text: The calendar then marked the seventeen hundred and seventy-fifth year of the Christian era. Celestial manifestations were granted to the British Isles in that auspicious age, much as they are today. A certain Mistress Westcott had just celebrated her twenty-fifth year of grace; her divine advent had been foretold by a visionary trooper of the Royal Dragoons, who declared that the very foundations of the capital were destined to be swallowed by the earth. Scarcely twelve years had elapsed since the phantom of Hen-Alley was finally silenced, having tapped out its spectral tidings in a manner identical to the ghosts of this present season—spirits who, it must be noted, possess a dismal lack of creative flair. Meanwhile, mundane dispatches of a more terrestrial nature had reached the Sovereign and his subjects from an assembly of colonists across the Atlantic; these documents, curiously enough, have exerted a far greater influence upon the destiny of mankind than any knockings ever produced by the brood of that aforementioned alleyway spook.
source_text: It was the year of Our Lord one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five. Spiritual revelations were conceded to England at that favoured period, as at this. Mrs. Southcott had recently attained her five-and-twentieth blessed birthday, of whom a prophetic private in the Life Guards had heralded the sublime appearance by announcing that arrangements were made for the swallowing up of London and Westminster. Even the Cock-lane ghost had been laid only a round dozen of years, after rapping out its messages, as the spirits of this very year last past (supernaturally deficient in originality) rapped out theirs. Mere messages in the earthly order of events had lately come to the English Crown and People, from a congress of British subjects in America: which, strange to relate, have proved more important to the human race than any communications yet received through any of the chickens of the Cock-lane brood.

text: In England, the state of public safety and civil order hardly justified any sense of national pride. Bold burglaries by armed gangs and highway robberies occurred nightly within the capital itself; families were openly advised not to leave for the country without first moving their furniture to secure warehouses for safekeeping. The man who was a respectable shopkeeper by day often became a masked bandit by night; if he were recognized and addressed by a peer while acting the part of “the Captain,” he would simply shoot his acquaintance through the head and gallop away. The mail coach was frequently ambushed by bands of robbers; in one instance, the guard killed three attackers before running out of ammunition, only to be slain by the remaining four, who then looted the mail in peace. Even the Lord Mayor of London, that most powerful of figures, was forced to surrender his purse at Turnham Green to a lone highwayman who robbed the great man in full view of his attendants. In the city's prisons, inmates fought pitched battles with their jailers, and the authorities responded by firing blunderbusses loaded with heavy shot into the fray. Thieves at Court drawing-rooms snipped diamond jewelry from the necks of the nobility, while in the slums of St. Giles, soldiers searching for contraband traded fire with the local mobs—and yet, no one found any of these events particularly remarkable. Amidst this turmoil, the hangman was perpetually in demand, though his efforts were as futile as they were frequent. He was forever busy: stringing up rows of varied criminals, executing a housebreaker on Saturday for a crime committed on Tuesday, branding the palms of convicts at Newgate, or burning seditious pamphlets at Westminster Hall. One day he took the life of a monstrous murderer, and the next, he hanged a pathetic thief who had stolen only sixpence from a farm boy.
source_text: In England, there was scarcely an amount of order and protection to justify much national boasting. Daring burglaries by armed men, and highway robberies, took place in the capital itself every night; families were publicly cautioned not to go out of town without removing their furniture to upholsterers’ warehouses for security; the highwayman in the dark was a City tradesman in the light, and, being recognised and challenged by his fellow-tradesman whom he stopped in his character of “the Captain,” gallantly shot him through the head and rode away; the mail was waylaid by seven robbers, and the guard shot three dead, and then got shot dead himself by the other four, “in consequence of the failure of his ammunition:” after which the mail was robbed in peace; that magnificent potentate, the Lord Mayor of London, was made to stand and deliver on Turnham Green, by one highwayman, who despoiled the illustrious creature in sight of all his retinue; prisoners in London gaols fought battles with their turnkeys, and the majesty of the law fired blunderbusses in among them, loaded with rounds of shot and ball; thieves snipped off diamond crosses from the necks of noble lords at Court drawing-rooms; musketeers went into St. Giles’s, to search for contraband goods, and the mob fired on the musketeers, and the musketeers fired on the mob, and nobody thought any of these occurrences much out of the common way. In the midst of them, the hangman, ever busy and ever worse than useless, was in constant requisition; now, stringing up long rows of miscellaneous criminals; now, hanging a housebreaker on Saturday who had been taken on Tuesday; now, burning people in the hand at Newgate by the dozen, and now burning pamphlets at the door of Westminster Hall; to-day, taking the life of an atrocious murderer, and to-morrow of a wretched pilferer who had robbed a farmer’s boy of sixpence.

text: A multitude of such occurrences, along with a thousand kindred trifles, unfolded within the borders of that notable year, seventeen hundred and seventy-five. Surrounded by these omens, even as the Hewer and the Reaper toiled unremarked, the two of the heavy jowls and that other couple of the dull and the radiant visages paraded with sufficient clamor, asserting their sovereign mandates with haughty confidence. Thus did the year seventeen hundred and seventy-five usher their High Mightinesses, and a sea of humble mortals—the figures of this narrative among them—down the winding tracks that awaited them.
source_text: All these things, and a thousand like them, came to pass in and close upon the dear old year one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five. Environed by them, while the Woodman and the Farmer worked unheeded, those two of the large jaws, and those other two of the plain and the fair faces, trod with stir enough, and carried their divine rights with a high hand. Thus did the year one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five conduct their Greatnesses, and myriads of small creatures--the creatures of this chronicle among the rest--along the roads that lay before them.

text: On a Friday night in late November, the road to Dover stretched ahead of the first individual involved in this narrative. For him, the path lay just beyond the Dover mail coach as it labored up Shooter’s Hill. He was trudging through the muck alongside the vehicle, as were the rest of the passengers. They weren't walking because they had any desire for exercise in such conditions, but because the combination of the steep incline, the heavy harness, the deep mud, and the coach itself was so burdensome. The horses had already stalled three times and had even tried to pull the carriage across the road in a rebellious attempt to head back toward Blackheath. However, the driver and the guard, through the combined force of reins and whip, had asserted their authority. They quelled a defiance that might have otherwise suggested these animals possessed the power of reason, forcing the team to surrender and return to their work.
source_text: It was the Dover road that lay, on a Friday night late in November, before the first of the persons with whom this history has business. The Dover road lay, as to him, beyond the Dover mail, as it lumbered up Shooter’s Hill. He walked up hill in the mire by the side of the mail, as the rest of the passengers did; not because they had the least relish for walking exercise, under the circumstances, but because the hill, and the harness, and the mud, and the mail, were all so heavy, that the horses had three times already come to a stop, besides once drawing the coach across the road, with the mutinous intent of taking it back to Blackheath. Reins and whip and coachman and guard, however, in combination, had read that article of war which forbade a purpose otherwise strongly in favour of the argument, that some brute animals are endued with Reason; and the team had capitulated and returned to their duty.

text: The traveler central to this account was mid-stride on the carriage step, just as the two other passengers behind him were preparing to board. He halted there, one foot inside the coach and the other out, while the men below remained on the road. All of them looked anxiously from the driver to the guard and back again, listening. The driver peered behind them and the guard did the same; even the spirited lead horse pricked its ears and looked back, its silent attention echoing the others' apprehension.
source_text: The passenger booked by this history, was on the coach-step, getting in; the two other passengers were close behind him, and about to follow. He remained on the step, half in the coach and half out of; they remained in the road below him. They all looked from the coachman to the guard, and from the guard to the coachman, and listened. The coachman looked back and the guard looked back, and even the emphatic leader pricked up his ears and looked back, without contradicting.

text: “Advance at a crawl, do you mark me? And if there be pistols in those scabbards of yours, keep your hands well clear of the leather. I am prone to a most sudden sort of error, and when I slip, the blunder is invariably made of lead. Now, step forward and let me take your measure.”
source_text: “Come on at a footpace! d’ye mind me? And if you’ve got holsters to that saddle o’ yourn, don’t let me see your hand go nigh ’em. For I’m a devil at a quick mistake, and when I make one it takes the form of Lead. So now let’s look at you.”

text: A horse and its rider emerged slowly through the swirling fog, pulling up beside the mail coach where the passenger stood waiting. The rider leaned down and, glancing up at the guard, passed a small, folded note to the passenger. His mount was winded, and both the horse and the man were caked in grime, spattered with mud from the animal’s hooves to the rider’s cap.
source_text: The figures of a horse and rider came slowly through the eddying mist, and came to the side of the mail, where the passenger stood. The rider stooped, and, casting up his eyes at the guard, handed the passenger a small folded paper. The rider’s horse was blown, and both horse and rider were covered with mud, from the hoofs of the horse to the hat of the man.

text: Having finished speaking, the traveler pulled open the carriage door and climbed inside. He received no assistance from the other occupants, who had already hurried to conceal their watches and money in their boots and were now collectively feigning sleep. This deception was born of no particular motive other than a desire to avoid the potential danger of any further interaction.
source_text: With those words the passenger opened the coach-door and got in; not at all assisted by his fellow-passengers, who had expeditiously secreted their watches and purses in their boots, and were now making a general pretence of being asleep. With no more definite purpose than to escape the hazard of originating any other kind of action.

text: Yet, while the bank lingered in his thoughts, and the coach remained a muddled reality—like the sensation of an ache suppressed by a heavy narcotic—a different current of consciousness flowed steadily through the night. He was traveling to pull someone from the depths of a grave.
source_text: But, though the bank was almost always with him, and though the coach (in a confused way, like the presence of pain under an opiate) was always with him, there was another current of impression that never ceased to run, all through the night. He was on his way to dig some one out of a grave.

text: Digging, digging, digging—until a restless stir from one of his companions prompted him to slide the window shut, hook his arm firmly through the leather strap, and study the two sleeping figures. Slowly, his concentration would slip, and they would once again dissolve into the twin images of the bank and the grave.
source_text: Dig--dig--dig--until an impatient movement from one of the two passengers would admonish him to pull up the window, draw his arm securely through the leathern strap, and speculate upon the two slumbering forms, until his mind lost its hold of them, and they again slid away into the bank and the grave.

text: With eyes and mouth wide in an expression of surprise, the waiter backed away from the table, transferring his napkin from his right arm to his left. He settled into an easy pose and stood there monitoring the patron as he ate and drank, as if from a high vantage point or a sentry post. It was the timeless tradition of servers throughout the ages.
source_text: Rounding his mouth and both his eyes, as he stepped backward from the table, the waiter shifted his napkin from his right arm to his left, dropped into a comfortable attitude, and stood surveying the guest while he ate and drank, as from an observatory or watchtower. According to the immemorial usage of waiters in all ages.

text: A bottle of fine claret after a meal does a man staring into the embers no real damage, aside from making him disinclined to work. Mr. Lorry had been resting for some time and had just poured his final glass with the total look of contentment only found in a healthy, older gentleman who has finished his wine, when the clatter of wheels echoed up the tight street and rumbled into the inn's courtyard.
source_text: A bottle of good claret after dinner does a digger in the red coals no harm, otherwise than as it has a tendency to throw him out of work. Mr. Lorry had been idle a long time, and had just poured out his last glassful of wine with as complete an appearance of satisfaction as is ever to be found in an elderly gentleman of a fresh complexion who has got to the end of a bottle, when a rattling of wheels came up the narrow street, and rumbled into the inn-yard.

text: “Sir, I am truly indebted to you; please accept my deepest gratitude. I was informed by the Bank that you would be the one to explain the specifics of this matter, and they cautioned me that the details would be quite extraordinary. I have done my utmost to steel myself for the news, and I am, quite naturally, very anxious to learn what it is.”
source_text: “Sir, I thank you indeed. I thank you very gratefully. It was told me by the Bank that the gentleman would explain to me the details of the business, and that I must prepare myself to find them of a surprising nature. I have done my best to prepare myself, and I naturally have a strong and eager interest to know what they are.”

text: “Miss Manette, I am a man of business. I have a professional obligation to fulfill. As you listen to what I have to say, please regard me as nothing more than a speaking machine—honestly, I am little else. If you will permit it, I would like to share with you the history of one of our firm's clients.”
source_text: “Miss Manette, I am a man of business. I have a business charge to acquit myself of. In your reception of it, don’t heed me any more than if I was a speaking machine--truly, I am not much else. I will, with your leave, relate to you, miss, the story of one of our customers.”

text: Mr. Lorry accepted the small, hesitant hand that reached out so trustingly, pressing it to his lips with a touch of formal ceremony. He then escorted the young woman back to her seat. Resting his left hand upon the chair’s back, he used his right to alternately stroke his chin, adjust the wig over his ears, or gesture to emphasize his points. He remained standing, looking down into her face as she sat looking up at him.
source_text: Mr. Lorry took the hesitating little hand that confidingly advanced to take his, and he put it with some ceremony to his lips. He then conducted the young lady straightway to her chair again, and, holding the chair-back with his left hand, and using his right by turns to rub his chin, pull his wig at the ears, or point what he said, stood looking down into her face while she sat looking up into his.

text: “You speak with composure, and indeed, you are composed. That is well.” (Yet his demeanor seemed less satisfied than his speech suggested.) “It is a professional affair. Look upon it as a matter of business—business that simply must be concluded. Now, if this doctor’s wife, though a woman of great fortitude and spirit, had endured such profound agony from this cause before her infant was born—”
source_text: “You speak collectedly, and you--_are_ collected. That’s good!” (Though his manner was less satisfied than his words.) “A matter of business. Regard it as a matter of business--business that must be done. Now if this doctor’s wife, though a lady of great courage and spirit, had suffered so intensely from this cause before her little child was born--”

text: It’s… it’s a professional matter. You’re rattling me, and I can hardly conduct business when I’m feeling so flustered. Let’s try to keep our wits about us. It would be quite a relief if you could tell me, for example, the total of nine times ninepence, or how many shillings make up twenty guineas. I would feel much more confident about your mental state if you could.
source_text: “A--a matter of business. You confuse me, and how can I transact business if I am confused? Let us be clear-headed. If you could kindly mention now, for instance, what nine times ninepence are, or how many shillings in twenty guineas, it would be so encouraging. I should be so much more at my ease about your state of mind.”

text: “But he has been—been recovered. He draws breath. Marred by time and trial, it is only too certain; a shattered vessel, it may well be; yet we shall harbor the fondest hopes. Nevertheless, he survives. Your father has been sheltered within the home of a former valet in Paris, and thither we are bound: I, to verify his features if I am able; you, to beckon him back to light, to tenderness, to labor, to stillness, and to grace.”
source_text: “But he has been--been found. He is alive. Greatly changed, it is too probable; almost a wreck, it is possible; though we will hope the best. Still, alive. Your father has been taken to the house of an old servant in Paris, and we are going there: I, to identify him if I can: you, to restore him to life, love, duty, rest, comfort.”

text: Mr. Lorry gently rubbed the hands that were gripping his arm. “Hush now, it’s alright! You see? You know the whole story now, both the good and the bad. You’re already on your way to that poor, mistreated man, and with a smooth crossing and a steady journey over land, you’ll be by his side very soon.”
source_text: Mr. Lorry quietly chafed the hands that held his arm. “There, there, there! See now, see now! The best and the worst are known to you, now. You are well on your way to the poor wronged gentleman, and, with a fair sea voyage, and a fair land journey, you will be soon at his dear side.”

text: The wine shop occupied a corner, appearing more respectable than most of its kind. Its owner, dressed in a yellow vest and green trousers, had stood outside watching the desperate scramble for the spilled wine. “It is no concern of mine,” he said, giving a final shrug of his shoulders. “The market people did this. Let them bring a replacement.”
source_text: The wine-shop was a corner shop, better than most others in its appearance and degree, and the master of the wine-shop had stood outside it, in a yellow waistcoat and green breeches, looking on at the struggle for the lost wine. “It’s not my affair,” said he, with a final shrug of the shoulders. “The people from the market did it. Let them bring another.”

text: The proprietor of the wine shop was a thirty-year-old with a thick, powerful neck and a soldierly bearing. He appeared to be a man of fiery disposition, for despite the biting cold, he wore no coat, merely carrying one draped over his shoulder. His sleeves were rolled up, leaving his tanned arms exposed to the elbows. He wore no hat, revealing only a head of short, tightly curled dark hair. He was dark-complexioned in every respect, with clear eyes set wide apart in a bold face. Though he seemed generally good-natured, there was a relentless quality about him; he was obviously a man of firm resolve and singular focus. He was not the sort of person one would wish to encounter charging through a narrow mountain pass with a chasm on either side, for he was utterly incapable of being turned aside.
source_text: This wine-shop keeper was a bull-necked, martial-looking man of thirty, and he should have been of a hot temperament, for, although it was a bitter day, he wore no coat, but carried one slung over his shoulder. His shirt-sleeves were rolled up, too, and his brown arms were bare to the elbows. Neither did he wear anything more on his head than his own crisply-curling short dark hair. He was a dark man altogether, with good eyes and a good bold breadth between them. Good-humoured looking on the whole, but implacable-looking, too; evidently a man of a strong resolution and a set purpose; a man not desirable to be met, rushing down a narrow pass with a gulf on either side, for nothing would turn the man.

text: The proprietor of the wine-shop allowed his gaze to roam the room until it fixed upon an aged man and a young woman ensconced in a corner. The rest of the assembly consisted of a pair at cards, two more at dominoes, and three others lingering by the bar to stretch their meager portions of spirits. As he returned to his place behind the counter, he perceived the elder man signal to his companion with a meaningful look that seemed to say, “He is the one we seek.”
source_text: The wine-shop keeper accordingly rolled his eyes about, until they rested upon an elderly gentleman and a young lady, who were seated in a corner. Other company were there: two playing cards, two playing dominoes, three standing by the counter lengthening out a short supply of wine. As he passed behind the counter, he took notice that the elderly gentleman said in a look to the young lady, “This is our man.”

text: The three patrons tipped their hats to Madame Defarge with a series of sweeping gestures. She acknowledged their salute with a slight tilt of her head and a quick, observant look. After a casual glance around the wine shop, she resumed her knitting with an air of profound serenity, becoming completely absorbed in her work.
source_text: The three customers pulled off their hats to Madame Defarge, with three flourishes. She acknowledged their homage by bending her head, and giving them a quick look. Then she glanced in a casual manner round the wine-shop, took up her knitting with great apparent calmness and repose of spirit, and became absorbed in it.

text: At length, they attained the landing’s summit, halting there for a third time. One final ascent remained, a flight more precipitous and constricted than the rest, which had to be conquered before the attic floor was won. The vintner, who had kept a steady lead while shadowing Mr. Vance’s every step—as if shrinking from the possibility of the young woman’s interrogation—wheeled about; probing the deep pockets of the cloak slung over his shoulder, he drew forth a key.
source_text: At last, the top of the staircase was gained, and they stopped for the third time. There was yet an upper staircase, of a steeper inclination and of contracted dimensions, to be ascended, before the garret story was reached. The keeper of the wine-shop, always going a little in advance, and always going on the side which Mr. Lorry took, as though he dreaded to be asked any question by the young lady, turned himself about here, and, carefully feeling in the pockets of the coat he carried over his shoulder, took out a key.

text: The conversation had been carried out in such a soft whisper that the young lady had not overheard a single word. Nevertheless, she was now shaking with such powerful emotion, and her expression revealed such intense worry and, most significantly, such overwhelming fear, that Mr. Lorry felt he must offer her a few words of comfort.
source_text: This dialogue had been held in so very low a whisper, that not a word of it had reached the young lady’s ears. But, by this time she trembled under such strong emotion, and her face expressed such deep anxiety, and, above all, such dread and terror, that Mr. Lorry felt it incumbent on him to speak a word or two of reassurance.

text: Motioning for them to stay back with a warning hand, he leaned down and peered through a narrow crack in the wall. He soon straightened up and rapped several times against the door, clearly intending only to create a disturbance. With that same purpose, he scraped the key across the wood a few times before fumbling it into the lock and turning it with as much heavy clatter as he could manage.
source_text: With an admonitory gesture to keep them back, he stooped, and looked in through the crevice in the wall. Soon raising his head again, he struck twice or thrice upon the door--evidently with no other object than to make a noise there. With the same intention, he drew the key across it, three or four times, before he put it clumsily into the lock, and turned it as heavily as he could.

text: The upper leaf of the divided door was pushed further inward and latched. A thick shaft of daylight spilled into the loft, illuminating the laborer as he sat with a half-finished boot resting on his knees, his hands gone still. His meager kit and various bits of hide were strewn across the floor and his low table. He wore a silver beard, unevenly shorn and brief, above a gaunt face dominated by strikingly brilliant eyes. The profound hollowness of his cheeks would have lent those eyes a cavernous size beneath his dark brows and disheveled white locks, regardless of their true scale; however, they were naturally vast, and now appeared hauntingly magnified. His sallow, shredded shirt hung open at the collar, revealing a torso shriveled and spent. He, along with his coarse linen smock, his sagging stockings, and the entirety of his wretched attire, had—during a protracted withdrawal from the open air—faded into such a singular, sickly shade of aged ivory that it was nearly impossible to distinguish the man from his rags.
source_text: The opened half-door was opened a little further, and secured at that angle for the time. A broad ray of light fell into the garret, and showed the workman with an unfinished shoe upon his lap, pausing in his labour. His few common tools and various scraps of leather were at his feet and on his bench. He had a white beard, raggedly cut, but not very long, a hollow face, and exceedingly bright eyes. The hollowness and thinness of his face would have caused them to look large, under his yet dark eyebrows and his confused white hair, though they had been really otherwise; but, they were naturally large, and looked unnaturally so. His yellow rags of shirt lay open at the throat, and showed his body to be withered and worn. He, and his old canvas frock, and his loose stockings, and all his poor tatters of clothes, had, in a long seclusion from direct light and air, faded down to such a dull uniformity of parchment-yellow, that it would have been hard to say which was which.

text: Her golden hair, which she kept in long ringlets, had been swept back in haste and now draped over her neck. Moving his hand forward by slow degrees, he gathered a strand to gaze upon it. In the middle of this act, his mind wandered, and with a heavy sigh, he returned to his shoemaking.
source_text: Her golden hair, which she wore in long curls, had been hurriedly pushed aside, and fell down over her neck. Advancing his hand by little and little, he took it up and looked at it. In the midst of the action he went astray, and, with another deep sigh, fell to work at his shoemaking.

text: After the attic had remained silent for some time, and his heavy breathing and trembling frame had finally surrendered to the stillness that inevitably follows a crisis—a symbol of the ultimate peace and silence that must eventually quiet the storm of human life—the others moved closer to lift the father and daughter from the floor. He had slowly collapsed onto the boards, lying in a state of utter, worn-out lethargy. She had lowered herself with him, cushioning his head upon her arm, while her loose hair fell over him like a curtain to shield him from the light.
source_text: When the quiet of the garret had been long undisturbed, and his heaving breast and shaken form had long yielded to the calm that must follow all storms--emblem to humanity, of the rest and silence into which the storm called Life must hush at last--they came forward to raise the father and daughter from the ground. He had gradually dropped to the floor, and lay there in a lethargy, worn out. She had nestled down with him, that his head might lie upon her arm; and her hair drooping over him curtained him from the light.

text: Neither Sterling nor Vane felt particularly inclined toward this departure, both harboring a secret wish that one of them might stay behind. Yet, the dual burdens of securing a post-chaise and horses, as well as validating their passports, allowed for no delay; with the afternoon light fading fast, they were compelled to partition the essential duties and hasten away to discharge them.
source_text: Both Mr. Lorry and Defarge were rather disinclined to this course, and in favour of one of them remaining. But, as there were not only carriage and horses to be seen to, but travelling papers; and as time pressed, for the day was drawing to an end, it came at last to their hastily dividing the business that was necessary to be done, and hurrying away to do it.

text: Each of these partners would have disinherited his son over the mere suggestion of modernizing Tellson’s. In this respect, the firm was much like the country, which often cast out its own citizens for recommending improvements to laws and customs that were clearly harmful, yet were considered all the more venerable for their very antiquity.
source_text: Any one of these partners would have disinherited his son on the question of rebuilding Tellson’s. In this respect the House was much on a par with the Country; which did very often disinherit its sons for suggesting improvements in laws and customs that had long been highly objectionable, but were only the more respectable.

text: So it was that Tellson’s had reached a state of absolute, triumphant inconvenience. Once you had wrenched open a door that resisted with a stubborn, rattling gasp, you tumbled down two steps into the bank, finding yourself in a cramped and wretched shop. At two small counters, ancient clerks caused your check to flutter as if caught in a draft while they scrutinized your signature by the filthiest windows imaginable. These panes were constantly pelted by the mud of Fleet Street and further obscured by thick iron bars and the oppressive shadow of Temple Bar. Should your affairs require an audience with "the House," you were confined to a sort of death-row cell in the rear; there, you might ponder a life of regret until the management arrived, hands in pockets, barely discernible in the murky twilight. Your cash was pulled from or deposited into decaying wooden drawers, which released clouds of fine rot into your lungs every time they were handled. The banknotes emitted a stale, damp scent, as though they were rapidly reverting to the shredded rags from which they were born. Any silver you left for safekeeping was stored near the local sewers, where the foul vapors stripped away its luster within forty-eight hours. Your legal deeds were shoved into makeshift vaults that had once been kitchens, where the humid air seemed to draw the very oil from the parchment. Lighter crates of family records were carried to an upstairs room—a hollow dining hall containing a massive table that never saw a feast. There, even in 1780, the tenderest letters from lost loves or small children were kept in the grim shadow of the severed heads displayed on Temple Bar, a display of mindless ferocity that would have seemed barbaric in the most savage of lands.
source_text: Thus it had come to pass, that Tellson’s was the triumphant perfection of inconvenience. After bursting open a door of idiotic obstinacy with a weak rattle in its throat, you fell into Tellson’s down two steps, and came to your senses in a miserable little shop, with two little counters, where the oldest of men made your cheque shake as if the wind rustled it, while they examined the signature by the dingiest of windows, which were always under a shower-bath of mud from Fleet-street, and which were made the dingier by their own iron bars proper, and the heavy shadow of Temple Bar. If your business necessitated your seeing “the House,” you were put into a species of Condemned Hold at the back, where you meditated on a misspent life, until the House came with its hands in its pockets, and you could hardly blink at it in the dismal twilight. Your money came out of, or went into, wormy old wooden drawers, particles of which flew up your nose and down your throat when they were opened and shut. Your bank-notes had a musty odour, as if they were fast decomposing into rags again. Your plate was stowed away among the neighbouring cesspools, and evil communications corrupted its good polish in a day or two. Your deeds got into extemporised strong-rooms made of kitchens and sculleries, and fretted all the fat out of their parchments into the banking-house air. Your lighter boxes of family papers went up-stairs into a Barmecide room, that always had a great dining-table in it and never had a dinner, and where, even in the year one thousand seven hundred and eighty, the first letters written to you by your old love, or by your little children, were but newly released from the horror of being ogled through the windows, by the heads exposed on Temple Bar with an insensate brutality and ferocity worthy of Abyssinia or Ashantee.

text: Truly, in that era, the noose was the favorite prescription of every trade, and nowhere more so than at Sterling’s. Since death is the final resolution for all earthly ills, why should the State not adopt it as its primary statute? Thus, the forger was sent to the scaffold; the passer of a fraudulent bill was sent to the scaffold; the clerk who broke a private seal was sent to the scaffold; the urchin who pilfered a few shillings was sent to the scaffold; the groom who rode off with a depositor’s stallion was sent to the scaffold; the minter of a leaden coin was sent to the scaffold; nearly every player of a discordant note in the vast anthem of Vice was sent to the scaffold. Not that such brutality ever checked the impulse to sin—indeed, the opposite was more likely true—but it effectively closed the file on every culprit, sparing the world further bother. In its time, Sterling’s, like the other great houses of the city, had claimed so many lives that, if the skulls of its victims had been piled before the entrance rather than secreted away, they would have eclipsed what meager daylight reached the ledgers in a grimly symbolic fashion.
source_text: But indeed, at that time, putting to death was a recipe much in vogue with all trades and professions, and not least of all with Tellson’s. Death is Nature’s remedy for all things, and why not Legislation’s? Accordingly, the forger was put to Death; the utterer of a bad note was put to Death; the unlawful opener of a letter was put to Death; the purloiner of forty shillings and sixpence was put to Death; the holder of a horse at Tellson’s door, who made off with it, was put to Death; the coiner of a bad shilling was put to Death; the sounders of three-fourths of the notes in the whole gamut of Crime, were put to Death. Not that it did the least good in the way of prevention--it might almost have been worth remarking that the fact was exactly the reverse--but, it cleared off (as to this world) the trouble of each particular case, and left nothing else connected with it to be looked after. Thus, Tellson’s, in its day, like greater places of business, its contemporaries, had taken so many lives, that, if the heads laid low before it had been ranged on Temple Bar instead of being privately disposed of, they would probably have excluded what little light the ground floor had, in a rather significant manner.

text: Stationed outside Tellson’s Bank—and never permitted within unless specifically summoned—was a general handyman who served as a porter, messenger, and the firm’s living emblem. He was a constant fixture during business hours, except when sent on a task, during which time his son stood in for him: a scruffy twelve-year-old boy who was the man’s exact likeness. It was understood that Tellson’s, with its dignified reserve, simply endured his presence. The bank had always permitted such a character to linger, and the passage of years had eventually swept this man into the position. His family name was Cruncher, and at his long-ago baptism in a Hounsditch church, where he had formally renounced the works of darkness through his sponsors, he had been christened Jerry.
source_text: Outside Tellson’s--never by any means in it, unless called in--was an odd-job-man, an occasional porter and messenger, who served as the live sign of the house. He was never absent during business hours, unless upon an errand, and then he was represented by his son: a grisly urchin of twelve, who was his express image. People understood that Tellson’s, in a stately way, tolerated the odd-job-man. The house had always tolerated some person in that capacity, and time and tide had drifted this person to the post. His surname was Cruncher, and on the youthful occasion of his renouncing by proxy the works of darkness, in the easterly parish church of Hounsditch, he had received the added appellation of Jerry.

text: Mr. Cruncher’s residence was located in a rather unpleasant district and consisted of only two rooms, even if one included a tiny storage space with a single window in the count. Nevertheless, the home was kept in excellent condition. Early as it was on that blustery March morning, the room where he remained in bed had already been scrubbed clean; a spotless white cloth was spread over the heavy wooden table, which was neatly set with cups and saucers for breakfast.
source_text: Mr. Cruncher’s apartments were not in a savoury neighbourhood, and were but two in number, even if a closet with a single pane of glass in it might be counted as one. But they were very decently kept. Early as it was, on the windy March morning, the room in which he lay abed was already scrubbed throughout; and between the cups and saucers arranged for breakfast, and the lumbering deal table, a very clean white cloth was spread.

text: Established at his post by a quarter to nine, well-prepared to doff his tricorn to the venerable figures entering Thorne & Co., Silas occupied his usual spot on that blustery March day. Beside him loitered Silas the Younger, except for those moments when he lunged past the archway to visit sharp physical and psychological torments upon any passing urchins diminutive enough to suit his cruel whims. The two, striking in their mutual likeness, watched the morning flow of Cheapside in quietude; with their heads pressed as closely together as their own pairs of eyes, they bore a remarkable resemblance to a couple of apes. This simian quality was further emphasized by the elder Silas’s penchant for chewing and ejecting fragments of straw, all while the boy’s bright, darting eyes remained as fixed upon his sire as they were upon the surrounding city life.
source_text: Encamped at a quarter before nine, in good time to touch his three-cornered hat to the oldest of men as they passed in to Tellson’s, Jerry took up his station on this windy March morning, with young Jerry standing by him, when not engaged in making forays through the Bar, to inflict bodily and mental injuries of an acute description on passing boys who were small enough for his amiable purpose. Father and son, extremely like each other, looking silently on at the morning traffic in Fleet-street, with their two heads as near to one another as the two eyes of each were, bore a considerable resemblance to a pair of monkeys. The resemblance was not lessened by the accidental circumstance, that the mature Jerry bit and spat out straw, while the twinkling eyes of the youthful Jerry were as restlessly watchful of him as of everything else in Fleet-street.

text: Navigating the sordid throng scattered across this grim arena with the practiced ease of someone used to moving unnoticed, the courier located his destination and passed the note through a small hatch in the door. In those days, spectators paid admission to watch the legal dramas at the Old Bailey much as they paid to gawk at the inmates of Bedlam—though the courtroom spectacle commanded a far higher price. Consequently, every entrance to the courthouse was strictly monitored, save for the societal pathways that led the accused to their ruin, which were perpetually left unbarred.
source_text: Making his way through the tainted crowd, dispersed up and down this hideous scene of action, with the skill of a man accustomed to make his way quietly, the messenger found out the door he sought, and handed in his letter through a trap in it. For, people then paid to see the play at the Old Bailey, just as they paid to see the play in Bedlam--only the former entertainment was much the dearer. Therefore, all the Old Bailey doors were well guarded--except, indeed, the social doors by which the criminals got there, and those were always left wide open.

text: Silas Thorne’s focus was pulled toward the usher, whom he observed threading a path toward Arthur Penhaligon with a scrap of parchment held aloft. Penhaligon sat at the long table surrounded by the fraternity of the bar; he was positioned close to the prisoner’s lead counsel, who was nearly submerged in a chaotic heap of documents, and almost directly across from a different bewigged gentleman. This second man, with his hands buried in his pockets, seemed—whenever Silas’s eyes fell upon him—to be utterly absorbed in contemplating the courtroom’s ceiling. Following a bout of rough coughing, a persistent rubbing of his stubbled chin, and a sequence of urgent gestures, Silas finally drew a look from Penhaligon. Having stood to search the room, the gentleman gave a faint, knowing nod before settling back into his chair.
source_text: Mr. Cruncher’s attention was here diverted to the door-keeper, whom he saw making his way to Mr. Lorry, with the note in his hand. Mr. Lorry sat at a table, among the gentlemen in wigs: not far from a wigged gentleman, the prisoner’s counsel, who had a great bundle of papers before him: and nearly opposite another wigged gentleman with his hands in his pockets, whose whole attention, when Mr. Cruncher looked at him then or afterwards, seemed to be concentrated on the ceiling of the court. After some gruff coughing and rubbing of his chin and signing with his hand, Jerry attracted the notice of Mr. Lorry, who had stood up to look for him, and who quietly nodded and sat down again.

text: The target of all this collective scrutiny and noise was a man of perhaps twenty-five years, well-proportioned and handsome, with a tanned complexion and dark eyes. He had the air of a young gentleman, though he was dressed simply in black or a deep shade of grey. His long, dark hair was tied back with a ribbon at the nape of his neck, a choice made for convenience rather than style. Just as a powerful emotion can manifest through any physical facade, the paleness brought on by his predicament was visible beneath his bronzed skin, revealing that the inner spirit is more potent than the effects of the sun. In all other respects, he remained remarkably calm; he offered a respectful bow to the Judge and stood in quiet composure.
source_text: The object of all this staring and blaring, was a young man of about five-and-twenty, well-grown and well-looking, with a sunburnt cheek and a dark eye. His condition was that of a young gentleman. He was plainly dressed in black, or very dark grey, and his hair, which was long and dark, was gathered in a ribbon at the back of his neck; more to be out of his way than for ornament. As an emotion of the mind will express itself through any covering of the body, so the paleness which his situation engendered came through the brown upon his cheek, showing the soul to be stronger than the sun. He was otherwise quite self-possessed, bowed to the Judge, and stood quiet.

text: It so happened that the movement turned his face toward the left side of the courtroom. Nearly level with his eyes, in that corner of the judge’s bench, sat two people who immediately caught his attention; he fixed his gaze upon them so instantly, and with such a visible transformation of his features, that everyone watching him turned to look at them as well.
source_text: It happened, that the action turned his face to that side of the court which was on his left. About on a level with his eyes, there sat, in that corner of the Judge’s bench, two persons upon whom his look immediately rested; so immediately, and so much to the changing of his aspect, that all the eyes that were turned upon him, turned to them.

text: The girl remained at her father’s side, one hand threaded through his arm while the other pressed against him. She huddled close, seeking refuge from the grim spectacle even as she felt a deep, aching sympathy for the man in the dock. Her countenance betrayed a singular, haunting mixture of fright and mercy, focused entirely upon the prisoner’s mortal danger. So stark and unaffected was her distress that even the most callous observers found themselves moved, and a quiet murmur began to circulate through the crowd: “Who are those people?”
source_text: His daughter had one of her hands drawn through his arm, as she sat by him, and the other pressed upon it. She had drawn close to him, in her dread of the scene, and in her pity for the prisoner. Her forehead had been strikingly expressive of an engrossing terror and compassion that saw nothing but the peril of the accused. This had been so very noticeable, so very powerfully and naturally shown, that starers who had had no pity for him were touched by her; and the whisper went about, “Who are they?”

text: Facing such sincere pity and youthful beauty was far more taxing for the prisoner than facing the entire assembly. Standing with her at the very brink of his grave, he found that the public’s prying gaze could no longer steady his nerves. His restless hand began to arrange the herbs before him into imaginary garden beds, and his struggle to regulate his breathing made his lips tremble as the color drained from them. Once more, the low drone of the crowd rose like the buzzing of great flies.
source_text: To be confronted with such pity, and such earnest youth and beauty, was far more trying to the accused than to be confronted with all the crowd. Standing, as it were, apart with her on the edge of his grave, not all the staring curiosity that looked on, could, for the moment, nerve him to remain quite still. His hurried right hand parcelled out the herbs before him into imaginary beds of flowers in a garden; and his efforts to control and steady his breathing shook the lips from which the colour rushed to his heart. The buzz of the great flies was loud again.

text: Whenever a central figure in a high-stakes drama displays a vivid facial expression, the captivated audience tends to mimic it instinctively. Her brow was furrowed with an agonizing intensity as she delivered her testimony, and during the quiet intervals while the Judge transcribed her words, she carefully studied the opposing attorneys to see how they were taking it. This same look of strain spread through every section of the gallery; indeed, the majority of faces in the room seemed to act as mirrors for the witness by the time the Judge looked up from his writing to scowl at that outrageous blasphemy concerning George Washington.
source_text: Any strongly marked expression of face on the part of a chief actor in a scene of great interest to whom many eyes are directed, will be unconsciously imitated by the spectators. Her forehead was painfully anxious and intent as she gave this evidence, and, in the pauses when she stopped for the Judge to write it down, watched its effect upon the counsel for and against. Among the lookers-on there was the same expression in all quarters of the court; insomuch, that a great majority of the foreheads there, might have been mirrors reflecting the witness, when the Judge looked up from his notes to glare at that tremendous heresy about George Washington.

text: Mr. Thorne, who had spent the duration of the trial contemplating the dark beams of the roof, did not shift his posture even as the room erupted in a flurry of movement. While his associate, Mr. Blackwood, frantically organized his briefs and leaned in to murmur with his assistants, casting anxious looks toward the jury box; while the gallery stirred with a renewed and restless curiosity; while even the Justice rose to pace the length of his bench with a frantic step that suggested a brewing agitation to those below; this solitary figure remained slumped in his chair. His frayed silk gown hung loosely about him, his unkempt wig rested precariously upon his head as though it had fallen there by accident, and with his hands thrust deep into his pockets, he kept his gaze fixed upon the ceiling as it had been since morning. A peculiar quality of dissolute abandonment in his manner gave him such a disreputable cast that it veiled the remarkable likeness he bore to the defendant—a resemblance that had been so vivid during the heat of the cross-examination. Observing him now, many in the crowd remarked to their neighbors that the two men seemed hardly to share a single feature. Old Barnaby, nudging the man beside him, muttered, “I’d bet a silver shilling that scoundrel never earns a farthing in fees. Doesn't look the sort to be trusted with the law, now does he?”
source_text: Mr. Carton, who had so long sat looking at the ceiling of the court, changed neither his place nor his attitude, even in this excitement. While his learned friend, Mr. Stryver, massing his papers before him, whispered with those who sat near, and from time to time glanced anxiously at the jury; while all the spectators moved more or less, and grouped themselves anew; while even my Lord himself arose from his seat, and slowly paced up and down his platform, not unattended by a suspicion in the minds of the audience that his state was feverish; this one man sat leaning back, with his torn gown half off him, his untidy wig put on just as it had happened to light on his head after its removal, his hands in his pockets, and his eyes on the ceiling as they had been all day. Something especially reckless in his demeanour, not only gave him a disreputable look, but so diminished the strong resemblance he undoubtedly bore to the prisoner (which his momentary earnestness, when they were compared together, had strengthened), that many of the lookers-on, taking note of him now, said to one another they would hardly have thought the two were so alike. Mr. Cruncher made the observation to his next neighbour, and added, “I’d hold half a guinea that _he_ don’t get no law-work to do. Don’t look like the sort of one to get any, do he?”

text: Nevertheless, Mr. Carton was more observant of the room's details than he seemed to be; for when Miss Manette’s head slumped against her father’s chest, he was the first to notice. He called out clearly, “Officer! Attend to that young woman. Help the gentleman get her out of here. Can’t you see she’s about to faint!”
source_text: Yet, this Mr. Carton took in more of the details of the scene than he appeared to take in; for now, when Miss Manette’s head dropped upon her father’s breast, he was the first to see it, and to say audibly: “Officer! look to that young lady. Help the gentleman to take her out. Don’t you see she will fall!”

text: Even in a much stronger light, one would have struggled to identify the hunched shoemaker of that Parisian attic in the present Dr. Manette, with his intelligent features and dignified posture. Yet, he remained a man who demanded a second glance, even before one heard the melancholic rhythm of his soft, somber voice or witnessed the fits of abstraction that periodically clouded his mind without warning. Although any mention of his long years of suffering could pull this darkness from his soul—as seen during the trial—it also emerged of its own accord. To an observer ignorant of his past, this sudden gloom was as inexplicable as if the physical shadow of the Bastille had been cast over him by the summer sun, even while the fortress itself remained hundreds of miles away.
source_text: It would have been difficult by a far brighter light, to recognise in Doctor Manette, intellectual of face and upright of bearing, the shoemaker of the garret in Paris. Yet, no one could have looked at him twice, without looking again: even though the opportunity of observation had not extended to the mournful cadence of his low grave voice, and to the abstraction that overclouded him fitfully, without any apparent reason. While one external cause, and that a reference to his long lingering agony, would always--as on the trial--evoke this condition from the depths of his soul, it was also in its nature to arise of itself, and to draw a gloom over him, as incomprehensible to those unacquainted with his story as if they had seen the shadow of the actual Bastille thrown upon him by a summer sun, when the substance was three hundred miles away.

text: None but his child possessed the capacity to dispel the dark melancholy that clouded his thoughts. She served as a luminous cord, binding him to a history preceding his anguish and a current existence outside of it; and the resonance of her speech, the radiance of her features, and the contact of her hand exerted a potent, restorative sway over him in nearly every instance. This was not universally true, as she remembered certain moments when her magic had faltered; however, these lapses were scarce and trivial, and she trusted they had reached their end.
source_text: Only his daughter had the power of charming this black brooding from his mind. She was the golden thread that united him to a Past beyond his misery, and to a Present beyond his misery: and the sound of her voice, the light of her face, the touch of her hand, had a strong beneficial influence with him almost always. Not absolutely always, for she could recall some occasions on which her power had failed; but they were few and slight, and she believed them over.

text: Still in his wig and robes, he faced his former client, squaring himself so sharply that he squeezed the blameless Mr. Lorry completely out of the group. “I am glad to have cleared your name, Mr. Darnay,” he said. “It was a shameful prosecution, truly wretched; but it was no less likely to win for being so.”
source_text: He still had his wig and gown on, and he said, squaring himself at his late client to that degree that he squeezed the innocent Mr. Lorry clean out of the group: “I am glad to have brought you off with honour, Mr. Darnay. It was an infamous prosecution, grossly infamous; but not the less likely to succeed on that account.”

text: For my part, my deepest wish is to forget that I even belong to this world. It offers me nothing of value—save for wine like this—just as I offer nothing to it. We are certainly not alike in that regard. Indeed, I am beginning to realize that we share very little in common at all, you and I.
source_text: “As to me, the greatest desire I have, is to forget that I belong to it. It has no good in it for me--except wine like this--nor I for it. So we are not much alike in that particular. Indeed, I begin to think we are not much alike in any particular, you and I.”

text: It was an era defined by heavy drinking, and most men indulged deeply. Social standards have shifted so drastically since then that a factual account of the volume of wine and punch a single man might consume in one night—all while maintaining his status as a refined gentleman—would strike a modern audience as a preposterous lie. The legal profession was by no means trailing other elite fields in its devotion to alcohol; nor was Mr. Stryver, who was already aggressively building a successful and wealthy practice. He was just as determined to outmatch his rivals in his drinking as he was in the grueling competition of the courtroom.
source_text: Those were drinking days, and most men drank hard. So very great is the improvement Time has brought about in such habits, that a moderate statement of the quantity of wine and punch which one man would swallow in the course of a night, without any detriment to his reputation as a perfect gentleman, would seem, in these days, a ridiculous exaggeration. The learned profession of the law was certainly not behind any other learned profession in its Bacchanalian propensities; neither was Mr. Stryver, already fast shouldering his way to a large and lucrative practice, behind his compeers in this particular, any more than in the drier parts of the legal race.

text: The junior aide, excluded from such private consultations, had already departed for the night, leaving the master of the chambers to answer the summons himself. He appeared in soft house-shoes and a voluminous morning-robe, his neck exposed for comfort where the cravat had been cast aside. His gaze bore that frantic, scorched intensity common to men of his indulgent habits—a look visible in the likenesses of every notorious rake since the days of the Hanging Judge, and one which persists, however much the painter’s brush seeks to mask it, across the galleries of every intemperate epoch.
source_text: The Stryver clerk, who never assisted at these conferences, had gone home, and the Stryver principal opened the door. He had his slippers on, and a loose bed-gown, and his throat was bare for his greater ease. He had that rather wild, strained, seared marking about the eyes, which may be observed in all free livers of his class, from the portrait of Jeffries downward, and which can be traced, under various disguises of Art, through the portraits of every Drinking Age.

text: A peaceful vessel belonged in such a quiet harbor, and indeed, one was found there. The Doctor resided on two floors of a grand, formal house where various trades supposedly operated by day, though they were rarely heard and were entirely deserted by night. In a rear building reached through a courtyard where a leafy plane-tree rustled, signs claimed that church organs were built, silver was engraved, and gold was hammered by a mysterious giant whose gilded arm thrust out from the wall of the front hall—as if he had beaten himself into precious metal and threatened a similar fate for every visitor. Little was ever seen or heard of these businesses, nor of the solitary tenant rumored to live upstairs, nor the obscure coach-trimmer said to keep an office below. Occasionally, a stray workman might cross the hall while pulling on his coat, a stranger might peer into the shadows, or a distant clink or heavy thump might echo from the courtyard. These, however, were only the exceptions that proved the rule: the sparrows in the plane-tree and the echoes in the street corner held undisputed sway from Sunday morning until Saturday night.
source_text: There ought to have been a tranquil bark in such an anchorage, and there was. The Doctor occupied two floors of a large stiff house, where several callings purported to be pursued by day, but whereof little was audible any day, and which was shunned by all of them at night. In a building at the back, attainable by a courtyard where a plane-tree rustled its green leaves, church-organs claimed to be made, and silver to be chased, and likewise gold to be beaten by some mysterious giant who had a golden arm starting out of the wall of the front hall--as if he had beaten himself precious, and menaced a similar conversion of all visitors. Very little of these trades, or of a lonely lodger rumoured to live up-stairs, or of a dim coach-trimming maker asserted to have a counting-house below, was ever heard or seen. Occasionally, a stray workman putting his coat on, traversed the hall, or a stranger peered about there, or a distant clink was heard across the courtyard, or a thump from the golden giant. These, however, were only the exceptions required to prove the rule that the sparrows in the plane-tree behind the house, and the echoes in the corner before it, had their own way from Sunday morning unto Saturday night.

text: “Since we find ourselves momentarily undisturbed, and are both individuals of a pragmatic bent,” he remarked, once they had returned to the parlor and settled into an easy rapport. “Pray, tell me—in his confidences with Clara, does the Master never touch upon those dark days of the loom, even now?”
source_text: “As we happen to be alone for the moment, and are both people of business,” he said, when they had got back to the drawing-room and had sat down there in friendly relations, “let me ask you--does the Doctor, in talking with Lucie, never refer to the shoemaking time, yet?”

text: The observation was more insightful than Mr. Lorry had anticipated. "You’re right," he admitted, "and it is a terrifying thing to consider. Still, Miss Pross, I can’t help but wonder if it is healthy for Doctor Manette to keep that trauma so tightly bottled up inside. In fact, it is this very concern and the anxiety it often brings me that has prompted me to speak so candidly with you now."
source_text: It was a profounder remark than Mr. Lorry had looked for. “True,” said he, “and fearful to reflect upon. Yet, a doubt lurks in my mind, Miss Pross, whether it is good for Doctor Manette to have that suppression always shut up within him. Indeed, it is this doubt and the uneasiness it sometimes causes me that has led me to our present confidence.”

text: The evening was so stiflingly humid that even with every door and window flung wide, the group felt overwhelmed by the heat. Once the tea things were cleared away, they all moved toward a window to gaze out into the thick, deepening dusk. Lucie remained close to her father, Darnay sat beside her, and Carton stood propped against the window frame. The long, white drapes billowed as sudden gusts from the approaching storm swept into the room, lifting the fabric toward the ceiling and waving it like the wings of ghosts.
source_text: The night was so very sultry, that although they sat with doors and windows open, they were overpowered by heat. When the tea-table was done with, they all moved to one of the windows, and looked out into the heavy twilight. Lucie sat by her father; Darnay sat beside her; Carton leaned against a window. The curtains were long and white, and some of the thunder-gusts that whirled into the corner, caught them up to the ceiling, and waved them like spectral wings.

text: Monseigneur, a powerful lord of the Court, was holding his fortnightly levee at his opulent Parisian mansion. He remained sequestered in his private suite, a sanctum sanctorum to the fawning masses gathered in the adjoining rooms. Monseigneur was about to consume his chocolate. While he was a man who could swallow much with little effort—and was even rumored by a few bitter critics to be busily devouring the wealth of France—his morning beverage could not reach his throat without the assistance of four robust attendants working alongside the Chef.
source_text: Monseigneur, one of the great lords in power at the Court, held his fortnightly reception in his grand hotel in Paris. Monseigneur was in his inner room, his sanctuary of sanctuaries, the Holiest of Holiests to the crowd of worshippers in the suite of rooms without. Monseigneur was about to take his chocolate. Monseigneur could swallow a great many things with ease, and was by some few sullen minds supposed to be rather rapidly swallowing France; but, his morning’s chocolate could not so much as get into the throat of Monseigneur, without the aid of four strong men besides the Cook.

text: Indeed. It required a full quartet of retainers, each shimmering in ostentatious finery, with their captain finding it impossible to breathe without at least a pair of gilded chronometers tucked into his waistcoat—mimicking the austere and holy example of the Duke—to escort the blessed cacao to the ducal mouth. One servant conveyed the silver pot into the sanctified air; a second whipped the dark liquid into a froth with a specialized rod; a third extended the ceremonial linen; and a fourth (the gentleman of the dual watches) decanted the steaming brew. It was quite out of the question for the Duke to relinquish a single one of these ministers and still preserve his lofty station before the eyes of the Almighty. The stain upon his lineage would have been indelible had his morning draft been so crudely served by only three men; to be attended by a mere two would have surely brought about his immediate demise.
source_text: Yes. It took four men, all four ablaze with gorgeous decoration, and the Chief of them unable to exist with fewer than two gold watches in his pocket, emulative of the noble and chaste fashion set by Monseigneur, to conduct the happy chocolate to Monseigneur’s lips. One lacquey carried the chocolate-pot into the sacred presence; a second, milled and frothed the chocolate with the little instrument he bore for that function; a third, presented the favoured napkin; a fourth (he of the two gold watches), poured the chocolate out. It was impossible for Monseigneur to dispense with one of these attendants on the chocolate and hold his high place under the admiring Heavens. Deep would have been the blot upon his escutcheon if his chocolate had been ignobly waited on by only three men; he must have died of two.

text: Monseigneur had attended a small late-night feast the evening before, where the theater and the opera were delightfully represented. He spent most of his nights at such gatherings, surrounded by captivating company. So refined and easily influenced was Monseigneur that these performers held far more sway over him regarding the tedious matters of state policy and national secrets than the actual needs of the French people. What a fortunate situation for France, as it always is for any nation so blessed—just as it was for England, for instance, during the lamented reign of that jovial Stuart king who sold his country out.
source_text: Monseigneur had been out at a little supper last night, where the Comedy and the Grand Opera were charmingly represented. Monseigneur was out at a little supper most nights, with fascinating company. So polite and so impressible was Monseigneur, that the Comedy and the Grand Opera had far more influence with him in the tiresome articles of state affairs and state secrets, than the needs of all France. A happy circumstance for France, as the like always is for all countries similarly favoured!--always was for England (by way of example), in the regretted days of the merry Stuart who sold it.

text: Having relieved his four servants of their duties and finished his chocolate, Monseigneur ordered the doors of his inner sanctum to be cast open and stepped out. Then followed such groveling, such desperate flattery, such spineless obedience and profound self-abasement! As for the prostration of body and spirit, no reverence was left for the Divine—which perhaps explains why those who worshipped Monseigneur so rarely bothered with Heaven.
source_text: Monseigneur having eased his four men of their burdens and taken his chocolate, caused the doors of the Holiest of Holiests to be thrown open, and issued forth. Then, what submission, what cringing and fawning, what servility, what abject humiliation! As to bowing down in body and spirit, nothing in that way was left for Heaven--which may have been one among other reasons why the worshippers of Monseigneur never troubled it.

text: The Count descended to the stone quadrangle, stepped into his waiting coach, and sped off. He had found few companions at the gala; he had stood in a pocket of isolation, and the Cardinal’s reception of him had been notably frigid. In light of this, it seemed to afford him a certain grim satisfaction to see the dregs of the city flee before his galloping horses, their lives held by a thread as they scrambled from his path. His driver steered with the ferocity of a cavalryman in mid-charge, yet this lethal speed provoked neither a frown nor a word of caution from the master. A murmur of protest was sometimes heard, even in that unhearing city and muted century, suggesting that in these tight thoroughfares lacking walkways, the haughty tradition of high-speed travel brutally crippled the poor. However, no one of consequence gave the matter a second thought, and in this, as in all things, the wretched populace was left to endure their misery as best they could.
source_text: Its owner went downstairs into the courtyard, got into his carriage, and drove away. Not many people had talked with him at the reception; he had stood in a little space apart, and Monseigneur might have been warmer in his manner. It appeared, under the circumstances, rather agreeable to him to see the common people dispersed before his horses, and often barely escaping from being run down. His man drove as if he were charging an enemy, and the furious recklessness of the man brought no check into the face, or to the lips, of the master. The complaint had sometimes made itself audible, even in that deaf city and dumb age, that, in the narrow streets without footways, the fierce patrician custom of hard driving endangered and maimed the mere vulgar in a barbarous manner. But, few cared enough for that to think of it a second time, and, in this matter, as in all others, the common wretches were left to get out of their difficulties as they could.

text: They were so thoroughly cowed, and their long history of enduring what such a man could do to them—both within the law and outside of it—was so bitter that not a voice, a hand, or even a glance was raised. Among the men, not a single one. But the woman who stood there knitting looked up steadily, staring the Marquis directly in the face. It was beneath his dignity to acknowledge her; his scornful eyes swept over her and the rest of the rats as he leaned back in his seat once more and gave the command: "Drive on!"
source_text: So cowed was their condition, and so long and hard their experience of what such a man could do to them, within the law and beyond it, that not a voice, or a hand, or even an eye was raised. Among the men, not one. But the woman who stood knitting looked up steadily, and looked the Marquis in the face. It was not for his dignity to notice it; his contemptuous eyes passed over her, and over all the other rats; and he leaned back in his seat again, and gave the word “Go on!”

text: The hamlet consisted of a single, miserable road, featuring a dilapidated brewery, a run-down tannery, a shabby tavern, a meager stable for coach horses, and a humble fountain—all the typical, destitute facilities. Its inhabitants were equally destitute. Everyone there was impoverished; many sat outside their homes, peeling a few stray onions for their evening meal, while others gathered at the fountain to wash wild greens, weeds, and whatever meager scraps of vegetation they could scavenge. Clear evidence of what caused their misery was everywhere; demands for state taxes, church tithes, and feudal dues, both local and national, were posted throughout the small settlement. These official decrees were so numerous and exacting that it seemed a miracle the entire village had not yet been swallowed whole.
source_text: The village had its one poor street, with its poor brewery, poor tannery, poor tavern, poor stable-yard for relays of post-horses, poor fountain, all usual poor appointments. It had its poor people too. All its people were poor, and many of them were sitting at their doors, shredding spare onions and the like for supper, while many were at the fountain, washing leaves, and grasses, and any such small yieldings of the earth that could be eaten. Expressive signs of what made them poor, were not wanting; the tax for the state, the tax for the church, the tax for the lord, tax local and tax general, were to be paid here and to be paid there, according to solemn inscription in the little village, until the wonder was, that there was any village left unswallowed.

text: The Marquis’s chateau was a massive, heavy structure, fronted by a wide stone courtyard and two curving staircases that joined at a terrace before the main entrance. It was an entirely stony affair, crowded with thick railings, urns, flowers, and the sculpted faces of men and lions, all carved from rock. It was as if a Gorgon’s head had looked over the building when it was finished two centuries ago, freezing it forever in its current state.
source_text: It was a heavy mass of building, that chateau of Monsieur the Marquis, with a large stone courtyard before it, and two stone sweeps of staircase meeting in a stone terrace before the principal door. A stony business altogether, with heavy stone balustrades, and stone urns, and stone flowers, and stone faces of men, and stone heads of lions, in all directions. As if the Gorgon’s head had surveyed it, when it was finished, two centuries ago.

text: “I would not use the word 'happily,' my dear nephew,” the uncle replied with refined courtesy; “I am not at all convinced of that. A sustained period of quiet reflection, bolstered by the benefits of isolation, might steer your fate far better than you are currently managing it. But there is no use in debating the point. As you say, I am at a disadvantage. Those modest tools of discipline, those subtle supports for the prestige of noble houses—those small favors that could so easily restrain you—can only be secured now through persistent pleading and political weight. They are sought by so many and granted to so few! It was not always so, but France has certainly changed for the worse. Our own recent forebears exercised the power of life and death over the local peasantry. From this very chamber, many such dogs were led out to the gallows; in the next room, my own bedchamber, one man was famously stabbed on the spot for some arrogant display of sensitivity regarding his daughter—*his* daughter! We have been stripped of many rights; a new philosophy has become the fashion; and upholding our social rank today might—I won't say it definitely would, but it might—bring us genuine trouble. It is all quite regrettable, quite regrettable indeed!”
source_text: “I would not say happily, my friend,” returned the uncle, with refined politeness; “I would not be sure of that. A good opportunity for consideration, surrounded by the advantages of solitude, might influence your destiny to far greater advantage than you influence it for yourself. But it is useless to discuss the question. I am, as you say, at a disadvantage. These little instruments of correction, these gentle aids to the power and honour of families, these slight favours that might so incommode you, are only to be obtained now by interest and importunity. They are sought by so many, and they are granted (comparatively) to so few! It used not to be so, but France in all such things is changed for the worse. Our not remote ancestors held the right of life and death over the surrounding vulgar. From this room, many such dogs have been taken out to be hanged; in the next room (my bedroom), one fellow, to our knowledge, was poniarded on the spot for professing some insolent delicacy respecting his daughter--_his_ daughter? We have lost many privileges; a new philosophy has become the mode; and the assertion of our station, in these days, might (I do not go so far as to say would, but might) cause us real inconvenience. All very bad, very bad!”

text: “And he has left me,” the nephew replied, “chained to a system I find horrific—accountable for its sins, yet powerless to alter them. I seek only to fulfill my mother’s dying wish and the silent plea in her final gaze, which begged me to show mercy and make amends. I am tormented by this search for help and authority, finding neither despite my desperate need.”
source_text: “And has left me,” answered the nephew, “bound to a system that is frightful to me, responsible for it, but powerless in it; seeking to execute the last request of my dear mother’s lips, and obey the last look of my dear mother’s eyes, which implored me to have mercy and to redress; and tortured by seeking assistance and power in vain.”

text: Should this inheritance ever fall to me, I will entrust it to someone more capable of gradually lifting the crushing weight that drags it down—if such a task is even possible. My hope is that the desperate people who are bound to this place, having been pushed to their absolute limits for so long, might find some relief in the next generation. But I want no part of it myself. A curse hangs over this estate and every inch of this land.
source_text: “If it ever becomes mine, it shall be put into some hands better qualified to free it slowly (if such a thing is possible) from the weight that drags it down, so that the miserable people who cannot leave it and who have been long wrung to the last point of endurance, may, in another generation, suffer less; but it is not for me. There is a curse on it, and on all this land.”

text: He was entirely aware of the impulses that governed his resolve. The season was at its height when, having lately returned to the capital from his scholarly post, he sought out the tranquil neighborhood of Bloomsbury, determined to find a chance to speak his heart to Dr. Saracen. As the evening light began to wane, he arrived with the full assurance that Clara had gone out with Mrs. Hedges.
source_text: That he had his reasons for this, he knew full well. It was again a summer day when, lately arrived in London from his college occupation, he turned into the quiet corner in Soho, bent on seeking an opportunity of opening his mind to Doctor Manette. It was the close of the summer day, and he knew Lucie to be out with Miss Pross.

text: He discovered the Doctor by a window, settled in an armchair with a book. The inner strength that had both helped him survive his past ordeals and heightened their intensity had slowly come back to him.
