He was now a truly spirited man, marked by a fixed sense of purpose, a powerful will, and a forceful way of moving through the world. This restored vitality sometimes made him slightly unpredictable or impulsive, just as his other returning abilities had at first; yet, such instances were never frequent and had become increasingly uncommon.
source_text: He found the Doctor reading in his arm-chair at a window. The energy which had at once supported him under his old sufferings and aggravated their sharpness, had been gradually restored to him. He was now a very energetic man indeed, with great firmness of purpose, strength of resolution, and vigour of action. In his recovered energy he was sometimes a little fitful and sudden, as he had at first been in the exercise of his other recovered faculties; but, this had never been frequently observable, and had grown more and more rare.

text: “I am well aware,” Julian began with quiet reverence, “how could I fail to perceive, Professor Halloway, having observed you both day after day, that the bond between you and Elena is of a nature so singular and so poignant, so shaped by the very trials that fostered it, that it finds few equals even in the profoundest love of father and daughter. I recognize—indeed, how could it be otherwise—that alongside the duty of the woman she has become, her heart carries toward you all the guileless trust and reliance of her earliest childhood. I understand that because she lacked a parent in her youth, she is now consecrated to you with a fervor belonging to her present self, yet entwined with the innocent attachment of those years when you were stolen from her. I know beyond any doubt that had you been raised from the dead to stand before her, you could hardly possess a more hallowed significance than that which she grants you every hour. I see that when she holds you, the hands of the babe, the girl, and the woman are all clasped about your neck. I know that in her devotion to you, she finds and loves her mother as a young bride, sees you as you were in your youth, and cherishes you through the darkness of your long ordeal and the grace of your return. I have felt this truth, by sun and by starlight, from the first moment I was welcomed into your home.”
source_text: “I know,” said Darnay, respectfully, “how can I fail to know, Doctor Manette, I who have seen you together from day to day, that between you and Miss Manette there is an affection so unusual, so touching, so belonging to the circumstances in which it has been nurtured, that it can have few parallels, even in the tenderness between a father and child. I know, Doctor Manette--how can I fail to know--that, mingled with the affection and duty of a daughter who has become a woman, there is, in her heart, towards you, all the love and reliance of infancy itself. I know that, as in her childhood she had no parent, so she is now devoted to you with all the constancy and fervour of her present years and character, united to the trustfulness and attachment of the early days in which you were lost to her. I know perfectly well that if you had been restored to her from the world beyond this life, you could hardly be invested, in her sight, with a more sacred character than that in which you are always with her. I know that when she is clinging to you, the hands of baby, girl, and woman, all in one, are round your neck. I know that in loving you she sees and loves her mother at her own age, sees and loves you at my age, loves her mother broken-hearted, loves you through your dreadful trial and in your blessed restoration. I have known this, night and day, since I have known you in your home.”

text: Do not imagine," said Darnay, hearing a note of sorrowful reproach in the older man’s voice, "that if my life were so arranged that marrying her would ever mean separating her from you, I would have dared to speak a word of my feelings. I would recognize such an act as not only futile, but deeply dishonorable. If I held any such intention, even as a remote possibility in the years ahead—if such a thought had ever been hidden in my heart or could ever find a place there—I would not be worthy to touch this hand I so respect.
source_text: “But, do not believe,” said Darnay, upon whose ear the mournful voice struck with a reproachful sound, “that if my fortune were so cast as that, being one day so happy as to make her my wife, I must at any time put any separation between her and you, I could or would breathe a word of what I now say. Besides that I should know it to be hopeless, I should know it to be a baseness. If I had any such possibility, even at a remote distance of years, harboured in my thoughts, and hidden in my heart--if it ever had been there--if it ever could be there--I could not now touch this honoured hand.”

text: The sensation of his touch remained on her father’s hand. Responding to the contact for a brief moment, though not without warmth, her father settled his hands upon the arms of his chair and looked up for the first time since the meeting had started. A visible struggle played across his features—a fight against that recurring expression that tended toward dark suspicion and terror.
source_text: His touch still lingered on her father’s hand. Answering the touch for a moment, but not coldly, her father rested his hands upon the arms of his chair, and looked up for the first time since the beginning of the conference. A struggle was evidently in his face; a struggle with that occasional look which had a tendency in it to dark doubt and dread.

text: My request is that if Miss Manette should ever come to you of her own accord and share a confidence like the one I have just entrusted to you, you will confirm what I have said and your belief in it. I hope you think well enough of me that you won't use your influence to turn her against me. I will say no more about how much I have at stake; this is my only request. As for the condition you have every right to demand in return, I will fulfill it immediately.
source_text: “It is, that if Miss Manette should bring to you at any time, on her own part, such a confidence as I have ventured to lay before you, you will bear testimony to what I have said, and to your belief in it. I hope you may be able to think so well of me, as to urge no influence against me. I say nothing more of my stake in this; this is what I ask. The condition on which I ask it, and which you have an undoubted right to require, I will observe immediately.”

text: “Now, Sydney, don’t let the name I am about to mention unsettle you,” Stryver remarked, preparing his companion with a show of forced warmth for the coming revelation. “I know you rarely mean half of what you say, and even if you were sincere, it would be of no consequence. I offer this brief introduction only because you once spoke of the young lady in such disparaging terms.”
source_text: “Now, don’t let my announcement of the name make you uncomfortable, Sydney,” said Mr. Stryver, preparing him with ostentatious friendliness for the disclosure he was about to make, “because I know you don’t mean half you say; and if you meant it all, it would be of no importance. I make this little preface, because you once mentioned the young lady to me in slighting terms.”

text: “You referred to the girl as a mere porcelain figurine. That girl is Miss Beaumont. If you were a man of any grace or subtle discernment in such matters, Elias, I might have taken umbrage at your choice of words; but you are not. You are utterly wanting in that faculty; I am therefore no more troubled by your speech than I would be by a blind man’s critique of my canvases, or a deaf man’s estimation of my sonatas.”
source_text: “You made mention of the young lady as a golden-haired doll. The young lady is Miss Manette. If you had been a fellow of any sensitiveness or delicacy of feeling in that kind of way, Sydney, I might have been a little resentful of your employing such a designation; but you are not. You want that sense altogether; therefore I am no more annoyed when I think of the expression, than I should be annoyed by a man’s opinion of a picture of mine, who had no eye for pictures: or of a piece of music of mine, who had no ear for music.”

text: “So there you have it, Syd,” Mr. Stryver remarked. “I’m not concerned with her wealth; she’s a delightful woman, and I’ve decided to follow my own inclinations. All things considered, I believe I can afford to indulge myself. In me, she will find a husband who is already quite prosperous, whose career is on a steep upward trajectory, and who possesses real prestige. It is a stroke of luck for her, certainly, but she is a person who deserves such good fortune. Are you surprised?”
source_text: “Now you know all about it, Syd,” said Mr. Stryver. “I don’t care about fortune: she is a charming creature, and I have made up my mind to please myself: on the whole, I think I can afford to please myself. She will have in me a man already pretty well off, and a rapidly rising man, and a man of some distinction: it is a piece of good fortune for her, but she is worthy of good fortune. Are you astonished?”

text: “Now, I advise you,” persisted Thorne, “to confront the matter directly. I have faced it after my own fashion; you must face it after yours. Take a wife. Secure someone to oversee your comfort. Pay no heed to your lack of relish for feminine company, nor your ignorance of their nature, nor your want of social grace. Seek someone out. Find a reputable woman with a modest inheritance—someone of the domestic or house-managing sort—and wed her as a hedge against hard times. That is precisely the arrangement for a man like you. Reflect upon it, Elias.”
source_text: “Now, let me recommend you,” pursued Stryver, “to look it in the face. I have looked it in the face, in my different way; look it in the face, you, in your different way. Marry. Provide somebody to take care of you. Never mind your having no enjoyment of women’s society, nor understanding of it, nor tact for it. Find out somebody. Find out some respectable woman with a little property--somebody in the landlady way, or lodging-letting way--and marry her, against a rainy day. That’s the kind of thing for _you_. Now think of it, Sydney.”

text: His route took him past Tellson’s, and since he was a client there and knew Mr. Lorry to be a close confidant of the Manettes, Stryver decided to stop in and share the glowing news of his Soho prospects. He pushed through the door, which gave a faint, raspy rattle, stumbled down the two steps, passed the pair of elderly clerks, and forced his way into the stale back office. There sat Mr. Lorry, working over massive ledgers ruled for accounting; the vertical iron bars on his window looked like they had been drawn for figures as well, as if everything under the sun were part of one great, calculated sum.
source_text: His way taking him past Tellson’s, and he both banking at Tellson’s and knowing Mr. Lorry as the intimate friend of the Manettes, it entered Mr. Stryver’s mind to enter the bank, and reveal to Mr. Lorry the brightness of the Soho horizon. So, he pushed open the door with the weak rattle in its throat, stumbled down the two steps, got past the two ancient cashiers, and shouldered himself into the musty back closet where Mr. Lorry sat at great books ruled for figures, with perpendicular iron bars to his window as if that were ruled for figures too, and everything under the clouds were a sum.

text: Stryver’s most striking quality was that he invariably seemed too massive for any room he occupied. He appeared so grossly oversized for Tellson’s that the elderly clerks in its furthest reaches looked up with reproachful glares, as if his mere presence were crushing them against the masonry. Even the bank itself, personified as a dignified figure reading the news in the distance, seemed to frown in irritation, as though Stryver’s aggressive head had been thrust directly into its respectable midsection.
source_text: It was Stryver’s grand peculiarity that he always seemed too big for any place, or space. He was so much too big for Tellson’s, that old clerks in distant corners looked up with looks of remonstrance, as though he squeezed them against the wall. The House itself, magnificently reading the paper quite in the far-off perspective, lowered displeased, as if the Stryver head had been butted into its responsible waistcoat.

text: “That is not quite what I meant. I wish to make it clear, Mr. Stryver,” said Mr. Lorry, his face flushing, “that I will not tolerate a single disparaging remark about that young lady from anyone. If I were to encounter a man—and I truly hope I never shall—whose sensibilities were so crude and whose nature so arrogant that he could not refrain from insulting her at this desk, not even the decorum of Tellson’s would stop me from telling him exactly what I think of him.”
source_text: “Not exactly so. I mean to tell you, Mr. Stryver,” said Mr. Lorry, reddening, “that I will hear no disrespectful word of that young lady from any lips; and that if I knew any man--which I hope I do not--whose taste was so coarse, and whose temper was so overbearing, that he could not restrain himself from speaking disrespectfully of that young lady at this desk, not even Tellson’s should prevent my giving him a piece of my mind.”

text: “Understood. Thank you. Well, Mr. Stryver, here is what I was getting at: it could be quite distressing for you to find you’ve misread the situation, just as it would be deeply uncomfortable for Dr. Manette and his daughter to have to reject you outright. You are aware of the privileged and happy position I hold within their household. If you agree—without me formally representing you or binding you to any course of action—I will take it upon myself to re-examine my perspective through some fresh, careful observation. If you are then unhappy with my conclusion, you are perfectly free to test the matter yourself. If, however, you find my judgment sound, and it remains unchanged, it might save everyone from an ordeal that is better avoided. What is your opinion?”
source_text: “Granted. Thank you. Well, Mr. Stryver, I was about to say:--it might be painful to you to find yourself mistaken, it might be painful to Doctor Manette to have the task of being explicit with you, it might be very painful to Miss Manette to have the task of being explicit with you. You know the terms upon which I have the honour and happiness to stand with the family. If you please, committing you in no way, representing you in no way, I will undertake to correct my advice by the exercise of a little new observation and judgment expressly brought to bear upon it. If you should then be dissatisfied with it, you can but test its soundness for yourself; if, on the other hand, you should be satisfied with it, and it should be what it now is, it may spare all sides what is best spared. What do you say?”

text: One midsummer afternoon, after Mr. Beaumont had informed his protégé that he had “abandoned his thoughts of domesticity” and taken his arrogance to the hills of Sussex, the fragrance of street-side blossoms still carried a fleeting grace for the fallen, a phantom of health for the infirm, and a whisper of lost youth for the aged. Through these urban canyons, Elias’s footsteps continued to wander. From being aimless and drifting, his stride became sharpened by a sudden resolve, and in the pursuit of that inner command, his path led him directly to the physician’s threshold.
source_text: On a day in August, when Mr. Stryver (after notifying to his jackal that “he had thought better of that marrying matter”) had carried his delicacy into Devonshire, and when the sight and scent of flowers in the City streets had some waifs of goodness in them for the worst, of health for the sickliest, and of youth for the oldest, Sydney’s feet still trod those stones. From being irresolute and purposeless, his feet became animated by an intention, and, in the working out of that intention, they took him to the Doctor’s door.

text: “Had it been within the realm of possibility, Miss Manette, for you to return the affection of the man who stands before you—squandered, dissolute, and ruined as you know him to be—he would be certain, even in his greatest happiness, that he could only bring you to wretchedness. He would burden you with sorrow and regret, staining your life and dragging you down into his own disgrace. I understand perfectly that you can feel no love for me; I do not seek it; indeed, I am grateful that such a thing can never be.”
source_text: “If it had been possible, Miss Manette, that you could have returned the love of the man you see before yourself--flung away, wasted, drunken, poor creature of misuse as you know him to be--he would have been conscious this day and hour, in spite of his happiness, that he would bring you to misery, bring you to sorrow and repentance, blight you, disgrace you, pull you down with him. I know very well that you can have no tenderness for me; I ask for none; I am even thankful that it cannot be.”

text: To no one else. No, Miss Manette, to nobody at all. If you will just bear with me for a moment longer, you will have done everything for me that can ever be done. I want you to know that you have been the final vision of my soul. Even in my wretchedness, I have not fallen so low that the sight of you with your father—and the home you have made here—hasn't awakened long-buried echoes I believed were dead within me. Since meeting you, I have been haunted by a regret I thought would never bother me again, and I’ve heard the call of old voices urging me upward, spirits I assumed were silenced forever. I have entertained vague notions of trying once more, of starting over, of casting off my laziness and vices to resume the struggle I’d long since surrendered. It was a dream, nothing more—a dream that vanishes and leaves the sleeper exactly where he first fell—but I want you to know that it was you who inspired it.
source_text: “To none. No, Miss Manette, to none. If you will hear me through a very little more, all you can ever do for me is done. I wish you to know that you have been the last dream of my soul. In my degradation I have not been so degraded but that the sight of you with your father, and of this home made such a home by you, has stirred old shadows that I thought had died out of me. Since I knew you, I have been troubled by a remorse that I thought would never reproach me again, and have heard whispers from old voices impelling me upward, that I thought were silent for ever. I have had unformed ideas of striving afresh, beginning anew, shaking off sloth and sensuality, and fighting out the abandoned fight. A dream, all a dream, that ends in nothing, and leaves the sleeper where he lay down, but I wish you to know that you inspired it.”

text: Don't try to persuade me otherwise, Miss Manette. I’ve tested my own character, and I know the truth. I see I am upsetting you, so I will finish quickly. When I remember this day, may I believe that the final secret of my life was entrusted to your gentle heart, and that it rests there in private, never to be shared with another soul?
source_text: “Entreat me to believe it no more, Miss Manette. I have proved myself, and I know better. I distress you; I draw fast to an end. Will you let me believe, when I recall this day, that the last confidence of my life was reposed in your pure and innocent breast, and that it lies there alone, and will be shared by no one?”

text: “What do you think you’re doing? What is all this shouting for? What are you trying to say to your own father, you little rascal? This boy is becoming quite a handful for me!” Mr. Cruncher said, looking him over. “Him and his yelling! Don’t let me hear another sound from you, or you’ll feel the weight of my hand. Do you hear me?”
source_text: “What d’ye mean? What are you hooroaring at? What do you want to conwey to your own father, you young Rip? This boy is a getting too many for _me_!” said Mr. Cruncher, surveying him. “Him and his hooroars! Don’t let me hear no more of you, or you shall feel some more of me. D’ye hear?”

text: The mob gleefully ripped these items to shreds and scattered the remnants, while local merchants scrambled to shutter their shops; in those days, a rioting crowd was a lawless, terrifying beast that knew no limits. The rabble had already reached the point of prying open the hearse to seize the coffin when a cleverer individual proposed a new plan: they should instead escort the body to its final resting place with a rowdy, festive parade. Since the group was desperate for any sort of direction, this idea was met with roaring approval. Immediately, the mourning coach was stuffed with eight people inside and a dozen more clinging to the exterior, while as many rioters as could possibly manage to hold on swarmed the roof of the hearse. Among the first to volunteer was Jerry Cruncher, who retreated to the far corner of the carriage, carefully hiding his spiky hair to ensure no one from Tellson’s Bank would recognize him.
source_text: These, the people tore to pieces and scattered far and wide with great enjoyment, while the tradesmen hurriedly shut up their shops; for a crowd in those times stopped at nothing, and was a monster much dreaded. They had already got the length of opening the hearse to take the coffin out, when some brighter genius proposed instead, its being escorted to its destination amidst general rejoicing. Practical suggestions being much needed, this suggestion, too, was received with acclamation, and the coach was immediately filled with eight inside and a dozen out, while as many people got on the roof of the hearse as could by any exercise of ingenuity stick upon it. Among the first of these volunteers was Jerry Cruncher himself, who modestly concealed his spiky head from the observation of Tellson’s, in the further corner of the mourning coach.

text: Throughout the dwindling hours, Silas Thorne maintained a sharp-eyed sentry over Martha, trapping her in a thicket of morose dialogue to prevent any quiet communion that might work against his favor. To this end, he pressed his son into the same wearying service, badgering the wretched woman with a catalog of old grudges rather than permitting her a single moment’s retreat into her own mind. A saint could have paid no higher tribute to the strength of a righteous plea than Silas did through his profound terror of her devotion. He resembled nothing so much as a man who publicly derides the supernatural, yet finds himself undone by the mere whisper of a haunting.
source_text: He devoted himself during the remainder of the evening to keeping a most vigilant watch on Mrs. Cruncher, and sullenly holding her in conversation that she might be prevented from meditating any petitions to his disadvantage. With this view, he urged his son to hold her in conversation also, and led the unfortunate woman a hard life by dwelling on any causes of complaint he could bring against her, rather than he would leave her for a moment to her own reflections. The devoutest person could have rendered no greater homage to the efficacy of an honest prayer than he did in this distrust of his wife. It was as if a professed unbeliever in ghosts should be frightened by a ghost story.

text: “Listen to me!” barked Mr. Cruncher. “I’ll have no nonsense tomorrow! If I, as a respectable businessman, manage to bring home a roast or two, I don’t want to see you refusing it in favor of plain bread. If I can afford to provide a little beer, don't you go swearing off it for water. When in Rome, you must do as the Romans do. Rome will be a very nasty customer for you if you don't—and make no mistake, I am your Rome.”
source_text: “And mind you!” said Mr. Cruncher. “No games to-morrow! If I, as a honest tradesman, succeed in providing a jinte of meat or two, none of your not touching of it, and sticking to bread. If I, as a honest tradesman, am able to provide a little beer, none of your declaring on water. When you go to Rome, do as Rome does. Rome will be a ugly customer to you, if you don’t. _I_’m your Rome, you know.”

text: “You’re sabotaging our very bread and butter! I can’t say how meager you’ll make our provisions with these kneeling antics and your heartless behavior. Look at your son: he’s yours, isn't he? He’s as thin as a rail. Do you call yourself a mother and not realize that a mother’s first duty is to keep her boy stuffed full?”
source_text: “With your flying into the face of your own wittles and drink! I don’t know how scarce you mayn’t make the wittles and drink here, by your flopping tricks and your unfeeling conduct. Look at your boy: he _is_ your’n, ain’t he? He’s as thin as a lath. Do you call yourself a mother, and not know that a mother’s first duty is to blow her boy out?”

text: Young Jerry now neared the gate, holding his breath as he moved. Squatting low in a corner to peer inside, he watched the three "fishermen" as they waded through the thick, wild grass. All around them, the headstones in the vast cemetery looked like white specters standing guard, while the church tower loomed overhead like the ghost of a colossal giant. The men did not crawl far before they came to a halt and stood up straight. Then, they began to fish.
source_text: It was now Young Jerry’s turn to approach the gate: which he did, holding his breath. Crouching down again in a corner there, and looking in, he made out the three fishermen creeping through some rank grass! and all the gravestones in the churchyard--it was a large churchyard that they were in--looking on like ghosts in white, while the church tower itself looked on like the ghost of a monstrous giant. They did not creep far, before they stopped and stood upright. And then they began to fish.

text: It should be enough for you," Mr. Cruncher snapped back, "to be the wife of a hard-working man, without cluttering your head with questions about when I’m working or when I’m not. A wife who truly honors and obeys would stay out of her husband’s business entirely. You call yourself a woman of faith? If you’re what passes for religious, I’d much rather have a sinner! You have no more natural sense of duty than the bottom of the Thames has for a pier post, and just like that riverbed, it’ll have to be hammered into you.
source_text: “It’s enough for you,” retorted Mr. Cruncher, “to be the wife of a honest tradesman, and not to occupy your female mind with calculations when he took to his trade or when he didn’t. A honouring and obeying wife would let his trade alone altogether. Call yourself a religious woman? If you’re a religious woman, give me a irreligious one! You have no more nat’ral sense of duty than the bed of this here Thames river has of a pile, and similarly it must be knocked into you.”

text: The hushed dispute ended with the laborer shucking off his mud-caked boots and stretching himself out upon the floorboards. After stealing a nervous glance at the man lying there, his calloused, rust-stained hands serving as a makeshift pillow, the boy settled back down and drifted back into sleep.
source_text: The altercation was conducted in a low tone of voice, and terminated in the honest tradesman’s kicking off his clay-soiled boots, and lying down at his length on the floor. After taking a timid peep at him lying on his back, with his rusty hands under his head for a pillow, his son lay down too, and fell asleep again.

text: The business of drinking had begun ahead of its accustomed hour within the taproom of Citizen Morel. As early as the stroke of six, pallid visages squinting through the iron-grated casements had observed others within, hunched over their measures of spirits. Morel dispensed a remarkably weak vintage at the best of seasons, but it appeared a singularly watery draft that he poured at this dawn. It was a tart liquid, besides, or one fast turning to vinegar, for its effect upon the temper of the drinkers was to render them sullen. No joyous spark of merriment flickered from the pressed skins of Morel’s grapes; rather, a low-burning ember that glowed in the shadows remained secreted in the bitter dregs.
source_text: There had been earlier drinking than usual in the wine-shop of Monsieur Defarge. As early as six o’clock in the morning, sallow faces peeping through its barred windows had descried other faces within, bending over measures of wine. Monsieur Defarge sold a very thin wine at the best of times, but it would seem to have been an unusually thin wine that he sold at this time. A sour wine, moreover, or a souring, for its influence on the mood of those who drank it was to make them gloomy. No vivacious Bacchanalian flame leaped out of the pressed grape of Monsieur Defarge: but, a smouldering fire that burnt in the dark, lay hidden in the dregs of it.

text: It was now the third consecutive morning that early crowds had gathered at Monsieur Defarge’s wine shop. The pattern had started on Monday, and now Wednesday had arrived. There was actually more quiet simmering than drinking taking place; from the moment the doors opened, many men lingered and whispered, drifting through the shadows despite not having a single coin to pay for a glass. Nevertheless, they remained as captivated by the atmosphere as if they could afford entire casks. They moved restlessly from bench to bench and from corner to corner, their hungry expressions revealing that they were devouring rumors in place of wine.
source_text: This had been the third morning in succession, on which there had been early drinking at the wine-shop of Monsieur Defarge. It had begun on Monday, and here was Wednesday come. There had been more of early brooding than drinking; for, many men had listened and whispered and slunk about there from the time of the opening of the door, who could not have laid a piece of money on the counter to save their souls. These were to the full as interested in the place, however, as if they could have commanded whole barrels of wine; and they glided from seat to seat, and from corner to corner, swallowing talk in lieu of drink, with greedy looks.

text: An air of stalled anticipation and a general vacancy of spirit might have been detected by the informants who peered through the tavern windows, for their eyes searched every quarter of the city, from the monarch’s gilded halls to the dungeon’s damp stone. Hands at cards grew heavy and still, those at the tiles distractedly piled them into fragile spires, and patrons sketched idle shapes in the dregs of spilled spirits upon the scarred timber. Mistress Thorne herself traced the embroidery of her bodice with a small bodkin, her senses attuned to a rhythm and a specter that remained yet unseen and unheard on the far horizon.
source_text: A suspended interest and a prevalent absence of mind, were perhaps observed by the spies who looked in at the wine-shop, as they looked in at every place, high and low, from the king’s palace to the criminal’s gaol. Games at cards languished, players at dominoes musingly built towers with them, drinkers drew figures on the tables with spilt drops of wine, Madame Defarge herself picked out the pattern on her sleeve with her toothpick, and saw and heard something inaudible and invisible a long way off.

text: Tumbling down the incline like maniacs in a desperate chase, the wretch collapses. His captors howl with glee as they wrench him upright. His countenance is a mask of grit and scarlet, yet his shackled hands cannot reach it; seeing his struggle, they jeer anew. They haul him into the hamlet, where the populace swarms to watch; they parade him past the grinding stones of the mill to the fortress gates. There, beneath the black cloak of night, the village sees the portal yawn open and devour him—precisely so!
source_text: “As they descend the hill like madmen running a race, he falls. They laugh and pick him up again. His face is bleeding and covered with dust, but he cannot touch it; thereupon they laugh again. They bring him into the village; all the village runs to look; they take him past the mill, and up to the prison; all the village sees the prison gate open in the darkness of the night, and swallow him--like this!”

text: Defarge and the three men exchanged grim, shadowed glances. Their expressions were heavy with suppressed rage and a thirst for vengeance as they listened to the countryman’s report; despite their secrecy, they carried themselves with a cold, commanding air. They resembled a crude tribunal: Jacques One and Two sat upon the old pallet-bed, chins propped in their hands and eyes fixed on the road-mender; Jacques Three, just as focused, knelt behind them, his restless fingers constantly tracing the nervous, twitching lines around his mouth and nose. Defarge stood between the listeners and the narrator, whom he had placed in the light of the window, his gaze shifting back and forth from the speaker to his comrades.
source_text: Defarge and the three glanced darkly at one another. The looks of all of them were dark, repressed, and revengeful, as they listened to the countryman’s story; the manner of all of them, while it was secret, was authoritative too. They had the air of a rough tribunal; Jacques One and Two sitting on the old pallet-bed, each with his chin resting on his hand, and his eyes intent on the road-mender; Jacques Three, equally intent, on one knee behind them, with his agitated hand always gliding over the network of fine nerves about his mouth and nose; Defarge standing between them and the narrator, whom he had stationed in the light of the window, by turns looking from him to them, and from them to him.

text: It’s a nightmare, gentlemen. How are the women and children supposed to fetch water now? Who could possibly gather to talk in the evening beneath such a shadow? Beneath it, did I say? When I departed on Monday evening just as the sun was sinking, I looked back from the hilltop and saw that shadow stretching over the church, the mill, and the prison—it seemed to crawl across the entire landscape, sirs, right to the edge of the world!
source_text: “It is frightful, messieurs. How can the women and the children draw water! Who can gossip of an evening, under that shadow! Under it, have I said? When I left the village, Monday evening as the sun was going to bed, and looked back from the hill, the shadow struck across the church, across the mill, across the prison--seemed to strike across the earth, messieurs, to where the sky rests upon it!”

text: These fools are utterly blind. Though they hold your very life in contempt and would sooner extinguish it, along with a hundred others like you, than lose one of their prized horses or dogs, they only know what your presence deigns to show them. Let them remain deceived a while longer; no amount of trickery could ever be too much for them.
source_text: “These fools know nothing. While they despise your breath, and would stop it for ever and ever, in you or in a hundred like you rather than in one of their own horses or dogs, they only know what your breath tells them. Let it deceive them, then, a little longer; it cannot deceive them too much.”

text: The mansion and the shack, the stone mask and the hanging corpse, the crimson stain upon the floor and the clear water of the village spring—vast expanses of earth, a whole province, even the entirety of France—lay beneath the midnight sky, reduced to a mere thread of light. In this way, an entire world, with its grandest heights and its lowest depths, is held within the glimmer of a star. And just as human science can refract a light ray to study its parts, so might superior minds read in our planet’s faint glow every thought and action, every sin and every virtue, of every soul living upon it.
source_text: Chateau and hut, stone face and dangling figure, the red stain on the stone floor, and the pure water in the village well--thousands of acres of land--a whole province of France--all France itself--lay under the night sky, concentrated into a faint hair-breadth line. So does a whole world, with all its greatnesses and littlenesses, lie in a twinkling star. And as mere human knowledge can split a ray of light and analyse the manner of its composition, so, sublimer intelligences may read in the feeble shining of this earth of ours, every thought and act, every vice and virtue, of every responsible creature on it.

text: My courageous wife," Defarge replied, standing before her with his head slightly inclined and his hands joined behind his back, like a submissive student attending to a teacher. "I am not questioning any of this. Yet it has been a long time in coming, and it is possible—as you know well, my dear—that it may not arrive within our own lifetimes.
source_text: “My brave wife,” returned Defarge, standing before her with his head a little bent, and his hands clasped at his back, like a docile and attentive pupil before his catechist, “I do not question all this. But it has lasted a long time, and it is possible--you know well, my wife, it is possible--that it may not come, during our lives.”

text: She spoke the salutation aloud, then added inwardly as she took up her stitching once more: “Ha! A fine morning; age near forty, height about five feet nine, hair of a raven hue, a countenance generally pleasing but cast in shadow, skin of a sallow tint, eyes like dark coal, a thin and elongated face, with a hooked nose that lacks symmetry, leaning markedly toward the left cheek to lend him a treacherous air! A fine morning to the whole lot of you!”
source_text: She said it aloud, but added to herself, as she resumed her knitting: “Hah! Good day, age about forty, height about five feet nine, black hair, generally rather handsome visage, complexion dark, eyes dark, thin, long and sallow face, aquiline nose but not straight, having a peculiar inclination towards the left cheek which imparts a sinister expression! Good day, one and all!”

text: Such high praise for the brandy was unprecedented, and Madame Defarge was too familiar with its cheap origins to take the comment seriously. She simply noted that the cognac was flattered before picking up her knitting again. The stranger watched her busy hands for a moment, seizing the chance to survey the shop’s interior.
source_text: It was the first time it had ever been so complimented, and Madame Defarge knew enough of its antecedents to know better. She said, however, that the cognac was flattered, and took up her knitting. The visitor watched her fingers for a few moments, and took the opportunity of observing the place in general.

text: It was a curious thing, but the inhabitants of Saint Antoine appeared to have a collective distaste for the rose in Madame Defarge’s head-dress. Two men had come in at different times, clearly intending to order wine, but the moment they saw that unusual addition, they paused. They made a show of scanning the room for a non-existent friend before retreating. Of those who had already been sitting there when the visitor arrived, not a single soul remained; they had all quietly vanished. The spy watched intently, yet he failed to catch any overt signal. They had simply drifted away with a listless, accidental air of poverty that seemed perfectly genuine and beyond reproach.
source_text: It was remarkable; but, the taste of Saint Antoine seemed to be decidedly opposed to a rose on the head-dress of Madame Defarge. Two men had entered separately, and had been about to order drink, when, catching sight of that novelty, they faltered, made a pretence of looking about as if for some friend who was not there, and went away. Nor, of those who had been there when this visitor entered, was there one left. They had all dropped off. The spy had kept his eyes open, but had been able to detect no sign. They had lounged away in a poverty-stricken, purposeless, accidental manner, quite natural and unimpeachable.

text: Actually," Madame interjected, looking up from her needlework and her soft humming, "we hear nothing of them anymore. We received word of their safe arrival, and perhaps a letter or two followed, but since that time, our lives have simply drifted apart—they followed their own path, and we followed ours—and all communication between us has ceased.
source_text: “In effect,” madame struck in, looking up from her work and her little song, “we never hear about them. We received the news of their safe arrival, and perhaps another letter, or perhaps two; but, since then, they have gradually taken their road in life--we, ours--and we have held no correspondence.”

text: Indeed, Mademoiselle Beaumont is to be married. But her choice has not fallen upon an Englishman; she weds a countryman, one who, like herself, is French by birth. And touching upon the fate of poor Baptiste—ah, the miserable soul! it was a savage, savage business—it is a most singular irony that she is to join herself to the nephew of that very Seigneur for whose murder Baptiste was swung so many cubits into the air; in other words, the current Seigneur himself. But he lives in shadow in London, renouncing his rank; there, he is known only as Mr. Julian Vane. Valeray is the name of his mother’s family, which he uses to mask his station.
source_text: “Yes, Miss Manette is going to be married. But not to an Englishman; to one who, like herself, is French by birth. And speaking of Gaspard (ah, poor Gaspard! It was cruel, cruel!), it is a curious thing that she is going to marry the nephew of Monsieur the Marquis, for whom Gaspard was exalted to that height of so many feet; in other words, the present Marquis. But he lives unknown in England, he is no Marquis there; he is Mr. Charles Darnay. D’Aulnais is the name of his mother’s family.”

text: Madame Defarge’s needles moved with unwavering rhythm, yet the information visibly rattled her husband. Try as he might to appear nonchalant behind the narrow counter while fumbling to light his pipe, his distress was evident, and his hands lacked their usual steadiness. Any spy worth his salt would have easily detected this lapse and filed the observation away for future use.
source_text: Madame Defarge knitted steadily, but the intelligence had a palpable effect upon her husband. Do what he would, behind the little counter, as to the striking of a light and the lighting of his pipe, he was troubled, and his hand was not trustworthy. The spy would have been no spy if he had failed to see it, or to record it in his mind.

text: Having delivered his one significant blow, for whatever it might eventually be worth, and finding no new patrons to offer him another opening, Mr. Barsad settled his tab and prepared to depart. Before leaving, he took care to remark with refined politeness that he anticipated the pleasure of meeting Monsieur and Madame Defarge once more. For several minutes after he had stepped out into the atmosphere of Saint Antoine, the husband and wife remained precisely as he had left them, fearing he might suddenly return.
source_text: Having made, at least, this one hit, whatever it might prove to be worth, and no customers coming in to help him to any other, Mr. Barsad paid for what he had drunk, and took his leave: taking occasion to say, in a genteel manner, before he departed, that he looked forward to the pleasure of seeing Monsieur and Madame Defarge again. For some minutes after he had emerged into the outer presence of Saint Antoine, the husband and wife remained exactly as he had left them, lest he should come back.

text: At twilight, when the district of Saint Antoine turned itself out into the streets, its people sat on doorsteps and leaned from windows, seeking a breath of air in the wretched alleys. Madame Defarge moved among them with her knitting, passing from group to group like a dark apostle—one of a type the world would be wise never to produce again. All the women there were knitting. They crafted useless things, yet the repetitive motion served as a physical substitute for sustenance. Their fingers worked in place of their idle jaws; had those skeletal hands ever gone still, the gnawing ache of famine in their bellies would have been far more agonizing.
source_text: In the evening, at which season of all others Saint Antoine turned himself inside out, and sat on door-steps and window-ledges, and came to the corners of vile streets and courts, for a breath of air, Madame Defarge with her work in her hand was accustomed to pass from place to place and from group to group: a Missionary--there were many like her--such as the world will do well never to breed again. All the women knitted. They knitted worthless things; but, the mechanical work was a mechanical substitute for eating and drinking; the hands moved for the jaws and the digestive apparatus: if the bony fingers had been still, the stomachs would have been more famine-pinched.

text: I have contemplated her features, obsessing a thousand times over the unborn soul from which I was violently sundered. If it lived. If it had emerged into the light, or if the mother’s sudden trauma had extinguished it in the dark. If it were a son destined to one day right the wrongs done to his kin. (During my long confinement, there were years when the craving for vengeance was a madness.) If it were a son who would never learn of his father’s fate; who might even come to judge me, imagining I had walked away from my life of my own accord. If it were a daughter who would eventually reach her prime.
source_text: “I have looked at her, speculating thousands of times upon the unborn child from whom I had been rent. Whether it was alive. Whether it had been born alive, or the poor mother’s shock had killed it. Whether it was a son who would some day avenge his father. (There was a time in my imprisonment, when my desire for vengeance was unbearable.) Whether it was a son who would never know his father’s story; who might even live to weigh the possibility of his father’s having disappeared of his own will and act. Whether it was a daughter who would grow to be a woman.”

text: “She revealed her children to me,” the physician from Beauvais recounted, “and they knew of my existence, having been raised to feel for my suffering. Whenever they walked past a fortress of the State, they gave its dark, looming walls a wide berth, glancing up at the barred windows and speaking only in hushed tones. Though she could not truly liberate me, I felt as if she led me back to myself after granting me these glimpses. Then, granted the mercy of weeping, I sank to my knees and gave her my blessing.”
source_text: “And she showed me her children,” said the Doctor of Beauvais, “and they had heard of me, and had been taught to pity me. When they passed a prison of the State, they kept far from its frowning walls, and looked up at its bars, and spoke in whispers. She could never deliver me; I imagined that she always brought me back after showing me such things. But then, blessed with the relief of tears, I fell upon my knees, and blessed her.”

text: “Elena, I summon the memory of those former griefs only for the sake of the love I bear you this evening—a devotion far beyond the reach of speech—and to praise the Almighty for the bounty of my joy. My spirit, in its most frantic wanderings, never climbed toward a bliss so profound as that which I have shared with you, nor dreamed of the radiant path that lies ahead for us both.”
source_text: “Lucie, I recall these old troubles in the reason that I have to-night for loving you better than words can tell, and thanking God for my great happiness. My thoughts, when they were wildest, never rose near the happiness that I have known with you, and that we have before us.”

text: Only Mr. Lorry was invited to the wedding; even the bridesmaid’s duties fell solely to the lean Miss Pross. Their marriage would not change where they lived, as they had expanded their home by taking over the upper rooms once attributed to a mythical, unseen tenant, and they were perfectly content with that.
source_text: There was no one bidden to the marriage but Mr. Lorry; there was even to be no bridesmaid but the gaunt Miss Pross. The marriage was to make no change in their place of residence; they had been able to extend it, by taking to themselves the upper rooms formerly belonging to the apocryphal invisible lodger, and they desired nothing more.

text: Everything, however, remained in its proper place; the room was hushed. He lay in a deep sleep, his white hair fanned out beautifully against the smooth pillow, his hands resting still upon the quilt. She set her unnecessary candle in the shadows, stole toward his bed, and pressed her lips to his; then, she leaned over him, watching him in the silence.
source_text: All things, however, were in their places; all was quiet; and he lay asleep, his white hair picturesque on the untroubled pillow, and his hands lying quiet on the coverlet. She put her needless candle in the shadow at a distance, crept up to his bed, and put her lips to his; then, leaned over him, and looked at him.

text: The wedding morning was radiant with sunlight as the small party gathered outside the Doctor’s closed door, where he remained in private conversation with Charles Darnay. They were all prepared to depart for the church: the lovely bride, Mr. Lorry, and Miss Pross. This occasion would have filled Miss Pross with unclouded joy—she having slowly accepted the inevitability of the match—were it not for her persistent, secret conviction that her brother Solomon was the only one truly worthy of the bride’s hand.
source_text: The marriage-day was shining brightly, and they were ready outside the closed door of the Doctor’s room, where he was speaking with Charles Darnay. They were ready to go to church; the beautiful bride, Mr. Lorry, and Miss Pross--to whom the event, through a gradual process of reconcilement to the inevitable, would have been one of absolute bliss, but for the yet lingering consideration that her brother Solomon should have been the bridegroom.

text: “Well then,” murmured Mr. Halloway, who seemed unable to exhaust his wonder for the bride, having circled her several times to observe every fold of her demure, charming gown; “well then, it was to this very purpose, my dear Beatrice, that I bore you across the salt spray when you were a mere mite! Mercy upon me! How scantily I understood the gravity of my errand! How little did I prize the great fortune I was then securing for my good friend, Mr. Edward!”
source_text: “And so,” said Mr. Lorry, who could not sufficiently admire the bride, and who had been moving round her to take in every point of her quiet, pretty dress; “and so it was for this, my sweet Lucie, that I brought you across the Channel, such a baby! Lord bless me! How little I thought what I was doing! How lightly I valued the obligation I was conferring on my friend Mr. Charles!”

text: “You were crying just a moment ago; I saw it myself, and I can’t say I’m surprised. A gift of silver like the one you’ve given them is enough to bring anyone to tears. There wasn’t a single fork or spoon in that set,” Miss Pross said, “that I didn’t weep over last night after the delivery arrived, until my eyes were too blurred to see.”
source_text: “You were, just now; I saw you do it, and I don’t wonder at it. Such a present of plate as you have made ’em, is enough to bring tears into anybody’s eyes. There’s not a fork or a spoon in the collection,” said Miss Pross, “that I didn’t cry over, last night after the box came, till I couldn’t see it.”

text: Offering his arm to his daughter, he escorted her down to the carriage Mr. Lorry had commissioned for the occasion. The rest of the party followed in a separate coach, and soon, within a local church shielded from the prying eyes of strangers, Charles Darnay and Lucie Manette were united in happy matrimony.
source_text: He gave his arm to his daughter, and took her down-stairs to the chariot which Mr. Lorry had hired in honour of the day. The rest followed in another carriage, and soon, in a neighbouring church, where no strange eyes looked on, Charles Darnay and Lucie Manette were happily married.

text: On this occasion, Mr. Lorry pretended to leave when he could elicit no response, and after staying away for an hour, he returned. During the interval, the Doctor had moved to the window seat to gaze down at the plane-tree; however, at the sound of Mr. Lorry’s reappearance, he slipped back to his workbench.
source_text: This time, Mr. Lorry feigned to go out when he could extract no answer from him, and, after remaining absent for an hour, returned. In the meanwhile, the Doctor had removed to the seat in the window, and had sat there looking down at the plane-tree; but, on Mr. Lorry’s return, he slipped away to his bench.

text: To the degree that his mind could be sounded without breaching those subtle and measured boundaries which Mr. Thorne deemed the only secure avenue of approach, it appeared initially that he believed his niece’s vows had been exchanged only the day before. A calculated reference, dropped as if by chance, to the current date and day of the week, compelled him to a silent arithmetic that left him visibly disquieted. In every other regard, however, he remained so perfectly master of himself that Mr. Thorne resolved to solicit the very expertise he required. And that expertise was to be the man’s own.
source_text: So far as it was possible to comprehend him without overstepping those delicate and gradual approaches which Mr. Lorry felt to be the only safe advance, he at first supposed that his daughter’s marriage had taken place yesterday. An incidental allusion, purposely thrown out, to the day of the week, and the day of the month, set him thinking and counting, and evidently made him uneasy. In all other respects, however, he was so composedly himself, that Mr. Lorry determined to have the aid he sought. And that aid was his own.

text: “My dear Manette,” Mr. Lorry finally said, speaking with his greatest sense of kindness and affection, “I am merely a man of business, quite unsuited to handle such complex and sensitive issues. I lack the necessary knowledge and the specific insight required; I am in need of direction. There is no one in this world whose guidance I would trust more than yours. Tell me, what causes such a setback to occur? Is there a risk of it happening again? Could a recurrence be avoided? If it were to return, how should it be managed? What is the root of it all? What can I do to help my friend? No one could be more truly eager to assist a companion than I am to help mine, if only I knew the way.”
source_text: “Now, my dear Manette,” said Mr. Lorry, at length, in his most considerate and most affectionate way, “I am a mere man of business, and unfit to cope with such intricate and difficult matters. I do not possess the kind of information necessary; I do not possess the kind of intelligence; I want guiding. There is no man in this world on whom I could so rely for right guidance, as on you. Tell me, how does this relapse come about? Is there danger of another? Could a repetition of it be prevented? How should a repetition of it be treated? How does it come about at all? What can I do for my friend? No man ever can have been more desirous in his heart to serve a friend, than I am to serve mine, if I knew how.

text: “Yet I am at a loss for how to strike the first spark in such an instance. Were your wisdom, insight, and long-practiced judgment to set me upon the correct path, I might achieve a great deal; however, remaining unguided and in the dark, my efforts are of little consequence. I entreat you to converse with me; help me to perceive the matter with greater lucidity, and show me how I may be of some genuine service.”
source_text: “But I don’t know how to originate, in such a case. If your sagacity, knowledge, and experience, could put me on the right track, I might be able to do so much; unenlightened and undirected, I can do so little. Pray discuss it with me; pray enable me to see it a little more clearly, and teach me how to be a little more useful.”

text: “I wouldn’t hold onto it,” Mr. Lorry remarked, shaking his head; he grew more resolute as he noticed the Doctor’s visible unease. “I would advise him to let it go. I only need your permission. I am certain it does no one any good. Come now! Give me your consent, like a true friend. For the sake of his daughter, my dear Manette!”
source_text: “I would not keep it,” said Mr. Lorry, shaking his head; for he gained in firmness as he saw the Doctor disquieted. “I would recommend him to sacrifice it. I only want your authority. I am sure it does no good. Come! Give me your authority, like a dear good man. For his daughter’s sake, my dear Manette!”

text: Late that evening, after the doctor had departed, Mr. Lorry retreated to his room equipped with a hatchet, saw, chisel, and hammer, accompanied by Miss Pross bearing a light. Behind closed doors, acting with a strange sense of guilt, Lorry broke the shoemaker’s bench into fragments. Miss Pross held the candle as though she were an accessory to a homicide—a part her somber appearance was well-suited for. They wasted no time burning the remains, which had been chopped into manageable sizes, in the kitchen fireplace; meanwhile, the tools, shoes, and leather scraps were hidden away in the garden. Destruction and secrecy felt so unnatural to these two honest souls that, as they performed the deed and erased the evidence, they looked and felt like conspirators in a terrible crime.
source_text: On the night of the day on which he left the house, Mr. Lorry went into his room with a chopper, saw, chisel, and hammer, attended by Miss Pross carrying a light. There, with closed doors, and in a mysterious and guilty manner, Mr. Lorry hacked the shoemaker’s bench to pieces, while Miss Pross held the candle as if she were assisting at a murder--for which, indeed, in her grimness, she was no unsuitable figure. The burning of the body (previously reduced to pieces convenient for the purpose) was commenced without delay in the kitchen fire; and the tools, shoes, and leather, were buried in the garden. So wicked do destruction and secrecy appear to honest minds, that Mr. Lorry and Miss Pross, while engaged in the commission of their deed and in the removal of its traces, almost felt, and almost looked, like accomplices in a horrible crime.

text: “As for that so-called 'great service,'” Carton said, “I have to confess, since you’ve put it that way, that it was merely a bit of professional showmanship. I’m not sure I cared one way or the other what happened to you when I did it. Mark my words! I said *when* I did it; I’m talking about how I felt back then.”
source_text: “As to the great service,” said Carton, “I am bound to avow to you, when you speak of it in that way, that it was mere professional claptrap, I don’t know that I cared what became of you, when I rendered it.--Mind! I say when I rendered it; I am speaking of the past.”

text: In this way, the soft flutter of celestial wings merged with the household's other reverberations, creating a sound that belonged less to the world and more to the divine. The faint moans of the breeze passing over a small grave in the garden joined this chorus, and Lucie could hear both in a quiet drone—reminiscent of the rhythmic respiration of a calm summer ocean resting against a beach—while young Lucie, alternating between a charmingly serious focus on her morning lessons and playing with a doll by her mother’s feet, prattled away in the languages of the Two Cities that were intertwined in her life.
source_text: Thus, the rustling of an Angel’s wings got blended with the other echoes, and they were not wholly of earth, but had in them that breath of Heaven. Sighs of the winds that blew over a little garden-tomb were mingled with them also, and both were audible to Lucie, in a hushed murmur--like the breathing of a summer sea asleep upon a sandy shore--as the little Lucie, comically studious at the task of the morning, or dressing a doll at her mother’s footstool, chattered in the tongues of the Two Cities that were blended in her life.

text: This trio of fledgling scholars, led by a Mr. Sterling who exhaled a patronage of the crudest sort, had been driven like sheep to that peaceful Soho retreat and offered as pupils to Clara’s husband. With a boisterous air, Sterling had declared, “Look here, Julian! A few scraps of sustenance for your modest household!” The graceful refusal of these charitable morsels had left Mr. Sterling bloated with a resentment he subsequently turned to pedagogical use; he warned his young charges to beware the haughty destitution of men like that pedant-fellow. It was also his custom to boast to his own wife, over glasses of robust claret, of the various snares Clara had once set to entrap him, and of the flinty, impenetrable shrewdness in his own character, madam, which had kept him from being caught. Some of his legal cronies, who occasionally partook of both the claret and the fabrication, excused the latter by suggesting that he had recounted the tale so frequently that he now accepted it as fact—an intensification of the primary vice so incorrigible that it surely justifies the removal of such a man to some desolate spot for a quiet, necessary hanging.
source_text: These three young gentlemen, Mr. Stryver, exuding patronage of the most offensive quality from every pore, had walked before him like three sheep to the quiet corner in Soho, and had offered as pupils to Lucie’s husband: delicately saying “Halloa! here are three lumps of bread-and-cheese towards your matrimonial picnic, Darnay!” The polite rejection of the three lumps of bread-and-cheese had quite bloated Mr. Stryver with indignation, which he afterwards turned to account in the training of the young gentlemen, by directing them to beware of the pride of Beggars, like that tutor-fellow. He was also in the habit of declaiming to Mrs. Stryver, over his full-bodied wine, on the arts Mrs. Darnay had once put in practice to “catch” him, and on the diamond-cut-diamond arts in himself, madam, which had rendered him “not to be caught.” Some of his King’s Bench familiars, who were occasionally parties to the full-bodied wine and the lie, excused him for the latter by saying that he had told it so often, that he believed it himself--which is surely such an incorrigible aggravation of an originally bad offence, as to justify any such offender’s being carried off to some suitably retired spot, and there hanged out of the way.

text: A yawning moat, a solitary drawbridge, and colossal stone ramparts punctuated by eight looming turrets; the scene was a chaos of artillery, rifles, flames, and soot. Suddenly, a bridge crashed down! “Push on, brothers, push on! Labor, Jacques One, Jacques Two, Jacques Ten Thousand, Jacques Twenty-Five Thousand! By every angel or every devil—take your pick—keep at it!” So shouted Defarge the tavern-keeper, still tethered to his cannon, the metal of which had long since become scorching to the touch.
source_text: Deep ditch, single drawbridge, massive stone walls, eight great towers, cannon, muskets, fire and smoke. One drawbridge down! “Work, comrades all, work! Work, Jacques One, Jacques Two, Jacques One Thousand, Jacques Two Thousand, Jacques Five-and-Twenty Thousand; in the name of all the Angels or the Devils--which you prefer--work!” Thus Defarge of the wine-shop, still at his gun, which had long grown hot.

text: High in the wall sat a narrow, glassless window, heavily barred and obstructed by a stone screen that required one to stoop low and peer upward just to see the sky. A small chimney rose nearby, its passage choked by thick iron crossbars a few feet up. On the hearth rested a mound of ancient, feathery wood ash. The room contained only a stool, a table, and a pallet of straw. Enclosing these were four soot-blackened walls, with a single rusted iron ring bolted into the masonry.
source_text: There was a small, heavily-grated, unglazed window high in the wall, with a stone screen before it, so that the sky could be only seen by stooping low and looking up. There was a small chimney, heavily barred across, a few feet within. There was a heap of old feathery wood-ashes on the hearth. There was a stool, and table, and a straw bed. There were the four blackened walls, and a rusted iron ring in one of them.

text: Casting a lethal glance toward the gaoler, he scrambled into the soot-stained alcove of the fireplace. He peered into the throat of the chimney, hammering his heavy pry-bar against the stones and wrenching at the iron lattice that barred the way. Presently, a rain of pulverized mortar and grit spilled forth, from which he shielded his eyes; then, amidst the grey dregs and a narrow cleft where his steel had bitten deep, he searched with a slow and wary hand.
source_text: With a menacing look at the turnkey he crawled upon the hearth, and, peering up the chimney, struck and prised at its sides with the crowbar, and worked at the iron grating across it. In a few minutes, some mortar and dust came dropping down, which he averted his face to avoid; and in it, and in the old wood-ashes, and in a crevice in the chimney into which his weapon had slipped or wrought itself, he groped with a cautious touch.

text: The mob was a heaving, restless tide, searching everywhere for Defarge. The voices of Saint Antoine rose in a roar, demanding that their wine-shop master take his place at the head of the guard over the governor who had held the Bastille and fired upon the citizens. Only with Defarge leading the way could they ensure the man was marched to the Hotel de Ville to face his reckoning. Without such vigilance, he might yet slip away, and the people’s blood—suddenly a precious thing after long years of being treated as worthless—would be left unavenged.
source_text: They found it surging and tossing, in quest of Defarge himself. Saint Antoine was clamorous to have its wine-shop keeper foremost in the guard upon the governor who had defended the Bastille and shot the people. Otherwise, the governor would not be marched to the Hotel de Ville for judgment. Otherwise, the governor would escape, and the people’s blood (suddenly of some value, after many years of worthlessness) be unavenged.

text: An ocean of dark and menacing waters, where waves collided in ruinous upheaval, its true depths still unplumbed and its raw power yet to be gauged. It was a pitiless tide of surging, violent figures—cries for retribution and expressions forged in the fires of agony until they were tempered against any touch of mercy.
source_text: The sea of black and threatening waters, and of destructive upheaving of wave against wave, whose depths were yet unfathomed and whose forces were yet unknown. The remorseless sea of turbulently swaying shapes, voices of vengeance, and faces hardened in the furnaces of suffering until the touch of pity could make no mark on them.

text: He is not dead! He was so terrified of us—and with good cause—that he staged his own death and held a grand, fake funeral. But they found him alive, hiding in the provinces, and have dragged him back. I just saw him myself, a captive being led toward the Hotel de Ville. I told you he had reason to tremble before us. Speak up, everyone! Was his fear not justified?
source_text: “Not dead! He feared us so much--and with reason--that he caused himself to be represented as dead, and had a grand mock-funeral. But they have found him alive, hiding in the country, and have brought him in. I have seen him but now, on his way to the Hotel de Ville, a prisoner. I have said that he had reason to fear us. Say all! _Had_ he reason?”

text: The men were formidable in their murderous fury as they glared from their windows, grabbed whatever makeshift weapons they could find, and surged down into the streets; however, the women were a sight to terrify even the most courageous. Leaving behind the meager chores of their poverty, abandoning their children and the elderly or sick who lay starving and exposed on the bare ground, they rushed out with hair flying wild. They goaded one another and themselves into a state of madness with frantic cries and violent movements. "The villain Foulon is caught, sister!" "Old Foulon is taken, mother!" "The scoundrel Foulon is captured, daughter!" Then, dozens more joined them, beating their chests and tearing at their hair, shrieking, "Foulon is alive! Foulon, who told the starving they could eat grass! Foulon, who told my aged father to eat grass when I had no bread to offer him! Foulon, who told my nursing infant to suck on grass when my own breasts were dry with hunger! Mother of God, this Foulon! Heaven, see our misery! Hear me, my dead child and my frail father: I swear on my knees, upon these very stones, to take my revenge on Foulon! Husbands, brothers, and sons—give us his blood! Give us his head! Give us his heart! Give us his body and soul! Tear Foulon to pieces and bury him in the earth, so that grass may grow from him!" Fueled by these screams, many of the women, whipped into a blind delirium, spun about and lashed out at their own friends until they collapsed in a feverish faint, saved from being trampled underfoot only by the men who pulled them away.
source_text: The men were terrible, in the bloody-minded anger with which they looked from windows, caught up what arms they had, and came pouring down into the streets; but, the women were a sight to chill the boldest. From such household occupations as their bare poverty yielded, from their children, from their aged and their sick crouching on the bare ground famished and naked, they ran out with streaming hair, urging one another, and themselves, to madness with the wildest cries and actions. Villain Foulon taken, my sister! Old Foulon taken, my mother! Miscreant Foulon taken, my daughter! Then, a score of others ran into the midst of these, beating their breasts, tearing their hair, and screaming, Foulon alive! Foulon who told the starving people they might eat grass! Foulon who told my old father that he might eat grass, when I had no bread to give him! Foulon who told my baby it might suck grass, when these breasts were dry with want! O mother of God, this Foulon! O Heaven our suffering! Hear me, my dead baby and my withered father: I swear on my knees, on these stones, to avenge you on Foulon! Husbands, and brothers, and young men, Give us the blood of Foulon, Give us the head of Foulon, Give us the heart of Foulon, Give us the body and soul of Foulon, Rend Foulon to pieces, and dig him into the ground, that grass may grow from him! With these cries, numbers of the women, lashed into blind frenzy, whirled about, striking and tearing at their own friends until they dropped into a passionate swoon, and were only saved by the men belonging to them from being trampled under foot.

text: Indeed, the multitude had by then stifled the Chamber of Judgment where the withered and malefic prisoner sat, and were hemorrhaging into the neighboring squares and alleys. The Durands, man and wife, the Nemesis, and Patriot Six, stood in the thickest of the vanguard, mere yards from his side in the hall.
source_text: No. They were all by that time choking the Hall of Examination where this old man, ugly and wicked, was, and overflowing into the adjacent open space and streets. The Defarges, husband and wife, The Vengeance, and Jacques Three, were in the first press, and at no great distance from him in the Hall.

text: Eventually, the morning climbed until a solitary, benevolent beam—a flicker of sanctuary—descended upon the aged captive’s brow. Such grace proved unbearable; the fragile veneer of apathy he had clutched for so long dissolved into nothingness, and in that heartbeat, the fury of the district finally claimed him as its own.
source_text: At length the sun rose so high that it struck a kindly ray as of hope or protection, directly down upon the old prisoner’s head. The favour was too much to bear; in an instant the barrier of dust and chaff that had stood surprisingly long, went to the winds, and Saint Antoine had got him!

text: He was tumbled down the stone steps and hauled back up, falling headlong, then onto his knees, his feet, and his back. Hundreds of hands battered him and choked him with clumps of grass and straw thrust into his face; though torn, bruised, and gasping for air, he never ceased his frantic pleading for mercy. At times he thrashed in a desperate frenzy, the crowd parting briefly to watch his agony; at others, he was a mere heavy weight dragged through a dense thicket of legs. They hauled him to a street corner beneath a hanging lantern, where Madame Defarge finally released her grip—as a cat might let go of a mouse—and watched him with a cold, silent composure while he begged for his life. Around them, the women shrieked with rage and the men grimly demanded his execution, shouting for him to be killed with grass in his mouth. Twice he was hoisted up only for the rope to snap, and twice the crowd caught his screaming form as he fell. Finally, the third rope held firm, and soon his head was raised high upon a pike, his mouth stuffed with enough grass to incite all of Saint Antoine to dance at the sight of it.
source_text: Down, and up, and head foremost on the steps of the building; now, on his knees; now, on his feet; now, on his back; dragged, and struck at, and stifled by the bunches of grass and straw that were thrust into his face by hundreds of hands; torn, bruised, panting, bleeding, yet always entreating and beseeching for mercy; now full of vehement agony of action, with a small clear space about him as the people drew one another back that they might see; now, a log of dead wood drawn through a forest of legs; he was hauled to the nearest street corner where one of the fatal lamps swung, and there Madame Defarge let him go--as a cat might have done to a mouse--and silently and composedly looked at him while they made ready, and while he besought her: the women passionately screeching at him all the time, and the men sternly calling out to have him killed with grass in his mouth. Once, he went aloft, and the rope broke, and they caught him shrieking; twice, he went aloft, and the rope broke, and they caught him shrieking; then, the rope was merciful, and held him, and his head was soon upon a pike, with grass enough in the mouth for all Saint Antoine to dance at the sight of.

text: The day’s dark harvest did not end there, for the Iron Quarter howled and danced until its collective veins burned anew, learning as night fell that the kinsman of the slain—another mocker of the wretched—was nearing the city under a guard of five hundred horsemen. The Quarter inscribed his villainies upon flaming broadsides and seized him—would have torn him from the heart of an empire to unite him with his fellow—hoisting his head and heart upon pikes to parade the day’s three trophies in a lupine march through the city.
source_text: Nor was this the end of the day’s bad work, for Saint Antoine so shouted and danced his angry blood up, that it boiled again, on hearing when the day closed in that the son-in-law of the despatched, another of the people’s enemies and insulters, was coming into Paris under a guard five hundred strong, in cavalry alone. Saint Antoine wrote his crimes on flaring sheets of paper, seized him--would have torn him out of the breast of an army to bear Foulon company--set his head and heart on pikes, and carried the three spoils of the day, in Wolf-procession through the streets.

text: Those dinners were sparse and meager, as devoid of meat as they were of any seasoning for the miserable bread. Yet, the bonds of companionship lent a kind of sustenance to the hard fare, striking faint glimmers of joy from it. Parents who had endured the day’s harshest trials played tenderly with their thin children; and lovers, even in such a bleak world, continued to love and to hope.
source_text: Scanty and insufficient suppers those, and innocent of meat, as of most other sauce to wretched bread. Yet, human fellowship infused some nourishment into the flinty viands, and struck some sparks of cheerfulness out of them. Fathers and mothers who had had their full share in the worst of the day, played gently with their meagre children; and lovers, with such a world around them and before them, loved and hoped.

text: His Excellency (frequently a gentleman of considerable personal merit) was regarded as a gift to the state, a source of courtly elegance, and a radiant archetype of the opulent life, among other such lofty descriptions; yet, the aristocracy as a whole had, through one means or another, reduced affairs to this wretched state. It was peculiar that the world, fashioned specifically for His Excellency’s enjoyment, should be so quickly drained and discarded! There must certainly be some lack of foresight in the divine plan! Nevertheless, such was the reality; and since the final drop of moisture had been bled from the parched earth, and the gears of the engine had been cranked so relentlessly that their teeth were sheared away, leaving them to spin without traction, His Excellency began to retreat from a crisis so plebeian and baffling.
source_text: Monseigneur (often a most worthy individual gentleman) was a national blessing, gave a chivalrous tone to things, was a polite example of luxurious and shining life, and a great deal more to equal purpose; nevertheless, Monseigneur as a class had, somehow or other, brought things to this. Strange that Creation, designed expressly for Monseigneur, should be so soon wrung dry and squeezed out! There must be something short-sighted in the eternal arrangements, surely! Thus it was, however; and the last drop of blood having been extracted from the flints, and the last screw of the rack having been turned so often that its purchase crumbled, and it now turned and turned with nothing to bite, Monseigneur began to run away from a phenomenon so low and unaccountable.

text: The stone-breaker made his way back, the grit of the path swirling ahead of him on the shifting gusts. He reached the communal well, pressing through the throng of scrawny oxen that had come for water, and appeared to murmur his secrets into their ears as he had to the rest of the hamlet. Once the people had consumed their sparse evening crusts, they did not retreat to their beds as was their habit, but emerged once more into the lanes to wait. A restless infection of hushed talk pervaded the air, joined by a collective, fixed gaze toward a solitary quarter of the night sky. Master Vane, the local overseer, grew troubled; he ascended to his rooftop in solitude, watching that same distant point. From the shelter of his chimneys, he observed the shadowed, expectant faces gathered at the water below, and dispatched a runner to the sexton with word that the iron tongue of the church bell might soon be summoned to cry out.
source_text: The mender of roads went home, with the dust going on before him according to the set of the wind, and was soon at the fountain, squeezing himself in among the lean kine brought there to drink, and appearing even to whisper to them in his whispering to all the village. When the village had taken its poor supper, it did not creep to bed, as it usually did, but came out of doors again, and remained there.
