A curious contagion of whispering was upon it, and also, when it gathered together at the fountain in the dark, another curious contagion of looking expectantly at the sky in one direction only. Monsieur Gabelle, chief functionary of the place, became uneasy; went out on his house-top alone, and looked in that direction too; glanced down from behind his chimneys at the darkening faces by the fountain below, and sent word to the sacristan who kept the keys of the church, that there might be need to ring the tocsin by-and-bye.

text: The messenger from the manor, his horse lathered in sweat, thundered through the village and raced up the rocky slope toward the prison on the cliff. By the gates, a cluster of officers stood watching the distant blaze, while a separate group of soldiers remained further off. “Help us, gentlemen—officers! The chateau is on fire; we can still save the valuables if you act now! Help, please!” The officers glanced at the soldiers, who kept their eyes fixed on the fire. No orders were given. With indifferent shrugs and tightened lips, they simply replied, “It must burn.”
source_text: The rider from the chateau, and the horse in a foam, clattered away through the village, and galloped up the stony steep, to the prison on the crag. At the gate, a group of officers were looking at the fire; removed from them, a group of soldiers. “Help, gentlemen--officers! The chateau is on fire; valuable objects may be saved from the flames by timely aid! Help, help!” The officers looked towards the soldiers who looked at the fire; gave no orders; and answered, with shrugs and biting of lips, “It must burn.”

text: Inevitably, the primary sanctuary and central rendezvous for the dispossessed nobility in London was Barrington’s. It is said that phantoms linger where their living selves once thrived; thus, the impoverished aristocrat, now stripped of his fortune, haunted the very vaults where his gold had once rested. Furthermore, it served as the destination for the most authentic dispatches from across the Channel, arriving there with the greatest speed. Additionally, Barrington’s was a charitable institution, showing remarkable generosity toward those former patrons who had tumbled from their lofty heights. Moreover, those prudent lords who had sensed the gathering gale and, fearing the seizure of their lands, had wisely transferred their assets to the bank, could always be traced there by their destitute kinsmen. One must also note that every fresh arrival from the Continent presented himself and his news at Barrington’s as an unspoken obligation. For these manifold reasons, the bank functioned as a premier clearing-house for Gallic affairs; so widely recognized was this role, and so frequent were the solicitations for information, that the clerks occasionally inscribed the freshest bulletins in brief and displayed them in the street-facing windows, allowing any passer-by hurrying past the city gate to grasp the state of the world at a glance.
source_text: As was natural, the head-quarters and great gathering-place of Monseigneur, in London, was Tellson’s Bank. Spirits are supposed to haunt the places where their bodies most resorted, and Monseigneur without a guinea haunted the spot where his guineas used to be. Moreover, it was the spot to which such French intelligence as was most to be relied upon, came quickest. Again: Tellson’s was a munificent house, and extended great liberality to old customers who had fallen from their high estate. Again: those nobles who had seen the coming storm in time, and anticipating plunder or confiscation, had made provident remittances to Tellson’s, were always to be heard of there by their needy brethren. To which it must be added that every new-comer from France reported himself and his tidings at Tellson’s, almost as a matter of course. For such variety of reasons, Tellson’s was at that time, as to French intelligence, a kind of High Exchange; and this was so well known to the public, and the inquiries made there were in consequence so numerous, that Tellson’s sometimes wrote the latest news out in a line or so and posted it in the Bank windows, for all who ran through Temple Bar to read.

text: One humid, fog-choked afternoon, Mr. Lorry sat at his desk while Charles Darnay leaned against it, the two of them conversing in low whispers. The somber office, which had once been reserved for private bank business, had been transformed into a crowded hub for news and was currently packed to capacity. It was roughly half an hour before the end of the business day.
source_text: On a steaming, misty afternoon, Mr. Lorry sat at his desk, and Charles Darnay stood leaning on it, talking with him in a low voice. The penitential den once set apart for interviews with the House, was now the news-Exchange, and was filled to overflowing. It was within half an hour or so of the time of closing.

text: “My good Mr. Sterling, it is my very identity as a son of France that forces this reflection—a thought I had not meant to voice aloud—to weigh so heavily upon me. It is impossible to avoid the hope, given my long-held compassion for the wretched and the inheritance I surrendered to them,” he remarked, slipping back into his pensive state, “that I might be heard, and that I might possess the influence to counsel them toward moderation. Even last night, after you had gone, while I was speaking with Elena—”
source_text: “My dear Mr. Lorry, it is because I am a Frenchman born, that the thought (which I did not mean to utter here, however) has passed through my mind often. One cannot help thinking, having had some sympathy for the miserable people, and having abandoned something to them,” he spoke here in his former thoughtful manner, “that one might be listened to, and might have the power to persuade to some restraint. Only last night, after you had left us, when I was talking to Lucie--”

text: Among the speakers was Stryver, a barrister of the King’s Bench nearing a high promotion and thus particularly loud on the subject. He detailed for Monseigneur his schemes for annihilating the common people and purging them from the earth, proposing methods as absurdly futile as trying to eliminate eagles by sprinkling salt on their tails. Darnay listened with a deep sense of revulsion, caught between the urge to leave to escape the noise and the impulse to stay and offer a rebuttal. As he hesitated, the events that were destined to occur began to take their final shape.
source_text: Among the talkers, was Stryver, of the King’s Bench Bar, far on his way to state promotion, and, therefore, loud on the theme: broaching to Monseigneur, his devices for blowing the people up and exterminating them from the face of the earth, and doing without them: and for accomplishing many similar objects akin in their nature to the abolition of eagles by sprinkling salt on the tails of the race. Him, Darnay heard with a particular feeling of objection; and Darnay stood divided between going away that he might hear no more, and remaining to interpose his word, when the thing that was to be, went on to shape itself out.

text: Having long lived in fear for my life at the hands of the villagers, I was eventually seized with great brutality and shame, then forced to travel on foot the entire distance to Paris. I have endured much hardship along that road. Nor is that the end of my misery; my home has been demolished—leveled completely to the earth.
source_text: “After having long been in danger of my life at the hands of the village, I have been seized, with great violence and indignity, and brought a long journey on foot to Paris. On the road I have suffered a great deal. Nor is that all; my house has been destroyed--razed to the ground.

text: “The offense for which I languish in this cell, Citizen formerly the Count, and for which I shall shortly be hauled before the bar to surrender my life—barring your most gracious intercession—is, I am informed, a betrayal of the sovereign people. They charge me with having conspired against the nation in the service of an exile. I protest in vain that my labors were for their sake, not their ruin, and were carried out in strict accordance with your mandates. I argue to no effect that, prior to the confiscation of your estates, I had already waived the levies they could no longer afford, sought no arrears, and initiated no legal seizures. The singular answer I receive is that I have served a fugitive, and the cry remains: where is this master of yours?”
source_text: “The crime for which I am imprisoned, Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, and for which I shall be summoned before the tribunal, and shall lose my life (without your so generous help), is, they tell me, treason against the majesty of the people, in that I have acted against them for an emigrant. It is in vain I represent that I have acted for them, and not against, according to your commands. It is in vain I represent that, before the sequestration of emigrant property, I had remitted the imposts they had ceased to pay; that I had collected no rent; that I had had recourse to no process. The only response is, that I have acted for an emigrant, and where is that emigrant?

text: By all that is holy, by justice, by mercy, and for the sake of your family’s honorable name, I beg of you, Monsieur who was once the Marquis, to rescue me and grant me my freedom. My only crime is that I remained loyal to you. Oh, Monsieur formerly the Marquis, I pray that you will now be loyal to me!
source_text: “For the love of Heaven, of justice, of generosity, of the honour of your noble name, I supplicate you, Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, to succour and release me. My fault is, that I have been true to you. Oh Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, I pray you be you true to me!

text: The dormant apprehension within Julian’s breast flared into sudden, stinging life upon reading the missive. The mortal danger facing a devoted steward—a man whose only offense was an unyielding loyalty to Julian’s own disgraced house—loomed before him with such accusatory weight that, as he paced the shadowed quadrangles of Gray's Inn weighing his duty, he felt driven to avert his eyes from every passerby.
source_text: The latent uneasiness in Darnay’s mind was roused to vigourous life by this letter. The peril of an old servant and a good one, whose only crime was fidelity to himself and his family, stared him so reproachfully in the face, that, as he walked to and fro in the Temple considering what to do, he almost hid his face from the passersby.

text: As Julian paced the chamber with his purpose now fixed, he determined that Clara and her father should remain unaware of his intent until he was well beyond their reach. He wished to spare Clara the lingering grief of a parting; and the old man, who habitually turned his gaze from the treacherous memories of his youth, should learn of the venture only as a deed accomplished, not as a source of agonizing suspense. Julian did not openly weigh how much his own inhibited state was a consequence of shielding the elder from those old, dark associations, but he understood that such a burden had quietly dictated the direction of his path.
source_text: As he walked to and fro with his resolution made, he considered that neither Lucie nor her father must know of it until he was gone. Lucie should be spared the pain of separation; and her father, always reluctant to turn his thoughts towards the dangerous ground of old, should come to the knowledge of the step, as a step taken, and not in the balance of suspense and doubt. How much of the incompleteness of his situation was referable to her father, through the painful anxiety to avoid reviving old associations of France in his mind, he did not discuss with himself. But, that circumstance too, had had its influence in his course.

text: The day proved a grueling trial, spent in the warmth of their presence while harboring the very first secret of their united years. It was no simple task to uphold a facade of calm before their utter guilelessness. However, watching Catherine move through her domestic tasks with such radiant peace, he steeled his resolve to shield her from the approaching crisis. He had nearly faltered, for it felt unnatural to take any step without her silent guidance, but the hours eventually slipped away. In the early shadows of the evening, he took his leave of her and their young daughter, her cherished namesake, with the pretense of a fleeting errand. Having already concealed a bag of essentials, he slipped away into the damp gloom of the crowded lanes, his breast heavy with a sorrow that surpassed the thickest fog.
source_text: It was a hard day, that day of being among them, with the first reservation of their joint lives on his mind. It was a hard matter to preserve the innocent deceit of which they were profoundly unsuspicious. But, an affectionate glance at his wife, so happy and busy, made him resolute not to tell her what impended (he had been half moved to do it, so strange it was to him to act in anything without her quiet aid), and the day passed quickly. Early in the evening he embraced her, and her scarcely less dear namesake, pretending that he would return by-and-bye (an imaginary engagement took him out, and he had secreted a valise of clothes ready), and so he emerged into the heavy mist of the heavy streets, with a heavier heart.

text: The captive managed his own mount, but a slack lead was fixed to the bridle, the end of which one of the revolutionaries kept lashed around his wrist. They departed in this fashion as the stinging rain lashed their faces, clattering at a rhythmic military trot over the jagged city stones and out onto the sludge-choked roads. In this manner they traveled without relief, save for the swapping of horses and the variation of their speed, across all the muddy miles that lay between them and the capital.
source_text: The escorted governed his own horse, but a loose line was attached to his bridle, the end of which one of the patriots kept girded round his wrist. In this state they set forth with the sharp rain driving in their faces: clattering at a heavy dragoon trot over the uneven town pavement, and out upon the mire-deep roads. In this state they traversed without change, except of horses and pace, all the mire-deep leagues that lay between them and the capital.

text: After spending nearly half an hour in his saddle observing the scene, Darnay was again approached by the same official, who commanded the guards to open the gates. The man handed the escort—a mix of the drunken and the sober—a receipt for their prisoner and told Darnay to get down. He complied, and the two patriots took his exhausted horse by the reins, turning back to ride away without ever entering the city.
source_text: When he had sat in his saddle some half-hour, taking note of these things, Darnay found himself confronted by the same man in authority, who directed the guard to open the barrier. Then he delivered to the escort, drunk and sober, a receipt for the escorted, and requested him to dismount. He did so, and the two patriots, leading his tired horse, turned and rode away without entering the city.

text: He was fully aware now that he had stumbled into perils far more grave than any he had envisioned when departing from England. He recognized that dangers had closed in on him rapidly and might continue to multiply with even greater speed. He could not deny that, had he been able to predict the occurrences of the past several days, he might have reconsidered this journey altogether. Yet, his apprehensions were not quite as bleak as they would appear when judged by the clarity of later history. However unsettled the future seemed, it remained veiled, and in that mystery there resided a certain blind hope. The ghastly, prolonged massacre that was soon to leave a crimson stain upon the sacred season of the harvest was as far from his imagination as if it were separated by a hundred thousand years. The “sharp, newborn female” named La Guillotine was a figure barely known to him, or to the common people, even by name. The horrific acts soon to be perpetrated were likely not yet even conceived in the minds of those who would eventually carry them out. How, then, could such things take shape in the clouded thoughts of a man so inherently gentle?
source_text: That he had fallen among far greater dangers than those which had developed themselves when he left England, he of course knew now. That perils had thickened about him fast, and might thicken faster and faster yet, he of course knew now. He could not but admit to himself that he might not have made this journey, if he could have foreseen the events of a few days. And yet his misgivings were not so dark as, imagined by the light of this later time, they would appear. Troubled as the future was, it was the unknown future, and in its obscurity there was ignorant hope. The horrible massacre, days and nights long, which, within a few rounds of the clock, was to set a great mark of blood upon the blessed garnering time of harvest, was as far out of his knowledge as if it had been a hundred thousand years away. The “sharp female newly-born, and called La Guillotine,” was hardly known to him, or to the generality of people, by name. The frightful deeds that were to be soon done, were probably unimagined at that time in the brains of the doers. How could they have a place in the shadowy conceptions of a gentle mind?

text: The official irritably impaled the document on a spindle, leaving Charles Darnay to wait for his next command for a full half-hour. Darnay spent the time either pacing the vaulted stone chamber or resting on a cold bench; in both instances, he was being held there so that the chief and his subordinates could burn his likeness into their memories.
source_text: He stuck the paper on a file, in an ill-humour, and Charles Darnay awaited his further pleasure for half an hour: sometimes, pacing to and fro in the strong arched room: sometimes, resting on a stone seat: in either case detained to be imprinted on the memory of the chief and his subordinates.

text: In the somber half-light of the jail, the new arrival followed his keeper through a labyrinth of corridors and stairways, as a succession of heavy doors thudded shut and were bolted behind them. At last, they entered an expansive, low-roofed vault teeming with prisoners of both sexes. The women were gathered around a long table, busy with reading, writing, and various forms of needlework; meanwhile, the men mostly stood behind their chairs or paced restlessly across the floor.
source_text: Through the dismal prison twilight, his new charge accompanied him by corridor and staircase, many doors clanging and locking behind them, until they came into a large, low, vaulted chamber, crowded with prisoners of both sexes. The women were seated at a long table, reading and writing, knitting, sewing, and embroidering; the men were for the most part standing behind their chairs, or lingering up and down the room.

text: A low hum of sympathy followed Charles Darnay as he walked across the hall toward a barred gate where the guard stood waiting. Many voices—the soft, pitying tones of women being particularly distinct—offered him words of hope and encouragement. He paused at the iron door to offer his sincere gratitude; then it swung shut behind him, and the spectral figures vanished from his sight forever.
source_text: There was a murmur of commiseration as Charles Darnay crossed the room to a grated door where the gaoler awaited him, and many voices--among which, the soft and compassionate voices of women were conspicuous--gave him good wishes and encouragement. He turned at the grated door, to render the thanks of his heart; it closed under the gaoler’s hand; and the apparitions vanished from his sight forever.

text: “My dear companion, I lead a protected life within these city walls. I was once an inmate of the Bastille. There is not a single revolutionary in Paris—nay, in all of France—who, recognizing me as a former captive of that fortress, would dare lay a hand on me, unless it were to smother me with affection or bear me aloft in victory. The suffering of my past has granted me an influence that saw us safely past the gates, secured word of Charles’s condition, and guided us to this very spot. I was certain of this outcome; I was convinced I could rescue Charles from his peril, and I shared this assurance with Lucie. What is that sound?” Once more, his hand reached for the windowpane.
source_text: “My dear friend, I have a charmed life in this city. I have been a Bastille prisoner. There is no patriot in Paris--in Paris? In France--who, knowing me to have been a prisoner in the Bastille, would touch me, except to overwhelm me with embraces, or carry me in triumph. My old pain has given me a power that has brought us through the barrier, and gained us news of Charles there, and brought us here. I knew it would be so; I knew I could help Charles out of all danger; I told Lucie so.--What is that noise?” His hand was again upon the window.

text: The writing ended there. Yet for the woman who received it, the words meant so much that she turned from Defarge toward his wife and kissed one of the hands that held the needles. It was a gesture of intense, heartfelt gratitude, but the hand offered no reaction—it simply fell, cold and heavy, and returned to its knitting.
source_text: That was all the writing. It was so much, however, to her who received it, that she turned from Defarge to his wife, and kissed one of the hands that knitted. It was a passionate, loving, thankful, womanly action, but the hand made no response--dropped cold and heavy, and took to its knitting again.

text: “My dear,” Mr. Lorry interjected, stepping in to clarify; “there are frequent riots in these streets; and, though it is unlikely they will ever bother you, Madame Defarge wishes to see the people she has the influence to protect during such turmoil, so that she may know them—that she might recognize their faces. I believe,” Mr. Lorry continued, his comforting tone wavering as the icy demeanor of the three visitors began to weigh on him, “that I have explained the situation correctly, Citizen Defarge?”
source_text: “My dear,” said Mr. Lorry, striking in to explain; “there are frequent risings in the streets; and, although it is not likely they will ever trouble you, Madame Defarge wishes to see those whom she has the power to protect at such times, to the end that she may know them--that she may identify them. I believe,” said Mr. Lorry, rather halting in his reassuring words, as the stony manner of all the three impressed itself upon him more and more, “I state the case, Citizen Defarge?”

text: The gloom surrounding Madame Defarge and her company seemed to loom so ominously over the girl that her mother instinctively sank to the ground, clutching the child to her heart. That dark, threatening presence then appeared to stretch further, enveloping both mother and daughter in its shadow.
source_text: The shadow attendant on Madame Defarge and her party seemed to fall so threatening and dark on the child, that her mother instinctively kneeled on the ground beside her, and held her to her breast. The shadow attendant on Madame Defarge and her party seemed then to fall, threatening and dark, on both the mother and the child.

text: The Doctor confided in Mr. Lorry, emphasizing a need for absolute discretion that went without saying, that the mob had led him through a scene of slaughter to the La Force prison. Once inside, he encountered a makeshift tribunal presiding over the captives, who were summoned individually to hear their fates. With brutal efficiency, the judges either condemned them to immediate massacre, granted them their freedom, or, on rare occasions, sent them back to their cells. When his escorts brought him before this council, he declared his identity and occupation, explaining that he had spent eighteen years as a secret, uncharged captive in the Bastille. At this, one of the men on the bench stood to verify his story, and that man was none other than Defarge.
source_text: To Mr. Lorry, the Doctor communicated under an injunction of secrecy on which he had no need to dwell, that the crowd had taken him through a scene of carnage to the prison of La Force. That, in the prison he had found a self-appointed Tribunal sitting, before which the prisoners were brought singly, and by which they were rapidly ordered to be put forth to be massacred, or to be released, or (in a few cases) to be sent back to their cells. That, presented by his conductors to this Tribunal, he had announced himself by name and profession as having been for eighteen years a secret and unaccused prisoner in the Bastille; that, one of the body so sitting in judgment had risen and identified him, and that this man was Defarge.

text: He had discovered, by consulting the registers on the table, that his son-in-law was still among the living, and he had petitioned the Tribunal—a grim assembly of men both asleep and awake, some stained with blood and others clean, some intoxicated and others sober—for the man's life. In the initial surge of enthusiasm for the Doctor, who was revered as a legendary victim of the old regime, the court had agreed to bring Charles Darnay forward for examination. He seemed on the verge of immediate release when the tide of favor was suddenly halted by an obscure complication, resulting in a brief, private consultation. The President then informed Doctor Manette that the prisoner must remain in jail, though he promised that, out of respect for the Doctor, the young man would be kept in safe custody. Upon a signal, the prisoner was returned to the cells; however, the Doctor pleaded so fervently to stay and ensure that his son-in-law was not accidentally or maliciously surrendered to the mob outside—whose lethal cries frequently eclipsed the court’s proceedings—that he was allowed to remain. He stayed within that blood-soaked hall until the immediate peril had passed.
source_text: That, hereupon he had ascertained, through the registers on the table, that his son-in-law was among the living prisoners, and had pleaded hard to the Tribunal--of whom some members were asleep and some awake, some dirty with murder and some clean, some sober and some not--for his life and liberty. That, in the first frantic greetings lavished on himself as a notable sufferer under the overthrown system, it had been accorded to him to have Charles Darnay brought before the lawless Court, and examined. That, he seemed on the point of being at once released, when the tide in his favour met with some unexplained check (not intelligible to the Doctor), which led to a few words of secret conference. That, the man sitting as President had then informed Doctor Manette that the prisoner must remain in custody, but should, for his sake, be held inviolate in safe custody. That, immediately, on a signal, the prisoner was removed to the interior of the prison again; but, that he, the Doctor, had then so strongly pleaded for permission to remain and assure himself that his son-in-law was, through no malice or mischance, delivered to the concourse whose murderous yells outside the gate had often drowned the proceedings, that he had obtained the permission, and had remained in that Hall of Blood until the danger was over.

text: The Professor’s present existence was fraught with tension, certainly; yet, the observant Mr. Sterling noted a fresh, bolstering dignity within him. No vanity tainted this self-assurance; it was a wholesome and justifiable merit, though he watched its emergence as a singular phenomenon. The Professor understood that, until this hour, his long confinement had been linked in the thoughts of his daughter and his companion solely with his private agony, his loss, and his fragility. Since this perception had shifted—and he realized that his former ordeal now granted him a peculiar authority upon which they both relied for Julian’s eventual rescue and freedom—he felt so uplifted by the transformation that he assumed the mantle of guidance, demanding that they, as the vulnerable, lean upon him as the resilient. The established roles between him and Clara had turned about, yet only through the mechanism of profound devotion and thankfulness; for his only satisfaction lay in offering protection to the one who had so tirelessly nurtured him. “A remarkable sight,” mused Mr. Sterling with his characteristic kindly insight, “yet entirely fitting and proper; therefore, assume the command, my good friend, and hold it fast; it could not rest with a more capable soul.”
source_text: This new life of the Doctor’s was an anxious life, no doubt; still, the sagacious Mr. Lorry saw that there was a new sustaining pride in it. Nothing unbecoming tinged the pride; it was a natural and worthy one; but he observed it as a curiosity. The Doctor knew, that up to that time, his imprisonment had been associated in the minds of his daughter and his friend, with his personal affliction, deprivation, and weakness. Now that this was changed, and he knew himself to be invested through that old trial with forces to which they both looked for Charles’s ultimate safety and deliverance, he became so far exalted by the change, that he took the lead and direction, and required them as the weak, to trust to him as the strong. The preceding relative positions of himself and Lucie were reversed, yet only as the liveliest gratitude and affection could reverse them, for he could have had no pride but in rendering some service to her who had rendered so much to him. “All curious to see,” thought Mr. Lorry, in his amiably shrewd way, “but all natural and right; so, take the lead, my dear friend, and keep it; it couldn’t be in better hands.”

text: There was neither respite nor mercy, no stillness or moment of relief, and no way to gauge the passing hours. Although the sun and moon continued their cycles as they had since the dawn of creation, all other reckoning of time had vanished. Chronology was lost in the country’s violent delirium, much as a patient loses track of the world in the heat of a wasting fever. One moment, shattering the city's heavy silence, the executioner presented the king’s head to the masses; then, appearing to follow in the very next breath, came the head of his beautiful queen, whose hair had turned silver during eight long months of wretched, widowed captivity.
source_text: There was no pause, no pity, no peace, no interval of relenting rest, no measurement of time. Though days and nights circled as regularly as when time was young, and the evening and morning were the first day, other count of time there was none. Hold of it was lost in the raging fever of a nation, as it is in the fever of one patient. Now, breaking the unnatural silence of a whole city, the executioner showed the people the head of the king--and now, it seemed almost in the same breath, the head of his fair wife which had had eight weary months of imprisoned widowhood and misery, to turn it grey.

text: Nevertheless, by that singular law of inversion which governs such upheavals, the time felt interminable even as it raced by in a fever. A central court of retribution in the capital, supported by a vast web of local juntas across the provinces; a decree of universal suspicion that dissolved every safeguard of life, surrendering the righteous to the whims of the wicked; dungeons gorged with the blameless who were granted no audience—these horrors became the accepted rhythm of existence, assuming the weight of ancient custom before they were many weeks old. Above all, one grisly figure grew as familiar as the horizon, as if it had stood before the eyes of men since the world was forged—the steel-edged lady known as the Great Leveler.
source_text: And yet, observing the strange law of contradiction which obtains in all such cases, the time was long, while it flamed by so fast. A revolutionary tribunal in the capital, and forty or fifty thousand revolutionary committees all over the land; a law of the Suspected, which struck away all security for liberty or life, and delivered over any good and innocent person to any bad and guilty one; prisons gorged with people who had committed no offence, and could obtain no hearing; these things became the established order and nature of appointed things, and seemed to be ancient usage before they were many weeks old. Above all, one hideous figure grew as familiar as if it had been before the general gaze from the foundations of the world--the figure of the sharp female called La Guillotine.

text: It was the favorite subject of every joke; the ultimate remedy for migraines, a permanent fix for graying hair, and a secret for a refined complexion. It was the Republic's Razor, delivering the closest shave of all: those who embraced the blade peered through its small frame and sneezed their last into the basket. It symbolized the rebirth of the species, supplanting the crucifix. Miniature versions were worn over hearts that had abandoned the old faith; it was venerated and trusted where the Savior was now forsaken.
source_text: It was the popular theme for jests; it was the best cure for headache, it infallibly prevented the hair from turning grey, it imparted a peculiar delicacy to the complexion, it was the National Razor which shaved close: who kissed La Guillotine, looked through the little window and sneezed into the sack. It was the sign of the regeneration of the human race. It superseded the Cross. Models of it were worn on breasts from which the Cross was discarded, and it was bowed down to and believed in where the Cross was denied.

text: Amidst these monstrosities and the foul progeny they sired, the Healer moved with a fixed purpose: certain of his authority, patient in his design, never faltering in the conviction that he would ultimately rescue his son-in-law. Yet the deluge of the era surged past, so potent and bottomless, and dragged the days away with such violence, that Julian had languished in his cell for fifteen months even as the Healer stood firm in his hope. To such a degree of malice and frenzy had the Insurrection swelled by that bleak December, that the southern currents were clogged with the remains of those cast into the depths by night, and rows of captives were mowed down under the cold glare of the winter sun. Still, the Healer navigated the carnage with a tranquil spirit. No man was more familiar to the city in those dark hours; no man inhabited a more singular position. Muted, compassionate, and vital within both clinic and keep, bestowing his skill upon the slayer and the slain, he remained a figure isolated. Through the ministry of his art, the aura and the history of the Citadel Captive severed him from the rest of humanity. He was neither doubted nor challenged, appearing as one who had been summoned back from the void after twenty years of death, or as a specter gliding through the world of men.
source_text: Among these terrors, and the brood belonging to them, the Doctor walked with a steady head: confident in his power, cautiously persistent in his end, never doubting that he would save Lucie’s husband at last. Yet the current of the time swept by, so strong and deep, and carried the time away so fiercely, that Charles had lain in prison one year and three months when the Doctor was thus steady and confident. So much more wicked and distracted had the Revolution grown in that December month, that the rivers of the South were encumbered with the bodies of the violently drowned by night, and prisoners were shot in lines and squares under the southern wintry sun. Still, the Doctor walked among the terrors with a steady head. No man better known than he, in Paris at that day; no man in a stranger situation. Silent, humane, indispensable in hospital and prison, using his art equally among assassins and victims, he was a man apart. In the exercise of his skill, the appearance and the story of the Bastille Captive removed him from all other men. He was not suspected or brought in question, any more than if he had indeed been recalled to life some eighteen years before, or were a Spirit moving among mortals.

text: Her outward appearance did not change significantly. The somber, dark dresses she and her daughter wore—reminiscent of funeral attire—were kept just as tidy and well-maintained as the vibrant clothes from their happier years. Though she grew pale and the look of intense worry that once appeared only briefly became a permanent fixture on her face, she remained quite lovely. In the evenings, while kissing her father goodnight, the grief she had suppressed all day would often break through, and she would tell him that, besides God, he was her only source of hope. He always replied with unwavering certainty: “Nothing can happen to him without my being aware of it, and I am sure I can save him, Lucie.”
source_text: She did not greatly alter in appearance. The plain dark dresses, akin to mourning dresses, which she and her child wore, were as neat and as well attended to as the brighter clothes of happy days. She lost her colour, and the old and intent expression was a constant, not an occasional, thing; otherwise, she remained very pretty and comely. Sometimes, at night on kissing her father, she would burst into the grief she had repressed all day, and would say that her sole reliance, under Heaven, was on him. He always resolutely answered: “Nothing can happen to him without my knowledge, and I know that I can save him, Lucie.”

text: Yet, he was not distant, for shortly she detected a restless commotion and a chorus of yells drawing near, which struck a cold dread into her heart. Seconds later, a torrent of people surged around the angle of the fortress wall, headed by the carpenter hand-in-hand with the Fury. There were surely five hundred in the throng, yet they capered with the frantic energy of five thousand devils. No music spurred them but their own raucous chanting. They moved to the anthem of the revolt, keeping a brutal rhythm that sounded like the synchronized gnashing of teeth. Men and women joined together, women with women, men with men, as the whims of the crowd dictated. At the start, they appeared as a mere whirlwind of crimson caps and coarse rags; but as they occupied the space and halted to gyrate around Elena, a ghostly image of a dance turned to lunacy rose among them. They lunged, retreated, slapped at each other’s palms, seized one another’s hair, spun in solitary circles, and then locked together to whirl in pairs until many collapsed from fatigue. While those lay gasping, the rest linked arms and spiraled in a great ring; then the circle broke into smaller wheels of two and four, turning and turning until they stopped as one, only to begin again, striking, clutching, and tearing, before reversing their motion to spin the other way. Suddenly they went still, paused, caught the beat anew, formed a line the width of the street, and, with heads ducked low and arms flung high, swept away in a screaming tide. No combat could have been as hideous as this celebration. It was the very essence of a fallen grace—a thing once wholesome now abandoned to the diabolical—a vigorous sport mutated into a poison for the blood and a stone for the heart. Whatever beauty lingered in the movement only made it more loathsome, proving how warped the gifts of nature had become. The virginal chest bared to the cold, the youthful head lost to such distraction, the dainty foot stepping through this sludge of gore and grime, were the true symbols of a world out of joint.
source_text: But, he was not far off, for presently she heard a troubled movement and a shouting coming along, which filled her with fear. A moment afterwards, and a throng of people came pouring round the corner by the prison wall, in the midst of whom was the wood-sawyer hand in hand with The Vengeance. There could not be fewer than five hundred people, and they were dancing like five thousand demons. There was no other music than their own singing. They danced to the popular Revolution song, keeping a ferocious time that was like a gnashing of teeth in unison. Men and women danced together, women danced together, men danced together, as hazard had brought them together. At first, they were a mere storm of coarse red caps and coarse woollen rags; but, as they filled the place, and stopped to dance about Lucie, some ghastly apparition of a dance-figure gone raving mad arose among them. They advanced, retreated, struck at one another’s hands, clutched at one another’s heads, spun round alone, caught one another and spun round in pairs, until many of them dropped. While those were down, the rest linked hand in hand, and all spun round together: then the ring broke, and in separate rings of two and four they turned and turned until they all stopped at once, began again, struck, clutched, and tore, and then reversed the spin, and all spun round another way. Suddenly they stopped again, paused, struck out the time afresh, formed into lines the width of the public way, and, with their heads low down and their hands high up, swooped screaming off. No fight could have been half so terrible as this dance. It was so emphatically a fallen sport--a something, once innocent, delivered over to all devilry--a healthy pastime changed into a means of angering the blood, bewildering the senses, and steeling the heart. Such grace as was visible in it, made it the uglier, showing how warped and perverted all things good by nature were become. The maidenly bosom bared to this, the pretty almost-child’s head thus distracted, the delicate foot mincing in this slough of blood and dirt, were types of the disjointed time.

text: The steadfast old gentleman remained at his post, having never once abandoned his duty. He and his ledgers were frequently called upon regarding assets that had been seized and declared national property. Whatever he could manage to preserve for the rightful owners, he saved. There was no man alive better suited to guard the interests of Tellson’s or to maintain a discreet silence.
source_text: The staunch old gentleman was still in his trust; had never left it. He and his books were in frequent requisition as to property confiscated and made national. What he could save for the owners, he saved. No better man living to hold fast by what Tellson’s had in keeping, and to hold his peace.

text: A horizon of bruised copper and sulfur, paired with the damp exhalations of the river, marked the onset of night. The shadows were deep as they pulled up before the Exchange. That formerly grand seat of the Aristocrat lay withered and forsaken. Across a blackened heap of refuse in the square, the bold script proclaimed: State Possession. The Republic, Whole and Singular. Liberty, Parity, Comity—or the Scaffold!
source_text: A murky red and yellow sky, and a rising mist from the Seine, denoted the approach of darkness. It was almost dark when they arrived at the Bank. The stately residence of Monseigneur was altogether blighted and deserted. Above a heap of dust and ashes in the court, ran the letters: National Property. Republic One and Indivisible. Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death!

text: Brief mercies were exchanged in haste, yet the separation concluded with startling speed. Such departures were the rhythm of the hour; the denizens of the Abbaye were already occupied with arranging parlor games and a modest musical recital for the night ahead. Though many pressed against the iron bars in a brief paroxysm of grief, a score of vacancies in the evening’s festivities required immediate substitutes. The interval before the final bolting of doors was fleeting, after which the halls would be surrendered to the prowling hounds that patrolled the gloom until dawn. These captives were neither callous nor indifferent; rather, their stoicism was a byproduct of an era defined by ruin. In a related fashion, a certain manic exaltation—a delirium that drove many to court the blade with reckless unconcern—was no simple display of vanity. It was a fevered contagion born of a society unmoored from its foundations. During an outbreak of plague, there exists within the soul a perverse lure toward the infection—a fleeting, horrific desire to succumb to the very thing that kills. Within every human heart dwell such strange mysteries, lying dormant until the right catastrophe summons them into the light.
source_text: There were hurried words of farewell and kindness, but the parting was soon over. It was the incident of every day, and the society of La Force were engaged in the preparation of some games of forfeits and a little concert, for that evening. They crowded to the grates and shed tears there; but, twenty places in the projected entertainments had to be refilled, and the time was, at best, short to the lock-up hour, when the common rooms and corridors would be delivered over to the great dogs who kept watch there through the night. The prisoners were far from insensible or unfeeling; their ways arose out of the condition of the time. Similarly, though with a subtle difference, a species of fervour or intoxication, known, without doubt, to have led some persons to brave the guillotine unnecessarily, and to die by it, was not mere boastfulness, but a wild infection of the wildly shaken public mind. In seasons of pestilence, some of us will have a secret attraction to the disease--a terrible passing inclination to die of it. And all of us have like wonders hidden in our breasts, only needing circumstances to evoke them.

text: The response resonated favorably with the assembly. Acclamations for the celebrated doctor thundered through the hall. Such was the volatility of the crowd’s passions that weeping instantly softened the brutal features of those who had just been scowling at the defendant with a murderous eagerness to haul him into the square and end his life.
source_text: This answer had a happy effect upon the audience. Cries in exaltation of the well-known good physician rent the hall. So capriciously were the people moved, that tears immediately rolled down several ferocious countenances which had been glaring at the prisoner a moment before, as if with impatience to pluck him out into the streets and kill him.

text: Professor Valerius was called to testify next. His profound standing in the public eye, coupled with the crystalline precision of his responses, struck a powerful chord; yet, as he continued, illustrating how the Prisoner had been his first true companion following his liberation from those decades of gloom; how the young man had remained in London, an unwavering guardian to both the Professor and his daughter throughout their displacement; and how, rather than being an ally to the British Monarchy, he had stood trial for his very life as a detractor of the Crown and a supporter of the American cause—as he laid out these facts with measured grace and the undeniable weight of sincerity, the hearts of the Tribunal and the rabble beat as one. When he eventually summoned the testimony of Mr. Sterling, a British gentleman in the gallery who had observed that foreign trial and could attest to its veracity, the jurors cried out that they had heard all they required, signaling their readiness to deliver a verdict should the Magistrate permit it.
source_text: Doctor Manette was next questioned. His high personal popularity, and the clearness of his answers, made a great impression; but, as he proceeded, as he showed that the Accused was his first friend on his release from his long imprisonment; that, the accused had remained in England, always faithful and devoted to his daughter and himself in their exile; that, so far from being in favour with the Aristocrat government there, he had actually been tried for his life by it, as the foe of England and friend of the United States--as he brought these circumstances into view, with the greatest discretion and with the straightforward force of truth and earnestness, the Jury and the populace became one. At last, when he appealed by name to Monsieur Lorry, an English gentleman then and there present, who, like himself, had been a witness on that English trial and could corroborate his account of it, the Jury declared that they had heard enough, and that they were ready with their votes if the President were content to receive them.

text: They hoisted him into a massive chair they had scavenged, perhaps from the courtroom itself or one of the nearby hallways. A red banner had been draped over it, and a pike topped with a crimson cap was lashed to the back. In this makeshift chariot of victory, despite the Doctor’s desperate pleas, he was carried toward his home on the shoulders of the mob. A chaotic sea of red caps surged all around him, throwing up such ravaged and distorted faces from its turbulent depths that he more than once questioned his own sanity, fearing he was actually in a tumbril on his way to the guillotine.
source_text: They put him into a great chair they had among them, and which they had taken either out of the Court itself, or one of its rooms or passages. Over the chair they had thrown a red flag, and to the back of it they had bound a pike with a red cap on its top. In this car of triumph, not even the Doctor’s entreaties could prevent his being carried to his home on men’s shoulders, with a confused sea of red caps heaving about him, and casting up to sight from the stormy deep such wrecks of faces, that he more than once misdoubted his mind being in confusion, and that he was in the tumbril on his way to the Guillotine.

text: In a surreal and frantic parade, embracing everyone they encountered and singling him out for all to see, they bore him onward. They stained the snowy avenues with the dominant revolutionary red as they wound through the city, just as they had once soaked those same streets in a darker crimson beneath the winter frost. In this fashion, they delivered him to the courtyard of his home. Her father had hurried ahead to prepare her; when her husband finally stood on his own feet before her, she fell senseless into his arms.
source_text: In wild dreamlike procession, embracing whom they met and pointing him out, they carried him on. Reddening the snowy streets with the prevailing Republican colour, in winding and tramping through them, as they had reddened them below the snow with a deeper dye, they carried him thus into the courtyard of the building where he lived. Her father had gone on before, to prepare her, and when her husband stood upon his feet, she dropped insensible in his arms.

text: As he gathered her to his breast, shielding her striking features from the riotous mob so that his salt-tears and her mouth might meet in a private grief, a small knot of the rabble began to sway. Suddenly, the fever took hold of every soul, and the plaza buckled under the weight of a manic reel. They hoisted a common girl into the abandoned seat to reign as the Spirit of Emancipation; then, cascading through the narrow lanes, flanking the dark river, and surging across the span, the wild dance ensnared them all and carried them into the distance.
source_text: As he held her to his heart and turned her beautiful head between his face and the brawling crowd, so that his tears and her lips might come together unseen, a few of the people fell to dancing. Instantly, all the rest fell to dancing, and the courtyard overflowed with the Carmagnole. Then, they elevated into the vacant chair a young woman from the crowd to be carried as the Goddess of Liberty, and then swelling and overflowing out into the adjacent streets, and along the river’s bank, and over the bridge, the Carmagnole absorbed them every one and whirled them away.

text: “Quite so, quite so, I shall be as wary as a cat,” declared Miss Tibbs; “though I must confess to this company my fervent wish that we encounter no garlic-scented or pipe-reeking displays of communal hugging out in the thoroughfares. Now, my Petal, do not dream of wandering from this fireside until I am once more among you! Tend to the beloved husband you have reclaimed, and keep your sweet face nestled upon his shoulder exactly as it rests this moment, until you see me again! Might I put a single inquiry to you, Dr. Sterling, before I take my leave?”
source_text: “Yes, yes, yes, I’ll be cautious,” said Miss Pross; “but I may say among ourselves, that I do hope there will be no oniony and tobaccoey smotherings in the form of embracings all round, going on in the streets. Now, Ladybird, never you stir from that fire till I come back! Take care of the dear husband you have recovered, and don’t move your pretty head from his shoulder as you have it now, till you see me again! May I ask a question, Doctor Manette, before I go?”

text: Mercifully ignorant of the burgeoning tragedy at their dwelling, Miss Thorne threaded through the suffocating lanes and crossed the span of the Old Bridge, her mind occupied with the inventory of necessary goods she must acquire. Beside her, carrying the wicker hamper, walked Silas. Both kept a vigilant watch upon the shops they passed, casting suspicious glances at every congregating crowd and altering their course to shun any knot of particularly fevered orators. It was a biting dusk; the vaporous river, obscured by flickering cressets and the clamor of iron, revealed the moored vessels where armorers toiled, forging weapons for the Revolutionary Host. Woe betide the wretch who deceived that legion or climbed its ranks through treachery! Better that his chin had stayed forever smooth, for the People’s Shearer took a head as easily as a whisker.
source_text: Happily unconscious of the new calamity at home, Miss Pross threaded her way along the narrow streets and crossed the river by the bridge of the Pont-Neuf, reckoning in her mind the number of indispensable purchases she had to make. Mr. Cruncher, with the basket, walked at her side. They both looked to the right and to the left into most of the shops they passed, had a wary eye for all gregarious assemblages of people, and turned out of their road to avoid any very excited group of talkers. It was a raw evening, and the misty river, blurred to the eye with blazing lights and to the ear with harsh noises, showed where the barges were stationed in which the smiths worked, making guns for the Army of the Republic. Woe to the man who played tricks with _that_ Army, or got undeserved promotion in it! Better for him that his beard had never grown, for the National Razor shaved him close.

text: “Come on,” Mr. Cruncher said. “Out with it, now.” (Which, incidentally, was more than he was usually capable of doing himself.) “Is it John Solomon or Solomon John? Your sister calls you Solomon, and she ought to know. But I know you as John. So, which of the two comes first? And there is the matter of the name Pross, as well. That wasn’t what you were called on the other side of the water.”
source_text: “Come!” said Mr. Cruncher. “Speak out, you know.” (Which, by the way, was more than he could do himself.) “John Solomon, or Solomon John? She calls you Solomon, and she must know, being your sister. And _I_ know you’re John, you know. Which of the two goes first? And regarding that name of Pross, likewise. That warn’t your name over the water.”

text: “Let me enlighten you,” Julian said softly. “My gaze chanced upon you, Mr. Vane, as you departed the shadows of the Châtelet while I stood observing its heavy walls some time ago. Your features possess a distinct quality, and I have a keen faculty for recollection. Spurred by the sight of you at that particular threshold, and mindful of your past link to the calamities of a friend who now languishes in sorrow, I took it upon myself to trail you. I followed you into this very cellar, keeping close, and found a place beside you. From your own reckless speech and the common talk of the ruffians who surround you, your true profession was easily unmasked. And so, what started as a wandering impulse has begun to take the form of a design, Mr. Vane.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you,” said Sydney. “I lighted on you, Mr. Barsad, coming out of the prison of the Conciergerie while I was contemplating the walls, an hour or more ago. You have a face to be remembered, and I remember faces well. Made curious by seeing you in that connection, and having a reason, to which you are no stranger, for associating you with the misfortunes of a friend now very unfortunate, I walked in your direction. I walked into the wine-shop here, close after you, and sat near you. I had no difficulty in deducing from your unreserved conversation, and the rumour openly going about among your admirers, the nature of your calling. And gradually, what I had done at random, seemed to shape itself into a purpose, Mr. Barsad.”

text: “On a different occasion, master,” he replied, shifting his ground, “for this here hour is ill-suited for the tellin’. What I stand by is this: he knows right well that old Miller weren’t never laid in that there casket. Let him swear he was, in so much as a single spit of a word, and I’ll either have him by the gullet and throttle him for a handful of silver;” Barnaby lingered on the remark, as if such a price were a gesture of great charity; “else I’ll step out and name him to the world.”
source_text: “At another time, sir,” he returned, evasively, “the present time is ill-conwenient for explainin’. What I stand to, is, that he knows well wot that there Cly was never in that there coffin. Let him say he was, in so much as a word of one syllable, and I’ll either catch hold of his throat and choke him for half a guinea;” Mr. Cruncher dwelt upon this as quite a liberal offer; “or I’ll out and announce him.”

text: “I am plagued by the conviction,” Mr. Sterling remarked, leveling a trembling finger at the man, “that you have exploited the venerable and distinguished firm of Halloway’s as a mere pretense, while pursuing a clandestine trade of a most scandalous sort. If this is so, anticipate no fellowship from me upon our arrival in London. If this is so, do not think I shall shield your disgrace. Halloway’s shall not be made a party to such villainy.”
source_text: “My mind misgives me much,” said Mr. Lorry, angrily shaking a forefinger at him, “that you have used the respectable and great house of Tellson’s as a blind, and that you have had an unlawful occupation of an infamous description. If you have, don’t expect me to befriend you when you get back to England. If you have, don’t expect me to keep your secret. Tellson’s shall not be imposed upon.”

text: “No, I certainly will not, sir,” Mr. Cruncher replied, as if the very idea were alien to his character—though he stopped short of a full denial. “What I would humbly suggest to you, sir, is this. On that stool by the counter sits my son, a boy I’ve raised to manhood who will run your errands, carry your messages, and perform any odd job until you are quite worn out, if that is your wish. If the situation were as you say—not that I’m admitting it, for I won’t lie to you—then let that boy take his father’s place and provide for his mother. Do not inform on the boy’s father, sir—I beg you—and let that father take up the trade of honest digging. Let him make amends for what he might have unburied—if such a thing ever happened—by burying them with a sincere will and a firm belief in keeping them safe hereafter. That, Mr. Lorry,” said Mr. Cruncher, wiping his brow with his arm to signal the conclusion of his speech, “is what I respectfully offer you. A man cannot see the dreadful things happening around him, all these headless bodies so common they are hardly worth the cost of transport, without being struck by serious reflections. These are mine, if things are indeed as you suspect, and I entreat you to remember that I spoke up for a good cause just now when I might have stayed silent.”
source_text: “No, I will _not_, sir,” returned Mr. Crunches as if nothing were further from his thoughts or practice--“which I don’t say it is--wot I would humbly offer to you, sir, would be this. Upon that there stool, at that there Bar, sets that there boy of mine, brought up and growed up to be a man, wot will errand you, message you, general-light-job you, till your heels is where your head is, if such should be your wishes. If it wos so, which I still don’t say it is (for I will not prewaricate to you, sir), let that there boy keep his father’s place, and take care of his mother; don’t blow upon that boy’s father--do not do it, sir--and let that father go into the line of the reg’lar diggin’, and make amends for what he would have undug--if it wos so--by diggin’ of ’em in with a will, and with conwictions respectin’ the futur’ keepin’ of ’em safe. That, Mr. Lorry,” said Mr. Cruncher, wiping his forehead with his arm, as an announcement that he had arrived at the peroration of his discourse, “is wot I would respectfully offer to you, sir. A man don’t see all this here a goin’ on dreadful round him, in the way of Subjects without heads, dear me, plentiful enough fur to bring the price down to porterage and hardly that, without havin’ his serious thoughts of things. And these here would be mine, if it wos so, entreatin’ of you fur to bear in mind that wot I said just now, I up and said in the good cause when I might have kep’ it back.”

text: “A myriad of anxieties might cloud her mind,” Julian remarked, “and each would serve only to deepen her distress. Let my name remain unspoken in her presence. Just as I maintained upon my arrival, it is far wiser that I avoid her sight altogether. I am capable of extending my aid, performing whatever humble tasks may benefit her, while remaining entirely unseen. You intend to visit her now, I trust? Her solitude must be quite overwhelming this evening.”
source_text: “She might think a thousand things,” Carton said, “and any of them would only add to her trouble. Don’t speak of me to her. As I said to you when I first came, I had better not see her. I can put my hand out, to do any little helpful work for her that my hand can find to do, without that. You are going to her, I hope? She must be very desolate to-night.”

text: Julian had scarcely slipped from view when he paused beneath the weak radiance of a guttering lamp to mark a scrap of paper with his lead. Resuming his pace with the unwavering gait of one who knew every turn by heart, he threaded through a succession of grim, fouled streets—alleys far more wretched than their wont, as even the primary arteries of the city lay abandoned to filth during those seasons of dread. He eventually halted at a druggist’s door just as the master was barring it with his own weary hands. It was a narrow, murky, warped little vault, tucked into a twisting, rising wynd, and kept by a narrow, murky, warped little man.
source_text: Sydney had not gone far out of sight, when he stopped in the middle of the street under a glimmering lamp, and wrote with his pencil on a scrap of paper. Then, traversing with the decided step of one who remembered the way well, several dark and dirty streets--much dirtier than usual, for the best public thoroughfares remained uncleansed in those times of terror--he stopped at a chemist’s shop, which the owner was closing with his own hands. A small, dim, crooked shop, kept in a tortuous, up-hill thoroughfare, by a small, dim, crooked man.

text: In a distant era, when his peers had celebrated him as a young man of remarkable potential, he had escorted his father to his final resting place. His mother had preceded them in death by many years. Those hallowed lines, recited at his father’s interment, surfaced in his memory as he navigated the dim avenues, amidst deep gloom, while the moon and the clouds drifted across the heavens far above. “I am the resurrection and the life, declares the Lord: he who has faith in me, though he were to die, yet shall he live; and everyone who lives and has faith in me shall never die.”
source_text: Long ago, when he had been famous among his earliest competitors as a youth of great promise, he had followed his father to the grave. His mother had died, years before. These solemn words, which had been read at his father’s grave, arose in his mind as he went down the dark streets, among the heavy shadows, with the moon and the clouds sailing on high above him. “I am the resurrection and the life, saith the Lord: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live: and whosoever liveth and believeth in me, shall never die.”

text: The night dragged on to its end. As he stood upon the bridge, listening to the river slapping against the stone walls of the Île de la Cité—where the scenic jumble of houses and the cathedral gleamed under the moon’s glow—a cold daybreak emerged, looking like a lifeless face staring down from the heavens. Then the night, along with the moon and the stars, turned ashen and passed away; for a brief interval, it seemed as though all of creation had been surrendered to the sovereignty of death.
source_text: The night wore out, and, as he stood upon the bridge listening to the water as it splashed the river-walls of the Island of Paris, where the picturesque confusion of houses and cathedral shone bright in the light of the moon, the day came coldly, looking like a dead face out of the sky. Then, the night, with the moon and the stars, turned pale and died, and for a little while it seemed as if Creation were delivered over to Death’s dominion.

text: “I was certain,” Villon declared, casting his gaze down toward Elise, who remained at the foot of the dais, her eyes fixed unwaveringly upon his own. “I was aware that the captive in question had been interred within a chamber designated as Cell Sixty-Four of the West Wing. This fact I learned from his own lips. While he labored at the cobbler’s bench under my supervision, he recognized no identity save for that of Sixty-Four, West Wing. On the day I manned the cannon, I vowed that should the fortress be taken, I would search that very room. The stronghold yielded. Accompanied by a turnkey and a compatriot from the Council, I ascended to the quarters. I scrutinized the space with the utmost care. Within a cavity in the hearth, where a block had been loosened and then reset, I discovered a manuscript. This is the document I recovered. I have taken pains to study various examples of Professor Valerius’s script. This script belongs to Professor Valerius. I now entrust this record, penned by the hand of Professor Valerius, to the custody of the Magistrate.”
source_text: “I knew,” said Defarge, looking down at his wife, who stood at the bottom of the steps on which he was raised, looking steadily up at him; “I knew that this prisoner, of whom I speak, had been confined in a cell known as One Hundred and Five, North Tower. I knew it from himself. He knew himself by no other name than One Hundred and Five, North Tower, when he made shoes under my care. As I serve my gun that day, I resolve, when the place shall fall, to examine that cell. It falls. I mount to the cell, with a fellow-citizen who is one of the Jury, directed by a gaoler. I examine it, very closely. In a hole in the chimney, where a stone has been worked out and replaced, I find a written paper. This is that written paper. I have made it my business to examine some specimens of the writing of Doctor Manette. This is the writing of Doctor Manette. I confide this paper, in the writing of Doctor Manette, to the hands of the President.”

text: I, Alexandre Manette, a luckless physician born in Beauvais and later a resident of Paris, compose this sorrowful testament within my grim cell in the Bastille during the final month of 1767. I write in snatched moments, struggling against every hardship. I intend to hide these pages within the chimney wall, where I have painstakingly carved a secret hollow to receive them. Perhaps a compassionate hand will discover them there, long after I and my grief have returned to the earth.
source_text: “I, Alexandre Manette, unfortunate physician, native of Beauvais, and afterwards resident in Paris, write this melancholy paper in my doleful cell in the Bastille, during the last month of the year, 1767. I write it at stolen intervals, under every difficulty. I design to secrete it in the wall of the chimney, where I have slowly and laboriously made a place of concealment for it. Some pitying hand may find it there, when I and my sorrows are dust.

text: I noted that the two figures were muffled in heavy capes, seemingly anxious to escape recognition. As they stood together by the coach’s entrance, I perceived that they were roughly my own age, or perhaps a trifle younger; they were, moreover, singularly alike in height, in gait, in the very pitch of their voices, and, so far as I could judge, in countenance also.
source_text: “I observed that they were both wrapped in cloaks, and appeared to conceal themselves. As they stood side by side near the carriage door, I also observed that they both looked of about my own age, or rather younger, and that they were greatly alike, in stature, manner, voice, and (as far as I could see) face too.

text: The coach rumbled beyond the city’s edge, clearing the Northern Gate to meet the winding country road. Scarcely two miles from the threshold—a span I only calculated later upon retracing those steps—the vehicle veered from the main thoroughfare, coming to a halt before a desolate manor. The three of us dismounted and followed a sodden, yielding trail through a courtyard where a choked fountain spilled over its rim, advancing toward the entrance. When the bell’s summons went unanswered for a moment too long, one of my escorts lashed out, striking the servant who finally appeared across the mouth with a weighted gauntlet.
source_text: “The carriage left the streets behind, passed the North Barrier, and emerged upon the country road. At two-thirds of a league from the Barrier--I did not estimate the distance at that time, but afterwards when I traversed it--it struck out of the main avenue, and presently stopped at a solitary house, We all three alighted, and walked, by a damp soft footpath in a garden where a neglected fountain had overflowed, to the door of the house. It was not opened immediately, in answer to the ringing of the bell, and one of my two conductors struck the man who opened it, with his heavy riding glove, across the face.

text: Upon a scattering of straw, with a pillow wedged beneath his skull, rested a lad of striking features—a country youth of perhaps seventeen years. He lay rigid, teeth locked and right fist knotted against his breast, his wide eyes burning with a fixed and upward stare. Though I bent a knee beside him, the place of his wound remained hidden; yet, it was clear that he was expiring from the deep puncture of some pointed steel.
source_text: “On some hay on the ground, with a cushion thrown under his head, lay a handsome peasant boy--a boy of not more than seventeen at the most. He lay on his back, with his teeth set, his right hand clenched on his breast, and his glaring eyes looking straight upward. I could not see where his wound was, as I kneeled on one knee over him; but, I could see that he was dying of a wound from a sharp point.

text: “Then, with that individual’s consent and even his assistance, his brother abducted her. Regardless of what I am certain she told him—revelations that will soon be made known to you, Doctor, if they are not already—the brother took her for his own brief amusement and whim. I watched her pass me on the road. When I delivered the news, my father’s heart shattered; he died without ever uttering the words that weighed upon his soul. I took my younger sister—for I have another—to a place beyond this man’s power, where she would at least never be *his* vassal. Afterward, I pursued the brother here, and last night I scaled the walls—a common dog, perhaps, but one with a sword in hand. Where is the loft window? It was somewhere in this spot.”
source_text: “‘Then, with that man’s permission and even with his aid, his brother took her away; in spite of what I know she must have told his brother--and what that is, will not be long unknown to you, Doctor, if it is now--his brother took her away--for his pleasure and diversion, for a little while. I saw her pass me on the road. When I took the tidings home, our father’s heart burst; he never spoke one of the words that filled it. I took my young sister (for I have another) to a place beyond the reach of this man, and where, at least, she will never be _his_ vassal. Then, I tracked the brother here, and last night climbed in--a common dog, but sword in hand.--Where is the loft window? It was somewhere here?’

text: I administered the medications once more and remained at her bedside until the night was nearly spent. Her piercing shrieks never lost their sharp intensity, nor did she ever falter in the clarity or the sequence of her speech. They were always the same: “My husband, my father, and my brother! One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, eleven, twelve. Hush!”
source_text: “I repeated the medicines I had given her, and I sat at the side of the bed until the night was far advanced. She never abated the piercing quality of her shrieks, never stumbled in the distinctness or the order of her words. They were always ‘My husband, my father, and my brother! One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, eleven, twelve. Hush!’

text: My pen moves with agonizing slowness, for the chill is unrelenting and I live in constant dread of being exposed and entombed in a lightless, subterranean vault; thus, I must shorten this chronicle. My mind, however, remains unclouded; it can summon, and could recount in full, every syllable ever traded between myself and those kinsmen.
source_text: “I write with so much difficulty, the cold is so severe, I am so fearful of being detected and consigned to an underground cell and total darkness, that I must abridge this narrative. There is no confusion or failure in my memory; it can recall, and could detail, every word that was ever spoken between me and those brothers.

text: At daybreak, a small box addressed to me was left at my door, containing a roll of gold coins. I had been weighing my options carefully from the start. That afternoon, I resolved to write a private letter to the Minister, detailing the two patients I had treated, the place I had gone, and every circumstance of the event. I understood the reach of the Court and the legal immunity of the nobility, and I expected my report to be ignored; nonetheless, I needed to unburden my mind. I had kept the matter strictly confidential, even from my wife, a fact I planned to include in my letter. I had no fear for my own safety, but I realized that others could be endangered if they were compromised by the knowledge I held.
source_text: “Early in the morning, the rouleau of gold was left at my door in a little box, with my name on the outside. From the first, I had anxiously considered what I ought to do. I decided, that day, to write privately to the Minister, stating the nature of the two cases to which I had been summoned, and the place to which I had gone: in effect, stating all the circumstances. I knew what Court influence was, and what the immunities of the Nobles were, and I expected that the matter would never be heard of; but, I wished to relieve my own mind. I had kept the matter a profound secret, even from my wife; and this, too, I resolved to state in my letter. I had no apprehension whatever of my real danger; but I was conscious that there might be danger for others, if others were compromised by possessing the knowledge that I possessed.

text: She was a gentle, merciful woman, though her marriage was a landscape of grief. How could she find peace? Her brother-in-law watched her with a dark, suspicious eye, his every word turned against her; she trembled before his authority as she did before her husband’s. As I helped her to the carriage, I saw a child within—a lovely boy of some two or three years.
source_text: “She was a good, compassionate lady, and not happy in her marriage. How could she be! The brother distrusted and disliked her, and his influence was all opposed to her; she stood in dread of him, and in dread of her husband too. When I handed her down to the door, there was a child, a pretty boy from two to three years old, in her carriage.

text: “Had the Almighty seen fit to move the cold hearts of either brother during these horrific years, allowing me even a shred of news regarding my beloved wife—just a single word to say if she lived or had died—I might have felt they weren't completely beyond His grace. But I am now convinced that the sign of the red cross has sealed their doom, and they are excluded from His mercy. Therefore, on this final night of 1767, I, Alexandre Manette, a wretched prisoner in my deepest suffering, denounce them and every one of their descendants to the eventual day of judgment. I condemn them before both Heaven and earth.”
source_text: “If it had pleased _God_ to put it in the hard heart of either of the brothers, in all these frightful years, to grant me any tidings of my dearest wife--so much as to let me know by a word whether alive or dead--I might have thought that He had not quite abandoned them. But, now I believe that the mark of the red cross is fatal to them, and that they have no part in His mercies. And them and their descendants, to the last of their race, I, Alexandre Manette, unhappy prisoner, do this last night of the year 1767, in my unbearable agony, denounce to the times when all these things shall be answered for. I denounce them to Heaven and to earth.”

text: It was unnecessary, before such a court and such a throng, to recount how the Morels had sequestered the parchment from the other relics paraded through the streets when the Fortress fell, or why they had nursed their secret until the hour of vengeance ripened. It was equally plain that this abhorred patronymic had been damned for years by the denizens of the Black Quarter, its every letter entwined within the grim record of the doomed. No mortal had ever lived whose goodness or deeds might have upheld him in that chamber on that day, set against the weight of so fierce a condemnation.
source_text: Little need, in presence of that tribunal and that auditory, to show how the Defarges had not made the paper public, with the other captured Bastille memorials borne in procession, and had kept it, biding their time. Little need to show that this detested family name had long been anathematised by Saint Antoine, and was wrought into the fatal register. The man never trod ground whose virtues and services would have sustained him in that place that day, against such denunciation.

text: It was all the more tragic for the condemned man that his accuser was a respected citizen, his own devoted friend, and his father-in-law. The mob was gripped by a fanatical desire to emulate the brutal civic virtues of the ancient world, craving sacrifices and self-destruction at the altar of the state.
