Thus, when the President announced—knowing his own head might otherwise roll—that the Republic’s faithful doctor would earn even greater merit by purging a loathsome family of aristocrats, and would surely feel a divine joy in making his daughter a widow and her child an orphan, the room exploded with patriotic madness, completely stripped of any human compassion.
source_text: And all the worse for the doomed man, that the denouncer was a well-known citizen, his own attached friend, the father of his wife. One of the frenzied aspirations of the populace was, for imitations of the questionable public virtues of antiquity, and for sacrifices and self-immolations on the people’s altar. Therefore when the President said (else had his own head quivered on his shoulders), that the good physician of the Republic would deserve better still of the Republic by rooting out an obnoxious family of Aristocrats, and would doubtless feel a sacred glow and joy in making his daughter a widow and her child an orphan, there was wild excitement, patriotic fervour, not a touch of human sympathy.

text: Rise, I beg of you! There is no reason for you to bow low in our presence. We understand now the immense trial you weathered in the days of old. We recognize the internal storm you endured when you first glimpsed the truth of my kin, and the weight you carried once it was confirmed. We see the instinctive loathing you wrestled with and defeated, purely for her beloved sake. Our hearts overflow with thanks and unwavering loyalty. May the grace of the heavens rest upon you!
source_text: “No, no! What have you done, what have you done, that you should kneel to us! We know now, what a struggle you made of old. We know, now what you underwent when you suspected my descent, and when you knew it. We know now, the natural antipathy you strove against, and conquered, for her dear sake. We thank you with all our hearts, and all our love and duty. Heaven be with you!”

text: At that moment, stepping from the lightless niche where he had remained motionless, Julian Vane moved to lift her. Only her father and the clerk, Mr. Halloway, stood by. A tremor ran through his arm as he raised her and pillowed her head upon him; yet, his bearing held something more than mere commiseration—a sudden, rising glow of hidden pride.
source_text: Then, issuing from the obscure corner from which he had never moved, Sydney Carton came and took her up. Only her father and Mr. Lorry were with her. His arm trembled as it raised her, and supported her head. Yet, there was an air about him that was not all of pity--that had a flush of pride in it.

text: Sydney Carton lingered in the street, momentarily uncertain of his next move. “Nine o'clock at Tellson’s Bank,” he murmured, his expression clouded with thought. “Would it be wise to make myself visible in the meantime? I believe so. It is best that these people recognize that a man like me is here; it is a prudent safeguard and perhaps a vital groundwork for what is to come. But I must be careful, incredibly careful. I need to think this through completely.”
source_text: Sydney Carton paused in the street, not quite decided where to go. “At Tellson’s banking-house at nine,” he said, with a musing face. “Shall I do well, in the mean time, to show myself? I think so. It is best that these people should know there is such a man as I here; it is a sound precaution, and may be a necessary preparation. But care, care, care! Let me think it out!”

text: Halting the progress of his feet, which had begun to drift toward a specific destination, he paced the length of the twilight-shrouded lane, weighing the gravity of his intended course. His initial intuition remained unshaken. “It is necessary,” he murmured, his resolve hardening, “that these inhabitants recognize a man of my character is in their midst.” With that, he set his sights upon the quarter of Saint-Victor.
source_text: Checking his steps which had begun to tend towards an object, he took a turn or two in the already darkening street, and traced the thought in his mind to its possible consequences. His first impression was confirmed. “It is best,” he said, finally resolved, “that these people should know there is such a man as I here.” And he turned his face towards Saint Antoine.

text: Defarge returned to the bar and remarked, "He does bear a slight resemblance." Madame countered with a sharp edge, "I tell you, he looks very much like him." Jacques Three spoke up in a soothing tone, "He occupies your thoughts so completely, you see, madame." The amiable Vengeance added with a mocking laugh, "Indeed! And you are anticipating the great pleasure of seeing him once more tomorrow!"
source_text: Defarge went back to the counter, and said, “Certainly, a little like.” Madame sternly retorted, “I tell you a good deal like.” Jacques Three pacifically remarked, “He is so much in your mind, see you, madame.” The amiable Vengeance added, with a laugh, “Yes, my faith! And you are looking forward with so much pleasure to seeing him once more to-morrow!”

text: "I’ve kept a close watch on his daughter," Madame stated once more. "I’ve scrutinized her on several occasions. I saw her today, and I’ve seen her many times before. I’ve tracked her in the courtroom and along the road by the prison. If I were to merely raise a finger—!" She seemed to lift it—the man listening never looked up from his documents—and then let it strike the counter with a sharp, wooden crack, as if the heavy blade had just plummeted.
source_text: “I have observed his daughter,” repeated madame; “yes, I have observed his daughter, more times than one. I have observed her to-day, and I have observed her other days. I have observed her in the court, and I have observed her in the street by the prison. Let me but lift my finger--!” She seemed to raise it (the listener’s eyes were always on his paper), and to let it fall with a rattle on the ledge before her, as if the axe had dropped.

text: At the dawn of those momentous days, when the Bastille was stormed, he discovered this very document and brought it back. In the dead of night, once this room was empty and the doors were barred, we read it together right here, under the glow of this lamp. Ask him yourself if that is not the truth.
source_text: “In the beginning of the great days, when the Bastille falls, he finds this paper of to-day, and he brings it home, and in the middle of the night when this place is clear and shut, we read it, here on this spot, by the light of this lamp. Ask him, is that so.”

text: Our final opportunity has vanished, and it was a slim one at best. Yes, it is better that he be brought to her now. But before you depart, I need you to give me your full attention for a moment. Do not question the conditions I am about to set or the pledge I am going to demand; I have my reasons, and they are sound ones.
source_text: “The last chance is gone: it was not much. Yes; he had better be taken to her. But, before you go, will you, for a moment, steadily attend to me? Don’t ask me why I make the stipulations I am going to make, and exact the promise I am going to exact; I have a reason--a good one.”

text: Carton leaned down to retrieve the coat that lay tangled about his feet. As he did, a small case the Doctor used for his daily schedules slipped out and fell softly to the floor. Carton picked it up and found a folded paper tucked inside. “We must examine this!” he urged, and Mr. Lorry signaled his approval. He opened the document and cried out, “Thank God!”
source_text: Carton stooped to pick up the coat, which lay almost entangling his feet. As he did so, a small case in which the Doctor was accustomed to carry the lists of his day’s duties, fell lightly on the floor. Carton took it up, and there was a folded paper in it. “We should look at this!” he said. Mr. Lorry nodded his consent. He opened it, and exclaimed, “Thank _God!_”

text: Your wealth is sufficient to purchase the swiftest transit to the shoreline. Your affairs have been in order for days, awaiting only the signal to depart for London. Early tomorrow, see that the carriage-team is readied, so that they stand poised for the journey precisely at the second hour of the afternoon.
source_text: “You have money, and can buy the means of travelling to the seacoast as quickly as the journey can be made. Your preparations have been completed for some days, to return to England. Early to-morrow have your horses ready, so that they may be in starting trim at two o’clock in the afternoon.”

text: Yet, it was no simple feat to steady his soul for the coming trial while the vision of his adored companion lingered so clearly in his mind. His grasp upon vitality was tenacious, and relinquishing it proved an arduous struggle; as he labored to pry one part free, his grip only tightened elsewhere, and the moment he compelled one hand to let go, the other would seize hold with fresh vigor. A restless urgency drove his every thought, a violent and fevered agitation of the breast that fought bitterly against the spirit of submission. Should he find even a fleeting instant of peace, the thought of the spouse and child who were to survive him seemed to cry out in defiance, branding his surrender as a mere act of self-indulgence.
source_text: Nevertheless, it was not easy, with the face of his beloved wife fresh before him, to compose his mind to what it must bear. His hold on life was strong, and it was very, very hard, to loosen; by gradual efforts and degrees unclosed a little here, it clenched the tighter there; and when he brought his strength to bear on that hand and it yielded, this was closed again. There was a hurry, too, in all his thoughts, a turbulent and heated working of his heart, that contended against resignation. If, for a moment, he did feel resigned, then his wife and child who had to live after him, seemed to protest and to make it a selfish thing.

text: Yet sleep pulled him back, revealing itself in glowing visions. He felt liberated and joyful, returned to the old house in Soho—though it bore no real resemblance to the house he knew. With an inexplicably light heart, he was with Lucie again, and she assured him that everything had been a dream, that he had never truly departed. After a brief lapse in memory, he felt as though he had already suffered and come back to her, deceased but at peace, yet somehow still himself. Another period of darkness passed, and then he awoke to the dismal morning, momentarily unaware of where he was or what had occurred, until the thought suddenly pierced his mind: "This is the day of my death!"
source_text: But, it beckoned him back in his sleep, and showed itself in shining forms. Free and happy, back in the old house in Soho (though it had nothing in it like the real house), unaccountably released and light of heart, he was with Lucie again, and she told him it was all a dream, and he had never gone away. A pause of forgetfulness, and then he had even suffered, and had come back to her, dead and at peace, and yet there was no difference in him. Another pause of oblivion, and he awoke in the sombre morning, unconscious where he was or what had happened, until it flashed upon his mind, “this is the day of my death!”

text: The metal bit grated within the lock and turned. Just as the door creaked open, a man spoke in a low, English murmur: “My face is a mystery to him; I have stayed well out of his sight. You must enter alone; I will keep watch close by. Do not linger!”
source_text: The key was put in the lock, and turned. Before the door was opened, or as it opened, a man said in a low voice, in English: “He has never seen me here; I have kept out of his way. Go you in alone; I wait near. Lose no time!”

text: “‘Had the path been different;’” Julian’s fingers again descended with a stealthy and vigilant grace; “‘I should never have exploited the longer reprieve. Had the path been different;’” his palm now hovered at the captive’s brow; “‘I would only have had more to account for in the final tally. Had the path been—’” Julian peered at the nib and saw it wandering into a series of senseless, drifting strokes.
source_text: “‘If it had been otherwise;’” Carton’s hand was again watchfully and softly stealing down; “‘I never should have used the longer opportunity. If it had been otherwise;’” the hand was at the prisoner’s face; “‘I should but have had so much the more to answer for. If it had been otherwise--’” Carton looked at the pen and saw it was trailing off into unintelligible signs.

text: Swiftly, yet with hands as steadfast as his own resolve, Carton donned the garments the captive had discarded, smoothing back his hair and securing it with the ribbon the prisoner had worn. Then, in a low voice, he called out, "Come in! Enter!" and the spy appeared before him.
source_text: Quickly, but with hands as true to the purpose as his heart was, Carton dressed himself in the clothes the prisoner had laid aside, combed back his hair, and tied it with the ribbon the prisoner had worn. Then, he softly called, “Enter there! Come in!” and the Spy presented himself.

text: Fool, fool!" Julian cried, his boot striking the floor with a sharp crack. "Have I not already bound my soul to this course, that you must dither while our time evaporates? Carry him yourself to the hidden square, settle him yourself within the coach, present him yourself to Master Thorne, and charge him yourself to offer no medicine but the wind. Tell him to hold fast to my words and his own oath from last evening—and then, begone!
source_text: “Man, man!” returned Carton, stamping his foot; “have I sworn by no solemn vow already, to go through with this, that you waste the precious moments now? Take him yourself to the courtyard you know of, place him yourself in the carriage, show him yourself to Mr. Lorry, tell him yourself to give him no restorative but air, and to remember my words of last night, and his promise of last night, and drive away!”

text: Jarvis Lorry has been the one to answer every inquiry. It is he who has stepped down and now stands with his hand upon the carriage door, responding to the cluster of officials. They move with unhurried steps around the vehicle and climb slowly onto the box to examine the meager luggage fastened to the roof; meanwhile, the local peasants huddle closer to the doors, staring in with a ravenous curiosity. A mother, carrying her small child, stretches out the infant’s tiny arm so that it might touch the wife of the condemned aristocrat who has been sent to the guillotine.
source_text: It is Jarvis Lorry who has replied to all the previous questions. It is Jarvis Lorry who has alighted and stands with his hand on the coach door, replying to a group of officials. They leisurely walk round the carriage and leisurely mount the box, to look at what little luggage it carries on the roof; the country-people hanging about, press nearer to the coach doors and greedily stare in; a little child, carried by its mother, has its short arm held out for it, that it may touch the wife of an aristocrat who has gone to the Guillotine.

text: To put it plainly," Madame Defarge remarked, emerging from her brief reverie, "I cannot rely on my husband in this affair. Since last night, I have felt that I dare not share the specifics of my intentions with him; furthermore, I fear that if I hesitate, he might alert them, and they will manage to escape.
source_text: “In a word,” said Madame Defarge, coming out of her short abstraction, “I cannot trust my husband in this matter. Not only do I feel, since last night, that I dare not confide to him the details of my projects; but also I feel that if I delay, there is danger of his giving warning, and then they might escape.”

text: Many women during that era were horribly scarred by the violence of the times, but none were more formidable than this pitiless woman as she moved through the streets. She possessed a bold and fearless spirit, a sharp intellect, and a relentless will. Her beauty was of a type that suggested both rigidity and malice, commanding an immediate, instinctive recognition of her strength from all who saw her. In such turbulent days, she was bound to rise to prominence regardless of the situation. Yet, having been nurtured since childhood on a dark sense of injustice and a deep-rooted hatred for an entire class, the revolution had forged her into a tigress. She was utterly without mercy; if any spark of compassion had ever existed within her, it had been completely extinguished.
source_text: There were many women at that time, upon whom the time laid a dreadfully disfiguring hand; but, there was not one among them more to be dreaded than this ruthless woman, now taking her way along the streets. Of a strong and fearless character, of shrewd sense and readiness, of great determination, of that kind of beauty which not only seems to impart to its possessor firmness and animosity, but to strike into others an instinctive recognition of those qualities; the troubled time would have heaved her up, under any circumstances. But, imbued from her childhood with a brooding sense of wrong, and an inveterate hatred of a class, opportunity had developed her into a tigress. She was absolutely without pity. If she had ever had the virtue in her, it had quite gone out of her.

text: Perceiving in this design a final prospect of offering genuine aid amidst the gathering peril, Miss Thorne welcomed the task with a desperate heartiness. Both she and Silas had watched the heavy carriage roll away, well aware of the identity of the passenger Elias had escorted, and had endured a brief eternity of ten minutes in the grip of breathless dread. They were currently finishing their own schemes for flight, even as the Widow Varga, pacing through the narrow lanes, drew ever closer to the solitary rooms where they conducted their hurried deliberations.
source_text: Seeing in this arrangement the hope of rendering real service in that pressing emergency, Miss Pross hailed it with joy. She and Jerry had beheld the coach start, had known who it was that Solomon brought, had passed some ten minutes in tortures of suspense, and were now concluding their arrangements to follow the coach, even as Madame Defarge, taking her way through the streets, now drew nearer and nearer to the else-deserted lodging in which they held their consultation.

text: “I am an Englishwoman,” Miss Halloway cried, “and I am driven to desperation. I care not a brass farthing for my own life. I know that each minute I hold you here improves the fortune of my Little Dove. If you dare lay a finger on me, I will tear every raven lock from your head!”
source_text: “I am a Briton,” said Miss Pross, “I am desperate. I don’t care an English Twopence for myself. I know that the longer I keep you here, the greater hope there is for my Ladybird. I’ll not leave a handful of that dark hair upon your head, if you lay a finger on me!”

text: The Vengeance lunged for the threshold, but Sarah, driven by a sudden, fierce intuition, threw her arms around the woman’s middle and locked her grip. Resistance and violence proved futile against her; Sarah possessed the iron-willed endurance of devotion, a power that invariably dwarfs the brittle fuel of malice. She squeezed with such ferocity that the other woman was hoisted clean off the floor as they grappled. Her adversary’s fingernails raked and bloodied her cheeks, yet Sarah kept her chin tucked low, maintaining her hold on the waist with a tenacity more absolute than the final, white-knuckled clutch of a soul lost at sea.
source_text: Madame Defarge made at the door. Miss Pross, on the instinct of the moment, seized her round the waist in both her arms, and held her tight. It was in vain for Madame Defarge to struggle and to strike; Miss Pross, with the vigorous tenacity of love, always so much stronger than hate, clasped her tight, and even lifted her from the floor in the struggle that they had. The two hands of Madame Defarge buffeted and tore her face; but, Miss Pross, with her head down, held her round the waist, and clung to her with more than the hold of a drowning woman.

text: Upon the cathedral’s stone porch, watching for the ascent of the death-carts, lingers the stool-pigeon. He peers into the leaden wagon: absent. He searches the next: still absent. The doubt begins to gnaw—“Has he cheated the bargain?”—but his expression softens into relief as his gaze falls upon the third.
source_text: On the steps of a church, awaiting the coming-up of the tumbrils, stands the Spy and prison-sheep. He looks into the first of them: not there. He looks into the second: not there. He already asks himself, “Has he sacrificed me?” when his face clears, as he looks into the third.

text: The clocks are just striking three, and the path cut through the crowd is curving around, entering the execution square for the final act. The walls of people pushed to either side now collapse and merge behind the last cart as it passes, for everyone is following the procession to the Guillotine. Arranged in front of it, sitting in chairs as if at a public performance, are several women busy with their knitting. On one of the front seats stands The Vengeance, scanning the area for her friend.
source_text: The clocks are on the stroke of three, and the furrow ploughed among the populace is turning round, to come on into the place of execution, and end. The ridges thrown to this side and to that, now crumble in and close behind the last plough as it passes on, for all are following to the Guillotine. In front of it, seated in chairs, as in a garden of public diversion, are a number of women, busily knitting. On one of the fore-most chairs, stands The Vengeance, looking about for her friend.

text: The man identified as Evrémonde steps down, and the seamstress is helped out right after him. He hasn't let go of her steady hand; he continues to hold it just as he promised. He tenderly positions her so she faces away from the thudding machine that tirelessly hoists and drops its blade, and she gazes into his eyes to offer her thanks.
source_text: The supposed Evrémonde descends, and the seamstress is lifted out next after him. He has not relinquished her patient hand in getting out, but still holds it as he promised. He gently places her with her back to the crashing engine that constantly whirrs up and falls, and she looks into his face and thanks him.

text: If it were not for you, kind stranger, I would never have found such peace, for I am by nature a timid creature, easily frightened. Without your help, I could not have lifted my heart toward the One who suffered and died so that we might find solace and strength in this hour. I truly believe you were sent to me by God.
source_text: “But for you, dear stranger, I should not be so composed, for I am naturally a poor little thing, faint of heart; nor should I have been able to raise my thoughts to Him who was put to death, that we might have hope and comfort here to-day. I think you were sent to me by Heaven.”

text: What has occupied my mind during our journey, and what I continue to ponder while looking into your gentle, steady face that gives me such courage, is this: if the Republic truly serves the needy, and if they find relief from hunger and their various miseries, she might last for many years; she might even endure until she is old.
source_text: “What I have been thinking as we came along, and what I am still thinking now, as I look into your kind strong face which gives me so much support, is this:--If the Republic really does good to the poor, and they come to be less hungry, and in all ways to suffer less, she may live a long time: she may even live to be old.”

text: While I wouldn’t claim that being born in a workhouse is the most fortunate or enviable fate a human being could face, I will say that in this specific case, it was the best possible thing that could have happened to Oliver Twist. The simple truth is that Oliver had a great deal of trouble starting to breathe—a tedious chore, perhaps, but one that habit has made essential to our continued survival. For a while, he lay gasping on a thin mattress, balanced precariously between this world and the next, with the scales tipped heavily toward the latter. Now, if Oliver had been surrounded by doting grandmothers, frantic aunts, seasoned nurses, and brilliant doctors during those few minutes, they undoubtedly would have managed to kill him in short order. However, since there was no one present except a tipsy old pauper woman and a parish surgeon who worked by contract, Oliver and Nature were left to fight it out alone. The result was that, after a few gasps, Oliver finally breathed, sneezed, and proceeded to inform the workhouse residents that the parish had a new mouth to feed. He did this by letting out a cry as loud as anyone could expect from a male infant who had been deprived of that useful tool, a voice, for a little over three minutes and a quarter.
source_text: Although I am not disposed to maintain that being born in a workhouse, is in itself the most fortunate and enviable circumstance that can possibly befall a human being, I do mean to say that in this particular instance, it was the best thing for Oliver Twist that could by possibility have occurred. The fact is, that there was considerable difficulty in inducing Oliver to take upon himself the office of respiration,—a troublesome practice, but one which custom has rendered necessary to our easy existence; and for some time he lay gasping on a little flock mattress, rather unequally poised between this world and the next: the balance being decidedly in favour of the latter. Now, if, during this brief period, Oliver had been surrounded by careful grandmothers, anxious aunts, experienced nurses, and doctors of profound wisdom, he would most inevitably and indubitably have been killed in no time. There being nobody by, however, but a pauper old woman, who was rendered rather misty by an unwonted allowance of beer; and a parish surgeon who did such matters by contract; Oliver and Nature fought out the point between them. The result was, that, after a few struggles, Oliver breathed, sneezed, and proceeded to advertise to the inmates of the workhouse the fact of a new burden having been imposed upon the parish, by setting up as loud a cry as could reasonably have been expected from a male infant who had not been possessed of that very useful appendage, a voice, for a much longer space of time than three minutes and a quarter.

text: Just as Oliver's first cry provided evidence of his lungs’ healthy operation, the mismatched quilt draped untidily over the iron bedstead rustled; the wan face of a young woman lifted feebly from the pillow, and in a voice so weak it could barely form the sounds, she whispered, “Let me see the child, and die.”
source_text: As Oliver gave this first proof of the free and proper action of his lungs, the patchwork coverlet which was carelessly flung over the iron bedstead, rustled; the pale face of a young woman was raised feebly from the pillow; and a faint voice imperfectly articulated the words, “Let me see the child, and die.”

text:
Throughout the next eight or ten months, Oliver was the target of a methodical system of betrayal and fraud. He was raised without a nursing mother. The workhouse officials formally reported the infant orphan’s starving and impoverished state to the parish authorities. With an air of solemnity, the parish leaders asked the workhouse staff if there were any women currently living in the facility who could provide Oliver Twist with the sustenance and maternal care he lacked. The workhouse staff submissively replied that there were none. 

Consequently, in a display of supposed benevolence and mercy, the parish decided to "farm" Oliver out. This involved sending him to a satellite workhouse three miles away, where twenty or thirty other young "violators" of the poverty laws sprawled across the floor all day, unburdened by the nuisance of adequate food or clothing. They were placed under the maternal watch of an elderly woman who accepted these little culprits for the price of seven and a half pence per head each week. Now, seven and a half pence a week buys a substantial amount of food for a child; one can purchase a great deal with such a sum—more than enough to overfill a small stomach and cause indigestion. 

This matron was a wise and experienced woman; she knew what was best for children, and she had an even keener sense of what was best for herself. Accordingly, she pocketed the majority of the weekly payment for her own purposes, leaving the parish’s rising generation with even smaller rations than the pittance originally intended for them. In this way, she discovered a new level of deprivation within an already desperate situation, proving herself to be a truly gifted philosopher of human endurance.
source_text: For the next eight or ten months, Oliver was the victim of a systematic course of treachery and deception. He was brought up by hand. The hungry and destitute situation of the infant orphan was duly reported by the workhouse authorities to the parish authorities. The parish authorities inquired with dignity of the workhouse authorities, whether there was no female then domiciled in “the house” who was in a situation to impart to Oliver Twist, the consolation and nourishment of which he stood in need. The workhouse authorities replied with humility, that there was not. Upon this, the parish authorities magnanimously and humanely resolved, that Oliver should be “farmed,” or, in other words, that he should be dispatched to a branch-workhouse some three miles off, where twenty or thirty other juvenile offenders against the poor-laws, rolled about the floor all day, without the inconvenience of too much food or too much clothing, under the parental superintendence of an elderly female, who received the culprits at and for the consideration of sevenpence-halfpenny per small head per week. Sevenpence-halfpenny’s worth per week is a good round diet for a child; a great deal may be got for sevenpence-halfpenny, quite enough to overload its stomach, and make it uncomfortable. The elderly female was a woman of wisdom and experience; she knew what was good for children; and she had a very accurate perception of what was good for herself. So, she appropriated the greater part of the weekly stipend to her own use, and consigned the rising parochial generation to even a shorter allowance than was originally provided for them. Thereby finding in the lowest depth a deeper still; and proving herself a very great experimental philosopher.

text: It is a well-known anecdote that a certain scholar once theorized a horse could be trained to survive without food entirely. He demonstrated this so effectively that he had narrowed the animal's diet to a single straw a day, and would undoubtedly have created a most energetic creature living on nothing at all, had it not died just one day before it was meant to enjoy its first satisfying meal of thin air. Unfortunately for the scientific principles of the woman to whom Oliver Twist was entrusted, her own methods tended to produce a similar result. At the precise moment a child learned to subsist on the smallest imaginable portion of the thinnest possible nourishment, it happened in nearly every case that the infant either grew ill from deprivation, fell into the grate through sheer neglect, or was accidentally smothered. In each instance, the pathetic creature was hurried off to the next world, there to meet the parents it had never had the chance to know in this one.
source_text: Everybody knows the story of another experimental philosopher who had a great theory about a horse being able to live without eating, and who demonstrated it so well, that he had got his own horse down to a straw a day, and would unquestionably have rendered him a very spirited and rampacious animal on nothing at all, if he had not died, four-and-twenty hours before he was to have had his first comfortable bait of air. Unfortunately for the experimental philosophy of the female to whose protecting care Oliver Twist was delivered over, a similar result usually attended the operation of _her_ system; for at the very moment when the child had contrived to exist upon the smallest possible portion of the weakest possible food, it did perversely happen in eight and a half cases out of ten, either that it sickened from want and cold, or fell into the fire from neglect, or got half-smothered by accident; in any one of which cases, the miserable little being was usually summoned into another world, and there gathered to the fathers it had never known in this.

text: “And despite a promised bounty of fifteen sovereigns, which was thereafter increased to thirty; despite the most exhaustive and, I might say, preternatural labors on the part of this vestry,” declared Grimshaw, “we have never managed to uncover the identity of his sire, or his mother’s legal residence, name, or social standing.”
source_text: “And notwithstanding a offered reward of ten pound, which was afterwards increased to twenty pound. Notwithstanding the most superlative, and, I may say, supernat’ral exertions on the part of this parish,” said Bumble, “we have never been able to discover who is his father, or what was his mother’s settlement, name, or condition.”

text: Quite so," the beadle remarked, clearly flattered by the praise. "I suppose I may be, Mrs. Mann." He drained the remainder of his gin and water before adding, "Since Oliver is now too old to stay on here, the board has decided he must return to the workhouse. I have come personally to escort him back. So, bring him out to me at once.
source_text: “Well, well,” said the beadle, evidently gratified with the compliment; “perhaps I may be. Perhaps I may be, Mrs. Mann.” He finished the gin-and-water, and added, “Oliver being now too old to remain here, the board have determined to have him back into the house. I have come out myself to take him there. So let me see him at once.”

text: Oliver was just about to declare his eager willingness to go with anyone at all when, looking up, he spotted Mrs. Mann. She had positioned herself behind the beadle’s chair and was brandishing her fist at him with a look of pure rage. He understood the warning immediately, for that fist had been pressed against his ribs too often not to be permanently etched into his memory.
source_text: Oliver was about to say that he would go along with anybody with great readiness, when, glancing upward, he caught sight of Mrs. Mann, who had got behind the beadle’s chair, and was shaking her fist at him with a furious countenance. He took the hint at once, for the fist had been too often impressed upon his body not to be deeply impressed upon his recollection.

text: Lacking any clear concept of what a "live board" entailed, Oliver was quite taken aback by the news, unsure if the revelation called for laughter or tears. He was granted no time for reflection, however, as Mr. Bumble delivered a sharp rap to his head with a cane to stir his senses, followed by a prod to his back to ensure he moved briskly. Ordered to follow, he was led into a sprawling, white-walled hall where roughly ten stout gentlemen sat assembled around a table. At the head of the group, perched in an armchair that rose above the others, sat an especially corpulent man with a notably circular and crimson countenance.
source_text: Not having a very clearly defined notion of what a live board was, Oliver was rather astounded by this intelligence, and was not quite certain whether he ought to laugh or cry. He had no time to think about the matter, however; for Mr. Bumble gave him a tap on the head, with his cane, to wake him up: and another on the back to make him lively: and bidding him to follow, conducted him into a large whitewashed room, where eight or ten fat gentlemen were sitting round a table. At the top of the table, seated in an arm-chair rather higher than the rest, was a particularly fat gentleman with a very round, red face.

text: “I have never been more certain of a single thing in my life,” remarked the individual in the ivory waistcoat, as he rapped against the gate and perused the placard the next morning. “I have never been more certain of anything, than I am that the boy is destined for the gallows.”
source_text: “I never was more convinced of anything in my life,” said the gentleman in the white waistcoat, as he knocked at the gate and read the bill next morning: “I never was more convinced of anything in my life, than I am that that boy will come to be hung.”

text: For a week following his sacrilegious crime of requesting a second helping, Oliver was kept in strict confinement within the dim, isolated cell where the board, in their infinite benevolence, had seen fit to place him. One might naturally assume that, had the boy possessed a proper regard for the grim forecast made by the gentleman in the white waistcoat, he might have validated that man's status as a seer by fastening a handkerchief to a wall hook and hanging himself from the other end. Yet, a significant hurdle stood in the way of such an act: the board, in a formal and sealed decree, had judged handkerchiefs to be an unnecessary extravagance and had permanently banned them from the possession of the poor. An even more profound barrier was Oliver's own innocence and tender age. He spent the daylight hours weeping inconsolably, and as the oppressive nights descended, he shielded his eyes with his small hands against the blackness. Huddled in a corner, he sought sleep, only to wake repeatedly in a fit of trembling, pressing himself against the masonry as though the frigid, unyielding stone offered some small sanctuary from the overwhelming desolation of his prison.
source_text: For a week after the commission of the impious and profane offence of asking for more, Oliver remained a close prisoner in the dark and solitary room to which he had been consigned by the wisdom and mercy of the board. It appears, at first sight not unreasonable to suppose, that, if he had entertained a becoming feeling of respect for the prediction of the gentleman in the white waistcoat, he would have established that sage individual’s prophetic character, once and for ever, by tying one end of his pocket-handkerchief to a hook in the wall, and attaching himself to the other. To the performance of this feat, however, there was one obstacle: namely, that pocket-handkerchiefs being decided articles of luxury, had been, for all future times and ages, removed from the noses of paupers by the express order of the board, in council assembled: solemnly given and pronounced under their hands and seals. There was a still greater obstacle in Oliver’s youth and childishness. He only cried bitterly all day; and, when the long, dismal night came on, spread his little hands before his eyes to shut out the darkness, and crouching in the corner, tried to sleep: ever and anon waking with a start and tremble, and drawing himself closer and closer to the wall, as if to feel even its cold hard surface were a protection in the gloom and loneliness which surrounded him.

text: The animal was lost in a deep reverie, perhaps contemplating whether he might be treated to a cabbage stalk or two once he had delivered the two bags of soot piled in his little cart. Thus, oblivious to the driver’s command, he simply continued to plod along his way.
source_text: The donkey was in a state of profound abstraction: wondering, probably, whether he was destined to be regaled with a cabbage-stalk or two when he had disposed of the two sacks of soot with which the little cart was laden; so, without noticing the word of command, he jogged onward.

text: Mr. Grimshaw snarled a vicious malediction at the beast at large, directing particular vitriol toward its eyes; overtaking the creature, he dealt a buffet to its forehead that would have undoubtedly crushed any skull save for that of a donkey. Then, grasping the reins, he gave the animal’s jaw a violent jerk as a subtle hint that it remained subject to his will, thereby forcing it to come about. He followed this with another strike to the head, intended merely to daze the brute until he should return. These preparations concluded, he approached the gate to examine the posted notice.
source_text: Mr. Gamfield growled a fierce imprecation on the donkey generally, but more particularly on his eyes; and, running after him, bestowed a blow on his head, which would inevitably have beaten in any skull but a donkey’s. Then, catching hold of the bridle, he gave his jaw a sharp wrench, by way of gentle reminder that he was not his own master; and by these means turned him round. He then gave him another blow on the head, just to stun him till he came back again. Having completed these arrangements, he walked up to the gate, to read the bill.

text: The man in the white waistcoat stood by the gate with his hands clasped behind his back, fresh from sharing his weighty opinions in the board-room. Having watched the brief scuffle between Mr. Gamfield and the donkey, he grinned broadly as the sweep approached the notice, sensing immediately that this was precisely the kind of master Oliver Twist required. Mr. Gamfield also smiled as he scanned the document; five pounds was exactly the windfall he had been hoping for. Regarding the child who came with the money, Gamfield was well aware of the meager workhouse rations and knew the boy would be suitably slight—the perfect size for navigating narrow chimney flues. He read the advertisement once more from start to finish, then tipped his fur cap in a show of modesty and addressed the gentleman in the white waistcoat.
source_text: The gentleman with the white waistcoat was standing at the gate with his hands behind him, after having delivered himself of some profound sentiments in the board-room. Having witnessed the little dispute between Mr. Gamfield and the donkey, he smiled joyously when that person came up to read the bill, for he saw at once that Mr. Gamfield was exactly the sort of master Oliver Twist wanted. Mr. Gamfield smiled, too, as he perused the document; for five pounds was just the sum he had been wishing for; and, as to the boy with which it was encumbered, Mr. Gamfield, knowing what the dietary of the workhouse was, well knew he would be a nice small pattern, just the very thing for register stoves. So, he spelt the bill through again, from beginning to end; and then, touching his fur cap in token of humility, accosted the gentleman in the white waistcoat.

text: "Step inside," the gentleman in the white waistcoat directed. Mr. Gamfield paused briefly to strike the donkey across the skull and give its jaw a sharp, cruel wrench—a parting warning not to bolt while he was away—before following the man into the very room where Oliver had first encountered him.
source_text: “Walk in,” said the gentleman in the white waistcoat. Mr. Gamfield having lingered behind, to give the donkey another blow on the head, and another wrench of the jaw, as a caution not to run away in his absence, followed the gentleman with the white waistcoat into the room where Oliver had first seen him.

text: The man in the white vest seemed quite entertained by this clarification, but his laughter was quickly stifled by a stern glance from Mr. Limbkins. The committee then began to confer among themselves for a few minutes, speaking in such hushed tones that only the phrases “cost-cutting measures,” “looked good in the ledgers,” and “issue a formal report” could be overheard. These fragments were only audible because they were repeated so often and with such heavy emphasis.
source_text: The gentleman in the white waistcoat appeared very much amused by this explanation; but his mirth was speedily checked by a look from Mr. Limbkins. The board then proceeded to converse among themselves for a few minutes, but in so low a tone, that the words “saving of expenditure,” “looked well in the accounts,” “have a printed report published,” were alone audible. These only chanced to be heard, indeed, on account of their being very frequently repeated with great emphasis.

text: Indeed, Silas," proclaimed Mr. Snudge. "The virtuous and holy board, who serve as the very father and mother you so sorely lack, Silas, are preparing to apprentice you. They mean to provide for your future and transform you into a man, even though the drain upon the public purse is four pounds ten! Four pounds ten, Silas! Ninety shillings—one hundred and eighty sixpences! And all lavished upon a miserable stray whom no living soul could ever bring themselves to pity.
source_text: “Yes, Oliver,” said Mr. Bumble. “The kind and blessed gentleman which is so many parents to you, Oliver, when you have none of your own: are a going to “prentice” you: and to set you up in life, and make a man of you: although the expense to the parish is three pound ten!—three pound ten, Oliver!—seventy shillins—one hundred and forty sixpences!—and all for a naughty orphan which nobody can’t love.”

text: While Mr. Sowerberry voiced these complaints with the righteous anger of a man who had been wronged, Mr. Bumble sensed that the remarks were beginning to cast a shadow over the parish’s reputation. Consequently, the official decided it would be prudent to pivot the conversation. With Oliver Twist already at the forefront of his mind, he chose to make the boy his next subject.
source_text: As Mr. Sowerberry said this, with the becoming indignation of an ill-used man; and as Mr. Bumble felt that it rather tended to convey a reflection on the honour of the parish; the latter gentleman thought it advisable to change the subject. Oliver Twist being uppermost in his mind, he made him his theme.

text: “Incidentally,” remarked Mr. Bumble, “you wouldn’t happen to know of anyone in search of a lad, would you? A parish apprentice who is currently a mere burden—a millstone, one might say, around the neck of the community? We offer generous inducements, Mr. Sowerberry, very generous.” Even as he spoke, Mr. Bumble lifted his staff toward the notice overhead and struck three sharp blows against the words “FIVE POUNDS,” which were emblazoned there in enormous, blocky letters.
source_text: “By the bye,” said Mr. Bumble, “you don’t know anybody who wants a boy, do you? A porochial ’prentis, who is at present a dead-weight; a millstone, as I may say, round the porochial throat? Liberal terms, Mr. Sowerberry, liberal terms?” As Mr. Bumble spoke, he raised his cane to the bill above him, and gave three distinct raps upon the words “five pounds”: which were printed thereon in Roman capitals of gigantic size.

text: “Charlotte,” said Mr. Sowerberry, who had trailed Oliver downstairs, “hand this boy those chilled scraps we saved for Trip. The dog hasn’t returned since this morning, so he can do without them. I imagine the lad isn’t too particular to eat them—are you, boy?”
source_text: “Here, Charlotte,” said Mr. Sowerberry, who had followed Oliver down, “give this boy some of the cold bits that were put by for Trip. He hasn’t come home since the morning, so he may go without ’em. I dare say the boy isn’t too dainty to eat ’em—are you, boy?”

text: Briefly, Arthur peered up the thoroughfare and down again, surveying the opposite pavement with the conviction that the invisible soul who had spoken through the lock-hole had simply wandered off to escape the frost; for he found the way deserted, save for a hulking parish-lad who sat upon a bollard before the shop, busy with a slab of buttered crust. This the fellow divided into wedges, tailored to the span of his mouth with a folding-blade, and dispatched with singular agility.
source_text: For a second or two, Oliver glanced up the street, and down the street, and over the way: impressed with the belief that the unknown, who had addressed him through the key-hole, had walked a few paces off, to warm himself; for nobody did he see but a big charity-boy, sitting on a post in front of the house, eating a slice of bread and butter: which he cut into wedges, the size of his mouth, with a clasp-knife, and then consumed with great dexterity.

text: Oliver had just finished unfastening the shutters. As he struggled toward the small side courtyard where they were kept, the weight of the first one caused him to stumble and shatter a window pane. Noah looked on with mock concern, assuring him that he was in for a beating before finally condescending to help. Mr. Sowerberry came downstairs a moment later, followed shortly by his wife. Once Noah’s prediction had been fulfilled and Oliver had indeed "caught it," the boy followed the older youth down the stairs to breakfast.
source_text: Oliver, having taken down the shutters, and broken a pane of glass in his effort to stagger away beneath the weight of the first one to a small court at the side of the house in which they were kept during the day, was graciously assisted by Noah: who having consoled him with the assurance that “he’d catch it,” condescended to help him. Mr. Sowerberry came down soon after. Shortly afterwards, Mrs. Sowerberry appeared. Oliver having “caught it,” in fulfilment of Noah’s prediction, followed that young gentleman down the stairs to breakfast.

text: “Draw toward the hearth, Silas,” Martha said. “I’ve kept back a choice morsel of gammon from the master’s own morning plate for you. Arthur, latch that door at Master Silas’s back, and gather those scraps I’ve left on the lid of the flour-bin. There is your brew; carry it off to that crate and drink it down, and be quick, for they’ll be wanting you to watch the counter. Do you mark me?”
source_text: “Come near the fire, Noah,” said Charlotte. “I saved a nice little bit of bacon for you from master’s breakfast. Oliver, shut that door at Mister Noah’s back, and take them bits that I’ve put out on the cover of the bread-pan. There’s your tea; take it away to that box, and drink it there, and make haste, for they’ll want you to mind the shop. D’ye hear?”

text: “Nay, do not solicit my poor counsel,” Mrs. Thornebridge protested with a flourish of martyred grace; “seek the opinion of another.” This was followed by a peal of frantic, shrill laughter that left Mr. Thornebridge in a state of considerable agitation. It is a well-established and highly regarded stratagem of the wedded state, and one frequently crowned with success. It served its purpose by instantly compelling Mr. Thornebridge to plead, as a distinct privilege, for the opportunity to divulge the very intelligence Mrs. Thornebridge was most impatient to acquire. After a brief and calculated pause, this indulgence was most magnanimously granted.
source_text: “No, no, don’t ask mine,” replied Mrs. Sowerberry, in an affecting manner: “ask somebody else’s.” Here, there was another hysterical laugh, which frightened Mr. Sowerberry very much. This is a very common and much-approved matrimonial course of treatment, which is often very effective. It at once reduced Mr. Sowerberry to begging, as a special favour, to be allowed to say what Mrs. Sowerberry was most curious to hear. After a short duration, the permission was most graciously conceded.

text: "Yes, sir," Oliver answered. He had been careful to stay out of sight throughout the meeting, his entire body still trembling at the mere memory of Mr. Bumble’s voice. However, his efforts to dodge the official’s gaze were quite unnecessary. That particular functionary, who remained deeply unsettled by the gentleman in the white waistcoat’s grim prophecy, felt it was best to ignore the boy now that the undertaker had taken him on trial. He intended to avoid the subject entirely until the seven-year apprenticeship was legally finalized, ensuring that any risk of the parish being burdened with Oliver again was permanently and officially eliminated.
source_text: “Yes, sir,” replied Oliver, who had carefully kept himself out of sight, during the interview; and who was shaking from head to foot at the mere recollection of the sound of Mr. Bumble’s voice. He needn’t have taken the trouble to shrink from Mr. Bumble’s glance, however; for that functionary, on whom the prediction of the gentleman in the white waistcoat had made a very strong impression, thought that now the undertaker had got Oliver upon trial the subject was better avoided, until such time as he should be firmly bound for seven years, and all danger of his being returned upon the hands of the parish should be thus effectually and legally overcome.

text: "Wait, wait!" the old woman hissed in a harsh whisper. "Is the burial to be tomorrow, or the day after, or perhaps even tonight? I was the one who prepared her body, so I must walk in the procession, you see. You must send me a heavy cloak—a thick, warm one—for the air is freezing. We ought to have wine and cake before we depart! But no matter; just send some bread—a simple loaf and a cup of water will do. Can we have some bread, sir?" she pleaded, clutching at the undertaker’s sleeve as he turned once more toward the door.
source_text: “Stop, stop!” said the old woman in a loud whisper. “Will she be buried tomorrow, or next day, or tonight? I laid her out; and I must walk, you know. Send me a large cloak: a good warm one: for it is bitter cold. We should have cake and wine, too, before we go! Never mind; send some bread—only a loaf of bread and a cup of water. Shall we have some bread, dear?” she said eagerly: catching at the undertaker’s coat, as he once more moved towards the door.

text: On the succeeding day—the starving kin having been meanwhile sustained by a meager crust and a wedge of rind provided by Mr. Grime himself—Arthur and his master revisited the squalid dwelling. There they found Mr. Grime already waiting, flanked by four gaunt men from the asylum who were to serve as the funeral party. A tattered sable cloak had been cast over the threadbare garments of the aged woman and her companion; and the unadorned casket, once the lid was fastened tight, was hoisted upon the shoulders of the laborers and borne out into the open street.
source_text: The next day, (the family having been meanwhile relieved with a half-quartern loaf and a piece of cheese, left with them by Mr. Bumble himself,) Oliver and his master returned to the miserable abode; where Mr. Bumble had already arrived, accompanied by four men from the workhouse, who were to act as bearers. An old black cloak had been thrown over the rags of the old woman and the man; and the bare coffin having been screwed down, was hoisted on the shoulders of the bearers, and carried into the street.

text: Whenever Mr. Grimshaw was commissioned to inter some opulent dowager or patriarch, whose deathbed had been besieged by a flock of distraught kinsmen, one might witness a curious transformation. These relations, who had previously paraded their heartbreak with such clamorous despair that it seemed no comfort could ever reach them, were suddenly found to be in the highest spirits imaginable. Once the public gaze was averted, they chattered with a vivacity and ease that suggested their recent bereavement was a trifle of no consequence. Widowers, likewise, sustained the departure of their better halves with a fortitude so unwavering it bordered on the sublime. As for the widows, they donned their funeral sables not as emblems of woe, but as though they were determined to prove that black lace and heavy veils could be fashioned into the most flattering of adornments. It was further noted that those mourners who had been seized by paroxysms of agony at the graveside underwent a miraculous recovery upon crossing their own thresholds; by the time the silver tea-service was brought out, their composure was entirely restored. Such displays of human resilience were most edifying to behold, and Arthur regarded these shifts in temperament with profound and innocent wonder.
source_text: For instance; when Sowerberry had an order for the burial of some rich old lady or gentleman, who was surrounded by a great number of nephews and nieces, who had been perfectly inconsolable during the previous illness, and whose grief had been wholly irrepressible even on the most public occasions, they would be as happy among themselves as need be—quite cheerful and contented—conversing together with as much freedom and gaiety, as if nothing whatever had happened to disturb them. Husbands, too, bore the loss of their wives with the most heroic calmness. Wives, again, put on weeds for their husbands, as if, so far from grieving in the garb of sorrow, they had made up their minds to render it as becoming and attractive as possible. It was observable, too, that ladies and gentlemen who were in passions of anguish during the ceremony of interment, recovered almost as soon as they reached home, and became quite composed before the tea-drinking was over. All this was very pleasant and improving to see; and Oliver beheld it with great admiration.

text: "No, sir, he hasn't run off. He’s turned violent," Noah insisted. "He tried to kill me, sir; then he went after Charlotte, and then the mistress. Oh, the pain is just dreadful! I'm in such agony, please, sir!" At this, Noah began to squirm and thrash his body in a series of eel-like maneuvers, making sure Mr. Bumble understood that Oliver Twist’s savage and bloody assault had caused him grave internal damage, from which he was currently suffering the most intense torture.
source_text: “No, sir, no. Not run away, sir, but he’s turned wicious,” replied Noah. “He tried to murder me, sir; and then he tried to murder Charlotte; and then missis. Oh! what dreadful pain it is! Such agony, please, sir!” And here, Noah writhed and twisted his body into an extensive variety of eel-like positions; thereby giving Mr. Bumble to understand that, from the violent and sanguinary onset of Oliver Twist, he had sustained severe internal injury and damage, from which he was at that moment suffering the acutest torture.

text: Only when he was finally left in the quiet shadows of the undertaker’s dismal workshop did Oliver surrender to the emotions that the day’s hardships would naturally provoke in a small child. He had met their mockery with a look of defiance and endured the whip without a sound; a fierce pride had surged in his chest, powerful enough to stifle any cry even if they had burned him alive. But now, with no one to watch or listen, he sank to his knees upon the floor. Hiding his face in his hands, he wept with a desperate sorrow that one can only hope—for the sake of our common humanity—few so young are ever driven to express.
source_text: It was not until he was left alone in the silence and stillness of the gloomy workshop of the undertaker, that Oliver gave way to the feelings which the day’s treatment may be supposed likely to have awakened in a mere child. He had listened to their taunts with a look of contempt; he had borne the lash without a cry: for he felt that pride swelling in his heart which would have kept down a shriek to the last, though they had roasted him alive. But now, when there were none to see or hear him, he fell upon his knees on the floor; and, hiding his face in his hands, wept such tears as, God send for the credit of our nature, few so young may ever have cause to pour out before him!

text: Oliver vividly recalled trotting down this very path beside Mr. Bumble when he was first taken from the farm to the workhouse. His route led him directly past the front of the cottage. His heart raced at the realization, and for a moment, he almost decided to turn back. Yet he had already covered so much ground, and retreating would waste far too much time. Besides, at such an early hour, there was little risk of being noticed, so he pressed on.
source_text: Along this same footpath, Oliver well-remembered he had trotted beside Mr. Bumble, when he first carried him to the workhouse from the farm. His way lay directly in front of the cottage. His heart beat quickly when he bethought himself of this; and he half resolved to turn back. He had come a long way though, and should lose a great deal of time by doing so. Besides, it was so early that there was very little fear of his being seen; so he walked on.

text: “Banish those anxieties from your thoughts,” the youth remarked. “I am bound for the city this very night, and I happen to know a most venerable gentleman residing there who will provide you a bed for a song—or rather, for nothing at all—provided a trusted acquaintance presents you. And does he hold me in high regard? Why, not in the slightest! He’s never heard of me. Never in this world. Most assuredly not!”
source_text: “Don’t fret your eyelids on that score,” said the young gentleman. “I’ve got to be in London tonight; and I know a ’spectable old gentleman as lives there, wot’ll give you lodgings for nothink, and never ask for the change—that is, if any gentleman he knows interduces you. And don’t he know me? Oh, no! Not in the least! By no means. Certainly not!”

text: The sudden proposal of a place to stay was far too enticing to decline, especially since it was paired with the promise that the old man would find Oliver a decent situation without delay. This led to a more open and trusting conversation, through which Oliver learned that his companion’s name was Jack Dawkins and that he was a particular favorite and student of the elderly gentleman they had discussed.
source_text: This unexpected offer of shelter was too tempting to be resisted; especially as it was immediately followed up, by the assurance that the old gentleman referred to, would doubtless provide Oliver with a comfortable place, without loss of time. This led to a more friendly and confidential dialogue; from which Oliver discovered that his friend’s name was Jack Dawkins, and that he was a peculiar pet and _protégé_ of the elderly gentleman before mentioned.

text: The old man smirked and, with a deep bow to Oliver, took his hand, expressing his hope for a long and close friendship. Immediately, the youth with the pipes stepped forward to shake both of Oliver's hands with great vigor—focusing particularly on the hand holding his small bundle. One boy was remarkably eager to hang up Oliver's cap, while another was kind enough to reach into his pockets so that, given his exhaustion, he wouldn't have to bother emptying them himself before bed. These displays of courtesy would likely have gone much further if the old man hadn't begun swinging his toasting-fork across the heads and shoulders of the overly affectionate boys.
source_text: The Jew grinned; and, making a low obeisance to Oliver, took him by the hand, and hoped he should have the honour of his intimate acquaintance. Upon this, the young gentleman with the pipes came round him, and shook both his hands very hard—especially the one in which he held his little bundle. One young gentleman was very anxious to hang up his cap for him; and another was so obliging as to put his hands in his pockets, in order that, as he was very tired, he might not have the trouble of emptying them, himself, when he went to bed. These civilities would probably be extended much farther, but for a liberal exercise of the Jew’s toasting-fork on the heads and shoulders of the affectionate youths who offered them.

text: Arthur finished his meager portion, and the old man then prepared him a mug of warm spirits, urging him to drain it at once, for the vessel was required by another guest. The boy complied as he was told. Scarcely a moment later, he felt himself hoisted softly onto a heap of coarse canvas bags; a profound and heavy slumber claimed him almost instantly.
source_text: Oliver ate his share, and the Jew then mixed him a glass of hot gin and water, telling him he must drink it off directly, because another gentleman wanted the tumbler. Oliver did as he was desired. Immediately afterwards he felt himself gently lifted on to one of the sacks; and then he sunk into a deep sleep.

text: Once he was certain of his privacy, the old man moved stealthily to the door and bolted it. From a hidden compartment in the floor—or so it appeared to Oliver—he produced a small box and placed it cautiously on the table. His eyes sparkled as he raised the lid to peer inside. Dragging a worn chair forward, he sat and withdrew a magnificent gold watch, shimmering with jewels.
source_text: After satisfying himself upon this head, the Jew stepped gently to the door: which he fastened. He then drew forth: as it seemed to Oliver, from some trap in the floor: a small box, which he placed carefully on the table. His eyes glistened as he raised the lid, and looked in. Dragging an old chair to the table, he sat down; and took from it a magnificent gold watch, sparkling with jewels.

text: Just as the miser finished speaking, his sharp, ink-black eyes, which had been wandering through some private void, settled abruptly upon Arthur’s face. The youth’s own eyes were pinned to the man’s in a state of quiet, bewildered interest; and while the moment of awareness was swifter than a lightning strike—lasting no longer than a single indrawn breath—it was sufficient to alert the old man that his actions had not gone unobserved.
source_text: As the Jew uttered these words, his bright dark eyes, which had been staring vacantly before him, fell on Oliver’s face; the boy’s eyes were fixed on his in mute curiousity; and although the recognition was only for an instant—for the briefest space of time that can possibly be conceived—it was enough to show the old man that he had been observed.

text: After the amusement had been rehearsed to the point of exhaustion, a pair of maidens arrived to visit the young fellow; these were known as Meg and Clara. They possessed an abundance of hair, gathered up with a conspicuous lack of precision, and their ankles showed a distinct carelessness regarding the state of their footwear. Though they might not have been termed beauties, they boasted a vivid flush of health and a solid, thriving appearance. Their manners being singularly easy and familiar, Arthur concluded they were very charming girls, a fact of which he felt entirely certain.
source_text: When this game had been played a great many times, a couple of young ladies called to see the young gentleman; one of whom was named Bet, and the other Nancy. They wore a good deal of hair, not very neatly turned up behind, and were rather untidy about the shoes and stockings. They were not exactly pretty, perhaps; but they had a great deal of colour in their faces, and looked quite stout and hearty. Being remarkably free and agreeable in their manners, Oliver thought them very nice girls indeed, as there is no doubt they were.

text: “Quite so,” the old man remarked; “that is, unless they happen to stumble upon something while they are out; and you can be certain they won’t overlook it if they do, my boy. Let them be your examples, my dear. Let them be your examples,” he added, striking the fire-shovel against the hearth to drive the point home. “Do exactly as they tell you and heed their advice in every matter—particularly the Dodger’s. He is bound for greatness himself, and he will make a man of you too, if you only follow his lead. Now, tell me, is my handkerchief trailing from my pocket?” he asked, stopping short.
source_text: “Yes,” said the Jew; “that is, unless they should unexpectedly come across any, when they are out; and they won’t neglect it, if they do, my dear, depend upon it. Make ’em your models, my dear. Make ’em your models,” tapping the fire-shovel on the hearth to add force to his words; “do everything they bid you, and take their advice in all matters—especially the Dodger’s, my dear. He’ll be a great man himself, and will make you one too, if you take pattern by him.—Is my handkerchief hanging out of my pocket, my dear?” said the Jew, stopping short.

text: Oliver puzzled over how a game of pickpocketing could possibly lead to a future of greatness. Yet, assuming the older man must know best given his years, he followed him silently to the table and was soon fully immersed in his peculiar new education.
source_text: Oliver wondered what picking the old gentleman’s pocket in play, had to do with his chances of being a great man. But, thinking that the Jew, being so much his senior, must know best, he followed him quietly to the table, and was soon deeply involved in his new study.

text: Finally, one morning, Oliver received the permission he had been so desperate to obtain. For several days, there had been no handkerchiefs to work on, and the meals had become increasingly sparse. These factors might have influenced the old man’s decision; regardless, he told Oliver he could leave and placed him under the shared supervision of Charley Bates and the Dodger.
source_text: At length, one morning, Oliver obtained the permission he had so eagerly sought. There had been no handkerchiefs to work upon, for two or three days, and the dinners had been rather meagre. Perhaps these were reasons for the old gentleman’s giving his assent; but, whether they were or no, he told Oliver he might go, and placed him under the joint guardianship of Charley Bates, and his friend the Dodger.

text: The elderly man appeared quite distinguished, his hair dusted with white powder and his eyes framed by gold-rimmed glasses. He wore a dark green coat accented with a collar of black velvet, paired with white trousers, and he tucked a polished bamboo walking stick neatly beneath his arm. Having plucked a volume from the outdoor stall, he remained there, engrossed in its contents as though he were relaxing in an armchair within the privacy of his personal library. He might well have imagined himself there, for his deep concentration suggested he was oblivious to the stall, the bustling road, the nearby youths, or anything at all save the work in his hands. He read with unwavering focus, flipping each page the moment he reached the final line and starting immediately at the top of the next, moving forward with intense curiosity and zeal.
source_text: The old gentleman was a very respectable-looking personage, with a powdered head and gold spectacles. He was dressed in a bottle-green coat with a black velvet collar; wore white trousers; and carried a smart bamboo cane under his arm. He had taken up a book from the stall, and there he stood, reading away, as hard as if he were in his elbow-chair, in his own study. It is very possible that he fancied himself there, indeed; for it was plain, from his abstraction, that he saw not the book-stall, nor the street, nor the boys, nor, in short, anything but the book itself: which he was reading straight through: turning over the leaf when he got to the bottom of a page, beginning at the top line of the next one, and going regularly on, with the greatest interest and eagerness.

text: Imagine Julian’s profound shock and trepidation as he lingered a few yards distant, staring with eyes strained to their very brims, at the sight of the Rogue thrusting a hand deep into the elder’s coat and retrieving a silken square! To witness him pass the prize to young Arthur; and at last to see the pair of them take flight, vanishing around the street corner at a desperate pace!
source_text: What was Oliver’s horror and alarm as he stood a few paces off, looking on with his eyelids as wide open as they would possibly go, to see the Dodger plunge his hand into the old gentleman’s pocket, and draw from thence a handkerchief! To see him hand the same to Charley Bates; and finally to behold them, both running away round the corner at full speed!

text: The entire sequence unfolded in a mere moment. At the precise second Oliver took flight, the elderly gentleman reached for his pocket, discovered his handkerchief was missing, and whirled about. Seeing the boy sprinting away at such a breakneck speed, he instinctively took him for the culprit. Bellowing “Stop thief!” with all his strength, he set off in pursuit, his book still clutched in his hand.
source_text: This was all done in a minute’s space. In the very instant when Oliver began to run, the old gentleman, putting his hand to his pocket, and missing his handkerchief, turned sharp round. Seeing the boy scudding away at such a rapid pace, he very naturally concluded him to be the depredator; and shouting “Stop thief!” with all his might, made off after him, book in hand.

text: Even though Oliver had been raised under the guidance of supposed thinkers, he was unfamiliar with the fundamental principle that survival is the primary law of existence. Had he known this, he might have been ready for what followed. Instead, the surprise only heightened his terror; he bolted like a gale, pursued by the elderly man and the two boys, who chased after him with loud, frantic cries.
source_text: Although Oliver had been brought up by philosophers, he was not theoretically acquainted with the beautiful axiom that self-preservation is the first law of nature. If he had been, perhaps he would have been prepared for this. Not being prepared, however, it alarmed him the more; so away he went like the wind, with the old gentleman and the two boys roaring and shouting behind him.

text: “Stop thief! Stop thief!” There is a magnetic power in that cry. The shopkeeper deserts his counter and the driver his wagon; the butcher flings down his tray, the baker his basket, the milkman his pail, and the messenger his parcels. The schoolboy abandons his marbles, the laborer his pickaxe, and the child his toy. Off they dash in a frantic, headlong scramble—shouting and screaming as they bowl over pedestrians at every turn. They rouse the neighborhood dogs and terrify the poultry, until every street, square, and courtyard rings with the deafening clamor.
source_text: “Stop thief! Stop thief!” There is a magic in the sound. The tradesman leaves his counter, and the car-man his waggon; the butcher throws down his tray; the baker his basket; the milkman his pail; the errand-boy his parcels; the school-boy his marbles; the paviour his pickaxe; the child his battledore. Away they run, pell-mell, helter-skelter, slap-dash: tearing, yelling, screaming, knocking down the passengers as they turn the corners, rousing up the dogs, and astonishing the fowls: and streets, squares, and courts, re-echo with the sound.

text: “Seize the rogue! Seize the rogue!” The alarm is echoed by a multitude of tongues, the gathering swarm intensifying at every intersection. Forward they dash, churning through the sludge and thundering across the flagstones; sashes are flung upward, citizens spill into the thoroughfare, and the rabble surges onward. An entire assembly abandons the puppet-show at the very climax of the drama to join the stampeding mass, amplifying the clamor and infusing the frantic summons with renewed force: “Seize the rogue! Seize the rogue!”
source_text: “Stop thief! Stop thief!” The cry is taken up by a hundred voices, and the crowd accumulate at every turning. Away they fly, splashing through the mud, and rattling along the pavements: up go the windows, out run the people, onward bear the mob, a whole audience desert Punch in the very thickest of the plot, and, joining the rushing throng, swell the shout, and lend fresh vigour to the cry, “Stop thief! Stop thief!”

text: “Catch the thief! Stop that thief!” A primal hunger for the hunt is buried deep within the human soul. One miserable, gasping child, suffocating from exhaustion, his face a mask of pure fright and his eyes full of torment, pushes his body to the breaking point to stay ahead of his trackers. As they trail him, gaining ground with every passing second, they greet his failing stamina with cheers of delight. “Stop that thief!” Yes, for heaven’s sake, catch him—if only as an act of mercy.
source_text: “Stop thief! Stop thief!” There is a passion _for hunting something_ deeply implanted in the human breast. One wretched breathless child, panting with exhaustion; terror in his looks; agony in his eyes; large drops of perspiration streaming down his face; strains every nerve to make head upon his pursuers; and as they follow on his track, and gain upon him every instant, they hail his decreasing strength with joy. “Stop thief!” Ay, stop him for God’s sake, were it only in mercy!

text: The chamber’s dimensions evoked a subterranean scullery, though it was deprived of even that modest light. An insufferable grime pervaded the space, for it was now Monday, and the room had served as a pen for six intoxicated souls since their apprehension on Saturday evening. Yet this is a trifling matter. In these local watch-houses, citizens are nightly immured for the most petty transgressions—a word deserving of particular attention—within pits so dismal that the cells of Newgate, inhabited by the most monstrous of condemned convicts, would seem like grand estates in contrast. Should any man harbor doubts, let him merely set the two side by side.
source_text: This cell was in shape and size something like an area cellar, only not so light. It was most intolerably dirty; for it was Monday morning; and it had been tenanted by six drunken people, who had been locked up, elsewhere, since Saturday night. But this is little. In our station-houses, men and women are every night confined on the most trivial charges—the word is worth noting—in dungeons, compared with which, those in Newgate, occupied by the most atrocious felons, tried, found guilty, and under sentence of death, are palaces. Let any one who doubts this, compare the two.

text: There is something about that boy’s face," the elderly man murmured to himself as he strolled away, pensively tapping his chin with the corner of his book. "Something that truly affects and intrigues me. Could he actually be innocent? He looked just like— Wait a moment," he exclaimed, stopping dead in his tracks and gazing up at the sky. "Good heavens! Where have I encountered a look like that before?
source_text: “There is something in that boy’s face,” said the old gentleman to himself as he walked slowly away, tapping his chin with the cover of the book, in a thoughtful manner; “something that touches and interests me. _Can_ he be innocent? He looked like— Bye the bye,” exclaimed the old gentleman, halting very abruptly, and staring up into the sky, “Bless my soul!—where have I seen something like that look before?”

text: The office was a wood-paneled front parlor. Mr. Fang sat behind a high bench at the far end of the room, while a wooden enclosure stood near the door. Little Oliver had already been placed inside this pen, shivering uncontrollably as he faced the intimidating scene.
source_text: The office was a front parlour, with a panelled wall. Mr. Fang sat behind a bar, at the upper end; and on one side of the door was a sort of wooden pen in which poor little Oliver was already deposited; trembling very much at the awfulness of the scene.

text: The elderly woman offered no response. Instead, she dried her eyes and then her glasses, which rested on the bedspread, handling them as if they were a natural extension of her own face. She brought Oliver a cool beverage to sip, then gave his cheek a gentle pat, warning him that he must remain perfectly still or risk falling ill again.
