source_text: The old lady made no reply to this; but wiping her eyes first, and her spectacles, which lay on the counterpane, afterwards, as if they were part and parcel of those features, brought some cool stuff for Oliver to drink; and then, patting him on the cheek, told him he must lie very quiet, or he would be ill again.

text: Three days later, he was strong enough to sit in a comfortable armchair, bolstered by a stack of pillows. Because he was still too frail to walk, Mrs. Bedwin had him carried downstairs to her small private parlor. Once he was settled by the warmth of the fire, the kind woman sat down beside him. She was so overjoyed to see his improvement that she immediately broke into a fit of heavy sobbing.
source_text: In three days’ time he was able to sit in an easy-chair, well propped up with pillows; and, as he was still too weak to walk, Mrs. Bedwin had him carried downstairs into the little housekeeper’s room, which belonged to her. Having him set, here, by the fire-side, the good old lady sat herself down too; and, being in a state of considerable delight at seeing him so much better, forthwith began to cry most violently.

text: While he spoke, he gestured quickly toward the portrait hanging above Oliver and then back to the boy’s own face. It was a perfect, living reflection. The eyes, the head, the mouth—every single feature was identical. For a brief second, the expression was so exactly the same that even the finest detail seemed to have been reproduced with jarring accuracy.
source_text: As he spoke, he pointed hastily to the picture over Oliver’s head, and then to the boy’s face. There was its living copy. The eyes, the head, the mouth; every feature was the same. The expression was, for the instant, so precisely alike, that the minutest line seemed copied with startling accuracy!

text: Should I require any further evidence of the disciplined, almost academic logic these young men applied to their precarious situation, I would find it in the way they abandoned the chase the moment the crowd’s attention locked onto Oliver, fleeing toward home by the most direct route possible. I do not mean to suggest that esteemed scholars typically take the shortest path to a conclusion; in fact, their habit is usually to extend the journey through long-winded diversions and intellectual stumbles, much like a drunkard reeling under the weight of too many overlapping thoughts. However, I do firmly maintain that it is the constant practice of many great thinkers to show remarkable foresight in guarding against any outcome that might personally inconvenience them. In their view, one may commit a small wrong to achieve a greater right, employing any means that the desired end appears to justify. The actual measure of that right or wrong—or even the boundary between the two—is left entirely to the discretion of the individual, to be decided by their own supposedly objective and clear-eyed assessment of their specific circumstances.
source_text: If I wanted any further proof of the strictly philosophical nature of the conduct of these young gentlemen in their very delicate predicament, I should at once find it in the fact (also recorded in a foregoing part of this narrative), of their quitting the pursuit, when the general attention was fixed upon Oliver; and making immediately for their home by the shortest possible cut. Although I do not mean to assert that it is usually the practice of renowned and learned sages, to shorten the road to any great conclusion (their course indeed being rather to lengthen the distance, by various circumlocutions and discursive staggerings, like unto those in which drunken men under the pressure of a too mighty flow of ideas, are prone to indulge); still, I do mean to say, and do say distinctly, that it is the invariable practice of many mighty philosophers, in carrying out their theories, to evince great wisdom and foresight in providing against every possible contingency which can be supposed at all likely to affect themselves. Thus, to do a great right, you may do a little wrong; and you may take any means which the end to be attained, will justify; the amount of the right, or the amount of the wrong, or indeed the distinction between the two, being left entirely to the philosopher concerned, to be settled and determined by his clear, comprehensive, and impartial view of his own particular case.

text: The Dodger didn't offer a word in response. Instead, he pulled his hat back on, tucked the tails of his oversized coat under his arm, and pressed his tongue firmly against his cheek. After tapping the bridge of his nose several times with a knowing and practiced gesture, he spun around and slipped away through the courtyard. Master Bates trailed behind him, his face unusually grave with thought.
source_text: The Dodger made no reply; but putting his hat on again, and gathering the skirts of his long-tailed coat under his arm, thrust his tongue into his cheek, slapped the bridge of his nose some half-dozen times in a familiar but expressive manner, and turning on his heel, slunk down the court. Master Bates followed, with a thoughtful countenance.

text: Once he had tossed back a brace of stiff brandies, Mr. Vance deigned to bestow a glance upon the nimble-fingered lads; this rare indulgence sparked a dialogue wherein the particulars of Arthur’s arrest were meticulously recounted, though the Spinner saw fit to weave in such creative flourishes and tactical departures from the facts as he judged most prudent for the moment.
source_text: After swallowing two of three glasses of spirits, Mr. Sikes condescended to take some notice of the young gentlemen; which gracious act led to a conversation, in which the cause and manner of Oliver’s capture were circumstantially detailed, with such alterations and improvements on the truth, as to the Dodger appeared most advisable under the circumstances.

text: It is difficult to guess how long they might have remained seated, staring at one another in a state of uncertainty that was far from pleasant. There is no need to speculate on the matter, however; the abrupt arrival of the two young women Oliver had met previously caused the conversation to flow once more.
source_text: How long they might have sat and looked at each other, in a state of uncertainty not the most pleasant of its kind, it is difficult to guess. It is not necessary to make any guesses on the subject, however; for the sudden entrance of the two young ladies whom Oliver had seen on a former occasion, caused the conversation to flow afresh.

text: In fairness to the young woman, she did not explicitly decline the invitation; she merely voiced a fervent desire to be “blessed” if she should ever consent. This was a sophisticated evasion, proving she possessed that innate gentility which cannot bring itself to wound a fellow soul with the harshness of a direct and pointed refusal.
source_text: It is due to the young lady to say that she did not positively affirm that she would not, but that she merely expressed an emphatic and earnest desire to be “blessed” if she would; a polite and delicate evasion of the request, which shows the young lady to have been possessed of that natural good breeding which cannot bear to inflict upon a fellow-creature, the pain of a direct and pointed refusal.

text: Yet, finding that none among the ragged culprits answered to the name of Julian, nor offered any clue to his fate, Eliza marched boldly toward the gruff official in the embroidered vest. There, she erupted into a display of such wretched grief and piercing woe—greatly enhanced by the frantic rattling of a large house-key and the clutching of a small wicker basket—as she piteously begged for the return of her darling brother.
source_text: But, as neither of these criminals answered to the name of Oliver, or knew anything about him, Nancy made straight up to the bluff officer in the striped waistcoat; and with the most piteous wailings and lamentations, rendered more piteous by a prompt and efficient use of the street-door key and the little basket, demanded her own dear brother.

text: “We must discover where he has gone, my children; he has to be found,” the old man cried in a fit of agitation. “Charley, you are to do nothing but prowl the streets until you bring back some word of him! Nancy, my dear, he must be recovered. I am relying on you—on you and the Artful—for everything! Wait, hold on,” he added, his hands trembling as he fumbled with a locked drawer. “Here is some money. I shall close the shop tonight; you know where to find me. Do not linger here another minute. Not a single second, my dears!”
source_text: “We must know where he is, my dears; he must be found,” said the Jew greatly excited. “Charley, do nothing but skulk about, till you bring home some news of him! Nancy, my dear, I must have him found. I trust to you, my dear,—to you and the Artful for everything! Stay, stay,” added the Jew, unlocking a drawer with a shaking hand; “there’s money, my dears. I shall shut up this shop tonight. You’ll know where to find me! Don’t stop here a minute. Not an instant, my dears!”

text: I beseech you, sir, do not say you mean to banish me!" Julian cried, struck with terror by the elder man’s grave delivery. "Do not cast me back to the wilderness of the streets. Let me stay and serve you. Only do not return me to that den of misery. Have pity, I implore you, on a friendless boy!
source_text: “Oh, don’t tell you are going to send me away, sir, pray!” exclaimed Oliver, alarmed at the serious tone of the old gentleman’s commencement! “Don’t turn me out of doors to wander in the streets again. Let me stay here, and be a servant. Don’t send me back to the wretched place I came from. Have mercy upon a poor boy, sir!”

text: “I hope that isn't the case,” the elderly man replied. “In fact, I don't believe you ever would. I have been misled in the past by people I sought to help, yet I feel a strong desire to trust you anyway. I am more concerned with your well-being than I can quite explain, even to myself. Those I loved most deeply are long gone; but while my life's joy was buried with them, I haven't turned my heart into a tomb or shut out my capacity to care. If anything, profound sorrow has only strengthened and purified my affections.”
source_text: “I hope not,” rejoined the old gentleman. “I do not think you ever will. I have been deceived, before, in the objects whom I have endeavoured to benefit; but I feel strongly disposed to trust you, nevertheless; and I am more interested in your behalf than I can well account for, even to myself. The persons on whom I have bestowed my dearest love, lie deep in their graves; but, although the happiness and delight of my life lie buried there too, I have not made a coffin of my heart, and sealed it up, forever, on my best affections. Deep affliction has but strengthened and refined them.”

text: Dusty. I am familiar with a companion who harbors a mutton-jowled youth; a 'grand boy,' as he is called; with a spherical cranium, crimsoned jowls, and staring orbs; a detestable creature; with a torso and limbs that appear to be bursting from the seams of his indigo attire; possessing the roar of a boatswain and the voracity of a beast. I recognize him! The miscreant!
source_text: “Mealy. I know a friend who has a beef-faced boy; a fine boy, they call him; with a round head, and red cheeks, and glaring eyes; a horrid boy; with a body and limbs that appear to be swelling out of the seams of his blue clothes; with the voice of a pilot, and the appetite of a wolf. I know him! The wretch!”

text: The front door swung wide. Oliver dashed one way and the girl another, as Mrs. Bedwin stood on the doorstep, calling out for the boy; but he was nowhere to be found. Oliver and the girl soon returned, out of breath, to report that they had found no trace of him.
source_text: The street-door was opened. Oliver ran one way; and the girl ran another; and Mrs. Bedwin stood on the step and screamed for the boy; but there was no boy in sight. Oliver and the girl returned, in a breathless state, to report that there were no tidings of him.

text: It is a notable circumstance, proving the immense gravity we assign to our private verdicts and the stubbornness with which we champion our crudest guesses, that while Mr. Barstow was hardly a man of ill intent, and would have been truly pained to see his friend victimized by a ruse, he did, in that fleeting moment, most passionately desire that young Edward should never show his face again.
source_text: It is worthy of remark, as illustrating the importance we attach to our own judgments, and the pride with which we put forth our most rash and hasty conclusions, that, although Mr. Grimwig was not by any means a bad-hearted man, and though he would have been unfeignedly sorry to see his respected friend duped and deceived, he really did most earnestly and strongly hope at that moment, that Oliver Twist might not come back.

text: Keep up that smirk," growled Thorne, clattering the heavy iron back into its stand and eyeing him with brutal disdain; "sneer all you like. You’ll never find much to mock, however, unless you’re watching me through a hangman’s cowl. I hold the leash on you, Silas; and by God, I mean to keep it tight. Mark me! If the trap opens for me, it opens for you too; so you’d best see to my welfare.
source_text: “Grin away,” said Sikes, replacing the poker, and surveying him with savage contempt; “grin away. You’ll never have the laugh at me, though, unless it’s behind a nightcap. I’ve got the upper hand over you, Fagin; and, d—me, I’ll keep it. There! If I go, you go; so take care of me.”

text: “Right enough, they do,” growled Thorne. “It was the week of the Great Fair when I was pinched; and there wasn’t a brass horn in the stalls whose thin bleat didn’t reach me. Once I was barred in for the dark hours, the roar and ruckus beyond the walls made that grim dungeon so stiflingly quiet that I could have cracked my skull against the iron-plated door for the madness of it.”
source_text: “Of course they can,” replied Sikes. “It was Bartlemy time when I was shopped; and there warn’t a penny trumpet in the fair, as I couldn’t hear the squeaking on. Arter I was locked up for the night, the row and din outside made the thundering old jail so silent, that I could almost have beat my brains out against the iron plates of the door.”

text: For a full half-hour they pressed through desolate and soot-stained lanes, meeting only a few stray figures whose desperate appearances suggested they shared the same wretched station in life as Blackwood himself. Eventually, they emerged into a particularly foul, cramped alley lined with shops for cast-off garments; the hound trotted forward, as if sensing his duties as a sentry were over, and came to a halt before the entrance of a shuttered and seemingly derelict storefront. The building was in a state of total decay, and nailed to the door was a weathered board announcing it was for lease—a sign that looked as though it had rotted there for a lifetime.
source_text: They walked on, by little-frequented and dirty ways, for a full half-hour: meeting very few people, and those appearing from their looks to hold much the same position in society as Mr. Sikes himself. At length they turned into a very filthy narrow street, nearly full of old-clothes shops; the dog running forward, as if conscious that there was no further occasion for his keeping on guard, stopped before the door of a shop that was closed and apparently untenanted; the house was in a ruinous condition, and on the door was nailed a board, intimating that it was to let: which looked as if it had hung there for many years.

text: “It warms my heart to find you in such fine health, my boy,” remarked the Master, inclining his head with a flourish of feigned reverence. “The Weaver will find you a commoner set of rags, my boy, lest you should ruin that gentleman’s attire. Why the lack of notice, my boy? Had you sent word of your return, we might have set a hot kettle on the fire for your arrival.”
source_text: “Delighted to see you looking so well, my dear,” said the Jew, bowing with mock humility. “The Artful shall give you another suit, my dear, for fear you should spoil that Sunday one. Why didn’t you write, my dear, and say you were coming? We’d have got something warm for supper.”

text: Fair or not," Sikes snapped, "just hand it over! Do you think Nancy and I have nothing better to do with our time than spend it tracking down and snatching back every kid who gets caught because of you? Hand it over, you greedy old bag of bones—give it here!
source_text: “Fair, or not fair,” retorted Sikes, “hand over, I tell you! Do you think Nancy and me has got nothing else to do with our precious time but to spend it in scouting arter, and kidnapping, every young boy as gets grabbed through you? Give it here, you avaricious old skeleton, give it here!”

text: "They look quite fine," Charley Bates remarked, twisting his features into various smirks as he pretended to read one of the books. "Beautifully written, wouldn't you say, Oliver?" Upon seeing the expression of utter dismay Oliver directed at his tormentors, young Bates—who possessed a sharp eye for the absurd—dissolved into another fit of laughter, even more boisterous than the one before.
source_text: “They’re very pretty,” said Charley Bates: who, with sundry grimaces, had been affecting to read one of the volumes in question; “beautiful writing, isn’t is, Oliver?” At sight of the dismayed look with which Oliver regarded his tormentors, Master Bates, who was blessed with a lively sense of the ludicrous, fell into another ectasy, more boisterous than the first.

text: “Courteous speech!” shrieked the woman, her fury manifesting in a way that was truly harrowing to behold. “Courteous speech, you rogue! As if you have merited anything of the sort from my lips. I was filching in the gutters for your sake before I had reached half the years of that child there!” she cried, indicating young Elias. “Twelve winters I have spent in this same wretched labor, bound to your command. Do you not recognize it? Speak the truth! Do you not recognize it?”
source_text: “Civil words!” cried the girl, whose passion was frightful to see. “Civil words, you villain! Yes, you deserve ’em from me. I thieved for you when I was a child not half as old as this!” pointing to Oliver. “I have been in the same trade, and in the same service, for twelve years since. Don’t you know it? Speak out! Don’t you know it?”

text: The young woman spoke no further; instead, she began clawing at her hair and ripping her garments in a sudden fit of rage. She lunged at the old man with a ferocity that surely would have left him scarred by her vengeance, had Sikes not intercepted her by the wrists at the critical moment. After a few desperate, futile attempts to break free, she collapsed into a dead faint.
source_text: The girl said nothing more; but, tearing her hair and dress in a transport of passion, made such a rush at the Jew as would probably have left signal marks of her revenge upon him, had not her wrists been seized by Sikes at the right moment; upon which, she made a few ineffectual struggles, and fainted.

text: Young Ned, seemingly invigorated by his mission, seized the forked stick and conducted Silas into a neighboring cook-room, which housed several of those very pallets upon which he had formerly languished. There, amidst fits of uncontrollable hilarity, he produced the precise set of ragged vestments Silas had so jubilantly abandoned at Mr. Halloway’s; the chance presentation of which to the Patriarch, by the junk-dealer who had bought them, had functioned as the very first hint regarding the boy’s sanctuary.
source_text: Master Bates, apparently much delighted with his commission, took the cleft stick: and led Oliver into an adjacent kitchen, where there were two or three of the beds on which he had slept before; and here, with many uncontrollable bursts of laughter, he produced the identical old suit of clothes which Oliver had so much congratulated himself upon leaving off at Mr. Brownlow’s; and the accidental display of which, to Fagin, by the Jew who purchased them, had been the very first clue received, of his whereabout.

text: “The rival coach service has a contract for these two and is taking them at a discount,” Mr. Bumble remarked. “They’re both in a sorry state, and we’ve realized it’s two pounds cheaper to move them than to bury them—assuming we can offload them onto another parish. I think we’ll manage it, provided they don’t drop dead on the journey just to spite us. Ha! ha! ha!”
source_text: “The opposition coach contracts for these two; and takes them cheap,” said Mr. Bumble. “They are both in a very low state, and we find it would come two pound cheaper to move ’em than to bury ’em—that is, if we can throw ’em upon another parish, which I think we shall be able to do, if they don’t die upon the road to spite us. Ha! ha! ha!”

text: “Whereas a young lad, by the name of Julian Thorne, vanished or was lured away on Friday evening last from his residence at Blackheath, and has not since been encountered. The aforementioned bounty will be paid to any person who shall provide such intelligence as may lead to the recovery of the said Julian Thorne, or serve to illuminate his former history, in which the advertiser is, for various reasons, most earnestly interested.”
source_text: “Whereas a young boy, named Oliver Twist, absconded, or was enticed, on Thursday evening last, from his home, at Pentonville; and has not since been heard of. The above reward will be paid to any person who will give such information as will lead to the discovery of the said Oliver Twist, or tend to throw any light upon his previous history, in which the advertiser is, for many reasons, warmly interested.”

text: It would be exhausting to recount the beadle’s full testimony, as it dragged on for nearly twenty minutes; however, the core of his story was that Oliver was a nameless orphan, the offspring of base and wicked parents. From his earliest days, the boy had supposedly exhibited nothing but deceit, ingratitude, and malice. His brief history in his hometown had ended after he launched a bloody and spineless attack on an innocent lad, subsequently fleeing his master’s house under the cover of night. To verify his own identity and the truth of his claims, Mr. Bumble placed the documents he had brought to the city upon the table. Crossing his arms once more, he waited in silence for Mr. Brownlow’s reaction.
source_text: It would be tedious if given in the beadle’s words: occupying, as it did, some twenty minutes in the telling; but the sum and substance of it was, that Oliver was a foundling, born of low and vicious parents. That he had, from his birth, displayed no better qualities than treachery, ingratitude, and malice. That he had terminated his brief career in the place of his birth, by making a sanguinary and cowardly attack on an unoffending lad, and running away in the night-time from his master’s house. In proof of his really being the person he represented himself, Mr. Bumble laid upon the table the papers he had brought to town. Folding his arms again, he then awaited Mr. Brownlow’s observations.

text: Throughout the house, the decaying shutters were drawn tight, their iron bars bolted firmly into the rotting wood. The only light that entered crept through small circular holes at the top, serving only to deepen the gloom and populate the rooms with unsettling shadows. One attic window at the rear remained unshuttered, though it was caged behind rusted bars. From this spot, Oliver would stare out for hours with a mournful expression, seeing nothing but a tangled wilderness of blackened chimneys, steep gables, and soot-covered rooftops. Now and then, a rough-looking head might appear over a distant parapet, only to be pulled back quickly. Because his window was nailed shut and caked with years of rain and smoke, it was difficult enough to even perceive the shapes of the buildings, let alone hope to be seen or heard. He was as utterly isolated as if he were living inside the copper ball atop St. Paul’s Cathedral.
source_text: In all the rooms, the mouldering shutters were fast closed: the bars which held them were screwed tight into the wood; the only light which was admitted, stealing its way through round holes at the top: which made the rooms more gloomy, and filled them with strange shadows. There was a back-garret window with rusty bars outside, which had no shutter; and out of this, Oliver often gazed with a melancholy face for hours together; but nothing was to be descried from it but a confused and crowded mass of housetops, blackened chimneys, and gable-ends. Sometimes, indeed, a grizzly head might be seen, peering over the parapet-wall of a distant house; but it was quickly withdrawn again; and as the window of Oliver’s observatory was nailed down, and dimmed with the rain and smoke of years, it was as much as he could do to make out the forms of the different objects beyond, without making any attempt to be seen or heard,—which he had as much chance of being, as if he had lived inside the ball of St. Paul’s Cathedral.

text: “Your education has been most neglected,” remarked the Sly One, inspecting his toes with an air of immense vanity after Arthur had completed the polish. “The Patron will make a proper tool of you, I expect, or you’ll be the only lad he ever took in who didn't yield a dividend. Best to get a move on; you’ll be wedded to the craft much sooner than you anticipate, and you’re only burning through precious minutes, Arthur.”
source_text: “You’ve been brought up bad,” said the Dodger, surveying his boots with much satisfaction when Oliver had polished them. “Fagin will make something of you, though, or you’ll be the first he ever had that turned out unprofitable. You’d better begin at once; for you’ll come to the trade long before you think of it; and you’re only losing time, Oliver.”

text: Young Bates supported these suggestions with several moralizing lectures of his own. Once he had finished, he and his companion, Mr. Dawkins, began a vivid account of the many thrills inherent to their way of life. They peppered this description with various hints to Oliver that his smartest move would be to win Fagin’s approval without further hesitation, using the same methods they had employed to earn it themselves.
source_text: Master Bates backed this advice with sundry moral admonitions of his own: which, being exhausted, he and his friend Mr. Dawkins launched into a glowing description of the numerous pleasures incidental to the life they led, interspersed with a variety of hints to Oliver that the best thing he could do, would be to secure Fagin’s favour without more delay, by the means which they themselves had employed to gain it.

text: Their discourse reached an abrupt conclusion at this juncture, for the patriarch had re-entered the dwelling, flanked by Mistress Meg and a fellow quite unfamiliar to the lad. This newcomer, though greeted boisterously by the Rogue as Silas Vane, had lingered upon the landing to trade a few playful flatteries with the lady before finally making his entrance.
source_text: The conversation proceeded no farther at this time, for the Jew had returned home accompanied by Miss Betsy, and a gentleman whom Oliver had never seen before, but who was accosted by the Dodger as Tom Chitling; and who, having lingered on the stairs to exchange a few gallantries with the lady, now made his appearance.

text: Following a brief private exchange between Fagin and the newcomer, they pulled their seats toward the warmth of the fire. Beckoning Oliver to sit at his side, Fagin steered the talk toward subjects he knew would capture their attention: the lucrative nature of their business, the Dodger’s remarkable talent, the pleasant company of Charley Bates, and Fagin’s own generous spirit. In time, these topics were fully spent, as was Mr. Chitling, who found that a few weeks in the house of correction took a heavy toll on one's energy. Miss Betsy soon took her leave, allowing the rest of the company to seek their rest.
source_text: After some words apart between the last comer and Fagin, they drew their chairs towards the fire; and the Jew, telling Oliver to come and sit by him, led the conversation to the topics most calculated to interest his hearers. These were, the great advantages of the trade, the proficiency of the Dodger, the amiability of Charley Bates, and the liberality of the Jew himself. At length these subjects displayed signs of being thoroughly exhausted; and Mr. Chitling did the same: for the house of correction becomes fatiguing after a week or two. Miss Betsy accordingly withdrew; and left the party to their repose.

text: Henceforth, Julian was rarely granted a moment’s privacy, being kept in the perpetual company of the two urchins who daily reenacted their habitual charade with the master. Whether these exercises were meant to sharpen their own dexterity or to entice Julian’s, the old man kept to himself. In the quiet hours, the elder would regale them with chronicles of the thefts he had committed in his prime, weaving in such whimsical and eccentric details that Julian found himself laughing aloud, his mirth surfacing in direct defiance of his more virtuous instincts.
source_text: From this day, Oliver was seldom left alone; but was placed in almost constant communication with the two boys, who played the old game with the Jew every day: whether for their own improvement or Oliver’s, Mr. Fagin best knew. At other times the old man would tell them stories of robberies he had committed in his younger days: mixed up with so much that was droll and curious, that Oliver could not help laughing heartily, and showing that he was amused in spite of all his better feelings.

text: Sludge clung heavily to the paving-stones, and a soot-colored fog choked the narrow lanes; the drizzle descended with a leaden persistence, making every surface feel frigid and greasy to the hand. It was an evening perfectly calibrated for a wretch like the usurer to be afoot. As he slunk through the gloom, hugging the damp masonry and the hollows of the doorways, the wizened figure appeared like some foul vermin spawned from the very mire and shadow through which he wove: emerging in the dark hours to scavenge for some choice carrion to satisfy his greed.
source_text: The mud lay thick upon the stones, and a black mist hung over the streets; the rain fell sluggishly down, and everything felt cold and clammy to the touch. It seemed just the night when it befitted such a being as the Jew to be abroad. As he glided stealthily along, creeping beneath the shelter of the walls and doorways, the hideous old man seemed like some loathsome reptile, engendered in the slime and darkness through which he moved: crawling forth, by night, in search of some rich offal for a meal.

text: This acknowledgement was voiced with a subtle tremor of unease, betraying a lingering uncertainty as to how it might be received; for Mr. Vane and his protégée had remained estranged since her defiant intervention on the child’s account. Whatever misgivings he harbored were promptly dispelled by the girl’s composure. She withdrew her boots from the iron grate, shifted her seat, and gestured for Vane to bring his own closer to the hearth, offering no further comment on their quarrel; the evening, after all, possessed a biting chill that brooked no ceremony.
source_text: The latter recognition was uttered with just enough of embarrassment to imply a doubt of its reception; for Mr. Fagin and his young friend had not met, since she had interfered in behalf of Oliver. All doubts upon the subject, if he had any, were speedily removed by the young lady’s behaviour. She took her feet off the fender, pushed back her chair, and bade Fagin draw up his, without saying more about it: for it was a cold night, and no mistake.

text: “He doesn’t,” Sikes sneered. “Or he refuses to, and it amounts to the same thing. Speak plainly and call things by their real names; don’t sit there twitching and dropping hints, as if you weren’t the very person who first dreamed up this robbery. What are you getting at?”
source_text: “No, he don’t,” sneered Mr. Sikes. “Or he won’t, and that’s the same thing. Speak out, and call things by their right names; don’t sit there, winking and blinking, and talking to me in hints, as if you warn’t the very first that thought about the robbery. Wot d’ye mean?”

text: “Ugh!” Miller barked, coming to a dead stop as the girl, without so much as turning her shoulders, flicked her eyes toward the old miser's face. “It matters little which role I play. You’re stranded without my muscle, and that's the truth; but a man learns to tread carefully when he's tethered to a creature of your sort.”
source_text: “Umph!” cried Sikes, stopping short, as the girl, scarcely moving her head, looked suddenly round, and pointed for an instant to the Jew’s face. “Never mind which part it is. You can’t do it without me, I know; but it’s best to be on the safe side when one deals with you.”

text: The old man gestured toward Nancy, who remained staring into the flames, and signaled that he wanted her out of the room. Sikes shrugged with an air of annoyance, as if he thought such secrecy was unneeded; however, he gave in and asked Nancy to go out and fetch him a pitcher of beer.
source_text: The Jew nodded his head towards Nancy, who was still gazing at the fire; and intimated, by a sign, that he would have her told to leave the room. Sikes shrugged his shoulders impatiently, as if he thought the precaution unnecessary; but complied, nevertheless, by requesting Miss Nancy to fetch him a jug of beer.

text: “Scare him?” Sikes echoed. “This won’t be some harmless act, believe me. If he shows any sign of trouble once the job begins, I’m going all the way. You won’t see him alive again, Fagin. Think long and hard about that before you send him out. Remember what I’ve told you!” the thief said, balancing a crowbar he had pulled from beneath the bed frame.
source_text: “Frighten him!” echoed Sikes. “It’ll be no sham frightening, mind you. If there’s anything queer about him when we once get into the work; in for a penny, in for a pound. You won’t see him alive again, Fagin. Think of that, before you send him. Mark my words!” said the robber, poising a crowbar, which he had drawn from under the bedstead.

text: Overwhelmed by a sudden surge of panic, the boy snapped the book shut and shoved it aside. Falling to his knees, he begged the heavens to keep him from such atrocities, pleading for a quick death rather than a future destined for such grisly and horrific acts. Slowly, his agitation subsided, and in a hushed, trembling voice, he prayed for deliverance from his current peril. He implored that if any mercy remained for a lonely orphan who had never known the love of kin or companions, it might reach him now, as he stood forsaken and isolated in the heart of such pervasive evil and guilt.
source_text: In a paroxysm of fear, the boy closed the book, and thrust it from him. Then, falling upon his knees, he prayed Heaven to spare him from such deeds; and rather to will that he should die at once, than be reserved for crimes, so fearful and appalling. By degrees, he grew more calm, and besought, in a low and broken voice, that he might be rescued from his present dangers; and that if any aid were to be raised up for a poor outcast boy who had never known the love of friends or kindred, it might come to him now, when, desolate and deserted, he stood alone in the midst of wickedness and guilt.

text: Oliver perceived that he possessed some influence over the girl’s conscience, and for a moment, he considered appealing to her pity for his defenseless condition. But then, the realization struck him that it was only eleven o’clock; the streets were still filled with people, surely some of whom would be willing to believe his account. As this thought took hold, he stepped forward and announced, with a hint of urgency, that he was ready.
source_text: Oliver could see that he had some power over the girl’s better feelings, and, for an instant, thought of appealing to her compassion for his helpless state. But, then, the thought darted across his mind that it was barely eleven o’clock; and that many people were still in the streets: of whom surely some might be found to give credence to his tale. As the reflection occured to him, he stepped forward: and said, somewhat hastily, that he was ready.

text: The young woman kept a firm hold on Oliver’s hand, continuing to whisper the same warnings and reassurances she had already given him. It was all happening so quickly that he barely had a moment to realize where he was or how he had arrived before the carriage came to a stop at the house the old man had sought out the previous evening.
source_text: The girl still held Oliver fast by the hand, and continued to pour into his ear, the warnings and assurances she had already imparted. All was so quick and hurried, that he had scarcely time to recollect where he was, or how he came there, when the carriage stopped at the house to which the Jew’s steps had been directed on the previous evening.

text: “Now,” growled the ruffian, gripping Elias’s wrist and jamming the cold iron so hard against his temple that the boy could not help but flinch. “If you utter a single word once we are under the sky, save to answer me, this lead will be in your skull before you can blink. If you are determined to speak without my permission, then you had best settle your affairs with the Almighty beforehand.”
source_text: “Well,” said the robber, grasping Oliver’s wrist, and putting the barrel so close to his temple that they touched; at which moment the boy could not repress a start; “if you speak a word when you’re out o’doors with me, except when I speak to you, that loading will be in your head without notice. So, if you _do_ make up your mind to speak without leave, say your prayers first.”

text: Eliza, her eyes barely lighting upon the lad, tossed him a scrap of silk to bind about his throat; Miller provided a coarse, heavy mantle to be fastened over his shoulders. So arrayed, the boy surrendered his hand to the ruffian, who paused only to indicate with a grim motion the firearm concealed within the pocket of his greatcoat. Gripping the small hand firmly in his own and murmuring a final word to Eliza, he pulled the child away into the gloom.
source_text: Nancy, scarcely looking at the boy, threw him a handkerchief to tie round his throat; Sikes gave him a large rough cape to button over his shoulders. Thus attired, he gave his hand to the robber, who, merely pausing to show him with a menacing gesture that he had that same pistol in a side-pocket of his great-coat, clasped it firmly in his, and, exchanging a farewell with Nancy, led him away.

text: They stepped out into a dismal morning, met by a biting wind and heavy rain under a sky of heavy, dark clouds. A night of torrential downpours had left the roads covered in deep pools, and the gutters were overflowing with runoff. A pale hint of dawn was just beginning to break, yet it seemed to heighten the misery of the surroundings rather than alleviate it; the weak light only served to wash out the glow of the streetlamps, failing to bring any warmth or brightness to the soaked rooftops and desolate streets. This part of town appeared entirely deserted, with every window tightly shuttered and the thoroughfares they walked through remaining silent and empty.
source_text: It was a cheerless morning when they got into the street; blowing and raining hard; and the clouds looking dull and stormy. The night had been very wet: large pools of water had collected in the road: and the kennels were overflowing. There was a faint glimmering of the coming day in the sky; but it rather aggravated than relieved the gloom of the scene: the sombre light only serving to pale that which the street lamps afforded, without shedding any warmer or brighter tints upon the wet house-tops, and dreary streets. There appeared to be nobody stirring in that quarter of the town; the windows of the houses were all closely shut; and the streets through which they passed, were noiseless and empty.

text: As the rhythmic markers of the road fell away, Julian found himself increasingly consumed by a restless dread concerning his escort’s hidden purpose. Chelsea, Fulham, Putney, and the river-crossings of Richmond were surrendered to the distance; nevertheless, they pressed forward with a persistence that suggested their odyssey had scarcely begun. Finally, they approached a low-roofed alehouse known as The Swan and Hoop, a short distance from where a solitary path veered into the gloom. At this spot, the cart lurched to a standstill.
source_text: As they passed the different mile-stones, Oliver wondered, more and more, where his companion meant to take him. Kensington, Hammersmith, Chiswick, Kew Bridge, Brentford, were all passed; and yet they went on as steadily as if they had only just begun their journey. At length, they came to a public-house called the Coach and Horses; a little way beyond which, another road appeared to run off. And here, the cart stopped.

text: The newcomer pondered this reasoning with an air of great solemnity; having done so, he grasped Sikes by the hand and declared him to be a truly fine man. Sikes merely retorted that the stranger was surely jesting—a conclusion that would have been even more certain had Sikes been sober at the time.
source_text: The stranger reflected upon this argument, with a very profound face; having done so, he seized Sikes by the hand: and declared he was a real good fellow. To which Mr. Sikes replied, he was joking; as, if he had been sober, there would have been strong reason to suppose he was.

text: Seven o’clock rang out as they passed Sunbury Church. A light from the ferry-house window across the road spilled outward, deepening the shadows around a dark yew tree and the graves nestled at its base. The muffled sound of falling water echoed nearby, while the leaves of the ancient tree rustled softly in the night breeze. It felt like a hushed melody composed for the peace of the dead.
source_text: As they passed Sunbury Church, the clock struck seven. There was a light in the ferry-house window opposite: which streamed across the road, and threw into more sombre shadow a dark yew-tree with graves beneath it. There was a dull sound of falling water not far off; and the leaves of the old tree stirred gently in the night wind. It seemed like quiet music for the repose of the dead.

text: “Can you hear me?” the same voice bellowed. “Bill Sikes is out in the hallway with no one to show him any respect, and you’re slumped there as if you’ve been taking laudanum with every meal. Are you any more alert now, or do I need to use this iron candlestick to wake you up properly?”
source_text: “Do you hear?” cried the same voice. “There’s Bill Sikes in the passage with nobody to do the civil to him; and you sleeping there, as if you took laudanum with your meals, and nothing stronger. Are you any fresher now, or do you want the iron candlestick to wake you thoroughly?”

text: The business concluded and Blackwood having glutted his ravenous appetite—Julian could manage nothing but a dry heel of bread which they had compelled him to swallow—the two men reclined upon their chairs to seek a brief interval of sleep. The boy remained huddled on his stool by the hearth; meanwhile, Gable, swaddled in a heavy rug, stretched himself out upon the floorboards just beyond the iron fender.
source_text: This done, and Sikes having satisfied his appetite (Oliver could eat nothing but a small crust of bread which they made him swallow), the two men laid themselves down on chairs for a short nap. Oliver retained his stool by the fire; Barney wrapped in a blanket, stretched himself on the floor: close outside the fender.

text: Now, for the first time, Oliver—nearly driven to madness by sorrow and fright—realized that the true purpose of their journey was burglary and theft, perhaps even murder. He wrung his hands together, a stifled cry of revulsion escaping his lips. A haze clouded his vision; a cold sweat broke out across his pale face; his strength deserted him, and he collapsed to his knees.
source_text: And now, for the first time, Oliver, well-nigh mad with grief and terror, saw that housebreaking and robbery, if not murder, were the objects of the expedition. He clasped his hands together, and involuntarily uttered a subdued exclamation of horror. A mist came before his eyes; the cold sweat stood upon his ashy face; his limbs failed him; and he sank upon his knees.

text: “In the name of all that is sacred, release me!” Arthur cried. “Allow me to flee into the open meadows and meet my end in solitude. I shall never trouble the capital again; never, I swear it! I implore you, do not force me to take what is not mine. By the love of the radiant spirits who dwell in the heights of Heaven, have pity on my soul!”
source_text: “Oh! for God’s sake let me go!” cried Oliver; “let me run away and die in the fields. I will never come near London; never, never! Oh! pray have mercy on me, and do not make me steal. For the love of all the bright Angels that rest in Heaven, have mercy upon me!”

text: “Pay close heed, you wretched scrap,” Blackwood hissed, producing a shuttered lantern from his greatcoat and thrusting its blinding beam directly into Julian’s trembling face. “I am hoisting you through that aperture. Take the light; creep stealthily up the stairs ahead of you, move through the narrow vestibule to the main entrance; unbolt the locks and grant us entry.”
source_text: “Now listen, you young limb,” whispered Sikes, drawing a dark lantern from his pocket, and throwing the glare full on Oliver’s face; “I’m a going to put you through there. Take this light; go softly up the steps straight afore you, and along the little hall, to the street door; unfasten it, and let us in.”

text: In the fleeting interval permitted him to master his terror, the lad had forged a grim determination that, regardless of the mortal peril, he would stake everything on a single rush for the staircase to awaken the household. Possessed by this desperate intent, he stole forward on the instant, treading with the silent caution of a shadow.
source_text: In the short time he had had to collect his senses, the boy had firmly resolved that, whether he died in the attempt or not, he would make one effort to dart upstairs from the hall, and alarm the family. Filled with this idea, he advanced at once, but stealthily.

text: A bell began to toll loudly, its clanging mixed with the crack of gunshots and the shouting of men, as he felt the jarring sensation of being rushed over rugged ground. Soon, the commotion grew faint and muddled in the distance; a lethal chill gripped the boy’s heart, and he drifted into total silence and darkness.
source_text: Then came the loud ringing of a bell, mingled with the noise of fire-arms, and the shouts of men, and the sensation of being carried over uneven ground at a rapid pace. And then, the noises grew confused in the distance; and a cold deadly feeling crept over the boy’s heart; and he saw or heard no more.

text: The evening was punishingly cold. A thick, frozen crust had formed over the snow, so that only the loose mounds gathered in corners and side streets were vulnerable to the shrieking wind. As if venting its fury on whatever prey it could find, the gale snatched up these drifts, spinning them into a thousand misty spirals before scattering them into the dark air. It was a bleak, piercing night that drove the fortunate to gather by a warm fire and give thanks for their sanctuary, while the starving and homeless were left with no choice but to lie down and die. Many exhausted outcasts close their eyes on the desolate streets at such times; regardless of their past sins, they are unlikely to wake in a world more cruel than the one they have left behind.
source_text: The night was bitter cold. The snow lay on the ground, frozen into a hard thick crust, so that only the heaps that had drifted into byways and corners were affected by the sharp wind that howled abroad: which, as if expending increased fury on such prey as it found, caught it savagely up in clouds, and, whirling it into a thousand misty eddies, scattered it in air. Bleak, dark, and piercing cold, it was a night for the well-housed and fed to draw round the bright fire and thank God they were at home; and for the homeless, starving wretch to lay him down and die. Many hunger-worn outcasts close their eyes in our bare streets, at such times, who, let their crimes have been what they may, can hardly open them in a more bitter world.

text: This was the state of the world abroad when Mrs. Gable, the superintendent of the parish house which has already been noted as the nursery of young Silas, settled herself before a bright fire in her own snug parlor. She looked, with no little pride, upon a tiny round table supporting a tray of matching size, stocked with the various implements for that most cherished of matronly comforts. In short, Mrs. Gable was preparing to refresh her spirits with a cup of tea. As her eye traveled from the table to the hearth, where a kettle of the most diminutive scale sang a thin song in a piping voice, her internal gratification was so heightened that she allowed herself the luxury of a smile.
source_text: Such was the aspect of out-of-doors affairs, when Mrs. Corney, the matron of the workhouse to which our readers have been already introduced as the birthplace of Oliver Twist, sat herself down before a cheerful fire in her own little room, and glanced, with no small degree of complacency, at a small round table: on which stood a tray of corresponding size, furnished with all necessary materials for the most grateful meal that matrons enjoy. In fact, Mrs. Corney was about to solace herself with a cup of tea. As she glanced from the table to the fireplace, where the smallest of all possible kettles was singing a small song in a small voice, her inward satisfaction evidently increased,—so much so, indeed, that Mrs. Corney smiled.

text: I remain your obedient servant, madam," declared Mr. Grimshaw, who had lingered upon the mat to scrape the mire from his soles and buffet the frost from his heavy cape; and who now stepped into the light, clutching a tricorn in one hand and a hempen parcel in the other. "Shall I draw the latch, madam?
source_text: “At your service, ma’am,” said Mr. Bumble, who had been stopping outside to rub his shoes clean, and to shake the snow off his coat; and who now made his appearance, bearing the cocked hat in one hand and a bundle in the other. “Shall I shut the door, ma’am?”

text: I have never," remarked Mr. Bumble, "seen matters reach such a disgraceful state. Only the day before yesterday, a man—and as you are a woman of experience, ma’am, I feel I may speak plainly—a fellow with scarcely a shred of clothing to his name (at which Mrs. Corney cast her eyes to the floor) went to the overseer’s house just as he was expecting guests for dinner. He insisted he be given relief, Mrs. Corney. Since he refused to move and was quite distressing the company, the overseer sent out a pound of potatoes and a half-pint of oatmeal. 'Good heavens!' cried the ungrateful scoundrel, 'what use is this to me? You might as well have given me a pair of iron spectacles!' 'Very well,' replied the overseer, snatching the food back at once, 'you shall have nothing else here.' 'Then I will simply die in the street!' the vagabond shouted. 'Oh no, you won't,' the overseer answered.
source_text: “I never,” said Mr. Bumble, “see anything like the pitch it’s got to. The day afore yesterday, a man—you have been a married woman, ma’am, and I may mention it to you—a man, with hardly a rag upon his back (here Mrs. Corney looked at the floor), goes to our overseer’s door when he has got company coming to dinner; and says, he must be relieved, Mrs. Corney. As he wouldn’t go away, and shocked the company very much, our overseer sent him out a pound of potatoes and half a pint of oatmeal. ‘My heart!’ says the ungrateful villain, ‘what’s the use of _this_ to me? You might as well give me a pair of iron spectacles!’ ‘Very good,’ says our overseer, taking ’em away again, ‘you won’t get anything else here.’ ‘Then I’ll die in the streets!’ says the vagrant. ‘Oh no, you won’t,’ says our overseer.”

text: The tea was prepared and served in absolute silence. Mr. Bumble, having draped a handkerchief across his knees to shield the glory of his breeches from stray crumbs, began to eat and drink. He punctuated these activities with an occasional heavy sigh, which appeared to have no ill effect on his hunger; instead, the outbursts seemed to only hasten his industrious progress through the tea and toast.
source_text: The tea was made, and handed in silence. Mr. Bumble, having spread a handkerchief over his knees to prevent the crumbs from sullying the splendour of his shorts, began to eat and drink; varying these amusements, occasionally, by fetching a deep sigh; which, however, had no injurious effect upon his appetite, but, on the contrary, rather seemed to facilitate his operations in the tea and toast department.

text: Had the matron shifted her seat to the right, she would have been singed by the hearth; had she moved to the left, she would have surely tumbled into Mr. Bumble’s embrace. Consequently, being a cautious woman who undoubtedly grasped these risks in a single look, she kept her position and presented Mr. Bumble with another serving of tea.
source_text: Now, if the matron had moved her chair to the right, she would have been scorched by the fire; and if to the left, she must have fallen into Mr. Bumble’s arms; so (being a discreet matron, and no doubt foreseeing these consequences at a glance) she remained where she was, and handed Mr. Bumble another cup of tea.

text: No, no, ma’am," the old woman answered, "it’s no use; she is well beyond any help now. I’ve seen many people pass away, from tiny infants to robust men, and I recognize the signs of approaching death well enough. But her mind is uneasy. In those few moments when the convulsions subside—which isn't often, for she is struggling terribly to let go—she insists she has something to tell you. She won't be able to go peacefully until you arrive, ma'am.
source_text: “No, no, mistress,” replied the old woman, “nobody can; she’s far beyond the reach of help. I’ve seen a many people die; little babes and great strong men; and I know when death’s a-coming, well enough. But she’s troubled in her mind: and when the fits are not on her,—and that’s not often, for she is dying very hard,—she says she has got something to tell, which you must hear. She’ll never die quiet till you come, mistress.”

text: Upon hearing this news, the respectable Mrs. Corney grumbled a series of insults against elderly women who could not even manage to die without intentionally bothering their social superiors. Wrapping herself in a heavy shawl she had quickly snatched up, she briefly asked Mr. Bumble to wait until her return, lest some urgent matter should arise. Ordering the messenger to move quickly and not spend the whole night limping up the stairs, she followed her out of the room with a sour expression, scolding the woman the entire way.
source_text: At this intelligence, the worthy Mrs. Corney muttered a variety of invectives against old women who couldn’t even die without purposely annoying their betters; and, muffling herself in a thick shawl which she hastily caught up, briefly requested Mr. Bumble to stay till she came back, lest anything particular should occur. Bidding the messenger walk fast, and not be all night hobbling up the stairs, she followed her from the room with a very ill grace, scolding all the way.

text: Mr. Mudge’s demeanor, when at last he was left to his own devices, was quite remarkable. He pried open the buffet, took a census of the teaspoons, appraised the weight of the sugar-grippers, and peered intently at a silver cream-pot to ensure it was of the genuine sort; then, having pacified his avarice, he set his three-cornered hat at a tilt and pranced with profound solemnity exactly four times around the mahogany.
source_text: Mr. Bumble’s conduct on being left to himself, was rather inexplicable. He opened the closet, counted the teaspoons, weighed the sugar-tongs, closely inspected a silver milk-pot to ascertain that it was of the genuine metal, and, having satisfied his curiosity on these points, put on his cocked hat corner-wise, and danced with much gravity four distinct times round the table.

text: The servant performed the task as bid, shaking her head to suggest the patient would not expire so quickly; having finished, she sat back down beside the fellow-watcher, who had by then returned. The mistress, with a look of irritation, pulled her shawl tight and took a seat at the bed’s end.
source_text: The attendant did as she was told: shaking her head meanwhile, to intimate that the woman would not die so easily; having done so, she resumed her seat by the side of the other nurse, who had by this time returned. The mistress, with an expression of impatience, wrapped herself in her shawl, and sat at the foot of the bed.

text: After a long interval of silence, the two elderly women stood from the bed and huddled over the fire, stretching their frail hands toward the warmth. The flames cast a haunting light across their sunken features, making their harsh appearance seem all the more unsettling as they leaned in to speak in hushed tones.
source_text: When they had sat in silence for some time, the two old women rose from the bed, and crouching over the fire, held out their withered hands to catch the heat. The flame threw a ghastly light on their shrivelled faces, and made their ugliness appear terrible, as, in this position, they began to converse in a low voice.

text: “She didn't say a single thing,” the other answered. “For a brief moment, she clawed and picked at her own arms, but I gripped her hands firmly, and she soon drifted away. There was so little life left in her that keeping her still was no trouble at all. I’m not as feeble as I look for an old woman, even if I do live on the parish dole; no, not by a long shot!”
source_text: “Not a word,” replied the other. “She plucked and tore at her arms for a little time; but I held her hands, and she soon dropped off. She hasn’t much strength in her, so I easily kept her quiet. I ain’t so weak for an old woman, although I am on parish allowance; no, no!”

text: “Pay heed to my words,” the expiring woman rasped, struggling as though to fan the last smoldering ember of her vitality into a flame. “Within these very walls—upon this wretched pallet—I once looked after a comely lass, brought to us with her feet mangled and raw from the road, all stained with the filth and gore of her journey. She delivered a son and perished in the labor. Stay—let me recollect—which year was it that saw her end?”
source_text: “Now listen to me,” said the dying woman aloud, as if making a great effort to revive one latent spark of energy. “In this very room—in this very bed—I once nursed a pretty young creetur’, that was brought into the house with her feet cut and bruised with walking, and all soiled with dust and blood. She gave birth to a boy, and died. Let me think—what was the year again!”

text: “The youth became the living image of his mother,” the woman muttered, lost in her own wandering thoughts and ignoring the question entirely. “I could never gaze upon his face without seeing her ghost in his features. That poor, lost girl! She was so tender, such a meek creature. Stay; the story is not quite finished. I have more to reveal, do I not?”
source_text: “The boy grew so like his mother,” said the woman, rambling on, and not heeding the question, “that I could never forget it when I saw his face. Poor girl! poor girl! She was so young, too! Such a gentle lamb! Wait; there’s more to tell. I have not told you all, have I?”

text: “Look here, Fagin,” he remarked, gesturing gloomily toward his leather boots. “Not a smear of polish on them since that night we both remember; not a lick of blacking, I swear! But stop staring at me like that, man. All things in due course. I won't speak a word of business until I’ve had my fill of food and drink; so bring out the provisions, and let me have a decent meal for the first time in three days!”
source_text: “See there, Faguey,” he said, pointing disconsolately to his top boots; “not a drop of Day and Martin since you know when; not a bubble of blacking, by Jove! But don’t look at me in that way, man. All in good time. I can’t talk about business till I’ve eat and drank; so produce the sustainance, and let’s have a quiet fill-out for the first time these three days!”

text: Bill was lugging him on his back, running flat out. We halted for a moment to carry him between the two of us; his head was lolling and he felt deathly cold. The law was right behind us—it was every man for himself then, a mad scramble to stay off the gallows. We split up and dumped the boy in a ditch. Whether he's still alive or not, I haven't a clue.
source_text: “Bill had him on his back, and scudded like the wind. We stopped to take him between us; his head hung down, and he was cold. They were close upon our heels; every man for himself, and each from the gallows! We parted company, and left the youngster lying in a ditch. Alive or dead, that’s all I know about him.”

text: Yet, as the old man glanced back and signaled his desire for solitude, and since the diminutive fellow found it difficult to extract himself from his seat, the Cripples was momentarily deprived of Mr. Lively's company. By the time he had managed to stand, the man had vanished; thus, after a fruitless attempt to catch a glimpse of him by straining on his tiptoes, Mr. Lively wedged himself back into his small chair. Sharing a somber nod with a woman in the shop across the way—a look heavy with mutual suspicion—he returned to his pipe with a grave and steady air.
source_text: But as the Jew, looking back, waved his hand to intimate that he preferred being alone; and, moreover, as the little man could not very easily disengage himself from the chair; the sign of the Cripples was, for a time, bereft of the advantage of Mr. Lively’s presence. By the time he had got upon his legs, the Jew had disappeared; so Mr. Lively, after ineffectually standing on tiptoe, in the hope of catching sight of him, again forced himself into the little chair, and, exchanging a shake of the head with a lady in the opposite shop, in which doubt and mistrust were plainly mingled, resumed his pipe with a grave demeanour.

text: The Broken Crutch—or just the Crutch, as it was titled by its regulars—was the public-house where Graves and his dog had formerly been observed. With a brief signal to the barman, Lowndes went straight upstairs. He opened a door and, sliding quietly into the chamber, peered about with nervous intent, shading his eyes as if scanning the shadows for one particular soul.
source_text: The Three Cripples, or rather the Cripples; which was the sign by which the establishment was familiarly known to its patrons: was the public-house in which Mr. Sikes and his dog have already figured. Merely making a sign to a man at the bar, Fagin walked straight upstairs, and opening the door of a room, and softly insinuating himself into the chamber, looked anxiously about: shading his eyes with his hand, as if in search of some particular person.

text: “Nothing,” replied the proprietor of the Cripples, for it was the man himself. “He won't stir until the coast is clear. You can be sure they're sniffing around down there; if he moved, he’d expose the whole business immediately. Barney’s doing just fine, or I’d have heard otherwise. I’d bet anything he’s handling things the right way. Just leave him to it.”
source_text: “None,” replied the landlord of the Cripples; for it was he. “He won’t stir till it’s all safe. Depend on it, they’re on the scent down there; and that if he moved, he’d blow upon the thing at once. He’s all right enough, Barney is, else I should have heard of him. I’ll pound it, that Barney’s managing properly. Let him alone for that.”

text: “Nay, nay,” the merchant stammered; for though he had sought the man with great urgency, he seemed strangely unburdened by the empty room. “Convey that I called for him, and that he must attend me this evening. No—make it the morrow. Since he is away, the morrow will serve well enough.”
source_text: “No, no,” said the Jew, hastily; as though, however desirous he might be to see the person in question, he was nevertheless relieved by his absence. “Tell him I came here to see him; and that he must come to me tonight. No, say tomorrow. As he is not here, tomorrow will be time enough.”

text: “Stop pestering me, Fagin,” the girl answered, lifting her head with a weary air. “If Bill didn't succeed this time, he'll manage it the next. He’s pulled off plenty of scores for you before, and he’ll do plenty more when he’s able; and when he isn't, he won't, so let that be the end of it.”
source_text: “Don’t worry me now, Fagin!” replied the girl, raising her head languidly. “If Bill has not done it this time, he will another. He has done many a good job for you, and will do many more when he can; and when he can’t he won’t; so no more about that.”

text: “The boy will have to take his chances just like the others,” Nancy cut in quickly. “I’ll say it again: I hope he’s dead and gone, beyond the reach of any more pain and far from your clutches—provided, of course, that Bill is alright. If Toby managed to escape, then Bill is surely safe; Bill is worth twice what Toby is any day of the week.”
source_text: “The boy must take his chance with the rest,” interrupted Nancy, hastily; “and I say again, I hope he is dead, and out of harm’s way, and out of yours,—that is, if Bill comes to no harm. And if Toby got clear off, Bill’s pretty sure to be safe; for Bill’s worth two of Toby any time.”

text: The mounting weight of the evidence left Mr. Monks visibly shaken. His denials grew increasingly half-hearted as their search continued to yield nothing; at last, he let out a few dark, mocking laughs and admitted that his overactive imagination must have gotten the better of him. He refused to speak of it any further that evening, abruptly noting that it was already after one in the morning. With that, the charming pair went their separate ways.
source_text: This accumulated testimony effectually staggered Mr. Monks. His protestations had gradually become less and less vehement as they proceeded in their search without making any discovery; and, now, he gave vent to several very grim laughs, and confessed it could only have been his excited imagination. He declined any renewal of the conversation, however, for that night: suddenly remembering that it was past one o’clock. And so the amiable couple parted.

text: Having followed him this distance on his journey back and concluded the necessary arrangements for the old woman’s burial, let us now look into the fate of young Oliver Twist to see if he remains in the ditch where Toby Crackit abandoned him.
source_text: And now that we have accompanied him so far on his road home, and have made all necessary preparations for the old woman’s funeral, let us set on foot a few inquires after young Oliver Twist, and ascertain whether he be still lying in the ditch where Toby Crackit left him.

text: Through a curious alignment of events, the others had experienced that same unsettling sensation at that exact second. It was evident, then, that the gate was the cause, particularly as there was no uncertainty about the timing; all three vividly remembered spotting the thieves at the very moment the change occurred.
source_text: By a remarkable coincidence, the other two had been visited with the same unpleasant sensation at that precise moment. It was quite obvious, therefore, that it was the gate; especially as there was no doubt regarding the time at which the change had taken place, because all three remembered that they had come in sight of the robbers at the instant of its occurance.

text: Bolstering one another with such talk, yet remaining huddled tight and glancing nervously about whenever a fresh gust rattled the branches, the three men hurried back to a tree where they had hidden their lantern, fearing its glow might show the thieves where to aim. Snatching up the light, they made for home at a brisk pace; long after their dark silhouettes had vanished from view, the flame could still be seen flickering and bobbing in the distance, like a ghostly vapor rising from the heavy, sodden air through which it was carried.
source_text: Encouraging each other with such converse as this; but, keeping very close together, notwithstanding, and looking apprehensively round, whenever a fresh gust rattled through the boughs; the three men hurried back to a tree, behind which they had left their lantern, lest its light should inform the thieves in what direction to fire. Catching up the light, they made the best of their way home, at a good round trot; and long after their dusky forms had ceased to be discernible, the light might have been seen twinkling and dancing in the distance, like some exhalation of the damp and gloomy atmosphere through which it was swiftly borne.

text: Tibbs, having ducked behind the heavy door to swing it wide, had no sooner glimpsed the small figure than he let out a sharp, sudden cry. Mr. Grimshaw, seizing the lad by a single wrist and a boot—sparing, by some providence, the mangled limb—hauled him bodily into the passage and deposited him at full length upon the cold floor.
source_text: Brittles, who had got behind the door to open it, no sooner saw Oliver, than he uttered a loud cry. Mr. Giles, seizing the boy by one leg and one arm (fortunately not the broken limb) lugged him straight into the hall, and deposited him at full length on the floor thereof.

text: Prattling without pause, he trailed Mr. Hedges toward the upper landing; and while this climb is in progress, the reader may be apprised that Mr. Sterling, a local surgeon known throughout the surrounding parishes simply as “the doctor,” had acquired a portly frame more through the expansion of his own mirth than the richness of his larder. He was as warm-hearted and robust, and withal as singular an old bachelor, as might be encountered in a journey of many weeks by any traveler now walking the earth.
source_text: Talking all the way, he followed Mr. Giles upstairs; and while he is going upstairs, the reader may be informed, that Mr. Losberne, a surgeon in the neighbourhood, known through a circuit of ten miles round as “the doctor,” had grown fat, more from good-humour than from good living: and was as kind and hearty, and withal as eccentric an old bachelor, as will be found in five times that space, by any explorer alive.

text: The two women found their patience tested far longer than Mr. Losberne had predicted, as the hours dragged by with Oliver still lost in a profound sleep. Twilight had already fallen by the time the sympathetic physician arrived with the news that the boy was finally conscious enough to talk. He explained that while Oliver was gravely ill and exhausted from blood loss, the child was so desperate to reveal something that it seemed better to let him speak immediately rather than force him to stay silent until morning, as the doctor would have otherwise required.
source_text: The patience of the two ladies was destined to undergo a longer trial than Mr. Losberne had led them to expect; for hour after hour passed on, and still Oliver slumbered heavily. It was evening, indeed, before the kind-hearted doctor brought them the intelligence, that he was at length sufficiently restored to be spoken to. The boy was very ill, he said, and weak from the loss of blood; but his mind was so troubled with anxiety to disclose something, that he deemed it better to give him the opportunity, than to insist upon his remaining quiet until next morning: which he should otherwise have done.

text: “The house is broken into,” the doctor remarked, “and two men catch a fleeting glimpse of a boy through a haze of gunsmoke and the frantic confusion of the night. Then, the very next morning, a lad arrives at this same house, and merely because his arm is bandaged, these men seize him—putting his life in peril—and insist he is the thief. Now, the real issue is whether their claims are supported by the truth; if they are not, what sort of predicament have these men created for themselves?”
source_text: “Here’s the house broken into,” said the doctor, “and a couple of men catch one moment’s glimpse of a boy, in the midst of gunpowder smoke, and in all the distraction of alarm and darkness. Here’s a boy comes to that very same house, next morning, and because he happens to have his arm tied up, these men lay violent hands upon him—by doing which, they place his life in great danger—and swear he is the thief. Now, the question is, whether these men are justified by the fact; if not, in what situation do they place themselves?”

text: Lamps were finally lit; and Blathers and Duff, trailed by the local constable, Brittles, Giles, and indeed the entire household, squeezed into the tiny room at the hallway’s end to examine the window. They then marched around to the lawn to peer back in from the outside. This was followed by the delivery of a candle to inspect the shutters, a lantern to search for footprints, and a pitchfork to poke through the shrubbery. After these tasks were completed before a breathless audience, they retreated inside, where Giles and Brittles were forced into a theatrical reenactment of their roles in the night's chaos. They performed this scene some six times, their stories diverging only slightly during the first attempt, but contradicting one another in a dozen major ways by the last. Once this was finished, Blathers and Duff cleared the room for a private conference, the secrecy and gravity of which would have made a high-level medical consultation over the most complex of ailments appear like simple playground talk.
source_text: Lights were then procured; and Messrs. Blathers and Duff, attended by the native constable, Brittles, Giles, and everybody else in short, went into the little room at the end of the passage and looked out at the window; and afterwards went round by way of the lawn, and looked in at the window; and after that, had a candle handed out to inspect the shutter with; and after that, a lantern to trace the footsteps with; and after that, a pitchfork to poke the bushes with. This done, amidst the breathless interest of all beholders, they came in again; and Mr. Giles and Brittles were put through a melodramatic representation of their share in the previous night’s adventures: which they performed some six times over: contradicting each other, in not more than one important respect, the first time, and in not more than a dozen the last. This consummation being arrived at, Blathers and Duff cleared the room, and held a long council together, compared with which, for secrecy and solemnity, a consultation of great doctors on the knottiest point in medicine, would be mere child’s play.

text: “I am inclined to disagree, Miss Catherine,” the physician sighed, his head moving in a slow, rhythmic denial. “I hardly think such evidence would suffice to clear him, neither in the eyes of the local magistrates nor before those of a more elevated station. What, in their estimation, is he? A common truant. Viewed through the lens of cynical logic and likelihood, his history remains a most questionable affair.”
source_text: “I doubt it, my dear young lady,” said the doctor, shaking his head. “I don’t think it would exonerate him, either with them, or with legal functionaries of a higher grade. What is he, after all, they would say? A runaway. Judged by mere worldly considerations and probabilities, his story is a very doubtful one.”

text: “The only certainty I can grasp,” remarked Mr. Sterling finally, lowering himself into a chair with a forced composure born of panic, “is that we are compelled to maintain a facade of absolute confidence. Our motives are virtuous, and in that righteousness we shall find our justification. The lad is currently gripped by a rising delirium, rendering further interrogation impossible; there, at least, is a small mercy. We have no choice but to play this hand as it lies, and if our best efforts yield a poor result, the blame cannot rest upon our shoulders. Enter!”
source_text: “All I know is,” said Mr. Losberne, at last: sitting down with a kind of desperate calmness, “that we must try and carry it off with a bold face. The object is a good one, and that must be our excuse. The boy has strong symptoms of fever upon him, and is in no condition to be talked to any more; that’s one comfort. We must make the best of it; and if bad be the best, it is no fault of ours. Come in!”

text: Oliver had been drifting in and out of sleep, yet he appeared more unwell and flushed with fever than he had at any point prior. With the doctor’s assistance, he managed to remain upright in bed for a brief moment, staring at the strangers without any comprehension of the situation; in truth, he seemed unable to recall where he was or anything that had recently occurred.
source_text: Oliver had been dozing; but looked worse, and was more feverish than he had appeared yet. Being assisted by the doctor, he managed to sit up in bed for a minute or so; and looked at the strangers without at all understanding what was going forward—in fact, without seeming to recollect where he was, or what had been passing.

text: Oliver continued to flourish and grow strong, nurtured by the collective kindness of Mrs. Maylie, Rose, and the generous Mr. Losberne. If sincere prayers born of profound gratitude find their way to heaven—and truly, what else could?—the blessings the boy invoked for his protectors took root in their hearts, spreading a quiet sense of serenity and contentment.
source_text: Meanwhile, Oliver gradually throve and prospered under the united care of Mrs. Maylie, Rose, and the kind-hearted Mr. Losberne. If fervent prayers, gushing from hearts overcharged with gratitude, be heard in heaven—and if they be not, what prayers are!—the blessings which the orphan child called down upon them, sunk into their souls, diffusing peace and happiness.

text: Poor boy," Rose said one day, as Oliver struggled weakly to voice the gratitude that reached his pale lips. "You will have many chances to help us, if you wish. We are going to the countryside, and my aunt wants you to come along. The peace and quiet, the fresh air, and the lovely spring weather will have you feeling better in just a few days. We'll find plenty of little jobs for you to do once you're strong enough for the effort.
source_text: “Poor fellow!” said Rose, when Oliver had been one day feebly endeavouring to utter the words of thankfulness that rose to his pale lips; “you shall have many opportunities of serving us, if you will.
