We are going into the country, and my aunt intends that you shall accompany us. The quiet place, the pure air, and all the pleasure and beauties of spring, will restore you in a few days. We will employ you in a hundred ways, when you can bear the trouble.”

text: "It would bring me more joy than I can put into words," the young lady answered. "To think that my dear, kind aunt was the instrument of saving someone from the terrible suffering you have described would be a profound pleasure; but to know that the person who received her compassion is genuinely thankful and devoted in return would delight me more than you can imagine. Do you follow my meaning?" she asked, observing Oliver’s pensive expression.
source_text: “You will make me happier than I can tell you,” replied the young lady. “To think that my dear good aunt should have been the means of rescuing any one from such sad misery as you have described to us, would be an unspeakable pleasure to me; but to know that the object of her goodness and compassion was sincerely grateful and attached, in consequence, would delight me, more than you can well imagine. Do you understand me?” she inquired, watching Oliver’s thoughtful face.

text: The stranger pursued them to the carriage door, shouting the most savage oaths and insults as he went. While Mr. Losberne turned to address the coachman, the man peered inside and fixed Oliver with a look so piercing and malevolent—so heavy with vengeful fury—that the boy would find it impossible to banish the image from his mind, waking or sleeping, for many months to come. He did not cease his violent shouting until the driver had returned to his seat; and as they pulled away, they could still see him some distance behind, stomping upon the earth and tearing at his own hair in a frantic display of either genuine or performative madness.
source_text: The man followed to the chariot door, uttering the wildest imprecations and curses all the way; but as Mr. Losberne turned to speak to the driver, he looked into the carriage, and eyed Oliver for an instant with a glance so sharp and fierce and at the same time so furious and vindictive, that, waking or sleeping, he could not forget it for months afterwards. He continued to utter the most fearful imprecations, until the driver had resumed his seat; and when they were once more on their way, they could see him some distance behind: beating his feet upon the ground, and tearing his hair, in transports of real or pretended rage.

text: They settled in an exquisite place. For Oliver, who had spent his years amidst grimy multitudes and the chaos of constant strife, it was as if he had stepped into a different world. Climbing roses and honeysuckle draped the cottage walls, ivy spiraled up the thick trees, and the garden blooms saturated the air with a wonderful fragrance. Close by sat a quiet churchyard, devoid of grand or hideous headstones; it was instead a field of modest, mossy mounds where the village’s departed found their rest. Oliver often walked among them, and reflecting on the miserable pit where his mother was buried, he would occasionally hide away to cry. But whenever he looked up at the infinite blue above, he stopped picturing her beneath the soil, and his grief, though lingering, lost its bitter sting.
source_text: It was a lovely spot to which they repaired. Oliver, whose days had been spent among squalid crowds, and in the midst of noise and brawling, seemed to enter on a new existence there. The rose and honeysuckle clung to the cottage walls; the ivy crept round the trunks of the trees; and the garden-flowers perfumed the air with delicious odours. Hard by, was a little churchyard; not crowded with tall unsightly gravestones, but full of humble mounds, covered with fresh turf and moss: beneath which, the old people of the village lay at rest. Oliver often wandered here; and, thinking of the wretched grave in which his mother lay, would sometimes sit him down and sob unseen; but, when he raised his eyes to the deep sky overhead, he would cease to think of her as lying in the ground, and would weep for her, sadly, but without pain.

text: Rose Maylie’s health declined with alarming speed; by midnight, she had drifted into a feverish delirium. A local physician remained at her bedside in constant attendance. After his initial examination, he drew Mrs. Maylie aside to confide that the illness was of a most perilous nature. “In truth,” he admitted, “her recovery would be nothing short of a miracle.”
source_text: Rose Maylie had rapidly grown worse; before mid-night she was delirious. A medical practitioner, who resided on the spot, was in constant attendance upon her; and after first seeing the patient, he had taken Mrs. Maylie aside, and pronounced her disorder to be one of a most alarming nature. “In fact,” he said, “it would be little short of a miracle, if she recovered.”

text: Daybreak arrived, and the small cottage fell into a heavy, lonely silence. Neighbors spoke only in hushed tones, and anxious faces appeared periodically at the gate, while women and children departed in tears. Throughout the entire day and long after nightfall, Oliver paced restlessly back and forth in the garden, his eyes drawn every moment to the sickroom above. He shuddered at the sight of the darkened window, which looked as though death itself were already lying within. Late that night, Mr. Losberne finally arrived. "It is a cruel blow," the doctor said, turning away as he spoke; "one so young and so dearly loved; but there is very little hope."
source_text: Morning came; and the little cottage was lonely and still. People spoke in whispers; anxious faces appeared at the gate, from time to time; women and children went away in tears. All the livelong day, and for hours after it had grown dark, Oliver paced softly up and down the garden, raising his eyes every instant to the sick chamber, and shuddering to see the darkened window, looking as if death lay stretched inside. Late that night, Mr. Losberne arrived. “It is hard,” said the good doctor, turning away as he spoke; “so young; so much beloved; but there is very little hope.”

text: A fresh dawn broke. The heavens beamed with a heartless splendor, casting light as if the earth were a stranger to grief; and though the gardens flourished in their peak of vigor, and the atmosphere vibrated with the pulse of living things, the gentle girl ebbed away. Arthur stole toward the weathered cemetery, where, perched upon a mossy rise, he surrendered to his tears and whispered his orisons in the stillness.
source_text: Another morning. The sun shone brightly; as brightly as if it looked upon no misery or care; and, with every leaf and flower in full bloom about her; with life, and health, and sounds and sights of joy, surrounding her on every side: the fair young creature lay, wasting fast. Oliver crept away to the old churchyard, and sitting down on one of the green mounds, wept and prayed for her, in silence.

text: The harsh toll of the church bell shattered those youthful reflections. It rang again and again, signaling a funeral. A modest group of mourners entered the gate, wearing white ribbons for the young life lost. They stood bareheaded at the grave; among the weeping crowd was a mother—a mother once. Yet the sun shone brilliantly, and the birds sang on.
source_text: A knell from the church bell broke harshly on these youthful thoughts. Another! Again! It was tolling for the funeral service. A group of humble mourners entered the gate: wearing white favours; for the corpse was young. They stood uncovered by a grave; and there was a mother—a mother once—among the weeping train. But the sun shone brightly, and the birds sang on.

text: Moisture gathered in Elias’s eyes as he summoned the memory of that singular hour which had inaugurated his long season of peace; meanwhile, the elder man shifted his stance to hide his countenance, lapsing into a profound and wordless stillness. Elias caught the faint, rhythmic catch of a breath—a sound remarkably like a sob—echoing more than once in the quiet air. Yet, he refrained from offering any new word, instinctively understanding the tumult within his friend’s breast. He remained a few paces off, affecting a deep interest in the arrangement of his wildflowers to grant the other his necessary solitude.
source_text: The tears stood in Oliver’s eyes as he recalled the scene which was the beginning of so much happiness; and the gentleman turned his face away, and remained silent, for some minutes. Oliver thought he heard him sob, more than once; but he feared to interrupt him by any fresh remark—for he could well guess what his feelings were—and so stood apart, feigning to be occupied with his nosegay.

text: Throughout this entire time, Mr. Giles, still wearing his white nightcap, had been sitting on the steps of the carriage with his elbows propped on his knees. He was busy dabbing his eyes with a blue cotton handkerchief covered in white polka dots. That the sincere man wasn't merely faking his distress was made perfectly clear by his bloodshot eyes when he finally looked up at the young gentleman who had turned to speak to him.
source_text: All this time, Mr. Giles, with the white nightcap on, had been sitting on the steps of the chaise, supporting an elbow on each knee, and wiping his eyes with a blue cotton pocket-handkerchief dotted with white spots. That the honest fellow had not been feigning emotion, was abundantly demonstrated by the very red eyes with which he regarded the young gentleman, when he turned round and addressed him.

text: During their progress, Silas found himself stealing frequent, inquisitive looks at the stranger. The man appeared to be in the middle of his third decade and was of average stature; his features were open and agreeable, and his manner was marked by an easy, winning grace. Despite the disparity of their years, the newcomer’s resemblance to the elderly matron was so pronounced that Silas would have had little trouble discerning their kinship, had the gentleman not already acknowledged her as his mother.
source_text: As they walked along, Oliver glanced from time to time with much interest and curiosity at the new comer. He seemed about five-and-twenty years of age, and was of the middle height; his countenance was frank and handsome; and his demeanor easy and prepossessing. Notwithstanding the difference between youth and age, he bore so strong a likeness to the old lady, that Oliver would have had no great difficulty in imagining their relationship, if he had not already spoken of her as his mother.

text: “I realize she deserves the finest and most genuine love a man's heart can provide,” said Mrs. Maylie. “I know her own loyalty and warmth demand a return that is both deep and lasting. If I didn't believe that, and if I weren't certain that a change in a loved one’s behavior would break her heart, I wouldn't find this task so difficult or feel such an internal conflict while trying to carry out what I believe is my strict duty.”
source_text: “I know that she deserves the best and purest love the heart of man can offer,” said Mrs. Maylie; “I know that the devotion and affection of her nature require no ordinary return, but one that shall be deep and lasting. If I did not feel this, and know, besides, that a changed behaviour in one she loved would break her heart, I should not feel my task so difficult of performance, or have to encounter so many struggles in my own bosom, when I take what seems to me to be the strict line of duty.”

text: I *have* thought about it!" he snapped back impatiently. "Mother, I’ve been weighing this for years—ever since I was old enough to have a serious thought. My feelings haven't changed, and they never will. Why should I endure the pain of staying silent when waiting won't do any good? No. Before I leave this place, Rose is going to hear me out.
source_text: “I _have_ considered!” was the impatient reply; “Mother, I have considered, years and years. I have considered, ever since I have been capable of serious reflection. My feelings remain unchanged, as they ever will; and why should I suffer the pain of a delay in giving them vent, which can be productive of no earthly good? No! Before I leave this place, Rose shall hear me.”

text: Mr. Giles approached the corner with significant gravity and a touch of intrigue, where he was favored with a brief, whispered dialogue with the doctor. Once the exchange concluded, he performed a series of elaborate bows and departed with a stride of remarkable stateliness. Though the nature of this conversation was kept secret from those in the parlor, the kitchen was quickly informed of its contents. Mr. Giles headed there at once, and after calling for a mug of ale, declared with an effective air of grandeur that his mistress, moved by his brave defense during the attempted burglary, had deposited twenty-five pounds into the local savings bank for his exclusive use. At this news, the two maidservants raised their hands in wonder. Mr. Giles, smoothing his shirt-front, demurred with a "No, no," requesting that they inform him if he ever appeared too arrogant toward his social inferiors. He then offered several other comments intended to showcase his humility; these were received with great acclaim and were, in truth, about as insightful and relevant as the pronouncements of great men typically tend to be.
source_text: Mr. Giles walked into the corner with much importance, and some wonder, and was honoured with a short whispering conference with the doctor, on the termination of which, he made a great many bows, and retired with steps of unusual stateliness. The subject matter of this conference was not disclosed in the parlour, but the kitchen was speedily enlightened concerning it; for Mr. Giles walked straight thither, and having called for a mug of ale, announced, with an air of majesty, which was highly effective, that it had pleased his mistress, in consideration of his gallant behaviour on the occasion of that attempted robbery, to deposit, in the local savings-bank, the sum of five-and-twenty pounds, for his sole use and benefit. At this, the two women-servants lifted up their hands and eyes, and supposed that Mr. Giles, pulling out his shirt-frill, replied, “No, no”; and that if they observed that he was at all haughty to his inferiors, he would thank them to tell him so. And then he made a great many other remarks, no less illustrative of his humility, which were received with equal favour and applause, and were, withal, as original and as much to the purpose, as the remarks of great men commonly are.

text: Julian found the hours far from burdensome, even though the young mistress had not yet emerged from her sickroom, and his evening airings were limited to occasional, brief wanderings with the elder Mrs. Sterling. He threw himself into his studies under the silver-haired scholar with a fresh intensity, working so diligently that the speed of his own mastery left him quite amazed. It was during the course of these quiet labors that he was abruptly shaken and filled with dread by a most startling turn of events.
source_text: Nor did Oliver’s time hang heavy on his hands, although the young lady had not yet left her chamber, and there were no evening walks, save now and then, for a short distance, with Mrs. Maylie. He applied himself, with redoubled assiduity, to the instructions of the white-headed old gentleman, and laboured so hard that his quick progress surprised even himself. It was while he was engaged in this pursuit, that he was greatly startled and distressed by a most unexpected occurrence.

text: On a lovely evening, as the initial shadows of dusk began to descend over the land, Oliver sat by his window, deeply immersed in his books. He had been studying them for quite a while; and, because the day had been exceptionally hot and he had spent much of it in strenuous activity, it was no slight to the authors, regardless of who they were, to observe that he gradually and quietly drifted off to sleep.
source_text: One beautiful evening, when the first shades of twilight were beginning to settle upon the earth, Oliver sat at this window, intent upon his books. He had been poring over them for some time; and, as the day had been uncommonly sultry, and he had exerted himself a great deal, it is no disparagement to the authors, whoever they may have been, to say, that gradually and by slow degrees, he fell asleep.

text: It was only a second, a fleeting glimpse before his eyes, and then they were gone. Yet the recognition had been mutual, and their expressions were now etched into his mind as permanently as if they had been carved in stone and placed before him since birth. He stood paralyzed for a moment; then, vaulting through the window into the garden, he cried out loudly for help.
source_text: It was but an instant, a glance, a flash, before his eyes; and they were gone. But they had recognised him, and he them; and their look was as firmly impressed upon his memory, as if it had been deeply carved in stone, and set before him from his birth. He stood transfixed for a moment; then, leaping from the window into the garden, called loudly for help.

text: Their efforts had yielded nothing. Not a single fresh footprint could be found anywhere. They stood now atop a small rise, overlooking the surrounding countryside for several miles in every direction. To the left, a village sat nestled in a hollow; however, to reach it from the path Oliver had indicated, the men would have had to cross a wide stretch of exposed ground, a feat they could not have accomplished so quickly. A dense wood bordered the meadows in a different direction, but they could not have reached that shelter for the same reason.
source_text: The search was all in vain. There were not even the traces of recent footsteps, to be seen. They stood now, on the summit of a little hill, commanding the open fields in every direction for three or four miles. There was the village in the hollow on the left; but, in order to gain that, after pursuing the track Oliver had pointed out, the men must have made a circuit of open ground, which it was impossible they could have accomplished in so short a time. A thick wood skirted the meadow-land in another direction; but they could not have gained that covert for the same reason.

text: The gentle girl’s eyes brimmed with tears as those words were uttered; and when a single drop fell into the flower over which she leaned, shimmering within its cup and heightening its beauty, it seemed as though the pure overflow of her youthful heart claimed a natural kinship with the loveliest things in the world.
source_text: There were tears in the eyes of the gentle girl, as these words were spoken; and when one fell upon the flower over which she bent, and glistened brightly in its cup, making it more beautiful, it seemed as though the outpouring of her fresh young heart, claimed kindred naturally, with the loveliest things in nature.

text: There is no goal more honorable, nothing more suited to a person's highest character, than the endeavor to win a heart like yours," the young man said, taking her hand in his. "Rose, my dearest Rose! I have loved you for years—truly, for years. I once hoped to build a reputation first, so I could return home with pride and tell you that I sought success only so you might share in it. In my daydreams, I imagined reminding you of the many quiet ways I showed my devotion as a boy, and asking for your hand as if to honor an unspoken vow we made long ago. That moment hasn't arrived; I have achieved no fame, and my youthful dreams are still just dreams. Nevertheless, I offer you the heart that has been yours for so long, and I stake my entire future on your answer.
source_text: “There is no pursuit more worthy of me: more worthy of the highest nature that exists: than the struggle to win such a heart as yours,” said the young man, taking her hand. “Rose, my own dear Rose! For years—for years—I have loved you; hoping to win my way to fame, and then come proudly home and tell you it had been pursued only for you to share; thinking, in my daydreams, how I would remind you, in that happy moment, of the many silent tokens I had given of a boy’s attachment, and claim your hand, as in redemption of some old mute contract that had been sealed between us! That time has not arrived; but here, with not fame won, and no young vision realised, I offer you the heart so long your own, and stake my all upon the words with which you greet the offer.”

text: “The future before you,” Rose replied with steady conviction, “is a magnificent one. Every honor that great talent and influential relatives can secure in public life is within your grasp. Yet those connections are prideful; I will not associate with those who might look with contempt upon the mother who gave me life, nor will I bring shame or failure upon the son of the woman who has so faithfully filled that mother’s place. In short,” she said, turning away as her fleeting strength began to fail her, “there is a shadow upon my name for which society punishes the innocent. I will not carry that burden into any family but my own, and the weight of that reproach shall rest on me alone.”
source_text: “The prospect before you,” answered Rose, firmly, “is a brilliant one. All the honours to which great talents and powerful connections can help men in public life, are in store for you. But those connections are proud; and I will neither mingle with such as may hold in scorn the mother who gave me life; nor bring disgrace or failure on the son of her who has so well supplied that mother’s place. In a word,” said the young lady, turning away, as her temporary firmness forsook her, “there is a stain upon my name, which the world visits on innocent heads. I will carry it into no blood but my own; and the reproach shall rest alone on me.”

text: “Wait, Elena! Only a moment more, I implore you!” Julian cried, dropping to his knees before her. “If my life had been cast in a darker mold—if I were destined for a path of quiet, nameless labor—if I were wretched, infirm, or utterly alone—would you have shunned me then? Or is it merely this prospect of worldly acclaim and silver that has birthed such a cruel doubt within you?”
source_text: “One word more, Rose. Dearest Rose! one more!” cried Harry, throwing himself before her. “If I had been less—less fortunate, the world would call it—if some obscure and peaceful life had been my destiny—if I had been poor, sick, helpless—would you have turned from me then? Or has my probable advancement to riches and honour, given this scruple birth?”

text: If your response is what I have only just dared to hope," Harry countered, "it will cast a light of joy across my solitary journey and illuminate the way forward. It is no small thing to transform the life of someone who loves you more than anything else with just a few short words. Oh, Rose—for the sake of my intense and lasting devotion, for all the pain I have borne for you and all you might yet condemn me to face—please, answer this single question!
source_text: “If your answer be what I almost dare to hope it is,” retorted Harry, “it will shed a gleam of happiness upon my lonely way, and light the path before me. It is not an idle thing to do so much, by the utterance of a few brief words, for one who loves you beyond all else. Oh, Rose: in the name of my ardent and enduring attachment; in the name of all I have suffered for you, and all you doom me to undergo; answer me this one question!”

text: I’d really rather you didn't mention this to them," Harry said, his words coming out in a rush. "It might make my mother feel like she has to write to me more often, and that’s just an added stress for her. Let’s keep this between us; just be sure to tell me everything. I’m relying on you.
source_text: “I would rather you did not mention it to them,” said Harry, hurrying over his words; “because it might make my mother anxious to write to me oftener, and it is a trouble and worry to her. Let it be a secret between you and me; and mind you tell me everything! I depend upon you.”

text: Certain elevations in social standing possess a unique prestige that transcends their material benefits, largely derived from the specific garments they demand. A general is defined by his uniform; a bishop by his ceremonial apron; a barrister by his flowing robes; a parish beadle by his gold-laced hat. Deprive the cleric of his vestments, or the official of his tricorn and lace, and what remains? Only men. Simple, unremarkable men. Respectability, and even a sense of the sacred, frequently depend more upon the cut of a coat or the fabric of a waistcoat than many would care to admit.
source_text: There are some promotions in life, which, independent of the more substantial rewards they offer, require peculiar value and dignity from the coats and waistcoats connected with them. A field-marshal has his uniform; a bishop his silk apron; a counsellor his silk gown; a beadle his cocked hat. Strip the bishop of his apron, or the beadle of his hat and lace; what are they? Men. Mere men. Dignity, and even holiness too, sometimes, are more questions of coat and waistcoat than some people imagine.

text: Mrs. Bumble recognized immediately that the turning point had arrived. She understood that this clash for domestic authority would be the final, decisive blow for either side. The moment she heard him mention the deceased, she collapsed into a chair. Shrieking that Mr. Bumble was a cold-hearted savage, she dissolved into a fit of hysterical sobbing.
source_text: Mrs. Bumble, seeing at a glance, that the decisive moment had now arrived, and that a blow struck for the mastership on one side or other, must necessarily be final and conclusive, no sooner heard this allusion to the dead and gone, than she dropped into a chair, and with a loud scream that Mr. Bumble was a hard-hearted brute, fell into a paroxysm of tears.

text: However, tears were not the sort of things that could penetrate Mr. Bumble’s spirit; his heart was effectively waterproof. Much like those resilient felt hats that only seem to improve after a soaking, his nerves grew firmer and more robust when met with a deluge of weeping. Because these outbursts signaled frailty and served as a silent admission of his superior standing, they filled him with a sense of haughty satisfaction. He gazed upon his wife with visible pleasure, warmly encouraging her to sob as much as she pleased, noting that the medical profession deemed such exertion quite beneficial to one's physical constitution.
source_text: But, tears were not the things to find their way to Mr. Bumble’s soul; his heart was waterproof. Like washable beaver hats that improve with rain, his nerves were rendered stouter and more vigorous, by showers of tears, which, being tokens of weakness, and so far tacit admissions of his own power, pleased and exalted him. He eyed his good lady with looks of great satisfaction, and begged, in an encouraging manner, that she should cry her hardest: the exercise being looked upon, by the faculty, as strongly conducive to health.

text: “Nine o’clock tonight,” the stranger said, producing a slip of paper and scrawling a remote address near the riverbank in a hand that revealed his inner turmoil. “Bring her to me at that hour. I shouldn’t have to urge you toward discretion; your own welfare depends on it.”
source_text: “At nine in the evening,” said the stranger, producing a scrap of paper, and writing down upon it, an obscure address by the water-side, in characters that betrayed his agitation; “at nine in the evening, bring her to me there. I needn’t tell you to be secret. It’s your interest.”

text: “Scarcely solitary, my sweet, and by no means vulnerable,” ventured Mr. Pumbleton, his speech rattling with a visible tremor of fright. “You have *me* at your side, my dear. And what is more,” he continued, his teeth knocking together as he spoke, “Mr. Blackwood is far too refined a gentleman to visit any harm upon a servant of the parish. He is well aware that my youth has fled, my love, and that I have grown somewhat withered in the stalk, as it were; but he has learned—I am quite certain it has reached his ears—that I am a most formidable warden, possessed of extraordinary vigor if I am once provoked. I simply need a little provoking; that is all.”
source_text: “Not alone, my dear, nor unprotected, neither,” submitted Mr. Bumble, in a voice tremulous with fear: “_I_ am here, my dear. And besides,” said Mr. Bumble, his teeth chattering as he spoke, “Mr. Monks is too much of a gentleman to attempt any violence on porochial persons. Mr. Monks is aware that I am not a young man, my dear, and also that I am a little run to seed, as I may say; but he has heerd: I say I have no doubt Mr. Monks has heerd, my dear: that I am a very determined officer, with very uncommon strength, if I’m once roused. I only want a little rousing; that’s all.”

text: As Mr. Bumble spoke, he made a pathetic show of clutching his lantern with an air of stern resolve. However, the panic written across his face clearly revealed that he needed quite a bit of prodding—a great deal, in fact—before he would attempt anything remotely aggressive, unless his opponents were the destitute or others who had been systematically broken down for that very purpose.
source_text: As Mr. Bumble spoke, he made a melancholy feint of grasping his lantern with fierce determination; and plainly showed, by the alarmed expression of every feature, that he _did_ want a little rousing, and not a little, prior to making any very warlike demonstration: unless, indeed, against paupers, or other person or persons trained down for the purpose.

text: I suspected as much the moment you walked in," Monks replied, observing the venomous glare the lady directed at her husband. "That is all for the best; I have far less hesitation in bargaining with two people when I see that only one of them truly calls the shots. I am in earnest. Look here!
source_text: “I thought as much, when you came in,” rejoined Monks, marking the angry glance which the lady darted at her spouse as she spoke. “So much the better; I have less hesitation in dealing with two people, when I find that there’s only one will between them. I’m in earnest. See here!”

text: “I will tell you everything in due time,” the woman said. “I believe she kept the jewelry for a long while, hoping it might eventually serve some better purpose. When she finally pawned it, she must have scraped together every cent to pay the annual interest, preventing the claim from expiring so she could reclaim it if its value ever came to light. But nothing ever came of it, and she died clutching that frayed, tattered slip of paper in her hand. The deadline was only two days away; I thought there might be some profit in it for me one day, so I went and redeemed the pledge myself.”
source_text: “In good time I’ll tell you.” said the woman. “I judge that she had kept the trinket, for some time, in the hope of turning it to better account; and then had pawned it; and had saved or scraped together money to pay the pawnbroker’s interest year by year, and prevent its running out; so that if anything came of it, it could still be redeemed. Nothing had come of it; and, as I tell you, she died with the scrap of paper, all worn and tattered, in her hand. The time was out in two days; I thought something might one day come of it too; and so redeemed the pledge.”

text: Encouraged by these words, the matron stepped toward the river's edge, and even Mr. Bumble, driven by a growing inquisitiveness, dared to follow her. The murky water, bloated by the heavy rains, surged rapidly below, and every other sound was lost to the roar of its churning and splashing against the slick, mossy pilings. A water-mill had once stood there; the current, foaming and agitated as it struck the few rotting stakes and broken fragments of machinery that remained, seemed to leap forward with a sudden burst of speed once it was clear of the obstacles that had unsuccessfully tried to block its frantic path.
source_text: Thus encouraged, the matron drew near to the brink; and even Mr. Bumble himself, impelled by curiousity, ventured to do the same. The turbid water, swollen by the heavy rain, was rushing rapidly on below; and all other sounds were lost in the noise of its plashing and eddying against the green and slimy piles. There had once been a water-mill beneath; the tide foaming and chafing round the few rotten stakes, and fragments of machinery that yet remained, seemed to dart onward, with a new impulse, when freed from the obstacles which had unavailingly attempted to stem its headlong course.

text: It was lucky that their talk concluded when it did, for Mr. Bumble, who had bent himself double in a bow until he was inches from the ladder's edge, would surely have fallen headlong into the lower room. He lit his own lantern from the one Monks had taken from the rope and now held; making no attempt to say anything further, he went down in silence, followed by his wife. Monks came last, lingering on the steps to make certain that no sounds could be heard but the pelting rain outside and the roar of the rushing water.
source_text: It was fortunate that the conversation terminated at this point, or Mr. Bumble, who had bowed himself to within six inches of the ladder, would infallibly have pitched headlong into the room below. He lighted his lantern from that which Monks had detached from the rope, and now carried in his hand; and making no effort to prolong the discourse, descended in silence, followed by his wife. Monks brought up the rear, after pausing on the steps to satisfy himself that there were no other sounds to be heard than the beating of the rain without, and the rushing of the water.

text: Under any other circumstances, his sharp rebuke and the aggressive tone he used would have had the intended impact. But the girl was so genuinely weak and spent that her head simply fell back against the chair; she lost consciousness before Mr. Sikes could even utter the usual string of curses he typically used to season his threats. He was quite unsure how to handle this unfamiliar crisis, as Nancy’s fits were normally the volatile kind that a person eventually thrashes through without much help. Sikes tried a bit of swearing, but finding that particular remedy entirely useless, he finally called out for assistance.
source_text: At any other time, this remonstrance, and the tone in which it was delivered, would have had the desired effect; but the girl being really weak and exhausted, dropped her head over the back of the chair, and fainted, before Mr. Sikes could get out a few of the appropriate oaths with which, on similar occasions, he was accustomed to garnish his threats. Not knowing, very well, what to do, in this uncommon emergency; for Miss Nancy’s hysterics were usually of that violent kind which the patient fights and struggles out of, without much assistance; Mr. Sikes tried a little blasphemy: and finding that mode of treatment wholly ineffectual, called for assistance.

text: The combined efforts to revive her, performed with great energy—particularly by Master Bates, who seemed to find his role in the business a source of immense amusement—soon achieved their purpose. The girl slowly regained consciousness and, stumbling to a chair by the bedside, buried her face in the pillow. This left Sikes to face the visitors, looking on with considerable surprise at their unexpected arrival.
source_text: These united restoratives, administered with great energy: especially that department consigned to Master Bates, who appeared to consider his share in the proceedings, a piece of unexampled pleasantry: were not long in producing the desired effect. The girl gradually recovered her senses; and, staggering to a chair by the bedside, hid her face upon the pillow: leaving Mr. Sikes to confront the new comers, in some astonishment at their unlooked-for appearance.

text: “Pipe this pigeon pasty, Ned!” the lad crowed, hauling out a monstrous dish; “the sort of dainty birds with joints so supple they’d vanish on the palate, leaving naught for a tooth to find; a great heap of top-shelf gunpowder tea, so fierce that a drop of steaming water would well-nigh shatter the pot; a mountain of brown sugar so fine and dark you’d think the laborers had blessed every grain with their own sweat to make it so sweet—aye, truly! Two crusty brown loaves; a brick of the finest saltless; a wedge of sharp Cheshire; and, to crown the feast, a flask of the heaviest ruin you ever tipped!”
source_text: “Sitch a rabbit pie, Bill,” exclaimed that young gentleman, disclosing to view a huge pasty; “sitch delicate creeturs, with sitch tender limbs, Bill, that the wery bones melt in your mouth, and there’s no occasion to pick ’em; half a pound of seven and six-penny green, so precious strong that if you mix it with biling water, it’ll go nigh to blow the lid of the tea-pot off; a pound and a half of moist sugar that the niggers didn’t work at all at, afore they got it up to sitch a pitch of goodness,—oh no! Two half-quartern brans; pound of best fresh; piece of double Glo’ster; and, to wind up all, some of the richest sort you ever lushed!”

text: “No! I’d bet my life you haven’t,” Sikes replied with a venomous smirk. “You’ve been busy weaving your schemes and plots every single hour I’ve spent here, alternating between chills and fever. Bill was to handle this, and Bill was to manage that—Bill was to do it all for a pittance the moment he recovered, being just desperate enough for your trade. If it hadn't been for the girl, I would have died.”
source_text: “No! I’ll pound it that you han’t,” replied Sikes, with a bitter grin. “You’ve been scheming and plotting away, every hour that I have laid shivering and burning here; and Bill was to do this; and Bill was to do that; and Bill was to do it all, dirt cheap, as soon as he got well: and was quite poor enough for your work. If it hadn’t been for the girl, I might have died.”

text: Accompanied by several similar boasts, Mr. Toby Crackit gathered his winnings and thrust them into his vest pocket with a disdainful look, as if such trifling silver coins were far too petty for a gentleman of his stature. This done, he sauntered from the room with such a show of grace and refinement that Mr. Chitling—who watched the man's retreating legs and boots with rapt fascination until they vanished—assured the company that the privilege of Toby’s acquaintance was a steal at fifteen sixpences a visit, and that he didn't value his own losses at the snap of a finger.
source_text: With these and other ejaculations of the same kind, Mr. Toby Crackit swept up his winnings, and crammed them into his waistcoat pocket with a haughty air, as though such small pieces of silver were wholly beneath the consideration of a man of his figure; this done, he swaggered out of the room, with so much elegance and gentility, that Mr. Chitling, bestowing numerous admiring glances on his legs and boots till they were out of sight, assured the company that he considered his acquaintance cheap at fifteen sixpences an interview, and that he didn’t value his losses the snap of his little finger.

text: Well then," Fagin remarked as they stepped out of the room, "I'll go and fetch that payment for you, Nancy. This key only opens a small cabinet where I store a few trifles the boys pick up, my dear. I never lock away any wealth, mostly because I have none to secure—ha! ha! ha!—nothing at all. It’s a wretched business, Nancy, and a thankless one, but I enjoy the company of the youth, so I endure it all, I endure it all. Quiet!" he hissed, quickly tucking the key into his vest. "Who’s that? Listen!
source_text: “Now,” said Fagin, when they had left the room, “I’ll go and get you that cash, Nancy. This is only the key of a little cupboard where I keep a few odd things the boys get, my dear. I never lock up my money, for I’ve got none to lock up, my dear—ha! ha! ha!—none to lock up. It’s a poor trade, Nancy, and no thanks; but I’m fond of seeing the young people about me; and I bear it all, I bear it all. Hush!” he said, hastily concealing the key in his breast; “who’s that? Listen!”

text: Pressing a skeletal finger to his withered lips, the miser bore a sputtering candle toward the threshold as a heavy tread resounded from the landing. He reached the door at the same instant as the stranger, who, bursting blindly into the room, was nearly upon the young woman before his eyes finally found her.
source_text: Laying his skinny forefinger upon his lip, the Jew carried a candle to the door, as a man’s step was heard upon the stairs without. He reached it, at the same moment as the visitor, who, coming hastily into the room, was close upon the girl before he observed her.

text: The girl moved closer to the table and showed no intention of leaving, even though she saw Monks gesturing toward her. The old man, perhaps fearing she might speak of the money if he tried to dismiss her, pointed toward the upper floor and led Monks from the room.
source_text: The girl drew closer to the table, and made no offer to leave the room, although she could see that Monks was pointing to her. The Jew: perhaps fearing she might say something aloud about the money, if he endeavoured to get rid of her: pointed upward, and took Monks out of the room.

text: The man hurried up the stairs. Nancy stayed behind, pale and gasping for breath, her lip trembling as she endured the loud, stinging insults the virtuous housemaids showered upon her. Their mockery only intensified when the man returned, bringing word that the young woman was to follow him to the upper floor.
source_text: The man ran upstairs. Nancy remained, pale and almost breathless, listening with quivering lip to the very audible expressions of scorn, of which the chaste housemaids were very prolific; and of which they became still more so, when the man returned, and said the young woman was to walk upstairs.

text: “Offer up your deepest gratitude, my lady,” the girl cried out, “that there were hands to hold you and walls to ward you in your youth, and that you were never cast into the teeth of winter and famine, or the madness of the tavern—and a horror deeper still—as I have been since my first cry. I speak the truth of it, for the shadow of the tenement and the mud of the street were my nursery, just as they shall be my winding-sheet.”
source_text: “Thank Heaven upon your knees, dear lady,” cried the girl, “that you had friends to care for and keep you in your childhood, and that you were never in the midst of cold and hunger, and riot and drunkenness, and—and—something worse than all—as I have been from my cradle. I may use the word, for the alley and the gutter were mine, as they will be my deathbed.”

text: He spotted my shadow on the wall while I was eavesdropping, hoping to learn the truth," the girl said. "Not many people could have scrambled out of sight fast enough to avoid being caught, but I managed it. After that, I didn't see him again until last night.
source_text: “He caught sight of my shadow on the wall as I listened, in the hope of finding out,” said the girl; “and there are not many people besides me that could have got out of their way in time to escape discovery. But I did; and I saw him no more till last night.”

text: “It is the honest truth, my lady, though it falls from such lips as mine,” the young woman answered. “He swore then, using profanities that are the common coin of my life but would be alien to your ears, that if he could satisfy his rage by taking the boy’s breath without risking the gallows, he would have done it. But since he dared not, he vowed to haunt the child's every step; and by wielding the boy's own dark origins against him, he might yet find his ruin. ‘In truth, Silas,’ he said, ‘wicked as you may be, you have never devised such traps as I shall lay for my young brother, Arthur.’”
source_text: “The truth, lady, though it comes from my lips,” replied the girl. “Then, he said, with oaths common enough in my ears, but strange to yours, that if he could gratify his hatred by taking the boy’s life without bringing his own neck in danger, he would; but, as he couldn’t, he’d be upon the watch to meet him at every turn in life; and if he took advantage of his birth and history, he might harm him yet. ‘In short, Fagin,’ he says, ‘Jew as you are, you never laid such snares as I’ll contrive for my young brother, Oliver.’”

text: He spoke with a grim and bitter sincerity, if ever a man did," the girl replied, shaking her head. "He is a man of terrible focus when his hatred is roused. I know many who commit far darker deeds, but I would rather listen to all of them a dozen times over than hear that Monks speak once. It is getting late, and I must return home before anyone suspects I have been out on such a mission. I have to hurry back.
source_text: “He spoke in hard and angry earnest, if a man ever did,” replied the girl, shaking her head. “He is an earnest man when his hatred is up. I know many who do worse things; but I’d rather listen to them all a dozen times, than to that Monks once. It is growing late, and I have to reach home without suspicion of having been on such an errand as this. I must get back quickly.”

text: I can't explain what it is," the girl replied. "I only know that it’s the truth, and not just for me, but for hundreds of others as miserable and broken as I am. I have to go back. I don't know if this is God punishing me for my sins, but I am pulled back to him through every bit of pain and abuse. I believe I’d still return, even if I knew for certain that he would be the one to kill me in the end.
source_text: “I don’t know what it is,” answered the girl; “I only know that it is so, and not with me alone, but with hundreds of others as bad and wretched as myself. I must go back. Whether it is God’s wrath for the wrong I have done, I do not know; but I am drawn back to him through every suffering and ill usage; and I should be, I believe, if I knew that I was to die by his hand at last.”

text: For women of your station, graced with such youth and innocence," the young woman answered with a haunting calm, "the surrender of the heart is a journey you may take in safety, for you are fortified by a home, by kin, and by the regard of many who cherish you. But when one like me—who looks to the shroud as her only certain shelter, and the ward-matron as her solitary mourner—anchors her ruined soul to a man, permitting him to inhabit the vast emptiness of a wretched life, what power on earth can heal her? Have compassion, Madam; pity us for clinging to this solitary remnant of our nature, only to see it twisted by a dark providence from a source of grace into a new engine of misery and despair.
source_text: “When ladies as young, and good, and beautiful as you are,” replied the girl steadily, “give away your hearts, love will carry you all lengths—even such as you, who have home, friends, other admirers, everything, to fill them. When such as I, who have no certain roof but the coffinlid, and no friend in sickness or death but the hospital nurse, set our rotten hearts on any man, and let him fill the place that has been a blank through all our wretched lives, who can hope to cure us? Pity us, lady—pity us for having only one feeling of the woman left, and for having that turned, by a heavy judgment, from a comfort and a pride, into a new means of violence and suffering.”

text: “You would be doing me the greatest favor, lady,” the girl answered, her hands twisting in distress, “if you could just end my life right now. I’ve felt more sickened by what I’ve become tonight than I ever have before, and it would mean something not to die in the same misery I’ve lived in. May God bless you, kind lady, and grant you as much happiness as the shame I have brought upon myself.”
source_text: “You would serve me best, lady,” replied the girl, wringing her hands, “if you could take my life at once; for I have felt more grief to think of what I am, tonight, than I ever did before, and it would be something not to die in the hell in which I have lived. God bless you, sweet lady, and send as much happiness on your head as I have brought shame on mine!”

text: Agitated by these conflicting thoughts, wavering between one path and the next before shrinking from them all as each new concern arose, Rose endured a night of restless anxiety. After further solitary deliberation the following day, she reached the difficult decision that she must seek Harry’s counsel.
source_text: Disturbed by these different reflections; inclining now to one course and then to another, and again recoiling from all, as each successive consideration presented itself to her mind; Rose passed a sleepless and anxious night. After more communing with herself next day, she arrived at the desperate conclusion of consulting Harry.

text: The words had scarcely left her mouth when Mr. Grimwig, who had been feigning interest in a heavy volume on the table, knocked it over with a resounding crash. Reclining abruptly in his chair, his face was stripped of every expression save for one of absolute astonishment, and he remained fixed in a long, vacant stare. Then, as if mortified by such a lapse in composure, he wrenched himself back into his former posture with a sudden convulsion. Staring straight ahead, he let out a low, drawn-out whistle that seemed to descend until it finally vanished into the very depths of his stomach.
source_text: The words no sooner escaped her lips, than Mr. Grimwig, who had been affecting to dip into a large book that lay on the table, upset it with a great crash, and falling back in his chair, discharged from his features every expression but one of unmitigated wonder, and indulged in a prolonged and vacant stare; then, as if ashamed of having betrayed so much emotion, he jerked himself, as it were, by a convulsion into his former attitude, and looking out straight before him emitted a long deep whistle, which seemed, at last, not to be discharged on empty air, but to die away in the innermost recesses of his stomach.

text: “Grant me the kindness, my dear girl, of ignoring this talk of my own virtue and compassion, for I assure you no one else has ever witnessed such traits in me. If you are able to provide any fragment of truth that might undo the grim impression I was compelled to form of that destitute youth, then I beseech you, in the name of Providence, let me have it this instant.”
source_text: “Do me the favour, my dear young lady, to leave entirely out of the question that goodness and benevolence of which you speak, and of which nobody else knows anything; and if you have it in your power to produce any evidence which will alter the unfavourable opinion I was once induced to entertain of that poor child, in Heaven’s name put me in possession of it.”

text: “I harbored no doubt of his return—none at all,” the elderly woman murmured, folding him into her bosom. “How robust he seems, and how much like a nobleman’s heir he is attired once more! In what far corner have you lingered through this great stretch of time? Truly, it is the same kind countenance, though less wan; the same mild expression, but far less melancholy. I have carried the image of his gentle smile in my mind every hour, cherished beside the memories of my own departed little ones, who were lost to me when I was yet a spirited young thing.” Continuing her chatter, at one moment holding Julian at a distance to gauge his growth and the next pulling him near to brush her hand through his locks, the dear soul wept and rejoiced against his neck in equal measure.
source_text: “He would come back—I knew he would,” said the old lady, holding him in her arms. “How well he looks, and how like a gentleman’s son he is dressed again! Where have you been, this long, long while? Ah! the same sweet face, but not so pale; the same soft eye, but not so sad. I have never forgotten them or his quiet smile, but have seen them every day, side by side with those of my own dear children, dead and gone since I was a lightsome young creature.” Running on thus, and now holding Oliver from her to mark how he had grown, now clasping him to her and passing her fingers fondly through his hair, the good soul laughed and wept upon his neck by turns.

text: “So it stands. It is obvious that we will face immense trouble uncovering the truth of this matter unless we can force this fellow, Monks, to yield completely. Such a feat can only be achieved through deception, by cornering him when he is away from his companions. If we were to have him seized now, we possess no real evidence against him. As far as we can tell, he has no direct involvement in the gang’s various thefts. Even if he weren't set free immediately, he would likely suffer no greater penalty than a short stint in jail as a common tramp; afterward, he would surely remain so resolutely silent that he might as well be entirely senseless and mute for all the help he would provide us.”
source_text: “Thus. It is quite clear that we shall have extreme difficulty in getting to the bottom of this mystery, unless we can bring this man, Monks, upon his knees. That can only be done by stratagem, and by catching him when he is not surrounded by these people. For, suppose he were apprehended, we have no proof against him. He is not even (so far as we know, or as the facts appear to us) concerned with the gang in any of their robberies. If he were not discharged, it is very unlikely that he could receive any further punishment than being committed to prison as a rogue and vagabond; and of course ever afterwards his mouth would be so obstinately closed that he might as well, for our purposes, be deaf, dumb, blind, and an idiot.”

text: They were a man and a woman, though they might be more aptly characterized as a male and a female. The former was a lanky, knock-kneed, bony individual with a shambling gait—one of those people to whom no definite age can be easily assigned, appearing as stunted men in their youth and as overgrown boys once they reach maturity. The woman was young but built with a sturdy, resilient frame, which she required to support the massive bundle fastened to her back. Her companion was not burdened by any such weight; from a stick propped over his shoulder there hung only a small parcel wrapped in a simple handkerchief, appearing light indeed. This advantage, along with his remarkably long legs, allowed him to easily stay a good half-dozen paces ahead of her. Occasionally, he would jerk his head back with an air of irritation, as if scolding her for her slowness and demanding she exert herself more.
source_text: They were a man and woman; or perhaps they would be better described as a male and female: for the former was one of those long-limbed, knock-kneed, shambling, bony people, to whom it is difficult to assign any precise age,—looking as they do, when they are yet boys, like undergrown men, and when they are almost men, like overgrown boys. The woman was young, but of a robust and hardy make, as she need have been to bear the weight of the heavy bundle which was strapped to her back. Her companion was not encumbered with much luggage, as there merely dangled from a stick which he carried over his shoulder, a small parcel wrapped in a common handkerchief, and apparently light enough. This circumstance, added to the length of his legs, which were of unusual extent, enabled him with much ease to keep some half-dozen paces in advance of his companion, to whom he occasionally turned with an impatient jerk of the head: as if reproaching her tardiness, and urging her to greater exertion.

text: Steadfast in his wary design, Mr. Prowle forged ahead without respite until he gained the crossroads of the Mitre at Islington, where the thickening press of wayfarers and the cacophony of dray-carts signaled, to his calculating mind, the authentic threshold of the capital. Halting only long enough to discern which avenues teemed most dangerously with life—and were thus most diligently to be shunned—he veered instead into the shadows of Goswell Way. He was presently submerged in that tangled knot of grimy lanes and neglected courts situated between the Old Bailey and the meat markets; a quarter which, having been spared by the tides of modern refinement, stood as a testament to the city’s enduring squalor, a dark pocket of wretchedness preserved in the very center of the thriving hive.
source_text: In pursuance of this cautious plan, Mr. Claypole went on, without halting, until he arrived at the Angel at Islington, where he wisely judged, from the crowd of passengers and numbers of vehicles, that London began in earnest. Just pausing to observe which appeared the most crowded streets, and consequently the most to be avoided, he crossed into Saint John’s Road, and was soon deep in the obscurity of the intricate and dirty ways, which, lying between Gray’s Inn Lane and Smithfield, render that part of the town one of the lowest and worst that improvement has left in the midst of London.

text: I've a mind to be a gentleman," Mr. Claypole said, kicking his legs out as he resumed a dialogue that had begun before Fagin arrived to overhear it. "No more of those dreary coffins for me, Charlotte; I’m choosing a life of refinement. And if you’d like, I’ll make a lady of you, too.
source_text: “So I mean to be a gentleman,” said Mr. Claypole, kicking out his legs, and continuing a conversation, the commencement of which Fagin had arrived too late to hear. “No more jolly old coffins, Charlotte, but a gentleman’s life for me: and, if yer like, yer shall be a lady.”

text: Fagin underscored his point by tapping the side of his nose with his right forefinger. Noah attempted to mimic the sign, though he met with little success, as his own nose was far too small to serve the purpose effectively. Nevertheless, Fagin seemed to take the clumsy effort as a mark of total agreement, and he began to serve the liquor that Barney had just brought in with a show of great warmth.
source_text: Fagin followed up this remark by striking the side of his nose with his right forefinger,—a gesture which Noah attempted to imitate, though not with complete success, in consequence of his own nose not being large enough for the purpose. However, Mr. Fagin seemed to interpret the endeavour as expressing a perfect coincidence with his opinion, and put about the liquor which Barney reappeared with, in a very friendly manner.

text: I only say this to make my point perfectly clear," the old man remarked, his eyebrows arching. "To achieve your ends, you are reliant on me. To keep my own small trade secure and hidden, I am reliant on you. Your welfare is your primary concern, just as mine is my own. Yet, the more you value your own skin, the more protective you must be of mine. So we return to what I said at the start—that this devotion to self-interest is the only thing keeping us united. It must be so, or we shall all face our downfall together.
source_text: “Only to show you my meaning clearly,” said the Jew, raising his eyebrows. “To be able to do that, you depend upon me. To keep my little business all snug, I depend upon you. The first is your number one, the second my number one. The more you value your number one, the more careful you must be of mine; so we come at last to what I told you at first—that a regard for number one holds us all together, and must do so, unless we would all go to pieces in company.”

text: “I have my doubts,” Fagin replied with a heavy sigh. “If no new evidence is produced, it will only be a minor conviction, and he’ll return to us in six weeks or so. But if they find more, he’s facing a long sentence. They know what a clever lad he is; they’ll make the Artful a lifer—nothing less than a lifer.”
source_text: “I’m doubtful about it,” replied Fagin, with a sigh. “If they don’t get any fresh evidence, it’ll only be a summary conviction, and we shall have him back again after six weeks or so; but, if they do, it’s a case of lagging. They know what a clever lad he is; he’ll be a lifer. They’ll make the Artful nothing less than a lifer.”

text: Vane was on the cusp of rendering these enigmatic syllables into the common parlance; and, once decoded, the dull-witted Gudgeon would have grasped that they signified that particular sentence, “perpetual exile to the colonies,” when the conversation was abruptly severed by the entrance of the Nimble Lad, his palms buried deep within his coat-flaps and his features wrenched into a grimace of half-playful agony.
source_text: Fagin was about to translate these mysterious expressions into the vulgar tongue; and, being interpreted, Mr. Bolter would have been informed that they represented that combination of words, “transportation for life,” when the dialogue was cut short by the entry of Master Bates, with his hands in his breeches-pockets, and his face twisted into a look of semi-comical woe.

text: Indeed, the elder had so deftly manipulated the lad’s capricious humours that young Tom, who had previously been inclined to see the incarcerated Stripling as a mere object of pity, now perceived him as the protagonist of a most rare and hilarious spectacle. He grew quite eager for the day when his old comrade should be afforded so grand a stage for the exercise of his particular gifts.
source_text: In fact, Mr. Fagin had so well humoured his young friend’s eccentric disposition, that Master Bates, who had at first been disposed to consider the imprisoned Dodger rather in the light of a victim, now looked upon him as the chief actor in a scene of most uncommon and exquisite humour, and felt quite impatient for the arrival of the time when his old companion should have so favourable an opportunity of displaying his abilities.

text: It was certainly Mr. Dawkins. He strolled into the office with his massive sleeves pushed back as usual, his left hand buried in his pocket and his hat held in his right. Moving ahead of the guard with a peculiar, rolling swagger that defied description, he took his stand in the dock and demanded in a clear, loud voice to know why he had been forced into such a shameful predicament.
source_text: It was indeed Mr. Dawkins, who, shuffling into the office with the big coat sleeves tucked up as usual, his left hand in his pocket, and his hat in his right hand, preceded the jailer, with a rolling gait altogether indescribable, and, taking his place in the dock, requested in an audible voice to know what he was placed in that ’ere disgraceful sitivation for.

text: Once Noah had seen the man secured in a solitary cell, he hurried back to the spot where he had parted with young Bates. He waited there for some time before the boy joined him, as Charley had cautiously remained in a secluded hiding place until he was sure that no unwanted followers had tracked his new acquaintance.
source_text: Having seen him locked up by himself in a little cell, Noah made the best of his way back to where he had left Master Bates. After waiting here some time, he was joined by that young gentleman, who had prudently abstained from showing himself until he had looked carefully abroad from a snug retreat, and ascertained that his new friend had not been followed by any impertinent person.

text: Despite her mastery of guile and pretense, Nancy was unable to fully suppress the inner turmoil sparked by her recent betrayal. She remained acutely aware that both the manipulative Fagin and the savage Sikes had entrusted her with their most private designs, never once doubting her absolute devotion. Though their crimes were abhorrent and her hatred for Fagin ran deep—blaming him for her descent into a hopeless pit of vice and misery—she still experienced flickers of remorse. She feared that her revelations would finally pull the net tight around the man who had evaded justice for so long, and that he might ultimately meet his end through her own hand, however much he deserved such a ruin.
source_text: Adept as she was, in all the arts of cunning and dissimulation, the girl Nancy could not wholly conceal the effect which the knowledge of the step she had taken, wrought upon her mind. She remembered that both the crafty Jew and the brutal Sikes had confided to her schemes, which had been hidden from all others: in the full confidence that she was trustworthy and beyond the reach of their suspicion. Vile as those schemes were, desperate as were their originators, and bitter as were her feelings towards Fagin, who had led her, step by step, deeper and deeper down into an abyss of crime and misery, whence was no escape; still, there were times when, even towards him, she felt some relenting, lest her disclosure should bring him within the iron grasp he had so long eluded, and he should fall at last—richly as he merited such a fate—by her hand.

text: Yet, these were but the aimless drifts of a consciousness unable to fully untether itself from past comrades and long-held ties, despite being firmly locked upon a solitary goal and committed to resisting any distraction. Her dread for Vane’s safety might have proven a more compelling reason to retreat while the hour allowed; yet, she had mandated that her silence be absolute, she had offered no trail that might expose him, and she had declined, even for his benefit, a harbor from the depravity and sorrow that surrounded her—what further proof of loyalty could she give! Her purpose was unyielding.
source_text: But, these were the mere wanderings of a mind unable wholly to detach itself from old companions and associations, though enabled to fix itself steadily on one object, and resolved not to be turned aside by any consideration. Her fears for Sikes would have been more powerful inducements to recoil while there was yet time; but she had stipulated that her secret should be rigidly kept, she had dropped no clue which could lead to his discovery, she had refused, even for his sake, a refuge from all the guilt and wretchedness that encompasses her—and what more could she do! She was resolved.

text: “Listen to you!” snarled Miller, wheeling about in his stool to glare at her. “Aye! And should you carry on for a moment more, the cur will take a hold of your windpipe and shake that caterwauling right out of you. What’s gotten into your head, you vixen? What is it?”
source_text: “Hear you!” repeated Sikes turning round in his chair to confront her. “Aye! And if I hear you for half a minute longer, the dog shall have such a grip on your throat as’ll tear some of that screaming voice out. Wot has come over you, you jade! Wot is it?”

text: Vane signaled with a slight movement of his chin that the matter should be dropped; soon enough, the young woman regained her usual quietude. Murmuring to Brant that she was unlikely to break out again, Vane grasped his hat and offered a brief valediction. He halted at the threshold, however, and glancing back, requested that a light be brought to guide him through the pitch-black passage of the stairs.
source_text: Fagin nodded to him to take no further notice just then; and, in a few minutes, the girl subsided into her accustomed demeanour. Whispering Sikes that there was no fear of her relapsing, Fagin took up his hat and bade him good-night. He paused when he reached the room-door, and looking round, asked if somebody would light him down the dark stairs.

text: Barnaby rose in absolute silence, the old man’s trembling fervor having passed into him like a sudden fever. They crept from the house and hurried through a tangled knot of back-alleys until they reached a tavern Barnaby knew well; it was the same squalid inn where he had found his first bed upon reaching the capital.
source_text: Noah started up without saying a word; for the Jew was in a state of such intense excitement that it infected him. They left the house stealthily, and hurrying through a labyrinth of streets, arrived at length before a public-house, which Noah recognised as the same in which he had slept, on the night of his arrival in London.

text: The cathedral bells rang out the final quarter before midnight as two shapes appeared upon the span of the river. The first, a woman moving with frantic haste, scanned the passing faces with an air of desperate expectation; the second, a man, clung to the deepest gloom of the masonry. He measured his progress by hers, pausing whenever she hesitated and gliding forward the moment she advanced, yet always maintaining a cautious interval. In this manner they traversed the bridge from the northern bank toward the southern shore, until the woman, seemingly thwarted in her search of the wayfarers, abruptly reversed her course. This sudden pivot did not catch her trailer unawares; retreating into the dark curve of a stone alcove above the water, he bent low over the ledge to mask his presence while she hurried by on the far pavement. Once she had regained her former lead, he stepped silently from his hiding place to resume the chase. Near the very crest of the bridge, she halted. The man likewise went still.
source_text: The church clocks chimed three quarters past eleven, as two figures emerged on London Bridge. One, which advanced with a swift and rapid step, was that of a woman who looked eagerly about her as though in quest of some expected object; the other figure was that of a man, who slunk along in the deepest shadow he could find, and, at some distance, accommodated his pace to hers: stopping when she stopped: and as she moved again, creeping stealthily on: but never allowing himself, in the ardour of his pursuit, to gain upon her footsteps. Thus, they crossed the bridge, from the Middlesex to the Surrey shore, when the woman, apparently disappointed in her anxious scrutiny of the foot-passengers, turned back. The movement was sudden; but he who watched her, was not thrown off his guard by it; for, shrinking into one of the recesses which surmount the piers of the bridge, and leaning over the parapet the better to conceal his figure, he suffered her to pass on the opposite pavement. When she was about the same distance in advance as she had been before, he slipped quietly down, and followed her again. At nearly the centre of the bridge, she stopped. The man stopped too.

text: A heavy haze drifted over the water, heightening the crimson glow of the fires burning on the small vessels docked at the piers and making the grimy buildings on the shore seem even darker and more obscure. The aged, soot-blackened warehouses on each bank rose with a dull weight from the crowded roofs and gables, looming sternly over a river too inky to reflect their massive silhouettes. While the tower of Saint Saviour’s and the spire of Saint Magnus—long the towering sentinels of the old bridge—could still be discerned in the darkness, the dense cluster of ships downstream and the many church steeples upstream were almost entirely lost to view.
source_text: A mist hung over the river, deepening the red glare of the fires that burnt upon the small craft moored off the different wharfs, and rendering darker and more indistinct the murky buildings on the banks. The old smoke-stained storehouses on either side, rose heavy and dull from the dense mass of roofs and gables, and frowned sternly upon water too black to reflect even their lumbering shapes. The tower of old Saint Saviour’s Church, and the spire of Saint Magnus, so long the giant-warders of the ancient bridge, were visible in the gloom; but the forest of shipping below bridge, and the thickly scattered spires of churches above, were nearly all hidden from sight.

text: They moved ahead, searching their surroundings with the look of those who held a slim expectation they doubted would ever be realized, when this new associate suddenly joined them. They halted with a sharp gasp of surprise but quickly suppressed it, for a man in the attire of a countryman passed close by—brushing against them, indeed—at that exact moment.
source_text: They walked onward, looking about them with the air of persons who entertained some very slight expectation which had little chance of being realised, when they were suddenly joined by this new associate. They halted with an exclamation of surprise, but suppressed it immediately; for a man in the garments of a countryman came close up—brushed against them, indeed—at that precise moment.

text: “The self-righteous would have stiffened their necks to behold me in my current misery, and spoken only of hellfire and divine retribution,” the girl lamented. “Oh, my lady, why are those who claim to be the Lord’s chosen never as compassionate to us fallen creatures as you? You, who possess the bloom and grace they have long since squandered, might be forgiven a touch of vanity; yet you are more modest than the lot of them, while they remain so terribly proud.”
source_text: “Your haughty religious people would have held their heads up to see me as I am tonight, and preached of flames and vengeance,” cried the girl. “Oh, dear lady, why ar’n’t those who claim to be God’s own folks as gentle and as kind to us poor wretches as you, who, having youth, and beauty, and all that they have lost, might be a little proud instead of so much humbler?”

text: “Ah,” the gentleman remarked. “A Muslim, after a thorough washing, faces the East to say his prayers. These pious folks, however, after scouring their expressions against the world until they’ve rubbed away every smile, turn just as consistently toward the gloomiest side of the heavens. If I must choose between the devotee and the hypocrite, I would much prefer the former!”
source_text: “Ah!” said the gentleman. “A Turk turns his face, after washing it well, to the East, when he says his prayers; these good people, after giving their faces such a rub against the World as to take the smiles off, turn with no less regularity, to the darkest side of Heaven. Between the Mussulman and the Pharisee, commend me to the first!”

text: “Back to my home, miss,” the girl answered. “To the kind of home I have spent my whole life building for myself. We must part here; I am certain to be watched. Go! Please, go! If I have done you any service at all, I ask only that you leave me and let me go my way in solitude.”
source_text: “Home, lady,” rejoined the girl. “To such a home as I have raised for myself with the work of my whole life. Let us part. I shall be watched or seen. Go! Go! If I have done you any service all I ask is, that you leave me, and let me go my way alone.”

text: Imagine that boy," Fagin continued, "were to turn informant—to betray us all—first seeking out the right people for the job, and then meeting them in the street to describe our faces, detailing every trait by which they might recognize us, and the very hideout where we could be most easily captured. Suppose he did all this, and further exposed a scheme we have all been part of—of his own free will; not because he was caught, cornered, tried, or lectured by a priest while starving on bread and water—but of his own accord; to satisfy his own desire; slipping out at night to find those most set against us and selling us out. Do you hear me?" the old man cried, his eyes flashing with fury. "If he were to do all that, what then?
source_text: “Suppose that lad,” pursued Fagin, “was to peach—to blow upon us all—first seeking out the right folks for the purpose, and then having a meeting with ’em in the street to paint our likenesses, describe every mark that they might know us by, and the crib where we might be most easily taken. Suppose he was to do all this, and besides to blow upon a plan we’ve all been in, more or less—of his own fancy; not grabbed, trapped, tried, earwigged by the parson and brought to it on bread and water,—but of his own fancy; to please his own taste; stealing out at nights to find those most interested against us, and peaching to them. Do you hear me?” cried the Jew, his eyes flashing with rage. “Suppose he did all this, what then?”

text: Fagin fixed a sharp look on the thief and signaled for him to stay quiet. He bent over the mattress on the floor, shaking the sleeper to wake him. Sikes leaned forward in his seat, watching with his hands braced on his knees, as if he were deeply curious about where all this interrogation and preparation would eventually lead.
source_text: Fagin looked hard at the robber; and, motioning him to be silent, stooped over the bed upon the floor, and shook the sleeper to rouse him. Sikes leant forward in his chair: looking on with his hands upon his knees, as if wondering much what all this questioning and preparation was to end in.

text: He remained rigid, anchored by a heavy dread. A single wheeze and a shift of the fingers had provoked him; in a fever of fright and malice, he had delivered blow upon blow. He once cast a cloak over the shape, but imagining those sightless eyes following him through the dark was more harrowing than watching them track the crimson light flickering on the ceiling. He ripped the garment back. It was merely a carcass—cold meat and gristle—but the sheer volume of that flesh and the depth of that blood were overwhelming.
source_text: He had not moved; he had been afraid to stir. There had been a moan and motion of the hand; and, with terror added to rage, he had struck and struck again. Once he threw a rug over it; but it was worse to fancy the eyes, and imagine them moving towards him, than to see them glaring upward, as if watching the reflection of the pool of gore that quivered and danced in the sunlight on the ceiling. He had plucked it off again. And there was the body—mere flesh and blood, no more—but such flesh, and so much blood!

text: Throughout the entire interval, he had not once averted his eyes from the corpse; no, not for a single moment. With his grim preparations finished, he retreated toward the door, walking backward and dragging the dog with him to prevent its paws from being stained again and tracking evidence of the murder into the streets. He closed the door quietly, turned the lock, pocketed the key, and left the house.
source_text: All this time he had, never once, turned his back upon the corpse; no, not for a moment. Such preparations completed, he moved, backward, towards the door: dragging the dog with him, lest he should soil his feet anew and carry out new evidence of the crime into the streets. He shut the door softly, locked it, took the key, and left the house.

text: He made his way through Islington and hiked up the slope in Highgate where the Whittington monument stands. He started back down Highgate Hill, his resolve faltering and his destination unknown. Veering right almost as soon as he had begun his descent, he followed a path through the fields along the edge of Caen Wood until he reached Hampstead Heath. After crossing the low ground by the Vale of Health, he climbed the opposite bank, crossed the road linking the two villages, and walked the rest of the way to the North End meadows. There, he stretched out under a hedge and drifted into sleep.
source_text: He went through Islington; strode up the hill at Highgate on which stands the stone in honour of Whittington; turned down to Highgate Hill, unsteady of purpose, and uncertain where to go; struck off to the right again, almost as soon as he began to descend it; and taking the foot-path across the fields, skirted Caen Wood, and so came on Hampstead Heath.
