Traversing the hollow by the Vale of Heath, he mounted the opposite bank, and crossing the road which joins the villages of Hampstead and Highgate, made along the remaining portion of the heath to the fields at North End, in one of which he laid himself down under a hedge, and slept.

text: Before long he was on his feet and moving once more, heading not deep into the rural landscape but back toward London along the highway. He doubled back again, crossing over the very ground he had just walked, then drifted through the open fields. He would collapse by the edge of a ditch to rest, only to bolt upright and strike out for another spot, repeating the cycle as he wandered on.
source_text: Soon he was up again, and away,—not far into the country, but back towards London by the high-road—then back again—then over another part of the same ground as he already traversed—then wandering up and down in fields, and lying on ditches’ brinks to rest, and starting up to make for some other spot, and do the same, and ramble on again.

text: The discourse of the fellows gathered there drifted from the surrounding soil to the tillers of it; and when these subjects were spent, it moved to the vintage of a certain patriarch interred that past Lord’s Day. The striplings among them held him to be of great antiquity, while the elders maintained he was practically a youth—no more advanced in seasons, one snowy-headed forebear insisted, than himself—with a good dozen years of breath remaining, had he but minded his health; had he but minded his health.
source_text: The conversation of the men assembled here, turned upon the neighbouring land, and farmers; and when those topics were exhausted, upon the age of some old man who had been buried on the previous Sunday; the young men present considering him very old, and the old men present declaring him to have been quite young—not older, one white-haired grandfather said, than he was—with ten or fifteen year of life in him at least—if he had taken care; if he had taken care.

text: “I’ll have it completely gone, sir,” the man replied, casting a knowing wink at the crowd, “before you can even walk across the room to reach it. Everyone, take a look at this dark mark on the gentleman’s hat. It is no wider than a small coin, yet it sits thicker than a large one. Whether it is a stain from wine, fruit, beer, water, paint, tar, mud, or even blood—”
source_text: “I’ll take it clean out, sir,” replied the man, winking to the company, “before you can come across the room to get it. Gentlemen all, observe the dark stain upon this gentleman’s hat, no wider than a shilling, but thicker than a half-crown. Whether it is a wine-stain, fruit-stain, beer-stain, water-stain, paint-stain, pitch-stain, mud-stain, or blood-stain—”

text: Occasionally, he would spin around with frantic resolve, intent on confronting the apparition even if the sight of it killed him. Yet his scalp prickled and his pulse froze, for the thing had pivoted with him, remaining always at his back. During the morning, he had managed to keep it in his line of sight, but now it lingered behind him—constant and inescapable. When he pressed his spine against a dirt embankment, he sensed it looming over him, its silhouette etched clearly against the frigid night sky. He flung himself down onto the road, lying flat on his back. There it remained by his head, mute and motionless—a living headstone, its epitaph written in blood.
source_text: At times, he turned, with desperate determination, resolved to beat this phantom off, though it should look him dead; but the hair rose on his head, and his blood stood still, for it had turned with him and was behind him then. He had kept it before him that morning, but it was behind now—always. He leaned his back against a bank, and felt that it stood above him, visibly out against the cold night-sky. He threw himself upon the road—on his back upon the road. At his head it stood, silent, erect, and still—a living grave-stone, with its epitaph in blood.

text: He passed a shed in an open field that offered a refuge for the night. Three towering poplars stood before the entrance, casting the interior into heavy shadow, while the wind sighed through their branches with a mournful wail. He found he could not travel further until daybreak; here, he pressed himself against the wall to endure a fresh onset of misery.
source_text: There was a shed in a field he passed, that offered shelter for the night. Before the door, were three tall poplar trees, which made it very dark within; and the wind moaned through them with a dismal wail. He _could not_ walk on, till daylight came again; and here he stretched himself close to the wall—to undergo new torture.

text: There Julian crouched, gripped by a private horror that defied description, his body convulsing as a deathly chill broke across his flesh. Then, cutting through the stillness, the wind delivered a frantic hubbub from afar—the discordant bellows of a mob seized by shock. To a soul so isolated, any human resonance, however threatening, was a reprieve from the void. The advent of a visible enemy reignited his spent spirit; he hauled himself to his feet and plunged headlong into the gloom.
source_text: And here he remained in such terror as none but he can know, trembling in every limb, and the cold sweat starting from every pore, when suddenly there arose upon the night-wind the noise of distant shouting, and the roar of voices mingled in alarm and wonder. Any sound of men in that lonely place, even though it conveyed a real cause of alarm, was something to him. He regained his strength and energy at the prospect of personal danger; and springing to his feet, rushed into the open air.

text: Once the frantic delirium had ebbed, the harrowing weight of his transgression surged back with redoubled intensity. He peered darkly at the clusters of men nearby, haunted by the suspicion that their hushed murmurs concerned him alone. At a silent command of his hand, the cur followed, and the pair retreated into the gloom with quiet footsteps. He skirted a heavy pump where several workers sat, and they hailed him to partake in their meager fare. He snatched up a portion of bread and salt-beef, but as he swallowed a deep pull of ale, he overheard the watchmen—newly arrived from the capital—deliberating upon the slaughter. "Word is he’s fled toward Bristol," one remarked, "but his time is short. The trackers are already afoot, and before another sun sets, the hue and cry will ring through every village in the land."
source_text: This mad excitement over, there returned, with ten-fold force, the dreadful consciousness of his crime. He looked suspiciously about him, for the men were conversing in groups, and he feared to be the subject of their talk. The dog obeyed the significant beck of his finger, and they drew off, stealthily, together. He passed near an engine where some men were seated, and they called to him to share in their refreshment. He took some bread and meat; and as he drank a draught of beer, heard the firemen, who were from London, talking about the murder. “He has gone to Birmingham, they say,” said one: “but they’ll have him yet, for the scouts are out, and by tomorrow night there’ll be a cry all through the country.”

text: He followed the urge immediately, sticking to the loneliest roads as he began his return trip, determined to stay out of sight just outside the city until twilight; then, taking a winding path inside, he would make his way directly to the specific location he had chosen.
source_text: He acted upon this impulse without delay, and choosing the least frequented roads began his journey back, resolved to lie concealed within a short distance of the metropolis, and, entering it at dusk by a circuitous route, to proceed straight to that part of it which he had fixed on for his destination.

text: Yet I am also well aware," the elderly man continued, "of the wretchedness, the slow-motion agony, and the long-drawn-out suffering of that mismatched marriage. I know how aimlessly and exhaustedly that miserable couple hauled their heavy shackles through a world that had become toxic to them both. I saw how stiff formalities were replaced by blatant insults; how apathy turned to resentment, resentment to bitterness, and bitterness to pure revulsion, until they finally tore their rattling connection apart. Retreating to opposite sides of their lives, they each carried a painful remnant—a bond that only death could truly unfasten—hiding it from society behind the most cheerful masks they could manage. Your mother was successful in this; she soon put it behind her. But for your father, that memory corroded and ate away at his heart for years.
source_text: “But I also know,” pursued the old gentleman, “the misery, the slow torture, the protracted anguish of that ill-assorted union. I know how listlessly and wearily each of that wretched pair dragged on their heavy chain through a world that was poisoned to them both. I know how cold formalities were succeeded by open taunts; how indifference gave place to dislike, dislike to hate, and hate to loathing, until at last they wrenched the clanking bond asunder, and retiring a wide space apart, carried each a galling fragment, of which nothing but death could break the rivets, to hide it in new society beneath the gayest looks they could assume. Your mother succeeded; she forgot it soon. But it rusted and cankered at your father’s heart for years.”

text: “The history I recount is one of hardship and profound lamentation, Julian,” Mr. Thorne replied, “as most authentic chronicles tend to be; were it a mere record of unalloyed delight, it would occupy but a moment’s breath. Eventually, that affluent kinsman—for whose social elevation your father had been traded, a fate far from unique in this world—passed away. Seeking to amend the wretchedness he had helped foster, he bequeathed that supposed remedy for every human sting: Wealth. Duty demanded your father’s instant departure for Florence, where the old man had sought a recovery that never came, dying instead amidst a tangle of unsettled business. He traveled there only to be struck down by a fatal fever; your mother, bearing you in her arms, fled Paris the instant the news arrived. He breathed his last a single day after she reached his side, having authored no testament—*no testament whatever*—thereby ensuring the entire fortune devolved upon her and yourself.”
source_text: “It is a true tale of grief and trial, and sorrow, young man,” returned Mr. Brownlow, “and such tales usually are; if it were one of unmixed joy and happiness, it would be very brief. At length one of those rich relations to strengthen whose interest and importance your father had been sacrificed, as others are often—it is no uncommon case—died, and to repair the misery he had been instrumental in occasioning, left him his panacea for all griefs—Money. It was necessary that he should immediately repair to Rome, whither this man had sped for health, and where he had died, leaving his affairs in great confusion. He went; was seized with mortal illness there; was followed, the moment the intelligence reached Paris, by your mother who carried you with her; he died the day after her arrival, leaving no will—_no will_—so that the whole property fell to her and you.”

text: During this portion of the account, Monks caught his breath and listened with an expression of sharp anticipation, even though he kept his gaze averted from the man speaking. When Mr. Brownlow stopped for a moment, Monks shifted his weight like someone who had just felt a sudden sense of reprieve, wiping the perspiration from his flushed face and hands.
source_text: At this part of the recital Monks held his breath, and listened with a face of intense eagerness, though his eyes were not directed towards the speaker. As Mr. Brownlow paused, he changed his position with the air of one who has experienced a sudden relief, and wiped his hot face and hands.

text: “Quite so,” the other man responded. “His hound has been observed skulking around one of his old retreats, and it’s almost certain the master is hiding there already, or will arrive under the cloak of night. Watchmen are stationed at every turn. I’ve consulted with the party assigned to apprehend him, and they assure me his flight is at an end. A government bounty of one hundred pounds is to be announced tonight.”
source_text: “Yes, yes,” replied the other. “His dog has been seen lurking about some old haunt, and there seems little doubt that his master either is, or will be, there, under cover of the darkness. Spies are hovering about in every direction. I have spoken to the men who are charged with his capture, and they tell me he cannot escape. A reward of a hundred pounds is proclaimed by Government tonight.”

text: Close to the stretch of the Thames where the Rotherhithe church stands, in an area where the riverside buildings are at their grimiest and the vessels on the water are blackened by coal dust and the soot from cramped, low-roofed houses, lies the most repulsive and peculiar of the many hidden pockets of London. It is a truly extraordinary place, one whose very name remains entirely unknown to the vast majority of the city’s inhabitants.
source_text: Near to that part of the Thames on which the church at Rotherhithe abuts, where the buildings on the banks are dirtiest and the vessels on the river blackest with the dust of colliers and the smoke of close-built low-roofed houses, there exists the filthiest, the strangest, the most extraordinary of the many localities that are hidden in London, wholly unknown, even by name, to the great mass of its inhabitants.

text: “Poor Bet! She went to view the body, to see if she could identify it,” Chitling replied, his expression sinking further into gloom. “She went completely out of her mind, screaming and shouting, slamming her head against the floorboards. They had to force her into a straightjacket and take her to the hospital—and that’s where she is now.”
source_text: “Poor Bet! She went to see the Body, to speak to who it was,” replied Chitling, his countenance falling more and more, “and went off mad, screaming and raving, and beating her head against the boards; so they put a strait-weskut on her and took her to the hospital—and there she is.”

text: Crackit went down to the door and returned followed by a man whose lower face was muffled in a handkerchief, another being tied over his head beneath his hat. He removed them slowly. A pallid face, sunken eyes, gaunt cheeks, three days’ stubble, emaciated limbs, and heavy, labored breathing; it was the mere ghost of Sikes.
source_text: Crackit went down to the door, and returned followed by a man with the lower part of his face buried in a handkerchief, and another tied over his head under his hat. He drew them slowly off. Blanched face, sunken eyes, hollow cheeks, beard of three days’ growth, wasted flesh, short thick breath; it was the very ghost of Sikes.

text: The three witnesses stood as if paralyzed by shock. They did not attempt to intervene while the child and the man grappled together on the dirt; the boy, oblivious to the heavy blows falling upon him, only tightened his desperate grip on the fabric at the murderer’s chest and continued to shriek for aid with every breath.
source_text: The three spectators seemed quite stupefied. They offered no interference, and the boy and man rolled on the ground together; the former, heedless of the blows that showered upon him, wrenching his hands tighter and tighter in the garments about the murderer’s breast, and never ceasing to call for help with all his might.

text: Find me a room where I can lock up this screaming little demon!" Sikes roared, pacing frantically while hauling the boy along as effortlessly as if he were an empty bag. "That door! Now!" He shoved the child inside, slid the bolt home, and twisted the key. "Is the door downstairs locked tight?
source_text: “Open the door of some place where I can lock this screeching Hell-babe,” cried Sikes fiercely; running to and fro, and dragging the boy, now, as easily as if he were an empty sack. “That door. Quick!” He flung him in, bolted it, and turned the key. “Is the downstairs door fast?”

text: Those in the front rank caught the shout, and a thousand voices swelled the refrain. Men clamored for scaling-frames, others for iron mauls; several dashed here and there with flaming brands in search of tools, only to return and bellow anew; a few exhausted their lungs in fruitless oaths and bitter invective; many surged onward with a frantic fervor, choking the way for those struggling behind; the most reckless sought to ascend by the leaden conduits and fissures in the stone; and the entire throng billowed in the murk like a harvest of grain threshed by a gale, uniting at intervals in a solitary, thunderous cry of rage.
source_text: The nearest voices took up the cry, and hundreds echoed it. Some called for ladders, some for sledge-hammers; some ran with torches to and fro as if to seek them, and still came back and roared again; some spent their breath in impotent curses and execrations; some pressed forward with the ecstasy of madmen, and thus impeded the progress of those below; some among the boldest attempted to climb up by the water-spout and crevices in the wall; and all waved to and fro, in the darkness beneath, like a field of corn moved by an angry wind: and joined from time to time in one loud furious roar.

text: The onlookers had been gripped by a sudden hush, peering at his frantic motions and doubting his design; yet, the instant they perceived his ruin, they raised a shout of savage execration to which all their previous din was but a murmur. Over and over the cry surged. Those too far removed to know the cause joined the clamor; it rang and echoed through the air; it seemed as if the entire city had emptied its dwellings to hurl a collective damnation at his soul.
source_text: The crowd had been hushed during these few moments, watching his motions and doubtful of his purpose, but the instant they perceived it and knew it was defeated, they raised a cry of triumphant execration to which all their previous shouting had been whispers. Again and again it rose. Those who were at too great a distance to know its meaning, took up the sound; it echoed and re-echoed; it seemed as though the whole city had poured its population out to curse him.

text: A fresh roar erupted. At that instant, word rippled through the throng that the door had finally been breached, and that the man who had first called for the ladder had successfully entered the room. The human tide shifted abruptly as the news spread from person to person; those watching from the windows, seeing the crowds on the bridges surging back, abandoned their posts and hurried into the street to join the mob now rushing headlong toward the house. Every man shoved and struggled against his neighbor, breathless with the desperate urge to get near the entrance and glimpse the villain as the officers hauled him out. The screams of those pinned to the point of suffocation or trampled underfoot in the chaos were horrific, and the narrow alleys became completely choked. Between the frantic rush of those trying to reclaim the space before the house and the desperate thrashing of others attempting to escape the crush, the immediate focus on the murderer was momentarily lost, though the collective hunger for his capture only grew more feverish.
source_text: There was another roar. At this moment the word was passed among the crowd that the door was forced at last, and that he who had first called for the ladder had mounted into the room. The stream abruptly turned, as this intelligence ran from mouth to mouth; and the people at the windows, seeing those upon the bridges pouring back, quitted their stations, and running into the street, joined the concourse that now thronged pell-mell to the spot they had left: each man crushing and striving with his neighbor, and all panting with impatience to get near the door, and look upon the criminal as the officers brought him out. The cries and shrieks of those who were pressed almost to suffocation, or trampled down and trodden under foot in the confusion, were dreadful; the narrow ways were completely blocked up; and at this time, between the rush of some to regain the space in front of the house, and the unavailing struggles of others to extricate themselves from the mass, the immediate attention was distracted from the murderer, although the universal eagerness for his capture was, if possible, increased.

text: Look, look right there!" Oliver cried, gripping Rose’s hand and pointing out the carriage window. "That is the stile I climbed over; those are the hedges I hid behind, terrified that someone would catch me and drag me back! Over there is the path across the fields, leading to the house where I was a small child. Oh Dick, my dear old friend, if only I could see you now!
source_text: “See there, there!” cried Oliver, eagerly clasping the hand of Rose, and pointing out at the carriage window; “that’s the stile I came over; there are the hedges I crept behind, for fear any one should overtake me and force me back! Yonder is the path across the fields, leading to the old house where I was a little child! Oh Dick, Dick, my dear old friend, if I could only see you now!”

text: Yet it was a sincere, vivid, and joyful reality. They pulled up at the entrance of the town’s principal hotel—a place Oliver had once viewed with such awe that he had mistaken it for a vast palace, though it now appeared curiously shrunken and less imposing. Mr. Grimwig was there to welcome them, greeting both the young lady and the elder woman with a grandfatherly affection as they stepped from the coach. He was the very picture of kindness, never once threatening to eat his own head; he remained remarkably pleasant even while stubbornly correcting an ancient postboy regarding the quickest route to London, insisting he knew the way best despite having only traveled it once while soundly asleep. Dinner was waiting, the bedrooms were ready, and every comfort seemed to have been arranged as if by magic.
source_text: But it was pure, earnest, joyful reality. They drove straight to the door of the chief hotel (which Oliver used to stare up at, with awe, and think a mighty palace, but which had somehow fallen off in grandeur and size); and here was Mr. Grimwig all ready to receive them, kissing the young lady, and the old one too, when they got out of the coach, as if he were the grandfather of the whole party, all smiles and kindness, and not offering to eat his head—no, not once; not even when he contradicted a very old postboy about the nearest road to London, and maintained he knew it best, though he had only come that way once, and that time fast asleep. There was dinner prepared, and there were bedrooms ready, and everything was arranged as if by magic.

text: Finally, when nine o’clock arrived and they had begun to assume the night's business was over, Mr. Losberne and Mr. Grimwig entered the room. They were followed by Mr. Brownlow and a man whose appearance made Oliver nearly cry out in shock. They informed him that this was his brother—the same individual he had encountered in the market town and later seen peering through his bedroom window with Fagin. Monks directed a look of pure, undisguised malice at the stunned boy before taking a seat near the door. Mr. Brownlow, clutching a handful of documents, walked over to a table where Rose and Oliver were sitting.
source_text: At length, when nine o’clock had come, and they began to think they were to hear no more that night, Mr. Losberne and Mr. Grimwig entered the room, followed by Mr. Brownlow and a man whom Oliver almost shrieked with surprise to see; for they told him it was his brother, and it was the same man he had met at the market-town, and seen looking in with Fagin at the window of his little room. Monks cast a look of hate, which, even then, he could not dissemble, at the astonished boy, and sat down near the door. Mr. Brownlow, who had papers in his hand, walked to a table near which Rose and Oliver were seated.

text: “The will,” Mr. Brownlow stated, interjecting for him, “was written in the same spirit as the letter. He spoke of the wretchedness his wife had caused him, and of your own defiant character, your malice, and the premature wickedness you showed as his only son—raised, as you were, to despise him. He bequeathed to you and your mother an annual allowance of eight hundred pounds each. The bulk of his wealth was split into two equal parts: one for Agnes Fleming, and the other for her child, provided it survived birth and reached adulthood. If the child were a girl, the inheritance was unconditional; but if it were a boy, he would only receive it on the condition that, during his youth, he never tarnished his name through any public act of disgrace, meanness, or cowardice. He did this to show his faith in the mother and his conviction—strengthened by his impending death—that the child would share her gentle spirit and integrity. Should he be disappointed in this hope, the money was to go to you. For only then, if both children proved equally unworthy, would he acknowledge your prior claim to his wealth—a claim you never held over his heart, having repulsed him with coldness and hostility since you were a small child.”
source_text: “The will,” said Mr. Brownlow, speaking for him, “was in the same spirit as the letter. He talked of miseries which his wife had brought upon him; of the rebellious disposition, vice, malice, and premature bad passions of you his only son, who had been trained to hate him; and left you, and your mother, each an annuity of eight hundred pounds. The bulk of his property he divided into two equal portions—one for Agnes Fleming, and the other for their child, if it should be born alive, and ever come of age. If it were a girl, it was to inherit the money unconditionally; but if a boy, only on the stipulation that in his minority he should never have stained his name with any public act of dishonour, meanness, cowardice, or wrong. He did this, he said, to mark his confidence in the mother, and his conviction—only strengthened by approaching death—that the child would share her gentle heart, and noble nature. If he were disappointed in this expectation, then the money was to come to you: for then, and not till then, when both children were equal, would he recognise your prior claim upon his purse, who had none upon his heart, but had, from an infant, repulsed him with coldness and aversion.”

text: “Is not the heart’s impulse merely the heart’s impulse, Mrs. Pringle?” the overseer argued. “Can it be said I lack sentiment—I, who oversaw his upbringing by the very statutes of the vestry—now that I find him perched here amidst such remarkably polished society! I held that lad in as much esteem as if he were my—my—my own sainted progenitor,” Mr. Pringle declared, faltering as he reached for a sufficiently lofty metaphor. “Master Silas, my dear, you surely recollect that pious gentleman in the ivory waistcoat? He was summoned to his eternal rest but six days since, in a mahogany casket with brass-finished handles, Silas.”
source_text: “Isn’t natur, natur, Mrs. Bumble?” remonstrated the workhouse master. “Can’t I be supposed to feel—_I_ as brought him up porochially—when I see him a-setting here among ladies and gentlemen of the very affablest description! I always loved that boy as if he’d been my—my—my own grandfather,” said Mr. Bumble, halting for an appropriate comparison. “Master Oliver, my dear, you remember the blessed gentleman in the white waistcoat? Ah! he went to heaven last week, in a oak coffin with plated handles, Oliver.”

text: “Should the legal code maintain such a premise,” remarked Mr. Pringle, wringing his headgear with fervent intensity, “then the law is a fool—a simpleton. If such is the vision of the law, the law is an unmarried man; and the most I might wish for the law is that its eye be opened by the lessons of experience—of experience.”
source_text: “If the law supposes that,” said Mr. Bumble, squeezing his hat emphatically in both hands, “the law is a ass—a idiot. If that’s the eye of the law, the law is a bachelor; and the worst I wish the law is, that his eye may be opened by experience—by experience.”

text: “The girl,” Monks answered, “after her father died in an unfamiliar place under an assumed name, leaving no letters, books, or even a shred of paper that might have revealed his true identity—she was taken in by some impoverished locals, who raised her as if she were their own.”
source_text: “The child,” replied Monks, “when her father died in a strange place, in a strange name, without a letter, book, or scrap of paper that yielded the faintest clue by which his friends or relatives could be traced—the child was taken by some wretched cottagers, who reared it as their own.”

text: “The motives that swayed me then shall sway me still,” Clara declared with quiet resolve. “If ever I owed an unyielding obligation to the woman whose mercy rescued me from a life of penury and wretchedness, surely that debt claims me most tonight. It is a conflict,” she said, “but one I meet with pride; it is a grief, but one my spirit shall endure.”
source_text: “The same reasons which influenced me then, will influence me now,” said Rose firmly. “If I ever owed a strict and rigid duty to her, whose goodness saved me from a life of indigence and suffering, when should I ever feel it, as I should tonight? It is a struggle,” said Rose, “but one I am proud to make; it is a pang, but one my heart shall bear.”

text: The courtroom was tiled from floor to ceiling with the features of the crowd. Probing, hungry eyes peered out from every available gap. From the ledge of the prisoner’s dock to the most cramped and distant corners of the galleries, every gaze was locked onto one man—Fagin. Before him and behind, overhead and underfoot, to his left and his right, he seemed to stand within a vast constellation, shimmering with the light of a thousand watchful eyes.
source_text: The court was paved, from floor to roof, with human faces. Inquisitive and eager eyes peered from every inch of space. From the rail before the dock, away into the sharpest angle of the smallest corner in the galleries, all looks were fixed upon one man—Fagin. Before him and behind: above, below, on the right and on the left: he seemed to stand surrounded by a firmament, all bright with gleaming eyes.

text: He gazed longingly at their faces as they exited, one after the other, as if trying to gauge which way the consensus was tilting; but the effort was futile. The guard touched his shoulder. He moved like an automaton toward the end of the dock and sat down. He wouldn't have even seen the chair if the man hadn't gestured toward it.
source_text: He looked, wistfully, into their faces, one by one when they passed out, as though to see which way the greater number leant; but that was fruitless. The jailer touched him on the shoulder. He followed mechanically to the end of the dock, and sat down on a chair. The man pointed it out, or he would not have seen it.

text: He cast his eyes toward the gallery once more. Amidst the stifling heat of the packed room, some onlookers were eating, while others used handkerchiefs to fan themselves. He spotted a young man sketching his features in a small pad. Wondering if the drawing was accurate, he watched with a bystander’s idle curiosity as the artist snapped his lead and carved a fresh point with a knife.
source_text: He looked up into the gallery again. Some of the people were eating, and some fanning themselves with handkerchiefs; for the crowded place was very hot. There was one young man sketching his face in a little note-book. He wondered whether it was like, and looked on when the artist broke his pencil-point, and made another with his knife, as any idle spectator might have done.

text: He was escorted through a flagstone chamber beneath the courtroom, where several defendants waited for their hearings while others conversed with friends pressed against a metal grate overlooking the yard. No one had come for him; yet, as he walked by, the other inmates drew back to ensure the crowd at the bars could see him clearly. The onlookers met him with a barrage of slurs, howling and hissing as he passed. He brandished his fist and would have spat at them, but his guards forced him onward, through a somber corridor lit by a few flickering lamps and into the heart of the jail.
source_text: They led him through a paved room under the court, where some prisoners were waiting till their turns came, and others were talking to their friends, who crowded round a grate which looked into the open yard. There was nobody there to speak to _him_; but, as he passed, the prisoners fell back to render him more visible to the people who were clinging to the bars: and they assailed him with opprobrious names, and screeched and hissed. He shook his fist, and would have spat upon them; but his conductors hurried him on, through a gloomy passage lighted by a few dim lamps, into the interior of the prison.

text: Others might have lived in this very cell—occupied this exact spot. The darkness was absolute; why hadn't they brought a lamp? This place had stood for ages. Dozens of men must have spent their final hours here. It was like sitting in a tomb littered with corpses—the hood, the rope, the bound arms, and the faces he recognized even through those ghastly veils. Light! He needed light!
source_text: Some of them might have inhabited that very cell—sat upon that very spot. It was very dark; why didn’t they bring a light? The cell had been built for many years. Scores of men must have passed their last hours there. It was like sitting in a vault strewn with dead bodies—the cap, the noose, the pinioned arms, the faces that he knew, even beneath that hideous veil.—Light, light!

text: The grim walls of Newgate, which had long concealed so much suffering and silent agony from the eyes and, quite often, the consciences of the world, had never contained a sight as horrifying as this. The few people who paused as they walked by, curious about the man scheduled for execution the following morning, would have found little rest that night had they actually been able to see him.
source_text: Those dreadful walls of Newgate, which have hidden so much misery and such unspeakable anguish, not only from the eyes, but, too often, and too long, from the thoughts, of men, never held so dread a spectacle as that. The few who lingered as they passed, and wondered what the man was doing who was to be hanged tomorrow, would have slept but ill that night, if they could have seen him.

text: Leaving that spot, the group proceeded through a succession of stout barriers, which were unfastened by warders stationed on the interior; having crossed a vacant courtyard, they mounted a steep, constricted staircase and reached a gallery flanked on the left by a sequence of massive doors. Gesturing for them to pause, the keeper struck one of these panels with his heavy iron ring. Two guards, after a moment of low-voiced consultation, stepped out into the hall, extending their weary frames as if savoring the short reprieve, and signaled for the newcomers to follow the gaoler into the room. They obeyed.
source_text: From this place, they passed through several strong gates, opened by other turnkeys from the inner side; and, having entered an open yard, ascended a flight of narrow steps, and came into a passage with a row of strong doors on the left hand. Motioning them to remain where they were, the turnkey knocked at one of these with his bunch of keys. The two attendants, after a little whispering, came out into the passage, stretching themselves as if glad of the temporary relief, and motioned the visitors to follow the jailer into the cell. They did so.

text: “Get him off to bed!” Fagin shrieked. “Are you listening, any of you? He’s been the—the—the source of this whole disaster. It’s worth every penny to bring him to this point—Bolter’s throat, Bill; forget the girl—slit Bolter’s throat as deep as you can. Saw his head right off!”
source_text: “Take him away to bed!” cried Fagin. “Do you hear me, some of you? He has been the—the—somehow the cause of all this. It’s worth the money to bring him up to it—Bolter’s throat, Bill; never mind the girl—Bolter’s throat as deep as you can cut. Saw his head off!”

text: The first gray streaks of morning were visible as they stepped back into the street. A massive congregation had already taken its place; the upper stories were packed with onlookers, some puffing on pipes and others distracted by games of chance; the mob beneath was a sea of movement, a cacophony of shoves, bickering, and ribaldry. All was pulse and vigor, save for the somber arrangement of wood and hemp at the square’s heart—the charcoal-colored dais, the heavy lintel, the coiled cord, and the whole ghastly architecture of the grave.
source_text: Day was dawning when they again emerged. A great multitude had already assembled; the windows were filled with people, smoking and playing cards to beguile the time; the crowd were pushing, quarrelling, joking. Everything told of life and animation, but one dark cluster of objects in the centre of all—the black stage, the cross-beam, the rope, and all the hideous apparatus of death.

text: Having secured a full pardon for testifying against Fagin, and deciding his former criminal life was far too hazardous, Mr. Noah Claypole spent some time searching for an easy way to make ends meet. Eventually, he established himself as a professional informant, a trade that allows him to live quite comfortably. His routine involves walking out once a week during Sunday services with Charlotte, who dresses quite respectably for the occasion. She makes a show of fainting at the doorstep of a kind-hearted innkeeper, and when Noah is sold a small glass of brandy to help her recover, he reports the violation the next day and keeps half the fine. On occasion, Mr. Claypole performs the fainting spell himself, but the profit remains identical.
source_text: Mr. Noah Claypole: receiving a free pardon from the Crown in consequence of being admitted approver against Fagin: and considering his profession not altogether as safe a one as he could wish: was, for some little time, at a loss for the means of a livelihood, not burdened with too much work. After some consideration, he went into business as an informer, in which calling he realises a genteel subsistence. His plan is, to walk out once a week during church time attended by Charlotte in respectable attire. The lady faints away at the doors of charitable publicans, and the gentleman being accommodated with three-penny worth of brandy to restore her, lays an information next day, and pockets half the penalty. Sometimes Mr. Claypole faints himself, but the result is the same.

text: Regarding Mr. Halloway and young Pipkin, they continue to occupy their customary stations, though time has polished the former’s scalp and turned the latter’s hair to a distinguished ash. They keep their quarters at the rectory, yet they dispense their labors with such democratic zeal between that house, Julian and Mr. Sterling, and Dr. Thorne, that the townspeople are still at a loss to determine which household can rightfully claim their allegiance.
source_text: As to Mr. Giles and Brittles, they still remain in their old posts, although the former is bald, and the last-named boy quite grey. They sleep at the parsonage, but divide their attentions so equally among its inmates, and Oliver and Mr. Brownlow, and Mr. Losberne, that to this day the villagers have never been able to discover to which establishment they properly belong.

text: Gabriel was his name, and throughout the workweek, he was a young man of practical sense, fluid movement, respectable attire, and an overall solid reputation. On Sundays, however, he became a man of vague intentions, prone to procrastination and burdened by his formal suit and umbrella. Generally, he felt he occupied that expansive, lukewarm middle ground between the parish’s devout regulars and its local drunks. He attended services, yet he would stifle a yawn by the time the congregation reached the Nicene Creed and found himself daydreaming about lunch when he was supposed to be listening to the sermon. To describe how he was viewed by the public: when his acquaintances were in a foul mood, they judged him a scoundrel; when they were happy, they saw him as a saint; and when they were neither, he was a man whose moral character was a sort of salt-and-pepper blend.
source_text: His Christian name was Gabriel, and on working days he was a young man of sound judgment, easy motions, proper dress, and general good character. On Sundays he was a man of misty views, rather given to postponing, and hampered by his best clothes and umbrella: upon the whole, one who felt himself to occupy morally that vast middle space of Laodicean neutrality which lay between the Communion people of the parish and the drunken section,—that is, he went to church, but yawned privately by the time the congregation reached the Nicene creed, and thought of what there would be for dinner when he meant to be listening to the sermon. Or, to state his character as it stood in the scale of public opinion, when his friends and critics were in tantrums, he was considered rather a bad man; when they were pleased, he was rather a good man; when they were neither, he was a man whose moral colour was a kind of pepper-and-salt mixture.

text: Yet an observant person, watching him cross one of his pastures on a remarkably warm and sunlit December morning, might have perceived Gabriel Oak in a different light. His face still retained the soft lines and colors of his youth, which had persisted well into his adult years; in the quiet corners of his expression, the boy he had been was still visible. His tall, sturdy frame was substantial enough to be commanding, had he ever bothered to carry himself with any sense of importance. But certain men, in both town and country, possess a psychological trait that has more to do with their spirit than their muscles: a habit of shrinking their physical presence through their own bearing. Out of a deep, almost saintly modesty—a constant sense that he had no special right to take up room in the world—Oak walked with an unassuming air and a slight, nearly imperceptible bend that was not quite a slouch. This could be seen as a shortcoming in someone who values their outward image more than their inner resilience, but Gabriel Oak was not a man who cared for such things.
source_text: But some thoughtful persons, who had seen him walking across one of his fields on a certain December morning—sunny and exceedingly mild—might have regarded Gabriel Oak in other aspects than these. In his face one might notice that many of the hues and curves of youth had tarried on to manhood: there even remained in his remoter crannies some relics of the boy. His height and breadth would have been sufficient to make his presence imposing, had they been exhibited with due consideration. But there is a way some men have, rural and urban alike, for which the mind is more responsible than flesh and sinew: it is a way of curtailing their dimensions by their manner of showing them. And from a quiet modesty that would have become a vestal, which seemed continually to impress upon him that he had no great claim on the world’s room, Oak walked unassumingly and with a faintly perceptible bend, yet distinct from a bowing of the shoulders. This may be said to be a defect in an individual if he depends for his valuation more upon his appearance than upon his capacity to wear well, which Oak did not.

text: He had just reached that time of life when the word "young" is no longer the automatic prefix to "man" in common description. He was at the most balanced stage of his development, for his intellect and his emotions were now distinctly partitioned: he had passed the years when youth indiscriminately mixes them into the form of impulse, and he had not yet reached the point where a wife and family weld them back together into the form of prejudice. In short, he was twenty-eight and a bachelor.
source_text: He had just reached the time of life at which “young” is ceasing to be the prefix of “man” in speaking of one. He was at the brightest period of masculine growth, for his intellect and his emotions were clearly separated: he had passed the time during which the influence of youth indiscriminately mingles them in the character of impulse, and he had not yet arrived at the stage wherein they become united again, in the character of prejudice, by the influence of a wife and family. In short, he was twenty-eight, and a bachelor.

text: Norcombe Hill, located near the solitary Toller-Down, was a landscape that impressed upon the observer a sense of near-absolute permanence. It was a plain, rounded swell of chalk and earth—one of those modest terrestrial curves that might remain unshaken during a final upheaval, even as more magnificent summits and sheer granite precipices crumble to the ground.
source_text: Norcombe Hill—not far from lonely Toller-Down—was one of the spots which suggest to a passer-by that he is in the presence of a shape approaching the indestructible as nearly as any to be found on earth. It was a featureless convexity of chalk and soil—an ordinary specimen of those smoothly-outlined protuberances of the globe which may remain undisturbed on some great day of confusion, when far grander heights and dizzy granite precipices topple down.

text: The ridge was cloaked on its eastern face by a venerable and moribund grove of oaks, the highest reaches of which etched a dark border over the summit, crowning the pale arc of the firmament like a bristling fringe. This night, the timber served to screen the western incline from the biting gusts that struck the copse and labored through the branches with a heavy muttering, or surged across the topmost limbs in a weary sigh. Down in the trench, the brittle leaves seethed and hissed in the restless air, a sudden draft occasionally prying a few loose to send them skittering wildly over the turf. A final scattering of the autumn’s dead had endured upon their native twigs until this deep mid-winter hour, and in their eventual fall, they struck the boles with a succession of crisp, percussive raps.
source_text: The hill was covered on its northern side by an ancient and decaying plantation of beeches, whose upper verge formed a line over the crest, fringing its arched curve against the sky, like a mane. To-night these trees sheltered the southern slope from the keenest blasts, which smote the wood and floundered through it with a sound as of grumbling, or gushed over its crowning boughs in a weakened moan. The dry leaves in the ditch simmered and boiled in the same breezes, a tongue of air occasionally ferreting out a few, and sending them spinning across the grass. A group or two of the latest in date amongst the dead multitude had remained till this very mid-winter time on the twigs which bore them and in falling rattled against the trunks with smart taps.

text: The music did not drift freely into the night air; it felt suppressed, lacking the strength to carry far or rise very high. The sound originated from a dim shape nestled against the hedge of the grove—a shepherd’s hut—which cast a silhouette so peculiar that an outsider would have struggled to guess its purpose or even recognize what it was.
source_text: The tune was not floating unhindered into the open air: it seemed muffled in some way, and was altogether too curtailed in power to spread high or wide. It came from the direction of a small dark object under the plantation hedge—a shepherd’s hut—now presenting an outline to which an uninitiated person might have been puzzled to attach either meaning or use.

text: The overall impression was that of a miniature Noah’s Ark resting on a small Ararat. It followed the conventional silhouette favored by toy-makers, a design that becomes fixed in the human mind from childhood as a lasting mental image. The cabin was mounted on small wheels, lifting its floor about a foot from the earth. These shepherds' huts are hauled into the pastures as lambing season nears, shielding the man during his mandatory nightly watch.
source_text: The image as a whole was that of a small Noah’s Ark on a small Ararat, allowing the traditionary outlines and general form of the Ark which are followed by toy-makers—and by these means are established in men’s imaginations among their firmest, because earliest impressions—to pass as an approximate pattern. The hut stood on little wheels, which raised its floor about a foot from the ground. Such shepherds’ huts are dragged into the fields when the lambing season comes on, to shelter the shepherd in his enforced nightly attendance.

text: This solo transition from farmhand to proprietor, burdened by a flock of sheep bought on credit, represented a vital turning point for Gabriel Oak, and he understood the weight of his circumstances perfectly. The first phase of his new career was the lambing of his ewes. Given that sheep had been his specialty since his youth, he was prudent enough not to delegate their care during this critical season to any hired worker or inexperienced hand.
source_text: This venture, unaided and alone, into the paths of farming as master and not as man, with an advance of sheep not yet paid for, was a critical juncture with Gabriel Oak, and he recognised his position clearly. The first movement in his new progress was the lambing of his ewes, and sheep having been his speciality from his youth, he wisely refrained from deputing the task of tending them at this season to a hireling or a novice.

text: The wind persisted in its assault on the corners of the hut, yet the flute music had fallen silent. A bright rectangle of light suddenly pierced the side of the building, revealing the dark outline of Farmer Oak. With a lantern in his grasp, he closed the door and occupied himself in that corner of the field for nearly twenty minutes. The light from his lamp flickered and faded as he worked, alternately illuminating his figure or casting him into darkness as he moved before or behind the flame.
source_text: The wind continued to beat about the corners of the hut, but the flute-playing ceased. A rectangular space of light appeared in the side of the hut, and in the opening the outline of Farmer Oak’s figure. He carried a lantern in his hand, and closing the door behind him, came forward and busied himself about this nook of the field for nearly twenty minutes, the lantern light appearing and disappearing here and there, and brightening him or darkening him as he stood before or behind it.

text: Elias’s gestures, while possessed of a muted vigor, were languid, and their cautious precision harmonized entirely with the nature of his calling. Because symmetry of form is born of function, it was impossible to ignore the inherent poise in his constant maneuvers through the huddle of ewes. Nevertheless, while he could, if the situation required, act or reason with a flash as volatile as that of any metropolitan wit, his peculiar excellence—be it ethical, corporeal, or intellectual—remained rooted in the stationary, rarely depending upon the fickle assistance of velocity.
source_text: Oak’s motions, though they had a quiet energy, were slow, and their deliberateness accorded well with his occupation. Fitness being the basis of beauty, nobody could have denied that his steady swings and turns in and about the flock had elements of grace. Yet, although if occasion demanded he could do or think a thing with as mercurial a dash as can the men of towns who are more to the manner born, his special power, morally, physically, and mentally, was static, owing little or nothing to momentum as a rule.

text: To be left in total solitude at night when one expects and desires company can be unnerving; yet, a far more distressing experience is to encounter an unknown presence when instinct, observation, memory, and every form of logical reasoning have combined to convince the mind that it is in absolute isolation.
source_text: To find themselves utterly alone at night where company is desirable and expected makes some people fearful; but a case more trying by far to the nerves is to discover some mysterious companionship when intuition, sensation, memory, analogy, testimony, probability, induction—every kind of evidence in the logician’s list—have united to persuade consciousness that it is quite in isolation.

text: Farmer Oak approached the grove and maneuvered through the low branches until he reached the windward side. A shadowy form nestled against the hill signaled the location of a shed, built into an excavation in the rise so that the rear of its roof rested nearly even with the soil. The front consisted of wooden planks fastened to posts and treated with tar to withstand the elements. Rays and specks of light filtered through gaps in the walls and ceiling, forming the collective glow that had drawn his attention. Oak moved to the back, where he leaned over the roof and peered through a small hole, gaining a clear view of the scene inside.
source_text: Farmer Oak went towards the plantation and pushed through its lower boughs to the windy side. A dim mass under the slope reminded him that a shed occupied a place here, the site being a cutting into the slope of the hill, so that at its back part the roof was almost level with the ground. In front it was formed of board nailed to posts and covered with tar as a preservative. Through crevices in the roof and side spread streaks and dots of light, a combination of which made the radiance that had attracted him. Oak stepped up behind, where, leaning down upon the roof and putting his eye close to a hole, he could see into the interior clearly.

text: Through a playful twist of fate—the kind of brief diversion Nature grants her children amidst her tireless labors—the girl’s cloak fell away, releasing a cascade of black hair over her red jacket. Oak knew her at once as the figure from the yellow wagon, the myrtles, and the mirror; or, more bluntly, as the woman who owed him two pence.
source_text: By one of those whimsical coincidences in which Nature, like a busy mother, seems to spare a moment from her unremitting labours to turn and make her children smile, the girl now dropped the cloak, and forth tumbled ropes of black hair over a red jacket. Oak knew her instantly as the heroine of the yellow waggon, myrtles, and looking-glass: prosily, as the woman who owed him twopence.

text: The rider appeared perfectly at ease on any part of her horse’s back. Once the plantation was behind her and there was no longer a need for her strange posture, she transitioned into a much more sensible stance. She lacked a side-saddle, and it was obvious that she could never maintain a secure grip on the polished leather by sitting sideways. Straightening up like a resilient young tree and ensuring she was unobserved, she took her seat astride the animal as the saddle required, despite the social impropriety, and trotted away toward Tewnell Mill.
source_text: The performer seemed quite at home anywhere between a horse’s head and its tail, and the necessity for this abnormal attitude having ceased with the passage of the plantation, she began to adopt another, even more obviously convenient than the first. She had no side-saddle, and it was very apparent that a firm seat upon the smooth leather beneath her was unattainable sideways. Springing to her accustomed perpendicular like a bowed sapling, and satisfying herself that nobody was in sight, she seated herself in the manner demanded by the saddle, though hardly expected of the woman, and trotted off in the direction of Tewnell Mill.

text: A sharp intuition forced him to snatch his eyes from hers as abruptly as if he had been caught in a trespass. The memory of the singular vanities she had enacted while passing through the grove stirred in the girl a nettled fluttering, succeeded by a scalding heat. It was a spectacle to see a woman redden who was rarely given to such displays; every part of the milkmaid’s visage was suffused with the most intense vermilion. From the delicate Eglantine, through the diverse shades of the Damask to the darkest Gallica, the countenance of Miller’s companion rapidly darkened; whereupon he, in a fit of delicacy, turned his head away.
source_text: A perception caused him to withdraw his own eyes from hers as suddenly as if he had been caught in a theft. Recollection of the strange antics she had indulged in when passing through the trees was succeeded in the girl by a nettled palpitation, and that by a hot face. It was a time to see a woman redden who was not given to reddening as a rule; not a point in the milkmaid but was of the deepest rose-colour. From the Maiden’s Blush, through all varieties of the Provence down to the Crimson Tuscany, the countenance of Oak’s acquaintance quickly graduated; whereupon he, in considerateness, turned away his head.

text: With the hour of the evening pail approaching, Thorne kept his accustomed vigil over the stone byre. A biting chill finally reached him; after scattering fresh litter about the yearling tegs, he retreated into his wheeled cabin and heaped more fuel upon the grate. To block the draft surging beneath the door, he wedged a heavy sack against the sill and pivoted the small dwelling slightly toward the south. Immediately, the wind began to hiss through a narrow air-hole—one of a pair cut into the opposing walls of the timber room.
source_text: As the milking-hour drew near, Oak kept his usual watch upon the cowshed. At last he felt cold, and shaking an extra quantity of bedding round the yearling ewes he entered the hut and heaped more fuel upon the stove. The wind came in at the bottom of the door, and to prevent it Oak laid a sack there and wheeled the cot round a little more to the south. Then the wind spouted in at a ventilating hole—of which there was one on each side of the hut.

text: When he opened his eyes, he found that the evening had faded into a sudden, unexpected twilight. The young woman with the lovely smile and bright teeth was right beside him. Most startlingly, his head was resting in her lap; his face and neck were uncomfortably damp, and her fingers were busy unfastening his collar.
source_text: On opening his eyes he found that evening had sunk to dusk in a strange manner of unexpectedness. The young girl with the remarkably pleasant lips and white teeth was beside him. More than this—astonishingly more—his head was upon her lap, his face and neck were disagreeably wet, and her fingers were unbuttoning his collar.

text: “Blast that cabin!” Silas grumbled. “I paid twelve sovereigns for that structure. I’ll sell it off and huddle under woven hurdles as the ancients did, nesting in a bundle of loose chaff! It served me just such a turn but a few days past!” To lend weight to his words, Silas brought his heavy fist down against the floor.
source_text: “Ah, the hut!” murmured Gabriel. “I gave ten pounds for that hut. But I’ll sell it, and sit under thatched hurdles as they did in old times, and curl up to sleep in a lock of straw! It played me nearly the same trick the other day!” Gabriel, by way of emphasis, brought down his fist upon the floor.

text: “I suppose I ought to,” Oak murmured, his mind elsewhere. He was striving to seize and savor the feeling of this proximity, his head pressed against her gown, before the instant vanished into the discard pile of history. He wished she could grasp his inner state, but he would have sooner tried to catch a scent in a net than attempt to bind his ethereal feelings within the heavy, blunt threads of human speech. Thus, he stayed quiet.
source_text: “Yes I suppose I should,” said Oak, absently. He was endeavouring to catch and appreciate the sensation of being thus with her, his head upon her dress, before the event passed on into the heap of bygone things. He wished she knew his impressions; but he would as soon have thought of carrying an odour in a net as of attempting to convey the intangibilities of his feeling in the coarse meshes of language. So he remained silent.

text: Gabriel was lost in such deep contemplation that the sheer weight of his thoughts etched faint creases into his forehead. When the outcome of a meeting is as likely to bring disaster as it is success, any deviation from what one anticipated creates a sharp sting of failure. Oak approached the door feeling somewhat disconcerted; the opening he had rehearsed in his mind bore no resemblance to the reality he now faced.
source_text: Gabriel meditated, and so deeply that he brought small furrows into his forehead by sheer force of reverie. Where the issue of an interview is as likely to be a vast change for the worse as for the better, any initial difference from expectation causes nipping sensations of failure. Oak went up to the door a little abashed: his mental rehearsal and the reality had had no common grounds of opening.

text: “This is all I intended to say,” she explained with urgency, though she was vaguely aware of the awkwardness of the situation she had created. “No man has won my heart yet, contrary to my aunt’s claim that I have a dozen suitors. I despise the idea of being treated like a man’s possession, though I suppose I might be spoken for eventually. Had I any romantic interest in you, I certainly wouldn’t have followed you like this; that would have been far too brazen! But I saw no shame in rushing to set the record straight regarding a falsehood you had been told.”
source_text: “What I meant to tell you was only this,” she said eagerly, and yet half conscious of the absurdity of the position she had made for herself—“that nobody has got me yet as a sweetheart, instead of my having a dozen, as my aunt said; I _hate_ to be thought men’s property in that way, though possibly I shall be had some day. Why, if I’d wanted you I shouldn’t have run after you like this; ’twould have been the _forwardest_ thing! But there was no harm in hurrying to correct a piece of false news that had been told you.”

text: “By my very life and spirit, I cannot think of a more senseless remark for a woman to utter,” Thorne declared. “But my dear,” he proceeded in a soothing, conciliatory tone, “do not be so!” He released a sigh of such unvarnished honesty—one so heavy that, resembling the soughing of a great cedar grove, it became a distinct vibration in the surrounding air. “Why must you refuse me?” he entreated, maneuvering around the sharp-leaved holly to reach her side.
source_text: “Upon my heart and soul, I don’t know what a maid can say stupider than that,” said Oak. “But dearest,” he continued in a palliative voice, “don’t be like it!” Oak sighed a deep honest sigh—none the less so in that, being like the sigh of a pine plantation, it was rather noticeable as a disturbance of the atmosphere. “Why won’t you have me?” he appealed, creeping round the holly to reach her side.

text: "Never," Oak insisted, his voice so charged with conviction that he seemed to be surging through the brush toward her by the sheer weight of his words. "There is one thing I am sure of in this world—only one—and that is that I will love you, ache for you, and continue to want you until my final breath." His tone held a deep, sincere sadness now, and his broad, sun-darkened hands shook with visible emotion.
source_text: “Never,” said Mr Oak, so earnestly that he seemed to be coming, by the force of his words, straight through the bush and into her arms. “I shall do one thing in this life—one thing certain—that is, love you, and long for you, and _keep wanting you_ till I die.” His voice had a genuine pathos now, and his large brown hands perceptibly trembled.

text: “It feels terribly wrong to refuse you when your devotion is so deep,” she said with a hint of anguish, glancing around as if seeking an escape from her moral predicament. “I truly wish I hadn’t run after you!” Nevertheless, she seemed to have a knack for quickly recovering her spirits, and she set her features into a look of coy defiance. “It wouldn’
source_text: “It seems dreadfully wrong not to have you when you feel so much!” she said with a little distress, and looking hopelessly around for some means of escape from her moral dilemma. “How I wish I hadn’t run after you!” However she seemed to have a short cut for getting back to cheerfulness, and set her face to signify archness. “It wouldn’t do, Mr. Oak. I want somebody to tame me; I am too independent; and you would never be able to, I know.”

text: It is often observed that while there is a clear road for falling into love, no such defined path exists for getting out of it. Some regard marriage as a shortcut to that destination, yet it is a cure known to fail. Separation, the remedy chance offered Gabriel Oak upon Bathsheba’s disappearance, might be effective for certain personalities, but it often serves to idealize the absent person for others—specifically those whose love, however quiet and steady, runs deep. Oak belonged to this even-tempered class of men, and he found the secret union of his soul with Bathsheba’s to be burning with a more refined intensity now that she was gone.
source_text: It may have been observed that there is no regular path for getting out of love as there is for getting in. Some people look upon marriage as a short cut that way, but it has been known to fail. Separation, which was the means that chance offered to Gabriel Oak by Bathsheba’s disappearance, though effectual with people of certain humours, is apt to idealize the removed object with others—notably those whose affection, placid and regular as it may be, flows deep and long. Oak belonged to the even-tempered order of humanity, and felt the secret fusion of himself in Bathsheba to be burning with a finer flame now that she was gone—that was all.

text: Gabriel owned a pair of dogs. George, the senior of the two, possessed a nose with a black tip and a thin rim of pink skin. His coat was patterned with irregular patches of white and charcoal grey; however, years of exposure to the elements had bleached the grey from the most prominent tufts, leaving them a rusty brown. It appeared as though the blue tones had vanished from the fur, much like the way indigo pigments fade in a landscape by Turner. While his coat had originally been hair, a lifetime spent among the sheep seemed to be gradually transforming it into a coarse, low-grade wool.
source_text: Gabriel had two dogs. George, the elder, exhibited an ebony-tipped nose, surrounded by a narrow margin of pink flesh, and a coat marked in random splotches approximating in colour to white and slaty grey; but the grey, after years of sun and rain, had been scorched and washed out of the more prominent locks, leaving them of a reddish-brown, as if the blue component of the grey had faded, like the indigo from the same kind of colour in Turner’s pictures. In substance it had originally been hair, but long contact with sheep seemed to be turning it by degrees into wool of a poor quality and staple.

text: George had originally been owned by a shepherd of loose morals and a vicious disposition, with the result that the dog understood the various shades of malice in every oath and curse more clearly than the most foul-mouthed scoundrel in the parish. Long experience had taught him to distinguish so finely between a civil "Come in!" and a "Damn you, come in!" that he knew to a hair’s breadth how fast he must retreat from the ewes' heels to escape a heavy blow from a sheep crook. Though he was getting on in years, he remained a sharp and thoroughly reliable animal.
source_text: This dog had originally belonged to a shepherd of inferior morals and dreadful temper, and the result was that George knew the exact degrees of condemnation signified by cursing and swearing of all descriptions better than the wickedest old man in the neighbourhood. Long experience had so precisely taught the animal the difference between such exclamations as “Come in!” and “D–––– ye, come in!” that he knew to a hair’s breadth the rate of trotting back from the ewes’ tails that each call involved, if a staggerer with the sheep crook was to be escaped. Though old, he was clever and trustworthy still.

text: Oak was a man of profound compassion; indeed, his natural empathy often dismantled his more calculated plans, guiding him forward as if by an inescapable force of nature. He had always been haunted by the grim truth that his flock’s ultimate destination was the butcher—that a moment inevitably arrived when every shepherd stood as a complete traitor to the helpless creatures he raised. His immediate reaction to the scene was one of deep grief for the premature end of these docile ewes and the lives of their unborn offspring.
source_text: Oak was an intensely humane man: indeed, his humanity often tore in pieces any politic intentions of his which bordered on strategy, and carried him on as by gravitation. A shadow in his life had always been that his flock ended in mutton—that a day came and found every shepherd an arrant traitor to his defenseless sheep. His first feeling now was one of pity for the untimely fate of these gentle ewes and their unborn lambs.

text: George’s offspring had performed his duties with such terrifying efficiency that he was judged too proficient to be allowed to live; consequently, he was led away and executed at noon that very day. His end served as another grim example of the misfortune that frequently haunts dogs and thinkers alike when they follow a line of logic to its absolute limit. It is the recurring tragedy of those who attempt to maintain perfect consistency in a world governed almost entirely by compromise.
source_text: George’s son had done his work so thoroughly that he was considered too good a workman to live, and was, in fact, taken and tragically shot at twelve o’clock that same day—another instance of the untoward fate which so often attends dogs and other philosophers who follow out a train of reasoning to its logical conclusion, and attempt perfectly consistent conduct in a world made up so largely of compromise.

text: Gabriel’s features had grown more pallid, his gaze more contemplative, and his general air more somber. He had endured a crucible of misery that had ultimately bestowed upon him more than it had stripped away. Though he had plummeted from his humble status as a master of the flocks into the lowest depths of misfortune, he had emerged with a stately composure he had never previously known. He now possessed a certain detachment from his own destiny—a quality that might lead a lesser man toward bitterness, but which, in his case, formed the very foundation of his nobility. In this manner, his humiliation had become his elevation, and his great loss had been his ultimate gain.
source_text: Gabriel was paler now. His eyes were more meditative, and his expression was more sad. He had passed through an ordeal of wretchedness which had given him more than it had taken away. He had sunk from his modest elevation as pastoral king into the very slime-pits of Siddim; but there was left to him a dignified calm he had never before known, and that indifference to fate which, though it often makes a villain of a man, is the basis of his sublimity when it does not. And thus the abasement had been exaltation, and the loss gain.

text: Gabriel climbed the gate once more, jumping down onto the tilled earth on the other side and heading straight across the field toward the fire. The blaze expanded rapidly, growing larger both from his approach and its own spreading heat, revealing the sharp outlines of haystacks as he drew near. The fire was centered in a rick-yard. A deep orange light began to bathe his exhausted face, and the front of his tunic and leggings were covered in a shifting pattern of shadows from thorny branches, cast as the light filtered through a bare hedge. In the intense glow, the metal curve of his shepherd’s crook gleamed like polished silver. He reached the perimeter fence and paused to catch his breath. The place appeared to be completely deserted.
source_text: Gabriel again mounted the gate, and, leaping down on the other side upon what he found to be ploughed soil, made across the field in the exact direction of the fire. The blaze, enlarging in a double ratio by his approach and its own increase, showed him as he drew nearer the outlines of ricks beside it, lighted up to great distinctness. A rick-yard was the source of the fire. His weary face now began to be painted over with a rich orange glow, and the whole front of his smock-frock and gaiters was covered with a dancing shadow pattern of thorn-twigs—the light reaching him through a leafless intervening hedge—and the metallic curve of his sheep-crook shone silver-bright in the same abounding rays. He came up to the boundary fence, and stood to regain breath. It seemed as if the spot was unoccupied by a living soul.

text: Before Elias stood a haphazard stack of forage, and the fire plunged into its dry depths with electric speed. The side facing the gale pulsed with a deep incandescence, brightening and fading in rhythm, much like the smoldering tip of a tobacco pipe. Presently an overhanging sheaf tumbled downward with a dry rustle; the blaze stretched upward, twisting in a hushed, steady drone that lacked any sharp snapping. Level strata of vapor drifted rearward like scudding mists, and from within these veils, concealed furnaces cast a glow that turned the translucent haze into a broad, radiant expanse of saffron. Single stalks in the front were overtaken by a crawling, scarlet heat, resembling tangles of vermillion larvae, while overhead there appeared phantasmal countenances of flame—protruding tongues, staring orbs, and various diabolical silhouettes—from which bursts of embers occasionally took flight like a sudden cloud of starlings.
source_text: This before Gabriel’s eyes was a rick of straw, loosely put together, and the flames darted into it with lightning swiftness. It glowed on the windward side, rising and falling in intensity, like the coal of a cigar. Then a superincumbent bundle rolled down, with a whisking noise; flames elongated, and bent themselves about with a quiet roar, but no crackle. Banks of smoke went off horizontally at the back like passing clouds, and behind these burned hidden pyres, illuminating the semi-transparent sheet of smoke to a lustrous yellow uniformity. Individual straws in the foreground were consumed in a creeping movement of ruddy heat, as if they were knots of red worms, and above shone imaginary fiery faces, tongues hanging from lips, glaring eyes, and other impish forms, from which at intervals sparks flew in clusters like birds from a nest.

text: “Good heavens, fire! A fine master but a wicked servant—or rather, a wicked servant and a fine master! Mark Clark, come quickly! Billy Smallbury, Maryann Money, Jan Coggan, and Matthew, over here!” Further shapes emerged through the thick smoke behind the shouting man, and Gabriel discovered that he was not alone but surrounded by a large group. Their shadows leaped and jolted against the backdrop, dictated by the erratic pulse of the fire rather than the movements of the people themselves. This assembly—typical of those who process thought through emotion and emotion through frantic action—fell to work with a singular and disorganized energy.
source_text: “O, man—fire, fire! A good master and a bad servant is fire, fire!—I mane a bad servant and a good master. Oh, Mark Clark—come! And you, Billy Smallbury—and you, Maryann Money—and you, Jan Coggan, and Matthew there!” Other figures now appeared behind this shouting man and among the smoke, and Gabriel found that, far from being alone he was in a great company—whose shadows danced merrily up and down, timed by the jigging of the flames, and not at all by their owners’ movements. The assemblage—belonging to that class of society which casts its thoughts into the form of feeling, and its feelings into the form of commotion—set to work with a remarkable confusion of purpose.

text: Thorne gripped the blunt ends of the bundles as if preparing for a turn at straw-dressing; then, bracing his heels and jamming his crook into the gaps, he hauled himself up the overhanging wall. He quickly straddled the topmost ridge and began to strike at the smoldering embers with his staff, hollering to the men below for a leafy bough, a ladder, and a dousing of water.
source_text: Oak seized the cut ends of the sheaves, as if he were going to engage in the operation of “reed-drawing,” and digging in his feet, and occasionally sticking in the stem of his sheep-crook, he clambered up the beetling face. He at once sat astride the very apex, and began with his crook to beat off the fiery fragments which had lodged thereon, shouting to the others to get him a bough and a ladder, and some water.

text: “True enough, I reckon, and a woman of vast means!” observed a fellow nearby. “She journeyed from far away to take up the lands of her kinsman, whose end was quite abrupt. That old fellow was said to tally his silver by the gallon jar. Nowadays, they tell that she keeps a ledger in every vault in Melchester, and treats a gold guinea with no more gravity than you and I would a common farthing—not a whit of difference, herdsman.”
source_text: “Ay, ’a b’lieve, and a rich one too!” said a bystander. “Lately ’a came here from a distance. Took on her uncle’s farm, who died suddenly. Used to measure his money in half-pint cups. They say now that she’ve business in every bank in Casterbridge, and thinks no more of playing pitch-and-toss sovereign than you and I do pitch-halfpenny—not a bit in the world, shepherd.”

text: The bailiff, who appeared almost frightened by the prospect of showing neighborly kindness, headed up the slope, while Oak continued toward the village. He was still stunned by the unexpected encounter with Bathsheba, happy to be in her presence once more but baffled by the speed of her change; the inexperienced girl from Norcombe had become a poised and commanding woman. It seems some women only require a crisis to prove they are capable of meeting it.
source_text: The bailiff who showed this nervous dread of loving his neighbour as himself, went up the hill, and Oak walked on to the village, still astonished at the reencounter with Bathsheba, glad of his nearness to her, and perplexed at the rapidity with which the unpractised girl of Norcombe had developed into the supervising and cool woman here. But some women only require an emergency to make them fit for one.

text: The small figure remained rooted beside the ancient beech, while Silas wound his way down toward the hearths of Kings-Hintock, otherwise known as Marsh-Mallow in the local dialect. He believed he had stood within the very shadow of a vast and quiet misery when he had encountered that slight, trembling being. Nevertheless, because discernment demands the tempering of one's sudden fancies, Silas laboured to suppress the memory of the moment.
