source_text: The young girl remained motionless by the tree, and Gabriel descended into the village of Weatherbury, or Lower Longpuddle as it was sometimes called. He fancied that he had felt himself in the penumbra of a very deep sadness when touching that slight and fragile creature. But wisdom lies in moderating mere impressions, and Gabriel endeavoured to think little of this.

text: The chamber within was illuminated solely by the ruddy glow of the kiln’s mouth, which flooded the floor with the level, sweeping brilliance of a sunset, projecting the shadows of every facial contour upward upon those gathered there. The stone tiles were hollowed into a track from the door to the fire, their entire surface worn into ripples. A bowed bench of raw, unpolished oak ran along one wall, and in a distant corner stood a simple bedstead, the personal berth of the maltster who so often rested there.
source_text: The room inside was lighted only by the ruddy glow from the kiln mouth, which shone over the floor with the streaming horizontality of the setting sun, and threw upwards the shadows of all facial irregularities in those assembled around. The stone-flag floor was worn into a path from the doorway to the kiln, and into undulations everywhere. A curved settle of unplaned oak stretched along one side, and in a remote corner was a small bed and bedstead, the owner and frequent occupier of which was the maltster.

text: Of course," agreed his son, a young man of sixty-five with a balding head and a solitary tooth in the upper left of his jaw. This tooth made quite a show of itself, standing out as conspicuously as a milestone on a grassy bank. "But Joe was the one who dealt with him most. Even so, my son William must have known the fellow before the rest of us—didn't you, Billy, before you moved away from Norcombe?
source_text: “Ay, sure,” said his son, a young man about sixty-five, with a semi-bald head and one tooth in the left centre of his upper jaw, which made much of itself by standing prominent, like a milestone in a bank. “But ’twas Joe had most to do with him. However, my son William must have knowed the very man afore us—didn’t ye, Billy, afore ye left Norcombe?”

text: Yes—just the other day, my youngest daughter Liddy and I were at my grandson’s christening," Billy went on. "We were speaking of this same family then. It was only this past Purification Day, shepherd—the time when the charity money is handed out to the deserving poor—and I remember it clearly because they all had to trudge up to the vestry together. Yes, this very man’s kin.
source_text: “Ay—the other day I and my youngest daughter, Liddy, were over at my grandson’s christening,” continued Billy. “We were talking about this very family, and ’twas only last Purification Day in this very world, when the use-money is gied away to the second-best poor folk, you know, shepherd, and I can mind the day because they all had to traypse up to the vestry—yes, this very man’s family.”

text: Jacob leaned down to pick up the "God-forgive-me," a towering, two-handled mug sitting amidst the hot ashes. The vessel was scorched and webbed with cracks from the fire, its outer surface layered with a rough buildup. This crust, especially thick in the deep curves of the handles where light hadn't touched for years, was a hardened mixture of spilled cider and soot baked solid by the heat. To any reasonable drinker, however, the mug was perfectly acceptable, given that the inside and the rim remained impeccably clean. This particular sort of vessel is called a "God-forgive-me" in Weatherbury and the neighboring parishes for reasons that remain vague; it is likely so named because its sheer volume makes any drinker feel a sense of shame upon seeing the bottom after draining it.
source_text: Jacob stooped to the God-forgive-me, which was a two-handled tall mug standing in the ashes, cracked and charred with heat: it was rather furred with extraneous matter about the outside, especially in the crevices of the handles, the innermost curves of which may not have seen daylight for several years by reason of this encrustation thereon—formed of ashes accidentally wetted with cider and baked hard; but to the mind of any sensible drinker the cup was no worse for that, being incontestably clean on the inside and about the rim. It may be observed that such a class of mug is called a God-forgive-me in Weatherbury and its vicinity for uncertain reasons; probably because its size makes any given toper feel ashamed of himself when he sees its bottom in drinking it empty.

text: When directed to check whether the spirits had reached the right heat, Jacob composedly used his finger as a thermometer, dipping it into the liquid. Once he judged it to be nearly ready, he picked up the vessel and politely tried to brush the ash from its underside with the hem of his linen smock, a gesture of hospitality intended for the newcomer, Shepherd Oak.
source_text: Jacob, on receiving the order to see if the liquor was warm enough, placidly dipped his forefinger into it by way of thermometer, and having pronounced it nearly of the proper degree, raised the cup and very civilly attempted to dust some of the ashes from the bottom with the skirt of his smock-frock, because Shepherd Oak was a stranger.

text: “Aye, I’ve sampled every manner of society. They hauled me to the Highfield Revels and into a boisterous, whirling pantomime, wherein ladies performed feats on horseback—perched atop the beasts in little more than their shifts; yet it failed to mend my spirits one whit. Following that, I served as a fetch-boy at the Ladies’ Ninepins Ground behind the Cooper’s Flagon in Shaston. It was a truly ungodly station, and a peculiar haunt for a pious fellow. I was forced to meet the gaze of the most profligate souls from dawn to dusk; nonetheless, it proved futile—the old affliction remained as stubborn as before. This sudden coloring has been a burden to my bloodline for centuries. Truly, it is a kind mercy of Heaven that I have fared no more poorly.”
source_text: “Oh ay, tried all sorts o’ company. They took me to Greenhill Fair, and into a great gay jerry-go-nimble show, where there were women-folk riding round—standing upon horses, with hardly anything on but their smocks; but it didn’t cure me a morsel. And then I was put errand-man at the Women’s Skittle Alley at the back of the Tailor’s Arms in Casterbridge. ’Twas a horrible sinful situation, and a very curious place for a good man. I had to stand and look ba’dy people in the face from morning till night; but ’twas no use—I was just as bad as ever after all. Blushes hev been in the family for generations. There, ’tis a happy providence that I be no worse.”

text: “And so he became completely turned around,” Coggan went on with a deadpan expression, implying that a proper story, like the turning tide, must follow its own path without interruption. “As he wandered through the woods in the dead of night, gripped by fear and unable to find any way out of the trees, he cried out, ‘A man is lost! A man is lost!’ An owl happened to be hooting ‘Whoo-whoo-whoo!’ from a nearby branch, as they do, shepherd” (Gabriel nodded), “and Joseph, trembling all over, answered, ‘Joseph Poorgrass, of Weatherbury, sir!’”
source_text: “—And so ’a lost himself quite,” continued Mr. Coggan, with an impassive face, implying that a true narrative, like time and tide, must run its course and would respect no man. “And as he was coming along in the middle of the night, much afeared, and not able to find his way out of the trees nohow, ’a cried out, ‘Man-a-lost! man-a-lost!’ A owl in a tree happened to be crying ‘Whoo-whoo-whoo!’ as owls do, you know, shepherd” (Gabriel nodded), “and Joseph, all in a tremble, said, ‘Joseph Poorgrass, of Weatherbury, sir!’”

text: “He is a man of keen parts, and it is a genuine solace to have such a guardian among us,” murmured Silas Mellows in a low, rhythmic drawl. “We should be brimming with gratitude that he does not peddle lewd verses instead of these honest strains; for the Creator might just as easily have fashioned the fellow as a profligate—a vessel of vice, to put it plainly—as the man he stands. Truly, for the sake of our wives and daughters, we must feel a profound sense of thanks.”
source_text: “He’s a clever man, and ’tis a true comfort for us to have such a shepherd,” murmured Joseph Poorgrass, in a soft cadence. “We ought to feel full o’ thanksgiving that he’s not a player of ba’dy songs instead of these merry tunes; for ’twould have been just as easy for God to have made the shepherd a loose low man—a man of iniquity, so to speak it—as what he is. Yes, for our wives’ and daughters’ sakes we should feel real thanksgiving.”

text: “Don’t play any more, shepherd,” said Susan Tall’s husband, the young man who had spoken once before. “I must be on my way, and while the music continues, I feel as though I am suspended by wires. If I were to leave and knew the tunes were still playing without me there to hear them, I would feel quite dejected.”
source_text: “Don’t ye play no more shepherd” said Susan Tall’s husband, the young married man who had spoken once before. “I must be moving and when there’s tunes going on I seem as if hung in wires. If I thought after I’d left that music was still playing, and I not there, I should be quite melancholy-like.”

text: “It was a case of theft, plain and simple. Word is that after Miss Everdene arrived home, she went back out to make sure everything was secure, as is her habit. On her way in, she caught Bailiff Pennyways sneaking down the granary steps with a half-bushel of barley. She lunged at him like a wildcat—there’s truly no girl as bold as she is—but this stays between us, of course?”
source_text: “Stealing it is. The news is, that after Miss Everdene got home she went out again to see all was safe, as she usually do, and coming in found Baily Pennyways creeping down the granary steps with half a bushel of barley. She fleed at him like a cat—never such a tomboy as she is—of course I speak with closed doors?”

text: He likewise considered how he might recover his few tools and volumes from the hamlet of Blackwood. *The Youth’s Reliable Guide*, *The Smith’s Essential Manual*, *A Treatise on Animal Ailments*, *Milton’s Great Epic*, *Bunyan’s Holy Journey*, *Defoe’s Island Tale*, *Johnson’s Lexicon*, and *Cocker’s Mathematics* formed the whole of his collection; and though it was a slender assembly, it was one from which he had gained more substantial wisdom through patient study than many a person of privilege has ever drawn from a mile of crowded shelves.
source_text: He also thought of plans for fetching his few utensils and books from Norcombe. _The Young Man’s Best Companion_, _The Farrier’s Sure Guide_, _The Veterinary Surgeon_, _Paradise Lost_, _The Pilgrim’s Progress_, _Robinson Crusoe_, Ash’s _Dictionary_, and Walkingame’s _Arithmetic_, constituted his library; and though a limited series, it was one from which he had acquired more sound information by diligent perusal than many a man of opportunities has done from a furlong of laden shelves.

text: Carved directly into the masonry, fluted pilasters graced the facade, while the chimneys rising above the roof were either paneled or cylindrical, their gables and finials still whispering of a Gothic ancestry. Velvety cushions of tawny moss blanketed the stone tiles, and clusters of houseleek sprouted from the eaves of the lower outbuildings nearby. A gravel path leading toward the road was so choked with silver-green moss at the edges that only a slender strip of the original brown stone remained exposed in the center. This neglect, coupled with the front’s somnolent air and the bustling activity on the opposite side, suggested that when the house was adapted for farming, its living essence had spun around within its shell to face the other way. Such inversions and strange stagnations are often the scars left by commerce on buildings—or indeed entire streets—that were first conceived for nothing but leisure.
source_text: Fluted pilasters, worked from the solid stone, decorated its front, and above the roof the chimneys were panelled or columnar, some coped gables with finials and like features still retaining traces of their Gothic extraction. Soft brown mosses, like faded velveteen, formed cushions upon the stone tiling, and tufts of the houseleek or sengreen sprouted from the eaves of the low surrounding buildings. A gravel walk leading from the door to the road in front was encrusted at the sides with more moss—here it was a silver-green variety, the nut-brown of the gravel being visible to the width of only a foot or two in the centre. This circumstance, and the generally sleepy air of the whole prospect here, together with the animated and contrasting state of the reverse façade, suggested to the imagination that on the adaptation of the building for farming purposes the vital principle of the house had turned round inside its body to face the other way. Reversals of this kind, strange deformities, tremendous paralyses, are often seen to be inflicted by trade upon edifices—either individual or in the aggregate as streets and towns—which were originally planned for pleasure alone.

text: Inside the room where the voices originated, Bathsheba and her companion-servant, Liddy Smallbury, could be found seated on the floor. They were sorting through a chaotic jumble of documents, books, vials, and discarded items—the leftover belongings of the former resident. Liddy, the great-granddaughter of the maltster, was nearly the same age as Bathsheba, her face a striking portrait of a spirited English country girl. Any lack of refinement in her features was easily overlooked because of her magnificent complexion; in the winter air, her full, round face held a gentle flush similar to those seen in the works of Terburg or Gerard Douw. Like the figures in those old Dutch paintings, her beauty was grounded and pleasant, stopping well short of the sublime. Though she was resilient, she wasn't as adventurous as Bathsheba, and she occasionally exhibited a seriousness that was equal parts genuine sentiment and a dutiful sense of decorum.
source_text: In the room from which the conversation proceeded Bathsheba and her servant-companion, Liddy Smallbury, were to be discovered sitting upon the floor, and sorting a complication of papers, books, bottles, and rubbish spread out thereon—remnants from the household stores of the late occupier. Liddy, the maltster’s great-granddaughter, was about Bathsheba’s equal in age, and her face was a prominent advertisement of the light-hearted English country girl. The beauty her features might have lacked in form was amply made up for by perfection of hue, which at this winter-time was the softened ruddiness on a surface of high rotundity that we meet with in a Terburg or a Gerard Douw; and, like the presentations of those great colourists, it was a face which kept well back from the boundary between comeliness and the ideal. Though elastic in nature she was less daring than Bathsheba, and occasionally showed some earnestness, which consisted half of genuine feeling, and half of mannerliness superadded by way of duty.

text: At that moment, a young boy approached and peered in at them. He was one of the Coggans, a family that, along with the Smallburys, was as frequent a name in the district as the Avons and Derwents are among English rivers. The lad was never without a loose tooth or a gashed finger to exhibit to his favored acquaintances, a display he performed with the pride of one elevated above the unblemished masses. In response to these minor calamities, onlookers were expected to exclaim, "Poor child!" with a note of congratulation mingled with their sympathy.
source_text: A little boy came up at this moment and looked in upon them. This child was one of the Coggans, who, with the Smallburys, were as common among the families of this district as the Avons and Derwents among our rivers. He always had a loosened tooth or a cut finger to show to particular friends, which he did with an air of being thereby elevated above the common herd of afflictionless humanity—to which exhibition people were expected to say “Poor child!” with a dash of congratulation as well as pity.

text: “Attend now: your governance has passed from a master’s hand to a mistress’s. My own capacity for husbandry is as yet unproven, even to myself; nevertheless, I mean to apply my full spirit to the task, and if your loyalty is true, you will find my own devotion to your welfare equally certain. Let no one among you—should any be so inclined, though I trust you are not—presume that my being a woman renders me incapable of discerning the line between upright conduct and shiftless knavery.”
source_text: “Now mind, you have a mistress instead of a master. I don’t yet know my powers or my talents in farming; but I shall do my best, and if you serve me well, so shall I serve you. Don’t any unfair ones among you (if there are any such, but I hope not) suppose that because I’m a woman I don’t understand the difference between bad goings-on and good.”

text: It was a night when sorrow might settle upon even the most radiant spirit without seeming at all out of place. For those of a sensitive nature, love transformed into a restless concern, optimism ebbed into apprehension, and firm belief faded into a mere longing. In such moments, the act of remembering did not provoke any bitterness over missed paths to greatness, nor did the thought of what lay ahead stir the soul toward any new or ambitious endeavor.
source_text: It was a night when sorrow may come to the brightest without causing any great sense of incongruity: when, with impressible persons, love becomes solicitousness, hope sinks to misgiving, and faith to hope: when the exercise of memory does not stir feelings of regret at opportunities for ambition that have been passed by, and anticipation does not prompt to enterprise.

text: By daylight, the river would have appeared as one of those deep, tranquil currents that moves with a uniform, gliding speed across its entire width, any fluctuation in pace being swiftly resolved by a small eddy. The signal met no response other than the liquid bubbling of one of these unseen vortexes—along with a few faint noises that a melancholic man might have called weeping, and a joyful man mirth—created by the water lapping against small obstructions elsewhere in the stream.
source_text: The river would have been seen by day to be of that deep smooth sort which races middle and sides with the same gliding precision, any irregularities of speed being immediately corrected by a small whirlpool. Nothing was heard in reply to the signal but the gurgle and cluck of one of these invisible wheels—together with a few small sounds which a sad man would have called moans, and a happy man laughter—caused by the flapping of the waters against trifling objects in other parts of the stream.

text: The specific curve of her perfect upper teeth and the sharply defined corners of her crimson mouth, visible as she tilted her head back with a defiant air to debate a tall man, hinted at a latent power. It suggested that this lithe young woman possessed a formidable capacity for romantic daring and the audacity to act upon it. Yet her eyes were marked by an unfailing softness. Had they been a lighter hue, this quality might have seemed like a hazy blur; because they were dark, however, it merely tempered what could have been a sharp, penetrating gaze into one of lucid transparency.
source_text: Something in the exact arch of her upper unbroken row of teeth, and in the keenly pointed corners of her red mouth when, with parted lips, she somewhat defiantly turned up her face to argue a point with a tall man, suggested that there was potentiality enough in that lithe slip of humanity for alarming exploits of sex, and daring enough to carry them out. But her eyes had a softness—invariably a softness—which, had they not been dark, would have seemed mistiness; as they were, it lowered an expression that might have been piercing to simple clearness.

text: Surprisingly for a lady of such blooming vigor, she consistently waited for a companion to finish their sentence before she would interpose her own. When the talk turned to trade, she defended her prices with a dealer’s resolve, yet sought to diminish those of her rivals with a persistence common to her sex. Nevertheless, there was a suppleness in her conviction that saved it from being called willful, and a certain guilelessness in her parsimony that ensured it never tasted of spite.
source_text: Strange to say of a woman in full bloom and vigor, she always allowed her interlocutors to finish their statements before rejoining with hers. In arguing on prices, she held to her own firmly, as was natural in a dealer, and reduced theirs persistently, as was inevitable in a woman. But there was an elasticity in her firmness which removed it from obstinacy, as there was a _naïveté_ in her cheapening which saved it from meanness.

text: One might say that married men in their forties are typically eager to cast a wandering eye toward any passably pretty woman they happen to pass. Much like people playing cards just for fun, the awareness that they are safe from the final penalty—the requirement to pay—makes them unusually bold in their observations. Bathsheba was convinced that this stoic individual was not a married man.
source_text: It may be said that married men of forty are usually ready and generous enough to fling passing glances at any specimen of moderate beauty they may discern by the way. Probably, as with persons playing whist for love, the consciousness of a certain immunity under any circumstances from that worst possible ultimate, the having to pay, makes them unduly speculative. Bathsheba was convinced that this unmoved person was not a married man.

text: The volume was cast open, its yellowed pages frayed at the most popular passages where the fingers of clumsy readers had once traced the lines to steady their gaze. Bathsheba hunted for a specific verse in the Book of Ruth until the solemn, sacred words caught her eye. They provoked in her a mixture of nervous excitement and shame; it was a moment where abstract Wisdom stood face-to-face with Folly in the flesh. This living Folly blushed, yet she remained firm in her resolve and rested the key upon the page. A rust-colored mark directly over the verse, left by the pressure of some iron object long ago, betrayed the fact that this was not the first time the old Bible had been employed for such a rite.
source_text: The book was opened—the leaves, drab with age, being quite worn away at much-read verses by the forefingers of unpractised readers in former days, where they were moved along under the line as an aid to the vision. The special verse in the Book of Ruth was sought out by Bathsheba, and the sublime words met her eye. They slightly thrilled and abashed her. It was Wisdom in the abstract facing Folly in the concrete. Folly in the concrete blushed, persisted in her intention, and placed the key on the book. A rusty patch immediately upon the verse, caused by previous pressure of an iron substance thereon, told that this was not the first time the old volume had been used for the purpose.

text: As twilight deepened on the evening of the fourteenth of February, Harrison took his evening meal by the glow of ancient, crackling logs. Upon the shelf above him rested a chronometer topped by a gilded hawk, and within the bird’s outstretched talons lay the missive Elena had delivered. To this spot the solitary man’s vision was perpetually drawn, until the thick crimson wax seemed a sanguine stain upon his very pupils; and as he consumed his food, he traced the hidden sentences in his mind’s eye, though the parchment itself sat far beyond the clarity of his physical gaze.
source_text: At dusk, on the evening of St. Valentine’s Day, Boldwood sat down to supper as usual, by a beaming fire of aged logs. Upon the mantel-shelf before him was a time-piece, surmounted by a spread eagle, and upon the eagle’s wings was the letter Bathsheba had sent. Here the bachelor’s gaze was continually fastening itself, till the large red seal became as a blot of blood on the retina of his eye; and as he ate and drank he still read in fancy the words thereon, although they were too remote for his sight—

text: “She will regret this—most certainly!” he declared. “Benjy Pennyways was neither an honorable man nor a faithful manager—a traitor as treacherous as Judas Iscariot himself. But the idea that she thinks she can manage everything on her own!” He fell silent, letting his head sway slowly from side to side several times. “In all my long years, I have never seen the like—never!”
source_text: “She’ll rue it—surely, surely!” he said. “Benjy Pennyways were not a true man or an honest baily—as big a betrayer as Judas Iscariot himself. But to think she can carr’ on alone!” He allowed his head to swing laterally three or four times in silence. “Never in all my creeping up—never!”

text: “The elders have been hauling her through the briars for her haughty ways,” remarked Julian Vance; “but to my mind, she should be given her head. Lord love her comely features—what wouldn't I give for a pressing of those scarlet lips!” The audacious Vance then emitted a distinct and recognizable smack of his own.
source_text: “These middle-aged men have been pulling her over the coals for pride and vanity,” said Mark Clark; “but I say, let her have rope enough. Bless her pretty face—shouldn’t I like to do so—upon her cherry lips!” The gallant Mark Clark here made a peculiar and well known sound with his own.

text: “It’s come to our ears that you’re a fellow of remarkable sense and talent, drover,” observed Silas Mudge, his voice quivering with a distinct unease from his refuge behind the cooper’s heavy bed-post. “To be gifted with such a sharp brain is a rare comfort, to be sure,” he continued, twitching with a restlessness born of a troubled spirit rather than any physical need. “We could only hope for a portion of such brilliance ourselves, couldn’t we, friends?”
source_text: “We hear that ye be a extraordinary good and clever man, shepherd,” said Joseph Poorgrass with considerable anxiety from behind the maltster’s bedstead, whither he had retired for safety. “’Tis a great thing to be clever, I’m sure,” he added, making movements associated with states of mind rather than body; “we wish we were, don’t we, neighbours?”

text: “Oh, Arthur—I have been so foolish! I was certain the stone church with the narrow spire was St. Jude’s, and I arrived at the portal precisely at half-past eleven, just as you instructed. I stayed until fifteen minutes before midday, only then discovering my error: I was at St. Mark’s. But I felt no great alarm, believing that tomorrow would serve our purpose just as well.”
source_text: “Oh, Frank—I made a mistake!—I thought that church with the spire was All Saints’, and I was at the door at half-past eleven to a minute as you said. I waited till a quarter to twelve, and found then that I was in All Souls’. But I wasn’t much frightened, for I thought it could be to-morrow as well.”

text: Physical causes and emotional consequences do not follow a predictable ratio. The psychological fallout from a mental impulse can be massive, even when the initial spark is laughably small. When women are in a capricious mood, their typical insight—whether through negligence or an innate blind spot—seems to fail them in this regard. Consequently, Bathsheba was destined to be utterly blindsided today.
source_text: Material causes and emotional effects are not to be arranged in regular equation. The result from capital employed in the production of any movement of a mental nature is sometimes as tremendous as the cause itself is absurdly minute. When women are in a freakish mood, their usual intuition, either from carelessness or inherent defect, seemingly fails to teach them this, and hence it was that Bathsheba was fated to be astonished to-day.

text: Boldwood watched her—not with cunning, judgment, or insight, but with a vacant stare, much like a harvester pausing to watch a steam engine rush past—as a thing entirely outside his world and only vaguely grasped. To him, women were distant curiosities rather than essential partners—celestial bodies of such vague form, motion, and endurance that he had never felt compelled to wonder if their paths were governed by the same rigid principles as his own, or if they were as truly chaotic as they appeared on the surface.
source_text: Boldwood looked at her—not slily, critically, or understandingly, but blankly at gaze, in the way a reaper looks up at a passing train—as something foreign to his element, and but dimly understood. To Boldwood women had been remote phenomena rather than necessary complements—comets of such uncertain aspect, movement, and permanence, that whether their orbits were as geometrical, unchangeable, and as subject to laws as his own, or as absolutely erratic as they superficially appeared, he had not deemed it his duty to consider.

text: He observed her ink-dark tresses, the precise geometry of her profile, and the soft, ivory swell of her throat. He marked then the fold of her lids, the dark glint of her eyes behind their lashes, and the intricate shell of her ear. Finally, he took in the grace of her silhouette, the heavy drape of her gown, and the very soles of her shoes.
source_text: He saw her black hair, her correct facial curves and profile, and the roundness of her chin and throat. He saw then the side of her eyelids, eyes, and lashes, and the shape of her ear. Next he noticed her figure, her skirt, and the very soles of her shoes.

text: This captivating woman had essentially proposed that he marry her. What could have driven her to such an unusual act? Boldwood’s inability to distinguish between merely accepting a presented opportunity and actively creating a new one was perfectly matched by Bathsheba’s own failure to recognize how monumental consequences can grow from the most trivial beginnings.
source_text: And this charming woman had in effect said to him, “Marry me.” Why should she have done that strange thing? Boldwood’s blindness to the difference between approving of what circumstances suggest, and originating what they do not suggest, was well matched by Bathsheba’s insensibility to the possibly great issues of little beginnings.

text: At that instant, Elara was dispassionately negotiating with a spirited young yeoman, tallying figures with an apathy that suggested his handsome features were nothing more than columns in a record book. It appeared certain that a temperament so flamboyant held little charm for a woman of her particular sensibilities. Yet Arthur felt a searing heat creep even to his fingertips, a budding resentment that forced him across the grim threshold of the slighted suitor’s torment. A sudden urge took hold of him to stride forward and sever their proximity. Such an intrusion was possible through a single pretext: a request to inspect her store of grain. Arthur quickly abandoned the thought. He found the demand impossible to utter; to require such beauty to engage in the commonalities of trade felt like a desecration, clashing violently with the exalted image he held of her.
source_text: She was at this moment coolly dealing with a dashing young farmer, adding up accounts with him as indifferently as if his face had been the pages of a ledger. It was evident that such a nature as his had no attraction for a woman of Bathsheba’s taste. But Boldwood grew hot down to his hands with an incipient jealousy; he trod for the first time the threshold of “the injured lover’s hell.” His first impulse was to go and thrust himself between them. This could be done, but only in one way—by asking to see a sample of her corn. Boldwood renounced the idea. He could not make the request; it was debasing loveliness to ask it to buy and sell, and jarred with his conceptions of her.

text: Bathsheba was acutely aware that she had finally breached his stoic defenses. She could feel his gaze following her every move, never letting her out of his sight. It was a victory, and had it happened naturally, the long wait would have only made the success more satisfying. Yet, because it was the result of a calculated trick, she valued it no more than she would a silk flower or a piece of wax fruit.
source_text: All this time Bathsheba was conscious of having broken into that dignified stronghold at last. His eyes, she knew, were following her everywhere. This was a triumph; and had it come naturally, such a triumph would have been the sweeter to her for this piquing delay. But it had been brought about by misdirected ingenuity, and she valued it only as she valued an artificial flower or a wax fruit.

text: Boldwood held the lease for the estate known as Little Weatherbury Farm, standing as the closest thing to an aristocrat this secluded corner of the parish could offer. Refined outsiders, whose lives centered on the city and who found themselves stranded in this rural pocket for a day, would catch the sound of spinning wheels and hope for a glimpse of the elite—a solitary lord, or at minimum, a country squire. However, it was never more than Mr. Boldwood departing for the morning. When the sound of wheels returned later, their expectations would rise once again, only to find it was merely Mr. Boldwood arriving home.
source_text: Boldwood was tenant of what was called Little Weatherbury Farm, and his person was the nearest approach to aristocracy that this remoter quarter of the parish could boast of. Genteel strangers, whose god was their town, who might happen to be compelled to linger about this nook for a day, heard the sound of light wheels, and prayed to see good society, to the degree of a solitary lord, or squire at the very least, but it was only Mr. Boldwood going out for the day. They heard the sound of wheels yet once more, and were re-animated to expectancy: it was only Mr. Boldwood coming home again.

text: Treading a rhythmic path behind the shifting forms of his livestock was Master Thorne. This barn was his sanctuary and his cell combined; here, once he had seen to the nourishment of his mute charges, the lonely man would pace and ponder into the twilight hours, remaining until the lunar glow filtered through the grime-streaked panes, or until a thick, impenetrable shadow claimed the room.
source_text: Pacing up and down at the heels of the animals was Farmer Boldwood himself. This place was his almonry and cloister in one: here, after looking to the feeding of his four-footed dependants, the celibate would walk and meditate of an evening till the moon’s rays streamed in through the cobwebbed windows, or total darkness enveloped the scene.

text: The events of Boldwood’s life were common, yet his spirit was far from typical. That stillness, which observers noted above all else and mistook for simple apathy, was actually the precarious balance of massive, conflicting internal forces. Once this equilibrium was shaken, he was instantly pushed to the edge. He was a man of extremes; a passion either governed him entirely or remained utterly hidden. His temperament was never moderate, but either stagnant or a torrent. He was always struck mortally, or he was missed altogether.
source_text: The phases of Boldwood’s life were ordinary enough, but his was not an ordinary nature. That stillness, which struck casual observers more than anything else in his character and habit, and seemed so precisely like the rest of inanition, may have been the perfect balance of enormous antagonistic forces—positives and negatives in fine adjustment. His equilibrium disturbed, he was in extremity at once. If an emotion possessed him at all, it ruled him; a feeling not mastering him was entirely latent. Stagnant or rapid, it was never slow. He was always hit mortally, or he was missed.

text: As Elara’s silhouette met the gaze of the landholder, it cast a radiance over him akin to the lunar glow striking a lonely spire. A man’s physical frame serves as the vessel, or perhaps the parchment, of his inner spirit, betraying whether he is guarded or candid, abundant or restrained. Sterling’s countenance had undergone a transformation from its habitual austerity; his features now confessed that he had ventured beyond his fortifications for the first time, gripped by a terrifying awareness of his own nakedness. Such is the common fate of formidable temperaments when they succumb to affection.
source_text: When Bathsheba’s figure shone upon the farmer’s eyes it lighted him up as the moon lights up a great tower. A man’s body is as the shell, or the tablet, of his soul, as he is reserved or ingenuous, overflowing or self-contained. There was a change in Boldwood’s exterior from its former impassibleness; and his face showed that he was now living outside his defences for the first time, and with a fearful sense of exposure. It is the usual experience of strong natures when they love.

text: Elara lifted her eyes as the movement came to a rest, spying the stockman near the threshold where he stood beneath the heavy boughs of a flowering rowan. To Elias, her features held the mercurial radiance of a morning in May; he remained a vigilant scholar of their every fluctuation and noted at once the shadow of a foreign intrusion, manifested in a sudden, vivid flush of embarrassment. Casting his eyes behind him, he likewise discovered Sterling.
source_text: Bathsheba looked up at the completion of the manœuvre and saw the farmer by the gate, where he was overhung by a willow tree in full bloom. Gabriel, to whom her face was as the uncertain glory of an April day, was ever regardful of its faintest changes, and instantly discerned thereon the mark of some influence from without, in the form of a keenly self-conscious reddening. He also turned and beheld Boldwood.

text: She shivered, turned, and murmured a greeting. His voice was so completely different from the start she had imagined. It was a quality of profound, hushed stillness—an emphasis on deep significance that remained barely articulated. Silence sometimes possesses a striking ability to manifest as the naked spirit of emotion, wandering free from its physical shell, and it is then more powerful than any spoken word. In this manner, brevity often reveals more than an abundance of speech. Boldwood had conveyed everything in that single greeting.
source_text: She trembled, turned, and said “Good morning.” His tone was so utterly removed from all she had expected as a beginning. It was lowness and quiet accentuated: an emphasis of deep meanings, their form, at the same time, being scarcely expressed. Silence has sometimes a remarkable power of showing itself as the disembodied soul of feeling wandering without its carcase, and it is then more impressive than speech. In the same way, to say a little is often to tell more than to say a great deal. Boldwood told everything in that word.

text: “I have attained my forty-second year,” he proceeded. “Perhaps I was rightly deemed an unyielding solitary; certainly, I considered myself such. During my younger days, I never envisioned myself bound by domestic ties, nor have I entertained such prospects as I grew older. Yet none of us remain static, and my own transformation was born entirely of your influence. Lately, I have felt with increasing weight that my current existence is profoundly lacking in every sense. Above all else, I long to call you my wife.”
source_text: “I am now forty-one years old,” he went on. “I may have been called a confirmed bachelor, and I was a confirmed bachelor. I had never any views of myself as a husband in my earlier days, nor have I made any calculation on the subject since I have been older. But we all change, and my change, in this matter, came with seeing you. I have felt lately, more and more, that my present way of living is bad in every respect. Beyond all things, I want you as my wife.”

text: Cainy Ball cranked the handle of Gabriel’s grindstone, his head bobbing in a somber, rhythmic motion with every revolution of the wheel. Oak stood much like a figure of Eros honing his arrows: his frame was slightly bowed, his weight pressed firmly against the shears, and his head was tilted to one side, with a focused compression of the lips and a squint of the eyes that defined his concentration.
source_text: Cainy Ball turned the handle of Gabriel’s grindstone, his head performing a melancholy see-saw up and down with each turn of the wheel. Oak stood somewhat as Eros is represented when in the act of sharpening his arrows: his figure slightly bent, the weight of his body thrown over on the shears, and his head balanced side-ways, with a critical compression of the lips and contraction of the eyelids to crown the attitude.

text: “I imagine it is so,” Elias remarked with a hollow sharpness, never ceasing the rhythmic revolution of the handle, his voice undulating in a measured surge and retreat as he bent and rose with the mechanism. The sound was channeled by his posture, cast at one moment deep into the loam beneath his feet and at the next sent drifting levelly across the orchard, while his vision remained fixed upon a solitary withered leaf resting in the dirt.
source_text: “I suppose not,” said Gabriel bitterly, and going on with his turning, his words rising and falling in a regular swell and cadence as he stooped or rose with the winch, which directed them, according to his position, perpendicularly into the earth, or horizontally along the garden, his eyes being fixed on a leaf upon the ground.

text: It could be said of this barn—a claim rarely applicable to the church or the castle of similar age and architecture—that the intent behind its initial construction was the very same purpose it served today. In a way that surpassed those other two medieval relics, the old barn represented a way of life that had remained untouched by the erosions of time. Here, the vision of the ancient craftsmen was in complete harmony with the outlook of the modern observer. Gazing at the weathered structure, one noted its current utility while reflecting on its heritage, feeling a profound satisfaction in such unbroken continuity—a sense of gratitude and pride in the enduring concept that had piled these stones together. The fact that four centuries had not exposed it as a mistake, nor provoked any animosity toward its function, nor incited any upheaval to destroy it, gave this simple gray monument of past minds a sense of peace, if not a certain dignity, that was often undermined by critical scrutiny in its religious and military equivalents. For once, the medieval and the modern occupied common ground. Its narrow windows, the worn arches and stonework, its orientation, and the faded chestnut beams of the roof did not reference a dead art of fortification or a forgotten religious doctrine. The protection and preservation of the body through the provision of food remains a science, a faith, and a constant human drive.
source_text: One could say about this barn, what could hardly be said of either the church or the castle, akin to it in age and style, that the purpose which had dictated its original erection was the same with that to which it was still applied. Unlike and superior to either of those two typical remnants of mediævalism, the old barn embodied practices which had suffered no mutilation at the hands of time. Here at least the spirit of the ancient builders was at one with the spirit of the modern beholder. Standing before this abraded pile, the eye regarded its present usage, the mind dwelt upon its past history, with a satisfied sense of functional continuity throughout—a feeling almost of gratitude, and quite of pride, at the permanence of the idea which had heaped it up. The fact that four centuries had neither proved it to be founded on a mistake, inspired any hatred of its purpose, nor given rise to any reaction that had battered it down, invested this simple grey effort of old minds with a repose, if not a grandeur, which a too curious reflection was apt to disturb in its ecclesiastical and military compeers. For once mediævalism and modernism had a common stand-point. The lanceolate windows, the time-eaten archstones and chamfers, the orientation of the axis, the misty chestnut work of the rafters, referred to no exploded fortifying art or worn-out religious creed. The defence and salvation of the body by daily bread is still a study, a religion, and a desire.

text: Thus the tide of talk was entirely hers. There is a garrulousness that conveys no substance, which characterized Elara; and there is a wordlessness that resonates with meaning, which belonged to Silas. Immersed in this soft and measured happiness, he turned the creature over onto her other flank, securing her head with his knee while he drove the clippers in rhythmic strokes about her neck; then over the side and along the ridge of her back, ending finally at the tail.
source_text: So the chatter was all on her side. There is a loquacity that tells nothing, which was Bathsheba’s; and there is a silence which says much: that was Gabriel’s. Full of this dim and temperate bliss, he went on to fling the ewe over upon her other side, covering her head with his knee, gradually running the shears line after line round her dewlap; thence about her flank and back, and finishing over the tail.

text: Yet the austerity of fortune would not suffer Silas’s morning contentment to remain whole. The tups, the matronly ewes, and the twice-shorn stock had already been denuded of their wool, and the hands were just commencing upon the yearlings and wethers, when Thorne’s fancy that Elara might remain to witness his skill through a further labor was sharply broken by the manifestation of Master Sterling in the barn's remotest alcove. No eye had marked his approach, yet his presence was now an established fact. Sterling moved within a singular aura of solemnity that cast a shadow over all in his vicinity; and the conversation, which Elara’s arrival had merely tempered, was now utterly extinguished.
source_text: But heartless circumstance could not leave entire Gabriel’s happiness of this morning. The rams, old ewes, and two-shear ewes had duly undergone their stripping, and the men were proceeding with the shearlings and hogs, when Oak’s belief that she was going to stand pleasantly by and time him through another performance was painfully interrupted by Farmer Boldwood’s appearance in the extremest corner of the barn. Nobody seemed to have perceived his entry, but there he certainly was. Boldwood always carried with him a social atmosphere of his own, which everybody felt who came near him; and the talk, which Bathsheba’s presence had somewhat suppressed, was now totally suspended.

text: As is typical for those with strong personalities, Bathsheba constantly drew the ire of men like Henery Fray. Her most glaring flaw was being far too vocal about her disagreements and not nearly expressive enough regarding her approvals. Just as an object’s color is defined by the light it reflects rather than what it absorbs, a person’s identity is shaped by their hostilities and rejections, while their benevolence is often dismissed as no real trait at all.
source_text: As usual with decided characters, Bathsheba invariably provoked the criticism of individuals like Henery Fray. Her emblazoned fault was to be too pronounced in her objections, and not sufficiently overt in her likings. We learn that it is not the rays which bodies absorb, but those which they reject, that give them the colours they are known by; and in the same way people are specialized by their dislikes and antagonisms, whilst their goodwill is looked upon as no attribute at all.

text: To celebrate the shearing, a long table was arranged on the lawn beside the house, with one end pushed over the ledge of the wide parlor window and a few feet into the room. Miss Everdene sat just inside the window, facing the length of the table. This positioned her at the head of the gathering without requiring her to sit directly among the men.
source_text: For the shearing-supper a long table was placed on the grass-plot beside the house, the end of the table being thrust over the sill of the wide parlour window and a foot or two into the room. Miss Everdene sat inside the window, facing down the table. She was thus at the head without mingling with the men.

text: The country squire was arrayed in a spirited fashion, his crisp frock-coat and snowy waistcoat presenting a vivid departure from his habitual somber tweeds. An inner radiance matched his outward display, manifesting in a rare and buoyant talkativeness. This lightheartedness was mirrored by Elara upon his approach, though the unwelcome sight of Miller—the overseer discharged for his dishonest dealings—momentarily unsettled her peace of mind.
source_text: The gentleman-farmer was dressed in cheerful style, in a new coat and white waistcoat, quite contrasting with his usual sober suits of grey. Inwardy, too, he was blithe, and consequently chatty to an exceptional degree. So also was Bathsheba now that he had come, though the uninvited presence of Pennyways, the bailiff who had been dismissed for theft, disturbed her equanimity for a while.

text: During the performance, young Bob Coggan fell prey to one of those peculiar impulses that seize children whenever their elders are being especially solemn. In an effort to suppress his laughter, he stuffed a wad of the tablecloth into his mouth; however, after remaining tightly bottled up for a moment, his amusement finally erupted through his nostrils. Joseph noticed the disruption and, his face flushing with sudden resentment, stopped singing at once. Coggan promptly boxed Bob’s ears.
source_text: But during this rendering young Bob Coggan exhibited one of those anomalies which will afflict little people when other persons are particularly serious: in trying to check his laughter, he pushed down his throat as much of the tablecloth as he could get hold of, when, after continuing hermetically sealed for a short time, his mirth burst out through his nose. Joseph perceived it, and with hectic cheeks of indignation instantly ceased singing. Coggan boxed Bob’s ears immediately.

text: The sun sank into a thick, amber mist; yet they remained, talking on until they were as joyous as the gods of Homeric myth. Bathsheba kept her seat by the window, busy with her knitting, though she would look up every so often to observe the darkening world outside. The slow-creeping twilight deepened and eventually swallowed them whole before any move was made to depart.
source_text: The sun went down in an ochreous mist; but they sat, and talked on, and grew as merry as the gods in Homer’s heaven. Bathsheba still remained enthroned inside the window, and occupied herself in knitting, from which she sometimes looked up to view the fading scene outside. The slow twilight expanded and enveloped them completely before the signs of moving were shown.

text: “I’ll grant this much to Higgins,” added Thatcher, “that whenever he truly resolves to perform a deed of high character—as I could tell by his look before he sat to his cup—he is generally able to see it through. I am proud to announce to the parish, friends, that he has pilfered nothing at all.”
source_text: “Well, I’ll say this for Pennyways,” added Coggan, “that whenever he do really make up his mind to do a noble thing in the shape of a good action, as I could see by his face he did to-night afore sitting down, he’s generally able to carry it out. Yes, I’m proud to say, neighbours, that he’s stole nothing at all.”

text: I will strive to care for you," she uttered, her voice wavering with a vulnerability that stood in stark contrast to her usual poise. "If I can find any reason to believe that I might be a worthy wife to you, then I shall truly be prepared to marry. However, Mr. Boldwood, a certain degree of caution regarding such a monumental step is only right for any woman, and I am not ready to offer a formal commitment tonight. I would prefer to ask for your patience for a few weeks more, until I can better understand my own heart and circumstances.
source_text: “I will try to love you,” she was saying, in a trembling voice quite unlike her usual self-confidence. “And if I can believe in any way that I shall make you a good wife I shall indeed be willing to marry you. But, Mr. Boldwood, hesitation on so high a matter is honourable in any woman, and I don’t want to give a solemn promise to-night. I would rather ask you to wait a few weeks till I can see my situation better.

text: I possess every confidence that once these forty days have elapsed—the period you mean to remain absent until the grain is gathered—I may finally vow to become your bride," she stated with unwavering poise. "However, mark my words well: I have not yet given you my oath.
source_text: “I have every reason to hope that at the end of the five or six weeks, between this time and harvest, that you say you are going to be away from home, I shall be able to promise to be your wife,” she said, firmly. “But remember this distinctly, I don’t promise yet.”

text: Of the many responsibilities Bathsheba had assumed by choosing to manage the estate without a bailiff, one particular duty was her nightly walk around the farmstead to ensure everything was secure before bed. Gabriel almost invariably anticipated her in this routine, patrolling the grounds with the vigilance of a dedicated sentry. However, his quiet protection was mostly hidden from his mistress, and the small portion she did notice was accepted with little appreciation. It seems women often decry the flightiness of men in love, yet they frequently meet true constancy with nothing but indifference.
source_text: Among the multifarious duties which Bathsheba had voluntarily imposed upon herself by dispensing with the services of a bailiff, was the particular one of looking round the homestead before going to bed, to see that all was right and safe for the night. Gabriel had almost constantly preceded her in this tour every evening, watching her affairs as carefully as any specially appointed officer of surveillance could have done; but this tender devotion was to a great extent unknown to his mistress, and as much as was known was somewhat thanklessly received. Women are never tired of bewailing man’s fickleness in love, but they only seem to snub his constancy.

text: Her route back to the residence led through a youthful grove of slender firs, established some years prior to shield the property from the northern winds. Because the overhead branches were so densely interlaced, the path remained somber even under a cloudless midday sun, felt like twilight by evening, turned as pitch as midnight at dusk, and became as profoundly dark as the ninth plague of Egypt at the deep of night. The setting was essentially a vast, low-roofed natural chamber, where a feathery ceiling was sustained by thin pillars of living timber, and the ground was layered with a soft, brownish carpet of fallen needles and weathered cones, interspersed with the occasional blade of grass.
source_text: Her way back to the house was by a path through a young plantation of tapering firs, which had been planted some years earlier to shelter the premises from the north wind. By reason of the density of the interwoven foliage overhead, it was gloomy there at cloudless noontide, twilight in the evening, dark as midnight at dusk, and black as the ninth plague of Egypt at midnight. To describe the spot is to call it a vast, low, naturally formed hall, the plumy ceiling of which was supported by slender pillars of living wood, the floor being covered with a soft dun carpet of dead spikelets and mildewed cones, with a tuft of grass-blades here and there.

text: This stretch of the lane always proved the pivot of her evening’s walk, though her earlier premonitions of hazard had been too dim to persuade her to bring an escort. Stealing along with the noiseless persistence of a ghost, Elara imagined she caught the sound of a gait entering the passage from the opposing direction. It was, without question, the scrape of a shoe. Her own steps instantly became as hushed as falling petals. She comforted herself with the thought that the way was a common thoroughfare and the wayfarer likely a neighbor bound for home; yet she felt a pang of regret that the encounter was to take place in the blackest recess of the wood, despite its proximity to her own gate.
source_text: This bit of the path was always the crux of the night’s ramble, though, before starting, her apprehensions of danger were not vivid enough to lead her to take a companion. Slipping along here covertly as Time, Bathsheba fancied she could hear footsteps entering the track at the opposite end. It was certainly a rustle of footsteps. Her own instantly fell as gently as snowflakes. She reassured herself by a remembrance that the path was public, and that the traveller was probably some villager returning home; regretting, at the same time, that the meeting should be about to occur in the darkest point of her route, even though only just outside her own door.

text: He also bent down, and the lantern sitting on the earth between them cast a beam from its open side, illuminating the fir needles and the tall, wet grass like a giant glowworm. The light shone upward into their countenances and threw massive shadows of the man and woman across the woods. These dark shapes twisted and broke against the rough bark of the trees until they finally dissolved into nothingness.
source_text: He too stooped, and the lantern standing on the ground betwixt them threw the gleam from its open side among the fir-tree needles and the blades of long damp grass with the effect of a large glowworm. It radiated upwards into their faces, and sent over half the plantation gigantic shadows of both man and woman, each dusky shape becoming distorted and mangled upon the tree-trunks till it wasted to nothing.

text: “Of course, miss; I am not made of stone.” He followed this with a sigh that was as full of playfulness as a sigh could be without becoming something else entirely. “I am grateful for beauty, even when it is tossed to me like a bone to a dog. These moments will be over far too soon!”
source_text: “Certainly, miss; I am not of steel.” He added a sigh which had as much archness in it as a sigh could possess without losing its nature altogether. “I am thankful for beauty, even when ’tis thrown to me like a bone to a dog. These moments will be over too soon!”

text: “Oh, miss—I’m almost embarrassed to say it—he’s a real playboy! But I know him to be sharp and refined, the sort who could have made a fortune and lived like a gentleman. He is such a clever, stylish young man! He is a doctor’s son by name, which is quite a lot; but in his very nature, he is the son of an earl!”
source_text: “Oh! miss—I blush to name it—a gay man! But I know him to be very quick and trim, who might have made his thousands, like a squire. Such a clever young dandy as he is! He’s a doctor’s son by name, which is a great deal; and he’s an earl’s son by nature!”

text: Indeed. He was raised with great care and attended the Casterbridge Grammar School for a long time. He mastered many languages during his stay; it was even rumored he progressed so far that he could transcribe Chinese in shorthand, though I cannot personally confirm it, as it was only hearsay. Regardless, he squandered his natural gifts and enlisted as a soldier; yet even then, he rose to the rank of sergeant without any effort at all. Ah, what a virtue it is to be of good birth; noble blood will always distinguish itself, even among the common ranks. And has he truly come back home, miss?
source_text: “Yes. And, he was brought up so well, and sent to Casterbridge Grammar School for years and years. Learnt all languages while he was there; and it was said he got on so far that he could take down Chinese in shorthand; but that I don’t answer for, as it was only reported. However, he wasted his gifted lot, and listed a soldier; but even then he rose to be a sergeant without trying at all. Ah! such a blessing it is to be high-born; nobility of blood will shine out even in the ranks and files. And is he really come home, miss?”

text: To him, recollections were a burden and expectations a luxury he did not require. By focusing his thoughts and feelings entirely on the immediate scene, he remained susceptible only to the trials of the moment. His perception of time was like a brief, occasional glimmer; that stretching of the mind into what has been and what might be—the very thing that renders the past a source of regret and the future a reason for caution—was unknown to Troy. In his world, the past was merely yesterday and the future only tomorrow; he never looked a day beyond.
source_text: He was a man to whom memories were an incumbrance, and anticipations a superfluity. Simply feeling, considering, and caring for what was before his eyes, he was vulnerable only in the present. His outlook upon time was as a transient flash of the eye now and then: that projection of consciousness into days gone by and to come, which makes the past a synonym for the pathetic and the future a word for circumspection, was foreign to Troy. With him the past was yesterday; the future, to-morrow; never, the day after.

text: Since Sergeant Troy never indulged in the habit of anticipation, he was entirely immune to disappointment. This benefit, however, was offset by a certain shrinking of his more refined emotions and perceptions. Yet a lack of depth is seldom perceived as a shortcoming by the person who lacks it; in this sense, a poverty of spirit or taste is unlike material hardship, for those who possess it are indifferent to their state, while those who might care are already beyond its reach. One does not feel the absence of what one has never possessed, and Troy felt no void where grander experiences might have been. Instead, knowing he found joy in things that more earnest people lacked, his own limited capacity felt more vast than theirs.
source_text: Sergeant Troy, being entirely innocent of the practice of expectation, was never disappointed. To set against this negative gain there may have been some positive losses from a certain narrowing of the higher tastes and sensations which it entailed. But limitation of the capacity is never recognized as a loss by the loser therefrom: in this attribute moral or æsthetic poverty contrasts plausibly with material, since those who suffer do not mind it, whilst those who mind it soon cease to suffer. It is not a denial of anything to have been always without it, and what Troy had never enjoyed he did not miss; but, being fully conscious that what sober people missed he enjoyed, his capacity, though really less, seemed greater than theirs.

text: He kept himself from crossing the boundary that separates fashionable indiscretions from the truly repulsive; therefore, while his ethics were never praised, the judgment he faced was often softened by an indulgent smile. This reception led him to become a dealer in the romantic exploits of others, serving to inflate his reputation as a man of the world rather than to offer any moral benefit to those who listened.
source_text: He never passed the line which divides the spruce vices from the ugly; and hence, though his morals had hardly been applauded, disapproval of them had frequently been tempered with a smile. This treatment had led to his becoming a sort of regrater of other men’s gallantries, to his own aggrandizement as a Corinthian, rather than to the moral profit of his hearers.

text: The extraordinary efficacy of adulation when directed toward the feminine heart is a concept so ubiquitous that it is uttered with the same unthinking regularity as a common maxim or a profession of faith, seldom considering the profound implications inherent in such a claim. Rarer still is this knowledge applied for the genuine benefit of the woman in question. For most, this truth is relegated to that shelf of dusty platitudes which demand some personal disaster to illuminate their terrifying depth. When considered with any degree of gravity, it is usually accompanied by the assumption that such praise must possess a grain of logic to take root. It speaks well of the masculine character that few seek to test this theory through deliberate trial, and perhaps it is fortunate for their peace of mind that chance has not forced the discovery upon them. Yet, the fact remains that a deceiver who skillfully ensnares a woman by drowning her in impossible fantasies can gain an influence bordering on the diabolical—a lesson learned by many through bitter and uninvited experience. Others claim to have mastered this dark art through calculated practice, continuing their cynical maneuvers with devastating success. Lieutenant Vane was of this latter sort.
source_text: The wondrous power of flattery in _passados_ at woman is a perception so universal as to be remarked upon by many people almost as automatically as they repeat a proverb, or say that they are Christians and the like, without thinking much of the enormous corollaries which spring from the proposition. Still less is it acted upon for the good of the complemental being alluded to. With the majority such an opinion is shelved with all those trite aphorisms which require some catastrophe to bring their tremendous meanings thoroughly home. When expressed with some amount of reflectiveness it seems co-ordinate with a belief that this flattery must be reasonable to be effective. It is to the credit of men that few attempt to settle the question by experiment, and it is for their happiness, perhaps, that accident has never settled it for them. Nevertheless, that a male dissembler who by deluging her with untenable fictions charms the female wisely, may acquire powers reaching to the extremity of perdition, is a truth taught to many by unsought and wringing occurrences. And some profess to have attained to the same knowledge by experiment as aforesaid, and jauntily continue their indulgence in such experiments with terrible effect. Sergeant Troy was one.

text: A splash of vivid crimson appeared from behind the cart, continuing to pitch hay as steadily as any of the laborers. This was the dashing sergeant, who had joined the harvest for his own amusement; certainly, no one could dispute that he was rendering the farm’s mistress a noble service through this spontaneous gift of his toil during such a frantic season.
source_text: From behind the waggon a bright scarlet spot emerged, and went on loading unconcernedly with the rest. It was the gallant sergeant, who had come haymaking for pleasure; and nobody could deny that he was doing the mistress of the farm real knight-service by this voluntary contribution of his labour at a busy time.

text: “You’re being unfair—but I won’t repeat what you said. I’m so grateful to have this token of your friendship, whatever the cost, that I won’t argue over your delivery. It really does matter to me, Miss Everdene. You might think a man is weak for wanting nothing more than a simple ‘good morning.’ Perhaps he is—I can't say. But you have never been a man looking at a woman, and that woman being you.”
source_text: “That’s unjust—but I won’t repeat the remark. I am too gratified to get such a mark of your friendship at any price to cavil at the tone. I _do_, Miss Everdene, care for it. You may think a man foolish to want a mere word—just a good morning. Perhaps he is—I don’t know. But you have never been a man looking upon a woman, and that woman yourself.”

text: I didn't really intend for you to take it at first, as it was my only small claim to status," he blurted out, quite frankly. "But I swear, I truly want you to have it now. No more games—please! Don't refuse me the pleasure of seeing you wear it for my sake. But then, you are so beautiful that you don't even feel the need to be gracious like everyone else.
source_text: “I did not quite mean you to accept it at first, for it was my one poor patent of nobility,” he broke out, bluntly; “but, upon my soul, I wish you would now. Without any shamming, come! Don’t deny me the happiness of wearing it for my sake? But you are too lovely even to care to be kind as others are.”

text: Elara watched from the grass as he worked to brush and dislodge the cluster from the branch, hoisting the skep with his free hand to catch the falling swarm. She stole a moment of privacy while his concentration was bound to the task, smoothing her stray locks and straightening her bodice. He climbed down, extending the hive at a distance, a shimmering wake of bees trailing behind him in the air.
source_text: Bathsheba looked on from the ground whilst he was busy sweeping and shaking the bees from the tree, holding up the hive with the other hand for them to fall into. She made use of an unobserved minute whilst his attention was absorbed in the operation to arrange her plumes a little. He came down holding the hive at arm’s length, behind which trailed a cloud of bees.

text: As the clock struck eight on this solstice eve, while the shimmering orb of fire in the west still raked the tops of the ferns with its long, indulgent rays, a gentle whispering of fabric rose from the greenery, and Marianne appeared in the clearing, the soft, verdant plumes caressing her as high as her arms. She stopped, pivoted, and retreated over the rise toward her own porch, from which vantage she cast a parting look at the hollow she had just fled, having resolved that she would not remain in that solitude after all.
source_text: At eight o’clock this midsummer evening, whilst the bristling ball of gold in the west still swept the tips of the ferns with its long, luxuriant rays, a soft brushing-by of garments might have been heard among them, and Bathsheba appeared in their midst, their soft, feathery arms caressing her up to her shoulders. She paused, turned, went back over the hill and half-way to her own door, whence she cast a farewell glance upon the spot she had just left, having resolved not to remain near the place after all.

text: The hollow was a natural, saucer-shaped basin about thirty feet wide, shallow enough for the sun to reach their heads. From the center, the sky was framed by a circular horizon of ferns that grew down the slopes and stopped abruptly at the bottom. Within this green belt, the floor was a thick, silken carpet of moss and grass, so soft that one's feet sank halfway into it.
source_text: The pit was a saucer-shaped concave, naturally formed, with a top diameter of about thirty feet, and shallow enough to allow the sunshine to reach their heads. Standing in the centre, the sky overhead was met by a circular horizon of fern: this grew nearly to the bottom of the slope and then abruptly ceased. The middle within the belt of verdure was floored with a thick flossy carpet of moss and grass intermingled, so yielding that the foot was half-buried within it.

text: “Observe,” Julian remarked, unsheathing the blade. As he tilted the steel into the afternoon glare, it cast back a brilliant recognition, pulsating with a vitality of its own. “To begin, we practice four strokes and four lunges to either side. The foot-soldier’s maneuvers are perhaps more complex than our own, to my thinking, yet they want for our sweeping grace. Their code requires seven slashes and three pierces. That is the mere preface. Now, our primary cut is as if you were scattering seed across a furrow—thus.” Elara witnessed a silver arc, inverted against the blue, before Julian’s hand became motionless once more. “The second, as if you were pruning a hedge—so. The third, as if you were harvesting the wheat—in this fashion. The fourth, as if you were flailing the floor—in that way. Then the sequence repeats on the left. The thrusts follow: one, two, three, four, right; one, two, three, four, left.” He cycled through the pattern again. “Shall you see them once more?” he inquired. “One, two—”
source_text: “Now,” said Troy, producing the sword, which, as he raised it into the sunlight, gleamed a sort of greeting, like a living thing, “first, we have four right and four left cuts; four right and four left thrusts. Infantry cuts and guards are more interesting than ours, to my mind; but they are not so swashing. They have seven cuts and three thrusts. So much as a preliminary. Well, next, our cut one is as if you were sowing your corn—so.” Bathsheba saw a sort of rainbow, upside down in the air, and Troy’s arm was still again. “Cut two, as if you were hedging—so. Three, as if you were reaping—so. Four, as if you were threshing—in that way. Then the same on the left. The thrusts are these: one, two, three, four, right; one, two, three, four, left.” He repeated them. “Have ’em again?” he said. “One, two—”

text: Not since the heavy sabre was first forged as the kingdom’s primary steel had such agility been witnessed in its handling as that exhibited by Sergeant Sterling; certainly, he had never found himself in a more inspired state for such a display than here, amidst the golden twilight and the tangled bracken with Elara. It could be claimed with absolute certainty, given the perilous intimacy of his lunges, that if the blade’s rapid trajectory had possessed the power to leave a visible trail in the atmosphere, the empty space remaining would have served as a precise cast of Elara’s standing form.
source_text: Never since the broadsword became the national weapon had there been more dexterity shown in its management than by the hands of Sergeant Troy, and never had he been in such splendid temper for the performance as now in the evening sunshine among the ferns with Bathsheba. It may safely be asserted with respect to the closeness of his cuts, that had it been possible for the edge of the sword to leave in the air a permanent substance wherever it flew past, the space left untouched would have been almost a mould of Bathsheba’s figure.

text: That brief interval sent the blood pulsing into her face and a stinging heat through her entire frame, reaching even the soles of her feet, as her emotions expanded until they completely overwhelmed her reason. It struck her with the force of the blow at Horeb, though it brought forth a torrent of tears rather than water. She felt the heavy burden of someone who had committed a grave transgression.
source_text: That minute’s interval had brought the blood beating into her face, set her stinging as if aflame to the very hollows of her feet, and enlarged emotion to a compass which quite swamped thought. It had brought upon her a stroke resulting, as did that of Moses in Horeb, in a liquid stream—here a stream of tears. She felt like one who has sinned a great sin.

text: A distinct strain of folly now began to weave into the complex qualities of Bathsheba Everdene’s character. This trait was foreign to her true essence; yet, arriving like a serum on the tip of Eros’s arrow, it eventually permeated her entire being. Although Bathsheba had too much sense to be ruled by her feminine impulses, she was too feminine to use that sense effectively. Perhaps nothing more baffles a man than a woman’s knack for believing flatteries she knows are false—except, indeed, her skepticism toward truths she knows are accurate.
source_text: We now see the element of folly distinctly mingling with the many varying particulars which made up the character of Bathsheba Everdene. It was almost foreign to her intrinsic nature. Introduced as lymph on the dart of Eros, it eventually permeated and coloured her whole constitution. Bathsheba, though she had too much understanding to be entirely governed by her womanliness, had too much womanliness to use her understanding to the best advantage. Perhaps in no minor point does woman astonish her helpmate more than in the strange power she possesses of believing cajoleries that she knows to be false—except, indeed, in that of being utterly sceptical on strictures that she knows to be true.

text: Gabriel witnessed this obsession and was troubled by it from his morning departure to the fields until his return, and often into the small hours of the night. His own unrequited love had been his primary burden; now, seeing Bathsheba falling into a snare was a far deeper grief that nearly eclipsed his personal heartache. This shift mirrored Hippocrates’ famous observation regarding how a more intense physical pain can mask a lesser one.
source_text: All this infatuation Gabriel saw, and was troubled thereby from the time of his daily journey a-field to the time of his return, and on to the small hours of many a night. That he was not beloved had hitherto been his great sorrow; that Bathsheba was getting into the toils was now a sorrow greater than the first, and one which nearly obscured it. It was a result which paralleled the oft-quoted observation of Hippocrates concerning physical pains.

text: “I have a fondness for the ranks, but for this individual I feel none,” he remarked with heavy earnestness. “His mastery of his craft has perhaps led him into folly; for the amusement he yields the village is but a tragedy for the girl. If he endeavors to address you, grant him only a brusque ‘Good evening’ and depart; when you perceive him on one path, take the alternate. Should he utter a jest, let the humor escape you and keep your lips set, and refer to him among the busybodies as ‘that peculiar gentleman’ or ‘that Lieutenant So-and-so.’ Speak of him as a man from a house gone to rack and ruin. You need not be coarse, only coolly indifferent, and thus quietly sever the connection.”
source_text: “I like soldiers, but this one I do not like,” he said, sturdily. “His cleverness in his calling may have tempted him astray, and what is mirth to the neighbours is ruin to the woman. When he tries to talk to ’ee again, why not turn away with a short ‘Good day’; and when you see him coming one way, turn the other. When he says anything laughable, fail to see the point and don’t smile, and speak of him before those who will report your talk as ‘that fantastical man,’ or ‘that Sergeant What’s-his-name.’ ‘That man of a family that has come to the dogs.’ Don’t be unmannerly towards en, but harmless-uncivil, and so get rid of the man.”

text: You are well aware, mistress, that I love you and always shall. I only say this to remind you that I would never wish to cause you any trouble; beyond that, I will set my feelings aside. I have failed to gain wealth or status, and I am not so foolish as to pursue you now that I am poor and you have risen so far above me. But Bathsheba, I beg you to consider this—both to maintain your reputation among the workers and out of simple fairness to a decent man who loves you as much as I do, you should be more cautious in how you behave toward this soldier.
source_text: “You know, mistress, that I love you, and shall love you always. I only mention this to bring to your mind that at any rate I would wish to do you no harm: beyond that I put it aside. I have lost in the race for money and good things, and I am not such a fool as to pretend to ’ee now I am poor, and you have got altogether above me.
