But Bathsheba, dear mistress, this I beg you to consider—that, both to keep yourself well honoured among the workfolk, and in common generosity to an honourable man who loves you as well as I, you should be more discreet in your bearing towards this soldier.”

text: No messenger could depart with the note until the morrow; yet, to quiet her restless spirit by relinquishing the page—and so, in a manner, launching the deed beyond recall—she stood to deliver it into the hands of whatever domestic might be found below in the kitchen.
source_text: It was impossible to send this letter till the next day; yet to quell her uneasiness by getting it out of her hands, and so, as it were, setting the act in motion at once, she arose to take it to any one of the women who might be in the kitchen.

text: “You wretched child! What malice of spirit permits you to give breath to such base rumors? You are a cold and pitiless thing.... But I shall see to it that neither you nor any soul in this parish, nor the borough beyond, dares to utter another word of it!” She broke into a restless stride, pacing a frantic path from the hearth to the threshold and back again.
source_text: “You graceless girl! How can you have the cruel heart to repeat what they say? Unfeeling thing that you are.... But _I’ll_ see if you or anybody else in the village, or town either, dare do such a thing!” She started off, pacing from fireplace to door, and back again.

text: “I have no wish to go over things again,” Liddy remarked, displaying the feminine poise of one small in stature; “but I no longer desire to remain in your service. If you please, I shall leave when the harvest is done, or at the end of the week, or even this very day. I fail to see why I should be mistreated and shouted at for no reason whatsoever!” the tiny woman concluded, her voice and bearing full of sudden importance.
source_text: “I don’t want to repeat anything,” said Liddy, with womanly dignity of a diminutive order; “but I don’t wish to stay with you. And, if you please, I’ll go at the end of the harvest, or this week, or to-day.... I don’t see that I deserve to be put upon and stormed at for nothing!” concluded the small woman, bigly.

text: The following evening, Bathsheba set off to fulfill a promise made to Liddy earlier that day, primarily to avoid Mr. Boldwood should he arrive to respond to her note in person. Her companion, as a sign of their recent reconciliation, had been granted a week’s leave to stay with her sister. This sister was married to a prosperous maker of fencing and livestock feeders who lived in a charming thicket of hazel trees just beyond Yalbury. It had been arranged that Miss Everdene would visit them for a short stay to examine several clever innovations the woodsman had integrated into his handiwork.
source_text: The next evening Bathsheba, with the idea of getting out of the way of Mr. Boldwood in the event of his returning to answer her note in person, proceeded to fulfil an engagement made with Liddy some few hours earlier. Bathsheba’s companion, as a gauge of their reconciliation, had been granted a week’s holiday to visit her sister, who was married to a thriving hurdler and cattle-crib-maker living in a delightful labyrinth of hazel copse not far beyond Yalbury. The arrangement was that Miss Everdene should honour them by coming there for a day or two to inspect some ingenious contrivances which this man of the woods had introduced into his wares.

text: After charging Gabriel and Maryann with the task of securing the house for the night, she stepped outdoors just as a brief thunderstorm concluded. The rain had cleansed the atmosphere and lightly dampened the earth's surface, though the ground underneath remained entirely dry. A crisp vitality radiated from every ridge and valley, as if the soil itself were exhaling a pure, sweet breath, and the joyful birds offered a chorus to the landscape. In front of her, the sky displayed a striking contrast: patches of violent light flickered around a veiled sun, which drifted toward the furthest northwestern edge of the horizon that the midsummer solstice would permit.
source_text: Leaving her instructions with Gabriel and Maryann, that they were to see everything carefully locked up for the night, she went out of the house just at the close of a timely thunder-shower, which had refined the air, and daintily bathed the coat of the land, though all beneath was dry as ever. Freshness was exhaled in an essence from the varied contours of bank and hollow, as if the earth breathed maiden breath; and the pleased birds were hymning to the scene. Before her, among the clouds, there was a contrast in the shape of lairs of fierce light which showed themselves in the neighbourhood of a hidden sun, lingering on to the farthest north-west corner of the heavens that this midsummer season allowed.

text: “With a light heart? Can a man betrayed into such searing torment find any cause for gladness? Having suffered ruin, how am I to affect the air of one who has triumphed? Good God, you are surely hollow of all feeling! Had I but known what a terrifyingly cruel nectar this was to prove, I would have fled your path, remained blind to your beauty, and stayed deaf to your very name. I confess all this to you, but what is it to your mind? You remain unmoved.”
source_text: “Cheerfully! Can a man fooled to utter heart-burning find a reason for being merry? If I have lost, how can I be as if I had won? Heavens you must be heartless quite! Had I known what a fearfully bitter sweet this was to be, how I would have avoided you, and never seen you, and been deaf of you. I tell you all this, but what do you care! You don’t care.”

text: “You might as well go; I have no further right to hold you. As for myself, I ought to find some solitary place to hide away and seek penance. I once loved a woman, and now I am sick with the shame of it. When I am gone, people will remember me only as a pathetic, lovesick fool. God—if only this rejection had happened in secret, so that my disgrace remained hidden and my status preserved! But it is of no use now; my reputation is destroyed, and the woman is not won. What a miserable shame it is—nothing but shame!”
source_text: “You may as well. I have no further claim upon you. As for me, I had better go somewhere alone, and hide—and pray. I loved a woman once. I am now ashamed. When I am dead they’ll say, miserable love-sick man that he was. Heaven—heaven—if I had got jilted secretly, and the dishonour not known, and my position kept! But no matter, it is gone, and the woman not gained. Shame upon him—shame!”

text: Bathsheba had remained as rigid as a statue until that moment, but now she buried her face in her hands, her mind reeling as she tried to process the scene that had just ended. The profound depths of intense emotion revealed by a man as stoic as Mr. Boldwood were both baffling and frightening. He was not simply a master of self-restraint; he was exactly the man he had just proven himself to be.
source_text: Bathsheba, who had been standing motionless as a model all this latter time, flung her hands to her face, and wildly attempted to ponder on the exhibition which had just passed away. Such astounding wells of fevered feeling in a still man like Mr. Boldwood were incomprehensible, dreadful. Instead of being a man trained to repression he was—what she had seen him.

text: Haunted by the fear of being seen as an overly sentimental girl, this sincere woman had become far too skilled at hiding her deep passions behind a facade of indifference. But now, her defenses had crumbled. In her state of agitation, she stopped her journey to pace back and forth, her fingers twitching at the air and her hand pressed to her brow as she wept in broken gasps. She eventually sat on a pile of stones by the road to reflect, staying there for a long time. Above the dark horizon, copper clouds formed headlands and shores around a translucent green stretch of the western sky. Deep purple tints soon washed over them, and as the restless earth rotated, she was brought toward a different view in the east: the arrival of flickering, uncertain stars. She looked up at their quiet struggles within the dark void, but she took in none of it. Her tormented spirit was elsewhere, lingering entirely with Troy.
source_text: With almost a morbid dread of being thought a gushing girl, this guileless woman too well concealed from the world under a manner of carelessness the warm depths of her strong emotions. But now there was no reserve. In her distraction, instead of advancing further she walked up and down, beating the air with her fingers, pressing on her brow, and sobbing brokenly to herself. Then she sat down on a heap of stones by the wayside to think. There she remained long. Above the dark margin of the earth appeared foreshores and promontories of coppery cloud, bounding a green and pellucid expanse in the western sky. Amaranthine glosses came over them then, and the unresting world wheeled her round to a contrasting prospect eastward, in the shape of indecisive and palpitating stars. She gazed upon their silent throes amid the shades of space, but realised none at all. Her troubled spirit was far away with Troy.

text: The hamlet of Weatherbury was as hushed as the cemetery at its center, and the living lay nearly as motionless as the dead. The church clock struck eleven. The air was so devoid of noise that the internal whirring of the machinery was audible just before the tolls, as was the sharp mechanical click when they ceased. The notes rang out with the indifferent persistence of a machine—echoing against the stone walls, rippling toward the drifting clouds, and seeping through the gaps into the vast, unexplored reaches of the night.
source_text: The village of Weatherbury was quiet as the graveyard in its midst, and the living were lying well-nigh as still as the dead. The church clock struck eleven. The air was so empty of other sounds that the whirr of the clock-work immediately before the strokes was distinct, and so was also the click of the same at their close. The notes flew forth with the usual blind obtuseness of inanimate things—flapping and rebounding among walls, undulating against the scattered clouds, spreading through their interstices into unexplored miles of space.

text: Only two types of people could have drifted into the enclosure with the spectral grace of that shadowy figure: a woman or a traveler. Given the late hour and the nature of the task, a woman was out of the question; therefore, the intruder could be nothing but a thief. He had likely recognized the household's vulnerability on this specific night and chosen it for his bold intrusion. To turn this suspicion into a certainty, a group of travelers was currently known to be camped down in Weatherbury Bottom.
source_text: Two varieties only of humanity could have entered the paddock with the ghostlike glide of that mysterious figure. They were a woman and a gipsy man. A woman was out of the question in such an occupation at this hour, and the comer could be no less than a thief, who might probably have known the weakness of the household on this particular night, and have chosen it on that account for his daring attempt. Moreover, to raise suspicion to conviction itself, there were gipsies in Weatherbury Bottom.

text: Bathsheba’s anxious reflections by the side of the road had finally brought her to the realization that only two paths remained in this disastrous situation. The first was to ensure Troy remained far from Weatherbury until Boldwood’s anger had finally abated; the second was to yield to Oak’s warnings and Boldwood’s condemnations by severing her connection to Troy entirely.
source_text: Bathsheba’s perturbed meditations by the roadside had ultimately evolved a conclusion that there were only two remedies for the present desperate state of affairs. The first was merely to keep Troy away from Weatherbury till Boldwood’s indignation had cooled; the second to listen to Oak’s entreaties, and Boldwood’s denunciations, and give up Troy altogether.

text: Was Bathsheba entirely unaware of the obvious irony that a lover’s embrace is the worst possible place to fortify a decision to leave him? Or did she realize, with a hidden thrill of pleasure, that by opting for this method of dismissal she was ensuring they would be together at least one last time?
source_text: Was Bathsheba altogether blind to the obvious fact that the support of a lover’s arms is not of a kind best calculated to assist a resolve to renounce him? Or was she sophistically sensible, with a thrill of pleasure, that by adopting this course for getting rid of him she was ensuring a meeting with him, at any rate, once more?

text: Night had fallen, and it was likely nearly ten o'clock. To achieve her aim, she had to abandon her visit to Liddy at Yalbury, return to Weatherbury Farm, hitch the horse to the gig, and depart for Bath immediately. The plan seemed unfeasible at first; the journey was a grueling trek even for a sturdy horse, and she had significantly underestimated the distance. It was a remarkably bold venture for a woman to undertake alone in the dark.
source_text: It was now dark, and the hour must have been nearly ten. The only way to accomplish her purpose was to give up her idea of visiting Liddy at Yalbury, return to Weatherbury Farm, put the horse into the gig, and drive at once to Bath. The scheme seemed at first impossible: the journey was a fearfully heavy one, even for a strong horse, at her own estimate; and she much underrated the distance. It was most venturesome for a woman, at night, and alone.

text: She lingered on the path, delaying her arrival at Blackwood until the village windows were dark and, most importantly, until Mr. Oakwell was safely confined to his house. Her strategy was to journey toward Exeter under the stars, meeting Lieutenant Vane at daybreak before he could begin his progress toward her. She meant to offer her final adieus and send him away; then, while the gelding recovered its strength, she would allow herself the luxury of tears before setting out for home the next morning. By this method, she could pace the horse mildly through the day, join Sarah at the mill by sunset, and slip back into Blackwood unobserved—leaving her secret journey entirely undetected. Such was Clara’s design. But, being unfamiliar with the local terrain, she had grievously misjudged the scale of the map, imagining the road to be scarcely half the length it truly was.
source_text: Her walk was slow, for she wished not to enter Weatherbury till the cottagers were in bed, and, particularly, till Boldwood was secure. Her plan was now to drive to Bath during the night, see Sergeant Troy in the morning before he set out to come to her, bid him farewell, and dismiss him: then to rest the horse thoroughly (herself to weep the while, she thought), starting early the next morning on her return journey. By this arrangement she could trot Dainty gently all the day, reach Liddy at Yalbury in the evening, and come home to Weatherbury with her whenever they chose—so nobody would know she had been to Bath at all. Such was Bathsheba’s scheme. But in her topographical ignorance as a late comer to the place, she misreckoned the distance of her journey as not much more than half what it really was.

text: Mr. Coggan poured the cider with a generous, unsparing hand into Cain’s wide, gasping mouth; half of it spilled down the side of the jug, and half of what reached his lips trickled down his neck, and half of what entered his throat went down the wrong pipe, only to be coughed and sputtered over the surrounding reapers in a fine cider mist. For a brief moment, this spray hung in the warm sunlight like a small, hazy breath.
source_text: Mr. Coggan poured the liquor with unstinted liberality at the suffering Cain’s circular mouth; half of it running down the side of the flagon, and half of what reached his mouth running down outside his throat, and half of what ran in going the wrong way, and being coughed and sneezed around the persons of the gathered reapers in the form of a cider fog, which for a moment hung in the sunny air like a small exhalation.

text: “I saw our mistress enter a kind of park, a place with benches and greenery, walking arm-in-arm with a soldier,” Cainy continued steadily, sensing that his news was having a profound effect on Gabriel’s heart. “I’m certain the man was Sergeant Troy. They sat together for more than half an hour, speaking of deeply moving things, and at one point she was weeping uncontrollably. When they finally emerged, her eyes were bright though she was pale as a lily; they gazed into each other’s faces with an intimacy as deep as any man and woman can know.”
source_text: “I seed our mis’ess go into a sort of a park place, where there’s seats, and shrubs and flowers, arm-in-crook with a sojer,” continued Cainy, firmly, and with a dim sense that his words were very effective as regarded Gabriel’s emotions. “And I think the sojer was Sergeant Troy. And they sat there together for more than half-an-hour, talking moving things, and she once was crying a’most to death. And when they came out her eyes were shining and she was as white as a lily; and they looked into one another’s faces, as far-gone friendly as a man and woman can be.”

text: “Leave ’un bide,” Silas Thorne put in. “The lad’s meaning is that the heavens and the ground in the great town of Bristol are not so very unlike our own. It is to our benefit to learn of distant places, and as such the youth’s words should be permitted, if I may say so.”
source_text: “Let en alone,” interposed Joseph Poorgrass. “The boy’s meaning is that the sky and the earth in the kingdom of Bath is not altogether different from ours here. ’Tis for our good to gain knowledge of strange cities, and as such the boy’s words should be suffered, so to speak it.”

text: And there were such immense, grand dwellings, and more folk a-walking the streets on a plain Monday than ever gathered for the Thornbury Revels at Midsummer. I visited many a majestic abbey and house of prayer, too. And how that vicar would plead with the heavens! He’d sink to his knees and clasp his hands together, letting the heavy gold bands on his fingers shine and spark right in your sight—wealth he’d surely gained for his most marvelous way of speaking to God! Truly, I’d give much to bide in such a town.
source_text: “And there were great large houses, and more people all the week long than at Weatherbury club-walking on White Tuesdays. And I went to grand churches and chapels. And how the parson would pray! Yes; he would kneel down and put up his hands together, and make the holy gold rings on his fingers gleam and twinkle in yer eyes, that he’d earned by praying so excellent well!—Ah yes, I wish I lived there.”

text: The farmer left through the gate immediately. He remained unforgiven—that was the heart of the matter. He had glimpsed the woman who was both his delight and his torment, sitting in the room where he had been welcomed as a special guest earlier that summer, and now she had refused to let him in.
source_text: The farmer instantly went out by the gate. He was unforgiven—that was the issue of it all. He had seen her who was to him simultaneously a delight and a torture, sitting in the room he had shared with her as a peculiarly privileged guest only a little earlier in the summer, and she had denied him an entrance there now.

text: Boldwood wasn't in a frame of mind to pick up on any nuances of tone. He went on, “I may as well be direct; and understand, I have no interest in debating morality, a woman’s reputation, or offering any judgment on your behavior. I’m here to propose a business deal.”
source_text: Boldwood’s present mood was not critical enough to notice tones. He continued, “I may as well speak plainly; and understand, I don’t wish to enter into the questions of right or wrong, woman’s honour and shame, or to express any opinion on your conduct. I intend a business transaction with you.”

text: As Boldwood spoke, his tone betrayed a painful awareness of just how fragile his position, his goals, and his methods had become. He had completely lost the steady, dignified air of his former self; the very plan he was now pursuing would have been dismissed by him as pathetically foolish only a few months earlier. We often see a powerful drive in a lover that is missing in a man who is free, yet the free man possesses a clarity of vision that the lover lacks entirely. Deep passion inevitably brings a certain narrowness of mind; while love intensifies emotion, it diminishes practical judgment. Boldwood was an extreme example of this truth: he knew nothing of Fanny Robin’s current life or her whereabouts, nor did he grasp what Troy was capable of, yet he spoke as if he did.
source_text: In making this statement Boldwood’s voice revealed only too clearly a consciousness of the weakness of his position, his aims, and his method. His manner had lapsed quite from that of the firm and dignified Boldwood of former times; and such a scheme as he had now engaged in he would have condemned as childishly imbecile only a few months ago. We discern a grand force in the lover which he lacks whilst a free man; but there is a breadth of vision in the free man which in the lover we vainly seek. Where there is much bias there must be some narrowness, and love, though added emotion, is subtracted capacity. Boldwood exemplified this to an abnormal degree: he knew nothing of Fanny Robin’s circumstances or whereabouts, he knew nothing of Troy’s possibilities, yet that was what he said.

text: “I have already weighed that possibility, and I have concluded that if your integrity cannot be moved, I can trust your—well, let’s call it pragmatism—to ensure you don't forfeit a potential five hundred pounds, nor make a bitter enemy of a man who is willing to be an exceptionally useful friend.”
source_text: “I had thought of that, and I have considered that if I can’t appeal to your honour I can trust to your—well, shrewdness we’ll call it—not to lose five hundred pounds in prospect, and also make a bitter enemy of a man who is willing to be an extremely useful friend.”

text: Beside Troy, Oak appeared somberly diminished, like a flickering candle outmatched by a brilliant gaslight. Feeling profoundly out of place, he stepped back outside with the intention of heading home; in his current state, he had no heart to witness the scene within the barn. He lingered for a moment at the threshold, however, as Troy began to speak.
source_text: In juxtaposition with Troy, Oak had a melancholy tendency to look like a candle beside gas, and ill at ease, he went out again, thinking he would go home; for, under the circumstances, he had no heart for the scene in the barn. At the door he paused for a moment: Troy was speaking.

text: Bathsheba swept out of the barn in a state of high indignation, the women and children following in her wake. The musicians, recognizing that they were merely hired hands rather than true guests, retreated quietly to their spring wagon and harnessed the horse. Consequently, Troy and the farmhands were left as the only ones remaining in the space. Oak, wishing to avoid appearing needlessly sullen, stayed for a brief interval before he, too, stood up and made a quiet exit, pursued by a jovial curse from the sergeant for declining another round of grog.
source_text: Bathsheba indignantly left the barn, followed by all the women and children. The musicians, not looking upon themselves as “company,” slipped quietly away to their spring waggon and put in the horse. Thus Troy and the men on the farm were left sole occupants of the place. Oak, not to appear unnecessarily disagreeable, stayed a little while; then he, too, arose and quietly took his departure, followed by a friendly oath from the sergeant for not staying to a second round of grog.

text: He returned to the barn, intent on finding help to cover the haystacks before the night was out. All was quiet within, and he would have moved on, assuming the group had dispersed, had a faint light not spilled through a knot-hole in the heavy doors. It was a deep, honeyed glow, appearing vividly yellow against the pale, greenish-white atmosphere of the night outside.
source_text: He went back to the barn to endeavour to obtain assistance for covering the ricks that very night. All was silent within, and he would have passed on in the belief that the party had broken up, had not a dim light, yellow as saffron by contrast with the greenish whiteness outside, streamed through a knot-hole in the folding doors.

text: The tapers nestled within the pine boughs had dwindled to their brass sockets, and in several places the surrounding needles were singed by the dying heat. Many flames had expired altogether; others smoldered and gave off a rank odor, weeping hot tallow onto the floorboards below. Beneath the table, and slumped against stools and benches in every posture save an upright one, lay the wretched figures of the laborers, their low-hanging heads resembling nothing so much as tangled mops or discarded brushes. In the center of this ruin, the scarlet coat of Major Sterling remained vivid and distinct as he reclined in his chair. Silas lay flat upon his spine, jaw agape, exhaling a rhythmic thunder that was echoed by his companions; the collective respiration of the prostrate assembly rose in a low, continuous rumble, like the hum of a distant city. Barnaby had coiled himself into a tight ball after the fashion of a badger, seemingly intent on presenting the smallest possible surface to the air, while behind him the slight, fading remnant of Peter Grey was barely visible. Vessels still cluttered the board, including a toppled pitcher from which a thin stream, tracing a precise path down the table’s center, spilled into the collar of the oblivious Miller in a persistent, rhythmic beat, much like the slow weeping of a stalactite within a cavern.
source_text: The candles suspended among the evergreens had burnt down to their sockets, and in some cases the leaves tied about them were scorched. Many of the lights had quite gone out, others smoked and stank, grease dropping from them upon the floor. Here, under the table, and leaning against forms and chairs in every conceivable attitude except the perpendicular, were the wretched persons of all the work-folk, the hair of their heads at such low levels being suggestive of mops and brooms. In the midst of these shone red and distinct the figure of Sergeant Troy, leaning back in a chair. Coggan was on his back, with his mouth open, huzzing forth snores, as were several others; the united breathings of the horizonal assemblage forming a subdued roar like London from a distance. Joseph Poorgrass was curled round in the fashion of a hedge-hog, apparently in attempts to present the least possible portion of his surface to the air; and behind him was dimly visible an unimportant remnant of William Smallbury. The glasses and cups still stood upon the table, a water-jug being overturned, from which a small rill, after tracing its course with marvellous precision down the centre of the long table, fell into the neck of the unconscious Mark Clark, in a steady, monotonous drip, like the dripping of a stalactite in a cave.

text: He had driven his iron spike—a slender shaft buffed to a sheen by years of labor—deep into the hayrick, serving to anchor the bundles in place of the usual timber brace. An azure luminescence flared at the sky's zenith, vibrating downward through the dark air until it hovered near the metal tip. This was the fourth of the great discharges. A second later, a sharp crack resounded—abrupt, crystalline, and brief. Silas understood his peril was now acute, and he determined to make his descent.
source_text: He had stuck his ricking-rod, or poniard, as it was indifferently called—a long iron lance, polished by handling—into the stack, used to support the sheaves instead of the support called a groom used on houses. A blue light appeared in the zenith, and in some indescribable manner flickered down near the top of the rod. It was the fourth of the larger flashes. A moment later and there was a smack—smart, clear, and short. Gabriel felt his position to be anything but a safe one, and he resolved to descend.

text: Hardly had Oak reached for his implements again when the fifth bolt lunged forth, striking with a viper’s agility and a demon's roar. The flash burned a vivid emerald green, followed by a deafening, bone-shaking peal of thunder. What had the brilliance momentarily unveiled? Looking out from the crest of the haystack, he glimpsed a dark silhouette in the clearing that appeared to be a woman. Could it truly be the parish's most daring inhabitant—Bathsheba? The figure advanced one solitary step before the blackness reclaimed her.
source_text: Before Oak had laid his hands upon his tools again out leapt the fifth flash, with the spring of a serpent and the shout of a fiend. It was green as an emerald, and the reverberation was stunning. What was this the light revealed to him? In the open ground before him, as he looked over the ridge of the rick, was a dark and apparently female form. Could it be that of the only venturesome woman in the parish—Bathsheba? The form moved on a step: then he could see no more.

text: “How dreadful!” she cried, seizing his sleeve. Gabriel turned, steadying her on her high perch by holding her arm. At that same instant, while his body was still pivoted, the light flared again, revealing a dark likeness of the hilltop poplar etched against the barn wall. It was the tree’s shadow, cast across the wood by a second flash from the west.
source_text: “How terrible!” she exclaimed, and clutched him by the sleeve. Gabriel turned, and steadied her on her aerial perch by holding her arm. At the same moment, while he was still reversed in his attitude, there was more light, and he saw, as it were, a copy of the tall poplar tree on the hill drawn in black on the wall of the barn. It was the shadow of that tree, thrown across by a secondary flash in the west.

text: Another brilliance seared the gloom. Elena was down in the stubble, hoisting a fresh bundle to her shoulder, and she met the blinding glare—and the subsequent roar—without a tremor, before ascending once more with her burden. A hollow silence then reigned for several minutes, through which the sharp strike of the wooden pins rang out as Caleb hammered them home with frantic haste. He dared to hope the tempest’s peak had drifted past. Then, the heavens fractured in a sudden, violent radiance.
source_text: The next flare came. Bathsheba was on the ground now, shouldering another sheaf, and she bore its dazzle without flinching—thunder and all—and again ascended with the load. There was then a silence everywhere for four or five minutes, and the crunch of the spars, as Gabriel hastily drove them in, could again be distinctly heard. He thought the crisis of the storm had passed. But there came a burst of light.

text: The atmosphere sharpened its chill and began to stir with a newfound violence. Cold drafts swirled in invisible currents against Silas’s weathered face. The gale shifted its course by a fraction, mounting in strength until, within minutes, every quarter of the firmament seemed to be in frantic motion. Fragments of the straw casing atop the wheat-ricks were snatched into the air in wild, chaotic spirals, requiring him to pin them down with heavy beams discovered nearby. This task finished, Silas bent his strength once more to the barley. A heavy bead of rain struck his forehead, the wind shrieked around every angle, the great elms shuddered to their very foundations, and the smaller limbs clashed in a wooden duel. Driving in pegs at every vulnerable point, he gradually insulated from ruin this tangible representation of six hundred guineas. The deluge began in earnest, and Silas soon felt the moisture tracing frozen, viscous channels down his back. At last, he was rendered little more than a saturated lump of humanity, the tinctures of his coat seeping out to form a dark reservoir at the base of his ladder. The rain lanced through the heavy air in slanted, crystalline needles, stretching in an uninterrupted line from the weeping clouds to his own person.
source_text: The air changed its temperature and stirred itself more vigorously. Cool breezes coursed in transparent eddies round Oak’s face. The wind shifted yet a point or two and blew stronger. In ten minutes every wind of heaven seemed to be roaming at large. Some of the thatching on the wheat-stacks was now whirled fantastically aloft, and had to be replaced and weighted with some rails that lay near at hand. This done, Oak slaved away again at the barley. A huge drop of rain smote his face, the wind snarled round every corner, the trees rocked to the bases of their trunks, and the twigs clashed in strife. Driving in spars at any point and on any system, inch by inch he covered more and more safely from ruin this distracting impersonation of seven hundred pounds. The rain came on in earnest, and Oak soon felt the water to be tracking cold and clammy routes down his back. Ultimately he was reduced well-nigh to a homogeneous sop, and the dyes of his clothes trickled down and stood in a pool at the foot of the ladder. The rain stretched obliquely through the dull atmosphere in liquid spines, unbroken in continuity between their beginnings in the clouds and their points in him.

text: "I truly believed she would have married you," Gabriel said. He didn't quite grasp the sheer intensity of Boldwood’s feelings—which might have otherwise made him hold his tongue—and he was determined not to avoid a hard truth just to spare himself the discomfort. "But that's how it is sometimes; life rarely follows our expectations," he added, speaking with the quiet composure of a man who had been hardened by misfortune without being broken by it.
source_text: “I thought my mistress would have married you,” said Gabriel, not knowing enough of the full depths of Boldwood’s love to keep silence on the farmer’s account, and determined not to evade discipline by doing so on his own. “However, it is so sometimes, and nothing happens that we expect,” he added, with the repose of a man whom misfortune had inured rather than subdued.

text: “Not so, Silas,” he continued, his voice possessing a levity as vacant as the grin upon a death’s-head: “the world made a far greater tragedy of the business than we ourselves ever felt. I confess to a stray pang of remorse on occasion, yet no lady has ever held dominion over my heart for any lasting duration. Well then, good day; I shall rely upon your honor to keep this confidence strictly between the two of us.”
source_text: “No, Gabriel,” he resumed, with a carelessness which was like the smile on the countenance of a skull: “it was made more of by other people than ever it was by us. I do feel a little regret occasionally, but no woman ever had power over me for any length of time. Well, good morning; I can trust you not to mention to others what has passed between us two here.”

text: One Saturday evening in October, Bathsheba’s gig was slowly ascending the slope. She sat passively in the second seat, while beside her walked a poised, well-built young man in a farmer’s suit of a remarkably modern cut. Though on foot, he managed the reins and whip, idly flicking the lash toward the horse’s ear for distraction. This was her husband, the former Sergeant Troy, who had purchased his discharge with Bathsheba’s wealth and was now reinventing himself as a progressive, spirited agriculturist. Traditionalists still referred to him as “Sergeant” whenever they crossed paths—a habit encouraged by his lingering military mustache and the rigid, soldierly posture that had become a permanent part of his frame and character.
source_text: One Saturday evening in the month of October Bathsheba’s vehicle was duly creeping up this incline. She was sitting listlessly in the second seat of the gig, whilst walking beside her in a farmer’s marketing suit of unusually fashionable cut was an erect, well-made young man. Though on foot, he held the reins and whip, and occasionally aimed light cuts at the horse’s ear with the end of the lash, as a recreation. This man was her husband, formerly Sergeant Troy, who, having bought his discharge with Bathsheba’s money, was gradually transforming himself into a farmer of a spirited and very modern school. People of unalterable ideas still insisted upon calling him “Sergeant” when they met him, which was in some degree owing to his having still retained the well-shaped moustache of his military days, and the soldierly bearing inseparable from his form and training.

text: “Yet, the dilemma of whether we shall witness the spectacle or keep to our parlor is of small consequence to the heart of the affair. Every wager is recorded and the ledgers sealed fast before the first rider even mounts, you can be certain of that. Whether the day yields a profit or a loss for my pocket, it will scarce affect our intention to travel there next Monday.”
source_text: “But the question of going to see the race or staying at home has very little to do with the matter. Bets are all booked safely enough before the race begins, you may depend. Whether it is a bad race for me or a good one, will have very little to do with our going there next Monday.”

text: “You shouldn't go there; and yet, hold on. Yes, perhaps just for tonight; I have no better option—more's the pity! Sleep there tonight and stay through tomorrow. Monday is the first day I'm free; so at ten o'clock sharp on Monday morning, meet me at Grey’s Bridge on the edge of town. I’ll bring whatever funds I can scrape together. You won't be in need—I’ll see to that, Fanny; and then I’ll find you a place to stay. Farewell until then. I’m a wretch—but goodbye!”
source_text: “You shan’t go there; yet, wait. Yes, perhaps for to-night; I can do nothing better—worse luck! Sleep there to-night, and stay there to-morrow. Monday is the first free day I have; and on Monday morning, at ten exactly, meet me on Grey’s Bridge just out of the town. I’ll bring all the money I can muster. You shan’t want—I’ll see that, Fanny; then I’ll get you a lodging somewhere. Good-bye till then. I am a brute—but good-bye!”

text: Upon reaching the summit where the road at last leveled, Elara turned her gaze backward. The stranger had risen and was now shrinking away from Julian, descending the gradient with a pained, unsteady stride past the third milestone from Kingsbury. Julian then closed the distance to his wife, vaulted into the phaeton, and snatched the ribbons from her hands. Without uttering a single syllable, he urged the animal into a sudden trot. A visible restlessness possessed him.
source_text: After advancing the distance which completed the ascent of the hill, Bathsheba turned her head. The woman was upon her feet, and Bathsheba saw her withdrawing from Troy, and going feebly down the hill by the third milestone from Casterbridge. Troy then came on towards his wife, stepped into the gig, took the reins from her hand, and without making any observation whipped the horse into a trot. He was rather agitated.

text: Guided by the distant glow of Casterbridge and the touch of her fingers, the woman selected two branches from the piles. These limbs were straight for about three or four feet before splitting into Y-shaped forks. She sat down, snapped off the smaller twigs, and carried the wood onto the road. Positioning a fork under each arm to act as a crutch, she tested them, tentatively putting her full weight—frail as she was—onto the sticks and swinging herself ahead. The girl had fashioned a physical means of support.
source_text: By the aid of the Casterbridge aurora, and by feeling with her hands, the woman selected two sticks from the heaps. These sticks were nearly straight to the height of three or four feet, where each branched into a fork like the letter Y. She sat down, snapped off the small upper twigs, and carried the remainder with her into the road. She placed one of these forks under each arm as a crutch, tested them, timidly threw her whole weight upon them—so little that it was—and swung herself forward. The girl had made for herself a material aid.

text: There she remained, a slumped and featureless mass, for more than ten minutes. A hollow morning wind began to thrum across the flats, unsettling the withered leaves that had lain still since the previous day. With a frantic exertion, the woman maneuvered onto her knees and finally hoisted herself upright. Steadying her frame with a single crutch, she attempted one step, then a second, and a third, wielding her supports now as mere walking-staves. She progressed thus until, descending the slope of Yalbury Hill, another stone marker emerged, and the start of a metal-railed fence appeared. Stumbling toward the first post, she clung to it and surveyed the landscape.
source_text: Here she lay, a shapeless heap, for ten minutes and more. The morning wind began to boom dully over the flats, and to move afresh dead leaves which had lain still since yesterday. The woman desperately turned round upon her knees, and next rose to her feet. Steadying herself by the help of one crutch, she essayed a step, then another, then a third, using the crutches now as walking-sticks only. Thus she progressed till descending Mellstock Hill another milestone appeared, and soon the beginning of an iron-railed fence came into view. She staggered across to the first post, clung to it, and looked around.

text: She was not a woman prone to speaking her thoughts; yet a crisis of the spirit often dissolves the distinctiveness of the frail, just as it clarifies the character of the brave. In a level voice she said once more, “I shall imagine the journey ends at the fifth marker yonder, and no further, and so find the fortitude to reach it.”
source_text: This woman was not given to soliloquy; but extremity of feeling lessens the individuality of the weak, as it increases that of the strong. She said again in the same tone, “I’ll believe that the end lies five posts forward, and no further, and so get strength to pass them.”

text: As six o’clock drew near, stirrings could be heard from within the structure that served as a sanctuary for this exhausted soul. A small door beside the main entrance opened, and a man peered out. He caught sight of the gasping bundle of clothes huddled there, went back inside to fetch a light, and returned. After retreating once more, he reappeared, this time accompanied by two women.
source_text: It was getting on towards six o’clock, and sounds of movement were to be heard inside the building which was the haven of rest to this wearied soul. A little door by the large one was opened, and a man appeared inside. He discerned the panting heap of clothes, went back for a light, and came again. He entered a second time, and returned with two women.

text: Once the morning meal was finished, she felt composed and steady—very much her normal self. She wandered toward the gate, intending to inspect a different section of the farm. Despite her household responsibilities, she still directed the estate's operations personally, though she often found that Gabriel Oak had already anticipated her needs and acted on them. Because of this, she began to view him with the honest devotion of a sister. Occasionally, she did recall him as a former admirer, briefly wondering what sort of husband he might have made, just as she sometimes wondered about Boldwood. Yet Bathsheba, for all her capacity for emotion, was not one for idle daydreaming. Such thoughts were fleeting and surfaced only when Troy’s lack of attention became especially hard to ignore.
source_text: After breakfast she was cool and collected—quite herself in fact—and she rambled to the gate, intending to walk to another quarter of the farm, which she still personally superintended as well as her duties in the house would permit, continually, however, finding herself preceded in forethought by Gabriel Oak, for whom she began to entertain the genuine friendship of a sister. Of course, she sometimes thought of him in the light of an old lover, and had momentary imaginings of what life with him as a husband would have been like; also of life with Boldwood under the same conditions. But Bathsheba, though she could feel, was not much given to futile dreaming, and her musings under this head were short and entirely confined to the times when Troy’s neglect was more than ordinarily evident.

text: A figure appeared on the road who looked much like Mr. Boldwood, and she soon realized it was indeed him. Bathsheba felt a sharp, uncomfortable flush rise to her cheeks as she looked on. While still quite far off, the farmer stopped and gestured to Gabriel Oak, who was on a path cutting through the field. The two men walked toward each other and began what seemed to be a very serious conversation.
source_text: She saw coming up the road a man like Mr. Boldwood. It was Mr. Boldwood. Bathsheba blushed painfully, and watched. The farmer stopped when still a long way off, and held up his hand to Gabriel Oak, who was in a footpath across the field. The two men then approached each other and seemed to engage in earnest conversation.

text: In this fashion, the hour elongated. Arthur Fennel presently approached, laboring behind a handcart of russets destined for the lady’s cellar. Harrison and Jude hailed him, lingering in brief discourse, until the trio separated and Arthur continued his ascent, the barrow creaking as he breasted the slope.
source_text: Thus they continued for a long time. Joseph Poorgrass now passed near them, wheeling a barrow of apples up the hill to Bathsheba’s residence. Boldwood and Gabriel called to him, spoke to him for a few minutes, and then all three parted, Joseph immediately coming up the hill with his barrow.

text: Fetch a harvest of winter greens and blossoms to cast over her casket—bring an abundance, that she might be hidden away in them. Cut branches of flowering viburnum, and dappled box, and yew, and southernwood; yes, and armfuls of late chrysanthemums. And let the old gelding, Trusty, draw her along, for they were well acquainted in better days.
source_text: “Carry with you some evergreens and flowers to put upon her coffin—indeed, gather a great many, and completely bury her in them. Get some boughs of laurustinus, and variegated box, and yew, and boy’s-love; ay, and some bunches of chrysanthemum. And let old Pleasant draw her, because she knew him so well.”

text: One of the men walked over to the side, pulled a piece of chalk from his pocket, and scrawled the name and a few other details across the lid in a large, messy script. (Presumably, such tasks are handled with more sensitivity today, usually with an engraved plate.) He draped a black cloth over the box—shabby but respectable—and the wagon's tailgate was locked back into place. One of the men handed a registration certificate to Poorgrass, and then both went inside, closing the door behind them. Their brief involvement with her had come to a permanent end.
source_text: One of the men then stepped up beside it, took from his pocket a lump of chalk, and wrote upon the cover the name and a few other words in a large scrawling hand. (We believe that they do these things more tenderly now, and provide a plate.) He covered the whole with a black cloth, threadbare, but decent, the tail-board of the waggon was returned to its place, one of the men handed a certificate of registry to Poorgrass, and both entered the door, closing it behind them. Their connection with her, short as it had been, was over for ever.

text: Wayfarers—for the modern species of *sightseer* had not yet diverged into a recognized category in that era—would occasionally observe, as they lifted their eyes to the branch-laden post, that although artists were partial to illustrating a signboard hung in such a fashion, they had never before witnessed so quintessential an example in its functional state. It was beside this same tree that the wain had been parked when Silas Thorne climbed inside on his maiden journey toward Kingsbridge; however, the heavy shroud of night had ensured that both the swinging emblem and the inn itself went entirely unnoticed.
source_text: Travellers—for the variety _tourist_ had hardly developed into a distinct species at this date—sometimes said in passing, when they cast their eyes up to the sign-bearing tree, that artists were fond of representing the signboard hanging thus, but that they themselves had never before noticed so perfect an instance in actual working order. It was near this tree that the waggon was standing into which Gabriel Oak crept on his first journey to Weatherbury; but, owing to the darkness, the sign and the inn had been unobserved.

text: Naturally, you’ll have another glass. A man feels twice his usual self once he’s had a taste. You are filled with such a grand, glowing warmth, and you fall to your labor with a sudden, easy vigor, so that every task snaps along like dry kindling. Of course, too much drink is a vice that leads straight to the Devil in his smoky den; yet many folk lack the natural talent for enjoying a draught, and since we have been blessed with such a sturdy capacity for it, we really ought to make the most of our gift.
source_text: “Of course, you’ll have another drop. A man’s twice the man afterwards. You feel so warm and glorious, and you whop and slap at your work without any trouble, and everything goes on like sticks a-breaking. Too much liquor is bad, and leads us to that horned man in the smoky house; but after all, many people haven’t the gift of enjoying a wet, and since we be highly favoured with a power that way, we should make the most o’t.”

text: It always starts up once I've spent a little while in a pub," Joseph Poorgrass remarked softly. "I begin to see two of everything, as if I were a holy man from the days of Noah, watching them enter the ark... Y-y-yes," he went on, overcome with emotion at the thought of being a cast-off, his eyes welling up. "I feel I’m too decent for this country; I really should have lived in the time of Genesis with the other men of faith. Then no one would have b-b-branded me a d-d-drunkard like this!
source_text: “It always comes on when I have been in a public-house a little time,” said Joseph Poorgrass, meekly. “Yes; I see two of every sort, as if I were some holy man living in the times of King Noah and entering into the ark.... Y-y-y-yes,” he added, becoming much affected by the picture of himself as a person thrown away, and shedding tears; “I feel too good for England: I ought to have lived in Genesis by rights, like the other men of sacrifice, and then I shouldn’t have b-b-been called a d-d-drunkard in such a way!”

text: Julian, observing that not one of the three possessed the sobriety required to pilot the dray for the rest of the way, held his tongue; he simply latched the door against them and strode to the cart, its form blurring into the damp haze and gathering murk of that blighted evening. He wrenched the mare’s nose from the circle of grass she had grazed to the roots, straightened the branches masking the casket, and steered the team forward into the sickly dark.
source_text: Gabriel, seeing that neither of the three was in a fit state to take charge of the waggon for the remainder of the journey, made no reply, but, closing the door again upon them, went across to where the vehicle stood, now getting indistinct in the fog and gloom of this mildewy time. He pulled the horse’s head from the large patch of turf it had eaten bare, readjusted the boughs over the coffin, and drove along through the unwholesome night.

text: In a sudden, desperate bid to shield Bathsheba from the worst of the coming blow, Gabriel glanced back at the chalk lettering scrawled across the coffin lid. The brief inscription read, "Fanny Robin and child." Reaching for his handkerchief, he cautiously erased the last two words until only the name "Fanny Robin" was left behind. With that, he stepped out of the room and made a silent exit through the front door.
source_text: Suddenly, as in a last attempt to save Bathsheba from, at any rate, immediate anguish, he looked again, as he had looked before, at the chalk writing upon the coffin-lid. The scrawl was this simple one, “_Fanny Robin and child_.” Gabriel took his handkerchief and carefully rubbed out the two latter words, leaving visible the inscription “_Fanny Robin_” only. He then left the room, and went out quietly by the front door.

text: “And why should I stir?” Honoria murmured with listless indifference. “The effort is too great—and the hearth is so warm, Bess.” Then, with a sudden, sharp intake of breath, she leaned forward and hissed in a frantic whisper, “Have you heard any dark rumors concerning Alice?” Scarcely had the words been uttered when a shadow of deep, unspeakable shame swept across her countenance, and she broke into a violent torrent of tears.
source_text: “Why don’t I?” said Bathsheba, desultorily. “It isn’t worth while—there’s a fire here, Liddy.” She suddenly exclaimed in an impulsive and excited whisper, “Have you heard anything strange said of Fanny?” The words had no sooner escaped her than an expression of unutterable regret crossed her face, and she burst into tears.

text: Five or ten minutes later, another knock sounded at the door. Liddy returned, stepping a short distance into the room and pausing with uncertainty until she finally spoke. “Maryann has just heard a most peculiar rumor, though I am certain it is false. We will surely learn the truth of the matter in a day or two.”
source_text: In five or ten minutes there was another tap at the door. Liddy reappeared, and coming in a little way stood hesitating, until at length she said, “Maryann has just heard something very strange, but I know it isn’t true. And we shall be sure to know the rights of it in a day or two.”

text: She was suddenly seized by a craving to speak with someone stronger than herself, seeking the fortitude to maintain her dignity and face her mounting suspicions with stoicism. Yet, she found no such ally within her own walls; she was already the most composed woman in the house. She needed to learn patience and the ability to withhold judgment, but she had no teacher. If only she could go to Gabriel Oak! She admired his quiet way of enduring hardship. Boldwood, despite his seemingly deeper and more intense passions, had never mastered the simple lesson Gabriel displayed in every look—that among life's many concerns, his own self-interest was never the most important. Oak viewed the horizon of his circumstances with a meditative detachment, indifferent to his own place in the middle of it all. That was the state she aspired to reach. However, Gabriel was not currently tortured by the same inner uncertainty that haunted her. She was sure he knew the truth about Fanny. If she approached him now and simply asked for the facts, his honor would force him to be honest. The relief would be overwhelming. No further words would be necessary; he understood her so well that no erratic behavior on her part could ever shock him.
source_text: She suddenly felt a longing desire to speak to some one stronger than herself, and so get strength to sustain her surmised position with dignity and her lurking doubts with stoicism. Where could she find such a friend? nowhere in the house. She was by far the coolest of the women under her roof. Patience and suspension of judgement for a few hours were what she wanted to learn, and there was nobody to teach her. Might she but go to Gabriel Oak!—but that could not be. What a way Oak had, she thought, of enduring things. Boldwood, who seemed so much deeper and higher and stronger in feeling than Gabriel, had not yet learnt, any more than she herself, the simple lesson which Oak showed a mastery of by every turn and look he gave—that among the multitude of interests by which he was surrounded, those which affected his personal well-being were not the most absorbing and important in his eyes. Oak meditatively looked upon the horizon of circumstances without any special regard to his own standpoint in the midst. That was how she would wish to be. But then Oak was not racked by incertitude upon the inmost matter of his bosom, as she was at this moment. Oak knew all about Fanny that he wished to know—she felt convinced of that. If she were to go to him now at once and say no more than these few words, “What is the truth of the story?” he would feel bound in honour to tell her. It would be an inexpressible relief. No further speech would need to be uttered. He knew her so well that no eccentricity of behaviour in her would alarm him.

text: She wept profusely over the silent duo resting in the casket—tears born of a tangled source, possessing a quality so elusive and complex that they could be characterized only as something far deeper than mere grief. Surely some remnant of her former passion must have lingered within Fanny’s remains for circumstances to have conspired to bring her back in such a quiet, unforced, yet devastatingly potent way. Fanny had accomplished the singular act—the act of death—through which a lowly existence is transmuted into a dignified one. To this, fate had added their meeting this evening, an event that, in Bathsheba’s fevered perception, transformed her rival’s defeat into victory, her shame into glory, and her misfortune into dominance; it cast a harsh, mocking glare upon Bathsheba herself, imbuing her entire world with a sense of bitter irony.
source_text: Her tears fell fast beside the unconscious pair in the coffin: tears of a complicated origin, of a nature indescribable, almost indefinable except as other than those of simple sorrow. Assuredly their wonted fires must have lived in Fanny’s ashes when events were so shaped as to chariot her hither in this natural, unobtrusive, yet effectual manner. The one feat alone—that of dying—by which a mean condition could be resolved into a grand one, Fanny had achieved. And to that had destiny subjoined this reencounter to-night, which had, in Bathsheba’s wild imagining, turned her companion’s failure to success, her humiliation to triumph, her lucklessness to ascendency; it had thrown over herself a garish light of mockery, and set upon all things about her an ironical smile.

text: Eliza’s features were encircled by those pale, flaxen tresses, and the provenance of the ringlet Arthur had guarded so closely was now beyond any dispute. To Isabel’s fevered mind, the blameless, ivory countenance appeared to glow with a faint, mocking triumph, as if the girl were paying back her own suffering with the pitiless precision of the ancient scriptures: “Fire for fire; bruise for bruise; stripe for stripe.”
source_text: Fanny’s face was framed in by that yellow hair of hers; and there was no longer much room for doubt as to the origin of the curl owned by Troy. In Bathsheba’s heated fancy the innocent white countenance expressed a dim triumphant consciousness of the pain she was retaliating for her pain with all the merciless rigour of the Mosaic law: “Burning for burning; wound for wound: strife for strife.”

text: Bathsheba grew so unsettled by her own mental state that she felt a desperate need to escape herself. The memory of Gabriel Oak kneeling in the dark that night resurfaced, and with a sudden, instinctive urge to follow his lead, she took hold of the thought. She resolved to go down on her knees and attempt to pray. Gabriel had found strength in prayer; she determined that she would as well.
source_text: Bathsheba became at this moment so terrified at her own state of mind that she looked around for some sort of refuge from herself. The vision of Oak kneeling down that night recurred to her, and with the imitative instinct which animates women she seized upon the idea, resolved to kneel, and, if possible, pray. Gabriel had prayed; so would she.

text: At first, he had remained rigidly upright. Now, within the frozen stillness of his body, a faint stir of motion became visible, much as the eye eventually finds light in the depths of a pitch-black night. He began to sink slowly forward. The tension in his face relaxed, his expression of shock shifting into a boundless, profound sorrow. Bathsheba watched him from the other side, her lips still parted and her gaze fractured by distress. The capacity for deep emotion is bound to the depth of one’s character; thus, despite all the hardships Fanny had endured—which were perhaps greater relative to her own frailty—she had likely never experienced the sheer, absolute weight of the agony that now overwhelmed Bathsheba.
source_text: He had originally stood perfectly erect. And now, in the well-nigh congealed immobility of his frame could be discerned an incipient movement, as in the darkest night may be discerned light after a while. He was gradually sinking forwards. The lines of his features softened, and dismay modulated to illimitable sadness. Bathsheba was regarding him from the other side, still with parted lips and distracted eyes. Capacity for intense feeling is proportionate to the general intensity of the nature, and perhaps in all Fanny’s sufferings, much greater relatively to her strength, there never was a time she suffered in an absolute sense what Bathsheba suffered now.

text: There was something so bizarre and jarring about such a vulnerable, childlike plea coming from a woman as capable and independent as Bathsheba. As Troy unclasped her arms from around his neck, he stared at her in utter confusion. It was a startling revelation that all women were fundamentally the same at their core, even those as outwardly different as Fanny and the woman standing before him; he could hardly recognize his proud wife in this desperate figure. It was as if Fanny’s own spirit had taken possession of her body. Yet this lasted only a few seconds. Once the initial surprise faded, his expression shifted back into a cold, domineering look that commanded her silence.
source_text: There was something so abnormal and startling in the childlike pain and simplicity of this appeal from a woman of Bathsheba’s calibre and independence, that Troy, loosening her tightly clasped arms from his neck, looked at her in bewilderment. It was such an unexpected revelation of all women being alike at heart, even those so different in their accessories as Fanny and this one beside him, that Troy could hardly seem to believe her to be his proud wife Bathsheba. Fanny’s own spirit seemed to be animating her frame. But this was the mood of a few instants only. When the momentary surprise had passed, his expression changed to a silencing imperious gaze.

text: If only the wife had stopped there. Yet, in such harrowing circumstances, her decision to speak was perhaps a mistake more easily understood, if not forgiven, than a more prudent silence would have been, now that her rival was merely a corpse. With a strenuous effort of self-control, she reined in the emotions she had been driven to reveal, drawing them back into herself.
source_text: Had the wife now but gone no further. Yet, perhaps, under the harrowing circumstances, to speak out was the one wrong act which can be better understood, if not forgiven in her, than the right and politic one, her rival being now but a corpse. All the feeling she had been betrayed into showing she drew back to herself again by a strenuous effort of self-command.

text: Her gaze wandered further across the landscape. As the first light of morning broke, the chilled atmosphere and soft hues made the frantic impulses and vows of the preceding night appear jarringly vivid. She noticed that her lap and hair were strewn with scarlet and amber leaves, which had drifted from the boughs to settle soundlessly upon her while she fitfully slept. Elara shook her skirts to clear them, causing a host of their kin lying nearby to rise and whirl away on the sudden draft, “resembling phantoms from a sorcerer fleeing.”
source_text: She looked further around. Day was just dawning, and beside its cool air and colours her heated actions and resolves of the night stood out in lurid contrast. She perceived that in her lap, and clinging to her hair, were red and yellow leaves which had come down from the tree and settled silently upon her during her partial sleep. Bathsheba shook her dress to get rid of them, when multitudes of the same family lying round about her rose and fluttered away in the breeze thus created, “like ghosts from an enchanter fleeing.”

text: That evening, the sun set in a deep, blood-like crimson, casting its final glow onto a dark, bruised cloud in the east. Against this somber backdrop, the western face of the church tower—the only part of the building visible from the farmhouse windows—stood out with sharp clarity, its weathercock atop the spire shimmering in the fading light. At six o’clock, the village youth gathered here as they always did for a game of Prisoners’ Base. This location had been used for the ancient game since time immemorial; the old stocks served as a convenient base near the churchyard boundary, where the ground had been beaten as hard and smooth as pavement by the players' feet. She watched the dark-haired boys darting to and fro, their white shirt-sleeves catching the sun, while the quiet evening air was occasionally broken by a shout or a burst of spirited laughter. After about fifteen minutes, the game came to a sudden halt, and the players vaulted over the wall, vanishing behind a yew tree. This tree was partially hidden by a beech that had turned into a mass of golden leaves, its branches forming dark patterns against the bright foliage.
source_text: The sun went down almost blood-red that night, and a livid cloud received its rays in the east. Up against this dark background the west front of the church tower—the only part of the edifice visible from the farm-house windows—rose distinct and lustrous, the vane upon the summit bristling with rays. Hereabouts, at six o’clock, the young men of the village gathered, as was their custom, for a game of Prisoners’ base. The spot had been consecrated to this ancient diversion from time immemorial, the old stocks conveniently forming a base facing the boundary of the churchyard, in front of which the ground was trodden hard and bare as a pavement by the players. She could see the brown and black heads of the young lads darting about right and left, their white shirt-sleeves gleaming in the sun; whilst occasionally a shout and a peal of hearty laughter varied the stillness of the evening air. They continued playing for a quarter of an hour or so, when the game concluded abruptly, and the players leapt over the wall and vanished round to the other side behind a yew-tree, which was also half behind a beech, now spreading in one mass of golden foliage, on which the branches traced black lines.

text: Upon reaching the town, Troy turned down a side street and passed through a set of gates topped by a sign that read, “Lester, stone and marble mason.” Scattered within the yard were headstones of every shape and size, already carved with dedications to the memory of anonymous people who had not yet passed away.
source_text: Reaching the town, Troy descended into a side street and entered a pair of gates surmounted by a board bearing the words, “Lester, stone and marble mason.” Within were lying about stones of all sizes and designs, inscribed as being sacred to the memory of unnamed persons who had not yet died.

text: Troy set his basket down beside the grave and slipped away for a few moments. When he came back, he was armed with a spade and a lantern, the glow of which he cast upon the marble while he read the carved words. He hooked the lantern onto the yew tree’s lowest branch and began taking various flower roots from his basket. There were bundles of snowdrops, hyacinths, and crocus bulbs, along with violets and double daisies to bloom in the early spring, plus carnations, pinks, picotees, lilies of the valley, forget-me-nots, summer’s farewell, meadow-saffron, and more, intended for the later months of the year.
source_text: Troy deposited his basket beside the tomb, and vanished for a few minutes. When he returned he carried a spade and a lantern, the light of which he directed for a few moments upon the marble, whilst he read the inscription. He hung his lantern on the lowest bough of the yew-tree, and took from his basket flower-roots of several varieties. There were bundles of snow-drop, hyacinth and crocus bulbs, violets and double daisies, which were to bloom in early spring, and of carnations, pinks, picotees, lilies of the valley, forget-me-not, summer’s farewell, meadow-saffron and others, for the later seasons of the year.

text: It is often suggested that the most accurate gauge of an artistic era’s vigor is the skill with which its masters execute the grotesque; in the realm of Gothic art, this point is certainly beyond dispute. Weatherbury tower represented an early instance of decorative parapets being used on a parish church instead of a cathedral, making its gargoyles—the necessary companions to such features—unusually striking. They were carved with immense boldness and designed with a level of originality that seemed to push the limits of human imagination. There was a peculiar balance to their deformity, a trait more common in Continental grotesques than in British ones of that period. No two of the eight figures were alike. An observer might conclude that nothing could surpass the hideousness of the carvings on the north side, until they walked around to the south. Of the two situated there, only the one on the southeastern corner is relevant to this account. It was too human to be a dragon, too impish to be a man, too animalistic to be a devil, and not bird-like enough to be a griffin. This repulsive stone being was sculpted as if covered in shriveled hide, with short, pricked ears and eyes that seemed to leap from their sockets. Its fingers and hands were hooked into the corners of its mouth, pulling the jaws apart to ensure the water it vomited could flow freely. While the upper teeth remained, the lower row had been entirely worn away. Crouched there, jutting two feet from the wall that supported its weight, the creature had spent four hundred years sneering at the landscape—silently in the sun, and with a wet, gurgling rattle in the rain.
source_text: It has been sometimes argued that there is no truer criterion of the vitality of any given art-period than the power of the master-spirits of that time in grotesque; and certainly in the instance of Gothic art there is no disputing the proposition. Weatherbury tower was a somewhat early instance of the use of an ornamental parapet in parish as distinct from cathedral churches, and the gurgoyles, which are the necessary correlatives of a parapet, were exceptionally prominent—of the boldest cut that the hand could shape, and of the most original design that a human brain could conceive. There was, so to speak, that symmetry in their distortion which is less the characteristic of British than of Continental grotesques of the period. All the eight were different from each other. A beholder was convinced that nothing on earth could be more hideous than those he saw on the north side until he went round to the south. Of the two on this latter face, only that at the south-eastern corner concerns the story. It was too human to be called like a dragon, too impish to be like a man, too animal to be like a fiend, and not enough like a bird to be called a griffin. This horrible stone entity was fashioned as if covered with a wrinkled hide; it had short, erect ears, eyes starting from their sockets, and its fingers and hands were seizing the corners of its mouth, which they thus seemed to pull open to give free passage to the water it vomited. The lower row of teeth was quite washed away, though the upper still remained. Here and thus, jutting a couple of feet from the wall against which its feet rested as a support, the creature had for four hundred years laughed at the surrounding landscape, voicelessly in dry weather, and in wet with a gurgling and snorting sound.

text: Perhaps for the first time since his youth, Julian, as he lingered beside the disturbed earth of the grave, felt a profound longing to be any man but himself. It is a rare occurrence for one possessed of such vital energy not to feel that the simple fact of his own identity is the very quality that renders his life more promising than the lives of those who might otherwise mirror him. Julian had realized, in his shallow fashion, countless times before, that he could never truly envy the station of another; to possess their world would require a different spirit, and he had never wished for any spirit but his own. He had been indifferent to the scandals of his birth, the turbulence of his career, and the flickering instability of his future, for these were merely the attributes of the central figure in a drama that would be nothing without him; it seemed only natural that the plot would eventually resolve itself into a graceful end. On this particular morning, however, the deception vanished, and Julian was seized by a sudden, sharp hatred for his own being. The immediacy of this change was more illusion than fact. A bank of coral that stops just short of the tide is no more visible to the sailor than if it did not exist, and the final, crowning growth is often mistaken for the creation of a thing that has, in truth, been building toward completion for a long time.
source_text: Almost for the first time in his life, Troy, as he stood by this dismantled grave, wished himself another man. It is seldom that a person with much animal spirit does not feel that the fact of his life being his own is the one qualification which singles it out as a more hopeful life than that of others who may actually resemble him in every particular. Troy had felt, in his transient way, hundreds of times, that he could not envy other people their condition, because the possession of that condition would have necessitated a different personality, when he desired no other than his own. He had not minded the peculiarities of his birth, the vicissitudes of his life, the meteor-like uncertainty of all that related to him, because these appertained to the hero of his story, without whom there would have been no story at all for him; and it seemed to be only in the nature of things that matters would right themselves at some proper date and wind up well. This very morning the illusion completed its disappearance, and, as it were, all of a sudden, Troy hated himself. The suddenness was probably more apparent than real. A coral reef which just comes short of the ocean surface is no more to the horizon than if it had never been begun, and the mere finishing stroke is what often appears to create an event which has long been potentially an accomplished thing.

text: He retreated slowly from the graveside. He made no effort to shovel the earth back, restore the flowers, or repair the site in any way. He simply surrendered his hand and renounced his schemes, both for the present moment and for all time. Departing the churchyard in silence while the townspeople still slept, he cut through the rear pastures and emerged stealthily onto the main road. A short while later, he had vanished from the village entirely.
source_text: He slowly withdrew from the grave. He did not attempt to fill up the hole, replace the flowers, or do anything at all. He simply threw up his cards and forswore his game for that time and always. Going out of the churchyard silently and unobserved—none of the villagers having yet risen—he passed down some fields at the back, and emerged just as secretly upon the high road. Shortly afterwards he had gone from the village.

text: Kingsport! The very utterance of the name seemed to shrink his stature and the gravity of his offenses; a span of some twelve miles now lay as a buffer between them. She felt a profound aversion to soliciting news from Martha concerning her husband’s whereabouts, having until this hour maintained a scrupulous silence on the matter; however, the entire household was already privy to the bitter rupture that had occurred, rendering any veil of secrecy a futile endeavor. Clara had arrived at that weary threshold where the shifting winds of public opinion no longer hold any power to wound.
source_text: Budmouth! The sound of the word carried with it a much diminished perspective of him and his deeds; there were thirteen miles interval betwixt them now. She hated questioning Liddy about her husband’s movements, and indeed had hitherto sedulously avoided doing so; but now all the house knew that there had been some dreadful disagreement between them, and it was futile to attempt disguise. Bathsheba had reached a stage at which people cease to have any appreciative regard for public opinion.

text: Evening settled slowly over the vast, watery expanse ahead. Nearby, where the coast curved to trace a dark ribbon against the horizon, a row of amber sparks began to flicker into view, signaling the location of Budmouth as the lamps were lit along the promenade. The rhythmic thud of their oars was the only distinct sound on the water, and as they rowed through the deepening shadows, the lights grew brighter, each casting a fiery blade deep into the waves. Finally, among the other hazy outlines, the silhouette of the ship they were heading for loomed into sight.
source_text: And now night drooped slowly upon the wide watery levels in front; and at no great distance from them, where the shoreline curved round, and formed a long riband of shade upon the horizon, a series of points of yellow light began to start into existence, denoting the spot to be the site of Budmouth, where the lamps were being lighted along the parade. The cluck of their oars was the only sound of any distinctness upon the sea, and as they laboured amid the thickening shades the lamp-lights grew larger, each appearing to send a flaming sword deep down into the waves before it, until there arose, among other dim shapes of the kind, the form of the vessel for which they were bound.

text: Thus Elara inhabited a world where her grandest designs lay in fragments. She was not of the sort who could manufacture optimism from thin air, distinguishing herself from those more pampered and narrow-minded sisters of the hearth, for whom hope is a mere rhythmic pulse that a warm fire and a full plate are enough to sustain. Recognizing with stark clarity that her misstep had been irredeemable, she resigned herself to her lot, lingering in a frigid stillness as she awaited the final curtain.
source_text: Hence Bathsheba lived in a perception that her purposes were broken off. She was not a woman who could hope on without good materials for the process, differing thus from the less far-sighted and energetic, though more petted ones of the sex, with whom hope goes on as a sort of clockwork which the merest food and shelter are sufficient to wind up; and perceiving clearly that her mistake had been a fatal one, she accepted her position, and waited coldly for the end.
