text: Finding her composure too shaken to guide her horse homeward with the same steadiness she had displayed that morning, Masterson, exercising the utmost tact and consideration for her distress, proposed either to secure a groom to take her place at the whip or to provide her a seat within his own carriage, a vehicle far more commodious than her modest trap. Such entreaties Elowen quietly refused, and the gentleman took his leave without further delay.
source_text: Being hardly in a condition to drive home as she had driven to town, Boldwood, with every delicacy of manner and feeling, offered to get her a driver, or to give her a seat in his phaeton, which was more comfortable than her own conveyance. These proposals Bathsheba gently declined, and the farmer at once departed.

text: Nearly thirty minutes passed before she forced herself to rally, eventually taking her seat and the reins just as she always did—looking, on the surface, as though nothing had changed. She departed the town through a winding side street and drove on slowly, her mind far from the road or the scenery. The first hints of twilight were appearing when she finally reached home; she climbed down without a word, left the horse with the stable boy, and went straight upstairs. Liddy was waiting for her on the landing. The news had arrived in Weatherbury half an hour ahead of her, and Liddy searched her mistress’s face for some sign of the truth. Bathsheba had nothing to say.
source_text: About half-an-hour later she invigorated herself by an effort, and took her seat and the reins as usual—in external appearance much as if nothing had happened. She went out of the town by a tortuous back street, and drove slowly along, unconscious of the road and the scene. The first shades of evening were showing themselves when Bathsheba reached home, where, silently alighting and leaving the horse in the hands of the boy, she proceeded at once upstairs. Liddy met her on the landing. The news had preceded Bathsheba to Weatherbury by half-an-hour, and Liddy looked inquiringly into her mistress’s face. Bathsheba had nothing to say.

text: Bathsheba reflected on how convinced everyone else seemed, finding it peculiar that she could not share their certainty. A startling thought struck her, bringing a sudden heat to her face. What if Troy had pursued Fanny into the next life? Could he have planned his own end, yet managed to make it look like a mere accident? However, this vision of how reality might diverge from appearances—intensified by her old resentment toward Fanny and the profound regret he had shown that night—did not prevent her from seeing a more probable truth. It was a possibility less tragic in nature, but for her, one that would be far more ruinous.
source_text: Then Bathsheba said to herself that others were assured in their opinion; strange that she should not be. A strange reflection occurred to her, causing her face to flush. Suppose that Troy had followed Fanny into another world. Had he done this intentionally, yet contrived to make his death appear like an accident? Nevertheless, this thought of how the apparent might differ from the real—made vivid by her bygone jealousy of Fanny, and the remorse he had shown that night—did not blind her to the perception of a likelier difference, less tragic, but to herself far more disastrous.

text: "They were bound to one another, heart and soul; it is only right they should depart as one," she murmured. "I was a ghost to their union, an intruder; why then should I cling to this remnant of her?" She gathered the lock in her palm and held it poised above the hungry grate. "Yet—I shall not burn it. I will preserve it in remembrance of her, the wretched girl!" she added, jerking her hand back from the heat.
source_text: “He was hers and she was his; they should be gone together,” she said. “I am nothing to either of them, and why should I keep her hair?” She took it in her hand, and held it over the fire. “No—I’ll not burn it—I’ll keep it in memory of her, poor thing!” she added, snatching back her hand.

text: In the eyes of those of more mature years, Bathsheba was perhaps more captivating now than ever before. The wilder impulses of her spirit had been refined; the ethereal vision she once embodied had proven herself grounded enough for the humble requirements of everyday life. She had successfully transitioned into this more reflective chapter of her existence without sacrificing much of the radiance that had defined her youth.
source_text: To the eyes of the middle-aged, Bathsheba was perhaps additionally charming just now. Her exuberance of spirit was pruned down; the original phantom of delight had shown herself to be not too bright for human nature’s daily food, and she had been able to enter this second poetical phase without losing much of the first in the process.

text: Shepherds arriving from distant regions set out two or three days, or sometimes a full week, before the fair. They drove their livestock slowly, covering no more than ten or twelve miles a day, and stopped each night at pre-selected fields rented along the way. There, the animals finally grazed after a day spent on the move without food. The shepherd walked at the back of his flock, a pack containing his supplies for the week cinched to his shoulders, using his crook as a staff for the long journey. Along the route, some sheep would inevitably grow weary or start to limp, and occasionally a lamb was born mid-trek. To manage these occurrences, a pony and cart often accompanied the flocks from the more remote areas, providing a ride for the weak or injured for the rest of the trip.
source_text: Shepherds who attended with their flocks from long distances started from home two or three days, or even a week, before the fair, driving their charges a few miles each day—not more than ten or twelve—and resting them at night in hired fields by the wayside at previously chosen points, where they fed, having fasted since morning. The shepherd of each flock marched behind, a bundle containing his kit for the week strapped upon his shoulders, and in his hand his crook, which he used as the staff of his pilgrimage. Several of the sheep would get worn and lame, and occasionally a lambing occurred on the road. To meet these contingencies, there was frequently provided, to accompany the flocks from the remoter points, a pony and waggon into which the weakly ones were taken for the remainder of the journey.

text: The Weatherbury estates were not a great distance from the hill, rendering such complex arrangements unnecessary for their journey. Yet the combined flocks of Bathsheba and Farmer Boldwood comprised a vast and precious assembly that required considerable care; for this reason, Gabriel, accompanied by Boldwood’s shepherd and Cain Ball, escorted them through the ancient, fading town of Kingsbere and up toward the plateau, with old George the dog following steadily in their wake.
source_text: The Weatherbury Farms, however, were no such long distance from the hill, and those arrangements were not necessary in their case. But the large united flocks of Bathsheba and Farmer Boldwood formed a valuable and imposing multitude which demanded much attention, and on this account Gabriel, in addition to Boldwood’s shepherd and Cain Ball, accompanied them along the way, through the decayed old town of Kingsbere, and upward to the plateau,—old George the dog of course behind them.

text: Before the morning was well underway, these thousands of gasping, noisy, and exhausted creatures had been herded into their stalls, with each flock’s dog tethered to the corner of its enclosure. Narrow walkways ran between the pens, which were soon teeming with merchants and buyers from across the region.
source_text: All these bleating, panting, and weary thousands had entered and were penned before the morning had far advanced, the dog belonging to each flock being tied to the corner of the pen containing it. Alleys for pedestrians intersected the pens, which soon became crowded with buyers and sellers from far and near.

text: “Now,” Coggan said, appealing in a sincere voice to the crowd that stood huddled against his shoulders, “did you ever hear of a woman so entirely unreasonable? I swear to you, neighbors, if I could only escape this human cider-press, those cursed women could ruin the whole display for all I care!”
source_text: “Now,” said Coggan, appealing in an earnest voice to the public at large as it stood clustered about his shoulder-blades, “did ye ever hear such onreasonable woman as that? Upon my carcase, neighbours, if I could only get out of this cheese-wring, the damn women might eat the show for me!”

text: Silas remained quiet, appearing content to be mollified for his companion’s sake, and they slowly neared the bottom of the gangway. Barnaby was flattened like a wooden puppet, and the small coin for his passage, which he had gripped tightly for the last hour, had grown so fervently hot in the damp clench of his agitated hand that the woman in tinsel and brass rings set with paste jewels, her visage and throat masked in white powder, who took the payment from him, let it fall at once, dreading that some mischief had been used to scald her skin. So they passed inside, and the heavy canvas, to a spectator in the field, became distended with a thousand small knots like those on a bag of grain, pushed out by the frantic press of heads, shoulders, and joints within.
source_text: Jan held his tongue, as if he had no objection to be pacified to please a friend, and they gradually reached the foot of the ladder, Poorgrass being flattened like a jumping-jack, and the sixpence, for admission, which he had got ready half-an-hour earlier, having become so reeking hot in the tight squeeze of his excited hand that the woman in spangles, brazen rings set with glass diamonds, and with chalked face and shoulders, who took the money of him, hastily dropped it again from a fear that some trick had been played to burn her fingers. So they all entered, and the cloth of the tent, to the eyes of an observer on the outside, became bulged into innumerable pimples such as we observe on a sack of potatoes, caused by the various human heads, backs, and elbows at high pressure within.

text: Peering through a slit in his dressing tent to scout the arena before entering, Troy discovered his wife seated in an elevated position, reigning as the tournament’s queen. He recoiled in sudden agitation; though his disguise masked his features, he was struck by the certainty that she would recognize his voice. Throughout the day, he had entertained the thought that someone from Weatherbury might appear and identify him, yet he had brushed the risk aside. "Let them see me," he had thought carelessly. But faced with Bathsheba herself, the reality proved far more daunting than his imagination had suggested. He realized now, with a sense of dread, that he had not nearly accounted for the gravity of such an encounter.
source_text: Troy, on peeping from his dressing-tent through a slit for a reconnoitre before entering, saw his unconscious wife on high before him as described, sitting as queen of the tournament. He started back in utter confusion, for although his disguise effectually concealed his personality, he instantly felt that she would be sure to recognize his voice. He had several times during the day thought of the possibility of some Weatherbury person or other appearing and recognizing him; but he had taken the risk carelessly. If they see me, let them, he had said. But here was Bathsheba in her own person; and the reality of the scene was so much intenser than any of his prefigurings that he felt he had not half enough considered the point.

text: The fair’s most expansive dining tent was managed by a publican from a nearby village. It was regarded as a perfectly respectable establishment for refreshment and repose, as "Host Trencher"—the playful moniker given to him by the regional press—was a solid, well-respected figure known for his culinary services throughout the district. The pavilion was partitioned into first- and second-class sections, with a private nook at the far end of the superior side reserved for the elite. This inner sanctum was separated from the main area by a serving counter, where the proprietor himself hurried to and fro in his shirt-sleeves and a white apron, appearing so comfortable in his surroundings that one might think he had spent his entire existence beneath a canvas roof. Within this secluded retreat sat a table and chairs that, once the candles were struck, presented an image of warmth and elegance, complete with a steaming urn, silver-plated pots for tea and coffee, fine porcelain cups, and rich fruit cakes.
source_text: The largest refreshment booth in the fair was provided by an innkeeper from a neighbouring town. This was considered an unexceptionable place for obtaining the necessary food and rest: Host Trencher (as he was jauntily called by the local newspaper) being a substantial man of high repute for catering through all the country round. The tent was divided into first and second-class compartments, and at the end of the first-class division was a yet further enclosure for the most exclusive, fenced off from the body of the tent by a luncheon-bar, behind which the host himself stood bustling about in white apron and shirt-sleeves, and looking as if he had never lived anywhere but under canvas all his life. In these penetralia were chairs and a table, which, on candles being lighted, made quite a cozy and luxurious show, with an urn, plated tea and coffee pots, china teacups, and plum cakes.

text: “I appreciate it,” Bathsheba said. “But I really must be on my way now. It was quite negligent of that man to keep me waiting until this late hour. I would have departed two hours ago if it weren’t for him. I hadn’t planned on coming inside at all, but there is nothing quite so restorative as a cup of tea, though I never would have had one without your help.”
source_text: “Thank you,” said Bathsheba. “But I must be going at once. It was great neglect in that man to keep me waiting here till so late. I should have gone two hours ago, if it had not been for him. I had no idea of coming in here; but there’s nothing so refreshing as a cup of tea, though I should never have got one if you hadn’t helped me.”

text: The original plan for the return to Weatherbury was for Oak to replace Poorgrass at the reins of Bathsheba’s carriage. By late afternoon, it was obvious that Joseph was suffering from his usual bout of double vision, rendering him an unreliable driver and protector. Gabriel, however, was so burdened by the management of Boldwood’s remaining sheep that Bathsheba decided, without a word to anyone, to drive herself home. She had often done so from the Casterbridge Market and was content to trust in providence for a safe trip. But after crossing paths with Boldwood at the refreshment tent—a meeting that was, at least for her, quite accidental—she found it difficult to decline his offer to accompany her on horseback. Twilight had fallen before she realized it, yet Boldwood insisted there was no need for worry, as the moon would rise within the half-hour.
source_text: The arrangement for getting back again to Weatherbury had been that Oak should take the place of Poorgrass in Bathsheba’s conveyance and drive her home, it being discovered late in the afternoon that Joseph was suffering from his old complaint, a multiplying eye, and was, therefore, hardly trustworthy as coachman and protector to a woman. But Oak had found himself so occupied, and was full of so many cares relative to those portions of Boldwood’s flocks that were not disposed of, that Bathsheba, without telling Oak or anybody, resolved to drive home herself, as she had many times done from Casterbridge Market, and trust to her good angel for performing the journey unmolested. But having fallen in with Farmer Boldwood accidentally (on her part at least) at the refreshment-tent, she found it impossible to refuse his offer to ride on horseback beside her as escort. It had grown twilight before she was aware, but Boldwood assured her that there was no cause for uneasiness, as the moon would be up in half-an-hour.

text: “Is it truly a righteous path? The indiscretions of my earlier days have proven that a woman beneath the world’s gaze must exercise the utmost vigilance just to preserve a modicum of respect, and I am desperate to remain blameless in this affair. And six years—why, we could all be resting in the churchyard by then, even if Julian never returns, though one cannot rule out his reappearance. Such reflections make the whole arrangement seem quite foolish. Is it not a wild idea, Silas? How he ever imagined such a thing is beyond my understanding. But tell me, is it wicked? You are my senior; you have seen more of life than I.”
source_text: “But is it right? Some rash acts of my past life have taught me that a watched woman must have very much circumspection to retain only a very little credit, and I do want and long to be discreet in this! And six years—why we may all be in our graves by that time, even if Mr. Troy does not come back again, which he may not impossibly do! Such thoughts give a sort of absurdity to the scheme. Now, isn’t it preposterous, Gabriel? However he came to dream of it, I cannot think. But is it wrong? You know—you are older than I.”

text: “It seems to me that your lack of affection is the very thing that strips this arrangement of its guilt. Had you been driven by some feverish impulse to ignore the shadow of your husband’s disappearance, it might well be a sin; yet a dispassionate pact made merely to assist him feels altogether different. To my mind, ma’am, the true transgression lies in the prospect of wedlock with a man you do not honestly and deeply love.”
source_text: “Well, your want of love seems to me the one thing that takes away harm from such an agreement with him. If wild heat had to do wi’ it, making ye long to over-come the awkwardness about your husband’s vanishing, it mid be wrong; but a cold-hearted agreement to oblige a man seems different, somehow. The real sin, ma’am in my mind, lies in thinking of ever wedding wi’ a man you don’t love honest and true.”

text: As the shadows lengthened, the hearth was built up within the vast, low-beamed parlor where the main stairs terminated, and the floor was stripped of its furnishings to accommodate the feet of the dancers. The centerpiece of the night’s fire—a gargantuan, unsplit length of timber—proved so burdensome that no man could lift or roll it; consequently, as the guests’ arrival neared, a pair of workers could be seen laboring to drag it into position, plying levers and rattling chains to force the great weight home.
source_text: As it grew later the fire was made up in the large long hall into which the staircase descended, and all encumbrances were cleared out for dancing. The log which was to form the back-brand of the evening fire was the uncleft trunk of a tree, so unwieldy that it could be neither brought nor rolled to its place; and accordingly two men were to be observed dragging and heaving it in by chains and levers as the hour of assembly drew near.

text: Boldwood had never shown such fussiness or been quite so impossible to satisfy regarding the cut of his clothes. As the tailor circled him—cinching the waist, adjusting the sleeves, and flattening the lapels—Boldwood remained intently engaged, a stark departure from his usual apathy. There had been times when the farmer would have dismissed such trivialities as immature, yet he offered no sharp or weary reprimand to this man who treated a minor fold in the fabric with the same gravity as a tectonic catastrophe in a distant land. Eventually, Boldwood admitted he was content enough and settled the account; the tailor exited the room at the exact moment Oak arrived to provide his daily update on the farm’s progress.
source_text: Never had Boldwood been so fastidious, unreasonable about the fit, and generally difficult to please. The tailor walked round and round him, tugged at the waist, pulled the sleeve, pressed out the collar, and for the first time in his experience Boldwood was not bored. Times had been when the farmer had exclaimed against all such niceties as childish, but now no philosophic or hasty rebuke whatever was provoked by this man for attaching as much importance to a crease in the coat as to an earthquake in South America. Boldwood at last expressed himself nearly satisfied, and paid the bill, the tailor passing out of the door just as Oak came in to report progress for the day.

text: “Nice work, Pennyways,” Troy chuckled, though a distinct note of worry crept into his voice as he went on. “What I really need to know is this: do you believe there’s actually something developing between her and Boldwood? I swear, I never would have imagined it! She must truly despise me. Have you managed to find out if she’s been encouraging his attention?”
source_text: “Ha-ha! Well done, Pennyways,” Troy had laughed, but it was with some anxiety that he said, “Now, what I want to know is this, do you think there’s really anything going on between her and Boldwood? Upon my soul, I should never have believed it! How she must detest me! Have you found out whether she has encouraged him?”

text: “I wouldn’t be too certain of that.... She is a remarkable specimen, Higgins, wouldn’t you say? Confess that you’ve never encountered a more noble or dazzling woman in all your days. On my word, when I beheld her again, I marveled at what sort of man I must be to have stayed away from her side for so long. And then there was that confounded spectacle holding me back—a burden I’ve at last cast off, mercifully.” He drew on his cigar in silence for a time, then inquired, “How did she strike you when you walked past her yesterday?”
source_text: “I’m not so sure of that.... She’s a handsome woman, Pennyways, is she not? Own that you never saw a finer or more splendid creature in your life. Upon my honour, when I set eyes upon her that day I wondered what I could have been made of to be able to leave her by herself so long. And then I was hampered with that bothering show, which I’m free of at last, thank the stars.” He smoked on awhile, and then added, “How did she look when you passed by yesterday?”

text: Boldwood lingered alone in his chamber for a time, fully dressed and prepared to welcome his guests. The nervous preoccupation he had felt regarding his appearance slowly ebbed away, giving way to a sense of profound gravity. He peered out from the window, observing the faint silhouettes of the trees against the horizon as the gathering twilight deepened into the dark of night.
source_text: As Boldwood continued awhile in his room alone—ready and dressed to receive his company—the mood of anxiety about his appearance seemed to pass away, and to be succeeded by a deep solemnity. He looked out of the window, and regarded the dim outline of the trees upon the sky, and the twilight deepening to darkness.

text: Why don't you simply send her a letter? You have certainly found yourself in a peculiar predicament, Sergeant. Should you choose to return, all these matters will inevitably be exposed, and they will hardly cast you in a favorable light. Truly, were I in your position, I would remain exactly as you are—an unmarried man known only as Francis. A decent wife is well and good, but even the finest spouse is not nearly as desirable as having no wife at all. That is my honest opinion, and I have been regarded as a rather shrewd fellow in my time.
source_text: “Why not write to her? ’Tis a very queer corner that you have got into, sergeant. You see all these things will come to light if you go back, and they won’t sound well at all. Faith, if I was you I’d even bide as you be—a single man of the name of Francis. A good wife is good, but the best wife is not so good as no wife at all. Now that’s my outspoke mind, and I’ve been called a long-headed feller here and there.”

text: “Listen, sergeant, my advice is this: if I were in your shoes, I’d head back overseas where you just came from—it isn’t too late to make a move. I wouldn’t go stirring up trouble and ruining your reputation just for the sake of living with her. That whole business about your acting is bound to be exposed, you know, despite what you think. Good lord, there’s going to be an absolute uproar if you show up right now, right in the middle of Boldwood’s Christmas party!”
source_text: “Well, sergeant, all I say is this, that if I were you I’d go abroad again where I came from—’tisn’t too late to do it now. I wouldn’t stir up the business and get a bad name for the sake of living with her—for all that about your play-acting is sure to come out, you know, although you think otherwise. My eyes and limbs, there’ll be a racket if you go back just now—in the middle of Boldwood’s Christmasing!”

text: “I truly wish we had shared the news right away,” the first man continued, sounding troubled. “This might lead to more damage than we realize. It is going to be a heavy burden for poor Mr. Boldwood. I wish Troy were—well, may God forgive me for such a thought! He’s a scoundrel for playing such cruel games with his wife. Nothing has gone well in Weatherbury since the day he arrived. Now, I don't have the heart to go inside. Let's stop at Warren’s for a few minutes first, shall we, neighbors?”
source_text: “I wish we had told of the report at once,” the first uneasily continued. “More harm may come of this than we know of. Poor Mr. Boldwood, it will be hard upon en. I wish Troy was in—Well, God forgive me for such a wish! A scoundrel to play a poor wife such tricks. Nothing has prospered in Weatherbury since he came here. And now I’ve no heart to go in. Let’s look into Warren’s for a few minutes first, shall us, neighbours?”

text: “But it really is! This is something that concerns everyone,” Samway insisted. “We know for a fact that the master is on the wrong path and she’s completely unaware of the truth. We ought to tell them immediately. Laban, you're the one who knows her best—you should be the one to go and ask for a word with her.”
source_text: “But it is! ’Tis a thing which is everybody’s business,” said Samway. “We know very well that master’s on a wrong tack, and that she’s quite in the dark, and we should let ’em know at once. Laban, you know her best—you’d better go and ask to speak to her.”

text: “You are equivocating! I speak, of course, of the pledge. I have no desire to impose myself or to invite the scrutiny of the world. But I must have your word. Think of it as a sterile contract, an arrangement between two parties long past the turbulence of desire.” Sterling knew the falsehood of his own words; he was consumed by the very heat he denied, yet he understood that this frigid pretense was the sole condition of her audience. “A commitment to wed when five years and nine months have lapsed. Surely, you must see that you owe me this much!”
source_text: “Now, that’s evasion! Why, the promise. I don’t want to intrude upon you at all, or to let it become known to anybody. But do give your word! A mere business compact, you know, between two people who are beyond the influence of passion.” Boldwood knew how false this picture was as regarded himself; but he had proved that it was the only tone in which she would allow him to approach her. “A promise to marry me at the end of five years and three-quarters. You owe it to me!”

text: Nevertheless, it had little impact now, for she spoke in a flat, toneless murmur that served as evidence for her own words: “I have no feelings about this at all. I am completely uncertain of the right course of action in this predicament, and I have no one to guide me. But I will give my word, if I must. I offer it as the repayment of a debt, contingent, of course, on my becoming a widow.”
source_text: However, it had not much effect now, for she said, in a passionless murmur which was in itself a proof of her words: “I have no feeling in the matter at all. And I don’t at all know what is right to do in my difficult position, and I have nobody to advise me. But I give my promise, if I must. I give it as the rendering of a debt, conditionally, of course, on my being a widow.”

text: “Oh, I cannot say—please, let me go!” she cried, her breath quickening as her chest began to heave. “I am terrified of what I should do! I truly wish to be fair to you, yet to do so feels like a betrayal of myself, and perhaps even a sin. There is such deep uncertainty regarding his death, and the thought is horrifying; let me consult a solicitor, Mr. Boldwood, to see if I am truly free to act!”
source_text: “Oh, I don’t know, pray let me go!” she said, her bosom beginning to rise. “I am afraid what to do! I want to be just to you, and to be that seems to be wronging myself, and perhaps it is breaking the commandments. There is considerable doubt of his death, and then it is dreadful; let me ask a solicitor, Mr. Boldwood, if I ought or no!”

text: The assembly turned in dazed confusion toward Thorne. Positioned behind him as he stood by the stone hearth was a gun-rack, a typical fixture of these country manors, built to house two barrels. When Elara shrieked within her husband’s violent embrace, the mask of wretched sorrow on Thorne’s face shattered. His vessels bulged with a sudden heat, and a manic brilliance ignited in his eyes. He wheeled around, snatched a rifle from its rest, primed the mechanism, and immediately leveled the blast at Julian.
source_text: In bewilderment they turned their eyes to Boldwood. At his back, as stood before the fireplace, was a gun-rack, as is usual in farmhouses, constructed to hold two guns. When Bathsheba had cried out in her husband’s grasp, Boldwood’s face of gnashing despair had changed. The veins had swollen, and a frenzied look had gleamed in his eye. He had turned quickly, taken one of the guns, cocked it, and at once discharged it at Troy.

text: “Elias,” she murmured mechanically as he crossed the threshold, tilting up a visage so hollowed that only its familiar architecture remained to mark her identity, all other color having vanished from the portrait. “Ride to Blackwood this instant for a physician. It is, I fear, a futile errand, but go. Mr. Sterling has shot my husband.”
source_text: “Gabriel,” she said, automatically, when he entered, turning up a face of which only the well-known lines remained to tell him it was hers, all else in the picture having faded quite. “Ride to Casterbridge instantly for a surgeon. It is, I believe, useless, but go. Mr. Boldwood has shot my husband.”

text: She was carried to a back room, where the medical attention that had been futile for Troy proved vital for Bathsheba. She succumbed to a string of fainting spells that appeared dangerously severe for a time. Once she was settled in bed and Oak received word that there was no immediate cause for alarm regarding her recovery, he left the house. Liddy stayed behind to watch over her mistress, listening as Bathsheba spent the long, agonizing hours of that miserable night whispering in a low moan, “It’s all my fault—how can I keep going? Oh God, how can I live like this?”
source_text: They took her away into a further room, and the medical attendance which had been useless in Troy’s case was invaluable in Bathsheba’s, who fell into a series of fainting-fits that had a serious aspect for a time. The sufferer was got to bed, and Oak, finding from the bulletins that nothing really dreadful was to be apprehended on her score, left the house. Liddy kept watch in Bathsheba’s chamber, where she heard her mistress, moaning in whispers through the dull slow hours of that wretched night: “Oh it is my fault—how can I live! O Heaven, how can I live!”

text: Time moved forward quickly into the month of March, to a blustery day that lacked both the warmth of the sun and the crispness of frost or dew. At the summit of Yalbury Hill, halfway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge where the main road crosses the ridge, a large crowd had assembled, their gazes frequently fixed on the northern horizon. This gathering consisted of a local throng, a contingent of javelin-men, and two trumpeters; in their center stood several carriages, one of which held the high sheriff. Among the spectators, many of whom had climbed the steep embankments flanking the road, were several men and boys from Weatherbury—including Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.
source_text: We pass rapidly on into the month of March, to a breezy day without sunshine, frost, or dew. On Yalbury Hill, about midway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge, where the turnpike road passes over the crest, a numerous concourse of people had gathered, the eyes of the greater number being frequently stretched afar in a northerly direction. The groups consisted of a throng of idlers, a party of javelin-men, and two trumpeters, and in the midst were carriages, one of which contained the high sheriff. With the idlers, many of whom had mounted to the top of a cutting formed for the road, were several Weatherbury men and boys—among others Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.

text: “I’m hoping for the best outcome,” Coggan remarked, “though I know it’s bound to be a grim one. Regardless, I won’t be attending the trial, and I’d suggest that those of you who aren't required to be there stay home. It would only rattle him further to see us all staring at him as if he were some kind of public spectacle.”
source_text: “Well, I hope for the best,” said Coggan, “though bad that must be. However, I shan’t go to the trial, and I’d advise the rest of ye that bain’t wanted to bide away. ’Twill disturb his mind more than anything to see us there staring at him as if he were a show.”

text: With her features concealed behind the mask of her fingers, Elara failed to observe a shadow that glided silently into the vestibule. The man, catching sight of her, made a tentative motion to withdraw, but then hesitated, remaining to study her in the gloom. It was some minutes before she finally looked up, her countenance glistening with tears and her eyes veiled by a watery film. "Mr. Sterling," she breathed, her composure shattered, "how long have you been watching me?"
source_text: Owing to Bathsheba’s face being buried in her hands she did not notice a form which came quietly into the porch, and on seeing her, first moved as if to retreat, then paused and regarded her. Bathsheba did not raise her head for some time, and when she looked round her face was wet, and her eyes drowned and dim. “Mr. Oak,” exclaimed she, disconcerted, “how long have you been here?”

text: Bathsheba headed home, her thoughts caught up in a fresh anxiety. Since this trouble was more an irritation than a tragedy, it proved beneficial by drawing her away from the persistent melancholy of her existence. She pondered Oak’s behavior and his apparent wish to avoid her, recalling various recent interactions. Though these moments seemed minor individually, they collectively suggested a clear reluctance to be near her. Eventually, it hit her with a sharp sense of grief that her final loyal follower was about to desert her. The man who had trusted her and defended her when the rest of the world turned away had finally, like everyone else, grown tired of the old loyalty, leaving her to face her struggles entirely alone.
source_text: Bathsheba went home, her mind occupied with a new trouble, which being rather harassing than deadly was calculated to do good by diverting her from the chronic gloom of her life. She was set thinking a great deal about Oak and of his wish to shun her; and there occurred to Bathsheba several incidents of her latter intercourse with him, which, trivial when singly viewed, amounted together to a perceptible disinclination for her society. It broke upon her at length as a great pain that her last old disciple was about to forsake her and flee. He who had believed in her and argued on her side when all the rest of the world was against her, had at last like the others become weary and neglectful of the old cause, and was leaving her to fight her battles alone.

text: The harvest months ebbed away under a shroud of dismal speculation, and the Nativity dawned, rounding out a full year of her recognized bereavement and more than two of her solitary existence. Searching her conscience, she found it remarkably odd that the memory most synonymous with the season—the catastrophe at Sterling’s estate—failed to stir her pulse; rather, she was gripped by a piercing belief that the community had cast her out, with Thorne acting as the silent captain of those who turned away. As she departed the sanctuary that morning, she cast her eyes about, longing for Thorne—whose resonant bass had echoed from the choir loft with a galling nonchalance—to perhaps wait for her along the path as in years past. He was there, trailing her down the walk as he always had. Yet, when Elena glanced back, he shifted his focus, and the moment he cleared the lych-gate and found the slightest pretext for a different route, he veered off and was lost to the shadows.
source_text: The autumn wore away gloomily enough amid these melancholy conjectures, and Christmas-day came, completing a year of her legal widowhood, and two years and a quarter of her life alone. On examining her heart it appeared beyond measure strange that the subject of which the season might have been supposed suggestive—the event in the hall at Boldwood’s—was not agitating her at all; but instead, an agonizing conviction that everybody abjured her—for what she could not tell—and that Oak was the ringleader of the recusants. Coming out of church that day she looked round in hope that Oak, whose bass voice she had heard rolling out from the gallery overhead in a most unconcerned manner, might chance to linger in her path in the old way. There he was, as usual, coming down the path behind her. But on seeing Bathsheba turn, he looked aside, and as soon as he got beyond the gate, and there was the barest excuse for a divergence, he made one, and vanished.

text: A vibrant hearth-glow pulsed against the glass, though the room beyond appeared quite vacant. She struck the door with a trembling hand, suddenly wary of the scandal that might attend a solitary woman visiting a bachelor’s house, regardless of his role as her supervisor or the professional nature of her errand. Then Julian pulled the door open, and a beam of moonlight illuminated the breadth of his brow.
source_text: A lively firelight shone from the window, but nobody was visible in the room. She tapped nervously, and then thought it doubtful if it were right for a single woman to call upon a bachelor who lived alone, although he was her manager, and she might be supposed to call on business without any real impropriety. Gabriel opened the door, and the moon shone upon his forehead.

text: Gabriel gazed at her for a long while, but the weak glow from the hearth revealed little of her expression. "Bathsheba," he whispered, a mix of tenderness and astonishment in his voice as he drew near. "If I could only be sure of one thing—if I knew whether you would ever let me love you, win your heart, and marry you after all—if I only knew that!"
source_text: Gabriel looked her long in the face, but the firelight being faint there was not much to be seen. “Bathsheba,” he said, tenderly and in surprise, and coming closer: “if I only knew one thing—whether you would allow me to love you and win you, and marry you after all—if I only knew that!”

text: I’m so happy I came!" she said with a sigh of relief, standing up from her seat. "I’ve thought about you so much more since I got the impression you didn’t even want to see me again. But I have to get going now, or I’ll be missed. Honestly, Gabriel," she added, laughing softly as they walked to the door, "it feels exactly as if I’ve come here to woo you—how scandalous!
source_text: “Oh, how glad I am I came!” she exclaimed, thankfully, as she rose from her seat. “I have thought so much more of you since I fancied you did not want even to see me again. But I must be going now, or I shall be missed. Why Gabriel,” she said, with a slight laugh, as they went to the door, “it seems exactly as if I had come courting you—how dreadful!”

text: The pair shared a hushed evening tea in Elara’s sitting-room later that same day, for it was settled that Silas should take up residence there. He was, as yet, without capital, a dwelling, or any effects of significant merit—notwithstanding his steady progress toward their acquisition—whereas Elara possessed a comparative surfeit of all three.
source_text: The two sat down very quietly to tea in Bathsheba’s parlour in the evening of the same day, for it had been arranged that Farmer Oak should go there to live, since he had as yet neither money, house, nor furniture worthy of the name, though he was on a sure way towards them, whilst Bathsheba was, comparatively, in a plethora of all three.

text: “My thanks; my thanks to you all,” Silas remarked. “A morsel and a draught shall be dispatched to The Crown for your pleasure straightway. I had a notion that we might well receive some such greeting from our old comrades, and I was even now saying as much to my bride.”
source_text: “Thank ye; thank ye all,” said Gabriel. “A bit and a drop shall be sent to Warren’s for ye at once. I had a thought that we might very likely get a salute of some sort from our old friends, and I was saying so to my wife but now.”

text: Known collectively as “The Iron Wastes,” a name bestowed upon the melancholy setting of this chronicle, are a dozen distinct moorlands merged into one; these tracts are fundamentally identical in their grim nature and outward form, though their ancient wholeness is now partially obscured by invasive patches of farmland—reclaimed with varying degrees of fortune—and by the deliberate geometry of new-grown forests.
source_text: Under the general name of “Egdon Heath,” which has been given to the sombre scene of the story, are united or typified heaths of various real names, to the number of at least a dozen; these being virtually one in character and aspect, though their original unity, or partial unity, is now somewhat disguised by intrusive strips and slices brought under the plough with varying degrees of success, or planted to woodland.

text: As a Saturday afternoon in November neared the hour of twilight, the sprawling, open wilderness of Egdon Heath grew darker with each passing minute. Above, a pale expanse of cloud obscured the sky like a vast tent, for which the entire moor served as the floor.
source_text: A Saturday afternoon in November was approaching the time of twilight, and the vast tract of unenclosed wild known as Egdon Heath embrowned itself moment by moment. Overhead the hollow stretch of whitish cloud shutting out the sky was as a tent which had the whole heath for its floor.

text: The landscape currently existed in total harmony with the human spirit—neither hideous nor mundane, but rather, like man, neglected yet persistent. It remained remarkably vast and enigmatic in its dark, unchanging character. Just as with those who have spent years in isolation, a sense of withdrawal marked its features. It possessed a solitary aspect that seemed to whisper of impending tragedy.
source_text: It was at present a place perfectly accordant with man’s nature—neither ghastly, hateful, nor ugly; neither commonplace, unmeaning, nor tame; but, like man, slighted and enduring; and withal singularly colossal and mysterious in its swarthy monotony. As with some persons who have long lived apart, solitude seemed to look out of its countenance. It had a lonely face, suggesting tragical possibilities.

text: This forgotten, outworn, bypassed tract of earth is entered in the Great Survey. Its character is preserved there as a scrubby, thorn-choked, desolate wasteland—“Erica.” Then follow its dimensions in archaic leagues; and, though the true scale of those ancient units remains a matter of some debate, the figures indicate that the reach of Blackwood Moor has scarcely contracted since that distant era. “Privilegium Fodiendi”—the entitlement to harvest peat—is cited in old scrolls pertaining to the region. “Clothed in bracken and gray lichen,” noted Camden of this same brooding expanse of land.
source_text: This obscure, obsolete, superseded country figures in Domesday. Its condition is recorded therein as that of heathy, furzy, briary wilderness—“Bruaria.” Then follows the length and breadth in leagues; and, though some uncertainty exists as to the exact extent of this ancient lineal measure, it appears from the figures that the area of Egdon down to the present day has but little diminished. “Turbaria Bruaria”—the right of cutting heath-turf—occurs in charters relating to the district. “Overgrown with heth and mosse,” says Leland of the same dark sweep of country.

text: In this place, the landscape provided clear, comprehensible truths—broad evidence that offered a sense of deep satisfaction. Egdon was a wild, rebellious entity that had never changed its nature. It was an adversary to civilization; since the dawn of plant life, its soil had been cloaked in the same ancient brown, the inherent and unchanging garment of this particular terrain. There was a touch of irony in its singular, aged coat, a silent critique of human vanity regarding fashion. A visitor on the heath dressed in modern styles and colors seems somewhat out of place. We instinctively desire the oldest and most basic human attire where the earth's own covering is so primal.
source_text: Here at least were intelligible facts regarding landscape—far-reaching proofs productive of genuine satisfaction. The untameable, Ishmaelitish thing that Egdon now was it always had been. Civilization was its enemy; and ever since the beginning of vegetation its soil had worn the same antique brown dress, the natural and invariable garment of the particular formation. In its venerable one coat lay a certain vein of satire on human vanity in clothes. A person on a heath in raiment of modern cut and colours has more or less an anomalous look. We seem to want the oldest and simplest human clothing where the clothing of the earth is so primitive.

text: To rest upon a thorny stump in the central valley of Egdon at the fading of the day, where the horizon is bounded entirely by the rising slopes of the heath, is to find a profound sense of stability. To realize that everything beneath and around has remained as immutable as the constellations since prehistoric times offers a necessary anchor to a mind weary of the relentless march of progress. This vast, untouched wilderness possesses a primal endurance that even the ocean cannot claim. One cannot truly call the sea old; driven by the sun and the moon, it is a restless entity, reborn in a year, a day, or a single hour. While the waters, the fields, and the people shifted, Egdon remained. Its contours were neither so steep as to be worn away by storms nor so level as to be buried by silt and flood. Aside from a weathered highway and an ancient burial mound—both long since absorbed into the natural landscape—not even the smallest ridge was the work of a plow or spade, standing instead as the direct imprint of the earth’s last great geological shift.
source_text: To recline on a stump of thorn in the central valley of Egdon, between afternoon and night, as now, where the eye could reach nothing of the world outside the summits and shoulders of heathland which filled the whole circumference of its glance, and to know that everything around and underneath had been from prehistoric times as unaltered as the stars overhead, gave ballast to the mind adrift on change, and harassed by the irrepressible New. The great inviolate place had an ancient permanence which the sea cannot claim. Who can say of a particular sea that it is old? Distilled by the sun, kneaded by the moon, it is renewed in a year, in a day, or in an hour. The sea changed, the fields changed, the rivers, the villages, and the people changed, yet Egdon remained. Those surfaces were neither so steep as to be destructible by weather, nor so flat as to be the victims of floods and deposits. With the exception of an aged highway, and a still more aged barrow presently to be referred to—themselves almost crystallized to natural products by long continuance—even the trifling irregularities were not caused by pickaxe, plough, or spade, but remained as the very finger-touches of the last geological change.

text: The elderly man understood what this signified. The traveler with the wagon was a reddleman—someone whose trade involved providing farmers with red ochre for their sheep. He belonged to a profession that was quickly disappearing from the region, holding a position in the rural landscape much like the dodo once held among animals. He was a strange, intriguing, and nearly vanished bridge between forgotten ways of living and those that now dominate.
source_text: The old man knew the meaning of this. The traveller with the cart was a reddleman—a person whose vocation it was to supply farmers with redding for their sheep. He was one of a class rapidly becoming extinct in Wessex, filling at present in the rural world the place which, during the last century, the dodo occupied in the world of animals. He is a curious, interesting, and nearly perished link between obsolete forms of life and those which generally prevail.

text: As they continued their journey, the reddleman would occasionally drift away from his companion and move to the back of the wagon, peering through a small window into its interior. Each time he looked, his expression was one of deep concern. He would then rejoin the old man, who might offer another observation about the local landscape, to which the reddleman would give a distracted reply before they both lapsed back into quiet. Neither of them found this silence uncomfortable; in such desolate regions, travelers often walk together for miles without speaking after their initial greetings. In these isolated places, proximity serves as a silent form of communication, especially since—unlike in a crowded city—such closeness can be ended at any moment. To choose to remain together is, in itself, a kind of conversation.
source_text: Now, as they thus pursued their way, the reddleman occasionally left his companion’s side, and, stepping behind the van, looked into its interior through a small window. The look was always anxious. He would then return to the old man, who made another remark about the state of the country and so on, to which the reddleman again abstractedly replied, and then again they would lapse into silence. The silence conveyed to neither any sense of awkwardness; in these lonely places wayfarers, after a first greeting, frequently plod on for miles without speech; contiguity amounts to a tacit conversation where, otherwise than in cities, such contiguity can be put an end to on the merest inclination, and where not to put an end to it is intercourse in itself.

text: Watching the ancient mound, the reclining man saw that its summit, formerly the highest mark on the horizon, was crowned by something taller. It projected from the rounded earth like a crest upon a casque. A dreamer might have mistaken the silhouette for one of the prehistoric builders, so far had the modern world retreated from the view. He seemed a final sentinel of his people, lingering a moment in thought before falling into the same eternal shadow as the rest of his vanished kin.
source_text: As the resting man looked at the barrow he became aware that its summit, hitherto the highest object in the whole prospect round, was surmounted by something higher. It rose from the semiglobular mound like a spike from a helmet. The first instinct of an imaginative stranger might have been to suppose it the person of one of the Celts who built the barrow, so far had all of modern date withdrawn from the scene. It seemed a sort of last man among them, musing for a moment before dropping into eternal night with the rest of his race.

text: The cause of her abrupt departure soon became clear. As she vanished from view on the right, a new figure, carrying a heavy load, appeared against the horizon on the left, climbed the burial mound, and set the weight down at the summit. Another followed, then a third, fourth, and fifth, until eventually the entire hilltop was crowded with people bearing similar burdens.
source_text: The reason of her sudden displacement now appeared. With her dropping out of sight on the right side, a newcomer, bearing a burden, protruded into the sky on the left side, ascended the tumulus, and deposited the burden on the top. A second followed, then a third, a fourth, a fifth, and ultimately the whole barrow was peopled with burdened figures.

text: And what of the newlyweds down at the Quiet Woman?" the other man asked, gesturing toward a faint glimmer in the direction of the far-off road, well away from where the reddleman was currently paused. "What is the truth behind their story? A man of your discernment must surely have the facts.
source_text: “And how about the new-married folks down there at the Quiet Woman Inn?” the other inquired, pointing towards a dim light in the direction of the distant highway, but considerably apart from where the reddleman was at that moment resting. “What’s the rights of the matter about ’em? You ought to know, being an understanding man.”

text: "I truly felt as happy as if someone had handed me a silver coin when I heard they’d been stopped," said a sincere voice. It belonged to Olly Dowden, a woman who supported herself by crafting brooms from the heath. She was naturally polite to friends and rivals alike, feeling a quiet appreciation toward the world just for the chance to keep living.
source_text: “I’m sure when I heard they’d been forbid I felt as glad as if anybody had gied me sixpence,” said an earnest voice—that of Olly Dowden, a woman who lived by making heath brooms, or besoms. Her nature was to be civil to enemies as well as to friends, and grateful to all the world for letting her remain alive.

text: “It may sound unlikely, but I’ve no real fondness for a rowdy wedding,” Silas Thorne remarked, his gaze once more drifting across the company. “I can’t find it in me to reproach Clara Penhaligon and young Master Vane for keeping things private, if I’m being honest. A marriage celebrated at home demands those endless country jigs that go on for hours; and they serve a man’s joints poorly once he’s seen his fortieth year.”
source_text: “Ah, now, you’d hardly believe it, but I don’t care for gay weddings,” said Timothy Fairway, his eyes again travelling round. “I hardly blame Thomasin Yeobright and neighbour Wildeve for doing it quiet, if I must own it. A wedding at home means five and six-handed reels by the hour; and they do a man’s legs no good when he’s over forty.”

text: “You’d be scholar enough to ink your name in the register,” remarked Thorne, “were you young enough to be tethered to a woman once more, like Silas and young Martha—which is a far cry from what Ned here could do, for he follows his sire’s lead in the way of letters. Ah, Ned, I remember well on my own wedding morning how I saw your father’s mark staring me down as I went to sign. He and your mother were the pair wed just before us, and there stood his cross with its arms spread wide like a great, looming scarecrow. A grim, black brand it was—the very spit of your father in ink! For the life of me, I couldn’t stop a laugh when I saw it, though I was as flushed as midsummer, what with the business of marrying, and the bride hanging heavy on my arm, and Barnaby and a dozen other rogues grinning at me through the chapel glass. But a moment later, a feather’s weight would have felled me, for I called to mind that if your parents had traded sharp words once, they’d been at it a hundred times since they’d been made one, and I saw myself as the next poor simpleton to stumble into that same thicket.... Aye—well, it was a rare day, truly!”
source_text: “Couldst sign the book, no doubt,” said Fairway, “if wast young enough to join hands with a woman again, like Wildeve and Mis’ess Tamsin, which is more than Humph there could do, for he follows his father in learning. Ah, Humph, well I can mind when I was married how I zid thy father’s mark staring me in the face as I went to put down my name. He and your mother were the couple married just afore we were and there stood they father’s cross with arms stretched out like a great banging scarecrow. What a terrible black cross that was—thy father’s very likeness in en! To save my soul I couldn’t help laughing when I zid en, though all the time I was as hot as dog-days, what with the marrying, and what with the woman a-hanging to me, and what with Jack Changley and a lot more chaps grinning at me through church window. But the next moment a strawmote would have knocked me down, for I called to mind that if thy father and mother had had high words once, they’d been at it twenty times since they’d been man and wife, and I zid myself as the next poor stunpoll to get into the same mess.... Ah—well, what a day ’twas!”

text: “Mama was certain the sky held no silver, for she’d consulted a neighbor who kept a calendar, a precaution she took for every son she bore. She lived in dread of that ancient proverb, ‘Hollow moon, half a man,’ which made her tremble for the spirit of every boy-child she raised. Do you truly reckon it a dark omen, Master Weaver, that the night of my birth was empty?”
source_text: “Mother know’d ’twas no moon, for she asked another woman that had an almanac, as she did whenever a boy was born to her, because of the saying, ‘No moon, no man,’ which made her afeard every man-child she had. Do ye really think it serious, Mister Fairway, that there was no moon?”

text: These words were directed at Susan Nunsuch; the next thing the onlookers noticed was the woman’s sturdy figure being swept toward the patch of ground where the fire had been lit. Mr. Fairway had hooked his arm around her waist before she could realize his intent, lifting her bodily into the air. The bonfire had faded into a ring of ash dotted with glowing embers and sparks, the brushwood having been completely consumed. Once they reached the center, he spun her around and around in a dance. She was a woman who rattled as she moved; besides her rigid corset of whalebone and wood, she wore wooden clogs in every season, rain or shine, to protect her boots from wear. As Fairway began to bounce her about, the clatter of the clogs, the groaning of her stays, and her startled shrieks combined into a loud and chaotic performance.
source_text: This was addressed to Susan Nunsuch; and the next circumstance of which the beholders were conscious was a vision of the matron’s broad form whisking off towards the space whereon the fire had been kindled. She was lifted bodily by Mr. Fairway’s arm, which had been flung round her waist before she had become aware of his intention. The site of the fire was now merely a circle of ashes flecked with red embers and sparks, the furze having burnt completely away. Once within the circle he whirled her round and round in a dance. She was a woman noisily constructed; in addition to her enclosing framework of whalebone and lath, she wore pattens summer and winter, in wet weather and in dry, to preserve her boots from wear; and when Fairway began to jump about with her, the clicking of the pattens, the creaking of the stays, and her screams of surprise, formed a very audible concert.

text: She was a lady of mature years, whose refined countenance bore the unmistakable stamp of a keen and penetrating intellect. Occasionally, she appeared to survey the world from a private summit, viewing horizons that remained hidden from the common gaze. An air of isolation clung to her; the very loneliness of the moorland seemed to have coalesced within her features as she emerged from its shadows. Her manner toward the local rustics reflected a cool disregard for their company, as well as a total indifference to their judgment of her solitary rambles in such a desolate place at twilight. Such poise implied a quiet sense of elevation above her neighbors, a sentiment explained by her lineage: for while she had wed a man of the soil, she was the offspring of a clergyman, and had once nurtured ambitions far grander than her present station.
source_text: She was a woman of middle-age, with well-formed features of the type usually found where perspicacity is the chief quality enthroned within. At moments she seemed to be regarding issues from a Nebo denied to others around. She had something of an estranged mien; the solitude exhaled from the heath was concentrated in this face that had risen from it. The air with which she looked at the heathmen betokened a certain unconcern at their presence, or at what might be their opinions of her for walking in that lonely spot at such an hour, thus indirectly implying that in some respect or other they were not up to her level. The explanation lay in the fact that though her husband had been a small farmer she herself was a curate’s daughter, who had once dreamt of doing better things.

text: The poor girl was simply overcome by her feelings, no doubt. It is only natural. People may say what they wish about him—he has cultivated several acres of heath, owns the local inn, and keeps a stock of ponies. His manners are those of a true gentleman. Besides, what is done is done, and it cannot be altered now.
source_text: “Poor little thing, her feelings got the better of her, no doubt. ’Tis nature. Well, they may call him what they will—he’ve several acres of heth-ground broke up here, besides the public house, and the heth-croppers, and his manners be quite like a gentleman’s. And what’s done cannot be undone.”

text: I knew her back when I was a boy, before I started this job. She asked me for a lift, but then she suddenly fainted. I picked her up and placed her inside, and she’s been there ever since. She’s been crying a great deal, though she’s barely spoken; the only thing she told me was that her wedding was supposed to happen this morning. I tried to get her to eat something, but she couldn't, and at last, she drifted off to sleep.
source_text: “I had met her as a lad before I went away in this trade. She asked then if she might ride, and then down she fell in a faint. I picked her up and put her in, and there she has been ever since. She has cried a good deal, but she has hardly spoke; all she has told me being that she was to have been married this morning. I tried to get her to eat something, but she couldn’t; and at last she fell asleep.”

text: The reddleman led the way with his lantern, climbing up first to help Mrs. Yeobright into the wagon. As the door opened, she noticed an improvised bed at the far end, screened by every piece of cloth the man owned to protect the sleeper from the red stains of his trade. A young girl lay there, wrapped in a heavy cloak. She was fast asleep, her face clearly revealed in the steady glow of the lantern.
source_text: The reddleman followed with the lantern, and, stepping up first, assisted Mrs. Yeobright to mount beside him. On the door being opened she perceived at the end of the van an extemporized couch, around which was hung apparently all the drapery that the reddleman possessed, to keep the occupant of the little couch from contact with the red materials of his trade. A young girl lay thereon, covered with a cloak. She was asleep, and the light of the lantern fell upon her features.

text: Yet the pervasive murk left nothing for the eye but the stark contours of the hills against the sky. The current behind the manor made itself known by the soft churning of eddies as it passed through the brittle, plumed reeds that rose like a defensive wall along the banks. Their location was signaled by a sound resembling the hushed litany of a devout multitude, created as they brushed one another in the sluggish breeze.
source_text: But the thick obscurity permitted only skylines to be visible of any scene at present. The water at the back of the house could be heard, idly spinning whirpools in its creep between the rows of dry feather-headed reeds which formed a stockade along each bank. Their presence was denoted by sounds as of a congregation praying humbly, produced by their rubbing against each other in the slow wind.

text: He was a relatively young man, and of his two primary attributes—his physique and his movement—it was the latter that first drew the observer's eye. His gait possessed a singular grace, a silent manifestation of a career spent in the pursuit of women. Following this, his more tangible features emerged, including a heavy shock of hair that overhung his face, giving his brow the pointed silhouette of a medieval Gothic shield. His neck was as smooth and perfectly round as a pillar, while the lower half of his body was delicately built. Taken altogether, he was a person whom no man would ever find reason to admire, but whom no woman would ever find reason to dislike.
source_text: He was quite a young man, and of the two properties, form and motion, the latter first attracted the eye in him. The grace of his movement was singular—it was the pantomimic expression of a lady-killing career. Next came into notice the more material qualities, among which was a profuse crop of hair impending over the top of his face, lending to his forehead the high-cornered outline of an early Gothic shield; and a neck which was smooth and round as a cylinder. The lower half of his figure was of light build. Altogether he was one in whom no man would have seen anything to admire, and in whom no woman would have seen anything to dislike.

text: “It is nearly a matter of simple justice for me,” Wildeve declared. “Consider the ordeal I endured to win her hand; the disgrace any man suffers when his wedding banns are publicly revoked—a humiliation doubled for one as burdened by sensitivity and dark melancholy as I am. I can never truly move past the shame of that rejection. A more callous individual would find satisfaction in the opportunity to spite your aunt by refusing to proceed any further with the marriage.”
source_text: “As a matter of justice it is almost due to me,” said Wildeve. “Think what I have gone through to win her consent; the insult that it is to any man to have the banns forbidden—the double insult to a man unlucky enough to be cursed with sensitiveness, and blue demons, and Heaven knows what, as I am. I can never forget those banns. A harsher man would rejoice now in the power I have of turning upon your aunt by going no further in the business.”

text: Scarcely had Silas drifted into slumber, and the initial breath from neighbor Miller’s lungs found its way through Silas’s oboe, when every soul in the pews sensed immediately that a master was in their midst. Necks would crane instinctively, and the whisper would go round, “Indeed, I knew it could be none other!” I recall one particular Sabbath vividly—a day for the double bass, and Miller had carried in his own great timbered beast. We were set to the Hundred-and-thirty-third, sung to the tune of ‘Lydia’; and upon reaching the verse, ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbor Miller, now fully caught in the fervor of his craft, plunged his bow across the strings with such magnificent violence that he very nearly cleaved the instrument in twain. Each pane of glass in the sanctuary shuddered as though a tempest had broken overhead. Aged Reverend Thorne raised his arms within his voluminous white vestments with a gesture so unstudied it seemed he wore but his everyday coat, appearing to murmur to his own heart, ‘Would that every parish were blessed with such a talent!’ Yet, in all of Oakhaven, there was not a single person who could hope to rival Miller.
source_text: “No sooner was Andrey asleep and the first whiff of neighbour Yeobright’s wind had got inside Andrey’s clarinet than everyone in church feeled in a moment there was a great soul among ’em. All heads would turn, and they’d say, ‘Ah, I thought ’twas he!’ One Sunday I can well mind—a bass viol day that time, and Yeobright had brought his own. ’Twas the Hundred-and-thirty-third to ‘Lydia’; and when they’d come to ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbour Yeobright, who had just warmed to his work, drove his bow into them strings that glorious grand that he e’en a’most sawed the bass viol into two pieces. Every winder in church rattled as if ’twere a thunderstorm. Old Pa’son Williams lifted his hands in his great holy surplice as natural as if he’d been in common clothes, and seemed to say hisself, ‘Oh for such a man in our parish!’ But not a soul in Kingsbere could hold a candle to Yeobright.”

text: “Well, he had been fading toward the grave for several months before he finally passed. In those days, the young women would race for shifts and dress-lengths at the Greenhill Fair, and my wife—who was then just a tall, spindly girl barely old enough to wed—competed alongside the others, for she was a fast runner before she grew heavy. When she returned, I asked her—we had only just begun courting then—‘What did you get, my sweet?’ She turned bright red in an instant and claimed she’d won a piece of gown-fabric. I suspected it was actually a smock, and I was right. It seems strange now, seeing how she speaks her mind today without the slightest blush, that she was once so bashful over such a small thing. But then she said—and this is what reminded me of the story—‘Whatever I’ve won, whether it’s meant for public view or not’ (she was quite the modest one back then), ‘I would rather have lost the race than seen what I saw. Poor Mr. Yeobright fell ill the moment he reached the fairgrounds and was forced to return home.’ That was the very last time he ever traveled beyond the borders of this parish.”
source_text: “Ah, well; he was looking for the earth some months afore he went. At that time women used to run for smocks and gown-pieces at Greenhill Fair, and my wife that is now, being a long-legged slittering maid, hardly husband-high, went with the rest of the maidens, for ’a was a good runner afore she got so heavy. When she came home I said—we were then just beginning to walk together—‘What have ye got, my honey?’ ‘I’ve won—well, I’ve won—a gown-piece,’ says she, her colours coming up in a moment. ’Tis a smock for a crown, I thought; and so it turned out. Ay, when I think what she’ll say to me now without a mossel of red in her face, it do seem strange that ’a wouldn’t say such a little thing then.... However, then she went on, and that’s what made me bring up the story, ‘Well, whatever clothes I’ve won, white or figured, for eyes to see or for eyes not to see’ (’a could do a pretty stroke of modesty in those days), ‘I’d sooner have lost it than have seen what I have. Poor Mr. Yeobright was took bad directly he reached the fair ground, and was forced to go home again.’ That was the last time he ever went out of the parish.”

text: Wildeve gave a quiet laugh, stood lost in thought for a second, and then drifted back into the parlor. There, his eyes rested on a bottle of wine sitting atop the mantel. “Ah, old Dowden!” he whispered to himself. He walked over to the kitchen door and called out, “Is anyone around who can deliver something to old Dowden?”
source_text: Wildeve laughed to himself, remained a moment thinking, and idly returned to the front room. Here his glance fell upon a bottle of wine which stood on the mantelpiece. “Ah—old Dowden!” he murmured; and going to the kitchen door shouted, “Is anybody here who can take something to old Dowden?”

text: Deep in the valley, the distant glimmer from the inn’s window persisted; it soon became evident that this light, or whatever lay behind it, was the true cause of the woman’s sigh, rather than her own movements or the surrounding night. She raised her left hand, which gripped a collapsed telescope. With a swift and practiced motion, she extended the instrument and, bringing it to her eye, aimed her gaze toward the steady beam of light radiating from the distant inn.
source_text: Far away down the valley the faint shine from the window of the inn still lasted on; and a few additional moments proved that the window, or what was within it, had more to do with the woman’s sigh than had either her own actions or the scene immediately around. She lifted her left hand, which held a closed telescope. This she rapidly extended, as if she were well accustomed to the operation, and raising it to her eye directed it towards the light beaming from the inn.

text: The flame kindled by her respiration flickered with great instability, and a transitory gleaming of flesh was the only portion of her visage it managed to unveil. This consisted merely of two peerless lips and a solitary cheek, her head being yet muffled in its coverings. She tossed the splinter aside, took the goblet in her hand, the optic tube beneath her arm, and resumed her walk.
source_text: The light raised by her breath had been very fitful, and a momentary irradiation of flesh was all that it had disclosed of her face. That consisted of two matchless lips and a cheek only, her head being still enveloped. She threw away the stick, took the glass in her hand, the telescope under her arm, and moved on.

text: A subtle trail wound along the ridge, and the woman followed its lead. Local inhabitants recognized it as a path, yet a casual traveler would have failed to spot it even in the sun's glare. For those who regularly frequented the moor, however, the route remained clear even at midnight. The secret to navigating these fading tracks when the air was too dark to reveal a major road lay in a refined sensitivity in one's feet—a trait born from years of nocturnal wandering in secluded areas. To an experienced walker, the distinction between the feel of untouched grass and the trampled remains of a narrow walkway is discernible even through the heaviest footwear.
source_text: Along the ridge ran a faint foot-track, which the lady followed. Those who knew it well called it a path; and, while a mere visitor would have passed it unnoticed even by day, the regular haunters of the heath were at no loss for it at midnight. The whole secret of following these incipient paths, when there was not light enough in the atmosphere to show a turnpike road, lay in the development of the sense of touch in the feet, which comes with years of night-rambling in little-trodden spots. To a walker practised in such places a difference between impact on maiden herbage, and on the crippled stalks of a slight footway, is perceptible through the thickest boot or shoe.

text: She made her way toward the small, persistent fire that had earlier captured the gaze of the men on Rainbarrow and Wildeve in the valley below. A soft radiance from its embers began to play across her features, and it soon became apparent that the blaze was not situated on level ground, but atop a prominent earthen embankment where two boundary fences met. Beyond this lay a ditch, dry for the most part except for a broad pool directly beneath the fire that was rimmed with a thick growth of heather and rushes. In the pool’s glassy surface, the fire’s reflection shimmered upside down.
source_text: Her course was in the direction of the small undying fire which had drawn the attention of the men on Rainbarrow and of Wildeve in the valley below. A faint illumination from its rays began to glow upon her face, and the fire soon revealed itself to be lit, not on the level ground, but on a salient corner or redan of earth, at the junction of two converging bank fences. Outside was a ditch, dry except immediately under the fire, where there was a large pool, bearded all round by heather and rushes. In the smooth water of the pool the fire appeared upside down.

text: No living soul could be discerned; yet periodically a pallid shape flickered above the crest of the ridge and retreated. This was a slight human hand, busy with the task of feeding the blaze, but to the eye it appeared entirely detached from any form, much like the spectral fingers that once wrote of ruin upon a palace wall. Every so often a stray coal tumbled from the bank, plunging into the water with a sudden, sharp sizzle.
source_text: Nobody was visible; but ever and anon a whitish something moved above the bank from behind, and vanished again. This was a small human hand, in the act of lifting pieces of fuel into the fire, but for all that could be seen the hand, like that which troubled Belshazzar, was there alone. Occasionally an ember rolled off the bank, and dropped with a hiss into the pool.

text: The young woman’s vitality was evident in the spirited way she ascended the slope. Rather than stepping down into the hollow, she kept to the ridge until she reached the spot where the fire glowed. The reason the flames burned so steadily was now clear: the fuel was made of dense, split logs—the gnarled trunks of old thorn trees that grew in small clusters across the hills. A stack of this wood sat in the crook of the bank, and from beside it, a young boy looked up at her. He was slowly feeding the fire, tossing in a fresh piece every few minutes with a listless air that suggested he had been at this work for hours, for his face looked quite worn.
source_text: The young lady—for youth had revealed its presence in her buoyant bound up the bank—walked along the top instead of descending inside, and came to the corner where the fire was burning. One reason for the permanence of the blaze was now manifest: the fuel consisted of hard pieces of wood, cleft and sawn—the knotty boles of old thorn trees which grew in twos and threes about the hillsides. A yet unconsumed pile of these lay in the inner angle of the bank; and from this corner the upturned face of a little boy greeted her eyes. He was dilatorily throwing up a piece of wood into the fire every now and then, a business which seemed to have engaged him a considerable part of the evening, for his face was somewhat weary.

text: After her careful observation of the rugged slopes and deep ravines, Eustacia could no longer hide her irritation. She muttered petulant remarks every so often, her words punctuated by sighs, and in the moments between those sighs, she would suddenly pause to listen. Stepping down from her vantage point, she wandered toward Rainbarrow once more, though this time she stopped before reaching it.
source_text: After her attentive survey of the wild slopes and hollow ravines a gesture of impatience escaped Eustacia. She vented petulant words every now and then, but there were sighs between her words, and sudden listenings between her sighs. Descending from her perch she again sauntered off towards Rainbarrow, though this time she did not go the whole way.

text: Concealed by the crumbling embankment, she remained motionless. Moments later, a sudden plash disturbed the stillness of the water outside. A child, listening, would have imagined a bullfrog taking flight; however, to a more discerning ear, the sound was that of a stone sinking fast. Beatrix stepped toward the water’s edge.
source_text: Here, screened by the outwork, she waited. In a few moments a splash was audible from the pond outside.
