Had the child been there he would have said that a second frog had jumped in; but by most people the sound would have been likened to the fall of a stone into the water. Eustacia stepped upon the bank.

text: “Certainly; for what else would bring me back?” Julian remarked with a prickle of resentment. “Not that my constancy carries much weight now, following your remarks on my failings—truths I might have uttered myself, perhaps, but which lose their dignity when voiced by you. Still, the affliction of a quickening heart is my lot, and I must bear its weight and the scorn of women alike. It has dragged me from the heights of architecture to the service of a public house; what further descent lies ahead remains a mystery.” He continued to regard her with a heavy, somber air.
source_text: “Yes; or why should I have come?” he said touchily. “Not that fidelity will be any great merit in me after your kind speech about my unworthiness, which should have been said by myself if by anybody, and comes with an ill grace from you. However, the curse of inflammability is upon me, and I must live under it, and take any snub from a woman. It has brought me down from engineering to innkeeping—what lower stage it has in store for me I have yet to learn.” He continued to look upon her gloomily.

text: I give you no thanks for that," she said, turning away as a smoldering anger spread through her like a buried fire. "Return to Rainbarrow if you wish, but you will not find me; call for me, but I shall not hear; and though you may try to lure me back, I will never give myself to you again.
source_text: “I don’t thank you for that,” she said, turning away, while indignation spread through her like subterranean heat. “You may come again to Rainbarrow if you like, but you won’t see me; and you may call, but I shall not listen; and you may tempt me, but I won’t give myself to you any more.”

text: Isolde exhaled—not the dainty gasp of a debutante, but a release of air that rattled her ribs like a sudden chill. On those rare occasions when a beam of sober judgment pierced her consciousness, exposing her companion's vanities with the harshness of a flare, she was seized by such a spasm. But the lucidity vanished instantly, and her affection endured. She was aware of his fickle games, yet she would not turn away. Disrupting the glowing logs with a sharp kick, she hurried inside and ascended to her room in total shadow. Through the faint whispers of fabric as she stripped in the dark, her breathing remained thick and heavy; and that same involuntary twitching revisited her frame even after she had succumbed, moments later, to the depths of sleep.
source_text: Eustacia sighed—it was no fragile maiden sigh, but a sigh which shook her like a shiver. Whenever a flash of reason darted like an electric light upon her lover—as it sometimes would—and showed his imperfections, she shivered thus. But it was over in a second, and she loved on. She knew that he trifled with her; but she loved on. She scattered the half-burnt brands, went indoors immediately, and up to her bedroom without a light. Amid the rustles which denoted her to be undressing in the darkness other heavy breaths frequently came; and the same kind of shudder occasionally moved through her when, ten minutes later, she lay on her bed asleep.

text: Eustacia Vye was made of the very stuff of the divine. Given a little polish, she would have been perfectly at home on Olympus. She possessed the temperament and drives that define an ideal goddess—which is to say, the very traits that make for a less-than-ideal woman. Had she been granted total dominion over the earth and its people, free to spin and sever the threads of fate at her whim, the world would scarcely have registered the change in power. There would have been the same unfairness of fortune, the same piling of rewards in one place and abuse in another, the same impulsive kindness over strict justice, the same constant paradoxes, and the same erratic shifts between tenderness and violence that we already suffer today.
source_text: Eustacia Vye was the raw material of a divinity. On Olympus she would have done well with a little preparation. She had the passions and instincts which make a model goddess, that is, those which make not quite a model woman. Had it been possible for the earth and mankind to be entirely in her grasp for a while, she had handled the distaff, the spindle, and the shears at her own free will, few in the world would have noticed the change of government. There would have been the same inequality of lot, the same heaping up of favours here, of contumely there, the same generosity before justice, the same perpetual dilemmas, the same captious alteration of caresses and blows that we endure now.

text: Her figure was curvaceous and substantial, her skin neither flushed nor pale, yet as soft to the touch as a passing cloud. To gaze upon her hair was to imagine that even the deepest winter lacked the darkness to cast such a shadow; it draped across her forehead like the fall of night smothering the final glow of the sunset.
source_text: She was in person full-limbed and somewhat heavy; without ruddiness, as without pallor; and soft to the touch as a cloud. To see her hair was to fancy that a whole winter did not contain darkness enough to form its shadow—it closed over her forehead like nightfall extinguishing the western glow.

text: She evoked the scent of damask blooms, the fire of garnets, and the stillness of an equatorial dusk; her temper suggested the dreams of the Lethe and the gravity of a processional hymn; her movements, the rhythmic pulse of the tide; her voice, the deep wood of a cello. In the fading light, with a mere shifting of her tresses, her form might have served as a model for the supreme queens of Olympus. A silver crescent poised behind her, a rusted helm upon her brow, or a circlet of stray dewdrops would have been ornaments enough to summon the presence of Diana, Minerva, or Juno, offering a resemblance to the ancient world as faithful as those portrayals found upon the walls of the most esteemed galleries.
source_text: Her presence brought memories of such things as Bourbon roses, rubies, and tropical midnight; her moods recalled lotus-eaters and the march in Athalie; her motions, the ebb and flow of the sea; her voice, the viola. In a dim light, and with a slight rearrangement of her hair, her general figure might have stood for that of either of the higher female deities. The new moon behind her head, an old helmet upon it, a diadem of accidental dewdrops round her brow, would have been adjuncts sufficient to strike the note of Artemis, Athena, or Hera respectively, with as close an approximation to the antique as that which passes muster on many respected canvases.

text: Yet her divine arrogance, her passions, and her furies were largely wasted on the desolate expanse of Egdon. Her influence was restricted, and the constant awareness of these boundaries had warped her character's growth. Egdon served as her personal underworld; since her arrival, she had absorbed much of its gloom, even as her inner self remained eternally at odds with it. Her physical presence reflected this simmering defiance, and the dark magnificence of her beauty was the visible layer of the heavy, suppressed passion within her. A genuine, infernal majesty rested upon her features—not as an affectation or something forced, but as a quality that had matured naturally over the years.
source_text: But celestial imperiousness, love, wrath, and fervour had proved to be somewhat thrown away on netherward Egdon. Her power was limited, and the consciousness of this limitation had biassed her development. Egdon was her Hades, and since coming there she had imbibed much of what was dark in its tone, though inwardly and eternally unreconciled thereto. Her appearance accorded well with this smouldering rebelliousness, and the shady splendour of her beauty was the real surface of the sad and stifled warmth within her. A true Tartarean dignity sat upon her brow, and not factitiously or with marks of constraint, for it had grown in her with years.

text: A thin band of black velvet was fastened around the crown of her head, restraining her thick, dark hair in a fashion that enhanced her somber majesty by casting irregular shadows over her brow. As Richter observed, nothing adorns a beautiful face quite like a narrow ribbon traced across the forehead. Though the local girls often wore colorful silks for the same effect and decorated themselves with metal jewelry, Eustacia Vye only laughed at the idea of such common ornaments and continued on her way.
source_text: Across the upper part of her head she wore a thin fillet of black velvet, restraining the luxuriance of her shady hair, in a way which added much to this class of majesty by irregularly clouding her forehead. “Nothing can embellish a beautiful face more than a narrow band drawn over the brow,” says Richter. Some of the neighbouring girls wore coloured ribbon for the same purpose, and sported metallic ornaments elsewhere; but if anyone suggested coloured ribbon and metallic ornaments to Eustacia Vye she laughed and went on.

text: What was the source of her innate poise? Did it flow from some hidden spring in her father’s Phaeacian ancestry, or was it a legacy of the Fitzalans and De Veres, inherited through her mother’s titled relations? It might simply have been a blessing from above—a fortunate meeting of natural forces. In recent years, she had lacked any opportunity to become common, for her life was a solitary one. Such isolation upon the moor makes vulgarity almost impossible to achieve. She could no more have been uncouth than the wild ponies, the bats, or the snakes that shared her landscape. Had she lived a restricted life in a town like Budmouth, she might have been utterly diminished.
source_text: Where did her dignity come from? By a latent vein from Alcinous’ line, her father hailing from Phæacia’s isle?—or from Fitzalan and De Vere, her maternal grandfather having had a cousin in the peerage? Perhaps it was the gift of Heaven—a happy convergence of natural laws. Among other things opportunity had of late years been denied her of learning to be undignified, for she lived lonely. Isolation on a heath renders vulgarity well-nigh impossible. It would have been as easy for the heath-ponies, bats, and snakes to be vulgar as for her. A narrow life in Budmouth might have completely demeaned her.

text: She possessed a certain boldness of intellect; indeed, when measured against the traditionalist community she lived in, her perspective was remarkably unique. This stemmed primarily from her innate drive to defy social expectations. Regarding leisure, she felt much like a horse put out to pasture that finds pleasure in watching its fellows toil along the road. Relaxation held value for her only when it coincided with the industry of others. Consequently, she loathed Sundays, when everything stood still, frequently remarking that the day’s quietude would be the death of her. Watching the local laborers in their Sabbath state—hands thrust in pockets, boots freshly greased but left unlaced in that typical Sunday fashion, strolling aimlessly past the stacks of peat and brushwood they had harvested all week and nudging the piles as if they had forgotten their purpose—filled her with a profound sense of gloom. To escape the dullness of this ill-timed day, she would rummage through cabinets filled with her grandfather’s outdated maps and miscellaneous junk, all while humming the rowdy folk songs usually reserved for Saturday nights. Conversely, on Saturday evenings she often sang hymns, and she made a point of reading scripture only on weekdays, ensuring she was never weighed down by the feeling of merely performing a religious chore.
source_text: Thus she was a girl of some forwardness of mind, indeed, weighed in relation to her situation among the very rearward of thinkers, very original. Her instincts towards social non-comformity were at the root of this. In the matter of holidays, her mood was that of horses who, when turned out to grass, enjoy looking upon their kind at work on the highway. She only valued rest to herself when it came in the midst of other people’s labour. Hence she hated Sundays when all was at rest, and often said they would be the death of her. To see the heathmen in their Sunday condition, that is, with their hands in their pockets, their boots newly oiled, and not laced up (a particularly Sunday sign), walking leisurely among the turves and furze-faggots they had cut during the week, and kicking them critically as if their use were unknown, was a fearful heaviness to her. To relieve the tedium of this untimely day she would overhaul the cupboards containing her grandfather’s old charts and other rubbish, humming Saturday-night ballads of the country people the while. But on Saturday nights she would frequently sing a psalm, and it was always on a weekday that she read the Bible, that she might be unoppressed with a sense of doing her duty.

text: The moment the forlorn lad retreated from the warmth of the embers, he gripped the silver tightly within his fist, seeking to draw some measure of bravery from the cold metal, and broke into a sprint. In truth, there was no great hazard in letting a child navigate this corner of Blackwood Moor by himself. The path to his threshold covered less than five hundred yards; his father’s lodge and a companion dwelling a short distance away made up the meager cluster known as Ashwood Rise. A third and final house, the residence of Major Thorne and Elara, sat detached from the others, standing as the most solitary of outposts upon these desolate, windswept slopes.
source_text: As soon as the sad little boy had withdrawn from the fire he clasped the money tight in the palm of his hand, as if thereby to fortify his courage, and began to run. There was really little danger in allowing a child to go home alone on this part of Egdon Heath. The distance to the boy’s house was not more than three-eighths of a mile, his father’s cottage, and one other a few yards further on, forming part of the small hamlet of Mistover Knap: the third and only remaining house was that of Captain Vye and Eustacia, which stood quite away from the small cottages and was the loneliest of lonely houses on these thinly populated slopes.

text: Once the lad had regained the summit of the hollow, he perceived the embers still glowing upon the ledge, their light having grown faint. In place of Lady Gwendolen’s isolated silhouette, two figures now appeared, the newcomer being a gentleman. The child skittered beneath the ridge to determine if he might safely disturb so magnificent a personage for his own inconsequential needs.
source_text: When the boy had reascended to the top of the valley he found the fire to be still burning on the bank, though lower than before. Beside it, instead of Eustacia’s solitary form, he saw two persons, the second being a man. The boy crept along under the bank to ascertain from the nature of the proceedings if it would be prudent to interrupt so splendid a creature as Miss Eustacia on his poor trivial account.

text: Daylight had failed and the clouds of dust had finally settled—he hoped they would never rise again. He walked on with firm intent, finding no reason for fear until he came within a few paces of the sandpit. A faint stirring in the gloom ahead brought him to a sudden standstill. The pause was fleeting, however, as the sound clarified into the rhythmic, steady chewing of two grazing creatures.
source_text: The light had gone, the rising dust had disappeared—he hoped for ever. He marched resolutely along, and found nothing to alarm him till, coming within a few yards of the sandpit, he heard a slight noise in front, which led him to halt. The halt was but momentary, for the noise resolved itself into the steady bites of two animals grazing.

text: The beasts stood directly in his trajectory, a fact that gave the youth no pause; he had grown up amidst the heavy hooves of stallions from his very cradle. Upon nearing them, however, the lad felt a flicker of curiosity to see that the small creatures did not bolt, each being restrained by a wooden hobble to curb its wandering; this was proof enough that they were already mastered. He could now peer into the heart of the hollow, which, sliced into the flank of the ridge, possessed a level floor. In the furthest shadows, the angular silhouette of a caravan appeared, its hind end toward him. A warm radiance spilled from within, throwing a dancing image upon the sheer bank of shale at the back of the clearing where the vehicle was stationed.
source_text: The animals were in the direct line of his path, but that the child thought little of; he had played round the fetlocks of horses from his infancy. On coming nearer, however, the boy was somewhat surprised to find that the little creatures did not run off, and that each wore a clog, to prevent his going astray; this signified that they had been broken in. He could now see the interior of the pit, which, being in the side of the hill, had a level entrance. In the innermost corner the square outline of a van appeared, with its back towards him. A light came from the interior, and threw a moving shadow upon the vertical face of gravel at the further side of the pit into which the vehicle faced.

text: In that instant, a moor-pony feeding in the shadows rattled the heavy shackle on its leg. At the sound, the rudd-man set down his needlework, lit the lantern hanging by the door, and emerged from the caravan. As he fixed the candle, the light struck his face; his eyes and teeth gleamed with a startling whiteness against his scarlet skin, presenting a ghoulish mask to the watching child. The boy knew well whose lair he had disturbed. Beings more sinister than simple wanderers were said to haunt the heath, and the rudd-man was the most fearsome of them all.
source_text: At this moment one of the heath-croppers feeding in the outer shadows was audibly shaking off the clog attached to its foot. Aroused by the sound, the reddleman laid down his stocking, lit a lantern which hung beside him, and came out from the van. In sticking up the candle he lifted the lantern to his face, and the light shone into the whites of his eyes and upon his ivory teeth, which, in contrast with the red surrounding, lent him a startling aspect enough to the gaze of a juvenile. The boy knew too well for his peace of mind upon whose lair he had lighted. Uglier persons than gipsies were known to cross Egdon at times, and a reddleman was one of them.

text: From the time that letter arrived on a distant autumn morning, the reddleman and Thomasin had not encountered one another until today. In the intervening years, he had drifted even further from her station by adopting the reddle trade, yet his private circumstances remained quite comfortable. Indeed, as his expenses were only a quarter of his earnings, he was by all accounts a prosperous man.
source_text: Since the arrival of that letter, on a certain autumn morning long ago, the reddleman and Thomasin had not met till today. During the interval he had shifted his position even further from hers than it had originally been, by adopting the reddle trade; though he was really in very good circumstances still. Indeed, seeing that his expenditure was only one-fourth of his income, he might have been called a prosperous man.

text: He kept to the same routine for four more nights, but without any luck. On the following evening, however—exactly one week after their last meeting—he caught sight of a woman’s figure moving along the ridge and the silhouette of a young man climbing up from the valley. They met in the shallow trench surrounding the burial mound, the original excavation where ancient Britons had once dug the earth to pile it high.
source_text: He pursued precisely the same course yet four nights longer, and without success. But on the next, being the day-week of their previous meeting, he saw a female shape floating along the ridge and the outline of a young man ascending from the valley. They met in the little ditch encircling the barrow—the original excavation from which it had been thrown up by the ancient British people.

text: You want my advice on this?" Eustacia Vye’s voice reached him, resonant and full of her usual impulsive fire. "My advice? It’s an insult to even speak to me that way—I won’t stand for it a moment longer!" She broke into tears. "I’ve loved you, and I’ve let that love show, much to my own sorrow; yet you can stand there so coldly and ask if I think it would be better for you to marry Thomasin. Better—naturally it would be. Marry her, then—she fits your social circle far better than I do!
source_text: “Wish to consult me on the matter?” reached his ears in the rich, impetuous accents of Eustacia Vye. “Consult me? It is an indignity to me to talk so—I won’t bear it any longer!” She began weeping. “I have loved you, and have shown you that I loved you, much to my regret; and yet you can come and say in that frigid way that you wish to consult with me whether it would not be better to marry Thomasin. Better—of course it would be. Marry her—she is nearer to your own position in life than I am!”

text: “No, I will not hear it! You must see that you are only tormenting me. Damon, you have not conducted yourself well; you have fallen low in my estimation. You failed to cherish my affection—the favor of a lady who once entertained far grander ambitions. But it was Thomasin’s doing. She lured you away, and she deserves to suffer for it. Where is she staying now? Not that I truly care, any more than I care for my own fate. Ah, how she would rejoice if I were dead and buried! I ask you, where is she?”
source_text: “But you shall not tell me! You must see that it is only harassing me. Damon, you have not acted well; you have sunk in my opinion. You have not valued my courtesy—the courtesy of a lady in loving you—who used to think of far more ambitious things. But it was Thomasin’s fault. She won you away from me, and she deserves to suffer for it. Where is she staying now? Not that I care, nor where I am myself. Ah, if I were dead and gone how glad she would be! Where is she, I ask?”

text: “I won’t thank you for that. I would actually detest it if everything were perfectly easy. In fact, I think I prefer it when you pull away from me every once in a while. Love is such a dreary affair when a partner is entirely predictable. It’s a scandal to admit it, I know, but it’s the truth!” She gave a little laugh. “The mere thought of it makes me feel quite gloomy. Don’t you dare offer me a tame kind of love, or you’ll be out the door!”
source_text: “I do not thank you for that. I should hate it to be all smooth. Indeed, I think I like you to desert me a little once now and then. Love is the dismallest thing where the lover is quite honest. O, it is a shame to say so; but it is true!” She indulged in a little laugh. “My low spirits begin at the very idea. Don’t you offer me tame love, or away you go!”

text: I care a little, but not enough to lose any sleep," the young man replied with an air of indifference. "No, that’s all in the past. I’ve found that there are two blossoms where I once thought there was only one. Perhaps there are three, or four, or any number of them just as fine as the first... My life has taken a strange turn. Who would have guessed that all this would happen to me?
source_text: “I care a little, but not enough to break my rest,” replied the young man languidly. “No, all that’s past. I find there are two flowers where I thought there was only one. Perhaps there are three, or four, or any number as good as the first.... Mine is a curious fate. Who would have thought that all this could happen to me?”

text: After a mere glance at Diggory Venn, she sensed that his mission was a peculiar one and that he was not as lowly as she had first assumed. Her proximity did not make him fidget or shuffle his feet; he lacked those nervous tells that usually betray a simple countryman when faced with a remarkable woman. When he asked if they might speak, she replied, “Yes, walk with me,” and continued on her way.
source_text: She seemed to feel, after a bare look at Diggory Venn, that the man had come on a strange errand, and that he was not so mean as she had thought him; for her close approach did not cause him to writhe uneasily, or shift his feet, or show any of those little signs which escape an ingenuous rustic at the advent of the uncommon in womankind. On his inquiring if he might have a conversation with her she replied, “Yes, walk beside me,” and continued to move on.

text: “Never—I will not, I will not!” she exclaimed with a sudden fire, entirely losing the aloofness she usually displayed toward the peddler. “To be treated with such insolence! All was progressing smoothly—I refuse to be humiliated—especially by a woman so far beneath me. You may play the part of her champion if you wish, but has she not brought this misery upon herself? Am I required to petition a mob of peasants before I follow my own desires? She placed herself between me and my purpose, and now that she is justly rebuked, she recruits you to solicit my pity!”
source_text: “No—I won’t, I won’t!” she said impetuously, quite forgetful of her previous manner towards the reddleman as an underling. “Nobody has ever been served so! It was going on well—I will not be beaten down—by an inferior woman like her. It is very well for you to come and plead for her, but is she not herself the cause of all her own trouble? Am I not to show favour to any person I may choose without asking permission of a parcel of cottagers? She has come between me and my inclination, and now that she finds herself rightly punished she gets you to plead for her!”

text: The red-dye peddler appeared optimistic; following her remark, his third effort felt as though it might actually succeed. "Since we’ve finally started to speak more candidly, miss," he said, "I’ll tell you what I’m prepared to suggest. As you know, I’m on the road quite often now that I’ve taken up this trade."
source_text: The reddleman looked hopeful; after these words from her his third attempt seemed promising. “As we have now opened our minds a bit, miss,” he said, “I’ll tell you what I have got to propose. Since I have taken to the reddle trade I travel a good deal, as you know.”

text: Once Diggory Venn had vanished from sight, Eustacia moved to the ridge and peered down the rugged, beautiful valley toward the sun, which sat in the direction of Wildeve’s house. The fog had settled enough that the uppermost branches of the trees and shrubs near his home were just visible, appearing to pierce through a giant white web that hid them from the daylight. It was clear her thoughts were pulled that way, drifting and circling around him as the only point on her horizon where her fantasies could take form. The man who had once been nothing more than a casual diversion—and would have stayed a mere hobby if he hadn't known exactly when to abandon her—was now the center of her yearning once more. The end of his pursuit had reignited her passion. The idle interest Eustacia once felt for Wildeve had been dammed up by Thomasin’s involvement, turning it into a flood. She had once enjoyed teasing him, but that was before he had gained another woman's favor. Often, a single drop of irony is enough to make a bland situation suddenly sharp and intriguing.
source_text: When Diggory Venn had gone quite away, Eustacia walked to the bank and looked down the wild and picturesque vale towards the sun, which was also in the direction of Wildeve’s. The mist had now so far collapsed that the tips of the trees and bushes around his house could just be discerned, as if boring upwards through a vast white cobweb which cloaked them from the day. There was no doubt that her mind was inclined thitherward; indefinitely, fancifully—twining and untwining about him as the single object within her horizon on which dreams might crystallize. The man who had begun by being merely her amusement, and would never have been more than her hobby but for his skill in deserting her at the right moments, was now again her desire. Cessation in his love-making had revivified her love. Such feeling as Eustacia had idly given to Wildeve was dammed into a flood by Thomasin. She had used to tease Wildeve, but that was before another had favoured him. Often a drop of irony into an indifferent situation renders the whole piquant.

text: The scarlet-stained traveler had departed from Isolde’s threshold with a leaden heart, convinced that Lucy’s prospects for contentment were utterly eclipsed. Yet, as he made his way back toward his itinerant home, he realized that one final avenue of hope remained unexplored; there, silhouetted against the dusk, was the austere figure of Mrs. Ashworth pacing toward The Silent Lady. He struck out across the heath to intercept her, reading in her careworn expression a confirmation that her errand to Julian was prompted by the very same anxieties that had compelled his own visit to Isolde.
source_text: The reddleman had left Eustacia’s presence with desponding views on Thomasin’s future happiness; but he was awakened to the fact that one other channel remained untried by seeing, as he followed the way to his van, the form of Mrs. Yeobright slowly walking towards the Quiet Woman. He went across to her; and could almost perceive in her anxious face that this journey of hers to Wildeve was undertaken with the same object as his own to Eustacia.

text: “I held that conviction until this very hour. Yet, in truth, why should a mere afternoon’s excursion to Oakhaven in his company work her any lasting injury? To any witness of her inherent virtue, such a suspicion must seem a grave injustice. Only this morning, I labored to encourage her union with Sterling—yes, I, madam—believing it my duty to honor her devotion. But I find myself questioning the rectitude of that course now. In any case, the effort failed. And thus, I offer myself in his stead.”
source_text: “I thought that till half an hour ago. But, after all, why should her going off with him to Anglebury for a few hours do her any harm? Anybody who knows how pure she is will feel any such thought to be quite unjust. I have been trying this morning to help on this marriage with Wildeve—yes, I, ma’am—in the belief that I ought to do it, because she was so wrapped up in him. But I much question if I was right, after all. However, nothing came of it. And now I offer myself.”

text: Genevieve followed his retreating shadow until it vanished into the night. She rested a hand against her brow and exhaled a long, weighted breath; then, those plush, poetic lips gave way to a vulgar necessity—a yawn. She felt an immediate sting of anger at having signaled, if only to her own soul, the waning of her fire. To confess to Julian’s mediocrity at this late hour was to acknowledge the depth of her own previous delusions. Moreover, the dawning sense that her heart was ruled by the spiteful spirit of the dog in the manger left her, for a moment, profoundly ashamed.
source_text: Eustacia watched his shadowy form till it had disappeared. She placed her hand to her forehead and breathed heavily; and then her rich, romantic lips parted under that homely impulse—a yawn. She was immediately angry at having betrayed even to herself the possible evanescence of her passion for him. She could not admit at once that she might have overestimated Wildeve, for to perceive his mediocrity now was to admit her own great folly heretofore. And the discovery that she was the owner of a disposition so purely that of the dog in the manger had something in it which at first made her ashamed.

text: She retreated indoors, weighed down by a singular wretchedness that was not quite sorrow. It was the specific clarity that marks the awakening of reason during the final hours of a misguided and fleeting affair. To recognize that the illusion is fading, even if it has not yet vanished entirely, is one of the most draining and strange intervals in the long journey from the birth of a passion to its ultimate conclusion.
source_text: She went indoors in that peculiar state of misery which is not exactly grief, and which especially attends the dawnings of reason in the latter days of an ill-judged, transient love. To be conscious that the end of the dream is approaching, and yet has not absolutely come, is one of the most wearisome as well as the most curious stages along the course between the beginning of a passion and its end.

text: Old Master Thorne had come home, and was occupied with decanting several gallons of fresh-delivered spirits into the cubic flasks of his heavy sideboard. When these private stores ran dry, he would betake himself to The Mute Virgin, where, warming his coat-tails at the hearth with a steaming glass in his grip, he would recount extraordinary fables of surviving a full decade beneath the keel of a man-of-war, along with various other maritime miracles, to the local laborers, who were far too eager for a free draught of bitter to voice the slightest skepticism regarding his veracity.
source_text: Her grandfather had returned, and was busily engaged in pouring some gallons of newly arrived rum into the square bottles of his square cellaret. Whenever these home supplies were exhausted he would go to the Quiet Woman, and, standing with his back to the fire, grog in hand, tell remarkable stories of how he had lived seven years under the waterline of his ship, and other naval wonders, to the natives, who hoped too earnestly for a treat of ale from the teller to exhibit any doubts of his truth.

text: The day was tranquil and bright, the hour nearing three; yet the winter solstice had arrived with such quietude that the sun’s shallow path made the time feel much later than the reality. In this place, there was little to warn a traveler that he must discard his summer reliance on the heavens as a reliable clock. Through the slow procession of weeks, the dawn had shifted its anchor from the northeast toward the south, and the dusk had retreated accordingly; yet the vastness of Blackmoor remained utterly unmoved by the change.
source_text: It was a fine and quiet afternoon, about three o’clock; but the winter solstice having stealthily come on, the lowness of the sun caused the hour to seem later than it actually was, there being little here to remind an inhabitant that he must unlearn his summer experience of the sky as a dial. In the course of many days and weeks sunrise had advanced its quarters from northeast to southeast, sunset had receded from northwest to southwest; but Egdon had hardly heeded the change.

text: Eustacia waited inside the dining room, a space that resembled a kitchen with its flagstone floor and cavernous fireplace. In the heavy stillness, as she stood there alone, the murmur of a conversation reached her through the chimney. She moved into the hearth to listen, glancing up the uneven, ancient shaft. Within its dark recesses, smoke drifted lazily toward the small patch of sky above, while a weak light filtered down to reveal layers of soot hanging from the flue like seaweed clinging to a rocky crevice.
source_text: Eustacia was indoors in the dining-room, which was really more like a kitchen, having a stone floor and a gaping chimney-corner. The air was still, and while she lingered a moment here alone sounds of voices in conversation came to her ears directly down the chimney. She entered the recess, and, listening, looked up the old irregular shaft, with its cavernous hollows, where the smoke blundered about on its way to the square bit of sky at the top, from which the daylight struck down with a pallid glare upon the tatters of soot draping the flue as seaweed drapes a rocky fissure.

text: Her grandfather spoke up, entering the discussion. “That young man should never have gone away. His father’s trade would have served him well, and he ought to have carried it on. I’ve no trust in these modern changes within a family. My father was a man of the sea, as was I, and my son would have followed our path, had I been given one.”
source_text: Her grandfather joined in the conversation. “That lad ought never to have left home. His father’s occupation would have suited him best, and the boy should have followed on. I don’t believe in these new moves in families. My father was a sailor, so was I, and so should my son have been if I had had one.”

text: “That city he inhabits is Paris,” Silas observed, “and folks say it’s the very ground where they severed the monarch’s neck in times gone by. My old mother used to dwell on that dark affair. ‘Si,’ she’d say to me, ‘I was but a slip of a girl then, and as I stood smoothing out my mother’s kerchiefs one day, the curate walked in and said, “They’ve shortened the king by a head, Martha; and heaven only knows what ruin comes next.”’”
source_text: “The place he’s been living at is Paris,” said Humphrey, “and they tell me ’tis where the king’s head was cut off years ago. My poor mother used to tell me about that business. ‘Hummy,’ she used to say, ‘I was a young maid then, and as I was at home ironing Mother’s caps one afternoon the parson came in and said, “They’ve cut the king’s head off, Jane; and what ’twill be next God knows.’”

text: It’s a wretched business regarding his cousin Thomasin. I’m surprised a man of Clym’s high standards would return home to such a scandal. We were certainly in a state when we learned they weren’t married, especially after serenading them as man and wife that night! I would be ashamed if a relative of mine were made such a fool of; it truly diminishes the family’s reputation.
source_text: “That’s a bad trouble about his cousin Thomasin. I wonder such a nice-notioned fellow as Clym likes to come home into it. What a nunnywatch we were in, to be sure, when we heard they weren’t married at all, after singing to ’em as man and wife that night! Be dazed if I should like a relation of mine to have been made such a fool of by a man. It makes the family look small.”

text: Elspeth bent her gaze back to the pears. “I am held up as a deterrent,” she whispered, “just as the brigand, the drunkard, and the card-sharp are. What a circle to be joined to! Is that truly my place? It is madness! Yet why, Aunt, does everyone persist in convincing me I belong there by their very treatment of me? Why am I not measured by my conduct? Look at me now, kneeling here to gather this fruit—do I bear the likeness of a ruined woman? I only wish that every lady of repute were as truly virtuous as I!” she exclaimed with passion.
source_text: Thomasin lowered her face to the apples again. “I am a warning to others, just as thieves and drunkards and gamblers are,” she said in a low voice. “What a class to belong to! Do I really belong to them? ’Tis absurd! Yet why, Aunt, does everybody keep on making me think that I do, by the way they behave towards me? Why don’t people judge me by my acts? Now, look at me as I kneel here, picking up these apples—do I look like a lost woman?... I wish all good women were as good as I!” she added vehemently.

text: “I definitely won’t do that. I have no desire to appear humbled in front of everyone in Egdon, or to be toyed with by a man like Wildeve. I believe we’ve gathered enough berries by now, so we should head back. Once we've finished decorating the house with these and putting up the mistletoe, it will be time for us to set out and meet him.”
source_text: “Of course I shall not. I do not wish to show myself beaten before all Egdon, and the sport of a man like Wildeve. We have enough berries now, I think, and we had better take them home. By the time we have decked the house with this and hung up the mistletoe, we must think of starting to meet him.”

text: Isolde started from her sleep. The sudden snap was the heavy blind in the hall below, which the servant-girl was unlatching to invite the morning—a gray light now doled out with the parsimony of the heavens in this bleak stretch of winter. “Would that I had beheld his countenance!” she sighed again. “The vision was meant for Mr. Sterling!”
source_text: Eustacia awoke. The cracking had been that of the window shutter downstairs, which the maid-servant was opening to let in the day, now slowly increasing to Nature’s meagre allowance at this sickly time of the year. “O that I had seen his face!” she said again. “’Twas meant for Mr. Yeobright!”

text: Yet such delicate aspirations were impractical amidst the lonely dwellings of Blackwood Moor. Though technically counted as congregants, the inhabitants were effectively exiled from any spiritual community. Guests who braved the distance to these remote cottages for the winter festivities chose to linger by the blazing logs, savoring spiced wine and potent cordials until their holiday concluded. Surrounded by a wilderness of drenching rains and treacherous drifts, they felt no impulse to struggle through the mire for miles, only to sit damp and bedraggled among the townspeople who lived within a stone’s throw of the altar and remained pristine. Elara perceived that the chances were remote that Julian Vane would attend a service at all during his short respite, and it would be a fruitless endeavor to navigate her carriage across the broken terrain in the vain hope of finding him there.
source_text: But these tender schemes were not feasible among the scattered inhabitants of Egdon Heath. In name they were parishioners, but virtually they belonged to no parish at all. People who came to these few isolated houses to keep Christmas with their friends remained in their friends’ chimney-corners drinking mead and other comforting liquors till they left again for good and all. Rain, snow, ice, mud everywhere around, they did not care to trudge two or three miles to sit wet-footed and splashed to the nape of their necks among those who, though in some measure neighbours, lived close to the church, and entered it clean and dry. Eustacia knew it was ten to one that Clym Yeobright would go to no church at all during his few days of leave, and that it would be a waste of labour for her to go driving the pony and gig over a bad road in hope to see him there.

text: Perhaps Joe, representing the side of Christendom, possessed a sweetheart, just as Jim, who fought for the Moslem side, had one of his own. While the costumes were being fashioned, Joe’s beloved might discover that Jim’s was adorning her partner’s surcoat with vibrant silk scallops along the hem, in addition to the ribbons on the visor—the bars of which, invariably made of half-inch wide colored strips hanging before the face, were mostly composed of that same material. Joe’s sweetheart would immediately apply brilliant silk to the scallops of his hem and, going a step further, attach tufts of ribbon to the shoulders. Jim’s sweetheart, refusing to be outdone, would then respond by affixing bows and rosettes over every available space.
source_text: It might be that Joe, who fought on the side of Christendom, had a sweetheart, and that Jim, who fought on the side of the Moslem, had one likewise. During the making of the costumes it would come to the knowledge of Joe’s sweetheart that Jim’s was putting brilliant silk scallops at the bottom of her lover’s surcoat, in addition to the ribbons of the visor, the bars of which, being invariably formed of coloured strips about half an inch wide hanging before the face, were mostly of that material. Joe’s sweetheart straight-way placed brilliant silk on the scallops of the hem in question, and, going a little further, added ribbon tufts to the shoulder pieces. Jim’s, not to be outdone, would affix bows and rosettes everywhere.

text: Eustacia’s countenance clouded. A party was planned at the Yeobrights', an event from which she was, as usual, excluded. She remained a stranger to these local festivities, having long regarded them as beneath her social orbit. Yet, had she been invited, what a chance it would have been to see the man whose presence was warming her spirit like the summer sun! To deepen that connection was a desired thrill; to sever it might restore her peace; but to leave things as they were was simply agonizing.
source_text: Eustacia’s face flagged. There was to be a party at the Yeobrights’; she, naturally, had nothing to do with it. She was a stranger to all such local gatherings, and had always held them as scarcely appertaining to her sphere. But had she been going, what an opportunity would have been afforded her of seeing the man whose influence was penetrating her like summer sun! To increase that influence was coveted excitement; to cast it off might be to regain serenity; to leave it as it stood was tantalizing.

text: Whatever you prefer," she said, her voice entirely devoid of emotion. "But this must be finished within a week. Now, there is only one thing I want you to do—wait here while I change into the dress, and then judge whether I am playing the part correctly. But first, let me check inside.
source_text: “As you like,” said she without the least emotion. “But it must be over in a week. Now, there is only one thing I want you to do—to wait while I put on the dress, and then to see if I do my part properly. But let me look first indoors.”

text: Quite so. It is imperative, naturally, that you remain out of sight. I intend to arrive a few minutes past the hour, fully attired, and convey the news that you are unable to attend. I have concluded that the wisest strategy is for me to dispatch you on a genuine errand, so that the pretext may be grounded in fact. Since our two heath-ponies are known to drift toward the meadows, you shall go tomorrow evening to see if they have wandered there. The remaining arrangements I will handle myself. You may depart now.
source_text: “Yes. Well, you of course must not appear. I will march in about five minutes late, ready-dressed, and tell them that you can’t come. I have decided that the best plan will be for you to be sent somewhere by me, to make a real thing of the excuse. Our two heath-croppers are in the habit of straying into the meads, and tomorrow evening you can go and see if they are gone there. I’ll manage the rest. Now you may leave me.”

text: Just then, the violins wound up with a sharp squeal, and the serpent let out a final note that seemed to rattle the very rafters. Once the relative silence from within signaled that the dancers had returned to their seats, Father Christmas stepped forward, unfastened the latch, and peered into the room.
source_text: At this moment the fiddles finished off with a screech, and the serpent emitted a last note that nearly lifted the roof. When, from the comparative quiet within, the mummers judged that the dancers had taken their seats, Father Christmas advanced, lifted the latch, and put his head inside the door.

text: As she delivered her lines, Eustacia kept her chin high and spoke with a forced coarseness, feeling reasonably confident that her disguise would hold. However, the intense focus required to maintain her ruse, the unfamiliarity of the setting, the flicker of the candlelight, and the way the fringed mask obscured her vision left her completely unable to identify the onlookers. Beyond a table laden with candles, she could just barely make out the shapes of faces, but nothing more.
source_text: During her declamation Eustacia held her head erect, and spoke as roughly as she could, feeling pretty secure from observation. But the concentration upon her part necessary to prevent discovery, the newness of the scene, the shine of the candles, and the confusing effect upon her vision of the ribboned visor which hid her features, left her absolutely unable to perceive who were present as spectators. On the further side of a table bearing candles she could faintly discern faces, and that was all.

text: That slow, rhythmic sinking toward the ground was a major reason Eustacia believed the Turkish Knight’s role, though not the briefest, was her ideal fit. To tumble straight from an upright posture to a flat sprawl—the fate of the other combatants—lacked the grace and modesty she required. Yet dying in the manner of the Turk, through a steady and stubborn collapse, was a feat she could manage with decorum.
source_text: This gradual sinking to the earth was, in fact, one reason why Eustacia had thought that the part of the Turkish Knight, though not the shortest, would suit her best. A direct fall from upright to horizontal, which was the end of the other fighting characters, was not an elegant or decorous part for a girl. But it was easy to die like a Turk, by a dogged decline.

text: Elara now counted herself among the casualties of the fray, though she avoided the floor by reclining against the upright clock-case, her head propped at a graceful height. As the performance unfolded between the Knight of the Cross, the Turkish Warrior, the Apothecary, and Father Yule, Elara—her duties finished—at last found the opportunity to scrutinize her surroundings and seek out the particular silhouette that had enticed her there.
source_text: Eustacia was now among the number of the slain, though not on the floor, for she had managed to sink into a sloping position against the clock-case, so that her head was well elevated. The play proceeded between Saint George, the Saracen, the Doctor, and Father Christmas; and Eustacia, having no more to do, for the first time found leisure to observe the scene round, and to search for the form that had drawn her hither.

text: To a person in their middle years, his features appeared youthful, yet a younger observer would likely have found no reason to label him as truly immature. It was a face that suggested not so much a specific span of time, but rather a profound depth of lived experience. While a simple tally of years might have sufficed to define Jared, Mahalaleel, and the other ancient patriarchs, the age of a modern man is gauged instead by the density and weight of his personal history.
source_text: To one of middle age the countenance was that of a young man, though a youth might hardly have seen any necessity for the term of immaturity. But it was really one of those faces which convey less the idea of so many years as its age than of so much experience as its store. The number of their years may have adequately summed up Jared, Mahalaleel, and the rest of the antediluvians, but the age of a modern man is to be measured by the intensity of his history.

text: They raised the mournful dirge that serves as the epilogue, a time when the slain performers regained their feet with a ghostly and hushed gravity, akin to the spectral battalions of the Great Captain at a midnight inspection. Shortly thereafter, the latch turned and Halloway entered, followed by Julian and another. They had lingered in the cold until the play’s end, much as the actors had previously waited for the dancers to finish their rounds.
source_text: They sang the plaintive chant which follows the play, during which all the dead men rise to their feet in a silent and awful manner, like the ghosts of Napoleon’s soldiers in the Midnight Review. Afterwards the door opened, and Fairway appeared on the threshold, accompanied by Christian and another. They had been waiting outside for the conclusion of the play, as the players had waited for the conclusion of the dance.

text: I really should have arrived sooner," Mr. Fairway remarked, pausing to scan the ceiling beam for a nail to hang his hat on. But seeing his usual spot occupied by mistletoe and every nail on the walls weighed down with bunches of holly, he finally set the hat aside by precariously balancing it between the candle box and the top of the clock case. "I should have been here earlier, ma'am," he resumed, looking more at ease, "but I know how these parties go, and how little space there is in people's homes at such times, so I thought I'd wait until things had settled down a bit.
source_text: “Well, I should have come earlier,” Mr. Fairway said and paused to look along the beam of the ceiling for a nail to hang his hat on; but, finding his accustomed one to be occupied by the mistletoe, and all the nails in the walls to be burdened with bunches of holly, he at last relieved himself of the hat by ticklishly balancing it between the candle-box and the head of the clock-case. “I should have come earlier, ma’am,” he resumed, with a more composed air, “but I know what parties be, and how there’s none too much room in folks’ houses at such times, so I thought I wouldn’t come till you’d got settled a bit.”

text: “Still, it wasn't through any merit of my own, and I take no pride in the matter,” the gratified old man remarked. “But I've never been able to rid myself of my peculiar whims; I’ll freely admit to those. Yes, Master Cantle was always known for such things, as everyone is aware. Yet I am a small figure indeed when set beside you, Mr. Clym.”
source_text: “And yet not my own doing, and I feel no pride in it,” said the pleased ancient. “But I can’t be cured of my vagaries; them I plead guilty to. Yes, Master Cantle always was that, as we know. But I am nothing by the side of you, Mister Clym.”

text: Yeobright re-entered the parlor, no longer accompanied by his cousin. He drew within a few feet of Eustacia before coming to a halt, as though some sudden reflection had once again seized him. He stood there, his eyes fixed upon her. Disturbed by his scrutiny, she looked away, wondering how much longer this agonizing interval was destined to endure. After lingering for several seconds, he finally continued on his way.
source_text: Yeobright returned to the room without his cousin. When within two or three feet of Eustacia he stopped, as if again arrested by a thought. He was gazing at her. She looked another way, disconcerted, and wondered how long this purgatory was to last. After lingering a few seconds he passed on again.

text: She arrived at the small gate of Thorn-Edge, yet before lifting the latch, she paused to survey the sprawling moorland one last time. The massive bulk of Raven-Mound rose darkly against the horizon, with the moon poised like a sentinel directly above its peak. The night was heavy with a frozen quietude. This prospect suddenly recalled to Elara’s mind a duty she had, until that very second, completely overlooked. She had vowed to meet Silas at the Mound at eight o'clock this night, to grant him a definitive response to his desperate petitions that they flee the country together.
source_text: She reached the wicket at Mistover Knap, but before opening it she turned and faced the heath once more. The form of Rainbarrow stood above the hills, and the moon stood above Rainbarrow. The air was charged with silence and frost. The scene reminded Eustacia of a circumstance which till that moment she had totally forgotten. She had promised to meet Wildeve by the Barrow this very night at eight, to give a final answer to his pleading for an elopement.

text: Blackmound had once more merged with the gloom when Thorne scaled the steep rise at its foot. As he crested the peak, a silhouette emerged from the dark soil immediately behind him. It was the lady’s secret messenger. He clapped Thorne upon the back. The anxious tavern-keeper and erstwhile surveyor recoiled as though a seraph’s touch had exposed his hidden frame.
source_text: Rainbarrow had again become blended with night when Wildeve ascended the long acclivity at its base. On his reaching the top a shape grew up from the earth immediately behind him. It was that of Eustacia’s emissary. He slapped Wildeve on the shoulder. The feverish young inn-keeper and ex-engineer started like Satan at the touch of Ithuriel’s spear.

text: Wildeve kept his eyes fixed on the earth near Venn’s boots until he let his gaze climb slowly up Diggory’s figure, bathed in the candle’s glow, to rest upon his features. “Ha! Well, I suppose this is my due, given how I’ve toyed with both women,” he remarked eventually, half to himself and half to Venn. “But of all the peculiar turns I’ve witnessed, the most bizarre is that you would work so directly against your own advantage to bring this to me.”
source_text: Wildeve stood looking on the ground beside Venn’s feet, till he allowed his eyes to travel upwards over Diggory’s form, as illuminated by the candle, to his head and face. “Ha-ha! Well, I suppose I deserve it, considering how I have played with them both,” he said at last, as much to himself as to Venn. “But of all the odd things that ever I knew, the oddest is that you should so run counter to your own interests as to bring this to me.”

text: He had gained the silvered rails and was about to unfasten the latch when the dwelling's entrance parted and swiftly fell shut. A feminine shape had retreated into the interior. In that same instant, a man who had evidently been conversing with her under the lintel stepped out from the building's shadow until he confronted Silas eye to eye. It was Ashworth once more.
source_text: He had reached the white palings and laid his hand upon the gate when the door of the house opened, and quickly closed again. A female form had glided in. At the same time a man, who had seemingly been standing with the woman in the porch, came forward from the house till he was face to face with Venn. It was Wildeve again.

text: Yes," Mrs. Yeobright said with a heavy heart, putting the letter aside. "If you believe you can marry him, then do it. And if Mr. Wildeve wants the wedding to be modest, let it be so. I can't do anything; the choice is yours now. My authority over your life ended the day you walked out of this house to follow him to Anglebury." She continued, her tone sharpening with bitterness, "I might as well ask why you’re even seeking my advice. If you had just married him without telling me, I could barely have found the strength to be angry—for the simple reason that, you poor girl, you have no better alternative.
source_text: “Yes,” Mrs. Yeobright said sadly, putting down the letter. “If you think you can marry him, do so. And since Mr. Wildeve wishes it to be unceremonious, let it be that too. I can do nothing. It is all in your own hands now. My power over your welfare came to an end when you left this house to go with him to Anglebury.” She continued, half in bitterness, “I may almost ask, why do you consult me in the matter at all? If you had gone and married him without saying a word to me, I could hardly have been angry—simply because, poor girl, you can’t do a better thing.”

text: The following day was surrendered to the numb routine of arrangement, as both women endeavored to bury themselves in taskwork to shun the spiritual shadow of the event. A fresh wardrobe and various effects were marshaled for Catherine, and their talk ran perpetually on the trivialities of the home, the better to stifle those secret anxieties touching her destiny as the mistress of Thorne’s house.
source_text: The next day was passed in mere mechanical deeds of preparation, both the women being anxious to immerse themselves in these to escape the emotional aspect of the situation. Some wearing apparel and other articles were collected anew for Thomasin, and remarks on domestic details were frequently made, so as to obscure any inner misgivings about her future as Wildeve’s wife.

text: I have decided to wear my blue silk after all," she remarked. "It is my wedding day, even if there is a certain somberness surrounding the occasion. I mean," she added, eager to ensure she wasn't misunderstood, "not that the day itself is unhappy, but rather that it follows so much heartache and misfortune.
source_text: “I have been thinking that I will wear my blue silk after all,” she said. “It is my wedding day, even though there may be something sad about the time. I mean,” she added, anxious to correct any wrong impression, “not sad in itself, but in its having had great disappointment and trouble before it.”

text: He was a young man from whom much was anticipated, yet outside of that singular promise, everything remained a muddle. It appeared just as likely that he would achieve success through some unconventional path as it was that he would ruin himself in an equally singular way. The one undeniable truth was that he could never stay stagnant within the station of his birth.
source_text: He had been a lad of whom something was expected. Beyond this all had been chaos. That he would be successful in an original way, or that he would go to the dogs in an original way, seemed equally probable. The only absolute certainty about him was that he would not stand still in the circumstances amid which he was born.

text: The minutiae of how this career was settled upon him do not demand a full accounting. When his father died, a compassionate local dignitary had stepped forward to provide the youth a beginning, which resulted in his being sent away to Kingsbridge. Though Ashworth felt little inclination for the move, it stood as the only practical door left open. From that town he progressed to the capital, and shortly thereafter to Paris, where he had lingered until the present time.
source_text: The details of this choice of a business for him it is not necessary to give. At the death of his father a neighbouring gentleman had kindly undertaken to give the boy a start, and this assumed the form of sending him to Budmouth. Yeobright did not wish to go there, but it was the only feasible opening. Thence he went to London; and thence, shortly after, to Paris, where he had remained till now.

text: “I’ll explain,” Yeobright said with a sudden, intense sincerity. “I’m actually glad for the chance to say this. I’ve moved back because, looking at the big picture, I might be slightly more useful here than in any other place. But that’s a realization I’ve only come to recently. When I first left home, I believed this area wasn't worth a second thought. I viewed our way of life here as beneath me. Conditioning your boots with oil instead of polish, or beating the dust off your jacket with a twig rather than a proper brush—I used to ask myself if anything could be more absurd.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you,” said Yeobright with unexpected earnestness. “I am not sorry to have the opportunity. I’ve come home because, all things considered, I can be a trifle less useless here than anywhere else. But I have only lately found this out. When I first got away from home I thought this place was not worth troubling about. I thought our life here was contemptible. To oil your boots instead of blacking them, to dust your coat with a switch instead of a brush—was there ever anything more ridiculous? I said.”

text: As my perspective shifted, the path I followed grew increasingly bleak. I realized I was trying to emulate people with whom I shared almost no common ground. I was striving to trade one way of life for another, only to find that this new existence was no better than the one I had known before. It was not superior; it was merely different.
source_text: “Well, as my views changed my course became very depressing. I found that I was trying to be like people who had hardly anything in common with myself. I was endeavouring to put off one sort of life for another sort of life, which was not better than the life I had known before. It was simply different.”

text: To gain true influence, one's primary strength should be a mastery over practical affairs. The most effective leaders are those who give structure to the vague sentiments their audience has long harbored but could never quite define. One who promotes intellectual growth while devaluing material ambition will likely only be heard by those who have already grown weary of the struggle for wealth. To suggest to the laboring poor that culture should take precedence over comfort may be theoretically sound, yet it disrupts a developmental path that humanity has followed for ages. In this sense, Yeobright’s attempt to convince the reclusive folk of Egdon that they could achieve a state of enlightened wisdom without first escaping their poverty was much like telling ancient Chaldean scholars that one might ascend to the highest celestial sphere without ever passing through the lower heavens.
source_text: In the interests of renown the forwardness should lie chiefly in the capacity to handle things. Successful propagandists have succeeded because the doctrine they bring into form is that which their listeners have for some time felt without being able to shape. A man who advocates æsthetic effort and deprecates social effort is only likely to be understood by a class to which social effort has become a stale matter. To argue upon the possibility of culture before luxury to the bucolic world may be to argue truly, but it is an attempt to disturb a sequence to which humanity has been long accustomed. Yeobright preaching to the Egdon eremites that they might rise to a serene comprehensiveness without going through the process of enriching themselves was not unlike arguing to ancient Chaldeans that in ascending from earth to the pure empyrean it was not necessary to pass first into the intervening heaven of ether.

text: Was Sterling’s temperament truly equable? By no means. An equable temperament is one devoid of any sharp inclination; it is the kind of mind of which it can be predicted that it will never lead its bearer to the cell of the lunatic, the fire of the martyr, or the cross of the rebel. Yet, by the same token, it will never see him acclaimed as a seer, venerated as a patriarch, or seated upon a throne. Its customary fruits are serenity and the commonplace. It yields the rhymes of a Whitehead, the portraits of a Lely, the diplomacy of a Grenville, the sermons of a Porteus; granting its tenants the ability to amass a fortune, to settle their accounts with precision, to depart the scene with decorum, to breathe their last in soft surroundings, and to earn the solid cenotaph which is frequently their just reward. It would never have suffered Sterling to engage in the folly of renouncing his profession to serve the common good.
source_text: Was Yeobright’s mind well-proportioned? No. A well proportioned mind is one which shows no particular bias; one of which we may safely say that it will never cause its owner to be confined as a madman, tortured as a heretic, or crucified as a blasphemer. Also, on the other hand, that it will never cause him to be applauded as a prophet, revered as a priest, or exalted as a king. Its usual blessings are happiness and mediocrity. It produces the poetry of Rogers, the paintings of West, the statecraft of North, the spiritual guidance of Tomline; enabling its possessors to find their way to wealth, to wind up well, to step with dignity off the stage, to die comfortably in their beds, and to get the decent monument which, in many cases, they deserve. It never would have allowed Yeobright to do such a ridiculous thing as throw up his business to benefit his fellow-creatures.

text: He made his way down the slope, soon arriving at the hearth of Hawthorn-Glade. His mother stood by the casement, shearing brittle stems from the potted ferns. She raised her eyes to him with a look of quiet bafflement at his continued presence, a shadow that had not left her brow for several days. He saw clearly that what the villagers at the tavern felt as mere interest was, in her, a deep-seated trepidation. Still, she voiced no inquiry, even as the unpacking of his great valise hinted at a long duration. Her very reticence demanded an account of his intentions more urgently than any cry.
source_text: He descended into the valley, and soon reached his home at Blooms-End. His mother was snipping dead leaves from the window-plants. She looked up at him as if she did not understand the meaning of his long stay with her; her face had worn that look for several days. He could perceive that the curiosity which had been shown by the hair-cutting group amounted in his mother to concern. But she had asked no question with her lips, even when the arrival of his trunk suggested that he was not going to leave her soon. Her silence besought an explanation of him more loudly than words.

text: It would be simple enough. Yet I won’t be "improving" myself in the sense you imagine; most would likely consider it a step backward. I detest my current line of work, and I hope to perform some significant service before I pass away. I plan to find that purpose as a schoolteacher—one dedicated to the poor and the unlearned, teaching them the very lessons that everyone else neglects.
source_text: “Very easily. But I shall not do better in the way you mean; I suppose it will be called doing worse. But I hate that business of mine, and I want to do some worthy thing before I die. As a schoolmaster I think to do it—a school-master to the poor and ignorant, to teach them what nobody else will.”

text: Noticing that Julian’s attention was caught with a peculiar intensity, Mrs. Thorne spoke with a shadow of anxiety to Silas: "You perceive a depth in that girl which escapes the rest of us. To my way of thinking, Miss Morelle is too steeped in sloth to possess any real allure. I have never heard it suggested that she is of any utility to her own person or to the world. Even in a wild place like Blackwood, a virtuous daughter is never mistaken for a weaver of spells."
source_text: Observing that Clym appeared singularly interested Mrs. Yeobright said rather uneasily to Sam, “You see more in her than most of us do. Miss Vye is to my mind too idle to be charming. I have never heard that she is of any use to herself or to other people. Good girls don’t get treated as witches even on Egdon.”

text: He moved across the gorse with singular purpose, treading a path as direct as an arrow, as if driven by some desperate necessity. His mother exhaled slowly and, giving up on her intention to see Thomasin, turned to head home. Although the evening haze was already blurring the valleys into indistinct shapes, the high ridges were still illuminated by the low, sharp rays of the setting winter sun. This light fell upon Clym as he strode forward, watched by every rabbit and bird in the brush, while his elongated shadow stretched out far in front of him.
source_text: He was, indeed, walking with a will over the furze, as straight as a line, as if his life depended upon it. His mother drew a long breath, and, abandoning the visit to Thomasin, turned back. The evening films began to make nebulous pictures of the valleys, but the high lands still were raked by the declining rays of the winter sun, which glanced on Clym as he walked forward, eyed by every rabbit and field-fare around, a long shadow advancing in front of him.

text: Six sturdy men stood in a row leading from the well’s mouth, gripping a heavy rope that wound over the pulley and into the darkness below. Fairway, with a thinner cord lashed around his waist and secured to one of the upright posts as a safety measure, leaned out over the opening. His right hand was clamped firmly onto the vertical line as it descended straight into the depths of the shaft.
source_text: Half a dozen able-bodied men were standing in a line from the well-mouth, holding a rope which passed over the well-roller into the depths below. Fairway, with a piece of smaller rope round his body, made fast to one of the standards, to guard against accidents, was leaning over the opening, his right hand clasping the vertical rope that descended into the well.

text: Isabella, her cheeks flushing as she noted the stir her outcry had caused among the company below, had retreated from the casement, though Julian continued to search the dark pane with a yearning eye. During his vigil, the laborers at the cistern finally drew up the bucket, the task completed without further accident. One fellow went to seek the Major, intending to ask how the broken windlass should be mended. The old soldier was found to be abroad, and in his stead Isabella appeared at the entrance and stepped forth. She had reassumed a graceful and lofty composure, quite distinct from the spirited desperation that had marked her earlier concern for Julian’s welfare.
source_text: Eustacia, who had reddened when she perceived the effect of her exclamation upon the group below, was no longer to be seen at the window, though Yeobright scanned it wistfully. While he stood there the men at the well succeeded in getting up the bucket without a mishap. One of them went to inquire for the captain, to learn what orders he wished to give for mending the well-tackle. The captain proved to be away from home, and Eustacia appeared at the door and came out. She had lapsed into an easy and dignified calm, far removed from the intensity of life in her words of solicitude for Clym’s safety.

text: Clym glanced toward the well, which stood empty now that the men had gone home for the day. "That’s quite a distance to go for spring water," he remarked after a brief pause. "But since you aren't fond of what’s in the pond, I’ll see if I can fetch some for you myself." He walked back to the well. "Yeah, I think I can make it work by fastening this bucket to it."
source_text: Clym looked towards the well, which was now deserted, the men having gone home. “It is a long way to send for spring-water,” he said, after a silence. “But since you don’t like this in the pond, I’ll try to get you some myself.” He went back to the well. “Yes, I think I could do it by tying on this pail.”

text: They parted ways, and Eustacia soon faded into the deepening gloom. She was overwhelmed by a sense of renewal; her past had been wiped away, and her life was only just starting. Clym did not immediately recognize the profound effect this meeting had on him. On his journey home, his clearest thought was that his ambitions had somehow been elevated. A captivating woman had become woven into the fabric of his future.
source_text: They separated, and Eustacia vanished in the increasing shade. She seemed full of many things. Her past was a blank, her life had begun. The effect upon Clym of this meeting he did not fully discover till some time after. During his walk home his most intelligible sensation was that his scheme had somehow become glorified. A beautiful woman had been intertwined with it.

text: Julian felt no inclination to dispel the notion, though his vision for the schooling of the populace was entirely distinct from any desire to use learning as a ladder for social climbing. He possessed no such vanity. He had reached that particular season of early manhood when the inherent tragedy of the human struggle first comes into sharp focus; and the weight of this truth causes all personal striving to falter for a time. In France, it is a common enough fashion to seek an early grave at this juncture; in England, we behave with far greater virtue, or far greater cowardice, depending on how one chooses to see it.
source_text: Yeobright was unwilling to disturb this idea, though his scheme was far enough removed from one wherein the education of youth should be made a mere channel of social ascent. He had no desires of that sort. He had reached the stage in a young man’s life when the grimness of the general human situation first becomes clear; and the realization of this causes ambition to halt awhile. In France it is not uncustomary to commit suicide at this stage; in England we do much better, or much worse, as the case may be.

text: Her understanding of life was remarkably profound, especially given that she had never truly participated in it. There are cases of people who, despite having no direct experience of the subjects they analyze, still possess a keen sense of how those subjects relate to one another. Blacklock, a poet blind from birth, was able to describe the visual world with startling precision; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave masterful lectures on the properties of color, teaching others about concepts they could see while he could not. In the social realm, these gifted individuals are often women; they are able to observe a world they have never set foot in and gauge the strength of forces they have only heard about. We label this gift intuition.
source_text: She had a singular insight into life, considering that she had never mixed with it. There are instances of persons who, without clear ideas of the things they criticize have yet had clear ideas of the relations of those things. Blacklock, a poet blind from his birth, could describe visual objects with accuracy; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave excellent lectures on colour, and taught others the theory of ideas which they had and he had not. In the social sphere these gifted ones are mostly women; they can watch a world which they never saw, and estimate forces of which they have only heard. We call it intuition.

text: To Mrs. Yeobright, the wider world was merely a collection of trends rather than individual spirits. She viewed society from a detached height, seeing communities as one might view the dense crowds in the works of Sallaert or Van Alsloot—great tides of humanity shifting and surging toward fixed ends, yet with every face lost in the sheer scale of the panorama.
source_text: What was the great world to Mrs. Yeobright? A multitude whose tendencies could be perceived, though not its essences. Communities were seen by her as from a distance; she saw them as we see the throngs which cover the canvases of Sallaert, Van Alsloot, and others of that school—vast masses of beings, jostling, zigzagging, and processioning in definite directions, but whose features are indistinguishable by the very comprehensiveness of the view.

text: “I am quite aware that your resolution preceded your acquaintance with her; yet, left to itself, such a scheme would have withered into mere rhetoric. It served as a charming subject for discourse, but as a practical course of action, it was preposterous. I had every confidence that, within a brief season, the absurdity of such martyrdom would have dawned upon you, and that you would have long since retreated to Vienna to occupy yourself with some more sensible pursuit. I grant that the mercantile world of textiles might have seemed a narrow theater for your particular talents, regardless of the fortune it promised. However, witnessing your profound delusion regarding this young woman, I find I must now view all your perceptions with the gravest skepticism.”
source_text: “Well, I know you had decided to attempt it before you saw her; but that would have ended in intentions. It was very well to talk of, but ridiculous to put in practice. I fully expected that in the course of a month or two you would have seen the folly of such self-sacrifice, and would have been by this time back again to Paris in some business or other. I can understand objections to the diamond trade—I really was thinking that it might be inadequate to the life of a man like you even though it might have made you a millionaire. But now I see how mistaken you are about this girl I doubt if you could be correct about other things.”

text: “She is exceptionally well-educated and would be an ideal administrator for a boarding school. I will admit that I have adjusted my perspective slightly out of respect for your opinion, which I hope satisfies you.
