I no longer intend to personally provide basic instruction to the underprivileged. I can achieve something more significant. I can establish a reputable private academy for the sons of farmers, and I can manage my own qualifications without interrupting the school’s operation. With this strategy, and the support of a wife like her—”
source_text: “She is excellently educated, and would make a good matron in a boarding-school. I candidly own that I have modified my views a little, in deference to you; and it should satisfy you. I no longer adhere to my intention of giving with my own mouth rudimentary education to the lowest class. I can do better. I can establish a good private school for farmers’ sons, and without stopping the school I can manage to pass examinations. By this means, and by the assistance of a wife like her——”

text: As he observed the distant horizon, a brownish smudge began to form on the moon's lower edge—the eclipse was underway. This was the designated moment, for the cosmic event had been drafted into earthly service as a signal for the lovers. At the sight, Yeobright’s focus snapped back to his surroundings; he stood, shook off his lethargy, and began to listen. The minutes ticked by, perhaps ten in all, as the shadow across the lunar disc grew noticeably larger. Suddenly, a rustling sound came from his left, and a cloaked figure looking toward the sky appeared at the base of the mound. Clym climbed down to meet her. In an instant, she was in his embrace, and he pressed his lips to hers.
source_text: While he watched the far-removed landscape a tawny stain grew into being on the lower verge—the eclipse had begun. This marked a preconcerted moment—for the remote celestial phenomenon had been pressed into sublunary service as a lover’s signal. Yeobright’s mind flew back to earth at the sight; he arose, shook himself and listened. Minute after minute passed by, perhaps ten minutes passed, and the shadow on the moon perceptibly widened. He heard a rustling on his left hand, a cloaked figure with an upturned face appeared at the base of the Barrow, and Clym descended. In a moment the figure was in his arms, and his lips upon hers.

text: As the initial radiance of love and beauty ceased to blind him, Yeobright began to realize the depth of his predicament. There were moments when he wished he had never met Eustacia, only to immediately reject the thought as heartless. He was struggling to maintain three conflicting interests: his mother’s faith in him, his career path as an educator, and Eustacia’s well-being. His passionate spirit would not allow him to sacrifice any of these, yet keeping all three alive seemed beyond his reach. Though his devotion was as idealistic as a poet’s, it had turned what was once a mere obstacle into a set of shackles. A situation that had been challenging enough when he was single-minded had become impossibly complex with Eustacia’s arrival. Just as his mother was starting to accept his professional shift, he had introduced a second, more painful complication, and the two together were more than she could endure.
source_text: Thus as his sight grew accustomed to the first blinding halo kindled about him by love and beauty, Yeobright began to perceive what a strait he was in. Sometimes he wished that he had never known Eustacia, immediately to retract the wish as brutal. Three antagonistic growths had to be kept alive: his mother’s trust in him, his plan for becoming a teacher, and Eustacia’s happiness. His fervid nature could not afford to relinquish one of these, though two of the three were as many as he could hope to preserve. Though his love was as chaste as that of Petrarch for his Laura, it had made fetters of what previously was only a difficulty. A position which was not too simple when he stood whole-hearted had become indescribably complicated by the addition of Eustacia. Just when his mother was beginning to tolerate one scheme he had introduced another still bitterer than the first, and the combination was more than she could bear.

text: A settled composure had at last overtaken him, and he surveyed the expanse of the moor. In the secluded clefts between the mounds that broke the valley's sweep, the nascent ferns were rising in lush profusion, preparing to eventually overtop the height of a tall man. He moved further down the incline, casting himself upon the earth at a point where a trail wound out from a shaded dip, and remained there. It was to this spot he had pledged to Elara that he would lead his mother this day, in the hope of a reconciliation. The venture had proved a total miscarriage.
source_text: By this time he was calm, and he looked over the landscape. In the minor valleys, between the hillocks which diversified the contour of the vale, the fresh young ferns were luxuriantly growing up, ultimately to reach a height of five or six feet. He descended a little way, flung himself down in a spot where a path emerged from one of the small hollows, and waited. Hither it was that he had promised Eustacia to bring his mother this afternoon, that they might meet and be friends. His attempt had utterly failed.

text: After he had rested there for a long while, brooding in a dark mood, he caught sight of a white silk bonnet rising above the ferns to his left. Yeobright knew instantly that it was the woman he loved. His heart shook off its lethargy, replaced by a sudden, glowing thrill; he leaped to his feet and exclaimed, “I was certain she would come.”
source_text: When he had reclined for some considerable time, gloomily pondering, he discerned above the ferns a drawn bonnet of white silk approaching from the left, and Yeobright knew directly that it covered the head of her he loved. His heart awoke from its apathy to a warm excitement, and, jumping to his feet, he said aloud, “I knew she was sure to come.”

text: “Ah, I see your thoughts have turned toward the philosophical. In a way, these bleak and insurmountable barriers are almost welcome; they permit us to gaze with indifference upon the cruel ironies that Fate so enjoys. I have heard of those who, when suddenly finding happiness, died from the sheer terror that they might not live to experience it. I found myself in that strange state of agitation recently, but I am relieved of it now. Let us continue our walk.”
source_text: “Ah—your mind runs off to the philosophical side of it. Well, these sad and hopeless obstacles are welcome in one sense, for they enable us to look with indifference upon the cruel satires that Fate loves to indulge in. I have heard of people, who, upon coming suddenly into happiness, have died from anxiety lest they should not live to enjoy it. I felt myself in that whimsical state of uneasiness lately; but I shall be spared it now. Let us walk on.”

text: They lingered, ready to say their final goodbyes. The land before them was a perfect, unbroken plane. The sun, perched on the horizon, sent its light flooding over the ground from between clouds of bronze and lilac that stretched out beneath a soft, pale-green sky. Every dark object on the earth facing the sun was veiled in a violet mist, against which swarms of humming gnats glowed, rising and dancing like embers in the air.
source_text: They stood still and prepared to bid each other farewell. Everything before them was on a perfect level. The sun, resting on the horizon line, streamed across the ground from between copper-coloured and lilac clouds, stretched out in flats beneath a sky of pale soft green. All dark objects on the earth that lay towards the sun were overspread by a purple haze, against which groups of wailing gnats shone out, rising upwards and dancing about like sparks of fire.

text: “It is surely within our right, for we have both attained our full majority. Nor have I pursued my calling for so long without setting aside a fair portion of my earnings; and if you are willing to occupy a simple cottage out on the moorland until I can secure a lease in Kingsport for the academy, we may live with a very modest expenditure.”
source_text: “We certainly can, both being of full age. And I have not followed my occupation all these years without having accumulated money; and if you will agree to live in a tiny cottage somewhere on the heath, until I take a house in Budmouth for the school, we can do it at a very little expense.”

text: The atmosphere had shifted drastically since the previous night. That hazy, amber sunset which had veiled Eustacia as he looked back for the last time had been a clear omen of the coming turn. It was one of those common occurrences in an English June when the day becomes as sodden and turbulent as late autumn. Chilly clouds hurried past in a singular mass, appearing like a painted backdrop in constant motion. Mists from distant lands arrived on the gusts, which swirled and broke around his form as he continued his journey.
source_text: The weather was far different from that of the evening before. The yellow and vapoury sunset which had wrapped up Eustacia from his parting gaze had presaged change. It was one of those not infrequent days of an English June which are as wet and boisterous as November. The cold clouds hastened on in a body, as if painted on a moving slide. Vapours from other continents arrived upon the wind, which curled and parted round him as he walked on.

text: Elias finally reached the edge of a stand of larch and oak, a plot reclaimed from the wild moorland in the very year he entered the world. Here the trees, burdened by the weight of their lush, rain-soaked leaves, were enduring a greater ruin than during the fiercest winter tempests, when naked branches are better armed to withstand the gale. These verdant young oaks were suffering fractures, contusions, and jagged rents, from which the life-blood of the sap would seep for weeks, leaving welts that would persist until the wood was finally cast into the flame. Every trunk groaned at its base, pivoting like a limb in a dry socket, and with each surge of the wind, a shuddering cry rose from the canopy as though the timber itself felt the torment. Nearby, in a cluster of gorse, a linnet attempted a melody; however, the breeze caught beneath its down until the feathers stood awry, tossed its tiny tail, and forced the creature into silence.
source_text: At length Clym reached the margin of a fir and beech plantation that had been enclosed from heath-land in the year of his birth. Here the trees, laden heavily with their new and humid leaves, were now suffering more damage than during the highest winds of winter, when the boughs are especially disencumbered to do battle with the storm. The wet young beeches were undergoing amputations, bruises, cripplings, and harsh lacerations, from which the wasting sap would bleed for many a day to come, and which would leave scars visible till the day of their burning. Each stem was wrenched at the root, where it moved like a bone in its socket, and at every onset of the gale convulsive sounds came from the branches, as if pain were felt. In a neighbouring brake a finch was trying to sing; but the wind blew under his feathers till they stood on end, twisted round his little tail, and made him give up his song.

text: Clym arrived at the vacant property around noon. It felt nearly as secluded as the home of Eustacia’s grandfather, though a dense ring of fir trees almost entirely enclosed the grounds, masking its proximity to the open heath. He traveled an additional mile to the village where the landlord lived and returned with him to the house; once the deal was struck, the man promised that at least one room would be habitable by the following day. Clym intended to stay there alone until the morning of his wedding, when Eustacia would finally join him.
source_text: Yeobright reached the empty house about midday. It was almost as lonely as that of Eustacia’s grandfather, but the fact that it stood near a heath was disguised by a belt of firs which almost enclosed the premises. He journeyed on about a mile further to the village in which the owner lived, and, returning with him to the house, arrangements were completed, and the man undertook that one room at least should be ready for occupation the next day. Clym’s intention was to live there alone until Eustacia should join him on their wedding-day.

text: For an entire week, Thomasin remained faithful to her promise. She made a conscious effort to downplay the significance of the wedding, sharing updates on the ongoing preparations and noting her own invitation to the ceremony. However, by the following week, she felt poorly and remained out of sight. The matter of the gold coins was still left unaddressed; Thomasin was too apprehensive to approach her husband about the money again, and Mrs. Yeobright had strictly forbidden her from doing so.
source_text: And for one week Thomasin literally fulfilled her word. She endeavoured to make light of the wedding; and brought news of the preparations, and that she was invited to be present. The next week she was rather unwell, and did not appear. Nothing had as yet been done about the guineas, for Thomasin feared to address her husband again on the subject, and Mrs. Yeobright had insisted upon this.

text: The day of the nuptials dawned. To an outside observer, nothing at Willow-Garth betrayed the slightest concern for the events unfolding at Black-Heath. A profound hush enveloped Julian’s mother’s dwelling, a stillness mirrored by the stagnant air within the rooms. Mrs. Thorne, having refused to witness the vows, remained seated at the morning table in the parlor adjoining the vestibule, her gaze fixed vacantly upon the sunlit threshold. In this very chamber, half a year prior, the festive midwinter gathering had assembled—the night Elena had arrived in secret, an uninvited shadow. The sole visitor now was a lone wren; perceiving no threat in the motionless figure, it ventured across the floor, beat its wings against the glass in a vain escape, and tangled itself among the potted geraniums. Disturbed by the frantic beating, the solitary woman rose, freed the captive creature, and stepped toward the entrance. She awaited Tamsin, who had sent word the previous evening requesting her inheritance and promising to call for the sum before the day was out.
source_text: The wedding morning came. Nobody would have imagined from appearances that Blooms-End had any interest in Mistover that day. A solemn stillness prevailed around the house of Clym’s mother, and there was no more animation indoors. Mrs. Yeobright, who had declined to attend the ceremony, sat by the breakfast table in the old room which communicated immediately with the porch, her eyes listlessly directed towards the open door. It was the room in which, six months earlier, the merry Christmas party had met, to which Eustacia came secretly and as a stranger. The only living thing that entered now was a sparrow; and seeing no movements to cause alarm, he hopped boldly round the room, endeavoured to go out by the window, and fluttered among the pot-flowers. This roused the lonely sitter, who got up, released the bird, and went to the door. She was expecting Thomasin, who had written the night before to state that the time had come when she would wish to have the money and that she would if possible call this day.

text: Nevertheless, Thomasin herself occupied only a small part of Mrs. Yeobright’s reflections as she gazed up the valley of the heath, a place alive with butterflies and the dry, rhythmic chirping of grasshoppers that formed a surrounding whisper. A family drama, for which the final arrangements were currently being made a few miles away, appeared as clearly in her mind’s eye as if it were unfolding right in front of her. She attempted to push the image aside and paced through her garden, yet her gaze repeatedly drifted toward the parish church serving Mistover; in her agitated state, her imagination seemed to pierce through the hills that blocked the building from her sight. The morning slowly passed. As the clock struck eleven, she wondered if the ceremony was already underway. It surely had to be. She continued to envision the scene at the church, where the groom would have now arrived with his bride. She pictured the small cluster of children waiting by the gate as the pony-drawn carriage—the mode of transport Thomasin had mentioned for their brief trip—pulled up. Then, she saw them in her mind as they entered, walked toward the chancel, and knelt, and the service seemed to proceed.
source_text: Yet Thomasin occupied Mrs. Yeobright’s thoughts but slightly as she looked up the valley of the heath, alive with butterflies, and with grasshoppers whose husky noises on every side formed a whispered chorus. A domestic drama, for which the preparations were now being made a mile or two off, was but little less vividly present to her eyes than if enacted before her. She tried to dismiss the vision, and walked about the garden plot; but her eyes ever and anon sought out the direction of the parish church to which Mistover belonged, and her excited fancy clove the hills which divided the building from her eyes. The morning wore away. Eleven o’clock struck—could it be that the wedding was then in progress? It must be so. She went on imagining the scene at the church, which he had by this time approached with his bride. She pictured the little group of children by the gate as the pony carriage drove up in which, as Thomasin had learnt, they were going to perform the short journey. Then she saw them enter and proceed to the chancel and kneel; and the service seemed to go on.

text: Wildeve arrived as evening approached. Since Thomasin’s marriage, Mrs. Yeobright had adopted a sort of grim civility toward him—the kind of forced warmth that eventually develops in all such unwanted relationships. The dream of what might have been is cast aside out of sheer fatigue, and exhausted human effort half-heartedly accepts the reality of the situation. To give him credit, Wildeve had behaved quite politely toward his wife’s aunt, so his entrance now caused her no surprise.
source_text: Towards evening Wildeve came. Since Thomasin’s marriage Mrs. Yeobright had shown him that grim friendliness which at last arises in all such cases of undesired affinity. The vision of what ought to have been is thrown aside in sheer weariness, and browbeaten human endeavour listlessly makes the best of the fact that is. Wildeve, to do him justice, had behaved very courteously to his wife’s aunt; and it was with no surprise that she saw him enter now.

text: “Thomasin has been unable to come as she promised,” he answered her worried inquiry, for she knew her niece was in desperate need of funds. “The captain arrived last night and personally urged her to join them today. Not wanting to cause offense, she decided to go. They collected her in the pony-chaise and will be bringing her back later.”
source_text: “Thomasin has not been able to come, as she promised to do,” he replied to her inquiry, which had been anxious, for she knew that her niece was badly in want of money. “The captain came down last night and personally pressed her to join them today. So, not to be unpleasant, she determined to go. They fetched her in the pony-chaise, and are going to bring her back.”

text: Her son was also present, and was now married. There could be no more fitting time than the present to hand over his share of the money. The opportunity this gift provided to show that she held no resentment against him brought a sense of comfort to the sorrowful mother’s heart.
source_text: Her son, too, was there, and was now married. There could be no more proper moment to render him his share of the money than the present. And the chance that would be afforded her, by sending him this gift, of showing how far she was from bearing him ill-will, cheered the sad mother’s heart.

text: The hour approached nine as Elias began his trek up the valley toward Raven’s Crag; but as the summer solstice was then at its height, the earliest gloaming had only just commenced to amber the surrounding hills. It was here that he detected the sound of speech, tracing it to a party of travelers moving through a swale in the ground ahead, where nothing but the crowns of their heads could be seen above the rim.
source_text: It was nearly nine o’clock when he began to ascend the vale towards Mistover; but the long days of summer being at their climax, the first obscurity of evening had only just begun to tan the landscape. At this point of his journey Christian heard voices, and found that they proceeded from a company of men and women who were traversing a hollow ahead of him, the tops only of their heads being visible.

text: “No rowdy misbehavior, I assume? You see, neighbors, if there were, it would set a terrible example for my father, given his loose character. But a dress length for a shilling, provided there’s no sorcery involved—it’s worth a look, and it won't cost me even thirty minutes. I’ll join you, then, if you’ll walk a short distance toward Mistover with me afterward, should it be dark by then and no one else is heading that way?”
source_text: “And no ba’dy gaieties, I suppose? You see, neighbours, if so, it would be setting father a bad example, as he is so light moral’d. But a gown-piece for a shilling, and no black art—’tis worth looking in to see, and it wouldn’t hinder me half an hour. Yes, I’ll come, if you’ll step a little way towards Mistover with me afterwards, supposing night should have closed in, and nobody else is going that way?”

text: “Observe, my friends,” the speaker resumed as the fresh arrivals gathered round the board. “We have five names inscribed already, and we seek but four more to complete the tally. I perceive in the countenances of these newcomers a certain sagacity—surely they are astute enough to grasp this fleeting chance to adorn their fair companions with finery for a mere pittance.”
source_text: “Now, gentlemen,” he continued, as the newcomers drew up to the table, “there’s five have entered, and we want four more to make up the number. I think, by the faces of those gentlemen who have just come in, that they are shrewd enough to take advantage of this rare opportunity of beautifying their ladies at a very trifling expense.”

text: "Good heavens!" Christian muttered under his breath. "To think I was born with such fortune and never realized it until this very moment! What strange objects these dice are—masters of everyone’s fate, yet they follow my lead! I’m certain I’ll never have to fear a thing again." He handled the dice one by one with a sense of wonder. "Listen, sir," he whispered conspiratorially to Wildeve, who was standing close to his left, "if I could only use this gift I have for making money grow, I might be able to do a favor for a close relative of yours, considering what I’m carrying for her—don't you think?" He gave the floor a significant tap with one of his boots, which was heavy with hidden gold.
source_text: “Well, to be sure!” said Christian, half to himself. “To think I should have been born so lucky as this, and not have found it out until now! What curious creatures these dice be—powerful rulers of us all, and yet at my command! I am sure I never need be afeared of anything after this.” He handled the dice fondly one by one. “Why, sir,” he said in a confidential whisper to Wildeve, who was near his left hand, “if I could only use this power that’s in me of multiplying money I might do some good to a near relation of yours, seeing what I’ve got about me of hers—eh?” He tapped one of his money-laden boots upon the floor.

text: Inside the tavern, the group’s conversation hummed along until a sudden distraction drew their eyes toward the cavernous hearth. This fireplace was immense, built with deep-set walls that concealed a private stone bench—a feature typical of the old wold dwellings—allowing an occupant to remain utterly hidden, so long as no flame burned to betray them, which was the case during this season of heat. From the gloom of that hollow, a lone object thrust forward into the radiance of the tallow candles. It was a pipe of fired clay, stained a deep russet. The company had only noticed its presence when a voice from the darkness behind it called out for a coal.
source_text: Within the room the men fell into chat till their attention was for a moment drawn to the chimney-corner. This was large, and, in addition to its proper recess, contained within its jambs, like many on Egdon, a receding seat, so that a person might sit there absolutely unobserved, provided there was no fire to light him up, as was the case now and throughout the summer. From the niche a single object protruded into the light from the candles on the table. It was a clay pipe, and its colour was reddish. The men had been attracted to this object by a voice behind the pipe asking for a light.

text: "Quite so," Wildeve remarked. To anyone familiar with the circumstances, it would have been clear that he was humiliated to find the package held actual currency rather than the trivial bauble he had envisioned at Blooms-End—a mere feminine trinket of no consequence to him. Mrs. Yeobright’s refusal carried a sharp sting, implying that his integrity was too suspect for him to be trusted as a reliable guardian of his wife’s property.
source_text: “No doubt,” said Wildeve. Any person who had known the circumstances might have perceived that Wildeve was mortified by the discovery that the matter in transit was money, and not, as he had supposed when at Blooms-End, some fancy nick-nack which only interested the two women themselves. Mrs. Yeobright’s refusal implied that his honour was not considered to be of sufficiently good quality to make him a safer bearer of his wife’s property.

text: "Fine," Wildeve replied, getting to his feet. Scouring the area by the lantern’s glow, he located a wide, level stone, positioned it between himself and Christian, and sat back down. He opened the lantern to brighten the area, focusing its beams onto the rock. Christian set down a shilling, Wildeve matched the bet, and each of them threw. Christian won. They played for two shillings, and Christian was victorious again.
source_text: “Very well,” said Wildeve, rising. Searching about with the lantern, he found a large flat stone, which he placed between himself and Christian, and sat down again. The lantern was opened to give more light, and its rays directed upon the stone. Christian put down a shilling, Wildeve another, and each threw. Christian won. They played for two, Christian won again.

text: Ever since their departure, Wildeve had been fixated on the low opinion his wife’s kin harbored toward him, a slight that galled him to his core. With each passing mile, he found himself sliding toward a retaliatory scheme, though he could not have said exactly when the impulse first took root. His aim was to provide Mrs. Yeobright with what he deemed a necessary lesson: to prove that, as her niece’s husband, he was the only rightful custodian of the woman’s fortune.
source_text: Wildeve had been brooding ever since they started on the mean estimation in which he was held by his wife’s friends; and it cut his heart severely. As the minutes passed he had gradually drifted into a revengeful intention without knowing the precise moment of forming it. This was to teach Mrs. Yeobright a lesson, as he considered it to be; in other words, to show her if he could that her niece’s husband was the proper guardian of her niece’s money.

text: He delved into the depths of his boot, retrieving one of Elspeth’s sequestered coins, still burning with the heat of his skin. Thorne had already deposited a gold sovereign upon the flat granite. Thus, the play recommenced. Thorne triumphed in the opening round, and Silas, emboldened, staked another, finding success in that turn. The luck ebbed and flowed, yet the steady accumulation gravitated toward Thorne. So total was their immersion that the world beyond their gaze ceased to exist; the level rock, the unshuttered lantern, the tumbling dice, and the few gilded leaves of fern within the circle of light became the sum of their reality.
source_text: He thrust his hand into the boot and withdrew one of poor Thomasin’s precious guineas, piping hot. Wildeve had already placed a sovereign on the stone. The game was then resumed. Wildeve won first, and Christian ventured another, winning himself this time. The game fluctuated, but the average was in Wildeve’s favour. Both men became so absorbed in the game that they took no heed of anything but the pigmy objects immediately beneath their eyes, the flat stone, the open lantern, the dice, and the few illuminated fern-leaves which lay under the light, were the whole world to them.

text: "It doesn't matter—I don't care!" he groaned, frantically unlacing his left boot to reach the other fifty coins. "I know the devil will pitch me into the flames with his pitchfork for what I’ve done tonight! But maybe I can still win, and then I’ll find a wife to stay awake with me through the dark so I won't have to be afraid. I won't! Here’s another one for you!" He slammed another gold coin onto the stone, and the dice cup began to rattle once more.
source_text: “I don’t care—I don’t care!” he moaned, and desperately set about untying his left boot to get at the other fifty. “The devil will toss me into the flames on his three-pronged fork for this night’s work, I know! But perhaps I shall win yet, and then I’ll get a wife to sit up with me o’ nights and I won’t be afeard, I won’t! Here’s another for’ee, my man!” He slapped another guinea down upon the stone, and the dice-box was rattled again.

text: Neither man took much notice of their environment, their vision narrowed to the small flat rock, which felt as vast and vital to them as a field of war. By this point, the game’s luck had turned; the reddleman was winning every round. Eventually, sixty guineas—Thomasin’s fifty and ten of Clym’s—had passed into his hands. Wildeve had grown reckless, desperate, and pushed to his limit.
source_text: But neither of the men paid much attention to these things, their eyes being concentrated upon the little flat stone, which to them was an arena vast and important as a battlefield. By this time a change had come over the game; the reddleman won continually. At length sixty guineas—Thomasin’s fifty, and ten of Clym’s—had passed into his hands. Wildeve was reckless, frantic, exasperated.

text: Her apprehension grew acute, but there was a certain steadfastness in Clym’s behavior that made her reluctant to approach him about the matter. At this juncture in their shared life, however, a specific occurrence provided the opening she needed. It took place one evening approximately six weeks after their wedding, triggered solely by Venn’s accidental diversion of the fifty guineas that had been destined for Yeobright.
source_text: Her anxiety reached a high pitch; but there was something in Clym’s undeviating manner which made her hesitate before sounding him on the subject. At this point in their experience, however, an incident helped her. It occurred one evening about six weeks after their union, and arose entirely out of the unconscious misapplication of Venn of the fifty guineas intended for Yeobright.

text: “A pittance from Julian Sterling? No—not a penny! What, madam, is the meaning of such a charge?” Elara’s indignation erupted with telltale speed; the secret weight of her past devotion to Sterling compelled her to assume that Mrs. Blackwood was equally aware of their history. She leaped to the conviction that she was being branded a recipient of shameful favors, fearing that her old suitor’s name was being invoked as a direct assault upon her honor.
source_text: “Money from Mr. Wildeve? No—never! Madam, what do you mean by that?” Eustacia fired up all too quickly, for her own consciousness of the old attachment between herself and Wildeve led her to jump to the conclusion that Mrs. Yeobright also knew of it, and might have come to accuse her of receiving dishonourable presents from him now.

text: “How can you suggest he needed protection from me?” Eustacia cried, her eyes brimming with indignant tears. “I haven't ruined him by marrying him! What have I done that’s so terrible for you to think so poorly of me? You had no right to turn him against me when I have never done anything to hurt you.”
source_text: “How can you imply that he required guarding against me?” cried Eustacia, passionate tears in her eyes. “I have not injured him by marrying him! What sin have I done that you should think so ill of me? You had no right to speak against me to him when I have never wronged you.”

text: “I see what you think of me,” Eustacia said, her voice thick with agitation. “You believe me capable of any depravity. What could be more loathsome than a wife who entertains a lover while poisoning her husband’s heart against his own kin? Yet that is the reputation you have assigned to me. Why then do you not come and wrench him from my side?”
source_text: “I understand you,” said Eustacia, breathless with emotion. “You think me capable of every bad thing. Who can be worse than a wife who encourages a lover, and poisons her husband’s mind against his relative? Yet that is now the character given to me. Will you not come and drag him out of my hands?”

text: You invited this; your doubts have goaded me into speaking of Julian with a severity I never intended. You will surely carry these complaints to him, and it will sow only wretchedness between us. I beg you to depart! You have proven yourself no friend.
source_text: “It was asked for; you have suspected me, and you have made me speak of my husband in a way I would not have done. You will let him know that I have spoken thus, and it will cause misery between us. Will you go away from me? You are no friend!”

text: This revelation of a hidden vein of hope within Eustacia’s heart troubled her husband. It was his first real encounter with the circuitous ways a woman might pursue her deepest longings. Yet his own resolve stayed firm, for all his deep affection for her. The only lasting impact of her words was to drive him back to his studies with even greater intensity, determined to secure the kind of practical success that would allow him to effectively counter her fancies with the weight of his own accomplishments.
source_text: This indication of an unexpected mine of hope in Eustacia’s bosom disconcerted her husband. It was the first time that he had confronted the fact of the indirectness of a woman’s movement towards her desire. But his intention was unshaken, though he loved Eustacia well. All the effect that her remark had upon him was a resolve to chain himself more closely than ever to his books, so as to be the sooner enabled to appeal to substantial results from another course in arguing against her whim.

text: One day, following a period of more intense exertion than usual, he woke with a peculiar discomfort in his eyes. The sun was beating directly against the window shades, and the moment he looked toward them, a sharp pang forced him to snap his eyelids shut. Every time he tried to look around the room, that same agonizing sensitivity to light flared up, and stinging tears flooded down his face. He had to tie a makeshift bandage over his brow while getting dressed, and he found he could not remove it as the day went on. Eustacia was deeply frightened by his condition. When they realized things were no better by the next morning, they decided to send for a doctor from Anglebury.
source_text: One morning, after a severer strain than usual, he awoke with a strange sensation in his eyes. The sun was shining directly upon the window-blind, and at his first glance thitherward a sharp pain obliged him to close his eyelids quickly. At every new attempt to look about him the same morbid sensibility to light was manifested, and excoriating tears ran down his cheeks. He was obliged to tie a bandage over his brow while dressing; and during the day it could not be abandoned. Eustacia was thoroughly alarmed. On finding that the case was no better the next morning they decided to send to Anglebury for a surgeon.

text: Chafing with impatience at the disruption of a task he was so eager to finish, Clym was transformed into an invalid. He was confined to a room where all light was barred, and his condition would have been one of total misery had Eustacia not read to him by the faint glow of a shielded lamp. He hoped for a quick recovery, but the surgeon’s third visit brought the grim news that while he might venture outside with protected eyes in a month, any prospect of resuming his work or reading a single page would have to be abandoned for a long time.
source_text: Fretting with impatience at this interruption to a task he was so anxious to hasten, Clym was transformed into an invalid. He was shut up in a room from which all light was excluded, and his condition would have been one of absolute misery had not Eustacia read to him by the glimmer of a shaded lamp. He hoped that the worst would soon be over; but at the surgeon’s third visit he learnt to his dismay that although he might venture out of doors with shaded eyes in the course of a month, all thought of pursuing his work, or of reading print of any description, would have to be given up for a long time to come.

text: Clym received the news with profound seriousness, yet he did not surrender to hopelessness. A sense of calm resolve, and even a certain brightness, began to settle over him. Total darkness was not to be his fate, and that realization sufficed. To be forced to view his surroundings through a permanent haze for the foreseeable future was a grim prospect, one that effectively ended his professional ambitions; however, Yeobright was a thorough stoic regarding misfortunes that merely diminished his social rank. Setting aside his concerns for Eustacia, he was content with the most modest existence, provided it allowed him to pursue his educational ideals. Establishing a small night-school in a cottage was one such possibility, and so his physical ailment failed to crush his spirit as it might have otherwise.
source_text: Clym was very grave at the intelligence, but not despairing. A quiet firmness, and even cheerfulness, took possession of him. He was not to be blind; that was enough. To be doomed to behold the world through smoked glass for an indefinite period was bad enough, and fatal to any kind of advance; but Yeobright was an absolute stoic in the face of mishaps which only affected his social standing; and, apart from Eustacia, the humblest walk of life would satisfy him if it could be made to work in with some form of his culture scheme. To keep a cottage night-school was one such form; and his affliction did not master his spirit as it might otherwise have done.

text: His existence had narrowed to a strangely minute scale, his entire world restricted to a small circle immediately surrounding him. The creeping and winged inhabitants of the heath were his constant associates, and they seemed to have inducted him into their society. Bees hummed with an air of intimacy near his head, crowding the heather and furze-blossoms at his side until the weight of their bodies pressed the flowers to the soil. The rare, amber-tinted butterflies found only on Egdon quivered in the very air he exhaled, alighting on his bent back and playing about the shining point of his hook as he swung it. Legions of emerald grasshoppers vaulted over his feet, landing haphazardly on their heads or backs like clumsy acrobats, or pursued noisy romances beneath the ferns with silent, drab-colored mates. Great flies, wild and unacquainted with kitchens or nets, buzzed around him without recognizing him as a human being. In the hollows of the bracken, snakes glided in vivid displays of blue and yellow, their scales gleaming with the fresh intensity that follows the shedding of a skin. Young rabbits ventured from their nests to warm themselves on the slopes, the fierce sun shining through their delicate ears until they glowed a translucent blood-red, exposing every fine vein. Not one of them felt any fear of him.
source_text: His daily life was of a curious microscopic sort, his whole world being limited to a circuit of a few feet from his person. His familiars were creeping and winged things, and they seemed to enroll him in their band. Bees hummed around his ears with an intimate air, and tugged at the heath and furze-flowers at his side in such numbers as to weigh them down to the sod. The strange amber-coloured butterflies which Egdon produced, and which were never seen elsewhere, quivered in the breath of his lips, alighted upon his bowed back, and sported with the glittering point of his hook as he flourished it up and down. Tribes of emerald-green grasshoppers leaped over his feet, falling awkwardly on their backs, heads, or hips, like unskilful acrobats, as chance might rule; or engaged themselves in noisy flirtations under the fern-fronds with silent ones of homely hue. Huge flies, ignorant of larders and wire-netting, and quite in a savage state, buzzed about him without knowing that he was a man. In and out of the fern-dells snakes glided in their most brilliant blue and yellow guise, it being the season immediately following the shedding of their old skins, when their colours are brightest. Litters of young rabbits came out from their forms to sun themselves upon hillocks, the hot beams blazing through the delicate tissue of each thin-fleshed ear, and firing it to a blood-red transparency in which the veins could be seen. None of them feared him.

text: “Eustacia! I didn’t notice you, though I saw something stirring,” he said softly. Stepping forward, he drew off his thick leather glove and took her hand. “Why do you speak so strangely? It is only a little old melody that caught my fancy when I was in Paris, and now it feels suited to our life together. Has your love for me truly vanished, then, just because I no longer look like a polished gentleman?”
source_text: “Eustacia! I did not see you, though I noticed something moving,” he said gently. He came forward, pulled off his huge leather glove, and took her hand. “Why do you speak in such a strange way? It is only a little old song which struck my fancy when I was in Paris, and now just applies to my life with you. Has your love for me all died, then, because my appearance is no longer that of a fine gentleman?”

text: “You persist in your own designs, then, and refuse to yield when I implore you to forsake this ignoble toil. Is there some hidden aversion you harbor that you should thwart my every wish? I am your wife, yet you will not hear me. Yes, I am your wife, whatever you may think!”
source_text: “Well, you follow out your own ideas, and won’t give in to mine when I wish you to leave off this shameful labour. Is there anything you dislike in me that you act so contrarily to my wishes? I am your wife, and why will you not listen? Yes, I am your wife indeed!”

text: Why do you drive me, Julian, to utter such harshness? I merit as much compassion as you—more, I believe. For you can sing! It would be a strange moment that found me singing beneath a cloud so dark. Believe me, my love, I could weep with a passion that would stun and confuse a spirit as elastic as yours. Even if you were careless of your own grief, you might have ceased your song out of mere pity for mine. Lord! were I a man in your stead, I would curse rather than sing.
source_text: “Why will you force me, Clym, to say bitter things? I deserve pity as much as you. As much?—I think I deserve it more. For you can sing! It would be a strange hour which should catch me singing under such a cloud as this! Believe me, sweet, I could weep to a degree that would astonish and confound such an elastic mind as yours. Even had you felt careless about your own affliction, you might have refrained from singing out of sheer pity for mine. God! if I were a man in such a position I would curse rather than sing.”

text: Recently, Eustacia had drifted into a state of near-total apathy. A desolate quality had settled into her striking eyes which, regardless of her character, would have moved anyone to pity who had seen her in the vibrant peak of her love for Clym. The emotional temperaments of the couple had begun to shift in inverse relation to their circumstances. Clym, despite his physical misfortune, remained remarkably cheerful; he even endeavored to offer comfort to his wife, who had never experienced a single moment of bodily suffering in her entire life.
source_text: Eustacia’s manner had become of late almost apathetic. There was a forlorn look about her beautiful eyes which, whether she deserved it or not, would have excited pity in the breast of anyone who had known her during the full flush of her love for Clym. The feelings of husband and wife varied, in some measure, inversely with their positions. Clym, the afflicted man, was cheerful; and he even tried to comfort her, who had never felt a moment of physical suffering in her whole life.

text: By five in the evening, she emerged from the house, fully prepared for her excursion. There was beauty enough in her presentation to win twenty new hearts. The defiant sorrow that marked her face while indoors was now tempered and obscured by her outdoor dress, which always possessed a soft, misty quality, free of any sharp outlines. Her face appeared from within it as if framed by a cloud, with no harsh transition between her complexion and the cloth. The day’s heat had hardly begun to lift, and she wandered across the bright hills at a slow pace, for there was no need to hurry her aimless wandering. Towering ferns swallowed her in their foliage whenever the path led that way, creating a landscape of miniature woods, even though not a single stalk would remain to sprout again in the coming year.
source_text: It was five in the afternoon when she came out from the house ready for her walk. There was material enough in the picture for twenty new conquests. The rebellious sadness that was rather too apparent when she sat indoors without a bonnet was cloaked and softened by her outdoor attire, which always had a sort of nebulousness about it, devoid of harsh edges anywhere; so that her face looked from its environment as from a cloud, with no noticeable lines of demarcation between flesh and clothes. The heat of the day had scarcely declined as yet, and she went along the sunny hills at a leisurely pace, there being ample time for her idle expedition. Tall ferns buried her in their leafage whenever her path lay through them, which now formed miniature forests, though not one stem of them would remain to bud the next year.

text: Young men adorned with blue and white rosettes stepped toward the girls, their faces glowing with effort. The young women, energized by the movement and the thrill of the night, blushed with a hue deeper than the many pink ribbons they wore. Girls with long ringlets, short curls, delicate tresses, and thick braids spun in circles; an observer might have wondered how such an attractive group of similar age, size, and spirit could have been gathered from just one or two small hamlets. In the background, a solitary, joyful man danced by himself with his eyes shut, completely unaware of his surroundings. A fire crackled beneath a pruned thorn tree a short distance away, where three kettles hung in a row. Nearby, older women were busy at a tea table, but Eustacia searched among them fruitlessly for the cattle-dealer’s wife. It was she who had suggested Eustacia come, promising to secure a warm and polite reception for her.
source_text: The young men wore blue and white rosettes, and with a flush on their faces footed it to the girls, who, with the excitement and the exercise, blushed deeper than the pink of their numerous ribbons. Fair ones with long curls, fair ones with short curls, fair ones with lovelocks, fair ones with braids, flew round and round; and a beholder might well have wondered how such a prepossessing set of young women of like size, age, and disposition, could have been collected together where there were only one or two villages to choose from. In the background was one happy man dancing by himself, with closed eyes, totally oblivious of all the rest. A fire was burning under a pollard thorn a few paces off, over which three kettles hung in a row. Hard by was a table where elderly dames prepared tea, but Eustacia looked among them in vain for the cattle-dealer’s wife who had suggested that she should come, and had promised to obtain a courteous welcome for her.

text: She completed her purpose, and by the hour she turned her steps back toward the site of the rustic ball, which lay directly upon her path to Highmains, the sun had begun its final descent. The evening had grown so profoundly still that the strains of the fiddles reached her from a great distance, pulsating with a fervor that seemed, remarkably, even more intense than when she had first departed. As she crested the hill, the sun had slipped entirely from view; yet this transition was of little consequence to Marcella or the dancers, for a heavy, golden moon was lifting itself before her, its pale beams not yet triumphant over the fading glow of the west. The revelry continued without pause, but a crowd of newcomers had now gathered to form a perimeter around the dancers, enabling Marcella to take her place among them, her presence shielded by the anonymity of the throng.
source_text: This she did, and by the time that she retraced her steps towards the scene of the gipsying, which it was necessary to repass on her way to Alderworth, the sun was going down. The air was now so still that she could hear the band afar off, and it seemed to be playing with more spirit, if that were possible, than when she had come away. On reaching the hill the sun had quite disappeared; but this made little difference either to Eustacia or to the revellers, for a round yellow moon was rising before her, though its rays had not yet outmastered those from the west. The dance was going on just the same, but strangers had arrived and formed a ring around the figure, so that Eustacia could stand among these without a chance of being recognized.

text: It was Julian. Not since the hour of his vows had their eyes met, when she had lingered in the shadows of the chancel and disquieted him by drawing back her lace to step forward and attest to the union. Yet she could not fathom why the mere sight of him now should provoke such a sudden, burning tide within her.
source_text: It was Wildeve. Till this moment he had not met her eye since the morning of his marriage, when she had been loitering in the church, and had startled him by lifting her veil and coming forward to sign the register as witness. Yet why the sight of him should have instigated that sudden rush of blood she could not tell.

text: Wildeve offered his arm and led her along the outer edge of the circle to the end of the line, where they joined the dancers. Within moments, they were absorbed into the choreography, steadily advancing toward the head of the set. Until they reached the midpoint, Eustacia regretted more than once that she had given in to his request; however, as they moved from the middle to the top, she felt that since she had ventured out for enjoyment, she was merely taking a natural path toward finding it. Once they were fully committed to the endless glides and spins that their new position as the lead couple required, Eustacia’s pulse began to race, leaving no room for further contemplation.
source_text: Wildeve gave her his arm and took her down on the outside of the ring to the bottom of the dance, which they entered. In two minutes more they were involved in the figure and began working their way upwards to the top. Till they had advanced halfway thither Eustacia wished more than once that she had not yielded to his request; from the middle to the top she felt that, since she had come out to seek pleasure, she was only doing a natural thing to obtain it. Fairly launched into the ceaseless glides and whirls which their new position as top couple opened up to them, Eustacia’s pulses began to move too quickly for long rumination of any kind.

text: Whispers of “Who are they?” began to circulate, though no one pursued the matter with any malice. Had Eustacia encountered these girls during their typical daily routines, the reception would have been far different; here, however, she was spared any pointed stares, as the celebration had inspired everyone to look their absolute best. Like the planet Mercury veiled by the glow of twilight, her constant radiance was overlooked within the momentary splendor of the event.
source_text: People began to say “Who are they?” but no invidious inquiries were made. Had Eustacia mingled with the other girls in their ordinary daily walks the case would have been different: here she was not inconvenienced by excessive inspection, for all were wrought to their brightest grace by the occasion. Like the planet Mercury surrounded by the lustre of sunset, her permanent brilliancy passed without much notice in the temporary glory of the situation.

text: Wildeve’s internal state was not difficult to discern. To him, impediments served as a nourishing sun that brought his passion to full bloom, and he was currently submerged in a feverish, beautiful agony. The chance to claim for a brief moment what belonged to another man for the rest of the year was a thrill he was uniquely suited to enjoy. He had long ago resumed his pining for Eustacia; indeed, the very act of signing the marriage register with Thomasin seemed to be the instinctive signal for his heart to retreat to its original obsession. The added complication of Eustacia’s own wedding was the final element required to make that return unavoidable.
source_text: As for Wildeve, his feelings are easy to guess. Obstacles were a ripening sun to his love, and he was at this moment in a delirium of exquisite misery. To clasp as his for five minutes what was another man’s through all the rest of the year was a kind of thing he of all men could appreciate. He had long since begun to sigh again for Eustacia; indeed, it may be asserted that signing the marriage register with Thomasin was the natural signal to his heart to return to its first quarters, and that the extra complication of Eustacia’s marriage was the one addition required to make that return compulsory.

text: Reconsider your command before you press me further. There is a witness here who has surrendered no detail of our secret trysts upon the High Tor—and he stands now in your husband’s confidence. If either of them should spy us together, who among them would ever believe that our meeting and dancing at the harvest revel was born of mere coincidence?
source_text: “You will think twice before you direct me to do that. Here is a man who has not forgotten an item in our meetings at Rainbarrow—he is in company with your husband. Which of them, seeing us together here, will believe that our meeting and dancing at the gipsy party was by chance?”

text: "I shall reflect on whether I know of anyone," Venn answered, speaking with a forced cheerfulness that held no real joy. He then offered a bow of his own unique fashioning and turned to leave. Thomasin reached out her hand to him; and though he harbored enough grief to provide for many a long regret, the reddleman departed without a single audible sigh.
source_text: “I will consider if I know of one,” replied Venn in that same light tone which meant no lightness. And then he bowed in a manner of his own invention and moved to go. Thomasin offered him her hand; and without a sigh, though with food for many, the reddleman went out.

text: At this hour, Thorne had set foot upon the desolate expanse of Blackwood Moor with no intent but to reach its far boundary; his involvement with the fortunes of the Sterling house was ostensibly concluded, and his own professional duties pressed for his care. Nonetheless, he felt a sudden, involuntary slide back into that well-worn groove of stratagem and watchfulness on Elara’s behalf.
source_text: On this occasion Venn had arrived on Egdon Heath only to cross to the other side—he had no further connection with the interests of the Yeobright family, and he had a business of his own to attend to. Yet he suddenly began to feel himself drifting into the old track of manœuvring on Thomasin’s account.

text: During this period, Wildeve was entirely free of any deliberate scheme; apart from their encounter at the village dance, he had not seen Eustacia once since her wedding day. Yet, a latent spirit of intrigue remained within him, evidenced by a recent sentimental routine. After nightfall, he would often wander toward Alderworth to contemplate the stars and her dwelling, eventually retracing his steps with lingering deliberation.
source_text: At this time, as has been seen, Wildeve was quite innocent of any predetermined act of intrigue, and except at the dance on the green he had not once met Eustacia since her marriage. But that the spirit of intrigue was in him had been shown by a recent romantic habit of his—a habit of going out after dark and strolling towards Alderworth, there looking at the moon and stars, looking at Eustacia’s house, and walking back at leisure.

text: In the meantime, a brief drama unfolded outside that spared Eustacia from any risk of compromising herself that night. While Wildeve was busy arranging his moth-signal, another figure approached the gate behind him. This man, carrying a gun, watched the other’s activities at the window for a moment, then stepped up to the house and knocked. He quickly slipped around the corner and vanished over the hedge.
source_text: Meanwhile a little drama had been acted outside which saved Eustacia from all possibility of compromising herself that evening at least. Whilst Wildeve had been preparing his moth-signal another person had come behind him up to the gate. This man, who carried a gun in his hand, looked on for a moment at the other’s operation by the window, walked up to the house, knocked at the door, and then vanished round the corner and over the hedge.

text: Since his intended sign had been eclipsed by that raucous pounding, Sterling withdrew, slipped through the gate, and hurried down the trail with no thought but to vanish unseen. Midway down the slope, the path skirted a cluster of gnarled hollies, which amidst the pervasive murk of the night loomed like the pupil of a blackened eye. As Sterling reached this spot, a sharp report shattered the silence, and several spent pellets pattered against the foliage at his side.
source_text: As his signal had been rendered futile by this uproarious rapping Wildeve withdrew, passed out at the gate, and walked quickly down the path without thinking of anything except getting away unnoticed. Halfway down the hill the path ran near a knot of stunted hollies, which in the general darkness of the scene stood as the pupil in a black eye. When Wildeve reached this point a report startled his ear, and a few spent gunshots fell among the leaves around him.

text: A short distance below Julian’s isolated cottage lay a tiny cluster of dwellings where one of the two beadles of the district of Oakhaven lived, and toward this man’s house Sterling went without delay. The first object to catch his eye upon entering was the heavy staff hanging from a wall-peg, a silent assurance that the power he sought was rooted there. When he spoke to the beadle’s wife, he found that the officer was not within; nevertheless, Sterling stated his intention to wait.
source_text: About half a mile below Clym’s secluded dwelling lay a hamlet where lived one of the two constables who preserved the peace in the parish of Alderworth, and Wildeve went straight to the constable’s cottage. Almost the first thing that he saw on opening the door was the constable’s truncheon hanging to a nail, as if to assure him that here were the means to his purpose. On inquiry, however, of the constable’s wife he learnt that the constable was not at home. Wildeve said he would wait.

text: By then, Silas had retreated from the wild scrub to wait upon Mrs. Wentworth. A certain warmth had characterized their relations since the hour she first grasped the fortuitous agency by which he had secured the return of the ancestral coins. She was struck by the unseasonable moment of his appearance, yet she entertained no thought of refusing his company.
source_text: Meanwhile Venn had left the heath and gone to call upon Mrs. Yeobright, with whom he had been on friendly terms since she had learnt what a providential countermove he had made towards the restitution of the family guineas. She wondered at the lateness of his call, but had no objection to see him.

text: Around the stroke of eleven, Mrs. Sterling ventured out onto the moor, bound for her son’s cottage with the heavy intent of forging a peace between them and his wife, Clara, just as she had pledged to the itinerant merchant. She had aspired to cover the greater part of the distance before the meridian sun achieved its full fury, yet once she was underway, it became clear that such haste was impossible. The celestial fire had seared the very soul of the wasteland, turning the once-vivid bells of the heather to a scorched russet under the merciless brilliance of the recent days. Every depression in the earth held a stagnant, oven-like heat, and the pale flint of the dried-up brooks, which now acted as dusty thoroughfares, appeared almost calcined by the long-standing thirst of the land.
source_text: It was about eleven o’clock on this day that Mrs. Yeobright started across the heath towards her son’s house, to do her best in getting reconciled with him and Eustacia, in conformity with her words to the reddleman. She had hoped to be well advanced in her walk before the heat of the day was at its highest, but after setting out she found that this was not to be done. The sun had branded the whole heath with its mark, even the purple heath-flowers having put on a brownness under the dry blazes of the few preceding days. Every valley was filled with air like that of a kiln, and the clean quartz sand of the winter water-courses, which formed summer paths, had undergone a species of incineration since the drought had set in.

text: She tracked the man she had been shown. He was dressed in earthy, reddish tones, merging with the surrounding landscape as effectively as a green larva disappears against the foliage it eats. While his pace surpassed Mrs. Yeobright’s whenever he was moving, she was able to stay a consistent distance behind him because of his tendency to stop at every patch of briars, where he would linger briefly. Each time she reached one of these thickets, she saw a handful of long, flexible stalks he had severed and laid out flat next to the track. It was obvious they were meant to be used as ties for bundles of kindling, which he planned to pick up on his way back.
source_text: She followed the figure indicated. He appeared of a russet hue, not more distinguishable from the scene around him than the green caterpillar from the leaf it feeds on. His progress when actually walking was more rapid than Mrs. Yeobright’s; but she was enabled to keep at an equable distance from him by his habit of stopping whenever he came to a brake of brambles, where he paused awhile. On coming in her turn to each of these spots she found half a dozen long limp brambles which he had cut from the bush during his halt and laid out straight beside the path. They were evidently intended for furze-faggot bonds which he meant to collect on his return.

text: The brush-gleaner remained so preoccupied with the toil of his progress that he never once glanced behind; thus, his shape, swathed in rough skins and heavy gloves, gradually appeared to her as little more than a living signpost guiding her through the waste. Suddenly, her attention was arrested by the distinct character of his movement. There was a particularity in his stride that stirred a dormant memory; and the manner of his walking betrayed his identity to her, much as the peculiar bearing of a messenger on the horizon once informed the ancient scouts of his name. "He treads with the very same rhythm as his father," she murmured; and then the revelation flooded her mind that this common workman was her own boy.
source_text: The furze-cutter was so absorbed in the business of his journey that he never turned his head; and his leather-legged and gauntleted form at length became to her as nothing more than a moving handpost to show her the way. Suddenly she was attracted to his individuality by observing peculiarities in his walk. It was a gait she had seen somewhere before; and the gait revealed the man to her, as the gait of Ahimaaz in the distant plain made him known to the watchman of the king. “His walk is exactly as my husband’s used to be,” she said; and then the thought burst upon her that the furze-cutter was her son.

text: Beside Clym’s home rose a small hill, topped with a cluster of firs that stretched so high into the sky that, from a distance, they seemed a mere dark speck floating over the crest. By the time Mrs. Yeobright reached this point, she felt deeply troubled, exhausted, and physically drained. She made her way up and sat in the cool shade to catch her breath and reflect on the best way to open a conversation with Eustacia. She wished to avoid upsetting a woman whose outward stillness hid a temperament even more powerful and restless than her own.
source_text: At one side of Clym’s house was a knoll, and on the top of the knoll a clump of fir trees so highly thrust up into the sky that their foliage from a distance appeared as a black spot in the air above the crown of the hill. On reaching this place Mrs. Yeobright felt distressingly agitated, weary, and unwell. She ascended, and sat down under their shade to recover herself, and to consider how best to break the ground with Eustacia, so as not to irritate a woman underneath whose apparent indolence lurked passions even stronger and more active than her own.

text: As had been noted, Wildeve was intent on visiting Eustacia openly by day, relying on the casual familiarity of a relative, now that the reddleman had detected and disrupted his secret night walks. The enchantment she had cast over him during their dance in the moonlight made it impossible for a man of his weak moral discipline to stay away entirely. He planned simply to meet her and her husband in a normal fashion, talk for a few minutes, and then leave. Every outward appearance would be perfectly proper, but the core reality would be enough to satisfy him—he would be in her sight. He didn't even wish for Clym to be away, for he suspected Eustacia might reject any scenario that threatened her dignity as a married woman, regardless of her private feelings for him. Women were often that way.
source_text: Wildeve, as has been stated, was determined to visit Eustacia boldly, by day, and on the easy terms of a relation, since the reddleman had spied out and spoilt his walks to her by night. The spell that she had thrown over him in the moonlight dance made it impossible for a man having no strong puritanic force within him to keep away altogether. He merely calculated on meeting her and her husband in an ordinary manner, chatting a little while, and leaving again. Every outward sign was to be conventional; but the one great fact would be there to satisfy him—he would see her. He did not even desire Clym’s absence, since it was just possible that Eustacia might resent any situation which could compromise her dignity as a wife, whatever the state of her heart towards him. Women were often so.

text: Wildeve blew a kiss to the lovely young woman he had been speaking with and stepped out into the garden. She followed him with her eyes as he moved down the path, climbed the stile at its end, and waded into the ferns beyond. The tall fronds brushed against his hips as he walked until he was eventually swallowed by the dense growth. When he had finally vanished, she turned away slowly and focused her attention back on the interior of the house.
source_text: Wildeve kissed his hand to the beautiful girl he addressed, and passed into the garden, where she watched him down the path, over the stile at the end, and into the ferns outside, which brushed his hips as he went along till he became lost in their thickets. When he had quite gone she slowly turned, and directed her attention to the interior of the house.

text: She extracted from the woven reed pouch she carried a weathered porcelain cup, fashioned without a handle in the antique style; it was one of six such pieces resting in the bag, remnants she had cherished since her girlhood and had brought with her this day as a humble token for Julian and Morwenna.
source_text: She drew from the small willow reticule that she carried in her hand an old-fashioned china teacup without a handle; it was one of half a dozen of the same sort lying in the reticule, which she had preserved ever since her childhood, and had brought with her today as a small present for Clym and Eustacia.

text: Within two hours she gained a rise some three-quarters of the distance from Fairwood to her own threshold, where a spray of wild mint had encroached upon the trail; there she rested upon the pungent pillow it formed. In front of her, a kingdom of ants had established a highway across the dust, where a burdened and ceaseless throng toiled. To peer down at them was like viewing a metropolis from a high balcony. She reflected that this frantic commerce had endured for years at this very spot—undoubtedly the swarms of old were the ancestors of the workers traveling there now. She leaned back to find a deeper stillness, and the pale eastern expanse of the firmament was as much a solace to her eyes as the mint was to her brow. As she watched, a great crane climbed into that side of the sky and steered his course toward the sun. He had risen dripping from some tarn in the valleys, and as he flew, the tips and linings of his wings, his legs, and his breast were so struck by the radiant beams that he appeared as if cast in molten silver. Up in the blue where he hung seemed a realm of grace and freedom, far from the terrestrial weight to which she was chained; and she wished that she might lift herself unmarred from the earth and glide as he glided then.
source_text: In two hours she reached a slope about three-fourths the whole distance from Alderworth to her own home, where a little patch of shepherd’s-thyme intruded upon the path; and she sat down upon the perfumed mat it formed there. In front of her a colony of ants had established a thoroughfare across the way, where they toiled a never-ending and heavy-laden throng. To look down upon them was like observing a city street from the top of a tower. She remembered that this bustle of ants had been in progress for years at the same spot—doubtless those of the old times were the ancestors of these which walked there now. She leant back to obtain more thorough rest, and the soft eastern portion of the sky was as great a relief to her eyes as the thyme was to her head. While she looked a heron arose on that side of the sky and flew on with his face towards the sun. He had come dripping wet from some pool in the valleys, and as he flew the edges and lining of his wings, his thighs and his breast were so caught by the bright sunbeams that he appeared as if formed of burnished silver. Up in the zenith where he was seemed a free and happy place, away from all contact with the earthly ball to which she was pinioned; and she wished that she could arise uncrushed from its surface and fly as he flew then.

text: The wind was knocked out of him, and the agonized scream that rose in his throat never made it past his lips. In the brief pause before he understood he had to do something, he lost all track of time and place. It felt as if he were a young boy again, out on this same moor with his mother just as they used to be at this time of day. Then he jolted back to the task at hand; leaning down even closer, he found that she was still breathing. Though her breath was faint, it remained steady, save for the occasional ragged gasp.
source_text: His breath went, as it were, out of his body and the cry of anguish which would have escaped him died upon his lips. During the momentary interval that elapsed before he became conscious that something must be done all sense of time and place left him, and it seemed as if he and his mother were as when he was a child with her many years ago on this heath at hours similar to the present. Then he awoke to activity; and bending yet lower he found that she still breathed, and that her breath though feeble was regular, except when disturbed by an occasional gasp.

text: He was still nearly a mile from home when his mother began to shift restlessly in his arms, as if the physical constraint of being carried had become unbearable to her. He eased her down onto his lap and surveyed their surroundings. Though they were currently far from any established road, they were no more than a mile from the cottages at Blooms-End where Fairway, Sam, Humphrey, and the Cantles lived. About fifty yards away stood an abandoned hut, built of earthen sod and roofed with thin turf. The plain silhouette of the isolated structure was visible, and he decided to head toward it. Upon reaching the entrance, he laid her down gently and hurried to cut an armful of dry ferns with his pocketknife. He scattered these inside the shed, which was completely open on one side, and placed his mother on the makeshift bed; then, he sprinted with all his strength toward Fairway’s house.
source_text: While he was yet nearly a mile from the house his mother exhibited signs of restlessness under the constraint of being borne along, as if his arms were irksome to her. He lowered her upon his knees and looked around. The point they had now reached, though far from any road, was not more than a mile from the Blooms-End cottages occupied by Fairway, Sam, Humphrey, and the Cantles. Moreover, fifty yards off stood a hut, built of clods and covered with thin turves, but now entirely disused. The simple outline of the lonely shed was visible, and thither he determined to direct his steps. As soon as he arrived he laid her down carefully by the entrance, and then ran and cut with his pocketknife an armful of the dryest fern. Spreading this within the shed, which was entirely open on one side, he placed his mother thereon; then he ran with all his might towards the dwelling of Fairway.

text: Susan appeared just then with the skillet, whereupon the living adder was dispatched and the heads of all three were struck off. The rest of the bodies, sliced into segments and split open, were tossed into the pan, which began to hiss and sputter over the flames. Soon, a trickle of translucent oil seeped from the remains; Clym dipped the edge of his handkerchief into the liquid and used it to soothe the wound.
source_text: Susan now arrived with the frying pan, when the live adder was killed and the heads of the three taken off. The remainders, being cut into lengths and split open, were tossed into the pan, which began hissing and crackling over the fire. Soon a rill of clear oil trickled from the carcases, whereupon Clym dipped the corner of his handkerchief into the liquid and anointed the wound.

text: Spending the evening in solitude was always tedious for her, but tonight it felt even more draining after the day's excitement. Those two visits had left her feeling unsettled and restless. She wasn't terribly worried about how she might be portrayed when Clym and his mother eventually talked, but she was deeply irritated. This frustration sharpened her focus, leaving her with a nagging regret that she hadn't just opened the door. She truly thought Clym had been awake—a defense that was honest enough in its own way—yet she knew she would still be judged for failing to respond to the first knock. However, rather than accepting her own role in the mess, she shifted the blame onto some shadowy, cosmic power, a vast architect of fate who had rigged her circumstances and controlled her destiny.
source_text: To be left to pass the evening by herself was irksome to her at any time, and this evening it was more irksome than usual by reason of the excitements of the past hours. The two visits had stirred her into restlessness. She was not wrought to any great pitch of uneasiness by the probability of appearing in an ill light in the discussion between Clym and his mother, but she was wrought to vexation, and her slumbering activities were quickened to the extent of wishing that she had opened the door. She had certainly believed that Clym was awake, and the excuse would be an honest one as far as it went; but nothing could save her from censure in refusing to answer at the first knock. Yet, instead of blaming herself for the issue she laid the fault upon the shoulders of some indistinct, colossal Prince of the World, who had framed her situation and ruled her lot.

text: So deeply was Elara immersed in the shifting prospects of Julian that she failed to register how much more intimately Arthur’s destiny was entwined with her own; thus, rather than proceeding to their appointed encounter, she paused to rest upon a lichen-covered crag. Her ruminations were abruptly severed by a call from the rear, and pivoting, she discovered her erstwhile paramour—now the gilded beneficiary of a grand inheritance—looming directly beside her.
source_text: So intent was Eustacia upon Wildeve’s fortunes that she forgot how much closer to her own course were those of Clym; and instead of walking on to meet him at once she sat down upon a stone. She was disturbed in her reverie by a voice behind, and turning her head beheld the old lover and fortunate inheritor of wealth immediately beside her.

text: I intend to travel first to Paris, staying through the winter and spring. From there, I shall visit Italy, Greece, Egypt, and Palestine before the onset of the summer heat. During the summer, I will go to America; then, by a route not yet finalized, I shall continue to Australia and around to India. I suspect that by then, I will have grown weary of the journey. I will likely return to Paris at that point, remaining there for as long as my funds permit.
source_text: “From here to Paris, where I shall pass the winter and spring. Then I shall go to Italy, Greece, Egypt, and Palestine, before the hot weather comes on. In the summer I shall go to America; and then, by a plan not yet settled, I shall go to Australia and round to India. By that time I shall have begun to have had enough of it. Then I shall probably come back to Paris again, and there I shall stay as long as I can afford to.”

text: Actually, it’s a quite ancient treatment—the traditional cure used by viper hunters, I believe," the doctor answered. "Hoffman, Mead, and I think the Abbé Fontana all cite it as a foolproof ointment. It was certainly the best course of action you could have taken, though I suspect other types of oil might have proven just as effective.
