source_text: “Well, it is a very ancient remedy—the old remedy of the viper-catchers, I believe,” replied the doctor. “It is mentioned as an infallible ointment by Hoffman, Mead, and I think the Abbé Fontana. Undoubtedly it was as good a thing as you could do; though I question if some other oils would not have been equally efficacious.”

text: Indeed, it was so; to offer excuses now would be a hollow labor. My treatment of Catherine was deplorable—I maintained a stony silence, and she found it impossible to grant me grace. Now she has passed beyond the veil. If I had only signaled my desire for peace a little sooner, and we had reconciled before her departure, this sorrow would not weigh so heavily. But I shunned her door, and she avoided mine, never suspecting how gladly I would have received her—that is the thought that haunts me. She died without knowing I was bound for her cottage that very evening, for her spirit was already too far gone to hear my confession. If only she had stepped across my threshold! I yearned for it daily. But it was never meant to happen.
source_text: “Yes, it was so; it is useless to excuse me! My conduct to her was too hideous—I made no advances; and she could not bring herself to forgive me. Now she is dead! If I had only shown myself willing to make it up with her sooner, and we had been friends, and then she had died, it wouldn’t be so hard to bear. But I never went near her house, so she never came near mine, and didn’t know how welcome she would have been—that’s what troubles me. She did not know I was going to her house that very night, for she was too insensible to understand me. If she had only come to see me! I longed that she would. But it was not to be.”

text: Yeobright was too deeply lost in the rambling thoughts brought on by his guilt to pay her any mind. He had been speaking this way incessantly throughout his illness. His initial grief had turned to pure despair following the unfortunate account from the boy who had heard Mrs. Yeobright’s final words—bitter things said in a moment of tragic misunderstanding. This distress had completely broken him, and he yearned for death as a laborer in the fields aches for the shade. It was a heart-wrenching sight: a man standing at the very center of sorrow. He constantly mourned his delayed journey to his mother’s house, an error that could never be undone, insisting he must have been possessed by some dark spirit to have forgotten his duty to go to her since she would not come to him. He would beg Eustacia to confirm his self-blame; yet she, tormented by a secret she dared not confess, claimed she could not offer an opinion. “That’s because you didn’t know my mother’s heart,” he would reply. “She was always ready to forgive if she were asked, but I must have seemed like a stubborn child to her, which made her appear unyielding. But she wasn't—she was just proud and reserved. Yes, I understand now why she held out against me for so long. She was waiting for me to come. I imagine she said a hundred times in her grief, ‘This is how he repays all my sacrifices!’ And I never went to her. When I finally set out to see her, it was too late. The thought of it is almost more than I can bear.”
source_text: But Yeobright was too deeply absorbed in the ramblings incidental to his remorseful state to notice her. During his illness he had been continually talking thus. Despair had been added to his original grief by the unfortunate disclosure of the boy who had received the last words of Mrs. Yeobright—words too bitterly uttered in an hour of misapprehension. Then his distress had overwhelmed him, and he longed for death as a field labourer longs for the shade. It was the pitiful sight of a man standing in the very focus of sorrow. He continually bewailed his tardy journey to his mother’s house, because it was an error which could never be rectified, and insisted that he must have been horribly perverted by some fiend not to have thought before that it was his duty to go to her, since she did not come to him. He would ask Eustacia to agree with him in his self-condemnation; and when she, seared inwardly by a secret she dared not tell, declared that she could not give an opinion, he would say, “That’s because you didn’t know my mother’s nature. She was always ready to forgive if asked to do so; but I seemed to her to be as an obstinate child, and that made her unyielding. Yet not unyielding—she was proud and reserved, no more.... Yes, I can understand why she held out against me so long. She was waiting for me. I dare say she said a hundred times in her sorrow, ‘What a return he makes for all the sacrifices I have made for him!’ I never went to her! When I set out to visit her it was too late. To think of that is nearly intolerable!”

text: Clym’s sorrow gradually ebbed as it exhausted its own force. His physical vigor returned, and a month after Thomasin’s visit, he could be seen pacing the garden paths. His countenance presented a strange fusion of endurance and hopelessness, a mingling of healthy color and deathly pallor. He maintained an unnatural silence regarding everything connected to his mother; though Eustacia sensed his thoughts remained fixed upon her, she was too relieved by this omission to ever broach the subject herself. While his earlier mental frailty had compelled him to speak from the heart, the restoration of his reason had brought with it a deep, brooding reserve.
source_text: Clym’s grief became mitigated by wearing itself out. His strength returned, and a month after the visit of Thomasin he might have been seen walking about the garden. Endurance and despair, equanimity and gloom, the tints of health and the pallor of death, mingled weirdly in his face. He was now unnaturally silent upon all of the past that related to his mother; and though Eustacia knew that he was thinking of it none the less, she was only too glad to escape the topic ever to bring it up anew. When his mind had been weaker his heart had led him to speak out; but reason having now somewhat recovered itself he sank into taciturnity.

text: “Aye, the dry spell holds, and a mercy it is. But I’ve come to bring word of a matter quite removed from our recent family trials. I was dispatched by the monied master of the Golden Lion—him we used to call the publican—to inform you that Mrs. Vane is doing bravely with a new daughter, born just as the clock struck one this afternoon, or thereabouts; and the talk is that waiting for this little soul is what has anchored them to the village ever since they came into their great fortune.”
source_text: “Yes, without a drop o’ rain, thank God. But I was coming to tell ’ee of something else which is quite different from what we have lately had in the family. I am sent by the rich gentleman at the Woman, that we used to call the landlord, to tell ’ee that Mrs. Wildeve is doing well of a girl, which was born punctually at one o’clock at noon, or a few minutes more or less; and ’tis said that expecting of this increase is what have kept ’em there since they came into their money.”

text: While Julian paced the apartments, he was seized by a sharp distaste for the removals necessary to reconcile his forebears’ hallowed furniture with Genevieve’s fashionable whims. The skeletal oak clock, its panel depicting the Assumption and its plinth the Gathering of the Nets; the corner hutch with its glazed door revealing the dapple-patterned china; the rotating server; the heavy wooden salvers; the wall-cistern with its burnished tap—to what exile would these venerable relics be consigned?
source_text: As he surveyed the rooms he felt strongly disinclined for the alterations which would have to be made in the time-honoured furnishing of his parents and grandparents, to suit Eustacia’s modern ideas. The gaunt oak-cased clock, with the picture of the Ascension on the door panel and the Miraculous Draught of Fishes on the base; his grandmother’s corner cupboard with the glass door, through which the spotted china was visible; the dumb-waiter; the wooden tea trays; the hanging fountain with the brass tap—whither would these venerable articles have to be banished?

text: “You heard from her own mouth that I had not been unkind; yet another was told by those same lips that I had indeed used her ill. My mother was never a creature of whim, shifting her mind every hour for no reason. How can it be, Thorne, that she should give such conflicting reports in so brief a space?”
source_text: “You had it from her lips that I had _not_ ill-treated her; and at the same time another had it from her lips that I _had_ ill-treated her? My mother was no impulsive woman who changed her opinion every hour without reason. How can it be, Venn, that she should have told such different stories in close succession?”

text: He remained in that same listless state throughout the afternoon. A neighbor prepared a bed for him in the house so that he would not have to leave and return the following morning; however, when he finally retired in the empty dwelling, he spent the long hours of the night wide awake, circling the same dark thoughts. Solving the enigma of this death felt more urgent than any problem belonging to the living world. Fixed in his mind was the striking image of a small boy entering the hut where Clym’s mother lay. The child’s wide eyes, his intense gaze, and the high-pitched voice that delivered those words had pierced his brain like sharp blades.
source_text: He continued in the same state all the afternoon. A bed was made up for him in the same house by a neighbour, that he might not have to return again the next day; and when he retired to rest in the deserted place it was only to remain awake hour after hour thinking the same thoughts. How to discover a solution to this riddle of death seemed a query of more importance than highest problems of the living. There was housed in his memory a vivid picture of the face of a little boy as he entered the hovel where Clym’s mother lay. The round eyes, eager gaze, the piping voice which enunciated the words, had operated like stilettos on his brain.

text: As Yeobright reached the cottage of Susan Nunsuch, the mother of the boy he was seeking, he found the household was not yet awake. Yet in these upland hamlets, the transition from sleep to activity is remarkably swift and uncomplicated. No thick barrier of yawning or grooming separates the world of the night from the world of the day. Yeobright rapped on the upstairs windowsill, which he could reach with his walking stick; within a few minutes, the woman came downstairs.
source_text: When Yeobright reached the cottage of Susan Nunsuch, the mother of the boy he sought, he found that the inmates were not yet astir. But in upland hamlets the transition from a-bed to abroad is surprisingly swift and easy. There no dense partition of yawns and toilets divides humanity by night from humanity by day. Yeobright tapped at the upper windowsill, which he could reach with his walking stick; and in three or four minutes the woman came down.

text: With those parting words, Yeobright stepped out from the small house. His eyes, staring fixedly into the void, glinted with a frigid light, while his mouth had taken on the tragic cast often portrayed in depictions of Oedipus. In his current state of mind, he was capable of the most extreme actions, yet his surroundings offered no outlet for them. Instead of being met by Eustacia’s pale visage or some mysterious male figure, he was confronted only by the indifferent face of the heath. Having endured the violent shifts of centuries, its ancient, weathered expanse made the frantic inner storm of a single human being seem entirely inconsequential.
source_text: With these words Yeobright went forth from the little dwelling. The pupils of his eyes, fixed steadfastly on blankness, were vaguely lit with an icy shine; his mouth had passed into the phase more or less imaginatively rendered in studies of Oedipus. The strangest deeds were possible to his mood. But they were not possible to his situation. Instead of there being before him the pale face of Eustacia, and a masculine shape unknown, there was only the imperturbable countenance of the heath, which, having defied the cataclysmal onsets of centuries, reduced to insignificance by its seamed and antique features the wildest turmoil of a single man.

text: A sense of the profound neutrality in the surrounding world gripped Julian during his frantic journey toward Blackwood. He had experienced this crushing of human heat by the lifeless material of the earth once before, though in that earlier instance, the sensation had merely dampened a sentiment far gentler than the one now consuming him. It had occurred as he stood taking leave of Elara within the damp, hushed lowlands that lay beyond the crest of the hills.
source_text: A consciousness of a vast impassivity in all which lay around him took possession even of Yeobright in his wild walk towards Alderworth. He had once before felt in his own person this overpowering of the fervid by the inanimate; but then it had tended to enervate a passion far sweeter than that which at present pervaded him. It was once when he stood parting from Eustacia in the moist still levels beyond the hills.

text: The sound of his entry must have woken her, for when he pushed open the door, she was standing before the mirror in her nightgown. She held the length of her hair in one hand, winding the heavy mass into a coil atop her head as she prepared to dress. Never one to offer the first word upon meeting, she allowed Clym to cross the room in silence without turning to look at him. He moved up behind her, and her gaze met his reflection in the glass. His face was grey, gaunt, and ghastly. Instead of turning toward him in pained surprise—as even a reserved woman like Eustacia would have done before she was weighed down by a secret—she remained perfectly still, watching him through the mirror. As she looked, the deep flush that warmth and heavy sleep had brought to her cheeks and neck faded away, and the deathly pallor of his countenance seemed to pass into hers. He was close enough to see this change, and the sight finally prompted him to speak.
source_text: The noise of his arrival must have aroused her, for when he opened the door she was standing before the looking glass in her nightdress, the ends of her hair gathered into one hand, with which she was coiling the whole mass round her head, previous to beginning toilette operations. She was not a woman given to speaking first at a meeting, and she allowed Clym to walk across in silence, without turning her head. He came behind her, and she saw his face in the glass. It was ashy, haggard, and terrible. Instead of starting towards him in sorrowful surprise, as even Eustacia, undemonstrative wife as she was, would have done in days before she burdened herself with a secret, she remained motionless, looking at him in the glass. And while she looked the carmine flush with which warmth and sound sleep had suffused her cheeks and neck dissolved from view, and the deathlike pallor in his face flew across into hers. He was close enough to see this, and the sight instigated his tongue.

text: “Since the hour permits, I shall be explicit, though you surely grasp my intent. It strikes me as singularly odd that you should be found quite so solitary during my time away. Pray, inform me: where is that gentleman who shared your company on the final afternoon of August? Is he concealed beneath the divan? Or perhaps wedged within the flue?”
source_text: “As there is ample time I will tell you, though you know well enough. I mean that it is extraordinary that you should be alone in my absence. Tell me, now, where is he who was with you on the afternoon of the thirty-first of August? Under the bed? Up the chimney?”

text: She hurried into her clothes while Yeobright paced the room with a somber expression. When she was finally ready, her small hands trembled so violently as she tried to secure her bonnet that she couldn't manage the ribbons. After a few moments, she gave up the struggle. Noticing this, he stepped toward her and said, "Let me tie them."
source_text: She hastily dressed herself, Yeobright moodily walking up and down the room the whole of the time. At last all her things were on. Her little hands quivered so violently as she held them to her chin to fasten her bonnet that she could not tie the strings, and after a few moments she relinquished the attempt. Seeing this he moved forward and said, “Let me tie them.”

text: He steadied her onto the veranda and, leaving her upon a bench, hastened to the rear where he scaled a ladder to a high casement. Slipping through the frame, he descended to unbolt the door from within. He then helped her into the chamber, which was furnished with a stiff horsehair davenport as massive as a hay-wain. She reclined there, and Julian draped her in a heavy mantle he had found hanging in the passage.
source_text: He supported her to the porch, and there depositing her on a seat hastened to the back, climbed to a window by the help of a ladder, and descending inside opened the door. Next he assisted her into the room, where there was an old-fashioned horsehair settee as large as a donkey wagon. She lay down here, and Charley covered her with a cloak he found in the hall.

text: Later, he looked in on her from time to time to check the fire, to ask if she had any needs, to remark that the wind had turned toward the west, or to offer to pick some blackberries for her; however, she met every one of these inquiries with a flat rejection or a cold lack of concern.
source_text: Afterwards he occasionally came in to see if the fire was burning, to ask her if she wanted anything, to tell her that the wind had shifted from south to west, to ask her if she would like him to gather her some blackberries; to all which inquiries she replied in the negative or with indifference.

text: The sudden ruin of her plans caused by their disappearance hit her with the force of a physical vacuum; she nearly lost consciousness. Who could have done this? Only one other soul was on the property. Eustacia turned instinctively toward the open window that surveyed the garden as far as its bordering ridge. Atop that height stood Charley, positioned high enough to look directly into the room. His eyes were fixed upon her with a look of desperate, watchful concern.
source_text: The instant quashing of her purpose by their absence affected her brain as a sudden vacuum affects the body—she nearly fainted. Who had done this? There was only one person on the premises besides herself. Eustacia involuntarily turned to the open window which overlooked the garden as far as the bank that bounded it. On the summit of the latter stood Charley, sufficiently elevated by its height to see into the room. His gaze was directed eagerly and solicitously upon her.

text: “What right have you to bar my exit?” she cried, her voice trembling with exhaustion. “I have brokered a ruinous debt with existence, and I am tired—so very tired of the struggle. Why did you intervene, Julian? What is the sting of the grave, if not the sorrow of those we leave? Yet I am spared even that, for not a single breath of mourning would follow me into the dark.”
source_text: “Why should I not die if I wish?” she said tremulously. “I have made a bad bargain with life, and I am weary of it—weary. And now you have hindered my escape. O, why did you, Charley! What makes death painful except the thought of others’ grief?—and that is absent in my case, for not a sigh would follow me!”

text: With this aim, he diligently sought to provide her with delightful diversions, bringing home curious treasures discovered on the heath. He gathered pale, trumpet-like mosses, scarlet-capped lichens, flint arrowheads used by Egdon’s ancient inhabitants, and sparkling crystals found within hollow stones. These he placed around the house in spots where she might stumble upon them as if by chance.
source_text: For this reason he busily endeavoured to provide her with pleasant distractions, bringing home curious objects which he found in the heath, such as white trumpet-shaped mosses, redheaded lichens, stone arrowheads used by the old tribes on Egdon, and faceted crystals from the hollows of flints. These he deposited on the premises in such positions that she should see them as if by accident.

text: Dusk descended, and Elara appeared entirely unmoved by the weight of the anniversary. She had retreated into the house after her long observation through the spyglass and had remained secluded ever since. Once the darkness was complete, Julian began to assemble the wood for the beacon, choosing the exact stretch of the embankment that Elara had favored on previous occasions.
source_text: The evening came, and Eustacia was still seemingly unconscious of the anniversary. She had gone indoors after her survey through the glass, and had not been visible since. As soon as it was quite dark Charley began to build the bonfire, choosing precisely that spot on the bank which Eustacia had chosen at previous times.

text: One cannot stop the spiteful from indulging in their gossip, but you need not be concerned. Whatever my private feelings, I swear on my honor never to voice them or act upon them until you give me leave. I recognize my obligation to Thomasin just as clearly as my duty to you, a woman so unfairly used. How may I help you?
source_text: “Well, there’s no preventing slanderers from having their fill at any time; but you need not be afraid. Whatever I may feel I promise you on my word of honour never to speak to you about—or act upon—until you say I may. I know my duty to Thomasin quite as well as I know my duty to you as a woman unfairly treated. What shall I assist you in?”

text: “I’ll give this some thought,” she said quickly. “I need to ask myself whether I can honestly rely on you as a friend, or if I must commit to you as a lover. If I decide to leave and want you to come with me, I’ll signal you at exactly eight o’clock one evening. That will be your cue to be ready with a horse and carriage at midnight that same night to drive me to the Budmouth docks in time for the morning boat.”
source_text: “I will think of this,” she said hurriedly. “Whether I can honestly make use of you as a friend, or must close with you as a lover—that is what I must ask myself. If I wish to go and decide to accept your company I will signal to you some evening at eight o’clock punctually, and this will mean that you are to be ready with a horse and trap at twelve o’clock the same night to drive me to Budmouth harbour in time for the morning boat.”

text: In the quiet of the parlor he had recently departed, Clara remained, her breath hitching in a series of troubled sighs. A sense of marital duty had prompted her that evening to mask all misgivings that Julian’s fascination with Elena had persisted beyond their wedding day. She possessed no absolute proof; and though Arthur was a cousin she held in the highest esteem, there was another whose proximity to her heart remained more absolute.
source_text: Meanwhile, at the house he had just left Thomasin sat sighing uneasily. Fidelity to her husband had that evening induced her to conceal all suspicion that Wildeve’s interest in Eustacia had not ended with his marriage. But she knew nothing positive; and though Clym was her well-beloved cousin there was one nearer to her still.

text: “Afraid?” he asked, patting her cheek as if she were a household pet. “I didn't think anything could rattle you. I suspect you’ve simply grown arrogant and find this place beneath you now that we’ve moved up in the world. Finding a new home is a tedious chore, but I couldn’t have started the search any sooner. Unless our ten thousand pounds had been a hundred thousand, we couldn't have afforded to ignore the need for caution.”
source_text: “Frightened?” he said, touching her cheek as if she were some domestic animal. “Why, I thought nothing could frighten you. It is that you are getting proud, I am sure, and don’t like living here since we have risen above our business. Well, it is a tedious matter, this getting a new house; but I couldn’t have set about it sooner, unless our ten thousand pounds had been a hundred thousand, when we could have afforded to despise caution.”

text: No, I don’t mind the delay—I would gladly stay here another twelve months before I’d risk anything where the baby is concerned. But I am troubled by the way you disappear every evening. Something is weighing on you, Damon; I am sure of it. You move about so despondently, and you look out at the heath as if it were a prison instead of a pleasant, wild place to wander.
source_text: “No—I don’t mind waiting—I would rather stay here twelve months longer than run any risk with baby. But I don’t like your vanishing so in the evenings. There’s something on your mind—I know there is, Damon. You go about so gloomily, and look at the heath as if it were somebody’s gaol instead of a nice wild place to walk in.”

text: A woman seated inside the cottage had caught sight of her and knew who she was in that fleeting flash of light. This was Susan, busy preparing a medicinal drink for her young son, who was often sickly but had now fallen gravely ill. She let her spoon fall, shook a fist at the spot where the figure had disappeared, and then returned to her task with a distracted, pensive air.
source_text: A woman who was sitting inside the cottage had seen and recognized her in that momentary irradiation. This was Susan herself, occupied in preparing a posset for her little boy, who, often ailing, was now seriously unwell. Susan dropped the spoon, shook her fist at the vanished figure, and then proceeded with her work in a musing, absent way.

text: The courier delivered the dispatch and went his way. The servant girl brought it to the Major, who noted it was intended for Elara. He turned the paper over repeatedly, suspecting the hand was that of her husband, though he lacked certainty. Determining she should receive it forthwith, he climbed the stairs; yet, peering through the keyhole, he found the room shrouded in darkness. In truth, Elara had collapsed upon the bed in her full attire, seeking to husband her strength for the impending voyage. Believing her asleep, the old man chose not to intrude; returning to the parlor, he leaned the letter against the mantel-clock to await the break of day.
source_text: He handed in a letter and went his way. The girl brought it to the captain, who found that it was directed to Eustacia. He turned it over and over, and fancied that the writing was her husband’s, though he could not be sure. However, he decided to let her have it at once if possible, and took it upstairs for that purpose; but on reaching the door of her room and looking in at the keyhole he found there was no light within, the fact being that Eustacia, without undressing, had flung herself upon the bed, to rest and gather a little strength for her coming journey. Her grandfather concluded from what he saw that he ought not to disturb her; and descending again to the parlour he placed the letter on the mantelpiece to give it to her in the morning.

text: He headed to bed at eleven, smoking for a while before turning out his lamp at eleven-thirty. Following his nightly routine, he raised the blind before climbing under the covers so he could check the wind's direction upon waking, as his window looked directly out at the flagstaff and its weather vane. He had just settled in when he was startled to see the white staff suddenly flare into view, appearing like a stroke of phosphorus painted against the dark night. There was only one possible cause: a light had suddenly been cast onto the pole from somewhere in the house. Since everyone else had already turned in for the night, the old man felt compelled to get back up, ease the window open, and peer outside. Eustacia's room was illuminated, and it was the glow from her window that had lit up the mast. Puzzled by what might have woken her, he stood there hesitating, considering whether to get the letter and slide it under her door, when he detected the faint rustle of clothing against the wall in the hallway.
source_text: At eleven o’clock he went to bed himself, smoked for some time in his bedroom, put out his light at half-past eleven, and then, as was his invariable custom, pulled up the blind before getting into bed, that he might see which way the wind blew on opening his eyes in the morning, his bedroom window commanding a view of the flagstaff and vane. Just as he had lain down he was surprised to observe the white pole of the staff flash into existence like a streak of phosphorus drawn downwards across the shade of night without. Only one explanation met this—a light had been suddenly thrown upon the pole from the direction of the house. As everybody had retired to rest the old man felt it necessary to get out of bed, open the window softly, and look to the right and left. Eustacia’s bedroom was lighted up, and it was the shine from her window which had lighted the pole. Wondering what had aroused her, he remained undecided at the window, and was thinking of fetching the letter to slip it under her door, when he heard a slight brushing of garments on the partition dividing his room from the passage.

text: “Is there any way out? Can I truly leave?” she wailed. “He isn't remarkable enough to justify my surrender—he simply cannot satisfy my longings. If he were a figure like Saul or Napoleon, it might be different; but to betray my marriage for him is such a pathetic indulgence. Besides, I lack the funds to depart on my own, and even if I could, what peace would I find? I am doomed to endure the coming year just as I have suffered through this one, with every year following the same pattern. I have struggled so desperately to be a woman of stature, yet fate has constantly conspired against me. I do not deserve this life!” she cried out in a fit of resentful defiance. “How cruel to be cast into such a poorly designed world! I was capable of so much, yet I have been wounded, ruined, and broken by forces far beyond my control. How heartless of the heavens to invent such agonies for me, when I have done nothing to offend them!”
source_text: “Can I go, can I go?” she moaned. “He’s not _great_ enough for me to give myself to—he does not suffice for my desire!... If he had been a Saul or a Bonaparte—ah! But to break my marriage vow for him—it is too poor a luxury!... And I have no money to go alone! And if I could, what comfort to me? I must drag on next year, as I have dragged on this year, and the year after that as before. How I have tried and tried to be a splendid woman, and how destiny has been against me!... I do not deserve my lot!” she cried in a frenzy of bitter revolt. “O, the cruelty of putting me into this ill-conceived world! I was capable of much; but I have been injured and blighted and crushed by things beyond my control! O, how hard it is of Heaven to devise such tortures for me, who have done no harm to Heaven at all!”

text: For this reason, Martha did not retire once the day’s labors were concluded, as would have been her custom under normal circumstances. In an effort to nullify the baleful influence she believed the enigmatic Rowena was exerting, the mother occupied herself with a macabre rite of folk-magic, intended to bring about debility, wasting, and total ruin upon the person it was meant to strike. It was a tradition well understood throughout the Great Fen in those times, and a practice that has not entirely faded from memory even today.
source_text: On this account Susan did not go to bed as soon as the evening’s work was over, as she would have done at ordinary times. To counteract the malign spell which she imagined poor Eustacia to be working, the boy’s mother busied herself with a ghastly invention of superstition, calculated to bring powerlessness, atrophy, and annihilation on any human being against whom it was directed. It was a practice well known on Egdon at that date, and one that is not quite extinct at the present day.

text: As the clock neared the midnight hour, observing that neither Thorne nor any other messenger had arrived, Elias withdrew to his chamber, where, notwithstanding his inner tumult, sleep eventually claimed him. His repose was tenuous, however, frayed by the lingering threads of his earlier suspense, so that a sudden pounding upon the door, occurring perhaps sixty minutes later, drew him instantly from his dreams. He stood and approached the casement. The torrent showed no sign of abating, and the wide reaches of the fell groaned with a soft, persistent sibilance under the weight of the water. In the absolute gloom, the landscape remained entirely hidden from view.
source_text: Between ten and eleven o’clock, finding that neither Fairway nor anybody else came to him, he retired to rest, and despite his anxieties soon fell asleep. His sleep, however, was not very sound, by reason of the expectancy he had given way to, and he was easily awakened by a knocking which began at the door about an hour after. Clym arose and looked out of the window. Rain was still falling heavily, the whole expanse of heath before him emitting a subdued hiss under the downpour. It was too dark to see anything at all.

text: At the time, he claimed he had taken them down merely to clean them. Now, however, he has admitted he removed them because he noticed Eustacia watching them with a peculiar interest; she later confessed to him that she was considering ending her life, though she swore him to silence and promised to put the thought aside forever. I don’t truly believe she has the nerve to actually use one, but it reveals the darkness that has been dwelling in her mind; and those who contemplate such an end once are likely to do so again.
source_text: “He said at the time that he took them down to clean. He has now owned that he took them because he saw Eustacia looking curiously at them; and she afterwards owned to him that she was thinking of taking her life, but bound him to secrecy, and promised never to think of such a thing again. I hardly suppose she will ever have bravado enough to use one of them; but it shows what has been lurking in her mind; and people who think of that sort of thing once think of it again.”

text: When a path is deeply familiar, the task of staying upon it is not overly difficult, as the feet recognize its unique feel; however, once abandoned, it is impossible to find again. Because her infant obstructed her view and distracted her thoughts, Thomasin eventually lost her way. This occurred as she descended an open hillside, roughly two-thirds of the distance home. Rather than attempting the hopeless search for such a faint trace of a trail, she pressed straight ahead, trusting her profound understanding of the land's shape—a knowledge of the terrain surpassed only by Clym or the wild ponies of the heath.
source_text: If the path is well known the difficulty at such times of keeping therein is not altogether great, from its familiar feel to the feet; but once lost it is irrecoverable. Owing to her baby, who somewhat impeded Thomasin’s view forward and distracted her mind, she did at last lose the track. This mishap occurred when she was descending an open slope about two-thirds home. Instead of attempting, by wandering hither and thither, the hopeless task of finding such a mere thread, she went straight on, trusting for guidance to her general knowledge of the contours, which was scarcely surpassed by Clym’s or by that of the heath-croppers themselves.

text: Finally, silence descended, leaving him with nothing to do but wait. He found it impossible to dispel the heavy gloom that had burdened him since his last encounter with Eustacia, yet he harbored a hope that his circumstances might be mended with money. He had talked himself into the belief that he could be both fair to his unassuming wife, by signing over half his estate to her, and gallantly devoted to a more powerful woman by joining his life to hers. Although he intended to follow Eustacia’s directions precisely—to leave her where she requested and depart if she so ordered—the enchantment she held over him only deepened. His heart raced at the thought that such commands would prove meaningless against their mutual desire to share a single destiny.
source_text: At last all was quiet, and he had nothing to do but to wait. By no effort could he shake off the oppression of spirits which he had experienced ever since his last meeting with Eustacia, but he hoped there was that in his situation which money could cure. He had persuaded himself that to act not ungenerously towards his gentle wife by settling on her the half of his property, and with chivalrous devotion towards another and greater woman by sharing her fate, was possible. And though he meant to adhere to Eustacia’s instructions to the letter, to deposit her where she wished and to leave her, should that be her will, the spell that she had cast over him intensified, and his heart was beating fast in the anticipated futility of such commands in the face of a mutual wish that they should throw in their lot together.

text: Silas hesitated, struck by the suspicion that this stationary coach might be the very instrument of his wife’s disappearance. The appearance of Silas instantly dispelled Julian’s calm, as he once again perceived the man as the mortal adversary from whom Elara must be sequestered by any means. Julian, therefore, maintained a guarded silence, praying that Silas would pass along the road without turning his attention toward the shadows.
source_text: He stopped as if in doubt whether this waiting vehicle could have anything to do with the flight of his wife or not. The sight of Yeobright at once banished Wildeve’s sober feelings, who saw him again as the deadly rival from whom Eustacia was to be kept at all hazards. Hence Wildeve did not speak, in the hope that Clym would pass by without particular inquiry.

text: Wildeve had meanwhile reached the near bank, where the light from Yeobright’s lantern cast a dappled and restless glow over the weir pool. This illumination revealed to the former engineer the churning paths of the currents as they tumbled from the sluice gates above. Across this scarred and distorted mirror of water, a dark shape drifted slowly, carried along by a receding current.
source_text: Wildeve meanwhile had arrived on the former side, and the light from Yeobright’s lamp shed a flecked and agitated radiance across the weir pool, revealing to the ex-engineer the tumbling courses of the currents from the hatches above. Across this gashed and puckered mirror a dark body was slowly borne by one of the backward currents.

text: Thorne could now perceive the drifting shape, however vaguely; and surmising from Sterling’s sudden dive that a rescue remained possible, he nearly threw himself in pursuit. Pausing to adopt a more calculated approach, he steadied his lantern against a piling to keep the light level, then raced toward the downstream edge of the pool where the stone embankment vanished. He leaped into the water and waded resolutely toward the deepening center. There the flow lifted him from his feet, and as he swam, the vortex pulled him toward the middle of the basin, where he saw Sterling flailing against the current.
source_text: Yeobright could now also discern the floating body, though but indistinctly; and imagining from Wildeve’s plunge that there was life to be saved he was about to leap after. Bethinking himself of a wiser plan, he placed the lamp against a post to make it stand upright, and running round to the lower part of the pool, where there was no wall, he sprang in and boldly waded upwards towards the deeper portion. Here he was taken off his legs, and in swimming was carried round into the centre of the basin, where he perceived Wildeve struggling.

text: Upon reaching the shore, they found Thomasin overwhelmed by sorrow, hunched over the two unresponsive figures already lying there. The horse and wagon were brought to the nearest section of the road, and it took only a short time to settle the three victims into the back. Venn led the horse forward, steadying Thomasin with his arm, while the other two men trailed behind until they arrived at the inn.
source_text: When they reached the bank there stood Thomasin, in a stress of grief, bending over the two unconscious ones who already lay there. The horse and cart were brought to the nearest point in the road, and it was the work of a few minutes only to place the three in the vehicle. Venn led on the horse, supporting Thomasin upon his arm, and the two men followed, till they reached the inn.

text: Thorne approached the hearth, his gaze fixated on the dancing fire in a hollow reverie. Wisps of steam curled from his damp gaiters and vanished into the chimney’s throat with the rising smoke, while his thoughts climbed the stairs to those who lay above. Two were now but cold clay; one had been snatched from the very threshold of the tomb; a fourth was broken by fever and mourning. He recalled the previous hour he had spent by this mantle, during the height of the wager; when Sterling moved with vigorous health; Elara was a vision of cheerful industry in the adjoining room; Julian and Isolde had just tasted the first fruits of their union, and the matriarch still held her place at Hearthstone. In that hour, the tapestry of their lives had seemed woven to last for decades. Yet, out of all that gathered circle, he alone stood as he had always stood, the only figure whom the intervening tragedies had failed to transform.
source_text: Venn drew near to the fireplace, and looked into the flames in an absent mood. The steam came from his leggings and ascended the chimney with the smoke, while he thought of those who were upstairs. Two were corpses, one had barely escaped the jaws of death, another was sick and a widow. The last occasion on which he had lingered by that fireplace was when the raffle was in progress; when Wildeve was alive and well; Thomasin active and smiling in the next room; Yeobright and Eustacia just made husband and wife, and Mrs. Yeobright living at Blooms-End. It had seemed at that time that the then position of affairs was good for at least twenty years to come. Yet, of all the circle, he himself was the only one whose situation had not materially changed.

text: For many months, the news of how Eustacia and Wildeve perished echoed across Egdon and into the distant countryside. Every detail of their romance was inflated, twisted, and embellished by local gossip until the actual facts were barely recognizable in the stories being told. Nevertheless, their abrupt passing did not diminish the dignity of either the man or the woman. Tragedy had claimed them with a certain elegance, ending their turbulent lives with a dramatic finality rather than letting them wither away through decades of aging, obscurity, and physical decline.
source_text: The story of the deaths of Eustacia and Wildeve was told throughout Egdon, and far beyond, for many weeks and months. All the known incidents of their love were enlarged, distorted, touched up, and modified, till the original reality bore but a slight resemblance to the counterfeit presentation by surrounding tongues. Yet, upon the whole, neither the man nor the woman lost dignity by sudden death. Misfortune had struck them gracefully, cutting off their erratic histories with a catastrophic dash, instead of, as with many, attenuating each life to an uninteresting meagreness, through long years of wrinkles, neglect, and decay.

text: Spring brought her a sense of peace, and summer offered further relief; by the arrival of autumn, she felt a deep comfort as her young daughter thrived, growing stronger and more aware with each passing day. External circumstances were also remarkably kind to Thomasin. Because Wildeve had died without a will, she and the child were his only legal heirs. After the estate had been processed, all outstanding debts settled, and the remaining assets from her husband’s uncle passed to her, the final sum to be invested for her and the girl’s future was nearly ten thousand pounds.
source_text: The spring came and calmed her; the summer came and soothed her; the autumn arrived, and she began to be comforted, for her little girl was strong and happy, growing in size and knowledge every day. Outward events flattered Thomasin not a little. Wildeve had died intestate, and she and the child were his only relatives. When administration had been granted, all the debts paid, and the residue of her husband’s uncle’s property had come into her hands, it was found that the sum waiting to be invested for her own and the child’s benefit was little less than ten thousand pounds.

text: Consequently, while others’ attempts at comfort proved useless, he discovered a private peace when left to his own devices. For a person of his simple tastes, the house and the annual hundred and twenty pounds inherited from his mother satisfied every material requirement. True wealth is determined not by the total sum possessed, but by the balance between one's expenses and one's income.
source_text: Thus, though words of solace were vainly uttered in his presence, he found relief in a direction of his own choosing when left to himself. For a man of his habits the house and the hundred and twenty pounds a year which he had inherited from his mother were enough to supply all worldly needs. Resources do not depend upon gross amounts, but upon the proportion of spendings to takings.

text: Clym’s curiosity drew him a few steps closer until he could peer through the glass. To his surprise, Diggory Venn stood inside, no longer a crimson-stained reddleman, but possessing the natural, pale complexion of a common man. He wore a clean white shirt, a pale floral waistcoat, a blue-speckled necktie, and a coat of dark bottle-green. There was nothing remarkable about his attire in itself, save for the total transformation it represented from his previous state. Every shade of red had been meticulously purged from his clothing; for those newly freed from their labors often dread nothing more than a reminder of the profession that secured their wealth.
source_text: Clym was curious enough to advance a little further and look in at the window. To his astonishment there stood within the room Diggory Venn, no longer a reddleman, but exhibiting the strangely altered hues of an ordinary Christian countenance, white shirt-front, light flowered waistcoat, blue-spotted neckerchief, and bottle-green coat. Nothing in this appearance was at all singular but the fact of its great difference from what he had formerly been. Red, and all approach to red, was carefully excluded from every article of clothes upon him; for what is there that persons just out of harness dread so much as reminders of the trade which has enriched them?

text: “I’m over at Stickleford—about two miles to the right of Alderworth, ma’am, just where the meadows begin. I’ve been thinking that if Mr. Yeobright ever wanted to drop by for a visit, he shouldn’t feel he has to stay away for lack of an invitation. I won’t stay for tea this afternoon, thank you, as I have some business I need to settle. Tomorrow is May Day, and the people from Shadwater have teamed up with a few of your neighbors to set up a pole just outside your fence on the heath, since it’s such a pleasant grassy spot.” Venn gestured with his elbow toward the patch of ground in front of the house. “I’ve been discussing it with Fairway,” he continued, “and I told him that before we put the pole up, we really ought to ask Mrs. Wildeve’s permission.”
source_text: “At Stickleford—about two miles to the right of Alderworth, ma’am, where the meads begin. I have thought that if Mr. Yeobright would like to pay me a visit sometimes he shouldn’t stay away for want of asking. I’ll not bide to tea this afternoon, thank’ee, for I’ve got something on hand that must be settled. ’Tis Maypole-day tomorrow, and the Shadwater folk have clubbed with a few of your neighbours here to have a pole just outside your palings in the heath, as it is a nice green place.” Venn waved his elbow towards the patch in front of the house. “I have been talking to Fairway about it,” he continued, “and I said to him that before we put up the pole it would be as well to ask Mrs. Wildeve.”

text: Venn departed shortly thereafter, and as evening fell, Yeobright wandered as far as Fairway’s cottage. It was a radiant sunset in May, and the birches lining the edge of the immense Egdon wasteland were cloaked in new leaves, as delicate as butterfly wings and as translucent as amber. Next to Fairway’s house was a clearing tucked away from the road, where the youth from the surrounding miles had gathered. A pole rested with one end upon a trestle, and the women were occupied with winding wildflowers around it from top to bottom. The ancient spirit of a festive England endured here with remarkable strength, and the symbolic rites traditionally tied to the seasons remained a lived reality on Egdon. In truth, the heart of such isolated hamlets is still pagan—in these corners, the veneration of nature, wild revelry, and traces of Teutonic ceremonies to forgotten deities seem to have somehow outlived the influence of medieval creeds.
source_text: Venn soon after went away, and in the evening Yeobright strolled as far as Fairway’s cottage. It was a lovely May sunset, and the birch trees which grew on this margin of the vast Egdon wilderness had put on their new leaves, delicate as butterflies’ wings, and diaphanous as amber. Beside Fairway’s dwelling was an open space recessed from the road, and here were now collected all the young people from within a radius of a couple of miles. The pole lay with one end supported on a trestle, and women were engaged in wreathing it from the top downwards with wild-flowers. The instincts of merry England lingered on here with exceptional vitality, and the symbolic customs which tradition has attached to each season of the year were yet a reality on Egdon. Indeed, the impulses of all such outlandish hamlets are pagan still—in these spots homage to nature, self-adoration, frantic gaieties, fragments of Teutonic rites to divinities whose names are forgotten, seem in some way or other to have survived mediæval doctrine.

text: He found himself so vexed by this latest complication that when the spirited brass ensemble arrived and burst into song—which occurred around the fifth hour, with a collective breath so formidable it threatened to unroof his cottage—he vacated his chambers by the back way, crossed the garden, slipped through the gate in the hawthorn, and escaped into the distance. He could not bring himself to witness such merriment today, though he had labored most sincerely to abide it.
source_text: He was so distressed by this new complexity that when the enthusiastic brass band arrived and struck up, which it did about five o’clock, with apparently wind enough among its members to blow down his house, he withdrew from his rooms by the back door, went down the garden, through the gate in the hedge, and away out of sight. He could not bear to remain in the presence of enjoyment today, though he had tried hard.

text: “What a preposterous display!” Margaret whispered, adopting a note of calculated derision. “To imagine that a fellow could be so witless as to go pining about like a schoolboy for a maiden’s glove! A man of substance, a wealthy orchardist with his pockets full, no less. What a shame!”
source_text: “How very ridiculous!” Thomasin murmured to herself, in a tone which was intended to be satirical. “To think that a man should be so silly as to go mooning about like that for a girl’s glove! A respectable dairyman, too, and a man of money as he is now. What a pity!”

text: The mystery remained unsolved for quite some time. Then, one afternoon, while Thomasin was in her room preparing for a stroll, she stepped out onto the landing to call for Rachel. Rachel was a girl of about thirteen whose job was to take the infant outside for fresh air, and she climbed the stairs as soon as she heard her name.
source_text: No clearing up of the mystery was granted her for some time. But one afternoon Thomasin was upstairs getting ready for a walk, when she had occasion to come to the landing and call “Rachel.” Rachel was a girl about thirteen, who carried the baby out for airings; and she came upstairs at the call.

text: Their exchange had taken place in a dip on the moorland near the ancient Roman road, a spot Thomasin often visited. One might have noticed that her encounter with Venn did nothing to discourage her from walking there in the days that followed. As for whether Venn avoided the place or was drawn back to it because of her, the answer could be easily inferred from Thomasin’s behavior roughly two months later that same year.
source_text: This conversation had passed in a hollow of the heath near the old Roman road, a place much frequented by Thomasin. And it might have been observed that she did not in future walk that way less often from having met Venn there now. Whether or not Venn abstained from riding thither because he had met Thomasin in the same place might easily have been guessed from her proceedings about two months later in the same year.

text: During this interval, Yeobright had frequently reflected on his obligations toward his cousin, Thomasin. He could not help but feel it a tragic inefficiency for such a gentle soul to be condemned, so early in life, to squander her charms amidst the lonely thickets and ferns. Yet he viewed this through the lens of a pragmatist rather than a lover. His former devotion to Eustacia had been the ultimate repository of his heart’s energy, leaving him with no further reserves of that profound passion to bestow. Thus, the logical conclusion was to avoid any prospect of marriage with Thomasin, even if it were meant as a kindness to her.
source_text: Throughout this period Yeobright had more or less pondered on his duty to his cousin Thomasin. He could not help feeling that it would be a pitiful waste of sweet material if the tender-natured thing should be doomed from this early stage of her life onwards to dribble away her winsome qualities on lonely gorse and fern. But he felt this as an economist merely, and not as a lover. His passion for Eustacia had been a sort of conserve of his whole life, and he had nothing more of that supreme quality left to bestow. So far the obvious thing was not to entertain any idea of marriage with Thomasin, even to oblige her.

text: If Clym’s own destiny were the only thing at stake, he would have asked Thomasin to marry him without hesitation. He had nothing to lose by honoring his late mother’s final wish. Yet, he was troubled by the idea of Thomasin being tied to the emotional shell of a man he felt himself to be. Only three interests still flickered within him. One was his nearly daily pilgrimage to the small graveyard where his mother rested; another was his equally regular nighttime visits to the more distant burial ground where Eustacia lay; and the last was his preparation for the only calling that seemed capable of quieting his restless spirit—serving as a wandering preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was hard to imagine that Thomasin would find any comfort in a husband defined by such grim preoccupations.
source_text: Had only Yeobright’s own future been involved he would have proposed to Thomasin with a ready heart. He had nothing to lose by carrying out a dead mother’s hope. But he dreaded to contemplate Thomasin wedded to the mere corpse of a lover that he now felt himself to be. He had but three activities alive in him. One was his almost daily walk to the little graveyard wherein his mother lay; another, his just as frequent visits by night to the more distant enclosure which numbered his Eustacia among its dead; the third was self-preparation for a vocation which alone seemed likely to satisfy his cravings—that of an itinerant preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was difficult to believe that Thomasin would be cheered by a husband with such tendencies as these.

text: The revelation caught me entirely unawares; yet, for all that, I am truly heartened by the intelligence. You have my full blessing, naturally, my sweet Beatrice. I wonder who the gentleman might be? I am quite at a loss for a name. And yet—no—it is the parish surgeon! Not that I intend to name him elderly, for his years are not so very advanced, after all. Truly, I observed a certain tenderness in his manner when he last attended your sickbed!
source_text: “I was taken rather by surprise. But, nevertheless, I am very glad to hear such news. I shall approve, of course, dear Tamsie. Who can it be? I am quite at a loss to guess. No I am not—’tis the old doctor!—not that I mean to call him old, for he is not very old after all. Ah—I noticed when he attended you last time!”

text: “How could I find the moral fortitude to wed, Silas, when the ghosts of two women lie at my door? Put the notion from your mind. After what I have wrought, it would be a grotesque travesty to enter a sanctuary and claim a bride. As Job once swore, I have made a pact with my own eyes; how then could I permit my heart to dwell upon a maiden?”
source_text: “How can I have the conscience to marry after having driven two women to their deaths? Don’t think such a thing, Humphrey. After my experience I should consider it too much of a burlesque to go to church and take a wife. In the words of Job, ‘I have made a covenant with mine eyes; when then should I think upon a maid?’”

text: The author wishes to clarify that the story’s original design never intended for Thomasin and Venn to wed. In the first draft, Venn was to remain a lonely, enigmatic figure, eventually slipping away from the heath to parts unknown, leaving Thomasin to her widowhood. Yet, the demands of publishing in installments forced a revision of this outcome. Readers are thus invited to adopt the ending they find most fitting; those who hold to a stricter aesthetic standard might find the original, more somber conclusion to be the rightful one.
source_text: [*] The writer may state here that the original conception of the story did not design a marriage between Thomasin and Venn. He was to have retained his isolated and weird character to the last, and to have disappeared mysteriously from the heath, nobody knowing whither—Thomasin remaining a widow. But certain circumstances of serial publication led to a change of intent. Readers can therefore choose between the endings, and those with an austere artistic code can assume the more consistent conclusion to be the true one.

text: Yes," the furze-dealer agreed, rising to his feet and thoroughly wiping his brow before passing the beeswax to Humphrey, who immediately took over the rubbing. "Not that the couple is in desperate need of one, but it’s only right to offer a gesture of friendship during such a chaotic transition in their lives. I provided both my daughters with a bed when they wed, and I’ve had enough feathers saved for another for the better part of a year. Now then, neighbors, I think we’ve applied plenty of wax. Grandfer Cantle, flip the ticking right-side out, and I’ll start pouring the feathers in.
source_text: “Yes,” said the furze-dealer, standing up, giving his forehead a thorough mopping, and handing the beeswax to Humphrey, who succeeded at the rubbing forthwith. “Not that this couple be in want of one, but ’twas well to show ’em a bit of friendliness at this great racketing vagary of their lives. I set up both my own daughters in one when they was married, and there have been feathers enough for another in the house the last twelve months. Now then, neighbours, I think we have laid on enough wax. Grandfer Cantle, you turn the tick the right way outwards, and then I’ll begin to shake in the feathers.”

text: “I have never laid eyes on a more leaden-footed creature than you, Barnaby,” Elder Silas remarked with sharp disapproval. “For all the sense you display, you might well be the offspring of some dullard who never ventured past the boundaries of Oakhaven. It appears that all the discipline and worldliness a father gains in the ranks amounts to naught when it comes to shaping the character of his boy. Regarding your particular disposition, I may as well have remained by the hearth and witnessed nothing of the world, just like the lot of you sitting here. Yet, as for my own soul, a bit of gallant fire has certainly left its mark, I can tell you that!”
source_text: “I never saw such a clumsy chap as you, Christian,” said Grandfer Cantle severely. “You might have been the son of a man that’s never been outside Blooms-End in his life for all the wit you have. Really all the soldiering and smartness in the world in the father seems to count for nothing in forming the nater of the son. As far as that chief Christian is concerned I might as well have stayed at home and seed nothing, like all the rest of ye here. Though, as far as myself is concerned, a dashing spirit has counted for sommat, to be sure!”

text: “Truly, my dear, beyond those matters, I would be much relieved if you might spare me. It sounds unfeeling, I know; but, dear Elara, I am certain I should be quite miserable in that throng—that is the plain reality of it. I intend to visit you often at your new dwelling, you see, so that my being away now will be of little account.”
source_text: “Well, dear, apart from that, if you would excuse me, I should be glad. I know it seems unkind; but, dear Thomasin, I fear I should not be happy in the company—there, that’s the truth of it. I shall always be coming to see you at your new home, you know, so that my absence now will not matter.”

text: Whereupon Mr. Grimsby flushed a vivid scarlet; for he was a man of reputed shrewdness who had lately gone bankrupt—to the profound shock of the neighborhood, as there appeared no logical pretext for his ruin—and had succeeded in furnishing the populace with much laughter and his lenders with precious little comfort, by settling his accounts at a rate of less than two cents to the dollar.
source_text: At which Mr. Snell turned red; for he was a very smart man and had just failed,--to everybody's surprise, since there was no reason in the world why he should fail,--and had created more merriment for the public than joy among his creditors, by paying a cent and a half on the dollar.

text: Returning the cigar to his lips, the doctor hugged one knee and stared pensively at a grimy, single-eared bust of Hippocrates looking down from a high bookshelf. He might have been reflecting on the hundreds of pro bono visits he had been graciously allowed to pay Uncle Capen over the course of ten years.
source_text: The Doctor put his cigar back into his mouth, clasped one knee in his hands, and fixed his eyes in meditation on a one-eared Hippocrates looking down with a dirty face from the top of a bookcase. Perhaps the Doctor was thinking of the two or three hundred complimentary visits he had been permitted to make upon Uncle Capen within ten years.

text: He began by recounting his first meeting with Uncle Capen some thirty years earlier, noting that even then, he had viewed him as an elderly man. He recalled telling him at the time that he was already descending into the twilight of his life, with one foot in the grave. The speaker highlighted Uncle Capen’s moral character, attributing his longevity to his virtuous habits. Since he had given up smoking roughly forty years prior, the speaker advised the young people in attendance to avoid tobacco if they hoped to reach such an advanced age. Furthermore, he had been a total abstainer from alcohol since turning seventy; the speaker urged those who wished to rival his lifespan to follow this example. The speaker concluded with a poignant reference to the relatives in the front pew, comparing their grief to that of John the Baptist’s disciples after his beheading. Then, another hymn was sung.
source_text: He began with some reminiscences of the first time he saw Uncle Capen, some thirty years before, and spoke of having viewed him even then as an aged man, and of having remarked to him that he was walking down the valley of life with one foot in the grave. He called attention to Uncle Capen's virtues, and pointed out their connection with his longevity. He had not smoked for some forty years; therefore, if the youth who were present desired to attain his age, let them not smoke. He had been a total abstainer, moreover, from his seventieth year; let them, if they would rival his longevity, follow his example. The good man closed with a feeling allusion to the relatives, in the front pew, mourning like the disciples of John the Baptist after his “beheadment” Another hymn was sung,--

text: “During the great parade in Washington,” he remarked, “that magnificent spectacle which served as the final act of the war, I was merely an observer, sidelined by a bullet wound that prevented me from marching. From a second-story window, I watched the columns pass, ready to achieve their ultimate victory by quietly dissolving back into the populace—a formidable, unstoppable force about to vanish, leaving behind nothing but the occasional one-armed man or a blue uniform jacket following a plow. Frequently now, when I shut my eyes, that scene returns: the brave ranks, the shredded colors, and the thunderous cheers that erupted as my own brigade, wearing their bright red scarves, marched past. Regardless of how small my role was as a simple soldier, no wealth on earth could ever purchase the pride I take in that brotherhood, nor the enduring memory of that grand review.”
source_text: “At the time of the grand review in Washington,” he said, “that mighty pageant that fittingly closed the drama of the war, I was a spectator, crippled then by a gun-shot wound, and unable to march. From an upper window I saw that host file by, about to record its greatest triumph by melting quietly into the general citizenship,--a mighty, resistless army about to fade and leave no trace, except here and there a one-armed man, or a blue flannel jacket behind a plough. Often now, when I close my eyes, that picture rises: that gallant host, those tattered flags; and I hear the shouts that rose when my brigade, with their flaming scarfs, went trooping by. Little as I may have done, as a humble member of that army, no earthly treasure could buy from me the thought of my fellowship with it, or even the memory of that great review.

text: “Observe the magnitude of this upheaval! Let us dwell upon its significance for a heartbeat. What a world of change it portends. Through the refinement of mechanical harvesters and the plummeting cost of the iron road, the sprawling, untamed pastures of the American interior now vie directly with the ancient estates of our own realm to fill the commoner's larder. It appears inevitable that, before this age passes, such pressure will shatter the monopoly of the landed lords, and in doing so, perhaps collapse the very pillars of the nobility.”
source_text: “And what a transition is this! Pause for a moment to consider it. How much does this imply. With the late improvements in agricultural machinery, with the cheapening of steel rails, the boundless prairie farms of the West are now brought into competition with the fields of Great Britain in supplying the Englishman's table, and seem not unlikely, within this generation, to break down the aristocratic holding of land, and so perhaps to undermine aristocracy itself.”

text: True," Apollos said, "that’s another point. I once received a message from my son who lives in Taunton. We were all so terrified and faint when we saw it that none of us dared to open it, and eventually the man who had driven it over had to open it for us.
source_text: “Trew,” said Apollos, “that's another thing. I hed a message come once-t from my son that lives to Taunton. We was all so sca't and faint when we see it, that we did n't none of us dast to open it, and finally the feller that druv over with it hed to open it fur us.”

text: Section IV.I: Of Restitution and Substitution. Should any corruption be revealed within this digital composition during the ninety days following its receipt, the recipient may seek a full recovery of such sums as were tendered. To do so, one must dispatch a formal missive detailing the grievance to the original purveyor. If the work was conveyed upon a physical vessel, said vessel must be surrendered in tandem with the petition. It remains the prerogative of the provider to furnish a flawless replacement in preference to a monetary return. In the case of electronic delivery, a secondary transmission may be offered. Yet, should this second endeavor also yield a defective result, the recipient retains the right to demand a final refund through written notice, thereby concluding all efforts at rectification.
source_text: 1.F.3. LIMITED RIGHT OF REPLACEMENT OR REFUND - If you discover a defect in this electronic work within 90 days of receiving it, you can receive a refund of the money (if any) you paid for it by sending a written explanation to the person you received the work from. If you received the work on a physical medium, you must return the medium with your written explanation. The person or entity that provided you with the defective work may elect to provide a replacement copy in lieu of a refund. If you received the work electronically, the person or entity providing it to you may choose to give you a second opportunity to receive the work electronically in lieu of a refund. If the second copy is also defective, you may demand a refund in writing without further opportunities to fix the problem.

text: As he dismissed her gratitude with a clumsy gesture and a few swallowed words, Elias obeyed her silent instruction to sit. He paused to marvel at the effortless fluidity of her movements before lurching toward a seat across from her, suddenly and painfully aware of his own bulk. This sensation was entirely novel. Throughout his years, he had never once considered whether he moved with elegance or ineptitude; such vanity had never breached his consciousness. He lowered himself tentatively onto the cushion's edge, tormented by the sudden prominence of his hands. No matter where he rested them, they felt intrusive. Julian was withdrawing from the study, and Elias tracked his retreat with a look of profound longing. He felt abandoned, cast away in this room with a woman who seemed more a ghost than a person. There was no publican here to provide a dram, no lad to fetch a flagon of porter—none of the common spirits that typically washed away the friction of a first meeting.
source_text: While he waved his hand and muttered that he had done nothing at all, he was obeying her behest by trying to get into a chair. He found time to admire the ease with which she sat down, then lurched toward a chair facing her, overwhelmed with consciousness of the awkward figure he was cutting. This was a new experience for him. All his life, up to then, he had been unaware of being either graceful or awkward. Such thoughts of self had never entered his mind. He sat down gingerly on the edge of the chair, greatly worried by his hands. They were in the way wherever he put them. Arthur was leaving the room, and Martin Eden followed his exit with longing eyes. He felt lost, alone there in the room with that pale spirit of a woman. There was no bar-keeper upon whom to call for drinks, no small boy to send around the corner for a can of beer and by means of that social fluid start the amenities of friendship flowing.

text: A tremor of shock hit him, and a faint flush of shame colored his weather-beaten face, though in his own mind it burned with the intensity of an open furnace door in the engine room. Crude matters like knife fights were clearly unsuitable topics for a lady’s ears. The refined people in novels, those from her station in life, did not discuss such brutality—perhaps they were entirely unaware that such things even existed.
source_text: He felt a shock himself, and a blush of embarrassment shone faintly on his sunburned cheeks, though to him it burned as hotly as when his cheeks had been exposed to the open furnace-door in the fire-room. Such sordid things as stabbing affrays were evidently not fit subjects for conversation with a lady. People in the books, in her walk of life, did not talk about such things—perhaps they did not know about them, either.

text: The dialogue faltered, leaving a hollow space in the air between them. With a flicker of uncertainty, she inquired about the thin, silver line marking his temple. He sensed then her struggle to inhabit his rugged dialect, and he decided instantly to discard his own speech and follow her into the softer terrain of hers.
source_text: There was a brief pause in the conversation they were trying to get started. Then she asked tentatively about the scar on his cheek. Even as she asked, he realized that she was making an effort to talk his talk, and he resolved to get away from it and talk hers.

text: “You were explaining why this fellow Swinburne didn't quite achieve greatness as a poet because—and that’s where you left off, miss,” he reminded her. As he spoke, he felt a sudden, sharp hunger within him, and the sound of her laughter sent exquisite shivers racing up and down his spine. It was like silver, he mused, like the delicate chiming of silver bells; and in an instant, he was whisked away to a distant land where, beneath a canopy of pink cherry blossoms, he smoked a cigarette and listened to the pagoda bells summoning straw-shod worshippers to prayer.
source_text: “You was saying that this man Swinburne failed bein’ a great poet because—an’ that was as far as you got, miss,” he prompted, while to himself he seemed suddenly hungry, and delicious little thrills crawled up and down his spine at the sound of her laughter. Like silver, he thought to himself, like tinkling silver bells; and on the instant, and for an instant, he was transported to a far land, where under pink cherry blossoms, he smoked a cigarette and listened to the bells of the peaked pagoda calling straw-sandalled devotees to worship.

text: “I’m certainly no weakling,” he remarked. “In the ordinary way of things, I have a stomach like iron. But at the moment, I’m suffering from a kind of mental indigestion; I simply can’t process most of what you’ve been saying. I was never trained for it, you see. I’ve always enjoyed books and poetry, and I’ve read what I could in my spare time, but I’ve never thought about them the way you do. That is why I’m unable to discuss them. I feel like a navigator lost on an unknown sea, lacking both a chart and a compass. Now, I want to find my bearings. Perhaps you can guide me. How did you come to learn all these things you’ve been explaining?”
source_text: “Yes, I ain’t no invalid,” he said. “When it comes down to hard-pan, I can digest scrap-iron. But just now I’ve got dyspepsia. Most of what you was sayin’ I can’t digest. Never trained that way, you see. I like books and poetry, and what time I’ve had I’ve read ’em, but I’ve never thought about ’em the way you have. That’s why I can’t talk about ’em. I’m like a navigator adrift on a strange sea without chart or compass. Now I want to get my bearin’s. Mebbe you can put me right. How did you learn all this you’ve ben talkin’?”

text: An instant later, vexed by his own arrogance, he clamped his hands onto the chair’s frame so violently that the nerves in his fingertips throbbed. In that same breath, he perceived a lady entering the parlor. He observed the girl rise and dart across the carpet to meet the newcomer. Embracing, they drifted toward him with their arms linked. This must be the mother, he concluded. She was a willow-thin blonde, imposing and refined, possessing a regal beauty. Her attire was exactly what he expected in such a residence, and his eyes traced the fluid movement of the fabric. The woman and her dress combined to remind him of actresses on a stage. Then came the memory of similar luminaries stepping into the London theaters while he stood watching in the rain, pushed back by the police into the damp gloom beyond the lights. His mind then vaulted to the Grand Hotel in Yokohama where, from the common dirt of the street, he had watched such noblewomen pass. A flurry of images—the city, the docks, the harbor of Japan—began to swirl before him. But he shoved the montage away, gripped by the necessity of the moment. He knew he must rise to be presented, and he labored to his feet, standing with his woolens sagging at the knees and his arms hanging with a clumsy weight, his jaw set for the coming trial.
source_text: The next moment, angry with himself for the boast, he had gripped the arms of the chair so savagely that every finger-end was stinging. At the same moment he became aware that a woman was entering the room. He saw the girl leave her chair and trip swiftly across the floor to the newcomer. They kissed each other, and, with arms around each other’s waists, they advanced toward him. That must be her mother, he thought. She was a tall, blond woman, slender, and stately, and beautiful. Her gown was what he might expect in such a house. His eyes delighted in the graceful lines of it. She and her dress together reminded him of women on the stage. Then he remembered seeing similar grand ladies and gowns entering the London theatres while he stood and watched and the policemen shoved him back into the drizzle beyond the awning. Next his mind leaped to the Grand Hotel at Yokohama, where, too, from the sidewalk, he had seen grand ladies. Then the city and the harbor of Yokohama, in a thousand pictures, began flashing before his eyes. But he swiftly dismissed the kaleidoscope of memory, oppressed by the urgent need of the present. He knew that he must stand up to be introduced, and he struggled painfully to his feet, where he stood with trousers bagging at the knees, his arms loose-hanging and ludicrous, his face set hard for the impending ordeal.

text: Though he defied Arthur’s characterization by appearing as a mild creature rather than a savage, his mind was racing to determine his next move. He was no submissive soul, and a subordinate role could never satisfy his inherently commanding temperament. He spoke only when forced, his dialogue mirroring his awkward approach to the table—halting and uneven as he searched a fragmented vocabulary. He hesitated over accurate words he feared he might mispronounce and discarded others he thought would be too coarse or unintelligible. All the while, he was burdened by the sense that this linguistic caution made him appear a simpleton, preventing him from conveying his inner depth. His inherent love of liberty rebelled against these social constraints just as his neck strained against the stiff, starched shackle of his collar. He knew he could not sustain the act. Possessing a mind of great power and sensitivity, his creative drive was both impatient and insistent. Whenever a thought or feeling surged within him, struggling to find shape and voice, he lost all sense of his surroundings, and his original manner of speech—the only tools he truly mastered—spilled out unchecked.
