source_text: But while he belied Arthur’s description, and appeared a gentle lamb rather than a wild man, he was racking his brains for a course of action. He was no gentle lamb, and the part of second fiddle would never do for the high-pitched dominance of his nature. He talked only when he had to, and then his speech was like his walk to the table, filled with jerks and halts as he groped in his polyglot vocabulary for words, debating over words he knew were fit but which he feared he could not pronounce, rejecting other words he knew would not be understood or would be raw and harsh. But all the time he was oppressed by the consciousness that this carefulness of diction was making a booby of him, preventing him from expressing what he had in him. Also, his love of freedom chafed against the restriction in much the same way his neck chafed against the starched fetter of a collar. Besides, he was confident that he could not keep it up. He was by nature powerful of thought and sensibility, and the creative spirit was restive and urgent. He was swiftly mastered by the concept or sensation in him that struggled in birth-throes to receive expression and form, and then he forgot himself and where he was, and the old words—the tools of speech he knew—slipped out.

text: He functioned as a stringed instrument; the sum of his experiences and his very awareness were the wires, while the surging music was a gale that struck them, stirring up a resonance of old recollections and new fantasies. He did more than just feel. Every sensation manifested as shape, pigment, and light, and whatever his mind envisioned was transformed into something tangible and ethereal. The boundaries of time dissolved as he drifted across a vast, glowing world, pursuing grand exploits and virtuous triumphs until he reached her—and then, with his arm around her, they traversed the majestic reaches of his inner kingdom together.
source_text: He was a harp; all life that he had known and that was his consciousness was the strings; and the flood of music was a wind that poured against those strings and set them vibrating with memories and dreams. He did not merely feel. Sensation invested itself in form and color and radiance, and what his imagination dared, it objectified in some sublimated and magic way. Past, present, and future mingled; and he went on oscillating across the broad, warm world, through high adventure and noble deeds to Her—ay, and with her, winning her, his arm about her, and carrying her on in flight through the empery of his mind.

text: He reached for the handle and stepped into the brightly lit room, where his sister sat with Bernard Higginbotham. She was mending a pair of his pants, while his thin frame was sprawled across two chairs, his feet hanging over the edge of the second in tattered carpet slippers. He peered over the top of the newspaper he was reading, revealing a pair of dark, dishonest, and piercing eyes. Martin Eden never looked at him without feeling a profound sense of loathing. Whatever his sister had seen in the man was entirely beyond his understanding. The man affected him like a piece of vermin, always triggering a sudden urge to crush him underfoot. “One of these days I’ll beat his face in,” was the silent promise he used to endure the man’s presence. Those eyes, predatory and cruel as a weasel’s, glared back at him with a look of grievance.
source_text: He fumbled for the knob and entered a lighted room, where sat his sister and Bernard Higginbotham. She was patching a pair of his trousers, while his lean body was distributed over two chairs, his feet dangling in dilapidated carpet-slippers over the edge of the second chair. He glanced across the top of the paper he was reading, showing a pair of dark, insincere, sharp-staring eyes. Martin Eden never looked at him without experiencing a sense of repulsion. What his sister had seen in the man was beyond him. The other affected him as so much vermin, and always aroused in him an impulse to crush him under his foot. “Some day I’ll beat the face off of him,” was the way he often consoled himself for enduring the man’s existence. The eyes, weasel-like and cruel, were looking at him complainingly.

text: His skin still prickling from the recent encounter with his brother-in-law, Martin Eden navigated the darkened rear corridor by touch. He entered his quarters—a cramped nook barely large enough to hold a bed, a washstand, and a single chair. Mr. Higginbotham’s parsimony was such that he refused to hire a servant while his wife could manage the labor; moreover, utilizing the maid’s room permitted them to house two lodgers rather than one. Martin set his volumes of Swinburne and Browning on the chair, discarded his coat, and sat on the bed. The springs let out a wheezing, metallic protest beneath his weight, but he remained oblivious. He began to unfasten his shoes, only to find himself staring at the opposite wall of white plaster, which was scarred by muddy brown streaks where rain had penetrated the roof. Upon this grimy backdrop, luminous visions began to shift and flicker. Neglecting his shoes, he gazed intently until his lips finally parted to whisper, “Ruth.”
source_text: Martin Eden, with blood still crawling from contact with his brother-in-law, felt his way along the unlighted back hall and entered his room, a tiny cubbyhole with space for a bed, a wash-stand, and one chair. Mr. Higginbotham was too thrifty to keep a servant when his wife could do the work. Besides, the servant’s room enabled them to take in two boarders instead of one. Martin placed the Swinburne and Browning on the chair, took off his coat, and sat down on the bed. A screeching of asthmatic springs greeted the weight of his body, but he did not notice them. He started to take off his shoes, but fell to staring at the white plaster wall opposite him, broken by long streaks of dirty brown where rain had leaked through the roof. On this befouled background visions began to flow and burn. He forgot his shoes and stared long, till his lips began to move and he murmured, “Ruth.”

text: He lifted his hand, rubbing his thumb across a palm toughened by labor and staring at the grime ground so deep into the skin that no brush could ever reach it. How different her hand had been! The memory sent a sharp thrill through him. It was like a rose petal, he thought—as cool and delicate as a snowflake. He had never imagined a woman’s hand could possess such incredible softness. He caught himself dreaming of what it would feel like to be caressed by her, then flushed with a sense of shame. Such a physical thought felt like an insult to her. To him, she was a pale, graceful spirit, existing on a plane far above the mundane flesh; yet the memory of her velvet palm lingered in his mind. He was used to the rough, sandpaper skin of factory girls and working women. He knew well enough why their hands were ruined, but hers... her hand was soft simply because she had never been forced to use it. A chasm opened between them at the staggering thought of a person who didn't have to work for a living. Suddenly, the reality of a leisure class towered over him like a bronze monument, arrogant and immense. He had worked for as long as he could remember, and his entire family had done the same. There was Gertrude; when her hands weren't hardened by endless chores, they were swollen and red like boiled beef from the laundry. And there was his sister Marian. She had worked at the cannery the previous summer, her slim hands now webbed with scars from tomato knives. Before that, she had lost the tips of two fingers to a cutting machine at the paper-box factory. He remembered the rigid, calloused palms of his mother as she lay in her coffin. Even his father had labored until his very last breath; the thick, leathery crusts on his hands must have been half an inch deep when he died. But her hands were soft—and so were her mother’s and her brothers’. That last realization stunned him; it was a powerful testament to the height of their social standing and the vast, unreachable distance that lay between her world and his own.
source_text: He held up his hand, rubbing the ball of the thumb over the calloused palm and gazing at the dirt that was ingrained in the flesh itself and which no brush could scrub away. How different was her palm! He thrilled deliciously at the remembrance. Like a rose-petal, he thought; cool and soft as a snowflake. He had never thought that a mere woman’s hand could be so sweetly soft. He caught himself imagining the wonder of a caress from such a hand, and flushed guiltily. It was too gross a thought for her. In ways it seemed to impugn her high spirituality. She was a pale, slender spirit, exalted far beyond the flesh; but nevertheless the softness of her palm persisted in his thoughts. He was used to the harsh callousness of factory girls and working women. Well he knew why their hands were rough; but this hand of hers . . . It was soft because she had never used it to work with. The gulf yawned between her and him at the awesome thought of a person who did not have to work for a living. He suddenly saw the aristocracy of the people who did not labor. It towered before him on the wall, a figure in brass, arrogant and powerful. He had worked himself; his first memories seemed connected with work, and all his family had worked. There was Gertrude. When her hands were not hard from the endless housework, they were swollen and red like boiled beef, what of the washing. And there was his sister Marian. She had worked in the cannery the preceding summer, and her slim, pretty hands were all scarred with the tomato-knives. Besides, the tips of two of her fingers had been left in the cutting machine at the paper-box factory the preceding winter. He remembered the hard palms of his mother as she lay in her coffin. And his father had worked to the last fading gasp; the horned growth on his hands must have been half an inch thick when he died. But Her hands were soft, and her mother’s hands, and her brothers’. This last came to him as a surprise; it was tremendously indicative of the highness of their caste, of the enormous distance that stretched between her and him.

text: He leaned back against the bed with a harsh, mocking laugh, finishing with his shoes. He felt like a fool, intoxicated by the mere sight of a woman’s features and the elegance of her soft, pale hands. Then, without warning, a vision materialized against the stained plaster of the wall. He found himself standing before a bleak tenement in the East End of London. It was night, and there stood Margey, a fifteen-year-old factory worker. He had escorted her home after the festivities, back to that miserable dwelling that was hardly fit for livestock. As he reached out to say goodnight, she offered her lips to be kissed, though he had initially resisted. A strange fear of her had held him back. But then her hand tightened around his in a feverish grip; he felt the rough scrape of her callouses against his skin, and a surge of deep compassion overcame him. He looked at her longing, hollow eyes and her stunted frame, which had been hurried from girlhood into a desperate, defensive maturity. Moved by a vast tolerance, he drew her into his arms and bent to kiss her. Her small cry of joy still resonated in his memory, and he could still feel her clutching at him like a cat. The poor, hungry thing! He stared at the phantom of that long-ago moment, his skin prickling just as it had then, while his heart swelled with pity. The scene was somber and slick, with a greasy rain falling upon the dark pavement. Then, a brilliant light broke across the wall, and rising through the old memory to replace it, Her face appeared—pale beneath a crown of golden hair, as far away and unreachable as a star.
source_text: He sat back on the bed with a bitter laugh, and finished taking off his shoes. He was a fool; he had been made drunken by a woman’s face and by a woman’s soft, white hands. And then, suddenly, before his eyes, on the foul plaster-wall appeared a vision. He stood in front of a gloomy tenement house. It was night-time, in the East End of London, and before him stood Margey, a little factory girl of fifteen. He had seen her home after the bean-feast. She lived in that gloomy tenement, a place not fit for swine. His hand was going out to hers as he said good night. She had put her lips up to be kissed, but he wasn’t going to kiss her. Somehow he was afraid of her. And then her hand closed on his and pressed feverishly. He felt her callouses grind and grate on his, and a great wave of pity welled over him. He saw her yearning, hungry eyes, and her ill-fed female form which had been rushed from childhood into a frightened and ferocious maturity; then he put his arms about her in large tolerance and stooped and kissed her on the lips. Her glad little cry rang in his ears, and he felt her clinging to him like a cat. Poor little starveling! He continued to stare at the vision of what had happened in the long ago. His flesh was crawling as it had crawled that night when she clung to him, and his heart was warm with pity. It was a gray scene, greasy gray, and the rain drizzled greasily on the pavement stones. And then a radiant glory shone on the wall, and up through the other vision, displacing it, glimmered Her pale face under its crown of golden hair, remote and inaccessible as a star.

text: Julian emerged from the amber remnants of sleep into a stifling mist that reeked of lye and sodden wool, a space vibrating with the discordant clamor of a desperate existence. Crossing the threshold, he encountered the rhythmic splashing of water and a sudden, sharp rebuke, punctuated by the heavy thud of a palm against skin as his sister discharged her resentment upon a whimpering child. The boy’s subsequent shriek lanced through him like a jagged blade. He felt a profound revulsion for the entire spectacle; the very air he inhaled seemed thick with a base and grinding misery. His mind drifted toward the serene elegance of the manor where Beatrice lived. There, life was an affair of the spirit, whereas here, it was a wretchedly corporeal struggle, devoid of any higher grace.
source_text: He awoke next morning from rosy scenes of dream to a steamy atmosphere that smelled of soapsuds and dirty clothes, and that was vibrant with the jar and jangle of tormented life. As he came out of his room he heard the slosh of water, a sharp exclamation, and a resounding smack as his sister visited her irritation upon one of her numerous progeny. The squall of the child went through him like a knife. He was aware that the whole thing, the very air he breathed, was repulsive and mean. How different, he thought, from the atmosphere of beauty and repose of the house wherein Ruth dwelt. There it was all spiritual. Here it was all material, and meanly material.

text: Alfred, come over here," he called to the weeping boy, reaching into his pocket as he spoke. He carried his money loose and uncounted, reflecting the same expansive, carefree manner with which he approached life. He pressed a quarter into the child’s hand and pulled him into a brief embrace to quiet his sobs. "Go on now and buy some candy, and make sure you share with your siblings. Be certain to get the kind that lasts the longest.
source_text: “Come here, Alfred,” he called to the crying child, at the same time thrusting his hand into his trousers pocket, where he carried his money loose in the same large way that he lived life in general. He put a quarter in the youngster’s hand and held him in his arms a moment, soothing his sobs. “Now run along and get some candy, and don’t forget to give some to your brothers and sisters. Be sure and get the kind that lasts longest.”

text: With a quick pinch, she slicked the trailing suds from her sodden forearms. He locked his hands around her broad waist and pressed a kiss to her humid, vapor-warmed lips. Tears pooled in her eyes, rising not from a surge of affection but from the profound fatigue of a body broken by toil. She shoved him back, but not before he glimpsed the wetness shimmering in her gaze.
source_text: With thumb and forefinger she swept the dripping suds first from one arm and then from the other. He put his arms round her massive waist and kissed her wet steamy lips. The tears welled into her eyes—not so much from strength of feeling as from the weakness of chronic overwork. She shoved him away from her, but not before he caught a glimpse of her moist eyes.

text:
He descended the narrow stairwell and stepped into the open air, drawing deep, desperate lungfuls of the evening. The stagnant atmosphere inside had begun to choke him, and Arthur’s relentless, vapid prattle had pushed his nerves to a jagged edge. More than once, he had felt a violent impulse to seize the boy by the scruff and bury his grinning face in the bowl of cold porridge. Every mindless syllable Arthur uttered seemed to widen the gulf between himself and Elena. How could a man mired in such common company ever hope to stand beside her?

The sheer scale of his predicament struck him with a heavy dread, the leaden weight of his birthright pinning him to the earth. It was as if every thread of his life—his sister’s cramped parlor, her noisy brood, the apprentice’s idiocy, and every familiar face—conspired to drag him back into the mud. The very flavor of his life had turned to ash. Until this moment, he had moved through his days with a blunt acceptance, viewing his lot as natural and sufficient. Doubts had only ever flickered when he retreated into novels, but those had been mere fables—glimpses of a porcelain reality that could never truly exist. Now, however, he had glimpsed that world in the flesh, a tangible paradise with Elena at its radiant heart. From this night on, his bread would taste of bile, his desires would cut like glass, and he would be haunted by a despair that was all the more cruel for its refusal to let go of hope.
source_text: He went downstairs and out into the street, breathing great breaths of air. He had been suffocating in that atmosphere, while the apprentice’s chatter had driven him frantic. There had been times when it was all he could do to refrain from reaching over and mopping Jim’s face in the mush-plate. The more he had chattered, the more remote had Ruth seemed to him. How could he, herding with such cattle, ever become worthy of her? He was appalled at the problem confronting him, weighted down by the incubus of his working-class station. Everything reached out to hold him down—his sister, his sister’s house and family, Jim the apprentice, everybody he knew, every tie of life. Existence did not taste good in his mouth. Up to then he had accepted existence, as he had lived it with all about him, as a good thing. He had never questioned it, except when he read books; but then, they were only books, fairy stories of a fairer and impossible world. But now he had seen that world, possible and real, with a flower of a woman called Ruth in the midmost centre of it; and thenceforth he must know bitter tastes, and longings sharp as pain, and hopelessness that tantalized because it fed on hope.

text: He had weighed the merits of the Berkeley and Oakland public libraries, ultimately choosing the latter because it was Ruth’s town. It seemed a plausible place to encounter her, and the hope of a chance meeting drew him there. Unfamiliar with the layout of such buildings, he drifted aimlessly through the fiction stacks until a slender, sharp-featured woman—who reminded him of a French girl—informed him that the reference materials were located on the upper floor. Too inexperienced to seek guidance from the desk clerk, he started his journey in the philosophy section. While he had heard of the subject, he never imagined so many words had been devoted to it. The massive, crowded shelves filled him with a sense of inadequacy, yet they also sparked a fierce intellectual curiosity. Here was a task that would test his mental strength. In the math department, he leafed through trigonometry textbooks, gazing at equations that looked like a foreign code. He could read his own language, but this was a different dialect entirely—one that Norman and Arthur mastered with ease. They were her brothers, and they belonged here. He fled the alcove in a fit of hopelessness, feeling the weight of the surrounding volumes pressing in on him from every direction.
source_text: He had debated between the Berkeley Free Library and the Oakland Free Library, and decided upon the latter because Ruth lived in Oakland. Who could tell?—a library was a most likely place for her, and he might see her there. He did not know the way of libraries, and he wandered through endless rows of fiction, till the delicate-featured French-looking girl who seemed in charge, told him that the reference department was upstairs. He did not know enough to ask the man at the desk, and began his adventures in the philosophy alcove. He had heard of book philosophy, but had not imagined there had been so much written about it. The high, bulging shelves of heavy tomes humbled him and at the same time stimulated him. Here was work for the vigor of his brain. He found books on trigonometry in the mathematics section, and ran the pages, and stared at the meaningless formulas and figures. He could read English, but he saw there an alien speech. Norman and Arthur knew that speech. He had heard them talking it. And they were her brothers. He left the alcove in despair. From every side the books seemed to press upon him and crush him.

text: A gnawing restlessness, much like a physical hunger, took hold of Martin Eden. He starved for a glimpse of the girl whose delicate hands had seized his life with a giant’s strength. He lacked the resolve to visit her, fearing he might arrive too soon and violate the formidable rules of etiquette. Instead, he spent long hours in the Oakland and Berkeley libraries, filling out membership forms for himself, his sisters Gertrude and Marian, and Jim—whose permission he bought with several rounds of beer. With four cards to borrow books, he burned the gaslight late into the night in his small room, for which Mr. Higginbotham charged him a weekly fee of fifty cents.
source_text: A terrible restlessness that was akin to hunger afflicted Martin Eden. He was famished for a sight of the girl whose slender hands had gripped his life with a giant’s grasp. He could not steel himself to call upon her. He was afraid that he might call too soon, and so be guilty of an awful breach of that awful thing called etiquette. He spent long hours in the Oakland and Berkeley libraries, and made out application blanks for membership for himself, his sisters Gertrude and Marian, and Jim, the latter’s consent being obtained at the expense of several glasses of beer. With four cards permitting him to draw books, he burned the gas late in the servant’s room, and was charged fifty cents a week for it by Mr. Higginbotham.

text: The mountain of literature he consumed served only to sharpen his inner turbulence. Each leaf of every volume offered a narrow aperture into the vast territory of human thought. His intellectual appetite gorged itself upon these pages, yet it grew only more ravenous with every chapter. Furthermore, he was adrift without a starting point, perpetually hampered by his lack of formal grounding. Basic allusions, which seemed transparent to any educated mind, remained frustratingly opaque to him. This ignorance extended even to the verses he devoured, which filled him with a frantic, wild joy. He sought out more of Dowson than the thin collection Clara had provided; "Cynara" spoke to him with a visceral clarity. Surely Clara could not grasp its true weight, he reasoned. How could a woman of her sheltered elegance fathom such dark depths? Soon he discovered the ballads of Kipling, and was intoxicated by the rhythmic pulse and raw allure they cast over the mundane world. He marvelled at the writer's kinship with the human condition and his piercing psychological insight. *Psychology*—the word itself was a fresh addition to Julian's burgeoning lexicon. He had invested in a heavy dictionary, a purchase that depleted his meager funds and hastened the hour of his return to the sea to earn more. This expenditure galled Mr. Sterling, who would have much preferred to see that silver applied toward the weekly rent.
source_text: The many books he read but served to whet his unrest. Every page of every book was a peep-hole into the realm of knowledge. His hunger fed upon what he read, and increased. Also, he did not know where to begin, and continually suffered from lack of preparation. The commonest references, that he could see plainly every reader was expected to know, he did not know. And the same was true of the poetry he read which maddened him with delight. He read more of Swinburne than was contained in the volume Ruth had lent him; and “Dolores” he understood thoroughly. But surely Ruth did not understand it, he concluded. How could she, living the refined life she did? Then he chanced upon Kipling’s poems, and was swept away by the lilt and swing and glamour with which familiar things had been invested. He was amazed at the man’s sympathy with life and at his incisive psychology. _Psychology_ was a new word in Martin’s vocabulary. He had bought a dictionary, which deed had decreased his supply of money and brought nearer the day on which he must sail in search of more. Also, it incensed Mr. Higginbotham, who would have preferred the money taking the form of board.

text: He did not venture near Ruth’s neighborhood during the day, but at night he lingered around the Morse house like a prowler, watching the windows and feeling a sense of reverence for the very walls that protected her. He narrowly avoided being spotted by her brothers on several occasions, and once he even followed Mr. Morse into the city, studying the man’s face beneath the streetlamps while secretly hoping for some fatal accident to occur so that he might rush in and save her father’s life. One night, his persistence was rewarded when he caught a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, her arms lifted as she styled her hair in front of a mirror. It lasted only a moment, yet for him, that moment stretched on as his blood turned to wine and raced through his heart. Then she lowered the shade. Now that he knew which room was hers, he returned often, hiding beneath a dark tree on the far side of the street and smoking one cigarette after another. One afternoon, he happened to see her mother leaving a bank, which served as another reminder of the vast social gulf between them. She belonged to the class of people who used banks; he had never once entered such a building, believing they were reserved solely for the most affluent and powerful members of society.
source_text: He dared not go near Ruth’s neighborhood in the daytime, but night found him lurking like a thief around the Morse home, stealing glimpses at the windows and loving the very walls that sheltered her. Several times he barely escaped being caught by her brothers, and once he trailed Mr. Morse down town and studied his face in the lighted streets, longing all the while for some quick danger of death to threaten so that he might spring in and save her father. On another night, his vigil was rewarded by a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, and her arms raised as she fixed her hair before a mirror. It was only for a moment, but it was a long moment to him, during which his blood turned to wine and sang through his veins. Then she pulled down the shade. But it was her room—he had learned that; and thereafter he strayed there often, hiding under a dark tree on the opposite side of the street and smoking countless cigarettes. One afternoon he saw her mother coming out of a bank, and received another proof of the enormous distance that separated Ruth from him. She was of the class that dealt with banks. He had never been inside a bank in his life, and he had an idea that such institutions were frequented only by the very rich and the very powerful.

text: In a sense, a profound spiritual transformation had taken hold of him. Her inherent grace and refinement had left their mark, instilling in his soul a desperate craving for personal cleanliness. He felt that only by purging his own grime could he ever be fit to breathe the same air as she did. He began scouring his teeth and scrubbing his skin with a coarse kitchen brush, at least until he spotted a proper nail-brush in a pharmacy window and realized its purpose. Upon buying it, a clerk’s glance at his hands prompted him to acquire a nail-file as well. After discovering a library book on physical health, he adopted a habit of taking cold baths every morning, a move that stunned Jim and baffled Mr. Higginbotham. The latter, resenting such modern eccentricities, even considered charging Martin extra for the water he consumed. His evolution extended to his attire as well; he had become acutely aware of the contrast between the shapeless, baggy trousers of the laboring class and the sharp, vertical creases worn by the men of the world above. Determined to bridge the gap, he commandeered his sister’s ironing board. His initial attempts were clumsy, resulting in a scorched pair of pants that had to be replaced. This unexpected cost only served to shorten the time he had left before he would be forced back to a life at sea.
source_text: In one way, he had undergone a moral revolution. Her cleanness and purity had reacted upon him, and he felt in his being a crying need to be clean. He must be that if he were ever to be worthy of breathing the same air with her. He washed his teeth, and scrubbed his hands with a kitchen scrub-brush till he saw a nail-brush in a drug-store window and divined its use. While purchasing it, the clerk glanced at his nails, suggested a nail-file, and so he became possessed of an additional toilet-tool. He ran across a book in the library on the care of the body, and promptly developed a penchant for a cold-water bath every morning, much to the amazement of Jim, and to the bewilderment of Mr. Higginbotham, who was not in sympathy with such high-fangled notions and who seriously debated whether or not he should charge Martin extra for the water. Another stride was in the direction of creased trousers. Now that Martin was aroused in such matters, he swiftly noted the difference between the baggy knees of the trousers worn by the working class and the straight line from knee to foot of those worn by the men above the working class. Also, he learned the reason why, and invaded his sister’s kitchen in search of irons and ironing-board. He had misadventures at first, hopelessly burning one pair and buying another, which expenditure again brought nearer the day on which he must put to sea.

text: He stopped with a chuckle and turned back toward them. Looking past their shoulders, he could see the crowd drifting beneath the glow of the streetlights. In the dimness where he stood, he could watch for her without being noticed. He was certain she would appear, as this was the path she always took home.
source_text: He halted with a laugh, and turned, facing them. Across their shoulders he could see the moving throng passing under the street lamps. Where he stood it was not so light, and, unseen, he would be able to see Her as she passed by. She would certainly pass by, for that way led home.

text: Seven days of relentless reading had slipped by since he first met Ruth Morse, yet he still could not summon the courage to visit her. Time after time he steeled himself to make the call, but his resolve always crumbled beneath the weight of his own insecurity. He was ignorant of the proper social hours and had no one to advise him, leaving him terrified of making a catastrophic mistake. Having abandoned his old friends and habits without finding new ones, he had nothing to do but read; the endless hours he spent immersed in text would have blinded a dozen men of lesser stamina. Yet his eyes remained clear, sustained by a body of exceptional strength. His mind, too, was like a field left fallow. It had remained untouched by abstract philosophy throughout his life and was now ready for the seed. Never having been dulled by formal education, his intellect clamped onto the wisdom of books with a predatory, unshakable grip.
source_text: A week of heavy reading had passed since the evening he first met Ruth Morse, and still he dared not call. Time and again he nerved himself up to call, but under the doubts that assailed him his determination died away. He did not know the proper time to call, nor was there any one to tell him, and he was afraid of committing himself to an irretrievable blunder. Having shaken himself free from his old companions and old ways of life, and having no new companions, nothing remained for him but to read, and the long hours he devoted to it would have ruined a dozen pairs of ordinary eyes. But his eyes were strong, and they were backed by a body superbly strong. Furthermore, his mind was fallow. It had lain fallow all his life so far as the abstract thought of the books was concerned, and it was ripe for the sowing. It had never been jaded by study, and it bit hold of the knowledge in the books with sharp teeth that would not let go.

text: Martin Eden’s mind was in a whirl when he departed several hours later, prompting a quick trip to the library to track down the meanings of a dozen unfamiliar terms. He emerged from the building carrying four heavy volumes: Blavatsky’s *Secret Doctrine*, *Progress and Poverty*, *The Quintessence of Socialism*, and *Warfare of Religion and Science*. Regrettably, he chose to begin with the *Secret Doctrine*. Each line was dense with complex, multi-syllabic words that eluded him. Propped up in bed, he found himself consulting the dictionary more frequently than the text itself. He looked up so many new terms that he would forget their definitions as soon as they reappeared, forcing him to search for them again. He eventually began recording these definitions in a journal, filling page after page with notes. Despite this, the meaning remained out of reach. By three in the morning, his brain felt chaotic, yet he had not grasped a single fundamental concept. When he finally looked up, the room seemed to pitch and roll like a vessel on a rough sea. In a fit of temper, he flung the *Secret Doctrine* across the room along with a string of curses, extinguished the light, and tried to sleep. The other three books proved just as difficult. His struggle was not due to a lack of intelligence, but rather a lack of intellectual discipline and the conceptual tools required for such high-level thought. Sensing this, he briefly considered the desperate plan of reading the entire dictionary from start to finish until he had mastered every single word.
source_text: Martin Eden’s head was in a state of addlement when he went away after several hours, and he hurried to the library to look up the definitions of a dozen unusual words. And when he left the library, he carried under his arm four volumes: Madam Blavatsky’s “Secret Doctrine,” “Progress and Poverty,” “The Quintessence of Socialism,” and “Warfare of Religion and Science.” Unfortunately, he began on the “Secret Doctrine.” Every line bristled with many-syllabled words he did not understand. He sat up in bed, and the dictionary was in front of him more often than the book. He looked up so many new words that when they recurred, he had forgotten their meaning and had to look them up again. He devised the plan of writing the definitions in a note-book, and filled page after page with them. And still he could not understand. He read until three in the morning, and his brain was in a turmoil, but not one essential thought in the text had he grasped. He looked up, and it seemed that the room was lifting, heeling, and plunging like a ship upon the sea. Then he hurled the “Secret Doctrine” and many curses across the room, turned off the gas, and composed himself to sleep. Nor did he have much better luck with the other three books. It was not that his brain was weak or incapable; it could think these thoughts were it not for lack of training in thinking and lack of the thought-tools with which to think. He guessed this, and for a while entertained the idea of reading nothing but the dictionary until he had mastered every word in it.

text: He found his refuge in poetry, reading it voraciously and taking particular delight in the more straightforward poets whose clarity he could easily grasp. He was a seeker of beauty, and in those verses, he found it in abundance. Like music, poetry resonated within his soul, and he was unaware that he was currently fortifying his intellect for the more demanding tasks ahead. Because his mind was still an unwritten ledger, he easily committed his favorite stanzas to memory, finding a deep, rhythmic pleasure in reciting the words aloud or murmuring them to himself. Then, on a library shelf, he came across Bulfinch’s “Age of Fable” and Gayley’s “Classic Myths” standing together. The encounter was a transformative spark that lit up his world of ignorance, driving him to consume poetry with even greater passion than before.
source_text: Poetry, however, was his solace, and he read much of it, finding his greatest joy in the simpler poets, who were more understandable. He loved beauty, and there he found beauty. Poetry, like music, stirred him profoundly, and, though he did not know it, he was preparing his mind for the heavier work that was to come. The pages of his mind were blank, and, without effort, much he read and liked, stanza by stanza, was impressed upon those pages, so that he was soon able to extract great joy from chanting aloud or under his breath the music and the beauty of the printed words he had read. Then he stumbled upon Gayley’s “Classic Myths” and Bulfinch’s “Age of Fable,” side by side on a library shelf. It was illumination, a great light in the darkness of his ignorance, and he read poetry more avidly than ever.

text: Both of my parents have passed away. I have two sisters; one is already married, and I expect the other will be soon enough. Then there’s a whole string of brothers—I’m the youngest—but they never helped anyone but themselves. They’ve just drifted around the world, always putting their own interests first. The eldest died in India. Two are in South Africa now, another is away on a whaling ship, and one is traveling with a circus as a trapeze artist. I suppose I’m just like the rest of them. I’ve been looking after myself since I was eleven, which is when my mother died. I’ll have to manage my own education, I guess, and what I need to know is where to start.
source_text: “My father and mother are dead. I’ve two sisters, one married, an’ the other’ll get married soon, I suppose. Then I’ve a string of brothers,—I’m the youngest,—but they never helped nobody. They’ve just knocked around over the world, lookin’ out for number one. The oldest died in India. Two are in South Africa now, an’ another’s on a whaling voyage, an’ one’s travellin’ with a circus—he does trapeze work. An’ I guess I’m just like them. I’ve taken care of myself since I was eleven—that’s when my mother died. I’ve got to study by myself, I guess, an’ what I want to know is where to begin.”

text: “You fail to finish the ends of your words. The word ‘and’ should be pronounced with the final ‘d,’ but you say ‘an.’ Similarly, you are inconsistent with ‘ing’ sounds, frequently dropping the ‘g’ altogether. You also have a habit of slurring by omitting starting letters and vowel blends. As for ‘them’—well, it is pointless to catalog every single error. What you really need is a lesson in grammar. I will fetch a textbook and show you how to start.”
source_text: “You don’t complete the endings. ‘A-n-d’ spells ‘and.’ You pronounce it ‘an’.’ ‘I-n-g’ spells ‘ing.’ Sometimes you pronounce it ‘ing’ and sometimes you leave off the ‘g.’ And then you slur by dropping initial letters and diphthongs. ‘T-h-e-m’ spells ‘them.’ You pronounce it—oh, well, it is not necessary to go over all of them. What you need is the grammar. I’ll get one and show you how to begin.”

text: Returning with the volume of syntax, she pulled a seat flush against his—a gesture of such casual grace that he questioned, too late, whether he ought to have aided her—and settled at his side. As she leafed through the text, their brows nearly met in the lamplight. He found it difficult to grasp her summary of the lessons ahead, so captivated was he by her intoxicating nearness. Yet, as she began to detail the architecture of conjugation, his distraction vanished into genuine wonder. The internal scaffolding of speech was a revelation to him, a glimpse into the very marrow of thought. He pressed closer to the script, and a stray lock of her hair grazed his face. He had known the darkness of a swoon only once before, and now the world seemed to tilt again. Breath failed him; his pulse hammered against his ribs, a frantic rhythm that threatened to choke him. In this proximity, the vast distance between their stations seemed suddenly spanned. Yet his adoration remained unsullied; she had not stepped down to his level, but rather he had been hoisted into her ethereal realm. His devotion was a form of piety, a trembling exaltation. Feeling as though he had stepped unbidden into a sacred shrine, he cautiously withdrew his head, severing the contact that hummed through him like a live current—a touch of which she remained entirely unconscious.
source_text: When she returned with the grammar, she drew a chair near his—he wondered if he should have helped her with the chair—and sat down beside him. She turned the pages of the grammar, and their heads were inclined toward each other. He could hardly follow her outlining of the work he must do, so amazed was he by her delightful propinquity. But when she began to lay down the importance of conjugation, he forgot all about her. He had never heard of conjugation, and was fascinated by the glimpse he was catching into the tie-ribs of language. He leaned closer to the page, and her hair touched his cheek. He had fainted but once in his life, and he thought he was going to faint again. He could scarcely breathe, and his heart was pounding the blood up into his throat and suffocating him. Never had she seemed so accessible as now. For the moment the great gulf that separated them was bridged. But there was no diminution in the loftiness of his feeling for her. She had not descended to him. It was he who had been caught up into the clouds and carried to her. His reverence for her, in that moment, was of the same order as religious awe and fervor. It seemed to him that he had intruded upon the holy of holies, and slowly and carefully he moved his head aside from the contact which thrilled him like an electric shock and of which she had not been aware.

text: His rapid evolution fascinated and astonished her. She noticed hidden layers of refinement blooming within him daily, as if his mind were fertile ground finally receiving nourishment. When she read Browning’s poetry to him, his unusual insights into debated verses frequently left her bewildered. She could not grasp that his raw experience with humanity and the world often made his readings more accurate than her academic ones. While his ideas appeared unsophisticated to her, she was frequently stirred by his bold intellectual leaps—trajectories so vast and celestial that she lost her way, left only to marvel at the sheer force of his untapped potential. Later, she performed music for him rather than merely at him, testing his spirit with melodies that reached depths she herself could not measure. He responded to music like a blossom turning toward the sun, moving swiftly from the simple tunes of the streets to the intricate classical compositions she had mastered. However, he showed an instinctive affinity for Wagner; once she explained the themes of the "Tannhäuser" overture, it captivated him more than anything else in her repertoire. To him, the piece felt like a direct reflection of his own existence. He saw his rough history in the Venusburg motif, while she became the embodiment of the Pilgrim’s Chorus; through this spiritual elevation, he ascended into a grand, ethereal struggle where the forces of light and darkness are locked in perpetual conflict.
source_text: His swift development was a source of surprise and interest. She detected unguessed finenesses in him that seemed to bud, day by day, like flowers in congenial soil. She read Browning aloud to him, and was often puzzled by the strange interpretations he gave to mooted passages. It was beyond her to realize that, out of his experience of men and women and life, his interpretations were far more frequently correct than hers. His conceptions seemed naive to her, though she was often fired by his daring flights of comprehension, whose orbit-path was so wide among the stars that she could not follow and could only sit and thrill to the impact of unguessed power. Then she played to him—no longer at him—and probed him with music that sank to depths beyond her plumb-line. His nature opened to music as a flower to the sun, and the transition was quick from his working-class rag-time and jingles to her classical display pieces that she knew nearly by heart. Yet he betrayed a democratic fondness for Wagner, and the “Tannhäuser” overture, when she had given him the clew to it, claimed him as nothing else she played. In an immediate way it personified his life. All his past was the _Venusburg_ motif, while her he identified somehow with the _Pilgrim’s Chorus_ motif; and from the exalted state this elevated him to, he swept onward and upward into that vast shadow-realm of spirit-groping, where good and evil war eternally.

text: He spent his days working and his evenings attending school, always keeping his sights set on the years to come. Eventually, he advanced to a night high school. Although he was earning a substantial income as a typesetter at just seventeen, his ambition pushed him toward something greater. He craved a lifelong profession rather than just a job, and he was more than willing to trade short-term comfort for long-term achievement. Setting his heart on the law, he joined my father’s practice as a simple office boy—can you believe it?—for a mere four dollars a week. Yet, because he had already learned the value of a penny, he still managed to save a portion of those meager wages.
source_text: “He worked in the daytime, and at night he went to night school. He had his eyes fixed always on the future. Later on he went to night high school. When he was only seventeen, he was earning excellent wages at setting type, but he was ambitious. He wanted a career, not a livelihood, and he was content to make immediate sacrifices for his ultimate gain. He decided upon the law, and he entered father’s office as an office boy—think of that!—and got only four dollars a week. But he had learned how to be economical, and out of that four dollars he went on saving money.”

text: I haven't finished my account yet," she remarked. "According to my father, he worked with a level of dedication unlike any other office assistant he’d ever hired. Mr. Butler was always driven. He was never late; in fact, he usually arrived at the office a few minutes ahead of schedule. Even so, he was incredibly disciplined with his time, dedicating every spare moment to his studies. He mastered bookkeeping and typing, and he even managed to pay for shorthand lessons by spending his evenings dictating for a court reporter in need of practice. He was quickly promoted to a clerkship and made himself indispensable. Father recognized his value and saw that he was destined for success. It was actually at Father's suggestion that he attended law school. He became an attorney, and he had barely returned to the firm before Father made him a junior partner. He is an extraordinary man. He has turned down a seat in the U.S. Senate several times, and Father claims he could be appointed to the Supreme Court whenever a vacancy appears, if he so chose. A life like his is an inspiration to us all. It demonstrates that a person with enough willpower can rise above their circumstances.
source_text: “But I have not finished my story,” she said. “He worked, so father says, as no other office boy he ever had. Mr. Butler was always eager to work. He never was late, and he was usually at the office a few minutes before his regular time. And yet he saved his time. Every spare moment was devoted to study. He studied book-keeping and type-writing, and he paid for lessons in shorthand by dictating at night to a court reporter who needed practice. He quickly became a clerk, and he made himself invaluable. Father appreciated him and saw that he was bound to rise. It was on father’s suggestion that he went to law college. He became a lawyer, and hardly was he back in the office when father took him in as junior partner. He is a great man. He refused the United States Senate several times, and father says he could become a justice of the Supreme Court any time a vacancy occurs, if he wants to. Such a life is an inspiration to all of us. It shows us that a man with will may rise superior to his environment.”

text: The skipper, a Norwegian with dull, glassy eyes, had somehow acquired a full collection of Shakespeare that he ignored entirely. Martin had taken to laundering the man’s garments as payment for the privilege of poring over the treasured books. Before long, he became so saturated with the dramas and the numerous lines that lodged themselves naturally in his memory that the entire world appeared to take the shape of an Elizabethan play. He even found himself thinking in the steady rhythm of blank verse. While this immersion refined his sense of rhythm and instilled a deep respect for elegant English, it simultaneously filled his head with a great deal of antiquated and useless language.
source_text: The captain was a fishy-eyed Norwegian who somehow had fallen into possession of a complete Shakespeare, which he never read, and Martin had washed his clothes for him and in return been permitted access to the precious volumes. For a time, so steeped was he in the plays and in the many favorite passages that impressed themselves almost without effort on his brain, that all the world seemed to shape itself into forms of Elizabethan tragedy or comedy and his very thoughts were in blank verse. It trained his ear and gave him a fine appreciation for noble English; withal it introduced into his mind much that was archaic and obsolete.

text: Those eight months had been highly productive; alongside his progress in eloquent speech and noble thought, he had discovered the depths of his own character. While he remained humble regarding his lack of knowledge, a new sense of inner power began to take root. He perceived a distinct gap between himself and his shipmates, though he was insightful enough to see that this difference was a matter of latent ability rather than current accomplishment. They shared the same tasks, yet within him stirred a restless energy suggesting he was capable of much more. The piercing beauty of his surroundings filled him with a bittersweet longing for Ruth’s company. He initially intended to describe these South Sea wonders solely for her, but his creative impulse soon demanded a broader stage. Then, in a moment of brilliant clarity, the grand ambition took hold: he would write. He would become the eyes, ears, and heart of the world, translating human experience into language. He would tackle every genre—poetry, fiction, and even Shakespearean drama. This was his destiny and the means by which he would prove worthy of Ruth. In his mind, the masters of literature were the world’s true giants, far transcending men like Mr. Butler, who valued only wealth and the prestige of the high courts.
source_text: The eight months had been well spent, and, in addition to what he had learned of right speaking and high thinking, he had learned much of himself. Along with his humbleness because he knew so little, there arose a conviction of power. He felt a sharp gradation between himself and his shipmates, and was wise enough to realize that the difference lay in potentiality rather than achievement. What he could do,—they could do; but within him he felt a confused ferment working that told him there was more in him than he had done. He was tortured by the exquisite beauty of the world, and wished that Ruth were there to share it with him. He decided that he would describe to her many of the bits of South Sea beauty. The creative spirit in him flamed up at the thought and urged that he recreate this beauty for a wider audience than Ruth. And then, in splendor and glory, came the great idea. He would write. He would be one of the eyes through which the world saw, one of the ears through which it heard, one of the hearts through which it felt. He would write—everything—poetry and prose, fiction and description, and plays like Shakespeare. There was career and the way to win to Ruth. The men of literature were the world’s giants, and he conceived them to be far finer than the Mr. Butlers who earned thirty thousand a year and could be Supreme Court justices if they wanted to.

text: Julian derived a secret pleasure from imagining his brother-in-law’s bewilderment on Sunday morning, the moment he would unfurl his *Gazette* and encounter the piece on the relic-hunters. At the first grey light, Julian was already at his own doorstep, his fingers moving with nervous haste through the dense Sunday edition. He conducted a second search, more methodical than the first, then refolded the paper and left it on the mat. He was thankful now that he had kept his literary ambitions a secret. On reflection, he realized he had been mistaken about the speed of the press. His submission lacked the urgency of a headline, and it was far more probable that the editor would write to him before the article ever went to print.
source_text: Martin contented himself by picturing his brother-in-law’s surprise on Sunday morning when he opened his _Examiner_ and saw the article on the treasure-hunters. Early that morning he was out himself to the front door, nervously racing through the many-sheeted newspaper. He went through it a second time, very carefully, then folded it up and left it where he had found it. He was glad he had not told any one about his article. On second thought he concluded that he had been wrong about the speed with which things found their way into newspaper columns. Besides, there had not been any news value in his article, and most likely the editor would write to him about it first.

text: By Friday evening, he had completed the twenty-one-thousand-word serial. He calculated that at two cents per word, the piece would earn him four hundred and twenty dollars. It was an impressive return for a single week’s labor. This sum exceeded any amount of money he had ever held at once, and he was unsure how he could possibly exhaust it. He had struck a gold mine, and he knew he could return to the source for more whenever he wished. His plans involved upgrading his wardrobe, subscribing to numerous periodicals, and purchasing the dozens of reference volumes he currently had to visit the library to use. Even so, a significant portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars remained untouched. This surplus troubled him until he hit upon the idea of employing a domestic worker for Gertrude and getting a bicycle for Marian.
source_text: On Friday night he finished the serial, twenty-one thousand words long. At two cents a word, he calculated, that would bring him four hundred and twenty dollars. Not a bad week’s work. It was more money than he had ever possessed at one time. He did not know how he could spend it all. He had tapped a gold mine. Where this came from he could always get more. He planned to buy some more clothes, to subscribe to many magazines, and to buy dozens of reference books that at present he was compelled to go to the library to consult. And still there was a large portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars unspent. This worried him until the thought came to him of hiring a servant for Gertrude and of buying a bicycle for Marian.

text: “Yet, might I not be uniquely suited for the life of a writer?” he wondered, feeling a private surge of triumph at his own phrasing. His restless imagination instantly cast the entire scene onto a massive mental screen, alongside a thousand other glimpses of his past—memories that were jagged and unrefined, marked by a crude, animalistic grit.
source_text: “But then, may not I be peculiarly constituted to write?” he queried, secretly exulting at the language he had used, his swift imagination throwing the whole scene and atmosphere upon a vast screen along with a thousand other scenes from his life—scenes that were rough and raw, gross and bestial.

text: He remained for dinner that night, and to Ruth’s great relief, he managed to win her father’s approval. They discussed the maritime profession—a field Martin understood thoroughly—and Mr. Morse later commented that he appeared to be a highly rational and intelligent young fellow. Because he was so careful to avoid slang and struggled to select the exact right words, Martin spoke with a measured slowness that actually helped him articulate his deepest insights. He felt significantly more relaxed than he had during his first visit almost a year ago, and his quiet modesty even impressed Mrs. Morse, who felt gratified by his obvious personal progress.
source_text: He stopped to dinner that evening, and, much to Ruth’s satisfaction, made a favorable impression on her father. They talked about the sea as a career, a subject which Martin had at his finger-ends, and Mr. Morse remarked afterward that he seemed a very clear-headed young man. In his avoidance of slang and his search after right words, Martin was compelled to talk slowly, which enabled him to find the best thoughts that were in him. He was more at ease than that first night at dinner, nearly a year before, and his shyness and modesty even commended him to Mrs. Morse, who was pleased at his manifest improvement.

text: Most individuals require a pilot to navigate, but I find I can dispense with such assistance. Having haunted the archives for so long, I am on the verge of total familiarity; I know which volumes to consult and which intellectual shores I mean to survey. By my reckoning, I shall make much better time on a solitary course. A squadron, after all, can only advance at the rate of its clumsiest boat, and a tutor is bound by the same law. They are unable to outstrip the common mass of their pupils, but I am free to dictate a tempo that no classroom could ever sustain.
source_text: “Some persons need guides, most persons do; but I think I can get along without them. I’ve spent a lot of time in the chart-room now, and I’m on the edge of knowing my way about, what charts I want to refer to, what coasts I want to explore. And from the way I line it up, I’ll explore a whole lot more quickly by myself. The speed of a fleet, you know, is the speed of the slowest ship, and the speed of the teachers is affected the same way. They can’t go any faster than the ruck of their scholars, and I can set a faster pace for myself than they set for a whole schoolroom.”

text:
He nearly blurted out that he would go much faster if she were by his side, as a vision flickered before him of an eternal expanse of radiant light and celestial darkness. In this dream, he drifted through the void with his arm around her, her pale golden hair brushing against his face. In that same moment, he felt the pathetic limitation of language. If only he could find the words to make her see what he saw! A sharp pang of longing struck him—a desperate urge to paint the scenes that flashed unbidden across the mirror of his mind. Suddenly, he grasped the edge of a great mystery. This was exactly what the master poets and great writers achieved; it was the source of their greatness. They possessed the power to articulate their thoughts, their feelings, and their observations. He thought of dogs dreaming in the sun, whimpering and yelping, yet unable to describe the visions that moved them. He had often wondered what they saw, and now he realized he was just like them: a dog napping in the heat. He witnessed magnificent, noble things, but he could only whine and bark at Ruth. 

But he would no longer be that sleeping dog. He would rouse himself, keep his eyes open, and labor until he had mastered the art. With clear sight and a free tongue, he would finally share the riches of his imagination with her. Other men had unlocked the secret of expression, turning words into loyal servants and arranging them so that the whole meant more than the sum of its parts. This brief insight moved him deeply, pulling him back into that cosmic dream of sunlit spaces and starry voids—until the silence of the room registered. He looked up to find Ruth watching him, a look of quiet amusement and a gentle smile playing in her eyes.
source_text: But I’d travel faster with you just the same, was what he wanted to blurt out, as he caught a vision of a world without end of sunlit spaces and starry voids through which he drifted with her, his arm around her, her pale gold hair blowing about his face. In the same instant he was aware of the pitiful inadequacy of speech. God! If he could so frame words that she could see what he then saw! And he felt the stir in him, like a throe of yearning pain, of the desire to paint these visions that flashed unsummoned on the mirror of his mind. Ah, that was it! He caught at the hem of the secret. It was the very thing that the great writers and master-poets did. That was why they were giants. They knew how to express what they thought, and felt, and saw. Dogs asleep in the sun often whined and barked, but they were unable to tell what they saw that made them whine and bark. He had often wondered what it was. And that was all he was, a dog asleep in the sun. He saw noble and beautiful visions, but he could only whine and bark at Ruth. But he would cease sleeping in the sun. He would stand up, with open eyes, and he would struggle and toil and learn until, with eyes unblinded and tongue untied, he could share with her his visioned wealth. Other men had discovered the trick of expression, of making words obedient servitors, and of making combinations of words mean more than the sum of their separate meanings. He was stirred profoundly by the passing glimpse at the secret, and he was again caught up in the vision of sunlit spaces and starry voids—until it came to him that it was very quiet, and he saw Ruth regarding him with an amused expression and a smile in her eyes.

text: “I thought you were offering a devotion,” she ventured with a brittle courage, though inwardly she felt her spirit shrivel and recoil. Never before had a profanity escaped the lips of a man within her circle, and she was stunned—not simply by the dictates of her strict upbringing, but by a deeper spiritual tremor, as if a harsh wind had suddenly torn through the quiet, gated sanctuary of her girlhood.
source_text: “It sounded as if you were praying,” she said bravely, but she felt herself inside to be withering and shrinking. It was the first time she had heard an oath from the lips of a man she knew, and she was shocked, not merely as a matter of principle and training, but shocked in spirit by this rough blast of life in the garden of her sheltered maidenhood.

text: Yet she pardoned him, surprised by how effortless it felt to do so. Somehow, overlooking his flaws had become easy. He hadn't been given the same start as other men, but he was striving so sincerely—and making real progress, too. It never occurred to her that there could be any other reason for her gentle attitude toward him. She was developing a deep affection for him, though she remained oblivious to it. She had no way of recognizing the change. After twenty-four years of calm composure without a single romantic entanglement, she lacked the insight to interpret her own emotions; having never felt the glow of true love, she didn't realize she was finally beginning to warm.
source_text: But she forgave, and with surprise at the ease of her forgiveness. Somehow it was not so difficult to forgive him anything. He had not had a chance to be as other men, and he was trying so hard, and succeeding, too. It never entered her head that there could be any other reason for her being kindly disposed toward him. She was tenderly disposed toward him, but she did not know it. She had no way of knowing it. The placid poise of twenty-four years without a single love affair did not fit her with a keen perception of her own feelings, and she who had never warmed to actual love was unaware that she was warming now.

text: After his treatise on "Abyssal Foraging," Julian penned an essay regarding maritime vocations, another on the tracking of pelagic turtles, and a final piece concerning the doldrums of the equator. Testing his range, he then attempted a work of fiction, and before his momentum faltered, he had produced six distinct narratives and mailed them to a variety of periodicals. He composed with a violent, obsessive fluency from sunrise until well past midnight, pausing only for hurried trips to the library stacks or to pay court to Clara. He was immersed in a state of absolute bliss. His vitality was strained to its utmost limit. He burned with a fever that refused to subside. The sublime rapture of the demiurge, that spark usually reserved for the divine, had become his own. The mundane world pressing in upon him—the stench of damp laundry and wilted greens, the bedraggled silhouette of his sister, and the derisive grin of Mr. Blackwood—was nothing more than a flickering shadow-play. Authenticity lived solely within his intellect, and the chronicles he birthed were the only solid pieces of truth he recognized.
source_text: Following the “Pearl-diving,” he wrote an article on the sea as a career, another on turtle-catching, and a third on the northeast trades. Then he tried, as an experiment, a short story, and before he broke his stride he had finished six short stories and despatched them to various magazines. He wrote prolifically, intensely, from morning till night, and late at night, except when he broke off to go to the reading-room, draw books from the library, or to call on Ruth. He was profoundly happy. Life was pitched high. He was in a fever that never broke. The joy of creation that is supposed to belong to the gods was his. All the life about him—the odors of stale vegetables and soapsuds, the slatternly form of his sister, and the jeering face of Mr. Higginbotham—was a dream. The real world was in his mind, and the stories he wrote were so many pieces of reality out of his mind.

text: He yearned to share his stories with Ruth but lacked the courage to do so yet. He decided to wait until he achieved publication, believing that only then would she truly appreciate the magnitude of his goals. In the meantime, he labored in solitude. No previous voyage had ever captivated him as much as this profound expedition into the world of ideas. He acquired textbooks on chemistry and physics, solving complex equations alongside his algebra exercises. Though he took scientific proofs on faith, his powerful imagination allowed him to visualize chemical reactions more vividly than many students who saw them firsthand. Martin pushed through the dense volumes, awestruck by the insights he was discovering about the universe. He had always taken the world for granted, but now he understood its intricate design—the constant harmony between matter and force. New explanations for old observations constantly struck him. He was mesmerized by the principles of mechanics, his mind frequently drifting back to the ropes and pulleys he had handled on ships. The science of navigation, which allowed vessels to find their way across the featureless ocean, finally became clear. As the secrets of tides and storms were revealed, the logic behind the trade winds made him fear he had written his recent article too hastily. He knew, at the very least, that he could improve upon it now. One afternoon, he joined Arthur for a visit to the University of California. There, with a hushed sense of religious wonder, he walked through the laboratories, witnessed live demonstrations, and listened to a physics professor address a hall of students.
source_text: He longed to read his stories to Ruth, but did not dare. He would wait till some were published, he decided, then she would understand what he had been working for. In the meantime he toiled on. Never had the spirit of adventure lured him more strongly than on this amazing exploration of the realm of mind. He bought the text-books on physics and chemistry, and, along with his algebra, worked out problems and demonstrations. He took the laboratory proofs on faith, and his intense power of vision enabled him to see the reactions of chemicals more understandingly than the average student saw them in the laboratory. Martin wandered on through the heavy pages, overwhelmed by the clews he was getting to the nature of things. He had accepted the world as the world, but now he was comprehending the organization of it, the play and interplay of force and matter. Spontaneous explanations of old matters were continually arising in his mind. Levers and purchases fascinated him, and his mind roved backward to hand-spikes and blocks and tackles at sea. The theory of navigation, which enabled the ships to travel unerringly their courses over the pathless ocean, was made clear to him. The mysteries of storm, and rain, and tide were revealed, and the reason for the existence of trade-winds made him wonder whether he had written his article on the northeast trade too soon. At any rate he knew he could write it better now. One afternoon he went out with Arthur to the University of California, and, with bated breath and a feeling of religious awe, went through the laboratories, saw demonstrations, and listened to a physics professor lecturing to his classes.

text: Yet he never abandoned his craft. A steady tide of tales spilled from his pen, and he experimented with the simpler meters—the light verse common to the popular monthlies—though he lost his bearings and threw away two weeks on a five-act tragedy in unrhymed verse, the swift rebuff of which by every editor he approached left him reeling. Later, he chanced upon the stark lines of a new master and composed a cycle of maritime poems inspired by those lean, rhythmic sketches. They were evocative works, rich in hue and daring, capturing the essence of the wandering life. “Tidal Rhythms,” he dubbed them, believing them the finest things he had yet conceived. He fashioned thirty of them in as many days, finishing one each night after completing a day’s prose that would have exhausted the weekly energy of a more celebrated novelist. This industry cost him nothing. It was not work. He was finally giving shape to his soul, and the long-stifled currents of beauty and mystery were at last breaking through his silent lips in a raw and powerful deluge.
source_text: But he did not neglect his writing. A stream of short stories flowed from his pen, and he branched out into the easier forms of verse—the kind he saw printed in the magazines—though he lost his head and wasted two weeks on a tragedy in blank verse, the swift rejection of which, by half a dozen magazines, dumfounded him. Then he discovered Henley and wrote a series of sea-poems on the model of “Hospital Sketches.” They were simple poems, of light and color, and romance and adventure. “Sea Lyrics,” he called them, and he judged them to be the best work he had yet done. There were thirty, and he completed them in a month, doing one a day after having done his regular day’s work on fiction, which day’s work was the equivalent to a week’s work of the average successful writer. The toil meant nothing to him. It was not toil. He was finding speech, and all the beauty and wonder that had been pent for years behind his inarticulate lips was now pouring forth in a wild and virile flood.

text: He didn't share his "Sea Lyrics" with anyone, not even the editors. He had grown wary of the publishing world, but it wasn't actually a lack of trust that stopped him from submitting the poems. They were so exquisite in his eyes that he felt a deep need to save them for Ruth, imagining a future, radiant moment when he would finally find the courage to read his work to her. In anticipation of that day, he kept the verses close, reciting them aloud and going over them until he had committed every line to memory.
source_text: He showed the “Sea Lyrics” to no one, not even to the editors. He had become distrustful of editors. But it was not distrust that prevented him from submitting the “Lyrics.” They were so beautiful to him that he was impelled to save them to share with Ruth in some glorious, far-off time when he would dare to read to her what he had written. Against that time he kept them with him, reading them aloud, going over them until he knew them by heart.

text: Every Tuesday she reserved for him; arriving as the shadows lengthened, he would remain for the evening repast and the concert that followed. These were the hallowed marks on his calendar. The grace of her home, so starkly opposed to the grimness of his own quarters, joined with her simple proximity to renew his vow to conquer the world. Though he possessed a natural affinity for beauty and a raw hunger to craft it, his labors were dedicated solely to her. He was, before all else, a suitor. All other aspirations were but servants to his passion.
source_text: One afternoon a week she gave to him, and arriving late, he usually stayed for dinner and for music afterward. Those were his red-letter days. The atmosphere of the house, in such contrast with that in which he lived, and the mere nearness to her, sent him forth each time with a firmer grip on his resolve to climb the heights. In spite of the beauty in him, and the aching desire to create, it was for her that he struggled. He was a lover first and always. All other things he subordinated to love.

text: Martin remained suspended in his elevated state, unable to descend. Even the sheer vulgarity of the insult failed to ground him; he was far beyond the reach of resentment or pain. Having glimpsed a magnificent revelation, he felt like a deity, possessed by a deep and unsettling compassion for the pathetic creature before him. He did not look at the man, and though his gaze swept past him, he saw nothing. Like a sleepwalker, he drifted from the room to get ready. It was only after he had reached his own quarters and was fixing his tie that he noticed a jarring sound echoing in his ears. Examining the memory, he identified it as Bernard Higginbotham’s parting snort—a noise that had somehow failed to penetrate his consciousness until that very moment.
source_text: But Martin could not come down from the height. Not even the bestiality of the allusion could bring him back to earth. Anger and hurt were beneath him. He had seen a great vision and was as a god, and he could feel only profound and awful pity for this maggot of a man. He did not look at him, and though his eyes passed over him, he did not see him; and as in a dream he passed out of the room to dress. It was not until he had reached his own room and was tying his necktie that he became aware of a sound that lingered unpleasantly in his ears. On investigating this sound he identified it as the final snort of Bernard Higginbotham, which somehow had not penetrated to his brain before.

text: She’s spent years tethered to machinery, working endless hours. In one's youth, the body is remarkably supple, and grueling labor molds it like soft clay to fit the demands of the job. I can often identify a man’s trade simply by watching him pass on the street. Consider me, for instance. Why do I lurch and sway as I move through this shop? It is the mark of all those years I spent at sea. Had I spent that same time herding cattle while my frame was still flexible, I wouldn’t be rolling like this, though I’d likely be bow-legged. It is no different for that girl. You must have seen the hardness in her eyes. She has never known the luxury of protection. She’s had to look out for herself, and a young woman cannot survive on her own and still keep her expression as soft and gentle as—well, as yours.
source_text: “She has worked long hours for years at machines. When one’s body is young, it is very pliable, and hard work will mould it like putty according to the nature of the work. I can tell at a glance the trades of many workingmen I meet on the street. Look at me. Why am I rolling all about the shop? Because of the years I put in on the sea. If I’d put in the same years cow-punching, with my body young and pliable, I wouldn’t be rolling now, but I’d be bow-legged. And so with that girl. You noticed that her eyes were what I might call hard. She has never been sheltered. She has had to take care of herself, and a young girl can’t take care of herself and keep her eyes soft and gentle like—like yours, for example.”

text: He glared at his own reflection, shaking a fist at the glass, before perching on the bedside to stare into space for a long while. Eventually, he pulled out his notebook and math text, losing himself in quadratic equations as the night wore on. The stars gradually paled, and the first gray light of morning began to wash against his window.
source_text: He shook his fist at himself in the glass, and sat down on the edge of the bed to dream for a space with wide eyes. Then he got out note-book and algebra and lost himself in quadratic equations, while the hours slipped by, and the stars dimmed, and the gray of dawn flooded against his window.

text: And so the monumental revelation commenced. He had attempted Spencer once before, but by starting with "Principles of Psychology," he had failed as miserably as he had with Madam Blavatsky. The text had been incomprehensible, and he had returned it without finishing it. But on this night, after his algebra and physics lessons and a brief effort at a sonnet, he climbed into bed and opened "First Principles." Dawn found him still immersed in the pages. Sleep was out of the question. He didn't even work on his writing that day. He remained on the bed until his body grew stiff, then moved to the hard floor, reading on his back with the book held aloft or shifting from side to side. He slept the following night and did his writing the next morning, but then the book's allure proved too great and he succumbed, reading all through the afternoon. He was oblivious to everything, including the fact that this was the very afternoon Ruth had reserved for him. He only became aware of the world around him when Bernard Higginbotham threw open the door and demanded to know if he thought they were running a restaurant.
source_text: So the great discovery began.
