Once before he had tried Spencer, and choosing the “Principles of Psychology” to begin with, he had failed as abjectly as he had failed with Madam Blavatsky. There had been no understanding the book, and he had returned it unread. But this night, after algebra and physics, and an attempt at a sonnet, he got into bed and opened “First Principles.” Morning found him still reading. It was impossible for him to sleep. Nor did he write that day. He lay on the bed till his body grew tired, when he tried the hard floor, reading on his back, the book held in the air above him, or changing from side to side. He slept that night, and did his writing next morning, and then the book tempted him and he fell, reading all afternoon, oblivious to everything and oblivious to the fact that that was the afternoon Ruth gave to him. His first consciousness of the immediate world about him was when Bernard Higginbotham jerked open the door and demanded to know if he thought they were running a restaurant.

text: Throughout his life, Martin Eden had been driven by an insatiable curiosity. It was this hunger for understanding that had propelled him into adventures across the globe. Yet, through the lens of Spencer’s philosophy, he was realizing that he had never truly known anything, and that no amount of sailing or wandering could have ever granted him such insight. He had merely skimmed the surface of existence, collecting isolated facts and forming shallow conclusions within a world he perceived as chaotic and governed by chance. While he had observed and logically analyzed the mechanics of a bird’s flight, he had never thought to question the biological process that had shaped birds into such complex living machines. The existence of such a developmental system had never crossed his mind. He had never guessed that there was a reason for their being; to him, they had always existed, appearing simply as a matter of course.
source_text: Martin Eden had been mastered by curiosity all his days. He wanted to know, and it was this desire that had sent him adventuring over the world. But he was now learning from Spencer that he never had known, and that he never could have known had he continued his sailing and wandering forever. He had merely skimmed over the surface of things, observing detached phenomena, accumulating fragments of facts, making superficial little generalizations—and all and everything quite unrelated in a capricious and disorderly world of whim and chance. The mechanism of the flight of birds he had watched and reasoned about with understanding; but it had never entered his head to try to explain the process whereby birds, as organic flying mechanisms, had been developed. He had never dreamed there was such a process. That birds should have come to be, was unguessed. They always had been. They just happened.

text: Spencer stood as the architect of all understanding, weaving every fragment of knowledge into a singular, cohesive system. He defined the fundamental nature of reality, revealing a cosmos so tangible and structured that it resembled those intricate model ships that sailors carefully assemble inside glass bottles. There was no room for randomness or caprice; everything was governed by law. It was these immutable rules that allowed a bird to take flight, and the very same principles that had once driven primordial ooze to churn and evolve, eventually sprouting the limbs and feathers required to transform into a creature of the air.
source_text: And here was the man Spencer, organizing all knowledge for him, reducing everything to unity, elaborating ultimate realities, and presenting to his startled gaze a universe so concrete of realization that it was like the model of a ship such as sailors make and put into glass bottles. There was no caprice, no chance. All was law. It was in obedience to law that the bird flew, and it was in obedience to the same law that fermenting slime had writhed and squirmed and put out legs and wings and become a bird.

text: He presented his monumental revelation to Ruth, attempting to convey the full measure of his awe and delight. However, she did not share his fervor. She accepted his words with a quiet reserve, appearing to have already encountered such concepts during her own schooling. The ideas failed to move her as intensely as they had moved him; he might have been surprised by this had he not reasoned that the material was simply familiar to her, while to him it was a startling new light. He found that Arthur and Norman also accepted evolution and had read Spencer, yet the work seemed to have made no significant impact on them. Meanwhile, Will Olney—the bespectacled youth with the unruly hair—dismissed Spencer with a caustic sneer, repeating the mocking refrain: “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”
source_text: He brought his great discovery to Ruth, sharing with her all his joy and wonder in it. But she did not seem to be so enthusiastic over it. She tacitly accepted it and, in a way, seemed aware of it from her own studies. It did not stir her deeply, as it did him, and he would have been surprised had he not reasoned it out that it was not new and fresh to her as it was to him. Arthur and Norman, he found, believed in evolution and had read Spencer, though it did not seem to have made any vital impression upon them, while the young fellow with the glasses and the mop of hair, Will Olney, sneered disagreeably at Spencer and repeated the epigram, “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”

text: Martin lived for those Sundays; they were the highlights of his week, primarily because they brought him close to Ruth, but also because they bridged the gap between him and the young men of her social standing. Regardless of their years of rigorous schooling, he discovered that he could hold his own intellectually, and their long discussions provided the perfect arena to test the complex grammar he had worked so tirelessly to learn. He no longer consulted guidebooks on manners, choosing instead to watch and learn from those around him. Apart from the moments when his own fervor took over, he remained intensely alert, tracking their every move to adopt the small graces and polished habits of their class.
source_text: Those Sundays were great days for Martin, greatest because he was with Ruth, and great, also, because they were putting him more on a par with the young men of her class. In spite of their long years of disciplined education, he was finding himself their intellectual equal, and the hours spent with them in conversation was so much practice for him in the use of the grammar he had studied so hard. He had abandoned the etiquette books, falling back upon observation to show him the right things to do. Except when carried away by his enthusiasm, he was always on guard, keenly watchful of their actions and learning their little courtesies and refinements of conduct.

text: For some time, Martin was struck by the realization that hardly anyone actually read Spencer. When he inquired at the library, the man behind the desk praised Spencer as a profound thinker, yet he clearly possessed no real knowledge of the philosopher’s work. Later, at a dinner attended by Mr. Butler, Martin steered the conversation toward the subject. Mr. Morse launched a bitter attack on the Englishman’s agnosticism, though he admitted he had never looked at *First Principles*; meanwhile, Mr. Butler confessed he had no use for the man, had never read a single sentence of his writing, and felt perfectly fine without him. Martin felt a flicker of doubt, and a less independent spirit might have bowed to the weight of public opinion. However, he found Spencer’s logic too compelling to ignore. To him, abandoning Spencer would be like a captain casting his compass and chronometer into the ocean. Instead, he committed himself to a rigorous study of evolution, his convictions strengthened by the supporting evidence of countless other scholars. As he delved deeper, he glimpsed vast, uncharted territories of knowledge, and he began to feel a constant, aching frustration that a day lasted only twenty-four hours.
source_text: The fact that Spencer was very little read was for some time a source of surprise to Martin. “Herbert Spencer,” said the man at the desk in the library, “oh, yes, a great mind.” But the man did not seem to know anything of the content of that great mind. One evening, at dinner, when Mr. Butler was there, Martin turned the conversation upon Spencer. Mr. Morse bitterly arraigned the English philosopher’s agnosticism, but confessed that he had not read “First Principles”; while Mr. Butler stated that he had no patience with Spencer, had never read a line of him, and had managed to get along quite well without him. Doubts arose in Martin’s mind, and had he been less strongly individual he would have accepted the general opinion and given Herbert Spencer up. As it was, he found Spencer’s explanation of things convincing; and, as he phrased it to himself, to give up Spencer would be equivalent to a navigator throwing the compass and chronometer overboard. So Martin went on into a thorough study of evolution, mastering more and more the subject himself, and being convinced by the corroborative testimony of a thousand independent writers. The more he studied, the more vistas he caught of fields of knowledge yet unexplored, and the regret that days were only twenty-four hours long became a chronic complaint with him.

text: “I claim no mastery of a single craft,” he remarked, shielding himself from Beatrice’s gaze. “Nor do I intend to narrow my focus so. The provinces of learning are far too numerous for any mortal to conquer even a fraction in his brief years. I must seek the whole of human wisdom. Should I require the labor of the expert, I shall simply find it preserved within their libraries.”
source_text: “I am not a specialist,” he said, in defence, to Ruth. “Nor am I going to try to be a specialist. There are too many special fields for any one man, in a whole lifetime, to master a tithe of them. I must pursue general knowledge. When I need the work of specialists, I shall refer to their books.”

text: Every philosopher of broad themes—the most profound intellects on earth—actually depends upon the specialist. Herbert Spencer followed this model. He drew his universal principles from the raw data of countless individual researchers. He would have required a thousand lifetimes to gather such evidence personally. Darwin was no different; he relied on the practical insights already gained by horticulturalists and livestock farmers.
source_text: “All thinkers on general subjects, the greatest minds in the world, in fact, rely on the specialists. Herbert Spencer did that. He generalized upon the findings of thousands of investigators. He would have had to live a thousand lives in order to do it all himself. And so with Darwin. He took advantage of all that had been learned by the florists and cattle-breeders.”

text: Julian tracked the debate intently, and while he acknowledged that Sterling’s logic was sound, he bristled at the dismissive manner the man showed toward Clara. In that moment, a new philosophy of devotion began to take shape. Reason, he realized, was irrelevant to the soul’s choice. It mattered little if the woman he adored reasoned poorly or well. Love existed on a plane far above mere intellect. If she failed to grasp the urgency of his artistic drive, she was not one bit less worthy of his heart. She was a creature of total grace, and her thoughts had no bearing on her essential beauty.
source_text: Martin followed the discussion closely, and while he was convinced that Olney was right, he resented the rather cavalier treatment he accorded Ruth. A new conception of love formed in his mind as he listened. Reason had nothing to do with love. It mattered not whether the woman he loved reasoned correctly or incorrectly. Love was above reason. If it just happened that she did not fully appreciate his necessity for a career, that did not make her a bit less lovable. She was all lovable, and what she thought had nothing to do with her lovableness.

text: “Nonsense!” Olney shot a spiteful look toward Ruth. “I suppose your next claim will be that you discovered Spencer through her—except you didn’t. She understands Darwin and the theory of evolution about as well as I understand the legends of King Solomon’s mines. What was that tongue-twisting definition of Spencer’s you hit us with the other day—the one about indefinite, incoherent homogeneity? Try that one on her and see if she grasps even a syllable of it. You see, that doesn't count as ‘culture’ to her. Anyway, I’m off; but if you start laboring over Latin, Martin, I’ll lose all respect for you.”
source_text: “Rats!” Olney looked at Ruth, and his expression was malicious. “I suppose you’ll be telling me next that you read Spencer on her recommendation—only you didn’t. And she doesn’t know anything more about Darwin and evolution than I do about King Solomon’s mines. What’s that jawbreaker definition about something or other, of Spencer’s, that you sprang on us the other day—that indefinite, incoherent homogeneity thing? Spring it on her, and see if she understands a word of it. That isn’t culture, you see. Well, tra la, and if you tackle Latin, Martin, I won’t have any respect for you.”

text: The conversation continued; she, with her soft but steady determination, kept returning to the necessity of a rigorous academic foundation and the value of Latin as a cornerstone for any profession. She described her vision of a truly accomplished man, a figure modeled largely after her father, though colored by certain unmistakable traits belonging to Mr. Butler. He lay on his back and listened with rapt attention, gazing upward and finding a quiet joy in the very movement of her lips as she spoke. Yet, his mind remained unpersuaded. There was nothing enticing in the life she envisioned, and he felt a heavy sense of disappointment coupled with a sharp, piercing love for her. Throughout her talk, she made no mention of his own work, and the manuscripts he had hoped to read to her sat forgotten on the grass.
source_text: They talked on; she, in her gently persistent way, returning always to the need of thorough grounding in education and to the advantages of Latin as part of the foundation for any career. She drew her ideal of the successful man, and it was largely in her father’s image, with a few unmistakable lines and touches of color from the image of Mr. Butler. He listened eagerly, with receptive ears, lying on his back and looking up and joying in each movement of her lips as she talked. But his brain was not receptive. There was nothing alluring in the pictures she drew, and he was aware of a dull pain of disappointment and of a sharper ache of love for her. In all she said there was no mention of his writing, and the manuscripts he had brought to read lay neglected on the ground.

text: Julian observed the bloodless pallor of Elara’s skin, the frantic dilation of her gaze, and the white-knuckled tension of her fingers with a hidden, predatory pride. He had won. The raw essence of his imagination had been transplanted from his consciousness into hers. It had struck deep. Whether she found it abhorrent or exquisite mattered little; it had enslaved her attention, compelling her into a breathless silence that rendered the rest of the world invisible.
source_text: He noted her pale face, her eyes wide and tense, and her clenched hands, with secret satisfaction. He had succeeded. He had communicated the stuff of fancy and feeling from out of his brain. It had struck home. No matter whether she liked it or not, it had gripped her and mastered her, made her sit there and listen and forget details.

text: Naturally, it was beautiful, but it possessed a quality far deeper than simple aesthetics—a sharp, piercing magnificence that rendered beauty a mere subordinate. He lay motionless on the earth, staring as the grim specter of a profound doubt loomed over him. He had fallen short. He was voiceless. He had witnessed one of the most remarkable things on earth, yet he had failed to find a way to give it voice.
source_text: Of course it was beautiful; but there was something more than mere beauty in it, something more stingingly splendid which had made beauty its handmaiden. He sprawled silently on the ground, watching the grisly form of a great doubt rising before him. He had failed. He was inarticulate. He had seen one of the greatest things in the world, and he had not expressed it.

text: “I suppose I do,” he admitted. “That uncertainty is what gives the journey its weight. It is not the state of being known, but the arduous climb toward it, that truly signifies. Besides, for me, such acclaim would be nothing more than a tool for a different end. I crave that distinction deeply, for that specific reason and that alone.”
source_text: “Yes, a little bit,” he confessed. “That is part of the adventure. It is not the being famous, but the process of becoming so, that counts. And after all, to be famous would be, for me, only a means to something else. I want to be famous very much, for that matter, and for that reason.”

text: She was too preoccupied with mapping out a feasible path for him to bother asking about that vague ambition he’d alluded to. Literature held no future for him; of that, she felt certain. His clumsy, adolescent efforts earlier that day had only confirmed it. Though he was a gifted talker, he lacked the ability to translate those thoughts into a literary form. When she measured him against the likes of Tennyson, Browning, or her most beloved prose stylists, he fell embarrassingly short. Still, she held back her full judgment. Her unusual fascination with him prompted her to stall for time. This urge to write was merely a trivial flaw, a phase he would eventually move past. Once he did, he could focus on the more substantial pursuits of the world. She was confident he would thrive then. His inner strength was so profound that failure seemed impossible—provided he abandoned his pen.
source_text: But she was too busy in her mind, carving out a career for him that would at least be possible, to ask what the ultimate something was which he had hinted at. There was no career for him in literature. Of that she was convinced. He had proved it to-day, with his amateurish and sophomoric productions. He could talk well, but he was incapable of expressing himself in a literary way. She compared Tennyson, and Browning, and her favorite prose masters with him, and to his hopeless discredit. Yet she did not tell him her whole mind. Her strange interest in him led her to temporize. His desire to write was, after all, a little weakness which he would grow out of in time. Then he would devote himself to the more serious affairs of life. And he would succeed, too. She knew that. He was so strong that he could not fail—if only he would drop writing.

text: The thought remained suspended as he surveyed the squalid limits of his attic, his eyes coming to rest with a heavy heart upon the mountain of thick envelopes gathering dust in the corner. The post had been cruel this week, and for want of a few pennies for stamps, he had watched the rejections accumulate. More would surely arrive with the dawn, and more still in the days to follow, until every wandering sheet of prose had retreated to its source. He lacked the means to cast them out again. His debt on the hired typewriter was a month in arrears, and what little silver remained in his pocket was already claimed by the mistress of the boarding house and the unrelenting fees of the hiring hall.
source_text: He had not finished the sentence, but looked about the mean little room and let his eyes dwell sadly upon a heap of returned manuscripts, still in their long envelopes, which lay in a corner on the floor. He had no stamps with which to continue them on their travels, and for a week they had been piling up. More of them would come in on the morrow, and on the next day, and the next, till they were all in. And he would be unable to start them out again. He was a month’s rent behind on the typewriter, which he could not pay, having barely enough for the week’s board which was due and for the employment office fees.

text: "Good God, we are nothing but beasts! Savage animals!" Martin whispered under his breath while tracking the brawl's every move. To his keen, perceptive eyes, the violence unfolded like a flickering kinetoscope. He found himself trapped between being a witness and a combatant. His recent months of intellectual growth and social polish recoiled from the spectacle, but then the present moment dissolved. The shadows of his history took hold of him, and he was once again the Martin Eden of old, back from a long voyage and trading blows with Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He felt the agony, the labor, the sweat, and the gore, experiencing a primal triumph each time his bare knuckles struck home.
source_text: “God! We are animals! Brute-beasts!” Martin muttered aloud, as he watched the progress of the fight. It was to him, with his splendid power of vision, like gazing into a kinetoscope. He was both onlooker and participant. His long months of culture and refinement shuddered at the sight; then the present was blotted out of his consciousness and the ghosts of the past possessed him, and he was Martin Eden, just returned from sea and fighting Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He suffered and toiled and sweated and bled, and exulted when his naked knuckles smashed home.

text: Then, after what felt like an eternity, as Cheese-Face’s strength began to fail, a sharp crack echoed through a flurry of punches. Martin’s right arm fell limp at his side; the bone had snapped. Everyone present recognized the sound, and Cheese-Face, sensing his advantage, pounced like a tiger to rain down blow after blow. When Martin’s crew surged forward to intervene, he drove them back with ragged, foul-mouthed oaths, his voice heavy with the groans of absolute agony and despair.
source_text: Next, after a seeming century or so, with Cheese-Face weakening fast, in a mix-up of blows there was a loud snap, and Martin’s right arm dropped to his side. It was a broken bone. Everybody heard it and knew; and Cheese-Face knew, rushing like a tiger in the other’s extremity and raining blow on blow. Martin’s gang surged forward to interfere. Dazed by the rapid succession of blows, Martin warned them back with vile and earnest curses sobbed out and groaned in ultimate desolation and despair.

text: The alarm blared, snapping Martin out of his slumber with a violence that would have left a less robust man with a throbbing headache. Although his rest had been deep, he transitioned to full awareness with feline speed, waking with a sense of excitement that his five-hour lapse into unconsciousness was finally over. He loathed the void of sleep. There were too many tasks to finish and too much of the world to experience. Resenting every minute lost to rest, he had already plunged his head into the washbasin before the clock’s ringing stopped, shivering with delight at the water’s icy sting.
source_text: The alarm-clock went off, jerking Martin out of sleep with a suddenness that would have given headache to one with less splendid constitution. Though he slept soundly, he awoke instantly, like a cat, and he awoke eagerly, glad that the five hours of unconsciousness were gone. He hated the oblivion of sleep. There was too much to do, too much of life to live. He grudged every moment of life sleep robbed him of, and before the clock had ceased its clattering he was head and ears in the washbasin and thrilling to the cold bite of the water.

text: The engineer, the gardener and his assistant, and a few hands from the stables were all gathered there. They ate in a rushed, somber silence, exchanging only a few words. As Martin ate and observed, he realized the immense distance he had traveled from their way of life. Their narrow intellectual horizons were disheartening, and he felt a growing urgency to leave. He devoured the unappetizing, watery meal as fast as the rest of them, letting out a heavy sigh of relief once he finally stepped out the kitchen door.
source_text: With them was the engineer, the gardener, and the assistant gardener, and two or three men from the stable. They ate hurriedly and gloomily, with but little conversation, and as Martin ate and listened he realized how far he had travelled from their status. Their small mental caliber was depressing to him, and he was anxious to get away from them. So he bolted his breakfast, a sickly, sloppy affair, as rapidly as they, and heaved a sigh of relief when he passed out through the kitchen door.

text: Upon returning to his room, he felt a sudden, unexpected wave of exhaustion, having lost track of the fact that he had been working on his feet for fourteen hours straight. He adjusted his alarm for six o’clock, figuring he could push through until one. He would use those hours to study. Removing his shoes to relieve his aching, swollen feet, he took his place at the table. He opened his copy of Fiske to the marked page, but struggling to grasp the content, he began the section again. The next thing he knew, he was waking up with cramped muscles, shivering from the cold mountain air drifting through the window. A glance at the clock revealed it was two in the morning; he had been unconscious for four hours. He stripped off his clothes and tumbled into bed, falling into a deep slumber the instant his head hit the pillow.
source_text: He was surprised at his weariness when he got into his room, forgetful of the fact that he had been on his feet and working without let up for fourteen hours. He set the alarm clock at six, and measured back five hours to one o’clock. He could read until then. Slipping off his shoes, to ease his swollen feet, he sat down at the table with his books. He opened Fiske, where he had left off to read. But he found trouble and began to read it through a second time. Then he awoke, in pain from his stiffened muscles and chilled by the mountain wind that had begun to blow in through the window. He looked at the clock. It marked two. He had been asleep four hours. He pulled off his clothes and crawled into bed, where he was asleep the moment after his head touched the pillow.

text: Not a moment lapsed without some new demand upon their strength. Elias waited for no signal, deferred to no fatigue, and moved with a frantic rhythm from one chore to the next. They starched two hundred formal shirts, a single practiced grab bunching the fabric so that the cuffs, collar, yoke, and breast-plate jutted from the circle of the right hand. At the same instant, the left hand hoisted the shirt’s bulk clear of the vat, and at the same instant, the right hand dived into the starch—a mixture so blistering that, to wring the cloth, they had to plunge their hands perpetually into a bucket of ice-cold water. And that night the labor stretched until eleven, processing the “fine starch”—all the pleated, ethereal, and intricate garments of the wealthy.
source_text: There was never an interval when something was not at hand to be done. Joe waited for nothing, waited on nothing, and went on the jump from task to task. They starched two hundred white shirts, with a single gathering movement seizing a shirt so that the wristbands, neckband, yoke, and bosom protruded beyond the circling right hand. At the same moment the left hand held up the body of the shirt so that it would not enter the starch, and at the same moment the right hand dipped into the starch—starch so hot that, in order to wring it out, their hands had to thrust, and thrust continually, into a bucket of cold water. And that night they worked till half-past ten, dipping “fancy starch”—all the frilled and airy, delicate wear of ladies.

text: I'm quitting," he declared. "I won't stand for it. I'm walking away for good. What's the point of me working like a dog all week, saving every minute I can, only for them to come along and dump all these extra starched pieces on me? This is a free country, and I'm going to tell that heavy-set boss exactly what I think of him. I won't be subtle about it, either. Plain English is plenty good enough for me. The nerve of him, sneaking in those extra starch jobs!
source_text: “I’m goin’ to quit,” he announced. “I won’t stand for it. I’m goin’ to quit it cold. What’s the good of me workin’ like a slave all week, a-savin’ minutes, an’ them a-comin’ an’ ringin’ in fancy-starch extras on me? This is a free country, an’ I’m to tell that fat Dutchman what I think of him. An’ I won’t tell ’m in French. Plain United States is good enough for me. Him a-ringin’ in fancy starch extras!”

text: Once more, on his days of rest, Arthur endured the sixty-league trek, seeking to bury the fatigue of the mill beneath the sharper fatigue of the road. After a quarter-year had passed, he made his third descent into the valley with Thomas. In the haze of the tavern, he shed his burdens and felt his pulse quicken; in that awakening, he recognized with startling clarity the creature he was becoming—not through the liquor, but through the labor. The intoxication was a result, not an origin. It pursued the toil as relentlessly as the shadow follows the light. No man could reach the summits by sinking into the state of a draft animal; such was the confession the brandy offered him, and he bowed his head in solemn assent. The spirit was a sage. It unmasked its own purpose to reveal a deeper ruin.
source_text: Again, at the week-ends, he ground out the one hundred and forty miles, obliterating the numbness of too great exertion by the numbness of still greater exertion. At the end of three months he went down a third time to the village with Joe. He forgot, and lived again, and, living, he saw, in clear illumination, the beast he was making of himself—not by the drink, but by the work. The drink was an effect, not a cause. It followed inevitably upon the work, as the night follows upon the day. Not by becoming a toil-beast could he win to the heights, was the message the whiskey whispered to him, and he nodded approbation. The whiskey was wise. It told secrets on itself.

text: I never felt the urge to drink while I was in the hospital. Strange, isn’t it? But after I’ve been slaving away all week, I just have to get loaded. Have you ever noticed how much cooks drink—and bakers, too? It’s the job. They really have no choice. Here, let me cover half the cost of that telegram.
source_text: “I never wanted to drink when I was in hospital. Funny, ain’t it? But when I’ve ben workin’ like a slave all week, I just got to bowl up. Ever noticed that cooks drink like hell?—an’ bakers, too? It’s the work. They’ve sure got to. Here, lemme pay half of that telegram.”

text: “Look at that!” he shouted. “And it’s all mine! It’s wide open. I could lie down under those trees and sleep for a thousand years if I felt like it. Come on, Mart, let's just leave it all behind. What’s the point in waiting another minute? That’s the land of leisure out there, and I’ve got my ticket in hand—and believe me, I’m never coming back!”
source_text: “Just look at it!” he cried. “An’ it’s all mine! It’s free. I can lie down under them trees an’ sleep for a thousan’ years if I want to. Aw, come on, Mart, let’s chuck it. What’s the good of waitin’ another moment. That’s the land of nothin’ to do out there, an’ I got a ticket for it—an’ it ain’t no return ticket, b’gosh!”

text: “It’s clear they never slaved in the steam-rooms at Fernwood Spa,” he remarked with a jagged laugh. “Labor is a virtue, or so the moralists preach, and God knows I’ve never been one to shy away from a hard day’s sweat. Yet even a virtue becomes a vice when taken to excess, and that infernal washhouse was surely the death of it. This is why I’ve signed for one final berth at sea. It shall be my last journey, for upon my return, I intend to force my way into the literary reviews. Of that, I am absolutely sure.”
source_text: “Then they never worked in the laundry at Shelly Hot Springs,” he laughed bitterly. “Toil is a good thing. It is necessary for human health, so all the preachers say, and Heaven knows I’ve never been afraid of it. But there is such a thing as too much of a good thing, and the laundry up there is one of them. And that’s why I’m going to sea one more voyage. It will be my last, I think, for when I come back, I shall break into the magazines. I am certain of it.”

text: “I find him intriguing, I imagine, in the same way one is drawn to a mastiff. There is undeniable merit in his character, yet he possesses traits I could never abide in—in a different sense. I have been contemplating the matter. He is given to oaths, to pipes, and to ale; he has even traded blows in anger—he confessed as much, and with a certain relish. He represents the antithesis of the man I should desire for a—” her voice trailed off into a hush—“consort. Moreover, his strength is overwhelming. My sovereign would be statuesque, lean, and raven-haired—a lithe and enchanting nobleman. No, I am in no peril of losing my heart to Silas Thorne. To do so would be the most wretched calamity imaginable.”
source_text: “He interests me, I suppose, like the bulldog. And there is much good in him, too; but there is much in him that I would not like in—in the other way. You see, I have been thinking. He swears, he smokes, he drinks, he has fought with his fists (he has told me so, and he likes it; he says so). He is all that a man should not be—a man I would want for my—” her voice sank very low—“husband. Then he is too strong. My prince must be tall, and slender, and dark—a graceful, bewitching prince. No, there is no danger of my falling in love with Martin Eden. It would be the worst fate that could befall me.”

text: A peculiar mixture of sorrow and joy settled upon Mrs. Morse. The academic girl, her scholar-daughter, had vanished, and in her stead stood a woman. The gamble had been a triumph. That singular emptiness in Ruth’s character was finally occupied, and the change had been achieved without any lasting harm. This coarse seaman had served as the catalyst; though Ruth did not love him, he had succeeded in awakening her to her own womanhood.
source_text: Mrs. Morse was strangely sad and happy. Her child-daughter, who was a bachelor of arts, was gone; but in her place was a woman-daughter. The experiment had succeeded. The strange void in Ruth’s nature had been filled, and filled without danger or penalty. This rough sailor-fellow had been the instrument, and, though Ruth did not love him, he had made her conscious of her womanhood.

text: “You are well aware that I harbor no secrets from you,” she began. “Yet, on occasion, you must be the one to inquire, as you have done today. I felt the urge to speak, but the path to the words seemed blocked. It is a prideful sort of shyness, I admit, but you have the means to bridge that distance for me. Now and then, you must simply ask; you must grant me the grace of an invitation.”
source_text: “You know I do nothing without telling you,” she began. “—Only, sometimes you must ask me, like this time. I wanted to tell you, but I did not know how. It is false modesty, I know it is that, but you can make it easy for me. Sometimes, like this time, you must ask me, you must give me a chance.”

text: As he moved through the diverse and shifting patterns of a restless world, he had mastered a fundamental principle: when entering an unfamiliar contest, one should always let the opponent lead. This tactic had proved invaluable a thousand times over, refining his ability to watch and wait. He knew how to study the unknown, lingering until a flaw or a point of vulnerability finally showed itself. It was much like a prize-fighter circling the ring, searching for a gap in a guard. And once that opening emerged, his long years of experience taught him to strike at it with absolute conviction.
source_text: In the course of getting acquainted with a varied world, whirling on through the ever changing phases of it, he had learned a rule of conduct which was to the effect that when one played a strange game, he should let the other fellow play first. This had stood him in good stead a thousand times and trained him as an observer as well. He knew how to watch the thing that was strange, and to wait for a weakness, for a place of entrance, to divulge itself. It was like sparring for an opening in fist-fighting. And when such an opening came, he knew by long experience to play for it and to play hard.

text: Muted by his lack of experience and an overwhelming fervor, Martin’s courtship was an unintentional, awkward affair conducted through physical proximity. She found the touch of his hand pleasant—and, in truth, something far more exquisite than mere pleasure. Martin was oblivious to the depth of her feeling, though he could sense that she did not find his touch repulsive. They did not often hold hands, except when greeting or parting, yet opportunities for their hands to graze one another were frequent: while maneuvering their bicycles, fastening the books of poetry they carried into the hills, or studying pages side by side. There were also moments when her hair would brush his cheek or their shoulders would touch as they leaned together over the beauty of the text. She would smile to herself at the sudden, stray impulses to ruffle his hair, while he, when they grew weary of reading, felt a profound desire to rest his head in her lap and dream with closed eyes of the future they would share. During past Sunday picnics at Shellmound and Schuetzen Parks, he had often rested his head on many laps, usually sleeping soundly and selfishly while girls shaded his face from the sun, looking down at him with a love he accepted with a lordly indifference. What had once been the easiest thing in the world now felt entirely out of reach; Ruth’s lap seemed like an impossible destination. Yet his very hesitation was the secret strength of his pursuit. It was this restraint that kept her from feeling alarmed. Being fastidious and shy herself, she remained unaware of the perilous direction their connection was taking. She drifted toward him subtly and unconsciously, while he, sensing that growing proximity, yearned to be bold but remained paralyzed by fear.
source_text: Tongue-tied by inexperience and by excess of ardor, wooing unwittingly and awkwardly, Martin continued his approach by contact. The touch of his hand was pleasant to her, and something deliciously more than pleasant. Martin did not know it, but he did know that it was not distasteful to her. Not that they touched hands often, save at meeting and parting; but that in handling the bicycles, in strapping on the books of verse they carried into the hills, and in conning the pages of books side by side, there were opportunities for hand to stray against hand. And there were opportunities, too, for her hair to brush his cheek, and for shoulder to touch shoulder, as they leaned together over the beauty of the books. She smiled to herself at vagrant impulses which arose from nowhere and suggested that she rumple his hair; while he desired greatly, when they tired of reading, to rest his head in her lap and dream with closed eyes about the future that was to be theirs. On Sunday picnics at Shellmound Park and Schuetzen Park, in the past, he had rested his head on many laps, and, usually, he had slept soundly and selfishly while the girls shaded his face from the sun and looked down and loved him and wondered at his lordly carelessness of their love. To rest his head in a girl’s lap had been the easiest thing in the world until now, and now he found Ruth’s lap inaccessible and impossible. Yet it was right here, in his reticence, that the strength of his wooing lay. It was because of this reticence that he never alarmed her. Herself fastidious and timid, she never awakened to the perilous trend of their intercourse. Subtly and unaware she grew toward him and closer to him, while he, sensing the growing closeness, longed to dare but was afraid.

text: “I believe I can remedy this without resorting to medicine,” Martin replied. “I can’t be certain, naturally, but I’m eager to give it a go. It is a matter of massage, really. I first picked up the technique from the Japanese—they are a people gifted in the art of bodywork, you know. Later, I refined the practice through the Hawaiians, who refer to it as *lomi-lomi*. It achieves much of what pharmaceuticals can do, along with several things they simply cannot.”
source_text: “I can cure it, I think, and without drugs,” was Martin’s answer. “I am not sure, of course, but I’d like to try. It’s simply massage. I learned the trick first from the Japanese. They are a race of masseurs, you know. Then I learned it all over again with variations from the Hawaiians. They call it _lomi-lomi_. It can accomplish most of the things drugs accomplish and a few things that drugs can’t.”

text: The moon had yet to climb the sky, and Elara, peering into the starlit abyss and sharing no word with Julian, was struck by a sudden wave of isolation. She stole a look at him. A gust of wind leaned the craft until the boards were awash, and he, steadying the tiller and the line, eased the sail while searching the darkness for the nearby northern shore. He did not notice her, and she studied him closely, musing on the curious distortion of character that moved a man of such obvious gifts to squander his vitality on the production of tales and verses destined for nothing but failure and the dust of mediocrity.
source_text: The moon had not yet risen, and Ruth, gazing into the starry vault of the sky and exchanging no speech with Martin, experienced a sudden feeling of loneliness. She glanced at him. A puff of wind was heeling the boat over till the deck was awash, and he, one hand on tiller and the other on main-sheet, was luffing slightly, at the same time peering ahead to make out the near-lying north shore. He was unaware of her gaze, and she watched him intently, speculating fancifully about the strange warp of soul that led him, a young man with signal powers, to fritter away his time on the writing of stories and poems foredoomed to mediocrity and failure.

text: A sense of insanity took hold, yet she chose to ignore its implications. Her individual identity faded, replaced by a fundamental feminine yearning; even the gentlest pressure of her weight seemed to fulfill that deep-seated craving. Her exhaustion had vanished completely. Martin remained silent. Had he spoken, the enchantment would have been shattered, but his quiet, loving restraint allowed the magic to endure. He felt lightheaded and overwhelmed, unable to grasp the reality of the situation. The experience felt so sublime that it seemed like a fever dream. He fought back a wild impulse to abandon the ropes and the helm just to pull her into his embrace. Sensing that such an action would be a mistake, he felt relieved that the demands of the boat kept his hands busy, shielding him from the lure of his own impulses. Nevertheless, he handled the vessel with intentional clumsiness, letting the wind escape the sails without shame to stretch out their progress toward the northern bank. Reaching the shore would force him to change course, and their physical connection would be severed. He navigated with such expertise that he slowed their momentum without the wranglers noticing, silently absolving his past grueling journeys for having prepared him for this exquisite night. His hard-won command over the elements and the craft allowed him to guide the boat while she rested beside him, her precious weight leaning upon his shoulder.
source_text: It was a madness, but she refused to consider the madness. She was no longer herself but a woman, with a woman’s clinging need; and though she leaned ever so lightly, the need seemed satisfied. She was no longer tired. Martin did not speak. Had he, the spell would have been broken. But his reticence of love prolonged it. He was dazed and dizzy. He could not understand what was happening. It was too wonderful to be anything but a delirium. He conquered a mad desire to let go sheet and tiller and to clasp her in his arms. His intuition told him it was the wrong thing to do, and he was glad that sheet and tiller kept his hands occupied and fended off temptation. But he luffed the boat less delicately, spilling the wind shamelessly from the sail so as to prolong the tack to the north shore. The shore would compel him to go about, and the contact would be broken. He sailed with skill, stopping way on the boat without exciting the notice of the wranglers, and mentally forgiving his hardest voyages in that they had made this marvellous night possible, giving him mastery over sea and boat and wind so that he could sail with her beside him, her dear weight against him on his shoulder.

text: A magnificent autumn day arrived, warm and slow-moving, charged with the quiet intensity of the shifting season. It was a true California Indian summer, marked by a misty sun and stray currents of air that left the stillness of the atmosphere undisturbed. Ethereal purple hazes, less like vapor and more like tapestries of pure pigment, tucked themselves into the hollows of the hills. San Francisco appeared as a faint smudge of smoke upon its ridges. The bay stretched out between them with the flat luster of liquid metal, its surface dotted with sailboats that sat frozen or wandered with the sluggish tide. Distant Tamalpais, shrouded in a silver glow, loomed beside the Golden Gate, which lay like a shimmering amber road under the sinking sun. Farther out, the vast and shadowy Pacific churned up great heaps of clouds on the horizon, sending them shoreward to signal the first cold gusts of the coming winter.
source_text: Came a beautiful fall day, warm and languid, palpitant with the hush of the changing season, a California Indian summer day, with hazy sun and wandering wisps of breeze that did not stir the slumber of the air. Filmy purple mists, that were not vapors but fabrics woven of color, hid in the recesses of the hills. San Francisco lay like a blur of smoke upon her heights. The intervening bay was a dull sheen of molten metal, whereon sailing craft lay motionless or drifted with the lazy tide. Far Tamalpais, barely seen in the silver haze, bulked hugely by the Golden Gate, the latter a pale gold pathway under the westering sun. Beyond, the Pacific, dim and vast, was raising on its sky-line tumbled cloud-masses that swept landward, giving warning of the first blustering breath of winter.

text: The volume tumbled from his lap to the earth, and they remained in a quiet stupor, looking out across the misty bay with gazes that wandered without seeing. Ruth stole a sidelong glance at his neck. She did not consciously move toward him; rather, she was pulled by an external power more compelling than gravity, as inevitable as destiny itself. It was a distance of only an inch, and she traversed it without any act of will. Her shoulder grazed his as softly as a butterfly alights on a blossom, and he returned the pressure with equal gentleness. She felt the weight of his shoulder against hers and the shudder that vibrated through him. This was her chance to pull away, but she had become a creature of instinct. Her movements had escaped her command—she was oblivious to restraint in the midst of this exquisite madness. His arm began to wind slowly behind her. She anticipated its gradual reach in a state of agonizing bliss. She waited for something she could not name, her breath coming short, her lips dry and burning, her pulse racing with a fever of expectation. The encircling arm rose and drew her in, pulling her close with a slow, tender grace. She could endure the suspense no longer. With a faint sigh and a sudden, unbidden impulse, she let her head sink onto his chest. He leaned down at once, and as his lips descended, hers rose eagerly to meet them.
source_text: The book slipped from his hands to the ground, and they sat idly and silently, gazing out over the dreamy bay with eyes that dreamed and did not see. Ruth glanced sidewise at his neck. She did not lean toward him. She was drawn by some force outside of herself and stronger than gravitation, strong as destiny. It was only an inch to lean, and it was accomplished without volition on her part. Her shoulder touched his as lightly as a butterfly touches a flower, and just as lightly was the counter-pressure. She felt his shoulder press hers, and a tremor run through him. Then was the time for her to draw back. But she had become an automaton. Her actions had passed beyond the control of her will—she never thought of control or will in the delicious madness that was upon her. His arm began to steal behind her and around her. She waited its slow progress in a torment of delight. She waited, she knew not for what, panting, with dry, burning lips, a leaping pulse, and a fever of expectancy in all her blood. The girdling arm lifted higher and drew her toward him, drew her slowly and caressingly. She could wait no longer. With a tired sigh, and with an impulsive movement all her own, unpremeditated, spasmodic, she rested her head upon his breast. His head bent over swiftly, and, as his lips approached, hers flew to meet them.

text: He tried to pull her closer once more, though the arm around her moved only slightly, held back by a fear of being too demanding. Then he felt her soften against him, and as she returned to his embrace, their lips met in another kiss.
source_text: He offered to draw her toward him again, but it was no more than a tentative muscular movement of the girdling arm, for he feared that he might be greedy. Then he felt her body yielding, and once again she was close in his arms and lips were pressed on lips.

text: To Martin Eden’s profound astonishment, she dissolved into a fit of weeping that required many kisses and tender embraces to soothe. All the while, a line from Kipling echoed through his mind: “The Colonel’s lady and Judy O’Grady are sisters under their skins.” It was a revelation; the novels he had consumed had led him to believe in a different reality. He had been under the impression that among the elite, only formal proposals were acceptable. In the rough world of his origin, it was natural for young men and women to win one another through physical closeness, but he had imagined such behavior impossible for the exalted figures on the social heights. Yet the books were wrong, and here was the evidence. The same silent pressures and caresses that were effective with working-class girls were equally potent with those of the high bourgeoisie. They were all made of the same substance, after all—sisters beneath the surface—and he might have realized as much had he applied his Spencerian studies. As he held Ruth and comforted her, he found great peace in the thought that the lady and the commoner were essentially identical. It brought her closer to him, making her seem attainable. Her skin was merely human flesh, like his own. There was no true barrier to their union; class was merely an external layer that could be shed. He recalled reading of slaves who had ascended to the Roman purple; if such a feat were possible, he could surely rise to her level. Despite her refined air and spiritual grace, she was, in her fundamental humanity, no different from Lizzie Connolly or any other girl of the streets. She was capable of the same love, the same fury, and certainly the same jealousy she was currently expressing through her final, lingering sobs in his arms.
source_text: And to Martin Eden’s supreme surprise, she burst into a storm of tears that took more kisses than one and many caresses to drive away. And all the while there was running through his head Kipling’s line: “_And the Colonel’s lady and Judy O’Grady are sisters under their skins_.” It was true, he decided; though the novels he had read had led him to believe otherwise. His idea, for which the novels were responsible, had been that only formal proposals obtained in the upper classes. It was all right enough, down whence he had come, for youths and maidens to win each other by contact; but for the exalted personages up above on the heights to make love in similar fashion had seemed unthinkable. Yet the novels were wrong. Here was a proof of it. The same pressures and caresses, unaccompanied by speech, that were efficacious with the girls of the working-class, were equally efficacious with the girls above the working-class. They were all of the same flesh, after all, sisters under their skins; and he might have known as much himself had he remembered his Spencer. As he held Ruth in his arms and soothed her, he took great consolation in the thought that the Colonel’s lady and Judy O’Grady were pretty much alike under their skins. It brought Ruth closer to him, made her possible. Her dear flesh was as anybody’s flesh, as his flesh. There was no bar to their marriage. Class difference was the only difference, and class was extrinsic. It could be shaken off. A slave, he had read, had risen to the Roman purple. That being so, then he could rise to Ruth. Under her purity, and saintliness, and culture, and ethereal beauty of soul, she was, in things fundamentally human, just like Lizzie Connolly and all Lizzie Connollys. All that was possible of them was possible of her. She could love, and hate, maybe have hysterics; and she could certainly be jealous, as she was jealous now, uttering her last sobs in his arms.

text: “You know, this time away has really granted me some clarity. I haven’t been draining my physical strength with labor, and I’ve stepped back from writing—at least, I haven't written anything intended for an audience. My days have been occupied solely with my devotion to you and my own private thoughts. I’ve read a little, though mostly just magazines to supplement my musings. I’ve been reflecting on my own nature, the world at large, and whether I can ever secure a position that is worthy of you. I’ve also been studying Spencer’s ‘Philosophy of Style,’ which helped me identify exactly what was missing from my work—or rather, what was wrong with it, and what is wrong with almost everything being published in the magazines these days.”
source_text: “You see, this vacation I have taken has given me perspective. I haven’t been working the life out of my body, and I haven’t been writing, at least not for publication. All I’ve done has been to love you and to think. I’ve read some, too, but it has been part of my thinking, and I have read principally magazines. I have generalized about myself, and the world, my place in it, and my chance to win to a place that will be fit for you. Also, I’ve been reading Spencer’s ‘Philosophy of Style,’ and found out a lot of what was the matter with me—or my writing, rather; and for that matter with most of the writing that is published every month in the magazines.”

text: The fact that Ruth doubted his talent as an author did not change Martin’s feelings or lower her in his estimation. During a brief respite from his work, he spent many hours looking inward, learning a great deal about his own character. He realized that he cherished beauty far more than he desired status, and that his ambition was mostly for Ruth’s benefit. This was the source of his powerful drive. He wanted to achieve public greatness—to "make it," as he put it—so that the woman he loved would be proud of him and consider him worthy of her.
source_text: That Ruth had little faith in his power as a writer, did not alter her nor diminish her in Martin’s eyes. In the breathing spell of the vacation he had taken, he had spent many hours in self-analysis, and thereby learned much of himself. He had discovered that he loved beauty more than fame, and that what desire he had for fame was largely for Ruth’s sake. It was for this reason that his desire for fame was strong. He wanted to be great in the world’s eyes; “to make good,” as he expressed it, in order that the woman he loved should be proud of him and deem him worthy.

text: He possessed a profound adoration for beauty, finding that the mere privilege of its service was reward enough. But his love for Ruth was deeper still. In his eyes, love was the world's most noble pursuit. It was the catalyst for his own radical transformation, the power that had molded a coarse sailor into a man of letters and art; thus, he valued love above all else, placing it higher than either scholarship or creative talent. He had already come to see that his intellect surpassed Ruth’s, just as it did those of her father and brothers. Despite her prestigious university background and her formal degree, his inherent mental force eclipsed her own. The brief period he had spent educating himself had provided him with a profound understanding of life, art, and human affairs—a mastery she could never truly hope to attain.
source_text: As for himself, he loved beauty passionately, and the joy of serving her was to him sufficient wage. And more than beauty he loved Ruth. He considered love the finest thing in the world. It was love that had worked the revolution in him, changing him from an uncouth sailor to a student and an artist; therefore, to him, the finest and greatest of the three, greater than learning and artistry, was love. Already he had discovered that his brain went beyond Ruth’s, just as it went beyond the brains of her brothers, or the brain of her father. In spite of every advantage of university training, and in the face of her bachelorship of arts, his power of intellect overshadowed hers, and his year or so of self-study and equipment gave him a mastery of the affairs of the world and art and life that she could never hope to possess.

text: Julian perceived these discrepancies, yet they failed to erode his adoration for Elena or her devotion to him. Love was a thing too exalted, and his own loyalty too profound, for him to tarnish it with petty critique. What relevance had her differing views on aesthetics, moral rectitude, the Terror, or universal franchise? These were products of the intellect, but love resided in a sphere beyond the reach of logic; it was transcendental. He would not debase it. He venerated it. Love occupied the alpine heights, far above the shadowed lowlands of rationality. It was a distilled state of being, the very crest of human experience, and its arrival was a rare providence. Influenced by the empirical philosophers he studied, he recognized the biological imperatives of desire; yet, by a sophisticated extension of that same logic, he concluded that the human soul attained its crowning glory in love. It was not a thing to be scrutinized, but a prize to be welcomed as life’s ultimate reward. To him, the lover was the most hallowed of creatures, and he found a fierce joy in the image of the "divinely intoxicated suitor," soaring above mundane concerns—above fortune and reputation, consensus and acclaim—surpassing the boundaries of breath to perish upon a kiss.
source_text: All this he realized, but it did not affect his love for her, nor her love for him. Love was too fine and noble, and he was too loyal a lover for him to besmirch love with criticism. What did love have to do with Ruth’s divergent views on art, right conduct, the French Revolution, or equal suffrage? They were mental processes, but love was beyond reason; it was superrational. He could not belittle love. He worshipped it. Love lay on the mountain-tops beyond the valley-land of reason. It was a sublimated condition of existence, the topmost peak of living, and it came rarely. Thanks to the school of scientific philosophers he favored, he knew the biological significance of love; but by a refined process of the same scientific reasoning he reached the conclusion that the human organism achieved its highest purpose in love, that love must not be questioned, but must be accepted as the highest guerdon of life. Thus, he considered the lover blessed over all creatures, and it was a delight to him to think of “God’s own mad lover,” rising above the things of earth, above wealth and judgment, public opinion and applause, rising above life itself and “dying on a kiss.”

text: The weeks ebbed away. Silas’s funds evaporated, and the arrival of an editor’s check seemed more a myth than a prospect. His major works had all been rejected and promptly recirculated, yet his commercial drudgery found no kinder reception. The narrow shelves of his pantry were soon stripped of their meager variety. Stranded with a partial bag of oats and a handful of withered plums, he found himself dining on that monotonous fare thrice daily for five days straight. At last, he attempted to trade upon his reputation for honesty. The local merchant, who had previously known only the color of Silas’s coin, refused further charity when the outstanding balance reached the grand total of four dollars and fifteen cents.
source_text: The weeks passed. Martin ran out of money, and publishers’ checks were far away as ever. All his important manuscripts had come back and been started out again, and his hack-work fared no better. His little kitchen was no longer graced with a variety of foods. Caught in the pinch with a part sack of rice and a few pounds of dried apricots, rice and apricots was his menu three times a day for five days hand-running. Then he startled to realize on his credit. The Portuguese grocer, to whom he had hitherto paid cash, called a halt when Martin’s bill reached the magnificent total of three dollars and eighty-five cents.

text: Eventually, he pawned his watch and later his bicycle, further depleting his food money to cover the cost of mailing out his manuscripts. He was discouraged by the lack of interest in his commercial writing; there were simply no buyers. When he measured his work against the content of newspapers, weekly journals, and pulp magazines, he was convinced his was better—significantly higher in quality than the norm—yet it remained unsold. He soon discovered that much of the press utilized syndicated "boilerplate" content and obtained the address for the association that provided it. The pieces he submitted were returned with a form letter, informing him that their own staff produced all the copy they required.
source_text: Later on he pawned his watch, and still later his wheel, reducing the amount available for food by putting stamps on all his manuscripts and sending them out. He was disappointed with his hack-work. Nobody cared to buy. He compared it with what he found in the newspapers, weeklies, and cheap magazines, and decided that his was better, far better, than the average; yet it would not sell. Then he discovered that most of the newspapers printed a great deal of what was called “plate” stuff, and he got the address of the association that furnished it. His own work that he sent in was returned, along with a stereotyped slip informing him that the staff supplied all the copy that was needed.

text: “You’ve missed the mark,” Clara insisted. “Every creative medium is defined by its own inherent constraints.” (She found herself summoning the memory of a symposium she had attended at the conservatory concerning the formal artifice of the humanities.) “Consider a portrait: it offers only a flat surface, yet you concede to the impression of depth and volume that the master’s hand projects upon it. In literature, similarly, the narrator must possess a divine insight. You regard it as quite proper for a writer to detail the hidden ruminations of a heroine, even though you are aware she was entirely isolated, and that neither the chronicler nor any witness could have possibly perceived her mind. This holds true for the theater, for marble, for the lyric stage—for every craft. One must simply yield to certain unavoidable fictions.”
source_text: “But you misunderstand,” Ruth protested. “Every form of art has its limitations.” (She was busy recalling a lecture she had heard at the university on the conventions of the arts.) “In painting there are only two dimensions to the canvas, yet you accept the illusion of three dimensions which the art of a painter enables him to throw into the canvas. In writing, again, the author must be omnipotent. You accept as perfectly legitimate the author’s account of the secret thoughts of the heroine, and yet all the time you know that the heroine was alone when thinking these thoughts, and that neither the author nor any one else was capable of hearing them. And so with the stage, with sculpture, with opera, with every art form. Certain irreconcilable things must be accepted.”

text: “No, not even for an instant. I am simply asserting my independence as an individual. I have only shared my thoughts to clarify why the clumsy, heavy-footed antics of Madame Tetralani ruin the symphonic experience for me. Every music critic in the world may be correct. But I am myself, and I refuse to surrender my own sensibilities to the collective opinion of mankind. If I find something unappealing, I simply do; there is no reason on earth why I should mimic a fondness for it just because the majority of people claim to enjoy it. I cannot allow popular fashion to dictate my personal tastes.”
source_text: “No, no, not for a moment. I merely maintain my right as an individual. I have just been telling you what I think, in order to explain why the elephantine gambols of Madame Tetralani spoil the orchestra for me. The world’s judges of music may all be right. But I am I, and I won’t subordinate my taste to the unanimous judgment of mankind. If I don’t like a thing, I don’t like it, that’s all; and there is no reason under the sun why I should ape a liking for it just because the majority of my fellow-creatures like it, or make believe they like it. I can’t follow the fashions in the things I like or dislike.”

text: The following morning, Martin Eden set aside his trivial commercial writing and, in a surge of creative intensity, forged an essay he called “The Philosophy of Illusion.” A postage stamp sent it on its way, though it was doomed to require many more stamps and endure a long succession of such journeys in the months to come.
source_text: And next day Martin Eden cast hack-work aside, and at white heat hammered out an essay to which he gave the title, “The Philosophy of Illusion.” A stamp started it on its travels, but it was destined to receive many stamps and to be started on many travels in the months that followed.

text: Elena Thorne lived in the marrow of indigence, intimately acquainted with its every indignity. To Clara, however, the word "poverty" denoted merely an unseemly state of affairs, a vague abstraction that marked the limit of her understanding. She was cognizant of Julian’s penury, yet in her imagination, she aligned his struggles with the humble origins of Benjamin Franklin or the early trials of industrial titans who had eventually triumphed. Furthermore, while she conceded that a lack of means was hardly pleasant, she clung to a genteel conviction that such hardship was character-building—a necessary goad that prodded any man of merit, provided he wasn't a spiritless laborer, toward achievement. Consequently, the revelation that Julian had surrendered his timepiece and winter coat to a moneylender caused her no distress. She viewed these sacrifices as promising developments, trusting that the sting of want would finally awaken his senses and force him to relinquish his literary delusions for a more practical calling.
source_text: Maria Silva was poor, and all the ways of poverty were clear to her. Poverty, to Ruth, was a word signifying a not-nice condition of existence. That was her total knowledge on the subject. She knew Martin was poor, and his condition she associated in her mind with the boyhood of Abraham Lincoln, of Mr. Butler, and of other men who had become successes. Also, while aware that poverty was anything but delectable, she had a comfortable middle-class feeling that poverty was salutary, that it was a sharp spur that urged on to success all men who were not degraded and hopeless drudges. So that her knowledge that Martin was so poor that he had pawned his watch and overcoat did not disturb her. She even considered it the hopeful side of the situation, believing that sooner or later it would arouse him and compel him to abandon his writing.

text: She observed his relentless labor and knew well the late hours he kept. Work was his life; she saw that he exceeded even her own industry, though his tasks were of a different kind. It astonished her to see that the more he starved, the more feverishly he worked. Occasionally, when she sensed his hunger was most desperate, she would bring him a fresh loaf of bread, disguising her kindness with clumsy jokes about his poor baking. At other times, she sent one of her small children to him with a large pitcher of hot soup, all the while wondering if she could truly justify taking food from her own family. Martin was deeply appreciative, understanding the reality of the poor and knowing that if true charity existed anywhere, it was found in such a sacrifice.
source_text: Likewise she watched his toils, and knew the measure of the midnight oil he burned. Work! She knew that he outdid her, though his work was of a different order. And she was surprised to behold that the less food he had, the harder he worked. On occasion, in a casual sort of way, when she thought hunger pinched hardest, she would send him in a loaf of new baking, awkwardly covering the act with banter to the effect that it was better than he could bake. And again, she would send one of her toddlers in to him with a great pitcher of hot soup, debating inwardly the while whether she was justified in taking it from the mouths of her own flesh and blood. Nor was Martin ungrateful, knowing as he did the lives of the poor, and that if ever in the world there was charity, this was it.

text: “So then—” She inhaled deeply, with the guileless air of a child, as she gave utterance to the furthest reaches of her ambition. “I desire a dairy farm—a prosperous dairy farm. Many cows, vast acreage, and rich, green grass. I would have it near San Lorenzo, for my sister resides there. I would sell my milk in the city of Oakland and amass a great fortune. Marco and Luca would not labor with the herd; they would attend their lessons. In time, they would become skilled engineers for the great railroad. Yes, that is the life I crave: the dairy farm.”
source_text: “Well, den—” She drew a big breath like a child, as she voiced to the uttermost all she cared to demand of life. “I lika da have one milka ranch—good milka ranch. Plenty cow, plenty land, plenty grass. I lika da have near San Le-an; my sister liva dere. I sella da milk in Oakland. I maka da plentee mon. Joe an’ Nick no runna da cow. Dey go-a to school. Bimeby maka da good engineer, worka da railroad. Yes, I lika da milka ranch.”

text: Drawing upon the meager bounty of his own future, Martin finally carried his only respectable suit to the pawnshop. His circumstances were dire enough to demand such a sacrifice, even though it meant severing his connection to Ruth. He owned no other presentable clothing, and while he could still frequent the local shops or visit his sister, he would never dare to set foot in the Morse home in such a shameful state of dress.
source_text: And from such largess, dispensed from his future, Martin turned and took his one good suit of clothes to the pawnshop. His plight was desperate for him to do this, for it cut him off from Ruth. He had no second-best suit that was presentable, and though he could go to the butcher and the baker, and even on occasion to his sister’s, it was beyond all daring to dream of entering the Morse home so disreputably apparelled.

text: This was undeniably a triumph. The envelope was far too slim to contain a returned manuscript, which could only mean an acceptance. He thought of the specific piece the *Transcontinental* held: "The Ring of Bells," a horror story totaling exactly five thousand words. Because prestigious magazines paid upon acceptance, a check surely waited inside. At two cents per word, the payment would be a hundred dollars. A hundred dollars! As he ripped the paper open, his various debts flashed through his mind: $3.85 for the grocer, a flat $4.00 for the butcher, $2.00 for the baker, and $5.00 for the fruit stand—$14.85 in all. Then came the rent at $2.50, plus another $2.50 for the coming month; the typewriter rental was $8.00 in arrears and $4.00 for the next month, bringing the tally to $31.85. Finally, he accounted for his pawned goods and their interest: his watch, overcoat, and suit at $5.50 each, and his bicycle at $7.75. Even with sixty percent interest, the grand total was only $56.10. He could almost see the numbers glowing in the air, performing the subtraction that left a remainder of $43.90. After clearing every debt and reclaiming every possession, he would still have a princely $43.90 in his pocket, with his room and typewriter secured for another month.
source_text: Of course this was good news. There was no manuscript in that thin envelope, therefore it was an acceptance. He knew the story in the hands of the _Transcontinental_. It was “The Ring of Bells,” one of his horror stories, and it was an even five thousand words. And, since first-class magazines always paid on acceptance, there was a check inside. Two cents a word—twenty dollars a thousand; the check must be a hundred dollars. One hundred dollars! As he tore the envelope open, every item of all his debts surged in his brain—$3.85 to the grocer; butcher $4.00 flat; baker, $2.00; fruit store, $5.00; total, $14.85. Then there was room rent, $2.50; another month in advance, $2.50; two months’ type-writer, $8.00; a month in advance, $4.00; total, $31.85. And finally to be added, his pledges, plus interest, with the pawnbroker—watch, $5.50; overcoat, $5.50; wheel, $7.75; suit of clothes, $5.50 (60 % interest, but what did it matter?)—grand total, $56.10. He saw, as if visible in the air before him, in illuminated figures, the whole sum, and the subtraction that followed and that gave a remainder of $43.90. When he had squared every debt, redeemed every pledge, he would still have jingling in his pockets a princely $43.90. And on top of that he would have a month’s rent paid in advance on the type-writer and on the room.

text: Martin was unaccustomed to being ill, and once Maria and her daughter had gone, he attempted to rise and get dressed. Through an immense effort of will, his head spinning and his eyes throbbing so intensely he couldn't keep them open, he forced himself out of bed, only to collapse against the table as his strength failed him. It took another half hour for him to struggle back to the mattress, where he was happy to lie still with his eyes shut, taking stock of his various aches and exhaustion. Maria checked on him several times to refresh the cold compresses on his forehead. Otherwise, she left him alone, having the sense not to bother him with idle talk. This filled him with a quiet appreciation, and he muttered under his breath, “Maria, you’re going to get that dairy farm, for sure, for sure.”
source_text: Martin was not used to sickness, and when Maria and her little girl left him, he essayed to get up and dress. By a supreme exertion of will, with rearing brain and eyes that ached so that he could not keep them open, he managed to get out of bed, only to be left stranded by his senses upon the table. Half an hour later he managed to regain the bed, where he was content to lie with closed eyes and analyze his various pains and weaknesses. Maria came in several times to change the cold cloths on his forehead. Otherwise she left him in peace, too wise to vex him with chatter. This moved him to gratitude, and he murmured to himself, “Maria, you getta da milka ranch, all righta, all right.”

text: It felt like an age since that letter from the *Transcontinental* had reached him, an eternity since that door had closed and a new chapter begun. He had given everything he had, holding nothing back, and now he lay broken. Had he not been so weakened by hunger, the influenza would never have claimed him. He was simply too exhausted to repel the sickness that had seized his frame. This was the inevitable consequence.
source_text: It seemed a life-time since he had received that letter from the _Transcontinental_, a life-time since it was all over and done with and a new page turned. He had shot his bolt, and shot it hard, and now he was down on his back. If he hadn’t starved himself, he wouldn’t have been caught by La Grippe. He had been run down, and he had not had the strength to throw off the germ of disease which had invaded his system. This was what resulted.

text: The light had grown sufficient for Arthur to make out the script, and the strain of it no longer registered against his exhaustion. *The Silver Shroud* was proposing thirty-five dollars for "The Maelstrom," a piece from his first forays into the gothic. He traced the sentences once more, then again. The correspondent admitted frankly that the prose lacked polish, yet the core premise was striking enough to warrant a sale. If he would permit them to excise a third of the text, the check would be mailed as soon as his confirmation reached their desk.
source_text: He could see to read, now, and he was unaware of the pain of the action. It was the _White Mouse_ that was offering him forty dollars, and the story was “The Whirlpool,” another of his early horror stories. He read the letter through again and again. The editor told him plainly that he had not handled the idea properly, but that it was the idea they were buying because it was original. If they could cut the story down one-third, they would take it and send him forty dollars on receipt of his answer.

text: Once Teresa had taken the letter to the mailbox, Martin reclined and began to reflect. As it turned out, the reports were accurate. The *White Mouse* issued payment upon accepting a piece. "The Whirlpool" ran three thousand words. If he trimmed it by a third, two thousand words would remain. A forty-dollar payment for that length meant a rate of two cents per word. Payment upon acceptance at two cents a word—the press had been honest. To think he had dismissed the *White Mouse* as a low-tier publication! Clearly, he lacked a true understanding of the magazine market. He had considered the *Transcontinental* a top-tier journal, yet it only offered a penny for every ten words. He had written off the *White Mouse* as insignificant, yet it paid twenty times the rate of the *Transcontinental* and provided immediate payment upon acceptance.
source_text: The letter despatched to the letter-box by Teresa, Martin lay back and thought. It wasn’t a lie, after all. The _White Mouse_ paid on acceptance. There were three thousand words in “The Whirlpool.” Cut down a third, there would be two thousand. At forty dollars that would be two cents a word. Pay on acceptance and two cents a word—the newspapers had told the truth. And he had thought the _White Mouse_ a third-rater! It was evident that he did not know the magazines. He had deemed the _Transcontinental_ a first-rater, and it paid a cent for ten words.
