He had classed the _White Mouse_ as of no account, and it paid twenty times as much as the_ Transcontinental_ and also had paid on acceptance.

text: Ruth managed to weave through the cramped quarters, navigating the narrow gap between the table and bed to reach Martin. Arthur, however, swung too wide and collided with a noisy crash into the pots and pans in Martin’s kitchen corner. He did not linger. With Ruth occupying the solitary chair and his escort duty finished, he retreated to the gate. There he stood, the focal point for seven wide-eyed Silvas who watched him like a carnival attraction. Surrounding the carriage were children from the entire neighborhood, all waiting eagerly for some dark or dramatic climax. In these streets, carriages appeared only for weddings or deaths. Since this was neither, it represented something beyond their understanding and was well worth the wait.
source_text: Ruth succeeded in veering right and left and right again, and in running the narrow passage between table and bed to Martin’s side; but Arthur veered too wide and fetched up with clatter and bang of pots and pans in the corner where Martin did his cooking. Arthur did not linger long. Ruth occupied the only chair, and having done his duty, he went outside and stood by the gate, the centre of seven marvelling Silvas, who watched him as they would have watched a curiosity in a side-show. All about the carriage were gathered the children from a dozen blocks, waiting and eager for some tragic and terrible dénouement. Carriages were seen on their street only for weddings and funerals. Here was neither marriage nor death: therefore, it was something transcending experience and well worth waiting for.

text: “No, it was for less than a week’s labor. Please hand me that gray-covered ledger on the far corner of the table.” He opened the book and began leafing through the pages at a brisk pace. “Yes, I was right. Four days for ‘The Ring of Bells’ and two for ‘The Whirlpool.’ That totals forty-five dollars for a week of work, or one hundred and eighty dollars a month. That exceeds any salary I could currently earn. Besides, I’m only just starting out. A thousand dollars a month isn't too much to aim for if I want to provide you with everything you deserve. Even a salary of five hundred a month would be insufficient. That forty-five dollars is merely the beginning. Just wait until I find my rhythm. Then you’ll see what I can really do.”
source_text: “No, for less than a week’s work. Please pass me that book over on the far corner of the table, the account book with the gray cover.” He opened it and began turning over the pages rapidly. “Yes, I was right. Four days for ‘The Ring of Bells,’ two days for ‘The Whirlpool.’ That’s forty-five dollars for a week’s work, one hundred and eighty dollars a month. That beats any salary I can command. And, besides, I’m just beginning. A thousand dollars a month is not too much to buy for you all I want you to have. A salary of five hundred a month would be too small. That forty-five dollars is just a starter. Wait till I get my stride. Then watch my smoke.”

text: I simply wasn't aware," he replied. "The possibility of lepers never entered my mind. After I abandoned the schooner and reached the shore, I struck out for the interior, looking for a place to hide. For three days, I sustained myself on wild guavas, *ohia*-apples, and bananas gathered from the jungle. On the fourth day, I stumbled upon a path—a simple foot-trail. It climbed steadily as it led further inland. This was the direction I intended to take, and the trail showed evidence of recent use. At one point, it followed the spine of a ridge that was as narrow as a knife's edge. The path atop the crest was less than three feet wide, with vertical cliffs on either side dropping hundreds of feet into the depths. A lone man, well-supplied with ammunition, could have defended that position against a hundred thousand.
source_text: “Because I didn’t know,” he answered. “I never dreamed of lepers. When I deserted the schooner and landed on the beach, I headed inland for some place of hiding. For three days I lived off guavas, _ohia_-apples, and bananas, all of which grew wild in the jungle. On the fourth day I found the trail—a mere foot-trail. It led inland, and it led up. It was the way I wanted to go, and it showed signs of recent travel. At one place it ran along the crest of a ridge that was no more than a knife-edge. The trail wasn’t three feet wide on the crest, and on either side the ridge fell away in precipices hundreds of feet deep. One man, with plenty of ammunition, could have held it against a hundred thousand.

text: I am a foolish thing," she murmured with a melancholy air. "Yet I cannot master my feelings. I am so deeply devoted to you, Julian—truly, I am. Perhaps I shall learn a more universal grace in time, but for now, I cannot help but envy the phantoms of your earlier years. And you know well, Julian, how crowded your history is with such spirits.
source_text: “I’m such a silly,” she said plaintively. “But I can’t help it. I do so love you, Martin, I do, I do. I shall grow more catholic in time, but at present I can’t help being jealous of those ghosts of the past, and you know your past is full of ghosts.”

text: Martin’s fortunes finally began to brighten. The day following Ruth’s visit, he received a check for three dollars from a New York scandal weekly in payment for three of his short poems. Two days later, a Chicago newspaper accepted his piece “Treasure Hunters,” promising ten dollars once it was printed. While the fee was small, the work represented his first real article, his very first venture into articulating his ideas for a public audience. To crown this run of luck, his second literary effort—an adventure serial for boys—was accepted by a juvenile magazine called *Youth and Age* before the week’s end. The manuscript was twenty-one thousand words long, and the sixteen dollars offered upon publication broke down to a meager seventy-five cents per thousand words. Nevertheless, Martin accepted the terms, well aware that it was only his second piece of work and that it was, in truth, a clumsy and meritless endeavor.
source_text: The sun of Martin’s good fortune rose. The day after Ruth’s visit, he received a check for three dollars from a New York scandal weekly in payment for three of his triolets. Two days later a newspaper published in Chicago accepted his “Treasure Hunters,” promising to pay ten dollars for it on publication. The price was small, but it was the first article he had written, his very first attempt to express his thought on the printed page. To cap everything, the adventure serial for boys, his second attempt, was accepted before the end of the week by a juvenile monthly calling itself _Youth and Age_. It was true the serial was twenty-one thousand words, and they offered to pay him sixteen dollars on publication, which was something like seventy-five cents a thousand words; but it was equally true that it was the second thing he had attempted to write and that he was himself thoroughly aware of its clumsy worthlessness.

text: To him, this modest amount felt like a windfall. The moment he reclaimed his wardrobe, he set off to see Ruth, unable to resist the urge to rattle the small collection of silver in his pocket along the way. He had lived in poverty for so long that he resembled a starving person who, once rescued, refuses to let a single scrap of food out of their sight; Martin simply could not keep his hand off the coins. This wasn't out of greed or a miserly nature, but because the money represented something far greater than its face value. It was the physical embodiment of his achievement, and the eagles engraved on the currency appeared to him as symbols of triumph.
source_text: In itself, this small sum seemed a fortune. Immediately on recovering his clothes he had gone to see Ruth, and on the way he could not refrain from jingling the little handful of silver in his pocket. He had been so long without money that, like a rescued starving man who cannot let the unconsumed food out of his sight, Martin could not keep his hand off the silver. He was not mean, nor avaricious, but the money meant more than so many dollars and cents. It stood for success, and the eagles stamped upon the coins were to him so many winged victories.

text: A subtle realization dawned on him that the world was, in fact, a magnificent place. It certainly looked far lovelier than it had before. For weeks, his surroundings had seemed bleak and oppressive; yet now, with his debts mostly settled, a few dollars rattling in his pocket, and the pride of accomplishment in his heart, the sun felt brilliantly warm. Even a sudden burst of rain that drenched unprepared pedestrians struck him as a joyful occurrence. When he had been hungry, he had agonized over the millions of others starving across the globe; now that he was well-fed, their suffering no longer weighed upon his mind. He forgot them entirely, and, being in love, thought instead of the world's countless lovers. Unbidden, the themes for new love poems began to stir within his imagination. Carried away by this creative fervor, he stepped off the streetcar two blocks past his stop, feeling no annoyance at the mistake.
source_text: It came to him insensibly that it was a very good world. It certainly appeared more beautiful to him. For weeks it had been a very dull and sombre world; but now, with nearly all debts paid, three dollars jingling in his pocket, and in his mind the consciousness of success, the sun shone bright and warm, and even a rain-squall that soaked unprepared pedestrians seemed a merry happening to him. When he starved, his thoughts had dwelt often upon the thousands he knew were starving the world over; but now that he was feasted full, the fact of the thousands starving was no longer pregnant in his brain. He forgot about them, and, being in love, remembered the countless lovers in the world. Without deliberately thinking about it, _motifs_ for love-lyrics began to agitate his brain. Swept away by the creative impulse, he got off the electric car, without vexation, two blocks beyond his crossing.

text: Arthur stepped into the Sterling parlor to find a gathering already in progress. Mrs. Sterling had used the visit of Clara’s cousins from the coast as a convenient shield for her true intention: drowning Clara’s interest in Arthur with a sea of ambitious young men. This social siege had commenced during Arthur’s recent travels and had reached a fever pitch. The hostess was insistent on showcasing men of tangible merit. Beyond the cousins, Eleanor and Grace, Arthur was introduced to two scholars—one of Greek and the other of Rhetoric; a lieutenant of the cavalry fresh from the island territories and an old childhood friend of Clara’s; a young man named Sterling, the personal assistant to the president of the Pacific Trust; and lastly, a prosperous bank manager, Frederick Low, a vigorous man of thirty-six. Low was a graduate of the state college, a member of the Metropolitan and Civic clubs, and a frequent stump speaker for the conservative ticket—a man, in every sense, on the ascent. The female contingent included a portrait painter, a cellist, and a Doctor of Social Science famous for her missions among the urban poor. But these women were peripheral to Mrs. Sterling’s design. They were the necessary scenery, the bait intended to attract the men of consequence she desired for her daughter.
source_text: He found a number of persons in the Morse home. Ruth’s two girl-cousins were visiting her from San Rafael, and Mrs. Morse, under pretext of entertaining them, was pursuing her plan of surrounding Ruth with young people. The campaign had begun during Martin’s enforced absence, and was already in full swing. She was making a point of having at the house men who were doing things. Thus, in addition to the cousins Dorothy and Florence, Martin encountered two university professors, one of Latin, the other of English; a young army officer just back from the Philippines, one-time school-mate of Ruth’s; a young fellow named Melville, private secretary to Joseph Perkins, head of the San Francisco Trust Company; and finally of the men, a live bank cashier, Charles Hapgood, a youngish man of thirty-five, graduate of Stanford University, member of the Nile Club and the Unity Club, and a conservative speaker for the Republican Party during campaigns—in short, a rising young man in every way. Among the women was one who painted portraits, another who was a professional musician, and still another who possessed the degree of Doctor of Sociology and who was locally famous for her social settlement work in the slums of San Francisco. But the women did not count for much in Mrs. Morse’s plan. At the best, they were necessary accessories. The men who did things must be drawn to the house somehow.

text: Ruth’s gaze drifted toward him often, struck by a mix of wonder and relief at the fluency with which he charmed her cousins. He remained remarkably composed, and being seated spared him the usual self-consciousness regarding his powerful frame. Though Ruth knew her cousins to be sharp-witted and bright—if only on the surface—she found their later enthusiasm for Martin almost difficult to fathom. Yet Martin, a seasoned wag among his own peers and a master of banter at social outings, found this playful verbal sparring simple enough to navigate. That evening, a sense of triumph seemed to steady him, offering a reassuring hand that allowed him to joke and laugh with an easy, undeterred grace.
source_text: Ruth’s eyes roved to him frequently to see how he was getting on, and she was surprised and gladdened by the ease with which he got acquainted with her cousins. He certainly did not grow excited, while being seated removed from him the worry of his shoulders. Ruth knew them for clever girls, superficially brilliant, and she could scarcely understand their praise of Martin later that night at going to bed. But he, on the other hand, a wit in his own class, a gay quizzer and laughter-maker at dances and Sunday picnics, had found the making of fun and the breaking of good-natured lances simple enough in this environment. And on this evening success stood at his back, patting him on the shoulder and telling him that he was making good, so that he could afford to laugh and make laughter and remain unabashed.

text: “It strikes me,” Julian persisted, “that a true comprehension of the land problem—or indeed, of any human inquiry—cannot be reached without a prior mastery of the very substance and architecture of life. How can we hope to interpret our codes and rituals, our faiths and social habits, without first fathoming not just the nature of the beings who devised them, but the raw material from which those beings were cast? Is a book any less a human artifact than the monuments and friezes of the pharaohs? Does anything exist within the known world that is not governed by the dictates of evolution? I am aware, of course, of the intricate systems tracing the refinement of various crafts, but they appear to me entirely too clinical. The individual is omitted. We have mapped the evolution of the implement, the stringed instrument, and the dance; yet we ignore the evolution of the human spirit—the unfolding of those primal, innate qualities that predated the first forged blade or the first primitive song. This is the element you overlook, and which I term biology. It is biology in its grandest, most all-encompassing form.”
source_text: “And it seems to me,” Martin continued, “that knowledge of the land question, in turn, of all questions, for that matter, cannot be had without previous knowledge of the stuff and the constitution of life. How can we understand laws and institutions, religions and customs, without understanding, not merely the nature of the creatures that made them, but the nature of the stuff out of which the creatures are made? Is literature less human than the architecture and sculpture of Egypt? Is there one thing in the known universe that is not subject to the law of evolution?—Oh, I know there is an elaborate evolution of the various arts laid down, but it seems to me to be too mechanical. The human himself is left out. The evolution of the tool, of the harp, of music and song and dance, are all beautifully elaborated; but how about the evolution of the human himself, the development of the basic and intrinsic parts that were in him before he made his first tool or gibbered his first chant? It is that which you do not consider, and which I call biology. It is biology in its largest aspects.

text: I realize I’m not being very clear, but I’ve been trying to forge this idea into words. It only occurred to me while you were speaking, so I wasn’t exactly prepared to present it. You yourself mentioned the human limitations that keep us from weighing every variable. Yet it seems to me that you are neglecting the biological dimension—the very material from which the tapestry of the arts is woven, and the underlying structure of every human deed and accomplishment.
source_text: “I know I express myself incoherently, but I’ve tried to hammer out the idea. It came to me as you were talking, so I was not primed and ready to deliver it. You spoke yourself of the human frailty that prevented one from taking all the factors into consideration. And you, in turn,—or so it seems to me,—leave out the biological factor, the very stuff out of which has been spun the fabric of all the arts, the warp and the woof of all human actions and achievements.”

text: Success had seemingly forgotten where Martin lived, and its heralds no longer visited his threshold. For twenty-five grueling days, ignoring every holiday and Sabbath, he labored over “The Shame of the Sun,” a massive treatise spanning thirty thousand words. It was a calculated strike against the mysticism of the Maeterlinck circle—an assault launched from the stronghold of empirical science against the world’s dreamers, yet one that still embraced a beauty consistent with proven truth. Soon after, he reinforced this campaign with two briefer works, “The Wonder-Dreamers” and “The Yardstick of the Ego.” Thus, he began to finance the endless pilgrimage of these manuscripts as they shuttled from one indifferent magazine to the next.
source_text: But success had lost Martin’s address, and her messengers no longer came to his door. For twenty-five days, working Sundays and holidays, he toiled on “The Shame of the Sun,” a long essay of some thirty thousand words. It was a deliberate attack on the mysticism of the Maeterlinck school—an attack from the citadel of positive science upon the wonder-dreamers, but an attack nevertheless that retained much of beauty and wonder of the sort compatible with ascertained fact. It was a little later that he followed up the attack with two short essays, “The Wonder-Dreamers” and “The Yardstick of the Ego.” And on essays, long and short, he began to pay the travelling expenses from magazine to magazine.

text: Heartened by a few modest successes, Martin returned to his commercial hack-work. Perhaps, after all, there was a career to be made here. Tucked away under his desk were the twenty short sketches that the newspaper syndicates had already rejected. He studied them to learn exactly how not to write for the papers, and in the process, he worked out the ideal formula. He realized that a newspaper story must never be tragic or end on a somber note; it should shun elegant language, nuanced ideas, and any real emotional complexity. Instead, it needed sentiment—masses of the virtuous, heavy-handed kind that had prompted him to cheer from the theater's highest gallery in his youth. It was the "God and Country" and "honest despite my poverty" variety of melodrama that defined the genre.
source_text: Encouraged by his several small sales, Martin went back to hack-work. Perhaps there was a living in it, after all. Stored away under his table were the twenty storiettes which had been rejected by the newspaper short-story syndicate. He read them over in order to find out how not to write newspaper storiettes, and so doing, reasoned out the perfect formula. He found that the newspaper storiette should never be tragic, should never end unhappily, and should never contain beauty of language, subtlety of thought, nor real delicacy of sentiment. Sentiment it must contain, plenty of it, pure and noble, of the sort that in his own early youth had brought his applause from “nigger heaven”—the “For-God-my-country-and-the-Czar” and “I-may-be-poor-but-I-am-honest” brand of sentiment.

text: Meanwhile, he was uncovering disturbing new realities about the magazine industry. Although *The Transcontinental* had printed "The Ring of Bells," the payment remained elusive. Martin, in dire need of the funds, sent a written demand, but he received nothing but a non-committal reply and an invitation to submit more stories. He had starved for two days awaiting that mail, and when it brought nothing, he was forced to return his bicycle to the pawnshop. He persisted in writing twice weekly to claim his five dollars, though he seldom received any acknowledgment. He was oblivious to the fact that *The Transcontinental* had been teetering on the edge of ruin for years—a low-grade, disreputable publication with a shaky circulation built on minor coercion and empty patriotism, supported by advertisements that were little more than acts of charity. He didn't realize that the magazine was the only thing keeping the editor and business manager afloat, and that they only managed to survive by constantly relocating to avoid rent and dodging every possible bill. Nor could he have suspected that his specific five dollars had been taken by the business manager to pay for house paint in Alameda. The manager was doing the labor himself on weekday afternoons because he couldn't afford union rates, and his last attempt to hire cheap, non-union help had ended with the man being yanked off a ladder and sent to the hospital with a broken collarbone.
source_text: In the meantime he was making fresh and alarming discoveries concerning the magazines. Though the _Transcontinental_ had published “The Ring of Bells,” no check was forthcoming. Martin needed it, and he wrote for it. An evasive answer and a request for more of his work was all he received. He had gone hungry two days waiting for the reply, and it was then that he put his wheel back in pawn. He wrote regularly, twice a week, to the _Transcontinental_ for his five dollars, though it was only semi-occasionally that he elicited a reply. He did not know that the _Transcontinental_ had been staggering along precariously for years, that it was a fourth-rater, or tenth-rater, without standing, with a crazy circulation that partly rested on petty bullying and partly on patriotic appealing, and with advertisements that were scarcely more than charitable donations. Nor did he know that the _Transcontinental_ was the sole livelihood of the editor and the business manager, and that they could wring their livelihood out of it only by moving to escape paying rent and by never paying any bill they could evade. Nor could he have guessed that the particular five dollars that belonged to him had been appropriated by the business manager for the painting of his house in Alameda, which painting he performed himself, on week-day afternoons, because he could not afford to pay union wages and because the first scab he had employed had had a ladder jerked out from under him and been sent to the hospital with a broken collar-bone.

text: After reviewing your letter, it appears you are under the mistaken impression that we compensate authors for unsolicited manuscripts. This is not our practice, and your submission was, of course, unrequested. We naturally assumed when your story arrived that you were aware of our terms. We can only express our sincere regret for this misunderstanding and assure you of our continued high regard. Thank you again for your contribution; we hope to see more of your work in the future.
source_text: “On rereading your letter it seems to us that you are laboring under the misapprehension that we pay for unsolicited manuscripts. This is not our custom, and of course yours was unsolicited. We assumed, naturally, when we received your story, that you understood the situation. We can only deeply regret this unfortunate misunderstanding, and assure you of our unfailing regard. Again, thanking you for your kind contribution, and hoping to receive more from you in the near future, we remain, etc.”

text: During this interval, he found himself possessed by a drive for perfection, an impulse that compelled him to rewrite and burnish his earlier compositions, such as “The Jostling Street,” “The Wine of Life,” “Joy,” and the “Sea Lyrics.” As had been his habit, even nineteen hours of daily labor seemed inadequate to satisfy his hunger for progress. He wrote and read with immense fervor, burying the agony of his tobacco withdrawal beneath a mountain of work. The gaudily labeled remedy Ruth had promised him was tucked away in the most remote corner of his bureau. The absence of the leaf tormented him most during his bouts of hunger, yet no matter how many times he suppressed the craving, it never lost its potency. To him, this discipline was his most significant accomplishment. Ruth, however, viewed his abstinence as nothing more than his simple duty. She had presented him with the cure, paid for with her own glove money, and then dismissed the sacrifice from her thoughts entirely within a few days.
source_text: He discovered in himself, at this period, a passion for perfection, under the sway of which he rewrote and polished “The Jostling Street,” “The Wine of Life,” “Joy,” the “Sea Lyrics,” and others of his earlier work. As of old, nineteen hours of labor a day was all too little to suit him. He wrote prodigiously, and he read prodigiously, forgetting in his toil the pangs caused by giving up his tobacco. Ruth’s promised cure for the habit, flamboyantly labelled, he stowed away in the most inaccessible corner of his bureau. Especially during his stretches of famine he suffered from lack of the weed; but no matter how often he mastered the craving, it remained with him as strong as ever. He regarded it as the biggest thing he had ever achieved. Ruth’s point of view was that he was doing no more than was right. She brought him the anti-tobacco remedy, purchased out of her glove money, and in a few days forgot all about it.

text: Silas was certain that a realm of profound intellect, of men with piercing and logical clarity, truly thrived. The volumes he had devoured provided the evidence, lifting his understanding far above the shallow expectations of the Whitcomb parlor. Somewhere beyond that parochial horizon, he was sure, existed spirits far more luminous than those he encountered at their dinner table. Through the pages of foreign society novels, he observed a class of people who debated statecraft and metaphysics with effortless grace. He discovered accounts of metropolitan gatherings—even on his own shores—where the creative and the brilliant merged their fires. In his earlier ignorance, he had naively assumed that a polished exterior and a life of leisure were synonymous with mental strength and aesthetic vitality. To his younger eyes, refined dress had seemed a natural corollary of a refined mind; he had been tricked into thinking that a university degree was the same thing as true sovereignty of the soul.
source_text: He knew that the great minds, the deep and rational thinkers, existed. He had his proofs from the books, the books that had educated him beyond the Morse standard. And he knew that higher intellects than those of the Morse circle were to be found in the world. He read English society novels, wherein he caught glimpses of men and women talking politics and philosophy. And he read of salons in great cities, even in the United States, where art and intellect congregated. Foolishly, in the past, he had conceived that all well-groomed persons above the working class were persons with power of intellect and vigor of beauty. Culture and collars had gone together, to him, and he had been deceived into believing that college educations and mastery were the same things.

text: Martin reflected on this until he understood that the distinction between these professionals—the lawyers, businessmen, and bankers—and the laborers he had always known was as superficial as the food they consumed, the attire they chose, or the streets where they resided. Without question, they lacked that intangible depth he felt in his own soul and encountered in literature. The Morses had presented the pinnacle of their social circle, and he found it wanting. Despite his poverty and his debts to the moneylenders, he knew he was the better of those he encountered at their home. When his single presentable suit was not in hock, he carried himself among them like a sovereign, burning with the resentment a prince might experience if exiled to live among simple goat-herds.
source_text: So Martin thought, and he thought further, till it dawned upon him that the difference between these lawyers, officers, business men, and bank cashiers he had met and the members of the working class he had known was on a par with the difference in the food they ate, clothes they wore, neighborhoods in which they lived. Certainly, in all of them was lacking the something more which he found in himself and in the books. The Morses had shown him the best their social position could produce, and he was not impressed by it. A pauper himself, a slave to the money-lender, he knew himself the superior of those he met at the Morses’; and, when his one decent suit of clothes was out of pawn, he moved among them a lord of life, quivering with a sense of outrage akin to what a prince would suffer if condemned to live with goat-herds.

text: “Merely because I label the populace as dim-witted and regard the notions of freedom, parity, and kinship as punctured illusions, you must not mistake me for a collectivist,” Julian observed, his expression light. “That I challenge the wisdom of the Enlightenment founders and the illogical Gauls who shaped their philosophy does not mean I share their creed. Truly, Mr. Sterling, you are far more aligned with the socialist cause than I, who stand as its declared foe.”
source_text: “Because I say Republicans are stupid, and hold that liberty, equality, and fraternity are exploded bubbles, does not make me a socialist,” Martin said with a smile. “Because I question Jefferson and the unscientific Frenchmen who informed his mind, does not make me a socialist. Believe me, Mr. Morse, you are far nearer socialism than I who am its avowed enemy.”

text: “I’m being completely sincere. You still cling to the idea of equality, yet you serve the interests of corporations that are actively dismantling it every single day. You label me a socialist because I reject equality, but I’m merely articulating the reality that you actually practice. The Republicans are enemies of equality, even if most of them use the term as a rallying cry while they work to undermine it. They destroy equality in its own name, which is why I find them so foolish. As for me, I am an individualist. I believe that life favors the fast and the powerful. That is the lesson I’ve taken from biology, or at least what I believe I’ve learned. Like I said, I am an individualist, and individualism is the inherent and permanent enemy of socialism.”
source_text: “Not at all. I speak in all seriousness. You still believe in equality, and yet you do the work of the corporations, and the corporations, from day to day, are busily engaged in burying equality. And you call me a socialist because I deny equality, because I affirm just what you live up to. The Republicans are foes to equality, though most of them fight the battle against equality with the very word itself the slogan on their lips. In the name of equality they destroy equality. That was why I called them stupid. As for myself, I am an individualist. I believe the race is to the swift, the battle to the strong. Such is the lesson I have learned from biology, or at least think I have learned. As I said, I am an individualist, and individualism is the hereditary and eternal foe of socialism.”

text: Under the glow of a magnificent autumn afternoon—one filled with the same unseasonable warmth that had blessed the day they first spoke of their love a year ago—Martin shared his "Love-cycle" with Ruth. They had returned to their usual spot on a grassy hill, arriving there as the day began to wane. She had frequently broken his rhythm with soft cries of delight, and now, as he set the final page of the manuscript down beside the others, he waited in anticipation for her response.
source_text: On a beautiful fall day, a day of similar Indian summer to that which had seen their love declared the year before, Martin read his “Love-cycle” to Ruth. It was in the afternoon, and, as before, they had ridden out to their favorite knoll in the hills. Now and again she had interrupted his reading with exclamations of pleasure, and now, as he laid the last sheet of manuscript with its fellows, he waited her judgment.

text: Martin was unaware that Ruth possessed no real feeling for the ecstasy of creation. She spoke the phrase often—it was from her lips that he had initially heard it. She had studied the subject and read about it during her time at the university while earning her degree; however, she lacked any true originality or creative spark, and her every cultural observation was merely a recycled echo of what others had already said.
source_text: Martin did not know that Ruth was unsympathetic concerning the creative joy. She used the phrase—it was on her lips he had first heard it. She had read about it, studied about it, in the university in the course of earning her Bachelorship of Arts; but she was not original, not creative, and all manifestations of culture on her part were but harpings of the harpings of others.

text: “That’s plenty,” she insisted, pulling herself away from the intoxicating pull of his strength by a sheer act of will. “I’ve spoken with my parents. I have never defied them so directly before, but I demanded to be heard, no matter how undutiful I seemed. They are still set against you, yet I told them repeatedly of my enduring love for you. At last, Father relented; he said that if you wish, you can start in his office immediately. He even suggested, entirely on his own, that he would pay you enough for us to marry and find a little cottage of our own. I think it was a wonderful gesture—don’t you?”
source_text: “There, that’s enough,” she urged, by an effort of will withdrawing herself from the fascination of his strength. “I have talked with father and mother. I never before asserted myself so against them. I demanded to be heard. I was very undutiful. They are against you, you know; but I assured them over and over of my abiding love for you, and at last father agreed that if you wanted to, you could begin right away in his office. And then, of his own accord, he said he would pay you enough at the start so that we could get married and have a little cottage somewhere. Which I think was very fine of him—don’t you?”

text: The resolution arrived unbidden, and so thoroughly was he submerged in the leaden gloom of his days that he lost all memory of the faint suggestion she had ventured regarding a new livelihood. She, meanwhile, aware that she had tested the limits of her influence, was content to defer his response until the hour arrived for her to press the matter once more.
source_text: The question was answered for him, and so oppressed was he by the grayness of life that he forgot the tentative proposition she had made for him to go to work. And she, having gone as far as she dared, was willing to wait the answer till she should bring the question up again.

text: “Yet there remains another truth,” Julian resumed, following a silence that weighed heavily upon them both. “I am certain of the strength I carry within; it is a secret I alone fully grasp, and I am convinced that triumph is inevitable. I refuse to be stifled. I am consumed by the necessity of expression—through poetry, through narrative, and through the essay. I do not demand that you trust in these ambitions, however. I do not even require your belief in my character or the merit of my work. What I implore of you instead is simply to offer me your heart, and to place your ultimate trust in the grace of love itself.”
source_text: “But there is more to be said,” he continued, after a pause painful to both. “I know what I have in me. No one knows that so well as I. I know I shall succeed. I will not be kept down. I am afire with what I have to say in verse, and fiction, and essay. I do not ask you to have faith in that, though. I do not ask you to have faith in me, nor in my writing. What I do ask of you is to love me and have faith in love.

text: “To be sure, this seems like pure delirium. It is the fruit of a strained spirit, of an intellect driven to the brink. But look at the cause: Why have I endured this? For you. To truncate my season of toil. To demand that Destiny arrive ahead of schedule. And that season is now complete. I have gauged my own strength. I declare that I gain more ground in a few weeks than the typical scholar gains in a decade. I am sure of it, I say. Were I not so starved for your sympathy, I would never speak of it. It is no empty pride. I calibrate my growth by the depth of the texts I have mastered. Your own kin are but unlettered primitives compared to me and the truths I have wrestled from the library while they were lost in dreams. In the past, I hungered for celebrity. Today, I care nothing for it. It is you I want; I am more famished for you than for sustenance, or shelter, or the world’s esteem. I hold a vision of sinking my head upon your bosom and slumbering for a thousand years, and that dream shall be made flesh before another winter passes.”
source_text: “Of course, this is sheer nonsense. It comes from nervousness, from an overwrought mind. But the point is: Why have I done this? For you. To shorten my apprenticeship. To compel Success to hasten. And my apprenticeship is now served. I know my equipment. I swear that I learn more each month than the average college man learns in a year. I know it, I tell you. But were my need for you to understand not so desperate I should not tell you. It is not boasting. I measure the results by the books. Your brothers, to-day, are ignorant barbarians compared with me and the knowledge I have wrung from the books in the hours they were sleeping. Long ago I wanted to be famous. I care very little for fame now. What I want is you; I am more hungry for you than for food, or clothing, or recognition. I have a dream of laying my head on your breast and sleeping an aeon or so, and the dream will come true ere another year is gone.”

text: It is you I love, not them, and nothing they do can harm me." A triumphant note rang through his voice. "I trust in your devotion rather than fearing their malice. Everything in this world may lose its way, but love never will. Love cannot fail unless it is a fragile thing that falters and collapses along the path.
source_text: “And I love you, not them, and nothing they do can hurt me.” Triumph sounded in his voice. “For I have faith in your love, not fear of their enmity. All things may go astray in this world, but not love. Love cannot go wrong unless it be a weakling that faints and stumbles by the way.”

text: Martin had run into his sister Gertrude by accident on Broadway—an encounter that proved to be both remarkably timely and deeply unsettling. Standing on the corner waiting for a streetcar, she had spotted him first, noting the gaunt, famished contours of his face and the frantic, burdened expression in his eyes. In truth, his situation was as dire as it looked. He had just come from a failed negotiation with a pawnbroker, having unsuccessfully tried to squeeze an additional loan out of his bicycle. With the arrival of the muddy autumn season, Martin had pawned the machine some time ago, choosing to keep his black suit instead.
source_text: Martin had encountered his sister Gertrude by chance on Broadway—as it proved, a most propitious yet disconcerting chance. Waiting on the corner for a car, she had seen him first, and noted the eager, hungry lines of his face and the desperate, worried look of his eyes. In truth, he was desperate and worried. He had just come from a fruitless interview with the pawnbroker, from whom he had tried to wring an additional loan on his wheel. The muddy fall weather having come on, Martin had pledged his wheel some time since and retained his black suit.

text: Martin’s fingers reflexively tightened around the gold coin. Even as he did so, he knew he should refuse it, and he felt himself caught in a painful surge of indecision. That small piece of metal represented more than money; it was nourishment, vitality, and the fuel his mind required to continue his craft. It held the potential for him to create something that might eventually earn him a fortune. In his mind, he could clearly see the two essays he had just finished. They lay beneath his table, resting on a stack of rejected manuscripts he couldn't afford to mail. He envisioned their titles exactly as he had typed them: “The High Priests of Mystery” and “The Cradle of Beauty.” He hadn't sent them out yet, though they were among his finest work. If only he had the postage! A sudden, deep conviction in his future success rose to join his hunger, and with a swift motion, he slid the coin into his pocket.
source_text: Martin’s hand instinctively closed on the piece of gold. In the same instant he knew he ought not to accept, and found himself struggling in the throes of indecision. That bit of gold meant food, life, and light in his body and brain, power to go on writing, and—who was to say?—maybe to write something that would bring in many pieces of gold. Clear on his vision burned the manuscripts of two essays he had just completed. He saw them under the table on top of the heap of returned manuscripts for which he had no stamps, and he saw their titles, just as he had typed them—“The High Priests of Mystery,” and “The Cradle of Beauty.” He had never submitted them anywhere. They were as good as anything he had done in that line. If only he had stamps for them! Then the certitude of his ultimate success rose up in him, an able ally of hunger, and with a quick movement he slipped the coin into his pocket.

text: Brissenden regarded him with a look of mingled compassion and reverence. “You are so terribly young, Martin, my boy—so very young. You will soar to great heights, but your wings are made of the thinnest gauze, dusted with the most delicate colors. Try not to burn them. Though, of course, you have already been singed. It clearly took some idealized woman to inspire that ‘Love-cycle,’ and that is the real pity of it.”
source_text: Brissenden looked at him pityingly and admiringly. “You are so young, Martin boy, so young. You will flutter high, but your wings are of the finest gauze, dusted with the fairest pigments. Do not scorch them. But of course you have scorched them already. It required some glorified petticoat to account for that ‘Love-cycle,’ and that’s the shame of it.”

text: Brissenden remained a constant mystery. Despite his monk-like features, his thinning blood harbored the soul of a dedicated hedonist. He faced death without fear, viewing the various ways of the world with a sharp and weary cynicism; nevertheless, as his end approached, he cherished every microscopic fragment of being. He was driven by a frantic urge to exist, to feel, to "wriggle through my brief moment in the stardust from which I emerged," as he once described it. In his pursuit of novel excitements and raw sensations, he had experimented with narcotics and ventured into many bizarre territories. He once confided to Martin that he had deliberately abstained from water for three days, solely to savor the sublime ecstasy of finally quenching such a profound thirst. Martin never discovered his true identity or origins. He was a man stripped of history, whose only prospect was the approaching tomb and whose current reality was a caustic, restless delirium of life.
source_text: But Brissenden was always an enigma. With the face of an ascetic, he was, in all the failing blood of him, a frank voluptuary. He was unafraid to die, bitter and cynical of all the ways of living; and yet, dying, he loved life, to the last atom of it. He was possessed by a madness to live, to thrill, “to squirm my little space in the cosmic dust whence I came,” as he phrased it once himself. He had tampered with drugs and done many strange things in quest of new thrills, new sensations. As he told Martin, he had once gone three days without water, had done so voluntarily, in order to experience the exquisite delight of such a thirst assuaged. Who or what he was, Martin never learned. He was a man without a past, whose future was the imminent grave and whose present was a bitter fever of living.

text: The door to the *Transcontinental* office was slightly open, and Martin, just as he was about to enter, was stopped in his tracks by a booming voice from inside. “That isn't the point, Mr. Ford,” the speaker shouted. Martin recognized the name from his letters; Ford was the man in charge. “The point is, can you pay me—and I mean right now, in cash? I don't give a damn about the *Transcontinental's* outlook or what you plan to turn it into next year. I want to be compensated for my labor. Let me make this clear: the Christmas edition won't see a printing press until I have the money in my hand. Good day. You can come see me when you have the funds.”
source_text: The door to the _Transcontinental_ office was ajar, and Martin, in the act of opening it, was brought to a sudden pause by a loud voice from within, which exclaimed:- “But that is not the question, Mr. Ford.” (Ford, Martin knew, from his correspondence, to be the editor’s name.) “The question is, are you prepared to pay?—cash, and cash down, I mean? I am not interested in the prospects of the _Transcontinental_ and what you expect to make it next year. What I want is to be paid for what I do. And I tell you, right now, the Christmas _Transcontinental_ don’t go to press till I have the money in my hand. Good day. When you get the money, come and see me.”

text: I must admit, I pictured you as a much older man. Your narrative displayed such scope and energy, such a seasoned outlook and profound insight. That story is a true work of genius—I sensed it after reading only the first few sentences. Let me describe the moment I first encountered it. Actually, wait; let me introduce you to the rest of the team first.
source_text: “I confess, though, I conceived you to be a much older man than you are. Your story, you know, showed such breadth, and vigor, such maturity and depth of thought. A masterpiece, that story—I knew it when I had read the first half-dozen lines. Let me tell you how I first read it. But no; first let me introduce you to the staff.”

text: Martin shook hands with an irritable-eyed, bald man whose features seemed remarkably young, though they were largely concealed by a pristine white beard. This beard was meticulously maintained by his wife every Sunday, a ritual during which she also made sure to shave the nape of his neck.
source_text: Martin found himself shaking hands with a cranky-eyed, bald-headed man, whose face looked youthful enough from what little could be seen of it, for most of it was covered by a snow-white beard, carefully trimmed—by his wife, who did it on Sundays, at which times she also shaved the back of his neck.

text: The two men watched Mr. White with anticipation, but he only laughed and gave a shrug. His conscience, at least, was clear. He had initially joined the *Transcontinental* to study the craft of magazine writing, yet he had primarily received an education in finance instead. The publication owed him four months of back pay, and he understood that the printer’s bills had to be settled long before the associate editor’s salary.
source_text: Both men looked expectantly at Mr. White, but that gentleman laughed and shrugged his shoulders. His conscience was clean at any rate. He had come into the _Transcontinental_ to learn magazine-literature, instead of which he had principally learned finance. The _Transcontinental_ owed him four months’ salary, and he knew that the printer must be appeased before the associate editor.

text: Mr. Ends confirmed he had the address and promised the check would be mailed at daybreak. Martin’s understanding of banking and financial drafts was murky, but he saw no reason why they couldn't simply hand him the payment today instead of waiting until the next morning.
source_text: Yes, Mr. Ends had the address, and the check would be mailed the first thing in the morning. Martin’s knowledge of banks and checks was hazy, but he could see no reason why they should not give him the check on this day just as well as on the next.

text: Yet in that very breath, with a fretful snap of his frame, Mr. Sterling pivoted as though to vacate the parlor. Simultaneously, Silas lunged, his hand clamping the man’s throat with such force that Sterling’s ivory beard, still meticulously groomed, was jerked upward toward the cornices at a sharp angle. To the paralyzed horror of Mr. Blackwood and Mr. Vance, they witnessed their administrator being thrashed with the same vigor one might use on a heavy woolen rug.
source_text: But at that moment, with an impatient movement, Mr. Ends turned as if about to leave the room. At the same instant Martin sprang for him, clutching him by the throat with one hand in such fashion that Mr. Ends’ snow-white beard, still maintaining its immaculate trimness, pointed ceilingward at an angle of forty-five degrees. To the horror of Mr. White and Mr. Ford, they saw their business manager shaken like an Astrakhan rug.

text: Arthur stood by the gate as Ruth made her way up Maria’s front stairs. The sharp staccato of a typewriter echoed through the house, and when Martin opened the door, she found him finishing the final page of a new work. Though she had come to ensure he would join them for Thanksgiving dinner, Martin began speaking of his own obsession before she could even mention the invitation.
source_text: Arthur remained at the gate while Ruth climbed Maria’s front steps. She heard the rapid click of the type-writer, and when Martin let her in, found him on the last page of a manuscript. She had come to make certain whether or not he would be at their table for Thanksgiving dinner; but before she could broach the subject Martin plunged into the one with which he was full.

text: Her indignation was plain to him as he retreated from the paddock gate. But what was the grievance? It was a miserable business that the bailiff had made off with Elena’s heifers, yet it was surely a mere accident of fortune. No soul could be held to account for it. It never struck him that he might have behaved a jot differently than he had. Well, perhaps he was somewhat at fault, he reconsidered, for turning his back on the appointment with the Steam Post. And then, she had never cared for “Quick-Step.”
source_text: She was angry with him, he concluded, as he returned from the gate. But why? It was unfortunate that the poundman had gobbled Maria’s cows. But it was only a stroke of fate. Nobody could be blamed for it. Nor did it enter his head that he could have done aught otherwise than what he had done. Well, yes, he was to blame a little, was his next thought, for having refused the call to the Railway Mail. And she had not liked “Wiki-Wiki.”

text: Martin found the enclosed letter to be clumsily hand-printed. It was a jumble of uneducated insults, asserting that the man known as Martin Eden was no true author, but a fraud who merely transcribed stories from old periodicals to sell as his own. The envelope bore a San Leandro postmark. Martin did not need a moment’s reflection to identify the culprit. Higginbotham’s poor grammar, his distinct slang, and his petty mindset were evident in every sentence. In every line, Martin recognized not a subtle touch, but the heavy-handed, vulgar script of his brother-in-law the grocer.
source_text: The enclosed letter Martin found to be crudely printed by hand. It was a hotchpotch of illiterate abuse of Martin, and of assertion that the “so-called Martin Eden” who was selling stories to magazines was no writer at all, and that in reality he was stealing stories from old magazines, typing them, and sending them out as his own. The envelope was postmarked “San Leandro.” Martin did not require a second thought to discover the author. Higginbotham’s grammar, Higginbotham’s colloquialisms, Higginbotham’s mental quirks and processes, were apparent throughout. Martin saw in every line, not the fine Italian hand, but the coarse grocer’s fist, of his brother-in-law.

text: Around that time, Martin’s standing in Maria’s eyes suffered a sharp decline. He discovered her in the kitchen one morning, moaning in agony with tears of exhaustion wetting her cheeks as she struggled fruitlessly to finish a massive load of ironing. He immediately diagnosed her ailment as the flu, treated her with a dose of hot whiskey—the dregs from the bottles Brissenden had provided—and commanded her to go to bed. But Maria was defiant. She insisted the laundry had to be completed and delivered that night; otherwise, there would be no food the next day for the seven young, starving Silvas.
source_text: It was about this time that Martin took a great slump in Maria’s estimation. He found her in the kitchen one morning groaning with pain, tears of weakness running down her cheeks, vainly endeavoring to put through a large ironing. He promptly diagnosed her affliction as La Grippe, dosed her with hot whiskey (the remnants in the bottles for which Brissenden was responsible), and ordered her to bed. But Maria was refractory. The ironing had to be done, she protested, and delivered that night, or else there would be no food on the morrow for the seven small and hungry Silvas.

text: Joe had taught Martin this clever trick back at Shelly Hot Springs. An old wheel hub, mounted to the end of a vertical post, served as the plunger. By securing this to a flexible pole anchored to the kitchen rafters, the hub could strike the woolens inside the barrel, allowing him to wash them thoroughly with just one hand.
source_text: Martin had learned the contrivance from Joe at the Shelly Hot Springs. The old wheel-hub, fixed on the end of the upright pole, constituted the plunger. Making this, in turn, fast to the spring-pole attached to the kitchen rafters, so that the hub played upon the woollens in the barrel, he was able, with one hand, thoroughly to pound them.

text: My writing days are over; I am nothing more than a clumsy amateur. You have shown me the hand of a true master craftsman. This is beyond brilliance; it exceeds the very definition of talent. It is reality driven to the point of frenzy, and every line rings with absolute certainty. I doubt you even recognize it, you stubborn theorist. No science could ever prove you wrong. This is a mocking truth, forged from the cold iron of the universe and braided with powerful melodies into a magnificent tapestry. I am finished; I am completely humbled. Actually, I have one more thing to say. Let me sell this for you.
source_text: “I shall never write again. I am a dauber in clay. You have shown me the work of the real artificer-artisan. Genius! This is something more than genius. It transcends genius. It is truth gone mad. It is true, man, every line of it. I wonder if you realize that, you dogmatist. Science cannot give you the lie. It is the truth of the sneer, stamped out from the black iron of the Cosmos and interwoven with mighty rhythms of sound into a fabric of splendor and beauty. And now I won’t say another word. I am overwhelmed, crushed. Yes, I will, too. Let me market it for you.”

text: Martin’s efforts to dissuade him proved useless. He argued that Brissenden’s animosity toward the magazines was pathological and extreme, claiming his behavior was far more shameful than that of the man who torched the Temple of Diana at Ephesus. Amidst this barrage of criticism, Brissenden calmly sipped his drink, agreeing with every insult Martin leveled—except for those concerning the editors. His loathing for them was absolute, and he easily surpassed Martin in vitriol whenever he turned his attention back to them.
source_text: In vain Martin strove to shake him from his decision. Martin told him that his hatred of the magazines was rabid, fanatical, and that his conduct was a thousand times more despicable than that of the youth who burned the temple of Diana at Ephesus. Under the storm of denunciation Brissenden complacently sipped his toddy and affirmed that everything the other said was quite true, with the exception of the magazine editors. His hatred of them knew no bounds, and he excelled Martin in denunciation when he turned upon them.

text: “By no means—not if your ambition is to see it in type. Press it upon the most venerable houses. It is possible some editor’s clerk will be mad enough or sodden enough with gin to recommend it. You have consumed the libraries. Their substance has been distilled in the retort of Julian Thorne’s spirit and decanted into ‘The Zenith’s Shadow,’ and the hour will come when Thorne is a man of renown, and this work shall be the very pillar of his prestige. Therefore, seek out a publisher—and do so with all haste.”
source_text: “By all means no—that is, if you want to see it in print. Offer it to the first-class houses. Some publisher’s reader may be mad enough or drunk enough to report favorably on it. You’ve read the books. The meat of them has been transmuted in the alembic of Martin Eden’s mind and poured into ‘The Shame of the Sun,’ and one day Martin Eden will be famous, and not the least of his fame will rest upon that work. So you must get a publisher for it—the sooner the better.”

text: Brissenden moved through the shadows until a sliver of light indicated a doorway. A knock followed by an invitation from inside opened the way, and Martin soon found himself shaking hands with Kreis, a striking man with dark features, brilliant white teeth, and a thick, drooping mustache. In the cramped back area that doubled as a kitchen and dining room, Mary, a sturdy young blonde, was busy washing dishes. The main room served as both a parlor and a bedroom. Strings of laundry hung so low from the ceiling that Martin initially missed the two men talking in a corner. They greeted Brissenden and his jugs of liquor with cheers, and Martin was introduced to Andy and Parry. He sat and listened closely as Parry described a boxing match he’d attended the night before, while Brissenden, clearly in his element, set to work mixing toddies and serving rounds of wine and whiskey-sodas. At his order to "bring in the crew," Andy headed out to summon the rest of the residents.
source_text: Brissenden advanced through the darkness till a thread of light marked the threshold of a door. A knock and an answer opened it, and Martin found himself shaking hands with Kreis, a handsome brunette man, with dazzling white teeth, a drooping black mustache, and large, flashing black eyes. Mary, a matronly young blonde, was washing dishes in the little back room that served for kitchen and dining room. The front room served as bedchamber and living room. Overhead was the week’s washing, hanging in festoons so low that Martin did not see at first the two men talking in a corner. They hailed Brissenden and his demijohns with acclamation, and, on being introduced, Martin learned they were Andy and Parry. He joined them and listened attentively to the description of a prize-fight Parry had seen the night before; while Brissenden, in his glory, plunged into the manufacture of a toddy and the serving of wine and whiskey-and-sodas. At his command, “Bring in the clan,” Andy departed to go the round of the rooms for the lodgers.

text: Martin had never felt any affinity for idealism, yet the way Norton articulated it proved to be a profound awakening. Its logical coherence spoke directly to Martin’s intellect, a merit seemingly lost on Kreis and Hamilton, who mocked Norton as a mere metaphysician—only for him to hurl the same insult back at them. Concepts of phenomenon and noumenon were traded like blows. They indicted him for trying to account for consciousness using only itself, while he accused them of linguistic trickery, claiming they derived theories from vocabulary rather than reality. This charge left them horrified, for it was the fundamental principle of their logic to begin with raw facts and only then assign them names.
source_text: Idealism had never attracted Martin, but the exposition it now received at the hands of Norton was a revelation. The logical plausibility of it, that made an appeal to his intellect, seemed missed by Kreis and Hamilton, who sneered at Norton as a metaphysician, and who, in turn, sneered back at them as metaphysicians. _Phenomenon_ and _noumenon_ were bandied back and forth. They charged him with attempting to explain consciousness by itself. He charged them with word-jugglery, with reasoning from words to theory instead of from facts to theory. At this they were aghast. It was the cardinal tenet of their mode of reasoning to start with facts and to give names to the facts.

text: He was acutely aware that the work he was producing was magnificent. "It will sell! It will sell!" was the constant refrain echoing in his mind. Of course it would. Finally, he was creating the kind of story that editors would scramble to acquire. The entire narrative revealed itself to him in sudden, electric bursts of insight. He paused just long enough to record a single paragraph in his journal. This was intended as the conclusion to "Overdue"; however, the book was so completely realized in his mind that he could pen the final lines weeks before he had even reached them. He weighed the unwritten story against the works of other maritime authors and felt it was vastly superior. "Only one man could rival this," he whispered aloud, "and that is Conrad. And it should be enough to make even him take notice, offer a handshake, and say, 'Well done, Martin, my boy.'"
source_text: He was conscious that it was great stuff he was writing. “It will go! It will go!” was the refrain that kept sounding in his ears. Of course it would go. At last he was turning out the thing at which the magazines would jump. The whole story worked out before him in lightning flashes. He broke off from it long enough to write a paragraph in his note-book. This would be the last paragraph in “Overdue”; but so thoroughly was the whole book already composed in his brain that he could write, weeks before he had arrived at the end, the end itself. He compared the tale, as yet unwritten, with the tales of the sea-writers, and he felt it to be immeasurably superior. “There’s only one man who could touch it,” he murmured aloud, “and that’s Conrad. And it ought to make even him sit up and shake hands with me, and say, ‘Well done, Martin, my boy.’”

text: You’re missing the point. I am telling you that your diagnosis is fundamentally flawed. I am not the one infected with the socialist virus; rather, it is you who are being hollowed out by its debilitating effects. For my part, I am a lifelong enemy of socialism, just as I am a fierce opponent of your bastardized democracy—a system that is nothing but a counterfeit socialism disguised in rhetoric that falls apart the moment you actually try to define it.
source_text: “That’s not the point. I mean to tell you that you are a poor diagnostician. I mean to tell you that I am not suffering from the microbe of socialism. I mean to tell you that it is you who are suffering from the emasculating ravages of that same microbe. As for me, I am an inveterate opponent of socialism just as I am an inveterate opponent of your own mongrel democracy that is nothing else than pseudo-socialism masquerading under a garb of words that will not stand the test of the dictionary.”

text: I am a reactionary—so absolute a traditionalist that my convictions are beyond your understanding, mired as you are in the deceptive illusions of our social order. You lack the vision to see through the facade. You only pretend to champion the survival of the fittest; I, however, truly embrace it. That is the distinction between us. Not long ago, I shared your outlook, having been swayed by the ideas of your circle. But I have realized that merchants and financiers are timid masters at best; they spend their lives wallowing in the muck of profit-seeking. Thus, I have returned to the ideals of aristocracy. I am the only true individualist in this room. I expect nothing from the state. I look only to the powerful leader, the man on horseback, to deliver the nation from its own decaying insignificance.
source_text: “I am a reactionary—so complete a reactionary that my position is incomprehensible to you who live in a veiled lie of social organization and whose sight is not keen enough to pierce the veil. You make believe that you believe in the survival of the strong and the rule of the strong. I believe. That is the difference. When I was a trifle younger,—a few months younger,—I believed the same thing. You see, the ideas of you and yours had impressed me. But merchants and traders are cowardly rulers at best; they grunt and grub all their days in the trough of money-getting, and I have swung back to aristocracy, if you please. I am the only individualist in this room. I look to the state for nothing. I look only to the strong man, the man on horseback, to save the state from its own rotten futility.”

text: A new surge of his intricate rage flared up, and Ruth felt a cold terror take hold of her. She had never seen him so consumed by fury, a state that remained entirely baffling and irrational to her understanding. Even so, through her fear ran the same magnetic threads of fascination that had always pulled her toward him—the same impulse that had forced her to lean in and, in a frantic, climactic moment, clasp her hands behind his neck. Though she felt wounded and insulted by what had transpired, she stayed trembling in his arms as he continued to mutter, “The animals! The wretched animals!” She remained there as he added, “I won’t trouble your dinner table again, my dear. They have no liking for me, and it is a mistake for me to impose my unwelcome presence on them. In truth, they are just as loathsome to me. It’s revolting. To think that in my ignorance I actually believed those in high society, with their grand estates, educations, and fortunes, were people of any real value!”
source_text: His complex anger flamed afresh, and Ruth was in terror of him. Never had she seen him so angry, and it was all mystified and unreasonable to her comprehension. And yet, through her very terror ran the fibres of fascination that had drawn and that still drew her to him—that had compelled her to lean towards him, and, in that mad, culminating moment, lay her hands upon his neck. She was hurt and outraged by what had taken place, and yet she lay in his arms and quivered while he went on muttering, “The beasts! The beasts!” And she still lay there when he said: “I’ll not bother your table again, dear. They do not like me, and it is wrong of me to thrust my objectionable presence upon them. Besides, they are just as objectionable to me. Faugh! They are sickening. And to think of it, I dreamed in my innocence that the persons who sat in the high places, who lived in fine houses and had educations and bank accounts, were worth while!”

text: “The floor is open to visitors for a brief five minutes,” the invalid insisted. “Rise and deliver your piece. Explain exactly why you loathe their collective vision. Lay bare your contempt for their narrow, gutter-born morality. Hurl the weight of the Overman at their heads and take the bruises you’ll surely earn for it. Provoke a skirmish; the friction will serve them well. They crave the debate as much as you hunger for the struggle. I confess, I hope to see you embrace their cause before my breath fails. It would provide a moral anchor for your life. Only such a faith will preserve you when the inevitable bitterness of the world finally descends upon you.”
source_text: “Outsiders are allowed five-minute speeches,” the sick man urged. “Get up and spout. Tell them why you don’t want socialism. Tell them what you think about them and their ghetto ethics. Slam Nietzsche into them and get walloped for your pains. Make a scrap of it. It will do them good. Discussion is what they want, and what you want, too. You see, I’d like to see you a socialist before I’m gone. It will give you a sanction for your existence. It is the one thing that will save you in the time of disappointment that is coming to you.”

text: Martin settled into his chair as a storm of voices broke out around him. Numerous men leaped to their feet, vying for the chairman’s notice. One by one, bolstered by roaring cheers and gesturing with frantic energy, they rose to dismantle his claims. It was a chaotic scene, but the chaos was strictly intellectual—a fierce collision of concepts. While some speakers drifted from the subject, most aimed their arguments straight at Martin. They challenged him with fresh lines of reasoning that forced him to reconsider his own, revealing not new biological principles, but innovative ways to interpret the old ones. They were far too invested in the debate to remain civil, and the chairman was frequently forced to strike his gavel to quell the mounting disorder.
source_text: Martin took his seat amidst an uproar of voices. A score of men were on their feet clamoring for recognition from the chair. And one by one, encouraged by vociferous applause, speaking with fire and enthusiasm and excited gestures, they replied to the attack. It was a wild night—but it was wild intellectually, a battle of ideas. Some strayed from the point, but most of the speakers replied directly to Martin. They shook him with lines of thought that were new to him; and gave him insights, not into new biological laws, but into new applications of the old laws. They were too earnest to be always polite, and more than once the chairman rapped and pounded for order.

text: As it happened, a fledgling correspondent occupied a seat in the gallery, sent there during a stagnant news cycle and driven by the press’s frantic appetite for the spectacular. He was not a particularly keen youth, but rather one possessed of a shallow, easy eloquence. The complexities of the debate were quite beyond his reach; instead, he basked in the smug certainty that he was far more refined than these verbose madmen of the laboring class. He maintained a profound reverence for the men of influence who sat in cushioned chairs and orchestrated the destinies of governments and broadsheets alike. Moreover, he pursued a singular professional ideal: to attain that height of journalistic mastery where one can manufacture a grand sensation out of absolutely nothing at all.
source_text: It chanced that a cub reporter sat in the audience, detailed there on a day dull of news and impressed by the urgent need of journalism for sensation. He was not a bright cub reporter. He was merely facile and glib. He was too dense to follow the discussion. In fact, he had a comfortable feeling that he was vastly superior to these wordy maniacs of the working class. Also, he had a great respect for those who sat in the high places and dictated the policies of nations and newspapers. Further, he had an ideal, namely, of achieving that excellence of the perfect reporter who is able to make something—even a great deal—out of nothing.

text: Truly, their praise concerns me not at all; I haven't the slightest regard for it. On the other hand, it is bound to make my standing with Evelyn’s kin rather strained. Her father has long insisted I am a common agitator, and this miserable business will only confirm his suspicions. Not that his judgment carries any weight—but what does it matter? I want to share what I’ve been crafting today. It is ‘The Arrears,’ naturally, and I’ve just about reached the halfway point.
source_text: “No, I really don’t care for their approval, not a whit. On the other hand, it’s very likely to make my relations with Ruth’s family a trifle awkward. Her father always contended I was a socialist, and this miserable stuff will clinch his belief. Not that I care for his opinion—but what’s the odds? I want to read you what I’ve been doing to-day. It’s ‘Overdue,’ of course, and I’m just about halfway through.”

text: By the next morning’s edition, Julian was introduced to a version of himself he had never met. “We are the avowed adversaries of the commonweal,” the column quoted him as asserting during a brief interrogation. “Make no mistake: we are not insurrectionists, but communalists.” When the correspondent suggested that the two ideologies were essentially the same, Julian was depicted as having offered a mute, shrugging concession. The text went on to map the supposed irregularities of his skull, citing them as evidence of a primitive character. His fingers were described as those of a common ruffian, and much was made of the predatory, blood-rimmed glare that supposedly defined his expression.
source_text: In the next morning’s paper Martin learned a great deal more about himself that was new to him. “We are the sworn enemies of society,” he found himself quoted as saying in a column interview. “No, we are not anarchists but socialists.” When the reporter pointed out to him that there seemed little difference between the two schools, Martin had shrugged his shoulders in silent affirmation. His face was described as bilaterally asymmetrical, and various other signs of degeneration were described. Especially notable were his thuglike hands and the fiery gleams in his blood-shot eyes.

text: The young journalist had been thorough. He had poked around until he unearthed Martin’s family background and even secured a picture of Higginbotham’s Cash Store, with Bernard Higginbotham posed prominently in front. The man was presented as a shrewd, respectable merchant who possessed no sympathy for his brother-in-law’s socialist leanings, nor for the man himself, whom he described as a shiftless idler who refused honest work and was destined for a prison cell. Hermann von Schmidt, Marian’s husband, had also been questioned. He branded Martin the family’s disgrace and formally disowned him. “He attempted to bleed me for money, but I put an end to that immediately,” Von Schmidt informed the reporter. “He knows better than to come around here looking for handouts. A man who won’t earn his keep is worthless, you can take my word for it.”
source_text: The cub had been industrious. He had scurried around and nosed out Martin’s family history, and procured a photograph of Higginbotham’s Cash Store with Bernard Higginbotham himself standing out in front. That gentleman was depicted as an intelligent, dignified businessman who had no patience with his brother-in-law’s socialistic views, and no patience with the brother-in-law, either, whom he was quoted as characterizing as a lazy good-for-nothing who wouldn’t take a job when it was offered to him and who would go to jail yet. Hermann von Schmidt, Marian’s husband, had likewise been interviewed. He had called Martin the black sheep of the family and repudiated him. “He tried to sponge off of me, but I put a stop to that good and quick,” Von Schmidt had said to the reporter. “He knows better than to come bumming around here. A man who won’t work is no good, take that from me.”

text: Julian’s indignation was sharp and unyielding. While Sterling viewed the entire debacle as a delightful farce, his amusement provided no solace to Julian, who foresaw the agonizing difficulty of justifying himself to Elena. Her father, Julian was certain, would greet the scandal with predatory joy, seizing upon it as the perfect instrument to sever their betrothal. He did not have to wait long to discover the depth of that hostility. The afternoon delivery brought a letter in Elena’s script. Julian accepted it from the courier and remained at the threshold, opening the envelope with a hollow sense of impending ruin. As he processed the words, his hand drifted instinctively toward his coat, searching for the loose tobacco and thin papers of his former life. He was oblivious to the fact that the pocket held nothing, unaware even that he had reached for the comforts of a habit he had long since discarded.
source_text: This time Martin was genuinely angry. Brissenden looked upon the affair as a good joke, but he could not console Martin, who knew that it would be no easy task to explain to Ruth. As for her father, he knew that he must be overjoyed with what had happened and that he would make the most of it to break off the engagement. How much he would make of it he was soon to realize. The afternoon mail brought a letter from Ruth. Martin opened it with a premonition of disaster, and read it standing at the open door when he had received it from the postman. As he read, mechanically his hand sought his pocket for the tobacco and brown paper of his old cigarette days. He was not aware that the pocket was empty or that he had even reached for the materials with which to roll a cigarette.

text: He studied the text once more, reading every word with deliberate care before sitting down to draft his response. He began by clarifying the points he had made during the socialist assembly, demonstrating that his actual statements were the exact opposite of what the paper had falsely reported. Yet, as the letter drew to a close, his composure broke, and he became like a man possessed by a divine longing, pleading for her affection. "I beg you for a reply," he wrote, "and in that reply, you must tell me only one thing. Do you love me? That is all I ask—the answer to that single question."
source_text: He read it through to the end, carefully, a second time, then sat down and replied. He outlined the remarks he had uttered at the socialist meeting, pointing out that they were in all ways the converse of what the newspaper had put in his mouth. Toward the end of the letter he was God’s own lover pleading passionately for love. “Please answer,” he said, “and in your answer you have to tell me but one thing. Do you love me? That is all—the answer to that one question.”

text: In the wake of her exit, he sat hunched and hollow-eyed on the edge of the cot, staring blindly into the gloom. Then, the ragged cover of a monthly, which had arrived in the early delivery and remained untouched, sent a jolt of clarity through his leaden thoughts. It was *The Lyceum*, he realized—the August issue—and it would finally contain "Evanescence." If only Aristhor were there to witness the sight!
