source_text: After she left him he sat drearily, with drooping shoulders, on the edge of the bed, gazing about him with lack-lustre eyes that saw nothing until the torn wrapper of a magazine, which had come in the morning’s mail and which lay unopened, shot a gleam of light into his darkened brain. It is _The Parthenon_, he thought, the August _Parthenon_, and it must contain “Ephemera.” If only Brissenden were here to see!

text: He lost all sense of the passing moments until, without warning, a pale horizontal streak began to crystallize within the darkness of his mind. It was a peculiar sight. But as the image gained clarity, he saw it was a limestone reef, smoking with the white fury of the southern swells. Soon, amidst the line of crashing foam, he identified a slender outrigger. In its stern sat a lithe, copper-skinned youth clad in a crimson wrap, his paddle flashing as it bit the water. He knew the boy well. It was Tari, the youngest son of the high chief, and the island was Moorea; past that thundering surf lay the lush groves of Afareaitu and the chief’s thatched hall where the river met the sea. The light was dying, and Tari was making his way back from the day’s catch. He was poised, waiting for the heave of a great roller to vault the coral barrier. Then the observer saw himself, seated forward in the craft as he had been so often in years past, his paddle held ready for Tari’s signal to dig deep when the sapphire wall of the ocean rose to claim them. In an instant, he was no longer watching from afar but was there in the wood; Tari was shouting, and they were both straining against the water, racing down the steep, liquid face of the flying sea. Under the prow, the water shrieked like a burst valve, the air dissolved into a cloud of spray, and with a heavy, resonant boom, the canoe slid into the velvet calm of the lagoon. Tari laughed, wiping the brine from his eyes, and together they paddled toward the crushed-shell beach where the grass walls of the village shone like burnished gold through the palms in the setting sun.
source_text: How long he sat there he did not know, until, suddenly, across his sightless vision he saw form a long horizontal line of white. It was curious. But as he watched it grow in definiteness he saw that it was a coral reef smoking in the white Pacific surges. Next, in the line of breakers he made out a small canoe, an outrigger canoe. In the stern he saw a young bronzed god in scarlet hip-cloth dipping a flashing paddle. He recognized him. He was Moti, the youngest son of Tati, the chief, and this was Tahiti, and beyond that smoking reef lay the sweet land of Papara and the chief’s grass house by the river’s mouth. It was the end of the day, and Moti was coming home from the fishing. He was waiting for the rush of a big breaker whereon to jump the reef. Then he saw himself, sitting forward in the canoe as he had often sat in the past, dipping a paddle that waited Moti’s word to dig in like mad when the turquoise wall of the great breaker rose behind them. Next, he was no longer an onlooker but was himself in the canoe, Moti was crying out, they were both thrusting hard with their paddles, racing on the steep face of the flying turquoise. Under the bow the water was hissing as from a steam jet, the air was filled with driven spray, there was a rush and rumble and long-echoing roar, and the canoe floated on the placid water of the lagoon. Moti laughed and shook the salt water from his eyes, and together they paddled in to the pounded-coral beach where Tati’s grass walls through the cocoanut-palms showed golden in the setting sun.

text: He slept deeply throughout the night, not moving until the postman’s morning arrival woke him. Martin felt weary and indifferent as he sifted through his correspondence without purpose. One slender envelope from a disreputable magazine held a check for twenty-two dollars. He had been hounding them for this payment for a year and a half. He looked at the sum with total apathy. The former spark of joy at receiving a publisher’s check had died out. Unlike his previous earnings, this one was not heavy with the hope of a grand future. To him, it was merely twenty-two dollars, and its only significance was that it would provide his next few meals.
source_text: He slept heavily all night, and did not stir until aroused by the postman on his morning round. Martin felt tired and passive, and went through his letters aimlessly. One thin envelope, from a robber magazine, contained a check for twenty-two dollars. He had been dunning for it for a year and a half. He noted its amount apathetically. The old-time thrill at receiving a publisher’s check was gone. Unlike his earlier checks, this one was not pregnant with promise of great things to come. To him it was a check for twenty-two dollars, that was all, and it would buy him something to eat.

text: He once traveled to San Francisco, intent on seeking out the "genuine article." Yet, at the very moment he reached the upstairs landing, he recoiled in sudden panic, fleeing back into the teeming streets of the slums. He was gripped by a strange dread at the prospect of hearing philosophy debated, and he hurried away like a fugitive, terrified that one of those authentic figures might happen by and recognize him.
source_text: Once he made a trip to San Francisco to look up the “real dirt.” But at the last moment, as he stepped into the upstairs entrance, he recoiled and turned and fled through the swarming ghetto. He was frightened at the thought of hearing philosophy discussed, and he fled furtively, for fear that some one of the “real dirt” might chance along and recognize him.

text: In its following edition, *The Parthenon* gloated over the sensation it had caused, taking shots at Sir John Value and callously monetizing Brissenden’s death. A major daily with a verified circulation of half a million ran a supposedly impromptu poem by Helen Della Delmar, in which she mocked and derided Brissenden. She was also responsible for a second piece, a parody written to lampoon him.
source_text: _The Parthenon_ came out in its next number patting itself on the back for the stir it had made, sneering at Sir John Value, and exploiting Brissenden’s death with ruthless commercialism. A newspaper with a sworn circulation of half a million published an original and spontaneous poem by Helen Della Delmar, in which she gibed and sneered at Brissenden. Also, she was guilty of a second poem, in which she parodied him.

text: Pulpits soon echoed with condemnations of “Evanescence,” and a particular rector, who had the temerity to champion its verses, was summarily excommunicated for his heterodoxy. The sprawling poem served as a font of global amusement. Lampoonists and illustrators seized upon it with howling mockery, and within the scandal-sheets of the day, witlings suggested that Arthur Sterling had confessed to Julian Fane, in the strictest confidence, that five stanzas of “Evanescence” would provoke a man to assault a mendicant, while ten would drive him headlong into the depths of the harbor.
source_text: Ministers began to preach sermons against “Ephemera,” and one, who too stoutly stood for much of its content, was expelled for heresy. The great poem contributed to the gayety of the world. The comic verse-writers and the cartoonists took hold of it with screaming laughter, and in the personal columns of society weeklies jokes were perpetrated on it to the effect that Charley Frensham told Archie Jennings, in confidence, that five lines of “Ephemera” would drive a man to beat a cripple, and that ten lines would send him to the bottom of the river.

text: During this time he drifted, resting and recovering from the exhaustive journey he had made through the world of ideas. When the check for three hundred and fifty dollars arrived from *The Parthenon*, he delivered it to the local attorney who managed Brissenden’s affairs for his family. Martin secured a receipt for the sum and, at the same time, provided a note for the hundred dollars Brissenden had previously lent him.
source_text: In the meantime he drifted, resting and recuperating after the long traverse he had made through the realm of knowledge. When _The Parthenon_ check of three hundred and fifty dollars was forwarded to him, he turned it over to the local lawyer who had attended to Brissenden’s affairs for his family. Martin took a receipt for the check, and at the same time gave a note for the hundred dollars Brissenden had let him have.

text: Eventually, a realization of his own solitude began to weigh on Martin. He possessed great physical strength and health, yet he suffered from a total lack of occupation. The end of his intellectual labors, the loss of Brissenden, and his break with Ruth had carved a hollow space in his soul—a space that could not be occupied by the idle pleasures of café life or the fragrance of Egyptian cigarettes. While the South Seas beckoned him, he felt that his affairs in the United States were not quite concluded. Two of his works were on the verge of publication, with others likely to follow. He could extract a significant sum from these ventures, and he resolved to wait until he could depart for the islands with a heavy purse. He recalled a specific valley and bay in the Marquesas that could be purchased for a thousand Chili dollars. The property spanned ten thousand acres, rising from a sheltered, horseshoe harbor to the jagged, mist-veiled peaks. It was a lush wilderness of tropical fruit and feral animals—pigs, chickens, and cattle—while high on the cliffs, wild goats were chased by packs of dogs. The entire region was untamed and devoid of human inhabitants. For a thousand Chili dollars, the valley and the bay could be his.
source_text: One day Martin became aware that he was lonely. He was healthy and strong, and had nothing to do. The cessation from writing and studying, the death of Brissenden, and the estrangement from Ruth had made a big hole in his life; and his life refused to be pinned down to good living in cafés and the smoking of Egyptian cigarettes. It was true the South Seas were calling to him, but he had a feeling that the game was not yet played out in the United States. Two books were soon to be published, and he had more books that might find publication. Money could be made out of them, and he would wait and take a sackful of it into the South Seas. He knew a valley and a bay in the Marquesas that he could buy for a thousand Chili dollars. The valley ran from the horseshoe, land-locked bay to the tops of the dizzy, cloud-capped peaks and contained perhaps ten thousand acres. It was filled with tropical fruits, wild chickens, and wild pigs, with an occasional herd of wild cattle, while high up among the peaks were herds of wild goats harried by packs of wild dogs. The whole place was wild. Not a human lived in it. And he could buy it and the bay for a thousand Chili dollars.

text: Julian allowed the subject to drift away, steering their talk into lighter currents. They dined in the hotel’s hall, where he indulged in vintage wines and rare delicacies; afterward, he claimed her for every dance, ignoring the room until her strength began to fail. He moved with an effortless grace, and she spun in a dizzying paradise, her forehead resting against his jacket, wishing the world might stop spinning right there. Later, they wandered deep into the grove where, in the timeless way of lovers, she sat upon the moss and he stretched out, his head heavy in her lap. He dozed fitfully while she ran her fingers through his hair, watching his quiet face and loving him with a total, unshielded heart. When he opened his eyes suddenly, he saw the naked devotion written in her gaze. Her lashes brushed her cheeks for a second, then her eyes met his again with a gentle, stubborn pride.
source_text: But Martin went no further into the matter. He led the conversation off into other channels. They had lunch in the restaurant, where he ordered wine and expensive delicacies and afterward he danced with her and with no one but her, till she was tired. He was a good dancer, and she whirled around and around with him in a heaven of delight, her head against his shoulder, wishing that it could last forever. Later in the afternoon they strayed off among the trees, where, in the good old fashion, she sat down while he sprawled on his back, his head in her lap. He lay and dozed, while she fondled his hair, looked down on his closed eyes, and loved him without reserve. Looking up suddenly, he read the tender advertisement in her face. Her eyes fluttered down, then they opened and looked into his with soft defiance.

text: Martin relished the conflict, feeling a brief revival of his former combative spirit. However, the sensation faded rapidly, leaving him burdened by a profound melancholy. He felt incredibly old—eons older than the reckless, happy-go-lucky friends of his youth. He had moved too far along his path to ever retreat. Their lifestyle, which he once embraced, now seemed repulsive to him. He felt let down by everything; he had become a total outsider. Just as the local beer tasted harsh, their presence felt unrefined. The distance between them was immense, an abyss created by the countless books he had read. He was an exile of his own making, having ventured so far into the intellectual wilderness that his old home was unreachable. Yet, being human, his natural desire for community remained starving. He hadn't found a new sanctuary. His old friends, his family, and the social elite all failed to understand him, and even the girl at his side—the one he placed on a pedestal—could not grasp his true self or the depth of his respect for her. A touch of bitterness colored his sorrow as he dwelled on his isolation.
source_text: Martin had enjoyed the fight, with a recrudescence of the old fighting thrills. But they quickly died away, and he was oppressed by a great sadness. He felt very old—centuries older than those careless, care-free young companions of his others days. He had travelled far, too far to go back. Their mode of life, which had once been his, was now distasteful to him. He was disappointed in it all. He had developed into an alien. As the steam beer had tasted raw, so their companionship seemed raw to him. He was too far removed. Too many thousands of opened books yawned between them and him. He had exiled himself. He had travelled in the vast realm of intellect until he could no longer return home. On the other hand, he was human, and his gregarious need for companionship remained unsatisfied. He had found no new home. As the gang could not understand him, as his own family could not understand him, as the bourgeoisie could not understand him, so this girl beside him, whom he honored high, could not understand him nor the honor he paid her. His sadness was not untouched with bitterness as he thought it over.

text: "The Zenith’s Decay" arrived in the waning days of October. As Julian severed the twine of the courier’s parcel and watched the six complimentary volumes spill across the table, a leaden grief took hold of him. He reflected on the frantic joy that would have consumed him had this moment arrived but a few months earlier, weighing that phantom ecstasy against his present, hollow indifference. This was his debut, his first published labor, yet his pulse remained stubbornly steady, mired in a quiet desolation. The milestone held no weight. Its only remaining utility was the potential for profit, and for wealth he felt nothing but a cold disregard.
source_text: “The Shame of the Sun” was published in October. As Martin cut the cords of the express package and the half-dozen complimentary copies from the publishers spilled out on the table, a heavy sadness fell upon him. He thought of the wild delight that would have been his had this happened a few short months before, and he contrasted that delight that should have been with his present uncaring coldness. His book, his first book, and his pulse had not gone up a fraction of a beat, and he was only sad. It meant little to him now. The most it meant was that it might bring some money, and little enough did he care for money.

text: He read the weekly reviews from the clipping bureau with the same cold indifference he had felt toward "The Shame of the Sun." The book was an evident success, which meant more gold in the coffers. He could now provide for Lizzie, fulfill his many promises, and still have enough remaining to build his castle of grass.
source_text: Just as emotionlessly as he had received “The Shame of the Sun” did he read the reviews of it that came in weekly from the clipping bureau. The book was making a hit, that was evident. It meant more gold in the money sack. He could fix up Lizzie, redeem all his promises, and still have enough left to build his grass-walled castle.

text: "How come you don't shop with me anymore?" the Portuguese grocer asked Martin that evening, stepping outside to flag him down as he got off the streetcar. Martin explained that he wasn't preparing his own meals these days, then went inside to enjoy a complimentary glass of wine. He noted that it was the highest quality bottle the grocer kept in stock.
source_text: “Why don’t you trade with me no more?” the Portuguese grocer asked Martin that evening, stepping out to hail him when he got off the car; and Martin explained that he wasn’t doing his own cooking any more, and then went in and had a drink of wine on the house. He noted it was the best wine the grocer had in stock.

text: “Maria,” Martin declared that night, “I am moving on. And you will be leaving this place yourself before long. You’ll be the one renting the house out, a landlady in your own right. You have a brother in the dairy trade over in San Leandro or Haywards. I want you to return all this laundry just as it is—unwashed, do you understand?—and go see him tomorrow. Tell him to come find me at the Metropole in Oakland. He’ll be able to recognize a quality milk ranch when he sees one.”
source_text: “Maria,” Martin announced that night, “I’m going to leave you. And you’re going to leave here yourself soon. Then you can rent the house and be a landlord yourself. You’ve a brother in San Leandro or Haywards, and he’s in the milk business. I want you to send all your washing back unwashed—understand?—unwashed, and to go out to San Leandro to-morrow, or Haywards, or wherever it is, and see that brother of yours. Tell him to come to see me. I’ll be stopping at the Metropole down in Oakland. He’ll know a good milk-ranch when he sees one.”

text: Wealth and acclaim cascaded upon him; he streaked across the firmament of letters like a meteor, more diverted than impressed by the clamor he provoked. A singular point troubled him, a triviality that would have baffled the public had they known of it. Yet the world would have found his preoccupation more perplexing than the trifle itself, which to him had grown monumental. Magistrate Sterling had requested his company at dinner. That was the minor detail, the seed of a paradox that was rapidly becoming an obsession. He had once been insolent to Sterling, treating him with raw contempt, yet the Magistrate, encountering him in the street, had offered a gracious invitation. Julian reflected on the many occasions he had met the man at the Hallowells’, during which Sterling had never once suggested a meal. Why had the invitation been withheld then? he asked himself. He had not transformed. He was still the same Julian Thorne. What constituted the change? Was it the mere fact that his prose now lived within the boards of a book? But the work was a thing of the past. It was not a feat he had performed since his rise. It was a victory achieved at the very hour Sterling was joining the chorus of scoffers, deriding his philosophy and his mind. Consequently, it was not for any inherent merit, but for a purely phantom prestige that Magistrate Sterling now desired his presence at the table.
source_text: Money poured in on him, fame poured in on him; he flashed, comet-like, through the world of literature, and he was more amused than interested by the stir he was making. One thing was puzzling him, a little thing that would have puzzled the world had it known. But the world would have puzzled over his bepuzzlement rather than over the little thing that to him loomed gigantic. Judge Blount invited him to dinner. That was the little thing, or the beginning of the little thing, that was soon to become the big thing. He had insulted Judge Blount, treated him abominably, and Judge Blount, meeting him on the street, invited him to dinner. Martin bethought himself of the numerous occasions on which he had met Judge Blount at the Morses’ and when Judge Blount had not invited him to dinner. Why had he not invited him to dinner then? he asked himself. He had not changed. He was the same Martin Eden. What made the difference? The fact that the stuff he had written had appeared inside the covers of books? But it was work performed. It was not something he had done since. It was achievement accomplished at the very time Judge Blount was sharing this general view and sneering at his Spencer and his intellect. Therefore it was not for any real value, but for a purely fictitious value that Judge Blount invited him to dinner.

text: He was kept fully occupied with the task of selling off his mountain of manuscripts. Editors were now inundating him with requests, having finally recognized that he was a true stylist whose work possessed genuine substance. Following the publication of “The Cradle of Beauty,” *The Northern Review* petitioned him for six more essays. He might have obliged them from his existing stock had *Burton’s Magazine* not stepped in with a speculative offer of five hundred dollars per piece for five essays. He responded by setting his price at a thousand dollars each. He could not forget that these same magazines had once rejected these very manuscripts with a mechanical, cold-blooded indifference. They had once made him suffer through their neglect, and now he intended to make them pay. *Burton’s* met his price for five, and the remaining four were snatched up at the same rate by *Mackintosh’s Monthly*, leaving the cash-strapped *Northern Review* unable to compete. In this manner, “The High Priests of Mystery,” “The Wonder-Dreamers,” “The Yardstick of the Ego,” “Philosophy of Illusion,” “God and Clod,” “Art and Biology,” “Critics and Test-tubes,” “Star-dust,” and “The Dignity of Usury” were released to the public, sparking a series of intellectual storms and lingering controversies that would resonate for a long time to come.
source_text: He was kept busy disposing of his heap of manuscripts. He was overwhelmed by requests from editors. It had been discovered that he was a stylist, with meat under his style. _The Northern Review_, after publishing “The Cradle of Beauty,” had written him for half a dozen similar essays, which would have been supplied out of the heap, had not _Burton’s Magazine_, in a speculative mood, offered him five hundred dollars each for five essays. He wrote back that he would supply the demand, but at a thousand dollars an essay. He remembered that all these manuscripts had been refused by the very magazines that were now clamoring for them. And their refusals had been cold-blooded, automatic, stereotyped. They had made him sweat, and now he intended to make them sweat. _Burton’s Magazine_ paid his price for five essays, and the remaining four, at the same rate, were snapped up by _Mackintosh’s Monthly, The Northern Review_ being too poor to stand the pace. Thus went out to the world “The High Priests of Mystery,” “The Wonder-Dreamers,” “The Yardstick of the Ego,” “Philosophy of Illusion,” “God and Clod,” “Art and Biology,” “Critics and Test-tubes,” “Star-dust,” and “The Dignity of Usury,”—to raise storms and rumblings and mutterings that were many a day in dying down.

text: The public, nonetheless, greeted the “Smoke of Joy” collection with a blend of skepticism and frostiness. The sheer boldness and unorthodox nature of the tales proved an affront to middle-class morality and ingrained biases. However, when a Parisian translation became an overnight sensation, the American and British markets followed the trend, buying the book in such numbers that Martin forced the staid firm of Singletree, Darnley & Co. to grant him a twenty-five percent royalty for a third book and thirty percent for a fourth. These two volumes gathered the rest of his short fiction, including pieces that were currently finishing their magazine runs. “The Ring of Bells” and his various horror stories filled one anthology; the other was comprised of “Adventure,” “The Pot,” “The Wine of Life,” “The Whirlpool,” “The Jostling Street,” and four additional works. The Lowell-Meredith Company secured the rights to his essays, while Maxmillian acquired his “Sea Lyrics” and the “Love-cycle,” the latter of which was serialized in the _Ladies’ Home Companion_ only after the payment of a truly exorbitant fee.
source_text: The public, however, received the “Smoke of Joy” collection rather dubiously and coldly. The audacity and unconventionality of the storiettes was a shock to bourgeois morality and prejudice; but when Paris went mad over the immediate translation that was made, the American and English reading public followed suit and bought so many copies that Martin compelled the conservative house of Singletree, Darnley & Co. to pay a flat royalty of twenty-five per cent for a third book, and thirty per cent flat for a fourth. These two volumes comprised all the short stories he had written and which had received, or were receiving, serial publication. “The Ring of Bells” and his horror stories constituted one collection; the other collection was composed of “Adventure,” “The Pot,” “The Wine of Life,” “The Whirlpool,” “The Jostling Street,” and four other stories. The Lowell-Meredith Company captured the collection of all his essays, and the Maxmillian Company got his “Sea Lyrics” and the “Love-cycle,” the latter receiving serial publication in the _Ladies’ Home Companion_ after the payment of an extortionate price.

text: Julian exhaled with relief as he dispatched the final bundle of prose. The palm-thatched villa and the white, copper-clad sloop were nearly within reach. He had, at last, tested Thorne’s grim theory that no work of true merit could ever find its way into the journals. His own triumph proved Thorne’s cynicism wrong.
source_text: Martin heaved a sigh of relief when he had disposed of the last manuscript. The grass-walled castle and the white, coppered schooner were very near to him. Well, at any rate he had discovered Brissenden’s contention that nothing of merit found its way into the magazines. His own success demonstrated that Brissenden had been wrong.

text: Elias approached Silas one afternoon—Elias, a man of the unyielding clay; and Silas turned to him with a sense of reprieve, absorbing the feverish details of a venture so transparently fraudulent it piqued his curiosity as a weaver of fictions rather than a seeker of profit. Pausing amidst his frantic oratory, Elias took a moment to observe that in the greater part of his "Radiance Rebuked," Silas had played the part of a common fool.
source_text: Kreis came to Martin one day—Kreis, of the “real dirt”; and Martin turned to him with relief, to receive the glowing details of a scheme sufficiently wild-catty to interest him as a fictionist rather than an investor. Kreis paused long enough in the midst of his exposition to tell him that in most of his “Shame of the Sun” he had been a chump.

text: He found certain elements in the journals quite entertaining. Every publication seemed to be laying claim to him. *Warren’s Monthly* announced to its readership that it was perpetually seeking out new voices and had been the first to present Martin Eden to the world. *The White Mouse* vied for credit, as did *The Northern Review* and *Mackintosh’s Magazine*, until they were all eclipsed by *The Globe*, which pointed exultantly to its back issues where the mutilated “Sea Lyrics” remained interred. *Youth and Age*, a periodical that had returned from the dead after evading its creditors, put forth an even earlier claim, though its only audience was children in the countryside. The *Transcontinental* offered a poised and persuasive explanation of how it had originally found Martin Eden, a story that *The Hornet* aggressively challenged by presenting “The Peri and the Pearl.” The quiet protestations of Singletree, Darnley & Co. were drowned out by the noise. Moreover, that house possessed no magazine of its own to make its assertions any louder.
source_text: There were things, however, in the magazines that amused him. All the magazines were claiming him. _Warren’s Monthly_ advertised to its subscribers that it was always on the quest after new writers, and that, among others, it had introduced Martin Eden to the reading public. _The White Mouse_ claimed him; so did _The Northern Review_ and _Mackintosh’s Magazine_, until silenced by _The Globe_, which pointed triumphantly to its files where the mangled “Sea Lyrics” lay buried. _Youth and Age_, which had come to life again after having escaped paying its bills, put in a prior claim, which nobody but farmers’ children ever read. The _Transcontinental_ made a dignified and convincing statement of how it first discovered Martin Eden, which was warmly disputed by _The Hornet_, with the exhibit of “The Peri and the Pearl.” The modest claim of Singletree, Darnley & Co. was lost in the din. Besides, that publishing firm did not own a magazine wherewith to make its claim less modest.

text: A tapping at the door pulled him back to the present. He hadn't been asleep, and his mind immediately jumped to the idea of a telegram, a letter, or perhaps a staff member returning with his fresh laundry. He was still preoccupied with Joe, wondering about his whereabouts, as he called out, “Come in.”
source_text: A knock at the door aroused him. He was not asleep, and his mind immediately connected the knock with a telegram, or letter, or perhaps one of the servants bringing back clean clothes from the laundry. He was thinking about Joe and wondering where he was, as he said, “Come in.”

text: My poor, tired head." She raised her hand, brushing her fingers tenderly through his hair. "Let your thoughts be still now. Let us begin again, today. I have loved you through everything. I realize I was weak to surrender to my mother’s demands; I should have stood firm. Yet I have heard you speak so many times with great compassion for the failings of mankind. Grant that same grace to me. I was mistaken. Forgive me.
source_text: “Poor, dear head.” She reached up a hand and passed the fingers soothingly through his hair. “Let it go around no more. Let us begin anew, now. I loved you all the time. I know that I was weak in yielding to my mother’s will. I should not have done so. Yet I have heard you speak so often with broad charity of the fallibility and frailty of humankind. Extend that charity to me. I acted mistakenly. Forgive me.”

text: “I do indeed pardon you,” he said with a flick of restless irritation. “It is a simple thing to overlook what was never truly a fault. Your conduct asks for no repentance. We each navigate by our own dim lanterns, and we can do no more than follow where they lead. I might just as easily beseech your forgiveness for my own inability to find work.”
source_text: “Oh, I do forgive,” he said impatiently. “It is easy to forgive where there is really nothing to forgive. Nothing that you have done requires forgiveness. One acts according to one’s lights, and more than that one cannot do. As well might I ask you to forgive me for my not getting a job.”

text: “I concede the truth of your words. I was a captive to my own timidity. My affection was too narrow, but I have mastered a deeper devotion. I love you in your entirety: for your present self, your past, and the very crucible that forged you. I prize the qualities that set you apart from my own world; though your philosophies elude me now, I know I am capable of reaching them. I will make their study my life’s work. Even your vices—the smoke and the coarse tongue—are facets of your being that I now hold dear. I am still a student of life. This quarter-hour has taught me more than years past. My presence here, this bold step, is the first fruit of my education. Oh, Julian!”
source_text: “I know that much you have said is so. I have been afraid of life. I did not love you well enough. I have learned to love better. I love you for what you are, for what you were, for the ways even by which you have become. I love you for the ways wherein you differ from what you call my class, for your beliefs which I do not understand but which I know I can come to understand. I shall devote myself to understanding them. And even your smoking and your swearing—they are part of you and I will love you for them, too. I can still learn. In the last ten minutes I have learned much. That I have dared to come here is a token of what I have already learned. Oh, Martin!—”

text: He tilted his head back and shut his eyes; much like a sobbing child who finds distraction from its misery by observing the sunbeams filtering through tear-streaked lashes, Martin drifted away from his ailment and Ruth’s presence. He was consumed by the sight of lush foliage, ignited by a searing sun that flared brilliantly against the inner surface of his eyelids. There was no comfort in that verdant glow. The light felt too brutal and intense. It pained him to behold it, yet he continued to stare, compelled by a force he did not understand.
source_text: He leaned his head back and closed his eyes; and like a child, crying, that forgets its grief in watching the sunlight percolate through the tear-dimmed films over the pupils, so Martin forgot his sickness, the presence of Ruth, everything, in watching the masses of vegetation, shot through hotly with sunshine that took form and blazed against this background of his eyelids. It was not restful, that green foliage. The sunlight was too raw and glaring. It hurt him to look at it, and yet he looked, he knew not why.

text: “I beg your indulgence,” he exclaimed, rising with a frantic suddenness. “My mind is quite unmoored; I had lost all sense of your being here.” He clutched his forehead as if to steady himself. “I am not quite whole today. I shall see you back to your door, but we must slip through the rear galley to remain unobserved. Lower your netting, and we shall pass through the shadows without incident.”
source_text: “Oh, forgive me,” he cried, springing to his feet. “I’m not myself, you know. I forgot you were here.” He put his hand to his head. “You see, I’m not just right. I’ll take you home. We can go out by the servants’ entrance. No one will see us. Pull down that veil and everything will be all right.”

text: Once more, she tried to pull her hand away. He felt a flicker of curiosity; now that she was no longer in danger, fear seemed to take hold of her. She appeared almost desperate to get away from him. Finding no logical reason for it, he assumed it was just her nerves. He held her hand firmly to stop her from withdrawing and continued walking beside her. Halfway down the block, he noticed a man in a long coat ducking back into a doorway. He stole a quick look as he passed and, despite the high, upturned collar, he was certain he recognized Norman, Ruth’s brother.
source_text: Again she started to remove her hand. He felt a momentary curiosity. Now that she was out of danger she was afraid. She was in almost a panic to be quit of him. He could see no reason for it and attributed it to her nervousness. So he restrained her withdrawing hand and started to walk on with her. Halfway down the block, he saw a man in a long overcoat shrink back into a doorway. He shot a glance in as he passed by, and, despite the high turned-up collar, he was certain that he recognized Ruth’s brother, Norman.

text: Upon returning to his room, he lay down and drifted into a sleep as peaceful as an infant’s. The night’s events had left no mark on him, as his mind had become unresponsive to any external influence. The brief flicker of affection he felt for Joe had vanished almost instantly; by the next minute, he was merely annoyed by the former laundry worker’s presence and the exhausting demand for conversation. Even the fact that he was set to depart for his cherished South Seas in five days held no meaning for him. He shut his eyes and rested soundly for eight continuous hours. He did not stir, nor did he shift his body or experience any dreams. Sleep had transformed into total oblivion, and every morning he woke with a sense of sorrow. Existence was a source of weariness and anxiety, and the very passage of time was a nuisance.
source_text: Once in his room he got into bed and slipped off to sleep as gently as a child. The occurrences of the evening had made no impression on him. His mind was dead to impressions. The glow of warmth with which he met Joe had been most fleeting. The succeeding minute he had been bothered by the ex-laundryman’s presence and by the compulsion of conversation. That in five more days he sailed for his loved South Seas meant nothing to him. So he closed his eyes and slept normally and comfortably for eight uninterrupted hours. He was not restless. He did not change his position, nor did he dream. Sleep had become to him oblivion, and each day that he awoke, he awoke with regret. Life worried and bored him, and time was a vexation.

text: “Listen, Arthur,” he said to his old comrade when they met the following morning, “there is a gentleman from Lyon with an establishment on Forty-second. He has secured his fortune and is eager to return to the Continent. It is a premier little steam-laundry, excellently fitted. There is your opportunity to find a permanent footing. Take this; procure some decent clothes and present yourself at this man’s office by ten o’clock. He has vetted the property on my behalf and will escort you through the works. If it pleases you, and you judge the value of fifteen thousand to be sound, give me the word and the deed is yours. Now, be on your way. My morning is crowded. I shall see you later.”
source_text: “Say, Joe,” was his greeting to his old-time working-mate next morning, “there’s a Frenchman out on Twenty-eighth Street. He’s made a pot of money, and he’s going back to France. It’s a dandy, well-appointed, small steam laundry. There’s a start for you if you want to settle down. Here, take this; buy some clothes with it and be at this man’s office by ten o’clock. He looked up the laundry for me, and he’ll take you out and show you around. If you like it, and think it is worth the price—twelve thousand—let me know and it is yours. Now run along. I’m busy. I’ll see you later.”

text: Silas was jubilant regarding the forge. The arrangements were finalized, and he was to take command of the place the following morning. Elias reclined upon the cot, his eyelids sealed, while the other’s voice droned on. His mind had wandered into a profound distance—a space so far removed that he was scarcely conscious of his own reflections. Only with a straining of the spirit did he manage an occasional word. This was Silas, a friend he had long cherished, yet the man’s zest for living was simply too piercing. The raucous collision of such energy against Elias’s blunted intellect was an agony. It was a sharp instrument digging into his frayed nerves. When Silas recalled their promise to one day box together, Elias felt a cry of pure anguish nearly rupture his throat.
source_text: Joe was delighted with the laundry. Everything was settled, and he would enter into possession next day. Martin lay on the bed, with closed eyes, while the other talked on. Martin’s thoughts were far away—so far away that he was rarely aware that he was thinking. It was only by an effort that he occasionally responded. And yet this was Joe, whom he had always liked. But Joe was too keen with life. The boisterous impact of it on Martin’s jaded mind was a hurt. It was an aching probe to his tired sensitiveness. When Joe reminded him that sometime in the future they were going to put on the gloves together, he could almost have screamed.

text: “Listen, Joe,” he said, “you aren’t stuck with that laundry. You don’t owe it a thing. You’re free to sell the shop whenever you like and spend every cent of the profit. If you ever grow weary of the grind and want to travel again, just walk away. You should do whatever it is that truly makes you happy.”
source_text: “You know, Joe,” he said, “that you are not tied down to that laundry. There are no strings on it. You can sell it any time and blow the money. Any time you get sick of it and want to hit the road, just pull out. Do what will make you the happiest.”

text: Existence struck him as a fierce, bleaching radiance that tormented the fragile sight of a convalescent. Through every hour of wakefulness, reality flared with a jagged brilliance that pressed upon his spirit. It was a searing, intolerable burden. This was Julian’s inaugural journey in the grand cabins; previously, his life at sea had been spent in the cramped forecastle or the suffocating, coal-blackened bowels of the hull. In those years, as he climbed the iron stairs from the furnace heat, he would catch fleeting sights of the elite in their crisp, pale garments, idling under canopies that shielded them from the elements. They were pampered by deferential attendants who catered to every trivial desire, and to Julian, their sphere of existence had seemed a literal heaven. Yet here he sat, the ship’s most distinguished guest, positioned at the captain’s right hand, while his heart wandered back to the soot and the steerage in a futile search for the paradise he had misplaced. He had failed to find a new sanctuary, and the old one was now beyond his reach.
source_text: Life was to him like strong, white light that hurts the tired eyes of a sick person. During every conscious moment life blazed in a raw glare around him and upon him. It hurt. It hurt intolerably. It was the first time in his life that Martin had travelled first class. On ships at sea he had always been in the forecastle, the steerage, or in the black depths of the coal-hold, passing coal. In those days, climbing up the iron ladders out the pit of stifling heat, he had often caught glimpses of the passengers, in cool white, doing nothing but enjoy themselves, under awnings spread to keep the sun and wind away from them, with subservient stewards taking care of their every want and whim, and it had seemed to him that the realm in which they moved and had their being was nothing else than paradise. Well, here he was, the great man on board, in the midmost centre of it, sitting at the captain’s right hand, and yet vainly harking back to forecastle and stoke-hole in quest of the Paradise he had lost. He had found no new one, and now he could not find the old one.

text: On the morning the *Cygnet* stalled in the windless latitudes, Julian’s despondency deepened into a sharper agony. Slumber had abandoned him. Having been saturated by heavy dreams, he was now condemned to wakefulness, forced to bear the bleached, relentless brilliance of being. He drifted through the ship like a ghost. The atmosphere hung heavy and damp, and even the fitful storms brought no coolness to the stifling heat. The very pulse of his blood felt like a burden. He trudged across the boards until his joints throbbed, then huddled in his lounge until the urge to move drove him upright once more. With a grim effort, he concluded the journal he had been reading and sought out a few books of verse from the vessel’s shelves. But the stanzas offered no sanctuary, and he returned, inevitably, to his rhythmic pacing.
source_text: The day the _Mariposa_ entered the doldrums, Martin was more miserable than ever. He could no longer sleep. He was soaked with sleep, and perforce he must now stay awake and endure the white glare of life. He moved about restlessly. The air was sticky and humid, and the rain-squalls were unrefreshing. He ached with life. He walked around the deck until that hurt too much, then sat in his chair until he was compelled to walk again. He forced himself at last to finish the magazine, and from the steamer library he culled several volumes of poetry. But they could not hold him, and once more he took to walking.

text: He glanced once more at the open porthole. Swinburne had provided the necessary clarity. Life was a malady, or rather, it had soured into something beyond endurance. “That dead men rise up never!” That single line moved him with a deep sense of thankfulness. It was the one act of mercy in the entire universe. When existence became a throbbing exhaustion, death stood ready to lull him into a perpetual sleep. What was he still lingering for? It was time to depart.
source_text: He looked again at the open port. Swinburne had furnished the key. Life was ill, or, rather, it had become ill—an unbearable thing. “That dead men rise up never!” That line stirred him with a profound feeling of gratitude. It was the one beneficent thing in the universe. When life became an aching weariness, death was ready to soothe away to everlasting sleep. But what was he waiting for? It was time to go.

text: He extinguished the cabin light to keep his movements hidden, then eased himself feet-first through the porthole. When his shoulders jammed, he pulled back inside to readjust, tucking one arm tight against his flank. The ship gave a timely lurch, helping him slide through until he was suspended by his hands. As soon as his feet dipped into the ocean, he let go. He found himself immersed in a pale, frothing turbulence. The *Mariposa*’s flank surged past like a massive, shadowed wall, its dark surface flickering with illuminated windows. She was moving fast. Before he could fully grasp it, the ship had pulled away, leaving him to tread water gently in the crackling foam of its trail.
source_text: Turning off the light in his room so that it might not betray him, he went out the port-hole feet first. His shoulders stuck, and he forced himself back so as to try it with one arm down by his side. A roll of the steamer aided him, and he was through, hanging by his hands. When his feet touched the sea, he let go. He was in a milky froth of water. The side of the _Mariposa_ rushed past him like a dark wall, broken here and there by lighted ports. She was certainly making time. Almost before he knew it, he was astern, swimming gently on the foam-crackling surface.

text: The primitive, unbidden drive for existence asserted itself. He stopped his rhythmic paddling, but as the tide crested over his lips, his limbs thrashed out in a sharp, upward surge. *The will to live*, he reflected, the notion eliciting a derisive scowl. Truly, he possessed a spirit of such magnitude that, through one ultimate act of defiance, it might extinguish itself and fall into silence.
source_text: It was the automatic instinct to live. He ceased swimming, but the moment he felt the water rising above his mouth the hands struck out sharply with a lifting movement. The will to live, was his thought, and the thought was accompanied by a sneer. Well, he had will,—ay, will strong enough that with one last exertion it could destroy itself and cease to be.

text: Silas and I made our way down to the amusement piers that afternoon, our pockets jingling with a shared four dollars and his mind desperate for a reprieve. His spirits were clouded by the disappearance of Elspeth O’Shea, the Mayo girl he meant to marry, who had been missing since she boarded a steamer for New York three months prior. She had carried three hundred dollars with her—two hundred from her own thrift and another hundred yielded from the sale of Silas’s inheritance, a snug cabin and a prize hog on the slopes of Glen Corrib. Since the arrival of the letter declaring she was on her way, Silas had received not a word nor a sign of her. He had placed notices in every newspaper in the city, but no trace of the girl could be found.
source_text: Tobin and me, the two of us, went down to Coney one day, for there was four dollars between us, and Tobin had need of distractions. For there was Katie Mahorner, his sweetheart, of County Sligo, lost since she started for America three months before with two hundred dollars, her own savings, and one hundred dollars from the sale of Tobin’s inherited estate, a fine cottage and pig on the Bog Shannaugh. And since the letter that Tobin got saying that she had started to come to him not a bit of news had he heard or seen of Katie Mahorner. Tobin advertised in the papers, but nothing could be found of the colleen.

text: Tobin and I headed for Coney Island, thinking a trip down the water slides and the scent of popcorn might lift his heavy spirits. But Tobin was a stubborn soul, and his gloom seemed part of his very being. He gritted his teeth at the squealing balloons and spat curses at the films; while he was happy to drink whenever someone offered, he held the puppet shows in contempt and was ready to throw punches at every photographer who came near.
source_text: So, to Coney me and Tobin went, thinking that a turn at the chutes and the smell of the popcorn might raise the heart in his bosom. But Tobin was a hardheaded man, and the sadness stuck in his skin. He ground his teeth at the crying balloons; he cursed the moving pictures; and, though he would drink whenever asked, he scorned Punch and Judy, and was for licking the tintype men as they came.

text: “You should know,” the man of destiny declared, “that my vocation is a literary one. I wander the world after dark, seeking human quirks among the crowds and truth in the stars above. When you found me, I was contemplating the elevated train alongside the moon. To me, the speeding transit is pure poetry and art, whereas the moon is but a dull, lifeless body moving by rote. These are personal views, however; in the literary trade, the standards are quite the opposite. It is my hope to write a book detailing the strange phenomena I have observed in this life.”
source_text: “Ye must know,” says the man of destiny, “that me walk in life is one that is called the literary. I wander abroad be night seeking idiosyncrasies in the masses and truth in the heavens above. When ye came upon me I was in contemplation of the elevated road in conjunction with the chief luminary of night. The rapid transit is poetry and art: the moon but a tedious, dry body, moving by rote. But these are private opinions, for, in the business of literature, the conditions are reversed. ’Tis me hope to be writing a book to explain the strange things I have discovered in life.”

text: In the foyer below was a mail-slot that would accept no letter, and a push-button from which no mortal hand could coax a ring. Also belonging to the scene was a card bearing the name “Mr. Julian Beaumont Smith.” The “Beaumont” had been brandished with pride during a former season of prosperity when its owner was paid thirty-five dollars a week. Now, with the income shrunken to twenty, the letters of “Beaumont” looked faint, as though they were seriously considering a retreat into a modest and unassuming B. But whenever Mr. Julian Beaumont Smith arrived at his flat above, he was called “Jules” and greeted with a fervent hug by Mrs. Julian Beaumont Smith, who...
source_text: In the vestibule below was a letter-box into which no letter would go, and an electric button from which no mortal finger could coax a ring. Also appertaining thereunto was a card bearing the name “Mr. James Dillingham Young.” The “Dillingham” had been flung to the breeze during a former period of prosperity when its possessor was being paid $30 per week. Now, when the income was shrunk to $20, the letters of “Dillingham” looked blurred, as though they were thinking seriously of contracting to a modest and unassuming D. But whenever Mr. James Dillingham Young came home and reached his flat above he was called “Jim” and greatly hugged by Mrs. James Dillingham Young, already

text: Finally, she discovered it. It seemed as though it had been crafted specifically for Jim and no one else. No other shop held anything comparable, though she had searched through every inventory in the city. It was a platinum watch chain, understated and elegant, demonstrating its worth through pure quality rather than gaudy decoration—the hallmark of all truly fine objects. It was a fitting companion even for the Watch itself. The moment her eyes fell upon it, she knew it belonged to Jim. The piece mirrored his own character: modest yet invaluable. They charged her twenty-one dollars for the treasure, and she rushed back home with her remaining eighty-seven cents. With such a chain attached, Jim could check the hour with pride in any setting. Despite the magnificence of the timepiece, he often glanced at it furtively because of the worn leather strap he was forced to use instead of a proper chain.
source_text: She found it at last. It surely had been made for Jim and no one else. There was no other like it in any of the stores, and she had turned all of them inside out. It was a platinum fob chain simple and chaste in design, properly proclaiming its value by substance alone and not by meretricious ornamentation—as all good things should do. It was even worthy of The Watch. As soon as she saw it she knew that it must be Jim’s. It was like him. Quietness and value—the description applied to both. Twenty-one dollars they took from her for it, and she hurried home with the 87 cents. With that chain on his watch Jim might be properly anxious about the time in any company. Grand as the watch was, he sometimes looked at it on the sly on account of the old leather strap that he used in place of a chain.

text: Jim halted just inside the entryway, standing as rigid as a pointer sensing game. His gaze remained pinned to Della, his eyes holding an undecipherable quality that struck fear into her heart. It wasn't fury, or astonishment, or judgment, or even revulsion; it was none of the emotions she had braced herself to encounter. He only continued to watch her intently, his features locked in that singular, haunting look.
source_text: Jim stopped inside the door, as immovable as a setter at the scent of quail. His eyes were fixed upon Della, and there was an expression in them that she could not read, and it terrified her. It was not anger, nor surprise, nor disapproval, nor horror, nor any of the sentiments that she had been prepared for. He simply stared at her fixedly with that peculiar expression on his face.

text: “There is no use in searching,” Elara said. “It has been bartered away—vanished and gone for good. It is the Eve of the Nativity, my love. Be gentle with me, for the sacrifice was made solely for you. Perhaps the strands upon my brow were indeed numbered,” she continued with a sudden, grave sweetness, “but no soul could ever calculate the depth of my devotion to you. Shall I set the cutlets to fire, Arthur?”
source_text: “You needn’t look for it,” said Della. “It’s sold, I tell you—sold and gone, too. It’s Christmas Eve, boy. Be good to me, for it went for you. Maybe the hairs of my head were numbered,” she went on with sudden serious sweetness, “but nobody could ever count my love for you. Shall I put the chops on, Jim?”

text: Behold, the Ornaments—the set of decorative pins Clara had worshipped through a Fifth Avenue window. They were exquisite, carved from genuine shell with borders of sparkling gems—the exact tint to suit the glorious, departed tresses. She knew their value well, and her spirit had longed for them with a hopeless, aching desire. Now the treasures were hers, but the heavy locks that should have worn such splendor were no more.
source_text: For there lay The Combs—the set of combs, side and back, that Della had worshipped long in a Broadway window. Beautiful combs, pure tortoise shell, with jewelled rims—just the shade to wear in the beautiful vanished hair. They were expensive combs, she knew, and her heart had simply craved and yearned over them without the least hope of possession. And now, they were hers, but the tresses that should have adorned the coveted adornments were gone.

text: As you know, the Magi were remarkably wise men who brought offerings to the baby in the manger. They were the ones who invented the tradition of giving Christmas presents. Since they were so intelligent, their gifts were undoubtedly practical, perhaps even coming with the option to exchange them if they were duplicates. I have clumsily told you the simple story of two naive young people in a small apartment who recklessly sacrificed their greatest treasures for one another. But let a final word be said to the clever people of today: of all who give gifts, these two were the most profound. Out of everyone who gives and receives, people like them are the wisest. Everywhere, they are the smartest. They are the true Magi.
source_text: The magi, as you know, were wise men—wonderfully wise men—who brought gifts to the Babe in the manger. They invented the art of giving Christmas presents. Being wise, their gifts were no doubt wise ones, possibly bearing the privilege of exchange in case of duplication. And here I have lamely related to you the uneventful chronicle of two foolish children in a flat who most unwisely sacrificed for each other the greatest treasures of their house. But in a last word to the wise of these days let it be said that of all who give gifts these two were the wisest. Of all who give and receive gifts, such as they are wisest. Everywhere they are wisest. They are the magi.

text: I ask you to imagine the setting—the marble-topped tables, the rows of leather-bound booths, the spirited crowd, and the women in their elegant evening finery, their attire a silent, vivid harmony of fashion, thrift, luxury, or art. Diligent waiters, ever-hopeful for a gratuity, move among the tables while the music offers a clever medley of the great composers. There is a constant stir of voices and laughter—and, most importantly, the tall, conical glasses of beer that tilt toward the lips like a ripe cherry dipping from its branch to meet the beak of a plundering jay. A sculptor from Mauch Chunk once remarked to me that the entire spectacle was authentically Parisian.
source_text: I invoke your consideration of the scene—the marble-topped tables, the range of leather-upholstered wall seats, the gay company, the ladies dressed in demi-state toilets, speaking in an exquisite visible chorus of taste, economy, opulence or art; the sedulous and largess-loving _garçons_, the music wisely catering to all with its raids upon the composers; the _mélange_ of talk and laughter—and, if you will, the Würzburger in the tall glass cones that bend to your lips as a ripe cherry sways on its branch to the beak of a robber jay. I was told by a sculptor from Mauch Chunk that the scene was truly Parisian.

text: As E. Rushmore Coglan rambled on about this tiny orb, I thought with amusement of a renowned author who addressed the entire world but remained devoted to Bombay. In a certain verse, he claims that the world's cities are locked in rivalry, and that the men born within them may travel extensively, yet they remain as attached to their home turf as a toddler to its mother’s apron strings. Even when traversing the deafening avenues of strange lands, he suggested, they remain devoted to their native soil—loyal, simple, and doting—treating the very name of their city as a binding pledge of honor. I felt a surge of satisfaction, for I had discovered Kipling’s mistake. Here was a man seemingly not formed from ordinary earth; a soul free from the petty arrogance of birthplace or borders, who, if he were to show any pride at all, would defend the whole world against the inhabitants of the Moon or Mars.
source_text: And as E. Rushmore Coglan prattled of this little planet I thought with glee of a great almost-cosmopolite who wrote for the whole world and dedicated himself to Bombay. In a poem he has to say that there is pride and rivalry between the cities of the earth, and that “the men that breed from them, they traffic up and down, but cling to their cities’ hem as a child to the mother’s gown.” And whenever they walk “by roaring streets unknown” they remember their native city “most faithful, foolish, fond; making her mere-breathed name their bond upon their bond.” And my glee was roused because I had caught Mr. Kipling napping. Here I had found a man not made from dust; one who had no narrow boasts of birthplace or country, one who, if he bragged at all, would brag of his whole round globe against the Martians and the inhabitants of the Moon.

text: The residents on the porch were in a state of agitation. Mr. Toomey, an insurance agent by instinct and an investigator by trade, stepped inside to evaluate the shriek. He emerged with the report that Mrs. Murphy’s young son, Mike, had vanished. Right behind him burst Mrs. Murphy—two hundred pounds of weeping hysterics, grasping at the empty air and wailing to the sky over the loss of thirty pounds of freckled trouble. It was pure melodrama, yet Mr. Toomey took a seat beside Miss Purdy, the milliner, and their hands met in a shared moment of compassion. The Walsh sisters, a pair of spinsters who grumbled daily about the noise in the corridors, asked immediately if anyone had bothered to check behind the clock.
source_text: The boarders on the steps were fluttered. Mr. Toomey, an insurance solicitor by birth and an investigator by profession, went inside to analyse the scream. He returned with the news that Mrs. Murphy’s little boy, Mike, was lost. Following the messenger, out bounced Mrs. Murphy—two hundred pounds in tears and hysterics, clutching the air and howling to the sky for the loss of thirty pounds of freckles and mischief. Bathos, truly; but Mr. Toomey sat down at the side of Miss Purdy, millinery, and their hands came together in sympathy. The two old maids, Misses Walsh, who complained every day about the noise in the halls, inquired immediately if anybody had looked behind the clock.

text: Colonel Thorne, who was seated beside his portly wife on the uppermost step, rose and fastened his waistcoat. “The infant is vanished?” he cried. “I shall traverse the entire metropolis.” Ordinarily, his spouse never permitted him abroad after dusk. But on this occasion, she commanded: “Begone, Alistair!” in a resonant contralto. “Any soul who can behold that mother’s anguish without flying to her assistance must surely possess a heart of flint.” “Bestow upon me a shilling or two, my dearest,” the Colonel added. “Stray children often wander great distances. I may require funds for the omnibus.”
source_text: Major Grigg, who sat by his fat wife on the top step, arose and buttoned his coat. “The little one lost?” he exclaimed. “I will scour the city.” His wife never allowed him out after dark. But now she said: “Go, Ludovic!” in a baritone voice. “Whoever can look upon that mother’s grief without springing to her relief has a heart of stone.” “Give me some thirty or—sixty cents, my love,” said the Major. “Lost children sometimes stray far. I may need carfares.”

text: Old Denny, who lived in a small back room on the fourth floor, sat on the bottom step and tried to read his newspaper by the light of the street lamp. He turned the page to finish an article about the carpenters’ strike while Mrs. Murphy wailed toward the moon, “Oh, Mike, for God’s sake, where is my little boy?”
source_text: Old man Denny, hall room, fourth floor back, who sat on the lowest step, trying to read a paper by the street lamp, turned over a page to follow up the article about the carpenters’ strike. Mrs. Murphy shrieked to the moon: “Oh, ar-r-Mike, f’r Gawd’s sake, where is me little bit av a boy?”

text: Mrs. Parker would begin by leading you through the double parlours. You would not dare interrupt her as she recited their many benefits and the sterling character of the gentleman who had occupied them for eight years. Eventually, you would manage to stammer out the confession that you were not a doctor or a dentist. Mrs. Parker’s reaction to this news was so profound that you could never again forgive your parents for failing to raise you in a profession that met the standards of her parlours.
source_text: First Mrs. Parker would show you the double parlours. You would not dare to interrupt her description of their advantages and of the merits of the gentleman who had occupied them for eight years. Then you would manage to stammer forth the confession that you were neither a doctor nor a dentist. Mrs. Parker’s manner of receiving the admission was such that you could never afterward entertain the same feeling toward your parents, who had neglected to train you up in one of the professions that fitted Mrs. Parker’s parlours.

text: And then—if you still wavered there, your feverish hand clutching three soggy dollars as you rasped out the truth of your miserable poverty—Mrs. Parker would no longer be your guide. She would bray a loud call for “Clara,” present you with her back, and march down the stairs. Then Clara, the maid, would lead you up the steep, carpeted ladder of the fourth floor to reveal the Skylight Room. It took up a mere seven-by-eight feet in the middle of the landing, squeezed between two pitch-black storage closets.
source_text: Then—oh, then—if you still stood on one foot, with your hot hand clutching the three moist dollars in your pocket, and hoarsely proclaimed your hideous and culpable poverty, nevermore would Mrs. Parker be cicerone of yours. She would honk loudly the word “Clara,” she would show you her back, and march downstairs. Then Clara, the coloured maid, would escort you up the carpeted ladder that served for the fourth flight, and show you the Skylight Room. It occupied 7×8 feet of floor space at the middle of the hall. On each side of it was a dark lumber closet or storeroom.

text: Each day, Miss Leeson departed for her employment. In the evenings, she often brought home handwritten drafts to transcribe on her typewriter. On those nights when she had no work, she would sit out on the high front stoop with the other lodgers. Miss Leeson was surely never intended for a cramped attic room when the plans for her soul were first drawn. She was a spirited creature, full of soft and playful whimsies. Once, she even indulged Mr. Skidder by listening to three acts of his unpublished comedy, "It’s No Kid; or, The Heir of the Subway."
source_text: Every day Miss Leeson went out to work. At night she brought home papers with handwriting on them and made copies with her typewriter. Sometimes she had no work at night, and then she would sit on the steps of the high stoop with the other roomers. Miss Leeson was not intended for a sky-light room when the plans were drawn for her creation. She was gay-hearted and full of tender, whimsical fancies. Once she let Mr. Skidder read to her three acts of his great (unpublished) comedy, “It’s No Kid; or, The Heir of the Subway.”

text: Mr. Skidder was particularly notable, as he had mentally cast her as the leading lady in a secret, unexpressed romantic play of his own creation. Then there was Mr. Hoover, a forty-five-year-old who was as portly and wealthy as he was dim-witted. The youthful Mr. Evans was also among them, feigning a dry, hollow cough in hopes that she might urge him to quit smoking. While the men collectively regarded her as the most delightful and spirited woman imaginable, the judgmental huffs from the women on the stairs remained entirely unrelenting.
source_text: Especially Mr. Skidder, who had cast her in his mind for the star part in a private, romantic (unspoken) drama in real life. And especially Mr. Hoover, who was forty-five, fat, flush and foolish. And especially very young Mr. Evans, who set up a hollow cough to induce her to ask him to leave off cigarettes. The men voted her “the funniest and jolliest ever,” but the sniffs on the top step and the lower step were implacable.

text: A time arrived later when Miss Leeson no longer brought home those daunting stacks of papers to transcribe. When she stepped out each morning, it wasn't to go to work; instead, she drifted from one firm to the next, her spirit slowly eroding under a steady rain of icy rejections delivered by arrogant young clerks. This pattern continued.
source_text: There came a time after that when Miss Leeson brought no formidable papers home to copy. And when she went out in the morning, instead of working, she went from office to office and let her heart melt away in the drip of cold refusals transmitted through insolent office boys. This went on.

text: Tenants of the city flat will surely second my assertion that theirs is the only genuine bliss. When a dwelling is filled with love, it can never be too cramped—let the sideboard collapse into a card table; let the hearth become a gymnasium, the secretary a guest-cot, the wash-basin a grand organ; let the very partitions squeeze inward, if they must, so long as you and your Clara are pinned between. But should the home be of the wretched sort, let it be vast and boundless—step through the gates of Gibraltar, hang your hat upon the Andes, your cloak upon the Horn of Africa, and find your exit through the frozen reaches of the Steppes.
source_text: Flat-dwellers shall indorse my dictum that theirs is the only true happiness. If a home is happy it cannot fit too close—let the dresser collapse and become a billiard table; let the mantel turn to a rowing machine, the escritoire to a spare bedchamber, the washstand to an upright piano; let the four walls come together, if they will, so you and your Delia are between. But if home be the other kind, let it be wide and long—enter you at the Golden Gate, hang your hat on Hatteras, your cape on Cape Horn and go out by the Labrador.

text: I’m teaching his daughter, Clementina. I’ve already grown incredibly fond of her. She’s a fragile girl, always dressed in white, with the most charming and unassuming personality. She’s only eighteen years old. I’ll be giving three lessons a week, and Joe—just imagine—it’s five dollars per session! I don’t mind the work at all, because once I find a few more students, I’ll be able to start my own lessons with Herr Rosenstock again. So, stop furrowing your brow, honey, and let’s enjoy a nice dinner.
source_text: “My pupil is his daughter Clementina. I dearly love her already. She’s a delicate thing—dresses always in white; and the sweetest, simplest manners! Only eighteen years old. I’m to give three lessons a week; and, just think, Joe! $5 a lesson. I don’t mind it a bit; for when I get two or three more pupils I can resume my lessons with Herr Rosenstock. Now, smooth out that wrinkle between your brows, dear, and let’s have a nice supper.”

text: “He came from clear across town. I wish you could’ve seen him, Dele—a heavy-set man wearing a wool scarf and chewing a quill toothpick. He noticed the sketch in Tinkle’s shop window and actually mistook it for a windmill at first. He was a good sport, though, and bought it regardless. He even ordered another—an oil study of the Lackawanna freight depot—to bring back with him. Music lessons? Forget it. I guess there’s still money to be made in Art.”
source_text: “All the way. I wish you could see him, Dele. Fat man with a woollen muffler and a quill toothpick. He saw the sketch in Tinkle’s window and thought it was a windmill at first. He was game, though, and bought it anyhow. He ordered another—an oil sketch of the Lackawanna freight depot—to take back with him. Music lessons! Oh, I guess Art is still in it.”

text: The pretty Anna fell upon her friend, shaking her with a mix of playful scolding and urgent pleading. Maggie Toole had actually found a man! Dear, faithful, plain Maggie—a wonderful companion, yet always overlooked for a dance or a moonlit bench in the park. How did it happen? When did it start? Who was the fellow?
source_text: The comely Anna pounced upon her friend, shook her, chided and beseeched her. Maggie Toole catch a fellow! Plain, dear, loyal, unattractive Maggie, so sweet as a chum, so unsought for a two-step or a moonlit bench in the little park. How was it? When did it happen? Who was it?

text: Beatrice swept across the dance floor like a nimble skiff towed by a formidable frigate. Truly, her escort validated every superlative whispered by her devoted confidante. He loomed several inches above the standard brawler of the district; his raven hair formed elegant ringlets, and his gaze and grin gleamed with a predatory brightness whenever he deigned to beam. The youths of the Anvil Club did not measure a man by his decorative qualities, but rather by his capacity for violence, his record in street-side skirmishes, and his knack for remaining at liberty despite the persistent efforts of the constabulary. Any fellow wishing to tether a shop-girl to his triumphal wagon would have disdained the manners of a fop. Such graces were deemed unsporting in the local arenas of love. Bulging deltoids, waistcoats gasping for breath over expansive ribs, an innate certainty of the male’s supreme rank in the natural order, and even the unhurried exhibition of bow-leggedness—these were the sanctioned munitions of the Anvil’s cavaliers. Thus, they observed the newcomer’s polite inclinations and studied elegance with their chins thrust out at a skeptical slant.
source_text: Across the floor Maggie sailed like a coquettish yacht convoyed by a stately cruiser. And truly, her companion justified the encomiums of the faithful chum. He stood two inches taller than the average Give and Take athlete; his dark hair curled; his eyes and his teeth flashed whenever he bestowed his frequent smiles. The young men of the Clover Leaf Club pinned not their faith to the graces of person as much as they did to its prowess, its achievements in hand-to-hand conflicts, and its preservation from the legal duress that constantly menaced it. The member of the association who would bind a paper-box maiden to his conquering chariot scorned to employ Beau Brummel airs. They were not considered honourable methods of warfare. The swelling biceps, the coat straining at its buttons over the chest, the air of conscious conviction of the supereminence of the male in the cosmogony of creation, even a calm display of bow legs as subduing and enchanting agents in the gentle tourneys of Cupid—these were the approved arms and ammunition of the Clover Leaf gallants. They viewed, then, genuflexions and alluring poses of this visitor with their chins at a new angle.

text: Yet this evening the common gourd had transmuted into a gilded chariot. Silas Thorne stood as a conquering suitor, and Elara Vance embarked upon her first gossamer-winged ascent. And though these figures of ancient lore and insect life be strangely entwined, they shall not drain one dram of nectar from the blossom-scented cadence of Elara’s solitary, peerless night.
source_text: But to-night the pumpkin had turned to a coach and six. Terry O’Sullivan was a victorious Prince Charming, and Maggie Toole winged her first butterfly flight. And though our tropes of fairyland be mixed with those of entomology they shall not spill one drop of ambrosia from the rose-crowned melody of Maggie’s one perfect night.

text: A few choice spirits lingered there, sporting heavy golden Albert chains and nursing stout cheroots; after some wag shared a bawdy tale, Sully Vance would retreat for a brisk half-hour’s exertion with the ten-pound iron weights. Truly, a funambulist’s feat across a jagged chasm was a trifle of no consequence when weighed against the hazard of a second waltz with Sully’s shop-girl flame. Precisely at ten, the luminous, florid countenance of “Hoss” McGinnis appeared in the doorway for its nightly vigil. He remained but five minutes, casting a benevolent wink at the ladies and dispensing prime Havanas to the eager young men.
source_text: But there would be sundry gentlemen there with large gold fob chains and black cigars; and somebody would tell a funny story, and then Dempsey would go back and work half an hour with the six-pound dumbbells. So, doing a tight-rope act on a wire stretched across Niagara was a safe terpsichorean performance compared with waltzing twice with Dempsey Donovan’s paper-box girl. At 10 o’clock the jolly round face of “Big Mike” O’Sullivan shone at the door for five minutes upon the scene. He always looked in for five minutes, smiled at the girls and handed out real perfectos to the delighted boys.

text: The two combatants were evenly paired. Dempsey was perhaps ten pounds lighter than his opponent, while O’Sullivan combined a broad frame with remarkable agility. Dempsey possessed a frozen gaze, a thin and commanding mouth, an indestructible jaw, a complexion as clear as a debutante’s, and the unshakable poise of a champion. The visitor displayed a more volatile disdain, unable to fully mask his obvious sneer. They were adversaries by a primal law established when the earth was still molten. Both were far too formidable and unique to share the pinnacle of their craft. Only one could remain standing.
source_text: The two gladiators were well matched. Dempsey had, perhaps, ten pounds of weight to give away. The O’Sullivan had breadth with quickness. Dempsey had a glacial eye, a dominating slit of a mouth, an indestructible jaw, a complexion like a belle’s and the coolness of a champion. The visitor showed more fire in his contempt and less control over his conspicuous sneer. They were enemies by the law written when the rocks were molten. They were each too splendid, too mighty, too incomparable to divide pre-eminence. One only must survive.

text: “Typically,” Dempsey continued with a gravelly sweetness, “the O’Sullivans in this area are familiar with their own. You escorted one of our ladies here, and we’d like to do right by you. If you have a family tree, let’s see some O’Sullivan buds on those branches. Or would you prefer we dug the truth out of you by the roots?”
source_text: “As a rule,” went on Dempsey, huskily sweet, “O’Sullivans in this district know one another. You escorted one of our lady members here, and we want a chance to make good. If you’ve got a family tree let’s see a few historical O’Sullivan buds come out on it. Or do you want us to dig it out of you by the roots?”

text: Sterling acknowledged Silas and the guild’s recorder, Alistair Thorne, with a sharp nod, then moved with purpose toward a heavy door at the far end of the room. Two more figures from the Mutual Benefit Society emerged to join the gathering. Julian Vane was now firmly within the grasp of the Committee of Conduct and Protocol. After a few whispered, urgent words, they guided him away through the same rear passage.
source_text: Dempsey nodded at Andy and William McMahan, the secretary of the club, and walked rapidly toward a door at the rear of the hall. Two other members of the Give and Take Association swiftly joined the little group. Terry O’Sullivan was now in the hands of the Board of Rules and Social Referees. They spoke to him briefly and softly, and conducted him out through the same door at the rear.
