text: The blade struck the wood with a sharp, metallic chime. Bare steel brandished inside the chambers of the Concordance Club! Such a transgression was without precedent in all their years. The entire room lapsed into a heavy, breathless stillness. Barney O’Shea prodded the dirk with the front of his boot, squinting at it like a collector of curiosities who has stumbled upon a relic of some forgotten, savage age.
source_text: The knife fell and rang upon the floor. Cold steel drawn in the rooms of the Give and Take Association! Such a thing had never happened before. Every one stood motionless for a minute. Andy Geoghan kicked the stiletto with the toe of his shoe curiously, like an antiquarian who has come upon some ancient weapon unknown to his learning.

text: “I was certain of it, Dempsey,” she remarked, her gaze turning lackluster even through her tears. “I was aware he was a Guinea; his actual name is Tony Spinelli. I rushed in as soon as I heard that you and he were brawling. Those Guineas are always armed with knives. But you don’t see the truth of it, Dempsey. I’ve never had a man of my own. I was tired of being the third wheel to Anna and Jimmy every night, so I convinced him to go by O’Sullivan and brought him here. I knew he wouldn’t stand a chance if he arrived as a Dago. I suppose I’ll be leaving the club for good now.”
source_text: “I knew it, Dempsey,” she said, as her eyes grew dull even in their tears. “I knew he was a Guinea. His name’s Tony Spinelli. I hurried in when they told me you and him was scrappin’. Them Guineas always carries knives. But you don’t understand, Dempsey. I never had a fellow in my life. I got tired of comin’ with Anna and Jimmy every night, so I fixed it with him to call himself O’Sullivan, and brought him along. I knew there’d be nothin’ doin’ for him if he came as a Dago. I guess I’ll resign from the club now.”

text: “Well,” he said, “a ‘Man About Town’ is a cross between a professional carouser and a high-society regular. He doesn’t quite—well, he exists somewhere between elite gala receptions and gritty underground prize fights. He doesn’t belong to the most prestigious private clubs, but he isn’t a member of some local blue-collar workers’ union either. I’m not entirely sure how to define him for you. You’ll find him wherever there’s something happening. I suppose he is a specific type. He’s in formal wear every night, knows the city's inner workings, and is on a first-name basis with every police officer and waiter in town. No, he doesn't hang around with the flashy, superficial crowd. You’ll generally see him on his own or with another man.”
source_text: “Why,” said he, “a ‘Man About Town’ is something between a ‘rounder’ and a ‘clubman.’ He isn’t exactly—well, he fits in between Mrs. Fish’s receptions and private boxing bouts. He doesn’t—well, he doesn’t belong either to the Lotos Club or to the Jerry McGeogheghan Galvanised Iron Workers’ Apprentices’ Left Hook Chowder Association. I don’t exactly know how to describe him to you. You’ll see him everywhere there’s anything doing. Yes, I suppose he’s a type. Dress clothes every evening; knows the ropes; calls every policeman and waiter in town by their first names. No; he never travels with the hydrogen derivatives. You generally see him alone or with another man.”

text: Permit us a brief excursion into the realm of domestic morality. There is something profoundly heartening in the spectacle of a family gathered over the Sunday gazette. The various folios have been distributed among them. The father is fixed in solemn contemplation of an illustration featuring a damsel at her morning gymnastics by an open window, inclining her form—but let us pass that by. The mother is quite absorbed in a puzzle, endeavoring to supply the elided characters in the word L_nd_n. The eldest daughters study the stock exchange columns with breathless interest, ever since a particular gentleman mentioned his recent gamble in Transcontinental Rail. Finally, young Percival, a youth of eighteen years attending the municipal high school, is lost in a treatise on the reconstruction of a discarded petticoat, for he harbors a secret ambition to carry off the embroidery medal at his graduation ceremony.
source_text: Let us continue with a moral digression. To see a family reading the Sunday paper gratifies. The sections have been separated. Papa is earnestly scanning the page that pictures the young lady exercising before an open window, and bending—but there, there! Mamma is interested in trying to guess the missing letters in the word N_w Yo_k. The oldest girls are eagerly perusing the financial reports, for a certain young man remarked last Sunday night that he had taken a flyer in Q., X. & Z. Willie, the eighteen-year-old son, who attends the New York public school, is absorbed in the weekly article describing how to make over an old skirt, for he hopes to take a prize in sewing on graduation day.

text: Soapy’s plans for the winter were hardly extravagant. He entertained no thoughts of cruising the Mediterranean or drifting under the drowsy southern skies of the Bay of Naples. What his spirit truly yearned for was a ninety-day stay on the Island. To Soapy, the height of comfort was three months of guaranteed food and shelter among friendly faces, safely tucked away from the biting north wind and the local police.
source_text: The hibernatorial ambitions of Soapy were not of the highest. In them there were no considerations of Mediterranean cruises, of soporific Southern skies drifting in the Vesuvian Bay. Three months on the Island was what his soul craved. Three months of assured board and bed and congenial company, safe from Boreas and bluecoats, seemed to Soapy the essence of things desirable.

text: Across the street stood an unassuming eatery that made no claims to elegance. It served those with hearty appetites and shallow pockets. While the plates and the air were heavy, the broth and the linens were remarkably thin. Into this establishment Soapy wandered, his worn-out shoes and ragged trousers passing without scrutiny. He took a seat and feasted upon beefsteak, pancakes, doughnuts, and pie. After finishing, he revealed to the waiter that he and even the smallest of coins were total strangers.
source_text: On the opposite side of the street was a restaurant of no great pretensions. It catered to large appetites and modest purses. Its crockery and atmosphere were thick; its soup and napery thin. Into this place Soapy took his accusive shoes and telltale trousers without challenge. At a table he sat and consumed beefsteak, flapjacks, doughnuts and pie. And then to the waiter be betrayed the fact that the minutest coin and himself were strangers.

text: With practiced efficiency, two servers deposited Silas upon the unyielding concrete, landing him squarely on his temple. He unfolded himself by degrees, much like the stiff extension of a folding yardstick, and slapped the grit from his coat. The sanctuary of a cell now felt like a vanishing mirage. The Penitentiary was a world away. A patrolman, loitering by a pharmacy window nearby, merely chuckled and sauntered off down the block.
source_text: Neatly upon his left ear on the callous pavement two waiters pitched Soapy. He arose, joint by joint, as a carpenter’s rule opens, and beat the dust from his clothes. Arrest seemed but a rosy dream. The Island seemed very far away. A policeman who stood before a drug store two doors away laughed and walked down the street.

text: A brilliant moon hung overhead, glowing with a peaceful light. The streets were nearly empty of cars and passersby, and from the eaves, the drowsy chirping of sparrows made the city feel, for a moment, like a quiet graveyard in the countryside. The hymn drifting from the organist’s pipes rooted Soapy to the iron railing, echoing a melody from a past where his life still held mothers and roses, aspirations and friendships, and a world of pure thoughts and clean collars.
source_text: The moon was above, lustrous and serene; vehicles and pedestrians were few; sparrows twittered sleepily in the eaves—for a little while the scene might have been a country churchyard. And the anthem that the organist played cemented Soapy to the iron fence, for he had known it well in the days when his life contained such things as mothers and roses and ambitions and friends and immaculate thoughts and collars.

text: The intersection of Soapy’s vulnerable mood and the sanctified aura of the ancient chapel effected an immediate and miraculous transformation within him. He gazed with instant revulsion at the depths to which he had sunk, realizing his life was now composed of squandered days, shameful cravings, extinguished ambitions, ruined talents, and the petty impulses that defined his hollow reality.
source_text: The conjunction of Soapy’s receptive state of mind and the influences about the old church wrought a sudden and wonderful change in his soul. He viewed with swift horror the pit into which he had tumbled, the degraded days, unworthy desires, dead hopes, wrecked faculties and base motives that made up his existence.

text: At an art gallery the other day, I noticed a painting that had been purchased for five thousand dollars. The artist was a scrappy young man from the West named Kraft, who held fast to a singular meal and a private philosophy. His mental sustenance was a deep-seated belief in the Flawless Aesthetic Balance of Nature. His actual theory of life, however, was built upon corned-beef hash with a poached egg. There was a narrative behind the canvas, so I returned home to let the ink flow. Kraft’s perspective—but that is not the beginning of the tale.
source_text: In an art exhibition the other day I saw a painting that had been sold for $5,000. The painter was a young scrub out of the West named Kraft, who had a favourite food and a pet theory. His pabulum was an unquenchable belief in the Unerring Artistic Adjustment of Nature. His theory was fixed around corned-beef hash with poached egg. There was a story behind the picture, so I went home and let it drip out of a fountain-pen. The idea of Kraft—but that is not the beginning of the story.

text: He let out a low, hearty grumble of appreciation. “That’s the woman for me,” he declared. “She can share my table for as long as she lives. I tell you, I’ve never laid eyes on a finer girl. I’m going back in there to ask for her hand. I reckon she won’t be interested in waiting tables anymore once she sees the heap of gold I’ve got.”
source_text: There he rumbled a roughly good-humoured protest. “That’s the girl for my money,” he declared. “She can eat out of my skillet the rest of her life. Why, I never see such a fine girl. I’m going back there and ask her to marry me. I guess she won’t want to sling hash any more when she sees the pile of dust I’ve got.”

text: Then, when our resources were spent and the gold-seeker, his faculties yet resilient, commenced again his rambling elegies for Clara, Sterling hissed a genteel but jagged taunt about the wretchedness of a miserly heart, prompting the man to crash down fistfuls of silver and banknotes, howling for a flood of liquor to submerge the insult.
source_text: Then, when our ammunition was gone and the Klondiker, still somewhat sober, began to babble again of Milly, Kraft whispered into his ear such a polite, barbed insult relating to people who were miserly with their funds, that the miner crashed down handful after handful of silver and notes, calling for all the fluids in the world to drown the imputation.

text: However, as I mentioned, I recently viewed a painting that commanded a price of five thousand dollars. It was titled "Boadicea," and the central figure appeared large enough to occupy the entire landscape. Yet, among the many admirers gathered before it, I suspect I was the only one who wished Boadicea would step right out of the canvas to serve me a plate of corned-beef hash with a poached egg.
source_text: But, as I said, I saw a painting the other day that was sold for $5,000. The title was “Boadicea,” and the figure seemed to fill all out-of-doors. But of all the picture’s admirers who stood before it, I believe I was the only one who longed for Boadicea to stalk from her frame, bringing me corned-beef hash with poached egg.

text: I entered the world as a yellow puppy, though the specifics of my birth date, location, lineage, and weight are entirely unknown. My very first memory involves an elderly woman at the intersection of Broadway and Twenty-third, who held me in a basket while trying to sell me to a stout lady. This "Old Mother Hubbard" was hyping me up for all she was worth, claiming I was a genuine Pomeranian-Hambletonian-Red-Irish-Cochin-China-Stoke-Pogis fox terrier. The heavy-set woman fumbled through the fabric scraps in her shopping bag until she finally cornered a five-dollar bill and handed it over. From that second on, I was a pampered pet—"mommy’s own precious little wootsey-squidlums." Tell me, dear reader, have you ever been lifted by a two-hundred-pound woman smelling of Camembert cheese and floral perfume while she nuzzles her nose all over you, chanting in a high-society soprano: “Oh, who’s a sweet little oodlum, doodlum, woodlum, toodlum, itsy-bitsy skoodlums?”
source_text: I was born a yellow pup; date, locality, pedigree and weight unknown. The first thing I can recollect, an old woman had me in a basket at Broadway and Twenty-third trying to sell me to a fat lady. Old Mother Hubbard was boosting me to beat the band as a genuine Pomeranian-Hambletonian-Red-Irish-Cochin-China-Stoke-Pogis fox terrier. The fat lady chased a V around among the samples of gros grain flannelette in her shopping bag till she cornered it, and gave up. From that moment I was a pet—a mamma’s own wootsey squidlums. Say, gentle reader, did you ever have a 200-pound woman breathing a flavour of Camembert cheese and Peau d’Espagne pick you up and wallop her nose all over you, remarking all the time in an Emma Eames tone of voice: “Oh, oo’s um oodlum, doodlum, woodlum, toodlum, bitsy-witsy skoodlums?”

text: I started to feel a real sense of pity for Hubby, I truly did. We looked so much alike that people couldn't help but stare whenever we went out; as a result, we abandoned the main roads where Morgan’s taxi usually roamed and took to navigating the frozen, soot-covered piles of old December snow in the neighborhoods where the poor lived.
source_text: I began to feel sorry for Hubby, dog my cats if I didn’t. We looked so much alike that people noticed it when we went out; so we shook the streets that Morgan’s cab drives down, and took to climbing the piles of last December’s snow on the streets where cheap people live.

text: In a peaceful spot on a secure street, I pulled my owner’s leash taut in front of an elegant, high-end bar. I lunged directly for the doors, whimpering with the same desperate urgency as a dog in a news report alerting the family that little Alice has become trapped in the creek while out gathering lilies.
source_text: At a quiet place on a safe street I tightened the line of my custodian in front of an attractive, refined saloon. I made a dead-ahead scramble for the doors, whining like a dog in the press despatches that lets the family know that little Alice is bogged while gathering lilies in the brook.

text: “Well, strike me blind,” the old man said with a smirk; “strike me blind if this yellow-tinted fellow isn't inviting me in for a glass. Let me see—how long has it been since I spared my shoe leather by propping a foot on the brass rail? I reckon I’ll—”
source_text: “Why, darn my eyes,” says the old man, with a grin; “darn my eyes if the saffron-coloured son of a seltzer lemonade ain’t asking me in to take a drink. Lemme see—how long’s it been since I saved shoe leather by keeping one foot on the foot-rest? I believe I’ll—”

text: You scruffy, woodchuck-hunting old mutt," I said to him. "You moon-howling, rabbit-tracking, egg-thieving beagle, don't you realize I have no desire to leave you? Can't you see we are both like those pups lost in the forest, while the mistress plays the part of the wicked uncle, chasing you with a kitchen towel and coming for me with flea ointment and a pink ribbon for my tail? Why don't we just give all that up and be partners for good?
source_text: “You old flea-headed woodchuck-chaser,” I said to him—“you moon-baying, rabbit-pointing, egg-stealing old beagle, can’t you see that I don’t want to leave you? Can’t you see that we’re both Pups in the Wood and the missis is the cruel uncle after you with the dish towel and me with the flea liniment and a pink bow to tie on my tail. Why not cut that all out and be pards forever more?”

text: When the day clerk arrived at eight o’clock, Ikey hurried toward Mrs. Riddle’s to learn how everything had turned out. But as he stepped out of the shop, none other than Chunk McGowan leaped from a passing streetcar and seized his hand—a man wearing a victor’s grin and glowing with absolute joy.
source_text: At eight o’clock in the morning the day clerk arrived and Ikey started hurriedly for Mrs. Riddle’s to learn the outcome. And, lo! as he stepped out of the store who but Chunk McGowan sprang from a passing street car and grasped his hand—Chunk McGowan with a victor’s smile and flushed with joy.

text: “Brought it off,” declared Barney, his smile radiating pure paradise. “Mabel reached the iron stairs precisely as planned, and we were before the parson at exactly nine-thirty and a fraction. She’s settled in the rooms now—whipped up a scramble this morning wearing a silk robe of cornflower blue—Lord, what a fortunate soul I've become!
source_text: “Pulled it off,” said Chunk with Elysium in his grin. “Rosy hit the fire-escape on time to a second, and we was under the wire at the Reverend’s at 9.30¼. She’s up at the flat—she cooked eggs this mornin’ in a blue kimono—Lord! how lucky I am! You must pace up some day, Ikey, and feed with us. I’ve got a job down near the bridge, and that’s where I’m heading for now.”

text: My dear Arthur," Aunt Beatrice sighed, "I wish you would not dwell so upon finance. Opulence is a trifle against a sincere bond; love is invincible. Had he but voiced his intentions sooner! She could never have turned our Julian away. But I fear the hour has slipped by, leaving him no occasion to speak. All your hoarded treasures cannot purchase the contentment your son requires.
source_text: “Oh, Anthony,” sighed Aunt Ellen, “I wish you would not think so much of money. Wealth is nothing where a true affection is concerned. Love is all-powerful. If he only had spoken earlier! She could not have refused our Richard. But now I fear it is too late. He will have no opportunity to address her. All your gold cannot bring happiness to your son.”

text: I ended up shelling out an extra three hundred dollars of my own money," Kelly remarked. "I had to exceed the original quote a bit. I secured most of the delivery wagons and taxis for five dollars each, but the heavy trucks and two-horse rigs generally pushed me to ten. The trolley drivers wanted ten, and some of the loaded haulers asked for twenty. The police were the most expensive—I paid fifty to a pair of them, and the others got twenty or twenty-five. But wasn’t the performance magnificent, Mr. Rockwall? I’m relieved William A. Brady didn't witness that little outdoor traffic spectacle. I wouldn't want to break his heart with such professional jealousy. And we didn't even have a rehearsal! Every man was there at the precise second. It took two hours before so much as a snake could have squeezed past Greeley’s statue.
source_text: “I paid out $300 more of my own,” said Kelly. “I had to go a little above the estimate. I got the express wagons and cabs mostly for $5; but the trucks and two-horse teams mostly raised me to $10. The motormen wanted $10, and some of the loaded teams $20. The cops struck me hardest—$50 I paid two, and the rest $20 and $25. But didn’t it work beautiful, Mr. Rockwall? I’m glad William A. Brady wasn’t onto that little outdoor vehicle mob scene. I wouldn’t want William to break his heart with jealousy. And never a rehearsal, either! The boys was on time to the fraction of a second. It was two hours before a snake could get below Greeley’s statue.”

text: To explain the matter, you are free to speculate that the scallops were unavailable, or that she had renounced lemon tarts for the duration of her fast, or that she had dined upon shallots, or that she had only just departed from a matinee at the Bijou. Then, finding every such supposition false, you will kindly allow the tale to continue.
source_text: To account for this you will be allowed to guess that the lobsters were all out, or that she had sworn ice-cream off during Lent, or that she had ordered onions, or that she had just come from a Hackett matinee. And then, all these theories being wrong, you will please let the story proceed.

text: Then the almanac turned deceitful, proclaiming the arrival of spring. But the season only appears when it is ready. The icy drifts of January remained as unyielding as stone throughout the side streets. Street organs continued to perform “In the Good Old Summertime” with their characteristic December spirit and flair. Men began signing thirty-day notes to purchase Easter finery, and janitors cut off the heat. When these circumstances arise, it is clear that the city remains firmly within winter’s grasp.
source_text: And then the almanac lied, and said that spring had come. Spring comes when it comes. The frozen snows of January still lay like adamant in the crosstown streets. The hand-organs still played “In the Good Old Summertime,” with their December vivacity and expression. Men began to make thirty-day notes to buy Easter dresses. Janitors shut off steam. And when these things happen one may know that the city is still in the clutches of winter.

text: In this shadowed, berry-fringed lane, Walter had courted her and won her heart. They had sat there together, braiding a crown of dandelions for her hair. He had been extravagant in his praise of how the yellow blossoms looked against her dark tresses; she had kept the wreath on, walking back to the house while her straw hat swung from her hand.
source_text: It was in this shaded and raspberried lane that Walter had wooed and won her. And together they had sat and woven a crown of dandelions for her hair. He had immoderately praised the effect of the yellow blossoms against her brown tresses; and she had left the chaplet there, and walked back to the house swinging her straw sailor in her hands.

text: Two weeks had gone by without a single letter from Walter, and the next entry on the menu was dandelions—dandelions served with an egg, though the egg was of no consequence. It was the dandelions that haunted her, the very blossoms Walter had once woven into a golden crown when he declared her his queen and his future wife. These first signs of spring had become her greatest source of grief, a painful reminder of the time when she had been most happy.
source_text: For she had received no letter from Walter in two weeks, and the next item on the bill of fare was dandelions—dandelions with some kind of egg—but bother the egg!—dandelions, with whose golden blooms Walter had crowned her his queen of love and future bride—dandelions, the harbingers of spring, her sorrow’s crown of sorrow—reminder of her happiest days.

text: As the clock struck six, the waiter delivered her evening meal and retrieved the typed menu. Sarah began to eat, but with a heavy sigh, she pushed aside the plate of dandelion greens and its crowning garnish of egg. Just as these once-vibrant blossoms, symbols of romance, had been reduced to a lowly vegetable, the hopes she had nurtured all summer had similarly withered and died. Though poets claim that love can sustain itself, Sarah could not bring herself to consume the very flowers that had decorated the first sacred feast of her heart’s true devotion.
source_text: At 6 o’clock the waiter brought her dinner and carried away the typewritten bill of fare. When Sarah ate she set aside, with a sigh, the dish of dandelions with its crowning ovarious accompaniment. As this dark mass had been transformed from a bright and love-indorsed flower to be an ignominious vegetable, so had her summer hopes wilted and perished. Love may, as Shakespeare said, feed on itself: but Sarah could not bring herself to eat the dandelions that had graced, as ornaments, the first spiritual banquet of her heart’s true affection.

text: Imagine yourself strolling along Broadway after a meal, savoring the final minutes of your cigar while debating whether to see a lighthearted tragedy or a solemn vaudeville show. Suddenly, you feel a hand rest upon your sleeve. You turn and find yourself staring into the mesmerizing eyes of a gorgeous woman, adorned in glittering diamonds and expensive Russian sables. She hastily shoves a steaming, buttered roll into your hand, whips out a small pair of scissors, and snips the second button from your coat. With a meaningful cry of “parallelogram!”, she vanishes down a side street, casting a frightened look back over her shoulder.
source_text: Suppose you should be walking down Broadway after dinner, with ten minutes allotted to the consummation of your cigar while you are choosing between a diverting tragedy and something serious in the way of vaudeville. Suddenly a hand is laid upon your arm. You turn to look into the thrilling eyes of a beautiful woman, wonderful in diamonds and Russian sables. She thrusts hurriedly into your hand an extremely hot buttered roll, flashes out a tiny pair of scissors, snips off the second button of your overcoat, meaningly ejaculates the one word, “parallelogram!” and swiftly flies down a cross street, looking back fearfully over her shoulder.

text: Semi-adventurers—those gallant and impressive characters—have always been plentiful. From the medieval Crusades to the American Palisades, they have populated our histories and provided endless fodder for the writers of period dramas. But each of these men was driven by a specific motive: a prize to claim, a victory to achieve, a grudge to satisfy, or a legacy to forge. Because they were bound by their own agendas, they could never be considered genuine devotees of pure adventure.
source_text: Half-adventurers—brave and splendid figures—have been numerous. From the Crusades to the Palisades they have enriched the arts of history and fiction and the trade of historical fiction. But each of them had a prize to win, a goal to kick, an axe to grind, a race to run, a new thrust in tierce to deliver, a name to carve, a crow to pick—so they were not followers of true adventure.

text: The intrepid piano dealer paused at the intersection to reflect. He then crossed the thoroughfare, walked a block, doubled back, and rejoined the northward surge of the crowd. Feigning indifference as he passed the distributor a second time, he casually accepted the small card offered to him. After walking a short distance, he examined the slip. It bore the same handwritten script as the first, spelling out the words “The Green Door.” Nearby, a few cards had been discarded on the sidewalk by other pedestrians. These had landed face-down, appearing blank. Rudolf turned them over, only to find that every single one was a printed advertisement for a local dental office.
source_text: The adventurous piano salesman halted at the corner and considered. Then he crossed the street, walked down a block, recrossed and joined the upward current of people again. Without seeming to notice the negro as he passed the second time, he carelessly took the card that was handed him. Ten steps away he inspected it. In the same handwriting that appeared on the first card “The Green Door” was inscribed upon it. Three or four cards were tossed to the pavement by pedestrians both following and leading him. These fell blank side up. Rudolf turned them over. Every one bore the printed legend of the dental “parlours.”

text: Silas retraced his steps toward the towering figure who stood sentinel beside the vitrine of clicking dentures. On this second approach, no slip of paper was extended toward him. Despite the absurdity of his flamboyant livery, the man possessed an inherent, formidable poise, distributing his advertisements with a selective elegance to some while permitting others to go ignored. At regular intervals, he barked a rough, indecipherable slogan that sounded like a cross between a tram-driver’s shout and a lyric from the opera house. Not only was the hand withheld, but Silas felt himself withered by a glance of frigid, nearly mocking superiority from that broad, polished face.
source_text: Rudolf walked slowly back to where the giant negro stood by the case of rattling teeth. This time as he passed he received no card. In spite of his gaudy and ridiculous garb, the Ethiopian displayed a natural barbaric dignity as he stood, offering the cards suavely to some, allowing others to pass unmolested. Every half minute he chanted a harsh, unintelligible phrase akin to the jabber of car conductors and grand opera. And not only did he withhold a card this time, but it seemed to Rudolf that he received from the shining and massive black countenance a look of cold, almost contemptuous disdain.

text: The ground floor, shuttered for the evening, appeared to be the province of furriers and milliners. Above it, a pulsing electric sign announced the dentist’s chambers. Higher still, a chaotic tangle of placards vied for attention, marking the offices of palmists, tailors, musicians, and physicians. At the very top, drawn curtains and the stark white of milk bottles on the ledges signaled the beginning of the residential quarters.
source_text: The first floor, now closed, seemed to house millinery or furs. The second floor, by the winking electric letters, was the dentist’s. Above this a polyglot babel of signs struggled to indicate the abodes of palmists, dressmakers, musicians and doctors. Still higher up draped curtains and milk bottles white on the window sills proclaimed the regions of domesticity.

text: Upon reaching Fifty-ninth Street, Jerry’s head began to droop and the reins went limp. However, the horse turned through the park gate of its own accord, embarking on its well-worn nightly route. The passenger leaned back, spellbound, inhaling the pure, invigorating scents of the foliage and the blossoms. The clever animal between the shafts, knowing the path by heart, settled into a leisurely, measured trot and kept strictly to the right side of the road.
source_text: When they reached Fifty-ninth street Jerry’s head was bobbing and his reins were slack. But his horse turned in through the park gate and began the old familiar nocturnal round. And then the fare leaned back, entranced, and breathed deep the clean, wholesome odours of grass and leaf and bloom. And the wise beast in the shafts, knowing his ground, struck into his by-the-hour gait and kept to the right of the road.

text: Half a hundred tables were crowded with royalty, draped in the finest fabrics and most precious stones the world could offer. Occasionally, one of them would glance inquisitively toward Jerry. They beheld an ordinary woman in a simple pink foulard silk, her face unexceptional but for a vibrant joy for living that the queens themselves secretly coveted.
source_text: At fifty tables sat princes and queens clad in all the silks and gems of the world. And now and then one of them would look curiously at Jerry’s fare. They saw a plain figure dressed in a pink silk of the kind that is tempered by the word “foulard,” and a plain face that wore a look of love of life that the queens envied.

text: We no longer lament or scatter ashes upon our brows at the mention of hell’s eternal fires. Even our ministers have begun to suggest that the Creator is composed of radium or ether—some scientific substance—and that the most a sinner need fear is a simple chemical transformation. While this is a comforting theory, a remnant of that old, righteous terror still haunts our modern hearts.
source_text: We no longer groan and heap ashes upon our heads when the flames of Tophet are mentioned. For, even the preachers have begun to tell us that God is radium, or ether or some scientific compound, and that the worst we wicked ones may expect is a chemical reaction. This is a pleasing hypothesis; but there lingers yet some of the old, goodly terror of orthodoxy.

text: Throughout her maiden year at the emporium, Clara received a weekly wage of five dollars. It might be an enlightening exercise to trace the geography of her existence on that sum. Are you unmoved? Very well; perhaps your curiosity demands more impressive figures. Six dollars is an impressive figure. I shall describe, then, how she subsisted on six dollars a week.
source_text: During her first year in the store, Dulcie was paid five dollars per week. It would be instructive to know how she lived on that amount. Don’t care? Very well; probably you are interested in larger amounts. Six dollars is a larger amount. I will tell you how she lived on six dollars per week.

text: No way!" Sadie cried out with admiration. "Aren't you the lucky one? Piggy is a real high-roller, and he always takes his dates to the most upscale spots. He even took Blanche to the Hoffman House one evening, where the music is incredible and you see all the elite. You’re going to have a fantastic time, Dulce.
source_text: “You never did!” exclaimed Sadie admiringly. “Well, ain’t you the lucky one? Piggy’s an awful swell; and he always takes a girl to swell places. He took Blanche up to the Hoffman House one evening, where they have swell music, and you see a lot of swells. You’ll have a swell time, Dulce.”

text: Once the door was securely bolted, Dulcie collapsed onto her bed, flattening the black feather on her hat, and wept for ten minutes. General Kitchener was her solitary companion, the very image of a chivalrous hero. He appeared to harbor some hidden grief, and his magnificent moustache was the stuff of dreams; she felt a slight intimidation from the mixture of sternness and warmth in his eyes. She frequently daydreamed that he might visit her one day, his saber clinking against his tall boots as he called her name. Once, when a boy rattled a chain against a streetlamp, she had even thrown open the window to check. But it was no use. She knew the General was far away in Japan, leading his forces against the fierce Turks, and he would never actually step out of his gold-leafed frame for her. Still, a single glance at him had defeated Piggy this evening. Yes, at least for tonight.
source_text: After the door was closed and locked, Dulcie fell upon her bed, crushing her black tip, and cried for ten minutes. General Kitchener was her only friend. He was Dulcie’s ideal of a gallant knight. He looked as if he might have a secret sorrow, and his wonderful moustache was a dream, and she was a little afraid of that stern yet tender look in his eyes. She used to have little fancies that he would call at the house sometime, and ask for her, with his sword clanking against his high boots. Once, when a boy was rattling a piece of chain against a lamp-post she had opened the window and looked out. But there was no use. She knew that General Kitchener was away over in Japan, leading his army against the savage Turks; and he would never step out of his gilt frame for her. Yet one look from him had vanquished Piggy that night. Yes, for that night.

text: Prince Michael reclined on his preferred park bench, a faint smile touching his lips. He found it an amusing thought that, should the whim strike him, he possessed the capital to purchase every one of the massive, glowing mansions lining the street before him. His reserves of gold, luxury vehicles, gems, masterpieces, and vast lands could rival the richest tycoon in Manhattan without even making a dent in his total fortune. He was a man entitled to dine alongside the world's monarchs. High society, the creative elite, the inner circles of power, the worship of beauties, the respect of the brilliant, and every luxury imaginable—all the sweetest rewards of existence were ready for the taking for Prince Michael of Valleluna whenever he wished to claim them. Instead, he opted to linger in tattered, grimy clothes on a public seat. Having sampled the offerings of a privileged life and found them unpalatable, he had voluntarily stepped out of his paradise for a time, seeking a different kind of distraction near the raw and vulnerable pulse of humanity.
source_text: Prince Michael sat on his favourite bench and smiled. It was a diverting thought to him that he was wealthy enough to buy every one of those close-ranged, bulky, window-lit mansions that faced him, if he chose. He could have matched gold, equipages, jewels, art treasures, estates and acres with any Croesus in this proud city of Manhattan, and scarcely have entered upon the bulk of his holdings. He could have sat at table with reigning sovereigns. The social world, the world of art, the fellowship of the elect, adulation, imitation, the homage of the fairest, honours from the highest, praise from the wisest, flattery, esteem, credit, pleasure, fame—all the honey of life was waiting in the comb in the hive of the world for Prince Michael, of the Electorate of Valleluna, whenever he might choose to take it. But his choice was to sit in rags and dinginess on a bench in a park. For he had tasted of the fruit of the tree of life, and, finding it bitter in his mouth, had stepped out of Eden for a time to seek distraction close to the unarmoured, beating heart of the world.

text: I understand the human heart as intimately as I know the natural world," the Prince said with solemn poise. "I am a scholar of philosophy, a master of the arts, and I possess wealth that is virtually limitless. There are very few earthly hardships that I cannot ease or resolve. I have studied your expression and found a spirit that is both honorable and deeply troubled. I urge you to accept my counsel or my assistance. Do not prove the intelligence in your eyes false by letting my outward appearance make you doubt my capacity to end your suffering.
source_text: “I know human nature as I do the trees and grass,” said the Prince, with earnest dignity. “I am a master of philosophy, a graduate in art, and I hold the purse of a Fortunatus. There are few mortal misfortunes that I cannot alleviate or overcome. I have read your countenance, and found in it honesty and nobility as well as distress. I beg of you to accept my advice or aid. Do not belie the intelligence I see in your face by judging from my appearance of my ability to defeat your troubles.”

text: “I will, Your Royal Highness,” he replied, his tone laced with feigned humility. “Do you see that house over there—the one where three of the upstairs windows are lit? Well, at six o'clock I was inside that building with the woman I am—or rather, was—engaged to. I’d been behaving poorly, my dear Prince—I’d been a bit of a troublemaker, and she’d caught wind of it. I was looking for forgiveness, of course—we’re always asking women to excuse our behavior, aren't we, Prince?”
source_text: “Your Royal Highness, I will,” he said, in tones of mock deference. “Do you see yonder house—the one with three upper windows lighted? Well, at 6 o’clock I stood in that house with the young lady I am—that is, I was—engaged to. I had been doing wrong, my dear Prince—I had been a naughty boy, and she had heard of it. I wanted to be forgiven, of course—we are always wanting women to forgive us, aren’t we, Prince?”

text: “I need time to reflect on this,” she declared. “One thing is certain: I will either grant you a total pardon, or I will never look upon your face again. There will be no compromises. At half-past eight,” she instructed, “at exactly half-past eight, you must watch the center window on the top floor. If I choose to forgive you, I shall hang a white silk scarf from that window. You will understand then that everything is restored to how it was, and you may return to me. If no scarf appears, you must accept that our relationship is over forever.” The young man finished his tale with a bitter edge. “That is why I have been fixated on that clock. The moment for the signal passed twenty-three minutes ago. Is it any wonder that I am a bit shaken, my tattered and bearded prince?”
source_text: “‘I want time to think it over,’ said she. ‘There is one thing certain; I will either fully forgive you, or I will never see your face again. There will be no half-way business. At half-past eight,’ she said, ‘at exactly half-past eight you may be watching the middle upper window of the top floor. If I decide to forgive I will hang out of that window a white silk scarf. You will know by that that all is as was before, and you may come to me. If you see no scarf you may consider that everything between us is ended forever.’ That,” concluded the young man bitterly, “is why I have been watching that clock. The time for the signal to appear has passed twenty-three minutes ago. Do you wonder that I am a little disturbed, my Prince of Rags and Whiskers?”

text: “By your very sovereign crown, it is impossible!” the young man cried out in a fit of despair. “You are a stranger to Clara’s habits, of course. She is a creature of absolute, unwavering punctuality; it was that very precision that first ensnared my heart. I have been handed my dismissal instead of a token of affection. By one minute past eight, I should have known the game was lost. I shall take the midnight express toward the frontier with Silas Thorne. Everything is over. I’ll try Silas’s cattle-range for a time, then lose myself in the Yukon and the bottom of a bottle. Fare you well—er—your Highness.”
source_text: “Never, on your principality!” exclaimed the young man, hopelessly. “You don’t know Marian—of course. She’s always on time, to the minute. That was the first thing about her that attracted me. I’ve got the mitten instead of the scarf. I ought to have known at 8.31 that my goose was cooked. I’ll go West on the 11.45 to-night with Jack Milburn. The jig’s up. I’ll try Jack’s ranch awhile and top off with the Klondike and whiskey. Good-night—er—er—Prince.”

text: The recognition between two travelers in a remote wilderness is immediate and absolute; the spiritual bond between a mother and her infant is instantaneous and profound. Without a moment’s delay, a master and his dog bridge the narrow gap between species, and the fleeting, wise communications shared by lovers are impossibly swift. Yet, all these connections are merely slow and fumbling exchanges of sentiment compared to a particular encounter that the sightseeing bus will soon unveil. You are about to learn—if the truth has not already struck you—which two inhabitants of this world can most rapidly peer into one another's souls when they finally meet face to face.
source_text: Swift and comprehensive is the recognition of white man for white man in African wilds; instant and sure is the spiritual greeting between mother and babe; unhesitatingly do master and dog commune across the slight gulf between animal and man; immeasurably quick and sapient are the brief messages between one and one’s beloved. But all these instances set forth only slow and groping interchange of sympathy and thought beside one other instance which the Rubberneck coach shall disclose. You shall learn (if you have not learned already) what two beings of all earth’s living inhabitants most quickly look into each other’s hearts and souls when they meet face to face.

text: Across the features of Mrs. Alistair Finch was inscribed a brief trilogy of the soul’s most satisfied conclusions. The first volume maintained the steadfast premise that Alistair Finch was a specimen of the highest order. The second was an appreciative treatise on the world, pronouncing it a thoroughly hospitable and elegant domain. The third volume confessed the exhilarating truth that, from their perch atop a sightseeing omnibus, they were sampling a velocity that passeth all understanding.
source_text: And on Mrs. James Williams’s face was recorded a little library of the world’s best thoughts in three volumes. Volume No. 1 contained the belief that James Williams was about the right sort of thing. Volume No. 2 was an essay on the world, declaring it to be a very excellent place. Volume No. 3 disclosed the belief that in occupying the highest seat in a Rubberneck auto they were travelling the pace that passes all understanding.

text: Directly in front of Mrs. James Williams sat a young woman in a baggy tan coat and a straw hat decorated with clusters of grapes and roses. It is only in the world of dreams or the displays of a milliner that one finds such a seasonal impossibility gathered in a single harvest. The girl watched with wide, trusting blue eyes as the man with the megaphone bellowed his conviction that the lives of millionaires were matters of great importance. During the pauses in his narration, she practiced a brand of silent philosophy, finding a stoic comfort in the rhythmic chewing of her pepsin gum.
source_text: Just in front of Mrs. James Williams sat a girl in a loose tan jacket and a straw hat adorned with grapes and roses. Only in dreams and milliners’ shops do we, alas! gather grapes and roses at one swipe. This girl gazed with large blue eyes, credulous, when the megaphone man roared his doctrine that millionaires were things about which we should be concerned. Between blasts she resorted to Epictetian philosophy in the form of pepsin chewing gum.

text: Stepping down to the ground, James Williams met his captors with a grin. He was already imagining the entertaining tale he would share back in Cloverdale about the time he was wrongly identified as a burglar. The sightseeing bus lingered, pausing out of consideration for its passengers. After all, what spectacle could be more captivating than this?
source_text: Descended to the ground, James Williams faced his captors with a smile. He was thinking what a good story he would have to tell in Cloverdale about having been mistaken for a burglar. The Rubberneck coach lingered, out of respect for its patrons. What could be a more interesting sight than this?

text: Sterling Vance was smoothing the creases of his dinner jacket in a cramped attic chamber. One iron sat warming over a hissing gas ring; the other was being driven with purpose across the wool to produce that razor-sharp line which would presently descend from his waistcoat to the uppers of his gleaming patent shoes. We are permitted to witness this portion of the gentleman’s toilette. The further details of his grooming are best left to the intuition of those who have navigated the straits of a modest income with such desperate ingenuity. He appears next as he steps from the threshold of the boarding-house, a figure of impeccable grace; poised, striking, and serene—a perfect imitation of a Manhattan man of leisure venturing forth, with a hint of fatigue, to meet the night’s entertainments.
source_text: Mr. Towers Chandler was pressing his evening suit in his hall bedroom. One iron was heating on a small gas stove; the other was being pushed vigorously back and forth to make the desirable crease that would be seen later on extending in straight lines from Mr. Chandler’s patent leather shoes to the edge of his low-cut vest. So much of the hero’s toilet may be intrusted to our confidence. The remainder may be guessed by those whom genteel poverty has driven to ignoble expedient. Our next view of him shall be as he descends the steps of his lodging-house immaculately and correctly clothed; calm, assured, handsome—in appearance the typical New York young clubman setting out, slightly bored, to inaugurate the pleasures of the evening.

text: Chandler earned a weekly wage of eighteen dollars. He worked at an architectural firm and, at twenty-two, held the conviction that architecture was a genuine art. He also privately believed—though he would never have dared to voice such a sentiment in New York—that the Flatiron Building’s design was far less impressive than that of the great cathedral in Milan.
source_text: Chandler’s honorarium was $18 per week. He was employed in the office of an architect. He was twenty-two years old; he considered architecture to be truly an art; and he honestly believed—though he would not have dared to admit it in New York—that the Flatiron Building was inferior in design to the great cathedral in Milan.

text: Upon reaching a particular junction, Elias paused, deliberating whether to pivot back toward the ornate and prestigious dining room where he was accustomed to feasting during his moments of deliberate extravagance. At that precise heartbeat, a slip of a girl rounded the brickwork at a trot, lost her purchase on a crust of frosted rime, and collapsed abruptly onto the paving stones.
source_text: At a certain corner he came to a standstill, proposing to himself the question of turning back toward the showy and fashionable restaurant in which he usually dined on the evenings of his especial luxury. Just then a girl scuddled lightly around the corner, slipped on a patch of icy snow and fell plump upon the sidewalk.

text: Such considerations flickered briefly across Julian’s mind, and he resolved to make the overture. It was an undeniable lapse in decorum, yet women of the laboring class often discarded such social niceties in these circumstances. They were typically astute observers of character, valuing their own sharp instincts far more than the hollow dictates of propriety. His twelve dollars, if distributed with careful grace, promised a banquet of significant quality for the pair. Such a feast would surely serve as a luminous interruption to the drab monotony of her daily existence, and her palpable delight would, in turn, magnify his own vanity and pleasure.
source_text: These thoughts passed swiftly through his mind, and he decided to ask her. It was a breach of etiquette, of course, but oftentimes wage-earning girls waived formalities in matters of this kind. They were generally shrewd judges of men; and thought better of their own judgment than they did of useless conventions. His ten dollars, discreetly expended, would enable the two to dine very well indeed. The dinner would no doubt be a wonderful experience thrown into the dull routine of the girl’s life; and her lively appreciation of it would add to his own triumph and pleasure.

text: I suspect," he told her with sincere gravity, "that your foot requires a longer rest than you realize. I’d like to propose a way for you to stay off it while doing me a kindness at the same time. I was just heading out for a solitary dinner when you came tumbling around the bend. Join me for a quiet meal and some conversation, and I’m certain that by then, your injured ankle will be strong enough to get you home.
source_text: “I think,” he said to her, with frank gravity, “that your foot needs a longer rest than you suppose. Now, I am going to suggest a way in which you can give it that and at the same time do me a favour. I was on my way to dine all by my lonely self when you came tumbling around the corner. You come with me and we’ll have a cozy dinner and a pleasant talk together, and by that time your game ankle will carry you home very nicely, I am sure.”

text: At that instant, the New York mania for status and the infectious drive to show off took hold of Towers Chandler. There on Broadway, amidst the glitter and the gaze of the crowd, he found himself on a stage of his own making. He had decided to spend one night portraying a wealthy man of fashion and taste. Clad in the perfect attire, he was beyond the reach of his better judgment; he was committed to the performance, and nothing could stop him from playing the role.
source_text: Then it was that the Madness of Manhattan, the frenzy of Fuss and Feathers, the Bacillus of Brag, the Provincial Plague of Pose seized upon Towers Chandler. He was on Broadway, surrounded by pomp and style, and there were eyes to look at him. On the stage of that comedy he had assumed to play the one-night part of a butterfly of fashion and an idler of means and taste. He was dressed for the part, and all his good angels had not the power to prevent him from acting it.

text: He began to regale Miss Marian with talk of social clubs and teas, of golf and riding and cotillions, alluding to European tours and a yacht moored at Larchmont. Seeing her captivated by this grandiosity, he reinforced his deception with subtle hints of immense wealth, casually mentioning names that the masses spoke of only with reverence. This was Chandler’s fleeting moment in the sun, and he was squeezing every bit of pleasure from it that he could. Yet, once or twice, the girl’s genuine character shimmered through the veil of vanity that his own ego had drawn across his vision.
source_text: So he began to prate to Miss Marian of clubs, of teas, of golf and riding and kennels and cotillions and tours abroad and threw out hints of a yacht lying at Larchmont. He could see that she was vastly impressed by this vague talk, so he endorsed his pose by random insinuations concerning great wealth, and mentioned familiarly a few names that are handled reverently by the proletariat. It was Chandler’s short little day, and he was wringing from it the best that could be had, as he saw it. And yet once or twice he saw the pure gold of this girl shine through the mist that his egotism had raised between him and all objects.

text: Once she had left her host, the young woman hurried across the city, eventually reaching a dignified and grand residence two blocks to the east. It stood along that thoroughfare known as the boulevard of wealth and worldly power. She slipped inside quickly and headed upstairs to a room where a beautiful woman, clad in an intricate gown, was peering out the window with a look of deep concern.
source_text: The girl, after leaving her entertainer, sped swiftly cross-town until she arrived at a handsome and sedate mansion two squares to the east, facing on that avenue which is the highway of Mammon and the auxiliary gods. Here she entered hurriedly and ascended to a room where a handsome young lady in an elaborate house dress was looking anxiously out the window.

text: “Oh, you impulsive creature!” the elder girl cried out as the other came inside. “When will you stop giving us such frights? Two hours have passed since you hurried off in that tattered old dress and Marie’s hat. Mother has been so distressed; she even sent Louis out in the motorcar to search for you. You really are a wicked, inconsiderate girl.”
source_text: “Oh, you madcap!” exclaimed the elder girl, when the other entered. “When will you quit frightening us this way? It is two hours since you ran out in that rag of an old dress and Marie’s hat. Mamma has been so alarmed. She sent Louis in the auto to try to find you. You are a bad, thoughtless Puss.”

text: “Ma’am,” he said, “the fellow on the opposite bench has tasked me with a message for you. If he’s a stranger and merely playing the rogue, give me the nod and I’ll fetch a constable in a few minutes’ time. But if you know him and his intentions are honest, then I’ll go ahead and recite the bit of flowery nonsense he’s sent your way.”
source_text: “Lady,” he said, “dat gent on de oder bench sent yer a song and dance by me. If yer don’t know de guy, and he’s tryin’ to do de Johnny act, say de word, and I’ll call a cop in t’ree minutes. If yer does know him, and he’s on de square, w’y I’ll spiel yer de bunch of hot air he sent yer.”

text: A performance!" she said, her voice slow and melodic, though it seemed to wrap her words in a thin, elusive veil of sarcasm. "A novel concept—something of a minstrel act, I assume. I was once acquainted with the man who sent you, so I don't think it will be necessary to involve the police. You may go ahead with your routine, but keep the volume down. It is a bit early for a public show, and we wouldn't want to draw a crowd.
source_text: “A song and dance!” she said, in a deliberate sweet voice that seemed to clothe her words in a diaphanous garment of impalpable irony. “A new idea—in the troubadour line, I suppose. I—used to know the gentleman who sent you, so I think it will hardly be necessary to call the police. You may execute your song and dance, but do not sing too loudly. It is a little early yet for open-air vaudeville, and we might attract attention.”

text: “Inform the gentleman that there is no need for me to restate my principles once more. He is fully aware of what they were and what they remain today. In relation to this matter, unwavering loyalty and honesty are my highest priorities. Tell him that I have scrutinized my own soul as thoroughly as one can, and I understand its vulnerabilities just as clearly as its requirements. This is precisely why I refuse to listen to his petitions, no matter what they might be. I did not judge him based on rumors or questionable proof, which is why I never leveled a formal accusation. Yet, since he insists on hearing what he already understands perfectly well, you may relay the facts to him.”
source_text: “Tell the gentleman that I need not repeat to him a description of my ideals. He knows what they have been and what they still are. So far as they touch on this case, absolute loyalty and truth are the ones paramount. Tell him that I have studied my own heart as well as one can, and I know its weakness as well as I do its needs. That is why I decline to hear his pleas, whatever they may be. I did not condemn him through hearsay or doubtful evidence, and that is why I made no charge. But, since he persists in hearing what he already well knows, you may convey the matter.

text: Inform him that I entered the conservatory from the rear that evening, intending to pick a rose for my mother. Tell him I saw him and Miss Ashburton together under the pink oleander. The scene was lovely, but their posture and intimacy were far too revealing to require any excuses. I walked out of the garden, abandoning the rose and my illusions at the same time. You may take that little drama back to your master.
source_text: “Tell him that I entered the conservatory that evening from the rear, to cut a rose for my mother. Tell him I saw him and Miss Ashburton beneath the pink oleander. The tableau was pretty, but the pose and juxtaposition were too eloquent and evident to require explanation. I left the conservatory, and, at the same time, the rose and my ideal. You may carry that song and dance to your impresario.”

text: Hand this letter to the lady," he said, "and ask her to read it. Tell her it should clarify the whole situation. Tell her that if she had only tempered her high ideals with a little trust, a great deal of heartache could have been avoided. Let her know that the loyalty she values so deeply has never once faltered. Tell her I am waiting for her response.
source_text: “Give that letter to the lady,” he said, “and ask her to read it. Tell her that it should explain the situation. Tell her that, if she had mingled a little trust with her conception of the ideal, much heartache might have been avoided. Tell her that the loyalty she prizes so much has never wavered. Tell her I am waiting for an answer.”

text: All at once, as he lay in stillness, the air grew thick with the potent, honeyed scent of mignonette. It swept in like a sharp gust of wind, possessing such clarity and presence that it seemed almost like a physical guest. "What is it, dear?" the man cried out, as though responding to a summons, as he leapt up and whirled around. The lush fragrance adhered to him, enveloping him completely. He reached into the void, his senses momentarily tangled and fused. How could a scent command him so forcefully? Surely he had heard a voice. And yet, was it not a sound that had reached out to touch him, to offer that soft caress?
source_text: Then, suddenly, as he rested there, the room was filled with the strong, sweet odour of mignonette. It came as upon a single buffet of wind with such sureness and fragrance and emphasis that it almost seemed a living visitant. And the man cried aloud: “What, dear?” as if he had been called, and sprang up and faced about. The rich odour clung to him and wrapped him around. He reached out his arms for it, all his senses for the time confused and commingled. How could one be peremptorily called by an odour? Surely it must have been a sound. But, was it not the sound that had touched, that had caressed him?

text: The tidying of the room had been half-hearted at best. Sprawled across the cheap fabric covering the dresser were several hairpins—those quiet, nondescript companions of women, adaptable to any whim but revealing nothing of the past. He overlooked them, knowing their anonymity was absolute. Rummaging through the dresser drawers, he stumbled upon a small, tattered handkerchief that had been cast aside. He pressed it to his face. It carried the sharp, provocative scent of heliotrope; he flung it to the floor. Another drawer yielded miscellaneous buttons, a playbill, a pawn ticket, a pair of stray marshmallows, and a volume on dream interpretation. In the final drawer, he discovered a woman’s black satin hair bow, an object that made him pause, caught between a chill and a fever. However, the satin bow was just another modest, mass-produced trinket of the feminine world, and it kept its secrets well.
source_text: The room had been but carelessly set in order. Scattered upon the flimsy dresser scarf were half a dozen hairpins—those discreet, indistinguishable friends of womankind, feminine of gender, infinite of mood and uncommunicative of tense. These he ignored, conscious of their triumphant lack of identity. Ransacking the drawers of the dresser he came upon a discarded, tiny, ragged handkerchief. He pressed it to his face. It was racy and insolent with heliotrope; he hurled it to the floor. In another drawer he found odd buttons, a theatre programme, a pawnbroker’s card, two lost marshmallows, a book on the divination of dreams. In the last was a woman’s black satin hair bow, which halted him, poised between ice and fire. But the black satin hair-bow also is femininity’s demure, impersonal, common ornament, and tells no tales.

text: He probed the dark fissures and forgotten nooks, unearthing nothing but stray bungs and spent cigarettes. These he overlooked with a weary, silent scorn. But upon discovering a chewed cigar butt trapped in the fibers of the rug, he ground it into the floor with a sharp and bitter imprecation. He ransacked the apartment from wall to wall. He turned up the bleak and trivial detritus of countless wandering occupants; yet of the woman he chased, who might have lived there and whose ghost seemed to linger in the draft, he discovered not one vestige.
source_text: He burrowed in crevices and corners, and found corks and cigarettes. These he passed in passive contempt. But once he found in a fold of the matting a half-smoked cigar, and this he ground beneath his heel with a green and trenchant oath. He sifted the room from end to end. He found dreary and ignoble small records of many a peripatetic tenant; but of her whom he sought, and who may have lodged there, and whose spirit seemed to hover there, he found no trace.

text: “Well, there was a bachelor who worked in the hauling trade. He moved out still owing me for a week. Before him, Mrs. Crowder and her two children stayed for four months; and prior to them was elderly Mr. Doyle, whose sons handled the rent. He kept the room for six months. That accounts for the past year, sir, and I don’t remember any further back than that.”
source_text: “Why, there was a single gentleman connected with the draying business. He left owing me a week. Before him was Missis Crowder and her two children, that stayed four months; and back of them was old Mr. Doyle, whose sons paid for him. He kept the room six months. That goes back a year, sir, and further I do not remember.”

text: A new truth dawned upon Maud’s reviving senses. Suddenly, she had risen from a hopeless, humble worshiper to a peer of the irresistible Beatrice. She was now a temptress herself, a mark for the Archer, a maiden who must show reserve while the victors feasted. Man had judged her waist attainable and her mouth delightful. The impulsive and amorous Mr. Higgins had, in effect, performed a miraculous feat of overnight renovation. He had taken the coarse burlap of her plainness, washed, dried, and refined it, returning it to her as delicate, patterned lace—the very gown of Aphrodite.
source_text: Another thing dawned upon Tildy’s recovering wits. In a moment she had advanced from a hopeless, lowly admirer to be an Eve-sister of the potent Aileen. She herself was now a man-charmer, a mark for Cupid, a Sabine who must be coy when the Romans were at their banquet boards. Man had found her waist achievable and her lips desirable. The sudden and amatory Seeders had, as it were, performed for her a miraculous piece of one-day laundry work. He had taken the sackcloth of her uncomeliness, had washed, dried, starched and ironed it, and returned it to her sheer embroidered lawn—the robe of Venus herself.

The party that supplied the faulty work may choose to provide a replacement copy instead of a refund. For works received via electronic transmission, the provider may offer a second chance to download the work rather than issuing a refund. If this second version is also flawed, you may submit a written demand for a refund, and the provider will be granted no further attempts to fix the issue.
source_text: 1.F.3. LIMITED RIGHT OF REPLACEMENT OR REFUND - If you discover a defect in this electronic work within 90 days of receiving it, you can receive a refund of the money (if any) you paid for it by sending a written explanation to the person you received the work from. If you received the work on a physical medium, you must return the medium with your written explanation. The person or entity that provided you with the defective work may elect to provide a replacement copy in lieu of a refund. If you received the work electronically, the person or entity providing it to you may choose to give you a second opportunity to receive the work electronically in lieu of a refund. If the second copy is also defective, you may demand a refund in writing without further opportunities to fix the problem.

text: Do not imagine, however, that my minor complaints or my longing for a solace I may never attain suggest any hesitation in my plans. My intentions are set in stone, and my departure is merely waiting for the weather to clear. Though the winter was brutally cold, the spring shows great promise and is arriving ahead of schedule, so I might actually embark earlier than planned. I will not be reckless; you know me well enough to trust my discretion and my concern for those whose lives are placed in my hands.
source_text: Yet do not suppose, because I complain a little or because I can conceive a consolation for my toils which I may never know, that I am wavering in my resolutions. Those are as fixed as fate, and my voyage is only now delayed until the weather shall permit my embarkation. The winter has been dreadfully severe, but the spring promises well, and it is considered as a remarkably early season, so that perhaps I may sail sooner than I expected. I shall do nothing rashly: you know me sufficiently to confide in my prudence and considerateness whenever the safety of others is committed to my care.

text: Yet victory will surely be the reward for my labors. Why should it be otherwise? I have already traveled this distance, carving a steady route across the uncharted ocean, with the very constellations serving as observers and proofs of my advancement. Why shouldn't I continue to push forward across this wild yet yielding expanse? What power can truly obstruct the steadfast courage and fixed purpose of a human being?
source_text: But success _shall_ crown my endeavours. Wherefore not? Thus far I have gone, tracing a secure way over the pathless seas, the very stars themselves being witnesses and testimonies of my triumph. Why not still proceed over the untamed yet obedient element? What can stop the determined heart and resolved will of man?

text: Around two in the afternoon, the fog finally lifted, revealing an endless expanse of jagged ice fields stretching toward the horizon in every direction. Some of my crewmates groaned in despair, and I felt a growing sense of unease myself, until a bizarre sight suddenly caught our attention and pulled our focus away from our own predicament. About half a mile off, we spotted a low-slung sled pulled by a team of dogs heading north; a figure that appeared human, though of immense, towering proportions, sat in the vehicle and guided the pack. We tracked the traveler’s swift progress through our telescopes until he eventually vanished behind the uneven ridges of the distant frozen landscape.
source_text: About two o’clock the mist cleared away, and we beheld, stretched out in every direction, vast and irregular plains of ice, which seemed to have no end. Some of my comrades groaned, and my own mind began to grow watchful with anxious thoughts, when a strange sight suddenly attracted our attention and diverted our solicitude from our own situation. We perceived a low carriage, fixed on a sledge and drawn by dogs, pass on towards the north, at the distance of half a mile; a being which had the shape of a man, but apparently of gigantic stature, sat in the sledge and guided the dogs. We watched the rapid progress of the traveller with our telescopes until he was lost among the distant inequalities of the ice.

text: This sight filled us with absolute amazement. We had believed ourselves to be hundreds of miles from any shore, yet this figure seemed to suggest that land was not nearly as remote as we had assumed. Nevertheless, since we were trapped by the surrounding ice, we found it impossible to pursue the trail we had been watching so intently.
source_text: This appearance excited our unqualified wonder. We were, as we believed, many hundred miles from any land; but this apparition seemed to denote that it was not, in reality, so distant as we had supposed. Shut in, however, by ice, it was impossible to follow his track, which we had observed with the greatest attention.

text: Some two hours later, the surge of the ground sea became audible, and before nightfall the ice fractured, setting our vessel free. We remained hove-to until morning, however, fearing the dark and those massive, drifting floes that wander after the pack breaks up. I seized this time to find a few hours of rest.
source_text: About two hours after this occurrence we heard the ground sea, and before night the ice broke and freed our ship. We, however, lay to until the morning, fearing to encounter in the dark those large loose masses which float about after the breaking up of the ice. I profited of this time to rest for a few hours.

text: You can imagine my utter amazement at hearing such a question from a man facing certain death, for whom I assumed my ship would be a sanctuary more valuable than any treasure the world could offer. I answered, nonetheless, that we were on a mission of exploration bound for the North Pole.
source_text: You may conceive my astonishment on hearing such a question addressed to me from a man on the brink of destruction and to whom I should have supposed that my vessel would have been a resource which he would not have exchanged for the most precious wealth the earth can afford. I replied, however, that we were on a voyage of discovery towards the northern pole.

text: Two days slipped away in this fashion before he was finally able to talk, and I often suspected that his agony had cost him his mind. When he had improved slightly, I moved him into my own cabin and cared for him as often as my duties would let me. I have never met a more captivating soul; his eyes are typically filled with a frantic, almost crazed look, yet there are times when a small gesture of help or a simple kindness makes his whole expression radiate a sense of goodness and warmth unlike anything I have ever seen. Most of the time, though, he is lost in a deep, dark despondency, sometimes grinding his teeth as if he is struggling under a weight of misery too heavy to bear.
source_text: Two days passed in this manner before he was able to speak, and I often feared that his sufferings had deprived him of understanding. When he had in some measure recovered, I removed him to my own cabin and attended on him as much as my duty would permit. I never saw a more interesting creature: his eyes have generally an expression of wildness, and even madness, but there are moments when, if anyone performs an act of kindness towards him or does him any the most trifling service, his whole countenance is lighted up, as it were, with a beam of benevolence and sweetness that I never saw equalled. But he is generally melancholy and despairing, and sometimes he gnashes his teeth, as if impatient of the weight of woes that oppresses him.

text: Such news piqued the traveler’s interest, prompting him to pose a series of inquiries regarding the path taken by the phantom—for so he designated the creature. Not long after, when we were at last in private, he remarked, “I have surely stirred your wonder, much as I have that of these worthy folk; yet you possess too much delicacy to press me for answers.”
source_text: This aroused the stranger’s attention, and he asked a multitude of questions concerning the route which the dæmon, as he called him, had pursued. Soon after, when he was alone with me, he said, “I have, doubtless, excited your curiosity, as well as that of these good people; but you are too considerate to make inquiries.”

text: Shortly thereafter, he asked if I believed the fracturing of the ice had destroyed the other sled. I told him I couldn't say for certain, as the ice hadn't broken apart until nearly midnight; the traveler might have reached a safe location before then, but I had no way of knowing for sure.
source_text: Soon after this he inquired if I thought that the breaking up of the ice had destroyed the other sledge. I replied that I could not answer with any degree of certainty, for the ice had not broken until near midnight, and the traveller might have arrived at a place of safety before that time; but of this I could not judge.

text: This concludes the chronicle of these remarkable circumstances as they have unfolded to this moment. Our guest’s vitality slowly revives, yet he remains shrouded in silence and manifests a distinct trepidation should any soul but me venture into his berth. Still, such is the sweetness and refinement of his bearing that he has won the regard of every hand on board, though they have shared but few words with him. I myself have grown to hold him in a brotherly affection, moved to profound sorrow by the weight of his enduring misery. One can only imagine the grandeur of his spirit in happier times, for even as a broken vessel, he retains a grace that is both poignant and irresistible.
source_text: Such is my journal of what relates to this strange occurrence up to the present day. The stranger has gradually improved in health but is very silent and appears uneasy when anyone except myself enters his cabin. Yet his manners are so conciliating and gentle that the sailors are all interested in him, although they have had very little communication with him. For my own part, I begin to love him as a brother, and his constant and deep grief fills me with sympathy and compassion. He must have been a noble creature in his better days, being even now in wreck so attractive and amiable.

text: “I concur with your sentiment,” the stranger answered. “We are incomplete beings, only partially realized, if someone more insightful, more virtuous, and more beloved than ourselves—the very definition of a true friend—does not offer their guidance to help refine our fragile and flawed characters. I once possessed such a friend, the most honorable of men, and so I feel qualified to speak on the nature of companionship. You are filled with hope and have your whole life ahead of you, with no reason to succumb to misery. But I—I have been stripped of everything and lack the strength to begin again.”
source_text: “I agree with you,” replied the stranger; “we are unfashioned creatures, but half made up, if one wiser, better, dearer than ourselves—such a friend ought to be—do not lend his aid to perfectionate our weak and faulty natures. I once had a friend, the most noble of human creatures, and am entitled, therefore, to judge respecting friendship. You have hope, and the world before you, and have no cause for despair. But I—I have lost everything and cannot begin life anew.”

text: Though his spirit is utterly shattered, no one possesses a more profound sensitivity to the splendors of the natural world. The starlit heavens, the vast sea, and every vista offered by these magnificent reaches still have the power to lift his soul above the terrestrial. Such a man leads a dual life; he may endure deep misery and be crushed by misfortune, yet when he retreats into his inner self, he becomes like a divine spirit wreathed in light, a sanctuary where neither grief nor folly can ever intrude.
source_text: Even broken in spirit as he is, no one can feel more deeply than he does the beauties of nature. The starry sky, the sea, and every sight afforded by these wonderful regions seem still to have the power of elevating his soul from earth. Such a man has a double existence: he may suffer misery and be overwhelmed by disappointments, yet when he has retired into himself, he will be like a celestial spirit that has a halo around him, within whose circle no grief or folly ventures.

text: It is easy to understand how much his offer to speak pleased me, yet I was hesitant to let him relive his suffering by recounting his past trials. I was profoundly eager to hear the promised tale, motivated by a blend of pure curiosity and a sincere wish to ease his burdens if it were within my power. I made sure to convey these conflicting emotions in my response.
source_text: You may easily imagine that I was much gratified by the offered communication, yet I could not endure that he should renew his grief by a recital of his misfortunes. I felt the greatest eagerness to hear the promised narrative, partly from curiosity and partly from a strong desire to ameliorate his fate if it were in my power. I expressed these feelings in my answer.

text: His daughter cared for him with profound devotion, yet she watched in anguish as their small reserve of money vanished, leaving no hope for future income. Nevertheless, Caroline Beaufort was endowed with a singular strength of character, and her resolve only hardened in the face of misfortune. She sought out sewing jobs and wove straw into braids, finding various ways to earn a meager income that was barely enough to sustain their existence.
source_text: His daughter attended him with the greatest tenderness, but she saw with despair that their little fund was rapidly decreasing and that there was no other prospect of support. But Caroline Beaufort possessed a mind of an uncommon mould, and her courage rose to support her in her adversity.
