She procured plain work; she plaited straw and by various means contrived to earn a pittance scarcely sufficient to support life.

text: After departing Italy, they journeyed through Germany and France. I was born in Naples, their firstborn, and accompanied them on their travels from my earliest infancy. For several years, I remained their only child. Deeply as they were devoted to one another, they seemed to possess a boundless wellspring of affection from which they drew endlessly to shower upon me. My first memories are of my mother’s gentle embraces and the look of kind satisfaction on my father’s face as he watched me. I was their delight and their pride, but more importantly, I was their child—a vulnerable, innocent being granted to them by Providence. They felt a solemn responsibility to nurture my goodness, knowing my future happiness or sorrow depended entirely on how they discharged their parental duties. Because they were so mindful of their obligations and so naturally tender, my infancy was a continuous lesson in patience and self-discipline. Yet, I was led by such a delicate, silken touch that my early life seemed like nothing but a long, unbroken span of happiness.
source_text: From Italy they visited Germany and France. I, their eldest child, was born at Naples, and as an infant accompanied them in their rambles. I remained for several years their only child. Much as they were attached to each other, they seemed to draw inexhaustible stores of affection from a very mine of love to bestow them upon me. My mother’s tender caresses and my father’s smile of benevolent pleasure while regarding me are my first recollections. I was their plaything and their idol, and something better—their child, the innocent and helpless creature bestowed on them by Heaven, whom to bring up to good, and whose future lot it was in their hands to direct to happiness or misery, according as they fulfilled their duties towards me. With this deep consciousness of what they owed towards the being to which they had given life, added to the active spirit of tenderness that animated both, it may be imagined that while during every hour of my infant life I received a lesson of patience, of charity, and of self-control, I was so guided by a silken cord that all seemed but one train of enjoyment to me.

text: In my sixteenth year, during our retreat to the family estate overlooking the lake, I bore witness to a tempest of unparalleled ferocity. It gathered behind the dark crags of the Jura, and the thunder erupted with a sudden, deafening roar that seemed to shake the very foundations of the world. I stood upon the threshold, captivated by the chaotic splendor of the elements. Without warning, a pillar of celestial fire descended upon a majestic elm that had long graced our lawn; once the brilliance faded, the great tree had vanished, leaving only a scorched and skeletal ruin. The following morning revealed a strange sight: the trunk had not merely split, but had been teased into a thousand silken filaments of timber. Never before had I seen such total annihilation.
source_text: When I was about fifteen years old we had retired to our house near Belrive, when we witnessed a most violent and terrible thunderstorm. It advanced from behind the mountains of Jura, and the thunder burst at once with frightful loudness from various quarters of the heavens. I remained, while the storm lasted, watching its progress with curiosity and delight. As I stood at the door, on a sudden I beheld a stream of fire issue from an old and beautiful oak which stood about twenty yards from our house; and so soon as the dazzling light vanished, the oak had disappeared, and nothing remained but a blasted stump. When we visited it the next morning, we found the tree shattered in a singular manner. It was not splintered by the shock, but entirely reduced to thin ribbons of wood. I never beheld anything so utterly destroyed.

text: Her passing was peaceful, and her face retained an expression of tenderness even in the stillness of death. I do not need to recount the agony of those who lose their most precious bonds to that permanent tragedy, nor describe the emptiness that haunts the soul and the desolation written upon the features. It takes a long time for the mind to accept that someone who was a constant presence—whose very life seemed an extension of our own—has vanished forever. It is hard to believe that those luminous eyes have gone dark and that a voice so familiar and cherished will never again be heard. These are the thoughts of the initial days; but as time confirms the reality of the loss, the true bitterness of mourning begins. Yet, who has not had a loved one snatched away by that cold hand? Why should I dwell on a sorrow that everyone has known and must eventually endure? Finally, a moment arrives when grieving becomes more of a habit than a necessity, and though a smile may feel like a betrayal, it is no longer forbidden. My mother was gone, but our responsibilities remained; we had to move forward with the world and count ourselves lucky as long as death had spared even one person we loved.
source_text: She died calmly, and her countenance expressed affection even in death. I need not describe the feelings of those whose dearest ties are rent by that most irreparable evil, the void that presents itself to the soul, and the despair that is exhibited on the countenance. It is so long before the mind can persuade itself that she whom we saw every day and whose very existence appeared a part of our own can have departed for ever—that the brightness of a beloved eye can have been extinguished and the sound of a voice so familiar and dear to the ear can be hushed, never more to be heard. These are the reflections of the first days; but when the lapse of time proves the reality of the evil, then the actual bitterness of grief commences. Yet from whom has not that rude hand rent away some dear connection? And why should I describe a sorrow which all have felt, and must feel? The time at length arrives when grief is rather an indulgence than a necessity; and the smile that plays upon the lips, although it may be deemed a sacrilege, is not banished. My mother was dead, but we had still duties which we ought to perform; we must continue our course with the rest and learn to think ourselves fortunate whilst one remains whom the spoiler has not seized.

text: The journey to the University of Leyden, which the late calamities had postponed, was once more a fixed necessity. I petitioned my father for a brief reprieve, a matter of some weeks. To my mind, it seemed a near-blasphemy to forsake the sepulchral stillness of our bereaved home so abruptly, only to be swept up in the frantic pulse of society. Though I was unpracticed in the art of mourning, its weight nonetheless unnerved me. I was hesitant to part from the faces of my remaining kin; most of all, I yearned to witness my gentle Elena find some glimmer of solace before my departure.
source_text: My departure for Ingolstadt, which had been deferred by these events, was now again determined upon. I obtained from my father a respite of some weeks. It appeared to me sacrilege so soon to leave the repose, akin to death, of the house of mourning and to rush into the thick of life. I was new to sorrow, but it did not the less alarm me. I was unwilling to quit the sight of those that remained to me, and above all, I desired to see my sweet Elizabeth in some degree consoled.

text: The candles burned low as we lingered. We found ourselves unable to sever the bond of our company or to force the bitter syllable of “Adieu” from our lips. At last, the word was spoken; we retreated to our chambers under the thin veil of seeking sleep, each harboring the illusion that the others were truly at rest. Yet when the first pallor of morning touched the sky and I stepped toward the coach prepared for my journey, I found them waiting—my father to grant a final prayer for my safety, Julian to offer one last steadfast clasp of the hand, and my beloved Clara to repeat her pleas for constant letters, her soft touch lingering on my sleeve as she performed those final, tender offices for her lifelong companion.
source_text: We sat late. We could not tear ourselves away from each other nor persuade ourselves to say the word “Farewell!” It was said, and we retired under the pretence of seeking repose, each fancying that the other was deceived; but when at morning’s dawn I descended to the carriage which was to convey me away, they were all there—my father again to bless me, Clerval to press my hand once more, my Elizabeth to renew her entreaties that I would write often and to bestow the last feminine attentions on her playmate and friend.

text: I went home without feeling discouraged, as I had already come to view the authors the professor condemned as obsolete; yet, I felt no more drawn to the subject in its modern form. M. Krempe was a short, stout man with a harsh voice and a disagreeable face, and his persona did little to endear me to his field of study. I have perhaps recounted my youthful conclusions on these matters with more logical coherence than they truly possessed at the time. Even as a child, I found the promises of modern scientists underwhelming. Driven by a youthful confusion and the lack of a proper mentor, I had looked backward through history, trading the findings of recent researchers for the fantasies of long-dead alchemists. Furthermore, I held the practical aims of modern science in contempt. It was a different matter entirely when the old masters pursued immortality and dominion; such ambitions, though vain, were magnificent. Now, however, the focus had shifted. The modern inquirer seemed dedicated only to dismantling the very visions that had originally sparked my interest. I was being asked to trade grand, limitless illusions for small and trivial realities.
source_text: I returned home not disappointed, for I have said that I had long considered those authors useless whom the professor reprobated; but I returned not at all the more inclined to recur to these studies in any shape. M. Krempe was a little squat man with a gruff voice and a repulsive countenance; the teacher, therefore, did not prepossess me in favour of his pursuits. In rather a too philosophical and connected a strain, perhaps, I have given an account of the conclusions I had come to concerning them in my early years. As a child I had not been content with the results promised by the modern professors of natural science. With a confusion of ideas only to be accounted for by my extreme youth and my want of a guide on such matters, I had retrod the steps of knowledge along the paths of time and exchanged the discoveries of recent inquirers for the dreams of forgotten alchemists. Besides, I had a contempt for the uses of modern natural philosophy. It was very different when the masters of the science sought immortality and power; such views, although futile, were grand; but now the scene was changed. The ambition of the inquirer seemed to limit itself to the annihilation of those visions on which my interest in science was chiefly founded. I was required to exchange chimeras of boundless grandeur for realities of little worth.

text: He then conducted me into his sanctum and elucidated the purposes of his manifold instruments, instructing me on the specific apparatus I should endeavor to acquire. He graciously offered the use of his own equipment once my mastery of the discipline had progressed sufficiently to ensure I would not disarrange their delicate configurations. Having also furnished the bibliography I had solicited, he bid me adieu, and I took my departure.
source_text: He then took me into his laboratory and explained to me the uses of his various machines, instructing me as to what I ought to procure and promising me the use of his own when I should have advanced far enough in the science not to derange their mechanism. He also gave me the list of books which I had requested, and I took my leave.

text: Because I devoted myself so intensely to my studies, my advancement was remarkably swift. My passion surprised my fellow students, and my technical skill soon rivaled that of my instructors. Professor Krempe would frequently ask after Cornelius Agrippa with a mocking grin, while Monsieur Waldman expressed sincere joy at my development. Two years passed in this fashion; I did not once return to Geneva, for I was entirely consumed by the search for the breakthroughs I hoped to achieve. Only those who have felt it can truly understand the allure of science. In other disciplines, one merely follows the path of predecessors to a known end, but science provides an inexhaustible source of wonder and discovery. Even a person of average intellect will surely master a subject if they focus on it exclusively; as I was utterly absorbed in my singular goal, my progress was so great that I eventually redesigned several chemical instruments, earning high praise from the university. Having mastered the theory and practice of natural philosophy to the extent that the Ingolstadt faculty could no longer teach me anything, I felt my stay was no longer necessary. I was preparing to return to my family and my home when an unexpected event occurred that delayed my departure.
source_text: As I applied so closely, it may be easily conceived that my progress was rapid. My ardour was indeed the astonishment of the students, and my proficiency that of the masters. Professor Krempe often asked me, with a sly smile, how Cornelius Agrippa went on, whilst M. Waldman expressed the most heartfelt exultation in my progress. Two years passed in this manner, during which I paid no visit to Geneva, but was engaged, heart and soul, in the pursuit of some discoveries which I hoped to make. None but those who have experienced them can conceive of the enticements of science. In other studies you go as far as others have gone before you, and there is nothing more to know; but in a scientific pursuit there is continual food for discovery and wonder. A mind of moderate capacity which closely pursues one study must infallibly arrive at great proficiency in that study; and I, who continually sought the attainment of one object of pursuit and was solely wrapped up in this, improved so rapidly that at the end of two years I made some discoveries in the improvement of some chemical instruments, which procured me great esteem and admiration at the university. When I had arrived at this point and had become as well acquainted with the theory and practice of natural philosophy as depended on the lessons of any of the professors at Ingolstadt, my residence there being no longer conducive to my improvements, I thought of returning to my friends and my native town, when an incident happened that protracted my stay.

text: I can see from your intensity and the hopeful wonder in your eyes, my friend, that you expect me to reveal the secret I possess; however, that cannot happen. If you hear my story through to its end, you will understand why I must be so guarded. I refuse to lead you, as reckless and passionate as I once was, toward your own certain destruction and misery. Let my experience, if not my advice, teach you how perilous the pursuit of knowledge can be, and how much more content is the man who considers his home to be the whole world than he who strives to become more than his nature permits.
source_text: I see by your eagerness and the wonder and hope which your eyes express, my friend, that you expect to be informed of the secret with which I am acquainted; that cannot be; listen patiently until the end of my story, and you will easily perceive why I am reserved upon that subject. I will not lead you on, unguarded and ardent as I then was, to your destruction and infallible misery. Learn from me, if not by my precepts, at least by my example, how dangerous is the acquirement of knowledge and how much happier that man is who believes his native town to be the world, than he who aspires to become greater than his nature will allow.

text: Such convictions sustained my will as I followed my ambition with tireless fervor. My face had turned ashen from constant study, and my frame grew gaunt from my self-imposed imprisonment. At times, when I stood on the very edge of discovery, I met with failure; yet I refused to let go of the hope that the next hour or the next day might bring success. I alone held the secret to which I had consecrated my life; the moon bore witness to my midnight industry as I tracked nature into her most secluded retreats with a breathless, unyielding intensity. No one can imagine the grim nature of my hidden labors as I scavenged through the unholy mists of the graveyard or experimented on living things to spark life into cold clay. My body shudders now and my vision falters at the memory, but then, a wild and unstoppable compulsion drove me; I seemed to have lost all sense of self, existing only for this one goal. It was a temporary delirium that left me feeling the weight of my actions even more sharply once the unnatural fever broke and I returned to my normal life. I gathered bones from charnel houses and, with sacrilegious hands, pried into the awesome mysteries of the human form. In a lonely attic chamber—a cell, in truth—cut off from the rest of the house by a gallery and a flight of stairs, I kept my workshop of foul invention; my eyes bulged with the strain of attending to every minute detail of my work. The anatomy theater and the slaughterhouse provided my materials; and though my soul often recoiled in horror at my tasks, I was propelled by a growing desperation as I brought my project toward its completion.
source_text: These thoughts supported my spirits, while I pursued my undertaking with unremitting ardour. My cheek had grown pale with study, and my person had become emaciated with confinement. Sometimes, on the very brink of certainty, I failed; yet still I clung to the hope which the next day or the next hour might realise. One secret which I alone possessed was the hope to which I had dedicated myself; and the moon gazed on my midnight labours, while, with unrelaxed and breathless eagerness, I pursued nature to her hiding-places. Who shall conceive the horrors of my secret toil as I dabbled among the unhallowed damps of the grave or tortured the living animal to animate the lifeless clay? My limbs now tremble, and my eyes swim with the remembrance; but then a resistless and almost frantic impulse urged me forward; I seemed to have lost all soul or sensation but for this one pursuit. It was indeed but a passing trance, that only made me feel with renewed acuteness so soon as, the unnatural stimulus ceasing to operate, I had returned to my old habits. I collected bones from charnel-houses and disturbed, with profane fingers, the tremendous secrets of the human frame. In a solitary chamber, or rather cell, at the top of the house, and separated from all the other apartments by a gallery and staircase, I kept my workshop of filthy creation; my eyeballs were starting from their sockets in attending to the details of my employment. The dissecting room and the slaughter-house furnished many of my materials; and often did my human nature turn with loathing from my occupation, whilst, still urged on by an eagerness which perpetually increased, I brought my work near to a conclusion.

text: The solstice came and went as I remained shackled, mind and spirit, to my solitary labor. It was a season of unparalleled radiance; the earth had rarely granted a more generous yield, nor the hillsides a more exuberant flowering, yet my senses were closed to the graces of the natural world. This same preoccupation that blinded me to the horizon also severed my ties to those distant kin whom I had not embraced in so many years. I recognized that my stillness caused them grief, for I recalled vividly my father’s warning: “I am convinced that while your heart is at peace, you will hold us in your thoughts and write with constancy. You must forgive me if I view any silence on your part as evidence that your more vital responsibilities have fallen into disarray.”
source_text: The summer months passed while I was thus engaged, heart and soul, in one pursuit. It was a most beautiful season; never did the fields bestow a more plentiful harvest or the vines yield a more luxuriant vintage, but my eyes were insensible to the charms of nature. And the same feelings which made me neglect the scenes around me caused me also to forget those friends who were so many miles absent, and whom I had not seen for so long a time. I knew my silence disquieted them, and I well remembered the words of my father: “I know that while you are pleased with yourself you will think of us with affection, and we shall hear regularly from you. You must pardon me if I regard any interruption in your correspondence as a proof that your other duties are equally neglected.”

text: At the time, I believed my father would be wrong to label my silence as a moral failing, yet I am now certain he was correct to find me culpable. A person at their best should always maintain a serene and steady spirit, never permitting a passing obsession or feverish ambition to shatter their inner peace. I am convinced that the quest for discovery is no exception to this law. If the work you undertake serves to dull your sympathies or strip away your appreciation for life’s most innocent delights, then that work is surely corrupt—or, at the very least, unsuited to the human soul. Had this principle been strictly followed, and had no man allowed his personal ambitions to disrupt the peace of his home, Greece might never have been enslaved, Caesar would have spared his republic, the Americas would have been discovered more slowly, and the empires of Mexico and Peru would never have been demolished.
source_text: I then thought that my father would be unjust if he ascribed my neglect to vice or faultiness on my part, but I am now convinced that he was justified in conceiving that I should not be altogether free from blame. A human being in perfection ought always to preserve a calm and peaceful mind and never to allow passion or a transitory desire to disturb his tranquillity. I do not think that the pursuit of knowledge is an exception to this rule. If the study to which you apply yourself has a tendency to weaken your affections and to destroy your taste for those simple pleasures in which no alloy can possibly mix, then that study is certainly unlawful, that is to say, not befitting the human mind. If this rule were always observed; if no man allowed any pursuit whatsoever to interfere with the tranquillity of his domestic affections, Greece had not been enslaved, Cæsar would have spared his country, America would have been discovered more gradually, and the empires of Mexico and Peru had not been destroyed.

text: My dear Clerval," I cried, "you have been so incredibly good to me. You spent this entire winter cooped up in my sickroom instead of focusing on your studies as you had planned. I truly don't know how I will ever make it up to you. I feel such deep regret for being the cause of your disappointment, but I know you will forgive me.
source_text: “Dearest Clerval,” exclaimed I, “how kind, how very good you are to me. This whole winter, instead of being spent in study, as you promised yourself, has been consumed in my sick room. How shall I ever repay you? I feel the greatest remorse for the disappointment of which I have been the occasion, but you will forgive me.”

text: Your health has been so desperately fragile, and though Arthur’s frequent dispatches are gracious, they are insufficient to soothe my anxieties on your account. You have been forbidden from the inkwell; yet a single word from you, dearest Julian, is required to settle our nerves. For weeks I expected every post to deliver that one line, and only my persistent counsel kept my father from setting out for Heidelberg. I spared him the rigors of such a journey, yet how often have I grieved my own inability to fly to your side! I am haunted by the image of your recovery being left to some indifferent, hired attendant, who could never intuit your needs or tend you with the devotion of your cousin. But that is past: Arthur writes that you are truly mending. I pray you will soon verify these reports with a letter in your own hand.
source_text: “You have been ill, very ill, and even the constant letters of dear kind Henry are not sufficient to reassure me on your account. You are forbidden to write—to hold a pen; yet one word from you, dear Victor, is necessary to calm our apprehensions. For a long time I have thought that each post would bring this line, and my persuasions have restrained my uncle from undertaking a journey to Ingolstadt. I have prevented his encountering the inconveniences and perhaps dangers of so long a journey, yet how often have I regretted not being able to perform it myself! I figure to myself that the task of attending on your sickbed has devolved on some mercenary old nurse, who could never guess your wishes nor minister to them with the care and affection of your poor cousin. Yet that is over now: Clerval writes that indeed you are getting better. I eagerly hope that you will confirm this intelligence soon in your own handwriting.

text: You surely recall Elara, who was always a particular favorite of yours; I remember your saying that if ever you fell into a melancholy, a single glimpse of her would suffice to dispel it. She possessed that quality of candid cheerfulness which the poets often ascribe to their most radiant muses. My mother became so deeply fond of her that she saw fit to provide the girl with an education far more sophisticated than was first planned. This generosity was met with the quietest, most sincere devotion; Elara never resorted to empty protestations of thanks, yet one could read a world of adoration in her eyes. Though her spirit was naturally buoyant and sometimes heedless, she remained keenly observant of my mother’s every nuance. Viewing her as the absolute standard of grace, she strove to adopt her very way of speaking and carrying herself, so that even now, Elara’s movements frequently bring my mother back to my mind.
source_text: “Justine, you may remember, was a great favourite of yours; and I recollect you once remarked that if you were in an ill humour, one glance from Justine could dissipate it, for the same reason that Ariosto gives concerning the beauty of Angelica—she looked so frank-hearted and happy. My aunt conceived a great attachment for her, by which she was induced to give her an education superior to that which she had at first intended. This benefit was fully repaid; Justine was the most grateful little creature in the world: I do not mean that she made any professions I never heard one pass her lips, but you could see by her eyes that she almost adored her protectress. Although her disposition was gay and in many respects inconsiderate, yet she paid the greatest attention to every gesture of my aunt. She thought her the model of all excellence and endeavoured to imitate her phraseology and manners, so that even now she often reminds me of her.

text: “My beloved Elizabeth!” I cried out once I had finished her letter. “I must reply immediately to quiet the fears they surely feel.” I managed to write, though the effort left me profoundly exhausted; yet my recovery had truly begun and continued without setback. Within another two weeks, I was finally strong enough to venture beyond my room.
source_text: “Dear, dear Elizabeth!” I exclaimed, when I had read her letter: “I will write instantly and relieve them from the anxiety they must feel.” I wrote, and this exertion greatly fatigued me; but my convalescence had commenced, and proceeded regularly. In another fortnight I was able to leave my chamber.

text: The warmer months dissolved amidst these pursuits, and I had intended to depart for my ancestral home as the leaves turned gold; however, unforeseen complications intervened, and before I could set out, the frost descended with such fury that the mountain passes were rendered impassable. Thus, my homecoming was deferred until the thaw of the following year. This postponement weighed heavily upon my spirit, for my heart yearned for the familiar streets of my youth and the society of those I held dear. I had tarried primarily from a sense of duty toward Adrian, being loath to abandon him to a city of strangers before he had established any local ties. Nevertheless, the winter months were endured with a certain lightness of heart; and though the vernal season was exceptionally sluggish in its approach, its eventual magnificence more than atoned for its long hesitation.
source_text: Summer passed away in these occupations, and my return to Geneva was fixed for the latter end of autumn; but being delayed by several accidents, winter and snow arrived, the roads were deemed impassable, and my journey was retarded until the ensuing spring. I felt this delay very bitterly; for I longed to see my native town and my beloved friends. My return had only been delayed so long, from an unwillingness to leave Clerval in a strange place, before he had become acquainted with any of its inhabitants. The winter, however, was spent cheerfully; and although the spring was uncommonly late, when it came its beauty compensated for its dilatoriness.

text: Two weeks passed in these wanderings. My health and spirits, long since recovered, drew even greater vitality from the fresh air, the simple events of our journey, and the steady companionship of my friend. My former studies had isolated me from humanity and made me a recluse, but Clerval revived my better nature; he taught me once again to find joy in the world and the bright faces of children. My wonderful friend! How truly you loved me, and how you labored to lift my mind to the level of your own. A selfish obsession had withered and narrowed my soul until your affection warmed my senses, returning me to the happy state of my youth—a time when I was loved by all and knew no grief. In my happiness, the natural world granted me the most exquisite sensations; a clear sky and green fields filled me with bliss. The season was truly magnificent, with spring flowers still in the hedges and summer buds beginning to swell. I was finally free from the thoughts that had burdened me so relentlessly the year before—an oppressive weight that, until now, I had been unable to cast aside.
source_text: We passed a fortnight in these perambulations: my health and spirits had long been restored, and they gained additional strength from the salubrious air I breathed, the natural incidents of our progress, and the conversation of my friend. Study had before secluded me from the intercourse of my fellow-creatures, and rendered me unsocial; but Clerval called forth the better feelings of my heart; he again taught me to love the aspect of nature, and the cheerful faces of children. Excellent friend! how sincerely you did love me, and endeavour to elevate my mind until it was on a level with your own. A selfish pursuit had cramped and narrowed me, until your gentleness and affection warmed and opened my senses; I became the same happy creature who, a few years ago, loved and beloved by all, had no sorrow or care. When happy, inanimate nature had the power of bestowing on me the most delightful sensations. A serene sky and verdant fields filled me with ecstasy. The present season was indeed divine; the flowers of spring bloomed in the hedges, while those of summer were already in bud. I was undisturbed by thoughts which during the preceding year had pressed upon me, notwithstanding my endeavours to throw them off, with an invincible burden.

text: Julian delighted in my lightheartedness and mirrored my every emotion with genuine warmth; he labored constantly to divert me, even as he voiced the grand sensations that stirred his own soul. The vastness of his intellect in those hours was nothing short of marvelous; his speech sparkled with invention, and frequently, in the manner of Eastern storytellers, he composed fables of extraordinary passion and magic. At other times, he recited the verses I most cherished or enticed me into spirited debates, which he defended with remarkable brilliance.
source_text: Henry rejoiced in my gaiety, and sincerely sympathised in my feelings: he exerted himself to amuse me, while he expressed the sensations that filled his soul. The resources of his mind on this occasion were truly astonishing: his conversation was full of imagination; and very often, in imitation of the Persian and Arabic writers, he invented tales of wonderful fancy and passion. At other times he repeated my favourite poems, or drew me out into arguments, which he supported with great ingenuity.

text: This past Thursday, May 7th, your two brothers, my niece, and I went for a stroll through Plainpalais. The evening was so mild and tranquil that we extended our walk much further than we usually do. It was already growing dark before we thought of returning, at which point we realized that William and Ernest, who had been running ahead, were nowhere to be found. We sat down on a bench to wait for them to reappear, but soon Ernest arrived alone, asking if we had seen his brother. He explained that they had been playing together when William ran off to hide; Ernest had searched for him in vain and waited a long while, but the boy never came back.
source_text: “Last Thursday (May 7th), I, my niece, and your two brothers, went to walk in Plainpalais. The evening was warm and serene, and we prolonged our walk farther than usual. It was already dusk before we thought of returning; and then we discovered that William and Ernest, who had gone on before, were not to be found. We accordingly rested on a seat until they should return. Presently Ernest came, and enquired if we had seen his brother; he said, that he had been playing with him, that William had run away to hide himself, and that he vainly sought for him, and afterwards waited for a long time, but that he did not return.

text: “Come, Victor. Don't dwell on thoughts of revenge against the murderer; instead, bring a sense of peace and kindness that will heal our minds rather than letting the pain fester. Enter this home of grief, my friend, with love for those who care for you, rather than hatred for your enemies.”
source_text: “Come, Victor; not brooding thoughts of vengeance against the assassin, but with feelings of peace and gentleness, that will heal, instead of festering, the wounds of our minds. Enter the house of mourning, my friend, but with kindness and affection for those who love you, and not with hatred for your enemies.

text: The trek homeward was a somber affair. At the outset, I had been consumed by a feverish desire to reach my destination, yearning to clasp my grieving companions and share in their heavy burden. But as the familiar spires of my childhood home rose to meet me, my haste evaporated. I was nearly crushed by the sheer volume of sentiment and memory that flooded my soul. I wandered through vistas of my youth that had been lost to me for six long years, wondering what ravages time might have wrought in my absence. One catastrophic event had already shattered my peace, yet I dreaded the myriad of smaller, creeping changes that might have transformed my world just as irrevocably. A cold apprehension froze my steps; I hesitated to move forward, haunted by a legion of formless terrors that made me quake, even as their exact shapes eluded my understanding.
source_text: My journey was very melancholy. At first I wished to hurry on, for I longed to console and sympathise with my loved and sorrowing friends; but when I drew near my native town, I slackened my progress. I could hardly sustain the multitude of feelings that crowded into my mind. I passed through scenes familiar to my youth, but which I had not seen for nearly six years. How altered every thing might be during that time! One sudden and desolating change had taken place; but a thousand little circumstances might have by degrees worked other alterations, which, although they were done more tranquilly, might not be the less decisive. Fear overcame me; I dared no advance, dreading a thousand nameless evils that made me tremble, although I was unable to define them.

text: I suspect, my dear companion, that I grow wearisome by lingering upon these opening scenes; yet they were days of relative peace, and I recall them with joy. My homeland, my adored home! Who but a native can know the rapture of beholding again your rivers, your peaks, and, above all, your exquisite lake!
source_text: I fear, my friend, that I shall render myself tedious by dwelling on these preliminary circumstances; but they were days of comparative happiness, and I think of them with pleasure. My country, my beloved country! who but a native can tell the delight I took in again beholding thy streams, thy mountains, and, more than all, thy lovely lake!

text: I hurried forward, captivated by the storm’s magnificent yet frightening power. This grand conflict in the sky lifted my spirits; I pressed my hands together and cried out, “William, my sweet angel! This is your funeral, this your mourning song!” Just as I spoke, I noticed a shape emerging from a nearby thicket; I froze, staring through the darkness, certain of what I saw. A sudden bolt of lightning cut through the gloom, revealing the figure with startling clarity. Its massive frame and repulsive features—far more grotesque than any human’s—told me at once that this was the miserable creature, the vile demon I had brought into existence. What was he doing here? Could he be—the very thought made me tremble—my brother’s killer? The moment the suspicion entered my mind, I was convinced of its truth; my teeth chattered, and I had to steady myself against a tree to keep from falling. The figure rushed past, vanishing into the shadows. No mortal man could have harmed that innocent child. *He* was the murderer; I had no doubt. The sheer weight of the realization was undeniable proof. I considered chasing the devil, but it would have been useless; another flash of lightning showed him clinging to the sheer cliffs of Mont Salêve, the mountain bordering the south of Plainpalais. He quickly reached the summit and disappeared.
source_text: While I watched the tempest, so beautiful yet terrific, I wandered on with a hasty step. This noble war in the sky elevated my spirits; I clasped my hands, and exclaimed aloud, “William, dear angel! this is thy funeral, this thy dirge!” As I said these words, I perceived in the gloom a figure which stole from behind a clump of trees near me; I stood fixed, gazing intently: I could not be mistaken. A flash of lightning illuminated the object, and discovered its shape plainly to me; its gigantic stature, and the deformity of its aspect more hideous than belongs to humanity, instantly informed me that it was the wretch, the filthy dæmon, to whom I had given life. What did he there? Could he be (I shuddered at the conception) the murderer of my brother? No sooner did that idea cross my imagination, than I became convinced of its truth; my teeth chattered, and I was forced to lean against a tree for support. The figure passed me quickly, and I lost it in the gloom. Nothing in human shape could have destroyed the fair child. _He_ was the murderer! I could not doubt it. The mere presence of the idea was an irresistible proof of the fact. I thought of pursuing the devil; but it would have been in vain, for another flash discovered him to me hanging among the rocks of the nearly perpendicular ascent of Mont Salêve, a hill that bounds Plainpalais on the south. He soon reached the summit, and disappeared.

text: Morning arrived, and I set out for the town. Finding the gates unbarred, I hurried toward my father’s house. My initial instinct was to disclose everything I knew about the killer and call for an immediate manhunt. However, I faltered when I considered the narrative I would have to share. A creature I had constructed and animated myself had encountered me at midnight among the jagged peaks of an unreachable mountain. I also thought of the nervous breakdown I had endured at the time of its birth, which would make an already far-fetched story sound like a lingering hallucination. I knew perfectly well that if anyone else had told me such a tale, I would have dismissed it as pure lunacy. Moreover, the creature’s bizarre capabilities would make any pursuit futile, even if I could convince my family to begin one. What point would there be in a hunt? No one could possibly apprehend a monster capable of scaling the precipitous cliffs of Mont Salêve. These considerations decided my course, and I chose to hold my tongue.
source_text: Day dawned; and I directed my steps towards the town. The gates were open, and I hastened to my father’s house. My first thought was to discover what I knew of the murderer, and cause instant pursuit to be made. But I paused when I reflected on the story that I had to tell. A being whom I myself had formed, and endued with life, had met me at midnight among the precipices of an inaccessible mountain. I remembered also the nervous fever with which I had been seized just at the time that I dated my creation, and which would give an air of delirium to a tale otherwise so utterly improbable. I well knew that if any other had communicated such a relation to me, I should have looked upon it as the ravings of insanity. Besides, the strange nature of the animal would elude all pursuit, even if I were so far credited as to persuade my relatives to commence it. And then of what use would be pursuit? Who could arrest a creature capable of scaling the overhanging sides of Mont Salêve? These reflections determined me, and I resolved to remain silent.

text: Before long, Clara entered the room. The passage of years had transformed her, bestowing a radiance that far eclipsed the simple prettiness of her girlhood. Though she retained her former sincerity and spirit, these traits were now elevated by a countenance reflecting deep thought and a tender soul. She embraced me with heartfelt tenderness. “Julian,” she cried, “your presence restores my courage. Perhaps you can discover the means to clear our innocent Martha. For if a soul so pure is judged a felon, who among us is truly safe? I trust in her virtue as implicitly as I trust my own. This tragedy strikes us twice over; we have lost our precious little Arthur, and now this girl, for whom I feel a sister’s love, is being dragged toward a far more wretched end. If she falls, I shall never taste happiness again. But it cannot be; she must be spared, and then I may yet find solace, even in the wake of our dear boy’s death.”
source_text: We were soon joined by Elizabeth. Time had altered her since I last beheld her; it had endowed her with loveliness surpassing the beauty of her childish years. There was the same candour, the same vivacity, but it was allied to an expression more full of sensibility and intellect. She welcomed me with the greatest affection. “Your arrival, my dear cousin,” said she, “fills me with hope. You perhaps will find some means to justify my poor guiltless Justine. Alas! who is safe, if she be convicted of crime? I rely on her innocence as certainly as I do upon my own. Our misfortune is doubly hard to us; we have not only lost that lovely darling boy, but this poor girl, whom I sincerely love, is to be torn away by even a worse fate. If she is condemned, I never shall know joy more. But she will not, I am sure she will not; and then I shall be happy again, even after the sad death of my little William.”

text: Justine’s demeanor was one of quiet composure. Clad in the black of mourning, her features—always pleasant—attained a striking beauty from the gravity of her situation. She appeared steady in her sense of innocence and did not falter, even as thousands of onlookers watched and reviled her; any sympathy her grace might have otherwise inspired was extinguished by the crowd's horror at the crime she was accused of committing. While she remained calm, her stillness was clearly forced; aware that her previous agitation had been interpreted as a sign of guilt, she had steeled herself to project an image of fortitude. As she entered the dock, her gaze searched the courtroom and soon found where we were seated. A brief tear seemed to cloud her eyes when she saw us, but she quickly regained her self-control, and a look of grieving tenderness appeared to confirm her absolute blamelessness.
source_text: The appearance of Justine was calm. She was dressed in mourning, and her countenance, always engaging, was rendered, by the solemnity of her feelings, exquisitely beautiful. Yet she appeared confident in innocence and did not tremble, although gazed on and execrated by thousands, for all the kindness which her beauty might otherwise have excited was obliterated in the minds of the spectators by the imagination of the enormity she was supposed to have committed. She was tranquil, yet her tranquillity was evidently constrained; and as her confusion had before been adduced as a proof of her guilt, she worked up her mind to an appearance of courage. When she entered the court she threw her eyes round it and quickly discovered where we were seated. A tear seemed to dim her eye when she saw us, but she quickly recovered herself, and a look of sorrowful affection seemed to attest her utter guiltlessness.

text: A wave of approval followed Elizabeth’s earnest and moving testimony, yet it was sparked by her selfless intervention rather than any sympathy for Justine. On the contrary, the public’s fury returned with even greater force, and they condemned the poor girl for what they saw as the ultimate betrayal. Justine wept as she listened to Elizabeth, but she offered no defense. My own nerves were frayed and my spirit was crushed throughout the trial. I was convinced of her innocence; I knew the truth. Was it possible that the creature who had—of this I had no doubt—killed my brother was now playing a cruel game, delivering an innocent woman to a shameful death? I could no longer endure the horror of the scene. Seeing that the crowd’s hostility and the judges’ cold stares had already doomed my helpless victim, I bolted from the courtroom in a fit of agony. The defendant’s suffering was nothing compared to mine; she was comforted by her own purity, but the teeth of regret sank deep into my soul and would not release their grip.
source_text: A murmur of approbation followed Elizabeth’s simple and powerful appeal, but it was excited by her generous interference, and not in favour of poor Justine, on whom the public indignation was turned with renewed violence, charging her with the blackest ingratitude. She herself wept as Elizabeth spoke, but she did not answer. My own agitation and anguish was extreme during the whole trial. I believed in her innocence; I knew it. Could the dæmon who had (I did not for a minute doubt) murdered my brother also in his hellish sport have betrayed the innocent to death and ignominy? I could not sustain the horror of my situation, and when I perceived that the popular voice and the countenances of the judges had already condemned my unhappy victim, I rushed out of the court in agony. The tortures of the accused did not equal mine; she was sustained by innocence, but the fangs of remorse tore my bosom and would not forgo their hold.

text: This was a devastating shock for Elizabeth, who had been so certain of Justine’s innocence. “I am heartbroken,” she lamented. “How can I ever trust in human virtue again? Justine, whom I loved and respected as a sister—how could she wear such a mask of purity only to deceive us? Her gentle eyes seemed completely free of malice or cunning, and yet she has committed a murder.”
source_text: This was a dire blow to poor Elizabeth, who had relied with firmness upon Justine’s innocence. “Alas!” said she. “How shall I ever again believe in human goodness? Justine, whom I loved and esteemed as my sister, how could she put on those smiles of innocence only to betray? Her mild eyes seemed incapable of any severity or guile, and yet she has committed a murder.”

text: Not long after, word reached us that the tragic girl had requested an audience with my cousin. My father was hesitant to let her go, yet he ultimately left the choice to her own sense of duty and heart. "I will go," Elizabeth resolved, "even if she is indeed guilty. But Victor, you must come with me; I cannot endure the visit alone." The very thought of this meeting was an agony to me, yet I found it impossible to refuse her.
source_text: Soon after we heard that the poor victim had expressed a desire to see my cousin. My father wished her not to go but said that he left it to her own judgment and feelings to decide. “Yes,” said Elizabeth, “I will go, although she is guilty; and you, Victor, shall accompany me; I cannot go alone.” The idea of this visit was torture to me, yet I could not refuse.

text: I admitted to the deed, but my admission was a falsehood. I confessed only to seek spiritual peace, yet that lie now burdens my heart more than any real sin I have committed. May the Lord have mercy on me! Ever since my conviction, the priest has hounded me; he threatened and intimidated me until I nearly believed I was the demon he described. He promised me excommunication and eternal fire if I remained stubborn in my denial. My lady, I was utterly alone; everyone looked upon me as a wretch destined for shame and damnation. What choice did I have? In a moment of weakness, I put my name to a lie, and only now do I feel the full weight of my misery.
source_text: “I did confess, but I confessed a lie. I confessed, that I might obtain absolution; but now that falsehood lies heavier at my heart than all my other sins. The God of heaven forgive me! Ever since I was condemned, my confessor has besieged me; he threatened and menaced, until I almost began to think that I was the monster that he said I was. He threatened excommunication and hell fire in my last moments if I continued obdurate. Dear lady, I had none to support me; all looked on me as a wretch doomed to ignominy and perdition. What could I do? In an evil hour I subscribed to a lie; and now only am I truly miserable.”

text: Justine shook her head in sorrow. “I no longer fear the end,” she said; “that particular agony has passed. God has bolstered my frailty, granting me the fortitude to face this ultimate trial. I depart from a world of grief and bitterness; if you will only keep me in your heart as one wrongly convicted, I can accept the destiny before me. Let my example show you, dear lady, how to yield with patience to the divine will.”
source_text: Justine shook her head mournfully. “I do not fear to die,” she said; “that pang is past. God raises my weakness and gives me courage to endure the worst. I leave a sad and bitter world; and if you remember me and think of me as of one unjustly condemned, I am resigned to the fate awaiting me. Learn from me, dear lady, to submit in patience to the will of heaven!”

text: In this manner, the unfortunate victim attempted to console both her companions and herself. She did indeed find the quiet acceptance she had sought. Yet I, the actual perpetrator, felt the undying worm of remorse alive within my breast, a sensation that permitted no hope or relief. Elizabeth also grieved and was heavy-hearted, but hers was the sorrow of the pure; it was like a cloud drifting across a luminous moon, momentarily veiling its brilliance without ever staining it. Agony and hopelessness had struck the very center of my heart; I carried an inner hell that nothing could ever douse. We remained with Justine for several hours, and it was only with the utmost struggle that Elizabeth could bring herself to leave. “I wish,” she cried out, “that I might perish with you, for I cannot bear to live in a world so full of wretchedness.”
source_text: Thus the poor sufferer tried to comfort others and herself. She indeed gained the resignation she desired. But I, the true murderer, felt the never-dying worm alive in my bosom, which allowed of no hope or consolation. Elizabeth also wept and was unhappy, but hers also was the misery of innocence, which, like a cloud that passes over the fair moon, for a while hides but cannot tarnish its brightness. Anguish and despair had penetrated into the core of my heart; I bore a hell within me which nothing could extinguish. We stayed several hours with Justine, and it was with great difficulty that Elizabeth could tear herself away. “I wish,” cried she, “that I were to die with you; I cannot live in this world of misery.”

text:
It was during this season that we sought refuge at our villa in Clairmont. This transition brought a profound relief to my spirit; the rigid locking of the town gates at the stroke of ten and the prohibition against lingering upon the waves past twilight had made our urban dwelling feel like a stifling cage. Now, liberty was mine. Frequently, once my kin had surrendered to sleep, I would unmoor the skiff and spend the dark watches upon the great expanse of the water. On certain nights, I let the canvas catch the breeze to carry me where it would; on others, having pulled the oars until I reached the center of the lake, I allowed the vessel to drift aimlessly while I surrendered to the agony of my own dark reflections. 

Amidst the profound stillness of the world, I felt myself the sole discordant note in an otherwise celestial landscape—save perhaps for the erratic flight of a bat or the coarse, rhythmic pulse of the marsh-dwellers that fell silent as I neared the bank. In those moments, I was frequently seized by the urge to cast myself into the obsidian depths, seeking a final shroud beneath the waves to bury my griefs and my crimes eternally. Yet, the memory of the noble and enduring Isabella stayed my hand; I loved her with a desperate devotion, and her very life was intertwined with my own. I considered, too, my father and my remaining brother. Could I, through a coward's exit, abandon them to the cruel whims of the demon I had unleashed upon their world?
source_text: About this time we retired to our house at Belrive. This change was particularly agreeable to me. The shutting of the gates regularly at ten o’clock and the impossibility of remaining on the lake after that hour had rendered our residence within the walls of Geneva very irksome to me. I was now free. Often, after the rest of the family had retired for the night, I took the boat and passed many hours upon the water. Sometimes, with my sails set, I was carried by the wind; and sometimes, after rowing into the middle of the lake, I left the boat to pursue its own course and gave way to my own miserable reflections. I was often tempted, when all was at peace around me, and I the only unquiet thing that wandered restless in a scene so beautiful and heavenly—if I except some bat, or the frogs, whose harsh and interrupted croaking was heard only when I approached the shore—often, I say, I was tempted to plunge into the silent lake, that the waters might close over me and my calamities for ever. But I was restrained, when I thought of the heroic and suffering Elizabeth, whom I tenderly loved, and whose existence was bound up in mine. I thought also of my father and surviving brother; should I by my base desertion leave them exposed and unprotected to the malice of the fiend whom I had let loose among them?

text: “As I contemplate the wretched end of poor Elara, Julian, the world no longer wears the fair mask it once displayed to my eyes. In my youth, the chronicles of cruelty and malice I encountered in literature seemed like fables of a bygone age, ghosts of history that could never touch the present. They were abstractions for the intellect, not burdens for the heart. But now, sorrow has crossed our threshold, and humanity reveals itself as a pack of wolves, each hungry for a brother's life. Perhaps I am too severe. The world stood united in its condemnation of her; and truly, had she been capable of such an atrocity, she would have been a demon in human form. To slay the child of her protector—a boy she had cradled from infancy and cherished as her own—simply for a handful of trinkets! While I abhor the taking of any life, I would agree that such a soul would be a blight upon the earth. But she was guiltless. My spirit knows it, my heart feels it; and seeing that you share this conviction, I am bolstered in my faith. Oh, Julian! When the face of a lie so perfectly mimics the countenance of truth, what security remains for our joy? I feel as though I tread the crumbling lip of a chasm, while a faceless multitude presses behind, eager to hurl me into the dark. Little Arthur and Elara are gone, victims of a shadow, while the true assassin wanders the earth unpunished—perhaps even honored. Yet, were I the one bound for the gallows in her stead, I would not trade my conscience for the freedom of that monster.”
source_text: “When I reflect, my dear cousin,” said she, “on the miserable death of Justine Moritz, I no longer see the world and its works as they before appeared to me. Before, I looked upon the accounts of vice and injustice that I read in books or heard from others as tales of ancient days or imaginary evils; at least they were remote and more familiar to reason than to the imagination; but now misery has come home, and men appear to me as monsters thirsting for each other’s blood. Yet I am certainly unjust. Everybody believed that poor girl to be guilty; and if she could have committed the crime for which she suffered, assuredly she would have been the most depraved of human creatures. For the sake of a few jewels, to have murdered the son of her benefactor and friend, a child whom she had nursed from its birth, and appeared to love as if it had been her own! I could not consent to the death of any human being, but certainly I should have thought such a creature unfit to remain in the society of men. But she was innocent. I know, I feel she was innocent; you are of the same opinion, and that confirms me. Alas! Victor, when falsehood can look so like the truth, who can assure themselves of certain happiness? I feel as if I were walking on the edge of a precipice, towards which thousands are crowding and endeavouring to plunge me into the abyss. William and Justine were assassinated, and the murderer escapes; he walks about the world free, and perhaps respected. But even if I were condemned to suffer on the scaffold for the same crimes, I would not change places with such a wretch.”

text: I listened to her words in a state of absolute torment. Though I had not committed the act myself, I was, for all intents and purposes, the actual killer. Elizabeth saw the misery written across my face and, gently taking my hand, said, “My dearest friend, you must try to compose yourself. God knows how deeply these tragedies have affected me, yet I am not as broken as you appear to be. There is a look of hopelessness, and occasionally a flash of vengeance, in your expression that truly frightens me. Dear Victor, you must let go of these dark impulses. Remember the friends who are still with you, who have placed all their hopes in your well-being. Have we lost the ability to bring you any joy? As long as we love one another and remain devoted, here in this beautiful and serene land of your birth, we can still find every quiet blessing. What is left to shatter our peace?”
source_text: I listened to this discourse with the extremest agony. I, not in deed, but in effect, was the true murderer. Elizabeth read my anguish in my countenance, and kindly taking my hand, said, “My dearest friend, you must calm yourself. These events have affected me, God knows how deeply; but I am not so wretched as you are. There is an expression of despair, and sometimes of revenge, in your countenance that makes me tremble. Dear Victor, banish these dark passions. Remember the friends around you, who centre all their hopes in you. Have we lost the power of rendering you happy? Ah! While we love, while we are true to each other, here in this land of peace and beauty, your native country, we may reap every tranquil blessing—what can disturb our peace?”

text: The first leg of my journey was spent on horseback, though I eventually swapped my mount for a mule, as it was steadier and less likely to be injured on such treacherous terrain. It was mid-August and the weather was fair, though it had been two months since Justine’s death—that wretched moment I considered the origin of all my grief. My heavy heart felt noticeably unburdened as I moved deeper into the Arve ravine. The towering peaks and sheer cliffs that loomed above, the roar of the river crashing against the stones, and the spray of the surrounding falls all spoke of a force as vast as the divine. In the presence of these elements, displayed in their most formidable glory, I no longer felt the need to fear or bow before any power less than the Creator. As I climbed higher, the valley became even more breathtaking. Ancient ruins clinging to pine-covered heights, the rushing river, and small cottages glimpsed through the trees formed a vista of remarkable beauty. But the scene was truly elevated to the sublime by the great Alps; their gleaming white peaks and domes rose above everything else, appearing like the landscape of a different world or the dwelling place of a celestial race.
source_text: I performed the first part of my journey on horseback. I afterwards hired a mule, as the more sure-footed and least liable to receive injury on these rugged roads. The weather was fine; it was about the middle of the month of August, nearly two months after the death of Justine, that miserable epoch from which I dated all my woe. The weight upon my spirit was sensibly lightened as I plunged yet deeper in the ravine of Arve. The immense mountains and precipices that overhung me on every side, the sound of the river raging among the rocks, and the dashing of the waterfalls around spoke of a power mighty as Omnipotence—and I ceased to fear or to bend before any being less almighty than that which had created and ruled the elements, here displayed in their most terrific guise. Still, as I ascended higher, the valley assumed a more magnificent and astonishing character. Ruined castles hanging on the precipices of piny mountains, the impetuous Arve, and cottages every here and there peeping forth from among the trees formed a scene of singular beauty. But it was augmented and rendered sublime by the mighty Alps, whose white and shining pyramids and domes towered above all, as belonging to another earth, the habitations of another race of beings.

text: As I traveled, I frequently felt a forgotten spark of joy resurface within me. A sudden curve in the path or the sight of a familiar landmark would evoke memories of the past, connecting me to the lighthearted spirit of my childhood. Even the wind seemed to whisper soft, consoling words, as if Nature were a mother urging me to stop my weeping. Yet, this gentle reprieve never lasted; I would inevitably find myself shackled once more to my sorrow, lost in the agony of my own mind. In those moments, I would press my horse onward, desperate to escape the world, my anxieties, and above all, my own soul. Or, in fits of deeper anguish, I would dismount and collapse onto the grass, utterly crushed by horror and hopelessness.
source_text: A tingling long-lost sense of pleasure often came across me during this journey. Some turn in the road, some new object suddenly perceived and recognised, reminded me of days gone by, and were associated with the lighthearted gaiety of boyhood. The very winds whispered in soothing accents, and maternal Nature bade me weep no more. Then again the kindly influence ceased to act—I found myself fettered again to grief and indulging in all the misery of reflection. Then I spurred on my animal, striving so to forget the world, my fears, and more than all, myself—or, in a more desperate fashion, I alighted and threw myself on the grass, weighed down by horror and despair.

text: We slumber; a phantom vision has the might to blight our rest. We wake; a solitary, drifting whim corrupts the light. We sense, imagine, or reflect; we exult or mourn, clutching a dear grief or flinging aside our burdens. It is all one: for, let it be bliss or agony, the gate of its exit remains unbarred. Our past shall never find its twin in the dawn; nothing abides but the law of change.
source_text: We rest; a dream has power to poison sleep. We rise; one wand’ring thought pollutes the day. We feel, conceive, or reason; laugh or weep, Embrace fond woe, or cast our cares away; It is the same: for, be it joy or sorrow, The path of its departure still is free. Man’s yesterday may ne’er be like his morrow; Nought may endure but mutability!

text: Just as I spoke, I suddenly glimpsed a figure in the distance, rushing toward me with a speed that defied nature. He leaped effortlessly over the deep cracks in the ice where I had trodden so tentatively; as he neared, his towering height seemed to far surpass that of any mortal. I felt a surge of unease; my vision clouded and a sudden lightheadedness took hold of me, yet the freezing mountain air quickly revived my spirit. As the shape drew closer—a sight both magnificent and repulsive—I recognized the miserable creature I had fashioned. I shuddered with a combination of fury and dread, determined to wait for him and then grapple in a fight to the finish. He reached me; his expression revealed a profound, bitter suffering joined with scorn and ill-will, while his ghastly, inhuman features were almost too revolting for the human eye to endure. But I took little note of this; my intense hatred had at first stolen my breath, and I found my voice only to assault him with a torrent of words filled with violent loathing and disdain.
source_text: As I said this I suddenly beheld the figure of a man, at some distance, advancing towards me with superhuman speed. He bounded over the crevices in the ice, among which I had walked with caution; his stature, also, as he approached, seemed to exceed that of man. I was troubled; a mist came over my eyes, and I felt a faintness seize me, but I was quickly restored by the cold gale of the mountains. I perceived, as the shape came nearer (sight tremendous and abhorred!) that it was the wretch whom I had created. I trembled with rage and horror, resolving to wait his approach and then close with him in mortal combat. He approached; his countenance bespoke bitter anguish, combined with disdain and malignity, while its unearthly ugliness rendered it almost too horrible for human eyes. But I scarcely observed this; rage and hatred had at first deprived me of utterance, and I recovered only to overwhelm him with words expressive of furious detestation and contempt.

text: “I anticipated this kind of welcome,” the creature said. “Society has always detested the miserable; imagine, then, the scorn I must face, being the most wretched of all living things. Even you, my maker, abhor and reject your own creation, though we are bound by a link that can only be broken by the death of one of us. You intend to take my life, but how can you treat the gift of existence so lightly? Fulfill your responsibilities to me, and I will honor my obligations to you and the rest of humanity. If you agree to my terms, I will leave you and everyone else in peace; however, if you refuse, I will feed the hunger of death until it is gorged on the blood of every friend you have left.”
source_text: “I expected this reception,” said the dæmon. “All men hate the wretched; how, then, must I be hated, who am miserable beyond all living things! Yet you, my creator, detest and spurn me, thy creature, to whom thou art bound by ties only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us. You purpose to kill me. How dare you sport thus with life? Do your duty towards me, and I will do mine towards you and the rest of mankind. If you will comply with my conditions, I will leave them and you at peace; but if you refuse, I will glut the maw of death, until it be satiated with the blood of your remaining friends.”

text: So I will spare you, my maker," he said, placing those hands I so detested over my eyes, though I thrust them away with a violent motion. "In this way, I hide the sight you find so repulsive. Even so, you may still listen and show me some mercy. I demand this of you by the virtues I once held. Hear my story; it is long and unusual, and the cold of this summit is too harsh for your delicate constitution. Come to the cabin on the mountain. The sun remains high in the sky; before it drops behind these frozen peaks to light another world, you will have heard my tale and can reach a verdict. It is your choice whether I depart from the realm of man forever to live a quiet life, or become a terror to your species and the architect of your own swift undoing.
source_text: “Thus I relieve thee, my creator,” he said, and placed his hated hands before my eyes, which I flung from me with violence; “thus I take from thee a sight which you abhor. Still thou canst listen to me and grant me thy compassion. By the virtues that I once possessed, I demand this from you. Hear my tale; it is long and strange, and the temperature of this place is not fitting to your fine sensations; come to the hut upon the mountain. The sun is yet high in the heavens; before it descends to hide itself behind your snowy precipices and illuminate another world, you will have heard my story and can decide. On you it rests, whether I quit for ever the neighbourhood of man and lead a harmless life, or become the scourge of your fellow creatures and the author of your own speedy ruin.”

text: With these words, he struck out across the frozen wasteland, and I pursued him in silence. My mind was a tumult of conflicting thoughts; I pondered the claims he had made and resolved, at the very least, to grant him an audience. A dark curiosity propelled me, but it was a burgeoning sense of mercy that finally fixed my purpose. Until this moment, I had viewed him only as the butcher of my young brother, and I sought desperately to know the truth of that bloody deed. Furthermore, I felt for the first time the heavy burden of a progenitor’s debt—the realization that I owed some modicum of solace to my handiwork before I dared denounce his cruelty. Bound by these considerations, I followed his lead. We navigated the slick ice and scaled the opposing crag. The atmosphere grew frigid as a grey rain began to fall; we retreated into the shelter, the wretch wearing a mask of victory while I felt only a profound despondency. I sat by the flames my loathsome host had stoked, and he began his narration.
source_text: As he said this he led the way across the ice; I followed. My heart was full, and I did not answer him, but as I proceeded, I weighed the various arguments that he had used and determined at least to listen to his tale. I was partly urged by curiosity, and compassion confirmed my resolution. I had hitherto supposed him to be the murderer of my brother, and I eagerly sought a confirmation or denial of this opinion. For the first time, also, I felt what the duties of a creator towards his creature were, and that I ought to render him happy before I complained of his wickedness. These motives urged me to comply with his demand. We crossed the ice, therefore, and ascended the opposite rock. The air was cold, and the rain again began to descend; we entered the hut, the fiend with an air of exultation, I with a heavy heart and depressed spirits. But I consented to listen, and seating myself by the fire which my odious companion had lighted, he thus began his tale.

text: Before long, a soft radiance crept across the firmament, instilling within me a sense of delight. I rose abruptly to witness a luminous orb ascending from the depths of the forest. I stared at the sight with a species of awe. Though its transit was gradual, it illuminated my way, allowing me to resume my quest for wild fruit. Still shivering, I happened upon a voluminous mantle beneath the boughs of a tree; I draped it about my person and reclined upon the earth. My intellect held no clear thoughts, for everything was muddled. I was conscious only of light, of the cravings of stomach and throat, and of the prevailing shadows; a multitude of noises echoed in my hearing, and diverse aromas assailed me from every quarter. The solitary feature I could truly discern was that brilliant satellite, and I anchored my vision upon it with contentment.
source_text: “Soon a gentle light stole over the heavens and gave me a sensation of pleasure. I started up and beheld a radiant form rise from among the trees. [The moon] I gazed with a kind of wonder. It moved slowly, but it enlightened my path, and I again went out in search of berries. I was still cold when under one of the trees I found a huge cloak, with which I covered myself, and sat down upon the ground. No distinct ideas occupied my mind; all was confused. I felt light, and hunger, and thirst, and darkness; innumerable sounds rang in my ears, and on all sides various scents saluted me; the only object that I could distinguish was the bright moon, and I fixed my eyes on that with pleasure.

text: The sun had been up for perhaps an hour when the urgent need for sustenance and sanctuary took hold of me; eventually, I caught sight of a small cabin situated upon a knoll, constructed, no doubt, for the use of some wandering herdsman. This was a spectacle entirely foreign to me, and I contemplated the dwelling with profound interest. Observing that the door stood ajar, I ventured inside. A withered man sat within, crouched beside a hearth where he was preparing his morning meal. At the sound of my approach, he turned, and upon beholding me, let out a piercing shriek; abandoning his home, he bounded across the meadows with a nimbleness that his frail and aged frame seemed scarcely to promise. His countenance, so unlike any I had previously encountered, and his sudden flight filled me with bewilderment. Yet I was captivated by the comforts of the cell; here, the frost and downpour were excluded; the floor remained parched; and it appeared to me then as magnificent a haven as the gates of Pandæmonium must have seemed to the exiled spirits after the torments of the fiery gulf. I fell hungrily upon the remains of the man’s repast, which consisted of loaf, curd, milk, and a draught of wine; the latter, however, proved unpalatable to my taste. Then, overcome by a heavy languor, I stretched myself out upon a heap of litter and drifted into a deep sleep.
source_text: “It was about seven in the morning, and I longed to obtain food and shelter; at length I perceived a small hut, on a rising ground, which had doubtless been built for the convenience of some shepherd. This was a new sight to me, and I examined the structure with great curiosity. Finding the door open, I entered. An old man sat in it, near a fire, over which he was preparing his breakfast. He turned on hearing a noise, and perceiving me, shrieked loudly, and quitting the hut, ran across the fields with a speed of which his debilitated form hardly appeared capable. His appearance, different from any I had ever before seen, and his flight somewhat surprised me. But I was enchanted by the appearance of the hut; here the snow and rain could not penetrate; the ground was dry; and it presented to me then as exquisite and divine a retreat as Pandæmonium appeared to the dæmons of hell after their sufferings in the lake of fire. I greedily devoured the remnants of the shepherd’s breakfast, which consisted of bread, cheese, milk, and wine; the latter, however, I did not like. Then, overcome by fatigue, I lay down among some straw and fell asleep.

text: I withdrew to this spot and lay down, relieved to have found a sanctuary, however wretched, from the biting winter and, more importantly, from the cruelty of mankind. When dawn broke, I crawled from my small hovel to survey the neighboring cottage, hoping to determine if I might stay in this newfound residence. It was built against the rear wall of the house, flanked on its exposed sides by a pigsty and a pool of clear water. I had entered through a single gap, but I now set about plugging every crack with wood and stones to ensure I remained unseen, though I left them loose enough to be moved whenever I wished to go out. The only light I received filtered through the sty, which proved quite adequate for me.
source_text: “Here, then, I retreated and lay down happy to have found a shelter, however miserable, from the inclemency of the season, and still more from the barbarity of man. As soon as morning dawned I crept from my kennel, that I might view the adjacent cottage and discover if I could remain in the habitation I had found. It was situated against the back of the cottage and surrounded on the sides which were exposed by a pig sty and a clear pool of water. One part was open, and by that I had crept in; but now I covered every crevice by which I might be perceived with stones and wood, yet in such a manner that I might move them on occasion to pass out; all the light I enjoyed came through the sty, and that was sufficient for me.

text: The elderly man had been lost in thought, but he brightened the moment his companions appeared, and they soon sat down to their meal. They finished eating quickly. Afterward, the young woman resumed her work organizing the cottage, while the old man spent a few minutes walking in the sun, leaning on the youth’s arm. The contrast between these two gentle souls was exceptionally beautiful. The elder man possessed silver hair and a face glowing with kindness and love; the younger was slender and graceful, with perfectly symmetrical features, yet his eyes and bearing reflected a deep, heavy sorrow. Eventually, the old man went back indoors, and the youth set off across the fields, carrying different tools than he had used earlier that morning.
source_text: “The old man had, in the meantime, been pensive, but on the appearance of his companions he assumed a more cheerful air, and they sat down to eat. The meal was quickly dispatched. The young woman was again occupied in arranging the cottage, the old man walked before the cottage in the sun for a few minutes, leaning on the arm of the youth. Nothing could exceed in beauty the contrast between these two excellent creatures. One was old, with silver hairs and a countenance beaming with benevolence and love; the younger was slight and graceful in his figure, and his features were moulded with the finest symmetry, yet his eyes and attitude expressed the utmost sadness and despondency. The old man returned to the cottage, and the youth, with tools different from those he had used in the morning, directed his steps across the fields.

text: I reclined upon my pallet of dry stalks, yet sleep remained a stranger to me. My mind dwelt incessantly upon the day’s revelations. I was most affected by the graceful serenity of those people, and I felt a profound yearning to seek their company, though I lacked the courage to do so.
