The memory of the previous night’s brutality from the savage townspeople was too vivid; I resolved, therefore, to remain hidden within my wretched shed, keeping a silent vigil to fathom the secret impulses that guided their every action.
source_text: “I lay on my straw, but I could not sleep. I thought of the occurrences of the day. What chiefly struck me was the gentle manners of these people, and I longed to join them, but dared not. I remembered too well the treatment I had suffered the night before from the barbarous villagers, and resolved, whatever course of conduct I might hereafter think it right to pursue, that for the present I would remain quietly in my hovel, watching and endeavouring to discover the motives which influenced their actions.

text: Supported by his son, the elderly man took a walk every day at noon, provided it wasn't raining—a term I learned for when water fell from the sky. These downpours were common, but strong winds would quickly dry the earth, and the weather became much more agreeable than it had been.
source_text: “The old man, leaning on his son, walked each day at noon, when it did not rain, as I found it was called when the heavens poured forth its waters. This frequently took place, but a high wind quickly dried the earth, and the season became far more pleasant than it had been.

text: It quickly became apparent that while the visitor spoke in distinct syllables and possessed a language of her own, she could not understand the dwellers, nor could they comprehend her. They made various gestures that remained obscure to me, yet I perceived that her arrival radiated a sudden light through the house, scattering their misery as the sun drives off the morning vapors. Julian seemed exceptionally joyous, welcoming his exotic guest with looks of pure enchantment. Seraphina, the soul of gentleness, pressed her lips to the hands of the lovely newcomer and, indicating her brother, made signs which I interpreted to mean that he had been steeped in melancholy until her arrival. A few hours passed in this manner, their faces reflecting a delight whose source I did not yet know. Soon, I noticed the frequent repetition of certain sounds which the guest echoed, signaling her attempt to learn their tongue; the thought instantly struck me that I should adopt these same instructions for my own benefit. The stranger acquired some twenty words in that first lesson; though many were sounds I had already mastered, I drew great profit from those that were unfamiliar.
source_text: “I soon perceived that although the stranger uttered articulate sounds and appeared to have a language of her own, she was neither understood by nor herself understood the cottagers. They made many signs which I did not comprehend, but I saw that her presence diffused gladness through the cottage, dispelling their sorrow as the sun dissipates the morning mists. Felix seemed peculiarly happy and with smiles of delight welcomed his Arabian. Agatha, the ever-gentle Agatha, kissed the hands of the lovely stranger, and pointing to her brother, made signs which appeared to me to mean that he had been sorrowful until she came. Some hours passed thus, while they, by their countenances, expressed joy, the cause of which I did not comprehend. Presently I found, by the frequent recurrence of some sound which the stranger repeated after them, that she was endeavouring to learn their language; and the idea instantly occurred to me that I should make use of the same instructions to the same end. The stranger learned about twenty words at the first lesson; most of them, indeed, were those which I had before understood, but I profited by the others.

text: The following morning, Felix departed for his daily labors, and once Agatha had concluded her customary chores, the Arabian woman seated herself at the old man’s feet. Taking up his guitar, she performed melodies of such enchanting beauty that they instantly drew tears of both grief and joy from my eyes. Then she sang, her voice flowing in a lush cadence that swelled and faded like the song of a woodland nightingale.
source_text: “The next morning Felix went out to his work, and after the usual occupations of Agatha were finished, the Arabian sat at the feet of the old man, and taking his guitar, played some airs so entrancingly beautiful that they at once drew tears of sorrow and delight from my eyes. She sang, and her voice flowed in a rich cadence, swelling or dying away like a nightingale of the woods.

text: Knowledge is a peculiar thing; once it takes hold, it fastens to the mind as stubbornly as lichen to a stone. There were times I longed to cast aside all thought and emotion, yet I realized there was only one way to escape this suffering: death—a state I dreaded even as I failed to comprehend it. I held virtue and noble sentiments in high regard, and I grew to love the kind dispositions and gentle manners of the cottagers. However, I was barred from any true connection with them. My only interactions were those I stole while remaining hidden and unknown, experiences that only sharpened my hunger for companionship rather than satisfying it. Agatha’s soft words and the radiant smiles of the lovely Arabian were never meant for me. The old man’s gentle guidance and Felix’s spirited conversation were not for me. I was a truly wretched and miserable soul.
source_text: “Of what a strange nature is knowledge! It clings to the mind when it has once seized on it like a lichen on the rock. I wished sometimes to shake off all thought and feeling, but I learned that there was but one means to overcome the sensation of pain, and that was death—a state which I feared yet did not understand. I admired virtue and good feelings and loved the gentle manners and amiable qualities of my cottagers, but I was shut out from intercourse with them, except through means which I obtained by stealth, when I was unseen and unknown, and which rather increased than satisfied the desire I had of becoming one among my fellows. The gentle words of Agatha and the animated smiles of the charming Arabian were not for me. The mild exhortations of the old man and the lively conversation of the loved Felix were not for me. Miserable, unhappy wretch!

text: Yet, where might my kin and companions be found? No sire had stood sentinel over my cradle, and no mother had ever graced me with her tender gaze or soft embrace. If such protectors had once existed, the ledger of my youth was now a total erasure, a hollow chasm where no light of memory could reach. Since the very dawn of my awareness, I had possessed this same towering frame and these same jagged features. Never had I looked upon a soul who shared my aspect, nor had any traveler ever sought my fellowship. What, then, was I? The inquiry echoed through my mind once more, met only by the sound of my own wretched lamentations.
source_text: “But where were my friends and relations? No father had watched my infant days, no mother had blessed me with smiles and caresses; or if they had, all my past life was now a blot, a blind vacancy in which I distinguished nothing. From my earliest remembrance I had been as I then was in height and proportion. I had never yet seen a being resembling me or who claimed any intercourse with me. What was I? The question again recurred, to be answered only with groans.

text: Felix had chanced upon the trial by accident, and the court’s verdict left him reeling with a sense of profound outrage. In that instant, he committed himself to a secret oath to liberate the man, immediately scouting for any possible opening. Following several unsuccessful efforts to enter the prison, he eventually located a heavily barred window in a secluded corner of the fortress. It overlooked the cell of the condemned merchant, who sat in irons, despondent as he awaited his brutal execution. Felix returned to the bars at night to reveal his plan of escape. Though the prisoner was shocked and elated, he attempted to secure Felix’s loyalty by promising him immense wealth. Felix brushed aside these offers with scorn; yet, when he encountered the exquisite Safie—who was granted access to her father and signaled her intense gratitude—the young man had to admit to himself that the captive held a treasure that would more than compensate for his labor and risk.
source_text: “Felix had accidentally been present at the trial; his horror and indignation were uncontrollable when he heard the decision of the court. He made, at that moment, a solemn vow to deliver him and then looked around for the means. After many fruitless attempts to gain admittance to the prison, he found a strongly grated window in an unguarded part of the building, which lighted the dungeon of the unfortunate Muhammadan, who, loaded with chains, waited in despair the execution of the barbarous sentence. Felix visited the grate at night and made known to the prisoner his intentions in his favour. The Turk, amazed and delighted, endeavoured to kindle the zeal of his deliverer by promises of reward and wealth. Felix rejected his offers with contempt, yet when he saw the lovely Safie, who was allowed to visit her father and who by her gestures expressed her lively gratitude, the youth could not help owning to his own mind that the captive possessed a treasure which would fully reward his toil and hazard.

text: The Turk was quick to notice the deep affection Felix felt for his daughter. Seeking to bind the young man more firmly to his cause, he promised that they should be wed as soon as he reached a place of refuge. Though Felix’s sense of honor forbade him from accepting the girl as a mere reward, he nevertheless cherished the hope of their marriage as the greatest possible source of future bliss.
source_text: “The Turk quickly perceived the impression that his daughter had made on the heart of Felix and endeavoured to secure him more entirely in his interests by the promise of her hand in marriage so soon as he should be conveyed to a place of safety. Felix was too delicate to accept this offer, yet he looked forward to the probability of the event as to the consummation of his happiness.

text: Throughout the subsequent days, as the intricate plans for the merchant’s liberation were set in motion, Elias’s resolve was stoked by various letters delivered from that exquisite girl. She had discovered a way to articulate her heart’s reflections in his own tongue, relying upon an aged servant of her father’s house who was well-versed in the language. Within these notes, she offered the most passionate thanks for the mercy he intended to show her sire, even as she quietly mourned the heavy misfortune of her own destiny.
source_text: “During the ensuing days, while the preparations were going forward for the escape of the merchant, the zeal of Felix was warmed by several letters that he received from this lovely girl, who found means to express her thoughts in the language of her lover by the aid of an old man, a servant of her father who understood French. She thanked him in the most ardent terms for his intended services towards her parent, and at the same time she gently deplored her own fate.

text: Safie recounted how her mother, a Christian Arab, had been captured and enslaved by the Turks; her grace and beauty had won the affection of Safie’s father, who eventually married her. The girl spoke with fervent pride of this woman, who, having been born free, deeply resented the chains of her later life. She had raised Safie in the doctrines of her own faith, urging her to cultivate a strength of mind and an autonomy of spirit that were typically forbidden to women in her father's culture. Although her mother died, these lessons were permanently engraved upon Safie’s soul. She was repulsed by the thought of returning to Asia to be confined within a harem, restricted to the frivolous and childish diversions that were ill-suited to a mind now devoted to grand concepts and the pursuit of virtue. The chance to marry a Christian and live in a nation where women were granted a social standing of their own was a truly captivating prospect.
source_text: “Safie related that her mother was a Christian Arab, seized and made a slave by the Turks; recommended by her beauty, she had won the heart of the father of Safie, who married her. The young girl spoke in high and enthusiastic terms of her mother, who, born in freedom, spurned the bondage to which she was now reduced. She instructed her daughter in the tenets of her religion and taught her to aspire to higher powers of intellect and an independence of spirit forbidden to the female followers of Muhammad. This lady died, but her lessons were indelibly impressed on the mind of Safie, who sickened at the prospect of again returning to Asia and being immured within the walls of a harem, allowed only to occupy herself with infantile amusements, ill-suited to the temper of her soul, now accustomed to grand ideas and a noble emulation for virtue. The prospect of marrying a Christian and remaining in a country where women were allowed to take a rank in society was enchanting to her.

text: Several days later, the merchant hurried into his daughter’s quarters to inform her that he believed his location in Leghorn had been exposed and that he would likely be handed over to the French government very soon. Consequently, he had chartered a vessel to take him to Constantinople and would be departing in just a few hours. He intended for his daughter to stay behind with a trusted servant so that she could join him later with the bulk of his wealth, which had not yet reached the city.
source_text: “A few days after, the Turk entered his daughter’s apartment and told her hastily that he had reason to believe that his residence at Leghorn had been divulged and that he should speedily be delivered up to the French government; he had consequently hired a vessel to convey him to Constantinople, for which city he should sail in a few hours. He intended to leave his daughter under the care of a confidential servant, to follow at her leisure with the greater part of his property, which had not yet arrived at Leghorn.

text: She attained a village nearly sixty miles from the sequestered home of Valancourt when her companion was seized by a fatal malady. Elara tended to her with the most selfless devotion, yet the woman expired, leaving the wanderer entirely isolated, a stranger to the local idiom and unversed in the habits of the realm. Providence, however, did not forsake her. The servant had uttered the name of their destination, and after her passing, the mistress of the inn ensured that Elara was guided safely to the threshold of her beloved.
source_text: “She arrived in safety at a town about twenty leagues from the cottage of De Lacey, when her attendant fell dangerously ill. Safie nursed her with the most devoted affection, but the poor girl died, and the Arabian was left alone, unacquainted with the language of the country and utterly ignorant of the customs of the world. She fell, however, into good hands. The Italian had mentioned the name of the spot for which they were bound, and after her death the woman of the house in which they had lived took care that Safie should arrive in safety at the cottage of her lover.”

text: Yet the *Epic of the Fall* stirred a far more profound and turbulent sea of passions within me. I pored over its pages, as I had the previous works that chanced into my path, accepting them as a literal chronicle of the world. The portrayal of an invincible Architect at war with His own handiwork commanded every ounce of my reverence and dread. Frequently, I mapped the poem’s circumstances onto my own bleak reality. Like the First Man, I appeared to be a solitary figure, severed from any lineage or kin; however, his station was fundamentally unlike my own. He had been fashioned by the Divine as a masterpiece, flourishing under the watchful eye of his Maker and granted the company of celestial spirits. I, by contrast, was a wretched vagabond, destitute and forsaken. More often, I found the Arch-Rebel to be the more accurate mirror of my soul, for whenever I witnessed the serene happiness of my guardians, the stinging bile of resentment rose up to choke me.
source_text: “But _Paradise Lost_ excited different and far deeper emotions. I read it, as I had read the other volumes which had fallen into my hands, as a true history. It moved every feeling of wonder and awe that the picture of an omnipotent God warring with his creatures was capable of exciting. I often referred the several situations, as their similarity struck me, to my own. Like Adam, I was apparently united by no link to any other being in existence; but his state was far different from mine in every other respect. He had come forth from the hands of God a perfect creature, happy and prosperous, guarded by the especial care of his Creator; he was allowed to converse with and acquire knowledge from beings of a superior nature, but I was wretched, helpless, and alone. Many times I considered Satan as the fitter emblem of my condition, for often, like him, when I viewed the bliss of my protectors, the bitter gall of envy rose within me.

text: I fought to suppress my anxieties and steel myself for the ordeal I intended to face in a few months. At times, I let my imagination drift beyond the limits of logic, wandering through a private paradise where I envisioned kind and beautiful beings who might empathize with my suffering and brighten my dark moods; their radiant faces seemed to offer smiles of profound comfort. But these were only delusions; no companion existed to ease my grief or share my inner life. I was utterly solitary. I recalled how Adam had petitioned his Maker for a mate, but where was my own? He had forsaken me, and in my profound resentment, I cursed his name.
source_text: “I endeavoured to crush these fears and to fortify myself for the trial which in a few months I resolved to undergo; and sometimes I allowed my thoughts, unchecked by reason, to ramble in the fields of Paradise, and dared to fancy amiable and lovely creatures sympathising with my feelings and cheering my gloom; their angelic countenances breathed smiles of consolation. But it was all a dream; no Eve soothed my sorrows nor shared my thoughts; I was alone. I remembered Adam’s supplication to his Creator. But where was mine? He had abandoned me, and in the bitterness of my heart I cursed him.

text: The autumn season slipped away. I watched with a mix of wonder and sadness as the leaves rotted and fell, and the world once again took on the desolate, frozen appearance it had when I first saw the woods beneath the moon. The harshness of the weather did not bother me, for my constitution was better suited to endure the cold than the summer heat. Nevertheless, I missed the beauty of the flowers, the songs of the birds, and the vibrant colors of the warmer months; as these faded, I concentrated more fully on the inhabitants of the cottage. Their joy did not seem to wane with the passing of summer. They cared for and comforted one another, their happiness so intertwined that it remained undisturbed by the grim changes in the world around them. The more I observed them, the more I longed to seek their protection and grace. My heart ached to be known and loved by these gentle people; to have their kind eyes rest upon me with affection was the very peak of my ambition. I refused to believe they would look at me with nothing but loathing and fear. They never turned away the destitute who came to their door. It was true that I sought a much greater prize than a bit of bread or a place to rest—I hungered for friendship and empathy—but I did not feel that I was entirely unworthy of receiving them.
source_text: “Autumn passed thus. I saw, with surprise and grief, the leaves decay and fall, and nature again assume the barren and bleak appearance it had worn when I first beheld the woods and the lovely moon. Yet I did not heed the bleakness of the weather; I was better fitted by my conformation for the endurance of cold than heat. But my chief delights were the sight of the flowers, the birds, and all the gay apparel of summer; when those deserted me, I turned with more attention towards the cottagers. Their happiness was not decreased by the absence of summer. They loved and sympathised with one another; and their joys, depending on each other, were not interrupted by the casualties that took place around them. The more I saw of them, the greater became my desire to claim their protection and kindness; my heart yearned to be known and loved by these amiable creatures; to see their sweet looks directed towards me with affection was the utmost limit of my ambition. I dared not think that they would turn them from me with disdain and horror. The poor that stopped at their door were never driven away. I asked, it is true, for greater treasures than a little food or rest: I required kindness and sympathy; but I did not believe myself utterly unworthy of it.

text: The frost returned, and a complete cycle of the months had elapsed since my awakening into this world. My faculties were now entirely devoted to the ambition of presenting myself before the family I so admired. I weighed many designs, but the one I finally adopted was to approach the hearth when the blind patriarch was solitary. I had sufficient discernment to realize that the monstrous aspect of my features was the source of the dread I provoked in others. My voice, while coarse, held nothing of the macabre; I hoped, therefore, that if I could earn the favor and advocacy of the elder Marceau while his children were away, he might act as a bridge to the acceptance of my younger hosts.
source_text: “The winter advanced, and an entire revolution of the seasons had taken place since I awoke into life. My attention at this time was solely directed towards my plan of introducing myself into the cottage of my protectors. I revolved many projects, but that on which I finally fixed was to enter the dwelling when the blind old man should be alone. I had sagacity enough to discover that the unnatural hideousness of my person was the chief object of horror with those who had formerly beheld me. My voice, although harsh, had nothing terrible in it; I thought, therefore, that if in the absence of his children I could gain the good will and mediation of the old De Lacey, I might by his means be tolerated by my younger protectors.

text: On a day when the sun sparkled across the crimson leaves covering the ground, bringing a sense of brightness despite the biting cold, Safie, Agatha, and Felix set off for a long stroll through the countryside. The elderly man, by his own choice, stayed behind alone in the cottage. Once his children had gone, he reached for his guitar and played several somber yet beautiful melodies, more haunting and lovely than any I had ever heard him perform. Initially, his face glowed with a quiet pleasure, but as he continued, a pensive sadness took hold; eventually, setting the instrument down, he sat motionless, lost in deep reflection.
source_text: “One day, when the sun shone on the red leaves that strewed the ground and diffused cheerfulness, although it denied warmth, Safie, Agatha, and Felix departed on a long country walk, and the old man, at his own desire, was left alone in the cottage. When his children had departed, he took up his guitar and played several mournful but sweet airs, more sweet and mournful than I had ever heard him play before. At first his countenance was illuminated with pleasure, but as he continued, thoughtfulness and sadness succeeded; at length, laying aside the instrument, he sat absorbed in reflection.

text: My pulse throbbed; this was the pivotal hour that would either validate my hopes or cement my despairs. The residents had gone to a festival nearby, and a profound stillness settled over the cottage and its surroundings. It was an ideal opening, yet when I attempted to move, my legs buckled and I slumped to the earth. I rose again, and gathering all the fortitude I could muster, I cast aside the timber I had used to hide my lair. The rush of fresh air invigorated me, and with a bolstered spirit, I stepped toward the cottage door.
source_text: “My heart beat quick; this was the hour and moment of trial, which would decide my hopes or realise my fears. The servants were gone to a neighbouring fair. All was silent in and around the cottage; it was an excellent opportunity; yet, when I proceeded to execute my plan, my limbs failed me and I sank to the ground. Again I rose, and exerting all the firmness of which I was master, removed the planks which I had placed before my hovel to conceal my retreat. The fresh air revived me, and with renewed determination I approached the door of their cottage.

text: I lingered for a second. I realized this was the crossroads that would either grant me eternal peace or condemn me to misery. I tried to summon enough courage to speak, but the sheer weight of the moment broke me. My strength failed, and I slumped into the chair, weeping openly. Just then, I heard the footsteps of the younger ones. Knowing I had to act immediately, I caught hold of the old man’s hand and cried out, "The time has come! Protect me! You and your family are the very friends I’ve sought. Do not forsake me now, in my darkest hour!"
source_text: “I paused. This, I thought, was the moment of decision, which was to rob me of or bestow happiness on me for ever. I struggled vainly for firmness sufficient to answer him, but the effort destroyed all my remaining strength; I sank on the chair and sobbed aloud. At that moment I heard the steps of my younger protectors. I had not a moment to lose, but seizing the hand of the old man, I cried, ‘Now is the time! Save and protect me! You and your family are the friends whom I seek. Do not you desert me in the hour of trial!’

text: Abominable, cruel architect! To what end was I quickened? Why, in that first breath, did I not quench the light of being you so recklessly granted? I cannot tell; the shadows of despondency had not yet eclipsed my spirit; my soul was then a furnace of wrath and bitter retaliation. I would have found a savage joy in razing that dwelling, sating my hunger with the screams of its inhabitants and the spectacle of their ruin.
source_text: “Cursed, cursed creator! Why did I live? Why, in that instant, did I not extinguish the spark of existence which you had so wantonly bestowed? I know not; despair had not yet taken possession of me; my feelings were those of rage and revenge. I could with pleasure have destroyed the cottage and its inhabitants and have glutted myself with their shrieks and misery.

text: I arrived at nightfall and withdrew into a secluded spot within the surrounding fields, contemplating how I might best approach you. I was weighed down by exhaustion and hunger, far too consumed by misery to take any pleasure in the mild evening air or the sight of the sun sinking behind the towering peaks of the Jura.
source_text: “It was evening when I arrived, and I retired to a hiding-place among the fields that surround it to meditate in what manner I should apply to you. I was oppressed by fatigue and hunger and far too unhappy to enjoy the gentle breezes of evening or the prospect of the sun setting behind the stupendous mountains of Jura.

text: “I meant to be rational. This fury is destroying me, yet you fail to see that you are the one responsible for its intensity. If even one soul showed me a spark of kindness, I would return it a thousand times over; for the sake of that single being, I would make peace with all of humanity. Instead, I am left dreaming of a happiness that remains out of reach. What I ask of you is fair and modest: I want a companion of the opposite sex, someone as deformed as I am. It is a meager solace, but it is the only one I can ever know, and it will be enough. We will be outcasts, certainly, severed from the rest of the world, but that will only draw us closer together. Our existence won't be joyful, but it will be peaceful and devoid of the torment I currently endure. Oh, my creator, grant me this happiness; let me feel thankful to you for just one favor. Let me see that I can evoke compassion in another living thing. Do not refuse my request.”
source_text: “I intended to reason. This passion is detrimental to me, for you do not reflect that _you_ are the cause of its excess. If any being felt emotions of benevolence towards me, I should return them a hundred and a hundredfold; for that one creature’s sake I would make peace with the whole kind! But I now indulge in dreams of bliss that cannot be realised. What I ask of you is reasonable and moderate; I demand a creature of another sex, but as hideous as myself; the gratification is small, but it is all that I can receive, and it shall content me. It is true, we shall be monsters, cut off from all the world; but on that account we shall be more attached to one another. Our lives will not be happy, but they will be harmless and free from the misery I now feel. Oh! My creator, make me happy; let me feel gratitude towards you for one benefit! Let me see that I excite the sympathy of some existing thing; do not deny me my request!”

text: “Should you grant my request, we shall vanish from the sight of your kind forever, seeking refuge in the trackless depths of the southern wilderness. My cravings are not those of common men; I seek no blood of the lamb or the calf to sate my hunger, for the forest’s humble offering of mast and berries is ample for my needs. My partner shall be of my own sort, finding contentment in the same simple provisions. We will find our rest on a bed of withered leaves, warmed by the same sun that nourishes the crops of your people. This is a life of harmony and reason I propose, and you must know that to reject it would be a stroke of senseless tyranny. Though you have shown me only scorn, I glimpse a flicker of pity in your expression; let me take hold of this brief grace and move you to grant the one thing I most desperately seek.”
source_text: “If you consent, neither you nor any other human being shall ever see us again; I will go to the vast wilds of South America. My food is not that of man; I do not destroy the lamb and the kid to glut my appetite; acorns and berries afford me sufficient nourishment. My companion will be of the same nature as myself and will be content with the same fare. We shall make our bed of dried leaves; the sun will shine on us as on man and will ripen our food. The picture I present to you is peaceful and human, and you must feel that you could deny it only in the wantonness of power and cruelty. Pitiless as you have been towards me, I now see compassion in your eyes; let me seize the favourable moment and persuade you to promise what I so ardently desire.”

text: “How fickle your heart remains! Only a moment ago, you seemed touched by my words, yet now you grow cold to my suffering once more. I vow to you, by the earth I walk upon and by the hands that shaped me, that if you grant me a companion, I shall abandon the world of men forever. We will seek out the most desolate wilderness to call our own. My malice will vanish, for I will finally know the comfort of sympathy. My life will pass in quiet peace, and in my final hour, I will not die cursing the one who gave me life.”
source_text: “How inconstant are your feelings! But a moment ago you were moved by my representations, and why do you again harden yourself to my complaints? I swear to you, by the earth which I inhabit, and by you that made me, that with the companion you bestow, I will quit the neighbourhood of man and dwell, as it may chance, in the most savage of places. My evil passions will have fled, for I shall meet with sympathy! My life will flow quietly away, and in my dying moments I shall not curse my maker.”

text: What is this? Do not toy with me; I insist on a response. If I am denied all bonds and affection, then bitterness and cruelty are my only inheritance. Yet the love of another would kill the root of my crimes, and I would vanish into a life of quiet obscurity. My malice is the product of an isolation I despise, and my better nature will surely emerge once I live alongside an equal. I will finally experience the emotions of a living soul and take my place in the human story from which I am now cast out.
source_text: “How is this? I must not be trifled with, and I demand an answer. If I have no ties and no affections, hatred and vice must be my portion; the love of another will destroy the cause of my crimes, and I shall become a thing of whose existence everyone will be ignorant. My vices are the children of a forced solitude that I abhor, and my virtues will necessarily arise when I live in communion with an equal. I shall feel the affections of a sensitive being and become linked to the chain of existence and events from which I am now excluded.”

text: “Your recent declarations on this matter, my dear Julian, have afforded me a greater measure of solace than I have known for a long season. If such are your true convictions, we may yet find ourselves restored to joy, despite the heavy clouds that currently hang over us. Indeed, it is that very darkness, which seems to have laid so firm a grasp upon your soul, that I am most anxious to dispel. Tell me, therefore, if you have any secret reluctance toward an immediate celebration of your wedding. Our trials have been many, and late calamities have stripped me of that peaceful repose so vital to my waning health and years. You are in the flower of your youth; and since you possess a sufficient estate, I cannot think that an early marriage would prove an obstacle to those designs of distinction and service you have surely conceived. Yet, do not believe I wish to impose my own vision of happiness upon you, or that a delay would cause me any profound grief. Receive my inquiry with an open heart, and answer me, I implore you, with perfect honesty.”
source_text: “The expression of your sentiments of this subject, my dear Victor, gives me more pleasure than I have for some time experienced. If you feel thus, we shall assuredly be happy, however present events may cast a gloom over us. But it is this gloom which appears to have taken so strong a hold of your mind that I wish to dissipate. Tell me, therefore, whether you object to an immediate solemnisation of the marriage. We have been unfortunate, and recent events have drawn us from that everyday tranquillity befitting my years and infirmities. You are younger; yet I do not suppose, possessed as you are of a competent fortune, that an early marriage would at all interfere with any future plans of honour and utility that you may have formed. Do not suppose, however, that I wish to dictate happiness to you or that a delay on your part would cause me any serious uneasiness. Interpret my words with candour and answer me, I conjure you, with confidence and sincerity.”

text: I set about the business of my departure, but a singular anxiety weighed upon my soul. By leaving, I abandoned my kin to a danger they could not fathom, defenseless against a malice I had provoked. Yet the wretch had sworn to haunt my steps; surely he would follow me to the English coast? This prospect was ghastly, yet it offered a grim comfort, as it presumed the safety of those I loved. I shuddered at the alternative. Throughout my long servitude to that monstrosity, I had allowed myself to be led by the whims of my own terror; and my present instincts whispered that the specter would pursue me, exempting my household from his dark designs.
source_text: I now made arrangements for my journey, but one feeling haunted me which filled me with fear and agitation. During my absence I should leave my friends unconscious of the existence of their enemy and unprotected from his attacks, exasperated as he might be by my departure. But he had promised to follow me wherever I might go, and would he not accompany me to England? This imagination was dreadful in itself, but soothing inasmuch as it supposed the safety of my friends. I was agonised with the idea of the possibility that the reverse of this might happen. But through the whole period during which I was the slave of my creature I allowed myself to be governed by the impulses of the moment; and my present sensations strongly intimated that the fiend would follow me and exempt my family from the danger of his machinations.

text: Where does he dwell now? Has this tender and radiant soul truly vanished into the void? Has that intellect, so full of brilliant concepts and magnificent dreams—a mind that shaped a whole world reliant upon its master's heartbeat—finally ceased to be? Does it linger now only as a shadow in my own recollection? No, I cannot believe it; though your graceful and luminous frame has fallen to ruin, your spirit remains to seek out and comfort your sorrowful friend.
source_text: And where does he now exist? Is this gentle and lovely being lost for ever? Has this mind, so replete with ideas, imaginations fanciful and magnificent, which formed a world, whose existence depended on the life of its creator;—has this mind perished? Does it now only exist in my memory? No, it is not thus; your form so divinely wrought, and beaming with beauty, has decayed, but your spirit still visits and consoles your unhappy friend.

text: I took some pleasure in the view, yet that delight was soured by both the weight of my past and the dread of my future. I was naturally suited for a life of quiet happiness. In my youth, I never knew what it was to be dissatisfied; if I ever felt a sense of listlessness, the splendor of nature or the heights of human achievement could always stir my heart and restore my vitality. But I am now a lightning-struck tree; the bolt has pierced my very soul. I realized then that I would live on only to display the wreckage of who I once was—a broken shell of a man, a figure of pity to the world and an unbearable burden to myself.
source_text: I enjoyed this scene, and yet my enjoyment was embittered both by the memory of the past and the anticipation of the future. I was formed for peaceful happiness. During my youthful days discontent never visited my mind, and if I was ever overcome by _ennui_, the sight of what is beautiful in nature or the study of what is excellent and sublime in the productions of man could always interest my heart and communicate elasticity to my spirits. But I am a blasted tree; the bolt has entered my soul; and I felt then that I should survive to exhibit what I shall soon cease to be—a miserable spectacle of wrecked humanity, pitiable to others and intolerable to myself.

text: We stayed in Oxford for quite some time, wandering through the surrounding countryside to seek out every landmark associated with that most stirring chapter of English history. Our small excursions were often lengthened by the many fascinating sights we encountered along the way. We made a pilgrimage to the tomb of the renowned Hampden and stood upon the very field where the patriot met his end. In those moments, my spirit was lifted from its wretched, degrading anxieties to dwell upon the noble ideals of freedom and self-denial for which these places stood as enduring symbols. For a fleeting interval, I ventured to cast aside my shackles and survey the world with a sense of pride and liberty; yet the iron had already pierced my soul too deeply, and I fell back, shivering and bereft of hope, into the misery of my own existence.
source_text: We passed a considerable period at Oxford, rambling among its environs and endeavouring to identify every spot which might relate to the most animating epoch of English history. Our little voyages of discovery were often prolonged by the successive objects that presented themselves. We visited the tomb of the illustrious Hampden and the field on which that patriot fell. For a moment my soul was elevated from its debasing and miserable fears to contemplate the divine ideas of liberty and self-sacrifice of which these sights were the monuments and the remembrancers. For an instant I dared to shake off my chains and look around me with a free and lofty spirit, but the iron had eaten into my flesh, and I sank again, trembling and hopeless, into my miserable self.

text: But he discovered that a traveler’s existence is marked by a persistent ache amidst its many delights. His emotions are perpetually strained; just as he begins to settle into a state of rest, he feels compelled to leave his comforts behind for the sake of something new. This fresh discovery soon occupies his mind, only to be abandoned in turn as he pursues further novelties.
source_text: But he found that a traveller’s life is one that includes much pain amidst its enjoyments. His feelings are for ever on the stretch; and when he begins to sink into repose, he finds himself obliged to quit that on which he rests in pleasure for something new, which again engages his attention, and which also he forsakes for other novelties.

text: A week later, we departed Edinburgh, journeying through Coupar and St. Andrew’s and following the winding banks of the Tay until we reached Perth, where our host awaited us. However, I found myself utterly unable to engage in lighthearted conversation with strangers or summon the social grace required of a guest; consequently, I informed Clerval of my desire to continue my Scottish travels unaccompanied. “Stay here and enjoy yourself,” I told him, “and let this place serve as our meeting point. I may be gone for a month or perhaps two, but I beg you not to question my movements. Grant me this brief interval of quiet and isolation; I trust that when I come back, I shall possess a more cheerful spirit, one better suited to your own lively disposition.”
source_text: We left Edinburgh in a week, passing through Coupar, St. Andrew’s, and along the banks of the Tay, to Perth, where our friend expected us. But I was in no mood to laugh and talk with strangers or enter into their feelings or plans with the good humour expected from a guest; and accordingly I told Clerval that I wished to make the tour of Scotland alone. “Do you,” said I, “enjoy yourself, and let this be our rendezvous. I may be absent a month or two; but do not interfere with my motions, I entreat you; leave me to peace and solitude for a short time; and when I return, I hope it will be with a lighter heart, more congenial to your own temper.”

text: Julian endeavored to divert me from my path, but finding my resolve firm, he ceased his objections. He urged me to correspond with him often. “I would choose to be your companion,” he said, “in those bleak wanderings, rather than stay among these unfamiliar folk; return quickly, then, my trusted friend, so that I may again find my bearings, which I can never truly possess while we are apart.”
source_text: Henry wished to dissuade me, but seeing me bent on this plan, ceased to remonstrate. He entreated me to write often. “I had rather be with you,” he said, “in your solitary rambles, than with these Scotch people, whom I do not know; hasten, then, my dear friend, to return, that I may again feel myself somewhat at home, which I cannot do in your absence.”

text: The island possessed a mere three wretched cottages, one of which was unoccupied when I reached its shores. I secured this dwelling for myself. It consisted of only two rooms, both of which bore the marks of extreme and grimy destitution. The thatched roof had caved in, the walls remained bare of plaster, and the door had slipped from its hinges. I commissioned its restoration, acquired some basic furniture, and moved in—a set of actions that would have surely drawn local scrutiny if the villagers’ perceptions had not been so deadened by their own agonizing poverty. Thus, I remained unobserved and undisturbed, scarcely acknowledged for the small gifts of sustenance and garments I offered, for profound misery has a way of numbing even the most fundamental human feelings.
source_text: On the whole island there were but three miserable huts, and one of these was vacant when I arrived. This I hired. It contained but two rooms, and these exhibited all the squalidness of the most miserable penury. The thatch had fallen in, the walls were unplastered, and the door was off its hinges. I ordered it to be repaired, bought some furniture, and took possession, an incident which would doubtless have occasioned some surprise had not all the senses of the cottagers been benumbed by want and squalid poverty. As it was, I lived ungazed at and unmolested, hardly thanked for the pittance of food and clothes which I gave, so much does suffering blunt even the coarsest sensations of men.

text: Meanwhile, I continued my ministrations, and the task was already nearing its fruition. I anticipated the end with a wavering and desperate optimism which I shrank from examining, yet it was clouded by shadowy intuitions of calamity that caused a cold heaviness to settle upon my spirit.
source_text: In the mean time I worked on, and my labour was already considerably advanced. I looked towards its completion with a tremulous and eager hope, which I dared not trust myself to question but which was intermixed with obscure forebodings of evil that made my heart sicken in my bosom.

text: Bondman, I formerly stooped to debate with you, but you have proven yourself undeserving of my patience. Do not forget that I possess the advantage; you imagine yourself suffering, yet I can render your existence so agonizing that you will curse the very sight of the sun. You are the source of my being, but I am the commander of your fate; yield!
source_text: “Slave, I before reasoned with you, but you have proved yourself unworthy of my condescension. Remember that I have power; you believe yourself miserable, but I can make you so wretched that the light of day will be hateful to you. You are my creator, but I am your master; obey!”

text: The shadows of twilight deepened as I remained upon the strand, consuming a humble wafer of grain to sate a hunger that had become quite piercing. Presently, a small craft neared the bank, and a boatman delivered a bundle of mail into my hands; there were dispatches from Lucerne, including a fervent appeal from Adrian, who begged me to return to his side. He confessed that he was squandering his days in aimless waiting, and that his partners in Bristol were demanding his arrival to conclude the contracts for his merchant venture in the Levant. He could delay his journey no longer; however, since his trip to the city might be the prelude to a far more distant voyage, he prayed that I might grant him the favor of my company while time allowed. He urged me, therefore, to forsake my lonely crag and meet him in Aberdeen, that we might travel toward the southern counties together. This communication served to rouse my flagging spirits, and I vowed to quit my isolated refuge within two days’ time.
source_text: The sun had far descended, and I still sat on the shore, satisfying my appetite, which had become ravenous, with an oaten cake, when I saw a fishing-boat land close to me, and one of the men brought me a packet; it contained letters from Geneva, and one from Clerval entreating me to join him. He said that he was wearing away his time fruitlessly where he was, that letters from the friends he had formed in London desired his return to complete the negotiation they had entered into for his Indian enterprise. He could not any longer delay his departure; but as his journey to London might be followed, even sooner than he now conjectured, by his longer voyage, he entreated me to bestow as much of my society on him as I could spare. He besought me, therefore, to leave my solitary isle and to meet him at Perth, that we might proceed southwards together. This letter in a degree recalled me to life, and I determined to quit my island at the expiration of two days.

text: The transformation of my inner state since the night the creature appeared was absolute. I had previously regarded my promise with a sense of hopeless resignation, believing it a burden that must be endured regardless of the cost; now, however, it felt as though a shroud had been lifted from my sight, granting me a sudden, piercing clarity. Not for a single moment did I consider returning to my work. Though the monster’s threats haunted my thoughts, I refused to believe that any voluntary action of mine should be taken to avert his wrath. I had resolved that to fashion another being like the first would be an act of the most profound and wicked selfishness, and I cast out every impulse that might have swayed me from this conviction.
source_text: Nothing could be more complete than the alteration that had taken place in my feelings since the night of the appearance of the dæmon. I had before regarded my promise with a gloomy despair as a thing that, with whatever consequences, must be fulfilled; but I now felt as if a film had been taken from before my eyes and that I for the first time saw clearly. The idea of renewing my labours did not for one instant occur to me; the threat I had heard weighed on my thoughts, but I did not reflect that a voluntary act of mine could avert it. I had resolved in my own mind that to create another like the fiend I had first made would be an act of the basest and most atrocious selfishness, and I banished from my mind every thought that could lead to a different conclusion.

text: Several hours elapsed in this fashion; but slowly, as the sun dipped toward the horizon, the wind softened into a light breeze and the breaking waves subsided. These were replaced by a heavy, rolling swell; I felt overcome by nausea and could scarcely maintain my grip on the rudder, when suddenly I glimpsed the silhouette of high land toward the south.
source_text: Some hours passed thus; but by degrees, as the sun declined towards the horizon, the wind died away into a gentle breeze and the sea became free from breakers. But these gave place to a heavy swell; I felt sick and hardly able to hold the rudder, when suddenly I saw a line of high land towards the south.

text: As this singular exchange persisted, I noted the throng of bystanders swelling in number. Their visages betrayed a volatile blend of inquisitiveness and resentment, a sight that stirred within me both a sense of harassment and a deepening dread. I sought the direction of the local tavern, yet my appeals were met with a stubborn silence. I attempted to advance, but a low rumble of voices rose from the mob as they trailed my steps and began to encircle me; just then, a fellow of coarse appearance stepped forward, laying a heavy hand upon my shoulder. "Come, sir," he commanded, "you must accompany me to Magistrate Vance's to offer a reckoning of your conduct."
source_text: While this strange dialogue continued, I perceived the crowd rapidly increase. Their faces expressed a mixture of curiosity and anger, which annoyed and in some degree alarmed me. I inquired the way to the inn, but no one replied. I then moved forward, and a murmuring sound arose from the crowd as they followed and surrounded me, when an ill-looking man approaching tapped me on the shoulder and said, “Come, sir, you must follow me to Mr. Kirwin’s to give an account of yourself.”

text: A violent delirium followed in the wake of these horrors. For two months, I lingered at the very threshold of the grave; my frantic utterances, I was later informed, were ghastly to endure, as I proclaimed myself the slayer of Arthur, of Elena, and of Sterling. At times, I beseeched my caretakers to help me extinguish the demon that haunted my existence; at others, I felt the phantom hands of the beast constricting my throat, and I howled in a convulsion of agony and fright. It was a singular mercy that I spoke in my mother tongue, for only Magistrate Thorne grasped the meaning of my words; yet my wild thrashing and desolate cries were more than enough to petrify the other onlookers.
source_text: A fever succeeded to this. I lay for two months on the point of death; my ravings, as I afterwards heard, were frightful; I called myself the murderer of William, of Justine, and of Clerval. Sometimes I entreated my attendants to assist me in the destruction of the fiend by whom I was tormented; and at others I felt the fingers of the monster already grasping my neck, and screamed aloud with agony and terror. Fortunately, as I spoke my native language, Mr. Kirwin alone understood me; but my gestures and bitter cries were sufficient to affright the other witnesses.

text: When the phantoms drifting through my mind solidified into focus, a burning ague seized me; a thick gloom weighed upon my chest. I was bereft of any tender voice to soothe my soul or any familiar hand to offer support. The surgeon visited to order his remedies, which an aged nurse concocted, yet he showed only a cold neglect while her countenance betrayed a singular cruelty. Who would concern themselves with the salvation of a killer, save for the executioner eager for his coin?
source_text: As the images that floated before me became more distinct, I grew feverish; a darkness pressed around me; no one was near me who soothed me with the gentle voice of love; no dear hand supported me. The physician came and prescribed medicines, and the old woman prepared them for me; but utter carelessness was visible in the first, and the expression of brutality was strongly marked in the visage of the second. Who could be interested in the fate of a murderer but the hangman who would gain his fee?

text: The time for the court sessions drew near. I had been incarcerated for three months already, and though I remained frail and haunted by the threat of another physical collapse, I was forced to travel nearly a hundred miles to the regional capital where the proceedings were to take place. Mr. Kirwin took it upon himself to manage every detail of my defense, from locating witnesses to preparing the case. I was fortunate enough to be spared the humiliation of a public trial, as the charges never reached a court with the power of life and death. The grand jury ultimately dismissed the indictment after evidence proved I was still in the Orkney Islands at the hour my friend's body was first found; two weeks after my arrival at the court town, I was released from custody.
source_text: The season of the assizes approached. I had already been three months in prison, and although I was still weak and in continual danger of a relapse, I was obliged to travel nearly a hundred miles to the country town where the court was held. Mr. Kirwin charged himself with every care of collecting witnesses and arranging my defence. I was spared the disgrace of appearing publicly as a criminal, as the case was not brought before the court that decides on life and death. The grand jury rejected the bill, on its being proved that I was on the Orkney Islands at the hour the body of my friend was found; and a fortnight after my removal I was liberated from prison.

text: Nevertheless, because I pressed for our departure from Ireland with such anxious desperation, my father decided it was wisest to give in. We secured passage on a ship bound for Havre-de-Grace and set sail from the Irish coast under a favorable wind. It was the middle of the night. I remained on deck, gazing up at the constellations and listening to the rhythm of the crashing waves. I welcomed the shroud of darkness that hid Ireland from my view, and my heart raced with a frantic delight at the thought of returning to Geneva. The horrors of the past felt like a ghastly nightmare; however, the wooden deck beneath me, the wind carrying me from that cursed shore, and the vast ocean around me served as grim reminders that this was no illusion. Clerval, my most beloved friend, had truly perished, a casualty of my own actions and the fiend I had brought to life. I traced the path of my existence in my mind: the peaceful years with my family in Geneva, the loss of my mother, and my move to Ingolstadt. I trembled as I recalled the manic fervor that drove me to assemble my ghastly foe, and I envisioned again that night he first drew breath. I could not bear to continue these reflections; a surge of agonizing emotions overcame me, and I broke into bitter tears.
source_text: Still, as I urged our leaving Ireland with such inquietude and impatience, my father thought it best to yield. We took our passage on board a vessel bound for Havre-de-Grace and sailed with a fair wind from the Irish shores. It was midnight. I lay on the deck looking at the stars and listening to the dashing of the waves. I hailed the darkness that shut Ireland from my sight, and my pulse beat with a feverish joy when I reflected that I should soon see Geneva. The past appeared to me in the light of a frightful dream; yet the vessel in which I was, the wind that blew me from the detested shore of Ireland, and the sea which surrounded me, told me too forcibly that I was deceived by no vision and that Clerval, my friend and dearest companion, had fallen a victim to me and the monster of my creation. I repassed, in my memory, my whole life; my quiet happiness while residing with my family in Geneva, the death of my mother, and my departure for Ingolstadt. I remembered, shuddering, the mad enthusiasm that hurried me on to the creation of my hideous enemy, and I called to mind the night in which he first lived. I was unable to pursue the train of thought; a thousand feelings pressed upon me, and I wept bitterly.

text: Throughout the long months of my detention, my sire had frequently listened to these same frantic protestations. At times, he appeared eager to probe the depths of my meaning; at others, he dismissed my words as the lingering echoes of a brain-fever, believing some dark hallucination born of my sickness had taken root in my recovering mind. I shunned all inquiry, guarding the grim truth of the entity I had summoned into being with a steadfast tongue. I was convinced that any such revelation would be branded as madness, a fear that effectively sealed my lips. Furthermore, I lacked the heart to betray a mystery so foul that it would surely poison my listener’s soul with a permanent and unnatural dread. Thus, I stifled my desperate longing for a kindred spirit’s pity, remaining mute when I would have surrendered everything to share my heavy burden. Nevertheless, such cries as I have described would occasionally escape me against my will. I could provide no context for them, yet their utterance offered a faint respite from the crushing weight of my hidden grief.
source_text: My father had often, during my imprisonment, heard me make the same assertion; when I thus accused myself, he sometimes seemed to desire an explanation, and at others he appeared to consider it as the offspring of delirium, and that, during my illness, some idea of this kind had presented itself to my imagination, the remembrance of which I preserved in my convalescence. I avoided explanation and maintained a continual silence concerning the wretch I had created. I had a persuasion that I should be supposed mad, and this in itself would for ever have chained my tongue. But, besides, I could not bring myself to disclose a secret which would fill my hearer with consternation and make fear and unnatural horror the inmates of his breast. I checked, therefore, my impatient thirst for sympathy and was silent when I would have given the world to have confided the fatal secret. Yet, still, words like those I have recorded would burst uncontrollably from me. I could offer no explanation of them, but their truth in part relieved the burden of my mysterious woe.

text: My father’s reaction to my words confirmed his fear that my sanity was failing, and he swiftly redirected our dialogue in an effort to guide my mind toward more stable ground. He was determined to suppress the memory of the tragedies we had faced in Ireland, choosing to never mention them himself and forbidding me from speaking of my own suffering.
source_text: The conclusion of this speech convinced my father that my ideas were deranged, and he instantly changed the subject of our conversation and endeavoured to alter the course of my thoughts. He wished as much as possible to obliterate the memory of the scenes that had taken place in Ireland and never alluded to them or suffered me to speak of my misfortunes.

text: Nevertheless, I worry that the very sentiments which caused you such anguish a year ago still endure, perhaps even strengthened by the passage of time. I am loath to trouble you now, while you are so burdened by recent tragedies, but a conversation I had with my uncle before he went away makes it vital that I offer some explanation before we meet again.
source_text: “Yet I fear that the same feelings now exist that made you so miserable a year ago, even perhaps augmented by time. I would not disturb you at this period, when so many misfortunes weigh upon you, but a conversation that I had with my uncle previous to his departure renders some explanation necessary before we meet.

text: Driven by such reflections, I penned a letter to Clara. My words were composed and filled with devotion. “I am haunted by the fear, my dearest,” I wrote, “that our earthly portion of joy is nearly spent; yet whatever light remains to me is reflected solely from you. Cast aside your fleeting worries; I dedicate my life and every pursuit of tranquility to you alone. I carry a solitary secret, Clara, a most grisly one; when it is at last disclosed, it will strike a deathly chill into your soul, and you will cease to wonder at my grief, marveling instead that I have not succumbed to the trials I have borne. I shall surrender this chronicle of pain and terror to you on the day after our vows are exchanged, for, my sweet companion, our union demands an absolute transparency. But until that moment arrives, I beseech you, do not speak of it. This I ask with the utmost gravity, confident that you will honor my plea.”
source_text: In this state of mind I wrote to Elizabeth. My letter was calm and affectionate. “I fear, my beloved girl,” I said, “little happiness remains for us on earth; yet all that I may one day enjoy is centred in you. Chase away your idle fears; to you alone do I consecrate my life and my endeavours for contentment. I have one secret, Elizabeth, a dreadful one; when revealed to you, it will chill your frame with horror, and then, far from being surprised at my misery, you will only wonder that I survive what I have endured. I will confide this tale of misery and terror to you the day after our marriage shall take place, for, my sweet cousin, there must be perfect confidence between us. But until then, I conjure you, do not mention or allude to it. This I most earnestly entreat, and I know you will comply.”

text: The brief peace I had found soon withered away. My memories ushered in a state of frenzy, and whenever I reflected on what had transpired, a true delirium seized my mind. I swung between fits of searing rage and depths of hollow despair. I became silent and unseeing, sitting in a frozen stupor, paralyzed by the overwhelming weight of the tragedies that had befallen me.
source_text: The tranquillity which I now enjoyed did not endure. Memory brought madness with it, and when I thought of what had passed, a real insanity possessed me; sometimes I was furious and burnt with rage, sometimes low and despondent. I neither spoke nor looked at anyone, but sat motionless, bewildered by the multitude of miseries that overcame me.

text: Merciful Heaven! Had I even for a fleeting second fathomed the demonic plot of my cruel antagonist, I would have chosen eternal banishment from my ancestral home, roaming the earth as a lonely fugitive, rather than have entered into this tragic covenant. But, as though he wielded some unholy glamour, the wretch had hidden his true aim from my vision; and while I imagined I was merely inviting my own execution, I was in truth precipitating the slaughter of a much more beloved soul.
source_text: Great God! If for one instant I had thought what might be the hellish intention of my fiendish adversary, I would rather have banished myself for ever from my native country and wandered a friendless outcast over the earth than have consented to this miserable marriage. But, as if possessed of magic powers, the monster had blinded me to his real intentions; and when I thought that I had prepared only my own death, I hastened that of a far dearer victim.

text: During that interval, I employed every possible safeguard to protect my life against a direct assault from my tormentor. I kept a pair of flintlocks and a keen blade always within reach, remaining perpetually alert to any trickery, and through this constant preparation, I found a renewed sense of composure. In truth, as the date neared, the creature’s menace began to feel like a phantom of my own imagination, unworthy of troubling my mind, while the bliss I sought in my nuptials seemed increasingly assured. As the hour of the ceremony approached and the world spoke of our union as a certainty beyond the reach of fate, I grew convinced that no tragedy could possibly hinder our joy.
source_text: In the meantime I took every precaution to defend my person in case the fiend should openly attack me. I carried pistols and a dagger constantly about me and was ever on the watch to prevent artifice, and by these means gained a greater degree of tranquillity. Indeed, as the period approached, the threat appeared more as a delusion, not to be regarded as worthy to disturb my peace, while the happiness I hoped for in my marriage wore a greater appearance of certainty as the day fixed for its solemnisation drew nearer and I heard it continually spoken of as an occurrence which no accident could possibly prevent.

text: Once the marriage rites were concluded, a great company gathered at my father’s house, yet it was arranged that Elizabeth and I would begin our journey by water, resting that night at Evian and continuing our travels the next morning. The day was bright and the wind proved steady; everything seemed to smile upon our wedding departure.
source_text: After the ceremony was performed a large party assembled at my father’s, but it was agreed that Elizabeth and I should commence our journey by water, sleeping that night at Evian and continuing our voyage on the following day. The day was fair, the wind favourable; all smiled on our nuptial embarkation.

text: “Try to be at peace, my dearest Victor,” Elizabeth said. “I trust there is nothing left to trouble your mind. Please believe that even if my face doesn't radiate a visible joy, I am truly content in my heart. A small voice inside warns me not to place too much faith in the future we've envisioned, yet I refuse to listen to such dark premonitions. Notice how quickly we are traveling and how the clouds, drifting around the summit of Mont Blanc, make this beautiful landscape even more captivating. Watch the countless fish darting through the transparent water; it is so clear we can see every single stone on the lakebed. What a glorious day this is! The whole natural world seems so joyful and calm.”
source_text: “Be happy, my dear Victor,” replied Elizabeth; “there is, I hope, nothing to distress you; and be assured that if a lively joy is not painted in my face, my heart is contented. Something whispers to me not to depend too much on the prospect that is opened before us, but I will not listen to such a sinister voice. Observe how fast we move along and how the clouds, which sometimes obscure and sometimes rise above the dome of Mont Blanc, render this scene of beauty still more interesting. Look also at the innumerable fish that are swimming in the clear waters, where we can distinguish every pebble that lies at the bottom. What a divine day! How happy and serene all nature appears!”

text: The gale, which until this hour had propelled our craft with singular velocity, slackened at twilight into a faint respiration; the mild air scarcely creased the surface of the deep and woke a gentle shivering among the coastal groves as we drew near the strand, from whence it carried the fragrant essence of new-mown fields and wild blossoms. The sun dipped below the world’s edge as our keel met the earth, and the instant I stepped upon the solid ground, those dormant agonies and terrors stirred anew—shadows destined to seize me in a grip that would never again be loosened.
source_text: The wind, which had hitherto carried us along with amazing rapidity, sank at sunset to a light breeze; the soft air just ruffled the water and caused a pleasant motion among the trees as we approached the shore, from which it wafted the most delightful scent of flowers and hay. The sun sank beneath the horizon as we landed, and as I touched the shore I felt those cares and fears revive which soon were to clasp me and cling to me for ever.

text: After she went inside, I spent some time pacing the hallways of the house, searching every corner that might offer a hiding place for my enemy. Finding no sign of him, I began to hope that some stroke of luck had prevented him from carrying out his threats, when suddenly a sharp, terrifying shriek rang out. It came from the room where Elizabeth had gone to rest. The moment I heard it, the full horror of the situation crashed down on me; my arms fell limp, and every muscle in my body froze. I could feel the blood crawling through my veins, a cold tingling sensation reaching my very fingertips. This paralysis lasted only a second; the scream echoed again, and I bolted toward the room.
source_text: She left me, and I continued some time walking up and down the passages of the house and inspecting every corner that might afford a retreat to my adversary. But I discovered no trace of him and was beginning to conjecture that some fortunate chance had intervened to prevent the execution of his menaces when suddenly I heard a shrill and dreadful scream. It came from the room into which Elizabeth had retired. As I heard it, the whole truth rushed into my mind, my arms dropped, the motion of every muscle and fibre was suspended; I could feel the blood trickling in my veins and tingling in the extremities of my limbs. This state lasted but for an instant; the scream was repeated, and I rushed into the room.

text: Almighty God! Why did death not claim me in that instant? Why must I remain to tell of the destruction of the gentlest spirit and the most perfect being to ever walk the earth? She lay there, limp and devoid of life, sprawled across the bed with her head hanging low, her ashen and mangled features partially masked by her own hair. Wherever I look, I am haunted by that same vision—her pale, slack form discarded by the killer upon her bridal bier. How could I see this and survive? Truly, life is a cruel, persistent thing that refuses to let go even when it is most loathed. For a brief moment, my mind went dark, and I collapsed onto the floor, senseless.
source_text: Great God! Why did I not then expire! Why am I here to relate the destruction of the best hope and the purest creature on earth? She was there, lifeless and inanimate, thrown across the bed, her head hanging down and her pale and distorted features half covered by her hair. Everywhere I turn I see the same figure—her bloodless arms and relaxed form flung by the murderer on its bridal bier. Could I behold this and live? Alas! Life is obstinate and clings closest where it is most hated. For a moment only did I lose recollection; I fell senseless on the ground.

text: Freedom, however, would have remained a sterile mercy had my restoration to sanity not coincided with a hunger for vengeance. As the shadow of my former calamities lengthened over my mind, I contemplated their source—the grotesque entity of my own forging, the vile apparition I had cast into the world to be my ruin. A delirious fury seized my heart at the mere thought of him, and I fervently entreated the heavens that he might fall into my clutches, allowing me to execute a terrible and absolute judgment upon his accursed life.
source_text: Liberty, however, had been a useless gift to me, had I not, as I awakened to reason, at the same time awakened to revenge. As the memory of past misfortunes pressed upon me, I began to reflect on their cause—the monster whom I had created, the miserable dæmon whom I had sent abroad into the world for my destruction. I was possessed by a maddening rage when I thought of him, and desired and ardently prayed that I might have him within my grasp to wreak a great and signal revenge on his cursed head.

text: “I am grateful,” I answered. “Please attend, then, to the statement I must deliver. It is a narrative so extraordinary that I would fear you might find it unbelievable, were it not for the inherent power of truth which, however incredible, demands belief. The events are too logically linked to be dismissed as a dream, and I have no reason to lie.” My demeanor as I spoke was solemn yet composed; I had already vowed within my heart to hunt my tormentor to the death, and this resolution stilled my agony, briefly reconciling me to existence. I then recounted my history concisely but with steady precision, noting each date accurately and never lapsing into bitter insults or emotional outbursts.
source_text: “I thank you,” replied I; “listen, therefore, to the deposition that I have to make. It is indeed a tale so strange that I should fear you would not credit it were there not something in truth which, however wonderful, forces conviction. The story is too connected to be mistaken for a dream, and I have no motive for falsehood.” My manner as I thus addressed him was impressive but calm; I had formed in my own heart a resolution to pursue my destroyer to death, and this purpose quieted my agony and for an interval reconciled me to life. I now related my history briefly but with firmness and precision, marking the dates with accuracy and never deviating into invective or exclamation.

text: My words brought about a noticeable shift in the expression of my listener. He had attended to my narrative with the vague acceptance one reserves for myths and hauntings; however, as soon as he was expected to intervene in an official capacity, his doubt returned in full force. Still, he responded with kindness, saying, "I would gladly offer any help I could in your hunt, but the being you have described seems to possess strengths that would mock my every effort. How could we possibly pursue a creature that roams across glaciers and dwells in depths where no man would ever risk going? Additionally, months have gone by since his crimes were committed, and it is impossible to know where he has traveled or what land he might be hiding in now."
source_text: This address caused a considerable change in the physiognomy of my own auditor. He had heard my story with that half kind of belief that is given to a tale of spirits and supernatural events; but when he was called upon to act officially in consequence, the whole tide of his incredulity returned. He, however, answered mildly, “I would willingly afford you every aid in your pursuit, but the creature of whom you speak appears to have powers which would put all my exertions to defiance. Who can follow an animal which can traverse the sea of ice and inhabit caves and dens where no man would venture to intrude? Besides, some months have elapsed since the commission of his crimes, and no one can conjecture to what place he has wandered or what region he may now inhabit.”

text: The life I led during those days was utterly repugnant to me, and only in the sanctuary of sleep could I experience any sense of gladness. O, holy rest! Many times, when my despair was most profound, I would fall into a slumber where my dreams offered a comfort bordering on bliss. It seemed as if protective spirits had arranged these fleeting hours of peace to ensure I possessed the fortitude to finish my grim journey. Had I been denied such respite, I would have certainly collapsed beneath my burdens. During the daylight hours, I was buoyed by the promise of the dark, for in my dreams I walked again with my friends, my wife, and through the landscapes of my native land. I saw once more the gentle face of my father, heard the sweet, clear notes of Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval in all his youthful vitality. Often, when worn down by a grueling day's march, I told myself that the sunlit world was the illusion and that I would wake to the truth only when night restored me to the arms of those I held dear. What a piercing, desperate love I bore for them! I reached out to their beloved ghosts even when I was awake, convincing myself they had never truly left me. At such times, the vengeance that scorched my heart would grow cold, and I followed the trail of the monster more as a divinely appointed chore—a mechanical impulse of some hidden force—than as the fervent obsession of my own spirit.
source_text: My life, as it passed thus, was indeed hateful to me, and it was during sleep alone that I could taste joy. O blessed sleep! Often, when most miserable, I sank to repose, and my dreams lulled me even to rapture. The spirits that guarded me had provided these moments, or rather hours, of happiness that I might retain strength to fulfil my pilgrimage. Deprived of this respite, I should have sunk under my hardships. During the day I was sustained and inspirited by the hope of night, for in sleep I saw my friends, my wife, and my beloved country; again I saw the benevolent countenance of my father, heard the silver tones of my Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval enjoying health and youth. Often, when wearied by a toilsome march, I persuaded myself that I was dreaming until night should come and that I should then enjoy reality in the arms of my dearest friends. What agonising fondness did I feel for them! How did I cling to their dear forms, as sometimes they haunted even my waking hours, and persuade myself that they still lived! At such moments vengeance, that burned within me, died in my heart, and I pursued my path towards the destruction of the dæmon more as a task enjoined by heaven, as the mechanical impulse of some power of which I was unconscious, than as the ardent desire of my soul.

text: Mocking demon! Once more I swear my revenge; again I condemn you, wretched creature, to a fate of agony and death. I will never abandon this hunt until either he or I have perished. Only then will I experience the joy of reuniting with Elizabeth and my lost companions, who are even now preparing to welcome me after this exhausting struggle and my long, nightmare journey.
source_text: Scoffing devil! Again do I vow vengeance; again do I devote thee, miserable fiend, to torture and death. Never will I give up my search until he or I perish; and then with what ecstasy shall I join my Elizabeth and my departed friends, who even now prepare for me the reward of my tedious toil and horrible pilgrimage!

text: Continuing my trek toward the north, the snowfall intensified and the bitter cold became nearly impossible to endure. The villagers remained huddled inside their cramped dwellings, and only the most resilient ventured out to hunt the beasts that hunger had driven from their dens. With the rivers locked under sheets of ice, I could no longer catch any fish, depriving me of my primary source of nourishment.
