source_text: As I still pursued my journey to the northward, the snows thickened and the cold increased in a degree almost too severe to support. The peasants were shut up in their hovels, and only a few of the most hardy ventured forth to seize the animals whom starvation had forced from their hiding-places to seek for prey. The rivers were covered with ice, and no fish could be procured; and thus I was cut off from my chief article of maintenance.

text: Several weeks prior, I had acquired a dog-drawn sledge, enabling me to race across the snow at an astonishing speed. I cannot say if the demon possessed similar equipment, but I realized that whereas I had previously been falling further behind each day, I was now gaining ground. When the ocean finally appeared on the horizon, he was only a single day ahead, and I harbored hopes of overtaking him before he reached the coast. Driven by this new spark of resolve, I hurried onward, reaching a desolate seaside village in two days. I asked the locals about the creature and obtained specific news. They spoke of a towering monster who had arrived the night before, brandishing a rifle and several pistols, whose ghastly appearance had driven the occupants of a secluded hut to flee in terror. He had plundered their winter provisions and piled them onto a sledge; then, having rounded up a large team of sled dogs, he harnessed them and departed that same night. To the relief of the traumatized villagers, he had struck out across the frozen sea in a direction where no land lay, and they speculated that he would soon meet his end, either by the shifting ice breaking apart or by the lethal, unending frost.
source_text: Some weeks before this period I had procured a sledge and dogs and thus traversed the snows with inconceivable speed. I know not whether the fiend possessed the same advantages, but I found that, as before I had daily lost ground in the pursuit, I now gained on him, so much so that when I first saw the ocean he was but one day’s journey in advance, and I hoped to intercept him before he should reach the beach. With new courage, therefore, I pressed on, and in two days arrived at a wretched hamlet on the seashore. I inquired of the inhabitants concerning the fiend and gained accurate information. A gigantic monster, they said, had arrived the night before, armed with a gun and many pistols, putting to flight the inhabitants of a solitary cottage through fear of his terrific appearance. He had carried off their store of winter food, and placing it in a sledge, to draw which he had seized on a numerous drove of trained dogs, he had harnessed them, and the same night, to the joy of the horror-struck villagers, had pursued his journey across the sea in a direction that led to no land; and they conjectured that he must speedily be destroyed by the breaking of the ice or frozen by the eternal frosts.

text: The count of days has long escaped me, yet I have weathered a wretchedness that only the inextinguishable thirst for a righteous vengeance could have steeled me to bear. Great, jagged ramparts of ice frequently choked my progress, and the deafening boom of the churning depths below seemed to herald my doom. Yet, the biting air would descend once more, knitting the fractured waves into a firm and traversable floor.
source_text: I cannot guess how many days have passed since then, but I have endured misery which nothing but the eternal sentiment of a just retribution burning within my heart could have enabled me to support. Immense and rugged mountains of ice often barred up my passage, and I often heard the thunder of the ground sea, which threatened my destruction. But again the frost came and made the paths of the sea secure.

text: Judging by the amount of food I had consumed, I estimated that my journey had lasted about three weeks. The constant delay of my expectations weighed heavily on my spirit, frequently drawing bitter tears of hopelessness from my eyes. Despair had nearly seized its victim, and I was close to collapsing under the weight of such wretchedness. On one occasion, after my exhausted animals had struggled with immense effort to reach the peak of a frozen slope, one of them succumbed to fatigue and died. I stared out at the vastness before me in agony, until my gaze suddenly locked onto a dark spot on the gloomy horizon. I squinted to make out what it was and let out a frantic cry of joy when I identified a sledge and the hulking, familiar silhouette within. A searing wave of hope suddenly rushed through me. Hot tears clouded my vision, and I brushed them away quickly so I wouldn't lose sight of the creature; yet the stinging drops continued to blur my eyes until, finally surrendering to my overwhelming emotions, I began to weep aloud.
source_text: By the quantity of provision which I had consumed, I should guess that I had passed three weeks in this journey; and the continual protraction of hope, returning back upon the heart, often wrung bitter drops of despondency and grief from my eyes. Despair had indeed almost secured her prey, and I should soon have sunk beneath this misery. Once, after the poor animals that conveyed me had with incredible toil gained the summit of a sloping ice mountain, and one, sinking under his fatigue, died, I viewed the expanse before me with anguish, when suddenly my eye caught a dark speck upon the dusky plain. I strained my sight to discover what it could be and uttered a wild cry of ecstasy when I distinguished a sledge and the distorted proportions of a well-known form within. Oh! With what a burning gush did hope revisit my heart! Warm tears filled my eyes, which I hastily wiped away, that they might not intercept the view I had of the dæmon; but still my sight was dimmed by the burning drops, until, giving way to the emotions that oppressed me, I wept aloud.

text: But I could not afford to hesitate. I uncoupled the dead dog from the harness, provided the survivors with a substantial meal, and after an hour’s rest—a delay that was as vital as it was unbearable—I resumed my pursuit. The sledge ahead remained visible, vanishing only momentarily behind the towering ridges of the ice. I was undeniably closing the distance, and when, after nearly two days of travel, I finally saw my enemy just a mile away, my heart leaped within my chest.
source_text: But this was not the time for delay; I disencumbered the dogs of their dead companion, gave them a plentiful portion of food, and after an hour’s rest, which was absolutely necessary, and yet which was bitterly irksome to me, I continued my route. The sledge was still visible, nor did I again lose sight of it except at the moments when for a short time some ice-rock concealed it with its intervening crags. I indeed perceptibly gained on it, and when, after nearly two days’ journey, I beheld my enemy at no more than a mile distant, my heart bounded within me.

text: Frankenstein realized I was documenting his history; he asked to review the pages and subsequently corrected and expanded them himself, focusing especially on restoring the true vigor of the dialogues he had with his nemesis. “Since you have seen fit to preserve my story,” he remarked, “I would not have a distorted version pass on to future generations.”
source_text: Frankenstein discovered that I made notes concerning his history; he asked to see them and then himself corrected and augmented them in many places, but principally in giving the life and spirit to the conversations he held with his enemy. “Since you have preserved my narration,” said he, “I would not that a mutilated one should go down to posterity.”

text: In my youth," he remarked, "I felt certain that I was meant for a monumental undertaking. While my emotions run deep, I once held a clarity of mind that made me capable of extraordinary feats. This sense of my own value sustained me where others might have faltered; I considered it a sin to squander in idle sorrow the abilities that could benefit humanity. Considering my finished labor—the animation of a sentient and reasoning being—I could not view myself as merely another ordinary dreamer. Yet that very pride, which bolstered me at the start, now only casts me deeper into despair. My ambitions and dreams have vanished, and much like the celestial being who craved absolute power, I am now bound in a perpetual torment. I possessed a fertile imagination alongside a rigorous capacity for logic and execution; through the marriage of these traits, I envisioned and then brought a man to life. Even today, I cannot recall the visions I had during that unfinished labor without a surge of emotion. My mind soared through the heavens, at times rejoicing in my own strength and at others consumed by the potential impact of my discovery. Since childhood, I was filled with grand expectations and a towering drive, yet look at how far I have fallen. Oh, my friend, had you encountered me in my former self, you would find me unrecognizable in this wretched condition. Melancholy seldom touched me; I felt carried forward by a grand fate, until I collapsed, never to stand again.
source_text: “When younger,” said he, “I believed myself destined for some great enterprise. My feelings are profound, but I possessed a coolness of judgment that fitted me for illustrious achievements. This sentiment of the worth of my nature supported me when others would have been oppressed, for I deemed it criminal to throw away in useless grief those talents that might be useful to my fellow creatures. When I reflected on the work I had completed, no less a one than the creation of a sensitive and rational animal, I could not rank myself with the herd of common projectors. But this thought, which supported me in the commencement of my career, now serves only to plunge me lower in the dust. All my speculations and hopes are as nothing, and like the archangel who aspired to omnipotence, I am chained in an eternal hell. My imagination was vivid, yet my powers of analysis and application were intense; by the union of these qualities I conceived the idea and executed the creation of a man. Even now I cannot recollect without passion my reveries while the work was incomplete. I trod heaven in my thoughts, now exulting in my powers, now burning with the idea of their effects. From my infancy I was imbued with high hopes and a lofty ambition; but how am I sunk! Oh! My friend, if you had known me as I once was, you would not recognise me in this state of degradation. Despondency rarely visited my heart; a high destiny seemed to bear me on, until I fell, never, never again to rise.”

text: I am grateful to you, Walton," he said, "for the kindness you show such a broken man. But when you talk of forming new bonds and finding new loves, do you truly believe anyone can replace those who are gone? Could any man ever be to me what Clerval was, or any woman become another Elizabeth? Even when there is no exceptional excellence to draw us in, the companions of our youth hold a specific power over our minds that a later friend can rarely achieve. They understand our natural temperaments, which, though they may be altered over time, are never truly erased; thus, they can judge our actions with a much clearer understanding of our true motives. A brother or sister would never suspect the other of dishonesty—unless such traits were evident from the start—while a newer friend, no matter how devoted, might still be viewed with an involuntary hint of suspicion. My friends were dear to me not only through long association, but because of their own inherent virtues; wherever I wander, Elizabeth’s gentle voice and Clerval’s conversation still echo in my ears. They are dead, and in this desolation, only one purpose keeps me alive. If I were committed to some noble cause or a design that benefited humanity, I might find a reason to live. But that is not my fate; I must hunt down and destroy the creature I brought into existence. Only then will my purpose on earth be complete, and I can finally welcome death.
source_text: “I thank you, Walton,” he said, “for your kind intentions towards so miserable a wretch; but when you speak of new ties and fresh affections, think you that any can replace those who are gone? Can any man be to me as Clerval was, or any woman another Elizabeth? Even where the affections are not strongly moved by any superior excellence, the companions of our childhood always possess a certain power over our minds which hardly any later friend can obtain. They know our infantine dispositions, which, however they may be afterwards modified, are never eradicated; and they can judge of our actions with more certain conclusions as to the integrity of our motives. A sister or a brother can never, unless indeed such symptoms have been shown early, suspect the other of fraud or false dealing, when another friend, however strongly he may be attached, may, in spite of himself, be contemplated with suspicion. But I enjoyed friends, dear not only through habit and association, but from their own merits; and wherever I am, the soothing voice of my Elizabeth and the conversation of Clerval will be ever whispered in my ear. They are dead, and but one feeling in such a solitude can persuade me to preserve my life. If I were engaged in any high undertaking or design, fraught with extensive utility to my fellow creatures, then could I live to fulfil it. But such is not my destiny; I must pursue and destroy the being to whom I gave existence; then my lot on earth will be fulfilled and I may die.”

text: I am writing to you while besieged by danger, uncertain if I shall ever again behold my beloved England or the even dearer friends who dwell there. I am hemmed in by towering peaks of ice that offer no path of escape and threaten to shatter my vessel at any moment. The valiant men I persuaded to join me look to me for some sign of rescue, yet I have no help to offer them. There is something profoundly horrific about our predicament, and yet my spirit and my hopes have not yet failed me. Still, it is an agonizing reflection that the lives of all these souls are placed in such jeopardy because of me. Should we perish, my own reckless ambitions are the cause.
source_text: I write to you, encompassed by peril and ignorant whether I am ever doomed to see again dear England and the dearer friends that inhabit it. I am surrounded by mountains of ice which admit of no escape and threaten every moment to crush my vessel. The brave fellows whom I have persuaded to be my companions look towards me for aid, but I have none to bestow. There is something terribly appalling in our situation, yet my courage and hopes do not desert me. Yet it is terrible to reflect that the lives of all these men are endangered through me. If we are lost, my mad schemes are the cause.

text: His oratory was delivered with a resonance so perfectly attuned to the shifting passions of his soul, and with a gaze so alight with noble ambition, that it was no marvel the mariners stood transfixed. They traded wordless glances, their tongues bound by the sheer weight of his conviction. I stepped forward then, urging them to withdraw and weigh his exhortations in the quiet of their own minds; I pledged that I would not force our prow deeper into the frozen latitudes should they remain set against it, yet I harbored a silent hope that, with prayerful reflection, their guttering spirits might once more catch fire.
source_text: He spoke this with a voice so modulated to the different feelings expressed in his speech, with an eye so full of lofty design and heroism, that can you wonder that these men were moved? They looked at one another and were unable to reply. I spoke; I told them to retire and consider of what had been said, that I would not lead them farther north if they strenuously desired the contrary, but that I hoped that, with reflection, their courage would return.

text: “And do you imagine,” the creature asked, “that I was then numb to pain and regret? He,” he continued, gesturing toward the corpse, “did not suffer in the final completion of the act—not a fraction of the agony that was mine during the slow, grueling process of its fulfillment. A ghastly selfishness drove me onward, even while my soul was poisoned by remorse. Do you truly think that Clerval’s cries were like music to my ears? My heart was designed for love and empathy, and when it was twisted by wretchedness into malice and hate, it did not endure that transformation without a level of torture you cannot even conceive.”
source_text: “And do you dream?” said the dæmon. “Do you think that I was then dead to agony and remorse? He,” he continued, pointing to the corpse, “he suffered not in the consummation of the deed. Oh! Not the ten-thousandth portion of the anguish that was mine during the lingering detail of its execution. A frightful selfishness hurried me on, while my heart was poisoned with remorse. Think you that the groans of Clerval were music to my ears? My heart was fashioned to be susceptible of love and sympathy, and when wrenched by misery to vice and hatred, it did not endure the violence of the change without torture such as you cannot even imagine.

text: “Adieu! I depart from your side, and in your silent form I look upon the final vestige of humanity my sight shall ever encounter. Farewell, Thorne! If life still pulsed within you, and with it the old hunger for my ruin, that malice would be more deeply glutted by my continued breath than by my demise. But your intent was otherwise; you sought my end only to forestall a greater harvest of sorrow. And if, through some mystery beyond my grasp, your spirit still perceives and suffers, you could never summon a wrath more cruel than the one I now harbor against myself. Broken as you were, my own misery remains the greater burden, for the acid of my guilt shall never cease to burn until the cold earth seals my spirit in eternal oblivion.”
source_text: “Farewell! I leave you, and in you the last of humankind whom these eyes will ever behold. Farewell, Frankenstein! If thou wert yet alive and yet cherished a desire of revenge against me, it would be better satiated in my life than in my destruction. But it was not so; thou didst seek my extinction, that I might not cause greater wretchedness; and if yet, in some mode unknown to me, thou hadst not ceased to think and feel, thou wouldst not desire against me a vengeance greater than that which I feel. Blasted as thou wert, my agony was still superior to thine, for the bitter sting of remorse will not cease to rankle in my wounds until death shall close them for ever.

text: "Call it *obstructing* you, then, if you prefer that phrasing." He continued by explaining he was merely an assistant cook and couldn't linger to chat, though he’d welcome the chance later; he confessed he was desperately curious to learn where I’d acquired my garments. As he turned to leave, he pointed out a figure in the distance, noting that the person was surely idle enough for my needs and was probably searching for me anyway. This was a lithe, graceful boy wearing coral-colored tights that made him resemble a bifurcated carrot, while the rest of his ensemble featured blue silk, fine lace, and delicate ruffles. He had long, golden curls and wore a feathered pink satin cap perched confidently over one ear. He looked amiable but walked with an air of immense self-satisfaction. He was so striking he could have been a painting. When he reached me, he examined me with a look of playful, bold curiosity, stated he had come to find me, and mentioned that he served as a page.
source_text: “_Hinder_ me, then, if the word please thee better.” Then he went on to say he was an under-cook and could not stop to gossip, though he would like it another time; for it would comfort his very liver to know where I got my clothes. As he started away he pointed and said yonder was one who was idle enough for my purpose, and was seeking me besides, no doubt. This was an airy slim boy in shrimp-colored tights that made him look like a forked carrot, the rest of his gear was blue silk and dainty laces and ruffles; and he had long yellow curls, and wore a plumed pink satin cap tilted complacently over his ear. By his look, he was good-natured; by his gait, he was satisfied with himself. He was pretty enough to frame. He arrived, looked me over with a smiling and impudent curiosity; said he had come for me, and informed me that he was a page.

text: The encounter was quite sharp, and I felt rather irritated. Yet, he remained entirely unfazed, appearing not to notice that any offense had been given. He began to talk and laugh with a cheerful, reckless, youthful air as we walked along, making himself my fast friend immediately. He peppered me with all sorts of questions about my history and my clothing, but he never waited for a response—he simply prattled on, as though he were unaware he had even asked and expected no reply, until he finally happened to mention that he was born at the start of the year 513.
source_text: It was pretty severe, but I was nettled. However, it never phazed him; he didn't appear to know he was hurt. He began to talk and laugh, in happy, thoughtless, boyish fashion, as we walked along, and made himself old friends with me at once; asked me all sorts of questions about myself and about my clothes, but never waited for an answer--always chattered straight ahead, as if he didn't know he had asked a question and wasn't expecting any reply, until at last he happened to mention that he was born in the beginning of the year 513.

text: An admirable vigor was manifest in nearly every expression; indeed, some possessed a certain sublimity and tenderness that shamed one's trivial censures into stillness. A most exalted benevolence and untainted virtue rested upon the features of the knight titled Sir Galahad, and similarly upon those of the sovereign; furthermore, a sense of imperial splendor and distinction was evident in the massive form and stately demeanor of Sir Launcelot of the Lake.
source_text: There was a fine manliness observable in almost every face; and in some a certain loftiness and sweetness that rebuked your belittling criticisms and stilled them. A most noble benignity and purity reposed in the countenance of him they called Sir Galahad, and likewise in the king's also; and there was majesty and greatness in the giant frame and high bearing of Sir Launcelot of the Lake.

text: I watched the lad with a sense of pity, noting the veil of profound sadness that stole over his expression. Glancing where he looked, I saw a venerable man with a long white beard, garbed in a loose black habit, who had risen and now stood by the table on precarious legs. His ancient head swayed with a frail motion as his dim, drifting eyes roved over the gathering. The same look of wretchedness I had seen in the young servant was visible on every face in the room—the silent endurance of brute animals who recognize that they must suffer without complaint.
source_text: I looked at the boy in sorrow; and as I looked I saw the cloud of a deep despondency settle upon his countenance. I followed the direction of his eye, and saw that a very old and white-bearded man, clothed in a flowing black gown, had risen and was standing at the table upon unsteady legs, and feebly swaying his ancient head and surveying the company with his watery and wandering eye. The same suffering look that was in the page's face was observable in all the faces around--the look of dumb creatures who know that they must endure and make no moan.

text: I had fallen into a wretched state by that time; indeed, I was barely lucid enough to follow the argument breaking out over the best way to execute me. Some even questioned whether I could be killed at all, suspecting that my garments were protected by sorcery. In truth, it was merely a common fifteen-dollar suit from a discount shop. Nevertheless, I remained coherent enough to register one striking fact: the casual vocabulary employed by this gathering of the realm’s highest nobility would have scandalized a hardened warrior. To call their language "indelicacy" would be a gross understatement. Yet, having read works like *Tom Jones* and *Roderick Random*, I was aware that the English aristocracy’s speech—and the loose morals such talk implied—had not truly been scrubbed clean until a century ago. Indeed, it was not until our own nineteenth century that the first recognizable examples of refined ladies and gentlemen finally emerged in English or even European history. If Sir Walter Scott had permitted his characters to speak with their actual voices rather than scripting them himself, we would have heard dialogue from the likes of Ivanhoe or the gentle Lady Rowena that would make a modern vagrant wince. Still, to those who are unknowingly crude, everything seems perfectly polite. King Arthur’s subjects had no inkling of their own vulgarity, and I possessed enough presence of mind not to point it out to them.
source_text: I was in a dismal state by this time; indeed, I was hardly enough in my right mind to keep the run of a dispute that sprung up as to how I had better be killed, the possibility of the killing being doubted by some, because of the enchantment in my clothes. And yet it was nothing but an ordinary suit of fifteen-dollar slop-shops. Still, I was sane enough to notice this detail, to wit: many of the terms used in the most matter-of-fact way by this great assemblage of the first ladies and gentlemen in the land would have made a Comanche blush. Indelicacy is too mild a term to convey the idea. However, I had read “Tom Jones,” and “Roderick Random,” and other books of that kind, and knew that the highest and first ladies and gentlemen in England had remained little or no cleaner in their talk, and in the morals and conduct which such talk implies, clear up to a hundred years ago; in fact clear into our own nineteenth century--in which century, broadly speaking, the earliest samples of the real lady and real gentleman discoverable in English history--or in European history, for that matter--may be said to have made their appearance. Suppose Sir Walter, instead of putting the conversations into the mouths of his characters, had allowed the characters to speak for themselves? We should have had talk from Rebecca and Ivanhoe and the soft lady Rowena which would embarrass a tramp in our day. However, to the unconsciously indelicate all things are delicate. King Arthur's people were not aware that they were indecent and I had presence of mind enough not to mention it.

text: My peculiar attire had caused such a stir of consternation that the assembly seemed genuinely gratified when the venerable Silas resolved the matter with a sudden flash of pragmatism. He scoffed at their bewilderment, asking why it had never crossed their minds to simply peel the fabric away. Before I could protest, I was rendered as stark as a winter branch. It was a marvel, really: in that crowded hall, I alone suffered the pangs of shame. The courtiers poked and prodded at the sight of me with the same clinical indifference they might accord a head of livestock. Even the Duchess regarded me with a child’s frank curiosity, noting aloud that the curvature of my shins was unlike any she had previously observed. It was the only bit of flattery I received that day—if one could truly call it flattery.
source_text: They were so troubled about my enchanted clothes that they were mightily relieved, at last, when old Merlin swept the difficulty away for them with a common-sense hint. He asked them why they were so dull--why didn't it occur to them to strip me. In half a minute I was as naked as a pair of tongs! And dear, dear, to think of it: I was the only embarrassed person there. Everybody discussed me; and did it as unconcernedly as if I had been a cabbage. Queen Guenever was as naively interested as the rest, and said she had never seen anybody with legs just like mine before. It was the only compliment I got--if it was a compliment.

text: I spent a full hour agonizing over that careless mistake, berating myself with every insult I could imagine. However, it eventually dawned on me that these people lacked any sense of logic; they simply never connected the dots. Their own conversations proved they wouldn't recognize a contradiction if it stared them in the face. With that realization, my mind was finally at ease.
source_text: I worried over that heedless blunder for an hour, and called myself a great many hard names, meantime. But finally it occurred to me all of a sudden that these animals didn't reason; that _they_ never put this and that together; that all their talk showed that they didn't know a discrepancy when they saw it. I was at rest, then.

text: The moment a man finds peace in this world, he invariably stumbles into a fresh anxiety. It struck me then that I had made a serious mistake: I had dispatched the boy to frighten his superiors with a vague threat, planning to invent the specifics of the disaster at my own convenience. Yet, those most willing to swallow a miracle are always the hungriest for a demonstration. What if I were called upon for a sample? Suppose they asked me to name the coming plague? I had indeed blundered; the lie should have been ready before the messenger was gone. “What can I say to buy myself some time?” I was back in the soup, sinking into the deepest sort of trouble.
source_text: But as soon as one is at rest, in this world, off he goes on something else to worry about. It occurred to me that I had made another blunder: I had sent the boy off to alarm his betters with a threat--I intending to invent a calamity at my leisure; now the people who are the readiest and eagerest and willingest to swallow miracles are the very ones who are hungriest to see you perform them; suppose I should be called on for a sample? Suppose I should be asked to name my calamity? Yes, I had made a blunder; I ought to have invented my calamity first. “What shall I do? what can I say, to gain a little time?” I was in trouble again; in the deepest kind of trouble...

text: A thousand desperate pleas arose, but I could not shorten the delay, for I could no longer remember the duration of a total eclipse. I was deeply perplexed and needed a moment to reflect. Something about this eclipse felt wrong, a fact that was profoundly unsettling. If this wasn't the event I expected, how could I be sure if this was truly the sixth century or just a dream? Oh, if I could only prove it was the latter! A sudden, joyous hope struck me. If the boy was right about the date and today was truly the twentieth, then this wasn't the sixth century. In a state of great excitement, I caught the monk’s sleeve and asked him for the day of the month.
source_text: There were a thousand pathetic protests, but I couldn't shorten up any, for I couldn't remember how long a total eclipse lasts. I was in a puzzled condition, anyway, and wanted to think. Something was wrong about that eclipse, and the fact was very unsettling. If this wasn't the one I was after, how was I to tell whether this was the sixth century, or nothing but a dream? Dear me, if I could only prove it was the latter! Here was a glad new hope. If the boy was right about the date, and this was surely the 20th, it _wasn't_ the sixth century. I reached for the monk's sleeve, in considerable excitement, and asked him what day of the month it was.

text: I’ve reached a decision, your Majesty. To provide a necessary lesson, I will let this gloom advance and shroud the world in night; however, whether I extinguish the sun permanently or bring back its light is a choice that rests with you. My conditions are these: you will remain the sovereign of all your lands, keeping every bit of the prestige and honor that belongs to a king. In return, you must appoint me as your permanent chief executive and minister, paying me one percent of any increase in state revenue I manage to generate beyond its current figures. If I can’t survive on that, I won’t ask for a handout from anyone. Does this arrangement suit you?
source_text: “I have reflected, Sir King. For a lesson, I will let this darkness proceed, and spread night in the world; but whether I blot out the sun for good, or restore it, shall rest with you. These are the terms, to wit: You shall remain king over all your dominions, and receive all the glories and honors that belong to the kingship; but you shall appoint me your perpetual minister and executive, and give me for my services one per cent of such actual increase of revenue over and above its present amount as I may succeed in creating for the state. If I can't live on that, I sha'n't ask anybody to give me a lift. Is it satisfactory?”

text: The stratagem succeeded. It was vital to prolong the suspense until the sun was entirely blotted out; otherwise, the crowd would have pressed me to banish the gloom, a miracle I was quite unable to deliver. Sending for my robes secured a short interval, but it was insufficient. I was compelled to devise another pretext. I argued that it was only reasonable to expect the sovereign might waver in the promises he had made while gripped by fear; therefore, I would allow the shadows to deepen for a time. If, after a fair trial, the king’s resolve remained firm, I would then permit the light to reappear. This proposal found no favor with the king or his court, yet I remained adamant.
source_text: My idea worked. I wanted to keep things as they were till the eclipse was total, otherwise they would be trying again to get me to dismiss the darkness, and of course I couldn't do it. Sending for the clothes gained some delay, but not enough. So I had to make another excuse. I said it would be but natural if the king should change his mind and repent to some extent of what he had done under excitement; therefore I would let the darkness grow a while, and if at the end of a reasonable time the king had kept his mind the same, the darkness should be dismissed. Neither the king nor anybody else was satisfied with that arrangement, but I had to stick to my point.

text:
Initially, one thing bothered me: the overwhelming public fascination I inspired. It seemed as though every soul in the kingdom was desperate for a glimpse of me. Word quickly spread that the eclipse had terrified the populace nearly to the point of collapse. While the darkness lasted, the entire country had been gripped by a pathetic hysteria; every chapel, cell, and monastery was packed with sobbing wretches convinced that Judgment Day had arrived. Then came the reports that the architect of this cataclysm was a newcomer, a sorcerer of immense power at King Arthur’s court. Rumor had it that he could have extinguished the sun as easily as a candle flame, and was on the verge of doing so until his compassion was won. He had supposedly broken his own spell and was now hailed as the savior who, single-handedly, had rescued the earth from ruin and humanity from death. 

Given that everyone accepted this tale as absolute truth, never even entertaining a doubt, it is easy to see why anyone in Britain would have trekked fifty miles just to see me. Naturally, I was the sole topic of conversation, and all other matters were forgotten; even the King found himself eclipsed in notoriety. Within a day, envoys started appearing, and they continued to pour in for two weeks. The village and the surrounding fields were swamped. I was forced to step outside several times a day to present myself to these worshipful, terrified crowds. While it became a tedious drain on my time, there was an undeniable pleasure in being the object of such reverence. Best of all, it turned old Merlin sick with jealousy, which gave me immense satisfaction. Yet, one detail baffled me: no one had requested an autograph. I mentioned this to Clarence, and good grief, I actually had to explain the concept to him. He informed me that, aside from a handful of clerics, not a single person in the land could read or write. Good heavens, imagine that.
source_text: One thing troubled me along at first--the immense interest which people took in me. Apparently the whole nation wanted a look at me. It soon transpired that the eclipse had scared the British world almost to death; that while it lasted the whole country, from one end to the other, was in a pitiable state of panic, and the churches, hermitages, and monkeries overflowed with praying and weeping poor creatures who thought the end of the world was come. Then had followed the news that the producer of this awful event was a stranger, a mighty magician at Arthur's court; that he could have blown out the sun like a candle, and was just going to do it when his mercy was purchased, and he then dissolved his enchantments, and was now recognized and honored as the man who had by his unaided might saved the globe from destruction and its peoples from extinction. Now if you consider that everybody believed that, and not only believed it, but never even dreamed of doubting it, you will easily understand that there was not a person in all Britain that would not have walked fifty miles to get a sight of me. Of course I was all the talk--all other subjects were dropped; even the king became suddenly a person of minor interest and notoriety. Within twenty-four hours the delegations began to arrive, and from that time onward for a fortnight they kept coming. The village was crowded, and all the countryside. I had to go out a dozen times a day and show myself to these reverent and awe-stricken multitudes. It came to be a great burden, as to time and trouble, but of course it was at the same time compensatingly agreeable to be so celebrated and such a center of homage. It turned Brer Merlin green with envy and spite, which was a great satisfaction to me. But there was one thing I couldn't understand--nobody had asked for an autograph. I spoke to Clarence about it. By George! I had to explain to him what it was. Then he said nobody in the country could read or write but a few dozen priests. Land! think of that.

text: You attempted to burn me to death despite my innocence, and lately, you’ve been trying to dismantle my professional standing. For that, I intend to call down a firestorm and level your tower, but I’m willing to give you a fair shot. If you believe you can shatter my spells and hold back the flames, then step up to the plate; it’s your turn to see what you can do.
source_text: “You wanted to burn me alive when I had not done you any harm, and latterly you have been trying to injure my professional reputation. Therefore I am going to call down fire and blow up your tower, but it is only fair to give you a chance; now if you think you can break my enchantments and ward off the fires, step to the bat, it's your innings.”

text: Merlin’s reputation had completely tanked. The king was ready to cut off his pay and even considered exiling him, but I stepped in to prevent it. I argued that he could still be useful for managing the weather and handling other minor chores, and I promised to give him a hand whenever his weak parlor tricks failed him. Not a single scrap of his tower remained, yet I arranged for the state to rebuild it, and I even suggested he take in some renters; however, he was far too proud for that. As for showing any gratitude, he never even offered a word of thanks. He was a difficult man to deal with, no matter how you looked at it; though, to be fair, you couldn’t really expect someone to be pleasant after being humiliated so thoroughly.
source_text: Merlin's stock was flat. The king wanted to stop his wages; he even wanted to banish him, but I interfered. I said he would be useful to work the weather, and attend to small matters like that, and I would give him a lift now and then when his poor little parlor-magic soured on him. There wasn't a rag of his tower left, but I had the government rebuild it for him, and advised him to take boarders; but he was too high-toned for that. And as for being grateful, he never even said thank you. He was a rather hard lot, take him how you might; but then you couldn't fairly expect a man to be sweet that had been set back so.

text: I was no mere phantom of a monarch; I was the true essence of authority, while the King himself was the shadow. My influence was immense—not a hollow title, as such things usually are, but the genuine article. I stood at the very headwaters of the world’s second great epoch, watching the narrow stream of history swell and widen as its powerful currents surged toward distant centuries. I could see the rise of other opportunists who would one day seek refuge behind various thrones: the De Montforts, Gavestons, and Villierses; the ambitious mistresses of France who orchestrated wars, and the royal favorites of Charles II. Yet, in that long procession of figures, I saw no one who was truly my equal. I was a singular entity, and I took pride in knowing that my position could not be challenged for thirteen hundred years. Truly, in terms of sheer power, I stood on level ground with the King. However, another institution existed that was slightly more formidable than the two of us combined: the Church. I have no intention of concealing that reality, nor could I. But that is a matter for another time; it will manifest itself soon enough. In those early days, at least, it presented no significant obstacle to me.
source_text: I was no shadow of a king; I was the substance; the king himself was the shadow. My power was colossal; and it was not a mere name, as such things have generally been, it was the genuine article. I stood here, at the very spring and source of the second great period of the world's history; and could see the trickling stream of that history gather and deepen and broaden, and roll its mighty tides down the far centuries; and I could note the upspringing of adventurers like myself in the shelter of its long array of thrones: De Montforts, Gavestons, Mortimers, Villierses; the war-making, campaign-directing wantons of France, and Charles the Second's scepter-wielding drabs; but nowhere in the procession was my full-sized fellow visible. I was a Unique; and glad to know that that fact could not be dislodged or challenged for thirteen centuries and a half, for sure. Yes, in power I was equal to the king. At the same time there was another power that was a trifle stronger than both of us put together. That was the Church. I do not wish to disguise that fact. I couldn't, if I wanted to. But never mind about that, now; it will show up, in its proper place, later on. It didn't cause me any trouble in the beginning --at least any of consequence.

text: Events moved forward, with a joust occurring almost every week. Every so often, the fellows—Lancelot and the others—would try to get me involved, but I’d tell them I’d join in eventually. There wasn't any rush, and I had far too many administrative gears to grease, fix, and set into motion.
source_text: Things ran along, a tournament nearly every week; and now and then the boys used to want me to take a hand--I mean Sir Launcelot and the rest--but I said I would by and by; no hurry yet, and too much government machinery to oil up and set to rights and start a-going.

text: Once those people fixated on an idea, there was simply no budging them. I realized this, so I didn't waste my breath trying to explain anything. As soon as Sir Sagramor was back on his feet, he let me know we had a debt to settle, scheduling a duel for three or four years in the future at the very tournament grounds where he’d first been offended. I told him I’d be ready whenever he returned. You see, he was setting off in search of the Holy Grail. All the men took a shot at the Grail sooner or later; it was a voyage that lasted several years. They would spend a long time diligently poking around, despite the fact that none of them had any clue where the Grail was actually located. Honestly, I don't think any of them expected to find it, nor would they have had any use for it if they had stumbled upon it. It was basically the Northwest Passage of that era. Every year, expeditions would head out "Grailing," and by the next year, search parties would be sent out to rescue them. It was great for one's reputation, but there wasn't a dime of profit in it. They even tried to get me to join in! Imagine that—the very idea made me laugh.
source_text: Well, whenever one of those people got a thing into his head, there was no getting it out again. I knew that, so I saved my breath, and offered no explanations. As soon as Sir Sagramor got well, he notified me that there was a little account to settle between us, and he named a day three or four years in the future; place of settlement, the lists where the offense had been given. I said I would be ready when he got back. You see, he was going for the Holy Grail. The boys all took a flier at the Holy Grail now and then. It was a several years' cruise. They always put in the long absence snooping around, in the most conscientious way, though none of them had any idea where the Holy Grail really was, and I don't think any of them actually expected to find it, or would have known what to do with it if he _had_ run across it. You see, it was just the Northwest Passage of that day, as you may say; that was all. Every year expeditions went out holy grailing, and next year relief expeditions went out to hunt for _them_. There was worlds of reputation in it, but no money. Why, they actually wanted _me_ to put in! Well, I should smile.

text: Every mine belonged to the crown, and they were quite numerous. In the past, they had been exploited with the same crude methods common to primitive peoples—simple pits gouged into the earth from which ore was carried up by hand in leather pouches, managing only a ton each day. I had, however, set to work as soon as possible to reorganize these operations upon a more rigorous, scientific foundation.
source_text: All mines were royal property, and there were a good many of them. They had formerly been worked as savages always work mines--holes grubbed in the earth and the mineral brought up in sacks of hide by hand, at the rate of a ton a day; but I had begun to put the mining on a scientific basis as early as I could.

text: Clarence was now twenty-two and served as my chief lieutenant, my indispensable right hand. He was a marvel, capable of rising to any occasion; there was simply no task he couldn't master. Lately, I had been mentoring him in the field of journalism, as the time felt right to launch a newspaper—nothing major, just a modest weekly for trial distribution in my various outposts of progress. He embraced the work instinctively; a true editor was hidden within him. He had already developed a dual nature: he spoke the local sixth-century dialect but wrote in the nineteenth-century style. His journalistic prose was steadily improving; it had already reached the standard of a rural Alabama paper, matching that region's editorial output perfectly in both content and tone.
source_text: Clarence was twenty-two now, and was my head executive, my right hand. He was a darling; he was equal to anything; there wasn't anything he couldn't turn his hand to. Of late I had been training him for journalism, for the time seemed about right for a start in the newspaper line; nothing big, but just a small weekly for experimental circulation in my civilization-nurseries. He took to it like a duck; there was an editor concealed in him, sure. Already he had doubled himself in one way; he talked sixth century and wrote nineteenth. His journalistic style was climbing, steadily; it was already up to the back settlement Alabama mark, and couldn't be told from the editorial output of that region either by matter or flavor.

text: No land was ever so plagued by wandering fabricators, both men and women alike. Scarcely a month passed without some vagrant arriving, usually peddling a story about a princess needing rescue from a distant castle where she was held by a lawless rogue, typically a giant. You would think the king’s first response to such a melodrama from a complete stranger would be to ask for credentials—or at least some directions to the castle and the best route to take. But no one ever thought of anything so practical. Instead, they swallowed these lies whole, never posing a single question. While I was away one day, another of these people appeared—a woman, this time—and spun the usual yarn. Her mistress was a prisoner in a vast, somber fortress along with forty-four other beautiful maidens, nearly all of them princesses; they had supposedly languished in that cruel captivity for twenty-six years. The masters of the castle were three monstrous brothers, each with four arms and a single eye—an eye centered in the forehead as large as a fruit. The specific type of fruit wasn't mentioned; it was their usual sloppiness with the details.
source_text: There never was such a country for wandering liars; and they were of both sexes. Hardly a month went by without one of these tramps arriving; and generally loaded with a tale about some princess or other wanting help to get her out of some far-away castle where she was held in captivity by a lawless scoundrel, usually a giant. Now you would think that the first thing the king would do after listening to such a novelette from an entire stranger, would be to ask for credentials--yes, and a pointer or two as to locality of castle, best route to it, and so on. But nobody ever thought of so simple and common-sense a thing at that. No, everybody swallowed these people's lies whole, and never asked a question of any sort or about anything. Well, one day when I was not around, one of these people came along--it was a she one, this time--and told a tale of the usual pattern. Her mistress was a captive in a vast and gloomy castle, along with forty-four other young and beautiful girls, pretty much all of them princesses; they had been languishing in that cruel captivity for twenty-six years; the masters of the castle were three stupendous brothers, each with four arms and one eye--the eye in the center of the forehead, and as big as a fruit. Sort of fruit not mentioned; their usual slovenliness in statistics.

text: I managed to hide my excitement with some effort when Clarence delivered the news. But he—he was unable to restrain himself at all. A constant stream of joy and gratitude poured from his lips; he was thrilled by my good luck and deeply thankful to the king for such a grand gesture of favor. He couldn't keep his body still for even a moment, spinning around the room in a lighthearted state of pure bliss.
source_text: By an effort, I contained my joy when Clarence brought me the news. But he--he could not contain his. His mouth gushed delight and gratitude in a steady discharge--delight in my good fortune, gratitude to the king for this splendid mark of his favor for me. He could keep neither his legs nor his body still, but pirouetted about the place in an airy ecstasy of happiness.

text: For my part, I could have damned the generosity that had saddled me with this favor, yet I buried my resentment for the sake of diplomacy and did my best to feign appreciation. I even went so far as to say I was delighted. In a sense, it was the truth; I felt every bit as joyful as a man who has just been scalped.
source_text: On my side, I could have cursed the kindness that conferred upon me this benefaction, but I kept my vexation under the surface for policy's sake, and did what I could to let on to be glad. Indeed, I _said_ I was glad. And in a way it was true; I was as glad as a person is when he is scalped.

text: It was obvious enough now why these fools never bothered to interrogate such liars for the specifics. There might have been a grain of truth buried somewhere within that girl, but I doubt a high-pressure hose could have washed it free; even standard blasting wouldn't have sufficed. It was a job for nothing less than dynamite. She was a total simpleton, yet the king and his knights hung on her every word as if she were reciting holy scripture. It really tells you everything you need to know about the entire group. And consider the primitive informality of this court: this roaming girl had no more trouble gaining an audience with the king in his palace than she would have had entering a poorhouse in my own country. In fact, he was delighted to see her and eager for her story; with a quest like hers to offer, she was as welcome as a fresh corpse is to a coroner.
source_text: Oh, well, it was reasonably plain, now, why these donkeys didn't prospect these liars for details. It may be that this girl had a fact in her somewhere, but I don't believe you could have sluiced it out with a hydraulic; nor got it with the earlier forms of blasting, even; it was a case for dynamite. Why, she was a perfect ass; and yet the king and his knights had listened to her as if she had been a leaf out of the gospel. It kind of sizes up the whole party. And think of the simple ways of this court: this wandering wench hadn't any more trouble to get access to the king in his palace than she would have had to get into the poorhouse in my day and country. In fact, he was glad to see her, glad to hear her tale; with that adventure of hers to offer, she was as welcome as a corpse is to a coroner.

text: Hardly had I wound up these musings when Arthur returned. I lamented the total lack of success I’d had with the maiden, for not one useful detail had surfaced to light our path to the fortress. The lad seemed struck by a curious sort of bewilderment, confessing he had been quite at a loss as to why I had bothered with such a cross-examination at all.
source_text: Just as I was ending-up these reflections, Clarence came back. I remarked upon the barren result of my efforts with the girl; hadn't got hold of a single point that could help me to find the castle. The youth looked a little surprised, or puzzled, or something, and intimated that he had been wondering to himself what I had wanted to ask the girl all those questions for.

text: Giving my word seemed a trivial thing—thirteen centuries was a long time off, and he was so earnest—so I agreed. Yet I found myself sighing despite my best efforts. There was no logic in it, certainly, since she had not yet come into the world. But that is our nature: when the heart is involved, we do not pause to reason; we only feel.
source_text: It was but a little thing to promise--thirteen hundred years or so--and he so eager; so I said Yes. But I sighed; I couldn't help it. And yet there was no sense in sighing, for she wasn't born yet. But that is the way we are made: we don't reason, where we feel; we just feel.

text: The impending journey dominated every conversation throughout that day and evening, and my companions were remarkably gracious, treating me with a deference that suggested they had quite forgotten their previous resentment. They seemed as fervent for me to snare those titans and liberate the venerable damsels as if the task had been entrusted to them. Truly, they were a decent lot—but perpetual adolescents, nothing more. They showered me with stratagems for tracking behemoths and methods for their capture; they recited ancient wards against sorcery and pressed upon me poultices and similar bric-a-brac to treat my future wounds. Yet, it never dawned on a single soul that if I were the prodigious warlock I purported to be, I should have no requirement for balms, instructions, or protective hexes—nor even for blade and harness—on any venture, even one against incendiary wyrms or fiends fresh from the pit. Least of all would I need them for such pathetic foes as these: those pedestrian trolls of the hinterlands.
source_text: My expedition was all the talk that day and that night, and the boys were very good to me, and made much of me, and seemed to have forgotten their vexation and disappointment, and come to be as anxious for me to hive those ogres and set those ripe old virgins loose as if it were themselves that had the contract. Well, they _were_ good children--but just children, that is all. And they gave me no end of points about how to scout for giants, and how to scoop them in; and they told me all sorts of charms against enchantments, and gave me salves and other rubbish to put on my wounds. But it never occurred to one of them to reflect that if I was such a wonderful necromancer as I was pretending to be, I ought not to need salves or instructions, or charms against enchantments, and, least of all, arms and armor, on a foray of any kind--even against fire-spouting dragons, and devils hot from perdition, let alone such poor adversaries as these I was after, these commonplace ogres of the back settlements.

text: I was supposed to have a quick breakfast and set off at first light, as was the custom, but I had a miserable time wrestling with my armor, which caused a bit of a delay. It’s a real chore to get into, given all the moving parts. First, you have to wrap a blanket or two around your torso to act as a cushion and keep the freezing iron off your skin. Then you pull on the chain mail sleeves and shirt—made of tiny, interlocking steel rings, it's a fabric so limp that if you drop it, it collapses into a heap like a pile of wet fishing nets. It’s incredibly heavy and easily the most uncomfortable thing imaginable to sleep in, yet plenty of people used it for pajamas—tax collectors, political agitators, and petty kings with questionable titles, that sort of crowd. Next, you put on your boots—essentially steel-roofed barges—and bolt those clumsy spurs to the heels. Then you strap the greaves to your shins and the cuisses to your thighs; once the back and breastplates are on, you really start to feel cramped. After that, you hook a steel half-skirt of overlapping bands to the chest piece; it hangs down in front but is notched out in the back so you can sit down, though it’s no more attractive or comfortable than an upside-down coal bucket. Then you belt on your sword, slide the pipe-like guards over your arms, pull on the iron gauntlets, and shove your head into a metal cage with a scrap of steel mesh dangling to protect your neck. There you are, packed in as tight as a candle in a mold. It’s certainly no time for dancing. Truly, a man encased like that is a nut hardly worth the effort of cracking; there is so little meat inside compared to the massive size of the shell.
source_text: I was to have an early breakfast, and start at dawn, for that was the usual way; but I had the demon's own time with my armor, and this delayed me a little. It is troublesome to get into, and there is so much detail. First you wrap a layer or two of blanket around your body, for a sort of cushion and to keep off the cold iron; then you put on your sleeves and shirt of chain mail--these are made of small steel links woven together, and they form a fabric so flexible that if you toss your shirt onto the floor, it slumps into a pile like a peck of wet fish-net; it is very heavy and is nearly the uncomfortablest material in the world for a night shirt, yet plenty used it for that--tax collectors, and reformers, and one-horse kings with a defective title, and those sorts of people; then you put on your shoes--flat-boats roofed over with interleaving bands of steel--and screw your clumsy spurs into the heels. Next you buckle your greaves on your legs, and your cuisses on your thighs; then come your backplate and your breastplate, and you begin to feel crowded; then you hitch onto the breastplate the half-petticoat of broad overlapping bands of steel which hangs down in front but is scolloped out behind so you can sit down, and isn't any real improvement on an inverted coal scuttle, either for looks or for wear, or to wipe your hands on; next you belt on your sword; then you put your stove-pipe joints onto your arms, your iron gauntlets onto your hands, your iron rat-trap onto your head, with a rag of steel web hitched onto it to hang over the back of your neck--and there you are, snug as a candle in a candle-mould. This is no time to dance. Well, a man that is packed away like that is a nut that isn't worth the cracking, there is so little of the meat, when you get down to it, by comparison with the shell.

text: Immediately, we were immersed in the wilderness. The dawn of autumn brought a refreshing chill to these timbered seclusions, making the early hours exceptionally graceful. From the heights, we gazed down upon lush emerald basins where rivers coiled through the landscape, interspersed with tufts of woodland and grand, solitary oaks that threw stark, dark silhouettes across the ground. Beyond these vales, mountain tiers draped in azure vapor receded in rolling waves toward the horizon; here and there, a faint smudge of ash or pearl atop a distant crest signaled the site of a stronghold. We traversed expansive, dew-laden commons with the silence of phantoms, our strides muffled by the springy sod. We drifted through clearings illuminated by a verdant glow—a hue born of the sun-soaked ceiling of boughs—while beside us, a freezing, translucent creek skipped and babbled over its rocky bed, producing a gentle, rhythmic sighing that was pleasant to the ear. At intervals, we turned from the light to penetrate the hallowed, opulent shadows of the deep woods. In that dimness, wary beasts flickered past, disappearing into the thicket before one could even fix a gaze upon the source of the rustle. Only the most industrious birds were awake, occupied with a melody in one quarter or a spat in another, while the muffled, distant thrumming of a woodpecker echoed from some unreachable sanctuary within the forest’s core. Then, at last, we would step back out into the dazzling brightness.
source_text: Straight off, we were in the country. It was most lovely and pleasant in those sylvan solitudes in the early cool morning in the first freshness of autumn. From hilltops we saw fair green valleys lying spread out below, with streams winding through them, and island groves of trees here and there, and huge lonely oaks scattered about and casting black blots of shade; and beyond the valleys we saw the ranges of hills, blue with haze, stretching away in billowy perspective to the horizon, with at wide intervals a dim fleck of white or gray on a wave-summit, which we knew was a castle. We crossed broad natural lawns sparkling with dew, and we moved like spirits, the cushioned turf giving out no sound of footfall; we dreamed along through glades in a mist of green light that got its tint from the sun-drenched roof of leaves overhead, and by our feet the clearest and coldest of runlets went frisking and gossiping over its reefs and making a sort of whispering music, comfortable to hear; and at times we left the world behind and entered into the solemn great deeps and rich gloom of the forest, where furtive wild things whisked and scurried by and were gone before you could even get your eye on the place where the noise was; and where only the earliest birds were turning out and getting to business with a song here and a quarrel yonder and a mysterious far-off hammering and drumming for worms on a tree trunk away somewhere in the impenetrable remotenesses of the woods. And by and by out we would swing again into the glare.

text: Taking a break was a luxury, as was the silence. But perfection is an elusive thing in this life. A while back, I’d carved a pipe and prepared some reasonably good tobacco—not the authentic variety, but the dried inner bark of a willow, a substitute used by the native tribes. These modest comforts had been stowed in the helmet, and while I had recovered them, I was left without matches.
source_text: It was good to have a rest--and peace. But nothing is quite perfect in this life, at any time. I had made a pipe a while back, and also some pretty fair tobacco; not the real thing, but what some of the Indians use: the inside bark of the willow, dried. These comforts had been in the helmet, and now I had them again, but no matches.

text: As time passed, a vexing realization slowly took hold: we were essentially immobilized. A novice in full harness cannot hope to board his steed without a great deal of help, and Sandy’s efforts alone were inadequate for the task. We had no choice but to wait for another traveler to happen by. I would have found the wait pleasant enough if it were silent, for my mind was crowded with ideas that needed sorting. I wanted to contemplate how any rational, or even marginally sane, men could have ever reconciled themselves to wearing armor, given its immense discomforts. It baffled me how they had maintained such a custom for ages when the agonies I was currently experiencing must have been their daily lot. I hoped to figure that out and perhaps find a way to end this absurdity and talk the people into letting the fashion perish; yet, serious thought was impossible. One simply could not think while Sandy was around.
source_text: Gradually, as the time wore along, one annoying fact was borne in upon my understanding--that we were weather-bound. An armed novice cannot mount his horse without help and plenty of it. Sandy was not enough; not enough for me, anyway. We had to wait until somebody should come along. Waiting, in silence, would have been agreeable enough, for I was full of matter for reflection, and wanted to give it a chance to work. I wanted to try and think out how it was that rational or even half-rational men could ever have learned to wear armor, considering its inconveniences; and how they had managed to keep up such a fashion for generations when it was plain that what I had suffered to-day they had had to suffer all the days of their lives. I wanted to think that out; and moreover I wanted to think out some way to reform this evil and persuade the people to let the foolish fashion die out; but thinking was out of the question in the circumstances. You couldn't think, where Sandy was.

text: Throughout those agonizing watches, while I remained paralyzed in a state of both glacial stillness and internal combustion—vexed as I was by that frantic colony of vermin—a single, intractable inquiry revolved ceaselessly within my exhausted brain: How do mortal men suffer this abominable harness? By what means have whole dynasties managed to tolerate such a burden? How is it possible for them to find repose at night, shadowed as they are by the grim certainty of the morrow’s tribulations?
source_text: All those trying hours whilst I was frozen and yet was in a living fire, as you may say, on account of that swarm of crawlers, that same unanswerable question kept circling and circling through my tired head: How do people stand this miserable armor? How have they managed to stand it all these generations? How can they sleep at night for dreading the tortures of next day?

text: By the time dawn finally broke, I was a total wreck: ragged, groggy, and drained from a lack of sleep; sore from tossing and turning, starving from the long fast, and dying for a bath to rid myself of the pests, all while being stiff with rheumatism. And how was the high-born lady, the titled aristocrat Demoiselle Alisande la Carteloise, getting along? Well, she was as lively as a squirrel; she had slept like the dead. As for a bath, it is likely that neither she nor any other noble in the country had ever had one, so she didn't feel its absence. By modern standards, those people were essentially just slightly refined savages. This noblewoman showed no eagerness for breakfast—which is another primitive trait. During their travels, these Britons were used to long periods without food and knew how to endure them; they also knew how to gorge themselves in preparation for a fast, much like a native hunter or an anaconda. In all probability, Sandy had fueled up for a three-day trek.
source_text: When the morning came at last, I was in a bad enough plight: seedy, drowsy, fagged, from want of sleep; weary from thrashing around, famished from long fasting; pining for a bath, and to get rid of the animals; and crippled with rheumatism. And how had it fared with the nobly born, the titled aristocrat, the Demoiselle Alisande la Carteloise? Why, she was as fresh as a squirrel; she had slept like the dead; and as for a bath, probably neither she nor any other noble in the land had ever had one, and so she was not missing it. Measured by modern standards, they were merely modified savages, those people. This noble lady showed no impatience to get to breakfast--and that smacks of the savage, too. On their journeys those Britons were used to long fasts, and knew how to bear them; and also how to freight up against probable fasts before starting, after the style of the Indian and the anaconda. As like as not, Sandy was loaded for a three-day stretch.

text:
We departed before dawn, Sandy mounted on her horse while I trailed behind on foot. Within thirty minutes, we encountered a band of tattered wretches gathered to repair the rutted track they called a road. They behaved with the submissiveness of beasts in my presence; when I suggested joining them for breakfast, they were so stunned by my apparent graciousness that they initially doubted my sincerity. My companion curled her lip in disdain and stepped away, remarking loudly enough for all to hear that she would just as soon dine with the livestock. This comment only made those poor souls uncomfortable because it drew attention to them, not because they felt insulted; they were beyond taking offense. Yet, legally speaking, they were neither slaves nor property. Through a cruel irony of language and law, they were classified as freemen. 

People of this rank—humble farmers and craftsmen—constituted seventy percent of the free populace. They were the true soul of the country, the only part that possessed any utility or deserved any measure of respect. To remove them would be to strip away the nation itself, leaving only the dregs: a king and a nobility who were idle, parasitic, and skilled only in destruction—utterly worthless in any logical society. Nevertheless, through a clever deception, this ornate minority had placed itself at the front of the parade rather than at the rear where it belonged. They had declared themselves the Nation, and the masses had acquiesced for so long that they now accepted this lie as an absolute truth; more than that, they believed it was the natural order of things. The clergy had convinced generations that this absurdity was divinely ordained. Failing to realize that the Almighty would hardly occupy Himself with such blatant cruelties, the people had simply ceased to question the matter, settling into a respectful and silent obedience.
source_text: We were off before sunrise, Sandy riding and I limping along behind. In half an hour we came upon a group of ragged poor creatures who had assembled to mend the thing which was regarded as a road. They were as humble as animals to me; and when I proposed to breakfast with them, they were so flattered, so overwhelmed by this extraordinary condescension of mine that at first they were not able to believe that I was in earnest. My lady put up her scornful lip and withdrew to one side; she said in their hearing that she would as soon think of eating with the other cattle--a remark which embarrassed these poor devils merely because it referred to them, and not because it insulted or offended them, for it didn't. And yet they were not slaves, not chattels. By a sarcasm of law and phrase they were freemen. Seven-tenths of the free population of the country were of just their class and degree: small “independent” farmers, artisans, etc.; which is to say, they were the nation, the actual Nation; they were about all of it that was useful, or worth saving, or really respect-worthy, and to subtract them would have been to subtract the Nation and leave behind some dregs, some refuse, in the shape of a king, nobility and gentry, idle, unproductive, acquainted mainly with the arts of wasting and destroying, and of no sort of use or value in any rationally constructed world. And yet, by ingenious contrivance, this gilded minority, instead of being in the tail of the procession where it belonged, was marching head up and banners flying, at the other end of it; had elected itself to be the Nation, and these innumerable clams had permitted it so long that they had come at last to accept it as a truth; and not only that, but to believe it right and as it should be. The priests had told their fathers and themselves that this ironical state of things was ordained of God; and so, not reflecting upon how unlike God it would be to amuse himself with sarcasms, and especially such poor transparent ones as this, they had dropped the matter there and become respectfully quiet.

text: And there I was, residing in a nation where the right to influence the government was reserved for only six people out of every thousand. For the other nine hundred and ninety-four to even hint at dissatisfaction or propose a reform would have made those six elites tremble in unison; to them, such a notion was nothing short of a wretched and treasonous betrayal. In effect, I had become a stakeholder in a firm where nearly everyone supplied the funding and the sweat, while a tiny clique appointed themselves the permanent board of directors and claimed every dividend. It struck me that these hundreds of swindled souls were in desperate need of a new deal. The more dramatic side of my personality would have loved to step down as Boss and incite a bloody insurrection that might blossom into a full revolution. However, I was well aware that any Jack Cade or Wat Tyler who attempts such a feat without first schooling his followers to a revolutionary standard is almost certain to be abandoned to his fate. I have never been fond of being left behind, if I do say so myself. Therefore, the "deal" I had been quietly refining in my mind followed a much different blueprint than those failed rebellions of the past.
source_text: And now here I was, in a country where a right to say how the country should be governed was restricted to six persons in each thousand of its population. For the nine hundred and ninety-four to express dissatisfaction with the regnant system and propose to change it, would have made the whole six shudder as one man, it would have been so disloyal, so dishonorable, such putrid black treason. So to speak, I was become a stockholder in a corporation where nine hundred and ninety-four of the members furnished all the money and did all the work, and the other six elected themselves a permanent board of direction and took all the dividends. It seemed to me that what the nine hundred and ninety-four dupes needed was a new deal. The thing that would have best suited the circus side of my nature would have been to resign the Boss-ship and get up an insurrection and turn it into a revolution; but I knew that the Jack Cade or the Wat Tyler who tries such a thing without first educating his materials up to revolution grade is almost absolutely certain to get left. I had never been accustomed to getting left, even if I do say it myself. Wherefore, the “deal” which had been for some time working into shape in my mind was of a quite different pattern from the Cade-Tyler sort.

text: “Listen, Elara, had I not witnessed such displays with my own eyes in this territory, I simply would not credit the report. But I have seen it, and I can envision those characters now, preening before that heraldry and carrying on in such a fashion. The ladies here truly behave as if they are quite out of their minds. And I mean your finest, too—the very cream of the courtly crop. The lowliest switchboard girl along a thousand miles of copper could teach lessons in poise, restraint, and common decency to the grandest duchess in the High King’s realm.”
source_text: “Now, if I hadn't seen the like myself in this country, Sandy, I wouldn't believe it. But I've seen it, and I can just see those creatures now, parading before that shield and acting like that. The women here do certainly act like all possessed. Yes, and I mean your best, too, society's very choicest brands. The humblest hello-girl along ten thousand miles of wire could teach gentleness, patience, modesty, manners, to the highest duchess in Arthur's land.”

text: Presently Sir Valerius wheeled his mount and bore down upon Alaric with his lance. Observing the charge, Sir Alaric braced his shield, and both knights couched their spears, colliding with the full fury of their galloping steeds; each warrior struck the other so violently in the center of his buckler, yet it was Alaric’s shaft that splintered.
source_text: “Then Sir Marhaus turned his horse and rode toward Gawaine with his spear. And when Sir Gawaine saw that, he dressed his shield, and they aventred their spears, and they came together with all the might of their horses, that either knight smote other so hard in the midst of their shields, but Sir Gawaine's spear brake--”

text: They continued their journey until they reached a deep, rocky ravine, where they came upon a clear-running stream. At the head of the water sat three maidens beside a beautiful spring. Sir Marhaus observed that since this land had been christened, no knight had ever passed through it without encountering some extraordinary adventure.
source_text: “And so they rode and came into a deep valley full of stones, and thereby they saw a fair stream of water; above thereby was the head of the stream, a fair fountain, and three damsels sitting thereby. In this country, said Sir Marhaus, came never knight since it was christened, but he found strange adventures--”

text: The shadows were stretching into dusk. It was mid-afternoon when Beatrice had first set about identifying the travelers for me, and she had made considerable progress—at least by her own measure. She would reach the point in due time, surely, but she was not a person who could be prodded into haste.
source_text: The sun was now setting. It was about three in the afternoon when Alisande had begun to tell me who the cowboys were; so she had made pretty good progress with it--for her. She would arrive some time or other, no doubt, but she was not a person who could be hurried.

text: Thus, the weary traveler’s sorrows quickly dissipated. He was a courageous man who had performed grand feats of valor in his day. His greatest fame stemmed from a quest much like my own, which he had once undertaken with a maiden named Maledisant. She had been as relentless a talker as Sandy, though of a harsher variety; while Sandy’s voice was a gentler tune, Maledisant’s tongue produced nothing but scorn and insults. Knowing his history well, I understood the deep pity in his eyes as he bid me farewell. He was convinced that I was enduring a truly miserable and trying ordeal.
source_text: So the poor colporteur's griefs vanished away. He was a brave fellow, and had done mighty feats of arms in his time. His chief celebrity rested upon the events of an excursion like this one of mine, which he had once made with a damsel named Maledisant, who was as handy with her tongue as was Sandy, though in a different way, for her tongue churned forth only railings and insult, whereas Sandy's music was of a kindlier sort. I knew his story well, and so I knew how to interpret the compassion that was in his face when he bade me farewell. He supposed I was having a bitter hard time of it.
