text: The miserable youth collapsed upon the stones, his slender frame knotting in a single, violent paroxysm of agony before he drew his final breath. A sharp, unbidden cry of pity broke from the elderly monarch’s lips, but a freezing glare from his consort caused him to stifle the sound instantly, severing his grief before it could find full voice. At a subtle gesture from his mother, Prince Gaheris withdrew to the vestibule to fetch the menials; meanwhile, the lady resumed her melodic conversation, her words flowing with a terrifying, untroubled grace.
source_text: Poor child! he slumped to the floor, twisted his silken limbs in one great straining contortion of pain, and was dead. Out of the old king was wrung an involuntary “O-h!” of compassion. The look he got, made him cut it suddenly short and not put any more hyphens in it. Sir Uwaine, at a sign from his mother, went to the anteroom and called some servants, and meanwhile madame went rippling sweetly along with her talk.

text: “Oh, pray, attend to your devoted maiden! Could one endowed with my modest arts truly address the vanquisher of Malakor in such a fashion without intending a mere drollery? Through my divinations, I had already envisioned your arrival, and by their light, I recognized you the very instant you entered this hall. I did but perform this small charade in the hope of provoking some manifestation of your skill, never doubting that you would strike the guards down with mystical fires, reducing them to heaps of ash—a wonder far exceeding my own capabilities, yet one I have long felt a childish eagerness to behold.”
source_text: “La, but do list to thine handmaid! as if one gifted with powers like to mine might say the thing which I have said unto one who has vanquished Merlin, and not be jesting. By mine enchantments I foresaw your coming, and by them I knew you when you entered here. I did but play this little jest with hope to surprise you into some display of your art, as not doubting you would blast the guards with occult fires, consuming them to ashes on the spot, a marvel much beyond mine own ability, yet one which I have long been childishly curious to see.”

text: The carnage lasted for an hour and a half, leaving behind a level of culinary devastation that was hard to fathom. Of the banquet’s centerpiece—the massive wild boar that had looked so grand and stout when the meal began—nothing remained but a skeletal frame resembling a hoop-skirt. This ruin was merely a symbol of the wreckage that had overtaken every other dish on the table.
source_text: The havoc continued an hour and a half, and unimaginable was the destruction of substantials. Of the chief feature of the feast --the huge wild boar that lay stretched out so portly and imposing at the start--nothing was left but the semblance of a hoop-skirt; and he was but the type and symbol of what had happened to all the other dishes.

text: As the desserts were brought out, the serious drinking commenced, and the conversation flowed just as freely. Enormous quantities of wine and mead were downed, and the entire party—men and women alike—slipped from a state of relaxed comfort into one of radiant joy, eventually becoming quite boisterous. The men shared anecdotes that were truly startling to the ear, yet not a single person blushed; when the punchlines were delivered, the room erupted in a collective roar that seemed to vibrate through the entire stone fortress. The ladies countered with scandalous tales that would have forced even the likes of Margaret of Navarre or Queen Elizabeth to retreat behind their handkerchiefs, but no one here sought cover; instead, they simply howled with laughter. Though these irreverent stories usually cast clergymen as their bawdy protagonists, the chaplain remained unbothered, joining in the merriment with gusto. In fact, when asked, he bellowed out a ballad that was as bold and suggestive as anything heard all night.
source_text: With the pastries and so on, the heavy drinking began--and the talk. Gallon after gallon of wine and mead disappeared, and everybody got comfortable, then happy, then sparklingly joyous--both sexes, --and by and by pretty noisy. Men told anecdotes that were terrific to hear, but nobody blushed; and when the nub was sprung, the assemblage let go with a horse-laugh that shook the fortress. Ladies answered back with historiettes that would almost have made Queen Margaret of Navarre or even the great Elizabeth of England hide behind a handkerchief, but nobody hid here, but only laughed --howled, you may say. In pretty much all of these dreadful stories, ecclesiastics were the hardy heroes, but that didn't worry the chaplain any, he had his laugh with the rest; more than that, upon invitation he roared out a song which was of as daring a sort as any that was sung that night.

text: The miserable sovereign was so thoroughly cowed and humbled that she lacked the nerve to even send the maestro to the gallows without first soliciting my approval. I felt a genuine pang of sympathy for her—as would anyone, for her distress was quite real—and I was therefore disposed to be as accommodating as reason allowed, having no desire to indulge in needless tyranny. I deliberated upon the matter with due gravity and finally summoned the orchestra to perform that "Abide With Me" once more, which they did. I saw then that her grievance was well-founded, and I granted her leave to execute the entire ensemble. This brief softening of my usual rigor had a salutary effect upon her. A leader gains nothing by the constant, heavy-handed application of absolute power, for such a habit wounds the legitimate pride of his ministers and ultimately compromises his own standing. To yield a small point now and then, when the stakes are low, is the far more prudent strategy.
source_text: The poor queen was so scared and humbled that she was even afraid to hang the composer without first consulting me. I was very sorry for her--indeed, any one would have been, for she was really suffering; so I was willing to do anything that was reasonable, and had no desire to carry things to wanton extremities. I therefore considered the matter thoughtfully, and ended by having the musicians ordered into our presence to play that Sweet Bye and Bye again, which they did. Then I saw that she was right, and gave her permission to hang the whole band. This little relaxation of sternness had a good effect upon the queen. A statesman gains little by the arbitrary exercise of iron-clad authority upon all occasions that offer, for this wounds the just pride of his subordinates, and thus tends to undermine his strength. A little concession, now and then, where it can do no harm, is the wiser policy.

text: With her mind finally at peace and her spirits lifted, the wine began to take hold of the queen once more, gaining the upper hand. It loosened her tongue, setting off a continuous, melodic stream of chatter. She truly was a relentless talker. It wouldn't have been appropriate for me to mention how late it was or how desperately I needed sleep. I found myself wishing I had slipped away to bed when I’d had the opportunity. Now I was forced to see it through; there was no other option. She droned on and on amidst the deep, eerie silence of the sleeping fortress until, eventually, a faint noise drifted up from far beneath us—a stifled scream so thick with agony that it made my skin prickle. The queen stopped, her eyes brightening with delight as she tilted her head like a bird listening for a call. The sound cut through the stillness again.
source_text: Now that the queen was at ease in her mind once more, and measurably happy, her wine naturally began to assert itself again, and it got a little the start of her. I mean it set her music going--her silver bell of a tongue. Dear me, she was a master talker. It would not become me to suggest that it was pretty late and that I was a tired man and very sleepy. I wished I had gone off to bed when I had the chance. Now I must stick it out; there was no other way. So she tinkled along and along, in the otherwise profound and ghostly hush of the sleeping castle, until by and by there came, as if from deep down under us, a far-away sound, as of a muffled shriek --with an expression of agony about it that made my flesh crawl. The queen stopped, and her eyes lighted with pleasure; she tilted her graceful head as a bird does when it listens. The sound bored its way up through the stillness again.

text: Her Majesty was profoundly incensed the following dawn upon discovering that neither Silas’s neck nor his meager holdings would be hers to claim. I informed her that this disappointment was a burden she must simply endure; while tradition and the statutes granted her dominion over both his life and his estate, the situation possessed mitigating factors, and thus, wielding the King’s seal, I had granted him clemency. The stag had been pillaging the poor wretch’s grain, and his strike had been one of impulsive rage rather than calculated theft; furthermore, he had dragged the carcass into the sovereign’s woods merely to obscure his trail. Blast the woman, I could not convince her that a flare of temper serves as a moral defense for slaying a deer—or a man—so I abandoned the effort and left her to her brooding. I had briefly hoped to illuminate the point by suggesting that her own recent outburst regarding the stable boy was a similar lapse, but the logic escaped her entirely.
source_text: The queen was a good deal outraged, next morning when she found she was going to have neither Hugo's life nor his property. But I told her she must bear this cross; that while by law and custom she certainly was entitled to both the man's life and his property, there were extenuating circumstances, and so in Arthur the king's name I had pardoned him. The deer was ravaging the man's fields, and he had killed it in sudden passion, and not for gain; and he had carried it into the royal forest in the hope that that might make detection of the misdoer impossible. Confound her, I couldn't make her see that sudden passion is an extenuating circumstance in the killing of venison--or of a person--so I gave it up and let her sulk it out. I _did_ think I was going to make her see it by remarking that her own sudden passion in the case of the page modified that crime.

text: Curse her, she was clever enough, possessing no shortage of wit, but her upbringing made her a fool—from a modern perspective, at least. To slay the page was no transgression; it was her prerogative, and she exercised it with a tranquil conscience, entirely unaware of any wrongdoing. She was the product of centuries of indoctrination in the unchallenged belief that a law granting her the power to kill a subject at will was fundamentally just and righteous.
source_text: No, confound her, her intellect was good, she had brains enough, but her training made her an ass--that is, from a many-centuries-later point of view. To kill the page was no crime--it was her right; and upon her right she stood, serenely and unconscious of offense. She was a result of generations of training in the unexamined and unassailed belief that the law which permitted her to kill a subject when she chose was a perfectly right and righteous one.

text: You’ve likely met the sort of person who would rather die than admit a complex word is over their head. In fact, the less they truly understand, the more pathetically determined they are to pretend your vocabulary hasn't left them in the dust. The queen was exactly like that, and her pride frequently resulted in the most idiotic mistakes. After a brief pause, her expression cleared as if she’d finally caught on, and she promised to handle the matter for me.
source_text: You have seen that kind of people who will never let on that they don't know the meaning of a new big word. The more ignorant they are, the more pitifully certain they are to pretend you haven't shot over their heads. The queen was just one of that sort, and was always making the stupidest blunders by reason of it. She hesitated a moment; then her face brightened up with sudden comprehension, and she said she would do it for me.

text: He admitted to being worn out, and his haggard face bore witness to it, yet he would not dismount. He declared he was in pursuit of the stove-polish man, punctuating the statement with a renewed volley of oaths. The traveling advertiser in question was Sir Ossaise of Surluse, a gallant knight of some renown, largely because he had once dared to measure his strength in the lists against the formidable Sir Gaheris—even if the encounter had ended in his defeat. He was a man of a cheerful, mocking temperament, someone for whom nothing in existence carried any weight. It was for this very quality that I had tasked him with generating a demand for stove polish. As stoves did not yet exist, the polish could hardly be taken seriously. The agent’s duty was merely to skillfully and bit by bit ready the public for the coming revolution, cultivating a preference for tidiness so that they would be prepared when the stove finally arrived on the scene.
source_text: He was aweary, he said, and indeed he looked it; but he would not alight. He said he was after the stove-polish man; and with this he broke out cursing and swearing anew. The bulletin-boarder referred to was Sir Ossaise of Surluse, a brave knight, and of considerable celebrity on account of his having tried conclusions in a tournament once, with no less a Mogul than Sir Gaheris himself--although not successfully. He was of a light and laughing disposition, and to him nothing in this world was serious. It was for this reason that I had chosen him to work up a stove-polish sentiment. There were no stoves yet, and so there could be nothing serious about stove-polish. All that the agent needed to do was to deftly and by degrees prepare the public for the great change, and have them established in predilections toward neatness against the time when the stove should appear upon the stage.

text: “To hell with him,” Sir Madok declared, “if I don’t beat him black and blue once I track him down, mark my words; for no knight named Ossaise or anyone else can insult me like this and stay alive, provided I can find him, a task I have sworn a solemn oath to complete this very day.”
source_text: “Blank-blank-blank him,” said Sir Madok, “an I do not stove-polish him an I may find him, leave it to me; for never no knight that hight Ossaise or aught else may do me this disservice and bide on live, an I may find him, the which I have thereunto sworn a great oath this day.”

text: I found myself wishing I had taken a different path. This was hardly the sort of encounter for a political strategist who was mentally drafting a non-violent transformation of society. It forced me to face the unavoidable truth that, despite all the soft-spoken rhetoric and high-minded philosophy, no nation has ever actually secured its liberty through polite conversation or moral appeals. It is a fundamental law that every revolution destined for success must start with bloodshed, no matter what peaceful methods might follow later. If history provides any lesson at all, it is that. What these people truly required was a Reign of Terror and a guillotine, and I was simply not the right person for the job.
source_text: I rather wished I had gone some other road. This was not the sort of experience for a statesman to encounter who was planning out a peaceful revolution in his mind. For it could not help bringing up the unget-aroundable fact that, all gentle cant and philosophizing to the contrary notwithstanding, no people in the world ever did achieve their freedom by goody-goody talk and moral suasion: it being immutable law that all revolutions that will succeed must _begin_ in blood, whatever may answer afterward. If history teaches anything, it teaches that. What this folk needed, then, was a Reign of Terror and a guillotine, and I was the wrong man for them.

text: This is a frequent occurrence—the way a spell might alter something in one man's sight while leaving it in its true form for another. You’ve heard of such things before, Sandy, even if you haven't witnessed it personally. But there is no real damage here. In truth, it’s a stroke of luck. If these ladies looked like swine to themselves and the rest of the world, we would be forced to break the enchantment, which might be impossible without knowing the exact ritual involved. It’s a dangerous business, too; if you try to reverse a spell without the correct formula, you're liable to make a mistake and turn your hogs into dogs, then cats, then rats, until you eventually reduce your subjects to nothing at all, or perhaps a scentless vapor that vanishes—which amounts to the same thing. Fortunately, only my vision is clouded, so there is no reason to undo the magic. These women remain noble ladies to you and everyone else; they won't suffer from my misunderstanding, for once I realize that a supposed pig is actually a lady, that is enough for me to treat her with the proper respect.
source_text: “This is a common case--the enchanting of a thing to one eye and leaving it in its proper form to another. You have heard of it before, Sandy, though you haven't happened to experience it. But no harm is done. In fact, it is lucky the way it is. If these ladies were hogs to everybody and to themselves, it would be necessary to break the enchantment, and that might be impossible if one failed to find out the particular process of the enchantment. And hazardous, too; for in attempting a disenchantment without the true key, you are liable to err, and turn your hogs into dogs, and the dogs into cats, the cats into rats, and so on, and end by reducing your materials to nothing finally, or to an odorless gas which you can't follow--which, of course, amounts to the same thing. But here, by good luck, no one's eyes but mine are under the enchantment, and so it is of no consequence to dissolve it. These ladies remain ladies to you, and to themselves, and to everybody else; and at the same time they will suffer in no way from my delusion, for when I know that an ostensible hog is a lady, that is enough for me, I know how to treat her.”

text: In her absence, while she retreated to weep her sorrows over the cold mutton, I distributed those lofty patents of nobility among the scullery maids and grooms. I proposed they might apply a brush to the floors where the high-born had lately loitered and paraded; yet they judged such a task to be of little merit and, more significantly, a perilous breach of etiquette that would surely provoke gossip. A breach of etiquette—that was the final word; here was a people who would sooner commit a felony than an original act. The staff insisted on the ancient way, a practice hallowed by centuries of use; they would merely scatter fresh straw throughout the halls, thus interring the remnants of the aristocratic presence. It was a parody of the earth’s own cycles: a scientific accumulation, a sedimentary archive of domestic life; an antiquarian might one day tunnel through the muck and discern, from the scraps of each distinct layer, the various dietary shifts the family had endured through the passing generations.
source_text: While she was gone to cry her farewells over the pork, I gave that whole peerage away to the servants. And I asked them to take a duster and dust around a little where the nobilities had mainly lodged and promenaded; but they considered that that would be hardly worth while, and would moreover be a rather grave departure from custom, and therefore likely to make talk. A departure from custom--that settled it; it was a nation capable of committing any crime but that. The servants said they would follow the fashion, a fashion grown sacred through immemorial observance; they would scatter fresh rushes in all the rooms and halls, and then the evidence of the aristocratic visitation would be no longer visible. It was a kind of satire on Nature: it was the scientific method, the geologic method; it deposited the history of the family in a stratified record; and the antiquary could dig through it and tell by the remains of each period what changes of diet the family had introduced successively for a hundred years.

text: The assembly of travelers mirrored Chaucer’s pilgrims in one distinct way: it comprised a cross-section of nearly every prominent trade and profession the nation possessed, reflected in a diverse array of garments. The group featured a mix of the aged and the young, men and women alike, some brimming with energy while others remained stoic. They journeyed atop horses and mules, yet not a single side-saddle was among them, for that specific convenience would not be introduced to England for another nine hundred years.
source_text: This company of pilgrims resembled Chaucer's in this: that it had in it a sample of about all the upper occupations and professions the country could show, and a corresponding variety of costume. There were young men and old men, young women and old women, lively folk and grave folk. They rode upon mules and horses, and there was not a side-saddle in the party; for this specialty was to remain unknown in England for nine hundred years yet.

text: From the world’s most distant corners, these men have drifted hither. A recluse prospers most where the tides of the pious run highest. Indeed, no manner of anchorite is absent from the fold. If a traveler should name some strange variety of devotee he believes exists only in a remote province, he need but poke through the hollows, dens, and bogs of this Sanctified Basin; whatever the creature’s pedigree, he shall surely find a living example crouched within.
source_text: “These have gathered there from the ends of the earth. A hermit thriveth best where there be multitudes of pilgrims. Ye shall not find no hermit of no sort wanting. If any shall mention a hermit of a kind he thinketh new and not to be found but in some far strange land, let him but scratch among the holes and caves and swamps that line that Valley of Holiness, and whatsoever be his breed, it skills not, he shall find a sample of it there.”

text: By early afternoon, we caught up with another group of travelers; however, this procession was devoid of any joy, laughter, or youthful exuberance. Though it contained people of every age—from the elderly and the middle-aged to young couples and nursing infants—not a single child smiled. Among the fifty people present, every face was downcast, marked by that fixed expression of utter hopelessness born from a long intimacy with despair. They were slaves. Heavy irons connected their bound hands and feet to leather belts at their waists, and everyone but the children was further linked into a single file by a chain running from collar to collar. They had traveled three hundred miles on foot in eighteen days, sustained only by the most pathetic scraps of food. At night, they slept in their chains, huddled together like animals. Their bodies were draped in rags that hardly qualified as clothing. The iron had worn the skin from their ankles, leaving ulcerated, worm-eaten sores; their bare feet were shredded, and every one of them limped. This group had originally numbered a hundred, but half had been sold along the way. The trader oversaw them from horseback, carrying a whip with a short handle and long, knotted lashes. He used it silently to strike the shoulders of those who faltered from pain, forcing them to stand straight. As we rode past, none of these miserable souls looked up or seemed to notice us. They produced no sound save for the rhythmic, haunting clank of chains as forty-three burdened feet rose and fell in unison, the entire line moving within its own thick cloud of dust.
source_text: Early in the afternoon we overtook another procession of pilgrims; but in this one was no merriment, no jokes, no laughter, no playful ways, nor any happy giddiness, whether of youth or age. Yet both were here, both age and youth; gray old men and women, strong men and women of middle age, young husbands, young wives, little boys and girls, and three babies at the breast. Even the children were smileless; there was not a face among all these half a hundred people but was cast down, and bore that set expression of hopelessness which is bred of long and hard trials and old acquaintance with despair. They were slaves. Chains led from their fettered feet and their manacled hands to a sole-leather belt about their waists; and all except the children were also linked together in a file six feet apart, by a single chain which led from collar to collar all down the line. They were on foot, and had tramped three hundred miles in eighteen days, upon the cheapest odds and ends of food, and stingy rations of that. They had slept in these chains every night, bundled together like swine. They had upon their bodies some poor rags, but they could not be said to be clothed. Their irons had chafed the skin from their ankles and made sores which were ulcerated and wormy. Their naked feet were torn, and none walked without a limp. Originally there had been a hundred of these unfortunates, but about half had been sold on the trip. The trader in charge of them rode a horse and carried a whip with a short handle and a long heavy lash divided into several knotted tails at the end. With this whip he cut the shoulders of any that tottered from weariness and pain, and straightened them up. He did not speak; the whip conveyed his desire without that. None of these poor creatures looked up as we rode along by; they showed no consciousness of our presence. And they made no sound but one; that was the dull and awful clank of their chains from end to end of the long file, as forty-three burdened feet rose and fell in unison. The file moved in a cloud of its own making.

text: I shall seize it from him; the extreme nature of this crisis renders the action necessary and just. And even if it did not, who would presume to impose laws upon the Church? The Church dictates the law to everyone; whatever she desires to do, she is permitted to do, regardless of whom it might harm. I will have it from him; you are to start immediately.
source_text: “But I will take it from him; it is a terrible emergency and the act is thereby justified. And if it were not so, who will give law to the Church? The Church giveth law to all; and what she wills to do, that she may do, hurt whom it may. I will take it from him; you shall begin upon the moment.”

text: It would have been far more sensible, certainly, for Valerius to set aside his dignity and abandon the field at once, for he was never going to coax a drop from that dry earth. He was a quintessential sorcerer of his era; which is to say, those legendary feats that secured his fame were invariably performed when no one but Valerius was there to witness them. He could hardly command the spring to flow before such a vast assembly; a throng of spectators was as fatal to a medieval wonder as a skeptic is to a modern séance. There is always some doubter present, ready to strike a match at the critical moment and expose the fraud. Yet, I had no wish for Valerius to retire until I was prepared to seize the stage with proper effect myself. That remained impossible until my apparatus arrived from the Citadel, a journey that would require at least two or three days.
source_text: Of course, it would have been best, all round, for Merlin to waive etiquette and quit and call it half a day, since he would never be able to start that water, for he was a true magician of the time; which is to say, the big miracles, the ones that gave him his reputation, always had the luck to be performed when nobody but Merlin was present; he couldn't start this well with all this crowd around to see; a crowd was as bad for a magician's miracle in that day as it was for a spiritualist's miracle in mine; there was sure to be some skeptic on hand to turn up the gas at the crucial moment and spoil everything. But I did not want Merlin to retire from the job until I was ready to take hold of it effectively myself; and I could not do that until I got my things from Camelot, and that would take two or three days.

text: I couldn't grasp every specific detail, but I understood the general point—and enough of it to feel a sense of guilt. It wasn't right to dump nineteenth-century jargon on a sixth-century novice and then criticize her for not following along, especially when she was trying her absolute best. It certainly wasn't her fault she couldn't bridge the gap, so I offered her an apology. Afterward, we strolled casually toward the hermit caves, chatting easily and feeling closer than ever.
source_text: I couldn't make it all out--that is, the details--but I got the general idea; and enough of it, too, to be ashamed. It was not fair to spring those nineteenth century technicalities upon the untutored infant of the sixth and then rail at her because she couldn't get their drift; and when she was making the honest best drive at it she could, too, and no fault of hers that she couldn't fetch the home plate; and so I apologized. Then we meandered pleasantly away toward the hermit holes in sociable converse together, and better friends than ever.

text: We meandered through that gallery of ascetics until dusk. It was a grotesque carnival of the spirit. Their singular ambition seemed to be a race toward the nadir of squalor, each competing to see who might nourish the most vibrant population of lice. Every gesture they made was saturated with a profound, insufferable vanity. One zealot found his glory in wallowing naked within the swamp, permitting every passing gadfly to feast upon his hide unhindered; another remained perpetually braced against a crag, performing his orisons with a theatrical fervor for the edification of the mob; a third chose to discard his humanity entirely, prowling the dust upon his knuckles like a hound. There was one who hauled a heavy yoke of iron through the years, never casting it aside; another who eschewed the bed for a standing rest among the nettles, snoring loudly only when the spectators were thickest. A matriarch of great age, shrouded only in the silver of her hair, was charred to a deep charcoal by five decades of sacred drought. Around each of these spectacles, throngs of travelers lingered in a state of pious stupor, coveting the unblemished grace that such hideous mortifications had extracted from a stern and watchful Providence.
source_text: We drifted from hermit to hermit all the afternoon. It was a most strange menagerie. The chief emulation among them seemed to be, to see which could manage to be the uncleanest and most prosperous with vermin. Their manner and attitudes were the last expression of complacent self-righteousness. It was one anchorite's pride to lie naked in the mud and let the insects bite him and blister him unmolested; it was another's to lean against a rock, all day long, conspicuous to the admiration of the throng of pilgrims and pray; it was another's to go naked and crawl around on all fours; it was another's to drag about with him, year in and year out, eighty pounds of iron; it was another's to never lie down when he slept, but to stand among the thorn-bushes and snore when there were pilgrims around to look; a woman, who had the white hair of age, and no other apparel, was black from crown to heel with forty-seven years of holy abstinence from water. Groups of gazing pilgrims stood around all and every of these strange objects, lost in reverent wonder, and envious of the fleckless sanctity which these pious austerities had won for them from an exacting heaven.

text: “Had any human endeavor been capable of severing the enchantment gripping this spring, the rite I have only now concluded would surely have succeeded. Yet it has faltered, and in this defeat, my darkest suspicions are made manifest; for this silence proves that the most terrible entity whispered of by the mystics of the Orient—he whose very title brings death to the tongue—has cast his shadow over this basin. No man walks this earth, nor shall any ever be born, possessed of the wit to pierce the mystery of such a curse, and lacking that hidden knowledge, no hand can undo the binding. These depths shall remain parched until the end of days, holy Sire. I have exhausted the limits of my craft. I pray you, grant me my departure.”
source_text: “If any labor of mortal might break the spell that binds these waters, this which I have but just essayed had done it. It has failed; whereby I do now know that that which I had feared is a truth established; the sign of this failure is, that the most potent spirit known to the magicians of the East, and whose name none may utter and live, has laid his spell upon this well. The mortal does not breathe, nor ever will, who can penetrate the secret of that spell, and without that secret none can break it. The water will flow no more forever, good Father. I have done what man could. Suffer me to go.”

text: That remark cut deep and made him recoil, for his track record as a forecaster was the most disastrous in the realm. Whenever he issued a storm warning for the coast, a week of total calm was certain to follow, and every time he predicted sunshine, it rained in torrents. I had kept him in the weather office specifically to ruin his reputation. However, that insult soured his mood, and instead of leaving to report my demise, he decided he would stay behind to savor the spectacle of it.
source_text: It was a home shot, and it made him wince; for he was the worst weather-failure in the kingdom. Whenever he ordered up the danger-signals along the coast there was a week's dead calm, sure, and every time he prophesied fair weather it rained brickbats. But I kept him in the weather bureau right along, to undermine his reputation. However, that shot raised his bile, and instead of starting home to report my death, he said he would remain and enjoy it.

text: As I set out for the chapel, the crowd bared their heads and retreated with reverence to clear a wide path for me, treating me like some sort of higher entity—and I was. I didn't doubt it for a second. I brought along a group of monks to work a night shift, instructing them in the mechanics of the pump and setting them to the task. It was clear that many of the folks outside intended to keep vigil with the water until morning, so it was only right that they should have as much of it as they wanted. To those monks, the pump was nothing short of a miracle itself; they were filled with awe and admiration for the extraordinary efficiency with which it operated.
source_text: When I started to the chapel, the populace uncovered and fell back reverently to make a wide way for me, as if I had been some kind of a superior being--and I was. I was aware of that. I took along a night shift of monks, and taught them the mystery of the pump, and set them to work, for it was plain that a good part of the people out there were going to sit up with the water all night, consequently it was but right that they should have all they wanted of it. To those monks that pump was a good deal of a miracle itself, and they were full of wonder over it; and of admiration, too, of the exceeding effectiveness of its performance.

text: I refuse to send it away, Father. I possess a secret understanding that reveals a mistake was made back then, when people believed building the bathhouse had driven the fountain off." The old man’s face began to glow with a deep fascination. "My insights tell me the bath was blameless in that disaster; it was actually brought about by an entirely different kind of sin.
source_text: “No, Father, I will not drive it away. I have mysterious knowledge which teaches me that there was an error that other time when it was thought the institution of the bath banished the fountain.” A large interest began to show up in the old man's face. “My knowledge informs me that the bath was innocent of that misfortune, which was caused by quite another sort of sin.”

text: “Greetings, Exchange! Do I reach the Citadel? Behold, let your spirit be gladdened if you have the grace to credit the miraculous when it arrives in a strange mask and reveals its glory in the most improbable of haunts—for here, in the living quick, stands the Governor, and with your very own ears shall you attend to his voice!”
source_text: “Hello Central! Is this you, Camelot?--Behold, thou mayst glad thy heart an thou hast faith to believe the wonderful when that it cometh in unexpected guise and maketh itself manifest in impossible places--here standeth in the flesh his mightiness The Boss, and with thine own ears shall ye hear him speak!”

text: The king, the queen, and much of the royal circle are setting out this very hour for your valley. They intend to pay holy tribute to the waters you restored, wash away their sins, and witness the spot where that demonic spirit supposedly vomited hellfire into the sky. If you listen carefully, you might hear me winking and see me smiling a knowing smile, for it was I who chose those particular flames from our stores and sent them along exactly as you ordered.
source_text: “The king and queen and many of the court do start even in this hour, to go to your valley to pay pious homage to the waters ye have restored, and cleanse themselves of sin, and see the place where the infernal spirit spouted true hell-flames to the clouds --an ye listen sharply ye may hear me wink and hear me likewise smile a smile, sith 'twas I that made selection of those flames from out our stock and sent them by your order.”

text: It was a welcome sound! Amidst the ring of telephones and the rush of instant communication with the world, I was finally drawing breath after a long period of choking. I realized then what a ghostly, stagnant, and lifeless nightmare this land had been to me, and how my mind had become so suppressed that I had grown used to the gloom almost beyond the point of noticing it.
source_text: It sounded good! In this atmosphere of telephones and lightning communication with distant regions, I was breathing the breath of life again after long suffocation. I realized, then, what a creepy, dull, inanimate horror this land had been to me all these years, and how I had been in such a stifled condition of mind as to have grown used to it almost beyond the power to notice it.

text: “Ah!” A collective gasp rippled through the onlookers. Not a single person in the crowd had considered such a straightforward tactic—the simple act of asking about someone who wasn't thousands of miles away. The magician was blindsided; he had never encountered a crisis like this in all his experience, and it left him paralyzed. He had no idea how to handle it. He looked dazed and bewildered, unable to utter a single syllable. “Well?” I pressed. “What are you waiting for? Is it possible you can instantly describe the actions of someone on the other side of the globe, yet you’re stumped by what a man standing ten feet away is doing? The people behind me can see exactly what I’m doing with my right hand—they’ll back you up if you get it right.” He remained speechless. “Fine, I’ll tell everyone why you won’t speak up: it’s because you don’t know. You, a sorcerer? My friends, this vagrant is nothing but a total sham and a liar.”
source_text: “Ah-h!” There was a general gasp of surprise. It had not occurred to anybody in the crowd--that simple trick of inquiring about somebody who wasn't ten thousand miles away. The magician was hit hard; it was an emergency that had never happened in his experience before, and it corked him; he didn't know how to meet it. He looked stunned, confused; he couldn't say a word. “Come,” I said, “what are you waiting for? Is it possible you can answer up, right off, and tell what anybody on the other side of the earth is doing, and yet can't tell what a person is doing who isn't three yards from you? Persons behind me know what I am doing with my right hand--they will indorse you if you tell correctly.” He was still dumb. “Very well, I'll tell you why you don't speak up and tell; it is because you don't know. _You_ a magician! Good friends, this tramp is a mere fraud and liar.”

text: The following dawn, I repaired to the telegraph station and ascertained that the sovereign had already traversed two outlying hamlets. I continued to chart his trajectory in this fashion throughout the subsequent day, holding my counsel all the while. By the third morning’s intelligence, it was clear that, barring some mishap, he would reach our gates by four o'clock. Yet, the town remained curiously indifferent; no banners were hoisted, nor were any royal welcomes being organized. It was a most peculiar state of affairs. Only one conclusion seemed plausible: that charlatan had been meddling in my business again. And so it proved. I consulted a certain friar of my acquaintance, who confirmed that the sorcerer had cast some fresh spells and announced that the court had abandoned their travel plans altogether. Imagine the audacity! It serves as a grim lesson on the fragility of fame in this realm. These folk had witnessed me perform the most spectacular feat of sorcery in the annals of man—the only miracle in living memory that actually served a useful purpose—and yet they were perfectly willing to follow the lead of a vagabond who possessed nothing but a silver tongue and an unverified pedigree.
source_text: Next day I went up to the telephone office and found that the king had passed through two towns that were on the line. I spotted his progress on the succeeding day in the same way. I kept these matters to myself. The third day's reports showed that if he kept up his gait he would arrive by four in the afternoon. There was still no sign anywhere of interest in his coming; there seemed to be no preparations making to receive him in state; a strange thing, truly. Only one thing could explain this: that other magician had been cutting under me, sure. This was true. I asked a friend of mine, a monk, about it, and he said, yes, the magician had tried some further enchantments and found out that the court had concluded to make no journey at all, but stay at home. Think of that! Observe how much a reputation was worth in such a country. These people had seen me do the very showiest bit of magic in history, and the only one within their memory that had a positive value, and yet here they were, ready to take up with an adventurer who could offer no evidence of his powers but his mere unproven word.

text: A particularly peculiar legal matter was brought to the king's attention. A young orphaned woman, who possessed a substantial inheritance, had wed a handsome but penniless young man. Her lands were situated within a territory governed by the Church. The local bishop, a haughty nobleman by birth, demanded the seizure of her property, arguing that her secret marriage had defrauded the Church of its feudal prerogative—specifically, the notorious "lord’s right" mentioned earlier. The punishment for evading this custom was the total forfeiture of her holdings. The woman’s defense rested on the claim that because the lordship belonged to the bishop, the right in question was non-transferable and had to be performed by him personally or not at all; however, an ancient ecclesiastical decree explicitly prohibited the bishop from such conduct. It was a truly strange legal puzzle.
source_text: One very curious case came before the king. A young girl, an orphan, who had a considerable estate, married a fine young fellow who had nothing. The girl's property was within a seigniory held by the Church. The bishop of the diocese, an arrogant scion of the great nobility, claimed the girl's estate on the ground that she had married privately, and thus had cheated the Church out of one of its rights as lord of the seigniory--the one heretofore referred to as le droit du seigneur. The penalty of refusal or avoidance was confiscation. The girl's defense was, that the lordship of the seigniory was vested in the bishop, and the particular right here involved was not transferable, but must be exercised by the lord himself or stand vacated; and that an older law, of the Church itself, strictly barred the bishop from exercising it. It was a very odd case, indeed.

text: The king had finally escaped his predicament, surely under conditions that satisfied both the Church and the nobility. Many eloquent justifications are written in defense of kingship, but the truth is simple: wherever universal suffrage exists, cruel laws cannot endure. Arthur’s subjects were admittedly poor candidates for a republic, having been diminished by centuries of royal oppression; yet even they would have been wise enough to dismantle the harsh statutes the king had just upheld if the matter were put to a free vote. A particular phrase has become so common that it is often mistaken for logic—the idea that a nation might not be “capable of self-government.” This suggests that there has ever been a people less fit to govern themselves than the self-anointed specialists who would presume to do it for them. In every era, the finest minds have risen in great numbers from the common folk, not the privileged few. No matter the general state of a nation’s intellect, the vast majority of its capability lies with the nameless poor; consequently, no society has ever lacked the internal strength to manage its own destiny. It is an undeniable fact that even the most enlightened monarchy lags behind the potential of a self-governing people, a truth that applies to every form of autocracy, from the most refined to the most base.
source_text: Well, the king was out of the hole; and on terms satisfactory to the Church and the rest of the aristocracy, no doubt. Men write many fine and plausible arguments in support of monarchy, but the fact remains that where every man in a State has a vote, brutal laws are impossible. Arthur's people were of course poor material for a republic, because they had been debased so long by monarchy; and yet even they would have been intelligent enough to make short work of that law which the king had just been administering if it had been submitted to their full and free vote. There is a phrase which has grown so common in the world's mouth that it has come to seem to have sense and meaning--the sense and meaning implied when it is used; that is the phrase which refers to this or that or the other nation as possibly being “capable of self-government”; and the implied sense of it is, that there has been a nation somewhere, some time or other which _wasn't_ capable of it--wasn't as able to govern itself as some self-appointed specialists were or would be to govern it. The master minds of all nations, in all ages, have sprung in affluent multitude from the mass of the nation, and from the mass of the nation only--not from its privileged classes; and so, no matter what the nation's intellectual grade was; whether high or low, the bulk of its ability was in the long ranks of its nameless and its poor, and so it never saw the day that it had not the material in abundance whereby to govern itself. Which is to assert an always self-proven fact: that even the best governed and most free and most enlightened monarchy is still behind the best condition attainable by its people; and that the same is true of kindred governments of lower grades, all the way down to the lowest.

text: I secured a private conference with the monarch and presented a plan. I argued that his impulse to commission the aristocracy into that particular legion was inspired, a masterstroke of statecraft. Indeed, I suggested he go further: commission five hundred more; in truth, appoint every blue-blooded cousin and idle lordling in the kingdom, until the officers outnumbered the rank-and-file tenfold. We would christen it the "Imperial Vanguard," the most illustrious and autonomous body in the realm, permitted to wage war by its own whims and wander the map at its leisure—a truly magnificent and lawless brotherhood. Such an arrangement would satisfy every vanity of the peerage, rendering them perfectly content. For the remainder of the forces, however, we would recruit from the common stock, officered by men of low birth but high merit. This secondary army would be held to the most rigorous discipline, stripped of all privilege, and tasked with the grueling, unceasing labor of the campaign. Thus, whenever the Vanguard grew weary and wished to gallop off in search of monsters or diversion, they might do so without concern, certain that the grim professionals behind them would keep the machinery of war grinding along exactly as before. The King was enchanted by the logic of it.
source_text: I had a private audience with the king, and made a proposition. I said it was quite right to officer that regiment with nobilities, and he couldn't have done a wiser thing. It would also be a good idea to add five hundred officers to it; in fact, add as many officers as there were nobles and relatives of nobles in the country, even if there should finally be five times as many officers as privates in it; and thus make it the crack regiment, the envied regiment, the King's Own regiment, and entitled to fight on its own hook and in its own way, and go whither it would and come when it pleased, in time of war, and be utterly swell and independent. This would make that regiment the heart's desire of all the nobility, and they would all be satisfied and happy. Then we would make up the rest of the standing army out of commonplace materials, and officer it with nobodies, as was proper--nobodies selected on a basis of mere efficiency--and we would make this regiment toe the line, allow it no aristocratic freedom from restraint, and force it to do all the work and persistent hammering, to the end that whenever the King's Own was tired and wanted to go off for a change and rummage around amongst ogres and have a good time, it could go without uneasiness, knowing that matters were in safe hands behind it, and business going to be continued at the old stand, same as usual. The king was charmed with the idea.

text: Observing that gave me a useful idea. I believed I had finally found a way to resolve a persistent and stubborn problem. The Pendragon bloodline was remarkably durable and exceptionally fertile. Every time a new child was born into this clan—which happened quite frequently—the public voiced exuberant delight while their hearts sank in genuine despair. The celebration was a facade, but the misery was real, for each birth signaled another demand for a Royal Grant. The list of these royals was extensive, creating an ever-growing strain on the treasury and posing a danger to the monarchy itself. However, Arthur refused to acknowledge this risk and ignored every proposal I made to replace these handouts with something else. Had I been able to convince him to occasionally fund one of these minor relatives from his personal wealth, I could have publicized it to great effect, improving his standing with the people. But he wouldn’t consider it. He held a near-religious devotion to the Royal Grant, viewing it as a kind of sanctified plunder. Nothing provoked his temper faster or more surely than an assault on that hallowed custom. Whenever I tried to gently suggest that no other self-respecting family in the country would stoop to begging for charity—well, I never managed to finish that thought. He always silenced me instantly and with absolute authority.
source_text: When I noticed that, it gave me a valuable notion. I thought I saw my way out of an old and stubborn difficulty at last. You see, the royalties of the Pendragon stock were a long-lived race and very fruitful. Whenever a child was born to any of these --and it was pretty often--there was wild joy in the nation's mouth, and piteous sorrow in the nation's heart. The joy was questionable, but the grief was honest. Because the event meant another call for a Royal Grant. Long was the list of these royalties, and they were a heavy and steadily increasing burden upon the treasury and a menace to the crown. Yet Arthur could not believe this latter fact, and he would not listen to any of my various projects for substituting something in the place of the royal grants. If I could have persuaded him to now and then provide a support for one of these outlying scions from his own pocket, I could have made a grand to-do over it, and it would have had a good effect with the nation; but no, he wouldn't hear of such a thing. He had something like a religious passion for royal grant; he seemed to look upon it as a sort of sacred swag, and one could not irritate him in any way so quickly and so surely as by an attack upon that venerable institution. If I ventured to cautiously hint that there was not another respectable family in England that would humble itself to hold out the hat--however, that is as far as I ever got; he always cut me short there, and peremptorily, too.

text: Naturally, I steered the conversation elsewhere. It was true that Guenever possessed great beauty, but when one considered her overall character, she was quite a disappointment. I made it a point not to meddle in such private matters—they were none of my business—yet I will confess that I hated watching the way things were unfolding. Time and again, she would ask me, “Sir Boss, have you seen Sir Lancelot anywhere?” but if she ever went around searching for the king with that same anxiety, I never happened to be there to see it.
source_text: Of course, I changed the Subject. Yes, Guenever was beautiful, it is true, but take her all around she was pretty slack. I never meddled in these matters, they weren't my affair, but I did hate to see the way things were going on, and I don't mind saying that much. Many's the time she had asked me, “Sir Boss, hast seen Sir Launcelot about?” but if ever she went fretting around for the king I didn't happen to be around at the time.

text: Elsewhere, some practitioners influenced a patient’s psyche without uttering a single word, yet they still managed to heal them. In other regions, specialists gathered the sick in a hall to offer prayers and stir their inner convictions, and those people left completely recovered. Whenever a monarch is unable to remedy the "king's evil," it is a sure sign that the most essential myth upholding his power—the people's faith in the divine right of their ruler—has vanished. When I was young, the English sovereigns had given up the practice of touching for the disease, but there was no real reason for such modesty; they probably would have effected a cure ninety-eight percent of the time.
source_text: In other places people operated on a patient's mind, without saying a word to him, and cured him. In others, experts assembled patients in a room and prayed over them, and appealed to their faith, and those patients went away cured. Wherever you find a king who can't cure the king's-evil you can be sure that the most valuable superstition that supports his throne--the subject's belief in the divine appointment of his sovereign--has passed away. In my youth the monarchs of England had ceased to touch for the evil, but there was no occasion for this diffidence: they could have cured it forty-nine times in fifty.

text: I flicked a copper down from the sill and secured my sheet; that primordial news-vendor vanished past the gable to procure my pence, and there he remains to this day. To hold a journal again was a singular luxury, yet I suffered a private pang of alarm when my sight landed upon the initial cluster of bold-faced titles. I had dwelled within a damp fog of veneration and stifling courtesy for so great a span that those ink-black shouts sent a thin, icy tremor racing through my marrow.
source_text: I dropped a nickel out of the window and got my paper; the Adam-newsboy of the world went around the corner to get my change; is around the corner yet. It was delicious to see a newspaper again, yet I was conscious of a secret shock when my eye fell upon the first batch of display head-lines. I had lived in a clammy atmosphere of reverence, respect, deference, so long that they sent a quivery little cold wave through me:

text: Admittedly, the publication sufficed as a preliminary venture; I was well aware of its utility, and yet it left me somewhat dissatisfied. The "Royal Bulletin" struck a more harmonious chord; indeed, its quiet, dignified reverence was a refreshing tonic after such unseemly impudence. Nevertheless, it invited further polish. Try as one might, a courtly record is inherently devoid of diversity. There is a profound tedium in its chronicles that resists every earnest endeavor to lend them luster or spirit. The most effective strategy—indeed, the only prudent one—is to mask the repetition of incident with a shifting variety of expression: one should peel the truth of its old casing and dress it in a new finery of syllables. It cheats the vision; the reader fancies he discovers a fresh occurrence, imagining a court bustling with endless vigor. Thus enticed, he consumes the entire column with relish, perhaps never realizing the whole banquet is but a gallon of water flavored by a single grain. Garrick’s style was respectable, being both austere and functional, yet I contend it was not the superior path.
source_text: Of course it was good enough journalism for a beginning; I knew that quite well, and yet it was somehow disappointing. The “Court Circular” pleased me better; indeed, its simple and dignified respectfulness was a distinct refreshment to me after all those disgraceful familiarities. But even it could have been improved. Do what one may, there is no getting an air of variety into a court circular, I acknowledge that. There is a profound monotonousness about its facts that baffles and defeats one's sincerest efforts to make them sparkle and enthuse. The best way to manage--in fact, the only sensible way--is to disguise repetitiousness of fact under variety of form: skin your fact each time and lay on a new cuticle of words. It deceives the eye; you think it is a new fact; it gives you the idea that the court is carrying on like everything; this excites you, and you drain the whole column, with a good appetite, and perhaps never notice that it's a barrel of soup made out of a single bean. Clarence's way was good, it was simple, it was dignified, it was direct and business-like; all I say is, it was not the best way:

text: For the rest of the session, my writing circulated from one group to another across that vast hall. I tracked its journey with a joyful gaze, sitting perfectly still, steeped in triumph and intoxicated by the moment. This was truly paradise; I was savoring a taste of heaven, even if I should never feel it again.
source_text: During all the rest of the seance my paper traveled from group to group all up and down and about that huge hall, and my happy eye was upon it always, and I sat motionless, steeped in satisfaction, drunk with enjoyment. Yes, this was heaven; I was tasting it once, if I might never taste it more.

text: Near the hour of rest, I led the Sovereign to my inner chambers to shear his head and instruct him in the awkward grace required by his new, humble weeds. While the aristocracy kept their hair fringed at the front and trailing to the shoulder behind, the meaner sort were cropped in a circle; the serfs, by contrast, remained unshorn, their hair a wild thicket. Thus, I upended a ceramic basin over his skull and severed every golden lock that peeked beneath the edge. I pruned his facial hair to a rough, uneven stubble, laboring to make the work look unskilled—a goal I reached with ease. It was a cruel sabotage of his features. Clad in clumsy leather soles and a shapeless gown of gritty brown flax that draped from his throat to his heels, his majesty evaporated. He was no longer the finest specimen in the land, but had turned into a remarkably plain and unappealing creature. Shorn and garbed in identical fashion, we could easily have been taken for tenant farmers, or herdsmen, or even draymen; indeed, we looked like any village laborer, for such clothing was the standard livery of the indigent, chosen for its durability and meager price. I do not imply it was a bargain for those with empty pockets, but rather that it was the most economical woven stuff available for a man’s dress—the most basic of manufactured goods.
source_text: About bedtime I took the king to my private quarters to cut his hair and help him get the hang of the lowly raiment he was to wear. The high classes wore their hair banged across the forehead but hanging to the shoulders the rest of the way around, whereas the lowest ranks of commoners were banged fore and aft both; the slaves were bangless, and allowed their hair free growth. So I inverted a bowl over his head and cut away all the locks that hung below it. I also trimmed his whiskers and mustache until they were only about a half-inch long; and tried to do it inartistically, and succeeded. It was a villainous disfigurement. When he got his lubberly sandals on, and his long robe of coarse brown linen cloth, which hung straight from his neck to his ankle-bones, he was no longer the comeliest man in his kingdom, but one of the unhandsomest and most commonplace and unattractive. We were dressed and barbered alike, and could pass for small farmers, or farm bailiffs, or shepherds, or carters; yes, or for village artisans, if we chose, our costume being in effect universal among the poor, because of its strength and cheapness. I don't mean that it was really cheap to a very poor person, but I do mean that it was the cheapest material there was for male attire--manufactured material, you understand.

text: “My Sovereign, I have been misread. Let me clarify. Foresight is of a dual nature. One form is the knack for discerning what is nearly at hand; the other is the power to perceive occurrences lost in the mists of remote centuries. Which of these talents, in your judgment, is the more potent?”
source_text: “Sire, I have been misunderstood. I will explain. There are two kinds of prophecy. One is the gift to foretell things that are but a little way off, the other is the gift to foretell things that are whole ages and centuries away. Which is the mightier gift, do you think?”

text: “Listen,” I proceeded, “I could easily manage both forms of prophecy—the distant and the immediate—if I cared to maintain the necessary practice. Yet I seldom employ anything but the long-range sort, for the other is quite beneath my station. It is better suited to Merlin’s class of charlatans—the ‘short-order’ seers, as we call them in the business. Occasionally, of course, I sharpen my wits and toss out a minor prediction, but it is a rare event indeed. You will surely remember the sensation it caused upon your arrival at the Valley of Holiness, given that I had foretold your coming and the very hour of your appearance several days before.”
source_text: “Now, then,” I continued, “I _could_ work both kinds of prophecy --the long and the short--if I chose to take the trouble to keep in practice; but I seldom exercise any but the long kind, because the other is beneath my dignity. It is properer to Merlin's sort --stump-tail prophets, as we call them in the profession. Of course, I whet up now and then and flirt out a minor prophecy, but not often--hardly ever, in fact. You will remember that there was great talk, when you reached the Valley of Holiness, about my having prophesied your coming and the very hour of your arrival, two or three days beforehand.”

text: By every rule of intellect, the opposite should surely hold; it ought to be a thousandfold more effortless to envision the finish than the start, since the former looms so near that a man without vision might almost perceive it. Indeed, the art of augury upends all reason, curiously casting the obvious into shadow while illuminating the impossible.
source_text: “And yet in reason it should clearly be the other way; it should be five hundred times as easy to foretell the last as the first, for, indeed, it is so close by that one uninspired might almost see it. In truth, the law of prophecy doth contradict the likelihoods, most strangely making the difficult easy, and the easy difficult.”

text: I personally explained the situation to the King, telling him that a dynamite bomb had done the work. This information didn't enlighten him at all; he remained just as ignorant as he was before. To him, though, it was a magnificent miracle and another blow to Merlin’s status. I made sure to clarify that this was such a rare feat it could only be repeated when atmospheric conditions were perfect. Otherwise, he would have requested an encore for every suitable target, which would have been a problem since I was out of explosives.
source_text: But I explained it to the king myself. I said it was done with a dynamite bomb. This information did him no damage, because it left him as intelligent as he was before. However, it was a noble miracle, in his eyes, and was another settler for Merlin. I thought it well enough to explain that this was a miracle of so rare a sort that it couldn't be done except when the atmospheric conditions were just right. Otherwise he would be encoring it every time we had a good subject, and that would be inconvenient, because I hadn't any more bombs along.

text: Thus the lesson proceeded. I coached Silas to mimic a dozen different souls crushed by fate and poverty. But it was a futile exercise; the words were empty sounds to him, as meaningless as the rustle of dry leaves. Language can never truly illuminate a hardship unless the listener has felt that same iron in his own soul. There are plenty of clever gentlemen who discourse pompously on the "toiling poor," soothing their consciences with the notion that a day of mental strain is vastly more exhausting than a day of physical drudgery, and should be paid accordingly. They believe this sincerely, for they have mastered the pen but never touched the plow. I, however, have tried my hand at both. For my part, no sum of gold exists that could tempt me to handle a shovel for thirty days straight, yet I would undertake the most taxing intellectual labor for next to nothing—and be quite content with the bargain.
source_text: And so on, and so on. I drilled him as representing in turn all sorts of people out of luck and suffering dire privations and misfortunes. But lord, it was only just words, words--they meant nothing in the world to him, I might just as well have whistled. Words realize nothing, vivify nothing to you, unless you have suffered in your own person the thing which the words try to describe. There are wise people who talk ever so knowingly and complacently about “the working classes,” and satisfy themselves that a day's hard intellectual work is very much harder than a day's hard manual toil, and is righteously entitled to much bigger pay. Why, they really think that, you know, because they know all about the one, but haven't tried the other. But I know all about both; and so far as I am concerned, there isn't money enough in the universe to hire me to swing a pickaxe thirty days, but I will do the hardest kind of intellectual work for just as near nothing as you can cipher it down--and I will be satisfied, too.

text: A faint rustle emanated from the gloom near the wooden rungs. It was the sovereign making his descent. I watched as he climbed down, one arm occupied by a heavy weight, the other guiding his passage. When he stepped into the pale light, I saw a girl of tender years resting against his chest, her consciousness fading as the pestilence claimed her. Here was the pinnacle of human courage; a confrontation with the reaper in the starkest of terms, where no prizes were offered and no banners waved. There were no throngs in velvet to cheer this lonely struggle, yet his resolve was as unshakable as when he faced an armed peer in the safety of his harness. In this hour, he reached a height of grandeur beyond any crown. I determined then that the hall of his forefathers needed a new monument—not another iron-clad warrior triumphing over a mythic foe, but a king in humble wool, carrying mortality itself so a mother’s heart might be stilled by one last glimpse of her child.
source_text: There was a slight noise from the direction of the dim corner where the ladder was. It was the king descending. I could see that he was bearing something in one arm, and assisting himself with the other. He came forward into the light; upon his breast lay a slender girl of fifteen. She was but half conscious; she was dying of smallpox. Here was heroism at its last and loftiest possibility, its utmost summit; this was challenging death in the open field unarmed, with all the odds against the challenger, no reward set upon the contest, and no admiring world in silks and cloth of gold to gaze and applaud; and yet the king's bearing was as serenely brave as it had always been in those cheaper contests where knight meets knight in equal fight and clothed in protecting steel. He was great now; sublimely great. The rude statues of his ancestors in his palace should have an addition--I would see to that; and it would not be a mailed king killing a giant or a dragon, like the rest, it would be a king in commoner's garb bearing death in his arms that a peasant mother might look her last upon her child and be comforted.

text: “You recognize the pattern well enough, having borne the brunt of it yourselves—for indeed, none of our station in this kingdom are ever spared. It is the same hollow, exhausted history. We labored and we persisted, and we called it a triumph merely because we drew breath at day's end; to claim anything more would be a falsehood. No misfortune arose that we could not endure until this season, when every calamity struck in a single, suffocating tide. Many winters past, the master of the hall saw fit to set his orchard upon our leasehold, and in the very heart of our richest soil—a stinging cruelty and a wretched affront.”
source_text: “Ye know it well yourselves, having suffered it--for truly none of our condition in Britain escape it. It is the old, weary tale. We fought and struggled and succeeded; meaning by success, that we lived and did not die; more than that is not to be claimed. No troubles came that we could not outlive, till this year brought them; then came they all at once, as one might say, and overwhelmed us. Years ago the lord of the manor planted certain fruit trees on our farm; in the best part of it, too--a grievous wrong and shame--”

text: We had barely gone a few yards when the sound of footsteps on gravel reached my ears. My heart leaped into my throat. It was vital that no one saw us leaving that house. I tugged at the king’s robe, and we scrambled back to take cover behind the corner of the cabin.
source_text: We had not moved four steps when I caught a sound as of footsteps upon gravel. My heart flew to my throat. We must not be seen coming from that house. I plucked at the king's robe and we drew back and took shelter behind the corner of the cabin.

text: We lingered for a duration in the dense shadow and silence, watching a ruby smudge on the horizon and straining to interpret a distant hum that ebbed and flowed fitfully through the night. At times it swelled, seeming for a moment more proximate; yet, when we anticipated it might reveal its source, it grew dull and subsided, its secret still guarded. We set off down the slope toward it, the winding track casting us at once into a nearly palpable darkness—an obscurity packed tight between two high ramparts of timber. We groped forward for perhaps half a mile, the murmur growing increasingly audible. The impending storm became more threatening, bringing occasional tremors of wind, pale flickers of lightning, and the low growls of remote thunder. I was at the front. I collided with an object—a soft, heavy mass that gave way slightly under my momentum; in that same instant, the lightning blazed, and a foot from my face hung the distorted visage of a man dangling from a branch! It seemed to twitch, though it was actually still. It was a morbid sight. Immediately, an ear-splitting crash of thunder followed, and the very floor of heaven gave way; the rain fell in a deluge. Regardless, we had to attempt to cut him down, for there was a chance that a spark of life remained, was there not? The lightning became rapid and piercing, the woods alternating between the clarity of midday and the void of midnight. One heartbeat the man was suspended before me in a fierce light, and the next he was vanished into the gloom. I told the Sovereign we must lower him. The Sovereign at once demurred.
source_text: We stood there awhile, in the thick darkness and stillness, looking toward the red blur in the distance, and trying to make out the meaning of a far-away murmur that rose and fell fitfully on the night. Sometimes it swelled up and for a moment seemed less remote; but when we were hopefully expecting it to betray its cause and nature, it dulled and sank again, carrying its mystery with it. We started down the hill in its direction, and the winding road plunged us at once into almost solid darkness--darkness that was packed and crammed in between two tall forest walls. We groped along down for half a mile, perhaps, that murmur growing more and more distinct all the time. The coming storm threatening more and more, with now and then a little shiver of wind, a faint show of lightning, and dull grumblings of distant thunder. I was in the lead. I ran against something--a soft heavy something which gave, slightly, to the impulse of my weight; at the same moment the lightning glared out, and within a foot of my face was the writhing face of a man who was hanging from the limb of a tree! That is, it seemed to be writhing, but it was not. It was a grewsome sight. Straightway there was an ear-splitting explosion of thunder, and the bottom of heaven fell out; the rain poured down in a deluge. No matter, we must try to cut this man down, on the chance that there might be life in him yet, mustn't we? The lightning came quick and sharp now, and the place was alternately noonday and midnight. One moment the man would be hanging before me in an intense light, and the next he was blotted out again in the darkness. I told the king we must cut him down. The king at once objected.

text: Our slumber lasted deep into the afternoon, and we awoke with an appetite that rendered even the meagerest peasant rations delicious to the King, particularly given the scarcity of the portion. The menu lacked any diversity, offering nothing but onions, salt, and that heavy black bread fashioned from fodder. The woman shared the details of the prior night’s calamity. Near midnight, while the household slept, the manor house was suddenly consumed by fire. The local folk hurried to assist, and every member of the family was rescued except for the master. His absence caused a panic, leading two gallant yeomen to give their lives searching the blazing ruins for that esteemed gentleman. Yet he was eventually discovered—or what remained of him—as a cold corpse. He lay in a thicket some three hundred yards distant, bound, gagged, and riddled with a dozen stab wounds.
source_text: We slept till far into the afternoon, and then got up hungry enough to make cotter fare quite palatable to the king, the more particularly as it was scant in quantity. And also in variety; it consisted solely of onions, salt, and the national black bread made out of horse-feed. The woman told us about the affair of the evening before. At ten or eleven at night, when everybody was in bed, the manor-house burst into flames. The country-side swarmed to the rescue, and the family were saved, with one exception, the master. He did not appear. Everybody was frantic over this loss, and two brave yeomen sacrificed their lives in ransacking the burning house seeking that valuable personage. But after a while he was found--what was left of him--which was his corpse. It was in a copse three hundred yards away, bound, gagged, stabbed in a dozen places.

text: The most agonizing aspect of the whole affair was the eagerness with which this subjugated people turned their violent hands upon their own peers to benefit their shared oppressor. This man and woman seemed convinced that in any dispute between a commoner and his lord, it was the natural and righteous duty of the entire lower class to defend the master, taking up his fight without ever pausing to consider the justice of the case. The man had even assisted in the execution of his neighbors, carrying out the deed with fervor despite knowing the accusations rested on nothing but flimsy suspicion devoid of evidence; yet, to him and his wife, there was nothing remotely ghastly about the betrayal.
source_text: The painful thing observable about all this business was the alacrity with which this oppressed community had turned their cruel hands against their own class in the interest of the common oppressor. This man and woman seemed to feel that in a quarrel between a person of their own class and his lord, it was the natural and proper and rightful thing for that poor devil's whole caste to side with the master and fight his battle for him, without ever stopping to inquire into the rights or wrongs of the matter. This man had been out helping to hang his neighbors, and had done his work with zeal, and yet was aware that there was nothing against them but a mere suspicion, with nothing back of it describable as evidence, still neither he nor his wife seemed to see anything horrible about it.

text: That was the crux of the matter. Deep down, a man is still a man. Eons of cruelty and tyranny cannot entirely strip away his inherent dignity; anyone who assumes otherwise is dead wrong. Even in the most downtrodden populations imaginable—the Russians, for instance, or the Germans—there is ample material to forge a republic. The necessary spirit exists within them, if only it could be drawn out from its cautious, fearful shell to topple every throne and trample the aristocracies that uphold them. We can still hope and trust that certain transformations are coming. My plan was a limited monarchy for the remainder of Arthur’s life, followed by the dissolution of the crown and the abolition of the nobility. Every aristocrat would be apprenticed to a productive trade, universal voting rights would be established, and the entire government would be handed over to the people, where it belongs. Truly, there was no reason to abandon my ambitions just yet.
source_text: There it was, you see. A man is a man, at bottom. Whole ages of abuse and oppression cannot crush the manhood clear out of him. Whoever thinks it a mistake is himself mistaken. Yes, there is plenty good enough material for a republic in the most degraded people that ever existed--even the Russians; plenty of manhood in them--even in the Germans--if one could but force it out of its timid and suspicious privacy, to overthrow and trample in the mud any throne that ever was set up and any nobility that ever supported it. We should see certain things yet, let us hope and believe.
