First, a modified monarchy, till Arthur's days were done, then the destruction of the throne, nobility abolished, every member of it bound out to some useful trade, universal suffrage instituted, and the whole government placed in the hands of the men and women of the nation there to remain. Yes, there was no occasion to give up my dream yet a while.

text: “Let us reach a plain understanding here and now, my dear Julian. It is true that I am merely a land steward, but do not imagine for a moment that I lack means. I have enjoyed a season of such extraordinary prosperity that the depth of my purse would leave you breathless. I tell you without exaggeration that I could fling away the cost of a dozen such banquets and never feel the loss of a single farthing!” With that, I gave my fingers a sharp, dismissive snap. I could feel my stature growing by leaps and bounds in Julian’s estimation, and as those final words left my lips, I stood before him a colossus of refinement and authority. “Thus, you must allow me my indulgence. You shall not provide a solitary groat toward this revelry; that is my final word.”
source_text: “Now let's understand each other on the spot, old friend. I am only a farm bailiff, it is true; but I am not poor, nevertheless. I have been very fortunate this year--you would be astonished to know how I have thriven. I tell you the honest truth when I say I could squander away as many as a dozen feasts like this and never care _that_ for the expense!” and I snapped my fingers. I could see myself rise a foot at a time in Marco's estimation, and when I fetched out those last words I was become a very tower for style and altitude. “So you see, you must let me have my way. You can't contribute a cent to this orgy, that's _settled_.”

text: “Still, it is significant; the very best a man can offer, given freely, always carries weight. It is equal to the finest effort of a prince and stands right alongside it—for even a monarch can do no more than his utmost. So, we shall go about and arrange this whole affair now, and you must not fret over the cost. I am a born profligate, truly one of the worst. Why, in a single week I have been known to spend—well, never mind that; you would never credit the amount anyway.”
source_text: “But it _is_ something; the best a man has, freely given, is always something, and is as good as a prince can do, and ranks right along beside it--for even a prince can but do his best. And so we'll shop around and get up this layout now, and don't you worry about the expense. I'm one of the worst spendthrifts that ever was born. Why, do you know, sometimes in a single week I spend --but never mind about that--you'd never believe it anyway.”

text: It amused Silas to his very soul to learn of so peculiar a figure; nevertheless, the news also served to arm him against the coming calamities. In my own experience, when one wanders the roads with a monarch who is feigning a lowlier state—and who manages to recall the deception only half the time—no degree of caution can ever be considered excessive.
source_text: It tickled Marco to the marrow to hear about such an odd character; but it also prepared him for accidents; and in my experience when you travel with a king who is letting on to be something else and can't remember it more than about half the time, you can't take too many precautions.

text: He was happy to comply. He filled them, and I carried them away. I didn't dare reveal that the miller-gun was a personal invention of mine, nor that I had issued a formal decree requiring every merchant in the realm to stock them and sell them at a state-mandated price. This was a mere pittance, and the shopkeeper kept the profit rather than the government. We supplied the items at no cost.
source_text: He would, with pleasure. He filled them, and I took them with me. I couldn't venture to tell him that the miller-gun was a little invention of my own, and that I had officially ordered that every shopkeeper in the kingdom keep them on hand and sell them at government price--which was the merest trifle, and the shopkeeper got that, not the government. We furnished them for nothing.

text: As the supplies arrived toward twilight that Friday, I found it nearly impossible to prevent the Bentons from falling into a swoon. They were certain that Arthur and I had courted financial ruin, and they viewed themselves as guilty partners in our insolvency. You see, beyond the banquet provisions, which were costly enough, I had procured several extravagances for their future household: a great store of white flour, a delicacy as foreign to their table as caviar to a hermit; a robust wooden dining table; two full pounds of salt—which they considered the height of profligacy—plus earthenware, benches, new attire, a cask of cider, and the like. I commanded the Bentons to remain silent regarding this opulence, for I wished to startle the guests and indulge in a bit of ostentation. Where the new garments were concerned, the simple pair behaved like children; they rose repeatedly throughout the night to see if the sun was up, finally dressing themselves a good hour before the first light. Their delight—indeed, their sheer mania—was so vivid and genuine that the spectacle paid me back in full for my interrupted slumber. The Prince, however, slept as he always did—like a corpse. The Bentons were forbidden from thanking him for the clothes, yet they exerted every effort to signal their profound appreciation. It was all for naught; he remained entirely oblivious to any change in their appearance.
source_text: Well, when that cargo arrived toward sunset, Saturday afternoon, I had my hands full to keep the Marcos from fainting. They were sure Jones and I were ruined past help, and they blamed themselves as accessories to this bankruptcy. You see, in addition to the dinner-materials, which called for a sufficiently round sum, I had bought a lot of extras for the future comfort of the family: for instance, a big lot of wheat, a delicacy as rare to the tables of their class as was ice-cream to a hermit's; also a sizeable deal dinner-table; also two entire pounds of salt, which was another piece of extravagance in those people's eyes; also crockery, stools, the clothes, a small cask of beer, and so on. I instructed the Marcos to keep quiet about this sumptuousness, so as to give me a chance to surprise the guests and show off a little. Concerning the new clothes, the simple couple were like children; they were up and down, all night, to see if it wasn't nearly daylight, so that they could put them on, and they were into them at last as much as an hour before dawn was due. Then their pleasure--not to say delirium--was so fresh and novel and inspiring that the sight of it paid me well for the interruptions which my sleep had suffered. The king had slept just as usual--like the dead. The Marcos could not thank him for their clothes, that being forbidden; but they tried every way they could think of to make him see how grateful they were. Which all went for nothing: he didn't notice any change.

text: I directed my attention toward him, and before the first third of the meal had passed, I had restored his good humor. In a land defined by rigid social hierarchies, such a task is effortless. You see, in a country of ranks and castes, a man is never permitted to be a whole man; he is merely a fraction of one, stunted and unable to reach his full stature. Once you demonstrate your superiority in position or wealth, the matter is settled—he simply yields. It becomes nearly impossible to insult him after that. I do not mean that he is literally beyond offense, only that it is so difficult to provoke him that the effort is rarely worth the time. I now commanded the blacksmith’s deep respect because I appeared immensely wealthy; had I possessed even a trivial noble title, I would have had his total adoration. This was true of any commoner in the realm, even if he were a giant of intellect and character and I was utterly devoid of both. It seemed this would always be the case so long as England endured. With a sudden prophetic clarity, I could look ahead and see the nation building monuments to its dreadful Georges and other royal figureheads—mere clothes-horses—while leaving uncelebrated the true creators of the modern world: Gutenberg, Watt, Arkwright, Whitney, Morse, Stephenson, and Bell.
source_text: However, I made a dead set at him, and before the first third of the dinner was reached, I had him happy again. It was easy to do--in a country of ranks and castes. You see, in a country where they have ranks and castes, a man isn't ever a man, he is only part of a man, he can't ever get his full growth. You prove your superiority over him in station, or rank, or fortune, and that's the end of it--he knuckles down. You can't insult him after that. No, I don't mean quite that; of course you _can_ insult him, I only mean it's difficult; and so, unless you've got a lot of useless time on your hands it doesn't pay to try. I had the smith's reverence now, because I was apparently immensely prosperous and rich; I could have had his adoration if I had had some little gimcrack title of nobility. And not only his, but any commoner's in the land, though he were the mightiest production of all the ages, in intellect, worth, and character, and I bankrupt in all three. This was to remain so, as long as England should exist in the earth. With the spirit of prophecy upon me, I could look into the future and see her erect statues and monuments to her unspeakable Georges and other royal and noble clothes-horses, and leave unhonored the creators of this world--after God--Gutenburg, Watt, Arkwright, Whitney, Morse, Stephenson, Bell.

text: Whenever I decide to strike someone, I don’t settle for a gentle nudge; that simply isn’t my style. If I’m going to hit at all, I intend to deliver a knockout blow. I never rush in blindly and risk a clumsy, half-hearted attempt. Instead, I keep my distance and close in slowly, ensuring the target never even suspects an attack is coming. Then, in a sudden flash, he’s flat on his back, completely bewildered as to how it happened. That was exactly my approach with Dowley. I began speaking in a casual, relaxed manner, as if I were merely making small talk to kill time. Not even the most experienced man alive could have tracked my starting point or predicted where I was ultimately going to land my strike.
source_text: Well, when I make up my mind to hit a man, I don't plan out a love-tap; no, that isn't my way; as long as I'm going to hit him at all, I'm going to hit him a lifter. And I don't jump at him all of a sudden, and risk making a blundering half-way business of it; no, I get away off yonder to one side, and work up on him gradually, so that he never suspects that I'm going to hit him at all; and by and by, all in a flash, he's flat on his back, and he can't tell for the life of him how it all happened. That is the way I went for brother Dowley. I started to talking lazy and comfortable, as if I was just talking to pass the time; and the oldest man in the world couldn't have taken the bearings of my starting place and guessed where I was going to fetch up:

text: Suppose, then, that within that gathering there are several souls harboring private malice toward him—individuals nursing some hidden grudge. If he is already widely detested by the town—perhaps for his arrogance, his riches, or some other grievance—is it not certain that the harmless clods and rubbish will soon be traded for the lethal weight of cobbles and masonry?
source_text: “Well, then, suppose he has a few personal enemies in that mob and here and there a man or a woman with a secret grudge against him--and suppose especially that he is unpopular in the community, for his pride, or his prosperity, or one thing or another--stones and bricks take the place of clods and cats presently, don't they?”

text: Dowley flinched, and it was plain to see. I could tell the blow had landed, yet he refused to let any word of protest escape his lips. The others, however, were far more outspoken, voicing their intense emotions quite clearly. They insisted they had seen the pillory in action enough times to know a person's slim chances there, and they vowed they would never agree to endure it if they could instead settle for the mercy of a swift hanging.
source_text: Dowley winced, visibly. I judged he was hit. But he didn't betray it by any spoken word. As for the others, they spoke out plainly, and with strong feeling. They said they had seen enough of the stocks to know what a man's chance in them was, and they would never consent to enter them if they could compromise on a quick death by hanging.

text: Anyway, to move on—for I believe I have sufficiently proven that the stocks should be abolished. I find many of our laws to be quite inequitable. For instance, if I commit an act that warrants the stocks, and you are aware of it but choose to remain silent, you will be the one punished if someone reports your failure to speak up.
source_text: “Well, to change the subject--for I think I've established my point that the stocks ought to be abolished. I think some of our laws are pretty unfair. For instance, if I do a thing which ought to deliver me to the stocks, and you know I did it and yet keep still and don't report me, _you_ will get the stocks if anybody informs on you.”

text: “...and further illustrating the recognized truth that with animals, the young—who might be viewed as the creature's unripened fruit—are the better sort, as everyone admits that when a goat is fully grown, its coat overheats and ruins the flavor of its meat; this defect, when joined with its various foul habits, its repulsive cravings, its unholy disposition, and its generally bitter moral quality—”
source_text: “--and further instancing the known truth that in the case of animals, the young, which may be called the green fruit of the creature, is the better, all confessing that when a goat is ripe, his fur doth heat and sore engame his flesh, the which defect, taken in connection with his several rancid habits, and fulsome appetites, and godless attitudes of mind, and bilious quality of morals--”

text: The situation had grown grave. We stayed quiet, waiting to see what would follow. The peasant struggled through his arduous ascent. The King pulled himself up and stood firm, readying a kick; as soon as the man’s head came within range, a heavy thud sounded, and the climber went sprawling to the earth. A fierce roar of rage rose from below as the mob surged in from every side, leaving us cornered and captive in the branches. Another man began to climb; the connecting limb had been spotted, and a volunteer started up the neighboring tree that served as our bridge. The King ordered me to act the part of Horatius and hold the crossing. For a time, the attackers came in quick succession, but it mattered little; the leader of each attempt received a blow that sent him falling as soon as he was close enough. The King’s spirits soared, his happiness absolute. He declared that if nothing ruined our chances, we were in for a fine night, for with these tactics, we could defend the tree against the entire countryside.
source_text: Matters were serious now. We remained still, and awaited developments. The peasant toiled his difficult way up. The king raised himself up and stood; he made a leg ready, and when the comer's head arrived in reach of it there was a dull thud, and down went the man floundering to the ground. There was a wild outbreak of anger below, and the mob swarmed in from all around, and there we were treed, and prisoners. Another man started up; the bridging bough was detected, and a volunteer started up the tree that furnished the bridge. The king ordered me to play Horatius and keep the bridge. For a while the enemy came thick and fast; but no matter, the head man of each procession always got a buffet that dislodged him as soon as he came in reach. The king's spirits rose, his joy was limitless. He said that if nothing occurred to mar the prospect we should have a beautiful night, for on this line of tactics we could hold the tree against the whole country-side.

text: We spent a grueling month wandering the countryside, enduring constant hardship and pain. By then, which Englishman was most preoccupied with the question of slavery? It was the king himself. From his former state of total indifference, he had become the most fervent observer of the cause. He had grown into the most venomous hater of the practice I had ever heard speak. Thus, I dared to pose a question I had first asked years earlier, which had then met with such a sharp rebuff that I had considered it dangerous to ever raise the matter again. Would he finally abolish slavery?
source_text: We had a rough time for a month, tramping to and fro in the earth, and suffering. And what Englishman was the most interested in the slavery question by that time? His grace the king! Yes; from being the most indifferent, he was become the most interested. He was become the bitterest hater of the institution I had ever heard talk. And so I ventured to ask once more a question which I had asked years before and had gotten such a sharp answer that I had not thought it prudent to meddle in the matter further. Would he abolish slavery?

text: A sudden tumult arose. We detected wails and shouts, and presently a female emerged in a state of frantic flight; upon observing our assembly, she cast herself among us and pleaded for sanctuary. A riotous crowd pursued her closely, brandishing firebrands, and they proclaimed her a conjurer who had cursed the local herds with a mysterious plague, performing her spells with the aid of a fiend in the shape of an ink-colored cat. The wretched creature had been pelted with rocks until her features were scarcely recognizable, so mangled was she by the assault. The rabble intended to consign her to the pyre.
source_text: Now came a diversion. We heard shrieks and yells, and soon a woman came running and crying; and seeing our group, she flung herself into our midst and begged for protection. A mob of people came tearing after her, some with torches, and they said she was a witch who had caused several cows to die by a strange disease, and practiced her arts by help of a devil in the form of a black cat. This poor woman had been stoned until she hardly looked human, she was so battered and bloody. The mob wanted to burn her.

text:
You grasp my design; you perceive the magnificent theatrical flourish I intended for our arrival at the citadel. The scheme was entirely sound, provided I could secure a sliver of tempered metal to fashion into a makeshift probe. With such a tool, I might spring the heavy iron mechanisms binding our wrists whenever the moment seemed ripe. Yet fortune is a fickle mistress, and for weeks no such scrap crossed my path. At last, however, my opportunity arrived. 

A merchant who had twice before attempted to bargain for me—without coming near a closed deal—returned once more. I felt no dread of being sold to him, for the ransom demanded for my head since the hour of my capture was absurdly high, drawing either outrage or mockery from every bidder. My master, however, was immovable; he demanded twenty gold pieces and would not abate a single copper. My companion, the Sovereign, was widely gaped at for his impressive frame, but his regal air made him a liability; no one desires a servant who looks born to command. I felt insulated by my own exorbitant cost. 

Thus, I did not expect to follow this man home, but I did expect to relieve him of a certain possession if he continued his visits. He wore a heavy wool cloak fastened by three steel pins. Twice he had frustrated me by keeping a cautious distance, but this time I triumphed. I plucked the lowermost clasp from his breast, and when he eventually noted its absence, he chalked the loss up to the rigors of the journey.
source_text: But you get my idea; you see what a stunning dramatic surprise I would wind up with at the palace. It was all feasible, if I could only get hold of a slender piece of iron which I could shape into a lock-pick. I could then undo the lumbering padlocks with which our chains were fastened, whenever I might choose. But I never had any luck; no such thing ever happened to fall in my way. However, my chance came at last. A gentleman who had come twice before to dicker for me, without result, or indeed any approach to a result, came again. I was far from expecting ever to belong to him, for the price asked for me from the time I was first enslaved was exorbitant, and always provoked either anger or derision, yet my master stuck stubbornly to it--twenty-two dollars. He wouldn't bate a cent. The king was greatly admired, because of his grand physique, but his kingly style was against him, and he wasn't salable; nobody wanted that kind of a slave. I considered myself safe from parting from him because of my extravagant price. No, I was not expecting to ever belong to this gentleman whom I have spoken of, but he had something which I expected would belong to me eventually, if he would but visit us often enough. It was a steel thing with a long pin to it, with which his long cloth outside garment was fastened together in front. There were three of them. He had disappointed me twice, because he did not come quite close enough to me to make my project entirely safe; but this time I succeeded; I captured the lower clasp of the three, and when he missed it he thought he had lost it on the way.

text: I stopped there, but the king was delighted and entirely satisfied. That night, we waited in silence for our fellow captives to drift off and signal their slumber in the usual way, for it is unwise to risk much on such desperate men if it can be avoided. It is always better to keep your own counsel. They likely tossed and turned no more than they did any other night, yet to my ears, it felt as though they would never settle into a steady rhythm of snoring. As the hours crawled by, I grew frantic that we would run out of darkness before our work was done. My impatience led me to make several premature movements that only served to hinder us; in the gloom, I could not so much as touch a padlock without it clattering, a sound that startled someone from his sleep and caused him to stir, waking the rest of the group in turn.
source_text: That was as far as I went, but the king was charmed and satisfied. That evening we waited patiently for our fellow-slaves to get to sleep and signify it by the usual sign, for you must not take many chances on those poor fellows if you can avoid it. It is best to keep your own secrets. No doubt they fidgeted only about as usual, but it didn't seem so to me. It seemed to me that they were going to be forever getting down to their regular snoring. As the time dragged on I got nervously afraid we shouldn't have enough of it left for our needs; so I made several premature attempts, and merely delayed things by it; for I couldn't seem to touch a padlock, there in the dark, without starting a rattle out of it which interrupted somebody's sleep and made him turn over and wake some more of the gang.

text: Only the unfree stood witness. They recounted how the most esteemed of their rank had shed his shackles and fled by means of some occult power, for the fastenings were neither forced nor the locks disturbed by any key. When the lord discovered his ruin, he was overtaken by a desperate fury and struck at his servants with a stout staff; they, however, rose against him and snapped his back, dealing such various and grievous wounds that he was brought swiftly to his reckoning.
source_text: “There was no witness but the slaves. They said the slave that was most valuable got free of his bonds and escaped in some strange way--by magic arts 'twas thought, by reason that he had no key, and the locks were neither broke nor in any wise injured. When the master discovered his loss, he was mad with despair, and threw himself upon his people with his heavy stick, who resisted and brake his back and in other and divers ways did give him hurts that brought him swiftly to his end.”

text: Therefore, to furnish myself with additional resources, I decided to locate several figures from my earlier days and reveal my identity. Such a move promised to deliver us from our difficulties without the aid of the armored gentry. However, I had to move with extreme care, as the gambit was delicate. I required a wardrobe of the highest order, yet a precipitous change in costume would be suspicious. I would have to progress by stages, acquiring one outfit after another from scattered merchants, each ensemble a notch more elegant than its predecessor, until I was eventually clad in the silks and velvets necessary for my scheme. With that, I commenced my journey.
source_text: Now, then, in order to increase the strings to my bow, I thought I would look up some of those people whom I had formerly recognized, and make myself known. That would help us out of our scrape, without the knights. But I must proceed cautiously, for it was a risky business. I must get into sumptuous raiment, and it wouldn't do to run and jump into it. No, I must work up to it by degrees, buying suit after suit of clothes, in shops wide apart, and getting a little finer article with each change, until I should finally reach silk and velvet, and be ready for my project. So I started.

text: To be honest, I desperately needed someone to rely on. My knights weren't going to arrive in time; they were at least three hours behind schedule. Absolutely nothing could save the King of England now, nor myself—which was the greater loss. It was more significant not just for my own sake, but for the nation, the only place on earth poised to emerge into true civilization. I felt physically ill. I fell silent, as there was nothing left to say. I understood his implication: if the runaway slave were captured, the delay would be overturned and the execution would proceed today. And then, the slave was found.
source_text: The fact is I did need to lean upon somebody. My knights couldn't arrive in time. They would be as much as three hours too late. Nothing in the world could save the King of England; nor me, which was more important. More important, not merely to me, but to the nation--the only nation on earth standing ready to blossom into civilization. I was sick. I said no more, there wasn't anything to say. I knew what the man meant; that if the missing slave was found, the postponement would be revoked, the execution take place to-day. Well, the missing slave was found.

text: Within moments, a third prisoner was thrashing in the air. It was a ghastly sight. I looked away for a heartbeat, and when I turned back, I lost sight of the king—they were blindfolding him! I was frozen; my body wouldn't move, my breath was caught in my throat, and I was utterly speechless. They finished covering his eyes and led him beneath the gallows. I couldn't break free from that suffocating helplessness. But the second I saw them tighten the noose around his neck, my paralysis shattered and I lunged to his rescue—and as I did, I cast one last look toward the horizon. Good lord! There they came, charging at full speed—five hundred armored knights on bicycles!
source_text: In a minute a third slave was struggling in the air. It was dreadful. I turned away my head a moment, and when I turned back I missed the king! They were blindfolding him! I was paralyzed; I couldn't move, I was choking, my tongue was petrified. They finished blindfolding him, they led him under the rope. I couldn't shake off that clinging impotence. But when I saw them put the noose around his neck, then everything let go in me and I made a spring to the rescue--and as I made it I shot one more glance abroad--by George! here they came, a-tilting!--five hundred mailed and belted knights on bicycles!

text: I stepped out then. But no roar of welcome met me. Instead, a stunned and expressive silence lingered for a beat, followed by a massive wave of laughter that began to roll through the sea of people, only to be cut dead by a sharp bugle blast. I was dressed in the most basic and practical of athletic outfits—flesh-toned tights from my throat to my heels, with blue silk ruffles around my waist, and nothing on my head. My horse was only average-sized, but he was sharp and lean, his muscles like coiled springs, and he was a pure racer. He was a gorgeous creature, his coat as glossy as satin, and he was as unadorned as the day he was born, except for his bridle and a ranger-saddle.
source_text: And then out I came. But I didn't get any shout. There was a wondering and eloquent silence for a moment, then a great wave of laughter began to sweep along that human sea, but a warning bugle-blast cut its career short. I was in the simplest and comfortablest of gymnast costumes--flesh-colored tights from neck to heel, with blue silk puffings about my loins, and bareheaded. My horse was not above medium size, but he was alert, slender-limbed, muscled with watchsprings, and just a greyhound to go. He was a beauty, glossy as silk, and naked as he was when he was born, except for bridle and ranger-saddle.

text: The crowd demanded a repeat, and then another, and yet another still. Once I had unhorsed five of their number, the armored ranks grew somber, pausing to hold a desperate council. They concluded that the time for mere pageantry was over and that they must deploy their most formidable legends. To the utter bewilderment of the assembly, my hempen coil brought down Sir Valerius, followed swiftly by the peerless Sir Percival. There was nothing left for them now but to reveal their ultimate gambit—to summon the paragon of paragons, the titan of the realm, the illustrious Sir Roland himself!
source_text: I got another encore; and another, and another, and still another. When I had snaked five men out, things began to look serious to the ironclads, and they stopped and consulted together. As a result, they decided that it was time to waive etiquette and send their greatest and best against me. To the astonishment of that little world, I lassoed Sir Lamorak de Galis, and after him Sir Galahad. So you see there was simply nothing to be done now, but play their right bower--bring out the superbest of the superb, the mightiest of the mighty, the great Sir Launcelot himself!

text: Imagine that three years have passed. Survey the landscape of England today; it is a joyful, flourishing land, remarkably changed. Educational institutions have sprouted everywhere, including several colleges and a scattering of respectable newspapers. Even the literary arts were beginning to blossom; Sir Dinadan the Humorist was the pioneer, releasing a tome of ancient jokes that I had already endured for thirteen hundred years. Had he simply excluded that putrid old gag about the lecturer, I might have remained silent; however, I found it utterly unbearable. I banned the volume and executed the author.
source_text: Consider the three years sped. Now look around on England. A happy and prosperous country, and strangely altered. Schools everywhere, and several colleges; a number of pretty good newspapers. Even authorship was taking a start; Sir Dinadan the Humorist was first in the field, with a volume of gray-headed jokes which I had been familiar with during thirteen centuries. If he had left out that old rancid one about the lecturer I wouldn't have said anything; but I couldn't stand that one. I suppressed the book and hanged the author.

text: Scarcely a knight remained in the entire kingdom who had not been put to some productive use. They journeyed across the territory in various helpful missionary roles; their inherent restlessness and extensive travel experience made them the most proficient distributors of modern culture available. Outfitted in heavy steel and bristling with swords and lances, they promoted their goods. If a prospect could not be convinced to accept a sewing machine on credit, or a melodeon, or a spool of barbed wire, or a temperance periodical, or any of the myriad products they carried, the knight simply eliminated the man and moved along.
source_text: There was hardly a knight in all the land who wasn't in some useful employment. They were going from end to end of the country in all manner of useful missionary capacities; their penchant for wandering, and their experience in it, made them altogether the most effective spreaders of civilization we had. They went clothed in steel and equipped with sword and lance and battle-axe, and if they couldn't persuade a person to try a sewing-machine on the installment plan, or a melodeon, or a barbed-wire fence, or a prohibition journal, or any of the other thousand and one things they canvassed for, they removed him and passed on.

text: My contentment was absolute. Events were gravitating toward a long-cherished objective with remarkable precision. I harbored two monumental designs, the most expansive of my various enterprises. The first was to subvert the established Orthodoxy and erect in its place a pluralistic, non-compulsory faith—not a state-sanctioned institution, but a system of private conscience. My second ambition was to eventually procure an edict declaring that, upon the passing of King Alaric, universal suffrage should be enacted for all citizens. This franchise would extend to every man, regardless of his mental acuity, and to every mother who, in her middle years, proved to possess as much sense as her twenty-one-year-old son. Alaric, being roughly forty like myself, likely had another thirty years of life; I felt confident that in such an interval, I could prime the citizenry for a spectacle unique in the annals of civilization: a comprehensive, peaceful political transformation. The ultimate aim was a republic. I must confess, though the admission brings a flush of shame, that I had begun to feel a sordid desire to serve as its first president. It seemed that, despite my lofty ideals, I was still susceptible to the standard vanities of the human heart.
source_text: I was very happy. Things were working steadily toward a secretly longed-for point. You see, I had two schemes in my head which were the vastest of all my projects. The one was to overthrow the Catholic Church and set up the Protestant faith on its ruins --not as an Established Church, but a go-as-you-please one; and the other project was to get a decree issued by and by, commanding that upon Arthur's death unlimited suffrage should be introduced, and given to men and women alike--at any rate to all men, wise or unwise, and to all mothers who at middle age should be found to know nearly as much as their sons at twenty-one. Arthur was good for thirty years yet, he being about my own age--that is to say, forty--and I believed that in that time I could easily have the active part of the population of that day ready and eager for an event which should be the first of its kind in the history of the world--a rounded and complete governmental revolution without bloodshed. The result to be a republic. Well, I may as well confess, though I do feel ashamed when I think of it: I was beginning to have a base hankering to be its first president myself. Yes, there was more or less human nature in me; I found that out.

text:
Clarence supported my revolutionary goals, though with a particular caveat. He favored a republic that abolished social castes but retained a hereditary monarchy at its helm instead of an elected leader. He argued that any people who had experienced the delight of idolizing a royal family could never be deprived of it without withering away from profound sadness. When I pointed out that kings were a hazard, he suggested substituting them with cats. He was certain a feline dynasty would serve every necessary function. They would be just as effective as any human royals, possess equal intellect, and display the same blend of virtues and vices, including a tendency to pick fights with other royal cats. They would be hilariously conceited and absurd without ever realizing it, and they would cost absolutely nothing. Furthermore, their claim to divine right would be as legitimate as any other royal line; "Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV, King by the Grace of God," would sound just as dignified as the same title applied to a typical human monarch in his finery. 

"Moreover," he noted in his polished, modern tongue, "the temperament of these cats would generally be far superior to that of the average king. This would provide a tremendous moral boost to the public, as a nation always patterns its ethics after its ruler. Since the veneration of royalty is based on emotion rather than logic, these elegant and harmless animals would easily become as hallowed as any other sovereign—perhaps even more so. People would eventually observe that they never hang, behead, or imprison anyone, nor do they perpetrate any cruelties or injustices. Thus, they would be entitled to a deeper level of affection and respect than a standard human king, and they would surely receive it. The gaze of a troubled world would soon settle upon this kind and gentle model, causing royal despots to vanish. Their subjects would fill those empty thrones with kittens from our own royal stock; we would become a sort of factory, exporting monarchs to every nation. Within forty years, all of Europe would be under feline rule, and we would be the ones supplying the cats. An age of eternal global peace would then begin... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow—fzt!—wow!"
source_text: Clarence was with me as concerned the revolution, but in a modified way. His idea was a republic, without privileged orders, but with a hereditary royal family at the head of it instead of an elective chief magistrate. He believed that no nation that had ever known the joy of worshiping a royal family could ever be robbed of it and not fade away and die of melancholy. I urged that kings were dangerous. He said, then have cats. He was sure that a royal family of cats would answer every purpose. They would be as useful as any other royal family, they would know as much, they would have the same virtues and the same treacheries, the same disposition to get up shindies with other royal cats, they would be laughably vain and absurd and never know it, they would be wholly inexpensive; finally, they would have as sound a divine right as any other royal house, and “Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV by the grace of God King,” would sound as well as it would when applied to the ordinary royal tomcat with tights on. “And as a rule,” said he, in his neat modern English, “the character of these cats would be considerably above the character of the average king, and this would be an immense moral advantage to the nation, for the reason that a nation always models its morals after its monarch's. The worship of royalty being founded in unreason, these graceful and harmless cats would easily become as sacred as any other royalties, and indeed more so, because it would presently be noticed that they hanged nobody, beheaded nobody, imprisoned nobody, inflicted no cruelties or injustices of any sort, and so must be worthy of a deeper love and reverence than the customary human king, and would certainly get it. The eyes of the whole harried world would soon be fixed upon this humane and gentle system, and royal butchers would presently begin to disappear; their subjects would fill the vacancies with catlings from our own royal house; we should become a factory; we should supply the thrones of the world; within forty years all Europe would be governed by cats, and we should furnish the cats. The reign of universal peace would begin then, to end no more forever.... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow--fzt!--wow!”

text: Sir Launcelot, decked out in his most opulent plate, marched through the grand hall toward the trading floor. He served as the exchange’s chairman, occupying the Siege Perilous—a seat he’d purchased from Sir Galahad—since the Knights of the Round Table had repurposed their legendary furniture for high finance. The price of a seat there was—well, the numbers were so staggering you wouldn't believe me if I told you. Launcelot was playing the role of a market bear; he’d cornered one of the emerging lines and was prepared to bleed the short-sellers dry that very day. Yet, he was still the same old Launcelot. The moment he peeked through the door and saw that his little favorite was unwell, his priorities shifted instantly. Bulls and bears could tear each other apart for all he cared; he was going to stay right there and do everything possible for little Hello-Central. And that is exactly what he did. He tossed his helmet into a corner and, within seconds, had replaced the wick in the alcohol lamp to get the croup-kettle steaming. By then, Sandy had already rigged a blanket tent over the cradle, and everything was ready.
source_text: Sir Launcelot, in his richest armor, came striding along the great hall now on his way to the stock-board; he was president of the stock-board, and occupied the Siege Perilous, which he had bought of Sir Galahad; for the stock-board consisted of the Knights of the Round Table, and they used the Round Table for business purposes now. Seats at it were worth--well, you would never believe the figure, so it is no use to state it. Sir Launcelot was a bear, and he had put up a corner in one of the new lines, and was just getting ready to squeeze the shorts to-day; but what of that? He was the same old Launcelot, and when he glanced in as he was passing the door and found out that his pet was sick, that was enough for him; bulls and bears might fight it out their own way for all him, he would come right in here and stand by little Hello-Central for all he was worth. And that was what he did. He shied his helmet into the corner, and in half a minute he had a new wick in the alcohol lamp and was firing up on the croup-kettle. By this time Sandy had built a blanket canopy over the crib, and everything was ready.

text: Sir Launcelot built up the steam; he and I packed the kettle with unslaked lime and carbolic acid, adding a dash of lactic acid before filling it with water and tucking the spout beneath the canopy. Once everything was prepared, we took our positions on either side of the crib to begin our vigil. Sandy was so moved and reassured that she readied a pair of long-stemmed pipes with willow-bark and sumach-tobacco, urging us to smoke as much as we liked. She promised the smoke wouldn't reach the child, and she was already quite used to the sight, being the first lady in the kingdom to ever see a man blow a cloud. There could hardly be a more serene or contented sight than Sir Launcelot in his grand armor, sitting in gracious composure with a long, white pipe. He was a truly fine man, a kind soul who seemed perfectly suited to a life of domestic happiness. But, naturally, there was Guenever—though it is useless to dwell on what is done and cannot be mended.
source_text: Sir Launcelot got up steam, he and I loaded up the kettle with unslaked lime and carbolic acid, with a touch of lactic acid added thereto, then filled the thing up with water and inserted the steam-spout under the canopy. Everything was ship-shape now, and we sat down on either side of the crib to stand our watch. Sandy was so grateful and so comforted that she charged a couple of church-wardens with willow-bark and sumach-tobacco for us, and told us to smoke as much as we pleased, it couldn't get under the canopy, and she was used to smoke, being the first lady in the land who had ever seen a cloud blown. Well, there couldn't be a more contented or comfortable sight than Sir Launcelot in his noble armor sitting in gracious serenity at the end of a yard of snowy church-warden. He was a beautiful man, a lovely man, and was just intended to make a wife and children happy. But, of course Guenever--however, it's no use to cry over what's done and can't be helped.

text: The medical experts advised that for the girl to regain her vigor, we had to get her away from home. Sea air was considered essential. Thus, we commissioned a warship and a staff of two hundred and sixty, spending a fortnight at sea before landing on the French shore. The doctors recommended we remain there for a while. The local ruler offered us his guest quarters, an invitation we accepted. If his home had been half as well-equipped as it was deficient, we would have been perfectly content; as it happened, we fared well enough in his strange, ancient castle by relying on the supplies and luxuries we brought from our vessel.
source_text: The doctors said we must take the child away, if we would coax her back to health and strength again. And she must have sea-air. So we took a man-of-war, and a suite of two hundred and sixty persons, and went cruising about, and after a fortnight of this we stepped ashore on the French coast, and the doctors thought it would be a good idea to make something of a stay there. The little king of that region offered us his hospitalities, and we were glad to accept. If he had had as many conveniences as he lacked, we should have been plenty comfortable enough; even as it was, we made out very well, in his queer old castle, by the help of comforts and luxuries from the ship.

text: Once a month had elapsed, I sent the vessel back for supplies and intelligence, expecting her return in just a few days. She would bring me word on the progress of a specific project I had launched. My goal was to supplant the traditional tournament with a pursuit that could drain off the knights' surplus energy, keeping these young nobles occupied and harmless while still fostering their most admirable quality—their robust competitive drive. I had been drilling a select group of them in secret for quite some time, and the moment for their first public appearance was now imminent.
source_text: At the end of a month I sent the vessel home for fresh supplies, and for news. We expected her back in three or four days. She would bring me, along with other news, the result of a certain experiment which I had been starting. It was a project of mine to replace the tournament with something which might furnish an escape for the extra steam of the chivalry, keep those bucks entertained and out of mischief, and at the same time preserve the best thing in them, which was their hardy spirit of emulation. I had had a choice band of them in private training for some time, and the date was now arriving for their first public effort.

text: But my focus was abruptly diverted from all else; our daughter began to fail again, and her illness became so dire that we were compelled to keep a constant vigil. We refused to let any outsider assist us, so the two of us shared the burden, taking alternating shifts day and night. Oh, Sandy—what a noble soul she possessed, so uncomplicated, sincere, and kind! She was an impeccable wife and mother, yet I had initially married her for no better reason than that, by the codes of chivalry, she was my legal property until some knight might win her from me in combat. She had scoured Britain in search of me, finally locating me at that grim execution outside London, and had quietly resumed her place by my side as though it were her birthright. As a New Englander, I feared this informal union would eventually disgrace her. She could not fathom why, but I silenced her objections and we held a proper wedding.
source_text: However, my attention was suddenly snatched from such matters; our child began to lose ground again, and we had to go to sitting up with her, her case became so serious. We couldn't bear to allow anybody to help in this service, so we two stood watch-and-watch, day in and day out. Ah, Sandy, what a right heart she had, how simple, and genuine, and good she was! She was a flawless wife and mother; and yet I had married her for no other particular reasons, except that by the customs of chivalry she was my property until some knight should win her from me in the field. She had hunted Britain over for me; had found me at the hanging-bout outside of London, and had straightway resumed her old place at my side in the placidest way and as of right. I was a New Englander, and in my opinion this sort of partnership would compromise her, sooner or later. She couldn't see how, but I cut argument short and we had a wedding.

text: A minute later, I rejoined my group. They had clearly been gripped by dark forebodings during my absence; it was written all over their faces. I called for an escort, and we galloped five miles to a ridge overlooking the sea. Where was my vast merchant fleet that had so recently crowded these glittering waters with the beauty of their white sails? They had all vanished. From one horizon to the other, not a single sail or plume of smoke remained—nothing but a dead and empty silence where there had once been such a bustling, energetic world.
source_text: In another minute I appeared in the presence of my train. They had been steeped in troubled bodings all this time--their faces showed it. I called an escort and we galloped five miles to a hilltop overlooking the sea. Where was my great commerce that so lately had made these glistening expanses populous and beautiful with its white-winged flocks? Vanished, every one! Not a sail, from verge to verge, not a smoke-bank--just a dead and empty solitude, in place of all that brisk and breezy life.

text: Naturally, I intended to take the train back to Camelot. A train! The station was as hollow as a cave. I pressed forward. The trip was nothing more than a repeat of everything I had already witnessed; Monday and Tuesday were identical to Sunday. I finally reached my destination in the dead of night. What had once been the most brilliantly illuminated town in the realm—shining like a sun resting on the horizon—was now nothing but a dark blotch against the sky. It was a mass of shadow thicker and more solid than the night itself, which only served to make its outline clearer. It struck me as symbolic, a grim omen that the Church was determined to keep its hold and snuff out my modern civilization in an instant. I saw no one moving in the gloomy streets. I felt my way along with a sinking heart. The great castle loomed like a black monolith on the hill, without a single spark of light. The drawbridge was lowered and the massive gates stood open; I walked in unchallenged, the click of my heels the only sound in the air—a ghostly noise that echoed through those enormous, empty courts.
source_text: Of course, I meant to take the train for Camelot. Train! Why, the station was as vacant as a cavern. I moved on. The journey to Camelot was a repetition of what I had already seen. The Monday and the Tuesday differed in no way from the Sunday. I arrived far in the night. From being the best electric-lighted town in the kingdom and the most like a recumbent sun of anything you ever saw, it was become simply a blot--a blot upon darkness--that is to say, it was darker and solider than the rest of the darkness, and so you could see it a little better; it made me feel as if maybe it was symbolical--a sort of sign that the Church was going to _keep_ the upper hand now, and snuff out all my beautiful civilization just like that. I found no life stirring in the somber streets. I groped my way with a heavy heart. The vast castle loomed black upon the hilltop, not a spark visible about it. The drawbridge was down, the great gate stood wide, I entered without challenge, my own heels making the only sound I heard--and it was sepulchral enough, in those huge vacant courts.

text: Lord, what a simpleton I was! By the week’s end, the massive and bitter truth finally hammered its way into my consciousness: the great bulk of the populace had tossed their bonnets and cheered for the commonwealth for a solitary day, and that was the limit of it! The clergy, the aristocracy, and the landed gentry had only to cast one colossal, icy scowl upon them to shrivel their spirits into those of cattle. From that instant, the herd began to drift back toward the pen—meaning the military camps—to surrender their worthless hides and their valuable wool to the "holy crusade." Even the wretches who had so recently worn shackles were now champions of this "divine cause," exalting it, praying for its success, and slobbering over it with the same maudlin zeal as every other peasant. To think of such human dross; to contemplate such utter madness!
source_text: Ah, what a donkey I was! Toward the end of the week I began to get this large and disenchanting fact through my head: that the mass of the nation had swung their caps and shouted for the republic for about one day, and there an end! The Church, the nobles, and the gentry then turned one grand, all-disapproving frown upon them and shriveled them into sheep! From that moment the sheep had begun to gather to the fold--that is to say, the camps--and offer their valueless lives and their valuable wool to the “righteous cause.” Why, even the very men who had lately been slaves were in the “righteous cause,” and glorifying it, praying for it, sentimentally slabbering over it, just like all the other commoners. Imagine such human muck as this; conceive of this folly!

text: “We have struggled to ignore our very essence—that we are sons of Britain. We sought to exalt cold intellect above our passions, and mandate above mercy; our thoughts may agree, but our souls rebel. While the foe was comprised only of the aristocracy, the squires, and the meager thousands of cavalrymen remaining from the old campaigns, we remained steadfast and certain. Each of the fifty young men standing in your sight believed, ‘They have sought this end; let them meet it.’ But stay! The world has changed—the whole of the nation is in the field against us! Oh, sir, I implore you—pause! These people are our brethren, our own blood and very breath; we love them. Do not demand that we dismantle our own home!”
source_text: “We have tried to forget what we are--English boys! We have tried to put reason before sentiment, duty before love; our minds approve, but our hearts reproach us. While apparently it was only the nobility, only the gentry, only the twenty-five or thirty thousand knights left alive out of the late wars, we were of one mind, and undisturbed by any troubling doubt; each and every one of these fifty-two lads who stand here before you, said, 'They have chosen--it is their affair.' But think!--the matter is altered--_All England is marching against us_! Oh, sir, consider! --reflect!--these people are our people, they are bone of our bone, flesh of our flesh, we love them--do not ask us to destroy our nation!”

text: That is true. They are thirty thousand strong, marching in ranks that stretch back for acres. Now, mark this: only the knights will ever reach the sand-belt. Then, a sudden incident will occur. Immediately afterward, the civilian masses in the rear will scatter, suddenly finding urgent business elsewhere. Since only the nobility are knights, only they will remain to face our music after that event. It is a certainty that we shall fight no one but those thirty thousand. Now, speak, and the decision is yours. Shall we avoid the battle and withdraw from the field?
source_text: “True. They are thirty thousand strong. Acres deep they will march. Now, observe: none but _they_ will ever strike the sand-belt! Then there will be an episode! Immediately after, the civilian multitude in the rear will retire, to meet business engagements elsewhere. None but nobles and gentry are knights, and _none but these_ will remain to dance to our music after that episode. It is absolutely true that we shall have to fight nobody but these thirty thousand knights. Now speak, and it shall be as you decide. Shall we avoid the battle, retire from the field?”

text: Finally, the details came into focus. The entire front line, extending back for what seemed like endless acres, was composed of cavalry—armored knights with plumes tossing. Suddenly, a trumpet blast rang out; the slow march ignited into a gallop, and the spectacle was truly breathtaking. That enormous, horseshoe-shaped wave swept downward, nearing the sandy perimeter. I caught my breath as they pressed closer—the sliver of green turf past the yellow sand grew thin, then thinner, until it was nothing but a ribbon before the horses, finally vanishing under their hooves. Good Lord! The whole leading edge of that force was hurled into the sky with a thunderous roar, transforming into a chaotic cyclone of rags and debris. A heavy wall of smoke clung to the earth, masking the rest of the throng from our eyes.
source_text: At last we could make out details. All the front ranks, no telling how many acres deep, were horsemen--plumed knights in armor. Suddenly we heard the blare of trumpets; the slow walk burst into a gallop, and then--well, it was wonderful to see! Down swept that vast horse-shoe wave--it approached the sand-belt--my breath stood still; nearer, nearer--the strip of green turf beyond the yellow belt grew narrow--narrower still--became a mere ribbon in front of the horses--then disappeared under their hoofs. Great Scott! Why, the whole front of that host shot into the sky with a thunder-crash, and became a whirling tempest of rags and fragments; and along the ground lay a thick wall of smoke that hid what was left of the multitude from our sight.

text: To the honorable commander of England’s rebelling aristocracy: Your resistance is futile. We have gauged your strength—if it even deserves that name. We know that, at most, you can muster only twenty-five thousand knights. You have, therefore, no hope of victory—none at all. Consider: we are well-armed, heavily fortified, and we number fifty-four. Fifty-four what? Men? No—*minds*, the most formidable in the world. Against such a power, raw physical force can no more prevail than the aimless waves can hope to shatter the granite cliffs of England. Take heed. We offer you your lives; for the sake of your families, do not reject this mercy. This is your final opportunity: lay down your weapons, surrender unconditionally to the Republic, and you will be forgiven.
source_text: TO THE HONORABLE THE COMMANDER OF THE INSURGENT CHIVALRY OF ENGLAND: You fight in vain. We know your strength—if one may call it by that name. We know that at the utmost you cannot bring against us above five and twenty thousand knights. Therefore, you have no chance—none whatever. Reflect: we are well equipped, well fortified, we number 54. Fifty-four what? Men? No, _minds_—the capablest in the world; a force against which mere animal might may no more hope to prevail than may the idle waves of the sea hope to prevail against the granite barriers of England. Be advised. We offer you your lives; for the sake of your families, do not reject the gift. We offer you this chance, and it is the last: throw down your arms; surrender unconditionally to the Republic, and all will be forgiven.

text: Once the darkness had fully descended, I cut the electricity to the perimeter fences and felt my way toward the embankment that edged our side of the great dynamite trench. I crawled to the crest and lay flat against the muddy incline to observe, though the night was too thick to discern anything. Silence reigned, heavy and absolute. To be sure, the common sounds of the countryside remained—the rush of wings, the drone of insects, the muffled barking of dogs, and the distant lowing of cows—but these did not pierce the stillness. Rather, they seemed to underscore it, lending the air a sense of macabre and heavy-hearted gloom.
source_text: As soon as it was good and dark, I shut off the current from all the fences, and then groped my way out to the embankment bordering our side of the great dynamite ditch. I crept to the top of it and lay there on the slant of the muck to watch. But it was too dark to see anything. As for sounds, there were none. The stillness was deathlike. True, there were the usual night-sounds of the country--the whir of night-birds, the buzzing of insects, the barking of distant dogs, the mellow lowing of far-off kine --but these didn't seem to break the stillness, they only intensified it, and added a grewsome melancholy to it into the bargain.

text: I electrified the third fence, followed quickly by the fourth and fifth, as the ranks surged forward to fill every gap. I sensed the moment for my grand finale had arrived, convinced that the entire host was now caught in our snare. Regardless, it was time to be certain. I pressed a switch, and fifty brilliant suns of electric light blazed to life along the edge of the precipice.
source_text: I sent a current through the third fence now; and almost immediately through the fourth and fifth, so quickly were the gaps filled up. I believed the time was come now for my climax; I believed that that whole army was in our trap. Anyway, it was high time to find out. So I touched a button and set fifty electric suns aflame on the top of our precipice.

text: It falls to me, Julian, to set down this record in his stead. He had proposed that we venture forth to see if any succor might be granted to the mangled. I protested the endeavor with great fervor. I maintained that if the casualties were many, our efforts would be futile, and it was surely unwise to trust our safety to those we had just broken. Yet his resolve was seldom altered once it had taken shape; thus, we severed the galvanic hum from the fences, summoned a small guard, clambered over the grisly barricade of slaughtered men, and moved onto the field. The first wretch who cried for aid sat leaned against the carcass of a comrade. When the Captain bent low to address him, the man knew him at once and drove a blade into his side. That soldier was Lord Valerius, as I learned by tearing away his helm. He will require no more assistance.
source_text: I, Clarence, must write it for him. He proposed that we two go out and see if any help could be accorded the wounded. I was strenuous against the project. I said that if there were many, we could do but little for them; and it would not be wise for us to trust ourselves among them, anyway. But he could seldom be turned from a purpose once formed; so we shut off the electric current from the fences, took an escort along, climbed over the enclosing ramparts of dead knights, and moved out upon the field. The first wounded man who appealed for help was sitting with his back against a dead comrade. When The Boss bent over him and spoke to him, the man recognized him and stabbed him. That knight was Sir Meliagraunce, as I found out by tearing off his helmet. He will not ask for help any more.

text: Our leader has not moved; he remains in a slumber as heavy as stone. If he does not rouse himself by nightfall, we will finally understand the true nature of his rest, and we shall carry his body to a secluded hollow deep within the cave where it can never be found or disturbed. As for the survivors, we have resolved that if any one of us manages to escape this place alive, he will record the event here and faithfully conceal this manuscript with our beloved chief, for it is his rightful property, whether he breathes or not.
source_text: The Boss has never stirred--sleeps like a stone. If he does not wake to-day we shall understand what kind of a sleep it is, and his body will then be borne to a place in one of the remote recesses of the cave where none will ever find it to desecrate it. As for the rest of us--well, it is agreed that if any one of us ever escapes alive from this place, he will write the fact here, and loyally hide this Manuscript with The Boss, our dear good chief, whose property it is, be he alive or dead.

text: Daybreak arrived just as I set the document aside. The rain had slowed to a trickle; the world appeared bleak and colorless, and the spent gale was whimpering itself into a hollow peace. I crossed to the wanderer’s room and stood by the door, which remained partly open. Hearing his voice within, I rapped against the frame. There was no reply, yet the murmuring went on. I peered inside. The man was supine, speaking in jagged, spirited bursts, his limbs flailing with the aimless agitation of the delirious. I stole inside and hovered over him. His rambling entreaties continued. I spoke a single word to anchor his wandering mind. At once, his clouded eyes and pallid face kindled with a sudden fire of joy, gratitude, and profound recognition.
source_text: The dawn was come when I laid the Manuscript aside. The rain had almost ceased, the world was gray and sad, the exhausted storm was sighing and sobbing itself to rest. I went to the stranger's room, and listened at his door, which was slightly ajar. I could hear his voice, and so I knocked. There was no answer, but I still heard the voice. I peeped in. The man lay on his back in bed, talking brokenly but with spirit, and punctuating with his arms, which he thrashed about, restlessly, as sick people do in delirium. I slipped in softly and bent over him. His mutterings and ejaculations went on. I spoke--merely a word, to call his attention. His glassy eyes and his ashy face were alight in an instant with pleasure, gratitude, gladness, welcome:

text: Mr. Direck represented a familiar American archetype. He bore a striking resemblance to those wholesome, well-groomed figures found in the pages of American periodicals—the cheerful men who grin while endorsing "Fizgig" products, or insist that "Wilkins" is the superior choice, or boast that their "Chesson" shirts never wrinkle. At this moment, however, his steady smile accompanied a different refrain: "Excellent. It’s English." He took immense delight in everything that deviated from the American norm, celebrating every detail he deemed authentically British. During the rail journey to London, the sight of the Cheshire hills divided into a patchwork of tiny fields had moved him to joyful laughter, and the discovery of a train carriage lacking a corridor provoked a satisfied chuckle. After a brief hesitation over the propriety of the gesture, he had bestowed a lavish tip upon the courteous, friendly guard. Later, he wandered through his London hotel in a state of near-rapture, exclaiming "Lordy! Lordy! My *word!*" as he confirmed the charming lack of telephones, central heating, or private bathrooms. During breakfast, a waiter who appeared to have stepped directly from the pages of Dickens feigned ignorance at the mention of "cereals" and served his egg in a porcelain cup identical to those depicted in *Punch* cartoons. When he finally ventured out to view the Thames, it seemed almost surreal in its diminutiveness; it was the most modest river he had ever encountered. He found himself fighting the urge to adopt a heavy drawl and stop a stranger to ask, "Say! Is this narrow, damp trench truly the legendary River Thames?"
source_text: Mr. Direck was a type of man not uncommon in America. He was very much after the fashion of that clean and pleasant-looking person one sees in the advertisements in American magazines, that agreeable person who smiles and says, "Good, it's the Fizgig Brand," or "Yes, it's a Wilkins, and that's the Best," or "My shirt-front never rucks; it's a Chesson." But now he was saying, still with the same firm smile, "Good. It's English." He was pleased by every unlikeness to things American, by every item he could hail as characteristic; in the train to London he had laughed aloud with pleasure at the chequer-board of little fields upon the hills of Cheshire, he had chuckled to find himself in a compartment without a corridor; he had tipped the polite yet kindly guard magnificently, after doubting for a moment whether he ought to tip him at all, and he had gone about his hotel in London saying "Lordy! Lordy! My _word!_" in a kind of ecstasy, verifying the delightful absence of telephone, of steam-heat, of any dependent bathroom. At breakfast the waiter (out of Dickens it seemed) had refused to know what "cereals" were, and had given him his egg in a china egg-cup such as you see in the pictures in _Punch_. The Thames, when he sallied out to see it, had been too good to be true, the smallest thing in rivers he had ever seen, and he had had to restrain himself from affecting a marked accent and accosting some passer-by with the question, "Say! But is this little wet ditch here the Historical River Thames?"

text: He possessed a naturally restless intellect that lent him both sharpness and zeal; furthermore, he was defined by a stubborn sense of innovation and a kind heart. Consequently, he was invariably spirited, occasionally expansive, and never base. He delighted in both writing and conversation. He spoke on every topic imaginable and held opinions on everything; he could no more refrain from forming thoughts than a dog can resist trailing a scent. He was forever investigating the very heels of reality. To many, he was a source of fascination and energy, while a select few found him profoundly irritating. He harbored a vast wealth of theories regarding global races and empires, societal structures and political bodies, landscaping and motorcars, the prospects of India and China, aesthetics, America, and the universal schooling of humanity... and every other subject of that nature.
source_text: His was a naturally irritable mind, which gave him point and passion; and moreover he had a certain obstinate originality and a generous disposition. So that he was always lively, sometimes spacious, and never vile. He loved to write and talk. He talked about everything, he had ideas about everything; he could no more help having ideas about everything than a dog can resist smelling at your heels. He sniffed at the heels of reality. Lots of people found him interesting and stimulating, a few found him seriously exasperating. He had ideas in the utmost profusion about races and empires and social order and political institutions and gardens and automobiles and the future of India and China and aesthetics and America and the education of mankind in general.... And all that sort of thing....

text: Mr. Britling glanced over his right shoulder, assessing the turn's radius and the distance. "I think," he said, "I'll just drive around the perimeter of the park. It will take us a little longer, but it’s easier than trying to reverse and maneuver here... These electric starters are remarkably convenient. Otherwise, I’d have to get out and manually crank the engine."
source_text: Mr. Britling looked over his right shoulder and considered problems of curvature and distance. "I think," he said, "I will go round outside the park. It will take us a little longer, but it will be simpler than backing and manoeuvring here now.... These electric starters are remarkably convenient things. Otherwise now I should have to get down and wind up the engine."

text: He spoke with a rapid, staccato rhythm, firing off every thought the instant it formed as if his mind were a fully loaded magazine. His delivery was nearly twice as fast as Mr. Direck’s, characterized by clipped words, concise phrasing, and a tendency to take linguistic shortcuts. This relentless pace left Mr. Direck feeling flustered and completely off-balance.
source_text: He had a quick and rather jerky way of speaking; he seemed to fire out a thought directly it came into his mind, and he seemed to have a loaded magazine of thoughts in his head. He spoke almost exactly twice as fast as Mr. Direck, clipping his words much more, using much compacter sentences, and generally cutting his corners, and this put Mr. Direck off his game.

text: This sudden verbal strike, launched while the American speaker is still unfolding his thoughts, is a common flaw in the interactions between these two peoples. It serves as a frequent source of confusion. Their fundamental concepts of dialogue are entirely different. The English are far less inclined to listen than Americans; they lack a natural sense of conversational reciprocity, and they often relegate their visitors to the role of a silent audience waiting for a turn that never arrives. Mr. Direck sat deep in his reclined chair, angled toward his famous host, interjecting "Yep" and "Sure" and "That *is* so" in the somber, dry tones he assumed an Englishman would expect, only slowly becoming aware of the situation.
source_text: That rapid attack while the transatlantic interlocutor is deploying is indeed a not infrequent defect of conversations between Englishmen and Americans. It is a source of many misunderstandings. The two conceptions of conversation differ fundamentally. The English are much less disposed to listen than the American; they have not quite the same sense of conversational give and take, and at first they are apt to reduce their visitors to the rôle of auditors wondering when their turn will begin. Their turn never does begin. Mr. Direck sat deeply in his slanting seat with a half face to his celebrated host and said "Yep" and "Sure" and "That _is_ so," in the dry grave tones that he believed an Englishman would naturally expect him to use, realising this only very gradually.

text:
Having finished his eulogy for the mechanical foresight that had finally put a motor within his grasp, Mr. Sterling drifted toward that most cherished preoccupation of the educated Briton: the systematic condemnation of his own country. He observed that the central arrangement of the levers allowed the Yankee firm to assemble the machine for either side of the highway with effortless indifference, catering with equal ease to the habits of the Continent or the eccentricities of the Isles. No home-grown carriage offered such flexibility. The English public, he argued, were victims of their own parochial traffic laws and antiquated measurements, yet they clung to these burdens with a stubborn, almost masochistic, satisfaction. 

The sudden flood of American imports was a modern marvel, another victory for the vast, coordinating intellect of the States. They were applying to the engine the same principles of uniformity they had once imposed upon the clock and the musket, achieving a wholesale precision while the British artisan still labored over individual parts. It remained a profound paradox that the very nation which had birthed the steam age and the specialization of toil should have retreated so far from the path of industrial order. This decline, he maintained, was the inevitable consequence of the classical air of the universities and the stagnant weight of the Anglican pulpit.
source_text: Mr. Britling, from his praise of the enterprise that had at last brought a car he could drive within his reach, went on to that favourite topic of all intelligent Englishmen, the adverse criticism of things British. He pointed out that the central position of the brake and gear levers in his automobile made it extremely easy for the American manufacturer to turn it out either as a left-handed or a right-handed car, and so adapt it either to the Continental or to the British rule of the road. No English cars were so adaptable. We British suffered much from our insular rule of the road, just as we suffered much from our insular weights and measures. But we took a perverse pride in such disadvantages. The irruption of American cars into England was a recent phenomenon, it was another triumph for the tremendous organising ability of the American mind. They were doing with the automobile what they had done with clocks and watches and rifles, they had standardised and machined wholesale, while the British were still making the things one by one. It was an extraordinary thing that England, which was the originator of the industrial system and the original developer of the division of labour, should have so fallen away from systematic manufacturing. He believed this was largely due to the influence of Oxford and the Established Church....

text:
These sorts of episodes are endless," Mr. Britling interjected with effortless ease. "You see, we suffer from both ends of the spectrum. Our industrialist class began as a group of rebels—essentially expanded artisans. They once possessed the innate drive and progressive spirit of the skilled worker. However, as soon as they gained wealth and enrolled their sons at Oxford, that spirit vanished. The manufacturing elite was quickly absorbed into the old aristocracy, whose schooling has always been rather elitist and detached—refined, sparse, and intentionally aloof. In America, you’ve managed to avoid a genuine conservative caste thus far. A lucky continent! By expelling your Loyalists, you were left with nothing but liberals and reformers. 

Our specific misfortune, however, is a breed of radical who is also a traditionalist snob. Figures like Ruskin and Morris, for instance, were just as regressive and hostile to science as any bishop or nobleman. They loathed machinery and despised scientific rigor, clinging to outdated intuition. Modern Socialists follow suit. They’ve convinced the public that a perfect car should be hand-forged by lone artisans using hammered copper, forged iron, and aged wood. This modern convenience of electric ignitions and lighting systems is utterly loathsome to the British psyche. We aren't merely behind the times; we are actively hostile. The English temperament has never truly accepted electricity—at least not the kind that flows through circuits. It feels too slick, too foreign. It brings to mind Italian cleverness—Volta, Galvani, Marconi, and the like. To us, respectable electricity is that dignified, impractical variety that produces long sparks from a hand-cranked glass wheel—the old frictional sort kept safely in Leyden jars. At the Claverings estate, they still won't allow electric doorbells. There was quite a scandal when the Solomonsons, during their tenancy, attempted to install them.
source_text: "There's no end of such little incidents," said Mr. Britling, cutting in without apparent effort. "You see, we get it on both sides. Our manufacturer class was, of course, originally an insurgent class. It was a class of distended craftsmen. It had the craftsman's natural enterprise and natural radicalism. As soon as it prospered and sent its boys to Oxford it was lost. Our manufacturing class was assimilated in no time to the conservative classes, whose education has always had a mandarin quality--very, very little of it, and very cold and choice. In America you have so far had no real conservative class at all. Fortunate continent! You cast out your Tories, and you were left with nothing but Whigs and Radicals. But our peculiar bad luck has been to get a sort of revolutionary who is a Tory mandarin too. Ruskin and Morris, for example, were as reactionary and anti-scientific as the dukes and the bishops. Machine haters. Science haters. Rule of Thumbites to the bone. So are our current Socialists. They've filled this country with the idea that the ideal automobile ought to be made entirely by the hand labour of traditional craftsmen, quite individually, out of beaten copper, wrought iron and seasoned oak. All this electric-starter business and this electric lighting outfit I have here, is perfectly hateful to the English mind.... It isn't that we are simply backward in these things, we are antagonistic. The British mind has never really tolerated electricity; at least, not that sort of electricity that runs through wires.
