Too slippery and glib for it. Associates it with Italians and fluency generally, with Volta, Galvani, Marconi and so on. The proper British electricity is that high-grade useless long-sparking stuff you get by turning round a glass machine; stuff we used to call frictional electricity. Keep it in Leyden jars.... At Claverings here they still refuse to have electric bells. There was a row when the Solomonsons, who were tenants here for a time, tried to put them in...."

text: Naturally," Mr. Britling interjected once more, "conservatism isn't some final, fixed state. When you think about it, we share the same ancestry as that ambitious friend of yours in Toledo. This cautiousness isn't a matter of genetics. Our history of vitality proves it isn't something in the atmosphere or the land itself. England has only grown stagnant and lazy because she has enjoyed such a long period of wealth and ease.
source_text: "Of course," Mr. Britling burst out again, "even conservatism isn't an ultimate thing. After all, we and your enterprising friend at Toledo, are very much the same blood. The conservatism, I mean, isn't racial. And our earlier energy shows it isn't in the air or in the soil. England has become unenterprising and sluggish because England has been so prosperous and comfortable...."

text: Precisely," Mr. Direck agreed. "The friend I was mentioning, a man named Robinson—a name that clearly marks him as being of genuine English descent—was, if I may say so, very much like yourself in build and coloring. In terms of his heritage, I would say he was—well, he was remarkably similar to you....
source_text: "Exactly," said Mr. Direck. "My friend of whom I was telling you, was a man named Robinson, which indicates pretty clearly that he was of genuine English stock, and, if I may say so, quite of your build and complexion; racially, I should say, he was, well--very much what you are...."

text: The gathering of guests left Mr. Direck feeling rather confused. That Mr. and Mrs. Britling sat at opposite ends of the table was obvious enough, and it seemed equally clear that the two barefoot children were their own. Beyond these few, however, the relationships became murky. There was a youth of seventeen or so, darker in complexion than the elder Britling yet possessing a similar freckled nose, who might have been a son from an earlier union or perhaps a stepson; he had a wild shock of hair and the awkward, elongated limbs of a boy who had grown several inches in the night. Nearby sat a distinctly German young man with a rosy face, cropped blonde hair, and spectacles. He wore a panama hat and was likely the tutor for the smaller boys. (Mr. Direck was also wearing a hat, having been warned in New York about the treacherous English weather, despite being the only one at the table to do so.) Finally, at the edge of the identifiable, sat a handsome young man with thick dark hair and vivid blue eyes whom the others called "Teddy." For this one, Mr. Direck settled on the title of "secretary."
source_text: The composition of the company was a matter for some perplexity to Mr. Direck. Mr. and Mrs. Britling were at either end of the table, that was plain enough. It was also fairly plain that the two barefooted boys were little Britlings. But beyond this was a cloud of uncertainty. There was a youth of perhaps seventeen, much darker than Britling but with nose and freckles rather like his, who might be an early son or a stepson; he was shock-headed and with that look about his arms and legs that suggests overnight growth; and there was an unmistakable young German, very pink, with close-cropped fair hair, glasses and a panama hat, who was probably the tutor of the younger boys. (Mr. Direck also was wearing his hat, his mind had been filled with an exaggerated idea of the treacheries of the English climate before he left New York. Every one else was hatless.) Finally, before one reached the limits of the explicable there was a pleasant young man with a lot of dark hair and very fine dark blue eyes, whom everybody called "Teddy." For him, Mr. Direck hazarded "secretary."

text: It’s really no bother. I enjoy being behind the wheel, at least the little I’ve done so far. Since we’re already out, we can return through Harborough High Oak to look into the Corner family lineage. They’re still quite common in that region. The route isn’t particularly hard; it’s just a bit steep and winding.
source_text: "It's no trouble. I like the driving. What I have had of it. And while we're at it, we'll come back by Harborough High Oak and look up the Corner pedigree. They're all over that district still. And the road's not really difficult; it's only a bit up and down and roundabout."

text: There is surely a singular charm," he observed, "in journeying here to rediscover your lost American ancestors upon our parish monuments and lichened slabs. You are doubtless aware of that country south of Worcester where the church walls are thick with the heraldic stars and bars of the departed Washingtons, though you’d be hard-pressed to find a living soul of that name today. Nor will you encounter many Claytons in Saffron Walden. Yet, much of this landscape is populated by families who have flourished here for six centuries or more. It gives this corner of East Anglia a far more visceral sense of antiquity than you’ll find in, say, Sussex or the Home Counties. Around these hills, the Ashbournes and the Wentworths remain, while toward Chelmsford and Witham, the Tyrrells and the Petres hold their ground. There are ancient oaks in the deer park at Hedingham that have heard the baying of wolves and the iron rattle of men-at-arms. Even the farmhouses are ringed by moats—a vestige of the days of the wolf. The great house itself is a magnificent Tudor pile, and the cottages, as you see, still keep their roofs of thatch.
source_text: "It must be very interesting," he said, "to come over here and pick up these American families of yours on the monuments and tombstones. You know, of course, that district south of Evesham where every other church monument bears the stars and stripes, the arms of departed Washingtons. I doubt though if you'll still find the name about there. Nor will you find many Hinkinsons in Market Saffron. But lots of this country here has five or six hundred-year-old families still flourishing. That's why Essex is so much more genuinely Old England than Surrey, say, or Kent. Round here you'll find Corners and Fairlies, and then you get Capels, and then away down towards Dunmow and Braintree Maynards and Byngs. And there are oaks and hornbeams in the park about Claverings that have echoed to the howling of wolves and the clank of men in armour. All the old farms here are moated--because of the wolves. Claverings itself is Tudor, and rather fine too. And the cottages still wear thatch...."

text: Soon, through a far-off portal, a pair of young lads came into brief view, guiding their diminutive but reliable velocipedes; they were followed after a short pause by the Austrian pedagogue. Then, a vast slate-colored tom sauntered across the stone quadrangle to settle with an air of quiet solemnity beside Mr. Halloway. The late-day heavens were a vibrant cerulean, streaked with a neat procession of globular, cotton-white clouds.
source_text: Presently through a distant gate the two small boys were momentarily visible wheeling small but serviceable bicycles, followed after a little interval by the German tutor. Then an enormous grey cat came slowly across the garden court, and sat down to listen respectfully to Mr. Britling. The afternoon sky was an intense blue, with little puff-balls of cloud lined out across it.

text: Every so often, a few of Mr. Britling’s casual comments led Mr. Direck to believe he was successfully conveying his initial observations as an American traveler. In reality, however, he wasn't allowed to express anything at all; Mr. Britling did all the talking. He sat next to his guest, letting a spray of ideas and insights dance like a joyful fountain in the bright sun.
source_text: Occasionally, from chance remarks of Mr. Britling's, Mr. Direck was led to infer that his first impressions as an American visitor were being related to his host, but as a matter of fact he was permitted to relate nothing; Mr. Britling did all the talking. He sat beside his guest and spirted and played ideas and reflections like a happy fountain in the sunshine.

text: The monologue of Mr. Thorne assumed the character of an expansive tide sweeping through Sterling’s intellect, carrying with it transient glimpses and sudden insights, wafting remnants of the teeming British scenes and resonances encountered over the previous week, diaphanous musings on venerable family titles and winsome kinswomen, shards of prearranged dialogues concerning Thorne’s renown across the Atlantic, the justification for the scholarly guild, and the lingering, elusive point of the Miller narrative....
source_text: Mr. Britling's talk became like a wide stream flowing through Mr. Direck's mind, bearing along momentary impressions and observations, drifting memories of all the crowded English sights and sounds of the last five days, filmy imaginations about ancestral names and pretty cousins, scraps of those prepared conversational openings on Mr. Britling's standing in America, the explanation about the lecture club, the still incompletely forgotten purport of the Robinson anecdote....

text: The British system of estates and the aristocracy were never officially designed, nor was our convoluted constitution ever part of a master plan. These things simply emerged, forming gradually like the concentric layers of an agate. Yet, there was a certain harmony to this development; it aligned so perfectly with the temperament of our people and the climate of our island. It offered such a sense of security that we naturally settled into it—and once you have settled in, it is nearly impossible to leave. We were already firmly rooted by the reign of Queen Anne, and it is doubtful we will ever truly escape. We are like the *Lingula*, that ancient shellfish found in the earliest fossils that still survives today; it adapted to its easy environment and never felt the need to evolve. Why bother? It merely expels any destabilizing influences. Our younger sons head abroad to build empires, while the surplus children of the working class move to the cities or emigrate to Canada and Australia. But none of that ever touches the core of the system.
source_text: "Nobody planned the British estate system, nobody planned the British aristocratic system, nobody planned the confounded constitution, it came about, it was like layer after layer wrapping round an agate, but you see it came about so happily in a way, it so suited the climate and the temperament of our people and our island, it was on the whole so cosy, that our people settled down into it, you can't help settling down into it, they had already settled down by the days of Queen Anne, and Heaven knows if we shall ever really get away again. We're like that little shell the _Lingula_, that is found in the oldest rocks and lives to-day: it fitted its easy conditions, and it has never modified since. Why should it? It excretes all its disturbing forces. Our younger sons go away and found colonial empires. Our surplus cottage children emigrate to Australia and Canada or migrate into the towns. It doesn't alter _this_...."

text: Mr. Direck’s gaze settled upon the barn, his expression shifting gradually from one of idle pleasure to a look of sharpening insight. Suddenly, he felt a firm compulsion to speak. He was so determined to voice his thought that he resolved to do so even if Mr. Britling continued his monologue without a single interruption.
source_text: Mr. Direck's eye had come to rest upon the barn, and its expression changed slowly from lazy appreciation to a brightening intelligence. Suddenly he resolved to say something. He resolved to say it so firmly that he determined to say it even if Mr. Britling went on talking all the time.

text: To my mind," Mr. Sterling observed, "the most remarkable thing is that this outbuilding has ceased to be an outbuilding, just as the courtyard has abandoned its rustic vocation. There is no longer a trace of grain or husk to be found inside, nor will there ever be again; it contains only a player-piano and a floor for dancing. If a heifer were to wander into this square, the inhabitants would fall over themselves to drive it away. They would view the creature as a most grotesque and alien sight.
source_text: "Well," said Mr. Direck, "the point that strikes me most about all this is that that barn isn't a barn any longer, and that this farmyard isn't a farmyard. There isn't any wheat or chaff or anything of that sort in the barn, and there never will be again: there's just a pianola and a dancing floor, and if a cow came into this farmyard everybody in the place would be shooing it out again. They'd regard it as a most unnatural object."

text: For the time being, he let the matter rest, yet throughout the afternoon Mr. Direck enjoyed the satisfaction of watching his idea drift through the quiet eddies of Mr. Britling’s mind. Even if the subject didn't resurface directly, its influence seemed to flicker in and out of view. He was guided around with tireless enthusiasm for the remainder of the day, catching only occasional sight of the other residents of the Dower House until the group finally reconvened at six o’clock that evening.
source_text: He left it at that for the time, but throughout the afternoon Mr. Direck had the gratification of seeing his thought floating round and round in the back-waters of Mr. Britling's mental current. If it didn't itself get into the stream again its reflection at any rate appeared and reappeared. He was taken about with great assiduity throughout the afternoon, and he got no more than occasional glimpses of the rest of the Dower House circle until six o'clock in the evening.

text:
To Mr. Sterling, the hamlet appeared like a canvas by Morland. A tavern projected its painted emblem, the Pembroke Crest, over the thoroughfare; beside it stood a stone basin—the sort where a Dickensian coachman might douse a hypocrite—and a jade-colored table sat expectantly by the portal. A village store and several delightful dwellings, each adorned with the Ashbourne insignia, framed the common, where a flock of geese marched in military precision. 

Passing beneath a lych-gate, Mr. Thorne guided his companion into the graveyard, where they discovered crumbling, lichen-clad markers, including a seventeenth-century stone carved with a grim death’s-head. Within the sanctuary, three massive funerary hatchments hung aloft, and a lateral chapel honored the Ashbournes and the Lords of Sedgewick. Its monuments ranged from colorful Tudor carvings to a cavernous stained-glass window of the most tawdry Victorian production. They also noted Gothic brasses of former rectors and a marble knight resting beside his dame, relics of a vanished dynasty that had held sway over Willow’s Reach long before the Ashbournes’ tenure. 

Upon exiting the porch, the pair encountered the ideal clergyman, Mr. Merryweather—a stout, jovial soul with a booming, inclusive laugh and a voice that seemed to wrap around them. "Exploring the motherland, eh?" he remarked to Mr. Sterling. "How splendid of you Americans to visit! Quite splendid..."
source_text: The village reminded Mr. Direck of Abbey's pictures. There was an inn with a sign standing out in the road, a painted sign of the Clavering Arms; it had a water trough (such as Mr. Weller senior ducked the dissenter in) and a green painted table outside its inviting door. There were also a general shop and a number of very pleasant cottages, each marked with the Mainstay crest. All this was grouped about a green with real geese drilling thereon. Mr. Britling conducted his visitor (through a lych gate) into the church-yard, and there they found mossy, tumble-down tombstones, one with a skull and cross-bones upon it, that went back to the later seventeenth century. In the aisle of the church were three huge hatchments, and there was a side chapel devoted to the Mainstay family and the Barons Homartyn, with a series of monuments that began with painted Tudor effigies and came down to a vast stained glass window of the vilest commercial Victorian. There were also mediæval brasses of parish priests, and a marble crusader and his lady of some extinguished family which had ruled Matching's Easy before the Mainstays came. And as the two gentlemen emerged from the church they ran against the perfect vicar, Mr. Dimple, ample and genial, with an embracing laugh and an enveloping voice. "Come to see the old country," he said to Mr. Direck. "So Good of you Americans to do that! So Good of you...."

text: I anticipate we shall encounter Hedges from the Foreign Office—or is it the Board of Trade?—and Lord Sterling, that fixture from the Exchequer. There will likely be a smattering of similar types, the sort we label the Ruling Order. Their wives, naturally. I also suspect the Duchess of Marbury might attend; she is particularly fervent regarding the Indian Question. And then there is the Honorable Beatrice Vane, whose beauty is whispered to be extraordinary, though I have never witnessed it. It is Lady Alvingham’s habit to insist I appear—not that I am a significant feature of these weekend diversions—but she likes me laid out upon the table, to be picked at should the whim strike, much like the radishes or the crystallized ginger. She always suggests I stay for Sunday luncheon, and I always decline because of the village cricket. So, I merely present myself for the Saturday afternoon.
source_text: "I expect we shall find Philbert from the Home Office--or is it the Local Government Board?--and Sir Thomas Loot, the Treasury man. There may be some other people of that sort, the people we call the Governing Class. Wives also. And I rather fancy the Countess of Frensham is coming, she's strong on the Irish Question, and Lady Venetia Trumpington, who they say is a beauty--I've never seen her. It's Lady Homartyn's way to expect me to come in--not that I'm an important item at these week-end social feasts--but she likes to see me on the table--to be nibbled at if any one wants to do so--like the olives and the salted almonds. And she always asks me to lunch on Sunday and I always refuse--because of the hockey. So you see I put in an appearance on the Saturday afternoon...."

text: Yes. But have you truly considered the implications of a civil war for the empire? Surely there are priorities in this world beyond this friction between Ulster’s "loyalists" and the Liberal ministry; this great empire must have concerns more vital than mere political maneuvering. You think you can intimidate the Home Rule government into a pathetic collapse that will restore the Tories to power. But suppose you fail. Suppose, instead, you actually succeed in inciting a civil war. Almost no one here or in Ireland desires it—I was there myself just a month ago—but when men carry loaded weapons, they eventually use them. And then the rage takes over. Few realize the permanent, festering wound that opens the moment the fighting starts. Imagine if a portion of the army mutinies and we are faced with a horrific and soul-crushing conflict across the water. India is watching these developments. Bengal might easily follow Ireland's lead. From that distance, sedition and treason remain sedition and treason, whether they are dressed in orange or green. And then, consider if the Germans choose that moment to strike!
source_text: "Yes. But do you really figure to yourself what a civil war may mean for the empire? Surely there are other things in the world besides this quarrel between the 'loyalists' of Ulster and the Liberal government; there are other interests in this big empire than party advantages? Yon think you are going to frighten this Home Rule government into some ridiculous sort of collapse that will bring in the Tories at the next election. Well, suppose you don't manage that. Suppose instead that you really do contrive to bring about a civil war. Very few people here or in Ireland want it--I was over there not a month ago--but when men have loaded guns in their hands they sometimes go off. And then people see red. Few people realise what an incurable sore opens when fighting begins. Suppose part of the army revolts and we get some extraordinary and demoralising fighting over there. India watches these things. Bengal may imitate Ireland. At that distance rebellion and treason are rebellion and treason whether they are coloured orange or green. And then suppose the Germans see fit to attack us!"

text: My only comfort in the midst of this upheaval," he remarked, "is a conversation I had last month with a young Irish girl in Meath. She was only twelve years old, and she took a liking to me—mostly, I suspect, because I joined her in a supposedly hazardous canoe she was forbidden from rowing alone. All through the day, the incessant arguments regarding the Irish Question had been swirling around her observant head. Once we were out on the water, she decided to enlighten me on a crucial point that everyone else seemed to ignore. 'You English,' she said, 'have a tendency to take all this conflict far too seriously. Don't let it trouble you... Half the time we’re just laughing at you. You really ought to just leave us be.'
source_text: "My one consolation," he said, "in this storm is a talk I had last month with a young Irishwoman in Meath. She was a young person of twelve, and she took a fancy to me--I think because I went with her in an alleged dangerous canoe she was forbidden to navigate alone. All day the eternal Irish Question had banged about over her observant head. When we were out on the water she suddenly decided to set me right upon a disregarded essential. 'You English,' she said, 'are just a bit disposed to take all this trouble seriously. Don't you fret yourself about it... Half the time we're just laffing at you. You'd best leave us all alone....'"

text: Observe this wretched display!" he shouted. "We stand before a genuine chance to bridge the divide between England and Ireland and lay these ancient sorrows to rest. Yet there appears to be no integrity, no wisdom, and no restraint among us to prevent it from being swallowed by such venomous spite and reciprocal provocation. Just as a glimmer of fortune reaches Ireland's shores... A plague on both your houses!
source_text: "But look at this miserable spectacle!" he cried. "Here is a chance of getting something like a reconciliation of the old feud of English and Irish, and something like a settlement of these ancient distresses, and there seems no power, no conscience, no sanity in any of us, sufficient to save it from this cantankerous bitterness, this sheer wicked mischief of mutual exasperation.... Just when Ireland is getting a gleam of prosperity.... A murrain on both your parties!"

text: The mindset behind all this recent defiance and unrest is—peculiar. It’s irritating, too... I can’t quite fathom it. It is the same pattern whether you examine the suffrage movement, the labor strikes, or this disaster in Ireland. It’s possible people have become too secure. You see, we are the products of several stable generations during which the pillars of society haven't truly shifted. We have been raised without any real threat—which is to say, without any real accountability. Not one of us—and I admit my own behavior contradicts my words—genuinely believes that life can ever be fundamentally altered again. All of this"—Mr. Britling made a broad, sweeping gesture—"appears as though it is destined to continue forever. The notion that the system could be destroyed seems unthinkable. It feels impossible that anything we do could actually break it. Lady Homartyn, for instance, cannot conceive of a world where she won't host weekend parties at Claverings, or where her mail and morning tea aren't brought to her bedside. Even if she manages to imagine a day when she herself is gone, she simply assumes someone else will be there to receive that tea. Her charming butler might worry about losing his position, but he cannot imagine a reality where such a position no longer exists to be lost. Old Asquith assumes we have always managed, and will always manage, by being subtly clever and advising everyone to 'wait and see.' And it is precisely because we are so certain that a total societal collapse is impossible that we allow ourselves to be so dangerously reckless in our individual crusades. 'Why shouldn’t women vote?' they ask. 'What’s the harm?' And then a bomb explodes in Westminster Abbey. 'Why shouldn’t Ulster make things impossible?' And suddenly some fanatic Carsonite is rushing off to Germany to flirt with treason over a vague hint from the Kaiser, buying up half a million rifles..."
source_text: "The psychology of all this recent insubordination and violence is--curious. Exasperating too.... I don't quite grasp it.... It's the same thing whether you look at the suffrage business or the labour people or at this Irish muddle. People may be too safe. You see we live at the end of a series of secure generations in which none of the great things of life have changed materially. We've grown up with no sense of danger--that is to say, with no sense of responsibility. None of us, none of us--for though I talk my actions belie me--really believe that life can change very fundamentally any more forever. All this",--Mr. Britling waved his arm comprehensively--"looks as though it was bound to go on steadily forever. It seems incredible that the system could be smashed. It seems incredible that anything we can do will ever smash the system. Lady Homartyn, for example, is incapable of believing that she won't always be able to have week-end parties at Claverings, and that the letters and the tea won't come to her bedside in the morning. Or if her imagination goes to the point of supposing that some day _she_ won't be there to receive the tea, it means merely that she supposes somebody else will be. Her pleasant butler may fear to lose his 'situation,' but nothing on earth could make him imagine a time when there will not be a 'situation' for him to lose. Old Asquith thinks that we always have got along, and that we always shall get along by being quietly artful and saying, 'Wait and see.' And it's just because we are all convinced that we are so safe against a general breakdown that we are able to be so recklessly violent in our special cases. Why shouldn't women have the vote? they argue. What does it matter? And bang goes a bomb in Westminster Abbey. Why shouldn't Ulster create an impossible position? And off trots some demented Carsonite to Germany to play at treason on some half word of the German Emperor's and buy half a million rifles....

text: Their dialogue was cut short by the arrival of Arthur, the secretary, and the young scholar from Bengal, both sodden but spirited, having just returned from the water. They reported that the lower grounds concealed a small lake, outfitted with a punt and a miniature island. As the group debated the merits of rowing, Mr. Sterling appeared from the parkland, engaged in a weighty confidence with the eldest son; the pair had clearly been occupied with a long, private ramble. A few voices suggested a match of shuttlecock. Mr. Thorne found himself drifting away from the crowd in the company of Lawrence Sterling, wandering through the rose-walk to catch the sunset’s final glow. Thorne took the chance to confirm his hunch that the boy was Mrs. Sterling’s stepson, and then, under the guise of admiring a far-off border of evening primroses, he maneuvered their walk past the trellis-work bower where the deepening shadows surely made it too dark for Miss Vane to continue her reading.
source_text: Their comparisons were interrupted by the appearance of "Teddy," the secretary, and the Indian young gentleman, damp and genial, as they explained, "from the boats." It seemed that "down below" somewhere was a pond with a punt and an island and a toy dinghy. And while they discussed swimming and boating, Mr. Carmine appeared from the direction of the park conversing gravely with the elder son. They had been for a walk and a talk together. There were proposals for a Badminton foursome. Mr. Direck emerged from the general interchange with Mr. Lawrence Carmine, and then strolled through the rose garden to see the sunset from the end. Mr. Direck took the opportunity to verify his impression that the elder son was the present Mrs. Britling's stepson, and he also contrived by a sudden admiration for a distant row of evening primroses to deflect their path past the arbour in which the evening light must now be getting a little too soft for Miss Corner's book.

text: Mr. Sterling felt that the ethical constants of the human spirit remained more fixed than the theories of science, and the two shared one of those casual inquiries into existence that sprout from a poorly defined disagreement and are eventually discarded rather than concluded. Julian impressed him as being more theoretical and removed than any young American student he had encountered—though he suspected this might be less a matter of nationality than a specific trait of the Vane bloodline. He seemed to have pursued a wider, more solitary course of reading while remaining notably less active. Furthermore, he exhibited a sense of composure and self-governance that was strikingly distinct.
source_text: Mr. Direck thought that the moral truths of human nature were steadier than science, and they had one of those little discussions of real life that begin about a difference inadequately apprehended, and do not so much end as are abandoned. Hugh struck him as being more speculative and detached than any American college youth of his age that he knew--but that might not be a national difference but only the Britling strain. He seemed to have read more and more independently, and to be doing less. And he was rather more restrained and self-possessed.

text: Before Mr. Direck could properly ask the young man about his career and worldview, the youth had already steered the talk toward the United States. He was incredibly keen to visit. "My father says in one of his books that over here we are just existing, whereas over there you are just beginning. It must be so inspiring to feel that your country is still being shaped."
source_text: Before Mr. Direck could begin a proper inquiry into the young man's work and outlook, he had got the conversation upon America. He wanted tremendously to see America. "The dad says in one of his books that over here we are being and that over there you are beginning. It must be tremendously stimulating to think that your country is still being made...."

text: He cast aside a diabolical suit of crimson and a bandit’s getup derived from one of Lady Thorne’s old velvet waistcoats, deciding at last upon a pair of raven-black silk hose. His limbs, he observed, were not without merit. From the manor’s great wardrobe he sampled various radiant fabrics, wavering over a scrap of imperial purple before selecting a heavy square of forest-green silk embroidered with golden birds and ornate scrollwork. He arranged this like a toga over his light silken vest—Julian had been adamant that a stiff shirt would impede his movements on the floor—and pinned it with a massive brooch of emerald paste. His head rose above the drapery with what he considered a fair degree of majesty. Julian suggested a silk fillet for his brow, which Mr. Thorne adjusted before the glass but ultimately rejected after a long silence. He was still weighing the effect of such a headband upon the sensibilities of Miss Vance when Julian left him to see to his own modest toilet. Alone at last, he used the pivoting glass and the wall mirror to examine his profile, a vanity he had been too self-conscious to indulge while the secretary remained. The total effect was most satisfactory.
source_text: He rejected a rather Mephistopheles-like costume of crimson and a scheme for a brigand-like ensemble based upon what was evidently an old bolero of Mrs. Britling's, and after some reflection he accepted some black silk tights. His legs were not legs to be ashamed of. Over this he tried various brilliant wrappings from the Dower House _armoire_, and chose at last, after some hesitation in the direction of a piece of gold and purple brocade, a big square of green silk curtain stuff adorned with golden pheasants and other large and dignified ornaments; this he wore toga fashion over his light silken under-vest--Teddy had insisted on the abandonment of his shirt "if you want to dance at all"--and fastened with a large green glass-jewelled brooch. From this his head and neck projected, he felt, with a tolerable dignity. Teddy suggested a fillet of green ribbon, and this Mr. Direck tried, but after prolonged reflection before the glass rejected. He was still weighing the effect of this fillet upon the mind of Miss Corner when Teddy left him to make his own modest preparations. Teddy's departure gave him a chance for profile studies by means of an arrangement of the long mirror and the table looking-glass that he had been too shy to attempt in the presence of the secretary. The general effect was quite satisfactory.

text: Teddy and the sons moved quickly to blow out the candles and clear the room, as two drivers emerged from the kitchen area to bring Mr. Lawrence Carmine’s car and the guests' vehicle around to the main entrance. The entire party drifted lightheartedly through the moonlight and past the massive trees toward the front of the house. There, Mr. Direck noticed the baby carriage—that enigmatic pram—waiting in the shadows just past the front door.
source_text: There was a rapid putting out of candles and a stowing away of things by Teddy and the sons, two chauffeurs appeared from the region of the kitchen and brought Mr. Lawrence Carmine's car and the visitor family's car to the front door, and everybody drifted gaily through the moonlight and the big trees to the front of the house. And Mr. Direck saw the perambulator waiting--the mysterious perambulator--a little in the dark beyond the front door.

text: Mr. Britling settled into an armchair by the hearth, casually hooking one leg over the side. Dressed in a dark velvet cloak and cap with matching silk hose, he bore a striking resemblance to a secondary figure—a court official, perhaps—in some theatrical drama of swords and intrigue. "I find these weekend dances and frolics to be remarkably restorative," he remarked. "Between that and our Sunday hockey, one begins the new week with a sharp and refreshed mind. Friday is invariably my most difficult day for work."
source_text: Mr. Britling seated himself in an armchair by the fireplace and threw one leg carelessly over the arm. In his black velvet cloak and cap, and his black silk tights, he was very like a minor character, a court chamberlain for example, in some cloak and rapier drama. "I find this week-end dancing and kicking about wonderfully wholesome," he said. "That and our Sunday hockey. One starts the new week clear and bright about the mind. Friday is always my worst working day."

text: Precisely. The local community was somewhat taken aback. The woman who had leased him the rooms came to see me in private. Teddy is quite defensive about his personal freedom; he perceives any request for an explanation as a slight. He probably told the elderly woman nothing more than, "This is Letty—she’s staying in my rooms now." I approached the topic with him very delicately. "Oh, that," he remarked, much like someone who had forgotten a minor detail. "We got married during the Christmas break. Would it be alright if I brought her to meet Mrs. Britling?" Eventually, we persuaded him to move into a small cottage I own. His wife is the daughter of a journalist and printer from Colchester. I’m not sure if you’ve had a conversation with her yet.
source_text: "Exactly. The village was a little startled. The cottager who had let the rooms came to me privately. Teddy is rather touchy on the point of his personal independence, he considers any demand for explanations as an insult, and probably all he had said to the old lady was, 'This is Letty--come to share my rooms.' I put the matter to him very gently. 'Oh, yes,' he said, rather in the manner of some one who has overlooked a trifle. 'I got married to her in the Christmas holidays. May I bring her along to see Mrs. Britling?' We induced him to go into a little cottage I rent. The wife was the daughter of a Colchester journalist and printer. I don't know if you talked to her."

text: The man is a perpetual interrogator. One offers any casual remark, only for him to commit it to a ledger in his garret, returning later to cross-examine you, delighting in the way your inconsistencies tangle. He views existence through a lens of systematic suspicion; his aim is to index and impale reality like a specimen. He rightly fears the world’s penchant for anarchy and its talent for hypocrisy. He stands in stark opposition to Julian, whose faith in the cosmos is so absolute it borders on insolence. Julian embodies our native nonchalance to a perilous degree. He might drop his gold chronometer in the tall grass of Highwood Chase and assume it would await his return a season hence—perhaps polished by a passing badger. He possesses the frantic energy of a squirrel, yet lacks the foresight to bury a single nut. To pose a query would be, for him, a breach of etiquette—a sign of unpardonable doubt. He assumes the world is functioning perfectly well without his interference. My Prussian guest, however, wears a mask of somber concern. He patrols his own perceptions like a diligent stationmaster. Observe how my library of gramophone discs is now flawlessly arranged and indexed? That was his doing. He fell upon the task the moment he crossed the threshold, driven by the same instinct that compels a stray feline to clear a cellar of vermin. Before his arrival, the music was a muddle. One simply accepted whatever providence pulled from the shelf.
source_text: "He's always asking questions. And you tell him any old thing and he goes and writes it down in his room upstairs, and afterwards asks you another like it in order to perplex himself by the variety of your answers. He regards the whole world with a methodical distrust. He wants to document it and pin it down. He suspects it only too justly of disorderly impulses, and a capacity for self-contradiction. He is the most extraordinary contrast to Teddy, whose confidence in the universe amounts almost to effrontery. Teddy carries our national laxness to a foolhardy extent. He is capable of leaving his watch in the middle of Claverings Park and expecting to find it a month later--being carefully taken care of by a squirrel, I suppose--when he happens to want it. He's rather like a squirrel himself--without the habit of hoarding. He is incapable of asking a question about anything; he would be quite sure it was all right anyhow. He would feel that asking questions betrayed a want of confidence--was a sort of incivility. But my German, if you notice,--his normal expression is one of grave solicitude. He is like a conscientious ticket-collector among his impressions. And did you notice how beautifully my pianola rolls are all numbered and catalogued? He did that. He set to work and did it as soon as he got here, just as a good cat when you bring it into the house sets to work and catches mice. Previously the pianola music was chaos. You took what God sent you.

text: He has a pale complexion, a somewhat stout build, and a moody nature, but there is also that buzzed hair—nearly bald—the soft physique, and the glasses; these traits feel engineered. Then there is his posture and his way of reasoning. His precision. Upon his arrival, he was quite a sight, wearing a student cap and a rucksack while carrying a violin; he looked like he’d stepped out of a story. No writer would dare invent a German who was so quintessentially German. Now, a young man from France or Italy or Russia might seem foreign, but he wouldn't bear the same unmistakable national imprint. He wouldn't be so transparently French or Italian or Russian. Other cultures aren't manufactured; they aren't built or designed but rather emerge from a thousand obscure origins. The Germans are a victory of sheer willpower. I recently observed that my sons shouted whenever they spoke German. "But one *must* shout when speaking German," Herr Heinrich replied. "That is what the schools teach." And they do. They train them to holler and puff out their chests, just as they train them to obey signs and avoid political thought. Even their own bodies don't seem to belong to them. My Herr Heinrich is relatively open-minded. He asked me recently, "Why should I devote myself to philology?" Then, reflecting, he said, "But I suppose it is my duty."
source_text: "He has the fair type of complexion, the rather full habit of body, the temperamental disposition, but in addition that close-cropped head, it is almost as if it were shaved, the plumpness, the glasses--those are things that are made. And the way he carries himself. And the way he thinks. His meticulousness. When he arrived he was delightful, he was wearing a student's corps cap and a rucksack, he carried a violin; he seemed to have come out of a book. No one would ever dare to invent so German a German for a book. Now, a young Frenchman or a young Italian or a young Russian coming here might look like a foreigner, but he wouldn't have the distinctive national stamp a German has. He wouldn't be plainly French or Italian or Russian. Other peoples are not made; they are neither made nor created but proceeding--out of a thousand indefinable causes. The Germans are a triumph of directive will. I had to remark the other day that when my boys talked German they shouted. 'But when one talks German one _must_ shout,' said Herr Heinrich. 'It is taught so in the schools.' And it is. They teach them to shout and to throw out their chests. Just as they teach them to read notice-boards and not think about politics. Their very ribs are not their own. My Herr Heinrich is comparatively a liberal thinker. He asked me the other day, 'But why should I give myself up to philology? But then,' he reflected, 'it is what I have to do.'"

text: They wandered toward a cluster of wicker chairs and canvas swings nestled between the towering elms and the flowering thickets, where the conversation eventually drifted to Highwood. "It is the most disciplined plot in the county," Sterling remarked. "One cannot blame Clara for its severity. Left to her own devices, she prefers the wild and the whimsical; she scatters seeds with a carefree hand, hoping for a riot of untamed color. She yearns for a beautiful chaos, yet it never quite materializes. The moment the General steps onto the gravel, the perennials seem to snap to attention of their own accord. There is even a specimen near the entrance—a willow, by all accounts. Any local veteran will swear to it. But since the General’s arrival, that tree has been straining to assume a military posture, with the most bizarre consequences. I happened to be walking past with old Barnaby recently. 'Have a look at that tall cypress,' he muttered. 'That’s a willow, surely,' I replied. 'No,' he insisted, 'it was a willow once, before the Colonel took command. But these days, sir, it’s a cypress.' And remarkably enough, it truly has become a cypress!"
source_text: They strolled towards a place of seats and hammocks between the big trees and the rose garden, and the talk turned for a time upon Rendezvous. "They have the tidiest garden in Essex," said Manning. "It's not Mrs. Rendezvous' fault that it is so. Mrs. Rendezvous, as a matter of fact, has a taste for the picturesque. She just puts the things about in groups in the beds. She wants them, she says, to grow anyhow. She desires a romantic disorder. But she never gets it. When he walks down the path all the plants dress instinctively.... And there's a tree near their gate; it used to be a willow. You can ask any old man in the village. But ever since Rendezvous took the place it's been trying to present arms. With the most extraordinary results. I was passing the other day with old Windershin. 'You see that there old poplar,' he said. 'It's a willow,' said I. 'No,' he said, 'it did used to be a willow before Colonel Rendezvous he came. But now it's a poplar.'... And, by Jove, it is a poplar!"...

text: There is something tragic about all this—this 'Kitchenerism,' if that's the word," Mr. Britling mused. "It spends its time rushing around, keeping itself wound tight, and frantically attempting to keep the rest of the country just as tense. And all for the sake of a war that might one day, somehow, break out with Germany. Assuming, that is, that Germany is truly insane. It is that conflict, like a fixed idea buzzing in Rendezvous' mind, that drives him toward Market Saffron at this very moment—he prefers the main roads because they are more grueling—pushing through the dust and the glare. Meanwhile, he could be right here, just passing the time in conversation...
source_text: "There is something tragical in all this--what shall I call it?--Kitchenerism," Mr. Britling reflected "Here you have it rushing about and keeping itself--screwed up, and trying desperately to keep the country screwed up. And all because there may be a war some day somehow with Germany. Provided Germany _is_ insane. It's that war, like some sort of bee in Rendezvous' brains, that is driving him along the road now to Market Saffron--he always keeps to the roads because they are severer--through all the dust and sunshine. When he might be here gossiping....

text: Yet, that detonation did not disturb or inconvenience Mr. Britling, Mr. Manning, or Mr. Direck in the least. Because it occurred far beyond the horizon of this spherical earth, it was impossible to hear or see from Matching’s Easy. Nevertheless, the blast was incredibly loud. It took place in an open area beside a river winding through a dense Oriental city—a place bristling with white minarets and surrounded by barren hills under a scorching afternoon sun. The sound came from a black package that Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria, with remarkable composure, had just hurled from his open-topped car, where it had landed moments earlier after being thrown from the quay. It exploded upon hitting the cobblestones directly beneath the front of the second vehicle in the motorcade, shattering the car’s front end and wounding both the aide-de-camp inside and several onlookers. The assailant was immediately seized by the crowd. The procession halted. A massive upheaval erupted among the vibrantly dressed throng, a feverish intensity that stood in sharp contrast to the Sunday tranquility of Matching’s Easy.
source_text: But neither Mr. Britling nor Mr. Manning nor Mr. Direck was interrupted or incommoded in the slightest degree by that report. Because it was too far off over the curve of this round world to be either heard or seen at Matching's Easy. Nevertheless it was a very loud report. It occurred at an open space by a river that ran through a cramped Oriental city, a city spiked with white minarets and girt about by bare hills under a blazing afternoon sky. It came from a black parcel that the Archduke Francis Ferdinand of Austria, with great presence of mind, had just flung out from the open hood of his automobile, where, tossed from the side of the quay, it had descended a few seconds before. It exploded as it touched the cobbled road just under the front of the second vehicle in the procession, and it blew to pieces the front of the automobile and injured the aide-de-camp who was in it and several of the spectators. Its thrower was immediately gripped by the bystanders. The procession stopped. There was a tremendous commotion amongst that brightly-costumed crowd, a hot excitement in vivid contrast to the Sabbath calm of Matching's Easy....

text: Mr. Carmine arrived in a car with two Native Americans and a man who had once been a prospector in Alaska. The family that had spent the night dancing at the Dower House reappeared, followed by a detached Mrs. Teddy carrying a specific hockey stick and Miss Corner pushing a stroller. More guests continued to arrive. At the first opportunity, Mr. Direck caught Miss Corner’s eye, and he lost all interest in anyone else.
source_text: Mr. Carmine arrived in an automobile with two Indians and a gentleman who had been a prospector in Alaska, the family who had danced overnight at the Dower House reappeared, and then Mrs. Teddy, very detached with a special hockey stick, and Miss Corner wheeling the perambulator. Then came further arrivals. At the earliest opportunity Mr. Direck secured the attention of Miss Corner, and lost his interest in any one else.

text: The ball darted swiftly back and forth across the pitch as our team mounted a fierce offensive. A chaotic mass of defenders and auxiliary players swarmed toward the vulnerable goal. Mr. Britling’s twelve-man squad was quickly losing any semblance of formation. One of the women from the sidecar and the courageous Indian gentleman had retreated to bolster the defense, joined by a man in spectacles who brandished his stick in flagrant violation of the rules. Mr. Direck’s captain and both of the Britling boys raced into the middle of the scramble. Mr. Britling, who struck Mr. Direck as being far too vocal and populist for a team leader, also hurried back to marshal his players with loud exhortations. "Pass it wide!" was the primary refrain of his instructions.
source_text: The ball executed some rapid movements to and fro across the field. Our side was pressing hard. There was a violent convergence of miscellaneous backs and suchlike irregulars upon the threatened goal. Mr. Britling's dozen was rapidly losing its disciplined order. One of the sidecar ladies and the gallant Indian had shifted their activities to the defensive back, and with them was a spectacled gentleman waving his stick, high above all recognised rules. Mr. Direck's captain and both Britling boys hurried to join the fray. Mr. Britling, who seemed to Mr. Direck to be for a captain rather too demagogic, also ran back to rally his forces by loud cries. "Pass outwardly!" was the burthen of his contribution.

text: The match resumed, and it quickly became apparent that the opening goal had been no indicator of the true intensity of the contest. Teddy and Cecily proved to be a formidable duo. Their impressive charges—bolstered by the aggressive yet skillful play of the Indian man on their right and directed by Mr. Britling’s boisterous shouting from the center—left Mr. Direck, the woman from the side-car, and Mr. Raeburn struggling to keep up. One rapid advance was only halted by the dust cloak, its heavy folds trapping the ball until help could arrive; another was repelled by a massive strike from Mr. Raeburn that sent the ball whistling inches past the youngest Britling’s head to the far side of the pitch. A third attempt saw Cecily flick a quick pass to the elder Britling son on her right, who deftly threaded the ball through the gaps in Mr. Lawrence Carmine’s defense to score. Following that, two more goals were recorded for Mr. Britling’s side in rapid succession.
source_text: The struggle was resumed. And soon it became clear that the first goal was no earnest of the quality of the struggle. Teddy and Cecily formed a terribly efficient combination. Against their brilliant rushes, supported in a vehement but effective manner by the Indian to their right and guided by loud shoutings from Mr. Britling (centre), Mr. Direck and the side-car lady and Mr. Raeburn struggled in vain. One swift advance was only checked by the dust cloak, its folds held the ball until help arrived; another was countered by a tremendous swipe of Mr. Raeburn's that sent the ball within an inch of the youngest Britling's head and right across the field; the third resulted in a swift pass from Cecily to the elder Britling son away on her right, and he shot the goal neatly and swiftly through the lattice of Mr. Lawrence Carmine's defensive movements. And after that very rapidly came another goal for Mr. Britling's side and then another.

text: Mr. Britling announced "Half Time," informing Mr. Direck that they paused for a five-minute rest and swapped sides once a team scored three goals. Everyone was perspiring and joyful, except the lady in the dust cloak, who remained perfectly composed. A competitive fire burned in every gaze, and the only shadow on Mr. Direck’s afternoon was a quiet, nagging worry about the durability of Mr. Raeburn’s trousers.
source_text: Then Mr. Britling cried out that it was "Half Time," and explained to Mr. Direck that whenever one side got to three goals they considered it was half time and had five minutes' rest and changed sides. Everybody was very hot and happy, except the lady in the dust cloak who was perfectly cool. In everybody's eyes shone the light of battle, and not a shadow disturbed the brightness of the afternoon for Mr. Direck except a certain unspoken anxiety about Mr. Raeburn's trousers.

text: At this point, Mr. Thorne was starting to grasp the fundamental maneuvers of the game. He was also recognizing that Beatrice was the most agile, swift, and tireless participant on the pitch. He served as her scout and distributor, developing a silent rapport with her. Any impulse to shield her had vanished completely. Facing them, Arthur and the girl from the sidecar, bolstered by Sterling, put up a formidable fight. Arthur was as lithe as a leopard. A "Four-Three" result seemed imminent, but then Arthur, the lanky Sikh, and Mrs. Arthur leveled the score. They nearly achieved the same feat to win, but Mr. Fletcher saved the day with a massive diagonal strike that found Mr. Thorne. He sprinted toward Sterling, feinted, and centered the ball to Beatrice. A frantic skirmish broke out on the left; Sterling knocked the ball out of bounds, and a young Pemberton threw it back in; the front line fumbled, but the cushioned matron recovered it. Onward once more! This final push will surely be the one!
source_text: By this time Mr. Direck was beginning to master the simple strategy of the sport. He was also beginning to master the fact that Cecily was the quickest, nimblest, most indefatigable player on the field. He scouted for her and passed to her. He developed tacit understandings with her. Ideas of protecting her had gone to the four winds of Heaven. Against them Teddy and a sidecar girl with Raeburn in support made a memorable struggle. Teddy was as quick as a cat. "Four-Three" looked like winning, but then Teddy and the tall Indian and Mrs. Teddy pulled square. They almost repeated this feat and won, but Mr. Manning saved the situation with an immense oblique hit that sent the ball to Mr. Direck. He ran with the ball up to Raeburn and then dodged and passed to Cecily. There was a lively struggle to the left; the ball was hit out by Mr. Raeburn and thrown in by a young Britling; lost by the forwards and rescued by the padded lady. Forward again! This time will do it!

text: Mr. Sterling emerged transformed, having applied a pungent liniment at Mrs. Thorne’s insistent suggestion, now attired in a somber dinner jacket and a generously knotted silk tie. He was suffused with a bodily contentment he had not tasted since departing the New York docks. Yet, this vigor possessed a quality distinct from the transatlantic variety. Where the American spirit felt crystalline and parched, this English vitality was—moist. His thoughts hovered with a quiet restlessness, like evening flies above a pool—devoid of sharp brilliance—and his limbs craved only repose. His bond with Elara matured with every shared look. Whenever their gazes locked, she offered a grin. He had grasped her tempo at last; abandoning his clumsy flatteries, he became her humble apprentice in the arts of the court and green. Following the evening meal, Mr. Thorne revisited his proposal for a motorcar outing on Monday.
source_text: Mr. Direck changed again, and after using some embrocation that Mrs. Britling recommended very strongly, came down in a black jacket and a cheerfully ample black tie. He had a sense of physical well-being such as he had not experienced since he came aboard the liner at New York. The curious thing was that it was not quite the same sense of physical well-being that one had in America. That is bright and clear and a little dry, this was--humid. His mind quivered contentedly, like sunset midges over a lake--it had no hard bright flashes--and his body wanted to sit about. His sense of intimacy with Cecily increased each time he looked at her. When she met his eyes she smiled. He'd caught her style now, he felt; he attempted no more compliments and was frankly her pupil at hockey and Badminton. After supper Mr. Britling renewed his suggestion of an automobile excursion on the Monday.

text: Past the village, Mr. Britling’s confidence seemed to rally. He picked up speed. But whenever other vehicles appeared or a side road came into view, his prudence returned. He stalked the road signs, creeping toward them in his lowest gear; he spent the whole morning driving like a man wary of hidden traps. Still, disaster caught him. For God demands something more from us than just being beyond reproach.
source_text: Beyond the village Mr. Britling seemed to gather confidence. The pace quickened. But whenever other traffic or any indication of a side way appeared discretion returned. Mr. Britling stalked his sign posts, crawling towards them on the belly of the lowest gear; he drove all the morning like a man who is flushing ambuscades. And yet accident overtook him. For God demands more from us than mere righteousness.

text: The motor started to emit a rhythmic clinking, and the vehicle’s momentum began to fade. At that moment, Mr. Britling noticed a small, earnest white sign bearing the words "Hidden Junction." Suddenly, he felt as though a side road might jump out from any direction. He disengaged the gears and jammed on the brakes, only to instantly regret the decision. But the engine, after three heavy, labored gasps, went dead. Clinging desperately to the steering wheel, Mr. Britling accidentally set the horn to a harsh blare, while his feet scrambled across the floorboards in a futile attempt to restart the motor. Mr. Direck sensed they were drifting backward, regardless of all the commotion. He grabbed for the emergency brake, but he was a moment too slow. With a soft, yielding sensation like settling into grease, the car tilted to one side and came to a halt with two of its wheels deep in the ditch.
source_text: The engine began to make a chinking sound, and the car lost pace. And then Mr. Britling saw a pleading little white board with the inscription "Concealed Turning." For the moment he thought a turning might be concealed anywhere. He threw out his clutch and clapped on his brake. Then he repented of what he had done. But the engine, after three Herculean throbs, ceased to work. Mr. Britling with a convulsive clutch at his steering wheel set the electric hooter snarling, while one foot released the clutch again and the other, on the accelerator, sought in vain for help. Mr. Direck felt they were going back, back, in spite of all this vocalisation. He clutched at the emergency brake. But he was too late to avoid misfortune. With a feeling like sitting gently in butter, the car sank down sideways and stopped with two wheels in the ditch.

text: The ruin of Gladys didn't merely spark a cycle of self-blame and regret stemming from the accident itself; it also triggered a vast array of secondary complications that were now tumbling clumsily through Mr. Britling’s consciousness. The loss of the vehicle proved to be remarkably troublesome in an entirely different way. It derailed certain intentions of his that existed well beyond the quiet life at Dower House, the place where Mr. Direck believed him to be so thoroughly anchored. For the duration of the weekend, Mr. Britling had been working hard to push several matters out of his head. Now, there was no longer any way to keep them at bay.
source_text: Now the smashing of Gladys was not only the source of a series of reproaches and remorses directly arising out of the smash; it had also a wide system of collateral consequences, which were also banging and blundering their way through the Britling mind. It was extraordinarily inconvenient in quite another direction that the automobile should be destroyed. It upset certain plans of Mr. Britling's in a direction growing right out from all the Dower House world in which Mr. Direck supposed him to be completely set and rooted. There were certain matters from which Mr. Britling had been averting his mind most strenuously throughout the week-end. Now, there was no averting his mind any more.

text: Mr. Britling was caught up in a romantic entanglement. To be precise, if one set aside his minor flirtations, this was his eighth such affair. He intended for his new motorcar, as soon as he could manage it with skill, to serve as a decisive means of resolving the various messy complications of this particular bond.
source_text: Mr. Britling was entangled in a love affair. It was, to be exact, and disregarding minor affinities, his eighth love affair. And the new automobile, so soon as he could drive it efficiently, was to have played quite a solvent and conclusive part in certain entangled complications of this relationship.

text: Mr. Thorne’s first union had been a season of radiant ecstasy. His second, by contrast, was a partnership of muted, domestic grays. There is much to be admired in that rigorous dogma which holds marriage to be not merely a mortal contract, but a perpetual union of spirits. Assuredly, Thorne would have possessed a more singular dignity, if not a more contented heart, had his romantic life withered alongside his first wife or persisted solely through his devotion to their son. He had wedded in the fever of youth, enjoying two years of honest affection, shared burdens, and those spirited disagreements that always ended in joy. A vital essence was surrendered to that time, one that could never be reclaimed. In his initial bereavement, he understood this perfectly—and then, eventually, he forgot. For while there is life, there is the restless imagination, which builds, forgets, and moves on.
source_text: Mr. Britling's first marriage had been a passionately happy one. His second was by comparison a marriage in neutral tint. There is much to be said for that extreme Catholic theory which would make marriage not merely lifelong but eternal. Certainly Mr. Britling would have been a finer if not a happier creature if his sentimental existence could have died with his first wife or continued only in his love for their son. He had married in the glow of youth, he had had two years of clean and simple loving, helping, quarrelling and the happy ending of quarrels. Something went out of him into all that, which could not be renewed again. In his first extremity of grief he knew that perfectly well--and then afterwards he forgot it. While there is life there is imagination, which makes and forgets and goes on.

text: The encounter with Edith occurred in a manner that shared no similarities with his memories of the late Mary. He met her through conventional social circles, whereas Mary had been a student at Newnham while he was a fellow at Pembroke; their discovery of one another had been a stroke of luck, characterized by chance and a touch of secrecy. That relationship had been marked by excitement and a certain impulsive energy. With Edith, however, he observed her, made a choice, and was required to court her. There was no sudden collision of souls, but rather a process of formal request and eventual agreement. Edith held a science degree from London University along with other similar credentials, and she viewed the world from beneath a wide brow and undulating brown hair with steady, observant eyes that seemed to hide nothing at all. This transparency was so unfathomable to Mr. Britling that he fell in love and married her primarily because of the tranquil mystery she seemed to possess. For a period after their wedding, he navigated those brown depths, intensely attempting to measure their true scale.
source_text: He met Edith under circumstances that did not in any way recall his lost Mary. He met her, as people say, "socially"; Mary, on the other hand, had been a girl at Newnham while he was a fellow of Pembroke, and there had been something of accident and something of furtiveness in their lucky discovery of each other. There had been a flush in it; there was dash in it. But Edith he saw and chose and had to woo. There was no rushing together; there was solicitation and assent. Edith was a Bachelor of Science of London University and several things like that, and she looked upon the universe under her broad forehead and broad-waving brown hair with quiet watchful eyes that had nothing whatever to hide, a thing so incredible to Mr. Britling that he had loved and married her very largely for the serenity of her mystery. And for a time after their marriage he sailed over those brown depths plumbing furiously.

text: Thus she moved through the years with an air of composure and grace, joining the ranks of those refined, cautious women who seek contentment in life’s secondary pleasures. She devoted herself to the quiet beauty of her home and garden—graceful, inanimate things that do not turn to wound their keeper—finding solace in aesthetic trifles and the comfort of a well-ordered existence. Furthermore, she took immense pride in the vitality and growth of her two young sons, whose lively presence provided a steady source of fulfillment. No one suspected, and perhaps she had even convinced herself to forget, the long seasons of shock and disenchantment, the quiet resentment and private weeping that had marked her slow realization. She had gradually understood that the unpredictable, vibrant man who had once pursued her with such desperate fervor had stopped loving her; his heart had eluded her grasp. It never occurred to her that she might have been the one to wound him, or that after his initial, chaotic, and misunderstood attempts to reach her, he had retreated into a shell of bitter isolation.
source_text: So she went through life, outwardly serene and dignified, one of a great company of rather fastidious, rather unenterprising women who have turned for their happiness to secondary things, to those fair inanimate things of household and garden which do not turn again and rend one, to aestheticisms and delicacies, to order and seemliness. Moreover she found great satisfaction in the health and welfare, the growth and animation of her own two little boys. And no one knew, and perhaps even she had contrived to forget, the phases of astonishment and disillusionment, of doubt and bitterness and secret tears, that spread out through the years in which she had slowly realised that this strange, fitful, animated man who had come to her, vowing himself hers, asking for her so urgently and persuasively, was ceasing, had ceased, to love her, that his heart had escaped her, that she had missed it; she never dreamt that she had hurt it, and that after its first urgent, tumultuous, incomprehensible search for her it had hidden itself bitterly away....

text: The inscrutable biological forces that had fashioned Julian Thorne had instilled in him a dogged conviction that somewhere on earth, within a single human breast, there resided the total fulfillment of his every longing and lack. He could conceive of a being, waiting in some unknown quarter, who possessed a depth of comprehension and a precision of sympathy that would resonate across the whole keyboard of his soul, from its most lyrical reaches to its most carnal depths. This bond would be so transformative that it would not only cast her—for this ideal was, of course, a woman—in a light of supreme loveliness, but would also achieve the near-impossible feat of making him feel beautiful and entirely at peace. Within the circle of her presence, there would be no self-doubt, no moral stumbles, and no finitude; there would be only delight and the pursuit of noble ends. Half the visionaries of the world yield to such a hope as instinctively as a migratory bird follows the season. They no more suspect it of being a mirage than a parched wanderer suspects the reality of a hidden well.
source_text: The mysterious processes of nature that had produced Mr. Britling had implanted in him an obstinate persuasion that somewhere in the world, from some human being, it was still possible to find the utmost satisfaction for every need and craving. He could imagine as existing, as waiting for him, he knew not where, a completeness of understanding, a perfection of response, that would reach all the gamut of his feelings and sensations from the most poetical to the most entirely physical, a beauty of relationship so transfiguring that not only would she--it went without saying that this completion was a woman--be perfectly beautiful in its light but, what was manifestly more incredible, that he too would be perfectly beautiful and quite at his ease.... In her presence there could be no self-reproaches, no lapses, no limitations, nothing but happiness and the happiest activities.... To such a persuasion half the imaginative people in the world succumb as readily and naturally as ducklings take to water. They do not doubt its truth any more than a thirsty camel doubts that presently it will come to a spring.

text: Over the weekend, Mr. Britling had succeeded in keeping his distress tightly contained. He had dispatched a short, noncommittal note to Mrs. Harrowdean, telling her he was reflecting on her words, and from that moment on, she had rarely entered his mind. More precisely, he had ensured that his attention was always occupied by more vivid interests. Now, however, in the hush of the late hours, the buried tension forced its way out and surged up to confront him.
source_text: Throughout the week-end Mr. Britling had kept this trouble well battened down. He had written to Mrs. Harrowdean a brief ambiguous note saying, "I am thinking over all that you have said," and after that he had scarcely thought about her at all. Or at least he had always contrived to be much more vividly thinking about something else. But now in these night silences the suppressed trouble burst hatches and rose about him.

text: How disastrously he had mismanaged the delicate machinery of his inner life! There was a period when he had embarked upon his desires and his principles as cheerfully as he had set out with the Phaeton toward Bishop’s Green. He possessed no more appetite for complexity than he did for the wayside mire. And now his spirit and his reputation were in a more pitiful condition than the battered, smoking Phaeton as the dray-horses hauled it away; for the carriage, at least, might be mended. But he—he was a threadbare tapestry of justifications. Not that he had ever stooped to justify himself. Yet there he stood! Remote, like a candle flickering at the far end of a vault, was that first sorrowful tale of a devotion as white as snow. It had reached its end by thirty, and he could almost weep that he had ever allowed his heart to stir again. He should have endured as a dignified recluse. Then Clara had arrived, Clara that guileless and accidental debtor. And there, once more, he should have clung to his resignation. He had clung to it—nine times out of ten. It is the tenth occasion, the singular, tempting minute, the flash of arrogant certainty—and your optimistic soul is back in the gutter.
source_text: What a mess he had made of the whole scheme of his emotional life! There had been a time when he had started out as gaily with his passions and his honour as he had started out with Gladys to go to Market Saffron. He had as little taste for complications as he had for ditches. And now his passions and his honour were in a worse case even than poor muddy smashed up Gladys as the cart-horses towed her off, for she at any rate might be repaired. But he--he was a terribly patched fabric of explanations now. Not indeed that he had ever stooped to explanations. But there he was! Far away, like a star seen down the length of a tunnel, was that first sad story of a love as clean as starlight. It had been all over by eight-and-twenty and he could find it in his heart to grieve that he had ever given a thought to love again. He should have lived a decent widower.... Then Edith had come into his life, Edith that honest and unconscious defaulter. And there again he should have stuck to his disappointment. He had stuck to it--nine days out of every ten. It's the tenth day, it's the odd seductive moment, it's the instant of confident pride--and there is your sanguine temperament in the ditch.

text: He summoned to mind the tea-time discourse at Blackwood, weighing the bureaucratic specimens he counted within his own circle. Imagine the sudden blow of an invasion! To be shielded by Vane and his coterie! Or, should the impact unseat them, to fall into the hands of those venomous Reactionaries—the bitter defenders of brittle autocracies and hollow entitlements who waited in the wings. The nation lacked a captain. In the stark clarity of the night, this truth settled upon him with a chilling finality. He was haunted by a specter of catastrophic confusion; he saw Lady Marbury and her *Gazette* clique erupting in a shrill, spiteful "patriotism," sowing havoc as the gale rose, only to eventually recognize a kindred nobility in the Prussians and negotiate a surrender based on that shared vanity. Rumors reached him of a Fleet ill-equipped with ordnance and lacking the heavy warships essential for a Continental struggle, its command fractured by petty academic disputes; yet, this maritime force remained their solitary bulwark. Throughout the realm, perhaps three hundred thousand souls could be gathered in a motley assembly. He harbored no trust in their gear, nor in their commanders. General Maitland, the sole officer who commanded his respect, had been driven to resign over a solicitor’s bungle regarding the Ulster impasse. He doubted any strategy had been drafted for the conflict Berlin might impose, nor was there any ledger of costs, nor any vision of the coming storm whatsoever.
source_text: He recalled the afternoon's talk at Claverings and such samples of our governmental machinery as he chanced to number among his personal acquaintance. Suppose suddenly the enemy struck! With Raeburn and his friends to defend us! Or if the shock tumbled them out of power, then with these vituperative Tories, these spiteful advocates of weak tyrannies and privileged pretences in the place of them. There was no leadership in England. In the lucid darkness he knew that with a terrible certitude. He had a horrible vision of things disastrously muffled; of Lady Frensham and her _Morning Post_ friends first garrulously and maliciously "patriotic," screaming her way with incalculable mischiefs through the storm, and finally discovering that the Germans were the real aristocrats and organising our national capitulation on that understanding. He knew from talk he had heard that the navy was weak in mines and torpedoes, unprovided with the great monitors needed for a war with Germany; torn by doctrinaire feuds; nevertheless the sea power was our only defence. In the whole country we might muster a military miscellany of perhaps three hundred thousand men. And he had no faith in their equipment, in their direction. General French, the one man who had his entire confidence, had been forced to resign through some lawyer's misunderstanding about the Irish difficulty. He did not believe any plans existed for such a war as Germany might force upon us, any calculation, any foresight of the thing at all.

text: Could the vast reach of the English hegemony, its global dominion, its iron-clad securities, and its disciplined routine be nothing more than a cosmic accident? He had interrogated both his country and his own soul on this point a thousand times. Fortune had certainly smiled upon him; he was wealthy, still possessing the vigor of his late prime. But was there not also some inherent quality, some worthy trait at play? Providence had gifted the English a strategic archipelago, a bracing atmosphere, and accessible seams of coal, yet was there not also a specific national excellence? He once held faith in that—a particular courage, a graceful nonchalance, a foundational pragmatism. A decade of political upheaval had eroded that belief profoundly. He gripped the remnants of it still, though without any real conviction. In the dark hours of the night, that comforting lie vanished entirely. As for his own life, he possessed a certain intellectual agility, perhaps, but his university appointment had occurred during a period of low competition; his position at the *Chronicle* was the result of a fortunate misunderstanding; and a chance encounter at a gala with a Duchess had secured his role in the Sterling exhibition. He had simply tumbled into roles that suited his temperament. These windfalls had never spurred him to greater labor. Because the path was smooth, he had elevated lethargy into a worldview. Now, at forty-two, he found himself lecturing the world—spending the entire retreat explaining to that visiting New Yorker, until the heavens themselves revolted and the car wreck silenced him—how the charming stagnation of the shires and the British habit of drifting were somehow the pinnacle of human achievement. A bloated, nationalistic creed. It was the sort of smugness *The Spectator* had peddled for half a century.
source_text: Was the whole prosperity of the British, the far-flung empire, the securities, the busy order, just their good luck? It was a question he had asked a hundred times of his national as of his personal self. No doubt luck had favoured him. He was prosperous, and he was still only at the livelier end of middle age. But was there not also a personal factor, a meritorious factor? Luck had favoured the British with a well-placed island, a hardening climate, accessible minerals, but then too was there not also a national virtue? Once he had believed in that, in a certain gallantry, a noble levity, an underlying sound sense. The last ten years of politics had made him doubt that profoundly. He clung to it still, but without confidence. In the night that dear persuasion left him altogether.... As for himself he had a certain brightness and liveliness of mind, but the year of his fellowship had been a soft year, he had got on to _The Times_ through something very like a misapprehension, and it was the chances of a dinner and a duchess that had given him the opportunity of the Kahn show. He'd dropped into good things that suited him. That at any rate was the essence of it. And these lucky chances had been no incentive to further effort. Because things had gone easily and rapidly with him he had developed indolence into a philosophy.
