Here he was just over forty, and explaining to the world, explaining all through the week-end to this American--until even God could endure it no longer and the smash stopped him--how excellent was the backwardness of Essex and English go-as-you-please, and how through good temper it made in some mysterious way for all that was desirable. A fat English doctrine. _Punch_ has preached it for forty years.

text: In the depths of these dark nights, the individual Mr. Britling would lie awake considering how poorly his own life was proceeding, while the universal Mr. Britling weighed the failures of the cosmos. His thoughts played out like a grand, intricate piece of music, where the heavy organ mourned the tragic fate of the human race and the shrill piccolo fretted over the private complications of Mrs. Harrowdean. The bass drum thundered with indignation at the Irish politicians, and the violins wept for the intellectual decay of the universities. Meanwhile, the trumpets heralded coming wars and ruin, the cymbals periodically struck a harsh note regarding the stalled automobile insurance, and the triangle rang out a sharp, persistent solo about a decaying gate-post he neglected during the day—reminding him how, as a result, the cows from the parish farm would invade the garden and consume his wife’s prize carnations.
source_text: On these black nights, when the personal Mr. Britling would lie awake thinking how unsatisfactorily Mr. Britling was going on, and when the impersonal Mr. Britling would be thinking how unsatisfactorily his universe was going on, the whole mental process had a likeness to some complex piece of orchestral music wherein the organ deplored the melancholy destinies of the race while the piccolo lamented the secret trouble of Mrs. Harrowdean; the big drum thundered at the Irish politicians, and all the violins bewailed the intellectual laxity of the university system. Meanwhile the trumpets prophesied wars and disasters, the cymbals ever and again inserted a clashing jar about the fatal delay in the automobile insurance, while the triangle broke into a plangent solo on the topic of a certain rotten gate-post he always forgot in the daytime, and how in consequence the cows from the glebe farm got into the garden and ate Mrs. Britling's carnations.

text: The following morning, Mr. Britling entered Mr. Direck’s room. He was rosy-cheeked from his bath and clad in a vibrant green-and-blue silk dressing gown; having already shaved, he showed no evidence of his late-night vigil. He had been heard whistling like a songbird while in the bathroom. "Did you sleep well?" he asked. "Splendid. I did as well. And your wrist and arm—I trust they didn't ache enough to disturb your rest?"
source_text: The next morning Mr. Britling came into Mr. Direck's room. He was pink from his morning bath, he was wearing a cheerful green-and-blue silk dressing gown, he had shaved already, he showed no trace of his nocturnal vigil. In the bathroom he had whistled like a bird. "Had a good night?" he said. "That's famous. So did I. And the wrist and arm didn't even ache enough to keep you awake?"

text: I used to entertain a certain apprehension regarding the German state," remarked Mr. Ashford, "but I have found myself placing more and more trust in the inherent sobriety of the German people, and in the Kaiser himself, for that matter. He is—if Herr Müller will forgive me for aligning with the wit of his own satirical broadsheets—perhaps a trifle stagey, perhaps a bit vain, but in his own spectacular, vivid fashion, his heart is set on concord. I am quite convinced his heart is set on concord....
source_text: "I used to be an alarmist about Germany," said Mr. Britling, "but I have come to feel more and more confidence in the sound common sense of the mass of the German population, and in the Emperor too if it comes to that. He is--if Herr Heinrich will permit me to agree with his own German comic papers--sometimes a little theatrical, sometimes a little egotistical, but in his operatic, boldly coloured way he means peace. I am convinced he means peace...."

text: It looked as if Mr. Direck wouldn’t be able to write any letters until his wrist had fully recovered. Teddy attempted to get him started on a typewriter, but Mr. Direck’s left hand proved far too clumsy. Suddenly, Mr. Britling recalled a strange quirk he possessed, one that Mr. Direck might also have without realizing it: the knack for mirror writing with his left hand. Back in his school days, Mr. Britling had stumbled upon the fact that while his right hand was painstakingly learning to write, his left hand had been secretly absorbing the same lessons. He found that by moving a pencil from right to left with his left hand, without focusing too hard on the page, he could create a perfect reverse of his usual script. Apparently, about three out of five people possess this hidden ability. After he demonstrated the trick, Miss Cecily Corner—who had stopped by casually to check on Mr. Direck—gave it a try, and then Mr. Direck followed suit. They all succeeded. Teddy then fetched a sheet of carbon paper, and by placing it backwards under the stationery, Mr. Direck was finally able to rejoin the world of correspondence.
source_text: It seemed as though Mr. Direck would be unable to write any letters until his wrist had mended. Teddy tried him with a typewriter, but Mr. Direck was very awkward with his left hand, and then Mr. Britling suddenly remembered a little peculiarity he had which it was possible that Mr. Direck might share unconsciously, and that was his gift of looking-glass writing with his left hand. Mr. Britling had found out quite by chance in his schoolboy days that while his right hand had been laboriously learning to write, his left hand, all unsuspected, had been picking up the same lesson, and that by taking a pencil in his left hand and writing from right to left, without watching what he was writing, and then examining the scrawl in a mirror, he could reproduce his own handwriting in exact reverse. About three people out of five have this often quite unsuspected ability. He demonstrated his gift, and then Miss Cecily Corner, who had dropped in in a casual sort of way to ask about Mr. Direck, tried it, and then Mr. Direck tried it. And they could all do it. And then Teddy brought a sheet of copying carbon, and so Mr. Direck, by using the carbon reversed under his paper, was restored to the world of correspondence again.

text: With the help of the two young boys, Herr Heinrich soon managed to haul a massive oak branch into his chamber, turning the entire room into the likeness of a bird sanctuary. "Billy will be most appreciative of this," Herr Heinrich remarked, appearing solemn and astute behind his spectacles. "It will grant him confidence in me. It will make him feel as though we are out in the forest together."
source_text: Assisted by the two small boys, Herr Heinrich presently got a huge bough of oak and brought it into his room, converting the entire apartment into the likeness of an aviary. "For this," said Herr Heinrich, looking grave and diplomatic through his glasses, "Billy will be very grateful. And it will give him confidence with me. It will make him feel we are in the forest together."

text: During the evening meal, the conversation revolved entirely around Billy, and the atmosphere was thick with sympathy. Herr Heinrich challenged the prevailing notion that the creature had behaved with a degree of cruelty, arguing instead that a mere lack of polish was Billy’s only shortcoming. He shared stories—tales he held in high regard—of the little fellow’s more affectionate and honorable nature. "Every time I provide his meal, he expresses his gratitude," Herr Heinrich stated. "He never forgets." Then, he hinted at more sinister suspicions. "As I passed the barn, I noticed a cat staring at me from behind a laurel bush," he remarked. "I’ve never cared for cats."
source_text: At supper Billy was the sole topic of conversation, and condolence was in the air. The impression that on the whole he had displayed rather a brutal character was combated by Herr Heinrich, who held that a certain brusqueness was Billy's only fault, and told anecdotes, almost sacred anecdotes, of the little creature's tenderer, nobler side. "When I feed him always he says, 'Thank you,'" said Herr Heinrich. "He never fails." He betrayed darker thoughts. "When I went round by the barn there was a cat that sat and looked at me out of a laurel bush," he said. "I do not like cats."

text: The day was coming when Mr. Britling would reexamine the history of that sunny July with incredulous detail, attempting to trace the true progression from the shocking crime in Sarajevo to Europe’s final, frantic descent into war. In one sense, the path was invisible; in another, it was so blatant he was astonished the world had not foreseen the coming ruin. The reality was that no direct connection existed; for two weeks, the Sarajevo murders slipped from public consciousness and the headlines, only to be resurrected as a justification for battle. Germany, bristling with arms and weary of her long, watchful vigil, observed the unfolding events and decided her moment had arrived, dragging the dead archduke from his tomb to serve her vast territorial ambitions.
source_text: A day was to come when Mr. Britling was to go over the history of that sunny July with incredulous minuteness, trying to trace the real succession of events that led from the startling crime at Sarajevo to Europe's last swift rush into war. In a sense it was untraceable; in a sense it was so obvious that he was amazed the whole world had not watched the coming of disaster. The plain fact of the case was that there was no direct connection; the Sarajevo murders were dropped for two whole weeks out of the general consciousness, they went out of the papers, they ceased to be discussed; then they were picked up again and used as an excuse for war. Germany, armed so as to be a threat to all the world, weary at last of her mighty vigil, watching the course of events, decided that her moment had come, and snatched the dead archduke out of his grave again to serve her tremendous ambition.

text: It is unlikely that anyone beyond the central command of Germany and Austria perceived the situation with such clarity. The strategic momentum lay entirely with the Germans. The thinkers of Russia, France, and Britain—those few who were actually beginning to confront the crisis directly—wrestled with far more convoluted dilemmas and deeper anxieties. In Mr. Britling’s view, the conference at Buckingham Palace served as a perfect illustration of the fragmentation and hesitation that persisted until the very moment hostilities erupted. He felt a deep-seated revulsion for the grim aggression of Sir Edward Carson; looking back, he envisioned that somber man with his sagging lip, sitting ponderous and unyielding at the table. Carson remained unmoved, even though the King had only just left the room after delivering a brief address filled with thinly disguised warnings of the approaching catastrophe.
source_text: It is doubtful if any one outside the directing intelligence of Germany and Austria saw anything so plain. The initiative was with Germany. The Russian brains and the French brains and the British brains, the few that were really coming round to look at this problem squarely, had a far less simple set of problems and profounder uncertainties. To Mr. Britling's mind the Round Table Conference at Buckingham Palace was typical of the disunion and indecision that lasted up to the very outbreak of hostilities. The solemn violence of Sir Edward Carson was intensely antipathetic to Mr. Britling, and in his retrospective inquiries he pictured to himself that dark figure with its dropping under-lip, seated, heavy and obstinate, at that discussion, still implacable though the King had but just departed after a little speech that was packed with veiled intimations of imminent danger...

text: Julian felt a profound intolerance for the betrayal of a stubborn pride and the dogged pursuit of a flawed logic. His own psychological frailties were cast in a different mold entirely. He was perpetually prepared to swap one conviction for another, always yielding to a better argument, confident that the bedrock of his spirit would ensure an eventual coherence. He detested Sterling then as a fox terrier might despise a mastiff—viewing him as a being undeniably more formidable and grand, yet infuriatingly, immeasurably more dull-witted.
source_text: Mr. Britling had no mercy in his mind for the treason of obstinate egotism and for persistence in a mistaken course. His own temperamental weaknesses lay in such different directions. He was always ready to leave one trail for another; he was always open to conviction, trusting to the essentials of his character for an ultimate consistency. He hated Carson in those days as a Scotch terrier might hate a bloodhound, as something at once more effective and impressive, and exasperatingly, infinitely less intelligent.

text: Life proceeded at Dower House as each inhabitant followed their own pursuits. Mr. Direck’s arm mended quickly, and he spent many hours with Cecily Corner discussing their personal ambitions, the nature of Utopias, and the literary works of Mr. Britling. He also sent for several Baedeker travel guides from a London shop, covering the landscapes of Holland, Belgium, Southern Germany, and Italy. After a moment of indecision, Herr Heinrich finally submitted his registration and deposit for the Esperanto Conference in Boulogne, while Billy allowed himself to be petted a few times, even if he still snapped with his usual speed and spirit. Furthermore, the concern regarding Hugh, Mr. Britling’s eldest son, turned out to be quite minor and was settled without any lasting difficulty.
source_text: And at Dower House they went about their businesses. Mr. Direck's arm healed rapidly; Cecily Corner and he talked of their objects in life and Utopias and the books of Mr. Britling, and he got down from a London bookseller Baedeker's guides for Holland and Belgium, South Germany and Italy; Herr Heinrich after some doubt sent in his application form and his preliminary deposit for the Esperanto Conference at Boulogne, and Billy consented to be stroked three times but continued to bite with great vigour and promptitude. And the trouble about Hugh, Mr. Britling's eldest son, resolved itself into nothing of any vital importance, and settled itself very easily.

text: Among all the world’s influences, Julian possessed the singular power to awaken Mr. Sterling’s unfortunate penchant for morbid foreboding. The boy resided closer to the marrow of his being and the sensitivity of his nerves than any other living soul. Over recent years, illuminated by the flickering torches of several romantic diversions, Sterling had gradually come to recognize this truth. Whatever Sterling perceived, he felt compelled to articulate; from this profound well of affection—an effortless warmth that surpassed the choreographed passions of his past—he had forged the philosophy he once shared with Mr. Thorne: that only through one’s offspring can a man touch the hem of selfless, unadulterated devotion. Yet this doctrine failed to account for the visceral, singular grip Julian held over him, a bond far more potent than the cheerful regard he felt for his younger step-sons. It was a discrepancy that Sterling, with a deliberate and practiced caution, chose never to scrutinize too closely.
source_text: Of all things in the world Hugh was the one that could most easily rouse Mr. Britling's unhappy aptitude for distressing imaginations. Hugh was nearer by far to his heart and nerves than any other creature. In the last few years Mr. Britling, by the light of a variety of emotional excursions in other directions, had been discovering this. Whatever Mr. Britling discovered he talked about; he had evolved from his realisation of this tenderness, which was without an effort so much tenderer than all the subtle and tremendous feelings he had attempted in his--excursions, the theory that he had expounded to Mr. Direck that it is only through our children that we are able to achieve disinterested love, real love. But that left unexplained that far more intimate emotional hold of Hugh than of his very jolly little step-brothers. That was a fact into which Mr. Britling rather sedulously wouldn't look....

text: Julian had forsaken the natural sciences for the easel following a solemn deliberation with his father, but the secret anxiety that gnawed at him was a newfound desire to recant. He longed to accompany Ashford to Edinburgh and—though a year had been squandered—reclaim his place in the lecture halls. He viewed this changeability as a shameful defect of will, mindful also of the heavy cost, and thus he endured a fortnight of inner turmoil before he could steel himself to confess the truth.
source_text: He gave up science for art after grave consultation with his father, and the real trouble that had been fretting him, it seemed, was that now he repented and wanted to follow Cardinal to Cambridge, and--a year lost--go on with science again. He felt it was a discreditable fluctuation; he knew it would be a considerable expense; and so he took two weeks before he could screw himself up to broaching the matter.

text: Or I could have bought her a massive, cream-colored car on a payment plan, only for her to wreck it. No, that kind of drama can wait. For now, I’ll just try my luck with one of those charming girls at the Cambridge tobacco shops and keep focusing on my degree for another year or two.
source_text: "Or bought a large cream-coloured motor-car for her on the instalment system, which she'd smashed up. No, that sort of thing comes later.... I'll just put myself down on the waiting list of one of those bits of delight in the Cambridge tobacco shops--and go on with my studies for a year or two...."

text: Was he really stifling a remarkable talent beneath the mundane weight of public affairs? When they finally wandered back from the garden pavilion for dinner in the golden evening light, he had given her his word that he would return to "The Silent Places" and see it through to completion. He also promised to reconsider the Irish pamphlet before he let it go to print.
source_text: Was he indeed burying a marvellous gift under the dust of current affairs? When at last in the warm evening light they strolled back from the summer-house to dinner he had definitely promised her that he would take up and finish "The Silent Places."... And think over the Irish pamphlet again before he published it....

text: That is the very point Mr. Sterling raises regarding you in his *Transatlantic Sketches*," she remarked. "He contends that the States encourage an excessive cultivation of the individual ego, and that whatever other faults they may possess, this is where they are most palpably in error. I encountered that passage only this morning, and it struck me instantly: 'Yes, that is precisely it! Mr. Vance is far too engaged in the over-elaboration of his own persona.'
source_text: "That is just what Mr. Britling says about you in his 'American Impressions,'" she said. "He says that America overdoes the development of personalities altogether, that whatever else is wrong about America that is where America is most clearly wrong. I read that this morning, and directly I read it I thought, 'Yes, that's exactly it! Mr. Direck is overdoing the development of personalities.'"

text: It’s true. I find talking with you both pleasant and frustrating. I’ve realized it’s because you are constantly dissecting my character and yours, which makes me feel self-conscious. It’s as if a spotlight is following me wherever I move. In a way, I actually enjoy the attention; it’s flattering, but later I find myself resenting the impact it has on me. I start trying to live up to the version of me you’ve described—performing my own personality, essentially. I feel this need to check my reflection to see if I’m maintaining the image. It’s precisely what Mr. Britling wrote about American women: they play a role, creating a narrative and an explanation for themselves that they are constantly trying to project to everyone else. And when you’re preoccupied with that, you lose the ability to think clearly about anything else.
source_text: "Yes. I like talking to you and I don't like talking to you. And I see now it is because you keep on talking of my Personality and your Personality. That makes me uncomfortable. It's like having some one following me about with a limelight. And in a sort of way I do like it. I like it and I'm flattered by it, and then I go off and dislike it, dislike the effect of it. I find myself trying to be what you have told me I am--sort of acting myself. I want to glance at looking-glasses to see if I am keeping it up. It's just exactly what Mr. Britling says in his book about American women. They act themselves, he says; they get a kind of story and explanation about themselves and they are always trying to make it perfectly plain and clear to every one. Well, when you do that you can't think nicely of other things."

text: You are certainly more a person of subtle nuances," remarked Mr. Direck. "And I must admit that it is those very subtleties that truly captivate me. Yet you still haven't told me, Miss Cissie, what you think I should do with my life. Here I am, you see, very much at your service. What sort of pursuit do you believe it is my duty to take up?
source_text: "You're certainly much more in half-tones," said Mr. Direck. "And I confess it's the half-tones get hold of me. But still you haven't told me, Miss Cissie, what you think I ought to do with myself. Here I am, you see, very much at your disposal. What sort of business do you think it's my duty to go in for?"

text: You return to that theme," Mr. Direck remarked. "I’ve never encountered anyone who embodies that particular spirit quite as you do. I wouldn't change it; it’s an essential part of you, and an essential part of this place. It is the very thing Mr. Britling is always suggesting without ever quite realizing he’s said it. It’s as if the events of our lives aren't what truly matters—as if there is something else entirely. One eventually falls into that way of thinking. It makes daily life, politics, the King, and the world of business seem like mere shadows, as if there were a deeper reality hidden just out of sight—around the corner—that we ought to be pursuing. I’ll admit, that feeling has taken hold of me, too. And in my mind, it’s inextricably linked to you. I don’t see any contradiction in that. I am in love with you—entirely, wholeheartedly—and there’s no point in being timid about it. I would sacrifice everything for your smallest whim right now; it’s as if love itself, rather than blood, pulses through my veins. But this doesn't pull me away from that higher purpose; it brings me closer to it. I feel that without you, I am capable of nothing, but with you—well, we wouldn't just settle into a quiet cottage or wander about with a Baedeker guide. Not for long, anyway. We would naturally stand side by side and *do*...
source_text: "You come back to that," said Mr. Direck. "I never met any one before who'd quite got that spirit as you have it. I wouldn't alter it. It's part of you. It's part of this place. It's what Mr. Britling always seems to be saying and never quite knowing he's said it. It's just as though all the things that are going on weren't the things that ought to be going on--but something else quite different. Somehow one falls into it. It's as if your daily life didn't matter, as if politics didn't matter, as if the King and the social round and business and all those things weren't anything really, and as though you felt there was something else--out of sight--round the corner--that you ought to be getting at. Well, I admit, that's got hold of me too. And it's all mixed up with my idea of you. I don't see that there's really a contradiction in it at all. I'm in love with you, all my heart's in love with you, what's the good of being shy about it? I'd just die for your littlest wish right here now, it's just as though I'd got love in my veins instead of blood, but that's not taking me away from that other thing. It's bringing me round to that other thing. I feel as if without you I wasn't up to anything at all, but _with_ you--We'd not go settling down in a cottage or just touring about with a Baedeker Guide or anything of that kind. Not for long anyhow. We'd naturally settle down side by side and _do_ ..."

text: Do you see that fallen timber over there? I rested upon it only the day before yesterday, my mind filled with thoughts of you. Won't you accompany me to that spot and sit a while? From that seat, one is granted a view—oh, a most charming English prospect!—offering a glimpse of the manor, those stands of trees, and the valley stretching toward the lily pond. I should so like to include you in my remembrance of it.
source_text: "You see that old felled tree there. I was sitting on it the day before yesterday and thinking of you. Will you come there and sit with me on it? When you sit on it you get a view, oh! a perfectly lovely English view, just a bit of the house and those clumps of trees and the valley away there with the lily pond. I'd love to have you in my memory of it...."

text: Upon reaching the small thicket, where the path was carpeted in moss and shielded by a screen of woven branches, he broke the silence. "Well, what's wrong with right here and now?" he asked. Cecily faced him, standing as straight as an arrow, her clear eyes full of unspoken devotion. He cupped her soft, cool face in his trembling hands and kissed her tender, half-parted lips. She shivered at the contact, and as he drew her closer to kiss her again, she pulled back. He did not insist. More silent than before, lost in thought and shaking with a strange internal intensity, these two outwardly calm young people made their way back to the Dower House.
source_text: And when they had reached the little covert, with its pathway of soft moss and its sheltering screen of interlacing branches, he broke the silence by saying, "Well, what's wrong with right here and now?" and Cecily stood up to him as straight as a spear, with gifts in her clear eyes. He took her soft cool face between his trembling hands, and kissed her sweet half-parted lips. When he kissed her she shivered, and he held her tighter and would have kissed her again. But she broke away from him, and he did not press her. And muter than ever, pondering deeply, and secretly trembling in the queerest way, these two outwardly sedate young people returned to the Dower House....

text: If they failed to repair the connection between them, the relationship would surely collapse, a prospect that filled Mr. Britling with an intense sense of frustration. She would inevitably turn to Oliver for solace and marry him; he knew her well enough to be certain she would flaunt her domestic bliss before him without restraint. Meanwhile, lacking any similar partner of his own, he would be relegated to a state of emotional solitude, left to manage his idle hours and his constant craving for praise and diversion entirely by himself. He would be consumed by envy. In such a state—and here he faced the fundamental truth—his writing would suffer. His work would fail to hold his interest so long as those restless, unspoken needs she once satisfied remained churning and unfulfilled.
source_text: And unless they could restore the bridge things would end, and Mr. Britling felt that the ending of things would involve for him the most extraordinary exasperation. She would go to Oliver for comfort; she would marry Oliver; and he knew her well enough to be sure that she would thrust her matrimonial happiness with Oliver unsparingly upon his attention; while he, on the other hand, being provided with no corresponding Olivette, would be left, a sort of emotional celibate, with his slack times and his afternoons and his general need for flattery and amusement dreadfully upon his own hands. He would be tormented by jealousy. In which case--and here he came to verities--his work would suffer. It wouldn't grip him while all these vague demands she satisfied fermented unassuaged.

text: Furthermore," Herr Heinrich cried, turning to face another of his frustrations, "how am I supposed to pack? Since I’ve been here, I’ve bought so many things: numerous books, two pairs of white flannel trousers, several shirts, and a metal gadget for developing Kodak film that I can’t even figure out how to use. All of this has to go into my small suitcase. And it simply won’t fit into my small suitcase!
source_text: "Also," cried Herr Heinrich, facing another aspect of his distresses, "how am I to pack my things? Since I have been here I have bought many things, many books, and two pairs of white flannel trousers and some shirts and a tin instrument that I cannot work, for developing privately Kodak films. All this must go into my little portmanteau. And it will not go into my little portmanteau!

text: Yet he did not keep his things packed for long. The taxi he had gone to the station to book that morning, using Teddy’s accommodating bike, arrived shortly to take him away. The whole family and the first group of the usual hockey players assembled around the vehicle to see him off. The older of the two younger boys placed a bulging rucksack onto the seat. Then, Herr Heinrich shook hands with everyone.
source_text: But he did not leave them packed up. The taxi-cab, to order which he had gone to the junction in the morning on Teddy's complaisant machine, came presently to carry him off, and the whole family and the first contingent of the usual hockey players gathered about it to see him off. The elder boy of the two juniors put a distended rucksack upon the seat. Herr Heinrich then shook hands with every one.

text: He stood for a while, taking in the scene. The balloon launch had drawn a modest crowd: village girls in white gloves and festive hats, young men in vivid ties and off-the-rack Sunday suits, parents, scouts, children, clerks in straw boaters, cyclists, and a mix of onlookers. Towering over the assembly was Mr. Cheshunt, the estate’s man-of-all-work. Standing on a table, he released the small balloons into the sky one at a time. They rose from his hand like clusters of multicolored grapes; some bobbed and dipped, others ascended with steady grace, while some whirled and eddied, drifting eastward on a gentle breeze—a string of bubbles set against the sky and the massive trees bordering the park. Off to the right, the striped canvas of the flower-show tents was visible; further still, the carousels churned out their melodies, the shooting galleries popped, and the swing boats creaked as they arched through the air. Partitioned off by a fence, the open parkland stretched out, where deer huddled under the great trees and watched the celebration with suspicion. Teddy and Hugh emerged from the throng around the balloons, hurrying back to their game of darts. Beside a solitary little tent, a man was putting up a bold notice: "Unlimited Teas, One Shilling." The perambulator was parked by the coconut shy, where Aunt Wilshire was still demonstrating her terrifying accuracy. She had already claimed twenty-seven prizes. A group of local children had been recruited to carry them, following her like a small retinue. Aunt Wilshire was a truly extraordinary old woman.
source_text: He stood for some moments watching the scene. The balloon start had gathered a little crowd of people, village girls in white gloves and cheerful hats, young men in bright ties and ready-made Sunday suits, fathers and mothers, boy scouts, children, clerks in straw hats, bicyclists and miscellaneous folk. Over their heads rose Mr. Cheshunt, the factotum of the estate. He was standing on a table and handing the little balloons up into the air one by one. They floated up from his hand like many-coloured grapes, some rising and falling, some soaring steadily upward, some spinning and eddying, drifting eastward before the gentle breeze, a string of bubbles against the sky and the big trees that bounded the park. Farther away to the right were the striped canvas tents of the flower-show, still farther off the roundabouts churned out their music, the shooting galleries popped, and the swing boats creaked through the air. Cut off from these things by a line of fencing lay the open park in which the deer grouped themselves under the great trees and regarded the festival mistrustfully. Teddy and Hugh appeared breaking away from the balloon race cluster, and hurrying back to their dart-throwing. A man outside a little tent that stood apart was putting up a brave-looking notice, "Unstinted Teas One Shilling." The Teddy perambulator was moored against the cocoanut shy, and Aunt Wilshire was still displaying her terrible prowess at the cocoanuts. Already she had won twenty-seven. Strange children had been impressed by her to carry them, and formed her retinue. A wonderful old lady was Aunt Wilshire....

text: This might slow our progress or make the objective more difficult, but it won't change the final result. We are going to win, of course. No other outcome is even conceivable. I never truly believed they were serious, but I see now that they were. This arrogant buildup and constant drilling, forty years of nationalistic posturing—sooner or later, it was bound to spill over into actual war.
source_text: "That may delay things; it may make the task harder; but it will not alter the end. Of course we are going to win. Nothing else is thinkable. I have never believed they meant it. But I see now they meant it. This insolent arming and marching, this forty years of national blustering; sooner or later it had to topple over into action...."

text: The speech he had delivered left Mr. Britling somewhat startled. He had not grasped, until the words actually left his mouth, the sheer scale of his fury and disdain for the German military machine that had so long cast a shadow over his world. He had insisted for years that they would never truly go to war—and yet, here they were. His anger was fueled by the bitter resentment of a defender who felt personally betrayed. He had only discovered the depth of his own combative convictions by hearing them aloud, and as he and his wife walked home through the village, he remained preoccupied with this sudden self-insight. He was somber and quiet, mentally sharpening scathing indictments of Krupp and the Kaiser. "Krupp and Kaiser"—he seized upon the easy, punchy alliteration. "It is the union of everything regressive about the Middle Ages with everything destructive about the modern age," he told himself.
source_text: Mr. Britling had been a little surprised by the speech he had made. He hadn't realised before he began to talk how angry and scornful he was at this final coming into action of the Teutonic militarism that had so long menaced his world. He had always said it would never really fight--and here it was fighting! He was furious with the indignation of an apologist betrayed. He had only realised the strength and passion of his own belligerent opinions as he had heard them, and as he walked back with his wife through the village to the Dower House, he was still in the swirl of this self-discovery; he was darkly silent, devising fiercely denunciatory phrases against Krupp and Kaiser. "Krupp and Kaiser," he grasped that obvious, convenient alliteration. "It is all that is bad in mediævalism allied to all that is bad in modernity," he told himself.

text: His mind was so preoccupied with these reflections that he stared at Mrs. Harrowdean’s overdue telegram for some time, unable to process a single word. It gradually dawned on him that he was duty-bound to visit her, but he found it easy to postpone his departure for the sake of a hockey match. Moreover, with a full moon expected, he felt that the silver glow of a summer night was a far more appropriate setting for the declarations he was meant to deliver than the hours of sunset and dinner. He returned to his internal theorizing about Germany, continuing his mental compositions right until the game officially commenced.
source_text: His brain was so busy with such stuff that for a time he stared at Mrs. Harrowdean's belated telegram without grasping the meaning of a word of it. He realised slowly that it was incumbent upon him to go over to her, but he postponed his departure very readily in order to play hockey. Besides which it would be a full moon, and he felt that summer moonlight was far better than sunset and dinner time for the declarations he was expected to make. And then he went on phrase-making again about Germany until he had actually bullied off at hockey.

text: He navigated the narrow lane by the dark dog-rose hedge, emerging onto the main road toward the village. Though several windows were illuminated, the streets were nearly deserted. In the distance, a cluster of lights glittered like rubies and topaz set against the silver shield of the night. The Flower Show remained in full swing, the late-hour discount having drawn in every remaining outsider.
source_text: He steered along the narrow road by the black dog-rose hedge, and so into the high road towards the village. The village was alight at several windows but almost deserted. Out beyond, a coruscation of lights burnt like a group of topaz and rubies set in the silver shield of the night. The festivities of the Flower Show were still in full progress, and the reduction of the entrance fee after seven had drawn in every lingering outsider. The roundabouts churned out their relentless music, and the bottle-shooting galleries popped and crashed. The well-patronised ostriches and motorcars flickered round in a pulsing rhythm; black, black, black, before the naphtha flares.

text: On that particular night, Mr. Britling’s spirit held space for little else but a fervent devotion to England. He cherished his country now as a true brotherhood of men. There would be no effortless triumph, and perhaps it was fortunate for a people so naturally complacent that the struggle would be arduous. Nevertheless, victory was the only path forward—the alternative was total annihilation.
source_text: There was little room in the heart of Mr. Britling that night for any love but the love of England. He loved England now as a nation of men. There could be no easy victory. Good for us with our too easy natures that there could be no easy victory. But victory we must have now--or perish....

text: A line of riders emerged from the gap between the cottages, followed by a grey wagon and a team hauling a heavy field gun. More horsemen came next, then another cannon. In the pale moonlight, the entire column appeared as a murky, brownish procession. An officer on horseback rode up to inspect him before returning to the intersection; at that time, England was not yet preoccupied with the threat of spies. Four more guns rolled past, trailed by a series of carts and more soldiers sitting rigidly in their saddles. No one sang or shouted; hardly a word was spoken, as the entire group moved with a sense of hushed, disciplined urgency. They eventually rumbled and clattered out of sight, leaving Mr. Britling alone in his car within the sleeping village. He restarted his engine and drove off, lost in thought.
source_text: Riding across the gap between the cottages was a string of horsemen, and then a grey cart, and then a team drawing a heavy object--a gun, and then more horsemen, and then a second gun. It was all a dim brown procession in the moonlight. A mounted officer came up beside him and looked at him and then went back to the cross-roads, but as yet England was not troubling about spies. Four more guns passed, and then a string of carts and more mounted men, sitting stiffly. Nobody was singing or shouting; scarcely a word was audible, and through all the column there was an effect of quiet efficient haste. And so they passed, and rumbled and jingled and clattered out of the scene, leaving Mr. Britling in his car in the dreaming village. He restarted his engine once more, and went his way thoughtfully.

text: The spirit of the road altered; the tangled enclosures vanished, as the columned pines and shadowed forests usurped the gnarled, hunched silhouettes of the hawthorn, the apple, and the oak. Dwellings became few and the world grew more and more desolate, until he might have imagined himself the sole wakeful figure abroad in all that vast and sleeping earth.
source_text: The character of the road changed; the hedges fell away, the pine trees and pine woods took the place of the black squat shapes of the hawthorn and oak and apple. The houses grew rarer and the world emptier and emptier, until he could have believed that he was the only man awake and out-of-doors in all the slumbering land....

text: He stated that he had come back out of a desperate need to write; the impulse was, in fact, quite pressing. He went directly to his chambers, turned on his lamp, prepared a cup of tea, and dressed in his nightclothes. The morning sun found him still laboring diligently over his pamphlet. He had decided to title the work: "And Now War Ends."
source_text: He explained he had returned because he wanted to write. He wanted indeed to write quite urgently. He went straight up to his room, lit his reading-lamp, made himself some tea, and changed into his nocturnal suit. Daylight found him still writing very earnestly at his pamphlet. The title he had chosen was: "And Now War Ends."

text: Arthur rose and dressed with a heavy, distracted deliberation. Upon reaching his suspenders, he paused, his hands going still; he pulled an atlas from the shelf to study the map of Northern France, lingering long over the Belgian frontier. He then consulted a nearby almanac, poring over the grim tallies of the great Continental legions. The sudden resonance of the breakfast chime was the only thing that broke his concentration.
source_text: He got out of bed and dressed in a slow, distraught manner. When he reached his braces he discontinued dressing for a time; he opened the atlas at Northern France, and stood musing over the Belgian border. Then he turned to Whitaker's Almanack to browse upon the statistics of the great European armies. He was roused from this by the breakfast gong.

text: Mrs. Arbuthnot grew visibly agitated as Mr. Sterling subjected her bundle of purchases to an overly meticulous inspection. "What earthly use is this stove-paste?" he demanded. She refused to acknowledge the question, sensing a deliberate attempt to dampen the spirit of her morning. She announced her immediate departure for the rectory. "Do not claim you were not apprised," she said. "I have a thousand tasks to attend to. There are the beans! I must direct the kitchen in the proper method of pickling the harvest. It is a fortunate year; the crop is truly endless. I called here simply to pass on the word." With this, she took her leave, clearly piqued by Mrs. Sterling’s listless apathy and Mr. Sterling’s relentless cynicism.
source_text: The lady was a little flurried by Mr. Britling's too detailed examination of her haul. "What good is blacking?" he asked. She would not hear him. She felt he was trying to spoil her morning. She declared she must get on back to her home. "Don't say I didn't warn you," she said. "I've got no end of things to do. There's peas! I want to show cook how to bottle our peas. For this year--it's lucky, we've got no end of peas. I came by here just for the sake of telling you." And with that she presently departed--obviously ruffled by Mrs. Britling's lethargy and Mr. Britling's scepticism.

text: Britain had not yet officially entered the conflict, but the heavens themselves seemed to be aligning toward that inevitable conclusion. It was as though a catastrophe of such immense proportions required a gradual onset. Mr. Britling harbored no uncertainty, even as a full-page advertisement in the *Daily News*—set in massive font by an anonymous sponsor—desperately urged the nation not to support "Russia the Terrible," the traditional bogeyman of emotional Radicals. The prevailing reports were broad and dramatic, though often lacking in accuracy. Claims circulated that German troops had invaded Belgium and the Netherlands, and that British ships were being impounded in the Kiel Canal. With a moratorium now in effect, the emerging stories of food hoarding proved that Mrs. Faber was just one of many highly-strung individuals reacting to the crisis.
source_text: England was not yet at war, but all the stars were marching to that end. It was as if an event so vast must needs take its time to happen. No doubt was left upon Mr. Britling's mind, though a whole-page advertisement in the _Daily News_, in enormous type and of mysterious origin, implored Great Britain not to play into the hands of Russia, Russia the Terrible, that bugbear of the sentimental Radicals. The news was wide and sweeping, and rather inaccurate. The Germans were said to be in Belgium and Holland, and they had seized English ships in the Kiel Canal. A moratorium had been proclaimed, and the reports of a food panic showed Mrs. Faber to be merely one example of a large class of excitable people.

text: Upon that particular theme, Mr. Thorne’s imagination flourished with extraordinary vigor. It had yielded a bountiful harvest of conjectures long before he crossed the threshold of his private room. He settled into his accustomed chair, yet the day’s labor remained neglected. He had unearthed a truth so transformative to his own existence that even the monumental crisis of the war was relegated, for a time, to the periphery of his concern.
source_text: On that text Mr. Britling's mind enlarged very rapidly. It produced a wonderful crop of possibilities before he got back to his study. He sat down to his desk, but he did not immediately take up his work. He had discovered something so revolutionary in his personal affairs that even the war issue remained for a time in suspense.

text: As long as it could only be addressed in fragments," Mr. Britling remarked. "And that is exactly where the immense potential of this conflict lies. Now, everything has become malleable. We have the chance to reshape the entire globe. Just a week ago, we were embroiled in bitter arguments over matters far too insignificant for human restlessness. Suddenly, we find ourselves at the dawn of a new era. This is a historic turning point. The world is now like clay, waiting for humanity to shape it as they see fit. This marks both the conclusion and the birth of an age. It is an event far more profound than the Reformation or the French Revolution... and we are living through it.
source_text: "So long as it could only be dealt with piecemeal," said Mr. Britling. "And that is just where the tremendous opportunity of this war comes in. Now everything becomes fluid. We can redraw the map of the world. A week ago we were all quarrelling bitterly about things too little for human impatience. Now suddenly we face an epoch. This is an epoch. The world is plastic for men to do what they will with it. This is the end and the beginning of an age. This is something far greater than the French Revolution or the Reformation.... And we live in it...."

text: The afternoon lingered, stretched thin by a climate of immense expectation. It was a day of singular obsession, the first in a grim lineage of shackled and ill-fated tomorrows. A conviction took hold that monumental upheavals were transpiring just beyond the reach of the senses, hidden behind the placid mask of the Kentish countryside. There was no refuge from this encroaching dread. It rendered every trivial pursuit fragmented and hollow. Sets of lawn tennis were attempted only to be severed mid-volley by the arrival of the late-day journals; any dialogue, no matter how far afield it began, inevitably gravitated back to the mobilization within the space of a few breaths.
source_text: That day of vast anticipations drew out into the afternoon. It was a day--obsessed. It was the precursor of a relentless series of doomed and fettered days. There was a sense of enormous occurrences going on just out of sound and sight--behind the mask of Essex peacefulness. From this there was no escape. It made all other interests fitful. Games of Badminton were begun and abruptly truncated by the arrival of the evening papers; conversations started upon any topic whatever returned to the war by the third and fourth remark....

text: A peculiar trait of the British temperament during the conflict was the stubborn tendency to regard the entire affair as a colossal farce. This inclination is woven through the vast majority of the nation’s contemporary letters and chronicles. Despite the carnage, the calculated malice, and the looming shadow of total ruin, that perspective endured. The national psyche simply declined to grant the Prussian offensive any sense of majesty or terror; they viewed the Kaiser and his heir not as formidable titans, but as mere caricatures. To the common mind, the adversary remained a frantic, witless soul, flushed with exertion, his eyes bulging with a desperate and manufactured ferocity. That he possessed a profound capacity for slaughter did nothing to diminish his fundamental absurdity. Even as the humor took on a macabre edge, it remained, at its core, a jest. The invader might reduce the world to a wasteland, yet he could never shake the islanders' conviction that he was merely playing the fool.
source_text: One remarkable aspect of the English attitude towards the war was the disposition to treat it as a monstrous joke. It is a disposition traceable in a vast proportion of the British literature of the time. In spite of violence, cruelty, injustice, and the vast destruction and still vaster dangers of the struggles, that disposition held. The English mind refused flatly to see anything magnificent or terrible in the German attack, or to regard the German Emperor or the Crown Prince as anything more than figures of fun. From first to last their conception of the enemy was an overstrenuous, foolish man, red with effort, with protruding eyes and a forced frightfulness of demeanour. That he might be tremendously lethal did not in the least obscure the fact that he was essentially ridiculous. And if as the war went on the joke grew grimmer, still it remained a joke. The German might make a desert of the world; that could not alter the British conviction that he was making a fool of himself.

text: This inclination remained evident as the afternoon progressed, surfacing in both offhand comments and intentional jokes. The younger boys had encountered the goose step, and the discovery filled them with a sense of wonder and amusement. They found it nearly impossible to believe that anyone would agree to move in such a preposterous manner. After experimenting with the gait themselves, they began a crusade to spread the practice. Letty and Cissie, who had stopped by the Dower House for tea and updates, were soon recruited to join Teddy and Hugh. The group of six moved across the lawn in exaggerated, scissor-like strides, laughing and swaying as they went. "Left," Hugh called out. "Left."
source_text: And this disposition kept coming to the surface throughout the afternoon, now in a casual allusion, now in some deliberate jest. The small boys had discovered the goose step, and it filled their little souls with amazement and delight. That human beings should consent to those ridiculous paces seemed to them almost incredibly funny. They tried it themselves, and then set out upon a goose-step propaganda. Letty and Cissie had come up to the Dower House for tea and news, and they were enrolled with Teddy and Hugh. The six of them, chuckling and swaying, marched, in vast scissor strides across the lawn. "Left," cried Hugh. "Left."

text: The initial thrusts were undoubtedly thwarted with considerable carnage, as were the more methodical onslaughts of the mid-August heat. Following these events, the reports from Namur dissolved into ambiguity, yet the English public clung to the conviction that the fortresses stood firm until the very hour the enemy entered the central metropolis. At the same time, the Republic’s armies were reclaiming their ancestral lands, moving into the border towns of the east; the Imperial hosts were surging across the northern frontiers; the *Monarch*, the *Siren*, and the *Thunderer* had been consigned to the deep by the press in a fictitious Mediterranean clash, and the African colonies were swiftly overtaken. The true ferocity and scale of the advance through the low countries remained hidden from the common view, permitting Mr. Thorne to persist in his worry that the struggle would conclude with undue haste, before its supposed virtues could be realized. Yet his fears were entirely groundless; the trials the war intended for Mr. Thorne were far more savage than anything he could envision, even in his most grandiose flights of fancy.
source_text: The first raiding attack was certainly repulsed with heavy losses, and so were the more systematic assaults on August the sixth and seventh. After that the news from Liège became uncertain, but it was believed in England that some or all of the forts were still holding out right up to the German entry into Brussels. Meanwhile the French were pushing into their lost provinces, occupying Altkirch, Mulhausen and Saarburg; the Russians were invading Bukovina and East Prussia; the _Goeben_, the _Breslau_ and the _Panther_ had been sunk by the newspapers in an imaginary battle in the Mediterranean, and Togoland was captured by the French and British. Neither the force nor the magnitude of the German attack through Belgium was appreciated by the general mind, and it was possible for Mr. Britling to reiterate his fear that the war would be over too soon, long before the full measure of its possible benefits could be secured. But these apprehensions were unfounded; the lessons the war had in store for Mr. Britling were far more drastic than anything he was yet able to imagine even in his most exalted moods.

text: I tell you, I never truly understood that war was a reality until I was caught in the middle of it. In America, we have no real concept of it. It is like the plague or a great famine—something found only in history books. We have lost the sense that it is something that actually happens. Only a handful of grandfathers, men like Judge Holmes and his wise contemporaries, ever speak of it. Otherwise, it is merely a game for children to play at. And then, quite suddenly, the streets are filled with people consumed by hatred and malice. You see respectable old gentlemen with white moustaches and decent fathers of families plotting to burn houses, to kill, and to spread terror. Even the pleasant young women are on the lookout for an Englishman to spit upon; I have been within their range and felt quite shaken more than once. The hardest thing to grasp is that they are actually committing these acts. Consider the small village of Visé near the Dutch border; some old man there supposedly fired a hunting rifle, and in response, they wiped the place off the map. They shot the inhabitants by the dozen, lining them up three deep to be executed, and then burned the town to the ground. Short of scalping, the most savage tribes could not have been more brutal. And these were supposedly disciplined German soldiers.
source_text: "I tell you I never knew there was such a thing as war until this happened to me. In America we don't know there is such a thing. It's like pestilence and famine; something in the story books. We've forgotten it for anything real. There's just a few grandfathers go around talking about it. Judge Holmes and sage old fellows like him. Otherwise it's just a game the kids play at.... And then suddenly here's everybody running about in the streets--hating and threatening--and nice old gentlemen with white moustaches and fathers of families scheming and planning to burn houses and kill and hurt and terrify. And nice young women, too, looking for an Englishman to spit at; I tell you I've been within range and very uncomfortable several times.... And what one can't believe is that they are really doing these things. There's a little village called Visé near the Dutch frontier; some old chap got fooling there with a fowling-piece; and they've wiped it out. Shot the people by the dozen, put them out in rows three deep and shot them, and burnt the place. Short of scalping, Red Indians couldn't have done worse. Respectable German soldiers....

text: Hardly a soul in Britain seems to harbor any inkling of the nightmare unfolding across the Channel. One hears the accounts—they circulate in the Dutch borderlands—and they leave one reeling. The invaders have embarked upon a campaign to systematically terrorize the populace; they have embraced a philosophy of cold, calculated horror. You cannot conceive of it... Truly... Violations. Cruelties of a nature that would stun a New Yorker. Things one simply does not mention... Well... Defilement. They have been despoiling women as a method of discipline, out in the open on the very merchant stalls of Louvain. It is an absolute fact. I had it from a gentleman who fled the region himself; he knew the families, he knew the terrain, he saw the wreckage. Our neighbors here fail to recognize that those victims are the same flesh and blood as the women in York or Hastings, or even here in Little Waltham for that matter. There remains this stubborn belief that the women of the Continent are of a separate, more resilient cast—better suited to endure such savagery. They cling to the notion that some celestial decree renders the English immune to such calamity. And yet, it is occurring not sixty leagues from where we stand. As far as I can tell, there is little enough to stop that shadow from stretching toward our own threshold.
source_text: "No one in England really seems to have any suspicion what is going on in Belgium. You hear stories--People tell them in Holland. It takes your breath away. They have set out just to cow those Belgians. They have started in to be deliberately frightful. You do not begin to understand.... Well.... Outrages. The sort of outrages Americans have never heard of. That one doesn't speak of.... Well.... Rape.... They have been raping women for disciplinary purposes on tables in the market-place of Liège. Yes, sir. It's a fact. I was told it by a man who had just come out of Belgium. Knew the people, knew the place, knew everything. People over here do not seem to realise that those women are the same sort of women that you might find in Chester or Yarmouth, or in Matching's Easy for the matter of that. They still seem to think that Continental women are a different sort of women--more amenable to that sort of treatment. They seem to think there is some special Providential law against such things happening to English people. And it's within two hundred miles of you--even now. And as far as I can see there's precious little to prevent it coming nearer...."

text: They are as certain of triumph as if London had already fallen. Their hearts are set on the capital. They are utterly convinced they will march through Belgium and occupy Paris by Sedan Day, using their Zeppelins and submarines to ruin your fleet before striking across the Channel. They claim England is the true prize of this Belgian incursion, and that France will be crushed merely in passing. They even brag of possessing artillery that can shell Dover from Calais. They are positive you cannot equip your own soldiers, certain that you cannot manufacture ten thousand rifles in a week. They will speak to any stranger on a train, detailing exactly how your downfall will be orchestrated. They discuss the matter with the cold detachment of men planning to round up cattle.
source_text: "And they're as sure of winning as if they had got London now. They mean to get London. They're cocksure they are going to walk through Belgium, cocksure they will get to Paris by Sedan day, and then they are going to destroy your fleet with Zeppelins and submarines and make a dash across the Channel. They say it's England they are after, in this invasion of Belgium. They'll just down France by the way. They say they've got guns to bombard Dover from Calais. They make a boast of it. They know for certain you can't arm your troops. They know you can't turn out ten thousand rifles a week. They come and talk to any one in the trains, and explain just how your defeat is going to be managed. It's just as though they were talking of rounding up cattle."

text: I always imagined war," Mr. Direck said, "as an event where the majority just watched and cheered while a specialized squad did the actual combat. But Germany isn't playing that game. I’ll admit, I’m terrified. This is the greatest conflict ever recorded; it’s the absolute peak of warfare. Last night, I dreamed of a grey deluge sweeping everything away before it. You, me, and Miss Corner—it's strange, isn't it, that she was there too?—were all struggling up a slope, climbing higher and higher as that tide chased us. It seemed to surge into a spray of human faces, steel helmets, and bayonets—with reaching hands—and blood. Listen, Mr. Britling, I know I'm a bit on edge, but I can tell you that you haven't even begun to understand the sheer force you've collided with here in England.
source_text: "I thought war," said Mr. Direck, "was a thing when most people stood about and did the shouting, and a sort of special team did the fighting. Well, Germany isn't fighting like that.... I confess it, I'm scared.... It's the very biggest thing on record; it's the very limit in wars.... I dreamt last night of a grey flood washing everything in front of it. You and me--and Miss Corner--curious thing, isn't it? that she came into it--were scrambling up a hill higher and higher, with that flood pouring after us. Sort of splashing into a foam of faces and helmets and bayonets--and clutching hands--and red stuff.... Well, Mr. Britling, I admit I'm a little bit overwrought about it, but I can assure you you don't begin to realise in England what it is you've butted against...."

text: Mr. Direck insisted he had no desire to dwell on unpleasantries, but he felt this was a reality the English people were forced to confront. He felt a natural kinship with them, as if he were truly one of their own. He had rushed back to them—it felt exactly like returning home—to warn them of the monumental force that was about to strike. He saw himself as a man standing before an approaching deluge, a vast and leaden flood. He could no longer suppress the weight of his thoughts. "Where is our army?" Letty suddenly asked.
source_text: Mr. Direck didn't, he declared, want to make disagreeable talk, but this was a thing people in England had to face. He felt like one of them himself--"naturally." He'd sort of hurried home to them--it was just like hurrying home--to tell them of the tremendous thing that was going to hit them. He felt like a man in front of a flood, a great grey flood. He couldn't hide what he had been thinking. "Where's our army?" asked Letty suddenly.

text: Then she began to speak fervently about the virtues of tranquility. She had never appreciated his exquisite "Silent Places" as deeply as she did in that moment. She felt a profound longing to retreat into such a dreamscape, seeking shelter from the brutality, betrayal, and mindless gossip of the world. The weight of reality had become unbearable. She wanted to vanish into a private sanctuary and quietly tend her own garden, just as Voltaire had once suggested. At night, she was sometimes too terrified even to undress. Her mind was filled with the imagined roar of artillery, the threat of coastal invasions, and the sight of ghoulish, masked scouts on motorcycles racing through the countryside.
source_text: Then she broke away into the praises of serenity. Never had she thought so much of his beautiful "Silent Places" as she did now. How she longed to take refuge in some such dreamland from violence and treachery and foolish rumours! She was weary of every reality. She wanted to fly away into some secret hiding-place and cultivate her simple garden there--as Voltaire had done.... Sometimes at night she was afraid to undress. She imagined the sound of guns, she imagined landings and frightful scouts "in masks" rushing inland on motor bicycles....

text:
Two primary impulses now governed the consciousness of Mr. Halloway. The first was a grand, almost ceremonial ambition: the desire to claim his portion of the struggle, to abandon the rhythmic comforts of The Priory and step into the fray. Precisely where he would tread or what labor he would perform remained indistinct. His mind’s eye conjured the image of a civilian partisan, clad in some practical yet unofficial attire, harrying a foreign invader from the shelter of a thicket. This ensemble would likely have mirrored the tweed-and-gaiter arrangement in which he had received Mr. Vance. Crucially, it would feature an armlet. Or perhaps he saw himself at a switchboard or a desk, executing some vital bureaucratic task where wit surpassed drill. Again, with an armlet. Weeks prior, the term "armlet" might have sounded obscure; now it stood as the essential emblem of the nation’s mobilization. 

He boarded the dawn express to London on Monday, determined to enlist in whatever capacity was available; to secure, as it were, his own badge of service without delay. The journals he purchased on the platform tempered his fear of Paris’s imminent collapse with a flicker of optimism, yet his purpose remained firm. The reports of relentless retreat and shattered lines had vanished. The German right wing was meeting a counter-offensive, potentially caught in a vice between the capital and the eastern fortresses, with the British pressing the flank. Though this news lightened his spirit, it did not lessen his grasp of the conflict’s staggering weight. Even with the enemy checked, the labor of driving them home would demand every soul. The struggle was a leviathan that would leave no life untouched. It required a total surrender of the self. He felt he must offer himself up entirely, allowing no hesitation to cloud that clarity. To remain idle now seemed a profound disgrace, a failure to defend the culture and security he had enjoyed against these disciplined, driven millions.
source_text: There were now two chief things in the mind of Mr. Britling. One was a large and valiant thing, a thing of heroic and processional quality, the idea of taking up one's share in the great conflict, of leaving the Dower House and its circle of habits and activities and going out--. From that point he wasn't quite sure where he was to go, nor exactly what he meant to do. His imagination inclined to the figure of a volunteer in an improvised uniform inflicting great damage upon a raiding invader from behind a hedge. The uniform, one presumes, would have been something in the vein of the costume in which he met Mr. Direck. With a "brassard." Or he thought of himself as working at a telephone or in an office engaged upon any useful quasi-administrative work that called for intelligence rather than training. Still, of course, with a "brassard." A month ago he would have had doubts about the meaning of "brassard"; now it seemed to be the very keyword for national organisation. He had started for London by the early train on Monday morning with the intention of immediate enrolment in any such service that offered; of getting, in fact, into his brassard at once. The morning papers he bought at the station dashed his conviction of the inevitable fall of Paris into hopeful doubts, but did not shake his resolution. The effect of rout and pursuit and retreat and retreat and retreat had disappeared from the news. The German right was being counter-attacked, and seemed in danger of getting pinched between Paris and Verdun with the British on its flank. This relieved his mind, but it did nothing to modify his new realisation of the tremendous gravity of the war. Even if the enemy were held and repulsed a little there was still work for every man in the task of forcing them back upon their own country. This war was an immense thing, it would touch everybody.... That meant that every man must give himself. That he had to give himself. He must let nothing stand between him and that clear understanding. It was utterly shameful now to hold back and not to do one's utmost for civilisation, for England, for all the ease and safety one had been given--against these drilled, commanded, obsessed millions.

text: Each soldier killed, many of those wounded, and almost every prisoner taken," he noted, "results in the loss of a firearm. We have already seen twenty-five thousand rifles vanish since the start of the war. In addition to the burden of equipping new regiments, we must replace those missing weapons for the reinforcements we send to the front. Are you aware of the current maximum weekly output of rifles in this country?
source_text: "Every dead man, many wounded men, most prisoners," he said, "mean each one a rifle lost. We have lost five-and-twenty thousand rifles alone since the war began. Quite apart from arming new troops we have to replace those rifles with the drafts we send out. Do you know what is the maximum weekly output of rifles at the present time in this country?"

text: Mr. Britling stepped out of his club around three-thirty with his thoughts in a tangle. His private vow to act quickly on his country’s behalf had been dampened by the baffling question of how to proceed. He continued to search for openings and avenues where none were to be found, overwhelmed by a mounting feeling of futility.
source_text: Mr. Britling emerged from his club about half-past three with his mind rather dishevelled and with his private determination to do something promptly for his country's needs blunted by a perplexing "How?" His search for doors and ways where no doors and ways existed went on with a gathering sense of futility.

text: Carmine’s expression betrayed none of the enthusiasm or nationalistic pride that Mr. Britling anticipated. Instead, he looked pale and drained, bearing the weariness of someone who hadn't rested in days. "You see," he said, offering a nearly sheepish explanation for the three stars on his cuff, "I previously served as a captain in the volunteers." He had been assigned to lead a reactivated volunteer unit responsible for guarding the bridges, utilities, manufacturing plants, and rail tunnels, along with various other essential but mundane tasks around Easinghampton. "I have to close down my home," Captain Carmine remarked, "and move into temporary rooms. I’ll admit, I detest the idea... Still, it surely won't go on for more than six months... But it’s miserable... Truly awful..."
source_text: But Carmine's face showed nothing of the excitement and patriotic satisfaction that would have seemed natural to Mr. Britling. He was white and jaded, as if he had not slept for many nights. "You see," he explained almost apologetically of the three stars upon his sleeve, "I used to be a captain of volunteers." He had been put in charge of a volunteer force which had been re-embodied and entrusted with the care of the bridges, gasworks, factories and railway tunnels, and with a number of other minor but necessary duties round about Easinghampton. "I've just got to shut up my house," said Captain Carmine, "and go into lodgings. I confess I hate it.... But anyhow it can't last six months.... But it's beastly.... Ugh!..."

text: Mr. Sterling reflected deeply on the insular nature of the nation’s military and administrative machinery. To his mind, this provided the essential explanation for every dissonance currently marring the metropolitan scene. The armed forces had long been a sequestered entity, forged for a narrow utility. It had acquired the rigid features of an exclusive guild, maintaining exacting standards within its own sphere while remaining profoundly inflexible. This seclusion was not a calculated affectation so much as a natural result of its specialized role. It engaged with the broader social fabric only through a few similarly distinct channels: the Court, the Cathedral, and the Playhouse. Beyond these narrow apertures, it viewed the sprawling civilian world as a misty, indifferent, and potentially hostile landscape—a place that merely spawned meddling politicians and withheld necessary funds—which it sought to either ignore with contempt or circumvent with craft. As soon as one perceived the profound distance between the military life and the realities of commerce or labor, and understood that the average citizen was a mere "layman" to the official mind, the utter incapacity of the Ministry to direct a massive national struggle became obvious. Those in power lacked any grasp of the fundamental rhythms of the country; they were oblivious to the origins of goods, operating on the assumption that anything required could be simply procured from a storefront. This was the inevitable perspective of a professional cadre under a government of cliques. No other outcome was possible. But now, through some urgent necessity, the populace had to seize control of the state it had for too long allowed to drift.
source_text: Mr. Britling dwelt upon this idea of the specialised character of the British army and navy and government. It seemed to him to be the clue to everything that was jarring in the London spectacle. The army had been a thing aloof, for a special end. It had developed all the characteristics of a caste. It had very high standards along the lines of its specialisation, but it was inadaptable and conservative. Its exclusiveness was not so much a deliberate culture as a consequence of its detached function. It touched the ordinary social body chiefly through three other specialised bodies, the court, the church, and the stage. Apart from that it saw the great unofficial civilian world as something vague, something unsympathetic, something possibly antagonistic, which it comforted itself by snubbing when it dared and tricking when it could, something that projected members of Parliament towards it and was stingy about money. Directly one grasped how apart the army lived from the ordinary life of the community, from industrialism or from economic necessities, directly one understood that the great mass of Englishmen were simply "outsiders" to the War Office mind, just as they were "outsiders" to the political clique, one began to realise the complete unfitness of either government or War Office for the conduct of so great a national effort as was now needed. These people "up there" did not know anything of the broad mass of English life at all, they did not know how or where things were made; when they wanted things they just went to a shop somewhere and got them. This was the necessary psychology of a small army under a clique government. Nothing else was to be expected. But now--somehow--the nation had to take hold of the government that it had neglected so long....

text: Naturally," Mr. Britling remarked, "none of this will be accomplished without a struggle. We cannot expect to reclaim a nation we’ve ignored for so long without experiencing significant domestic tension. Yet, here in England, we are capable of making these transitions without resorting to an uprising. That is where our true power lies...
source_text: "Of course," said Mr. Britling, "these things are not going to be done without a conflict. We aren't going to take hold of our country which we have neglected so long without a lot of internal friction. But in England we can make these readjustments without revolution. It is our strength....

text: It seems this event should have heralded its arrival with a clarion blast. Yet, until this moment, it has merely been a distant clamor, a disturbance noted as if from an adjacent residence. Now, the kingdom gradually stirs. The metropolis resembles a confused dreamer emerging from heavy rest to find conflagration and threat, upheaval and frantic appeals for assistance, right at the doorstep. The avenues convey precisely this mood. Citizens are watchful and attentive. One senses that at any second, during a hush or a lull, there might arrive from the far horizon, drifting over the chimney pots, the dim resonance of cannons or the faint, pained wails of small French or Belgian townships in their hour of torment.
source_text: "One feels the thing ought to have come upon us like the sound of a trumpet. Instead, until now, it has been like a great noise, that we just listened to, in the next house.... And now slowly the nation awakes. London is just like a dazed sleeper waking up out of a deep sleep to fire and danger, tumult and cries for help, near at hand. The streets give you exactly that effect. People are looking about and listening. One feels that at any moment, in a pause, in a silence, there may come, from far away, over the houses, faint and little, the boom of guns or the small outcries of little French or Belgian villages in agony...."

text: They began to deliberate over the necessary finances; a loan might be required for Teddy’s equipment, should his commission be granted. There were several other minor costs to settle as well. Mr. Britling pushed away a foolish notion that he was essentially funding Teddy’s journey into a reality that neither the young man nor Letty fully comprehended.
source_text: They fell to discussing ways and means; there might have to be a loan for Teddy's outfit, if he did presently secure a commission. And there were one or two other little matters.... Mr. Britling dismissed a ridiculous fancy that he was paying to send Teddy away to something that neither that young man nor Letty understood properly....

text: One April night, he was puzzled by a disturbance among the pheasants and the sound of dogs barking in the distance. To his total surprise, he then heard a faint crackling like far-off fireworks and spotted a ghostly, yellowish shape—resembling a fountain pen—streaking across the eastern sky, caught intermittently in the beam of a flickering searchlight. After rubbing his eyes and staring again, he realized he was looking at a Zeppelin, flying over Essex on its way toward London.
source_text: And one night in April he was perplexed by a commotion among the pheasants and a barking of distant dogs, and then to his great astonishment he heard noises like a distant firework display and saw something like a phantom yellowish fountain-pen in the sky far away to the east lit intermittently by a quivering search-light and going very swiftly. And after he had rubbed his eyes and looked again, he realised that he was looking at a Zeppelin--a Zeppelin flying Londonward over Essex.

text:
Mr. Van der Pant had frequently taken aim at the Zeppelins. Whenever the airships loomed over Antwerp, the residents would gather on their rooftops to fire at them. He held the vessels in complete contempt, using mocking gestures to convey his disdain for their presence. They were powerless unless they flew low, he insisted, and if they did, they became easy targets.
