He described one that had descended only to be riddled with gunfire; it had been forced to jettison its entire payload—which fortunately struck an empty field—simply to make a crippled escape. He dismissed the reports in the English press as utter nonsense; not a single Zeppelin had participated in the final bombardment. 

As he recounted these events, the Britlings listened, following as best they could and offering questions in a fractured Anglo-French. Sitting before them was a man who, just days prior, had been cycling through the streets of Antwerp, steering around shell craters, pools of blood, and the severed limbs of women. He had watched houses go up in flames from incendiary strikes and had once been blown off his bicycle by the concussive blast of a shell. These nightmares were not merely part of the same world; they were present, sharing a meal at their very table.
source_text: Mr. Van der Pant had had shots at various Zeppelins. When the Zeppelins came to Antwerp everybody turned out on the roofs and shot at them. He was contemptuous of Zeppelins. He made derisive gestures to express his opinion of them. They could do nothing unless they came low, and if they came low you could hit them. One which ventured down had been riddled; it had had to drop all its bombs--luckily they fell in an open field--in order to make its lame escape. It was all nonsense to say, as the English papers did, that they took part in the final bombardment. Not a Zeppelin.... So he talked, and the Britling family listened and understood as much as they could, and replied and questioned in Anglo-French. Here was a man who but a few days ago had been steering his bicycle in the streets of Antwerp to avoid shell craters, pools of blood, and the torn-off arms and shoulder-blades of women. He had seen houses flaring, set afire by incendiary bombs, and once at a corner he had been knocked off his bicycle by the pouff of a bursting shell.... Not only were these things in the same world with us, they were sitting at our table.

text: He recounted a harrowing tale of a woman tethered to her bed by infirmity, and of her husband, who had stepped onto the terrace to watch the silver airship. The sky groaned with the thunder of the batteries. At intervals, he would lean back into the room to describe the spectacle, but eventually, his voice failed and he looked in no more. She called his name, then called again, her heart quickening. With a desperate, agonizing effort, she hoisted herself up to peer through the window. At first, the image refused to make sense. He was slumped over the railing, his head canted at a horrific, impossible angle. It was shattered and unrecognizable. He had been struck by a jagged shard of metal launched from the city’s own defenses.
source_text: He told one grim story of an invalid woman unable to move, lying in bed in her _appartement_, and of how her husband went out on the balcony to look at the Zeppelin. There was a great noise of shooting. Ever and again he would put his head back into the room and tell her things, and then after a time he was silent and looked in no more. She called to him, and called again. Becoming frightened, she raised herself by a great effort and peered through the glass. At first she was too puzzled to understand what had happened. He was hanging over the front of the balcony, with his head twisted oddly. Twisted and shattered. He had been killed by shrapnel fired from the outer fortifications....

text: Mr. Britling stalled for time, promising to "investigate the matter," and thus managed to delay Mr. Van der Pant for two days. Eventually, he resolved to travel to London with him to conduct his search firsthand. While the trip failed to reunite Mr. Van der Pant with his family, it led Mr. Britling to recognize the deep validity of a remark by Herr Heinrich—one he had once dismissed as insignificant, yet which had remained lodged in his mind. "The English," Herr Heinrich had observed, "lack a grasp of indexing, which is the very foundation of all efficient organization."
source_text: Mr. Britling temporised by saying he would "make inquiries," and put Mr. Van der Pant off for two days. Then he decided to go up to London with him and "make inquiries on the spot." Mr. Van der Pant did not discover his family, but Mr. Britling discovered the profound truth of a comment of Herr Heinrich's which he had hitherto considered utterly trivial, but which had nevertheless stuck in his memory. "The English," Herr Heinrich had said, "do not understanding indexing. It is the root of all good organisation."

text: Observe Madame. Her very nature is cast in a mold entirely distinct from the women of our own circle. Consider modern pursuits—tennis, cycling, mixed schooling, the latest dances, or university degrees—and then try to reconcile them with her presence. It would be as jarring as imagining a nun clad in equestrian trousers. She is a creature of focused femininity, a specialist in the art of being a woman whose true domain remains the domestic hearth. Her style is one of flowing, elegant skirts and deliberate grace; her face seems shielded, for no Eastern silk could provide a more impenetrable screen than her serene Catholic composure. Catholicism has devised its own invisible purdah. She shares a closer bond with that charming Indian woman in her exquisite silks—the one Carmine introduced alongside her towering husband last year—than she does with girls like Letty or Cissie. That lady also attempted a game of hockey, and her performance was quite similar to the way Madame Van der Pant approached the sport.
source_text: "Look at Madame. She's built on a fundamentally different plan from any of our womenkind here. Tennis, the bicycle, co-education, the two-step, the higher education of women.... Say these things over to yourself, and think of her. It's like talking of a nun in riding breeches. She's a specialised woman, specialising in womanhood, her sphere is the home. Soft, trailing, draping skirts, slow movements, a veiled face; for no Oriental veil could be more effectual than her beautiful Catholic quiet. Catholicism invented the invisible purdah. She is far more akin to that sweet little Indian lady with the wonderful robes whom Carmine brought over with her tall husband last summer, than she is to Letty or Cissie. She, too, undertook to play hockey. And played it very much as Madame Van der Pant played it....

text: And," Mr. Britling continued, adopting the exact cadence a clergyman might use for his sixth sermon point, "it is exactly this Northern inclination that this global upheaval is set to liberate. This conflict is battering its way through Europe, shattering domestic lives, scattering and mingling households, leading Madame Van der Pant to take up hockey and sending André to scale trees with my young hoodlums; it is annihilating young men by the millions, warping the ratio of the sexes for an entire generation, drawing women into the spheres of trade and industry, liquidating the generational wealth that sustained so many in their elegant lethargy, and saturating the earth with peculiar misgivings and radical thoughts.
source_text: "And," said Mr. Britling, in very much the tones in which a preacher might say "Sixthly," "it is just all this Northern tendency that this world struggle is going to release. This war is pounding through Europe, smashing up homes, dispersing and mixing homes, setting Madame Van der Pant playing hockey, and André climbing trees with my young ruffians; it is killing young men by the million, altering the proportions of the sexes for a generation, bringing women into business and office and industry, destroying the accumulated wealth that kept so many of them in refined idleness, flooding the world with strange doubts and novel ideas...."

text: Unlike his wife, whose traditional Catholic background made her exceptionally diplomatic, Mr. Van der Pant lacked any sense of subtlety. He made it abundantly clear that he found the British postal and telegraph systems sluggish and disorganized, while he viewed the management of the Great Eastern railway lines as both wasteful and incompetent. He remarked that the laborers in the fields and the workmen he observed near the junction moved with a lethargy and lack of focus unlike anything he had ever encountered. It baffled him that Mr. Britling still lit his home with acetylene instead of electricity. He felt the price of fresh eggs was absurdly high, and his assessment of the local pig-farming was scathing. The roads, he complained, were not proper routes but a confusing maze; he even drew mocking maps on the tablecloth with his finger to show the absurdity of the lanes around the Dower House. He was stunned by the lack of a café in Matching’s Easy, and he dismissed the local "public house"—which he had visited with high expectations—as no true public space at all, but rather a dismal spot for solitary drinking. These were all complaints Mr. Britling might have made himself, yet coming from a Belgian refugee, he found them absolutely galling.
source_text: Mr. Van der Pant had not the extreme tactfulness of his so typically Catholic wife; he made it only too plain that he thought the British postal and telegraph service slow and slack, and the management of the Great Eastern branch lines wasteful and inefficient. He said the workmen in the fields and the workmen he saw upon some cottages near the junction worked slowlier and with less interest than he had ever seen any workman display in all his life before. He marvelled that Mr. Britling lit his house with acetylene and not electric light. He thought fresh eggs were insanely dear, and his opinion of Matching's Easy pig-keeping was uncomplimentary. The roads, he said, were not a means of getting from place to place, they were a _dédale_; he drew derisive maps with his finger on the table-cloth of the lane system about the Dower House. He was astonished that there was no Café in Matching's Easy; he declared that the "public house" to which he went with considerable expectation was no public house at all; it was just a sly place for drinking beer.... All these were things Mr. Britling might have remarked himself; from a Belgian refugee he found them intolerable.

text: They claimed the "angler" had snagged them using a snare made of hair. They brought forward the man himself, clearly beaming with pride at his skill. He described how he had mastered this particular technique during his time as a stoker in New York Harbor. The man placed such absolute trust in Mr. Britling that the gentleman felt like an accomplice to his crime—a significant breach of the laws and social norms from before the war. It was undeniable that these trout were the same ones Mr. Pumshock, the stockbroker who fancied himself a country gentleman, had so diligently protected in the waters of the Easy. Previously, the local community had been obliged to view Pumshock’s fish with a reverence that was nearly religious. Only a year ago, young Snooker had spent a month in prison for poaching just one of them. Now, however, the old rules no longer applied.
source_text: The "fisherman," they said, had got them with a hair noose. They produced the fisherman, of whom they were manifestly proud. It was, he explained, a method of fishing he had learnt when in New York Harbour. He had been a stoker. He displayed a confidence in Mr. Britling that made that gentleman an accessory after his offence, his very serious offence against pre-war laws and customs. It was plain that the trout were the trout that Mr. Pumshock, the stock-broker and amateur gentleman, had preserved so carefully in the Easy. Hitherto the countryside had been forced to regard Mr. Pumshock's trout with an almost superstitious respect. A year ago young Snooker had done a month for one of those very trout. But now things were different.

text: The first realizations of this shift reached him through accounts of the Kaiser’s demeanor and the hostility of the Berlin populace when war was declared, as well as the rough handling of British subjects trying to return home. Across the country, these travelers were subjected to insults and abuse—the sudden eruption of a long-simmering resentment. While the British ambassador was being hounded out of Berlin by a jeering mob, the German representative in England enjoyed a final, quiet walk through St. James’s Park, completely unbothered. One particular detail seized Mr. Britling’s imagination: the story of a group of young women who had gathered on a railway platform. As the British ambassador’s train passed by, they sang "Deutschland, Deutschland über Alles" to his drawn blinds. Mr. Britling could picture them vividly—likely dressed in white, with radiant faces and bright eyes, lifting their voices in that powerful, unrestrained manner characteristic of modern Germany.
source_text: The first intimations of this that struck upon his mind were the news of the behaviour of the Kaiser and the Berlin crowd upon the declaration of war, and the violent treatment of the British subjects seeking to return to their homes. Everywhere such people had been insulted and ill-treated. It was the spontaneous expression of a long-gathered bitterness. While the British ambassador was being howled out of Berlin, the German ambassador to England was taking a farewell stroll, quite unmolested, in St. James's Park.... One item that struck particularly upon Mr. Britling's imagination was the story of the chorus of young women who assembled on the railway platform of the station through which the British ambassador was passing to sing--to his drawn blinds--"Deutschland, Deutschland über Alles." Mr. Britling could imagine those young people, probably dressed more or less uniformly in white, with flushed faces and shining eyes, letting their voices go, full throated, in the modern German way....

text: Mr. Thorne was but one of a vast throng who desired to map the contours of the German mind in that season. The labor to fathom a society that had grown so suddenly incomprehensible became a singular phenomenon of the English intellect during the early months of the struggle. The national mood was defined by a profound and absolute amazement. Every German text that offered a glimpse into the source of this concentrated malice saw unprecedented circulation; the philosophies of Clausewitz, Treitschke, and Nietzsche provided the foundation for a thousand essays and tireless debates. One witnessed weary clerks on their daily commutes and laborers heading home from the docks poring over these dense, peculiar volumes. Like Mr. Thorne, they were all inquiring into the nature of the force that had collided with their world. They were attempting to explain the sudden, violent tempest of enmity that was sweeping over them from the center of Europe.
source_text: Mr. Britling was only one of a multitude who wanted to feel the bumps of Germany at that time. The effort to understand a people who had suddenly become incredible was indeed one of the most remarkable facts in English intellectual life during the opening phases of the war. The English state of mind was unlimited astonishment. There was an enormous sale of any German books that seemed likely to illuminate the mystery of this amazing concentration of hostility; the works of Bernhardi, Treitschke, Nietzsche, Houston Stewart Chamberlain, became the material of countless articles and interminable discussions. One saw little clerks on the way to the office and workmen going home after their work earnestly reading these remarkable writers. They were asking, just as Mr. Britling was asking, what it was the British Empire had struck against. They were trying to account for this wild storm of hostility that was coming at them out of Central Europe.

text: Melodies and moonbeams blended with an earnest German sensibility and robust singing. There was a grand picnic in a Stuhl Wagen, and the boys played disciplined circle games with the German children, while Hugh shared long walks and private thoughts with Kurt and Karl. The festivities reached a peak with a spirited dance, accompanied by Romani musicians found by Mr. Britling. During the revelry, the Germans demonstrated amusing contests involving baskets, potatoes, and forfeits, while the English introduced their hosts to the free-spirited two-step. The company sang "Britannia, Rule the Waves" and "Deutschland, Deutschland über Alles" in unison. Adam Meyer climbed onto a chair to deliver a grand oration in a dialect more pronounced than ever, as everyone enjoyed beer and syrups in the moonlight beneath the linden tree.
source_text: Music, moonlight, the simple German sentiment, the hearty German voices, the great picnic in a Stuhl Wagen, the orderly round games the boys played with the German children, and the tramps and confidences Hugh had with Kurt and Karl, and at last a crowning jollification, a dance, with some gipsy musicians whom Mr. Britling discovered, when the Germans taught the English various entertaining sports with baskets and potatoes and forfeits and the English introduced the Germans to the licence of the two-step. And everybody sang "Britannia, Rule the Waves," and "Deutschland, Deutschland über Alles," and Adam Meyer got on a chair and made a tremendous speech more in dialect than ever, and there was much drinking of beer and sirops in the moonlight under the linden....

text: Truly delightful people in a land filled with light! Yet his mind was haunted by the persistent shadow of the Visé murders and the massacres at Dinant and Louvain. It was a bloody, horrific violence inflicted on a blameless population, unjustly invaded and brutally handled—slaughter carried out with a sickening veneer of moral justification and racial arrogance.
source_text: Right pleasant people in a sunny land! Yet here pressing relentlessly upon his mind were the murders of Visé, the massacres of Dinant, the massacres of Louvain, murder red-handed and horrible upon an inoffensive people, foully invaded, foully treated; murder done with a sickening cant of righteousness and racial pretension....

text: For too long, the British character had been hollowed out by myths: a feigned devotion to a distant, unmoving monarchy, a hollow religiosity within the state church, and a charade of liberty in a government controlled by party hacks. Economic justice was a farce in a system where the promoter, the exploiter, and the loan shark held every advantage over the laborer and the craftsman; such a society could not long sustain a truly heroic fervor. It had purchased its ease at the cost of its people’s integrity. Every institution was tainted, the national soul stifled by a mountain of pretenses. Yet, when finally confronted by a ruthless and formidable foe—an adversary characterized by an unprecedented level of social organization and scientific precision—the nation seemed briefly galvanized by a rare spirit of purpose. The common folk and the young were radiant. Men of every rank marched away to perish, fueled by a magnificent vision of the conflict. The old lethargy retreated from view. But as that first wave of passion ebbed, the complacency returned. Soon, the elder statesmen, the merchants, the socialites, and the idle wealthy began to shake off their brief moral awakening. The familiar patterns of thought and conduct resumed. The war, which had opened with such theatrical force, failed to reach a resolution; there was no swift, glorious triumph and no sudden, noble collapse. Instead came the agony of stalemate, the harshest trial for a people lacking discipline. Long stretches of monotonous exhaustion set in. Even before the struggle at the Yser reached its peak, the war’s sense of narrative had dissolved. It was no longer a story of victory or defeat, but a colossal burden of attrition. It had devolved into a grueling, stagnant resistance against an overwhelming tide of misery.
source_text: Too long had British life been corrupted by the fictions of loyalty to an uninspiring and alien Court, of national piety in an official Church, of freedom in a politician-rigged State, of justice in an economic system where the advertiser, the sweater and usurer had a hundred advantages over the producer and artisan, to maintain itself now steadily at any high pitch of heroic endeavour. It had bought its comfort with the demoralisation of its servants. It had no completely honest organs; its spirit was clogged by its accumulated insincerities. Brought at last face to face with a bitter hostility and a powerful and unscrupulous enemy, an enemy socialistic, scientific and efficient to an unexampled degree, it seemed indeed to be inspired for a time by an unwonted energy and unanimity. Youth and the common people shone. The sons of every class went out to fight and die, full of a splendid dream of this war. Easy-going vanished from the foreground of the picture. But only to creep back again as the first inspiration passed. Presently the older men, the seasoned politicians, the owners and hucksters, the charming women and the habitual consumers, began to recover from this blaze of moral exaltation. Old habits of mind and procedure reasserted themselves. The war which had begun so dramatically missed its climax; there was neither heroic swift defeat nor heroic swift victory. There was indecision; the most trying test of all for an undisciplined people. There were great spaces of uneventful fatigue. Before the Battle of the Yser had fully developed the dramatic quality had gone out of the war. It had ceased to be either a tragedy or a triumph; for both sides it became a monstrous strain and wasting. It had become a wearisome thrusting against a pressure of evils....

text: Beneath the crushing weight of the conflict, the composure of the realm splintered, revealing a spite that was less methodical than that of the enemy, yet perhaps more unsettling. Because no state hand had molded the national character for the service of the Crown, the public spirit turned irritable and frantic, much like a sick man who has never learned self-restraint. It ceased to strike only at the invader, choosing instead to lash out at imagined turncoats and slackers; it dissipated its strength in a tangled, widening thicket of domestic feuds and recriminations. At one moment, the perceived lethargy of the Premier was the target; at another, the continental sympathies of the High Chancellor, or the reckless daring of the Naval Secretary. A frantic search for infiltrators and those of foreign extraction began in every corner of the land, though it stopped short of the aristocracy. There were outbursts against "subversives," against men of intellect, against scientists, and even the intimate associates of the Field Marshal. Day after day, Arthur Penhaligon studied the morning papers, his spirit sinking deeper into a profound and heavy disillusionment.
source_text: Under that strain the dignity of England broke, and revealed a malignity less focussed and intense than the German, but perhaps even more distressing. No paternal government had organised the British spirit for patriotic ends; it became now peevish and impatient, like some ill-trained man who is sick, it directed itself no longer against the enemy alone but fitfully against imagined traitors and shirkers; it wasted its energies in a deepening and spreading net of internal squabbles and accusations. Now it was the wily indolence of the Prime Minister, now it was the German culture of the Lord Chancellor, now the imaginative enterprise of the First Lord of the Admiralty that focussed a vindictive campaign. There began a hunt for spies and of suspects of German origin in every quarter except the highest; a denunciation now of "traitors," now of people with imaginations, now of scientific men, now of the personal friend of the Commander-in-Chief, now of this group and then of that group.... Every day Mr. Britling read his three or four newspapers with a deepening disappointment.

text: Thus did Mr. Britling’s ideas take the form of words as he wandered one cold and somber night in March, crossing a reed-filled meadow under a heavy, clouded sky. The agonizing shriek of a small animal caught in a distant thicket had prompted this grim line of reasoning. "Is existence a struggle against some primal curse?" he mused. "How often I find myself drifting back toward the tenets of Christian theology... and that notion of Redemption through the pouring out of blood."
source_text: So Mr. Britling's thoughts shaped themselves in words as he prowled one night in March, chill and melancholy, across a rushy meadow under an overcast sky. The death squeal of some little beast caught suddenly in a distant copse had set loose this train of thought. "Life struggling under a birth curse?" he thought. "How nearly I come back at times to the Christian theology!... And then, Redemption by the shedding of blood."

text: Yet such was Mr. Thorne’s understanding of the ancient heresies, not of the established creed. He slipped for a moment into the hazy memories of his former scholarship. What had been the Gnostic tenet? That the God of the ancient scriptures was the Adversary of the new? But no—that was the belief of the Manichaeans!
source_text: But that was Mr. Britling's idea of Gnosticism, not of orthodox Christianity. He went off for a time into faded reminiscences of theological reading. What had been the Gnostic idea? That the God of the Old Testament was the Devil of the New? But that had been the idea of the Manichæans!...

text: During those restless nocturnal wanderings in the first winter of the conflict, Mr. Britling conceived of a new manuscript. This work was meant to descend far deeper than the shallow, glib observations of "And Now War Ends." He called it the "Anatomy of Hate," intending it to be a rigorous study of how hatred functions as a necessary remedy for human sloth. He was convinced that as long as men remained complacent, they had to remain fierce. This certainty weighed heavily upon him.
source_text: It was during these prowling nights in the first winter of the war that Mr. Britling planned a new writing that was to go whole abysses beneath the facile superficiality of "And Now War Ends." It was to be called the "Anatomy of Hate." It was to deal very faithfully with the function of hate as a corrective to inefficiency. So long as men were slack, men must be fierce. This conviction pressed upon him....

text: Suddenly and with a jarring force, the malice of the war—which until then had been merely a stagnant collection of rumors, reports, and vague suspicions—reached into the quiet of Essex to strike a blow of senseless cruelty into the very soul of Mr. Britling. In the late afternoon, a telegram arrived from Filmington-on-Sea, where Aunt Wilshire had been nursing her grievances in a boarding-house after traveling through the Yorkshire moors. She had been "gravely wounded" during a German air raid the previous night. The morning papers had not uttered a word about the attack. She was asking for him.
source_text: Abruptly and shockingly, this malignity of warfare, which had been so far only a festering cluster of reports and stories and rumours and suspicions, stretched out its arm into Essex and struck a barb of grotesque cruelty into the very heart of Mr. Britling. Late one afternoon came a telegram from Filmington-on-Sea, where Aunt Wilshire had been recovering her temper in a boarding-house after a round of visits in Yorkshire and the moorlands. And she had been "very seriously injured" by an overnight German air raid. It was a raid that had not been even mentioned in the morning's papers. She had asked to see him.

text: The narrative felt as though it had been torn from a surreal Jules Verne adventure; there was the stillness of the ancient hamlet, the townsfolk drifting toward sleep, the mute streets, and the vast, starry heavens. Then, for ten fleeting minutes, came the thunder of artillery and the crash of breaking glass, succeeded by scattered flames, the piercing scream of a terrified child, and frightened figures hurrying about with lanterns. Soon, the sky was hollow once more, the attackers gone.
source_text: The story was like a page from some fantastic romance of Jules Verne's; the peace of the little old town, the people going to bed, the quiet streets, the quiet starry sky, and then for ten minutes an uproar of guns and shells, a clatter of breaking glass, and then a fire here, a fire there, a child's voice pitched high by pain and terror, scared people going to and fro with lanterns, and the sky empty again, the raiders gone....

text: In that dark interval, Silas Thorne was busy strangling Baron von Luft and shattering his crown. The Baron perished that night, and the High Sovereign met a bloody end; a dozen lesser souls were sacrificed to the vengeful ghost of Mother Marsden; many grisly acts were performed before Silas’s inner tempest finally subsided. Then, quite abruptly, he grew weary of the slaughter; the images were cast off, vanishing utterly from the theater of his mind.
source_text: Mr. Britling was at that moment killing Count Zeppelin and beating out his brains. Count Zeppelin was killed that night and the German Emperor was assassinated; a score of lesser victims were offered up to the _manes_ of Aunt Wilshire; there were memorable cruelties before the wrath and bitterness of Mr. Britling was appeased. And then suddenly he had had enough of these thoughts; they were thrust aside, they vanished out of his mind.

text: Before the war, such notions had never crossed Mr. Britling’s mind. They surfaced now, only to be resisted by the very essence of his character. For several weeks, his intellect had struggled with this puzzle, both in his waking hours and in the quiet of his subconscious. He pondered it during his solitary patrols as a special constable, and it projected itself in horrific metaphors across the shadows of his sleep. "Does a spirit of unadulterated cruelty truly exist? Can any being, even the most barbaric, genuinely grasp the suffering it creates, or do they hurt others simply to cause pain?" He invoked countless memories and visions to testify before the court of his own reason. Lost in these inquiries, he no longer felt the biting cold or his solitude. He sat there, judging the nature of all existence beneath the distant, apathetic stars.
source_text: These were thoughts that Mr. Britling had never faced before the war. They came to him now, and they came only to be rejected by the inherent quality of his mind. For weeks, consciously and subconsciously, his mind had been grappling with this riddle. He had thought of it during his lonely prowlings as a special constable; it had flung itself in monstrous symbols across the dark canvas of his dreams. "Is there indeed a devil of pure cruelty? Does any creature, even the very cruellest of creatures, really apprehend the pain it causes, or inflict it for the sake of the infliction?" He summoned a score of memories, a score of imaginations, to bear their witness before the tribunal of his mind. He forgot cold and loneliness in this speculation. He sat, trying all Being, on this score, under the cold indifferent stars.

text: What was this sense of correctness that lay hidden beneath brutality? What fueled this surge of malice, this irritation sparked by weak acts of compassion and softheartedness? Was it truly a purely wicked force, or was it instead a raw instinct—unseeing for the moment—that was essentially as noble as mercy, perhaps even surpassing mercy in its final significance?
source_text: What was this rightness that lurked below cruelty? What was the inspiration of this pressure of spite, this anger that was aroused by ineffective gentleness and kindliness? Was it indeed an altogether evil thing; was it not rather an impulse, blind as yet, but in its ultimate quality _as good as mercy_, greater perhaps in its ultimate values than mercy?

text: For weeks, this concept had been coalescing in Mr. Britling’s mind; it had matured and taken form as he tinkered with the opening pages of his essay, "The Anatomy of Hate." He now confronted the question directly: does there not exist a constructive and remedial urge behind all animosity? Is this malice perhaps merely the primitive ancestor of the rigorous order found in a creative society?
source_text: This idea had been gathering in Mr. Britling's mind for many weeks; it had been growing and taking shape as he wrote, making experimental beginnings for his essay, "The Anatomy of Hate." Is there not, he now asked himself plainly, a creative and corrective impulse behind all hate? Is not this malignity indeed only the ape-like precursor of the great disciplines of a creative state?

text: His early letters spoke at length about the appeal of strict routine and the outdoors. "The struggle of deciding what to do with my life is finally over," Hugh wrote. "I have surrendered my agency, and it brings a profound sense of relief. Instead of having to convince myself to get out of bed, a bugle makes the call. There is no room for hesitation, no chance to lie there and argue the point with myself. I’m beginning to understand why men enter monasteries and subject themselves to rules. You are carried along in a kind of moral vehicle instead of having to trudge down the road on your own."
source_text: The earlier letters told much of the charms of discipline and the open air. "All the bother about what one has to do with oneself is over," wrote Hugh. "One has disposed of oneself. That has the effect of a great relief. Instead of telling oneself that one ought to get up in the morning, a bugle tells you that.... And there's no nonsense about it, no chance of lying and arguing about it with oneself.... I begin to see the sense of men going into monasteries and putting themselves under rules. One is carried along in a sort of moral automobile instead of trudging the road...."

text: In his initial correspondence, he grappled with the basic principles of military drill and order, debating whether such routines were truly vital. He started with the conviction that their value was mostly overstated. Yet, as time passed, he recognized that these fundamentals were the bedrock of a true soldier. "When a crisis strikes," he decided, "one can never be sure which side of a man will emerge—his noble spirit or his primal fears. He might exhibit a magnificent bravery, or a sudden, desperate retreat. A learned habit is far more reliable than a fleeting instinct. Discipline, therefore, builds a routine of steady and valiant behavior. If you maintain your composure, you have the chance to be heroic. If you lose it, your training will sustain you."
source_text: And in his early letters he was greatly exercised by the elementary science of drill and discipline, and the discussion of whether these things were necessary. He began by assuming that their importance was overrated. He went on to discover that they constituted the very essentials of all good soldiering. "In a crisis," he concluded, "there is no telling what will get hold of a man, his higher instincts or his lower. He may show courage of a very splendid sort--or a hasty discretion. A habit is much more trustworthy than an instinct. So discipline sets up a habit of steady and courageous bearing. If you keep your head you are at liberty to be splendid. If you lose it, the habit will carry you through."

text: Another day of marching," he noted. "Solely because they have no other way to occupy us. There is no real marksmanship training because there are no rifles to be had. Half our hours are squandered. If you calculate half a week for every man in this army, you’ll find that we are losing centuries every single week. If the majority of these recruits had simply been registered and then allowed to return to their lives while officers were trained and equipment was secured—and if they were only then drilled as quickly as possible—it would have been vastly better for the nation. In one sense, we remain untrained; in another, we are becoming stagnant. It’s infuriating. I feel as though we are being poorly handled.
source_text: "Route marching again," he wrote. "For no earthly reason than that they can do nothing else with us. We are getting no decent musketry training because there are no rifles. We are wasting half our time. If you multiply half a week by the number of men in the army you will see we waste centuries weekly.... If most of these men here had just been enrolled and left to go about their business while we trained officers and instructors and got equipment for them, and if they had then been put through their paces as rapidly as possible, it would have been infinitely better for the country.... In a sort of way we are keeping raw; in a sort of way we are getting stale.... I get irritated by this. I feel we are not being properly done by.

text: My dear Father, consider this a letter of pure frustration. I need to vent to someone, and none of the other fellows here are suitable. I might deserve to be lined up and shot for saying so, but I’m declaring here and now that every officer in this battalion ranked higher than captain is a total incompetent—and that goes for a few of the captains as well. Some are pleasant enough but have effectively shut down their minds, while others are simply unpleasant and brainless. Judging by your latest letter to the paper, they seem to be a standard sample of the officer class tasked with winning this war. They are typically lazy, yet when they are finally motivated, they become obsessively fussy. The time they ought to spend sharpening their intellects and improving their tactical skills is instead wasted on staying "fit." In reality, they are fit for nothing. They can't manage a thirty-mile march without losing half the men, losing track of our food and quarters, and leaving us to go hungry for thirty-six hours. They can’t even count past five, having never learned to use their brains for simple arithmetic. I’ve concluded this war will be like the Battle of Inkerman on a massive scale. We in the ranks will have to get the job done ourselves. True leadership is off the table.
source_text: "My dear Parent, this is a swearing letter. I must let go to somebody. And somehow none of the other chaps are convenient. I don't know if I ought to be put against a wall and shot for it, but I hereby declare that all the officers of this battalion over and above the rank of captain are a constellation of incapables--and several of the captains are herewith included. Some of them are men of a pleasant disposition and carefully aborted mental powers, and some are men of an unpleasant disposition and no mental powers at all. And I believe--a little enlightened by your recent letter to _The Times_--that they are a fair sample of the entire 'army' class which has got to win this war. Usually they are indolent, but when they are thoroughly roused they are fussy. The time they should spend in enlarging their minds and increasing their military efficiency they devote to keeping fit. They are, roughly speaking, fit--for nothing. They cannot move us thirty miles without getting half of us left about, without losing touch with food and shelter, and starving us for thirty-six hours or so in the process, and they cannot count beyond the fingers of one hand, not having learnt to use the nose for arithmetical operations.... I conclude this war is going to be a sort of Battle of Inkerman on a large scale. We chaps in the ranks will have to do the job. Leading is 'off.'...

text: Word has it that the bond between the ranks and the brass in this new army is a thing of beauty. Maybe I’ll come to appreciate my commander eventually—but not today. Not until I’ve managed to forget the mess of movements that led to us taking Cheasingholt. He blunders through his actual duties without a flicker of shame, yet he would sooner face death than tie his boots in anything but the approved pattern. Truly, my bitterness toward the officer class is meant for him alone. I finally understand why soldiers are driven to kill their own superiors. But honestly, even setting aside my fatigue and fury, this whole deployment was handled quite miserably.
source_text: "They say the relations of men and officers in the new army are beautiful. Some day I may learn to love my officer--but not just yet. Not till I've forgotten the operations leading up to the occupation of Cheasingholt.... He muffs his real job without a blush, and yet he would rather be shot than do his bootlaces up criss-cross. What I say about officers applies only and solely to him really.... How well I understand now the shooting of officers by their men.... But indeed, fatigue and exasperation apart, this shift has been done atrociously...."

text: I imagine the ordeal has startled him out of his soldierly performance and back into his genuine self. He is remarkably like a scruffy mongrel, a mix of wiry terrier with a touch of Airedale. He is the sort of stray you find yourself liking, despite the foulness of everything he’s been poking his nose into. He is as tough as iron, but—good heavens—he is an absolutely pathetic boxer.
source_text: "I think it is because the affair has surprised him right out of acting Ortheris and Tommy Atkins for a bit, into his proper self. He's frightfully like some sort of mongrel with a lot of wiry-haired terrier and a touch of Airedale in it. A mongrel you like in spite of the flavour of all the horrid things he's been nosing into. And he's as hard as nails and, my dear daddy! he can't box for nuts."

text: He felt—as he had several times in recent years—that he was only just starting to truly see Hugh. Such a continuous rediscovery of one's offspring is common to any attentive father. He had always seen Hugh as a mere youth, yet here was a man standing over him, speaking as one peer to another. This person, so remarkably new, polished and distinct, filled Mr. Britling with a profound sense of astonishment and pride.
source_text: He had a feeling--every now and again in the last few years he had had the same feeling--as though he was only just beginning to discover Hugh. This perpetual rediscovery of one's children is the experience of every observant parent. He had always considered Hugh as a youth, and now a man stood over him and talked, as one man to another. And this man, this very new man, mint new and clean and clear, filled Mr. Britling with surprise and admiration.

text: Hugh possessed an independent mind; he analyzed his situation from every perspective, guided by a sense of duty rather than self-interest. He was more than a mere follower or a dreamer lost in his own vanity. If he served as a genuine reflection of his peers, then this new generation was a vast improvement over the one Mr. Britling had known.
source_text: Hugh thought for himself; he thought round and through his position, not egotistically but with a quality of responsibility. He wasn't just hero-worshipping and imitating, just spinning some self-centred romance. If he was a fair sample of his generation then it was a better generation than Mr. Britling's had been....

text: Mr. Britling’s thoughts veered toward the bitter injustice of the rejected volunteer. He was pierced by a sense of deep dishonor, watching noble young men march toward carnage while those over forty remained tucked away in safety. The absurdity of the arrangement galled him. Here were the fathers—men who had already played their parts, seen their best days, and justified their existence—forbidden from training or being of any real use. They were left in a state of shameful security, relegated to the hollow duties of special constables, while their innocent sons, with all their untapped potential, were swallowed by the lethal trenches. When it was over, the war would surely leave the earth populated only by the infirm, the aged, and the children.
source_text: At that Mr. Britling's mind went off at a tangent to the grievance of the rejected volunteer. It was acutely shameful to him that all these fine lads should be going off to death and wounds while the men of forty and over lay snug at home. How stupid it was to fix things like that! Here were the fathers, who had done their work, shot their bolts, returned some value for the costs of their education, unable to get training, unable to be of any service, shamefully safe, doing April fool work as special constables; while their young innocents, untried, all their gathering possibilities of service unbroached, went down into the deadly trenches.... The war would leave the world a world of cripples and old men and children....

text: Hugh was vague about his specific location and daily responsibilities. His letters were now subject to military censorship, and any precise details might result in his mail being withheld. Mr. Britling largely pictured him stationed a short distance behind the front lines, amidst a bleak, level landscape where the boom of heavy artillery echoed. He used photographs from the weekly papers to help construct these scenes. Occasionally, he imagined Hugh further back in quaint, ancient towns like Beauvais, seeing groups of soldiers lingering outside cafés. At other times, he envisioned the Pas de Calais as the massive British military hub it was rumored to be. In these thoughts, the background was filled with picket lines of horses, canvas tents, and grey wagons, with Hugh in the foreground—his arms bare, holding a bucket.
source_text: Hugh was not very explicit about his position or daily duties. What he wrote now had to pass through the hands of a Censor, and any sort of definite information might cause the suppression of his letter. Mr. Britling conceived him for the most part as quartered some way behind the front, but in a flat, desolated country and within hearing of great guns. He assisted his imagination with the illustrated papers. Sometimes he put him farther back into pleasant old towns after the fashion of Beauvais, and imagined loitering groups in the front of cafés; sometimes he filled in the obvious suggestions of the phrase that all the Pas de Calais was now one vast British camp. Then he crowded the picture with tethered horses and tents and grey-painted wagons, and Hugh in the foreground--bare-armed, with a bucket....

text: The clarity of this reality is rooted in the countless journals retrieved by our forces from the pockets of the fallen and the maimed. During the war’s initial phases, every infantryman of the Kaiser was strictly enjoined to record his daily thoughts; it was a formal command designed to sustain his psychological investment in the struggle. From these thousands of recovered testimonies, a singular truth emerges: these men are not soldiers by instinct. Unlike our own troops, they are merely orderly, educated civilians propelled into the fray. They require a lens of high romance or heavy tragedy to make the violence bearable, framing the conflict as a moral imperative or a stroke of destiny. They must shout their patriotic hymns and invoke the Divine to bolster their spirits. They possess none of that effortless affinity for the sport of battle that defines our own men.
source_text: "What makes all this so plain are the diaries the French and English have been finding on the dead. You know at the early state of the war every German soldier was expected to keep a diary. He was ordered to do it. The idea was to keep him interested in the war. Consequently, from the dead and wounded our people have got thousands.... It helps one to realise that the Germans aren't really soldiers at all. Not as our men are. They are obedient, law-abiding, intelligent people, who have been shoved into this. They have to see the war as something romantic and melodramatic, or as something moral, or as tragic fate. They have to bellow songs about 'Deutschland,' or drag in 'Gott.' They don't take to the game as our men take to the game....

text: A certain calcification occurs out here, affecting the spirit as much as the flesh. We exist on a separate plane entirely. Before I left, you spoke of the "Manor-world" and the "Beyond." Well, this is the Beyond. It is an alien place. Our boys still show a lingering softness for trivialities—stray cats, or a sprig of green. Even near the reserve lines, you’ll find small, vibrant plots tended by the infantry. But don't imagine for a moment that we harbor any gentleness for the broken, and to be blunt, there isn't a shred of compassion remaining for the foe. Not a grain. That sentiment has vanished. They say in the beginning men were careful with the injured and solemn with the slain. That’s a ghost story now. Gas, massacres, and horrors beyond naming have seen to that. Everything has turned to flint. Now, the men are more likely to jeer at a comrade who takes a bullet, or curse one whose injuries prove inconvenient. Just recently, a massive dead Prussian lay before the Suffolk lines. He turned into a stench, so they hauled him in and shoveled him under behind the parapet. Once he was covered, some well-meaning fellow was hammering a plank over the mound—"A Mother’s Fritz" or some such—when a heavy shell struck. It threw the man twenty paces, bleeding, and tossed Fritz back into the light. He was launched skyward. He spun through the air like a broken weather-vane. The whole company thought it a magnificent prank on the burial party. For a while, no one would go near him, and he lay several yards from his first resting place. Eventually, as the rot grew unbearable, a few stretcher-bearers buried him once more, labeling the spot "Fritz’s Final Sleep." Naturally, the luck of the front held, and he was unearthed again. In fragments this time. The trench erupted in mockery, shouting "Bravo, Fritz!" and "Wrong day for the Second Coming, old boy!"...
source_text: "There's a kind of hardening not only of the body but of the mind through all this life out here. One is living on a different level. You know--just before I came away--you talked of Dower-House-land--and outside. This is outside. It's different. Our men here are kind enough still to little things--kittens or birds or flowers. Behind the front, for example, everywhere there are Tommy gardens. Some are quite bright little patches. But it's just nonsense to suppose we are tender to the wounded up here--and, putting it plainly, there isn't a scrap of pity left for the enemy. Not a scrap. Not a trace of such feeling. They were tender about the wounded in the early days--men tell me--and reverent about the dead. It's all gone now. There have been atrocities, gas, unforgettable things. Everything is harder. Our people are inclined now to laugh at a man who gets hit, and to be annoyed at a man with a troublesome wound. The other day, they say, there was a big dead German outside the Essex trenches. He became a nuisance, and he was dragged in and taken behind the line and buried. After he was buried, a kindly soul was putting a board over him with 'Somebody's Fritz' on it, when a shell burst close by. It blew the man with the board a dozen yards and wounded him, and it restored Fritz to the open air. He was lifted clean out. He flew head over heels like a windmill. This was regarded as a tremendous joke against the men who had been at the pains of burying him. For a time nobody else would touch Fritz, who was now some yards behind his original grave. Then as he got worse and worse he was buried again by some devoted sanitarians, and this time the inscription was 'Somebody's Fritz. R.I.P.' And as luck would have it, he was spun up again. In pieces. The trench howled with laughter and cries of 'Good old Fritz!' 'This isn't the Resurrection, Fritz.'...

text: Mr. Halloway retreated to Old Orchard, his mind clouded by a heavy, gnawing unease. He was haunted by the conviction that Leo’s situation was far more precarious than the fragile hopes he had offered Sybilla; "unaccounted for" was a designation that likely hid a brutal and lonely end. He felt a deep sorrow for the boy, yet an even sharper pang for the young woman, whose every word and movement hinted at terrors far darker than his own. Most agonizing of all was that irrational sense of debt, the realization that his own hand had smoothed the path to Leo’s enlistment. He found it remarkable, almost unnerving, that Sybilla had not yet lashed out at him with the accusations he felt he deserved.
source_text: Mr. Britling returned to the Dower House perplexed and profoundly disturbed. He had a distressful sense that things were far more serious with Teddy than he had tried to persuade Letty they were; that "wounded and missing" meant indeed a man abandoned to very sinister probabilities. He was distressed for Teddy, and still more acutely distressed for Mrs. Teddy, whose every note and gesture betrayed suppositions even more sinister than his own. And that preposterous sense of liability, because he had helped Teddy to get his commission, was more distressful than it had ever been. He was surprised that Letty had not assailed him with railing accusations.

text: As I promised, I will describe the trenches. The essential thing to understand is their narrowness; they are a kind of inverted wall, resembling massive fissures in the earth more than anything else. Perhaps I should begin by explaining how we reached them. We set out around one in the morning, weighed down by every piece of equipment a soldier could possibly carry. In addition, I lugged a kettle filled with water, while most of the men had bundles of firewood and some carried extra bread. We marched from our quarters along the road for a mile or more before cutting across the fields. Eventually, we reached a ridge and descended into a labyrinth of zigzagging trenches that led toward the front line. These trenches are deeper than a man’s height, so you can see nothing; it is like walking through a corridor of mud. You simply trudge along in single file. Every so often, someone stumbles into a rainwater drainage pit, curses at a patch of soft ground, or bumps into the man ahead of him. This seems to go on for hours. It certainly lasted an hour, so I expect we covered two or three miles. At one point, we crossed a hollow and a ditch where the trench was reinforced with sandbags and spanned by a plank. Stars were visible overhead, and occasionally, the flares they launch would illuminate the trench edges, providing a brief glimpse of a distant treetop or a factory roof. For a while afterward, it was more difficult to proceed because the glare left us blinded. Then, just as you began to believe this slog would never end, we reached the support trenches behind the firing line and found the men we were relieving ready to depart.
source_text: "And now about those trenches--as I promised. The great thing to grasp is that they are narrow. They are a sort of negative wall. They are more like giant cracks in the ground than anything else.... But perhaps I had better begin by telling how we got there. We started about one in the morning ladened up with everything you can possibly imagine on a soldier, and in addition I had a kettle--filled with water--most of the chaps had bundles of firewood, and some had extra bread. We marched out of our quarters along the road for a mile or more, and then we took the fields, and presently came to a crest and dropped into a sort of maze of zigzag trenches going up to the front trench. These trenches, you know, are much deeper than one's height; you don't see anything. It's like walking along a mud-walled passage. You just trudge along them in single file. Every now and then some one stumbles into a soakaway for rainwater or swears at a soft place, or somebody blunders into the man in front of him. This seems to go on for hours and hours. It certainly went on for an hour; so I suppose we did two or three miles of it. At one place we crossed a dip in the ground and a ditch, and the trench was built up with sandbags up to the ditch and there was a plank. Overhead there were stars, and now and then a sort of blaze thing they send up lit up the edges of the trench and gave one a glimpse of a treetop or a factory roof far away. Then for a time it was more difficult to go on because you were blinded. Suddenly just when you were believing that this sort of trudge was going on forever, we were in the support trenches behind the firing line, and found the men we were relieving ready to come back.

text: Doubtless you anticipate a chronicle of the Kaiser’s men, of barrages and the thunder of the front, but the reality is far more tedious. I have yet to lay eyes on a living foe; no shell has fallen within a hundred paces of my position, and I remain entirely unburdened by any medals for valor. My primary achievement has been to coat myself in a layer of muck so thick it defies vocabulary. Though I labor incessantly, I have neither pulled a trigger nor engaged in the slightest skirmish. Our days are consumed by the drudgery of maintenance—shoring up the breastworks with a cautious eye toward the sharpshooters, lugging heavy tins of rations from the support lines, and extending our saps at an oblique angle into the waste. The simple mechanics of existence—preparing food and tidying our quarters—devour the hours. We boil tea in massive cauldrons within a communal cellar shared by two companies, then hoist the vessels on staves to carry them back to our sections. We scrub our tins, we scrub ourselves, we scrape away the stubble. The ritual of the midday meal occupies half the afternoon. It is less like a crusade and more akin to a damp, extended holiday in the woods. For this rotation, we are interspersed with some veterans from the Kentish ranks who understand the local rhythms. Consequently, we find ourselves assigned the lion's share of the manual labor. Eventually, we shall be granted a sector of our own to hold.
source_text: "And now you expect me to tell of Germans and the fight and shelling and all sorts of things. _I haven't seen a live German_; I haven't been within two hundred yards of a shell burst, there has been no attack and I haven't got the V.C. I have made myself muddy beyond describing; I've been working all the time, but I've not fired a shot or fought a ha'porth. We were busy all the time--just at work, repairing the parapet, which had to be done gingerly because of snipers, bringing our food in from the rear in big carriers, getting water, pushing our trench out from an angle slantingways forward. Getting meals, clearing up and so on takes a lot of time. We make tea in big kettles in the big dug-out, which two whole companies use for their cooking, and carry them with a pole through the handles to our platoons. We wash up and wash and shave. Dinner preparation (and consumption) takes two or three hours. Tea too uses up time. It's like camping out and picnicking in the park. This first time (and next too) we have been mixed with some Sussex men who have been here longer and know the business.... It works out that we do most of the fatigue. Afterwards we shall go up alone to a pitch of our own....

text: A few of us ventured out to shore up a gap in the embankment that stood only one sack thick, the very spot where a man had been struck earlier that day. We stacked the wall four-deep to the very crest. During the labor, I was haunted by the prospect of being caught in a searchlight’s pale bloom, and I felt a constant, crawling dread that something would find me from the rear. I forced my eyes forward; had I turned once, I should have spent the night looking back. All the while, our own rifles cracked just above our heads, the lead whistling past to convince the enemy we were still huddled safely within the trench.
source_text: "Some of us went out to strengthen a place in the parapet that was only one sandbag thick, where a man had been hit during the day. We made it four bags thick right up to the top. All the while you were doing it, you dreaded to find yourself in the white glare of a searchlight, and you had a feeling that something would hit you suddenly from behind. I had to make up my mind not to look round, or I should have kept on looking round.... Also our chaps kept shooting over us, within a foot of one's head. Just to persuade the Germans that we were not out of the trench....

text: The sight of that plunge drew an involuntary cry from your throat. It plummeted, seemed to regain a fleeting moment of control, and then pitched back into a terminal dive. The engine’s roar grew steadily more deafening as it dropped. I had never witnessed anything fall with such terrifying velocity. We saw it strike the earth amidst a cluster of ruined buildings on the ridge behind us. It flipped over and disintegrated instantly, shattered into a thousand jagged fragments.
source_text: "It made you say 'Ooooo!' to see that dive. It came down, seemed to get a little bit under control, and then dive down again. You could hear the engine roar louder and louder as it came down. I never saw anything fall so fast. We saw it hit the ground among a lot of smashed-up buildings on the crest behind us. It went right over and flew to pieces, all to smithereens....

text: The suspicion was confirmed four days later when another letter arrived from Hugh, recounting his second stint in the trenches. This time, the situation had been far more volatile; they had endured heavy shelling and a German assault. Because he was writing to his father, he spoke with greater openness, his message scrawled hastily in pencil.
source_text: Four days later this suspicion was confirmed by the arrival of another letter from Hugh in which he told of his second spell in the trenches. This time things had been much more lively. They had been heavily shelled and there had been a German attack. And this time he was writing to his father, and wrote more freely. He had scribbled in pencil.

text: The calamity involving Teddy and his wife struck him with devastating force. He recoiled at the memory of her ashen face and the silent indictment in her stare. He realized he could never summon the courage to offer her his solitary defense: "I did not restrain Hugh. Had I done that, you might truly have cause to hold me responsible."
source_text: The disaster to Teddy and Mrs. Teddy hit him hard. He winced at the thought of Mrs. Teddy's white face; the unspoken accusation in her eyes. He felt he could never bring himself to say his one excuse to her: "I did not keep Hugh back. If I had done that, then you might have the right to blame."

text: A cacophony of voices reached Mr. Britling’s ears, speaking of social backlash, of industrialists plotting against labor unions, and of rapacious landlords and shippers profiting from the chaos. He heard of incompetence and treachery within the very halls of government, of pervasive religious hypocrisy and bigotry, and of shameless self-promotion written in the blood of the fallen. There were reports of market manipulation and the senseless, grating tyranny in India and Egypt. It came as a jolt to him that Hugh perceived the conflict as little more than madness, possessing a stark grasp of its inherent pointlessness. The boy compelled his father to confront what he had already begun to suspect: that even by the metrics of glory or conquest, the war had long ago become a grotesque absurdity. There had to be a way out of this gore-stained impasse that granted victory to no one, producing only unprecedented waste and slaughter. The vast majority of people across the globe must have been desperate for peace, ready to secure it at any fair price, yet the world seemed to lack the strength to stop the daily butchery and establish a harmony that was more than a mere interval between wars. Every day the newspapers arrived with their clinical tallies of combat, loss, and ruin—ships lost at sea, towns reduced to rubble. Yet there was never a resolution, nor even a glimmer of an end.
source_text: There came drifting to Mr. Britling's ears a confusion of voices, voices that told of reaction, of the schemes of employers to best the trade unions, of greedy shippers and greedy house landlords reaping their harvest, of waste and treason in the very households of the Ministry, of religious cant and intolerance at large, of self-advertisement written in letters of blood, of forestalling and jobbery, of irrational and exasperating oppressions in India and Egypt.... It came with a shock to him, too, that Hugh should see so little else than madness in the war, and have so pitiless a realisation of its essential futility. The boy forced his father to see--what indeed all along he had been seeing more and more clearly. The war, even by the standards of adventure and conquest, had long since become a monstrous absurdity. Some way there must be out of this bloody entanglement that was yielding victory to neither side, that was yielding nothing but waste and death beyond all precedent. The vast majority of people everywhere must be desiring peace, willing to buy peace at any reasonable price, and in all the world it seemed there was insufficient capacity to end the daily butchery and achieve the peace that was so universally desired, the peace that would be anything better than a breathing space for further warfare.... Every day came the papers with the balanced story of battles, losses, destructions, ships sunk, towns smashed. And never a decision, never a sign of decision.

text: Mr. Britling felt a sharp impulse to champion them. "A business," he said bitingly, "is not like an estate, which sits and grows wealthy for its owner. These men cannot leave behind some prying underling when they depart for service. Tens of thousands of middle-class men have ruined themselves and discarded every future prospect just to join this war.
source_text: Mr. Britling was stung to defend them. "A business," he said acidly, "isn't like land, which waits and grows rich for its owner. And these people can't leave ferrety little agents behind them when they go off to serve. Tens of thousands of middle-class men have ruined themselves and flung away every prospect they had in the world to go to this war."

text: Mrs. Alvington soon found it essential to extricate Julian Thorne from the Duchess of Marbury’s heavy-handed displays of national fervor. He drifted out into the late-season garden—where the dahlias bloomed with an unprecedented, fiery brilliance—accompanied by Sinclair, Colonel Vance, and a diminutive woman who soon proved herself to be singularly astute. A cloud of gloom hung over the little assembly. "This habit of indiscriminate finger-pointing strikes me as the most deplorable—and indeed, the most threatening—aspect of our current temperament," Thorne remarked, following the quiet reprieve that came once they were safely beyond the Duchess's reach.
source_text: It presently became necessary for Lady Homartyn to rescue Mr. Britling from the great lady's patriotic tramplings. He found himself drifting into the autumnal garden--the show of dahlias had never been so wonderful--in the company of Raeburn and the staff officer and a small woman who was presently discovered to be remarkably well-informed. They were all despondent. "I think all this promiscuous blaming of people is quite the worst--and most ominous--thing about us just now," said Mr. Britling after the restful pause that followed the departure from the presence of Lady Frensham.

text: The carnage would surely intensify were we to appoint—for instance—Sterling and Mountjoy and Lord Pressley and the Duchess of Albury, with perhaps Vance and Blackwood thrown into the mix—as a supposedly Resolute Cabinet. . . . I would sooner accept Pressley as a lone tyrant than endure that. . . . We cannot abruptly disown our heritage and reinvent our soul. We turned a deaf ear to the critics of our age. We never deigned to scrub clean or renovate our ancient schools. We have stubbornly repelled instruction. We have opted to preserve our peculiar national vanities: the bench of mitred lords and the theater of partisan strife. The compulsory Latin and the superior academic sneer. And the Duchess of Albury. And the whole stagnant lot of it. And this is where it has brought us! . . . Well, curse the luck, we are committed now; we must labor through the mire—with the hands we have—as the men we have become.
source_text: "They'd get killed all the more if you had--let us say--Carson and Lloyd George and Northcliffe and Lady Frensham, with, I suppose, Austin Harrison and Horatio Bottomley thrown in--as a Strong Silent Government.... I'd rather have Northcliffe as dictator than that.... We can't suddenly go back on the past and alter our type. We didn't listen to Matthew Arnold. We've never thoroughly turned out and cleaned up our higher schools. We've resisted instruction. We've preferred to maintain our national luxuries of a bench of bishops and party politics. And compulsory Greek and the university sneer. And Lady Frensham. And all that sort of thing. And here we are!... Well, damn it, we're in for it now; we've got to plough through with it--with what we have--as what we are."

text: The small, knowledgeable woman suddenly mentioned her two sons, noting that one had recently returned, injured, from Suvla Bay. His accounts had left her deeply troubled. She insisted that the general population remained completely ignorant of what had truly transpired at the Battle of Anafarta. It had been a catastrophic disaster, a crushing defeat for the British forces. The logistical planning was incompetent; coordination was nonexistent, and nothing arrived where or when it was needed. For instance, the water distribution failed entirely, leaving the soldiers delirious with thirst. She identified a specific regiment that had been left without backup; when they finally retreated, the two units ended up fighting each other in the trenches, oblivious to the actual enemy. There was no leadership, no synergy, and no cohesive strategy. She claimed that some of the artillery had been abandoned back in Egypt, and portions of the supply train remained missing even now, lost somewhere in the Levant. Initially, Sir Ian Hamilton wasn't even on the scene, having failed to arrive on schedule. It was the senseless squandering of an entire military force—and such a promising one, at that. According to her son, the debacle signaled the absolute collapse of the Dardanelles campaign.
source_text: The little well-informed lady remarked abruptly that she had two sons; one was just home wounded from Suvla Bay. What her son told her made her feel very grave. She said that the public was still quite in the dark about the battle of Anafarta. It had been a hideous muddle, and we had been badly beaten. The staff work had been awful. Nothing joined up, nothing was on the spot and in time. The water supply, for example, had gone wrong; the men had been mad with thirst. One regiment which she named had not been supported by another; when at last the first came back the two battalions fought in the trenches regardless of the enemy. There had been no leading, no correlation, no plan. Some of the guns, she declared, had been left behind in Egypt. Some of the train was untraceable to this day. It was mislaid somewhere in the Levant. At the beginning Sir Ian Hamilton had not even been present. He had failed to get there in time. It had been the reckless throwing away of an army. And so hopeful an army! Her son declared it meant the complete failure of the Dardanelles project....

text: On three occasions this year," Raeburn remarked, "triumph has eluded us because of the sheer incompetence of our administrative staff. There is little point in seeking out individual culprits; the issue is one of national character. It stems from the fact that the graduates of our elite schools have never been taught to be punctual, to arrive at work precisely on time, to organize their own gear, or to perform any task with speed and precision—at least, nothing they can manage to delegate to others. One cannot expect individuals who are naturally relaxed to maintain a state of rigorous efficiency for long. Their entire upbringing and heritage work against it. They have a profound aversion to being perceived as overly fastidious; an Englishman would prefer to be a complete failure than to be labeled a prig. This is the reason we have forfeited three battles that should have been ours—along with countless lives, vast supplies, and time of immeasurable value. A full year has been squandered, and the morale of our nation has been broken.
source_text: "Three times this year," said Raeburn, "we have missed victories because of the badness of our staff work. It's no good picking out scapegoats. It's a question of national habit. It's because the sort of man we turn out from our public schools has never learnt how to catch trains, get to an office on the minute, pack a knapsack properly, or do anything smartly and quickly--anything whatever that he can possibly get done for him. You can't expect men who are habitually easy-going to keep bucked up to a high pitch of efficiency for any length of time. All their training is against it. All their tradition. They hate being prigs. An Englishman will be any sort of stupid failure rather than appear a prig. That's why we've lost three good fights that we ought to have won--and thousands and thousands of men--and material and time, precious beyond reckoning. We've lost a year. We've dashed the spirit of our people."

text: No map was ever drawn for the campaign, and it is improbable we shall devise one for the armistice," Thorne observed, adding: "Meanwhile, Lady Ashcroft’s set will be straining every nerve to stifle the rising pressure. They shall exhume the Irish Troubles at the first opportunity, though I suspect the Ulster card will no longer suffice to keep them afloat.
source_text: "We've never produced a plan for the war, and it isn't likely we shall have one for the peace," said Raeburn, and added: "and Lady Frensham's little lot will be doing their level best to sit on the safety-valve.... They'll rake up Ireland and Ulster from the very start. But I doubt if Ulster will save 'em."

text: In the meantime, I have news for you. I’ve survived a major engagement, a real battle, and I haven't sustained a single scratch. I even managed to capture two prisoners with my own bare hands. Men were being cut down right beside me, but I didn't find it troubling at all. It was every bit as exhilarating as those fierce contests we used to have around the hockey goal. I wasn't bothered by anything until later. But that evening in the trench, I stepped on something slick—the memory still revolts me. And then, after the worst was over, one of my mates was killed—it seemed so senseless, so unjust. I find myself dwelling on those two moments so much that the earlier combat feels like a dream. It feels more like a story I’ve read or a sketch in the *Illustrated London News* than something I actually experienced. I had wondered so often how it would feel and how I would hold up that, when it finally happened, the whole thing felt strangely flat and ordinary.
source_text: "Meanwhile I've got something to tell you. I've been through a fight, a big fight, and I haven't got a scratch. I've taken two prisoners with my lily hand. Men were shot close to me. I didn't mind that a bit. It was as exciting as one of those bitter fights we used to have round the hockey goal. I didn't mind anything till afterwards. Then when I was in the trench in the evening I trod on something slippery--pah! And after it was all over one of my chums got it--sort of unfairly. And I keep on thinking of those two things so much that all the early part is just dreamlike. It's more like something I've read in a book, or seen in the _Illustrated London News_ than actually been through. One had been thinking so often, how will it feel? how shall I behave? that when it came it had an effect of being flat and ordinary.

text: The rumors of our spring shortages in ordnance and shell have finally been silenced. Last night, the barrage began—a relentless hammering that persisted until the first grey streaks of dawn. The din was beyond anything I’d known. From our vantage, we watched the enemy lines erupt like a chain of exploding peaks; we could stand tall without fear of a sniper’s lead, as their earthworks were tossed into the sky. We had been pulled back into secondary ditches to escape the inevitable retaliation, but the response was meager. For once, our batteries held the high hand. A strange tension settled over us, reminiscent of the hush before a proctor distributes a final examination. Then the moment arrived. Clambering out of the earth is a peculiar sensation, akin to a chick breaking its shell; the horizon suddenly gapes wide and terrifying. Our cannons never seemed to cease their roar. "Forward! Forward!" the lieutenants urged. A collective roar went up as we surged. Seeing a comrade fall only spurs your legs to move quicker. Only the tangled thickets of wire force a pause—one must mind his footing in that rusted web. The vulnerability is what haunts you. After months of burrowing, the open air feels like a cold skinning. You sprint toward the opposing line with the frantic desperation of a lost child seeking shelter. I cannot fathom the courage it would take to turn one’s back to that storm; to retreat seems a greater feat of will than to advance. A sharp, dry hunger for the blade takes hold of you. Yet, they didn't stand their ground. They cast aside their gear and fled. We were upon them so swiftly we overtook a few in the following trench. I leaped into the hollow, hearing a rustle behind me, and discovered two of them huddled in a dugout, playing at invisibility. Their hands shot up the moment I turned. Had they resisted, I am not sure I would have struck; the urge for violence had evaporated. In that moment, I felt a bizarre, quiet kinship with them.
source_text: "They say we hadn't got enough guns in the spring or enough ammunition. That's all right now--anyhow. They started in plastering the Germans overnight, and right on until it was just daylight. I never heard such a row, and their trenches--we could stand up and look at them without getting a single shot at us--were flying about like the crater of a volcano. We were not in our firing trench. We had gone back into some new trenches, at the rear--I think to get out of the way of the counter fire. But this morning they weren't doing very much. For once our guns were on top. There was a feeling of anticipation--very like waiting for an examination paper to be given out; then we were at it. Getting out of a trench to attack gives you an odd feeling of being just hatched. Suddenly the world is big. I don't remember our gun fire stopping. And then you rush. 'Come on! Come on!' say the officers. Everybody gives a sort of howl and rushes. When you see men dropping, you rush the faster. The only thing that checks you at all is the wire twisted about everywhere. You don't want to trip over that. The frightening thing is the exposure. After being in the trenches so long you feel naked. You run like a scared child for the German trench ahead. I can't understand the iron nerve of a man who can expose his back by turning to run away. And there's a thirsty feeling with one's bayonet. But they didn't wait. They dropped rifles and ran. But we ran so fast after them that we caught one or two in the second trench.
