I got down into that, heard a voice behind me, and found my two prisoners lying artful in a dug-out. They held up their hands as I turned. If they hadn't I doubt if I should have done anything to them. I didn't feel like it. I felt _friendly_.

text: I have no memory of assisting or even pitying the wounded during that time, nor did the fallen move me beyond a practical need to step over rather than on them. I was consumed by a single, driving thought: we had to reinforce the trench and make it defensible before the enemy attempted to return. Once that was done, we had to hold our ground. I was driven by nothing but the singular urge to prevail; there was simply no room for any other thought in my mind.
source_text: "I don't remember that I helped or cared for a wounded man all the time, or felt anything about the dead except to step over them and not on them. I was just possessed by the idea that we had to get the trench into a sheltering state before they tried to come back. And then stick there. I just wanted to win, and there was nothing else in my mind....

text: He looked stunned. "Bloody," he muttered, "bloody." He locked eyes with me and suddenly smirked. You remember we once had two fights because of his use of that word—I think I mentioned those scraps to you back then. He couldn't box to save his life, but he had plenty of grit, and he found it ironic that I was now standing there, too shell-shocked to protest the old offense. "I thought you were a goner," he said. "I’m in a state—a bloody mess, aren't I? Bleeding like a pig. Bloody—that’s the word for it. Bloody! I didn't know I had so much of it in me."
source_text: "He looked amazed. 'Bloody,' he said, 'bloody.' He fixed his eyes on me, and suddenly grinned. You know we'd once had two fights about his saying 'bloody,' I think I told you at the time, a fight and a return match, he couldn't box for nuts, but he stood up like a Briton, and it appealed now to his sense of humour that I should be standing there too dazed to protest at the old offence. 'I thought _you_ was done in,' he said. 'I'm in a mess--a bloody mess, ain't I? Like a stuck pig. Bloody--right enough. Bloody! I didn't know I 'ad it _in_ me.'

text: As I was still clumsily reaching for my water bottle, he crumpled. He seemed to lose all thought of Ortheris. Suddenly, he spoke a few words that tore right through me. His face twisted into the expression of a cranky child refusing to go to sleep. "I didn't want to be left out," he muttered peevishly. "And now I’m finished!" Then—just like that—he looked utterly miserable and resentful, and he died. He turned his head slightly to the side, as if he were merely annoyed by the whole ordeal. He drifted into unconsciousness and simply flickered out.
source_text: "And then while I was fumbling with my water-bottle, he collapsed. He forgot all about Ortheris. Suddenly he said something that cut me all to ribbons. His face puckered up just like the face of a fretful child which refuses to go to bed. 'I didn't want to be aut of it,' he said petulantly. 'And I'm done!' And then--then he just looked discontented and miserable and died--right off. Turned his head a little way over. As if he was impatient at everything. Fainted--and fluttered out.

text: In any case, your son is safe and unhurt, even if he is currently struggling with bitterness and sorrow. We felt a great sense of relief that night. Rumors are circulating that we will soon be granted some time off and a chance to refit. Our casualties were quite high, though we have received official praise. Through it all, the Essex has performed well. I can’t count on returning home permanently just yet, but there is always the possibility of a forty-eight-hour pass back in England.
source_text: "But, anyhow, your son is sound and well--if sorrowful and angry. We were relieved that night. And there are rumours that very soon we are to have a holiday and a refit. We lost rather heavily. We have been praised. But all along, Essex has done well. I can't reckon to get back yet, but there are such things as leave for eight-and-forty hours or so in England....

text: To harbor a secret was contrary to Arthur’s every instinct—especially one involving a complex ethical dilemma—but he recognized that Beatrice and Professor Vance were ill-equipped to entertain with composure a matter that, in a more serene setting, would be a compelling philosophical problem. Yet he was not made for silence; he was made for the explicit declaration.
source_text: It was entirely against his habits of mind to hide anything--more particularly an entanglement with a difficult proposition--but he perceived quite clearly that neither Cecily nor Mr. Britling were really to be trusted to listen calmly to what, under happier circumstances, might be a profoundly interesting moral complication. Yet it was not in his nature to conceal; it was in his nature to state.

text: For a period they spoke of Hugh, yet Mr. Britling remained restless and ill-tempered, seemingly ready to make Mr. Direck answer for the War Office’s failures, the betrayal by Bulgaria, the evasiveness of Romania, or any other frustration currently vexing his spirit. They shared an uneasy, hurried lunch. Afterward, they withdrew to the study to smoke.
source_text: They talked of Hugh for a time, but Mr. Britling was fitful and irritable and quite prepared to hold Mr. Direck accountable for the laxity of the War Office, the treachery of Bulgaria, the ambiguity of Roumania or any other barb that chanced to be sticking into his sensibilities. They lunched precariously. Then they went into the study to smoke.

text: Enough about the war! Don't come here and preach to me—not when you were scouring archives just a year ago to trace your Essex relatives. Drop it! Explanations? I have no interest in your explanations. And"—he mimicked his visitor's pretentious drawl and pedantic reasoning—"complicated propositions.
source_text: "Well, let it be Nix on the war! Don't come here and talk to me! You who were searching registers a year ago to find your Essex kin. Let it be Nix! Explanations! What do I want with explanations? And"--he mocked his guest's accent and his guest's mode of thought--"dif'cult prap'sitions."

text: He stood up and lingered, caught in a moment of indecision. He was well aware that his judgment of America was absurdly biased and his conduct toward his trusting guest was shockingly rude; he knew there was no excuse for ending their conversation with such abrupt hostility. Yet, the outburst brought a wave of immense relief. As for making amends—absolutely not. He was glad he had spoken his mind.
source_text: He got up and stood irresolute. He knew he was being preposterously unfair to America, and outrageously uncivil to a trusting guest; he knew he had no business now to end the talk in this violent fashion. But it was an enormous relief. And to mend matters--_No!_ He was glad he'd said these things....

text: For the past several weeks, he had dwelled on this possibility almost incessantly, yet now that it was actually happening, he felt as though he had never given it a moment’s thought. He felt a desperate need to retreat into solitude so he could face this hideous and overwhelming truth alone, without any disturbance or outside interference.
source_text: He had been thinking of this possibility for the last few weeks almost continuously, and yet now that it had come to him he felt that he had never thought about it before, that he must go off alone by himself to envisage this monstrous and terrible fact, without distraction or interruption.

text: That evening, Eleanor summoned a desperate strength to shatter the cage of reserve that had always entombed her soul. Never before had she yearned so fiercely for a moment of unstudied grace; never had she felt so paralyzed by her own caution and the iron-clad habits of a lifetime’s restraint. She was ravaged by a borrowed sorrow. It seemed to her that she would gladly have surrendered her very life to offer her husband some measure of peace, yet she could conceive of no gesture that might bridge the distance between them. She slipped from the dining room into the dim hallway and ascended the stairs with ghostly quiet until she reached his door. There, she waited. No sound emerged from the room beyond. She extended a trembling hand and began to turn the latch, but the sudden, sharp click of the mechanism struck her like a physical blow. Appalled by her own daring, she recoiled. With a face of blanched despair, she turned and fled through the shadows to the sanctuary of her own chamber.
source_text: That night Mrs. Britling made the supreme effort of her life to burst the prison of self-consciousness and inhibition in which she was confined. Never before in all her life had she so desired to be spontaneous and unrestrained; never before had she so felt herself hampered by her timidity, her self-criticism, her deeply ingrained habit of never letting herself go. She was rent by reflected distress. It seemed to her that she would be ready to give her life and the whole world to be able to comfort her husband now. And she could conceive no gesture of comfort. She went out of the dining-room into the hall and listened. She went very softly upstairs until she came to the door of her husband's room. There she stood still. She could hear no sound from within. She put out her hand and turned the handle of the door a little way, and then she was startled by the loudness of the sound it made and at her own boldness. She withdrew her hand, and then with a gesture of despair, with a face of white agony, she flitted along the corridor to her own room.

text: Across England, where the attire of bereavement had once been uncommon, women and children now walked in the October sun wearing fresh black garments. One met these new sorrows everywhere: mothers grieving for sons, wives for husbands, lives splintered and hopes extinguished. The dyers were kept busy staining colorful clothes a somber black. Meanwhile, the number of maimed and broken men continued to grow. This was the reality in England, but it was even more so in France and Russia, across the Allied nations, and throughout Germany and Austria. Extending into Asia Minor and Egypt, India, Japan, and Italy, the world was filled with lamentation, a landscape of loss, poverty, and profound distress.
source_text: All over England now, where the livery of mourning had been a rare thing to see, women and children went about in the October sunshine in new black clothes. Everywhere one met these fresh griefs, mothers who had lost their sons, women who had lost their men, lives shattered and hopes destroyed. The dyers had a great time turning coloured garments to black. And there was also a growing multitude of crippled and disabled men. It was so in England, much more was it so in France and Russia, in all the countries of the Allies, and in Germany and Austria; away into Asia Minor and Egypt, in India and Japan and Italy there was mourning, the world was filled with loss and mourning and impoverishment and distress.

text: Evelyn and she had shared a long stillness beside the embers. She had been at her woolwork—a task she performed with singular ineptitude—while Evelyn made a pretense of her reading, all the while tracking her with surreptitious glances. Evelyn contemplated the sluggish, laboured expansion of the loose knitting for some time, noting the undercurrent of bitter frustration in every jerk of the needles. At length, she felt compelled to voice her protest.
source_text: Cissie and she had been sitting in silence before the fire. She had been knitting--she knitted very badly--and Cissie had been pretending to read, and had been watching her furtively. Cissie eyed the slow, toilsome growth of the slack woolwork for a time, and the touch of angry effort in every stroke of the knitting needles. Then she was stirred to remonstrance.

text: I am forged of cold iron, Martha. My spirit is my mother’s legacy, far removed from my father’s mildness. Julian is my entire world; he is the sole repository of whatever softness I once held. If that light is snuffed out, then let the darkness come. I have no intention of whimpering through my remaining days like those wretched, mourning creatures who crawl across the earth. If they have taken my man, I shall take their lives. It will be a reckoning of blood for blood and ruin for ruin. I shall hunt down the very instigators of this carnage, drawing close enough to feel their breath, and I will put an end to them and their lineage.
source_text: "I'm hard stuff, Cissie. I take after mother more than father. Teddy is my darling. All the tenderness of my life is Teddy. If it goes, it goes.... I won't crawl about the world like all these other snivelling widows. If they've killed my man I shall kill. Blood for blood and loss for loss. I shall get just as close to the particular Germans who made this war as I can, and I shall kill them and theirs....

text: Not long ago, such a poetic fantasy might have captivated Cissie’s imagination. She was only a year or two past her phase of theatrical melodrama, but she had definitely moved on. She was maturing now, gaining a more nuanced insight. People, she was starting to understand, rarely act with such simplicity. They swear oaths of loyalty that aren't truly binding; they love with genuine intent, yet always with reservations. There are no grand acts of vengeance, only small, spiteful ones; no lifelong crusades for justice, except for the cold, persistent machinery of the law. Life never possesses the intense focus that romantic stories demand. Instead, there is transition and a tendency to forget. Everything eventually dissolves. Even Teddy’s tragic history would be altered by the steady march of time. Even the cruelty of the German princes would eventually be viewed through more complicated lenses. Was it possible for Letty to maintain her current harsh and horrific perspective for years? Surely the intensity would fade; new layers of life would eventually cover it all.
source_text: Only a little while ago Cissie's imagination might have been captured by so romantic a dream. She was still but a year or so out of the stage of melodrama. But she was out of it. She was growing up now to a subtler wisdom. People, she was beginning to realise, do not do these simple things. They make vows of devotion and they are not real vows of devotion; they love--quite honestly--and qualify. There are no great revenges but only little mean ones; no life-long vindications except the unrelenting vengeance of the law. There is no real concentration of people's lives anywhere such as romance demands. There is change, there is forgetfulness. Everywhere there is dispersal. Even to the tragic story of Teddy would come the modifications of time. Even to the wickedness of the German princes would presently be added some conflicting aspects. Could Letty keep things for years in her mind, hard and terrible, as they were now? Surely they would soften; other things would overlay them....

text: A wave of memories washed over her, recalling Letty in countless schoolgirl escapades—moments of bold ambition and inevitable collapse. She saw Letty fearful, Letty irritated, and Letty throwing herself into grand designs, soaring with impressive vigor for a while before falling short. Cissie had seen her sister tearful and unkempt, broken by shame and humiliation. She knew Letty’s very essence. Poor Letty! Poor, dear Letty! With a sudden, piercing insight, Cissie understood what was transpiring in her sister’s thoughts. This frantic plotting for retribution was merely a mental game Letty played to shield herself from the crushing alternative to her hopes; it was a symptom of fragility, not of power. It was a subtle form of collapse. She simply could not look the cold fact of Teddy’s death in the eye. It was more than she could endure. Thus, she was crafting this fantasy of a vengeful mission to prepare for the moment she could no longer deny the truth. She was already half-convinced, though she would not fully yield until her dream-world was complete. If this state of limbo continued, she might solidify this delusion so thoroughly that it would eventually consume her mind. By that point, she would have also reconciled her life at Matching’s Easy with the elaborate fabric of her obsession.
source_text: There came a rush of memories of Letty in a dozen schoolgirl adventures, times when she had ventured, and times when she had failed; Letty frightened, Letty vexed, Letty launching out to great enterprises, going high and hard and well for a time, and then failing. She had seen Letty snivelling and dirty; Letty shamed and humiliated. She knew her Letty to the soul. Poor Letty! Poor dear Letty! With a sudden clearness of vision Cissie realised what was happening in her sister's mind. All this tense scheming of revenges was the imaginative play with which Letty warded off the black alternative to her hope; it was not strength, it was weakness. It was a form of giving way. She could not face starkly the simple fact of Teddy's death. That was too much for her. So she was building up this dream of a mission of judgment against the day when she could resist the facts no longer. She was already persuaded, only she would not be persuaded until her dream was ready. If this state of suspense went on she might establish her dream so firmly that it would at last take complete possession of her mind. And by that time also she would have squared her existence at Matching's Easy with the elaboration of her reverie.

text: She drifted through the house, feigning preparation for that monumental labor she was destined never to perform; she would pore over topographies of the Ruhr; she would scour the gazettes for word of foreign chancellors and princes; she might even make a listless attempt to find a post in Lucerne or Hamburg. Perhaps she would purchase a dagger or a pistol. Eventually, once the treaty was signed and the Continental kinsmen returned for their holidays, she would begin to haunt the perimeters of Balmoral or the Long Walk, a silent shadow at the gates.
source_text: She would go about the place then, fancying herself preparing for this tremendous task she would never really do; she would study German maps; she would read the papers about German statesmen and rulers; perhaps she would even make weak attempts to obtain a situation in Switzerland or in Germany. Perhaps she would buy a knife or a revolver. Perhaps presently she would begin to hover about Windsor or Sandringham when peace was made, and the German cousins came visiting again....

text: Furthermore, she sensed he was a truly fine man; he was so gentle and dependable, possessing a deliberate strength and a scrupulous integrity. There was a vast, innocent simplicity about him, joined with a profound commitment to justice. He was entirely vulnerable in her presence, and she could wound or trouble him as she pleased. Yet she could never hope to alter his deeply rooted beliefs.
source_text: Withal, as she felt and perceived, he was such a good thing, such a very good thing; so kind, so trustworthy, with a sort of slow strength, with a careful honesty, a big good childishness, a passion for fairness. And so helpless in her hands. She could lash him and distress him. Yet she could not shake his slowly formed convictions.

text: He arrived at Matching’s Easy after she summoned him to provide his definitive account of Teddy’s fate, wearing an outfit he didn't usually wear. In his own mind, the attire was so magnificent that it seemed to scream, resonating like a trumpet blast as he traveled through London to Liverpool Street station; it felt like a garment of international significance, a look he believed would echo all the way to Berlin. Yet it was actually so ordinary, so typical of what everyone wore then, that Cissie hardly noticed it when she met him at the station, her mind heavy with Letty’s suffering. She felt rather than saw that he looked more vigorous and handsome than he had since he first caught her attention in that eccentric cloak Teddy had found for him during those happy days before death had touched their world. Letty, too, remained distant and unpredictable, seeing nothing special in the suit he found so remarkable.
source_text: When he came down to Matching's Easy at her summons to bear his convincing witness of Teddy's fate, he came in an unwonted costume. It was a costume so wonderful in his imagination that it seemed to cry aloud, to sound like a trumpet as he went through London to Liverpool Street station; it was a costume like an international event; it was a costume that he felt would blare right away to Berlin. And yet it was a costume so commonplace, so much the usual wear now, that Cissie, meeting him at the station and full of the thought of Letty's trouble, did not remark it, felt indeed rather than observed that he was looking more strong and handsome than he had ever done since he struck upon her imagination in the fantastic wrap that Teddy had found for him in the merry days when there was no death in the world. And Letty too, resistant, incalculable, found no wonder in the wonderful suit.

text: Elena stepped into the corridor with a stony countenance and a gaze that perceived nothing, yet it felt as though a crushing mantle had been stripped from her. It was finished; there was no further room for expectation or dread. She would have been revolted to acknowledge that her spirit was, at last, unburdened.
source_text: She walked out with a set face and eyes that seemed unseeing, and yet it was as if some heavy weight had been lifted from her shoulders. It was over; there was no more to hope for and there was nothing more to fear. She would have been shocked to realise that her mind was relieved.

text: She yearned for solitude, needing to escape the scrutiny of others. She felt like a creature that, after a long and nightmarish gestation, had finally emerged into a stark, cold morning. It was necessary for her to inhabit her own skin and expand her consciousness within this joyless light—a new reality where Teddy was absent, and where neither retribution nor restitution for his loss would ever exist. Teddy was behind her now.
source_text: She wanted to be alone. She wanted to be away from every eye. She was like some creature that after a long nightmare incubation is at last born into a clear, bleak day. She had to feel herself; she had to stretch her mind in this cheerless sunshine, this new world, where there was to be no more Teddy and no real revenge nor compensation for Teddy. Teddy was past....

text: He fell silent for a while. Yet they drew strength from each other, fully aware of the mutual solace they provided. There was a natural sense of kinship and ease between them. Having been wounded by the same blow, they possessed an innate understanding of one another's pain. It was nothing like the empty consolation offered by those who had never known such profound love or terror.
source_text: He said no more for a time. But the two of them comforted one another, and knew that they comforted each other. They had a common feeling of fellowship and ease. They had been stricken by the same thing; they understood how it was with each other. It was not like the attempted comfort they got from those who had not loved and dreaded....

text: I never intended to portray him as anything beyond a mere man. I was frequently provoked by him, and just as often disillusioned. He was possessed of a thousand frailties, all of which were visible to our circle. Yet we did not resent them; rather, they served to anchor our devotion. And that peculiar, erratic brilliance! That bottomless well of sagacity. Then there was the exquisite, fragile architecture of his mind—the vivid recollections held within that beloved head, his fleeting vagaries, and those spontaneous sparks of invention....
source_text: "I don't make out he wasn't human. Often and often I have been angry with him, and disappointed in him. There were all sorts of weaknesses in him. We all knew them. And we didn't mind them. We loved him the better. And his odd queer cleverness!.... And his profound wisdom. And then all this beautiful and delicate fabric, all those clear memories in his dear brain, all his whims, his sudden inventions....

text: But we need it to arrive swiftly. The world has grown exhausted by this slaughter, tired of the constant grieving, the squandering of our riches, and the loss of our sons and our beloveds. We crave it soon, and to achieve it quickly, we must labor to make it so. We must offer up our lives—or whatever remains of them.
source_text: "Only we want it soon. The world is weary of this bloodshed, weary of all this weeping, of this wasting of substance and this killing of sons and lovers. We want it soon, and to have it soon we must work to bring it about. We must give our lives. What is left of our lives....

text: No," Mr. Britling insisted. "That is a question for the theologians to resolve. They have been far too reckless with their descriptions of the Divine, entertaining foolish, absolute notions—that He is all-powerful, that He is everything at once. But human reason knows better. Every genuine religious impulse contradicts it. In truth, the actual God of the Christians is Christ, not an abstract Almighty; a humiliated and wounded God fixed to a cross of physical matter. One day, He will surely prevail. But it is unjust to claim He orchestrates everything now, or to frame a moral case against Him. You have been deceived by the errors of the priesthood. God is not absolute; He is finite. A limited God who fights in His own vast, overarching way just as we fight in our own petty, fragile ways—a God who is *among* us—that is the heart of true religion. I am in complete agreement with you. For if I believed in an omnipotent God who observed these battles, these deaths, and the terrible waste of this war—capable of halting it, yet permitting it for His own sport—I would spit in His vacant face.
source_text: "No," said Mr. Britling. "It is the theologians who must answer that. They have been extravagant about God. They have had silly absolute ideas--that He is all powerful. That He's omni-everything. But the common sense of men knows better. Every real religious thought denies it. After all, the real God of the Christians is Christ, not God Almighty; a poor mocked and wounded God nailed on a cross of matter.... Some day He will triumph.... But it is not fair to say that He causes all things now. It is not fair to make out a case against him. You have been misled. It is a theologian's folly. God is not absolute; God is finite.... A finite God who struggles in his great and comprehensive way as we struggle in our weak and silly way--who is _with_ us--that is the essence of all real religion.... I agree with you so--Why! if I thought there was an omnipotent God who looked down on battles and deaths and all the waste and horror of this war--able to prevent these things--doing them to amuse Himself--I would spit in his empty face...."

text: I didn't either. But I do at this moment. It has finally revealed itself to me, and I see it with total clarity. I see it so vividly that I am stunned it wasn't obvious to me all along. You see, it becomes quite simple to believe in God—to see how intricate, magnificent, and compassionate He is—when you reflect on those young men who, by the thousands and hundreds of thousands, gave up their lives. Yes, and there were German boys who did exactly the same. The cruelty, the unfairness, the raw violence—they perceived it all differently. They sacrificed themselves; they gave everything they had. All those precious lives, so full of potential and light.
source_text: "Nor did I. But I do now.... I have suddenly found it and seen it plain. I see it so plain that I am amazed that I have not always seen it.... It is, you see, so easy to understand that there is a God, and how complex and wonderful and brotherly He is, when one thinks of those dear boys who by the thousand, by the hundred thousand, have laid down their lives.... Ay, and there were German boys too who did the same.... The cruelties, the injustice, the brute aggression--they saw it differently. They laid down their lives--they laid down their lives.... Those dear lives, those lives of hope and sunshine....

text: It is so clear to me," Mr. Britling said. "If the world held nothing more than our simple kindness toward one another, or the love that moved you to tears in this soft October sun, or the deep affection I have for Hugh—if everything else was stripped away and replaced by cruelty, scorn, and bitter filth—it would still be a certainty that a God of love and righteousness exists. Even if there were no other proof of the divine in this world but the goodness we found in our two boys, and if no other light guided us but the love we hold for each other...
source_text: "But it's so plain to me," said Mr. Britling. "If there was nothing else in all the world but our kindness for each other, or the love that made you weep in this kind October sunshine, or the love I bear Hugh--if there was nothing else at all--if everything else was cruelty and mockery and filthiness and bitterness, it would still be certain that there was a God of love and righteousness. If there were no signs of God in all the world but the godliness we have seen in those two boys of ours; if we had no other light but the love we have between us....

text: His getaway followed shortly thereafter. It had taken remarkably little time for him to break free. He had been confined within a Belgian railway station, held in a waiting area alongside several French captives, when Allied aircraft launched an assault on the junction. The explosion claimed the lives of their sentry and two of the other detainees. Amidst the ensuing turmoil, the remaining men managed to flee into the nearby streets. Wagons filled with hay were ablaze, and a nearby fuel depot was on the verge of igniting. "In such circumstances, flight was the only option. To be discovered roaming the area would have meant certain execution."
source_text: Then afterwards he had escaped. In quite a little time he had escaped. He had been in a railway station somewhere in Belgium; locked in a waiting-room with three or four French prisoners, and the junction had been bombed by French and British aeroplanes. Their guard and two of the prisoners had been killed. In the confusion the others had got away into the town. There were trucks of hay on fire, and a store of petrol was in danger. "After that one was bound to escape. One would have been shot if one had been found wandering about."

text: My life has returned with you!" Letty cried. "Up until this very day, I held onto the faith that you’d come back. But today—I lost heart. I thought the real world had ended, the love and the joy of it all, and that I had nothing left but to endure and guard your memory. Your poor arm. Your poor, dear arm. I imagined myself just being kind to people and pretending you were still alive somewhere. I won't mind about the arm for long. I’m glad you went, but I’m so much gladder that you’re home and can never leave me again. I shall be your right hand, my dear, and your left, and all the hands you need. Both of mine shall replace your lost one. You will have three hands now, instead of only two.
source_text: "Life's come back to me with you!" said Letty. "Until just to-day I've believed you'd come back. And to-day--I doubted.... I thought it was all over--all the real life, love and the dear fun of things, and that there was nothing before me, nothing before me but just holding out--and keeping your memory.... Poor arm. Poor arm. And being kind to people. And pretending you were alive somewhere.... I'll not care about the arm. In a little while.... I'm glad you've gone, but I'm gladder you're back and can never go again.... And I will be your right hand, dear, and your left hand and all your hands. Both my hands for your dear lost left one. You shall have three hands instead of two...."

text: The conversation had shifted to the battalions from the North who had marched up to replace the Kentish ranks following the skirmish in which Julian was taken. It soon grew apparent that Mr. Thorne was speaking of his own brothers-in-arms. "I am hardly the solitary American to have pledged himself to the Dominion—for the length of the struggle."
source_text: They had spoken about the Canadians who had come up and relieved the Essex men after the fight in which Teddy had been captured. And then it was manifest that Mr. Direck was talking of his regiment. "I'm not the only American who has gone Canadian--for the duration of the war."

text: The letters and photographs together served as a poignant reminder of how decent and amiable humanity could be. That is, until the savage impulses of nationalism begin their violent trampling, until paranoia and grasping selfishness infect the mind, and until the idiots with high explosives shatter that fundamental goodness into cries of rage and spatters of blood. People are remarkably gentle—right until the moment they become cruel. Suddenly, his head was filled with stories of human kindness defying the malice of war: accounts of wounded Germans and Englishmen helping each other while stranded in the dark mud between the lines, of the camaraderie shared between prisoners and their captors, and of the Saxons mingling with the British on Christmas Day. He remembered seeing the photographs of that scene in one of the daily papers.
source_text: The letters reinforced the photographs in their reminder how kind and pleasant a race mankind can be. Until the wild asses of nationalism came kicking and slaying amidst them, until suspicion and jostling greed and malignity poison their minds, until the fools with the high explosives blow that elemental goodness into shrieks of hate and splashes of blood. How kindly men are--up to the very instant of their cruelties! His mind teemed suddenly with little anecdotes and histories of the goodwill of men breaking through the ill-will of war, of the mutual help of sorely wounded Germans and English lying together in the mud and darkness between the trenches, of the fellowship of captors and prisoners, of the Saxons at Christmas fraternising with the English.... Of that he had seen photographs in one of the daily papers....

text: The Germany he addressed was destined to be a land of widows and orphans, of gaunt children and broken veterans, of the elderly and the destitute—men who had buried their kin, their friends, and their aspirations. No victory on land or at sea could prevent this outcome. France, Russia, and eventually Britain would share this fate, each to their own degree. Before the conflict, there was no Germany an Englishman could truly speak to; it had been nothing but a looming threat, a terrifying march of boots. Trying to reason with it then would have been as impossible as flagging down the Kaiser’s blaring motorcade on the Unter den Linden to request a quiet chat. But the nation that had viewed those early advances with a hesitant pride now had eyes filled with blood and tears. She had trusted and obeyed, yet no genuine triumph had arrived. Still, she continued to fight, bleeding and suffering, squandering her own riches and those of the entire world for no reason other than total depletion—so skilled she was, so loyal, so arrogant, and so profoundly misguided. And the German mind, whatever its character before the war, was now merely a survivor, something lingering by a reading lamp just as he sat by his, pondering, grieving, calculating the toll, and peering into a dark future.
source_text: The Germany to which he wrote would be a nation of widows and children, rather pinched boys and girls, crippled men, old men, deprived men, men who had lost brothers and cousins and friends and ambitions. No triumph now on land or sea could save Germany from becoming that. France too would be that, Russia, and lastly Britain, each in their degree. Before the war there had been no Germany to which an Englishman could appeal; Germany had been a threat, a menace, a terrible trampling of armed men. It was as little possible then to think of talking to Germany as it would have been to have stopped the Kaiser in mid career in his hooting car down the Unter den Linden and demand a quiet talk with him. But the Germany that had watched those rushes with a slightly doubting pride had her eyes now full of tears and blood. She had believed, she had obeyed, and no real victory had come. Still she fought on, bleeding, agonising, wasting her substance and the substance of the whole world, to no conceivable end but exhaustion, so capable she was, so devoted, so proud and utterly foolish. And the mind of Germany, whatever it was before the war, would now be something residual, something left over and sitting beside a reading-lamp as he was sitting beside a reading-lamp, thinking, sorrowing, counting the cost, looking into the dark future....

text: When I sat down to write to you, I intended only to speak of your son and mine. Yet I feel that the specifics of our shared grief need not be voiced; they are understood in the silence between us. What must be addressed, however, is the urgent necessity of ending all war, a task that rests solely on our shoulders and those of our fellow men. We owe this effort to the memory of our sons, to our heritage, and to everything that makes us human. War is no longer a human struggle; the chemist and the industrialist have stripped it of that. My boy was shot through the eye, his life extinguished by a man who never even knew what he had done. Consider the weight of that. It is clear to me, as it must be to you and the entire world, that modern conflict is now merely the lighting of a fuse that leads to total ruin. There is nothing else for one rational man to write to another about in these times but the salvation of humanity from the scourge of war.
source_text: _When I sat down to write to you I had meant only to write to you of your son and mine. But I feel that what can be said in particular of our loss, need not be said; it can be understood without saying. What needs to be said and written about is this, that war must be put an end to and that nobody else but you and me and all of us can do it. We have to do that for the love of our sons and our race and all that is human. War is no longer human; the chemist and the metallurgist have changed all that. My boy was shot through the eye; his brain was blown to pieces by some man who never knew what he had done. Think what that means!... It is plain to me, surely it is plain to you and all the world, that war is now a mere putting of the torch to explosives that flare out to universal ruin. There is nothing for one sane man to write to another about in these days but the salvation of mankind from war._

text: Beyond this point, Mr. Britling’s jottings became more scattered. They retained a certain sequence, yet they were no longer continuous. His thoughts had bounded across intervals that his hand lacked the time to record. He was starting to see that his letter to the elderly couple in Pomerania was becoming an impossibility. It had veered off into a formal academic discourse.
source_text: From this point onward Mr. Britling's notes became more fragmentary. They had a consecutiveness, but they were discontinuous. His thought had leapt across gaps that his pen had had no time to fill. And he had begun to realise that his letter to the old people in Pomerania was becoming impossible. It had broken away into dissertation.

text: Your son, as you must be aware, was perpetually dreaming of a global harmony similar to the one I am envisioning; he possessed a generosity of spirit that transcended his own nation. To him, war and enmity were simply the results of profound misunderstanding. He was convinced that a world capable of articulating its thoughts clearly would inevitably live in peace. This is why he was always strategizing to improve and spread a universal language, such as Esperanto or Ido, to serve as a common bond. My own child was driven by a related, perhaps even more expansive, ambition: the ideal of a unified human science, a pursuit that acknowledges no sovereign, no border, and no racial divide.
source_text: _Your boy, as no doubt you know, dreamt constantly of such a world peace as this that I foreshadow; he was more generous than his country. He could envisage war and hostility only as misunderstanding. He thought that a world that could explain itself clearly would surely be at peace. He was scheming always therefore for the perfection and propagation of Esperanto or Ido, or some such universal link. My youngster too was full of a kindred and yet larger dream, the dream of human science, which knows neither king nor country nor race_....

text: We must dedicate ourselves to a common duty. We must apply every faculty of the mind and every impulse of the soul to the refinement and realization of a true commonwealth, ensuring the final eclipse of the monarchs and the hierarchs and the bands of opportunists, the profiteers and the oligarchs who have lured humanity into this abyss of malice and slaughter—where our youth have vanished—and where we struggle blindly even now.
source_text: _"Let us pledge ourselves to service. Let us set ourselves with all our minds and all our hearts to the perfecting and working out of the methods of democracy and the ending for ever of the kings and emperors and priestcrafts and the bands of adventurers, the traders and owners and forestallers who have betrayed mankind into this morass of hate and blood--in which our sons are lost--in which we flounder still...."_
